\sum _ { k } A _ { k } ( x ) B _ { k } ( x ) = \sum _ { i } A _ { i } ^ { \prime } ( y ) B _ { i } ^ { \prime } ( y ) ;
$$

this last expression is seen to be an invariant.

Conversely, when $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { k } A _ { k } ( x ) C _ { k } ( x ) } \end{array}$ is invariant, $C _ { \kappa } ( \boldsymbol { x } )$ is a contragradient if $A _ { k } ( x )$ is a cogradient, and $C _ { \kappa } ( \boldsymbol { x } )$ is a cogradient if $A _ { k } ( x )$ is a contragradient.Eddington (1923) called this the quotient theorem. The proof is easy.From the relation

$$
\sum _ { k } A _ { k } ( x ) C _ { k } ( x ) = \sum _ { i } A _ { i } ^ { \prime } ( y ) C _ { i } ^ { \prime } ( y ) = \sum _ { k } \sum _ { j } { \frac { \partial \phi _ { k } } { \partial y _ { j } } } A _ { j } ^ { \prime } ( y ) C _ { k } ( x )
$$

we obtain

$$
\sum _ { i } A _ { i } ^ { \prime } ( y ) \left[ C _ { i } ^ { \prime } ( y ) - \sum _ { k } \frac { \partial \phi _ { k } } { \partial y _ { i } } C _ { k } ( x ) \right] = 0 .
$$

Hence, $C _ { i } ( { \boldsymbol { x } } )$ is a contragradient.

Next consider a quadratic differential form

$$
d s ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { i , j } ^ { 1 , . . . . , n } g _ { i j } d x _ { i } d x _ { j } , \qquad g _ { i j } = g _ { j i } .
$$

Let the result of substitution

$$
y _ { i } = f _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

be

$$
d s ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { i , j } ^ { 1 , \dotsc \dotsc } g _ { i , j } ^ { \prime } d y _ { i } d y _ { j } .
$$

Then

$$
d s ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { i , j } \sum _ { \alpha , \beta } g _ { i , j } ^ { \prime } { \frac { \partial f _ { i } } { \partial x _ { \alpha } } } { \frac { \partial f _ { j } } { \partial x _ { \beta } } } d x _ { \alpha } d x _ { \beta } = \sum _ { \alpha , \beta } \sum _ { i , j } g _ { \alpha , \beta } ^ { \prime } { \frac { \partial f _ { \alpha } } { \partial x _ { i } } } { \frac { \partial f _ { \beta } } { \partial x _ { j } } } d x _ { i } d x _ { j } ,
$$

and hence

$$
g _ { i j } = \sum _ { \alpha , \beta } g _ { \alpha , \beta } ^ { \prime } \frac { \partial f _ { \alpha } } { \partial x _ { i } } \frac { \partial f _ { \beta } } { \partial x _ { j } } \cdot
$$

Thus, $g _ { i j }$ is a contragradient. Denote by $g ^ { j i }$ the minor of $g _ { i j }$ in the determinant $\left\| g _ { i j } \right\|$ ; then

$$
\sum _ { j } g ^ { i j } g _ { j k } = \delta _ { i k } , \qquad g ^ { i j } = g ^ { j i } .
$$

By the quotient theorem, $g ^ { i j }$ is a cogradient.

Let

$$
T = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i , j } g _ { i j } \dot { q } _ { i } \dot { q } _ { j }
$$

in the Lagrangian function and let $Q _ { i }$ be a component of the external force. Then

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \sum _ { j } g _ { i j } \dot { q } _ { j } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { j , k } \frac { \partial g _ { j k } } { \partial q _ { i } } \dot { q } _ { j } \dot { q } _ { k } = Q _ { i } .
$$

If we write

$$
[ { ^ { j } } _ { i } { ^ { k } } ] = { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \left[ { \frac { \partial g _ { i j } } { \partial q _ { k } } } + { \frac { \partial g _ { i k } } { \partial q _ { j } } } - { \frac { \partial g _ { j k } } { \partial q _ { i } } } \right] ,
$$

which is Christoffel's symbol of the first kind, then

$$
\sum _ { j } g _ { i j } \ddot { q } _ { j } + \sum _ { j , k } [ { ^ j } _ { i } { ^ k } ] \dot { q } _ { j } \dot { q } _ { k } = Q _ { i } .
$$

We may also write

$$
\{ { \bf \ l } ^ { j } { } _ { i } { } ^ { k } \} = \sum _ { k } g ^ { h i } [ { \bf \ l } ^ { j } { } _ { h } { } ^ { k } ] ,
$$

which is Christoffel's symbol of the second kind.Then

$$
\ddot { q } _ { i } = - \sum _ { j , k } \{ { ^ j _ { i } } ^ { k } \} \dot { q } _ { j } \dot { q } _ { k } + Q ^ { i } ,
$$

where $\begin{array} { r } { Q ^ { i } = \sum _ { j } g ^ { i j } Q _ { j } } \end{array}$ . If the external force $Q _ { i }$ is zero,then (14) represents the geodesic (Ricci and Levi-Civita, 1900； Levi-Civita, 1923, 1925). We have

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { 2 } q _ { i } } { d s ^ { 2 } } } + \sum _ { j , k } \left\{ { \vphantom { \frac { d ^ { 2 } q _ { i } } { d s } } } ^ { j } \right\} { \frac { d q _ { j } } { d s } } { \frac { d q _ { k } } { d s } } = 0
$$

in the Riemannian space described by equations (13). If there exists an integral $F ( { \dot { q } } _ { 1 } , { \dot { q } } _ { 2 } , . . . , { \dot { q } } _ { n } ; q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , . . . , q _ { n } ; t ) =$ constant for (14), then

$$
{ \frac { \partial F } { \partial t } } + \sum _ { i } \left[ { \frac { \partial F } { \partial q _ { i } } } { \dot { q } } _ { i } - { \frac { \partial F } { \partial { \dot { q } } _ { i } } } \left( Q ^ { i } - \sum _ { j , k } \{ _ { i } ^ { j } { } ^ { k } \} { \dot { q } } _ { j } { \dot { q } } _ { k } \right) \right] = 0 .
$$

The problem of determining the $Q ^ { i }$ which satisfy this equation identically when $_ { \tau }$ is given,is called Bertrand's problem.

Take

$$
L = { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \sum _ { i , j } g _ { i j } { \dot { q } } _ { i } { \dot { q } } _ { j } - V ( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , \ldots , q _ { n } ) , \qquad \| g _ { i j } \| \neq 0 .
$$

Then, by our definition,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \displaystyle { \dot { p } _ { i } = \frac { \partial L } { \partial \dot { q } _ { i } } = \sum _ { j } g _ { i j } \dot { q } _ { j } } , } \end{array}
$$

or

$$
\dot { q } _ { j } = \sum _ { i } g ^ { j i } \dot { p } _ { i } .
$$

But

$$
\sum _ { i , j } g _ { i j } \dot { q } _ { i } \dot { q } _ { j } = \sum _ { i , j } \sum _ { \alpha , \beta } g _ { i j } g ^ { i \alpha } g ^ { j \beta } \phi _ { \alpha } \dot { p } _ { \beta } = \sum _ { \alpha , \beta } g ^ { \alpha \beta } \dot { p } _ { \alpha } \dot { p } _ { \beta } .
$$

Hence

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \cal H } = \sum _ { i } \hbar _ { i } \dot { q } _ { i } - { \cal L } = \sum _ { i , j } g _ { i j } \dot { q } _ { i } \dot { q } _ { j } - { \cal L } } } \\ { { \displaystyle = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i , j } g _ { i j } \dot { q } _ { i } \dot { q } _ { j } + V = { \cal T } + V = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { \alpha , \beta } g ^ { \alpha \beta } \dot { p } _ { \alpha } \dot { p } _ { \beta } + V . } } \end{array}
$$

The principle of least action of Maupertuis (Poincaré, l893)，when $H = h _ { ; }$ is

$$
\delta \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t _ { 1 } } \left( \sum _ { i } { \dot { q } } _ { i } { \frac { \partial L } { \partial { \dot { q } } _ { i } } } \right) d t = 0 ,
$$

or

$$
\ S \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t _ { 1 } } 2 T d t = 0 .
$$

We may eliminate $t$ from this by noting

$$
\left( { \frac { d s } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { i , j } g _ { i j } { \dot { q } } _ { i } { \dot { q } } _ { j } = 2 T ,
$$

that is, $d t = d s / \sqrt { 2 T }$ then

$$
\delta \int _ { s _ { 0 } } ^ { s _ { 1 } } \sqrt { 2 T } d s = 0 ,
$$

or, with $U = - V ,$

$$
\delta \int _ { s _ { 0 } } ^ { s _ { 1 } } \sqrt { 2 ( U + h ) } \ d s = 0 .
$$

The variable $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ is the arc length defined by

$$
d s ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { i , j } g _ { i j } d q _ { i } d q _ { j }
$$

in the $\pmb { n }$ -dimensional Riemannian space of equations (l3). If we fix $t _ { 0 }$ and $t _ { 1 }$ in this space, the extremal of the integral of $2 \sqrt { ( U + h ) }$ -which is a function of $q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , \ldots , q _ { n }$ over the arc length-represents the actual motion. This expression appears in Jacobi's lectures (Jacobi， 1884） on dynamics and is the foundation of the geometry of motion (Synge, 1926).

As a special case, consider the motion of a particle not subject to any external force. Then $U = 0$ and the variational principle is $\delta \int d s = 0  _ { ; }$ that is,the geodesic line of the shortest arc length on the Riemannian space defined by the above $d s ^ { 2 }$ .However, this theorem holds only in limited intervals； for example, the arc length larger than $\pmb { \pi }$ of a great circle on a spherical surface is not the shortest distance.The use of a minimum is limited to the comparison among all nearby curves.Let $B$ be the pole of a point $A$ (Figure 4).The great circles between $\boldsymbol { A }$ and $B$ are all of the same arc length. That is,

$$
A \alpha B C = A \beta B + B C = A \beta + \beta B + B C .
$$

Let $\beta$ be a nearby point of $B$ and draw a great circle $\beta C .$ Now

$$
\beta C < \beta B + B C .
$$

Hence, $A \beta + \beta C <$ the great circle $A \alpha B C$ ，Thus, there occur two cases when we attempt to draw the shortest paths in each direction from a point on the surface.

![](images/797a518d1f3a5c58207cdbf9d0d3e7da4cd8d2be988153225b7fac827a8577ff.jpg)

![](images/47d349e3f8fa67d551f107137f91c20f4db1acc9bb4a9c3560dbf984cde8d737.jpg)  
FIGURE 5

Two nearby shortest curves either remain close to each other but never intersect,or they do intersect；in the latter case the shortest curves have an envelope. The first case occurs on a developable surface or on a concave-convex surface-for example,on a hyperboloid of one sheet or on a hyperbolic paraboloid.The second case occurs, for instance, on an ellipsoid of revolution.In cases of the latter kind, the shortest curves starting from the same point on a surface do not intersect in the same way as do those on the surface of a sphere. They form an envelope around the pole of the point. It looks as though there were no shortest curve inside the envelope. But there is at least a curve something like EFG of Figure 5. The curve EFG does not represent the shortest curve beyond the point $F$ (Jacobi, l884).The works of Hadamard, Poincaré, and Birkhoff in the topological study of a dynamical system discuss the principle of least action geometrically by geodesic lines.

Now put

$$
d \tau = U \frac { d s } { \sqrt { 2 T } } , \mathrm { o r } d s = \frac { d \tau \sqrt { 2 T } } { U } , \mathrm { o r } d \tau = U d t .
$$

Then the principle of the least action takes the form

$$
\delta \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t _ { 1 } } \sqrt { 2 ( U + h ) T } d t = 0 ,
$$

or

$$
\delta \int _ { \tau _ { 0 } } ^ { \tau _ { 1 } } \sqrt { 2 \Bigl ( 1 + \frac { h } { U } \Bigr ) \frac { T } { U } } d \tau = 0 .
$$

Write

$$
\mathfrak { H } = 1 , \qquad \mathfrak { U } = \frac { h } { U } , \qquad \mathfrak { T } = \frac { T } { U } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i , j } U g _ { i j } \frac { d q _ { i } } { d \tau } \frac { d q _ { j } } { d \tau } .
$$

This is the case in which we take $\tau$ in place of $t .$ The variable $\boldsymbol { \tau }$ is that employed by Sundman for the regularization of the three-body problem. The Lagrangian function is now ${ \mathfrak { L } } = { \mathfrak { T } } + { \mathfrak { U } }$ . If we put

$$
r _ { i } = \frac { \partial \mathfrak { L } } { \partial ( d q _ { i } / d \tau ) } ,
$$

Then we may write

$$
{ \mathfrak { H } } = { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \sum _ { i , j } U g _ { i j } { \frac { d q _ { i } } { d \tau } } { \frac { d q _ { j } } { d \tau } } - { \frac { h } { U } } = { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \sum _ { i , j } { \frac { g ^ { i j } } { U } } r _ { i } r _ { j } - { \frac { h } { U } } ,
$$

and the Hamiltonian equations become

$$
\frac { d q _ { i } } { d \tau } = \frac { \partial \mathcal { S } } { \partial r _ { i } } , \frac { d r _ { i } } { d \tau } = - \frac { \partial \mathcal { S } } { \partial q _ { i } } .
$$

Here, $\mathfrak { H } = 1$ .Suppose that $V$ is a homogeneous form of degree $- 1$ in $q$ ,and that $g ^ { i j }$ is homogeneous of degree zero in $q$ .This is the case of the three-body problem. Then,

$$
\sum _ { i } q _ { i } \frac { \partial \mathfrak { H } } { \partial q _ { i } } = \mathfrak { H } = 1 , \qquad \sum _ { i } r _ { i } \frac { \partial \mathfrak { H } } { \partial r _ { i } } = 2 \mathfrak { H } = 2 .
$$

From the Hamiltonian equations we obtain

$$
\sum _ { i } q _ { i } { \frac { d r _ { i } } { d \tau } } = - 1 , \sum _ { i } r _ { i } { \frac { d q _ { i } } { d \tau } } = 2 .
$$

Hence, by integrating $d ( \sum _ { i } q _ { i } r _ { i } ) / d \tau = 1$ ，we obtain

$$
U \cdot \sum _ { i , j } g _ { i j } q _ { i } { \frac { d q _ { j } } { d \tau } } = \tau - \tau _ { 0 } ,
$$

or

$$
\sum _ { i , j } g _ { i j } q _ { i } { \frac { d q _ { j } } { d t } } = \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t } U d t .
$$

This formula has been deduced by Dziobek (1900). Rewriting this, we put

$$
\sum _ { i } ^ { } { p _ { i } q _ { i } } = \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t } { U d t } .
$$

This expression has been obtained by Wintner (1936).

When $L$ contains linear terms in $\dot { q } _ { i }$ ,that is,in the case of an irreversible dynamical system,we write

$$
L = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i , j } g _ { i j } \dot { q } _ { i } \dot { q } _ { j } + \sum _ { i } a _ { i } \dot { q } _ { i } - V ( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , . . . , q _ { n } ) ,
$$

and assume that $g _ { i j }$ and $a _ { i }$ are functions of $q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , \ldots , q _ { n } .$ . Then the energy integral becomes

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \cal H } = \sum _ { i } \rlap / { P } _ { i } \dot { q } _ { i } - { \cal L } = \sum _ { i } \rlap / { \partial } \frac { \partial { \cal L } } { \partial \dot { q } _ { i } } \dot { q } _ { i } - { \cal L } } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i , j } g _ { i j } \dot { q } _ { i } q _ { j } + V ( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , . . . , q _ { n } ) = h , } } \end{array}
$$

and we see that the linear terms in $\dot { q } _ { i }$ do not contribute to the energy; that is,the linear terms represent only the Coriolis force. In such a case,

and

$$
{ \frac { \partial L } { \partial { \dot { q } } _ { i } } } = { \dot { p } } _ { i } = \sum _ { j } g _ { i j } { \dot { q } } _ { j } + a _ { i } , \qquad { \dot { q } } _ { j } = \sum _ { k } \left( { \dot { p } } _ { k } - a _ { k } \right) g ^ { k j } ,
$$

$$
{ \cal L } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { \alpha , \beta } g ^ { \alpha \beta } \phi _ { \alpha } \phi _ { \beta } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { \alpha , \beta } g ^ { \alpha \beta } a _ { \alpha } a _ { \beta } - V ,
$$

$$
H = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { \alpha , \beta } g ^ { \alpha \beta } p _ { \alpha } \phi _ { \beta } - \sum _ { i } a ^ { i } p _ { i } + V + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { \alpha , \beta } g ^ { \alpha \beta } a _ { \alpha } a _ { \beta } ,
$$

$$
T = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i , j } g _ { i j } \dot { q } _ { i } \dot { q } _ { j } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { \alpha , \beta } g ^ { \alpha \beta } ( \beta _ { \alpha } - a _ { \alpha } ) ( \dot { p } _ { \beta } - a _ { \beta } ) ,
$$

where

$$
\sum _ { i } a _ { i } g ^ { i k } = a ^ { k } .
$$

# 1.5GEOMETRY OF MOTION

The principle of least action in $\pmb { n }$ -dimensional Riemannian space (15) has been expressed in the form (l6).A problem of dynamics is thus reduced to the study of the trajectories in the space (l5)by the variational principle (16). Synge (l926) called the line element $d s$ given by (15) the kinematical line element.The line element $d s$ determined by the variational principle (17)

$$
d s ^ { 2 } = 2 ( h - V ) T , d t ^ { 2 } = ( h - V ) \sum _ { i , j } g _ { i j } d q _ { i } d q _ { j }
$$

is called the action line element. We can also consider the $( n + 1 )$ -dimensional Riemannian space

$$
d s ^ { 2 } = 2 L d t ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { i , j } g _ { i j } d q _ { i } d q _ { j } - 2 V d t ^ { 2 }
$$

and the $( n + 1 )$ -dimensional Finsler space (Cartan, 1934). Any problem of dynamics can be related to suitably chosen curves.

Consider a kinematical space defined by (l5). Let $C$ and $C ^ { * }$ be two curves touching mutually at some point $o$ ,and $P$ and $P ^ { * }$ be two points on each of these curves such that $O P = O P ^ { * } = s .$ The expression

$$
\kappa ( C , C ^ { * } ) = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { s \to 0 } 2 { \frac { \overline { { { P P ^ { * } } } } } { s ^ { 2 } } }
$$

is called the relative curvature of $C$ and $C ^ { * }$ ，where $\overbrace { P P ^ { * } }$ is the shortest distance between the two points. If $C ^ { * }$ is a geodesic line, then $\pmb { \kappa }$ gives the curvature of $C$ Put

$$
\kappa _ { i } = \frac { d ^ { 2 } q _ { i } } { d s ^ { 2 } } + \sum _ { j , k } \binom { j } { i } \frac { d q _ { j } } { d s } \frac { d q _ { k } } { d s } ;
$$

then $\kappa _ { i }$ indicates the direction of the principal normal of the curve, and

$$
\kappa ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { i , j } g _ { i j } \kappa _ { i } \kappa _ { j } .
$$

The relative curvature, if we define $\kappa ^ { * }$ similarly with respect to $C ^ { * }$ ,is

$$
[ \kappa ( C , C ^ { * } ) ] ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { i , j } g _ { i j } ( \kappa _ { i } - \kappa _ { i } ^ { * } ) ( \kappa _ { j } - \kappa _ { j } ^ { * } ) .
$$

The velocity vector $v _ { i } = d q _ { i } / d t$ is represented by

$$
v = \biggl ( \sum _ { i , j } g _ { i j } { \frac { d q _ { i } } { d t } } { \frac { d q _ { j } } { d t } } \biggr ) ^ { 1 / 2 } = { \frac { d s } { d t } } = \sqrt { 2 T } .
$$

Define

$$
X _ { \mathfrak { i } } = { \frac { d X _ { \mathfrak { i } } } { d t } } + \sum _ { \mathfrak { j } , \mathfrak { k } } \{ ^ { { \mathfrak { j } } } { } _ { \mathfrak { i } } ^ { \ { \mathfrak { k } } } \} X _ { \mathfrak { j } } { \frac { d q _ { \mathfrak { k } } } { d t } }
$$

as the covariant derivative of $X _ { i }$ ; then,

$$
f _ { i } = v _ { \boldsymbol { I } i } = \frac { d v _ { i } } { d t } + \sum _ { j , k } \{ { ^ j } _ { i } ^ { k } \} v _ { j } v _ { k } = \frac { d ^ { 2 } q _ { i } } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \sum _ { j , k } \{ { ^ j } _ { i } ^ { k } \} \frac { d q _ { j } } { d t } \frac { d q _ { k } } { d t } ,
$$

or

$$
f _ { i } = \frac { d ^ { 2 } s } { d t ^ { 2 } } \frac { d q _ { i } } { d s } + \Big ( \frac { d s } { d t } \Big ) ^ { 2 } \kappa _ { i } ,
$$

that is, the acceleration vector is on the same plane as the tangent $d q _ { i } / d s$ and the principal normal $\kappa _ { i } ,$ and is expressed by

$$
f = \biggl ( \sum _ { i , j } g _ { i j } f _ { i } f _ { j } \biggr ) ^ { 1 / 2 } ,
$$

or by

$$
f _ { i } = \frac { d T } { d s } \frac { d q _ { i } } { d s } + 2 \kappa _ { i } T ,
$$

since $d s = { \sqrt { 2 T } } \cdot d t$ 、The equations of motion $f _ { i } = Q _ { i }$ are linear with respect to $_ { T }$ and $Q _ { i }$ Hence,if several external forces act on a holonomic dynamical system simultaneously, the kinetic energy of the system equals the sum of kinetic energies for the dynamical system acted on by each single external force separately.This is called Bonnet’s theorem (Bianchi, 1899, 1922).

If the trajectories in question are geodesics, then $\kappa _ { i } = 0$ ，and the vector $f _ { i }$ lies in the tangential direction.Hence, if $Q _ { i }$ has a component only in the direction tangential to the trajectory, then and only then does the motion take place on a geodesic.

Let $C ^ { * }$ be the actual trajectory. Let $C$ and $C ^ { * }$ be in contact at a point $P .$ Then

$$
4 T ^ { 2 } ( \kappa _ { i } - \kappa _ { i } ^ { * } ) = 2 T ( f _ { i } - f _ { i } ^ { * } ) - \left( \frac { d T } { d t } - \frac { d T ^ { * } } { d t } \right) \frac { d q _ { i } } { d t } .
$$

By substituting in the expression for $\kappa ( C , C ^ { * } )$ we obtain

$$
8 T ^ { 3 } [ \kappa ( C , C ^ { * } ) ] ^ { 2 } = 2 T \sum _ { j , k } g _ { j k } ( f _ { j } - Q _ { j } ) ( f _ { k } - Q _ { k } ) ,
$$

since

$$
f _ { i } ^ { * } = Q _ { i } , \frac { d T } { d t } = \sum _ { j } \frac { d q _ { j } } { d t } Q _ { j } .
$$

Consider three curves, all with the same velocity vector at a point $\scriptstyle { R }$ and all touching at that point.Let the frst curve be an arbitrary tra-jectory $C$ satisfying the condition of constraint, let the second be the actual trajectory $C ^ { * }$ satisfying the condition of constraint,and let the third be the actual trajectory $C ^ { * * }$ in the case of no constraint. The condition for conservation of energy,

$$
\frac { d T } { d t } = \sum _ { j } Q _ { j } \frac { d q _ { j } } { d t } ,
$$

is satisfied for $C ^ { * }$ . Let the equations of motion be

$$
f _ { i } ^ { * } = Q _ { i } + P _ { i }
$$

and the condition of constraint be

$$
\sum _ { i } { P _ { i } } \delta q _ { i } = 0 .
$$

Then

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \sum _ { j , k } g _ { j k } f _ { j } ^ { * } \delta q _ { k } = \sum _ { j , k } g _ { j k } ( Q _ { j } + P _ { j } ) \ \delta q _ { k } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad = \sum _ { j , k } g _ { j k } Q _ { j } \delta q _ { k } . } } \end{array}
$$

Denote an arbitrary infinitesimal displacement by $\delta \tau$ and let $\delta { { q } _ { i } } = \left( { { f } _ { i } } - { { f } _ { i } } ^ { * } \right) \delta \tau$ satisfy the condition of constraint; then

$$
\sum _ { j , k } g _ { j k } f _ { j } ^ { * } ( f _ { k } - f _ { k } ^ { * } ) = \sum _ { j , k } g _ { j k } Q _ { j } ( f _ { k } - f _ { k } ^ { * } ) .
$$

Thus

$$
\begin{array} { r l } {  { \mathbb { B } T ^ { 3 } \{ [ \kappa ( C , C ^ { + * } ) ] ^ { 2 } - [ \kappa ( C ^ { * } , C ^ { * * } ) ] ^ { 2 } \} } } \\ & { = 2 T \sum _ { j , k } \beta _ { k } [ f _ { j , \ell } - f _ { j } ^ { * } f _ { k } ^ { * } - 2 ( f _ { j } - f _ { j } ^ { * } ) Q _ { k } ] } \\ & { \quad - ( \frac { d T } { d t } - \sum _ { j , k } g _ { \mathcal { M } } Q _ { j } \frac { d q _ { k } } { d t } ) ^ { 2 } } \\ & { = 2 T \sum _ { j , k } g _ { \mathcal { M } } ( f _ { j } - f _ { j } ^ { * * } ) ( f _ { k } - f _ { k } ^ { * * } ) } \\ & { \quad - ( \frac { d T } { d t } - \sum _ { j , k } g _ { \mathcal { M } } Q _ { \mathcal { M } } \frac { d q _ { k } } { d t } ) ^ { 2 } . } \end{array}
$$

If the conservation of energy is satisfied also for $C .$ ，the last term vanishes and the right-hand member is positive.Hence

$$
\kappa ( C , C ^ { * } ) > \kappa ( C ^ { * } , C ^ { * * } ) .
$$

Thus,if the constraint is imposed on a holonomic dynamical system, the actual trajectory has the smallest curvature of all curves with the same tangent that satisfy the condition of constraint. This is called the principle of smallest curvature.

# 1.6EQUIVALENCE OF DYNAMICAL SYSTEMS

Consider two dynamical systems represented, respectively, by the following Lagrangian equations

$$
{ \frac { d } { d t } } \left( { \frac { \partial T } { \partial { \dot { q } } _ { i } } } \right) - { \frac { \partial T } { \partial q _ { i } } } = Q _ { i } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } ^ { * } } { \partial \dot { \mathfrak { q } } _ { i } } \right) - \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial \mathfrak { q } _ { i } } = \mathfrak { D } _ { i } ^ { * } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

If these two equations can be brought to the same form by the transformation

$$
\mathfrak { q } _ { i } = \phi _ { i } ( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , . . . , q _ { n } ) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

then the two dynamical systems are said to be equivalent according to Stackel (1891).

Suppose that the tensors $g _ { i j }$ and $\mathfrak { g } _ { i j } ^ { * }$ in

$$
T = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i , j } g _ { i j } \dot { q } _ { i } \dot { q } _ { j } , \qquad \mathfrak { T } ^ { * } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i , j } \mathfrak { g } _ { i j } ^ { * } \dot { \mathfrak { q } } _ { i } \dot { \mathfrak { q } } _ { j }
$$

do not depend on $t$ but only on $q$ and $\mathfrak { q }$ ,respectively. Let

$$
d s ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { i , j } g _ { i j } d q _ { i } d q _ { j } , \qquad d \hat { s } ^ { * 2 } = \sum _ { i , j } \mathfrak { g } _ { i j } ^ { * } d { \mathfrak { q } } _ { i } d { \mathfrak { q } } _ { j } ,
$$

and the result of substitution of ${ \mathfrak { q } } _ { i } = \phi _ { i } ( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , \dotsc , q _ { n } )$ in $\mathfrak { T } ^ { * }$ and $\mathfrak { Q } ^ { * }$ be $\mathfrak { T } , \mathfrak { Q }$ , respectively, and denote them by

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \mathfrak { T } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { j , k } \mathfrak { g } _ { j k } \dot { q } _ { j } \dot { q } _ { k } } , } \\ { { \displaystyle \mathfrak { Q } _ { i } = \sum _ { j } \mathfrak { Q } _ { j } ^ { * } \frac { \partial \phi _ { j } } { \partial q _ { i } } , \qquad d \hat { \mathfrak { s } } ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { j , k } \mathfrak { g } _ { j k } d q _ { j } d q _ { k } ; } } \end{array}
$$

then the Lagrangian equations take the form

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { \displaystyle { \ddot { q } } _ { \rho } + \sum _ { \lambda , \mu } \{ { ^ \lambda } { _ \rho } ^ { \mu } \} { \dot { q } } _ { \lambda } { \dot { q } } _ { \mu } = Q ^ { \rho } , ~ } } & { { ~ Q ^ { \rho } = \displaystyle \sum _ { \sigma } g ^ { \rho \sigma } Q _ { \sigma } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \ddot { q } } _ { \rho } + \sum _ { \lambda , \mu } \{ { ^ \lambda } { _ \rho } ^ { \mu } \} ^ { * } { \dot { q } } _ { \lambda } { \dot { q } } _ { \mu } = \mathfrak { Q } ^ { \rho } , ~ } } & { { ~ \mathfrak { Q } ^ { \rho } = \displaystyle \sum _ { \sigma } \mathfrak { g } ^ { \rho \sigma } \mathfrak { Q } _ { \sigma } , } } \end{array}
$$

Here $\{ ^ { \lambda } { } _ { \rho } { } ^ { \mu } \} ^ { * }$ is the Christoffel symbol for $d \hat { \mathfrak { s } } ^ { 2 }$ .In order that the two dynamical systems be equivalent we should have

$$
\{ { \^ { \lambda } } _ { \rho } { ^ { \mu } } \} = \{ { ^ { \lambda } } _ { \rho } { ^ { \mu } } \} ^ { * } , \quad \quad Q ^ { \rho } = \mathfrak { D } ^ { \rho } \quad \quad ( \lambda , \mu , \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

These equations are satisfied by ${ \mathfrak { g } } _ { i k } = c g _ { i k }$ $\overset { \cdot } { \underset { \cdot } { c } } =$ constant) and

$$
{ \mathfrak { Q } } _ { \rho } = \sum _ { \lambda } { \mathfrak { g } } _ { \rho \lambda } { \cal Q } ^ { \lambda } = \sum _ { \lambda } { \mathfrak { g } } _ { \rho \lambda } \sum _ { \mu } { g } ^ { \lambda \mu } { \cal Q } _ { \mu } = c { \cal Q } _ { \rho } .
$$

These are the conditions for the identity of the two dynamical systems in space and in time. However, if we consider only the orbital curves, the condition for the equivalence of two dynamical systems is different.

Put $\dot { \mathfrak { q } } _ { i } = d \mathfrak { q } _ { i } / d \tau$ in (18) and (19). Then (19) becomes

$$
\frac { d } { d \tau } \left( \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial \dot { q } _ { i } ^ { * } } \right) - \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial q _ { i } } = \mathfrak { T } _ { i } , \qquad \dot { q } _ { i } ^ { * } = \frac { d q _ { i } } { d \tau } ,
$$

$$
\mathfrak { T } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \frac { d \hat { \mathfrak { s } } } { d \tau } \right) ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { \lambda , \mu } \mathfrak { g } _ { \lambda \mu } \dot { q } _ { \lambda } ^ { * } \dot { q } _ { \mu } ^ { * } , \qquad \mathfrak { Q } _ { i } = \mathfrak { Q } _ { i } ( q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { n } ) .
$$

Let

$$
d t = \psi ( s ) d s , d \tau = \chi ( \hat { s } ) d \hat { s } .
$$

Let the arc length and the time correspond in the two equivalent orbital curves (Appell,1892). Then

$$
d \tau = \frac { d t } { f ( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , . . . , q _ { n } , \dot { q } _ { 1 } , \dot { q } _ { 2 } , . . . , \dot { q } _ { n } ) } \cdot
$$

This is a transformation of time. The simplest such transformation is $d \tau = d t / { \sqrt { c } } .$ In particular,if $c = - 1$ ，then $d \tau = - i d t$ ； such motions are called conjugate. Put $d \hat { \mathfrak { s } } ^ { 2 } = C d \mathfrak { s } ^ { 2 }$ ， $\mathfrak { H } _ { i } = c Q _ { i }$ ; then $d \tau = { \sqrt { C / c } } d t$ and is what Painlevé (1894) called the ordinary correspondence.When

$$
Q _ { i } = - { \frac { V ( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , . . . , q _ { n } ) } { q _ { i } } } , \qquad T + V = h ,
$$

then the orbit is a geodesic line with arc length $d s ^ { * } = \sqrt { ( h - V ) } d s .$ Denote the corresponding quantities of the equivalent systems $( d \mathfrak { s } , \mathfrak { B } )$ and $( d s , V )$ by

$$
d \mathfrak { s } = \sqrt { \propto V + \beta } \ d \mathfrak { s } , \mathfrak { B } = \frac { \gamma V + \delta } { \alpha V + \beta } ,
$$

or

$$
h = - \frac { \beta \mathfrak { h } - \delta } { \alpha \mathfrak { h } - \gamma } ;
$$

then

$$
d \hat { \mathfrak { s } } ^ { * } = 2 ( \mathfrak { h } - \mathfrak { B } ) d \hat { \mathfrak { s } } = \sqrt { \gamma - { \alpha } \mathfrak { h } } d { s } ^ { * } .
$$

This Darboux's transformation expresses the ordinary correspondence

between $( d s , V )$ and $( d \mathfrak { s } , \mathfrak { s } )$ in those cases where the potential exists (Prange, 1935).

When there is no external force, the function $f$ in the transformation of time is

$$
f = C \left( { \frac { G } { \mathfrak { G } } } \right) ^ { 1 / ( n + 1 ) } ,
$$

where $G$ and $\mathfrak { G }$ are the determinants of $g$ and $\mathfrak { g }$ ,respectively. Then the condition for the correspondence between $d s ^ { 2 }$ and $d \hat { \mathfrak { s } } ^ { 2 }$ is according to Levi-Civita (1896,1936),and Datzeff (1936)

$$
\{ \bar { \tau } _ { j } \bar { s } \} ^ { * } = \{ \bar { r } _ { j } \bar { s } \} , \qquad \{ \bar { r } _ { \bar { r } } \bar { s } \} ^ { * } = \{ \bar { r } _ { \bar { r } } \bar { s } \} - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \partial \log f } { \partial q _ { s } } ,
$$

$$
\{ { \stackrel { r } { r } } { } ^ { r } \} ^ { * } = \{ { \stackrel { r } { r } } { } ^ { r } \} = \frac { \partial \log f } { \partial q _ { r } } .
$$

We have defined the covariant derivative of $X _ { i }$ to be

$$
X _ { I i } = \frac { d X _ { i } } { d t } + \sum _ { i , k } \{ ^ { j } { } _ { i } { } ^ { k } \} X _ { j } \frac { d q _ { k } } { d t } .
$$

Substituting in $d s ^ { 2 }$ the covariant derivative of $\int ^ { 2 } { \mathfrak { g } } _ { \lambda \mu }$ ，we obtain

$$
( f ^ { 2 } \mathfrak { g } _ { r s } ) _ { I t } + ( f ^ { 2 } \mathfrak { g } _ { s t } ) _ { I r } + ( f ^ { 2 } \mathfrak { g } _ { t r } ) _ { I s } = 0 .
$$

The integral for the geodesic line in the space with line element ds is expressed by

$$
\left( { \frac { G } { \mathfrak { G } } } \right) ^ { 2 / ( n + 1 ) } \sum _ { \lambda , \mu } \mathfrak { g } _ { \lambda \mu } { \dot { q } } _ { \lambda } { \dot { q } } _ { \mu } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t . }
$$

We can discuss the case when an extremal force is acting on the system by writing

$$
d \tau ^ { 2 } = \frac { d t ^ { 2 } } { f ^ { 2 } ( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , . . . , q _ { n } ) } \biggl \{ 1 - \sum _ { r , s } c _ { r s } \dot { q } _ { r } \dot { q } _ { s } \biggr \} .
$$

Thus,the existence of a quadratic integral in momentum shows the existence of a corresponding line element.

# CANONICAL TRANSFORMATIONS

# 1.7BILINEAR COVARIANTS

A linear homogeneous expression of differentials

$$
\pi _ { \alpha } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) d x _ { i }
$$

is called a Pfaffian (Weber,1900；Goursat, l922；Cartan, 1922,1937; Schouten and v. d. Kulk, l949). Consider a transformation

$$
x _ { k } = \phi _ { k } ( y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { n } ) \qquad ( k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

with the Jacobian

$$
\partial ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) / \partial ( y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { n } ) \neq 0 .
$$

A Pfaffian is transformed to a Pfaffian.As

$$
d x _ { k } = \sum _ { i } { \frac { \partial \phi _ { k } } { \partial y _ { i } } } d y _ { i } ,
$$

we have

$$
\sum _ { k } X _ { k } d x _ { k } = \sum _ { k } \sum _ { i } X _ { k } { \frac { \partial \phi _ { k } } { \partial y _ { i } } } d y _ { i } .
$$

Consider another differential $\delta \boldsymbol { x } _ { j }$ ; then,

$$
\delta x _ { k } = \sum _ { k } \frac { \partial \phi _ { k } } { \partial y _ { i } } \delta y _ { i } .
$$

Furthermore, since

$$
\begin{array} { l } { d \delta x _ { k } = \displaystyle \sum _ { i } \frac { \partial \phi _ { k } } { \partial y _ { i } } d \delta y _ { i } + \displaystyle \sum _ { i , j } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \phi _ { k } } { \partial y _ { i } \partial y _ { j } } d y _ { j } \delta y _ { i } , } \\ { \displaystyle } \\ { \delta d x _ { k } = \displaystyle \sum _ { i } \frac { \partial \phi _ { k } } { \partial y _ { i } } \delta d y _ { i } + \displaystyle \sum _ { i , j } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \phi _ { k } } { \partial y _ { i } \partial y _ { j } } \delta y _ { j } d y _ { i } , } \end{array}
$$

we obtain

$$
d \delta x _ { k } - \delta d x _ { k } = \sum _ { i } \frac { \partial \phi _ { k } } { \partial y _ { i } } ( d \delta y _ { i } - \delta d y _ { i } ) .
$$

Thus, $d \delta x _ { k } \mathrm { ~ - ~ } \delta d x _ { k }$ varies cogradiently to $d { x _ { k } }$ or $\delta \ v { x } _ { k }$ .The terms $d { \boldsymbol { x } } _ { k }$ and $( d \delta x _ { k } - \delta d x _ { k } )$ are said to be covariants. The expression $d \delta x _ { k } \mathrm { ~ - ~ } \delta d x _ { k }$ is linear in $d$ and 8. Hence we call it a bilinear covariant. Let $f$ be a function of $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ,$ ；then

$$
\sum _ { k } \left( d \delta x _ { k } - \delta d x _ { k } \right) \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { k } } = \sum _ { i } \left( d \delta y _ { i } - \delta d y _ { i } \right) \frac { \partial f } { \partial y _ { i } }
$$

is an invariant. Denote the Pfaffian by

$$
\pi _ { d } = \sum _ { i } X _ { i } d x _ { i } = \sum _ { k } Y _ { k } d y _ { k } ;
$$

then, by the above theorem,

$$
\sum _ { k } X _ { k } ( d \delta x _ { k } - \delta d x _ { k } ) = \sum _ { i } Y _ { i } ( d \delta y _ { i } - \delta d y _ { i } ) .
$$

Applying the operator δ on (20), we obtain

$$
\delta \pi _ { d } = \sum _ { k } \delta X _ { k } d x _ { k } + \sum _ { k } X _ { k } \delta d x _ { k } = \sum _ { i } \delta Y _ { i } d y _ { i } + \sum _ { i } Y _ { i } \delta d y _ { i } .
$$

Similarly,

$$
d \pi _ { \delta } = \sum _ { k } d X _ { k } \delta x _ { k } + \sum _ { k } X _ { k } d \delta x _ { k } = \sum _ { i } d Y _ { i } \delta y _ { i } + \sum _ { i } Y _ { i } d \delta y _ { i } .
$$

The difference of these two expressions is

$$
d \pi _ { \delta } - \delta \pi _ { d } = \sum _ { k } ( d x _ { k } \delta x _ { k } - \delta X _ { k } d x _ { k } ) = \sum _ { i } ( d Y _ { i } \delta y _ { i } - \delta Y _ { i } d y _ { i } ) .
$$

If (20） exists between two Pfaffians, then we have (2la). In other words

$$
\sum _ { j , k } \left( \frac { \partial X _ { k } } { \partial x _ { j } } - \frac { \partial X _ { j } } { \partial x _ { k } } \right) d x _ { j } \delta x _ { k } = \sum _ { i , l } \left( \frac { \partial Y _ { i } } { \partial y _ { l } } - \frac { \partial Y _ { l } } { \partial y _ { i } } \right) d y _ { l } \delta y _ { j } .
$$

Either side of (22) is called a bilinear covariant of $\pi _ { d }$ 、This has been studied by Lipschitz and Frobenius (see Weyl, 1939). Lie did not employ bilinear covariants, but Poincaré (l893） and Cartan (1922） discussed them with regard to integral invariants.

Suppose that $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ are independent and not connected by any functional relation.Then $d { x } _ { 1 }$ $\mathbf { \Phi } _ { 1 } , d x _ { 2 } , \dots , d x _ { n } , \delta x _ { 1 } , \delta x _ { 2 } , \dots , \delta x _ { n }$ are mutually independent differentials and so are $d y _ { 1 } , d y _ { 2 } , . . . , d y _ { n } , \delta y _ { 1 } , \delta y _ { 2 } , . . . , \delta y _ { n } .$ If the differentials δ are all arbitrary, then the necessary and sufficient condition for

$$
\delta \pi _ { d } - d \pi _ { \delta } = \sum _ { i , j } \left( { \frac { \partial X _ { i } } { \partial x _ { j } } } - { \frac { \partial X _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } } \right) d x _ { i } \delta x _ { j } = 0
$$

is that

$$
\psi _ { j } \equiv \sum _ { i } \left( \frac { \partial X _ { i } } { \partial x _ { j } } - \frac { \partial X _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } \right) d x _ { i } = 0 .
$$

This expression is called the associated Pfaffan to the Pfafffian $\textstyle \sum _ { i } X _ { i } d x _ { i }$ Similarly, if the Jacobian $\partial ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) / \partial ( y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { n } ) \neq 0$ ，then

the differentials δ are arbitrary,and the necessary and sufficient condition for

is that

$$
\delta \pi _ { d } - d \pi _ { \delta } = \sum _ { i , j } \left( { \frac { \partial Y _ { i } } { \partial y _ { j } } } - { \frac { \partial Y _ { j } } { \partial y _ { i } } } \right) d y _ { i } \delta y _ { j } = 0
$$

$$
\psi _ { j } \equiv \sum _ { i } \left( { \frac { \partial Y _ { i } } { \partial y _ { j } } } - { \frac { \partial Y _ { j } } { \partial y _ { i } } } \right) d y _ { i } = 0 .
$$

The necessary and sufficient condition for the Pfaffian $\textstyle \sum _ { i } X _ { i } d x _ { i }$ to be a perfect differential is, as we shall immediately see,

$$
\frac { \partial X _ { i } } { \partial x _ { j } } - \frac { \partial X _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } = 0 \qquad ( i , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Hence,the necessary and sufficient condition for the Pfaffian $\textstyle \sum _ { i } X _ { i } d x _ { i }$ to be a perfect differential is $\psi _ { j } = 0$ ，or $\delta \pi _ { d } - d \pi _ { \delta } = 0$

Theorem: The necessary and sufficient condition for a Pfaffian to be a perfect differential is that the bilinear covariants are zero.

Corollary: The bilinear covariant of a Pfaffan which is a perfect differential is zero.

We now prove the theorem.The necessary and sufficient condition for a Pfaffian $\textstyle \sum _ { i } X _ { i } d x _ { i }$ to be a perfect differential is that

$$
\frac { \partial X _ { i } } { \partial x _ { j } } - \frac { \partial X _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } = 0 \qquad ( i , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

If $\textstyle \sum _ { i } X _ { i } d x _ { i }$ is a perfect differential $d Z _ { i }$ then $X _ { i } = \partial Z / \partial x _ { i }$ and hence

$$
{ \frac { \partial { X } _ { i } } { \partial { x } _ { j } } } = { \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } { Z } } { \partial { x } _ { i } \partial { x } _ { j } } } = { \frac { \partial { X } _ { j } } { \partial { x } _ { i } } } .
$$

Thus, the condition is necessary. In order to prove that the condition is sufficient, that is, that $\textstyle \sum _ { i } X _ { i } d x _ { i }$ is a perfect differential if (24) is true, we carry out Mayer's transformation

$$
x _ { 1 } = x _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } + \lambda _ { 1 } , \qquad x _ { i } = x _ { i } ^ { 0 } + \lambda _ { 1 } \lambda _ { i } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Then $d x _ { 1 } = d \lambda _ { 1 }$ ， $d { \pmb x } _ { i } = \lambda _ { i } d \lambda _ { 1 } + \lambda _ { 1 } d \lambda _ { i }$ . Denote the result of this transformation in $X _ { i }$ by $[ X _ { i } ]$ ; then,

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } d x _ { i } = \lambda _ { 1 } \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { n } \left[ X _ { j } \right] d \lambda _ { j } + \sum _ { j = 2 } \lambda _ { j } [ X _ { j } ] d \lambda _ { 1 } + \left[ X _ { 1 } \right] d \lambda _ { 1 } .
$$

As the associated Pfaffan is zero,by our assumption, so is the right-hand side of this last equation. Hence

$$
- \frac { \partial } { \partial \lambda _ { i } } \left( \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { j } [ X _ { j } ] + [ X _ { 1 } ] \right) + \frac { \partial } { \partial \lambda _ { 1 } } \left( \lambda _ { 1 } \sum _ { i = 2 } ^ { n } \left[ X _ { i } \right] \right) = 0 ( i , j = 2 , 3 , . . .
$$

that is,

$$
[ X _ { i } ] + \lambda _ { 1 } { \frac { \partial [ X _ { i } ] } { \partial \lambda _ { 1 } } } - [ X _ { i } ] - \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { j } { \frac { \partial [ X _ { j } ] } { \partial \lambda _ { i } } } - { \frac { \partial [ X _ { 1 } ] } { \partial \lambda _ { i } } } = 0 \quad ( i = 2 , 3 , . . .
$$

Thus

$$
\lambda _ { 1 } \frac { \partial [ X _ { i } ] } { \partial \lambda _ { 1 } } = \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { j } \frac { \partial [ X _ { j } ] } { \partial \lambda _ { i } } + \frac { \partial [ X _ { 1 } ] } { \partial \lambda _ { i } } \qquad ( i = 2 , 3 , . . . , n ) .
$$

To prove that $\textstyle \sum _ { i } X _ { i } d x _ { i }$ is a perfect differential, it is sufficient to show that there exists a function $U$ such that $\begin{array} { r } { \partial U / \partial \lambda _ { 1 } = \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { j } [ X _ { j } ] + [ X _ { 1 } ] _ { ! } } \end{array}$ that is,

$$
U = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \lambda _ { 1 } } \left( \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { j } [ X _ { j } ] + [ X _ { 1 } ] \right) d \lambda _ { 1 } ,
$$

or

$$
{ \frac { \partial U } { \partial \lambda _ { i } } } = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \lambda _ { 1 } } \left\{ \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { j } \frac { \partial } { \partial \lambda _ { i } } \left[ X _ { j } \right] + \left[ X _ { i } \right] + { \frac { \partial [ X _ { 1 } ] } { \partial \lambda _ { i } } } \right\} d \lambda _ { 1 } \qquad ( i = 2 , 3 , . . . , n )
$$

or, by (25),

$$
\frac { \partial U } { \partial \lambda _ { i } } = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \lambda _ { 1 } } \left\{ [ X _ { \mathrm { i } } ] + \lambda _ { 1 } \frac { \partial } { \partial \lambda _ { 1 } } \left[ X _ { \mathrm { i } } \right] \right\} d \lambda _ { 1 } = \lambda _ { 1 } [ X _ { \mathrm { i } } ] \qquad ( i = 2 , 3 , . . . , n ) .
$$

We now have

$$
\begin{array} { l } { d U = \lambda _ { 1 } \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 2 } ^ { n } \left[ X _ { i } \right] d \lambda _ { i } + \displaystyle \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { j } [ X _ { j } ] d \lambda _ { 1 } + \left[ X _ { 1 } \right] d \lambda _ { 1 } } \\ { = \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 2 } ^ { n } \left[ X _ { i } \right] ( \lambda _ { 1 } d \lambda _ { i } + \lambda _ { i } d \lambda _ { 1 } ) + \left[ X _ { 1 } \right] d \lambda _ { 1 } } \\ { = \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } d ( x _ { i } - x _ { i } ^ { 0 } ) . } \end{array}
$$

Hence

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { i } d x _ { i } = d U ( x _ { 1 } - x _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } , x _ { 2 } - x _ { 2 } ^ { 0 } , . . . , x _ { n } - x _ { n } ^ { 0 } ) ,
$$

a perfect differential.

# 1.8CONTACT TRANSFORMATIONS

A transformation from $( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , . . . , q _ { n } ; p _ { 1 } , p _ { 2 } , . . . , p _ { n } )$ to

$$
( Q _ { 1 } , Q _ { 2 } , . . . , Q _ { n } ; P _ { 1 } , P _ { 2 } , . . . , P _ { n } )
$$

is called a contact transformation if it makes $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } P _ { i } d Q _ { i } - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \beta _ { i } d q _ { i } } \end{array}$ ex-pressed as a function of $( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , . . . , q _ { n } ; p _ { 1 } , p _ { 2 } , . . . , p _ { n } )$ ,a perfect differential of a function of $( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , . . . , q _ { n } ; \beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } , . . . , \beta _ { n } )$

For a contact transformation there exists a perfect differential such that

$$
\sum _ { i } { ( P _ { i } d Q _ { i } - \dot { p _ { i } } d q _ { i } ) } = d W .
$$

In other words, the difference of two differentials $\textstyle \sum _ { i } P _ { i } d Q _ { i }$ and $\sum _ { j } \pmb { \hat { p } _ { j } } d q _ { j }$ is a perfect differential. The necessary and sufficient condition for a Pfaffian to be a perfect differential is that the bilinear covariant is zero. The condition is expressed by taking the bilinear covariant of (26)as

$$
\sum _ { i } { ( \delta P _ { i } d Q _ { i } - d P _ { i } \delta Q _ { i } ) } = \sum _ { i } { ( \delta p _ { i } d q _ { i } - d p _ { i } \delta q _ { i } ) } .
$$

Hence we obtain the following

Theorem : $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { i } \left( \hat { \partial } p _ { i } \ : d q _ { i } \ : - \ : d p _ { i } \ : \hat { \partial } q _ { i } \right) } \end{array}$ is invariant by a contact transformation.

Conversely,if the relation (27） is given，the bilinear covariant $\Sigma _ { i } ( \delta P _ { i } d Q _ { i } - d P _ { i } \delta Q _ { i } ) - \Sigma _ { i } ( \delta p _ { i } d q _ { i } - d p _ { i } \delta q _ { i } )$ ，when transformed as a function of $( q _ { i } , p _ { i } )$ by the transformation $( Q _ { i } , P _ { i } )  ( q _ { i } , \pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { i } )$ , is zero.Hence the difference $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { i } P _ { i } d Q _ { i } - \sum _ { i } \dot { p _ { i } } d q _ { i } } \end{array}$ of the Pfaffians corresponding to each member of (27） must be a perfect differential. Therefore,this transformation is a contact transformation.

Theorem: The necessary and sufficient condition for a contact transformation is that $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { i } \left( \hat { \partial } \phi _ { i } \ : d q _ { i } - \ : d p _ { i } \ : \hat { \partial } q _ { i } \right) } \end{array}$ be an invariant.

The most general form of a contact transformation is

$$
d Z - \sum _ { i } P _ { i } d Q _ { i } = \rho ( z , \phi _ { i } , q _ { i } ) \cdot \Big ( d z - \sum _ { i } \phi _ { i } d q _ { i } \Big ) , \qquad \rho \neq 0 ,
$$

as discussed by Lie. Goursat (1923) called this an inhomogeneous contact

transformation, and the form just discussed a homogeneous contact transformation. An inhomogeneous contact transformation, if we put

$$
z = q _ { n + 1 } , \qquad e _ { n + 1 } = { \frac { \hbar _ { n + 1 } } { \rho } } , \qquad - \hbar _ { i } \hbar _ { n + 1 } = \bar { p } _ { i } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

$$
Z = { \cal Q } _ { n + 1 } , \qquad - { \cal P } _ { i } { \cal P } _ { n + 1 } = { \bar { \cal P } } _ { i } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

can be reduced to a homogeneous contact transformation

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \bar { P } _ { i } d Q _ { i } + P _ { n + 1 } d Q _ { n + 1 } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \bar { p } _ { i } d q _ { i } + p _ { n + 1 } d q _ { n + 1 } .
$$

Hence,an inhomogeneous contact transformation can be treated as a special case of a homogeneous contact transformation.

# 1.9LAGRANGE'S AND POISSON'S BRACKETS

Supposing that $( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , . . . , q _ { n } ; \beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } , . . . , \beta _ { n } )$ are functions of $\pmb { u }$ and $v$ ； we call the expression

$$
[ u , v ] = \sum _ { i } \left\{ { \frac { \partial q _ { i } } { \partial u } } { \frac { \partial \phi _ { i } } { \partial v } } - { \frac { \partial q _ { i } } { \partial v } } { \frac { \partial \phi _ { i } } { \partial u } } \right\}
$$

the Lagrangian bracket. If $u = v _ { \mathrm { { i } } }$ then $[ u , v ] \equiv 0$ .It is also obvious that $[ u , v ] = - [ v , u ]$ . Carry out the transformation $( q _ { i } , p _ { i } )  ( Q _ { i } , P _ { i } )$ in the bilinear covariant $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { i } \left( \hat { \partial } p _ { i } \ : d q _ { i } - \ : d p _ { i } \ : \hat { \partial } q _ { i } \right) } \end{array}$ . Then

$$
\begin{array} { r c l } { { { \displaystyle \sum _ { i } } \ ( \delta { \phi } _ { i } \ d _ { i } - \ d { \phi } _ { i } \ \delta { q } _ { i } ) = \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { j , k } } [ Q _ { j } , Q _ { k } ] ( \delta Q _ { k } \ d Q _ { j } - d Q _ { k } \ \delta Q _ { j } ) } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { + \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { j , k } } [ Q _ { j } , P _ { k } ] ( \delta P _ { k } \ d Q _ { j } - d P _ { k } \ \delta Q _ { j } ) } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { + \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { j , k } } [ P _ { j } , Q _ { k } ] ( \delta Q _ { k } \ d P _ { j } - d Q _ { k } \ \delta P _ { j } ) } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { + \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { j , k } } [ P _ { j } , P _ { k } ] ( \delta P _ { k } \ d P _ { j } - d P _ { k } \ \delta P _ { j } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

The necessary and sufficient condition for $( q _ { i } , p _ { i } )  ( Q _ { i } , P _ { i } )$ to be a contact transformation is equality of the bilinear covariants:

$$
\sum _ { i } { ( \delta p _ { i } d q _ { i } - d p _ { i } \delta q _ { i } ) } = \sum _ { j } { ( \delta P _ { j } d Q _ { j } - d P _ { j } \delta Q _ { j } ) } .
$$

Comparing these two equations with arbitrary $d$ and & leads to the following

Theorem: The necessary and sufficient condition for a contact transformation is

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { { } [ Q _ { i } , P _ { j } ] = \delta _ { i j } , \qquad [ P _ { i } , P _ { j } ] = 0 , \qquad [ Q _ { i } , Q _ { j } ] = 0 , } } \\ { { { } [ P _ { i } , Q _ { j } ] = - \delta _ { i j } \qquad ( i , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) . } } \end{array}
$$

The expression

$$
( u , v ) = \sum _ { i } \left( \frac { \partial u } { \partial q _ { i } } \frac { \partial v } { \partial \rho _ { i } } - \frac { \partial u } { \partial \rho _ { i } } \frac { \partial v } { \partial q _ { i } } \right)
$$

is called the Poisson bracket. We see that $( u , u ) = 0$ and $( u , v ) = - ( v , u )$ Let $( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n } , u _ { n + 1 } , \ldots , u _ { 2 n } )$ be mutually independent functions of $( q _ { i } , p _ { i } )$ , such that

$$
\partial ( u _ { 1 } , . . . , u _ { n } , u _ { n + 1 } , . . . , u _ { 2 n } ) / \partial ( q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { n } , p _ { 1 } , . . . , p _ { n } ) \neq 0 .
$$

Then， conversely, $( q _ { i } , p _ { i } )$ are mutually independent functions of $( u _ { 1 } , . . . , u _ { n } , u _ { n + 1 } , . . . , u _ { 2 n } )$ . Since

$$
\sum _ { \alpha = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \frac { \partial q _ { j } } { \partial u _ { \alpha } } \frac { \partial u _ { \alpha } } { \partial q _ { i } } = \delta _ { i j } , \qquad \sum _ { \alpha = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \frac { \partial p _ { j } } { \partial u _ { \alpha } } \frac { \partial u _ { \alpha } } { \partial p _ { i } } = \delta _ { i j } ,
$$

$$
\sum _ { \alpha = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \frac { \partial p _ { j } } { \partial u _ { \alpha } } \frac { \partial u _ { \alpha } } { \partial q _ { i } } = 0 , \qquad \sum _ { \alpha = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \frac { \partial q _ { j } } { \partial u _ { \alpha } } \frac { \partial u _ { \alpha } } { \partial p _ { i } } = 0 \qquad ( i , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

we can prove, by adding the partial products of (28) and (30), that

$$
\sum _ { \alpha = 1 } ^ { 2 n } ( u _ { \alpha } , u _ { r } ) [ u _ { \alpha } , u _ { s } ] = \sum _ { i } \left( \frac { \partial u _ { r } } { \partial \boldsymbol { \phi } _ { i } } \frac { \partial \boldsymbol { \phi } _ { i } } { \partial u _ { s } } + \frac { \partial u _ { r } } { \partial q _ { i } } \frac { \partial q _ { i } } { \partial u _ { s } } \right) = \delta _ { r s } .
$$

Hence, the product of the determinant formed of $( u _ { i } , u _ { j } )$ and the determinant formed of $[ u _ { i } , u _ { j } ]$ is unity.A matrix in which the rows and columns are exchanged is called a transposed matrix of the original matrix. The matrix formed of the Lagrangian brackets,and the transposed matrix of the matrix formed of the Poisson brackets,are reciprocal. Since the determinant formed of $[ u _ { i } , u _ { j } ]$ is nonzero, reciprocity leads to the

Theorem: The necessary and suffcient condition for a contact transformation is

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { ( Q _ { i } , P _ { j } ) = \delta _ { i j } , ~ ( P _ { i } , P _ { j } ) = 0 , ~ ( Q _ { i } , Q _ { j } ) = 0 , } } \\ { { ~ ( P _ { i } , Q _ { j } ) = - \delta _ { i j } ~ ( i , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) . } } \end{array}
$$

To prove this theorem directly,write $\begin{array} { r l } { \sum _ { j } { \bigl ( } \delta P _ { j } { \mathrm { ~ } } d Q _ { j } { \mathrm { ~ - ~ } } d P _ { j } { \mathrm { ~ } } \delta Q _ { j } { \mathrm { ) } } } \end{array}$ as a function of $\delta p _ { i } , d p _ { i } , \delta q _ { i } , d q _ { i }$ with the help of the Poisson brackets and show that it is equal to $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { j } \big ( \delta p _ { j } \ : d q _ { j } - \ : d p _ { j } \ : \delta q _ { j } \big ) } \end{array}$ . In the course of this proof we see that the value of each determinant is equal to

$$
\| ( u _ { i } , u _ { j } ) \| = ( - 1 ) ^ { n ^ { 2 } + n } , \qquad \| [ u _ { i } , u _ { j } ] \| = ( - 1 ) ^ { n ^ { 2 } + n } .
$$

The condition for an inhomogeneous transformation to be a contact transformation is expressed by

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { ( ( Q _ { i } , Q _ { j } ) ) = 0 , \qquad ( ( P _ { i } , P _ { j } ) ) = 0 , \qquad ( ( Z , Q _ { i } ) ) = 0 , } \\ & { ( ( P _ { i } , Z ) ) = \rho P _ { i } , \qquad ( ( P _ { i } , Q _ { j } ) ) = \delta _ { i j } { \cdot } \rho , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
{ \frac { \delta } { \delta q _ { i } } } = { \frac { \partial } { \partial q _ { i } } } + \beta _ { i } { \frac { \partial } { \partial z } } , \qquad ( ( U , V ) ) = \sum _ { i } \left( { \frac { \partial U } { \partial q _ { i } } } { \frac { \partial V } { \delta q _ { j } } } - { \frac { \partial V } { \partial q _ { i } } } { \frac { \partial U } { \delta q _ { j } } } \right) .
$$

The Poisson brackets have an important bearing on Dirac's quantum mechanics (1925)．By Dirac's $q$ -numbers，which do not obey the commutative rule,

$$
\frac { i h } { 2 \pi } ( q _ { r } , q _ { s } ) , \frac { i h } { 2 \pi } ( \rho _ { r } , \rho _ { s } ) , \frac { i h } { 2 \pi } ( q _ { r } , \rho _ { s } )
$$

are supposed to correspond to Heisenberg's uncertainty relations

$$
q _ { r } q _ { s } - q _ { s } q _ { r } = 0 , \qquad \hbar _ { r } \hbar _ { s } - \hbar _ { s } \hbar _ { r } = 0 , \qquad q _ { r } \hbar _ { s } - \hbar _ { s } q _ { r } = \frac { i \hbar } { 2 \pi } \delta _ { r s }
$$

and are obtained by contact transformations.The Poisson brackets are useful in the application of special relativity theory,to the theory of electrons, radiation fields,and meson fields (Synge,1960).

A linear canonical transformation is called exceptional if it does not satisfy (29).Ura (l95l） proved the following necessary and sufficient conditions for the existence of a linear canonical exceptional transformation: that constants $\lambda _ { i j }$ and $\mu _ { i j } ( i = 1 , . . . , n ; j = 1 , . . . , l \equiv 2 n - 2 )$ can be found such that if

$$
X _ { j } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { i j } p _ { i } - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \mu _ { i j } q _ { i } ~ ( j = 1 , . . . , l \equiv 2 n - 2 ) ,
$$

and,inversely, if the $2 n$ variables $q _ { i } , p _ { i }$ are substituted in the Hamiltonian function $H$ as functions of $ { l }$ variables $X _ { j { \mathrm { : } } }$ then $H$ is expressed as functions of these $\boldsymbol { l }$ variables

$$
H ( q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { n } , \beta _ { 1 } , . . . , \beta _ { n } ) = K ( X _ { 1 } , . . . , X _ { l } ) .
$$

He also showed that if a linear canonical exceptional transformation is known， then two linearly independent integrals can be found and, inversely,if two linear independent integrals are known, then a linear canonical exceptional transformation can be derived from these two integrals.

# I.IO CANONICAL TRANSFORMATIONS

Consider a Pfaffian

$$
\omega _ { d } = \sum _ { i } \hbar _ { i } d q _ { i } - H d t
$$

and form the corresponding bilinear covariant

$$
\sum _ { i } \left( \delta p _ { i } d q _ { i } - d p _ { i } \delta q _ { i } \right) - \delta H d t - d H \delta t .
$$

Substituting

$$
\delta H = \sum _ { i } { \frac { \partial H } { \partial \rho _ { i } } } \delta \rho _ { i } + \sum _ { i } { \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { i } } } \delta q _ { i } + { \frac { \partial H } { \partial t } } \delta t
$$

in this expression, we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \delta \omega _ { d } - d \omega _ { \delta } = \sum _ { i } \left. - \left( d p _ { i } + \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { i } } d t \right) \delta q _ { i } + \left( d q _ { i } - \frac { \partial H } { \partial p _ { i } } d t \right) \delta p _ { i } \right. } \\ { \displaystyle ~ + \left( d H - \frac { \partial H } { \partial t } d t \right) \delta t . } \end{array}
$$

Equate the coefficients of $\delta q _ { i } , \delta { p _ { i } }$ ， $\delta t$ in this expression to zero-that is, equate the Pfaffian associated with (23) to zero; then,corresponding to (23), we obtain

$$
\frac { d \phi _ { i } } { d t } = \triangledown - \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { i } } , \frac { d q _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial \phi _ { i } } , \frac { d H } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial t } .
$$

The last equation has been proved to be a result of the former pair of $2 n$ equations.Hence, the Hamiltonian equations (33） are the associated Pfaffians corresponding to (23) and can be replaced by the bilinear covariant (32).

Consider a contact transformation

$$
\sum _ { i } { p _ { i } d q _ { i } } - H d t = \sum _ { i } { p _ { i } d Q _ { i } } - K d \tau + d W ,
$$

where $P _ { i } , Q _ { i } , K , \tau , W$ are all functions of $q _ { i } , p _ { i } , H , t .$ .The bilinear covariant on the right-hand side is, corresponding to (26),

$$
\sum _ { i } { ( \delta P _ { i } d Q _ { i } - d P _ { i } \delta Q _ { i } ) - \delta K d \tau } + d K \delta \tau .
$$

As the transformation is a contact transformation, this bilinear covariant should equal the bilinear covariant of (32). Since $\delta P _ { i } , \delta Q _ { i } , \delta K , \delta \tau ,$ and $\delta \pmb { \hat { p } _ { i } }$ ， $\delta q _ { i } , \ \delta H , \ \delta t$ are arbitrary and mutually independent, the associated Pfaffian

$$
\frac { d \boldsymbol { P _ { i } } } { d \tau } = \begin{array} { l l l } { - \frac { \partial \boldsymbol { K } } { \partial Q _ { i } } , } & { \frac { d Q _ { i } } { d \tau } = \frac { \partial \boldsymbol { K } } { \partial P _ { i } } , } & { \frac { d \boldsymbol { K } } { d \tau } = \frac { \partial \boldsymbol { K } } { \partial \tau } } \end{array}
$$

is the consequence of (33).Hence the canonical form of the differential equations is preserved by a contact transformation. In particular, put $\tau = t$ ; then

$$
\sum _ { i } { p _ { i } } d q _ { i } - H d t = \sum _ { i } P _ { i } d Q _ { i } - K d t + d W
$$

and

$$
{ \boldsymbol { p } } _ { i } = { \frac { \partial W } { \partial q _ { i } } } , \qquad { \boldsymbol { P } } _ { i } = - { \frac { \partial W } { \partial Q _ { i } } } , \qquad H = K - { \frac { \partial W } { \partial t } } .
$$

This is a condition for a contact transformation, provided that

$$
\Big \| \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } W } { \partial q _ { i } \partial Q _ { j } } \Big \| \neq 0 .
$$

Schering calls $W$ the substitution function.

Rewriting (35) in the form

$$
\sum _ { i } p _ { i } d q _ { i } - H d t = d \Bigl ( \sum _ { i } P _ { i } Q _ { i } \Bigr ) - \sum _ { i } Q _ { i } d P _ { i } - K d t + d W
$$

gives us another form of the condition for canonical transformation:

$$
{ \bf \nabla } \phi _ { i } = \frac { \partial S } { \partial q _ { i } } , \qquad Q _ { i } = \frac { \partial S } { \partial P _ { i } } , \qquad H = K - \frac { \partial S } { \partial t } .
$$

provided that

$$
\left\| \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } S } { \partial q _ { i } \partial P _ { j } } \right\| \neq 0 .
$$

Here $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { i } P _ { i } Q _ { i } + W = S , } \end{array}$ and $s$ is called the principal function.

Thus,a contact transformation defined by (36) or (38) preserves the canonical form of the differential equations (33). In general, a transformation that preserves the canonical form of differential equations is called a canonical transformation.A contact transformation is a canonical transformation, but a canonical transformation is not necessarily a contact transformation.Either (36) or (38) is a suffcient but not a necessary condition for canonicity.

Suppose that the differential equations

$$
{ \frac { d \beta _ { i } } { d t } } = \ - { \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { i } } } , { \frac { d q _ { i } } { d t } } = { \frac { \partial H } { \partial \beta _ { i } } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

are transformed to

$$
{ \frac { d { \cal P } _ { i } } { d t } } = \mathrm { ~ - } { \frac { \partial K } { \partial Q _ { i } } } , { \frac { d Q _ { i } } { d t } } = { \frac { \partial K } { \partial P _ { i } } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

and that the transformation is not a contact transformation.Lie (1922) tried to obtain the transformation in the form

$$
\sum _ { i } { p _ { i } } d q _ { i } = \sum _ { i } G _ { i } d H _ { i } + d V .
$$

He considered $H _ { 1 }$ among $H _ { i }$ to be a given function and $\lambda _ { i } , \mu _ { i } , U ,$ tobe arbitrary functions of $G _ { 1 } , \ldots , G _ { n } , H _ { 1 } , \ldots , H _ { n }$ His result is that if ${ \cal L } _ { i } = \lambda _ { i } + \partial U / \partial H _ { i }$ and $M _ { i } = \mu _ { i } + \partial U / \partial G _ { i } ,$ then $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { i } Z _ { i } d H _ { i } + \sum _ { i } M _ { i } d G _ { i } } \end{array}$ can be most generally written in the form $\Sigma _ { i } P _ { i } d Q _ { i } + d W$ According to Carathéodory (l930) this problem has nothing to do with canonicity. Consider two integral curves

$$
q _ { i } = q _ { i } ( t , c _ { 1 } , . . . , c _ { 2 n } ) , \qquad \hbar = \hbar ( t , c _ { 1 } , . . . , c _ { 2 n } ) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

and

$$
\mathrm { ~  ~ { ~ \psi ~ } ~ } = \mathrm { ~  ~ { ~ \cal { Q } } ~ } _ { j } ( t , C _ { 1 } , \mathrm { ~  ~ { ~ \psi ~ } ~ } _ { 1 } , \mathrm { ~  ~ { ~ \cal ~ { ~ \psi ~ } ~ } ~ } _ { j _ { n } } ) , \quad \mathrm { ~  ~ { ~ \cal ~ { ~ P } ~ } ~ } _ { j } = { \cal P } _ { j } ( t , C _ { 1 } , \mathrm { ~  ~ { ~ \psi ~ } ~ } _ { 1 } , \mathrm { ~  ~ { ~ \psi ~ } ~ } _ { 2 } ) , \quad \mathrm { ~  ~ { ~ \psi ~ } ~ } ( j = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) ,
$$

and the relations

$$
\begin{array} { r } { c _ { k } = \phi _ { k } ( C _ { 1 } , . . . , C _ { 2 n } ) \qquad ( k = 1 , 2 , . . . , 2 n ) , } \end{array}
$$

where the $\phi _ { k }$ are arbitrary functions. The two integral curves are made to correspond by these relations. If we eliminate $_ { 4 n }$ constants $C _ { l } , c _ { l }$ from these $6 n$ equations, then we obtain a transformation (not necessarily canonical). Caratheodory (l935) then defined a canonical transformation by

$$
\sum _ { i } P _ { i } d Q _ { i } = \sum _ { i } \hbar _ { i } d q _ { i } + d \Omega
$$

and called $\Omega$ the generating function. If

$$
\sum _ { j } \hbar _ { j } { \frac { \partial Q _ { i } } { \partial \dot { p } _ { j } } } \equiv \dot { 0 , } \qquad P _ { i } = \sum _ { j } \hbar _ { j } { \frac { \partial P _ { i } } { \partial \dot { p } _ { j } } } \qquad ( i , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

then $Q _ { i }$ and $P _ { i }$ are homogeneous of order zero and one respectively in $\mathbf { \nabla } \mathbf { \mathcal { P } } _ { i }$ ， and the canonical transformation is called homogeneous. Caratheodory proved that a homogeneous canonical transformation is also a homo-geneous contact transformation. Next, he took $_ r$ pairs from $\not p _ { i } , q _ { i }$ and put

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \xi _ { \alpha } = q _ { \alpha } , \qquad \eta _ { \alpha } = p _ { \alpha } , \qquad } & { \xi _ { \kappa } = p _ { \kappa } , \qquad \eta _ { \kappa } = - q _ { \kappa } } \\ & { \big ( \alpha = 1 , 2 , \ldots , r ; \kappa = r + 1 , \ldots , n } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
Q _ { i } ( q _ { j } , \pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { j } ) = \Xi _ { i } ( \xi _ { j } , \pmb { \mathscr { \eta } } _ { j } )
$$

and then proved that we can arrange matters so that

$$
\frac { \partial ( \Xi _ { 1 } , . . . , \Xi _ { n } ) } { \partial ( \phi _ { 1 } , . . . , \ L _ { q _ { n } } ) } \neq 0 .
$$

Solving

$$
Q _ { i } = Q _ { i } ( q _ { j } , \pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { j } ) , ~ P _ { i } = P _ { i } ( q _ { j } , \pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { j } )
$$

for such $\ p _ { 1 } , . . . , p _ { r } , q _ { \tau + 1 } , . . . , q _ { n } .$ we obtain

$$
\frac { \partial \chi _ { \alpha } } { \partial q _ { \beta } } = \frac { \partial \chi _ { \beta } } { \partial q _ { \alpha } } , \qquad \frac { \partial \chi _ { \alpha } } { \partial \phi _ { \kappa } } = \frac { \partial \chi _ { \kappa } } { \partial q _ { \alpha } } , \qquad \frac { \partial \chi _ { \kappa } } { \partial \phi _ { \lambda } } = \frac { \partial \chi _ { \lambda } } { \partial \phi _ { \kappa } } \qquad ( \alpha , \beta = 1 , . . . , r ) ,
$$

where

$$
: \chi _ { \alpha } ( q _ { \beta } , \phi _ { \lambda } , Q _ { i } ) , \qquad q _ { \kappa } = - \chi _ { \kappa } ( q _ { \beta } , \phi _ { \lambda } , Q _ { i } ) \qquad ( \kappa , \lambda = r + 1 , . . . , n ) .
$$

It can be shown that the generating function $\Omega ( q _ { \beta } , p _ { \lambda } , Q _ { i } )$ is determined by

$$
- \chi _ { \alpha } = \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial q _ { \alpha } } , \qquad - \chi _ { \kappa } = \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial p _ { \kappa } } .
$$

Let $\Omega _ { 0 }$ be a function satisfying these equations and $W ( Q _ { i } )$ be an arbitrary function of $Q _ { i }$ . Then the most general generating function $\Omega$ is written

$$
\Omega ( q _ { \alpha } , \beta _ { \kappa } , Q _ { i } ) = \Omega _ { 0 } ( q _ { \beta } , \beta _ { \lambda } , Q _ { i } ) + W ( Q _ { i } ) .
$$

Hence， the most general canonical transformation， according to Caratheodory, is

$$
{ \mit p } _ { \alpha } = - \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial q _ { \alpha } } , \qquad { \cal q } _ { \kappa } = \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial { \mit p } _ { \kappa } } , \qquad { \cal P } _ { i } = \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial Q _ { i } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$


<!-- chunk 0002: pages 71-140 -->
Ura (l946) proved that a canonical transformation is identical with a contact transformation if the Hamiltonian function is kept invariant for $n = 1$ ; he also deduced for a case of general $\pmb { n }$ the necessary and sufficient condition for a canonical transformation, by using a null divisor in the abstract algebra.Miyahara (l954) considered noncontact transformations in general that keep the transformed equations canonical.

Now we return to our main topic and suppose that $W$ does not depend on $t$ in (35) or (36).In this case we obtain

$$
\sum _ { i } { p _ { i } } d q _ { i } = \sum _ { i } P _ { i } d Q _ { i } + d V ,
$$

and $V$ does not depend on t. Levi-Civita and Amaldi (1927) called such a transformation a completely canonical transformation. From (36) we obtain

$$
\sum _ { i } { p _ { i } } d q _ { i } - \sum _ { i } P _ { i } d Q _ { i } = ( H - K ) d t + d W .
$$

As the left-hand side contains neither $t$ nor $d t$ ,we obtain

$$
H - K + { \frac { \partial W } { \partial t } } = 0 .
$$

Put $\begin{array} { r } { W = V + T , } \end{array}$ where $V$ does not depend on $t$ and $_ { T }$ is a function of $t$ and is zero, by our assumption.Hence, $H = K$ ; also, $( q _ { i } , p _ { i } )  ( Q _ { i } , P _ { i } )$ is a contact transformation.

A canonical transformation does not necessarily contain differentials. Write

$$
a = \sum _ { i } q _ { i } ^ { 2 } , \qquad b = \sum _ { i } q _ { i } \phi _ { i } , \qquad c = \sum _ { i } \phi _ { i } ^ { 2 } ,
$$

$$
A = \sum _ { j } Q _ { j } ^ { 2 } , ~ B = \sum _ { j } Q _ { j } P _ { j } , ~ C = \sum _ { j } P _ { j } ^ { 2 } .
$$

Then a regularizing transformation of Levi-Civita

$$
A = a c ^ { 2 } , \qquad B = - \ b , \qquad C = { \frac { 1 } { c } } ,
$$

is a canonical transformation.

Consider a contact transformation $( q _ { i } , p _ { i } )  ( Q _ { i } , P _ { i } )$

$$
{ \bf \nabla } \phi _ { i } = \frac { \partial U } { \partial q _ { i } } , \qquad - P _ { i } = \frac { \partial U } { \partial Q _ { i } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

with an arbitrary function $U$ under the condition that there exist $\pmb { r }$ relations

$$
\phi _ { j } ( q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { n } , Q _ { 1 } , . . . , Q _ { n } ) = 0 ( j = 1 , . . . , r )
$$

among $q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { n } ; Q _ { 1 } , . . . , Q _ { n }$ ，We use in place of $U$ an expression $U + \lambda _ { 1 } \phi _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \lambda _ { \tau } \phi _ { \tau }$ with $\pmb { r }$ parameters $\lambda _ { 1 } , . . . , \lambda _ { \tau }$ ，which we call Lagrange's indeterminate multipliers. Then we have a contact transformation

$$
{ \boldsymbol { \jmath } } _ { i } = { \frac { \partial U } { \partial q _ { i } } } + \lambda _ { 1 } { \frac { \partial \phi _ { 1 } } { \partial q _ { i } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \lambda _ { r } { \frac { \partial \phi _ { r } } { \partial q _ { i } } } ,
$$

$$
P _ { i } = - { \frac { \partial U } { \partial Q _ { i } } } - \lambda _ { 1 } { \frac { \partial \phi _ { 1 } } { \partial Q _ { i } } } - \cdot \cdot \cdot - \lambda _ { r } { \frac { \partial \phi _ { r } } { \partial Q _ { i } } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

If we eliminate $\lambda _ { 1 } , . . . , \lambda _ { r }$ from these $2 n$ equations and the $_ r$ relations $\phi _ { j } = 0$ ，then we obtain $q _ { i }$ and $\mathbf { \nabla } \mathbf { \mathcal { P } } _ { i }$ as functions of $Q _ { i }$ and $P _ { i : }$ and the Pfaffian can be written

$$
\sum _ { i } P _ { i } d Q _ { i } - H d t + d U + \lambda _ { 1 } d \phi _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \lambda _ { r } d \phi _ { r } .
$$

We can easily derive equations (36) by using the integral invariants discussed in Section 1.2. Consider a trajectory as an integral curve in the $( 2 n + 1 )$ -dimensional space for the Hamiltonian equations

$$
\frac { d q _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial p _ { i } } , \frac { d p _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { i } } , \frac { d H } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial t } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

and the variational curves from this trajectory as functions of two parameters $\pmb { \alpha }$ and $\beta$ .Such variational curves form a cylinder around the original trajectory. Let $\beta =$ constant be a closed curve $C$ surrounding this cylinder.From Cartan's integral invariant

$$
I = \int _ { C } \omega _ { \delta } = \int _ { C } \sum _ { i } \beta _ { i } \ \delta q _ { i } - H \delta t
$$

we obtain

$$
d I = \int _ { C } \sum _ { i } \left( d p _ { i } \delta q _ { i } - d q _ { i } \delta p _ { i } \right) - d H \delta t + d t \delta H .
$$

As δ is the symbol of variation due to the variation of the parameter $\pmb { \alpha }$ ， $d I$ should equal zero in order that $\pmb { I }$ be an integral invariant for any

arbitrary value of $\propto$ , that is,for any values of $\delta q _ { i } , \delta { p _ { i } }$ ,St.Hence,by putting $2 n + 1$ coeffcients of δ in the expression for $d I$ equal to zero, we obtain

$$
d p _ { i } + \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { i } } d t = 0 , \qquad - d q _ { i } + \frac { \partial H } { \partial p _ { i } } d t = 0 , \qquad - d H + \frac { \partial H } { \partial t } d t = 0
$$

$$
( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

in other words,the Hamiltonian canonical equations (33).

Next consider an integral invariant

$$
I = \int \varpi _ { \delta } , \varpi _ { \delta } = \sum _ { i } P _ { i } d Q _ { i } - K d t ,
$$

associated with

$$
{ \frac { d Q _ { i } } { d t } } = { \frac { \partial K } { \partial P _ { i } } } , { \frac { d P _ { i } } { d t } } = - { \frac { \partial K } { \partial Q _ { i } } } , { \frac { d K } { d t } } = { \frac { \partial K } { \partial t } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

and suppose that the transformation from $( q _ { i } , p _ { i } , H , t )$ to $( Q _ { i } , P _ { i } , K , t )$ makes the difference of $\omega _ { \delta }$ and $\varpi _ { \delta }$ a perfect differential. Since an integral invariant along a closed curve is unaltered by the addition of a perfect differential, the canonical form of the differential equations is preserved after this transformation.The condition for form preservation is expressed by

$$
\sum _ { i } \not p _ { i } \delta q _ { i } - \sum _ { i } P _ { i } \delta Q _ { i } - ( H - K ) \delta t = \delta W ,
$$

and we obtain a condition for this that is equivalent to (36) for contact transformations.

Again we vary both parameters $\pmb { \alpha }$ and $\beta$ .Let the points $A , B , C ,$ and $_ D$ correspond to $( \alpha , \beta )$ ， $( \alpha + \delta \alpha , \beta )$ ， $( \alpha , \beta + \delta \beta )$ ,and $( \alpha + \delta \alpha , \beta + \delta \beta )$ ， respectively,and take $\int \omega$ along the closed curve ABCD.Then we have $\omega _ { \delta }$ along $A B$ ， $\omega _ { \delta } ^ { \prime }$ along $A C$ ， $\omega _ { \delta } + \delta ^ { \prime } \omega _ { \delta }$ along $C D$ ,and $\omega _ { \delta } ^ { \prime } + \delta \omega _ { \delta } ^ { \prime }$ along $B D$ Hence

$$
\int _ { A B C D } = \delta \omega _ { \delta } ^ { \prime } - \delta ^ { \prime } \omega _ { \delta } = \omega ^ { \prime } ( \delta , \delta ^ { \prime } ) .
$$

The integral along the closed curve of this bilinear expression remains invariant when we move the closed curve along the corresponding trajectory. It is a double integral

$$
\iint \omega ^ { \prime } = \iint \sum _ { i } \delta p _ { i } \ \delta q _ { i } - \delta H \delta t
$$

over a two-dimensional area. If we apply an extended form of Stokes' theorem, then this expresson equals the integral

$$
\int \omega = \int { \Bigl ( } \sum _ { i } \beta _ { i } \delta q _ { i } - H \delta t { \Bigr ) }
$$

integrated along the closed curve forming the boundary of the twodimensional area. In the sense of Poincare's integral invariants (1889, 1893),which are integrals over the values at the same $t _ { : }$ ， $\iint { \sum _ { i } \delta p _ { i } \delta \dot { q } _ { i } }$ is an integral invariant. Poincaré calls $\iint \omega ^ { \prime }$ an absolute integral invariant and $\int \omega$ a relative integral invariant. The former is not restricted to either a closed or an open domain of integration；the latter is restricted to a closed domain.The fact thatan integral invariant $\iint \omega ^ { \prime }$ exists is equivalent to the fact that the given differential equations have the momentumenergy tensor $\omega _ { \delta }$ ·

Taylor(1935, l937) and Lewis (1937) considered integral invariants that are intermediate between those of Poincaré and Cartan. Denote by $\boldsymbol { x } _ { j }$ a set of $q _ { i }$ and $\mathbf { \nabla } \beta _ { i }$ Integrals of $x _ { j } = f _ { j } [ t ; x _ { k } ( t ) ]$ over the points $t = t ( u ) + t _ { 0 } $ $x _ { j } = f _ { j } [ t ( u ) + t _ { 0 } ; x _ { k } ( u ) ]$ are called by Taylor associated integral invariants. When $t$ is fixed,the integral invariant reduces to Poincare's. When $t _ { 0 }$ is not a constant and is an arbitrary function of $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \dotsc , u _ { p } ,$ it reduces to Cartan's (see Weitzenbock,1923).

Birkhoff (1927,1928) considered a variational principle of Pfaff:

$$
\delta \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t _ { 1 } } \left\{ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 2 m } X _ { i } \dot { x } _ { i } + Z \right\} d t = 0 .
$$

The integral is linear with respect to all the derivatives of first order; also their coefficients $X _ { 1 } , . . . , X _ { 2 m } , Z$ are arbitrary functions of $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { 2 m }$ ，provided that the determinant

$$
\left\| \frac { \partial X _ { i } } { \partial x _ { j } } - \frac { \partial X _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } \right\| \neq 0 \qquad ( i , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , 2 m ) .
$$

If we develop the variational expression, we obtain

$$
\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { 2 m } \left( \frac { \partial X _ { i } } { \partial x _ { j } } - \frac { \partial X _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } \right) \frac { d x _ { j } } { d t } - \frac { \partial Z } { \partial x _ { i } } = 0 \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 2 m ) .
$$

This integral is unchanged by transformations under the integral sign from variables $\boldsymbol { x } _ { j }$ to $y _ { j }$ 、 If we multiply each of these equations by $d x _ { i } / d t$ and add, then we obtain $d Z / d t = 0$ or $Z =$ constant. It corresponds to the energy integral of the Hamiltonian differential equations. Féraud (1929) discussed the relation between this principle of Birkhoff's and the integral invariants of Cartan.

# I.II NONHOLONOMIC CONSTRAINT

Consider a nonholonomic constraint (Section 1.1)

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { d ^ { \prime } \kappa _ { i } = a _ { i 1 } d q _ { 1 } + a _ { i 2 } d q _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + a _ { i n } d q _ { n } + a _ { i 0 } d t = 0 } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , k ) , } } \end{array}
$$

where $q _ { i } , \dot { q } _ { i }$ are functions of $t .$ .This is not a perfect differential according to the definition. The variables $\kappa _ { i }$ are called quasi-coordinates. Any possible displacement of the dynamical system must always satisfy (39). Any increment of velocity must satisfy $\delta \dot { q } _ { i } = d ( \delta q _ { i } ) / d t$ 、That is,the incremental operators $d$ and & must satisfy a commutative law $\delta d q _ { i } = d \delta q _ { i }$ Since any neighboring motion must also satisfy the constraint, we have

$$
d ( a _ { i 1 } \ : \delta q _ { 1 } + a _ { i 2 } \ : \delta q _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + a _ { i n } \ : \delta q _ { n } + a _ { i 0 } \ : \delta t ) = 0 .
$$

By (39),

$$
\delta ( a _ { i 1 } d q _ { 1 } + a _ { i 2 } d q _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + a _ { i n } d q _ { n } + a _ { i 0 } d t ) = 0 .
$$

Owing to the commutative law for $d$ and δ, we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { d ^ { \prime } ( \delta ^ { \prime } \kappa _ { i } ) \ - \ \delta ^ { \prime } ( d ^ { \prime } \kappa _ { i } ) \ = \displaystyle \sum _ { j , k } \Big ( \frac { \partial a _ { i k } } { \partial q _ { j } } - \frac { \partial a _ { i j } } { \partial q _ { k } } \Big ) ( d q _ { j } \ \delta q _ { k } \ - \ \delta q _ { j } \ d q _ { k } ) } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad + \ d t \sum _ { j } \Big ( \frac { \partial a _ { i j } } { \partial t } - \frac { \partial a _ { i 0 } } { \partial q _ { j } } \Big ) \ \delta q _ { j } . } } \end{array}
$$

The right-hand member becomes zero when and only when $d ^ { \prime } \kappa _ { i }$ is a perfect differential. Denote the result of solving (39) by

$$
\begin{array} { r } { d q _ { \lambda } = A _ { 1 \lambda } d ^ { \prime } \kappa _ { 1 } + A _ { 2 \lambda } d ^ { \prime } \kappa _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { k \lambda } d ^ { \prime } \kappa _ { k } \qquad } \\ { + A _ { k + 1 , \lambda } d ^ { \prime } \kappa _ { k + 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { n \lambda } d ^ { \prime } \kappa _ { n } + A _ { 0 \lambda } d ^ { \prime } \kappa _ { 0 } \qquad } \\ { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \end{array}
$$

Substituting this in the preceding equation,we obtain

$$
