\begin{array} { r l } { d ^ { * } \left[ \delta ^ { * } \kappa _ { * \downarrow } \right] - \delta ^ { * } ( d ^ { * } \kappa _ { * } ) = } & { \displaystyle \sum _ { \alpha , \alpha } \left[ \sum _ { \kappa _ { \Delta } , \Delta } \left( \frac { \partial \alpha _ { \Delta } } { \partial \beta _ { \alpha } } - \frac { \partial \alpha _ { \Delta } } { \partial \beta _ { \alpha } } \right) A _ { \alpha } A _ { \alpha } \right] } \\ & { \quad \times \left( d ^ { * } \kappa _ { \alpha } \delta ^ { * } \kappa _ { * } - \delta ^ { * } \kappa _ { \alpha } d ^ { * } \kappa _ { * } \right) } \\ & { \quad + \ d \displaystyle \sum _ { \alpha ^ { * } } \Bigg [ \displaystyle \sum _ { \kappa _ { \Delta } , \alpha } \left( \frac { \partial \alpha _ { \Delta } } { \partial \beta _ { \alpha } } - \frac { \partial \alpha _ { \Delta } } { \partial \beta _ { \alpha } } \right) ( d _ { \alpha } A _ { \alpha } - A _ { \alpha } A _ { \alpha } ) } \\ & { \qquad \quad + \displaystyle \sum _ { \kappa } \left( \frac { \partial \alpha _ { \Delta } } { \partial \alpha } - \frac { \partial \alpha _ { \Delta } } { \partial \beta _ { \alpha } } \right) A _ { \alpha } \Bigg ] \delta ^ { * } } \\ & { = \displaystyle \sum _ { \alpha ^ { * } } \frac { c _ { \alpha } ^ { \Delta } ( d ^ { * } \kappa _ { \alpha } \delta ^ { * } \kappa _ { * } - \delta ^ { * } \kappa _ { \alpha } d ^ { * } \kappa _ { \alpha } ) } { \alpha ^ { * } } } \\ & { \quad + \ d \displaystyle \sum _ { \alpha ^ { * } } c _ { \alpha } ^ { \dagger } \kappa _ { \alpha } \delta ^ { * } \kappa _ { * } , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
c _ { \sigma \tau } ^ { i } = - c _ { \tau \sigma } ^ { i } , \qquad c _ { \sigma \sigma } ^ { i } = 0 .
$$

This equation has been called by Heun (l901) the transitivity equation. The constants $c _ { \sigma \tau } ^ { i }$ correspond to the structure constants in Lie's theory of transformation groups (see Section 4.2).

If (39） does not contain $t$ explicitly, then Lagrange's equations are

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { \partial T } { \partial \dot { q } _ { i } } \right) - \frac { \partial T } { \partial q _ { i } } = Q _ { i } + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { k } \lambda _ { j } a _ { j i } ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { 1 i } d q _ { i } = 0 , . . . , \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { k i } d q _ { i } = 0 .
$$

If we use the quasi-coordinates

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \delta ^ { \prime } \kappa _ { n - k + 1 } = 0 , \ldots , \delta ^ { \prime } \kappa _ { n } = 0 , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \hat { \imath } q _ { i } = A _ { 1 i } \delta ^ { \prime } \kappa _ { 1 } + A _ { 2 i } \delta ^ { \prime } \kappa _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { n - k , i } \delta ^ { \prime } \kappa _ { n - k } . } } \end{array}
$$

we can cast the Lagrangian equations in the form (Hamel, 1924)

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - k } \left\{ \sum _ { \lambda = i } ^ { n } A _ { i \lambda } \Big [ \frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { \partial T } { \partial \dot { q } _ { \lambda } } \right) - \frac { \partial T } { \partial q _ { \lambda } } - Q _ { \lambda } \Big ] \right\} \delta ^ { \prime } \kappa _ { i } = 0 .
$$

Thus,

$$
\sum _ { \lambda = 1 } ^ { n } A _ { i \lambda } \Big [ \frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { \partial T } { \partial \dot { q } _ { \lambda } } \right) - \frac { \partial T } { \partial q _ { \lambda } } \Big ] = \sum _ { \lambda = 1 } ^ { n } A _ { i \lambda } Q _ { \lambda } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - k ) ,
$$

$$
\sum _ { \lambda = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { j \lambda } d q _ { \lambda } = 0 \qquad ( j = n - k + 1 , . . . , n ) .
$$

It has been seen that a holonomic dynamical system can be studied by referring to a Riemannian space (Section l.5). Similarly,a nonholonomic dynamical system can be discussed in a nonholonomic space. Suppose that there exist $n - m$ Pfaffians

$$
d x _ { i } = \sum _ { \alpha = m + 1 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { i } ^ { \alpha } d ^ { \prime } s _ { \alpha } \qquad ( \alpha , \beta = m + 1 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

in a space $R _ { n }$ such that

$$
d ^ { \prime } s _ { \alpha } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { \alpha } ^ { i } d x _ { i } = 0 ,
$$

where

$$
\sum _ { \alpha = m + 1 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { i } ^ { \alpha } \lambda _ { \alpha } ^ { j } = \delta _ { i j } , \qquad \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \lambda _ { \alpha } ^ { i } \lambda _ { i } ^ { \beta } = \delta _ { \alpha \beta } \qquad ( i , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

and $d ^ { \prime } s _ { \alpha }$ is not a perfect differential. If $d ^ { \prime } s _ { \alpha }$ were a perfect differential, then it would be possible to choose $x _ { i }$ so that $d s _ { \alpha } = d x _ { \alpha } = 0$ Thus $R _ { n }$ would reduce to $R _ { m }$ and the line element of the space would be such that

$$
d s ^ { 2 } = d s _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + d s _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + d s _ { m } ^ { 2 } .
$$

The space $R _ { n }$ with $\pmb { n }$ quasi-coordinates $x _ { i }$ constitutes a nonholonomic space $V _ { n } ^ { m }$ . The geometry of nonholonomic spaces has been studied by Vranceanu (1929，1935，196l)，Schouten (1929,1954)，and Yano (1945,1949).

# THE HAMILTON-JACOBI PARTIAL DIFFERENTIAL EQUATION

# 1.12THE HAMILTON-JACOBI THEOREM

If we apply a contact transformation

$$
{ \bf \nabla } \cdot { \bf \nabla } \phi _ { i } = \frac { \partial } { \partial q _ { i } } W ( q _ { i } , Q _ { i } , t ) , \qquad P _ { i } = - \frac { \partial } { \partial Q _ { t } } W ( q _ { i } , Q _ { i } , t ) ,
$$

$$
H = K - \frac { \partial } { \partial t } W ( q _ { i } , Q _ { i } , t ) ,
$$

to Hamilton's canonical equations

$$
\frac { d q _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial } { \partial p _ { i } } H ( q _ { i } , p _ { i } , t ) , \frac { d p _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial } { \partial q _ { i } } H ( q _ { i } , p _ { i } , t ) ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

then these equations (41） become Hamilton's canonical equations

$$
\frac { d Q _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial K } { \partial \phi _ { i } } , \frac { d P _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial K } { \partial Q _ { i } } ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Suppose that $K \equiv 0$ ,then the solution is

$$
Q _ { i } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t s } = \beta _ { i } , \qquad P _ { i } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t s } = \alpha _ { i } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

This solution represents an equilibrium position； that is, the state of motion in the variables $Q _ { i } , P _ { i }$ is an equilibrium state.Hence, if we can find such a function $W ( q _ { i } , Q _ { i } , t )$ , then we can obtain the solution of the original Hamilton equations (4l） from the equilibrium solution (43) by the transformation (40),provided that

$$
\biggl \| \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } { \cal W } } { \partial q _ { i } \partial Q _ { j } } \biggr \| \ne 0 ,
$$

that is, provided that the second equation of (40) can be solved for $q _ { i }$ Thus the solution of (41) reduces to obtaining $W ( q _ { i } , Q _ { i } , t )$

Put $\kappa \equiv 0$ in (40); then

$$
{ \frac { \partial W } { \partial t } } + H = 0 .
$$

Since $\boldsymbol { \mathit { \Pi } } _ { H }$ is a function of $q _ { i } , p _ { i } , t _ { i }$ ，we consider $q _ { i }$ and $t$ as the independent variables and substitute the first equation of (4O) in the place of $\mathbf { \nabla } \phi _ { i }$ Then we obtain a partial differential equation for determining the unknown function $W$ as a function of $n + 1$ variabies $q _ { i }$ and $t$ ：

$$
\frac { \partial W } { \partial t } + H \left( q _ { i } , \frac { \partial W } { \partial q _ { i } } , t \right) = 0 .
$$

This is called the Hamilton-Jacobi partial differential equation. A function $W ( q _ { i } , p _ { i } , t )$ satisfying equation (44） with $\pmb { n }$ arbitrary independent con-stants $\alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n } ,$ besides $\scriptstyle q _ { i } , t$ (any additive constant to $t$ is not counted) is called a complete integral.By‘independent constants"’ is meant that we can choose the $\alpha _ { i }$ such that the $( \partial W / \partial q _ { i } ) _ { t = 0 }$ can have arbitrary values, and in this case (44) is satisfied.

Hence, when we know a complete integral $W ( q _ { i } , Q _ { i } , t )$ and when

$$
\Delta \equiv \left\| \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } { W } } { \partial { q _ { i } } \partial { \alpha _ { j } } } \right\| \neq 0 ,
$$

then we can obtain the general integral of (4l) in the form $\ p _ { i } ( t , \alpha _ { i } , \beta _ { i } )$ ， $q _ { i } ( t , \alpha _ { i } , \beta _ { i } )$ , by solving the equations

$$
\phi _ { i } = \frac { \partial } { \partial q _ { i } } W ( q _ { i } , \alpha _ { i } , t ) , \qquad \beta _ { i } = - \frac { \partial } { \partial \alpha _ { i } } W ( q _ { i } , \alpha _ { i } , t ) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n
$$

derived from (41). Transforming back to the original variables $q _ { i } , \ p _ { i } ,$ we obtain the solution of (4l)；and,indeed, this is the general integral, as this solution contains $2 n$ integration constants $\alpha _ { i } , \beta _ { i }$ .This is called Jacobi's theorem.

Asa corollary of Jacobi's theorem we see that if $\pmb { H }$ does not contain $t$ explicitly, then the complete integral of (45) is

$$
W = - h t + V ( q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { n } , \alpha _ { 1 } , . . . , \alpha _ { n - 1 } , h ) ,
$$

where $h$ is one of the integration constants.

If we put (46) in (45), we obtain

$$
H { \Big ( } q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { n } , { \frac { \partial V } { \partial q _ { 1 } } } , . . . , { \frac { \partial V } { \partial q _ { n } } } { \Big ) } = h .
$$

If $V ( q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { n } , \alpha _ { 1 } , . . . , \alpha _ { n - 1 } , h )$ is an integral of (47) for an arbitrary value of $h$ ,then $K \equiv 0$ in (40);and substituting (46) in (40) gives

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \displaystyle p _ { i } = \frac { \partial V } { \partial q _ { i } } } & { { } \quad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) , } \\ { \displaystyle } & { { } } \\ { \displaystyle \beta _ { j } = - \frac { \partial V } { \partial \alpha _ { j } } } & { { } \quad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - 1 ) , } \\ { \displaystyle } & { { } } \\ { \displaystyle t - t _ { 0 } = \frac { \partial V } { \partial h } } & { { } } \end{array}
$$

The integration constants are $\alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n - 1 } , h , \beta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \beta _ { n - 1 } , t _ { 0 }$ Equations (48) can be solved for $\not p _ { i } , q _ { i }$ as functions of

$$
\alpha _ { 1 } , \dotsc , \alpha _ { n - 1 } , h , \beta _ { 1 } , \dotsc , \beta _ { n - 1 } , t - t _ { 0 } ,
$$

provided that (44a) does not equal zero when $\boldsymbol { h }$ is substituted for $\alpha _ { 1 }$

That the integral $V$ is the complete integral of (47） follows from $\Delta \neq 0$ 、It may be seen in the following manner.Put

$$
\Delta _ { 1 } = \left\| \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } V } { \partial q _ { i } \partial \alpha _ { j } } \right\| \qquad ( i , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - 1 ) .
$$

Then (44a) can be writen

$$
\Delta = \left\| \begin{array} { c c c } { { \Delta _ { 1 } } } & { { \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } V } { \partial q _ { i } \partial h } } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { { \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } V } { \partial q _ { n } \partial \alpha _ { j } } } } & { { \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } V } { \partial q _ { n } \partial h } } } \end{array} \right\| .
$$

Multiply each row by $\partial H / \partial { \boldsymbol { p } } _ { i }$ and suppose that $\pmb { H }$ contains at least one of the $\mathbf { \nabla } \mathbf { \mathcal { P } } _ { i }$ say ${ \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { \pmb { \mathscr { n } } }$ ,so that $\partial H / \partial p _ { n } \neq 0 .$ Then

$$
\sum _ { r } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } V } { \partial q _ { r } \partial \alpha _ { j } } \frac { \partial H } { \partial \beta _ { r } } = \sum _ { r } \frac { \partial H } { \partial \beta _ { r } } \frac { \partial \beta _ { r } } { \partial \alpha _ { j } } ~ ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - 1 ) .
$$

Here it is supposed that $\pmb { H }$ in $H = h$ is a function of $q _ { i }$ and $\partial V / \partial q _ { i }$ ,and that $V$ is a function of $q _ { i }$ and $\alpha _ { i }$ As $\boldsymbol { H }$ contains $\alpha _ { i }$ only through $\partial V / \partial q _ { i } .$ that is, through $\pmb { \mathscr { p } _ { i } }$ ，this expression is the derivative of $\pmb { H }$ with respect to $\alpha _ { j }$ Since the right-hand side of $H = h$ gives $\partial h / \partial \alpha _ { j } = 0$ ，this expression should equal zero.From the last column we obtain

$$
\sum _ { r } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } V } { \partial q _ { r } \partial h } \frac { \partial H } { \partial \phi _ { r } } = \sum _ { r } \frac { \partial H } { \partial \rho _ { r } } \frac { \partial \phi _ { r } } { \partial h } = 1 .
$$

Hence,

$$
\Delta = \frac { 1 } { \partial H / \partial \boldsymbol { p } _ { n } } \left\| \begin{array} { c c } { \Delta _ { 1 } } & { \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } V } { \partial q _ { i } \partial h } } \\ { \frac { \partial H } { \partial \boldsymbol { \beta } _ { n } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } V } { \partial q _ { n } \partial \alpha _ { j } } } & { \frac { \partial H } { \partial \boldsymbol { p } _ { n } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } V } { \partial q _ { n } \partial h } } \end{array} \right\|
$$

$$
= \frac { 1 } { \partial H / \partial \boldsymbol { \phi } _ { n } } \| \boldsymbol { \Delta } _ { 1 }  \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } H } { \partial q _ { i } \partial h } \| = \frac { 1 } { \partial H / \partial \boldsymbol { \phi } _ { n } } \boldsymbol { \Delta } _ { 1 } .
$$

Thus, the condition that $V$ should be the complete integral is $\Delta _ { 1 } \neq 0$ Here $H = h$ expresses that the total energy of the system is constant.

Instead of supposing that $\boldsymbol { h }$ is a function of only one independent variable $\alpha _ { i }$ as in the foregoing treatment, we now suppose that $h$ is a

function of $\pmb { n }$ independent variables $\alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n } ,$ and that $\pmb { h }$ does not contain $q _ { i }$ and $t .$ .Then (46) may be written

$$
W = - h ( \alpha _ { 1 } , . . . , \alpha _ { n } ) t + V ( q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { n } , \alpha _ { 1 } , . . . , \alpha _ { n } )
$$

and (4la) takes the form

$$
\phi _ { i } = \frac { \partial V } { \partial q _ { i } } , \qquad \frac { \partial V } { \partial \alpha _ { i } } = n _ { i } t - \beta _ { i } , \qquad n _ { i } = \frac { \partial h } { \partial \alpha _ { i } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

In this form, which Poincaré employed,all $\alpha _ { i }$ appear symmetrically in the formulas.

Another corollary of.Jacobi's theorem is that if $\pmb { H }$ does not contain $q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { k } ( k < n )$ , and hence can be written

$$
H = H ( t , q _ { k + 1 } , . . . , q _ { n } , \phi _ { 1 } , . . . , \phi _ { n } ) ,
$$

then, putting

$$
W = \alpha _ { 1 } q _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \alpha _ { k } q _ { k } + V ( t , q _ { k + 1 } , \cdot \cdot \cdot , q _ { n } , \alpha _ { 1 } , \cdot \cdot \cdot , \alpha _ { n } )
$$

in the Hamilton-Jacobi partial differential equation (45),we obtain

$$
{ \frac { \partial V } { \partial t } } + H { \Big ( } t , q _ { k + 1 } , . . . , q _ { n } , \alpha _ { 1 } , . . . , \alpha _ { k } , { \frac { \partial V } { \partial q _ { k + 1 } } } , . . . , { \frac { \partial V } { \partial q _ { n } } } { \Big ) } = 0 .
$$

If we can obtain $V ( t , { q _ { k + 1 } } , \ldots , { q _ { n } } , { \alpha _ { 1 } } , \ldots , { \alpha _ { k } } , { \alpha _ { k + 1 } } , \ldots , { \alpha _ { n } } )$ with arbitrary constants $\alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n } ,$ then $W = \alpha _ { 1 } q _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \alpha _ { k } q _ { k } + V$ is the complete integral of (45),and the motion is described by

$$
\begin{array} { l l l } { \displaystyle { \beta _ { i } = - q _ { i } - \frac { \partial V } { \partial \alpha _ { i } } , \qquad \hbar = \alpha _ { i } } } & { \displaystyle { \quad ( i = 1 , . . . , k ) , } } \\ { \displaystyle { } } & { \displaystyle { \beta _ { j } = - \frac { \partial V } { \partial \alpha _ { j } } , \qquad \hbar _ { j } = \frac { \partial V } { \partial q _ { j } } \qquad ( j = k + 1 , . . . , n ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Finally, if $\pmb { H }$ contains neither $t$ nor $q _ { 1 } , \ldots , q _ { k }$ ， then we can proceed in a similar manner by putting

$$
W = - h t + \alpha _ { 1 } q _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \alpha _ { k } q _ { k } + V ( q _ { k + 1 } , \cdot \cdot \cdot , q _ { n } , \alpha _ { 1 } , \cdot \cdot \cdot , \alpha _ { n } ) .
$$

Lehmann-Filhés (l904） discussed the case of incomplete integrals, that is,integrals of the Hamilton-Jacobi partial differential equation that do not contain the necessary number of integration constants.

# 1.13HYDRODYNAMICAL PROOF OF JACOBI's THEOREM

We consider Hamilton's canonical equations (4l） as describing the motion of the molecules of a fluid. The molecules are at $q _ { i } = q _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ ， $\mathbf { \nabla } \phi _ { i } = \mathbf { \nabla } \phi _ { i } ^ { \left( 0 \right) }$ at $t = t _ { 0 } ,$ and we seek the values of $q _ { i }$ and $\mathbf { \nabla } \phi _ { i }$ at time $t .$ This is the Lagrangian problem in the hydrodynamic equations of motion. If, instead, we seek the values .of $\mathbf { \nabla } \phi _ { i }$ for the molecules passing through the points $q _ { i }$ at time $t ,$ ,then the problem is Euler's.

According to Euler's point of view, we consider the $q _ { i }$ and $t$ as the independent variables and suppose $\mathbf { \nabla } \phi _ { \mathbf { i } }$ to be functions of these $n + 1$ variables. Then the second equation of (4l) can be written

$$
\frac { \partial { p _ { i } } } { \partial t } + \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { i } } + \sum _ { r } \frac { \partial H } { \partial { p _ { r } } } \frac { \partial { p _ { r } } } { \partial { q _ { i } } } = 0 .
$$

As $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { \Pi } } ^ { H }$ is a known function of $q _ { i } , \mathbf { \boldsymbol { \phi } } _ { i }$ and $t$ $\partial H / \partial q _ { i }$ and $\partial H / \partial { \pmb \phi } _ { i }$ are known functions of $q _ { i } , \mathbf { \boldsymbol { \beta } } _ { i } ,$ and $t .$ .These equations are now considered a system of partial differential equations for $\pmb { n }$ unknown functions $\mathbf { \nabla } \phi _ { i }$ ，that is, for $\pmb { n }$ unknown functions of $q _ { i }$ and $t .$ Suppose that the $\mathbf { \nabla } \mathbf { \mathcal { P } } _ { \mathbf { i } }$ can be derived from a potential, that is,they satisfy

$$
\frac { \partial { p } _ { i } } { \partial q _ { k } } - \frac { \partial { p } _ { k } } { \partial q _ { i } } = 0 \qquad ( i , k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ;
$$

then there exists potential $W$ such that $\pmb { \mathscr { p } _ { i } } = \partial W / \partial q _ { i }$

Thus, the above partial differential equations for the $\beta _ { i }$ are reduced to a partial differential equation for $W$ ，that is, the second term in the parentheses of the equation

$$
\frac { \partial { p _ { i } } } { \partial t } + \left( \frac { \partial { H } } { \partial { q _ { i } } } + \sum _ { r } \frac { \partial { H } } { \partial { p _ { r } } } \frac { \partial { p _ { r } } } { \partial { q _ { i } } } \right) = 0
$$

is the partial derivative $\partial \overline { { H } } / \partial q _ { i }$ in which the $\pmb { \mathscr { p } _ { i } }$ are supposed to be functions of the $n + 1$ independent variables $q _ { i }$ and $t .$ By substituting $\rho _ { i } = \partial W / \partial q _ { i }$ in this equation we obtain

$$
\frac { \partial } { \partial q _ { i } } \left( \frac { \partial W } { \partial t } + \overline { { H } } \right) = 0 .
$$

Hence we determine $W$ $\mathrm { f r o m } \partial W / \partial t + \overline { { H } } = 0$ and let $W$ be the complete integral with constants of integration $\alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n }$ 、Even if we replace the $\alpha _ { i }$ by $\alpha _ { i } + d \alpha _ { i }$ in $W _ { : }$ ,the partial differential equation is satisfied; therefore

$$
\frac { \partial ^ { 2 } W } { \partial t \partial \alpha _ { i } } + \sum _ { r } \frac { \partial H } { \partial \beta _ { r } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } W } { \partial q _ { r } \partial \alpha _ { i } } = 0 ,
$$

because the $\alpha _ { i }$ are contained in $H ( q _ { i } \ p _ { i } , t )$ only through $\mathbf { \nabla } \phi _ { i }$ ,that is,only through $\partial W / \partial q _ { i }$ $( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ .Furthermore,from the first set of

Hamilton's canonical equations (4l), we have

$$
\left( \frac { \partial } { \partial t } + \sum _ { \tau } \frac { d q _ { \tau } } { d t } \frac { \partial } { \partial q _ { r } } \right) \frac { \partial W } { \partial \alpha _ { i } } = 0 ,
$$

or

$$
{ \frac { d } { d t } } { \frac { \partial W } { \partial \alpha _ { i } } } = 0 .
$$

Hence,

$$
{ \frac { \partial W } { \partial \alpha _ { i } } } = \beta _ { i } = { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } } .
$$

Thus Jacobi's theorem has been proved on hydrodynamical foundations. This proof is due to Einstein (1917).

# 1.14VARIATION OF CONSTANTS

In order to solve the canonical equations

$$
\frac { d q _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial p _ { i } } , \frac { d \phi _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { i } } ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

put

$$
H = H _ { 0 } + H _ { 1 } ,
$$

where the solution of

$$
{ \frac { d q _ { i } } { d t } } = { \frac { \partial H _ { 0 } } { \partial p _ { i } } } , { \frac { d \phi _ { i } } { d t } } = - { \frac { \partial H _ { 0 } } { \partial q _ { i } } } \qquad ( i = 1 , . . . , n )
$$

is assumed to be known. This solution is obtained by solving the Hamilton-Jacobi partial differential equation

$$
\frac { \partial W } { \partial t } + H _ { 0 } \Big ( q _ { i } , \frac { \partial W } { \partial q _ { i } } , t \Big ) = 0 .
$$

Let the solution be

$$
\begin{array} { r } { q _ { i } = q _ { i } ( t , \alpha _ { 1 } , . . . , \alpha _ { n } , \beta _ { 1 } , . . . , \beta _ { n } ) , } \\ { \dot { p } _ { i } = p _ { i } ( t , \alpha _ { 1 } , . . . , \alpha _ { n } , \beta _ { 1 } , . . . , \beta _ { n } ) , } \end{array}
$$

with $2 n$ integration constants $\alpha _ { 1 } , . . . , \alpha _ { n } , \beta _ { 1 } , . . . , \beta _ { n }$

Next,take the Hamiltonian function $H = H _ { 0 } + H _ { 1 }$ and consider $\alpha _ { i }$ and $\beta _ { i }$ to be no longer constant but to vary according to the differential equations that we have already derived.We employ our usual procedure to solve the differential equations in $\alpha _ { i }$ and $\beta _ { i }$ ; that is, we drop terms of small magnitude and integrate; then,inserting the neglected terms, we consider the integration constants so obtained as variable,and thus form a new system of differential equations for the variation of these constants. This principle is called the variation of constants, or sometimes the Lagrangian method.

Suppose that we have obtained the complete integral $W ( q _ { i } , \alpha _ { i } , t )$ of (53). If $\lVert \partial ^ { 2 } W / \partial q _ { i } \partial \alpha _ { j } \rVert \neq 0$ in

$$
{ \boldsymbol { \beta } } _ { i } = { \frac { \partial W } { \partial q _ { i } } } , \qquad { \boldsymbol { \beta } } _ { i } = - { \frac { \partial W } { \partial \alpha _ { i } } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

then we can solve for $\pmb { \mathscr { p } _ { i } }$ and $q _ { i }$ as functions of $\alpha _ { i } , \beta _ { i }$ and $t$ from (55). By the result in Section 1.12, the original differential equations (50) are transformed to

$$
{ \frac { d Q _ { i } } { d t } } = { \frac { \partial K } { \partial P _ { i } } } , { \frac { d P _ { i } } { d t } } = - { \frac { \partial K } { \partial Q _ { i } } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

by the transformation

$$
\dot { p _ { i } } = \frac { \partial W } { \partial q _ { i } } , \qquad P _ { i } = - \frac { \partial W } { \partial Q _ { i } } , \qquad K = H + \frac { \partial W } { \partial t } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

If $H = H _ { 0 } ;$ ，then the solution is

$$
P _ { i } = \beta _ { i } , \qquad Q _ { i } = \alpha _ { i } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

If we substitute

$$
K = H + { \frac { \partial W } { \partial t } } = H - H _ { 0 } = H _ { 1 }
$$

for $\pmb { K }$ , then we obtain

$$
\frac { d \alpha _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H _ { 1 } } { \partial \beta _ { i } } , \frac { d \beta _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H _ { 1 } } { \partial \alpha _ { i } } ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

If $H = H _ { 0 }$ ，then $\alpha _ { i }$ and $\beta _ { i }$ are constants.The equations (56） give the variation of these constants. $H _ { 1 }$ is taken in general to be small compared with $H _ { 0 }$ ; it is called the perturbation.

In the foregoing we have considered the variation of canonical constants. Let $A _ { 1 } , A _ { 2 } , . . . , A _ { 2 n }$ ，be any set of independent integration constants that are functions of the canonical constants

$$
\alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n } , \beta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \beta _ { n } .
$$

Then

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { d A _ { r } } { d t } = \displaystyle \sum _ { i } \frac { \partial A _ { r } } { \partial \alpha _ { i } } \frac { d \alpha _ { i } } { d t } + \sum _ { i } \frac { \partial A _ { r } } { \partial \beta _ { i } } \frac { d \beta _ { i } } { d t } } \\ & { \quad = \displaystyle \sum _ { i } \left( \frac { \partial A _ { r } } { \partial \alpha _ { i } } \frac { \partial K } { \partial \beta _ { i } } - \frac { \partial A _ { r } } { \partial \beta _ { i } } \frac { \partial K } { \partial \alpha _ { i } } \right) } \\ & { \quad = \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \frac { \left( \partial A _ { r } \right)} { \partial \alpha _ { i } } \frac { \partial K } { \partial A _ { s } } \frac { \partial A _ { s } } { \partial \beta _ { i } } - \frac { \partial A _ { r } } { \partial \beta _ { i } } \frac { \partial K } { \partial A _ { s } } \frac { \partial A _ { s } } { \partial \alpha _ { i } }  } \\ & { \quad \quad = \displaystyle \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \frac { \partial K } { \partial A _ { s } } \left( A _ { r } , A _ { s } \right) , } \end{array}
$$

where $( A _ { r } , A _ { s } )$ is the Poisson bracket.Hence

$$
\frac { d A _ { \tau } } { d t } = \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \left( A _ { r } , A _ { s } \right) \frac { \partial H _ { 1 } } { \partial A _ { s } } \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , 2 n ) .
$$

In the usual perturbation calculation in celestial mechanics we take, for example, the six integration constants in the elliptic motion of a planet around the sun - neglecting the perturbations of the orbit due to a third body. The six integration constants $A _ { r }$ which we take are the semimajor axis，eccentricity，longitude of the perihelion，orbital inclination to the fundamental plane, longitude of the ascending node, and epoch of the perihelion passage； these are called the Keplerian elements. These Keplerian elements are functions of the canonical constants in the Keplerian eliptic motion. The perturbing Hamiltonian $H _ { 1 }$ is taken to represent perturbations due to the action of other bodies or due to the derivation of the figure of the attracting body from a spherical mass distribution. In general, $H _ { 1 }$ is multiplied by the perturbing mass and hence is small compared with the solar mass,in the order of one in several thousands. $H _ { 1 }$ is also small when the perturbing body is much farther away than the attracting body.

A similar consideration is important also in quantum mechanics. When we superpose the effect of an electromagnetic feld or a light quantum or some other particle on the motion of an electron around an atomic nucleus, the superposed effect is considered a perturbation added to the attraction between the electron and the nucleus. The equation of motion in the unperturbed case, that is, the Schrodinger equation or the Dirac equation,is integrated. When the perturbation is taken into account, the integration constants are supposed to vary;we obtain the variation by solving the differential equations for the perturbation (Corson, 1951).

In celestial mechanics and quantum mechanics we can reach a better approximation by including a part of the perturbation in the first integration and applying the principle of the variation of constants to the remaining part.The equations of motion in the perturbation calculations of celestial mechanics can be solved to a high degree of approximation because of the smallness of the perturbing masses. But in quantum mechanics there already appears in the second approximation a trace of the nonconvergence peculiar to this method. Even in celestial mechanics there is a serious difficulty in that the method does not lead to uniform convergence, as will be shown in later chapters.

# 1.15LIOUVILLE's THEOREM

The Lagrangian function $L$ in the Hamiltonian $\begin{array} { r } { H = \sum _ { i } { ( \partial L / \partial { \dot { q } } _ { i } ) } { \dot { q } } _ { i } - L } \end{array}$ is divided into terms of homogeneous degree with regard to $\dot { q } _ { i }$ and is written $L = L _ { 2 } + L _ { 1 } + L _ { 0 } $ so that

$$
\sum _ { i } \ ( \partial L _ { 2 } / \partial \dot { q } _ { i } ) \dot { q } _ { i } = 2 L _ { 2 } , \qquad \sum _ { i } ( \partial L _ { 1 } / \partial \dot { q } _ { i } ) \dot { q } _ { i } = L _ { 1 } , \qquad \sum _ { i } ( \partial L _ { 0 } / \partial \dot { q } _ { i } ) \dot { q } _ { i }
$$

Then, $H = L _ { 2 } - L _ { 0 }$ If $_ { T }$ is homogeneous of degree 2 with regard to $\dot { q } _ { i }$ and if the external force function $U$ does not contain $\dot { q } _ { i }$ , then we can take $\begin{array} { r } { L _ { 2 } = T _ { \mathrm { { 2 } } } } \end{array}$ ， $\mathbf { \delta } _ { L _ { 0 } } = U$ in ${ \cal L } = T + U .$ Hence, $H = T - U .$

Scholars of the Paris school—Darboux，Bertrand，and others - studied dynamical systems that admit integrals homogeneous of degree 1 or 2 in the velocity.As an example Liouville considered a dynamical system with $_ { T }$ and $U$ of the form

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { 2 T = [ \phi _ { 1 } ( q _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \phi _ { n } ( q _ { n } ) ] [ A _ { 1 } ( q _ { 1 } ) \dot { q } _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { n } ( q _ { n } ) \dot { q } _ { n } ^ { 2 } ] , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { U = \frac \psi \phi = \frac { \psi _ { 1 } ( q _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \psi _ { n } ( q _ { n } ) } { \phi _ { 1 } ( q _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \phi _ { n } ( q _ { n } ) } . } } \end{array}
$$

In this case we can solve the problem without integrating the differential equations but with quadrature alone. This is called Liouville's theorem. We have

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { { \displaystyle H = T - U = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \phi [ A _ { 1 } ( q _ { 1 } ) \dot { q } _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { n } ( q _ { n } ) \dot { q } _ { n } ^ { 2 } ] - \frac { \psi } { \phi } } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { \displaystyle \dot { p } _ { i } = \frac { \partial T } { \partial \dot { q } _ { i } } = \phi A _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) \dot { q } _ { i } } . } } \end{array}
$$

This theorem can be extended to an irreversible dynamical system. Put $\begin{array} { r } { U = ( \psi / \phi ) + \sum _ { i } a _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) \dot { q } _ { i } } \end{array}$ ; then $\begin{array} { r } { L _ { 1 } = \sum _ { i } a _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) \dot { q } _ { i } } \end{array}$ But $L _ { 1 }$ dt is a perfect

differential; hence we can omit this term, according to the theorem by Birkhoff described in Section 1.1.

Take the Hamilton-Jacobi partial differential equation for the Liouville case

$$
\frac { 1 } { 2 \phi } \left[ \frac { ( \partial V / \partial q _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } } { A _ { 1 } ( q _ { 1 } ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \frac { ( \partial V / \partial q _ { n } ) ^ { 2 } } { A _ { n } ( q _ { n } ) } \right] - \frac { \psi _ { 1 } ( q _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \psi _ { n } ( q _ { n } ) } { \phi _ { 1 } ( q _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \phi _ { n } ( q _ { n } ) } = h ,
$$

that is,

$$
\sum _ { i } { \frac { 1 } { A _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) } } \left( { \frac { \partial V } { \partial q _ { i } } } \right) ^ { 2 } = 2 \sum _ { i } [ \psi _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) + h \phi _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) ] .
$$

Let the complete integral of the partial differential equation be $\begin{array} { r } { V = \sum _ { i } V _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) } \end{array}$ ， that is, the sum of functions each containing a single variable ${ { q } _ { i } }$ .Then,

$$
V _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) = \int d q _ { i } \sqrt { 2 A _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) [ \psi _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) + h \phi _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) + \alpha _ { i } ] } ,
$$

with the condition that $\alpha _ { 1 } + \alpha _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \alpha _ { n } = 0$ among the integration constants $\alpha _ { i }$ .As $\alpha _ { n } = { } - ( \alpha _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \alpha _ { n - 1 } )$ , the motion, according to (48), is given by

$$
\frac { \partial V } { \partial \alpha _ { j } } - \frac { \partial V } { \partial \alpha _ { n } } = - \beta _ { j } \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - 1 ) ,
$$

$$
t - t _ { 0 } = \frac { \partial V } { \partial h } , \qquad \frac { \partial V } { \partial q _ { i } } = \rlap / p _ { i } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Write

$$
F _ { j } ( q _ { j } ) = \sqrt { \psi _ { j } ( q _ { j } ) + h \phi _ { j } ( q _ { j } ) + \alpha _ { j } } \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - 1 ) ;
$$

then,

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \displaystyle \int \sqrt { \frac { A _ { 1 } ( q _ { 1 } ) } { 2 F _ { 1 } ( q _ { 1 } ) } } d q _ { 1 } + } & { \beta _ { 1 } = \displaystyle \int \sqrt { \frac { A _ { 2 } ( q _ { 2 } ) } { 2 F _ { 2 } ( q _ { 2 } ) } } d q _ { 2 } + \beta _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot } \\ { \displaystyle } & { \qquad = \int \sqrt { \frac { A _ { n - 1 } ( q _ { n - 1 } ) } { 2 F _ { n - 1 } ( q _ { n - 1 } ) } } d q _ { n - 1 } + \beta _ { n - 1 } } \\ { \displaystyle } & { \qquad = \int \sqrt { \frac { A _ { n } ( q _ { n } ) } { 2 F _ { n } ( q _ { n } ) } } d q _ { n } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
t - t _ { 0 } = \int \phi _ { 1 } ( q _ { 1 } ) { \sqrt { { \frac { A _ { 1 } ( q _ { 1 } ) } { 2 F _ { 1 } ( q _ { 1 } ) } } } } d q _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \int \phi _ { n } ( q _ { n } ) { \sqrt { { \frac { A _ { n } ( q _ { n } ) } { 2 F _ { n } ( q _ { n } ) } } } } d q _ { n } .
$$

Thus we obtain the solution by taking $V$ as the sum of functions each containing a single variable $q _ { i }$ .Such a procedure is called the separation of variables; and most practical problems in dynamics can be solved by this means. Examples are Keplerian elliptic motion， the one-body problem in general relativity theory, the motion of a particle attracted by two fixed mass centers, the Stark effect in the classical quantum theory, the Sommerfeld theory on the fine structure of spectral lines due to the relativity shift,and the nonperturbed quantum-mechanical Kepler problem.Vinti's treatment of the motion of an earth satellite in the gravitational field of the distorted earth is another example (see Section 2.21).Robertson (l928) and Eisenhardt (1934) applied the separation of variables to the Schrodinger equation. In the following we enumerate a few examples. Such motions have an interesting bearing on quasiperiodic functions and almost periodic functions,as will be seen in the next chapter (Wintner,1938).

For the motion of a third body around two fixed centers with masses m and $m ^ { \prime }$ we employ elliptic coordinates (Charlier,l906,1906a;Hiltebeitel, 1911;Badaljan, 1934;see also Section 2.24):

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \lambda = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( r + r ^ { \prime } ) , \qquad \mu = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( r - r ^ { \prime } ) , } \end{array}
$$

where $\pmb { r }$ and $\pmb { r } ^ { \prime }$ denote the distances of the third body from the two fixed centers,which are in turn separated by a distance $2 c$ Then

$$
2 T = ( \lambda ^ { 2 } - \mu ^ { 2 } ) \bigg ( \frac { \dot { \lambda } ^ { 2 } } { \lambda ^ { 2 } - c ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \dot { \mu } ^ { 2 } } { c ^ { 2 } - \mu ^ { 2 } } \bigg ) ,
$$

$$
U = \frac { 1 } { \lambda ^ { 2 } - \mu ^ { 2 } } [ ( m + m ^ { \prime } ) \lambda - ( m - m ^ { \prime } ) \mu ] .
$$

The problem studied by Weierstrass(1894) of the geodesics on the surface of an ellipsoid is a special case of this problem.

The motion of an electron with mass $_ m$ and electric charge $- e$ in the Coulomb feld of an atomic nucleus of charge $z _ { e }$ superposed on the uniform electrostatic field of intensity $\pmb { { \cal E } }$ can be described, if we suppose that the atomic nucleus is at rest and denote the distance to the electron by $r _ { \mathrm { { : } } }$ ,by the Hamiltonian function (Schwarzschild, 19l6; Epstein, 1916, 1919, 1922)

$$
H = { \frac { m } { 2 } } \left( { \dot { x } } ^ { 2 } + { \dot { y } } ^ { 2 } + { \dot { z } } ^ { 2 } \right) - { \frac { e ^ { 2 } Z } { r } } + e E .
$$

Transform to parabolic coordinates by

$$
z + i r = { \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } ( \xi + i \eta ) ^ { 2 } , \qquad x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } = r ^ { 2 } ,
$$

or by

$x = \xi \eta \cos { \phi } , \qquad y = \xi \eta \sin { \phi } , \qquad z = \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ( \xi ^ { 2 } - \eta ^ { 2 } ) , \qquad r = \xi \eta ;$ then,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle T = \frac { m } { 2 } \{ ( \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } ) ( \dot { \xi } ^ { 2 } + \dot { \eta } ^ { 2 } ) + \xi ^ { 2 } \eta ^ { 2 } \dot { \phi } ^ { 2 } \} , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle U = \frac { 2 e ^ { 2 } Z } { \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } e E ( \xi ^ { 2 } - \eta ^ { 2 } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

The motion of an electron with charge $- e$ around an atomic nucleus of charge $Z e$ was treated in accordance with the special relativity theory by Sommerfeld (1916, 1924),and by Gleich (1926):

$$
T = c ^ { 2 } ( m - m _ { 0 } ) = c ^ { 2 } m _ { 0 } \Big ( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - \beta ^ { 2 } } } - 1 \Big ) ,
$$

and

$$
H = m _ { 0 } c ^ { 2 } \Big ( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - \beta ^ { 2 } } } - 1 \Big ) - \frac { e ^ { 2 } Z } { r } ,
$$

where

$$
m = \frac { m _ { 0 } } { \sqrt { 1 - \beta ^ { 2 } } } , \qquad \beta = \frac { v } { c } ,
$$

and $m _ { 0 }$ denotes the rest mass of the electron and $^ { c }$ the velocity of light.

# 1.16STACKEL's THEOREM‘

Suppose that a Hamilton-Jacobi equation

$$
H { \Big ( } q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { n } , { \frac { \partial V } { \partial q _ { 1 } } } , . . . , { \frac { \partial V } { \partial q _ { n } } } { \Big ) } = h
$$

can be integrated by the separation of variables, so that

$$
V = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } V ^ { ( i ) } { \left( q _ { i } \right) } .
$$

Then (58) takes the form

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \sum _ { k } A _ { k } { \Big ( } { \frac { \partial V } { \partial q _ { k } } } { \Big ) } ^ { 2 } - ( U + h ) = 0 .
$$

How shall we write $A _ { k }$ and $U$ as functions of $q _ { i }$ in order to solve the problem by the separation of variables in the form (59)?

Denote, for convenience, $\partial V / \partial q _ { k } = V _ { k } ,$ where $V _ { k }$ is a function of $q _ { k }$ only.Then (60） becomes

$$
\sum _ { k } A _ { k } V _ { k } ^ { 2 } = 2 ( U + h ) .
$$

The complete integral $V$ of this equation is a function of $q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { n }$ and $\alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n }$ .Differentiate (61） with respect to $\alpha _ { k }$ ：

$$
A _ { 1 } \frac { \partial ( V _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) } { \partial \alpha _ { k } } + A _ { 2 } \frac { \partial ( V _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } ) } { \partial \alpha _ { k } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { n } \frac { \partial ( V _ { n } ^ { 2 } ) } { \partial \alpha _ { k } } = 2 \frac { \partial h } { \partial \alpha _ { k } } \quad ( k = 1 . 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

As $V$ is the complete integral，we have $\Delta = \lVert \partial ^ { 2 } V / \partial q _ { i } \partial \alpha _ { j } \rVert \neq 0$ But $\Delta _ { 2 } = \| \partial ( V _ { j } ^ { 2 } ) / \partial \alpha _ { i } \| = 2 ^ { n } V _ { 1 } V _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot V _ { n } \Delta$ Because $\Delta \neq 0$ ，we have $\Delta _ { 2 } \neq 0$ Hence we can solve the linear simultaneous equations (62) for $A _ { 1 } , . . . , A _ { n }$ ：

$$
A _ { i } = \frac { 2 } { \Delta _ { 2 } } \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial h } { \partial \alpha _ { j } } \frac { \partial \Delta _ { 2 } } { \partial [ \partial ( V _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) / \partial \alpha _ { j } ] } \cdot
$$

Then (61） takes the form

$$
U = - h + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { V _ { j } ^ { 2 } } { \Delta _ { 2 } } \frac { \partial \Delta _ { 2 } } { \partial [ \partial ( V _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) / \partial \alpha _ { j } ] } \frac { \partial h } { \partial \alpha _ { j } } \cdot
$$

Now

$$
\frac { 1 } { \Delta _ { 2 } } \frac { \partial \Delta _ { 2 } } { \partial [ \partial ( V _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) / \partial \alpha _ { j } ] }
$$

is a minor of $\Delta _ { 2 }$ with regard to $\partial ( V _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) / \partial \alpha _ { j }$ and

$$
\Delta _ { 2 } = \sum _ { j } \frac { \partial ( V _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) } { \partial \alpha _ { j } } \frac { \partial \Delta _ { 2 } } { \partial [ \partial ( V _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) / \partial \alpha _ { j } ] } \cdot
$$

Hence

$$
U = \sum _ { j } \sum _ { i } \left[ V _ { i } ^ { 2 } \frac { \partial h } { \partial \alpha _ { j } } - h \frac { \partial ( V _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) } { \partial \alpha _ { j } } \right] \frac { 1 } { \Delta _ { 2 } } \frac { \partial \Delta _ { 2 } } { \partial [ \partial ( V _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) / \partial \alpha _ { j } ] } .
$$

We have made no assumption in (63) and (64) other than that $V$ is the complete integral. Assume now that the problem can be solved by the separation of variables in the form (59). Then, because of (61), $V _ { i }$ is a function of $q _ { i }$ only. As $\Delta _ { 2 } \neq 0$ , we can solve (62), and the result is expressed in the form (63).The derivative $\partial ( V _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) / \partial \alpha _ { j }$ is a function of $q _ { i }$ only. Thus,

$$
\Delta _ { 2 } = \| \phi _ { i j } ( q _ { i } ) \| ,
$$

where $\phi _ { i j } ( q _ { i } ) = \partial ( V _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) / \partial \alpha _ { j } ,$ Put

$$
\psi _ { i j } ( q _ { i } ) = V _ { i } ^ { 2 } \frac { \partial h } { \partial \alpha _ { j } } - h \frac { \partial ( V _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) } { \partial \alpha _ { j } } ,
$$

which is a function of $q _ { i }$ only; then we obtain the following

Theorem: In order that

$$
H = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i } A _ { i } \bigg ( \frac { \partial V } { \partial q _ { i } } \bigg ) ^ { 2 } - U = h
$$

can be solved by the separation of variables,there must exist two sets of single-valued finite continuous functions $\phi _ { i j } ( q _ { i } )$ and $\psi _ { i j } ( q _ { i } )$ , such that $\Delta _ { 2 }$ ， in (65), formed with these functions does not vanish; that any $\phi _ { i j } ( q _ { i } )$ does not change its sign;and that $A _ { i }$ and $U$ are of the forms

$$
A _ { i } = \frac { 2 } { \Delta _ { 2 } } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial h } { \partial \alpha _ { j } } \frac { \partial \Delta _ { 2 } } { \partial \phi _ { i j } } , \qquad U = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \psi _ { i j } ( q _ { i } ) \frac { 1 } { \Delta _ { 2 } } \frac { \partial \Delta _ { 2 } } { \partial \phi _ { i j } } .
$$

This is called Stackel’s theorem (Stackel,1905; Kneser,1894; Ahrens,1895). Substitute (63) and (64) in (60).Referring to the relation

$$
\sum _ { i } \frac { 1 } { \Delta _ { 2 } } \frac { \partial \Delta _ { 2 } } { \partial \phi _ { i j } } \phi _ { i j } = 1 ,
$$

we have

$$
\frac { 1 } { \Delta _ { 2 } } \sum _ { i } \sum _ { j } \frac { \partial \Delta _ { 2 } } { \partial \phi _ { i j } } \left[ \left( \frac { \partial V } { \partial q _ { i } } \right) ^ { 2 } \frac { \partial h } { \partial \alpha _ { j } } - \psi _ { i j } ( q _ { i } ) - \frac { h } { n } \phi _ { i j } \right] = 0 .
$$

Hence,

$$
\left( \frac { \partial V } { \partial q _ { i } } \right) ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { j } ^ { n } \frac { 1 } { \partial h / \partial \alpha _ { j } } \left[ \psi _ { i j } ( q _ { i } ) \ : + \ : \frac { h } { n } \ : \phi _ { i j } ( q _ { i } ) \right] ,
$$

or

$$
V = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \int \sqrt { \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left\{ \left[ \psi _ { i j } ( q _ { i } ) + \frac { h } { n } \phi _ { i j } ( q _ { i } ) \right] \bigg / \frac { \partial h } { \partial \alpha _ { j } } \right\} } d q _ { i } .
$$

This $V$ satisfies (6l)，and contains the independent variables $\alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n }$ (independent because $\Delta _ { 2 } \neq 0$ ).Hence,the solution of our problem is

$$
\frac { \partial V } { \partial \alpha _ { k } } = \nu _ { k } t - \beta _ { k } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \int \frac { ( 1 / 2 n ) ( \partial h / \partial \alpha _ { k } ) \phi _ { i k } ( q _ { i } ) \ d q _ { i } } { \sqrt { \sum _ { j = i } ^ { n } [ \psi _ { i j } ( q _ { i } ) + ( h / n ) \phi _ { i j } ( q _ { i } ) ] } } ,
$$

where $\nu _ { k } = \partial h / \partial \alpha _ { k } \mathrm { a n d } \beta _ { k }$ are the necessary $\pmb { n }$ integration constants. Putting

$$
\Phi _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) \equiv 4 n ^ { 2 } \biggl [ \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \psi _ { i j } ( q _ { i } ) + \frac { h } { n } \phi _ { i j } ( q _ { i } ) \biggr ] ,
$$

we obtain

$$
d t = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { \frac { \phi _ { i k } ( q _ { i } ) d q _ { i } } { \sqrt { \Phi _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) } } } \qquad ( k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

If we write $x _ { k } = \nu _ { k } t - \beta _ { k } = ( \partial h / \partial \alpha _ { k } ) t - \beta _ { k }$ ，then $q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , \ldots , q _ { n }$ are expressed as functions of $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ ，which, in turn,are linear in $t$ In particular,we can write

$$
\Phi _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) = ( q _ { i } - a _ { i } ) ( b _ { i } - q _ { i } ) \Psi _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) ,
$$

where $\phi _ { i j } ( q _ { i } )$ and $\Psi _ { i } ( q _ { i } )$ are single-valued, finite, and continuous in $a _ { i } \\\leqq _ { i } \\\\leqq b _ { i } ,$ and $\phi _ { i j } ( q _ { i } )$ does not change its sign. The function $\Psi _ { i } ( q _ { i } )$ is always positive and never becomes zero. The determinant $\| \phi _ { i j } ( \dot { q } _ { i } ) \|$ is always positive or always negative and never becomes zero.In such a case

$$
x _ { j } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \int _ { a _ { i } } ^ { b _ { i } } \frac { \phi _ { i j } ( q _ { i } ) \ d q _ { i } } { \sqrt { ( q _ { i } - { a _ { i } } ) ( b _ { i } - { q _ { i } } ) \Psi _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) } } ,
$$

and $q _ { i }$ is single-valued, finite, and continuous, and has $\pmb { n }$ fold period

$$
2 \omega _ { i 1 } , 2 \omega _ { i 2 } , . . . , 2 \omega _ { i n } ,
$$

where

$$
\omega _ { i j } = \int _ { a _ { i } } ^ { b _ { i } } \frac { \phi _ { i j } ( q _ { i } ) ~ d q _ { i } } { \sqrt { ( q _ { i } - a _ { i } ) ( b _ { i } - q _ { i } ) \Psi _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) } } \cdot
$$

Note that a dynamical system that can be integrated by Liouville's theorem can be integrated by the separation of variables according to Stäckel's theorem；however, the converse is only true for dynamical systems with two degrees of freedom (Rao,1935,1935a; Vrkljan, 1929, 1930).

In quantum mechanics the separation of variables has an important bearing on the quantization. From ${ \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { \mathrm { { i } } } = \partial V / \partial q _ { i }$ the action

$$
V _ { i } = \int \not p _ { i } d q _ { i } = \int \not p _ { i } \dot { q } _ { i } d t
$$

is computed by integrating over a closed curve in the variable $q _ { i }$ and quantization is applied to the action itself. Write $V _ { i }$ as ${ { J } _ { i } }$ and put $J _ { i } = n _ { i } h$ with Planck's constant $h$ .The constant $n _ { i }$ is a positive integer. We assume that there is a limit function of $\begin{array} { r } { J = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } J _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) } \end{array}$ as $\pmb { n }$ approaches infinity. The integration constant $\alpha _ { i }$ measures the energy of the quantum state, and $\beta _ { i }$ measures the instantaneous electron configuration in the atomic system defined by the values of $\alpha _ { i }$ . The $_ { n }$ variables $\alpha _ { i }$ are not independent, as the total energy of the system is fixed. Let $\nu _ { i }$ be the frequency and $E _ { i } = \nu _ { i } J _ { i }$ be the energy of the ith quantum state according to Bohr, then the total energy is $\begin{array} { r } { E = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } E _ { i } } \end{array}$ . Transform to the conjugate variables ${ \ w } _ { i }$ and $J _ { i }$ by

$$
w _ { i } = \frac { \partial V } { \partial J _ { i } } , \qquad \ p _ { i } = \frac { \partial V } { \partial q _ { i } } ;
$$

then $q _ { i }$ is a multiply periodic function with period $2 \pi$ for each ${ \boldsymbol { \varpi } } _ { i }$ . As we obtain these variables in the form

$$
w _ { i } = \nu _ { i } t - \beta _ { i } , \qquad \nu _ { i } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial J _ { i } } ,
$$

$q _ { i }$ is expressed in multiple Fourier series

$$
q _ { i } = \sum _ { j _ { 1 } , . . . , j _ { n } } C _ { j _ { 1 } j _ { 2 } . . . j _ { n } } ^ { ( i ) } \exp { [ 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } ( j _ { 1 } w _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + j _ { n } w _ { n } ) ] } ,
$$

where $C _ { j _ { 1 } j _ { 2 } . . . j _ { n } } ^ { ( i ) }$ is a function of $J _ { 1 } , J _ { 2 } , \ldots , J _ { n }$ only. Poincaré calls the variable ${ \boldsymbol { \ w } } _ { i }$ the angular variable and $J _ { i }$ the linear variable. In quantum mechanics $J _ { i }$ is called the action variable. Write $E _ { j } - E _ { i } = h \nu _ { i j } $ according to Einstein; this is the energy of the light quantum absorbed or emitted by the transition from the ith quantum state to the jth. This is the quantum theory of radiation of Bohr and Einstein (Bohr,l923,l924；Kramers, 1920).Kneser (l921） has discussed the separation of variables as regards the quantization according to Sommerfeld (1916), Schwarzschild (1916) and Epstein (1916, 1919).

# 1.17CONDITION FOR SEPARABILITY

The condition for the separability of variables in the Hamiltonian equations has been obtained by Morera (l881） for $n = 2$ and by

Dall'Aqua （1908,1912） for $\mathscr { n } = 3$ Liouville's and Stäckel's cases are concrete examples.Both theorems are concerned with the existence of integrals that are quadratic in the momenta (cf. Liouville,1895; Levi-Civita,1896;Painlevé,1897;Fues,1925;Weinacht, 1928).

In order that the solution of the Hamilton-Jacobi equation

$$
H ( { \boldsymbol { p } } _ { 1 } , . . . , { \boldsymbol { p } } _ { n } , { \boldsymbol { q } } _ { 1 } , . . . , { \boldsymbol { q } } _ { n } ) = h , \qquad { \boldsymbol { p } } _ { i } = { \frac { \partial V } { \partial q _ { i } } } ,
$$

can be written in the form $\begin{array} { r } { V = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } V _ { i } , } \end{array}$ , it is necessary and sufficient that

$$
{ \frac { d { \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { i } } { d q _ { j } } } = 0 \qquad ( i \neq j )
$$

for all combinations of $i , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ,$ in which $\mathbf { \nabla } \mathbf { \mathcal { P } } _ { i }$ is considered a function of $q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { n }$ . Under this condition

$$
\frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { i } } + \frac { \partial H } { \partial p _ { i } } \frac { \partial p _ { i } } { \partial q _ { i } } = 0 .
$$

If none of the $\partial H / \partial \mathbf { \phi } _ { i }$ vanish, then

$$
\frac { \partial { p _ { i } } } { \partial q _ { i } } = - \frac { \partial { H } } { \partial q _ { i } } \bigg / \frac { \partial { H } } { \partial { p _ { i } } } .
$$

Hence the condition for the separability of variables is

$$
{ \frac { d } { d q _ { j } } } \left( { \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { i } } } { \Big / } { \frac { \partial H } { \partial \not p _ { i } } } \right) = 0 \qquad ( i \not = j ) ,
$$

where

$$
\frac { d } { d q _ { j } } = \frac { \partial } { \partial q _ { j } } + \frac { \partial p _ { j } } { \partial q _ { j } } \frac { \partial } { \partial p _ { j } } .
$$

By using (74) for $\partial { \pmb \rho } _ { i } / \partial q _ { i }$ we have as the necessary and sufficient condition for the separability of variables that $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { \Pi } } ^ { H }$ should satisfy the $n ( n - 1 ) / 2$ equations

$$
\frac { \partial H } { \partial \boldsymbol { \beta } _ { i } } \frac { \partial H } { \partial \boldsymbol { \phi } _ { j } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } H } { \partial q _ { i } \partial q _ { j } } - \frac { \partial H } { \partial \boldsymbol { \phi } _ { i } } \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { j } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } H } { \partial q _ { i } \partial \boldsymbol { \phi } _ { j } } - \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { i } } \frac { \partial H } { \partial \boldsymbol { \phi } _ { j } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } H } { \partial \boldsymbol { \phi } _ { i } \partial q _ { j } } + \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { i } } \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { j } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } H } { \partial p _ { i } \partial p _ { j } } =
$$

Let

$$
T = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { r , s } a _ { r s } \dot { q } _ { r } \dot { q } _ { s }
$$

and let the contravariant quantity to $a _ { r s }$ be $a ^ { s r }$ ; then,

Now insert

$$
\mathfrak { T } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { r , s } a ^ { r s } \phi _ { r } \phi _ { s } , \quad \quad H = \mathfrak { T } - U .
$$

$$
{ \dot { q } } _ { i } = { \frac { \partial { \mathfrak { T } } } { \partial \rho _ { i } } } , \qquad \left( { \frac { \partial { \mathfrak { T } } } { \partial q _ { i } } } \right) _ { p } = - \left( { \frac { \partial T } { \partial q _ { i } } } \right) _ { \dot { q } }
$$

in (75)and put the coefficients of the fourth, second,and zero-th degree terms equal to zero; then

$$
\frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial \mathfrak { p } _ { j } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \mathfrak { T } } { \partial { q } _ { i } \partial { q } _ { j } } - \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial \mathfrak { p } _ { i } } \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial { q } _ { j } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \mathfrak { T } } { \partial { q } _ { i } \partial \mathfrak { p } _ { j } } - \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial { q } _ { i } } \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial \mathfrak { p } _ { j } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \mathfrak { T } } { \partial \mathfrak { p } _ { i } \partial { q } _ { j } } + \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial { q } _ { i } } \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial { q } _ { j } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \mathfrak { T } } { \partial \mathfrak { p } _ { i } \partial \mathfrak { p } _ { j } } = 0 ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial \beta _ { i } } \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial \beta _ { j } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } U } { \partial q _ { i } \partial q _ { j } } - \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial \beta _ { i } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \mathfrak { T } } { \partial q _ { i } \partial \beta _ { j } } \frac { \partial U } { \partial q _ { j } } - \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial \beta _ { j } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \mathfrak { T } } { \partial q _ { j } \partial \beta _ { i } } \frac { \partial U } { \partial q _ { i } } } \\ &  \phantom { \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial \beta _ { i } } \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial \beta _ { j } } \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial q _ { i } \partial q _ { j } } - \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial \beta _ { i } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \mathfrak { T } } { \partial q _ { i } \partial \beta _ { j } } \frac { \partial U } { \partial q _ { j } } - \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial \beta _ { j } } \frac { \partial U } { \partial q _ { i } } \frac { \partial U } { \partial q _ { i } } } \end{array}
$$

$$
{ \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } { \mathfrak { T } } } { \partial \beta _ { i } \ \partial \beta _ { j } } } { \frac { \partial U } { \partial q _ { i } } } { \frac { \partial U } { \partial q _ { j } } } = a ^ { i j } { \frac { \partial U } { \partial q _ { i } } } { \frac { \partial U } { \partial q _ { j } } } = 0 \qquad ( i \neq j ) .
$$

Equation (76) determines the form of $\mathfrak { T }$ ,and (77)and (78) determine $U _ { ☉ }$ Let us transform (76) first.Write

$$
\sigma _ { i j } = - \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial \beta _ { j } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \mathfrak { T } } { \partial \beta _ { i } \ \partial q _ { j } } + a ^ { i j } \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial q _ { j } } .
$$

As $\partial ^ { 2 } \mathfrak { T } / \partial p _ { i } \partial p _ { j } = a ^ { i j }$ , equation (76) becomes, when $_ i$ is fixed,

$$
\frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial \mathfrak { p } _ { i } } \left( \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial \mathfrak { p } _ { j } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \mathfrak { T } } { \partial { q } _ { i } \ \partial { q } _ { j } } - \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial { q } _ { j } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \mathfrak { T } } { \partial { q } _ { i } \ \partial { \mathfrak { p } } _ { j } } \right) + \frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial { q } _ { i } } { \sigma } _ { i j } = 0 .
$$

Here $\partial \mathfrak { T } / \partial \mathfrak { p } _ { i }$ is linear with respect to $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ ,and ${ \partial \mathfrak { T } } / { \partial q _ { i } }$ and $\sigma _ { i j }$ are quadratic with respect to $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ .In order that this formula shall hold identically,one of the latter two expressions must be divisible by ${ \partial \mathfrak { T } } / { \partial { p } _ { i } }$ .But

$$
\frac { \partial \mathfrak { T } } { \partial q _ { i } } = - \frac { \partial T } { \partial q _ { i } } = - \sum _ { r , s } \frac { \partial a _ { r s } } { \partial q _ { i } } \dot { q } _ { r } \dot { q } _ { s } .
$$

Hence,in order that $\partial \mathfrak { T } / \partial q _ { i }$ be divisible by $\partial \mathfrak { T } / \partial \mathfrak { p } _ { i }$ , we should have

$$
\frac { \partial a _ { r s } } { \partial q _ { i } } = 0 \qquad ( r , s \neq i ) .
$$

# Furthermore

$$
\sigma _ { i j } = { \dot { q } } _ { j } \sum _ { l } a ^ { i l } { \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } T } { \partial { \dot { q } } _ { l } \partial q _ { j } } } - a ^ { i j } { \frac { \partial T } { \partial q _ { j } } } .
$$

Hence, in order that this expression be divisible by ${ \partial \mathfrak { T } } / { \partial { p } _ { i } } = { { \dot { q } } _ { i } } ;$ the following must be true:

$$
a ^ { i j } \frac { \partial a _ { r s } } { \partial q _ { j } } = 0 , ~ \sum _ { l = 1 } ^ { n } a ^ { i l } \frac { \partial a _ { r l } } { \partial q _ { j } } - a ^ { i j } \frac { \partial a _ { r j } } { \partial q _ { j } } = 0 ,
$$

$$
\sum _ { l } a ^ { i l } { \frac { \partial a _ { j l } } { \partial q _ { j } } } - { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } a ^ { i j } { \frac { \partial a _ { j j } } { \partial q _ { j } } } = 0 \qquad ( j , r , s \neq i ; r , s \neq j ) .
$$

Thus either (79) or (80) must be valid.

In order that all expressions ${ \partial \mathfrak { T } } / { \partial q _ { i } }$ shall be divisible by ${ \partial \mathfrak { T } } / { \partial { p } _ { \mathfrak { i } } }$ the Christoffel symbol of the first kind must be zero, i.e.,

$$
[ \stackrel { i } { \prime } \bar { \jmath } ] = 0 ,
$$

or the Riemannian curvature tensor (Bianchi,1899, 1922,1924)

$$
F _ { r s , i j } = \frac { \partial } { \partial q _ { j } } \{ _ { s } ^ { r ~ i } \} - \frac { \partial } { \partial q _ { i } } \{ _ { s } ^ { r ~ j } \} + \sum _ { l } \left( \{ _ { l } ^ { r ~ i } \} \{ _ { s } ^ { l ~ j } \} - \{ _ { l } ^ { r ~ j } \} \{ _ { s } ^ { l ~ i } \} \right)
$$

should be zero, that is,

$$
{ \cal F } _ { r s , i j } = 0 .
$$

In other words, the Riemannian curvature of the Riemann space $\begin{array} { r } { d s ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { r , s } a _ { r s } d x _ { s } d x _ { r } } \end{array}$ should be zero,i.e., the space should be differentially transformed to a Euclidean $\pmb { n }$ -dimensional flat space. Several examples with $d s ^ { 2 }$ for the expressions of $_ { T }$ which satisfy either of these conditions were given by Levi-Civita (l904) and Dall'Aqua (l908，1912） (cf. Kneser,1921; Weinacht,1928; Fues,1925).Weinacht (1928) has given several separable examples and solved the problem by referring to elliptic coordinates.

In order that the wave equation in Schrodinger's wave mechanics,

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \sqrt { H } \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { i } } \left( \frac { H _ { i } } { \sqrt { H } } \frac { \partial \Psi } { \partial x _ { i } } \right) + k ^ { 2 } ( E - V ) \Psi = 0 ,
$$

$$
H = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } H _ { i } ( x _ { i } ) ,
$$

be solvable by the separation of variables in the form $\begin{array} { r } { \Psi = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \Psi _ { i } ( x _ { i } ) } \end{array}$ ，

it is necessary that the coefficients of $\partial ^ { \mathbf { 2 } \mathbf { 4 } \Gamma } / \partial x _ { i } ^ { 2 }$ and ${ \partial \Psi } / { \partial x _ { i } }$ differ by a factor which is a function of only a single variable $\pmb { x _ { i } }$ .Hence

$$
\frac { \sqrt { H } } { H _ { i } } \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { i } } \left( \frac { H _ { i } } { \sqrt { H } } \right)
$$

is a function of only a single variable $\pmb { x _ { i } }$ and

$$
H _ { i } = \sqrt { H } a _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { i - 1 } , x _ { i + 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) f _ { i } ( x _ { i } ) ,
$$

and the wave equation is of the form

$$
\sum _ { i } { \sqrt { H } } { \frac { a _ { i } } { \Psi } } { \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { i } } } \left( f _ { i } { \frac { \partial \Psi } { \partial x _ { i } } } \right) + k ^ { 2 } ( E - V ) = 0 .
$$

This equation has a solution containing $\pmb { n }$ constants $\alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n }$ beside $E$ . We construct

$$
\phi _ { i j } ( x _ { i } ) = \frac { \partial } { \partial \alpha _ { j } } \left[ \frac { 1 } { \Psi _ { i } } \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { i } } \left( f _ { i } \frac { \partial \Psi _ { i } } { \partial x _ { i } } \right) \right]
$$

by choosing $\alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n } \ s$ that $\| \phi _ { i j } ( { \pmb x } _ { i } ) \| \neq 0$ . Then the wave equation becomes

$$
\sum _ { i } { \sqrt { H } a _ { i } \phi _ { i j } } = - k ^ { 2 } \delta _ { 1 j }
$$

and ${ \sqrt { H } } a _ { i } = - k ^ { 2 } \phi ^ { i 1 }$ .Hence, $H _ { i } = - k ^ { 2 } f _ { i } ( x _ { i } ) \phi ^ { i 1 }$ where $\phi \phi ^ { i j }$ is the minor of $\left\| \phi _ { i j } \right\|$ and does not depend on $\pmb { x _ { i } }$ in the same way as $a _ { i }$ Hence ${ \sqrt { H } } \phi = c =$ constant. Thus we should have

$$
V = \sum _ { i } \phi ^ { i 1 } \chi _ { i } ( x _ { i } ) ,
$$

$$
\chi _ { i } = \alpha _ { 1 } \phi _ { i 1 } ( x _ { i } ) - \frac { 1 } { \Psi _ { i } } \frac { d } { d x _ { i } } \Big ( f _ { i } \frac { d \Psi _ { i } } { d x _ { i } } \Big ) ,
$$

or, by puting

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \Phi _ { i j } ( x _ { i } ) = - k ^ { 2 } f _ { i } ( x _ { i } ) ^ { - 1 } \phi _ { i j } ( x _ { i } ) , } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { F _ { i } ( x _ { i } ) = - k ^ { 2 } C ^ { - 1 / \eta } f _ { i } ( x _ { i } ) , } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { X _ { i } ( x _ { i } ) = - k ^ { 2 } f _ { i } ( x _ { i } ) \chi _ { i } ( x _ { i } ) , } } \end{array}
$$

it is necessary that

$$
\mathrm { ~  ~ \bar { ~ \psi ~ } { ~ \psi ~ } ~ } = \mathrm { ~  ~ \bar { ~ \psi ~ } { ~ \psi ~ } ~ } \psi = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \Phi ^ { i 1 } X _ { i } ( \dot { x } _ { i } ) , \qquad \sqrt { H } \Phi = \prod _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } ( F _ { i } ( x _ { i } ) ) ^ { - 1 } .
$$

To prove that this condition is sufficient, first note that

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \Phi ^ { i 1 } \Omega _ { i } ( x _ { i } ) = 0 ,
$$

where

$$
\Omega _ { i } ( x _ { i } ) = \frac { 1 } { F _ { i } \Psi _ { i } } \frac { d } { d x _ { i } } \left( F _ { i } \frac { d \Psi _ { i } } { d x _ { i } } \right) + k ^ { 2 } ( \alpha _ { 1 } \Phi _ { i 1 } - X _ { 1 } ) .
$$

This relation represents the circumstance that a vector $( \Psi _ { 1 } , \Psi _ { 2 } , . . . , \Psi _ { n } )$ is orthogonal to a vector $( \Phi ^ { 1 1 } , . . . , \Phi ^ { n 1 } )$ . It is known that the vector $( \Phi _ { 1 j } , . . . , \Phi _ { n j } )$ $( j = 2 , 3 , . . . , n )$ has such a property. Hence, the vector $( \Psi _ { 1 } , . . . , \Psi _ { n } )$ should be represented by a linear combination of the vectors $\Phi$ .We write the constant coefficients of this linear expression as $- k ^ { 2 } \alpha _ { 2 } , . . . , - k ^ { 2 } \alpha _ { n }$ ；then

$$
\Omega _ { i } ( x _ { i } ) = - k ^ { 2 } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { j } \Phi _ { i j } ( x _ { i } ) .
$$

Hence $\Psi _ { i }$ is determined by the following equation (Robertson, 1928; Brillouin,1926,1926a,1935; Eisenhardt,1934,1948,1949;Rao, 1935, 1935a; Kemble,1937):

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { F _ { i } ( x _ { i } ) } } { \frac { d } { d x _ { i } } } \left[ F _ { i } ( x _ { i } ) { \frac { d \Psi _ { i } } { d x _ { i } } } \right] + k ^ { 2 } \biggl [ \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { j } \Phi _ { i j } ( x _ { i } ) - X _ { i } ( x _ { i } ) \biggr ] \Psi _ { i } = 0 .
$$

The convergence of the solution in eigenfunction expansions in the separable cases of the Schrodinger equation has been proved by Titchmarsh (1955,1958).

# HAMILTONIAN MATRIX

# 1.18HAMILTONIAN MATRIX

Operations on a matrix obey the rules for a determinant. A matrix obtained by an interchange of the rows and the columns of a matrix $M$ is called a transposed matrix and denoted by $M ^ { \prime }$ . When the number of rows equals the number of columns, the array is called a square matrix and the number of rows or columns is called the order.A matrix whose diagonal elements are all 1 while the remaining elements are all O, is a unit matrix $\boldsymbol { \varepsilon }$ or $\epsilon .$ A matrix $M ^ { - 1 }$ such that $M M ^ { - 1 } = E$ is the inverse matrix of $M .$ .The determinant formed of a square matrix $M$ is denoted by det $M .$ Obviously det $\varepsilon = 1$ .A matrix such that det $M \neq 0$ is said to be nonsingular; otherwise, singular. A matrix whose elements are all zero is a null matrix $\omega$

Two fixed nonsingular matrices $\pmb { A }$ and $B$ satisfy the symmetry relation $( A H B ) ^ { \prime } = A H B$ for any symmetric matrix $H = H ^ { \prime }$ if and only if there exists a number $s \neq 0$ such that $\boldsymbol { A } ^ { \prime } = s \boldsymbol { B }$ Put $M ^ { \prime } = A ^ { \prime } B ^ { - 1 }$ ; then the condition $( A H B ) ^ { \prime } = A H B$ is equivalent to $M H = H M ^ { \prime }$ ,where $H = H ^ { \prime }$ is arbitrary and may be chosen to be $\pmb { \cal E }$ Hence $M = M ^ { \prime }$ .Now $M H = H M$ holds for any $H = H ^ { \prime }$ if and only if $M$ is of the form $s E$ This is evident if $M$ is a diagonal matrix. If it is not diagonal, then $M = M ^ { \prime }$ is transformed by means of an orthogonal matrix $R$ into a diagonal matrix $R M R ^ { - 1 }$ ，whereas $R$ transforms the set of all matrices $H = H ^ { \prime }$ into itself. Thus $R M R ^ { - 1 }$ is a diagonal matrix and is equal to $s E .$ Finally, $s \neq 0$ ，because $s E = M = M ^ { \prime } = A ^ { \prime } B ^ { - 1 }$ is the product of two non-singular matrices. Hence $A ^ { \prime } = s B$ ， $s \neq 0$ (Wintner, 1934; Williamson, 1936,1937,1939, 1940).

Consider a matrix composed of a null matrix $\omega$ of order $_ { n }$ and a unit matrix $\epsilon$ of order $\pmb { n }$ ，such as

$$
G = \binom { \omega } { \epsilon } \quad - \epsilon \biggr ) ,
$$

in contrast to

$$
E = \left( \begin{array} { l l } { { \epsilon } } & { { \omega } } \\ { { \omega } } & { { \epsilon } } \end{array} \right) ;
$$

then,

$$
G = - G ^ { - 1 } = - G ^ { \prime } .
$$

This is the matrix formed by the coefficients of & and $d$ in a bilinear covariant expression (Section 1.7) and is naturally nonsingular.

A system of $2 n$ ordinary differential equations of the first order that are linear and homogeneous and do not contain $t$ explicitly is canonical if and only if there exists a symmetric matrix $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { \Pi } } ^ { H }$ such that the differential equations can be written in the form

$$
G { \dot { x } } = H x .
$$

In fact,this is (up to the factor 2) the matrix of the $2 n$ -ary quadratic form representing the Hamiltonian function. Since $G ^ { - 1 } = - G ,$ we can write

$$
\dot { x } = - G H x , \qquad H = H ^ { \prime } .
$$

A nonsingular matrix $c$ independent of $t$ is called Hamiltonian if the transformation $\boldsymbol { x } = C \boldsymbol { y }$ transforms any differential system of this form to a differential system of the same form (Section 1.3).

If we substitute $\boldsymbol { x } = C \boldsymbol { y }$ in the above equation, we obtain $\dot { y } = - G K y .$ where $K = - G C ^ { - 1 } G H C$ since $G = - G ^ { - 1 }$ . Hence, $C$ is a Hamiltonian matrix if and only if $\kappa$ is the matrix of a $2 n$ ary quadratic form, that is, if and only if $K = K ^ { \prime }$ whenever $H = H ^ { \prime }$ . We can write $K = K ^ { \prime }$ in the form $( A H B ) ^ { \prime } = A H B$ by putting $A = C ^ { - 1 } G$ and $B = C G$ ，so that det $A \neq 0$ det $B \neq 0$ ,i.e., $A ^ { \prime } = s B$ Hence $C ^ { \prime } G C = s G$ ，

$$
| { \bf d e t } C | = | s | ^ { n } > 0 ,
$$

if we write $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ instead of $- s ^ { - 1 }$

If a matrix $C$ of order $2 n$ with real elements satisfies

$$
C ^ { \prime } G C = s G ,
$$

then it is said to be Hamiltonian (Wintner, l937, 1947;van Kampen and Wintner, 1936).When $s$ is a constant different from zero, $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ is called the multiplier of $C$ (van Kampen and Wintner, 1936, l938; Wintner, 1937). If det $G \neq 0$ ,then $| \mathsf { d e t } C | = | s | ^ { n }$ ·

The matrix

$$
K = - G C ^ { - 1 } G H C
$$

belonging to the transformed Hamiltonian function takes the form

$$
K = s ^ { - 1 } C ^ { \prime } H C .
$$

Thus the necessary and sufficient condition (83) for a Hamiltonian matrix $C$ is that the bilinear form in cogradient variables belonging to the matrix $G$ be a relative invariant of a nonsingular linear substitution if and only if the matrix of this substitution is a Hamiltonian matrix. The group of the Hamiltonian matrices, in contrast to the rotation and reflection groups of Euclidean space, cannot be characterized by the absolute invariance of a bilinear form (Section 4.2). For example, if

$$
C = \binom { \epsilon } { \omega } \ _ { - \epsilon } ^ { \omega } ) ,
$$

then $C ^ { \prime } G C = - G \neq G$ although $\left| \operatorname* { d e t } C \right| = \left| ( - 1 ) ^ { n } \right| = 1$

Since $s \neq 0$ ,a Hamiltonian matrix is nonsingular. If $c$ is Hamiltonian, then $C ^ { \prime }$ is also Hamiltonian and has the same multiplier.When $s = 1$ ， then $C$ and $C ^ { - 1 }$ have the same eigenvalues; that is, the roots of

$$
{ \bf d e t } \left( C - \lambda E \right) = 0
$$

and the roots of det $( C ^ { - 1 } - \lambda E ) = 0$ are the same.For, by the definition of a Hamiltonian matrix, $C ^ { \prime } = G C ^ { - 1 } G ^ { - 1 }$ or $G ^ { - 1 } C ^ { \prime } G = C ^ { - 1 }$ .Hence

$$
G ^ { - 1 } ( C ^ { \prime } - \lambda E ) G = C ^ { - 1 } - \lambda E .
$$

The totality of eigenvalues is called the spectrum; it may be discrete or continuous (Section 20.1).

Take a set of $_ m$ functions $Y ( y _ { 1 } , \dots , y _ { m } )$ and consider the square matrix $A = A ( Y )$ ，of order $m$ ，and a vector $B = B ( Y )$ . A vector is a matrix with a single column consisting of the vector components. Suppose that there exists a scalar function $g = g ( Y )$ satisfying

$$
B + A { \mathrm { ~ g r a d } } f = { \mathrm { g r a d } } g ,
$$

in the whole domain of the vector space $Y$ for any scalar function $f = f ( Y )$ .Then for all functions $f$ and for a given pair $\boldsymbol { A }$ and $B$ ，there exists a function $g$ satisfying (84) if and only if $B$ has the form of the gradient of a scalar function and $A$ has the form $s E _ { : }$ ，where $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ is a scalar independent of $\boldsymbol { Y }$ and $\boldsymbol { \varepsilon }$ is a unit matrix of order $\pmb { m }$ . It is assumed that $A$ ， $B$ and $f , g$ all have continuous partial derivatives of first and second order.

The sufficiency of these conditions is obvious. To prove their necessity: suppose that there exists a function $g$ for any function f. Choose $f = f ( Y )$ independent of $Y$ ; then,from (84), $B$ is a gradient. Hence,if we choose $f = f ( y _ { 1 } , \dots , y _ { m } )$ as an arbitrary polynomial $q ( y _ { i } )$ of the ith vectorcomponent of $Y$ ,and denote by $A _ { i } = A _ { i } ( Y )$ the vector whose components are the elements of the ith column of $A = A ( Y )$ ，then the vector $q ( y _ { i } ) A _ { i } ( Y )$ is a gradient for every polynomial $q ( y _ { i } )$ . Hence $A ( Y )$ isa diagonal matrix, since the integrability condition is satisfied by a gradient, and the ith diagonal element of $A ( Y )$ is a function of $y _ { i }$ alone. Furthermore, since $A$ grad $f$ is a gradient for any $f ,$ we see - by choosing i and $j$ arbitrarily and putting $f ( Y ) = y _ { i } y _ { j }$ - that the diagonal elements of $A ( Y )$ are all equal and hence $A = s E$ for a constant $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$

# 1.19CANONICAL TRANSFORMATION

Put

$$
x _ { i } = q _ { i } , \qquad x _ { n + i } = p _ { i } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

and take a vector $X = ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 2 n } )$ ; then Hamilton's equations can be written

$$
G \dot { X } = \operatorname { g r a d } _ { x } H , \qquad H = H ( X , t ) ,
$$

where a dot denotes total differentiation with respect to the time $t _ { : }$ and the subscript of“grad’’ refers to the space in which the partial differentiations are performed.

Now transform the phase space $\boldsymbol { \cal X }$ for any fixed $t$ into a phase space $\pmb { Y }$ by means of

$$
Y = Y ( X ; t ) , \qquad Y = ( y _ { 1 } , \dots , y _ { 2 n } ) , \qquad X = ( x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { 2 n } ) .
$$

Assume that these $2 n$ functions have continuous partial derivatives of the second order considered as functions of $2 n$ variables and $t _ { : }$ ,and that the transformation builds a locally one-to-one correspondence between the two phase spaces for any fixed $t _ { : }$ ， the Jacobian of order $2 n$ being everywhere different from zero.A transformation satisfying these properties is called canonical if it transforms any canonical system (85) into a canonical system such that

$$
G \dot { Y } = \operatorname { g r a d } _ { Y } K , K = K ( Y , t ) .
$$

Let $c$ be the Jacobian matrix of the transformation (86) for a fixed $t _ { i }$ so that the jth element in the kth column of the nonsingular $2 n$ order square matrix $C$ is the partial derivative of $y _ { j }$ with respect to $\pmb { x } _ { k }$ .The matrix $c$ can be considered a function $C ( Y , t )$ of $\pmb { Y }$ and $t$ rather than a function of $\pmb { X }$ and $t .$ By direct differentiation we obtain

$$
\dot { Y } = C \dot { X } + Y _ { t } ,
$$

where the subscript $t$ refers to a partial differentiation with respect to $t$ furthermore

$$
X = X ( Y , t )
$$

is the inverse transformation of (86). Also,for any function $f ,$

$$
\operatorname { g r a d } _ { x } f = C ^ { \prime } \operatorname { g r a d } _ { Y } f ,
$$

where $C ^ { \prime }$ is the transposed matrix of $C$ ; finally,

$$
\mathbf { g r a d } _ { Y } Y _ { t } = C _ { t } C ^ { - 1 } .
$$

From (88) and (89) we see that (86),which is not assumed to be a constant transformation, transforms (85） into

$$
G C ^ { - 1 } ( \dot { Y } - Y _ { t } ) = C ^ { \prime } \operatorname { g r a d } _ { Y } H .
$$

Referring to the definition of $G$ ， we can put this equation in the form

$$
G { \dot { Y } } = G Y _ { t } - G C G C ^ { \prime } \operatorname { g r a d } _ { Y } H .
$$

Comparing this equation with (87),we see that (86) is a canonical transformation if and only if there exists a $K = K ( Y , t )$ for any $H ,$ such that

$$
G Y _ { t } - G C G C ^ { \prime } \operatorname { g r a d } _ { Y } H = \operatorname { g r a d } _ { Y } K ,
$$

where $H = H ( X , t )$ is expressed as a function of $\pmb { Y }$ and $t$ by (86a). Compare(91） with (84) by keeping $t$ fixed; then we see that (86) is a canonical transformation ifand only if the vector $B = G Y _ { t }$ is the gradient of a scalar function with respect to $Y$ for any fixed $t ,$ and that the matrix $A = - G C G C ^ { \prime }$ is of the form $s E$ for any fixed $t _ { : }$ where $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ is a scalar function independent of $\pmb { Y } _ { : }$ and $\boldsymbol { \varepsilon }$ is the unit matrix of order $2 n$ .Hence the transformation (86) is canonical if and only if there exist two scalars

$$
R = R ( Y , t ) , \qquad s = s ( t ) ,
$$

such that

$$
G Y _ { t } = \operatorname { g r a d } _ { Y } R , \quad \quad C ^ { \prime } G C = s G .
$$

If this condition is satisfied, then the Hamiltonian function for (87), because of (91), is

$$
K = s H + R .
$$

The first condition of (92) cannot be applied directly to a given transformation (86), since $\scriptstyle { R }$ is unknown. But it can be shown that there exists for a given transformation (86) a function $R$ satisfying this condition if and only if the matrix $C ^ { \prime } G C _ { \mathrm { { i } } }$ ，considered as a function of $\pmb { Y }$ and $t ,$ is independent of t.In fact, the function $R$ exists for a given transformation (86) satisfying this condition ifand only ifgrady $G Y _ { t }$ is a symmetric matrix at every point of the $( 2 n + 1 )$ -dimensional domain in question. Since we can verify that $\begin{array} { r } { \mathrm { g r a d } _ { \boldsymbol { Y } } G Y _ { t } = G \mathrm { g r a d } _ { \boldsymbol { Y } } Y _ { t } , } \end{array}$ we have grady $G Y _ { t } = G C _ { t } C ^ { - 1 }$ by (90). Hence there exists a function $R$ if and only if $G C _ { t } C ^ { - 1 }$ is a symmetric matrix, that is,is equal to the matrix $( G C _ { t } C ^ { - 1 } ) ^ { \prime }$ ; in other words, if and only if

$$
C _ { t } ^ { \prime } G C + C ^ { \prime } G C _ { t } \equiv ( C ^ { \prime } G C ) _ { t }
$$

is the null-matrix for any $\pmb { Y }$ and $t ,$ that is, ifand only if $C ^ { \prime } G C$ is a function of $\pmb { Y }$ alone.

The scalar $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ in the necessary and sufficient condition (92) is independent of the vector $\pmb { Y }$ in the phase space for any fixed t.
