This fact is due to (84).The pair of conditions (92) implies that the scalar $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ should be independent both of $\pmb { Y }$ and t.A transformation (86) can be shown to be canonical if and only if there exists an $s \neq 0$ independent of both $\pmb { Y }$ and $t ,$ such that the Jacobian matrix $C = C ( Y , t )$ of (86) is a Hamiltonian matrix for any $\pmb { Y }$ and $t$ with the constant $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ as the multiplier.This is because (86) is canonical if and only if (92) can be satisfed by a suitable choice of the functions $\pmb { R }$ and $\pmb { S } \cdot$ The first condition of (92) is satisfied if and only if $C ^ { \prime } G C$ is independent of $t$ ,that is, $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ is a constant by the second condition of (92), since $s \neq 0$ .Hence,a transformation (86) is canonical if and only if its Jacobian matrix $C = C ( Y , t )$ determines a linear sub-stitution under which the bilinear form in cogradient variables belonging to the skew-symmetric matrix $G$ is a relative invariant with a multiplier $s \neq 0$ and is independent both of $\pmb { Y }$ and ${ \pmb t } .$

It is clear from the definition of a canonical transformation that the set of canonical transformations defined in a common $( 2 n + 1 )$ -dimensional domain forms a group.If $R _ { 1 } = R _ { 1 } ( Y , t )$ belongs to a canonical transformation which is substituted into another canonical transformation characterized by $R _ { 2 } = R _ { 2 } ( Y , t )$ and $s _ { 2 } .$ ，then the function $\scriptstyle { R }$ characterizing the composite transformation is ${ \cal R } = s _ { 2 } { \cal R } _ { 1 } + { \cal R } _ { 2 }$ .If $\because C , R , s$ characterize a canonical transformation, then $C ^ { - 1 } , \ - R / s , 1 / s$ characterize the inverse transformation. The multiplier $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ is uniquely determined by the canonical transformation, while the function $R$ remains indeterminate up to an additive term which is a function of $t$ alone.

If (86）satisfies the first condition of (92) for any $t$ and $\mathbf { { \mathit { Y } } _ { i } }$ and the second condition for a fixed $t$ and any $Y _ { z }$ ，then (86) is a canonical transformation.For,if the first condition of (92) is satisfied for any $t$ and $Y _ { : }$ ， then $C ^ { \prime } G C$ is independentof $t$ ,so that the second condition of (92) holds for any $t$ and $Y$ if it holds for a single $t$ and any Y. Call a transformation (86) conseruative if it does not contain $t$ explicitly.If (86) is a contact transformation and $t _ { 0 }$ is a fixed value of $t$ ,then the conservative transformation defined by $Y = Y ( X , t _ { 0 } )$ is canonical. In fact, any conservative transformation satisfies the first condition of (92) if we put $R \equiv 0$ ; it also satisfies the second condition for any $t$ or no $t .$ 、Thus (86） need not be canonical even if the conservative transformation $Y = Y ( X , t _ { 0 } )$ is canonical for any fixed $t = t _ { 0 }$ (van Kampen and Wintner, 1936).

# 1.20PFAFF'S CRITERION

Put

$$
y _ { i } = v _ { i } , \qquad y _ { n + i } = u _ { i } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

in (86); then (86) is a canonical transformation ifand only if there exists a function $\scriptstyle { R }$ for which the Pfaffian (Section 1.7)

$$
s \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { p _ { i } d q _ { i } } - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { u _ { i } d v _ { i } } + R d t
$$

is a perfect differential by (86). Consider a Pfaffian

$$
{ \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } s X ^ { \prime } G d X - { \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } Y ^ { \prime } G d Y + R d t ,
$$

where an expression such as $Z ^ { \prime } A W$ denotes the bilinear form belonging to the square matrix $\pmb { A }$ and the vectors $z$ and $W _ { ; }$ ，so that $Z ^ { \prime } A W$ is

considered as the product of three matrices. Whether the transformation (86) be canonical or not, the difference of the two Pfaffians (94) and (95) is a perfect differential of the function

$$
s \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { p _ { i } q _ { i } } - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } u _ { i } v _ { i } .
$$

Now (95) is a perfect differential in virtue of (86) if and only if (86) is canonical.In fact,(95) becomes a Pfaffian

$$
{ \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } ( s X ^ { \prime } G - Y ^ { \prime } G C ) ~ d X + ( R - { \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } Y ^ { \prime } G Y _ { t } ) ~ d t
$$

by (86),where $\pmb { Y }$ and $\textstyle \boldsymbol { Y } _ { t }$ are considered as functions of $X$ and $t .$ This last Pfaffian is a perfect differential of a function of $2 n + 1$ variables $X$ and $t$ if and only if (i) the partial derivatives of the covariant vector

$$
{ \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } ( s X ^ { \prime } G - Y ^ { \prime } G C )
$$

with respect to $t$ is the covariant gradient of the scalar $R \mathrm { ~ - ~ } _ { 2 } ^ { 1 } Y ^ { \prime } G Y _ { t }$ with respect to the vector $X$ and (ii） the Stokes alternating derivative of this covariant vector with respect to the vector $X$ is identically zero. It can be shown that conditions (i） and (ii） are equivalent to the two conditions of (92).

Actually, condition (i) is satisfied if and only if

$$
\begin{array} { r } { - \frac 1 2 ( Y ^ { \prime } G C ) _ { t } = ( \mathrm { g r a d } _ { x } R ) ^ { \prime } - \frac 1 2 [ \mathrm { g r a d } _ { x } \left( Y ^ { \prime } G Y _ { t } \right) ] ^ { \prime } , } \end{array}
$$

or, when transposed,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { - \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( C ^ { \prime } G ^ { \prime } Y ) _ { t } = \operatorname { g r a d } _ { X } R - \frac { 1 } { 2 } C ^ { \prime } G Y _ { t } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } C _ { t } ^ { \prime } G Y , } \end{array}
$$

that is, if and only if

$$
C ^ { \prime } G Y _ { t } = C ^ { \prime } \mathrm { g r a d } _ { \mathrm { Y } } R .
$$

Since this can be writen in the form of the first condition of (92), condition (i) is equivalent to this first condition of (92). Furthermore, since an alternating derivative is transformed by any transformation (86) as a tensor,and since the alternating derivative of $X ^ { \prime } G$ with respect to $\ b X$ is $G$ by definition, then the alternating derivative of $Y ^ { \prime } G C$ with respect to $X$ is $C ^ { \prime } G C$ and condition (ii） is satisfied if and only if the matrix ${ \scriptstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ( s G - C ^ { \prime } G C )$ is identically zero.Hence,condition (ii） is equivalent to the second condition of (92) (van Kampen and Wintner 1936; Wintner 1947).

# I.2IPOLAR FACTORIZATION

A matrix with complex elements is called symplectic according to Weyl (1939). If two symplectic matrices $A = \left( a _ { j k } \right)$ and $B = ( b _ { j k } )$ are such that $a _ { j k } = b _ { j k }$ for all $j$ and $k$ ，then $A = B$ Let $\bar { A } = ( \bar { a } _ { j k } )$ be a conjugate matrix of $A ( a _ { j k } )$ such that $a _ { k j } = \bar { a } _ { j k } .$ ，where a bar means the complex conjugate, so that $\scriptstyle A ^ { \prime } = { \overline { { A } } }$ and $\overline { { A ^ { \prime } } } = A$ The matrix $\pmb { A }$ determines a bilinear form

$$
A = A ( x , y ) = \sum _ { k , j } a _ { j k } y _ { j } x _ { k }
$$

and

$$
A ( x _ { j } ) = { \frac { \partial A ( x , y ) } { \partial y _ { j } } } = \sum _ { k } a _ { j k } x _ { k } ,
$$

that is,

$$
A ( x ) = ( a ) ( x ) .
$$

If $a _ { k j } = \bar { a } _ { j k }$ ，then $A ( x , y )$ is called a Hermitian form.The Hermitian form $A ( x , \bar { x } ) = \overline { { { A ( x , \bar { x } ) ^ { \prime } } } } = \overline { { { A ^ { \prime } ( x , \bar { x } ) } } }$ is real. If $A ( x , { \bar { x } } )$ equals zero when all $_ x$ are equal to zero and never changes its sign,then it is called definite. If the sign is positive, i.e., $A ( x , { \bar { x } } ) > 0$ ，then it is called a Hermitian form. The product of two bilinear forms $A ( x , y )$ and $B ( x , y )$ is defined by

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \cal C } = \sum _ { l } \displaystyle \frac { \partial A } { \partial x _ { l } } \displaystyle \frac { \partial B } { \partial y _ { l } } = \sum _ { l } \sum _ { j } a _ { j l } y _ { j } \sum _ { k } b _ { l k } x _ { k } } } \\ { { = \sum _ { j , k } y _ { j } x _ { k } \sum _ { l } a _ { j l } b _ { l k } = ( c _ { j k } ) , } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
c _ { j k } = \sum _ { l } a _ { j l } b _ { l k } .
$$

Operate with

$$
B ( { \boldsymbol x } _ { l } ) = \sum _ { k } b _ { l k } { \boldsymbol x } _ { k }
$$

on

$$
A ( x _ { j } ) = \sum _ { l } a _ { j l } x _ { l } .
$$

Then we obtain

$$
A B ( x _ { j } ) = \sum _ { l , k } a _ { j l } b _ { l k } x _ { k } = \sum _ { k } x _ { k } \sum _ { l } a _ { j l } b _ { l k } = \sum _ { k } c _ { j k } x _ { k } ,
$$

that is, a bilinear form $c$ ; also $( A B ) ^ { \prime } = B ^ { \prime } A ^ { \prime }$ ,and $\overline { { ( A B ) } } = \bar { A } \bar { B }$

The simplest Hermitian form is the unit-form $\begin{array} { r } { E = E ( x , y ) = \sum \bar { x } y . } \end{array}$ We have $E ( x , \bar { x } ) = \textstyle \sum ( x ) ^ { 2 }$ .A form $U$ such that $\overline { { U } } ^ { \prime } U = E _ { \mathrm { { ; } } }$ $\overline { { U ^ { \prime } } } = U ^ { - 1 }$ ， $U \neq 0$ , is called unitary.

Take

$$
R ( z , t ) = \sum _ { j , k } t _ { j k } t _ { j } z _ { k } , \qquad x _ { j } = \frac { \partial R ( z , t ) } { \partial t _ { j } } ,
$$

and transform a Hermitian form $\pmb { A }$ to $R ^ { - 1 } A R$ Then $X = A ( x , { \bar { x } } )$ is transformed to $Z = \overline { { { R ^ { \prime } } } } A R ( z , \bar { z } ) = X ,$ where $X$ is a Hermitian form. For, $\pmb { X }$ equals zero if all $_ x$ are zero and $z$ equals zero if all $_ z$ are zero. $X$ is positive for all values of $_ x$ and $z$ is positive for all $z .$ Hence,

$$
\overline { { Z ^ { \prime } } } = \overline { { ( \vec { R } ^ { \prime } A R ) ^ { \prime } } } = Z .
$$

Thus,a transformation $R$ transforms a Hermitian form $\pmb { A }$ to a Hermitian form $\overline { { R ^ { \prime } A R } }$ Let a transformation of a Hermitian form $\pmb { A }$ to a Hermitian form $R ^ { - 1 } A R$ be $R = U ,$ $\overline { { R ^ { \prime } } } = R ^ { - 1 }$ ; then the new Hermitian form gives a new Hermitian transformation.

Let the $N$ transformations of a group $G _ { n }$ of a finite order $\pmb { n }$ be $A _ { l }$ $( l = 1 , 2 , . . . , N )$ ,and $H$ be any Hermitian form.The Hermitian form

$$
\mathbf { \Pi } _ { \mathbf { H } } = \sum _ { l } \overline { { A _ { l } ^ { \prime } } } H A _ { l } ( \pmb { x } , \pmb { \bar { x } } )
$$

is an absolute invariant by all transformations of $G _ { n }$ . If the invariant of $G _ { n }$ is $\boldsymbol { \varepsilon }$ ,then $G _ { n }$ is formed exclusively by unitary transformations and is itself unitary. Consider a transformation $_ { T }$ such that ${ \overline { { T ^ { \prime } } } } = T .$ Let $\pmb { \sigma }$ be a real parameter and $i = \sqrt { - 1 }$ . Then it can be proved that a transformation

$$
\begin{array} { r } { U = ( \sigma E + i T ) ^ { - 1 } ( \sigma E - i T ) } \\ { = ( \sigma E - i T ) ( \sigma E + i T ) ^ { - 1 } } \end{array}
$$

is unitary. A unitary transformation forms a unitary group. It can be shown that this group is transitive (Section 4.27; Weyl, 1923, 1931, 1939;Wintner,1929).

A Hermitian transformation $\pmb { H }$ and its transform $U ^ { - 1 } H U$ generated by a unitary transformation are considered to be equivalent. Take $\dot { H } = ( h _ { j k } ) , \dot { h _ { j k } } = \bar { h } _ { k j } , \overline { { H ^ { \prime } } } = H ;$ then det $H \neq 0$ Let $\boldsymbol { r }$ be a root of the characteristic equation $\Delta = \Delta ( r ) = \operatorname* { d e t } \left( H - r E \right) = 0 .$ ，There exists at least one point $\xi$ such that $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { k } h _ { j k } \xi _ { k } = r \xi _ { j } } \end{array}$ or $H ( \xi ) = r \xi$ . Hence,a Hermitian transformation keeps at least one point $\xi$ invariant in the space of $\pmb { \xi }$

By a suitable unitary transformation a Hermitian form of order $\pmb { n }$ can be decomposed into a Hermitian form $H _ { n - 1 }$ of order $n - 1$ and a Hermitian form $h _ { n n } x _ { n } y _ { n }$ of order $_ { n }$ , as can be proved by considering a unitary transformation $W = [ w _ { j k } ]$ and its subtransformation

$$
\begin{array} { r } { W _ { j k } = \partial | W | / \partial w _ { j k } , } \end{array}
$$

such that $\overline { { W ^ { \prime } } } W = E ,$ $W ^ { \prime - 1 } = \overline { { W } } _ { \mathrm { { i } } }$ that is, $( \operatorname * { d e t } W ) ^ { - 1 } W _ { j k } = { \bar { w } } _ { j k }$

A matrix $C = \left( c _ { j k } \right)$ is called normal if $c _ { j k } = 0$ for $j \neq k$ or $C$ is of the form $\begin{array} { r } { C = \sum _ { j } c _ { j j } x _ { j } y _ { j } . } \end{array}$ f $R ^ { - 1 } A R$ formed of two matrices $R$ and $A$ is normal, then $R$ is called a normalizing matrix of $\pmb { A }$ .When the characteristic equation for the transformation has only simple roots, then there always exists a normalizing matrix for $\pmb { A }$ .When the characteristic equation has multiple roots,then $A$ cannot generally be normalized. Jordan (Turnbull and Aitken,1932;Wedderburn,1934) has proved that any transformation $\pmb { A }$ of finite order $_ { n }$ can be normalized. If the characteristic equation $\Delta = \Delta ( r ) = \operatorname* { d e t } \left( A - r E \right) = 0$ has a root $\pmb { r }$ of multipicity $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ ，then the minor determinants of $\Delta$ of orders $n - p + 1$ ， $n - \phi + 2 , . . . , n - 1 , n$ vanish for this value of $_ r$ 、This property holds also for any Hermitian matrix, as well as for any unitary matrix.

It can be proved that the matrix obtained by multiplying a Hermitian matrix by itself $m$ times is also Hermitian,and that fora given Hermitian matrix $\pmb { H }$ there exists one and only one Hermitian matrix defined by $A ^ { m } = H .$

Let $P$ be an arbitrary matrix with det $P \neq 0$ ； then $H = \bar { P } ^ { \prime } P$ is Hermitian. Conversely,any Hermitian matrix can be put in the form $H = \bar { P } ^ { \prime } P .$ . The matrix $P$ is of the form $P = U \sqrt { H }$ with a unitary matrix $\boldsymbol { U }$ (Autonne, 1902).

A Hermitian form does not in general form a group. If a group G has a Hermitian form $\boldsymbol { H }$ as an absolute invariant, then the group $H ^ { 1 / 2 } { \tt G } H ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ is unitary and has the Hermitian $\boldsymbol { \varepsilon }$ as its absolute invariant. It can be proved that a group that has two distinct Hermitian forms as its invariants is decomposable.

When we limit ourselves to the real domain, then a Hermitian form $H = ( h _ { j k } )$ - such that $h _ { j k } = h _ { k j } = \bar { h } _ { j k }$ 一is real and symmetric at the same time;and a unitary transformation $U$ — such that $\overline { { U } } = U$ —is orthogonal, $U ^ { \prime } U = E$ 、An orthogonal group is transitive in the real domain. Thus a Hermitian form in the real domain has at least an orthogonal normal form, that is,a real unitary form.A multiple root of a real Hermitian form is also a real Hermitian form (Autonne, l902; Williamson, 1935).

In virtue of this result of polar factorization it can be seen that there exists for any nonsingular matrix $A$ one and only one positive-definite and symmetric matrix $P = P ^ { \prime }$ and one and only one orthogonal matrix $R = R ^ { \prime - 1 }$ such that $A = P R$ 、It can be proved that both these polar factors $P$ and $R$ of any Hamiltonian matrix $C$ are Hamiltonian matrices (Wintner, 1934).

In fact,if we substitute $C = P R$ (where $P = P ^ { \prime }$ ， $R ^ { \prime } = R ^ { - 1 }$ ）inthe formula (83),with $\vert \operatorname* { d e t } C \vert = \vert s \vert ^ { n } > 0$ ，then we obtain

$$
R ^ { - 1 } P G P R = s G .
$$

Hence

$$
P _ { \mathrm { i } } R _ { \mathrm { 1 } } = P _ { \mathrm { 2 } } R _ { \mathrm { 2 } } ,
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { R _ { 1 } = G , \quad } & { R _ { 2 } = \pm R G R ^ { - 1 } , } \\ { P _ { 1 } = P , \quad } & { P _ { 2 } = \pm s R _ { 2 } P ^ { - 1 } R _ { 2 } ^ { - 1 } \quad } & { ( \pm s = | s | ) . } \end{array}
$$

Orthogonal matrices form a group containing $\scriptstyle { R }$ and $\pm G ,$ since

$$
G ^ { \prime } = - G = G ^ { - 1 } ,
$$

so that $R _ { 1 }$ and $R _ { 2 }$ are orthogonal matrices. On the other hand, $P _ { 1 }$ and $P _ { 2 }$ are positive-definite matrices, because the reciprocal matrix of a positivedefinite matrix $P$ is positive-definite and remains so after an orthogonal transformation and multiplication by a positive number $\pm s = | s |$ · Accordingly, $R _ { 1 } = R _ { 2 }$ and $P _ { 1 } = P _ { 2 }$ ， owing to the uniqueness of the polar factorization of the nonsingular matrix $P _ { 1 } R _ { 1 } = P _ { 2 } R _ { 2 }$ .Hence

$$
{ \cal G } = \pm R G R ^ { - 1 } , ~ { \cal P } = \pm s G { \cal P } ^ { - 1 } { \cal G } ^ { - 1 } ,
$$

where

$$
R ^ { - 1 } = R ^ { \prime } , \qquad P ^ { \prime } = P .
$$

Consequently

$$
C ^ { \prime } G C = s G , \qquad | \mathbf { d e t } C | = | s | ^ { n } > 0
$$

is satisfied by $C = R$ and by $C = P$ , if we choose the multiplier $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ equal to $\pm 1 , \pm s ,$ respectively. Thus $R$ and $P$ are Hamiltonian matrices.

Since the product of two Hamiltonian matrices is Hamiltonian,it follows that it is sufficient for normalization to know those positive-definite or orthogonal Hamiltonian matrices, that is, those matrices that represent dilatations and rotations or reflections in a Euclidean space of $2 n$ dimensions. The projection of an orthogonal transformation of the $2 n -$ dimensional phase space on the $\pmb { n }$ -dimensional coordinate space need not be an orthogonal transformation of this $\pmb { n }$ -dimensional space.Write a Hamiltonian matrix $c$ in the form

$$
C = { \binom { \alpha \beta } { \gamma \delta } } ,
$$

so that $\alpha , \beta , \gamma ,$ δ are matrices of order $_ n$ ,and substitute the defnition of $G$ of Section 1.18 and of this matrix $c$ in condition (83） with $C ^ { \prime } = C ^ { - 1 }$ of $2 n$ -ary orthogonality； then, $\gamma = - \beta$ ， $\delta = \alpha$ if $s > 0$ ，and $\gamma = \beta$ ， $\delta = - \alpha$ if $s < 0$ Hence,if $c$ is a Hamiltonian matrix, it is a $2 n$ -ary orthogonal matrix if and only if it has either of the particular forms

$$
C = \left( \begin{array} { r r } { { \alpha } } & { { \beta } } \\ { { - \beta } } & { { \alpha } } \end{array} \right) \qquad ( s = 1 ) ,
$$

or

$$
C = { \binom { \alpha } { \beta } } \quad { \overset { \beta } { \_ } } \quad \quad ( s = - 1 ) .
$$

Williamson (l936a) extended such polar representation to singular matrices.He also (1935) discussed the nonsingular pencils of Hermitian matrices.

# I.22PFAFFIAN DYNAMICAL SYSTEM

Birkhoff (1928) noticed that Hamiltonian equations in dynamics can be regarded as a special type arising from the more general Pfaffian variational principle

$$
\delta \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t _ { 1 } } \left( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } P _ { j } \dot { p } _ { j } + Q \right) d t = 0 ,
$$

in which the integral is linear in allof the frst derivatives with arbitrary functions $\ P _ { 1 } , . . . , P _ { n } ,$ $Q$ of the variables $\pmb { / _ { 1 } , \ldots , \pmb { / _ { n } } }$ as coefficients, and $\pmb { n }$ is even. In fact, the Hamiltonian equations are derived from the variational problem

$$
\delta \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t _ { 1 } } \left( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \dot { p } _ { j } \dot { q } _ { j } - H \right) d t = 0 ,
$$

or

$$
\delta \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t _ { 1 } } { \cal L } d t = 0 , \qquad { \cal L } = { \cal L } _ { 0 } + { \cal L } _ { 1 } + { \cal L } _ { 2 } ,
$$

$$
H = - L + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } { / } { \hat { q } } _ { j } { \hat { q } } _ { j } ,
$$

where

$$
\frac { d q _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial \dot { p } _ { i } } , \frac { d \dot { p } _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { i } } ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

If we develop this general Pfaffan variational principle explicitly, then we obtain

$$
\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left( { \frac { \partial P _ { i } } { \partial { \dot { p } } _ { j } } } - { \frac { \partial P _ { j } } { \partial p _ { i } } } \right) { \frac { d p _ { j } } { d t } } - { \frac { \partial Q } { \partial p _ { i } } } = 0 \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

These equations are those of a degenerate Lagrangian problem with $\begin{array} { r } { L _ { 2 } = 0 , L _ { 1 } = \sum _ { j } P _ { j } \dot { p } _ { j } , L _ { 0 } = Q } \end{array}$ , so that there exists a particular integral $Q =$ constant, which reduces to the energy integral in the Hamiltonian case.

The conservative Hamiltonian principle

$$
\delta \int \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \dot { p _ { i } } \frac { d q _ { i } } { d t } - H ( \dot { p } _ { 1 } , . . . , \dot { p } _ { n } , q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { n } ) \right) d t = 0
$$

has been generalized by Birkhoff (1928) to the Pfaffian principle

$$
\delta \int \bigg ( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 2 n } R _ { i } d x _ { i } - H d t \bigg ) = 0 ,
$$

or

$$
\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \left( { \frac { \partial R _ { i } } { \partial x _ { j } } } - { \frac { \partial R _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } } \right) { \frac { d x _ { j } } { d t } } = { \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { i } } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , 2 n ) ,
$$

where

$$
H = H ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 2 n } ) , \qquad R _ { i } = R _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 2 n } )
$$

are $2 n + 1$ given functions of $2 n$ coordinates, and

$$
\operatorname* { d e t } \left| \frac { \partial R _ { i } } { \partial x _ { j } } - \frac { \partial R _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } \right| \neq 0 .
$$

Equations (97),which admit the integral $H =$ constant,are invariant for any coordinate transformation.Most of the essential properties of the Hamiltonian system hold also for the Pfaffian cases.

The variational equations (Section 4.2O） belonging to the given periodic solution $x _ { i } = x _ { i } ( t ) ~ ( i = 1 , 2 , . ~ . ~ . , 2 n )$ ，

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \displaystyle \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \left( \frac { \partial R _ { i } } { \partial x _ { j } } - \frac { \partial R _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } \right) \frac { d \xi _ { i } } { d t } + \displaystyle \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \sum _ { l = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \left( \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } R _ { i } } { \partial x _ { l } \partial x _ { j } } - \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } R _ { j } } { \partial x _ { l } \partial x _ { i } } \right) \xi _ { l } \frac { d x _ { j } } { d t } } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad = \displaystyle \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } H } { \partial x _ { j } \partial x _ { i } } \xi _ { j } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 2 n ) } \end{array}
$$

can be written

$$
\frac { d \xi _ { i } } { d t } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { 2 n } a _ { i j } ( t ) \xi _ { j } \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , 2 n ) .
$$

Interchange $_ i$ and $j ;$ and write $\eta$ in place of $\xi$ in (98); then,

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \left( \frac { \partial R _ { i } } { \partial x _ { j } } - \frac { \partial R _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } \right) \frac { d \eta _ { i } } { d t } + \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \sum _ { l = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \left( \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } R _ { i } } { \partial x _ { l } \partial x _ { j } } - \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } R _ { j } } { \partial x _ { l } \partial x _ { i } } \right) \eta _ { l } \frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad = \displaystyle - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } H } { \partial x _ { i } \partial x _ { j } } \eta _ { i } \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , 2 n ) . } \end{array}
$$

Furthermore, the expression

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \sum _ { l = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \bigg ( \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } R _ { i } } { \partial x _ { l } \partial x _ { j } } - \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } R _ { j } } { \partial x _ { l } \partial x _ { i } } \bigg ) \bigg ( \xi _ { j } \eta _ { i } \frac { d x _ { l } } { d t } - \xi _ { l } \eta _ { i } \frac { d x _ { j } } { d t } - \xi _ { j } \eta _ { l } \frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } \bigg )
$$

is the sum of six trilinear forms, three of which are the negatives of the three others.Multiplying (98） by $\pmb { \eta _ { i } }$ and (100） by $\xi _ { j } .$ ，we obtain $_ { 4 n }$ relations.Adding these, we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \Big ( \frac { \partial R _ { i } } { \partial x _ { j } } - \frac { \partial R _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } \Big ) \Big ( \frac { d \xi _ { j } } { d t } \eta _ { i } + \frac { d \eta _ { i } } { d t } \xi _ { j } \Big ) } } \\ & { } & { + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \sum _ { l = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \Big ( \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } R _ { i } } { \partial x _ { l } \partial x _ { j } } - \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } R _ { j } } { \partial x _ { l } \partial x _ { i } } \Big ) \Big ( \xi _ { l } \eta _ { i } \frac { d x _ { j } } { d t } + \xi _ { j } \eta _ { l } \frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } \Big ) = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

The sum of the two vanishing expressions (l01) and (102) is the time derivative of the bilinear covariant

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 2 n } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { 2 n } s _ { i j } ( t ) \xi _ { j } ( t ) \eta _ { i } ( t ) ,
$$

where

$$
{ s } _ { i j } ( t ) = \frac { \partial { R } _ { i } } { \partial { x } _ { j } } - \frac { \partial { R } _ { j } } { \partial { x } _ { i } } ,
$$

which is associated with the Pfaffian

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 2 n } R _ { i } d x _ { i } - H d t .
$$

The value of this bilinear covariant (Section 1.7) is independent of $t$ for two arbitrary solutions $\xi , \eta$ of the variational equations belonging to the solution $x _ { i } = x _ { i } ( t ) ~ ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 2 n )$

It follows that the characteristic equation associated with (98) or (99) is always reciprocal. This is Wintner's (l93l） generalized form of Birkhoff's theorem. The skew-symmetric nonsingular bilinear form (103)

is the Pfaffian generalization of Poincaré's bilinear differential invariant corresponding to the Hamiltonian-type integral invariant

$$
\iint \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \delta p _ { i } \delta q _ { i } .
$$

The integral invariants of the Pfaffian systems have been studied by Feraud (1929).

The property of polar factorization can be extended to such Pfaffian dynamical systems.In the linear conservative case this has the form

$$
{ \dot { y } } = S ^ { - 1 } F y ,
$$

where $F = F ^ { \prime }$ and $s$ is nonsingular and equal to $- S ^ { \prime }$ but not necessarily equal to $G$ ， so that (lO4) is more general than the equations

$$
\dot { x } = - G H x .
$$

It can be proved that these Pfaffian systems may be reduced to the Hamiltonian. If $y = T x ,$ where $_ { T }$ is a nonsingular matrix, then (104) is equivalent to

$$
\dot { x } = ( T ^ { \prime } S T ) ^ { - 1 } ( T ^ { \prime } F T ) x .
$$

Now $H = T ^ { \prime } F T = H ^ { \prime }$ since $F = F ^ { \prime }$ .If $S = - S ^ { \prime }$ and det $\boldsymbol { s } \neq 0$ ，then there exists a nonsingular matrix $_ { T }$ such that $T ^ { \prime } S T = G = - G ^ { - 1 }$ (Weyl, 1931). Using this matrix $_ { T }$ in $y = T x ,$ ,we obtain (104a).

The definition of $G$ gives, for any $H = H ^ { \prime }$ and any integer $m \geq 0$

$$
G ( - G H ) ^ { m } G ^ { - 1 } = - G G ( - H G ) ^ { m - 1 } H G ^ { - 1 } = ( - 1 ) ^ { m } ( H G ) ^ { m }
$$

and

$$
[ ( G H ) ^ { m } ] ^ { \prime } = ( H ^ { \prime } G ^ { \prime } ) ^ { m } = ( - 1 ) ^ { m } ( H G ) ^ { m } .
$$

Hence,

$$
G ( - G H ) ^ { m } G ^ { - 1 } = [ ( G H ) ^ { m } ] ^ { \prime } .
$$

Consequently

$$
G [ \exp { ( - G H ) } ] G ^ { - 1 } = [ \exp { ( G H ) } ] ^ { \prime } ,
$$

where exp $\pmb { A }$ is defined for any $\pmb { A }$ by means of the exponential series. Since exp $( - A ) = ( \exp A ) ^ { - 1 }$ , it is clear that $C ^ { \prime } G C = s G _ { \cdot }$

$$
| { \bf d e t } C | = | s | ^ { m } > 0 ,
$$

is satisfied by $C = \exp { ( G H ) } , s = 1$ Hence, exp $( G H )$ is for any $H = H ^ { \prime }$ a Hamiltonian matrix of determinant $+ 1$ (Williamson, 1939, 1940).

On the other hand,any solution ${ \boldsymbol x } = { \boldsymbol x } ( t )$ of $\dot { \boldsymbol { x } } = B \boldsymbol { x } ,$ where $\boldsymbol { B }$ is independent of $t$ ,is related to the vector ${ \pmb x } ( 0 )$ of the initial constant by the linear transformation

$$
\begin{array} { r } { { \boldsymbol x } ( t ) = L ( t ) { \boldsymbol x } ( 0 ) , } \end{array}
$$

where $L ( t )$ denotes the matrix exp $( B t )$ . If we apply this to (104a), with $H = H ^ { \prime }$ and $B = - G H ,$ then—since $- t H$ is,like $H ,$ real and symmetric for $- \infty < t < + \infty$ - we obtain this result: the cyclic transformation group $L ( t )$ generated by the infinitesimal transformation (104a) consists of Hamiltonian matrices and det $L ( t ) = + 1$ for any finite $t$ (see Section 4.26 et seq. on the Lie groups).

Consider such interesting subgroups of $C$ as

$$
C _ { 1 } = { \binom { \alpha } { \omega } } , \quad \omega \biggr ) , \qquad C _ { 2 } = { \binom { \omega } { \gamma } } \quad \beta \biggr ) .
$$

We obtain the usual condition of contragradiency for $C _ { 1 }$

$$
\alpha ^ { \prime } = s \delta ^ { - 1 } , \qquad s \neq 0 ;
$$

and for $C _ { 2 }$

$$
\beta ^ { \prime } = - s \gamma ^ { - 1 } , \qquad s \neq 0 ,
$$

as the necessary and sufficient condition for the Hamiltonian character of the matrices (Wintner,1934). (See Section 4.14).

Vrkljan, V. S. 1929 Z. Physik,59,718. 1930Ibid.,65,280.

# Quasi-Periodic Motion

# SIMPLY PERIODIC MOTION

# 2.1LEBESGUE'S INTEGRALS

A collection of definite elements related to their totality by a certain law is called a set.Any two sets that can be made to correspond to each other are equivalent.A set of natural numbers is said to have the power of denumerable numbers.A set of rational numbers and a set of algebraic numbers determined as the roots of an algebraic equation with rational coefficients have the power of denumerable numbers.By representing such a number as the coordinate of a point on the straight line interval between O and l,we can find a number belonging to this set in any small but finite segment in this interval. Such being the case, the rational numbers and the algebraic numbers are said to be euerywhere dense in the interval. But the set of all points in the interval has the power of the continuum, because such a set cannot be made to correspond to any set of rational or algebraic numbers.

Describe a circle with a small but finite radius $\pmb { r }$ around a point $\pmb { P }$ The region inside this circle is called the $\pmb { r }$ -neighborhood.If there is no point belonging to the set $M$ in the neighborhood of $P _ { i }$ ，then $P$ is said to be isolated.If there are infinitely many points of the set $M$ other than $P$ in the $\pmb { r }$ -neighborhoodof $P$ then $P$ is called an accumulation point or a limit point of the set $M .$ A set consisting of accumulation points is called a derived set.A set containing its derived set is said to be closed.A set coinciding with its derived set is called a perfect set.A perfect set cannot have the power of denumerable numbers but has the power of the continuum according to Cantor.

A sum set (union, Vereinigungsmenge) of the sets $M _ { 1 } , M _ { 2 } , . . . , M _ { k }$ is a set $M$ containing all points that are in any of these $k$ sets.A product set of the sets $M _ { 1 } , M _ { 2 } , . . . , M _ { k }$ (intersection,Durchschnit） is a set of points that are common to all these $k$ sets.A set that is a part of a set is called a subset. A subset $M _ { 1 }$ of $M$ (written $M _ { 1 } \subset M$ ） consisting of all points not contained in a subset $M _ { 2 }$ of $M$ is called the complement of $M _ { 2 }$ in $M$ when $M _ { 1 } + M _ { 2 } = M .$ A complementary set of a closed set is an open set.

If there is a number larger or smaller than any member of the set $M ,$ then the number is called the upper bound or the lower bound of $M .$ If the upper and the lower bound both exist, then the set $M$ is said to be bounded.There should be at least one accumulation point for any bounded set consisting of an infinite number of elements (Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem). If there is only one accumulation point in a bounded set, then the set is said to be convergent and the accumulation point is called the limit. The largest and smallest accumulation points are denoted respectively by ${ \mathfrak { s } } \mathfrak { T } \overline { { \operatorname { i m } } } M ^ { \prime } $ or“ lim sup $M ^ { \prime \prime }$ and“lim $M ^ { \prime \prime }$ or“ lim inf M," and are called the superior and inferior limits, respectively. A set consisting of only interior points of a set $M$ is said to be open.A bounded closed set can be covered by a finite number of open sets.This is Heine-Borel’s covering theorem.

If all subsets consisting of an infinite number of elements selected from a set $M$ possess accumulation points, then the set $M$ is called compact. A number $\| { x , y } \|$ can be made to correspond to a pair of two points $_ { x }$ and $y$ ，such that $\left\| x , x \right\| = 0 , \left\| x , y \right\| = \left\| y , x \right\| > 0$ for $x \neq y$ ,and

$$
\| x , y \| + \| y , z \| \geq \| x , z \| .
$$

This number is called the distance of the two points,and such a set is said to be metrical. If the set of points $( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ is metrical, then the set constitutes a metrical space.When

$$
\left\| x , y \right\| = [ ( x _ { 1 } - y _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } + ( x _ { 2 } - y _ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + ( x _ { n } - y _ { n } ) ^ { 2 } ] ^ { 1 / 2 } ,
$$

the space is called Euclidean. The necessary and sufficient condition for a set $M$ of a Euclidean space $R _ { n }$ to be compact is that a finite number $\pmb { A }$ can be found such that $\left| x _ { i } \right| < A$ $( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ ，for any point $( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ of $M .$

If every point of a set is itself an accumulation point, then the set is called dense in itself. A perfect set is dense in itself.A set $M$ is nowhere dense in a set $N$ if $M$ is not dense in any subset of $N .$ If a set $M$ cannot be represented by a sum of its closed subsets, then $M$ is called connected. A sum of connected sets that are nonoverlapping-i.e.，any two of which have no common point — is connected.Any two points $\scriptstyle { a ^ { \prime } }$ and $ { \boldsymbol { a } } ^ { \prime \prime }$ of a connected set $M$ can be connected by a finite number of sequences $a ^ { \prime } = a _ { 0 } , a _ { 1 } , . . . , a _ { l - 1 } , a _ { l } = a ^ { \prime \prime }$ ,belonging to the set $M$ such that

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \left\| a _ { i - 1 } , a _ { i } \right\| \le \rho \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , l ) . } \end{array}
$$

A closed connected set is called a continuum.A connected set is dense in itself. A continuum is a perfect set.

If there is in $M$ a denumerably infinite number of subsets that are dense in themselves,then $M$ is said to be separable.A compact set is separable.Any set of a Euclidean space is separable.If there exists an $\pmb { n }$ for a given $\epsilon$ such that $\| a _ { n ^ { \prime } } , a _ { n ^ { \prime \prime } } \| < \epsilon$ for $n ^ { \prime } \geq n$ $n { ' } \stackrel { } { = } n$ ，then the sequence $\left\{ a _ { n } \right\}$ is said to be a Cauchy sequence. If a Cauchy sequence from a set $M$ possesses an accumulation point belonging to $M _ { : }$ ，then it is called complete.A complete set is closed and a compact closed set is complete. A closed subset of a Euclidean space is complete.

Let $\pmb { A }$ be a system of sets $M$ in an $\pmb { n }$ -dimensional metrical space $R _ { n }$ Let a function $\phi ( M )$ be such that: (i) $\phi ( M ) \geq 0$ for all $M$ belonging to $A$ ； (ii) $\phi ( M ) = 0$ for any empty set, i.e., for any set containing no element; (iii) $\phi ( M ) \cong \phi ( M _ { 1 } )$ for a subset $M _ { 1 }$ of $M$ that is, for $M _ { 1 } \subset M$ ； and (iv) $\phi ( { \cal M } ) \le \phi ( { \cal M } _ { 1 } ) + \phi ( { \cal M } _ { 2 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot$ for $M _ { 1 } + M _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot = M ,$ where $M _ { 1 } , M _ { 2 } , .$ are a denumerably infinite number of sets in the system $\pmb { A }$ ，Then the function $\phi ( M )$ is called a measure function. Let $\begin{array} { r } { m ( J ) = \prod _ { k = 1 } ^ { n } \left( b _ { k } - a _ { k } \right) } \end{array}$ be the measure function of an open domain $J$ of an $\pmb { n }$ -dimensional space $a _ { k } < x _ { k } < b _ { k }$ $( k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ . Let an arbitrary set $M$ in the metrical space $R _ { n }$ be covered by at most a denumerable number of open sets, by various methods. The sum set of such open sets contains $M .$ The sum of the measure functions of such open sets may differ according to the method of covering. Call the lower bound of these sums the outer measure; that is, the lower bound of the outer measures of all open sets containing $M$ is called the outer measure of $M$ and denoted by $m _ { a } ( M )$ . Let a set $M$ be covered by an open set $o$ The difference between the outer measure of $O$ and the outer measure of the complementary set of $M , O \mathrm { ~ - ~ } M ,$ is called the inner measure $m _ { i } ( M )$ ; that is, $m _ { a } ( O ) - m _ { a } ( O - M ) = m _ { i } ( M )$ . In general: $m _ { i } ( M ) \ \leqq m _ { a } ( M )$ .If $M _ { 1 } \subset M ,$ then $m _ { i } ( M _ { 1 } ) \ \leq m _ { i } ( M )$ .A set $M$ such that

$$
m _ { i } ( M ) = m _ { a } ( M ) = m ( M )
$$

is called measurable according to Lebesgue.

Any set constructed by a finite or a denumerable number of operations such as addition,subtraction,multiplication - finite and infinite一 from measurable sets is measurable.Such sets are called Borel sets.The operations just mentioned constitute a field called the Borel feld.

Let $M _ { 1 } + M _ { 2 }$ and $M _ { 1 } M _ { 2 }$ be respectively the sum set and the product set of two sets $M _ { 1 }$ and $M _ { 2 }$ . We have

$$
\begin{array} { r } { m _ { a } ( M _ { 1 } + M _ { 2 } ) + m _ { a } ( M _ { 1 } M _ { 2 } ) \leq m _ { a } ( M _ { 1 } ) + m _ { a } ( M _ { 2 } ) , } \\ { m _ { i } ( M _ { 1 } + M _ { 2 } ) + m _ { i } ( M _ { 1 } M _ { 2 } ) \geq m _ { i } ( M _ { 1 } ) + m _ { i } ( M _ { 2 } ) . } \end{array}
$$

If $M _ { 1 }$ and $M _ { 2 }$ are both measurable, that is, if

$$
m _ { i } ( M _ { 1 } ) = m _ { a } ( M _ { 1 } ) = m ( M _ { 1 } ) , ~ m _ { i } ( M _ { 2 } ) = m _ { a } ( M _ { 2 } ) = m ( M _ { 2 } ) ,
$$

then

$$
m _ { i } ( M _ { 1 } + M _ { 2 } ) = m _ { a } ( M _ { 1 } + M _ { 2 } ) , \qquad m _ { i } ( M _ { 1 } M _ { 2 } ) = m _ { a } ( M _ { 1 } M _ { 2 } ) ,
$$

and the sum set and the product set are measurable. We have

$$
m ( M _ { 1 } + M _ { 2 } ) + m ( M _ { 1 } M _ { 2 } ) = m ( M _ { 1 } ) + m ( M _ { 2 } ) .
$$

In particular, if $M _ { 1 }$ and $M _ { 2 }$ are nonoverlapping,i.e., possess no common point, then

$$
m ( M _ { 1 } + M _ { 2 } ) = m ( M _ { 1 } ) + m ( M _ { 2 } ) , m ( M _ { 1 } M _ { 2 } ) = 0 .
$$

The sum set and the product set of a denumerably infinite number of measurable sets are both measurable.A measure cannot be negative. The measure of an empty set is zero. The measure of the set containing all points between O and 1 is one. The measure of the set containing a denumerably infinite number of points, such as rational numbers or algebraic numbers, is zero.

For a point $^ { a }$ of $M _ { : }$ i.e., $a \in M ,$ ，the set of values of $f ( a )$ is called a function $f .$ For various sequences $\left\{ a _ { n } \right\}$ converging to a point $_ { a }$ of $M$ ,the point function lim $\scriptstyle \operatorname* { s u p } _ { n \to \infty } f ( a _ { n } )$ is denoted by $G ( a ; f , M )$ ,and

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } { \mathrm { i n f } } f ( a _ { n } )
$$

by $g ( a ; f , M )$ .If $G ( a ; f , M ) = g ( a ; f , M )$ , then $f$ is said to be continuous at $a \in M .$ A function that is continuous at all points of a set $M$ - that is, a function for which $G ( f , M ) = g ( f , M )$ —is said to be continuous in $M .$ If there exists a $\rho > 0$ for a given $\epsilon > 0$ such that $| f ( a ^ { \prime } ) - f ( a ^ { \prime \prime } ) | < \epsilon$ for any two points $\scriptstyle { a ^ { \prime } }$ and $\pmb { a } ^ { \prime \prime }$ of $M$ whose distance apart is less than $\rho$ ， then the bounded function $f$ is said to be uniformly continuous in $M$

If $M$ is compact and closed, then any function continuous in $M$ is uniformly continuous.The function

$$
\omega ( a ; f , M ) = G ( a ; f , M ) - g ( a ; f , M )
$$

is called the fluctuation of $f$ in $M .$ In order that $\omega ( a ; f , M ) = 0$ it is necessary and sufficient that $f$ should be continuous in M. Otherwise, $f$ is said to be discontinuous.

If the discontinuity point of $f$ is compact in $M _ { i }$ ，then the finite but discontinuous function $f$ is said to be totally discontinuous; if it is not compact, then it is said to be pointwise discontinuous.A function continuous on a perfect bounded set $M$ is called a function of Baire's class 0.A continuous function represented as the limit function of a sequence $\{ f _ { \mathfrak { n } } \}$ of continuous functions $f _ { n }$ is a function of Baire's class 1. If $f _ { n }$ belongs to Baire's class $\pmb { \alpha }$ but $f = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } f _ { n }$ does not belong to any Baire's class lower than $\pmb { \alpha }$ ，then $f$ is said to be of Baire's class $\propto$ ，A discontinuous function which is represented as the limit of functions of Baire's class $\pmb { \alpha }$ is said to be of Baire's class $\propto + ~ 1$ .

Let $f ( x )$ be a nonnegative bounded function of $_ x$ Draw a graph with coordinates $_ x$ and $y$ such that $y = f ( x )$ . The set of values of $y$ such that $0 \leq y \leqq f ( x )$ for a measurable $_ n$ dimensional set $M$ of $_ x$ is called an ordinate set and denoted by $\Omega \{ M ; f ( x ) \}$ . Denote the measure of this measurable space of $x , y$ by $\mu$ ; then

$$
\mu _ { a } [ \Omega \{ M ; f ( x ) \} ] = { \overline { { \int _ { M } f ( x ) ~ d x } } } , \qquad \mu _ { i } [ \Omega \{ M ; f ( x ) \} ] = \underbrace { \int _ { M } f ( x ) ~ d x } _ { \qquad }
$$

Call these integrals the upper and lower Lebesgue integrals of $f$ over the set $M .$ If $\Omega \{ M ; f ( x ) \}$ is measurable, then these two integrals coincide. If the measure is finite, then $f ( x )$ is called integrable or summable in Lebesgue's sense. $\mu [ \Omega \{ M ; f ( x ) \} ] = \int _ { M } f ( x ) \ d x$ is called a Lebesgue integral of $f$ on $M .$

When $f ( x )$ is a constant, that is, $f = h > 0$ ，then $\Omega ( M ; h )$ is an open ordinate set represented by a cylinder of height $h$ with base $M .$ The $( n + 1 )$ -dimensional measure of this cylinder is $\mu [ \Omega \{ M ; h \} ] = h { \cdot } m ( M )$ It is equal to zero for $m ( M ) = 0$ For $h = \infty$ and for a measurable $M$ with $m ( M ) > 0$ ，the measure of $\Omega \{ M ; \infty \}$ is measurable with infinite measure. If $m ( M ) = 0 $ then

$$
\mu [ \Omega \{ M ; \infty \} ] = \operatorname * { l i m } _ { k \to \infty } \mu [ \Omega \{ M ; k \} ] = \operatorname * { l i m } _ { k \to \infty } [ k \cdot m ( M ) ] = 0 ;
$$

that is, an ordinate set erected over a set of measure zero is itself of measure zero.

Denote a subset of $M _ { : }$ ， such that $f < \alpha$ ,by $M ( f < \alpha ) = M _ { \alpha }$ . It can be shown that $M _ { \alpha }$ is measurable for all $\alpha \geq 0$ Since $M ( f > 1 )$ ， $M ( f > 2 ) , \ldots$ are measurable, $M ( x = \infty )$ is measurable. If $M ( f = 0 )$ is measurable then $M ( f \geq \alpha )$ is measurable for all nonnegative $\pmb { \alpha }$ .The set $M ( f < \alpha )$ is also measurable. Functions such as $f$ are said to be measurable according to Lebesgue. Thus the measurability of a function can be deduced from the measurability of an ordinate set. Conversely，the existence of the integral $\int _ { M } f ( x ) \ d x$ can be deduced, if the measure is finite, from the measurability of the function $f ( x )$ . Since $m ( M _ { \infty } ) = 0$ (as is now evident),this function $f$ becomes infinite only on sets of measure zero,if $f$ is integrable on $M .$

Let $f ( x )$ be a measurable nonpositive function defined on a set $M$ of $_ { x }$ with finite measure. Construct the mirror image of this function with respect to $y = 0$ .Denote the ordinate set of a nonnegative function $- f$ by $\Omega ^ { + } \{ M ; - f \}$ ,and let its mirror image be $\Omega ^ { - } \{ M ; f \}$ .If $\Omega ^ { - } \{ M ; f \}$ is measurable, then take

$$
\int _ { M } f d x = - \mu [ \Omega ^ { - } \{ M ; f \} ] .
$$

Since

$$
\mu [ \Omega ^ { - } \{ M ; f \} ] = \mu [ \Omega ^ { + } \{ M ; - f \} ] ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\int _ { M } f d x = - \int _ { M } \left( - f \right) d x .
$$

Hence the properties for a nonnegative function can be extended to those for a nonpositive function,and consequently to those for any function with arbitrary sign. Thus it can be shown that $M ( f \lesseqqgtr \ - \alpha )$ ， $M ( f < - \alpha )$ ， $M ( f \gtrapprox - \alpha )$ ， $M ( f > - \alpha )$ are measurable if $f \lessgtr 0$ is integrable on $M .$

Consider a function which can have either sign on a measurable set $M$ with finite measure. Denote $f ^ { + } = \mathrm { m } \mathbf { a x } ( f , 0 ) , f ^ { - } = \mathrm { m i n } ( 0 , f )$ ； then, $f = f ^ { + } + f ^ { - }$ . Put

$$
\Omega ^ { + } \{ f ^ { + } \} = \Omega ^ { + } \{ f \} , \qquad \Omega ^ { - } \{ f ^ { - } \} = \Omega ^ { - } \{ f \} .
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \mu _ { a } [ \Omega ^ { + } \{ f \} ] - \mu _ { i } [ \Omega ^ { - } \{ f \} ] = \overline { { { \displaystyle \int _ { M } f d x } } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \mu _ { i } [ \Omega ^ { + } \{ f \} ] - \mu _ { a } [ \Omega ^ { - } \{ f \} ] = \int _ { M } f d x } } \\ { { \displaystyle \mu _ { i } [ \Omega ^ { + } \{ f \} ] - \mu _ { a } [ \Omega ^ { - } \{ f \} ] = \int _ { M } f d x } } \end{array}
$$

are called the upper and lower integrals of $f ,$ respectively. If these two integrals are equal, then

$$
\int _ { M } f d x = \mu [ \Omega ^ { + } \{ f \} ] - \mu [ \Omega ^ { - } \{ f \} ] = \int _ { M } f ^ { + } \ d x + \int _ { M } f ^ { - } \ d x
$$

is called the integral off.For $f$ to be integrable it is necessary that $f ^ { + }$ and $f ^ { - }$ are both integrable.We say that $f$ is measurable on $M$ if $\Omega ^ { + } \{ f \}$ and $\Omega ^ { - } \{ f \}$ are both measurable.

The sum of a finite number of measurable functions $f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , \ldots , f _ { n }$ on $M ,$ for which the operation $\textstyle \sum _ { t = 1 } ^ { \infty } f _ { i }$ is applicable, is measurable on a set $M ^ { * } \subset M .$ There exists $\operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } f _ { n } ,$ which is on a measurable set and is measurable on the set. Suppose that an infinite sequence of finite and integrable functions $f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , \ldots$ on $M$ converges to a finite function $f \circ \mathbf { n } M$ (except sets of measure zero). Let $f _ { 1 } + f _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + f _ { n } = S _ { n }$ ； then the $S _ { n }$ are integrable. If there exists an integrable function $\Psi$ that is the upper bound of the absolute values of all $S _ { n } { \mathrm { : } }$ then $f$ is integrable and

$$
\int _ { M } f d x = \int _ { M } \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } f _ { n } d x = \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \int _ { M } f _ { n } d x .
$$

Thus a convergent infinite series of finite integrable functions can be integrated term by term.If, in particular, there is a constant $\pmb { c }$ independent of $_ { n }$ such that $| S _ { n } | < c ;$ ,that is,if the partial sums $S _ { n }$ are uniformly bounded, then the series can be integrated term by term.

Lebesgue defined an auxiliary function $f _ { n }$ for an integer $_ { n }$ corresponding to a nonnegative measurable function $f$ on $M$ by the condition $f _ { n } = f$ for any point $P$ where $f \leqslant n$ ,and $f _ { n } = n$ for any point $P$ where $f > n$ The function $f _ { n }$ is bounded and measurable.We have

$$
\int _ { M } f d P = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } \int _ { M } f _ { n } d P .
$$

If this limit is finite,then $f ,$ according to Lebesgue, is summable on $M .$ $\operatorname { I f } f$ is nonpositive, a definition of summability can still be made. If $M$ is the sum of a finite or a denumerably infinite number of nonoverlapping sets $M _ { 1 }$ ， $M _ { 2 } , \ldots$ ，then the integral on $M$ is equal to the sum of integrals on $M _ { 1 } , M _ { 2 } , \dots$ Thus the sum of summable functions is summable,and the integral of the sum equals the sum of the integrals of each function. $\operatorname { I f } f$ is summable on a set $M$ and $M ^ { * } \subset M ,$ then the integral offon $M ^ { * }$ tends to zero with the measure of $M ^ { * }$ .If,further, the functions of a sequence $f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , \ldots , f _ { n } , \ldots .$ are summable on a set $M _ { : }$ ，if their absolute values are smaller than a positive summable function $\Phi$ independent of $\pmb { n }$ ,and if the sequence admits a limit function $F _ { : }$ ，then $F$ is summable and

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } \int _ { M } f _ { n } d P = \int _ { M } F d P .
$$

This theorem is due to Lebesgue.

Let a function $f$ be defined on a measurable set $M$ with finite measure and be integrable. Consider a function $\bar { f }$ which coincides with $f$ on $M$ but is equal to zero outside $M ,$ i.e., on a complementary set of $M .$ A set function on a set such that $F ( U ) = \int _ { U } \bar { f } d x$ with respect to an arbitrary measurable set $U$ in $R _ { n }$ is measurable and is called an indefinite integral of $\bar { f } .$ Let a function $\pmb { F }$ of finite values on a set $\boldsymbol { U }$ with finite measure be such that $F ( U ) = F ( U _ { 1 } ) + F ( U _ { 2 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot$ for $U = U _ { 1 } + U _ { 2 } + \cdots$ ,where $U$ is the sum set of denumerably infinite nonoverlapping measurable sets $U _ { 1 } , U _ { 2 } , \dots$ .This property is called the complete additivity of the set function $F ( U )$ . Let ${ \pmb F }$ be such that with a given positive $\epsilon$ there exists a constant δ so that $| F ( U ) | < \epsilon$ for $m ( U ) < \delta$ Then $F$ is said to be totally continuous. An indefinite integral has three properties: $( i )$ , it has a finite value for any finite set of finite measure; $( i i )$ , it/is completely additive; and $( i i i )$ , it is totally continuous. If the inequality

$$
g \cdot m ( U ) \leq \int _ { U } f d x = F ( U ) \leq G \cdot m ( U )
$$

(the mean value theorem)， holds in a set $U$ of an integrable function $f ,$ then

$$
g \leq { \frac { F ( U ) } { m ( U ) } } \leq G .
$$

Let the set function $F ( U )$ possess the above three properties. In a sequence of sets $U _ { 1 }$ $, U _ { 2 } , \ldots$ which converges to $X$ for $n \to \infty$ , we consider a set $K _ { n }$ containing $X$ and one of $U _ { n }$ .If $m ( U _ { n } ) / m ( K _ { n } ) \ \geq \ \alpha$ for a positive $\pmb { \alpha }$ ，and if $\operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } \left[ F ( U _ { n } ) / m ( U _ { n } ) \right]$ exists，then the limit is called the derivative of $F ( U )$ at $X ,$ and ${ \pmb F }$ is differentiable at $x .$ Consider at each point $\pmb { X }$ of a set $M$ of finite outer measure an admissble sequence of closed sets $V$ convergent to $X .$ Then finite or denumerably infinite sets $V _ { 1 } , V _ { 2 } , \ldots$ can be chosen from the sequence so that: (i), $V _ { 1 } , V _ { 2 } , \ldots$ are nonoverlapping; (ii), $M$ can be covered (except sets of measure zero) by the sets $V _ { 1 } , V _ { 2 } , \ldots$ so that $\begin{array} { r } { m _ { a } [ M - M ( \sum _ { n } V _ { n } ) ] = 0 } \end{array}$ ; and (iii), for a given $\epsilon > 0$ we have $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { n } m ( V _ { n } ) < m _ { a } ( M ) + \epsilon } \end{array}$ This is called Vitali’s covering theorem. On the basis of this theorem Lebesgue has shown that a finite, totally continuous, and completely additive set function ${ \pmb F }$ is almost everywhere differentiable (i.e., except for sets of measure zero) and is equal to an indefinite integral of its derivative.

If a function $\Phi$ can be found such that

$$
\Phi ( J ) = \Phi ( J _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \Phi ( J _ { n } ) \quad \mathrm { f o r } \quad J = J _ { 1 } + J _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + J _ { n } ,
$$

with nonoverlapping sets $\boldsymbol { J } _ { 1 } , \boldsymbol { J } _ { 2 } , \ldots$ . and that with a given $\epsilon > 0$ a constant δ can be chosen such that

$$
\biggr | \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \Phi ( J _ { i } ) \biggr | < \epsilon \qquad \mathrm { i f } \qquad \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m ( J _ { i } ) < \delta ,
$$

then $\Phi$ is called an interval function. The displayed properties are called complete additivity and total continuity，respectively.Let $f$ be an integrable function in $a \leq x \leq b$ ，and $a \leq \alpha < \beta \leq b$ be a closed interval $J = [ \alpha , \beta ]$ .Then

$$
\int _ { J } f d x = \int _ { \alpha } ^ { \beta } f d x ,
$$

and

$$
\int _ { \alpha } ^ { \beta } f d x = \int _ { \alpha } ^ { \gamma } f d x + \int _ { \gamma } ^ { \beta } f d x
$$

for $\alpha < \gamma < \beta .$ .The indefinite integral, owing to the relation

$$
\Phi ( J ) = \Phi [ \alpha , \beta ] = \int _ { \alpha } ^ { \beta } f d x ,
$$

is represented by an interval function and can be written

$$
\Phi ( J ) = F ( \beta ) - F ( \alpha ) , F = \int _ { \gamma } ^ { x } f d x .
$$

Consider $a \equiv x _ { 1 } < x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } < x _ { 2 } < \cdot \cdot \cdot < x _ { n } < x _ { n } ^ { \prime } \equiv b$ contained in the interval ${ \cal J } ^ { o } = [ a , b ]$ . If $\begin{array} { r } { \left| \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \Phi ( x _ { i } , x _ { i } ^ { \prime } ) \right| } \end{array}$ is bounded, then

$$
\Big | \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \{ F ( { \pmb x } _ { i } ^ { \prime } ) - F ( { \pmb x } _ { i } ) \} \Big |
$$

is bounded. The function is then said to be of bounded variation on $J ^ { o }$ ·

The integral of a bounded function $f ( { \pmb x } )$ in the interval $[ a , b ]$ depends on the interval. This interval function obeys the mean value theorem relative to $f ( { \pmb x } )$ and has the property of complete additivity. Such an integral is called a Riemann integral, which is an ordinary integral. $\mathbf { I f } f ( { \pmb x } )$ is integrable in the Riemann sense then $f ( x )$ is measurable and the Lebesgue integral of $f$ coincides with the Riemann integral (Vallée Poussin,1916;Hahn,1921; Baire,1925;Hobson,1926; Sierpinski,1928, 1934；Schlesinger and Plessner, 1926； Kestelman, 1937； Saks, 1937; Lebesgue，1930；Halmos，1950；McShane，1947；Thielman，1953; Natanson, 1955, 1960; Whitney, 1957; Bourbaki,1963).

# 2.2FOURIER SERIES

If we can determine $N = N ( \epsilon )$ independent of $_ { x }$ for a given $\epsilon$ such that

$$
| f _ { n + 1 } ( x ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + f _ { n + k } ( x ) | < \epsilon ,
$$

with an integer $k$ and an integer $n > N$ for all values of $_ { x }$ in the given interval, then the sum $\textstyle \sum _ { n } f _ { n } ( x )$ is said to be uniformly convergent in that interval. If $\textstyle \sum _ { n } f _ { n } ( x )$ is uniformly convergent and $f _ { n } ( x )$ is continuous at $\boldsymbol { x } = \boldsymbol { x } _ { 0 } ,$ ，then $\begin{array} { r } { F ( { \boldsymbol { x } } ) = \sum _ { n } f _ { n } ( { \boldsymbol { x } } ) } \end{array}$ is also continuous at $\scriptstyle x = x _ { 0 }$ and is summable term by term, that is,

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { x = x _ { 0 } } \sum _ { n } f _ { n } ( x ) = \sum _ { n = 0 } ^ { \infty } \operatorname* { l i m } _ { x = x _ { 0 } } f _ { n } ( x ) .
$$

If a series is uniformly convergent and each of its terms is integrable, then $F ( { \pmb x } )$ is also integrable and summable term by term. If each term of $\textstyle \sum _ { n } f _ { n } ( x )$ is differentiable and $\textstyle \sum _ { n } f _ { n } ^ { \prime } ( x )$ is uniformly convergent, (where $f ^ { \prime } ( x )$ is the derivative o $\mathbf { f } f ( { \pmb x } ) )$ and if $\textstyle \sum _ { n } f _ { n } ( x )$ is uniformly convergent at least at one point, then $\textstyle \sum _ { n } f _ { n } ( x )$ is uniformly convergent everywhere, and the derived function of $\pmb { F } ( \pmb { x } )$ is $\begin{array} { r } { F ^ { \prime } ( x ) = \overline { { \sum } } _ { n } f _ { n } ^ { \prime } ( x ) } \end{array}$ ; that is, the series is differentiable term by term (Knopp, 1931).

A series of the form

$$
f ( x ) = { \frac { a _ { 0 } } { 2 } } + \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { \infty } { \bigl ( } a _ { k } \cos k x + b _ { k } \sin k x { \bigr ) }
$$

is called a trigonometric series. If the series is uniformly convergent it represents a periodic function of period $2 \pi$ .Then a sum of finite number of terms of (1） is also called a trigonometric series and represents a singlevalued continuous function. If the absolute value of such a function $f ( x )$ is integrable, then

$$
a _ { k } = { \frac { 1 } { \pi } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } f ( t ) \cos k t d t , \qquad b _ { k } = { \frac { 1 } { \pi } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } f ( t ) \sin k t d t ;
$$

these are called the Fourier coefficients. When $k = 0$ , the function equals $a _ { 0 } / 2$ In such cases the expression of the form (1) is called a Fourier series.

Let $f ( x )$ be continuous in a closed interval $[ a , b ]$ and let ${ \boldsymbol { x } } _ { 1 } , { \boldsymbol { x } } _ { 2 }$ be any two points in this interval.Write the maximum of $| f ( x _ { 2 } ) - f ( x _ { 1 } ) |$ ,with $| x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } | \leq \delta$ as $\omega ( \delta )$ . The quantity $\omega ( \delta )$ is called the modulus of continuity of $f ( x )$ in $[ a , b ]$ . If $\omega ( \delta ) \ \leqq M \delta$ (where $M$ is a constant),it is said to satisfy the Lipschitz condition.If the modulus of continuity $\omega ( \delta )$ of a function $f ( x )$ satisfies the Dini-Lipschitz condition

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { \delta  0 } \omega ( \delta ) \log ( 1 / \delta ) = 0 ,
$$

then the Fourier series of $f ( x )$ converges uniformly to $f ( x )$

If $| f ( x ) |$ is integrable in $[ a , b ]$ , then

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { k \to \infty } \int _ { a } ^ { b } f ( x ) \sin k x d x = 0 , \qquad \operatorname* { l i m } _ { k = \infty } \int _ { a } ^ { b } f ( x ) \cos k x d x = 0 .
$$

$\operatorname { I f } f ( x )$ is continuous and stepwise differentiable in $[ 0 , 2 \pi ]$ - that is,if $f ( { \pmb x } )$ has at most a finite number of discontinuity points in any finite interval - and if $\left| f ^ { \prime } ( x ) \right|$ is integrable, then

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { k \to \infty } k a _ { k } = 0 , \qquad \operatorname* { l i m } _ { k \to \infty } k b _ { k } = { \frac { f ( + 0 ) - f ( - 0 ) } { \pi } } \cdotp
$$

Thus, a Fourier series is integrable term by term.

$\operatorname { I f } f ( x )$ is continuous in $[ 0 , 2 \pi ]$ and is such that $f ( 0 ) = f ( 2 \pi )$ , then the finite sum of trigonometric functions

$$
T _ { n } ( x ) = { \frac { a _ { 0 } } { 2 } } + \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { n } { \bigl ( } \alpha _ { k } \cos k x + \beta _ { k } \sin k x { \bigr ) }
$$

exists for a given $\epsilon > 0$ ,such that the relation $| f ( x ) - T _ { n } ( x ) | \leq \epsilon$ would hold uniformly in $[ 0 , 2 \pi ]$ . This is called the Weierstrassian approximation theorem. If $f ( x )$ is continuous in $[ a , b ]$ ， then for a given $\epsilon$ there exists a polynomial $P ( { \pmb x } )$ such that $| f ( { \pmb x } ) - P ( { \pmb x } ) | \le \epsilon$ uniformly in $[ a , b ]$ . This is another form of the Weierstrassan approximation theorem, using not a trigonometric series but by a series of polynomials.

The Fourier coefficients $a _ { k }$ and $b _ { k }$ can be obtained by minimizing $\int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } ( f ( x ) - T _ { n } ( x ) ) ^ { 2 } d x$ among thesum of trigonometrical functions of the form (3), or by

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } ( f ( x ) - T _ { n } ( x ) ) ^ { 2 } d x = 0 .
$$

This is sometimes called the minimum property of the Fourier coefficients. $\mathbf { I f } f ( { \pmb x } )$ is a quadratically integrable function, then

$$
{ \frac { a _ { 0 } } { 2 } } + \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { \infty } { ( a _ { k } ^ { 2 } + b _ { k } ^ { 2 } ) }
$$

converges to an integral

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { \pi } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } ( f ( x ) ) ^ { 2 } d x .
$$

This is called the completeness theorem of Fourier series.Accordingly, if the Fourier coefficients $\operatorname { o f } f ( { \pmb x } )$ are all zero, then $f ( x )$ is identically zero,and two such series with the same corresponding Fourier coefficients are identical.

The sum

$$
S _ { n } = { \frac { a _ { 0 } } { 2 } } + \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { k }
$$

is called the Fourier partial sum. Then

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle S _ { n } ( \xi ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } \int _ { \xi - \pi } ^ { \xi + \pi } f ( x ) \frac { \sin { ( 2 n + 1 ) } \frac { x - \xi } { 2 } } { \sin { \frac { x - \xi } { 2 } } } d x } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ = \frac { 1 } { \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } \left[ f ( \xi + 2 t ) + f ( \xi - 2 t ) \right] \frac { \sin { ( 2 n + 1 ) } t } { \sin { t } } d t . } } \end{array}
$$

Riemann proved that this $S _ { n } ( \xi )$ depends only on the property of $f ( x )$ at $x = \xi$ and that the integral on the right-hand side of this equation (if the lower limit $o$ of the integral is replaced by a fixed S） converges uniformly to zero as $\pmb { n }$ approaches infinity, for all values of $\xi$

In general, when $\int _ { a } ^ { b } { \bar { f } } ( x ) \ d x$ exists, $f ( x )$ is said to belong to class $L _ { 1 }$ ； when $\int _ { a } ^ { b } | f ( x ) | ^ { 2 } d x$ exists, $f ( { \pmb x } )$ is said to belong to class $L _ { 2 }$ .The integrals are taken in the Lebesgue sense. If $[ a , b ]$ is finite, then $L _ { 2 }$ contains $L _ { 1 }$ ； because, if $| f ( { \pmb x } ) |$ is large, then so is $| f ( x ) | ^ { 2 }$ . But $L _ { 2 }$ and $L _ { 1 }$ are not correlated in the infinite interval $[ - \infty , + \infty ]$ .If $f ( x )$ and $g ( x )$ both belong to class $L _ { 2 }$ ，then $f ( { \pmb x } ) { \pmb g } ( { \pmb x } )$ belongs to $L _ { 1 }$ and

$$
\left( \int _ { a } ^ { b } \left| f ( x ) g ( x ) \right| d x \right) ^ { 2 } \leq \int _ { a } ^ { b } | f ( x ) | ^ { 2 } d x \int _ { a } ^ { b } | g ( x ) | ^ { 2 } d x .
$$

This is called the Schwarz inequality.In fact, since $( | f ( { \pmb x } ) | + \lambda | g ( { \pmb x } ) | ) ^ { 2 }$ is integrable, the inequality $2 | f ( x ) g ( x ) | \leq | f ( x ) | ^ { 2 } + | g ( x ) | ^ { 2 }$ gives

$$
\begin{array} { l } { 0 \displaystyle \equiv \int _ { a } ^ { b } ( | f ( x ) | + \lambda | g ( x ) | ) ^ { 2 } d x } \\ { \displaystyle } \\ { = \int _ { a } ^ { b } | f ( x ) | ^ { 2 } d x + 2 \lambda \int _ { a } ^ { b } | f ( x ) g ( x ) | d x + \lambda ^ { 2 } \int _ { a } ^ { b } | g ( x ) | ^ { 2 } d x , } \end{array}
$$

a quadratic expression in $\lambda ,$ in which the discriminant cannot be positive.

We now introduce the Riesz-Fischer theorem :

If $\scriptstyle \sum _ { n } ^ { + \infty } = - \infty \ \left| a _ { n } \right| ^ { 2 }$ is convergent, then there exists a function $f ( { \pmb x } )$ in $[ - \pi , + \pi ]$ ,belonging to class $L _ { 2 }$ ,such that

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } \int _ { - \pi } ^ { \pi } { \bigg | } f ( x ) - \sum _ { k = - n } ^ { n } a _ { k } e ^ { i k x } { \bigg | } ^ { 2 } d x = 0 .
$$

Suppose that we are given a sequence of functions $\{ f _ { \pi } ( x ) \}$ of the class $L _ { 2 }$ in $[ a , b ]$ or in $[ - \infty , + \infty ]$ and that

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { m , n \to \infty } \int _ { a } ^ { b } | f _ { m } ( x ) - f _ { n } ( x ) | ^ { 2 } d x = 0 .
$$

Then there exists a function $f ( x )$ of class $L _ { 2 }$ that satisfies

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } \int _ { a } ^ { b } | f _ { n } ( x ) - f ( x ) | ^ { 2 } d x = 0 .
$$

In such a case $f _ { n } ( x )$ is said to converge to $f ( x )$ in the mean,and is written $f ( x ) = \operatorname { l . i . m . } _ { n  \infty } { \mathcal { f } } _ { n } ( x )$ . This means that we exclude a set of points of measure zero. It does not mean that $f ( x )$ converges at all points $_ x$

By stating that a sequence of functions $\{ \phi _ { \mathfrak { n } } ( { \mathfrak { x } } ) \}$ is orthogonal we mean that the functions are of class $L _ { 2 }$ in $[ a , b ]$ or $[ - \infty , + \infty ]$ and satisfy $\int \phi _ { m } ( x ) { \overline { { \phi _ { n } ( x ) } } } d x = 0$ $( m \neq n )$ in that interval, where $\overline { { \phi ( { \pmb x } ) } }$ denotes the conjugate complex function of $\phi ( { \pmb x } )$ .If $\int | \phi _ { n } ( x ) | ^ { 2 } d x = 1$ ，then the sequence is said to be normal. The minimum value of

$$
\int \left| { f ( x ) - \sum _ { k = - n } ^ { n } a _ { k } \phi _ { k } ( x ) } \right| ^ { 2 } d x
$$

is attained at $a _ { k } = \int f \phi _ { k } d x .$ There exists a function $f ( x )$ of class $L _ { 2 }$ such that $\begin{array} { r } { \operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } \int | f _ { n } - ^ { \bullet } f | ^ { 2 } d x = 0 . \operatorname { I f } f ( x ) } \end{array}$ is of class $L _ { 2 }$ and $\{ \phi _ { \mathfrak { n } } \}$ is a sequence of normal functions, then there exists a function $g ( x )$ of class $L _ { 2 }$ such that

$$
g ( x ) \sim f ( x ) - \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \phi _ { n } ( x ) \int f ( x ) { \overline { { \phi _ { n } ( x ) } } } d x .
$$

Furthermore, $\int g ( x ) { \overline { { \phi _ { n } ( x ) } } } \ d x = 0$ for all values of $\pmb { n }$ .If $f ( x )$ belongs to class $L _ { 2 }$ and

$$
f ( x ) \sim \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \phi _ { n } ( x ) \int f ( x ) { \overline { { \phi _ { n } ( x ) } } } \ d x ,
$$

then the sequence of functions $\{ \phi _ { \mathfrak { n } } \}$ is said to be closed or complete. In order that $\{ \phi _ { \mathfrak { n } } \}$ be complete,it is necessary and suffcient that for a given $\epsilon > 0$ there should exist a function $f ( { \pmb x } )$ of class $L _ { 2 }$ ,an integer $N _ { : }$ and a sum $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { N } a _ { k } \phi _ { k } ( x ) } \end{array}$ such that

$$
\int { \Big | } f ( x ) - \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { N } a _ { k } \phi _ { k } ( x ) { \Big | } ^ { 2 } d x < \epsilon .
$$

Also, in such a case,

$$
\int | f ( x ) | ^ { 2 } d x = \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \left| \int f \bar { \phi } _ { n } d x \right| ^ { 2 } .
$$

This is called the Parseval theorem.

In the theories of perturbation in celestial mechanics we usually employ trigonometric series at present. Those theorems and properties are indispensable for criticism of the theories discussed in the following chapters (Schlesinger and Plessner,1926；Zygmund, 1935；Hobson, 1926；Tonelli,1928；Kaczmarz and Steinhaus, 1935;Lebesgue,1906; Vallée Poussin,1919; Bernstein,1926;Hilb and Riesz,1924;Rogosinski, 1930；Denjoy，l94l， 1949；Hardy and Rogosinski， 1950； Natanson, 1955,1960; Kolmogorov and Fomin, 1961).

# 2.3LAGRANGE'S THEOREM

Let $f ( x )$ bea regular function inside a domain $\pmb { A }$ bounded bya simple closed curve $C$ in a complex plane of $\pmb { x } _ { ; }$ and $\theta ( z )$ be a regular function inside and on the boundary of $C$ Let $^ { a }$ be a zero point of $\theta ( z )$ inside $C ,$ such that $^ { a }$ is a simple root of $\theta ( z ) = 0$ .By a“simple curve” we mean a continuous curve without any double or any multiple point； that is,a curve that does not intersect itself. Let $_ { x }$ be a given point inside $\pmb { A }$ and $| \theta ( x ) | < | \theta ( z ) |$ for all points $_ z$ inside $C .$ Then,by Cauchy’s theorem in the theory of analytic functions, the complex integral

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi i } } \int _ { c } f ( z ) { \frac { \theta ^ { \prime } ( z ) } { \theta ( z ) } } d z
$$

represents the sum of the residues inside $C$ 、In fact, if we write the development of $\theta ( z )$ around $^ { a }$

$$
\theta ( z ) = A _ { 1 } ( z - a ) ^ { \tau } + A _ { 2 } ( z - a ) ^ { \tau + 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

then

$\theta ^ { \prime } ( z ) = A _ { 1 } r ( z - a ) ^ { r - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , \qquad f ( z ) = f ( a ) + ( z - a ) f ^ { \prime } ( a ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot$ ：， and, in addition,

$$
\frac { f ( z ) \theta ^ { \prime } ( z ) } { \theta ( z ) } - \frac { \eta f ( a ) } { z - a }
$$

is regular at $^ { a }$

Hence

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi i } } \int _ { c } { \frac { d z } { z - a } } = 1 , \qquad { \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi i } } \int _ { c } { \frac { d z } { ( z - a ) ^ { \tau } } } = 0 \qquad ( r \neq 1 ) ,
$$

and, hence, the residue of $f ( z ) \theta ^ { \prime } ( z ) / \theta ( z )$ is $\gamma f ( a )$ . From this follows the theorem. In particular, if we put $f ( z ) = 1$ , then the integral

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi i } } \int _ { c } { \frac { \theta ^ { \prime } ( z ) } { \theta ( z ) } } d x
$$

gives the total number of zero points of $\theta ( z )$ inside $C$

In the statement of this theorem we put $\theta ( z ) \ : - \ : \theta ( { \pmb x } )$ in place of $\theta ( z )$ Then, since $| \theta ( x ) | < | \theta ( z ) |$ on $C _ { s }$ ，we can easily show that the total number of zero points of $\theta ( z ) - \theta ( { \boldsymbol { x } } )$ inside $C$ is

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { { \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi i } } \displaystyle \int _ { c } { \frac { \theta ^ { \prime } ( z ) ~ d z } { \theta ( z ) _ { , } - ~ \theta ( x ) } } = { \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi i } } \displaystyle \left[ \int _ { c } { \frac { \theta ^ { \prime } ( z ) } { \theta ( z ) } } d z + \theta ( x ) \displaystyle \int _ { c } { \frac { \theta ^ { \prime } ( z ) } { ( \theta ( z ) ) ^ { 2 } } } d z + \cdot \cdot \cdot \right] } } \\ { { = { \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi i } } \displaystyle \int _ { c } { \frac { \theta ^ { \prime } ( z ) ~ d z } { \theta ( z ) } } . } } \end{array}
$$

This integral represents the number of zero points of $\theta ( z ) \ : - \ : \theta ( { \boldsymbol { x } } )$ inside $C .$ By our assumption, $\theta ( z )$ has only one root - indeed,one simple root 一 $^ { a }$ inside $C .$ Thus $\theta ( z ) - \theta ( { \boldsymbol { x } } )$ has only a simple zero point $z = x$ inside $C .$ Hence,by Cauchy's theorem,

$$
f ( x ) = { \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi i } } \int _ { c } { \frac { f ( z ) \theta ^ { \prime } ( z ) \ d z } { \theta ( z ) \ - \ \theta ( x ) } } \cdotp
$$

We expand the integrand, since $| \theta ( { \pmb x } ) | < \theta ( z )$ , in the form

$$
\begin{array} { l } { f ( x ) = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi i } \left[ \displaystyle \sum _ { n = 0 } ^ { \infty } ( \theta ( x ) ) ^ { n } \int _ { c } \frac { f ( z ) \theta ^ { \prime } ( z ) } { ( \theta ( z ) ) ^ { n + 1 } } d z \right] } \\ { = \displaystyle \sum _ { n = 0 } ^ { \infty } A _ { n } ( \theta ( x ) ) ^ { n } , \qquad A _ { n } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi i } \int _ { c } \frac { f ( z ) \theta ^ { \prime } ( z ) \ d z } { ( \theta ( z ) ) ^ { n + 1 } } . } \end{array}
$$

Or, from integration by parts,

$$
A _ { n } = { \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi i n } } \int _ { C } { \frac { f ^ { \prime } ( z ) } { ( \theta ( z ) ) ^ { n } } } d z .
$$

Now we write $\theta ( z ) = ( z - a ) \theta _ { 1 } ( z )$ . The function $\theta _ { 1 } ( z )$ is regular on and inside $c$ and does not vanish. Hence,

$$
{ \cal A } _ { n } = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi i n } \int _ { \cal C } \frac { f ^ { \prime } ( z ) ~ d z } { ( z - a ) ^ { n } ( \theta _ { 1 } ( z ) ) ^ { n } } = \frac { 1 } { n ! } \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } } { d a ^ { n - 1 } } \left[ \frac { f ^ { \prime } ( a ) } { ( \theta _ { 1 } ( a ) ) ^ { n } } \right] ,
$$

because

$$
\frac { 1 } { 2 \pi i } \int _ { c } \frac { f ( z ) ~ d z } { ( z - a ) ^ { n } } = \frac { 1 } { ( n - 1 ) ! } f ^ { ( n - 1 ) } ( a ) .
$$

Especially for $n = 0$ , using Cauchy's residue theorem again gives

$$
A _ { 0 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi i } \int _ { c } \frac { f ( z ) \theta ^ { \prime } ( z ) } { \theta _ { 1 } ( z ) } \frac { d z } { z - a } = f ( a ) .
$$

Thus, if we put

$$
\theta _ { 1 } ( z ) = \frac { 1 } { \phi ( z ) } , \qquad \theta ( x ) = t , \qquad \frac { z - a } { \phi ( z ) } = \theta ( z ) ,
$$

then

$$
| \theta ( x ) | < \left| \frac { z - a } { \phi ( z ) } \right| \qquad \mathrm { o n } ~ C .
$$

This leads to the following theorem of Lagrange (Whittaker and Watson, 1924;Goursat, 1918):

Let $f ( z ) , \phi ( z )$ be regular on and inside a closed curve $C$ around $^ { a }$ and let $t$ be such that $| t \phi ( z ) | < | z - a |$ holds for all points $z$ on $C$ Then the equation for x, $x = a + t \phi ( x )$ , admits one root inside $C$ Any function regular on and inside $C$ can be expanded in an integral power series of $\pmb { t }$ in the form

$$
f ( x ) = f ( a ) + \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \frac { t ^ { n } } { n ! } \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } } { d a ^ { n - 1 } } [ f ^ { \prime } ( a ) ( \phi ( a ) ) ^ { n } ] .
$$

# 2.4ASYMPTOTIC MOTION AND LIBRATION

Consider a dynamical system with one degree of freedom

$$
{ \frac { d q } { d t } } = { \frac { \partial H } { \partial \dot { p } } } , { \frac { d \dot { p } } { d t } } = - { \frac { \partial H } { \partial q } } , H = { \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } A \dot { p } ^ { 2 } - U ( q ) .
$$

The corresponding Hamilton-Jacobi partial diferential equation is

$$
A ( q ) \bigg ( \frac { \partial V } { \partial q } \bigg ) ^ { 2 } = 2 ( U + \alpha ) ,
$$

and the solution is

$$
V = \int \ { \sqrt { \frac { 2 ( U + \alpha ) } { A ( q ) } } } d q , \qquad t - \ t _ { 0 } = { \frac { \partial V } { \partial a } } = \int { \frac { d q } { \sqrt { 2 ( U + \alpha ) A ( q ) } } } \cdotp
$$

Take as the independent variable in place of $t$ a new variable called a mechanical variable, which increases monotonically and continuously with $t$ and becomes infinite together with t.In our case the mechanical variable is $q$ ; writing $2 ( U + \alpha ) A ( q ) = F ( q )$ gives

$$
\left( { \frac { d q } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } = F ( q ) .
$$

By our assumption, $F ( q )$ is continuous; it either does or does not vanish for some values of $q$

First suppose that $F ( q )$ does not vanish and is always positive. If $F ( q )$ were negative, then the motion would be imaginary. Further, $F ( q )$ is supposed to be an even function of $q$ ,and to remain finite for any finite value of $\pmb q$ ,and,in particular, to be periodic with period $2 \pi$ with regard to $q$ As $F ( q ) \neq 0$ , it can be expanded by the Weierstrassian approximation theorem of Section 2.2 in a Fourier series

$$
( F ( q ) ) ^ { - 1 / 2 } = A _ { 0 } + A _ { 1 } \cos q + A _ { 2 } \cos 2 q + \cdot \cdot \cdot . .
$$

By our assumption this does not vanish, so that

$$
| A _ { 0 } | > | A _ { 1 } | + | A _ { 2 } | + \cdots ,
$$

which is a suffcient condition. Hence

$$
t - t _ { 0 } = \int ( F ( q ) ) ^ { - 1 / 2 } d q = A _ { 0 } q + A _ { 1 } \sin q + { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } A _ { 2 } \sin 2 q + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

and

$$
| A _ { 0 } | > | A _ { 1 } | + \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } | A _ { 2 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

Since the frst term is the largest, we integrate this expresson with respect to $q$ from 0 to $2 \pi _ { : }$ obtaining $2 T = 2 \pi A _ { 0 }$ .By Lagrange's theorem of Section 2.3 we can expand $q$ in the form

$$
q = \frac { t - t _ { 0 } } { A _ { 0 } } + \sum _ { j = 1 } A _ { j } \sin { \left( j \frac { t - t _ { 0 } } { A _ { 0 } } \right) } ,
$$

if $| \phi ( q ) | < | t - t _ { 0 } - A _ { 0 } q |$ ，where $t - t _ { 0 } = A _ { 0 } q + \phi ( q )$ . Hence,when $\pmb { t }$ increases by ${ 2 T } _ { \ O }$ ，the variable $q$ increases by $2 \pi$ .Thus $q$ ,regarded as an angular variable,rotates through the whole circumference of a circle from $q = 0$ to $q = 2 \pi$ This is called the case of revolution.

Next, suppose that $F ( q )$ can become zero. $q$ regarded as an angular variable cannot take all values but it oscillates between two values of $q$ smaller than $2 \pi$ ； that is, it cannot pass through the value $^ { a }$ for which $F ( a ) = 0$ In fact, if we put $F ( q ) = ( q - a ) ^ { m } \phi ( a ) , \phi ( a ) \neq 0$ ，then $\phi ( a )$ can be expanded in a Taylor series and

$$
\frac { d q } { ( q - a ) ^ { m / 2 } } \left[ C _ { 0 } + C _ { 1 } ( q - a ) + C _ { 2 } ( q - a ) ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \right] = \pm d t .
$$

In particular, if $\pmb { m }$ is odd, then

$$
\frac { 2 } { ( q - a ) ^ { m / 2 - 1 } } \left[ \frac { C _ { 0 } } { m - 2 } + \frac { C _ { 1 } } { m - 4 } ( q - a ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot \right] = \mp ( t - t _ { 0 } ) ;
$$

if $_ { \pmb { m } }$ is even, then

$$
_ { \imath / 2 - 1 } \log { ( q - a ) } + \frac { 2 } { ( q - a ) ^ { m / 2 - 1 } } \left[ \frac { C _ { 0 } } { m - 2 } + \frac { C _ { 1 } } { m - 4 } ( q - a ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot \right]
$$

When $q$ approaches the value $^ { a }$ ,the absolute value of the left-hand side increases; hence, $t$ increases, provided that $m / 2 \geq 1$ . The variable $q$ does not approach $^ { a }$ for any finite value of $t .$ Suppose that $q$ does not pass through $\pmb { a }$ This is called a case of asymptotic motion. If $m = 1$ and $^ { a }$ is a simple root of $F ( q ) = 0 .$ then

$$
q - a = A _ { 1 } ( t - t _ { 0 } ) ^ { 2 } + A _ { 2 } ( t - t _ { 0 } ) ^ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

For $q = a _ { \mathrm { : } }$ $t = t _ { 0 }$ ； thus $q$ reaches $^ { a }$ for a finite value of $t$ but never through $_ { a }$ ; it turns back and follows the same route in the opposite sense. Such a point is called an ordinary point.

Let two consecutive roots of $F ( q ) = 0$ be $_ { a }$ and $b$ . Then

$$
\left( \frac { d q } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } = ( q - a ) ^ { m } ( b - q ) ^ { n } \psi ( q ) , \qquad \psi ( a ) \ne 0 , \qquad \psi ( b ) \ne 0 ;
$$

or

$$
\left( \frac { d q } { d w } \right) ^ { 2 } = \epsilon ^ { 2 } ( q - a ) ^ { m } ( b - q ) ^ { n } , \left( \frac { d w } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { \epsilon ^ { 2 } } \psi ( q ) .
$$

Take w such that $w = 0$ for $t = 0$ , and suppose that $d t$ and dw are of the same sign. The variable ${ \pmb w }$ is the mechanical variable. If $q$ is initially between $^ { a }$ and $b$ ，then $q$ always lies between $^ { a }$ and $b$

Let $m = n = 1$ ，then

$$
\frac { d q } { d w } = \epsilon \sqrt { ( q - a ) ( b - q ) } \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad q = a \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { \epsilon w } { 2 } + b \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { \epsilon w } { 2 } .
$$

Hence, $q$ is a periodic function of $\boldsymbol { \omega }$ with period $2 \pi / \epsilon$ with respect to $\boldsymbol { \omega }$ The variable $q$ is in this case a periodic function of $t$ ,also. Actually,

$$
\int _ { 0 } ^ { w } \frac { \epsilon d w } { \sqrt { \psi ( q ) } } = t .
$$

The variable $q$ does not change when w increases by $2 \pi / \epsilon$ ，while $t$ increases by $2 T .$ Since $q$ is a periodic function of $\epsilon w$ ，

$$
