\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle T = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { \epsilon d w } { \sqrt { \psi ( a \cos ^ { 2 } { ( \epsilon w / 2 ) } + b \sin ^ { 2 } { ( \epsilon w / 2 ) } ) } } } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ = \int _ { a } ^ { b } \frac { d q } { \sqrt { ( q - a ) ( b - q ) \psi ( q ) } } . } } \end{array}
$$

In order that the periods with respect to ${ \boldsymbol w }$ and to $t$ be equal, $\epsilon$ should equal ${ \pi } / T .$ Thus $q$ is periodic with respect to $t$ ； from the Weierstrassian approximation theorem,

$$
q = { \scriptstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } B _ { 0 } + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { \infty } B _ { j } \cos j \frac { \pi t } { T }  .
$$

In this case $q$ does not make the whole revolution on the circumference of a circle； it oscillates between two limits smaller than $2 \pi$ around $\begin{array} { r } { q = \frac { 1 } { 2 } B _ { 0 } } \end{array}$ . This is called a libration. In a region where $F ( q )$ is positive between two consecutive simple roots of $F ( q ) = 0$ ,there isa libration. It is necessary to state in what variable the libration takes place. The motion with regard to $q$ is a libration, but in our case it is also a revolution with regard to w (Hagihara, 1944;Brown, l932). The phenomenon of libration is very important in celestial mechanics.The moon librates around its axis of rotation with respect to the line connecting the moon and the earth; this is called the physical libration and causes more than half the surface area of the moon to be visible from the earth.The perisaturnia of some satellites of Saturn librate around the perisaturnia or the nodes of other satellite orbits.The librations of the satellites of Jupiter are most remarkable.Librations also occur in the motion of some asteroids.

If $_ m$ or $\boldsymbol { n }$ or both equal or exceed 2,an asymptotic motion takes place. The moving point approaches the limit point but never passes through it in the limit as $t$ approaches infinity. When two simple zero points $q = a$ and $q = b$ coincide and become a double zero point, then the moving point $C$ comes to rest at that double point if the domain of real motion degenerates to this single point；but the real motion is asymptotic from both sides of the double zero point if the domain of imaginary motion degenerates to this single point. When the root is multiple in an odd order, then the moving point is either at rest at a point in the domain of real motion or approaches it asymptotically. Furthermore,in the domain between the largest real root and $+ \infty$ and in the domain between the smallest real root and $- \infty$ , the motion is asymptotic for $q = + \infty$ and $q = - \infty$ ,respectively. The motion, beginning with $q = + \infty$ or $q = - \infty$ at $t = - \infty$ ,reaches the largest or the smallest real simple zero point in a finite time and returns to the initial point for $t = + \infty$ . When the real zero point is multiple, the motion is asymptotic to that point.

In order to study the motion at $q = \pm \infty$ ,we write $q = 1 / u _ { \colon }$ as in the theory of analytic functions, and study the motion at $u = 0$ Put

$$
F ( q ) = C _ { n } q ^ { n } + C _ { n - 1 } q ^ { n - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + C _ { 0 } ;
$$

then,

$$
d t = - \frac { d u } { u ^ { 2 - n / 2 } \sqrt { C _ { n } + C _ { n - 1 } u + \cdot \cdot \cdot + C _ { 0 } u ^ { n } } } \cdot
$$

If $n \neq 2$ ， the motion is asymptotic at $u = 0$ or $q = \infty$ .If $n = 2$ ，the motion is also asymptotic.But,if the domain of real motion extends from the largest real simple zero point to $+ \infty$ ，or from $- \infty$ to the smallest

real simple zero point, then we can transform the real domain to a real finite domain by

$$
q = \frac { \alpha v + \beta } { \gamma v + \delta }
$$

as in the theory of elliptic functions,modular functions,and automorphic functions, the motion is a libration with regard to the variable $v$ in that domain, that is, the singular character at $q = \infty$ with regard to $v$ is only an apparent one. For example, if we take $F ( q ) = ( q - a ) ( b - q )$ ，then if we write $q = c + 1 / v$ $( a < c < b )$ ,the motion is a libration between $- 1 / ( c - a )$ and $+ 1 / ( b - c )$ with regard to $v$

When $F ( q )$ is a third-degree polynomial with respect to $q , \psi ( q )$ may become zero and the above condition is not satisfed.When $F ( q )$ isa fourth degree polynomial and has four real zero points $a < b < c < d ,$ then the real motion of libration takes place in the real domain in which the initial position is situated. But, if the domain extends to values smaller than $^ { a }$ or larger than $d ,$ the motion is asymptotic at $q = - \infty$ or $q = + \infty$ . The polynomial $F ( q )$ can then be transformed to its canonical form

$$
{ \cal F } ( q ) = 4 q ^ { 3 } - g _ { 2 } q - g _ { 3 } ,
$$

in the theory of elliptic functions, where $g _ { 2 }$ and $g _ { 3 }$ are the invariants of quartic forms. One zero point is driven to infinity (Hagihara, 1931; Appell and Lacour, 1922; Halphen, 1886).

More generally, when $F ( q )$ is an algebraic polynomial, the behavior of the motion can be discussed as it is in the theory of hyperelliptic functions (Appell and Goursat, 1895；Baker, 1897, i907；Bliss,1933; Osgood,1932; Roberts and Hartford, 1938; see also Sections 6.23, 6.25).

# CONDITIONALLY PERIODIC MOTIONS

# 2.5MULTIPLY PERIODIC FUNCTIONS

When a real function $\pmb { F } ( \pmb { x } )$ satisfies the condition $F ( { \boldsymbol { \mathscr { x } } } ) = F ( { \boldsymbol { \mathscr { x } } } + { \boldsymbol { \omega } } )$ with a real constant $\omega$ for all real values of $_ x$ ,the function $\pmb { F } ( \pmb { x } )$ is said to have a period $\omega$ . With any positive or negative integer $\pmb { \tau }$ we have

$$
\begin{array} { r } { F ( { \pmb x } ) = F ( { \pmb x } + { \pmb \tau } { \pmb \omega } ) ; } \end{array}
$$

that is, if $\pmb { \omega }$ is a period, then its positive integral multiple or its negative integral multiple is also a period. Suppose that $\boldsymbol { F } ( \boldsymbol { x } )$ has another real period $\omega ^ { \prime }$ .If $F ( { \pmb x } ) = F ( { \pmb x } + { \pmb \omega } ^ { \prime } )$ , then $\scriptstyle { \tau ^ { \prime } \omega ^ { \prime } }$ with any positive or negative integer $\scriptstyle { \pmb { \tau } } ^ { \prime }$ is also a period.Hence, $\tau \omega + \tau ^ { \prime } \omega ^ { \prime }$ is also a period.

Assume that $F ( x )$ has no infinitely small period. If $\boldsymbol { F } ( \boldsymbol { x } )$ has two real periods $\omega$ and $\omega ^ { \prime }$ , then we can choose $\tau \omega + \tau ^ { \prime } \omega ^ { \prime }$ as small as we please by taking two integers $\boldsymbol { \tau }$ and $\tau ^ { \prime }$ suitably,as will bedescribedin Kronecker's theorem in Section 2.8. But, since by our assumption $\boldsymbol { F } ( \boldsymbol { x } )$ has no infinitely small period,the two periods $\omega$ and $\omega ^ { \prime }$ must be commensurable; that is, they must be in the ratio of two integers. Consider now the ratio ${ \pmb \beta } / q$ of two mutually prime integers. If $\pmb { \omega }$ is a period, then any other period must be in the form $\scriptstyle { \phi \omega } / { q }$ If we choose $\pmb { \tau }$ and $\scriptstyle \tau ^ { \prime }$ such that

$$
\tau q + \tau ^ { \prime } p = 1 ,
$$

then $\tau \omega + \tau ^ { \prime } \hbar \omega / q = \omega / q$ and $\omega / q$ is also a period. By assumption there should be the smallest number $\omega / q _ { 1 }$ among all periods of the form $\omega / q$ Hence,there should be necessarily a smallest period if there is no infinitely small period.

Next we consider a function $F ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ of $\pmb { n }$ variables $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ,$ and suppose that when $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ increase by $\omega _ { i 1 } , \omega _ { i 2 } , . . . , \omega _ { i n } ,$ respectively, the function $F ( x )$ has the same value. Then we say that $\pmb { F } ( \pmb { x } )$ is multiply periodic with the period $\mathbf { \Psi } ( \omega _ { i } ) = ( \omega _ { i 1 } , \omega _ { i 2 } , . . . , \omega _ { i n } )$ . The result of multiplying every element $\omega _ { i k } - k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n -$ of $\left( \omega _ { i } \right)$ by a fixed integer is also a multiple period. If $\left( \omega _ { i } \right)$ and $\left( \omega _ { j } \right)$ are both multiple periods of $\pmb { F } ( \pmb { x } )$ ， then $( \omega _ { i } + \omega _ { j } ) = ( \omega _ { i 1 } + \omega _ { j 1 } , . . . , \omega _ { i n } + \omega _ { j n } )$ is also a multiple period $\operatorname { o f } F ( x )$ $\begin{array} { r } { ( \tau \omega _ { i } + \tau ^ { \prime } \omega _ { j } ) = ( \tau \omega _ { i 1 } + \tau ^ { \prime } \omega _ { j 1 } , } \end{array}$ $\tau \omega _ { i n } + \tau ^ { \prime } \omega _ { j n } )$ is also a multiple period. Generally,

$$
\left( \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { m } \tau _ { k } \omega _ { k 1 } , \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { m } \tau _ { k } \omega _ { k 2 } , . . . , \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { m } \tau _ { k } \omega _ { k n } \right) \qquad ( m \leq n )
$$

is also a multiple period.When there is no relation of the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { \tau _ { 1 } } { \omega _ { 1 j } } + \frac { \tau _ { 2 } } { \omega _ { 2 j } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \frac { \tau _ { m } } { \omega _ { m j } } = 0 ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) , } \end{array}
$$

with positive or negative integers $\tau _ { 1 } , \tau _ { 2 } , . . . , \tau _ { m }$ (all of which do not vanish at the same time),then the multiple periods $\left( \omega _ { i } \right)$ (all of which do not vanish at the same time for $i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m )$ , are said to be independent.When a multiple period $\left( \omega _ { l } \right)$ is connected with other multiple periods $\left( \omega _ { i } \right)$ by a linear homogeneous relation, then the multiple period $\left( \omega _ { l } \right)$ is said to belong to a field of multiple periods $\left( \omega _ { i } \right)$ ,and the $_ m$ multiple periods $\left( \omega _ { i } \right)$ are called the primitive periods of order $_ m$ (Esclangon,1902,1903,1904, 1916).

In general, a system admitting the operations of addition and multi-


<!-- chunk 0003: pages 141-210 -->
plication,excluding the commutative law in multiplication, is called a ring. If a ring has at least one nonzero element, if $a x = b$ $( a \ne 0 )$ can be solved for $\pmb { x } _ { i }$ ,and if the ring obeys the commutative law, then it is called a field.By this definition there must bea unit element in a field and also a reciprocal element (Van der Waerden, l937). When $F ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } )$ cannot be reduced to a function of a smaller number of variables $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { p } , \textcircled { } < n _ { \cdot }$ ，by a linear transformation from $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ;$ the function is $\boldsymbol { F }$ called irreducible.

The multiple period $\left( \omega _ { i 1 } , \omega _ { i 2 } , . . . , \omega _ { i n } \right)$ can be represented as the components of a vector $\left( \omega _ { i } \right)$ in an $\pmb { n }$ -dimensional space.The function $F ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } )$ can be considered to belong to a point $( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } )$ of that space.Suppose that there is at least one point at which the function $F ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } )$ is continuous for all variables $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ on the straight line $\Delta$ ：

$$
x _ { j } = a _ { j } + \omega _ { i j } t \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

in this space. Any such function with this property -in particular, a function that is continuous and irreducible-must be a constant; otherwise, it cannot have an infinitely small period.

For the proof of this theorem of Esclangon we first suppose that $F$ does have an infinitely small period. (Eventually we will show that this is impossible.）As it is irreducible,the function

$$
\Phi ( t ) = F ( a _ { 1 } + \omega _ { i 1 } t , a _ { 2 } + \omega _ { i 2 } t , . . . , a _ { n } + \omega _ { i n } t )
$$

would not be a constant on the corresponding straight line $\Delta$ for any set of values of $\omega _ { i 1 } , \omega _ { i 2 } , . . . , \omega _ { i n }$ 、In fact,if it were a constant on a certain straight line,it would be expressible as a function of a smaller number of variables and become reducible by a transformation that makes the straight line one of the coordinate axes. Now we draw a sphere with radius l and the origin $O ( a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , . . . , a _ { n } )$ as center,and let the points $\omega _ { i j } / \big | \omega _ { i } \big |$ ,where $| \omega _ { i } | = | \sqrt { \omega _ { i 1 } ^ { 2 } + \omega _ { i 2 } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \ \omega _ { i n } ^ { 2 } } |$ $( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ corre-spond to the points on the surface of the sphere. Let $\{ \epsilon _ { 1 } \} , \{ \epsilon _ { 2 } \} , \ldots \{ \epsilon _ { n } \}$ be a set of sequences, each tending to zero. Then there will be a multiple period $\left( \omega _ { i } \right)$ with its absolute values $\left| \omega _ { i } \right|$ each smaller than the corresponding $\epsilon$ ，Denote them by $( \omega ^ { \prime } ) , ( \omega ^ { \prime \prime } ) , \ldots , ( \omega ^ { ( k ) } ) , \ldots ,$ and represent them by the respective points $P ^ { \prime } , P ^ { \prime \prime } , . . . , P ^ { ( k ) } ,$ ... on the surface of the sphere.The sequence of points $P ^ { \prime } , P ^ { \prime \prime } , . . . , P ^ { ( k ) } , . . .$ will have its limit point $P ^ { ( o ) } \big ( \omega ^ { ( o ) } \big )$ on the sphere.There should be at least one multiple period with its absolute value smaller than $\epsilon$ inside a small circle on the sphere with $P ^ { ( o ) }$ as its center. Take a point $Q$ in the space,and a point $Q ^ { \prime }$ on the straight line through $Q$ drawn parallel to $O P ^ { ( o ) }$ . It is required to prove that there is a point $Q _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ in the neighborhood of $Q$ such that

$$
F ( Q _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ) = F ( Q ) .
$$

Draw straight lines $\Delta _ { k } , k = 1 , 2 , \ldots ,$ which pass, respectively, through points $( \omega _ { k } ) , 2 ( \omega _ { k } ) , . . . , \phi ( \omega _ { k } )$ ， $k = 1 , 2 , \ldots ,$ with $Q$ as origin. These points form a point set $E$ 、For example,all points on $\boldsymbol { Q } \boldsymbol { Q } ^ { \prime }$ are the limit points of this point set $\boldsymbol { \varepsilon }$ .We draw, with $Q ^ { \prime }$ as center,a hemisphere $s$ with radius $\rho$ and a hemisphere $S ^ { \prime }$ with radius $\rho / 2$ . Since the straight lines $\Delta _ { k }$ have their limit $\boldsymbol { Q } \boldsymbol { Q } ^ { \prime }$ ， there are an infinite number of points belonging to the hemisphere $S ^ { \prime }$ . Among these points corresponding to the multiple periods $( \omega _ { k j } )$ there should certainly be at least one of absolute value less than $\rho / 2$ .Let $\Delta _ { k }$ be the straight line corresponding to one such point.Then the hemisphere $S$ should contain in its interior one point $Q _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ at the ends of the lines through $( \omega _ { k j } ) , 2 ( \omega _ { k j } ) , \ldots .$ As we can make the radius of $s$ as small as we please, $Q ^ { \prime }$ should be a limit point of $\textstyle E .$ Hence, all points on $Q Q ^ { \prime }$ are the limit points of $\pmb { { \cal E } }$ Now at the points belonging to the point set $E _ { : }$ ，the function $F$ takes the value $F ( Q )$ . If $\boldsymbol { F }$ is continuous at $Q ^ { \prime }$ ,then its value should be $F ( Q )$ . Since it is irreducible, $F$ cannot be a constant on a straight line parallel to $\boldsymbol { Q } \boldsymbol { Q } ^ { \prime }$ .Let $\boldsymbol { Q } \boldsymbol { Q } ^ { \prime }$ be a straight line on which $\pmb { F }$ is not a constant. On our assumption concerning $F _ { : }$ ，there must certainly be a point $Q ^ { \prime }$ at which $F$ is continuous on the line $Q Q ^ { \prime }$ .Let $Q$ be another arbitrary point on the straight line; then

$$
F ( Q ) = F ( Q ^ { \prime } )
$$

must hold. Thus $F$ must be a constant on $Q Q ^ { \prime }$ . This is contradictory to our assumption. Hence, $F$ cannot have an infinitely small period.

An irreducible function with a finite multiple period does not neces-sarily satisfy the above assumption. To do this (so Esclangon proved) the function should be a continuous function, or a pointwise discontinuous function (as defined by Baire) or a totally discontinuous function (Section 2.1;Baire, 1925;de la Vallée Poussin,1916; Hahn,l92l).When the points of discontinuity of a function $f$ are compact - that is, when any partial set with an infinite number of elements taken out from a point set composed of such discontinuity points has always its limit point 一 then the discontinuous function $f$ is said to be totally discontinuous and, when the discontinuity points offare not compact $f$ is said to be pointwise discontinuous, according to Baire.However, if an irreducible function has an infinitely small period, then the function must be a totally discontinuous function, in Baire's sense,at least on a certain straight line.We do not consider such a function with an infinitely smal period. Esclangon calls an irreducible continuous function with a finite multiple period a uniformly continuous quasi-periodic function. From now on we consider only uniformly continuous quasi-periodic functions and call them merely quasi-periodic functions.Then

$$
\Phi ( t ) = F ( a _ { 1 } + \omega _ { i 1 } t , . . . , a _ { n } + \omega _ { i n } t )
$$

should be periodic for $t$ in the same direction, that is,along $\left( \omega _ { i } \right)$ , whatever the values of $a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots , a _ { n }$ are. The smallest common period of $\Phi ( t )$ as we vary $( a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots , a _ { n } )$ should be in the form $( \omega _ { i } ) / q$ ,where $q$ is an integer. Hence, $\pmb { F }$ has a multiple period $( \omega _ { i 1 } / q , \omega _ { i 2 } / q , . ~ . ~ . , \omega _ { i n } / q )$ . Of course, the case when $\Phi ( t )$ is continuous in $t$ is a particular case of our assumption; therefore $F$ should have the smallest multiple periods.

Consider only the minimum periods among the periods of the same direction in the $\pmb { n }$ -dimensional space.We construct a parallelogram with $\left( \omega _ { i } \right)$ and $\scriptstyle ( \omega _ { j } )$ as the two sides and a latice system obtained by translating $\left( \omega _ { i } \right)$ and $( \omega _ { j } )$ by positive and negative integral multiples of the remaining $( \omega ) ^ { \circ } \mathbf { s }$ ，The periods $\tau _ { i } ( \omega _ { i } ) + \tau _ { j } ( \omega _ { j } )$ are all represented by this lattice system. In general,we take $\left( \omega _ { k } \right)$ ， $k = 1 , 2 , . . . , m$ ，as the basis period and obtain a lattice system consisting of an $_ { n }$ -dimensional parallelopiped. We call the parallelopiped formed by the $m$ vectors $\left( \omega _ { k } \right)$ the basis parallelopiped.When $n = m$ ,this lattice system flls up the $_ { n }$ dimensional space.We transform this by a linear homogeneous transformation with positive or negative integers $\tau _ { j k }$ as the coefficients:

$$
\left( \omega _ { j } ^ { \prime } \right) = \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { n } \tau _ { j k } \cdot \left( \omega _ { k } \right) \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

The transformed period $( \omega _ { j } ^ { \prime } )$ also belongs to the basis parallelopiped. Moreover, if $\| \pmb { \tau } _ { i j } \| = \pm 1$ ,then the volume of the basis parallelopiped is invariant.This is a unimodular transformation.Here the vector $\left( \omega _ { j } ^ { \prime } \right)$ is also independent and is a basis period of this parallelopiped.Let $\left( \omega _ { j } ^ { \prime } \right)$ be transformed into $( 0 , 0 , . . . , 0 , \omega _ { j j } ^ { \prime } , 0 , . . . , 0 )$ by such a transformation. Put $\omega _ { j j } ^ { \prime } = \omega _ { j }$ .Then the transformed basis periods are in the canonical form:

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \begin{array} { c c c c c } { \omega _ { 1 } } & { 0 } & { } & { } & { 0 } \\ { } & { } & { } & { } & { } \\ { 0 } & { \omega _ { 2 } } & { } & { } & { 0 } \\ { \vdots } & { } & { } & { } & { } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { } & { } & { \omega _ { n } . } \end{array} } \end{array}
$$

Apply the same unimodular transformation to $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ and denote the result by $w _ { 1 } , w _ { 2 } , \ldots , w _ { n }$ Then

$$
w _ { j } = \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { n } \tau _ { j k } x _ { k } \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

This means that the vectors representing the original periods $( \omega _ { j } )$ become the new coordinate axes ${ { w } _ { k } }$ . These w-axes are not necessarily orthogonal. If $m \textless n$ ，then only $\textstyle n - m$ coordinate axes are transformed by this operation.We further change the scale of length of these coordinates such that the transformed parallelopiped has the sides

$$
\begin{array} { c c c c } { { 2 \pi } } & { { 0 } } & { { } } & { { 0 } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { { 0 } } & { { 2 \pi } } & { { } } & { { 0 } } \\ { { \vdots } } & { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { { 0 } } & { { 0 } } & { { } } & { { 2 \pi . } } \end{array}
$$

Denote the result of this transformation of $F ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } )$ by

$$
F ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) = \Psi ( w _ { 1 } , w _ { 2 } , . . . , w _ { n } ) .
$$

This can be writen by Weierstrass's auxiliary theorem, which is proved in Section 2.6, immediately following,in the form

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \Psi ( w _ { 1 } , w _ { 2 } , . . . , w _ { n } ) } \\ { = \sum _ { \nu _ { 1 } , \nu _ { 2 } , . . . , \nu _ { n } } C _ { \nu _ { 1 } \nu _ { 2 } . . . \nu _ { n } } \exp { [ \sqrt { - 1 } \left( \nu _ { 1 } w _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \nu _ { n } w _ { n } \right) ] } . } \end{array}
$$

This result is of great importance in the quantum theory of Bohr and Kramers.

# 2.6WEIERSTRASSS AUXILIARY THEOREM

Suppose that an integral function $g ( v , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ of $n + 1$ variables $v , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ is periodic with period 1 with respect to $v .$ We have then $g ( v + m , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) = g ( v , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } )$ ， for any arbitrary integer $\pmb { m }$ .An integral function is a function that can be developed in a Taylor series with respect to all the independent variables. Now put

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { g _ { 0 } ( v , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } g ( v , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } g ( - v , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { g _ { 1 } ( v , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \sin 2 \pi v } ( \frac { 1 } { 2 } g ( v , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) - \frac { 1 } { 2 } g ( - v , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Then $g _ { 0 }$ and $g _ { 1 }$ are both integral functions of $v , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ Actually the

denominator of $g _ { 1 }$ vanishes only when $2 v$ is equal to an arbitrary integer $m$ Writing $v = ( m / 2 ) + h$ gives

$$
\sin 2 \left( { \frac { m } { 2 } } + h \right) \pi = \pm \sin 2 h \pi ,
$$

$$
g { \Big ( } { - } \frac { m } { 2 } - h , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } { \Big ) } = g { \Big ( } \frac { m } { 2 } - h , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } { \Big ) } .
$$

Hence

$$
g \Big ( \frac { m } { 2 } + h , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } \Big ) - g \Big ( - \frac { m } { 2 } - h , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } \Big ) = h P ( h , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) ,
$$

where $P$ isitselfanintegralfunctionofits variables.Thus, $g _ { 1 } ( v , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ is finite at $v = m / 2$

Now define $G _ { 0 } ( s , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } )$ by varying $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ arbitrarily in the following manner.If

$$
G _ { 0 } ( s , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) = g _ { 0 } ( v , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) ,
$$

with the variable $v$ satisfying $s = \cos 2 \pi v$ for any finite value of $s$ ，then $G _ { 0 } ( s , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } )$ has a definite finite value for any finite values of $s , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ Let $v ^ { \prime }$ be one of the values of $v$ satisfying $s ^ { \prime } = \cos 2 \pi v ^ { \prime }$ for a given $s ^ { \prime }$ ; then any other value of $v$ satisfying this equation is represented by $\pm v ^ { \prime } + m$ For all such values of $v$ the function $g _ { 0 } ( v , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ has the same value. Take $s$ and $s ^ { \prime }$ nearly equal to each other and put $h = 2 \pi ( v - v ^ { \prime } )$ ; then, since

$$
s - s ^ { \prime } = - \sin 2 \pi v ^ { \prime } \Big ( h - { \frac { h ^ { 3 } } { 3 ! } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \Big ) - \cos 2 \pi v ^ { \prime } \Big ( { \frac { h ^ { 2 } } { 2 ! } } - \cdot \cdot \cdot \Big ) ,
$$

we can determine the value of $\boldsymbol { v } - \boldsymbol { v } ^ { \prime }$ satisfying this equation if

$$
\sin 2 \pi v ^ { \prime } \neq 0 .
$$

When sin $2 \pi v ^ { \prime } = 0$ ,then $( \boldsymbol { v } - \boldsymbol { v } ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 }$ is expressed as an integral power series of $s - s ^ { \prime }$ . In the latter case, $v ^ { \prime } = m / 2$ and

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { g _ { 0 } ( v , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) = { \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } g \biggl ( - \frac { m } { 2 } + ( v - v ^ { \prime } ) , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } \biggr ) } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } \qquad - { \bf \sigma } { \bf { \bar { 2 } } } g \biggl ( - \frac { m } { 2 } - ( v - v ^ { \prime } ) , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } \biggr ) } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { = { \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } g \biggl ( \frac { m } { 2 } + ( v - v ^ { \prime } ) , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } \biggr ) } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } \qquad - { \bf \sigma } { \bf { \bar { 2 } } } g \biggl ( \frac { m } { 2 } - ( v - v ^ { \prime } ) , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } \biggr ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Hence,the expansion of $G _ { 0 } ( v , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ has only even powers of $\boldsymbol { v } - \boldsymbol { v } ^ { \prime }$ . In either case $G _ { 0 } ( s , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } )$ can be expanded in an integral power series of $s - s ^ { \prime }$ ， $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ . Hence, $G _ { 0 } ( s , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ is an integral function of $s , \pmb { x } _ { 1 } , \dots , \pmb { x } _ { n }$ and is equal to $g _ { 0 } ( v , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ ,where

$$
s = \cos 2 \pi v .
$$

Similarly, it can be proved that there exists an integral function $G _ { 1 } ( s , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ that becomes $g _ { 1 } ( v , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ if $s = \cos 2 \pi v$ Hence,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { g ( v , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) = G _ { 0 } ( \cos 2 \pi v , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) \phantom { x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x x } } \\ { + G _ { 1 } ( \cos 2 \pi v , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) \sin 2 \pi v . } \end{array}
$$

We now apply this theorem to the function $\phi ( v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , . . . , v _ { n } )$ satisfying

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \phi ( v _ { 1 } + 1 , v _ { 2 } , . . . , v _ { n } ) = \phi ( v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , . . . , v _ { n } ) , } \\ & { } \\ & { \phi ( v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } + 1 , . . . , v _ { n } ) = \phi ( v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , . . . , v _ { n } ) , } \\ & { \vdots } \\ & { \phi ( v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , . . . , v _ { n } + 1 ) = \phi ( v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , . . . , v _ { n } ) , } \end{array}
$$

and, in general,

$$
\phi ( v _ { 1 } + m _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } + m _ { 2 } , . . . , v _ { n } + m _ { n } ) = \phi ( v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , . . . , v _ { n } )
$$

with positive or negative integers $m _ { 1 } , m _ { 2 } , \ldots , m _ { n }$ ，This function can be written in the form

$$
G _ { 0 } ( \cos 2 \pi v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , . . . , v _ { n } ) + G _ { 1 } ( \cos 2 \pi v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , . . . , v _ { n } ) \sin 2 \pi v _ { 1 } ,
$$

and $G _ { 0 }$ and $G _ { 1 }$ each have period l with respect to $v _ { 2 } , \ldots , v _ { n }$ Hence

$$
\begin{array} { r } { G _ { \epsilon , 1 } ( \cos 2 \pi v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , \dotsc , v _ { n } ) = G _ { \epsilon , 0 } ( \cos 2 \pi v _ { 1 } , \cos 2 \pi v _ { 2 } , v _ { 3 } , \dotsc , v _ { n } ) \qquad } \\ { + G _ { \epsilon , 1 } ( \cos 2 \pi v _ { 1 } , \cos 2 \pi v _ { 2 } , v _ { 3 } , \dotsc , v _ { n } ) \cos 2 \pi \omega . } \end{array}
$$

where $\epsilon = 0$ ,1.Both $G _ { \epsilon , \mathfrak { o } }$ and $G _ { \epsilon , 1 }$ have period 1 with respect to $v$ By repeating this procedure we show that $\phi ( v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , . . . . , v _ { n } )$ can be written in the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \begin{array} { r l } { \displaystyle \sum _ { \epsilon _ { 1 } = 0 } ^ { 1 } \sum _ { \epsilon _ { 2 } = 0 } ^ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \sum _ { \epsilon _ { n } = 0 } ^ { 1 } G _ { \epsilon _ { 1 } , \epsilon _ { 2 } , \cdot \cdot \cdot , \epsilon _ { n } } ( \cos 2 \pi v _ { 1 } , \cos 2 \pi v _ { 2 } , . . . , \cos 2 \pi v _ { n } ) } & { } \\ { \times ( \sin 2 \pi v _ { 1 } ) ^ { \epsilon _ { 1 } } ( \sin 2 \pi v _ { 2 } ) ^ { \epsilon _ { 2 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot ( \sin 2 \pi \pi v _ { n } ) } & { \mathrm { o r ~ } \alpha . \cdot } \end{array} } \end{array}
$$

The coefficient $G _ { \epsilon _ { 1 } , \epsilon _ { 2 } , \dots , \epsilon _ { n } }$ is an integral function of

$$
\cos 2 \pi v _ { 1 } , \cos 2 \pi v _ { 2 } , . . . , \cos 2 \pi v _ { n } .
$$

# Writing

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \cos 2 \pi v _ { \alpha } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \exp \left( 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \ v _ { \alpha } \right) \ + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \exp \left( - 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \ v _ { \alpha } \right) } \\ { \sqrt { - 1 } \sin 2 \pi v _ { \alpha } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \exp \left( 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \ v _ { \alpha } \right) \ - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \exp \left( - 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \ v _ { \alpha } \right) } \end{array}
$$

permits the preceding expression to be written in a convergent power series of $_ { n }$ exponential functions

exp $( 2 \pi { \sqrt { - 1 } } \ v _ { n } )$ ,exp $( - 2 \pi { \sqrt { - 1 } } v _ { n } )$

Hence

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , . . . , v _ { n } ) = \displaystyle \sum _ { \lambda _ { 1 } , \mu _ { 1 } , . . . , \lambda _ { n } , \mu _ { n } } A _ { \lambda _ { 1 } \mu _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot \lambda _ { n } \mu _ { n } } } } \\ { { \mathrm { } \times \mathrm { e x p } \left\{ 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \ [ ( \lambda _ { 1 } - \mu _ { 1 } ) v _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + ( \lambda _ { n } - \mu _ { n } ) v _ { 1 } \right. } } \\ { { \left. ( \lambda _ { 1 } , \mu _ { 1 } , . . . , \lambda _ { n } , \mu _ { n } = 0 , 1 , . . . , \alpha \right.}  }  \end{array}
$$

Since this series is unconditionally convergent, we replace

$$
\lambda _ { 1 } - \mu _ { 1 } , . . . , \lambda _ { n } - \mu _ { n }
$$

by $\nu _ { 1 } , \nu _ { 2 } , . . . , \nu _ { n } ;$ respectively. This gives a convergent series

$$
\phi ( v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , . . . , v _ { n } ) \qquad \\  = \sum _ { \scriptstyle \nu _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , . . . , \nu _ { n } \atop v _ { 1 } , \nu _ { 2 } , . . . , \nu _ { n } } C _ { v _ { 1 } \nu _ { 2 } . . . \nu _ { n } } \exp { [ 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \ ( \nu _ { 1 } v _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \nu _ { n } v _ { n } ) ] }
$$

Since this is, moreover,uniformly convergent,

$$
\int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \phi ( v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , . . . , v _ { n } ) \exp \left[ - 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \left( \nu _ { 1 } v _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \nu _ { n } v _ { n } \right) \right] d v _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot 
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { = \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu _ { 1 } , \dots , \mu _ { n } } C _ { \mu _ { 1 } \cdot \dots \cdot \mu _ { n } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } } } \\ { { \mathrm { e x p } \left\{ 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \ [ ( \mu _ { 1 } - \nu _ { 1 } ) v _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + ( \mu _ { n } - \nu _ { n } ) v _ { n } ] \right\} d v _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d v _ { n } } } \\ { { = C _ { v _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot v _ { n } } \qquad ( \mu _ { 1 } , \mu _ { 2 } , \dots , \mu _ { n } = - \infty , \ldots , + \infty ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Hence the coefficients of the series (9） are expressed, similarly to the

Fourier coefficients, in the form (Weierstrass,1866,1895; Osgood,1924; Hobson, 1926)

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { C _ { \nu _ { 1 } \cdots \nu _ { n } } = \displaystyle \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \phi ( v _ { 1 } , . . . , v _ { n } ) } } \\ { { \qquad \times \exp \left[ - 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \left( \nu _ { 1 } v _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \nu _ { n } v _ { n } \right) \right] d v _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d v _ { n } . } } \end{array}
$$

# 2.7CONDITIONALLY PERIODIC MOTION

A conditionally periodic motion has been studied by Staude (1887, 1889) for a dynamical system with two degrees of freedom and by Stäckel (1891,1893) for a general case.

From Stackel's assumption (Section 1.16) concerning the integral functions $\phi _ { i k } ( q _ { i } )$ and $\Phi _ { i } ( q _ { i } )$ , there follows

$$
d t = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { \frac { \phi _ { i k } ( q _ { i } ) d q _ { i } } { \sqrt { \Phi _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) } } } \qquad ( k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Denote the consecutive zero points of $\Phi _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) = 0$ by

$$
a _ { i } < b _ { i } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

and let the initial value of $q _ { i }$ be in this interval.

Writing

$$
\frac { d q _ { i } } { ( q _ { i } - a _ { i } ) ^ { m _ { \mathrm { t } } / 2 } ( b _ { i } - q _ { i } ) ^ { n _ { \mathrm { t } } / 2 } } = \epsilon _ { i } d w _ { i } ,
$$

$$
\Phi _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) = ( q _ { i } - a _ { i } ) ^ { m _ { i } } ( b _ { i } - q _ { i } ) ^ { n _ { i } } \Psi _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) , ~ F _ { i k } ( q _ { i } ) = \frac { \phi _ { i k } ( q _ { i } ) } { \sqrt { \Psi _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) } } ,
$$

we may rewrite equation (ll) to read

$$
d t = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } F _ { i k } ( q _ { i } ) \epsilon _ { i } d w _ { i } \qquad ( k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

f $\Delta = \| \phi _ { i k } \| \neq 0$ , then we get

$$
\frac { 1 } { E } \frac { \partial E } { \partial \phi _ { i k } } = \frac { 1 } { \Delta } \frac { \partial \Delta } { \partial \phi _ { i k } }
$$

by formal logarithmic differentiation of $E = \Delta / \sqrt { \Psi _ { 1 } \Psi _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \Psi _ { n } }$ However,

$$
\frac { 1 } { E } \frac { \partial E } { \partial F _ { i k } } = \sqrt { \Psi _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) } \frac { 1 } { \Delta } \frac { \partial \Delta } { \partial \phi _ { i k } }
$$

from the relation

$$
\frac { \partial E } { \partial \phi _ { i k } } = \frac { \partial E } { \partial F _ { i k } } \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { \Psi _ { i } ( q _ { i } ) } } .
$$

Since the expressions $A _ { i } ( q _ { i } )$ of (60)and (61) of Section 1.16 cannot all be zero, at least one $\partial \Delta / \partial \phi _ { i k }$ is not zero. Hence,at least one $\partial E / \partial F _ { i k }$ $( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ is not zero for any value of $k$ As, $\phi _ { i k }$ in the interval $( a _ { i } , b _ { i } )$ becomes neither zero nor infinity, the determinant $\Delta$ —and, accordingly, $E$ -becomes neither zero nor infinity.Hence, solving (12) gives

$$
\frac { d t } { E } = \frac { \epsilon _ { 1 } d w _ { 1 } } { E _ { 1 } } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = \frac { \epsilon _ { n } d w _ { n } } { E _ { n } } ,
$$

where $E _ { j }$ is the determinant in which all elements of the jth column of the determinant $E \equiv \| F _ { i k } ( q _ { i } ) \|$ are replaced by 1. If we choose suitably the signs of $\epsilon _ { 1 } , \epsilon _ { 2 } , \ldots , \epsilon _ { n } $ ，then we can make $w _ { 1 } , w _ { 2 } , \ldots , w _ { n }$ increase monotonically while $t$ increases from $- \infty$ to $+ \infty$ ,because none of $E _ { i } / E$ becomes zero or infinity in this interval. Thus $w _ { i }$ is the mechanical variable and there will be an asymptotic motion or a libration according to the discussion in Section 2.4.

Assume that $m _ { i } = n _ { i } = 1 \ ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ ; then

$$
q _ { i } = a _ { i } \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { \epsilon _ { i } w _ { i } } { 2 } + b _ { i } \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { \epsilon _ { i } w _ { i } } { 2 } .
$$

Integrate (12) by putting $G _ { i k } ( q _ { i } ) = \int F _ { i k } ( q _ { i } ) \epsilon _ { i } d w _ { i }$ ，and denote the integration constants by $\beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } , . . . , \beta _ { n }$ .Then

$$
t + \beta _ { k } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } G _ { i k } ( w _ { i } ) \qquad ( k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Take $\epsilon _ { i } = \pm 1$ and let ${ \boldsymbol { \ w } } _ { i }$ increase by $2 \pi$ Then

$$
+ \beta _ { k } + 2 \omega _ { j k } = G _ { j k } ( w _ { j } + 2 \pi ) + et { } { ' } \sum _ { \stackrel { i = 1 } { i \neq j } } ^ { n } G _ { i k } ( w _ { i } ) ( j , k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

where we exclude $i = j$ in the summation $\Sigma ^ { \prime }$ . Thus

$$
2 \omega _ { j k } = G _ { j k } ( w _ { j } + 2 \pi ) - G _ { j k } ( w _ { j } ) = \int _ { w _ { j } } ^ { w _ { j } + 2 \pi } F _ { j k } ( q _ { j } ) \cdot \epsilon _ { j } d w _ { j } ,
$$

where $q _ { j }$ is a periodic function of $w _ { j }$ Hence,

$$
\omega _ { j k } = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } F _ { j k } ( q _ { j } ) ~ d w _ { j } ~ ( j , k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

or

$$
\omega _ { j k } = \int _ { a _ { j } } ^ { b _ { j } } \frac { \phi _ { j k } ( q _ { j } ) ~ d q _ { j } } { \sqrt { ( q _ { j } - a _ { j } ) ( b _ { j } - q _ { j } ) \Psi _ { j } ( q _ { j } ) } } \cdot
$$

Thus $t + \beta _ { \kappa }$ increases by $\omega _ { j k }$ as $w _ { j }$ increases by $2 \pi$ . Conversely， by considering $w _ { 1 } , w _ { 2 } , \ldots , w _ { n }$ as functions of $t + \beta _ { 1 } , t + \beta _ { 2 } , . . . , t + \beta _ { n } ,$ we see that $\boldsymbol { w } _ { j }$ increases by $2 \pi$ while $w _ { 1 } , w _ { 2 } , \ldots , w _ { j - 1 } , w _ { j + 1 } , \ldots , w _ { n }$ remain unaltered, as $t + \beta _ { 1 } , t + \beta _ { 2 } , . . . , t + \beta _ { n }$ increase, respectively, by

$$
2 \omega _ { j 1 } , 2 \omega _ { j 2 } , . . . , 2 \omega _ { j n } .
$$

From (13) and from reference to (12),

$$
d \beta _ { k } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } F _ { i k } ( q _ { i } ) \epsilon _ { i } d w _ { i } \qquad ( k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

By solving these equations we obtain

$$
d w _ { j } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } E _ { j i } d \beta _ { i } \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

as $E \neq 0$ ，where $E _ { j i }$ is the minor of the determinant $E .$ Thus

$$
\beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } , . . . , \beta _ { n }
$$

vary continuously as $w _ { 1 } , w _ { 2 } , \ldots , w _ { n }$ vary continuously. Hence

$$
w _ { 1 } , w _ { 2 } , \ldots , w _ { n }
$$

are single-valued functions of $t + \beta _ { 1 } , t + \beta _ { 2 } , . . . , t + \beta _ { n } .$ If we set

$q _ { k } = f _ { k } ( t + \beta _ { 1 } , t + \beta _ { 2 } , . . . , t + \beta _ { n } ) \qquad ( k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,$

then

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { f _ { k } ( t + \beta _ { 1 } + 2 \omega _ { j 1 } , t + \beta _ { 2 } + 2 \omega _ { j 2 } , . . . , t + \beta _ { n } + 2 \omega _ { j n } ) } \\ & { \qquad = f _ { k } ( t + \beta _ { 1 } , t + \beta _ { 2 } , . . . , t + \beta _ { n } ) ( j , k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) . } \end{array}
$$

Such functions are multiply periodic functions (Section 2.5); the $\omega _ { j k }$ are given by (l4).By a transformation

$$
\pi ( t + \beta _ { j } ) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \omega _ { i j } u _ { i } \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

we change to the variables $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { n } .$ ，by assuming $\Omega \equiv \left\| \omega _ { j k } \right\| \ne 0 _ { \mathrm { { i } } }$ and suppose that the functions $f _ { k } ( t + \beta _ { 1 } , t + \beta _ { 2 } , . . . , t + \beta _ { n } )$ are changed to $g _ { k } ( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , . . . , u _ { n } )$ . Then the above property is expressed by

$$
\begin{array} { l } { g _ { k } ( u _ { 1 } + 2 \pi , u _ { 2 } , . . . , u _ { n } ) = g _ { k } ( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } + 2 \pi , . . . , u _ { n } ) = \cdot \cdot \cdot } \\ { \qquad = g _ { k } ( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , . . . , u _ { n } + 2 \pi ) \qquad } \\ { \qquad = g _ { k } ( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , . . . , u _ { n } ) \qquad ( k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) . } \end{array}
$$

Generally, we have the relations with positive or negative integers $m _ { 1 } , m _ { 2 } , \ldots , m _ { n }$ ：

$$
g _ { k } ( u _ { 1 } + 2 m _ { 1 } \pi , u _ { 2 } + 2 m _ { 2 } \pi , . . . , u _ { n } + 2 m _ { n } \pi ) = g _ { k } ( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , . . . , u _ { n } ) .
$$

Such functions satisfy the condition (8) of Weierstrass and hence can be expanded in form (9) by Weierstrass's auxiliary theorem. We have

$$
g _ { k } = \sum _ { \nu _ { 1 } , . . . , \nu _ { n } } C _ { \nu _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot \nu _ { n } } ^ { ( k ) } \exp \left[ \sqrt { - 1 } \left( \nu _ { 1 } u _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \nu _ { n } u _ { n } \right) \right]
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \pi ^ { 2 } C _ { \nu _ { 1 } \cdots \nu _ { n } } ^ { ( k ) } = \displaystyle \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \cdot \cdot \cdot \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } g _ { k } ( u _ { 1 } , . . . , u _ { n } ) } \ ~ } \\ { { \times \ \exp \left[ - \sqrt { - 1 } \ ( \nu _ { 1 } u _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \nu _ { n } u _ { n } ) \right] d u _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d u _ { n } . } } \end{array}
$$

From (15)

$$
\Omega u _ { j } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial \omega _ { j i } } \pi ( t + \beta _ { i } ) \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Thus $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ can be expressed by linear functions of $t$ ; actually, $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ increase by the amounts

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { 1 } { \Omega } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial \omega _ { j i } } 2 \pi T ,
$$

respectively, as $t$ increases by $2 T .$ Hence,in order that the motion be periodic with period $2 T$ with respect to $t _ { : }$ ，these increments to $u _ { i }$ should be integral multiples of $2 \pi$ ,that is, either $\pmb { n }$ equations

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { \frac { 1 } { \Omega } } { \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial \omega _ { j i } } } = { \frac { m _ { j } } { T } } \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

or $\pmb { n }$ equations

$$
T = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } \omega _ { i j } \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

should hold simultaneously. Staude (1887, 1889), Stäckel (1912),and Ahrens (1895) called such motions conditionally periodic motions. Our mode of presentation is different from Charlier (19o6).It is a modified form of Weierstrass (1895).

The problem of solving the set of differential equations (l1） and expressing $q _ { i }$ as functions of $t + \beta _ { j }$ is called the problem of inversion.It has been discussed since the end of the last century in the inversion problem of elliptic integrals and general algebraic integrals; the addition theorem of these integrals has been developed by Abel, not only in the theory of multiply periodic functions but also in its application to algebraic geometry. The formula (69)of Section 1.16 resembles the expression in the addition theorem of algebraic functions (Section 6.25), but there are important differences: the functions $\Phi _ { i }$ in the same expres-sion are all different, and the periods in our case are not complex, as in the theory of algebraic functions,but real. Moreover, the expressions $\Phi _ { i } ( q _ { i } )$ and $\phi _ { i k } ( q _ { i } )$ in our case are not polynomials. We can simplify polynomials by a projective transformation or a Cremona transformation. In the one-body problem of relativity theory,as willbe shown in Section 2.17, the functions $\Phi _ { i } ( q _ { i } )$ are cubic equations and the theory of elliptic functions can be applied directly.In the three-body problem of Newtonian mechanics a similar situation appears in the theory of libration.

Suppose that $\pmb { n }$ uniform (single-valued and continuous) integrals

$$
C _ { i } ( q _ { i } , . . . , q _ { n } , \phi _ { 1 } , . . . , \phi _ { n } ) = C _ { i } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

are mutually in involution (as will be defined in Chapter 4) and let $\Phi _ { 2 n }$ be a phase space of $2 n$ dimensions of $q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { n } , p _ { 1 } , . . . , p _ { n }$ .Suppose that every point $P$ of $\Phi _ { 2 n }$ is at a finite distance from any other, and that two arbitrary points can be connected by a path of finite length described on $\Phi _ { 2 n }$ ， and,moreover, that at least one of the determinants of order $\pmb { n }$ formed from the matrix

$$
\left( { \frac { \partial C _ { i } } { \partial q _ { j } } } , { \frac { \partial C _ { i } } { \partial p _ { k } } } \right) \qquad ( i , j , k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

is not zero. In such a case, write $\begin{array} { r } { V ( P ) = \int _ { P _ { 0 } } ^ { P _ { 1 } } \sum _ { i } \phi _ { i } \ d q _ { i } } \end{array}$ ，where the path of integration is chosen so as to be on $\Phi _ { 2 n }$ ,and replace the assumption of Stackel. $\int { \phi _ { i } d q _ { i } }$ has the dimensionality of action. In classical quantum theory this action is put equal to an integral multiple of Planck's constant in the case when the variables can be separated in Stäckel's manner (Born, 1925).

Put

$$
\frac { \partial V } { \partial C _ { i } } = w _ { i } , \quad \quad \frac { \partial V } { \partial q _ { i } } = p _ { i } ,
$$

and transform from the canonical variables $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { i } , \ q _ { i }$ to $C _ { i } , \ w _ { i }$ Replace $C _ { 1 } , C _ { 2 } , . . . , C _ { n }$ by $\pmb { n }$ integrals in involution (Section 4.5) and suppose that the variables $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ determined by

$$
\frac { \partial V } { \partial v _ { i } } = u _ { i } , \qquad \frac { \partial V } { \partial q _ { i } } = \dot { p } _ { i }
$$

have periods

Then $( q _ { i } , p _ { i } )$ can be expanded in trigonometric series with the sum of integral multiples of $2 \pi u _ { i }$ as arguments, as in (16). Then $v _ { i } = \gamma _ { i } ,$ $u _ { i } = \nu _ { i } t + \epsilon _ { i } ,$ , and we obtain a result similar to Stackel's (Mineur, 1936). The assumption as to the form of $V ( P )$ is fairly strong, like Stackel’s.

We have transformed the canonically conjugate variables from $\not p _ { i } , q _ { i }$ to $\alpha _ { i } , \beta _ { i }$ . The variables $\beta _ { i }$ are contained only in $\pmb { u }$ ,as can be seen from (18); hence, only in ${ \pmb w }$ ，Their conjugate variables $\alpha _ { i }$ appear in $F _ { j k }$ in the expressions for $\omega _ { j k }$ ,and in the coefficients of the transformations from $\beta$ to $\pmb { u }$ and from $\boldsymbol { u }$ to w.We write the canonically conjugate variable of $\boldsymbol { u }$ as $v .$ The variable $q$ is periodic with respect to $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { u } }$ ，as may be seen from (16); and $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { u } }$ is contained only in the arguments of exponentials. Hence, the coefficients $C _ { \nu _ { 1 } \dots \nu _ { n } }$ should be functions of $v$ ; actually, $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { u } }$ is linear in $t$ and periodic with period $2 \pi$ .Such a variable is called an angular variable. On the other hand, $v$ does not have this property and is called a linear variable.Thus the coordinates and momenta are expressed in the form

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { q _ { i } = q _ { i } ( v _ { 1 } , \dotsc , v _ { n } ; u _ { 1 } , \dotsc , u _ { n } ) , \qquad u _ { j } = \nu _ { j } t + \epsilon _ { j } , } \\ & { } \\ & { \dot { p } _ { i } = \dot { p } _ { i } ( v _ { 1 } , \dotsc , v _ { n } ; u _ { 1 } , \dotsc , u _ { n } ) , } \end{array}
$$

where $\epsilon _ { j }$ are the integration constants and $\nu _ { j }$ are functions of $v$ . These equations, $2 n$ in number, give the relations between $\phi , q , u , v .$ Eliminating $\boldsymbol { u }$ gives $_ n$ relations among $\displaystyle { \phi , q } _ { \mathrm { : } }$ and $v$ .These severe conditions are necessary for a conditionally periodic motion. This has been proved by Levi-Civita (1933) by the use of Weierstrass's auxiliary theorem (Section 2.6).Fues (1925） proved that the necessary and sufficient condition for a conditionally periodic motion is that the surface of action $V =$ constant can be divided into a finite number of distinct sheets of surfaces.This circumstance has been important in Bohr's quantum theory based on his correspondence principle (see Born, 1925).

# 2.8KRONECKER's THEOREM

Suppose that we are given real numbers $\mu _ { 1 } , \ldots , \mu _ { m } , \tau$ and a positive number $\epsilon$ . Then there exists a constant $C = C ( \epsilon , m )$ , independent of $\tau _ { : }$ and $m$ integers $g _ { 1 } , \ldots , g _ { m }$ ，such that $_ m$ inequalities

$$
| t \mu _ { i } - g _ { i } | < \epsilon \mathrm { ~ \qquad ~ } ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m )
$$

hold for all values of $t$ in the interval $\tau < t < \tau + C$

This is known as Kronecker's theorem (1899). It has been proved by Lettenmeyer (1923), Bohr (1923),and Thomas (1924) (see also Hardy and Litlewood, 1914; Weyl, 19i6). The most elegant proof is by Bohr, later extended by Bacon (l934)； this proceeds as follows:

Consider m complex numbers exp $( 2 \pi { \sqrt { - 1 } } \ t \mu _ { j } )$ . It is sufficient to prove that the differences of these numbers each from exp $( 0 ) = 1$ are at most $\epsilon$ ,that is, the upper bound $L _ { F }$ of $| F ( t ) |$ , where

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { F ( t ) = 1 + \exp \left( 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \mathrm { \Delta } t \mu _ { 1 } \right) + \exp \left( 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \mathrm { \Delta } t \mu _ { 2 } \right) + \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \\ { { \mathrm { \Omega } + \exp \left( 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \mathrm { \Delta } t \mu _ { m } \right) } } \end{array} ,
$$

is,for all real values of $t$ ， equal to the upper bound $L _ { G } = m + 1$ of$G ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { m } )$ ， where

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \widetilde { \mathfrak { z } } ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m } ) = 1 + \exp { ( 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \ x _ { 1 } ) } + \exp { ( 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \ x _ { 2 } ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot } \\ { + \exp { ( 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \ x _ { m } ) } . \qquad } \end{array}
$$

We consider

$$
[ F ( t ) ] ^ { p } = [ 1 + \exp { ( 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \ t \mu _ { 1 } ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \exp { ( 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \ t \mu _ { m } ) } ] ^ { p }
$$

with a positive integer $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { p } }$ . Any two terms of the expansion of this expression do not coincide, because $\mu _ { i }$ are mutually linearly independent, that is, the number of terms in the expansion equals the number of terms in the expansion of $[ G ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m } ) ] ^ { p }$ ; indeed, corresponding terms have the same coefficients.Hence,

$$
F _ { p } = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { t \to \infty } { \frac { 1 } { 2 t } } \int _ { - t } ^ { t } | [ F ( t ) ] ^ { p } | ^ { 2 } d t
$$

is equal to

$$
G _ { p } = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } | \{ G ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { m } ) \} ^ { p } | ^ { 2 } d x _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { m } ,
$$

and both are equal to the sum of squares of the coefficients in these two expansions. It is easy to see that

$$
( G _ { p } ) ^ { 1 / 2 p }  L _ { G } = m + 1
$$

for $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }  \infty$ .Hence, $\vert G ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { m } ) \vert$ attains its upper bound $L _ { G }$ at the point $( 0 , 0 , \ldots , 0 )$ of the $_ m$ -dimensional parallelopiped or the $_ m$ -dimensional torus $0 \leq x _ { 1 } \leq 1 , . . . , 0 \leq x _ { m } \leq 1$ . Because $F _ { p } = G _ { p }$ ，therefore $F _ { p } ^ { 1 / 2 p } \to L _ { G }$ But，because $F _ { p } ^ { 1 / 2 p } \lessapproxeq L _ { F }$ for all values of $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ ，we obtain $L _ { F } \gtrapprox L _ { G }$ .Thus the theorem is proved.

We can prove the following theorem as a corollary to Kronecker's theorem:Suppose that there is no linear homogeneous relation with integer coefficients among $\mu _ { 1 } , \mu _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mu _ { m }$ . Given arbitrary numbers $k _ { 1 } , . . . , k _ { m }$ and $\epsilon > 0$ , we can obtain $t$ and the integers $g _ { 1 } , \ldots , g _ { m }$ ，such that

$$
| \mu _ { i } t - k _ { i } - g _ { i } | < \epsilon \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

In order to prove this theorem we take， following Bohl (1893), $\delta < | \mu _ { i } | ~ ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m )$ . According to Kronecker's Theorem we can choose $m ^ { 2 }$ expressions

$$
s _ { i } ^ { ( j ) } = \mu _ { i } t ^ { ( j ) } - g _ { i } ^ { ( j ) } \qquad ( i , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

such that their absolute values are smaller than $\epsilon > 0$ .If the determinant $\left\| s _ { i } ^ { ( j ) } \right\| \neq 0$ , then we take $\epsilon < 4 \delta / m$ and determine $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { j }$ by

$$
\hbar _ { 1 } s _ { i } ^ { ( 1 ) } + \hbar _ { 2 } s _ { i } ^ { ( 2 ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \hbar _ { m } s _ { i } ^ { ( m ) } = k _ { i } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

If we replace each $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { j }$ by an integer that differs from $\phi _ { j }$ at most by $\textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } }$ ， then the difference between the left-hand side of this equation and $k _ { i }$ becomes smaller than $\delta / 2$ and we can take $s _ { i } ^ { ( j ) }$ smaller than $\scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } }$ .Hence the problem is solved.

But in order to see that $\left\| s _ { i } ^ { ( j ) } \right\| \neq 0$ ,we tentatively assume that $\left\| s _ { i } ^ { ( j ) } \right\| = 0$ This should give

$$
{ \left| \begin{array} { l l l l l l l l } { 1 } & { 0 } & { } & { 0 } \\ { t ^ { ( 1 ) } } & { s _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) } } & { } & { s _ { m } ^ { ( 1 ) } } \\ { t ^ { ( 2 ) } } & { s _ { 1 } ^ { ( 2 ) } } & { } & { s _ { m } ^ { ( 2 ) } } \\ { \vdots } & { } & { } & { } & { } \\ { t ^ { ( m ) } } & { s _ { 1 } ^ { ( m ) } } & { } & { } & { s _ { m } ^ { ( m ) } } \end{array} \right| } = { \left| \begin{array} { l l l l l } { 1 } & { - \mu _ { 1 } } & { } & { - \mu _ { m } } \\ { t ^ { ( 1 ) } } & { - g _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) } } & { } & { - g _ { m } ^ { ( 1 ) } } \\ { t ^ { ( 2 ) } } & { - g _ { 1 } ^ { ( 2 ) } } & { } & { - g _ { m } ^ { ( 2 ) } } \\ { \vdots } & { } & { } & { } & { } \\ { t ^ { ( m ) } } & { - g _ { 1 } ^ { ( m ) } } & { } & { - g _ { m } ^ { ( m ) } } \end{array} \right| } = 0 .
$$

The second determinant is obtained by multiplying each element in the first column of the first determinant by the appropriate $\mu _ { i }$ and subtracting it from each column. $g _ { i } ^ { ( j ) }$ are integers,and there is no linear homogeneous relation with integer coefficients among $\mu _ { 1 } , \mu _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mu _ { m } .$ Hence, the coefficients of $t ^ { ( j ) }$ in the expansion of the determinant with respect to the first column are all different from zero.As the coefficient of l in this expansion is an integer, such a relation cannot exist, with $t ^ { ( j ) }$ either rational or irrational.Hence, the corollary is proved.

The estimate of $\epsilon$ and $C$ in (21） has been recently made by Siegel (Landau 1927,1929,1935),van der Corput (1921,1931,1932） and Schonberg (1928).

We have obtained in Section 2.7

$$
\begin{array} { r } { f \Big ( t + \beta _ { 1 } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } 2 m _ { i } \omega _ { i 1 } , t + \beta _ { 2 } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } 2 m _ { i } \omega _ { i 2 } , . . . , t + \beta _ { n } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } 2 m _ { i } \omega } \\ { = f ( t + \beta _ { 1 } , t + \beta _ { 2 } , . . . , t + \beta _ { n } ) . } \end{array}
$$

However, we can make

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } 2 m _ { i } \omega _ { i j } - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } 2 m _ { i } \omega _ { i 1 } ~ ( j = 2 , 3 , . . . , n )
$$

smaller than any arbitrarily given number $\eta$

In fact, it is sufficient to prove

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } 2 m _ { i } \omega _ { i j } - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } 2 m _ { i } \omega _ { i 1 } = \eta _ { j } , \qquad \left. \eta _ { j } \right. < \eta \qquad ( j = 2 , 3 , . . . , n ) .
$$

By solving these equations we see that at least one of the determinants of order $n - 1$ formed from the matrix

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \left( \begin{array} { l l l l l l } { \omega _ { 1 2 } - \omega _ { 1 1 } } & { \omega _ { 2 2 } - \omega _ { 2 1 } } & { } & { } & { \omega _ { n 2 } - \omega _ { n 1 } } \\ { \omega _ { 1 3 } - \omega _ { 1 1 } } & { \omega _ { 2 3 } - \omega _ { 2 1 } } & { } & { } & { \omega _ { n 3 } - \omega _ { n 1 } } \\ { \vdots } & { } & { } & { } & { } & { } \\ { \omega _ { 1 n } - \omega _ { 1 1 } } & { \omega _ { 2 n } - \omega _ { 2 1 } } & { } & { } & { \omega _ { n n } - \omega _ { n 1 } } \end{array} \right) } \end{array}
$$

is not zero.Because,if all of these determinants were zero, then the determinant of order $\pmb { n }$ formed from this matrix by writing

$$
\omega _ { 1 1 } , \omega _ { 2 1 } , . . . , \omega _ { n 1 }
$$

in the first row would be $\Omega$ .We have assumed that $\Omega \equiv \left. \omega _ { i j } \right. \ne 0$ Hence,(23) is proved.

We now solve the equations (23) by writing

$$
\Omega _ { 1 } = \left. \omega _ { i j } - \omega _ { i 1 } \right. \qquad ( i , j = 2 , 3 , . . . , n ) \colon
$$

$$
2 m _ { i } + 2 m _ { n } \Big [ \frac { 1 } { \Omega _ { 1 } } \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial \Omega _ { 1 } } { \partial \omega _ { i j } } \left( \omega _ { n j } - \omega _ { n 1 } \right) \Big ] = \frac { 1 } { \Omega _ { 1 } } \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial \Omega _ { 1 } } { \partial \omega _ { i j } } \eta _ { j }
$$

$$
( j = 2 , 3 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Hence, if we take

$$
\mu _ { i } = \frac { 1 } { \Omega _ { 1 } } \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial \Omega _ { 1 } } { \partial \omega _ { i j } } \left( \omega _ { n j } - \omega _ { n 1 } \right) , \qquad t = - m _ { n } , \quad g _ { i } = m _ { i } , \quad m = n - 1
$$

then we can solve $\epsilon _ { j }$ from the given constants $\eta _ { j }$ by

$$
\frac { 1 } { \Omega _ { 1 } } \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial \Omega _ { 1 } } { \partial \omega _ { i j } } \eta _ { j } = \epsilon _ { i } ~ ( i = 2 , 3 , . . . , m ) .
$$

If we take $\epsilon$ such that $\epsilon _ { i } < \epsilon$ ，then a direct application of Kronecker's theorem (21) shows that we can find $\prime , g _ { 1 } , \ldots , g _ { m }$ such that

$$
| \mu _ { i } t - g _ { i } | < \epsilon _ { i } \qquad ( i = 2 , 3 , . . . , m ) ;
$$

that is, we can find $m _ { 1 } , m _ { 2 } , . . . , m _ { n - 1 }$ Hence, there exists a set of integers $m _ { 1 } , m _ { 2 } , \ldots , m _ { n }$ satisfying (23), however small we may take $\eta$ ·

With such a set of $m _ { 1 } , m _ { 2 } , . . . , m _ { n - 1 } , m _ { n }$ we form $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } 2 m _ { j } \omega _ { j 1 } = T } \end{array}$ ： then we can write (22) in the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { f ( t + T + \beta _ { 1 } , t + T + \beta _ { 2 } + \eta _ { 2 } , . . . , t + T + \beta _ { n } + \eta _ { n } ) \qquad } \\ { = f ( t + \beta _ { 1 } , t + \beta _ { 2 } , . . . , t + } \end{array}
$$

But, as we have proved that the function $f$ is a continuous function of its variables,we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \vert f ( t + T + \beta _ { 1 } , t + T + \beta _ { 2 } + \eta _ { 2 } , . . . , t + T + \beta _ { n } + \eta _ { n } ) } } \\ & { } & { \quad - f ( t + T + \beta _ { 1 } , t + T + \beta _ { 2 } , . . . , t + T + \beta _ { n } ) \vert } \\ & { } & { \quad < M \sum _ { i = 2 } ^ { n } \eta _ { i } < M ( n - } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
M = \mathrm { m a x } \left| \frac { \partial f } { \partial \beta _ { i } } \right| \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

Hence,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \begin{array} { r } { \dot { \left( t + T + \beta _ { 1 } , t + T + \beta _ { 2 } , . . . , t + T + \beta _ { n } \right) } } \\ { - f ( t + \beta _ { 1 } , t + \beta _ { 2 } , . . . , t + \beta _ { n } ) | < M ( n - 1 } \end{array} } \end{array}
$$

Thus, by taking $_ { T }$ large enough, we can make the difference of the values of $f$ for $t$ and $t + T$ smaller than the given number $\eta _ { ; }$ ，however small $\eta$ may be.In other words, there exists a constant $_ { T }$ such that the differences at $t$ and $t + T$ of the coordinates $q _ { i }$ and the momenta $\mathbf { \nabla } \mathbf { \mathcal { P } } _ { i }$ $( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ of the dynamical system in question can be made as small as we please.In other words, the dynamical system in question can return to a state of motion as near as we please to the initial state of motion, as $t$ increases indefinitely； that is,if any trajectory in the $2 n$ dimensional phase space of Gibbs for $\pmb { \mathscr { p } _ { i } }$ and $q _ { i }$ once recedes from its initial position, it returns again indefinitely close to this initial position.

By using the terminology of point-set theory we see that this phase space of Gibbs is filled up euerywhere densely by the trajectory of motion (Section 2.l).This result is only for the case of simple motions, such as those discussed here (the general case will be discussed in a later chapter). One particular example is that of a two-dimensional simple pendulum describing a Lissajous figure when the ratio of the periods of the motion in the two perpendicular directions is irrational. In the next section, we will discuss the motion of molecules in a cube as the simplest example.

# 2.9MOTION OF MOLECULES IN A CUBE

Take a square with its sides $x = 0 , { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ; y = 0 , { \frac { 1 } { 2 } }$ Suppose that any molecule moving in a straight line inside this square makes a perfect reflection when it strikes any of the sides of the square. The mirror image of the trajectory with respect to the reflecting side is also a straight line. Let $P ( a , b )$ bea point inside the square.The point,of which the images are $( a + m , b + n )$ ， $+ \ { m , - b + n } ) , ( - a + m , b + n ) , ( - a$ $( - a + m , - b + n )$ on the xy plane,where $m$ and $\pmb { n }$ are integers,moves in a straight line $x = a + \alpha t$ $y = b + \beta t$ on that plane.In order that the point shall return to its initial position we should have $x = a + m$ ， $y = b + n$ Hence, $m = \alpha t$ ， $n = \beta t$ ; that is, the ratio $\alpha \colon \beta$ should be rational. If it is irrational, the moving point comes indefinitely near, but not exactly back to, its initial position. Denoting the point by $\xi , \eta$ we obtain

$$
| a + \alpha t - ( \xi + m ) | < \epsilon , \qquad | b + \beta t - ( \eta + n ) | < \epsilon ,
$$

and we can determine two integers $_ m$ and $\boldsymbol { n }$ such that these inequalities are satisfied with an arbitrarily given number $\epsilon$ by the corollary of Kronecker's theorem described in Section 2.8.

Next, consider a three-dimensional cube,with the sides $\textstyle x = 0 , { \frac { 1 } { 2 } }$ ； $y = 0 , \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } }$ ； $z = 0 , { \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } }$ . In order that the trajectory represented by the straight line $L$ ，

$$
x = a + \alpha t , \qquad y = b + \beta t , \qquad z = c + \gamma t ,
$$

return to its initial position, that is, in order that the trajectory be periodic, it is necessary and sufficient that the double ratio $\alpha { : } \beta { : } \gamma$ should be rational.That is, there should exist the relations

$$
A \alpha + B \beta + C \gamma = 0 , A ^ { \prime } \alpha + B ^ { \prime } \beta + C ^ { \prime } \gamma = 0 ,
$$

with integers $A , B , C , A ^ { \prime } , B ^ { \prime } , C ^ { \prime }$ .It is supposed here that the coefficients are such that $A { : } B { : } C$ and $A ^ { \prime } { : } B ^ { \prime } { : } C ^ { \prime }$ are both irreducible ratios. We specify two classes of nonperiodic trajectory: the first,in which there is only one such linear relation among $\alpha , \beta , \gamma _ { ; }$ ,and the second,in which there is no such relation.

First suppose that there is only one relation, $A \alpha + B \beta + C \gamma = 0$ with integer coefficients. The path of a moving point proceeding in the direction with direction cosines $A , B _ { \mathrm { { i } } }$ $C$ is perpendicular to the straight line $L$ . The plane $S$ ， given by

$$
A ( x - a ) + B ( y - b ) + C ( z - c ) = 0 ,
$$

contains the straight line $L$ and is perpendicular to the straight line with direction cosines $A , B , C .$ .We carry out a symmetric transformation on $S$ with respect to the cube surface that is cut by $\mathbb { S } .$ This gives a plane $S ^ { \prime }$ . Carry out again a symmetric transformation on $S ^ { \prime }$ with respect to the cube surface that is cut by the plane $S ^ { \prime }$ . Repeat these operations. The parts of the surfaces $S , S ^ { \prime } , \ldots$ inside the given cube form a closed polyhedron $\Pi$ of a finite number of faces. The edges are all on the surface of the cube. Let $A a + B b + C c = D$ .Then the equations for the planes containing the faces of $\Pi$ are

$$
\Sigma \colon \pm \ A x \ \pm \ B y \ \pm \ C z - \ ( D \ \pm \ A m \ \pm \ B n \ \pm \ C p ) \ = 0 ,
$$

with integers $m , n , \hbar$ . If a point on some plane $\Sigma$ is inside the cube, then its distance from the origin (0,0,O) is smaller than the length of the diagonal ${ \sqrt { 3 } } / 2$ of the cube; this gives

$$
\left| \frac { D \pm A m \pm B n \pm C p } { \sqrt { A ^ { 2 } + B ^ { 2 } + C ^ { 2 } } } \right| < \sqrt { 3 } / 2 .
$$

This moving point, the molecule, takes no value other than

$$
D , D \pm 1 , D \pm 2 , \ldots
$$

There are only a finite number of absolute values less than

$$
\sqrt { 3 } \sqrt { A ^ { 2 } + B ^ { 2 } + C ^ { 2 } } / 2 .
$$

Hence, there are only a finite number of planes $\Sigma$ satisfying this inequality. Moreover, the parts of planes inside the cube are polygons whose sides always also belong to contiguous polygons. Thus, the polyhedron is closed.

Since the image of our trajectory $\boldsymbol { L }$ is on the plane $S _ { : }$ ，the part of $L$ inside the cube is a straight-line segment on the polyhedron $\Pi$ ; hence, the trajectory is always on the surface of the polyhedron $\Pi$ .Thus,it is not everywhere dense inside the cube. It can be shown that the trajectory approaches indefinitely any point on the surface of $\Pi$ if it is not periodic.Take a point $( \xi , \eta , \zeta )$ on $s$ and consider

$$
A ( \xi - a ) + B ( \eta - b ) + C ( \zeta - c ) = 0 .
$$

We can determine $t$ and integers $m , n , \beta$ such that

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { | a + \alpha t - ( \xi + m ) | < \epsilon , \qquad | b + \beta t - ( \eta + n ) | < \epsilon , } } \\ { { | c + \gamma t - ( \zeta + \rlap / p ) | < \epsilon , } } \end{array}
$$

by the corollary of Kronecker's theorem. Hence, one of the ratios $\alpha / \beta$ ， $\beta / \gamma , \gamma / \alpha$ is irrational.

Next, suppose that there is no linear relation of the form

$$
A \alpha + B \beta + C \gamma = 0 .
$$

In this case the trajectory returns infinitely near any given position. We can choose $t$ and integers $m , n , \beta$ ，such that

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { | a + \alpha t - ( \xi + m ) | < \epsilon , \qquad | b + \beta t - ( \eta + n ) | < \epsilon , } } \\ { { | c + \gamma t - ( \zeta + \rlap / p ) | < \epsilon } } \end{array}
$$

by the corollary of Kronecker's theorem (Konig and Szücs, 1913).

In this example the trajectory is everywhere dense in a space or in a subspace of lower dimensionality contained in that space. But the case of rational ratios $\alpha { : } \beta { : } \gamma$ is very rare; that is, the probability of occurrence of rational ratios is infinitely small compared with the probability of occurrence of irrational ratios. The appearance of irrational ratios defines the regional recurrence.It will be discussed later, together with its Lebesgue measure.In the terminology of point-set theory the measure of the point set of rational numbers is zero,but the case in which the trajectory is everywhere dense except a point set of measure zero is called quasi-ergodic and will be discussed in a later chapter.

# 2.I0QUASI-PERIODIC FUNCTION OF BOHL

Let $\mu _ { 1 } , \mu _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mu _ { m }$ be $_ m$ nonzero and linearly independent numbers; that is, there is no linear homogeneous relation with integer coeffcients among these numbers.Let $t$ be a variable,and $U _ { j }$ a rational function of sin $2 \pi  { p _ { j } t }$ and cos $2 \pi \phi _ { j } t$ $( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , m )$ . Assume that a trigonometric series $U _ { 1 } + U _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + U _ { j } + \cdot \cdot \cdot$ is uniformly convergent in the whole real domain $_ { T }$ of $t$ ; assume, that is, that with a positive integer $_ { n }$ for each $\epsilon > 0$ ， the expression $| U _ { l + 1 } + U _ { l + 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + U _ { l + s - 1 } |$ can be made smaller than $\epsilon$ for an integer $l > n$ and an integer $s > 0$ for all values of $t$ belonging to $T .$ ，In other words,assume that this series represents a function $\psi ( t )$ in $T .$ ，Assume, furthermore, that we can choose an $\eta > 0$ so that $| \psi ( t + \tau ) - \psi ( t ) | < \epsilon$ for all numbers $\epsilon > 0$ ，with $t$ and $\pmb { \tau }$ both belonging to $T _ { : }$ ,and that $\mu _ { i } \tau$ $( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m )$ differ at most by $\eta$ from the corresponding integers. Then there exists a uniformly continuous function $\Psi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { m } )$ of period 1 for each variable $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m } ;$ such that

$$
\Psi ( \mu _ { 1 } t , \mu _ { 2 } t , . . . , \mu _ { m } t ) = \psi ( t )
$$

for all values of $t$

We now prove the first theorem of Bohl(1893,1906; see also Esclangon, 1902,1903,1904,1916).

When

$$
a _ { i } \equiv b _ { i } ( \mathrm { m o d } 1 ) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

we say that $( a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots , a _ { m } )$ and $( b _ { 1 } , b _ { 2 } , . . . , b _ { m } )$ belong to the same class. When $( \eta _ { 1 } t , \eta _ { 2 } t , . . . , \eta _ { m } t )$ belongs to a certain class, we say that $t$ belongs to that class.We now present the three following properties.First: there is one and only one class for each value of $t .$ 、Second:we can choose $\mu _ { 1 } , \mu _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mu _ { m }$ so that $\mu _ { 1 } t - k _ { 1 } , \mu _ { 2 } t - k _ { 2 } , . . . , \mu _ { m } t - k _ { m }$ differ from integers $g _ { 1 } , g _ { 2 } , \ldots , g _ { m } .$ ,respectively,at most by $\epsilon$ ,according to Kronecker's theorem. Hence there exists a set $( \mu _ { 1 } t , \mu _ { 2 } t , . . . , \mu _ { m } t )$ such that

$$
\mu _ { 1 } t , \mu _ { 2 } t , . . . , \mu _ { m } t
$$

differ from $k _ { 1 } , k _ { 2 } , \ldots , k _ { m } ;$ ， respectively, at most by $\epsilon$ ,however

$$
k _ { 1 } , k _ { 2 } , \ldots , k _ { m }
$$

may be given. Third: when $( a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , . . . . , a _ { m } )$ and $( b _ { 1 } , b _ { 2 } , . . . . , b _ { m } )$ are both smaller than $\eta > 0$ ,we denote the values of $t$ belonging to these two sets of numbers respectively by $t _ { \alpha }$ and $t _ { b }$ . Then $| \psi ( t _ { a } ) - \psi ( t _ { b } ) | < \epsilon .$ For, since

$$
| \mu _ { i } ( t _ { a } - t _ { b } ) | = | b _ { i } - a _ { i } | < \eta \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

it should follow according to our assumption that

$$
| \psi ( t _ { a } + t _ { b } - t _ { a } ) - \psi ( t _ { a } ) | < \epsilon .
$$

Now construct the function $\Psi$ . If there is a value of $t$ belonging to the class $( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m } )$ ，then we can form $\psi ( t )$ . Otherwise proceed as follows. Let $\epsilon _ { 1 } , \epsilon _ { 2 } , . . .$ be a sequence of positive numbers tending to zero; let $\eta _ { 1 } , \eta _ { 2 } , \ldots$ correspond respectively to $\epsilon _ { 1 } , \epsilon _ { 2 } , . . . .$ Let $( x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , x _ { m } ^ { \prime } )$ be a class of numbers that differ from the corresponding $( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m } )$ at most by $\eta _ { 1 } / 2$ ; let there be a value of $t$ belonging to that class. (This is possible because of the second property.）Denote the corresponding value of $\psi$ by $\psi _ { 1 }$ .Let $( x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } , x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } , \ldots , x _ { m } ^ { \prime \prime } )$ be a class of numbers that differ from the corresponding $( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m } )$ at most by $\eta _ { 2 } / 2$ and let there be a value of $t$ belonging to that class. Denote the corresponding $\psi$ by $\psi _ { 2 }$ The sequence $\psi _ { 1 } , \psi _ { 2 } , \ldots$ obtained by repeating this process indefinitely converges to a definite limit.In fact, each number in

$$
\left( x _ { 1 } ^ { ( \mu ) } , x _ { 2 } ^ { ( \mu ) } , \dots , x _ { m } ^ { ( \mu ) } \right)
$$

differs from the corresponding number in $\left( x _ { 1 } ^ { ( v ) } , x _ { 2 } ^ { ( v ) } , \ldots , x _ { m } ^ { ( v ) } \right)$ at most by $( \eta _ { \# } + \eta _ { \nu } ) / 2$ ,which cannot be larger than either $\eta _ { \mu }$ or $\eta _ { v }$ . Hence,

$$
\vert \psi _ { \mu } - \psi _ { v } \vert < \epsilon _ { \mu } , \epsilon _ { v } .
$$

As the sequence $\epsilon _ { 1 }$ ， $\epsilon _ { 2 } .$ ... converges to zero, we can choose an integer $N$ such that $\epsilon _ { n } < \epsilon$ for $n > N$ for every $\epsilon$ Hence, $\vert \psi _ { \mu } - \psi _ { \nu } \vert < \epsilon$ for $\mu , \nu > N ,$ and the sequence $\psi _ { 1 } , \psi _ { 2 } , \ldots$ converges.Furthermore, $\psi _ { i }$ converges to the same value, however the sequence $\epsilon _ { 1 } , \epsilon _ { 2 } , . . .$ ，and the corresponding sequence $\eta _ { 1 } , \eta _ { 2 } , \ldots$ may be chosen, because, if we choose another set of values $\bar { \epsilon } _ { 1 } , \bar { \epsilon } _ { 2 } , \dots$ and $\bar { \eta } _ { 1 } , \bar { \eta } _ { 2 } , \dots .$ ，we can choose $\epsilon _ { y }$ and $\bar { \epsilon } _ { v }$ smaller than $\epsilon$ for $\nu > n$ Anumber in $\left( \hat { x } _ { 1 } ^ { ( \nu ) } , \hat { x } _ { 2 } ^ { ( \nu ) } , \ldots , \hat { x } _ { m } ^ { ( \nu ) } \right)$ differs from the corresponding number in $\left( x _ { 1 } ^ { ( \nu ) } , x _ { 2 } ^ { ( \nu ) } , \dots , x _ { m } ^ { ( \nu ) } \right)$ at most by $( \eta _ { v } + \bar { \eta } _ { v } ) / 2$ Hence, $| \bar { \psi } _ { v } - \psi _ { v } | < \epsilon$ for $\nu > n$ ,and the sequence

$$
| \bar { \psi } _ { 1 } - \psi _ { 1 } | , | \bar { \psi } _ { 2 } - \psi _ { 2 } | , . . .
$$

converges to zero.Let the limit to which this sequence converges be the value of $\Psi$ at $( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m } )$

Next we must prove that the function $\Psi$ thus obtained is uniformly continuous.By assigning the corresponding $\eta > 0$ for a given $\epsilon > 0$ we consider $( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m } )$ , respectively, in the open domain

$$
( a _ { 1 } , a _ { 1 } + \eta ) , ( a _ { 2 } , a _ { 2 } + \eta ) , . . . , ( a _ { m } , a _ { m } + \eta ) .
$$

The values of $\Psi$ in this open domain at two points - for which a $t$ exists belonging to the same class - differ from each other at most by $\epsilon$ If the two points in the open domain do not belong to the same class,or if one of the two points does not belong to the same class, then we choose two points of the same class by the second property above stated; we can make the values at these two points differ from each other at most by the given $\epsilon > 0 .$ .Thus we can make the difference of the corresponding values of $\Psi$ arbitrarily close to the difference of the values of $\psi$ at these latter two points.Hence, the two values of $\Psi$ differ at most by $\epsilon$ ,and the function $\Psi$ is uniformly continuous.

Thus the function $\Psi$ has one determinate finite value at each point and has period l for each of the variables $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m }$ ,and,furthermore, is uniformly continuous everywhere.Hence

$$
\Psi ( \mu _ { 1 } t , \mu _ { 2 } t , . . . , \mu _ { m } t ) = \psi ( t ) .
$$

This proves the first theorem of Bohl.

We now come to the second theorem of Bohl.

Let $f ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m } )$ be an everywhere single-valued continuous and periodic function,having period 1 with respect to each variable.Then we can choose a single-valued continuous and periodic function

$$
\phi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { m } )
$$

such that the functions $\phi$ and $f$ differ at most by a given $\epsilon > 0$ everywhere;and such that

$$
\frac { \partial ^ { \nu _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \nu _ { m } } } { \partial x _ { 1 } ^ { \nu } \cdot \cdot \cdot \partial x _ { m } ^ { \nu _ { m } } } \phi
$$

exists, if $\nu _ { 1 } , \nu _ { 2 } , . . . , \nu _ { m } \leq 2$ ， and is everywhere finite, single-valued, and continuous.

In the case of one variable this theorem reduces to Fourier's and is obviously true. Supposing that this theorem is valid for $m - 1$ variables, we propose to prove by mathematical induction that it holds true for $m$ variables.

Let $n _ { 1 } , n _ { 2 } , \ldots , n _ { m }$ be integers. Suppose that we have chosen the domain of $x _ { i }$ to be $n _ { i } \delta _ { i } \equiv x _ { i } \equiv ( n _ { i } + 1 ) \delta _ { i }$ $( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m )$ ， such that the fluctuation (Section 2.1) of the values of $f$ in this domain is smaller than $\epsilon / m$ If $x _ { i } / \delta _ { i }$ $( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m )$ are integers, then by our assumption we can find a function $\phi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { m - 1 } , n \delta _ { m } )$ that satisfies the conditions of the theorem and differs from $f ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m - 1 } , n \delta _ { m } )$ at most by $( m - 1 ) \epsilon / m$ everywhere for all values of the integer $\pmb { n }$ Furthermore

$$
\phi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { m - 1 } , n \delta _ { m } + 1 ) = \phi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { m - 1 } , n \delta _ { m } ) .
$$

If $n \delta _ { m } < x _ { m } < ( n + 1 ) \delta _ { m }$ ,then $\phi$ is determinate for $x _ { m } = n \delta _ { m }$ if we put

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \phi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m } ) } } \\ & { \cdot } & { = \frac { 1 } { 2 } [ \phi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m - 1 } , ( n + 1 ) \delta _ { m } ) + \phi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m - 1 } , n \delta _ { m } , } \\ & { } & { \quad \quad + \ \frac { 1 } { 2 } [ \phi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m - 1 } , ( n + 1 ) \delta _ { m } ) - \phi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m - 1 } , \tau } \\ & { } & { \quad \quad \times \ [ \frac { 9 } { 8 } \sin \frac { \pi } { \delta _ { m } } ( x _ { m } - \frac { 2 n + 1 } { 2 } \delta _ { m } ) + \frac { 1 } { 8 } \sin \frac { 3 \pi } { \delta _ { m } } ( x _ { m } - \frac { 2 n + 1 } { 2 } \delta _ { m } )  } \end{array}
$$

The expression in sines lies between $^ { - 1 }$ and $+ 1$ or equals either of them.
