Hence, $| f ( x _ { 1 } , . ~ . ~ . , x _ { m } ) - \phi ( x _ { 1 } , . ~ . ~ . , x _ { m } ) |$ is less than or equal to either $| f ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { m - 1 } , x _ { m } ) - \phi ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { m - 1 } , n \delta _ { m } ) |$ or

$$
| f ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { m - 1 } , x _ { m } ) - \phi ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { m - 1 } , ( n + 1 ) \delta _ { m } ) | .
$$

Hence, $| f - \phi |$ must be smaller than $\epsilon$ 、We can prove the second property of the theorem in a similar manner.

Finally, we state the third theorem of Bohl.

Let $\phi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { m } )$ be a single-valued continuous and periodic function for all variables $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m }$ ; and let

$$
\frac { \partial ^ { n _ { 1 } + \cdots + n _ { m } } } { \partial x _ { 1 } ^ { n _ { 1 } } \cdots \partial x _ { m } ^ { n _ { m } } } \phi ,
$$

with $n _ { i } \le M _ { i } , M _ { i } > 2 ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m )$ ， be single-valued continuous and finite everywhere. Then

$$
\frac { \partial ^ { \nu _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \nu _ { m } } } { \partial x _ { 1 } ^ { \nu _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot \partial x _ { m } ^ { \nu _ { m } } } \phi , \qquad \nu _ { i } \leq M _ { i } - 2 \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m )
$$

can be developed in a multiple Fourier series of $\pmb { m }$ variables.

This is Weierstrass's auxiliary theorem, proved in Section 2.6. Summarizing these three theorems leads to the following

Theorem : Consider a positive number $\epsilon > 0$ and a function

$$
f ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m } )
$$

that is single-valued and continuous everywhere and periodic with period l for all variables. Then we can find a uniformly and absolutely convergent trigonometric series

M $\begin{array} { r } { \frac { \mathfrak { t } _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot \epsilon _ { m } } { n _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot n _ { m } } \cos { ( 2 \pi n _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } - \epsilon _ { 1 } \pi ) } \cos { ( 2 \pi n _ { 2 } x _ { 2 } - \epsilon _ { 2 } \pi ) } \cdot \cdot \cdot \cos { ( 2 \pi n _ { m } x _ { m } - \epsilon _ { m } \pi ) } } \end{array}$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \vert x _ { 1 } - \epsilon _ { 1 } \pi \rangle \cos { ( 2 \pi n _ { 2 } x _ { 2 } - \epsilon _ { 2 } \pi ) } \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { ( \epsilon _ { 1 } , \epsilon _ { 2 } , . . . , \epsilon _ { m } = 0 , \frac 1 2 ;  } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { {  n _ { 1 } , n _ { 2 } , . . . , n _ { m } = 0 , 1 , 2 , . . . ) , } } \end{array}
$$

that differs from $f$ at most by $\epsilon$ everywhere.

This trigonometric series can be replaced by a trigonometric series with a finite number of terms.Take a sequence of positive numbers $\sigma _ { 1 } , \sigma _ { 2 } , \ldots$ converging to zero,and a trigonometric series $S _ { \nu }$ of a finite number of terms with arguments

$$
2 \pi n _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } - \epsilon _ { 1 } \pi , 2 \pi n _ { 2 } x _ { 2 } - \epsilon _ { 2 } \pi , . . . , 2 \pi n _ { m } x _ { m } - \epsilon _ { m } \pi ,
$$

such that $S _ { v }$ differs from our function $\Psi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { m } )$ at most by $\sigma _ { v }$ everywhere. Let $U _ { 0 } = S _ { 1 }$ $\mathbf { \Sigma } = S _ { 1 } , U _ { 1 } = S _ { 2 } - S _ { 1 } , U _ { 2 } = S _ { 3 } - S _ { 2 } , \dotsc ;$ then $U _ { v }$ is a rational integral function of cos $2 \pi x _ { \mu }$ and sin $2 \pi x _ { \mu }$ $( \mu = 1 , 2 , . . . , m )$ ， and $U _ { 0 } + U _ { 1 } + U _ { 2 } + \cdots$ is a uniformly convergent series that can be made to differ from $\Psi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { m } )$ at most by a given $\epsilon .$ Accordingly $\psi ( t )$ ，also,can be approximated by a series of this form.Hence, the theorem of Bohl enunciated in the beginning has been proved.

Such a class of functions $\psi ( t )$ is called a quasi-periodic function of Bohl. Bohl (l906） called it a periodic function in a wider sense.Esclangon (1902,1904,l916) called it a quasi-periodic function,and the function $\Psi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { m } )$ the associated function. In the case where the number of the variables $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m }$ is infinite,with the power of denumerable numbers,Bohr （1923,1925）called this function $\psi ( t )$ a limit-periodic function. When $x _ { n } = n \xi$ $( n = 1 , 2 , \ldots )$ ，it is the ordinary multiply periodic function discussed in Section 2.5 and can be expanded in a multiple Fourier series.It is a special case of Bohr's almost periodic functions (1925,1926).The important role of such a class of functions in celestial mechanics will be described in later chapters.It can easily be seen that the conditionally periodic motions discussed in Section 2.7 can be represented by quasi-periodic functions.

The trajectories of an $\pmb { n }$ -dimensional motion represented by a quasi-periodic function fill an $\pmb { n }$ -dimensional torus everywhere densely; that is, the motion is stable in Poisson's sense (Poincaré, 1899).

It can be shown that for a quasi-periodic function $f ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } )$ the time mean and the space mean both exist and are equal; that is,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \operatorname* { l i m } _ { T \to \infty } \frac { 1 } { T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } f ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) d t } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad = \int _ { x _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot \int _ { x _ { n } } f ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) d x _ { 1 } d x _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n } . } \end{array}
$$

This is the ergodic property of a quasi-periodic function. We shall discuss ergodicity in detail in a later chapter.

# ALMOST PERIODIC FUNCTIONS

# 2.11DEFINITION

The necessary and sufficient condition for a function $f ( { \pmb x } )$ , defined in the interval $- \infty < x < + \infty$ ，to be an everywhere continuous simply periodic function with period $2 \pi / k$ ，is that it should be uniformly approximated by a trigonometric series of the form $\sum _ { n } ^ { N } = \ l _ { - N } b _ { n } e ^ { i n k x }$ according to Weierstrass's theorem of Section 2.6. Let $k _ { 1 } , k _ { 2 } , \ldots , k _ { m }$ be positive integers.The necessary and sufficient condition for a continuous function $f ( x )$ to be uniformly approximated by a trigonometric series of the form

$$
\sum _ { n } b _ { n } \exp { [ i ( n _ { 1 } k _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + n _ { m } k _ { m } ) x ] }
$$

is that, given $\epsilon > 0$ ,we should be able to find $\delta > 0$ ,for which

$$
| f ( x + \tau ) - f ( x ) | \ \equiv \ \epsilon \qquad ( - \infty < x < + \infty ) _ { }
$$

holds true for the values of $\pmb { \tau } _ { : }$ ，such that $k _ { 1 } \tau , k _ { 2 } \tau , . . . , k _ { m } \tau$ differ from the corresponding integral multiples of $2 \pi$ at most by δ, according to Bohl, as discussed in Section 2.l0. In such a case, $\tau = \tau ( f , \epsilon )$ is called the translation number, belonging to $\epsilon _ { \scriptscriptstyle \mathrm { : } }$ ， $\operatorname { o f } f ( x )$ ,and the exponents in this series are integral multiples of $k _ { 1 } , k _ { 2 } , \ldots , k _ { m }$ by $n _ { 1 } , n _ { 2 } , \ldots , n _ { m } .$ ，respectively.

Now we consider an arbitrary sequence of numbers $\lambda _ { n }$ as the exponents. A class of functions represented by a series of such a form has been considered by Bohr (1923a,1925,1925a,1925b,1926).

Construct a series of finite terms

$$
s ( x ) = \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } a _ { n } e ^ { i \lambda _ { n } x } , ( - \infty < \lambda < + \infty ) ,
$$

with complex numbers $a _ { n }$ and real numbers $\lambda _ { n }$ .A class of functions that can be uniformly approximated by such a sum $\pmb { s } ( \pmb { x } )$ — that is, a class of continuous functions $f ( { \pmb x } ) = { \pmb u } ( { \pmb x } ) + i { \pmb v } ( { \pmb x } )$ ，such that for a given $\epsilon > 0$ we can find an $s ( { \pmb x } )$ that satisfies $| f ( { \pmb x } ) - s ( { \pmb x } ) | \ \le \epsilon .$ $( - \infty < x < + \infty ) -$ is called a closed set (abgeschlossen Hille)of functions $\{ s ( \pmb { x } ) \}$ and is written $H \{ s ( { \pmb x } ) \}$ .The sequence of numbers $\lambda _ { n }$ is of power higher than the denumerable numbers and corresponds to $\ u _ { 1 } = n k \ ( n = 0 , \pm 1 , \pm 2 , . . . )$ for the case of simply periodic functions.A real number $\tau ( f , \epsilon )$ that satisfies

$$
| f ( x + \tau ) - f ( x ) | \ \equiv \ \epsilon \qquad ( - \infty < x < + \infty )
$$

is called the translation number (Verschiebungszahl),belonging to $\epsilon$ ,of $f ( x )$ · If there exists a length $L$ that contains at least one value of the set $E$ of real numbers $\boldsymbol { \tau }$ in any interval $\alpha < x < \alpha + L$ ，then $\boldsymbol { \varepsilon }$ is said to be relatively dense.If there exists for any $\epsilon > 0$ a relatively dense set of translation numbers, belonging to $\epsilon$ ,of a function $f ( { \pmb x } )$ that is continuous in $- \infty < x < + \infty$ ，then $f ( { \pmb x } )$ is called an almost periodic function. To be precise, such a function $f ( { \pmb x } )$ should be called uniformly almost periodic, but the adverb “uniformly’ is omitted in the following (Section 2.5; Besicovitch, 1932).A quasi-periodic function is a special case of an almost periodic function.A simply periodic function is a special case of a quasi-periodic function.

$\mathbf { I f } f ( { \pmb x } )$ is an almost periodic function, then $f ( { \pmb x } + { \pmb c } )$ and $f ( c x )$ with an arbitrary constant $\pmb { c }$ are almost periodic；also $\pmb { u } ( \pmb { x } )$ and ${ \pmb v } ( { \pmb x } )$ are almost periodic. The sum and the product of two almost periodic functions $f ( { \pmb x } )$ and $g ( x )$ ,and $1 / f ( { \pmb x } )$ (if the lower bound of $| f ( { \pmb x } ) |$ is not zero) are almost periodic.More generally, the sum and the product of continuous almost periodic functions $f _ { 1 } ( x ) , f _ { 2 } ( x ) , . . . , f _ { N } ( x )$ are almost periodic.In particu-lar, the sum $\begin{array} { r } { s ( { \pmb x } ) = \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } a _ { n } e ^ { i \lambda _ { n } { \pmb x } } } \end{array}$ is almost periodic. The limit function $f ( x )$ of almost periodic functions $f _ { 1 } ( x ) , f _ { 2 } ( x ) , \ldots$ to which they converge uniformly in $- \infty < x < + \infty$ ，and hence any function uniformly approximated by the sum $s ( x )$ , is also almost periodic. Hence, the class of functions belonging to the set $H \{ s ( { \pmb x } ) \}$ is almost periodic. Bohr's theory of almost periodic functions is based on the proof for the identification of such almost periodic functions with the set $H \{ s ( { \pmb x } ) \}$ ·

Substitute $\scriptstyle { \boldsymbol { x } } ^ { \prime }$ for $x \mathrm { ~ - ~ } \tau$ in (25)，then,as $\pmb { \tau }$ isin $0 < x ^ { \prime } < L _ { \mathrm { { i } } }$ the inequality (25） becomes $| f ( x ^ { \prime } ) - f ( x ) | \leq \epsilon .$ Let $B ^ { \prime }$ be the upper bound $\operatorname { o f } f ( x )$ in this interval; then it is evident that $| f ( x ) | \le B ^ { \prime } + \epsilon$ and that $f ( x )$ is bounded.Hence, an almost periodic function is bounded.

Choose $\pmb { x } _ { 1 }$ and $x _ { 2 }$ in the interval between $- L$ and $+ L$ .If $\left| x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } \right| \leqq \delta _ { \mathrm { { ; } } }$ then $\left| f ( x _ { 2 } ) - f ( \pmb { x } _ { 1 } ) \right| \le \epsilon .$ Let $| f ( x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ) - f ( x _ { 1 } ) | \leq \epsilon , | f ( x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ) - f ( x _ { 2 } ) | \leq \epsilon ,$ $x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 1 } - \tau , x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 2 } - \tau _ { 3 }$ ,and choose $\pmb { x } _ { 2 } ^ { \prime }$ and $\pmb { x } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ in the interval between $- L$ and $+ L$ such that $| x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } - x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } | < \delta$ ,then, since $\left| f ( x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ) - f ( x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ) \right| \leq 3 \epsilon ,$ it is evident that an almost periodic function $f ( x )$ is uniformly continuous (Section 2.1） in the interval $- \infty < x < + \infty$

Take a sequence of real numbers $h _ { 1 } , h _ { 2 } , \ldots$ ，that converge to zero, and let $f ( x )$ be an almost periodic function. If $f ^ { \prime } ( x )$ is uniformly continuous in the sequence of functions

$$
\phi _ { n } ( x ) = { \frac { 1 } { h _ { n } } } \left\{ f ( x + h _ { n } ) - f ( x ) \right\} = f ^ { \prime } ( x + \theta _ { n } h _ { n } ) , \qquad 0 < \theta _ { n } < 1
$$

$$
( n = 1 , 2 , \ldots ) ,
$$

then we can find δ such that $| f ^ { \prime } ( x ^ { \prime } ) - f ^ { \prime } ( x ) | \ \triangleq \ \epsilon$ if $| x ^ { \prime } - x | \leq \delta$ for a given $\epsilon$ 、As the sequence $\left\{ h _ { n } \right\}$ converges to zero, there exists an $N$ such that $\left| \theta _ { n } h _ { n } \right| \leqq \delta$ for $n \leq N .$ Since $\left| \phi ( { \boldsymbol { x } } ) - f ^ { \prime } ( { \boldsymbol { x } } ) \right| \leq \epsilon$ for such an $_ { n }$ ，the function $\phi _ { n } ( x )$ converges uniformly to $f ^ { \prime } ( x )$ . Thus $\phi _ { n } ( x )$ , accordingly its limit function, is almost periodic.Hence, the derived function $f ^ { \prime } ( x )$ of an almost periodic function $f ( { \pmb x } )$ is almost periodic.

As an example, consider a simply periodic function $e ^ { i \lambda x }$ and a translation number $\pmb { \tau }$ such that $| e ^ { i \lambda \tau } - 1 | \le \epsilon . \operatorname { F o r } \epsilon < 1$ there exists δ such that $| e ^ { i \delta } - 1 | < \epsilon$ Hence,& satisfies $\left| \lambda \tau \right| \leq \delta { \pmod { 2 \pi } }$ .For $N$ almost periodic functions $e ^ { i \lambda _ { 1 } x } , . . . , e ^ { i \lambda _ { N } x }$ and $\delta < \pi$ there should exist $\epsilon ( \delta )$ and at least one almost periodic function in an interval of length $L$ Hence, if $N$ inequalities

$$
\big | \lambda _ { n } \tau \big | \le \delta ( \mathrm { m o d } 2 \pi ) \qquad ( n = 1 , 2 , . . . , N ) ,
$$

and $\delta > 0$ are given, then there should exist at least one $\pmb { \tau }$ satisfying these inequalities in any interval of length $L$ This is Kronecker’s theorem, proved in Section 2.8.

# 2.12PROPERTIES OF ALMOST PERIODIC FUNCTIONS

We propose the following important theorems:

Theorem:The necessary and sufficient condition for the indefinite integral $F ( x ) = \int _ { 0 } ^ { x } f ( x ) d x$ of an almost periodic function $f ( x )$ to be almost periodic is that $\boldsymbol { F } ( \boldsymbol { x } )$ should be bounded.

In fact,let the upper and the lower bounds of $F ( x )$ be, respectively, $k _ { 2 }$ and $k _ { 1 } , k _ { 2 } > k _ { 1 }$ .If $k _ { 1 } = k _ { 2 } .$ then $\boldsymbol { F } ( \boldsymbol { x } )$ is a constant. It is sufficient to prove that for a given $\epsilon > 0$ there exists an $\epsilon _ { 1 }$ such that the translation number, belonging to $\epsilon _ { : }$ of $F ( x )$ is the translation number of $f ( x )$ ，belonging to $\epsilon _ { 1 } = \epsilon _ { 1 } ( \epsilon , f ( x ) )$ ·

There are two values $\pmb { x } _ { 1 }$ and $x _ { 2 }$ satisfying $F ( x _ { 1 } ) < k _ { 1 } + \epsilon / 6 _ { ; }$ $F ( x _ { 2 } ) > k _ { 2 } - \epsilon / 6$ 、Denote the smaller of the two by $\xi$ and the distance $\| x _ { 2 } - x _ { 1 } \|$ by $d .$ Choose $l _ { 0 } = L ( \xi / 6 d )$ so that in any interval of length $l _ { 0 }$ there is at least a translation number $\tau = \tau ( \epsilon / 6 d ) \ \mathrm { o f } \ f ( x )$ , belonging to $\epsilon _ { 1 } = \epsilon / 6 d .$ We can choose $\pmb { \tau }$ so that $\xi + \tau$ is in the interval $( \alpha , \alpha + l _ { 0 } )$ and accordingly $x _ { 1 } + \tau = z _ { 1 }$ $x _ { 2 } + \tau = z _ { 2 }$ are in the interval $( \alpha , \alpha + L _ { 0 } )$ with $L _ { 0 } = l _ { 0 } + d . $ Thus

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { { \cal F } ( z _ { 2 } ) - { \cal F } ( z _ { 1 } ) = { \cal F } ( x _ { 2 } ) - { \cal F } ( x _ { 1 } ) + \displaystyle \int _ { z _ { 1 } } ^ { z _ { 2 } } f ( y ) d y - \displaystyle \int _ { x _ { 1 } } ^ { x _ { 2 } } f ( y ) d y } } \\ { { ~ = { \cal F } ( x _ { 2 } ) - { \cal F } ( x _ { 1 } ) + \displaystyle \int _ { x _ { 1 } } ^ { x _ { 2 } } [ f ( y + \tau ) - f ( y ) ] d y } } \\ { { ~ \geq { \cal F } ( x _ { 2 } ) - { \cal F } ( x _ { 1 } ) - d \displaystyle \frac { \epsilon } { 6 d } } } \\ { { ~ > k _ { 2 } - k _ { 1 } - \displaystyle \frac { 2 \epsilon } { 6 } - \displaystyle \frac { \epsilon } { 6 } = k _ { 2 } - k _ { 1 } - \displaystyle \frac { \epsilon } { 2 } . } } \end{array}
$$

Since this inequality is satisfied for $F ( z _ { 1 } ) < k _ { 1 } + \epsilon / 2 , F ( z _ { 2 } ) > k _ { 2 } - \epsilon / 2$ therefore the two values $z _ { 1 }$ and $z _ { 2 }$ satisfying these inequalities are in any interval $( \alpha , \alpha + L _ { 0 } )$

For any arbitrarily given $_ x$ ,take $_ z$ such that $F ( z _ { 1 } ) < k _ { 1 } + \epsilon / 2$ in the interval $( x , x + L _ { 0 } )$ ; then

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \langle x + \tau \rangle - F ( x ) = F ( z _ { 1 } + \tau ) - F ( z _ { 1 } ) + \displaystyle \int _ { x } ^ { x + \tau } f ( y ) d y - \displaystyle \int _ { z _ { 1 } } ^ { z _ { 1 } + \tau } f ( y ) } & { } \\ { = F ( z _ { 1 } + \tau ) - F ( z _ { 1 } ) + \displaystyle \int _ { x } ^ { z _ { 1 } } f ( y ) d y - \displaystyle \int _ { x + \tau } ^ { z _ { 1 } + \tau } f ( y ) d \tau } \\ { > \displaystyle k _ { 1 } - \left( k _ { 1 } + \frac { \epsilon } { 2 } \right) - \displaystyle \left. \int _ { x } ^ { z _ { 1 } } \left[ f ( y + \tau ) - f ( y ) \right] d y \right. } & { } \\ { > - \frac { \epsilon } { 2 } - L _ { 0 } \frac { \epsilon } { 2 L _ { 0 } } = - \epsilon . } \end{array}
$$

Next, take $z _ { 2 }$ such that $F ( z _ { 2 } ) > k _ { 2 } - \epsilon / 2$ in the interval $( x , x + L _ { 0 } )$ ； then it can similarly be proved that

$$
| F ( x + \tau ) - F ( x ) | < \epsilon .
$$

From these two inequalities we obtain

$$
| F ( x + \tau ) - F ( x ) | \leq \epsilon .
$$

The Mean Value Theorem: There exists the following mean value for an almost periodic function $f ( x )$ ：

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { T \to \infty } { \frac { 1 } { T } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } f ( x ) \ d x = \Re \{ f ( x ) \} .
$$

In fact, let $L ( \epsilon )$ be the translation number of $\scriptstyle \int ( x )$ , belonging to $\epsilon$ ,and $\pmb { n }$ be a positive integer; then there exists a translation number $\tau _ { \nu }$ belonging to $\epsilon$ in the interval $( \nu T , \nu T + L )$ for

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { n T } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { n T } f ( x ) \ d x = \sum _ { v = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } { \frac { 1 } { n T } } \int _ { v T } ^ { ( v + 1 ) T } f ( x ) \ d x .
$$

Hence

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \int _ { \nu T } ^ { ( \nu + 1 ) T } f ( x ) ~ d x = \int _ { \nu T - \tau _ { \nu } } ^ { ( \nu + 1 ) T - \tau _ { \nu } } ~ f ( x + \tau _ { \nu } ) ~ d x } \\ { = \displaystyle \left( \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } + \int _ { \nu T - \tau _ { \nu } } ^ { 0 } + \int _ { T } ^ { ( \nu + 1 ) T - \tau _ { \nu } } \right) f ( x + \tau _ { \nu } ) ~ d x . } \end{array}
$$

The lengths of the intervals in the last two integrals do not exceed $L$ ； that is, each of these integrals has a smaller absolute value than $\Gamma L ,$ where $\Gamma$ is the upper bound of $| f ( x ) |$ . Owing to the inequality

$$
\left| \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } \left[ f ( x + \tau _ { v } ) - f ( x ) \right] d x \right| \le \epsilon T ,
$$

the first integral satisfes

$$
\left| { \frac { 1 } { n T } } \int _ { v T } ^ { ( v + 1 ) T } f ( x ) \ d x - { \frac { 1 } { n T } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } f ( x ) \ d x \right| \leq { \frac { 1 } { n } } \left( \epsilon + { \frac { 2 \Gamma L } { T } } \right) .
$$

Hence

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \left| \frac { 1 } { n T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { n T } f ( x ) ~ d x - \frac { 1 } { T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } f ( x ) ~ d x \right| } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad \leq \sum _ { \nu = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } \left| \frac { 1 } { n T } \int _ { \nu T } ^ { ( \nu + 1 ) T } f ( x ) ~ d x - \frac { 1 } { n T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } f ( x ) ~ d x \right| } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad \leq \epsilon + \frac { 2 \Gamma L } { T } . } \end{array}
$$

Since

$$
\left| \int _ { 0 } ^ { T ^ { \prime } } f ( x ) \ d x - \int _ { 0 } ^ { n T } f ( x ) \ d x \right| \leq \Gamma T
$$

for $n T < T ^ { \prime } \leq ( n + 1 ) T _ { ! }$ ，therefore

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { T ^ { \prime } } \int _ { \circ } ^ { \pi } f ( x ) d x - \frac { 1 } { n T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty \pi } f ( x ) d x \bigg | } \\ & { \qquad \le \bigg | \left( \frac { 1 } { T ^ { \prime } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } - \frac { 1 } { T ^ { \prime } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty \pi } \right) f ( x ) d x \bigg | + \bigg | \left( \frac { 1 } { T ^ { \prime } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty t } - \frac { 1 } { n T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty \pi } \right) f ( x ) \bigg | , } \\ & { \qquad \le \displaystyle \frac { \Gamma } { n } + \frac { 1 } { n } \bigg | \frac { 1 } { n T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi \pi } f ( x ) d x \bigg | } \\ & { \qquad \le \displaystyle \frac { \Gamma } { n } + \frac { 1 } { n } \bigg | \frac { 1 } { T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \tau } f ( x ) d x \bigg | + \frac { \epsilon } { n } + \frac { 2 \Gamma L } { n T } } \\ & { \qquad \le \displaystyle \frac { 2 \Gamma } { n } + \frac { \epsilon } { n } + \frac { 2 \Gamma L } { n T } . } \end{array}
$$

From (26)and (27) we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \Bigg | \Bigg | \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { T ^ { \prime } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T ^ { \prime } } f ( x ) d x - \frac { 1 } { T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } f ( x ) d x \Bigg | } \\ & { \qquad \leq \Bigg | \left( \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { T ^ { \prime } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T ^ { \prime } } - \frac { 1 } { n T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { n T } \right) f ( x ) d x \Bigg | + \Bigg | \left( \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { n T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { n T } - \frac { 1 } { T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } \right) f ( x ) } \\ & { \qquad \leq \displaystyle \frac { 2 \Gamma } { n } + \Big ( \epsilon + \frac { 2 \Gamma L } { T } \Big ) \Big ( 1 + \frac { 1 } { n } \Big ) \leq \frac { 2 \Gamma } { n } + 2 \Big ( \epsilon + \frac { 2 \Gamma L } { T } \Big ) . } \end{array}
$$

If we choose $T _ { 1 }$ and $T _ { 2 }$ ,respectively,in the intervals $[ n _ { 1 } T , ( n _ { 1 } + 1 ) T ]$ and $[ n _ { 2 } T , ( n _ { 2 } + 1 ) T ]$ ，where $n _ { 1 } , n _ { 2 } > n$ ，then

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } & { { \bigg | } { \frac { 1 } { T _ { 1 } } } { \int _ { 0 } ^ { T _ { 1 } } } f ( x ) \ d x - { \frac { 1 } { T _ { 2 } } } { \int _ { 0 } ^ { T _ { 2 } } } f ( x ) \ d x { \bigg | } } \\ & { \qquad \leq { \bigg | } { \bigg ( } { \frac { 1 } { T _ { 1 } } } { \int _ { 0 } ^ { T _ { 1 } } } - { \frac { 1 } { T } } { \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } } { \bigg ) } f ( x ) \ d x { \bigg | } + { \bigg | } { \bigg ( } { \frac { 1 } { T _ { 2 } } } { \int _ { 0 } ^ { T _ { 2 } } } - { \frac { L } { T } } { \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } } { \bigg ) } f ( y ) \ d y } \\ & { \qquad \leq { \frac { 4 \Gamma } { n } } + 4 { \bigg ( } \epsilon + { \frac { 2 \Gamma L } { T } } { \bigg ) } . } \end{array} }
$$

For a given $\epsilon$ we choose $T$ so that $2 \Gamma L / T \not \equiv \epsilon$ and $n$ so that $4 \Gamma / n \ \leq \ \epsilon$ then,for $T _ { 1 } , T _ { 2 } > n T ,$

$$
\left| { \frac { 1 } { T _ { 1 } } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T _ { 1 } } f ( x ) \ d x - { \frac { 1 } { T _ { 2 } } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T _ { 2 } } f ( x ) \ d x \right| \ \leq \ 9 \epsilon .
$$

$\mathbb { f } \epsilon$ is made sufficiently small, then $( 1 / T ) \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } f ( x ) d x$ converges uniformly to its limit for $T \to \infty$ , according to Cauchy's theorem. Hence, there exists the limit function $\mathfrak { M } \{ f ( x ) \}$ in the statement of the mean value theorem.

Since $f ( x )$ and $f ( x + a )$ are both almost periodic, and, since

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { T } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } f ( x + a ) \ d x = { \frac { 1 } { T } } \int _ { a } ^ { a + T } f ( x ) \ d x ,
$$

therefore

$$
{ \underset { x } { \mathfrak { M } } } \{ f ( x + a ) \} = { \underset { x } { \mathfrak { M } } } \{ f ( x ) \} .
$$

Since $f ( x + a )$ and $f ( x )$ have the same translation number, we have

$$
\left| \operatorname* { \mathfrak { M } } _ { x } \{ f ( x ) \} - \frac { 1 } { T } \int _ { a } ^ { a + T } f ( x ) \ d x \right| \ \le \epsilon + \frac { 2 \Gamma L } { T } ;
$$

thati $( 1 / T ) \int _ { a } ^ { a + T } f ( x ) d x$ tends anifargt $\mathfrak { M } \{ f ( x ) \}$ as $_ { T }$ increases

Theorem : If $f ( x )$ is almost periodic, then $f ( { \pmb x } + t ) { \pmb \bar { f } } ( t )$ is also almost periodic，where $\bar { f } ( t )$ is the conjugate complex function of $f ( t )$ . The convolution function (Faltung)

$$
g ( \pmb { x } ) = \mathfrak { M } \{ f ( \pmb { x } + t ) \bar { f } ( t ) \}
$$

is also almost periodic.

In fact, $f ( { \pmb x } )$ being uniformly continuous, there exists a $\delta ( \epsilon )$ for any $\epsilon$ such that $| f ( { \pmb x } _ { 1 } ) - f ( { \pmb x } _ { 2 } ) | \ \le \ \epsilon$ for $| x _ { 1 } - x _ { 2 } | \le \delta = \delta ( \epsilon )$ .Hence,it should follow that

$$
| g ( x + h ) - g ( x ) | = \left| \mathfrak { M } \{ [ f ( x + h + t ) - f ( x + t ) ] \bar { f } ( t ) \} \right| \le \epsilon \Gamma
$$

for $| h | \leqq \delta .$ But, since

$$
| g ( x + \tau ) - g ( x ) | = \left| \mathfrak { M } \{ [ f ( x + \tau + t ) - f ( x + t ) ] \bar { f } ( t ) \} \right| \le \epsilon ,
$$

therefore the translation number $\pmb { \tau }$ of $f ( x )$ , belonging to $\epsilon / \Gamma _ { : }$ is also the translation number of $g ( x )$ , belonging to $\epsilon$

As a special case of this property, the function

$$
g _ { T } ( x ) = \frac { 1 } { T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \tau } f ( x + t ) \bar { f } ( t ) d t
$$

is also almost periodic. Hence ${ \mathfrak { M } } \{ g ( x ) \} = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { T \to \infty } { \mathfrak { M } } \{ g _ { T } ( x ) \}$ . But, since

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \mathfrak { M } \{ g _ { T } ( x ) \} = } & { \underset { x  \infty } { \operatorname* { l i m } } \frac { 1 } { X } \int _ { 0 } ^ { x } ( \frac { 1 } { T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { x } f ( x + t ) \bar { f } ( t ) \ d t ) d x } \\ & { = \underset { x  \infty } { \operatorname* { l i m } } \frac { 1 } { T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { x } ( \frac { 1 } { X } \int _ { 0 } ^ { x } f ( x + t ) \ d x ) \bar { f } ( t ) \ d t } \\ & { = \frac { 1 } { T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { x } \mathfrak { M } \{ f ( x + t ) \} \bar { f } ( t ) \ d t } \\ & { = \frac { 1 } { T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { x } \mathfrak { M } \{ f ( x ) \} \bar { f } ( t ) \ d t = \mathfrak { M } \{ f ( x ) \} \cdot \frac { 1 } { T } \int _ { 0 } ^ { x } \bar { f } ( t ) \ d t , } \end{array}
$$

therefore

$$
\mathfrak { M } \{ g ( x ) \} = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { T \to \infty } \mathfrak { M } \{ g _ { T } ( x ) \} = \mathfrak { M } \{ f ( x ) \} \cdot \mathfrak { M } \{ \bar { f } ( t ) \} .
$$

Hence

$$
{ \mathfrak { M } } \{ g ( x ) \} = | { \mathfrak { M } } \{ f ( x ) \} | ^ { 2 } .
$$

Theorem on the Fourier Coeffcients :

$$
a ( \lambda ) = \mathfrak { M } \{ f ( x ) e ^ { - i \lambda x } \}
$$

is zero for any value of $\lambda$ , except, at most, for a denumerable set of $\lambda$

In fact, as the product $f ( x ) e ^ { - i \lambda x }$ of two almost periodic functions $f ( x )$ and $e ^ { - i \lambda x }$ is almost periodic, the mean value

$$
a ( \lambda ) = \mathfrak { M } \{ f ( x ) e ^ { - i \lambda x } \}
$$

exists with the real number $\lambda$ Consider $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } b _ { n } e ^ { i \lambda _ { n } x } } \end{array}$ where $\lambda _ { n }$ are all different.The function $\begin{array} { r } { | f ( { \boldsymbol x } ) - \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } b _ { n } e ^ { i \lambda _ { n } { \boldsymbol x } } | ^ { 2 } } \end{array}$ is almost periodic；thus

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \mathfrak { M } \Bigg \{ \bigg | f ( x ) - \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } b _ { n } e ^ { i \lambda _ { n } x } \bigg | ^ { 2 } \Bigg \} } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad = \mathfrak { M } \Bigg \{ \left[ f ( x ) - \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } b _ { n } e ^ { i \lambda _ { n } x } \right] \left[ \bar { f } ( x ) - \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } \bar { b } _ { n } e ^ { - i \lambda _ { n } x } \right] \bigg \} . } \end{array}
$$

However, the mean value of a sum is equal to the sum of the mean values. Hence

$$
{ \mathfrak { M } } { \bigg \{ } { \bigg | } f ( x ) - \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } b _ { n } e ^ { i \lambda _ { n } x } { \bigg | } ^ { 2 } { \bigg \} } = { \mathfrak { M } } \{ f ( x ) { \bar { f } } ( x ) \} - \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } { \bar { b } } _ { n } { \mathfrak { M } } \{ f ( x ) e ^ { - i \lambda _ { n } x } \}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle - \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } b _ { n } \mathfrak { M } \{ \bar { f } ( x ) e ^ { i \lambda _ { n } x } \} } \\ { \displaystyle + \sum _ { m = 1 } ^ { N } \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } b _ { n } \bar { b } _ { n } \mathfrak { M } \{ e ^ { i ( \lambda _ { m } - \lambda _ { n } ) x } \} . } \end{array}
$$

Here $ { \mathfrak { M } } \{ e ^ { i \lambda _ { n } x } \} = 0$ for $\lambda \neq 0$ and $= 1$ for $\lambda = 0$ .Thus,this formula is written

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle = \mathfrak { M } \{ | f ( x ) | ^ { 2 } \} - \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } \bar { b } _ { n } a ( \lambda _ { n } ) - \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } b _ { n } \bar { a } ( \lambda _ { n } ) + \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } b _ { n } \bar { b } _ { n } } } \\ { { \displaystyle = \mathfrak { M } \{ | f ( x ) | ^ { 2 } \} - \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } | a ( \lambda _ { n } ) | ^ { 2 } + \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } | b _ { n } - a ( \lambda _ { n } ) | ^ { 2 } . } } \end{array}
$$

The expression (31) has a minimum value at $\bar { b } _ { n } = a ( \lambda _ { n } )$ with a given $\lambda _ { n }$ ； thus

$$
{ \mathfrak { M } } \bigg \{ \bigg | f ( x ) - \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } a ( \lambda _ { n } ) e ^ { i \lambda _ { n } x } \bigg | ^ { 2 } \bigg \} = { \mathfrak { M } } \{ | f ( x ) | ^ { 2 } \} - \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } | a ( \lambda _ { n } ) | ^ { 2 } .
$$

But this right-hand member cannot be negative, and

$$
\sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } | a ( \lambda _ { n } ) | ^ { 2 } \leq \mathfrak { M } \{ | f ( x ) | ^ { 2 } \} .
$$

Now denote the sets of values of $\lambda$ as follows :

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { | a ( \lambda ) | > 1 } & { \mathrm { a s } \quad \lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } , . . . , \lambda _ { n _ { 1 } } } \\ { 1 \geq | a ( \lambda ) | > \frac { 1 } { 2 } } & { \mathrm { a s } \quad \lambda _ { n _ { 1 } + 1 } , \lambda _ { n _ { 1 } + 2 } , . . . , \lambda _ { n _ { 2 } } } \\ { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \geq | a ( \lambda ) | > \frac { 1 } { 3 } } & { \mathrm { a s } \quad \lambda _ { n _ { 2 } + 1 } , \lambda _ { n _ { 2 } + 2 } , . . . , \lambda _ { n _ { 3 } } } \end{array}
$$

and so on.

Evidently the set of all values of $\lambda$ such that $| a ( \lambda ) | > 0$ is at most denumerable.Hence, $a ( \lambda ) = 0$ except,at most,for a denumerable set.

Let the values of $\lambda$ corresponding to these nonzero values of $a ( \lambda )$ be $\Lambda _ { 1 } , \Lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots$ We call such $\Lambda _ { 1 } , \Lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots$ the Fourier exponents of $\dot { f } ( \pmb { x } )$ , and the

corresponding mean values $a ( \Lambda _ { 1 } ) = A _ { 1 }$ $a ( \Lambda _ { 2 } ) = A _ { 2 } , . . .$ the Fourier coefficients. We write

$$
f ( x ) \sim \sum _ { n } A _ { n } e ^ { i \Lambda _ { n } x }
$$

and call it the Fourier series of $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { f } } ( \pmb { x } )$ .If

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { N  \infty } \mathfrak { M } \bigg \{ \bigg | f ( x ) - \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } A _ { n } e ^ { i A _ { n } x } \bigg | ^ { 2 } \bigg \} = 0 ,
$$

then we say that the Fourier series $\textstyle \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } A _ { n } e ^ { i \Lambda _ { n } { \boldsymbol { x } } }$ converges in the mean to $f ( x )$ .If $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } a _ { n } e ^ { i \lambda _ { n } x } e ^ { - i \lambda x } } \end{array}$ is uniformly convergent in $- \infty < x < + \infty$ ， then we can construct the mean values term by term,and

$$
a ( \lambda ) = \mathfrak { M } \{ f ( x ) e ^ { - i \lambda x } \} = \mathfrak { M } \{ \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } a _ { n } e ^ { i \lambda _ { n } x } \cdot e ^ { - i \lambda x } \}
$$

is equal to $a _ { n }$ for $\lambda = \lambda _ { n }$ and equal to zero for $\lambda \neq \lambda _ { n }$ If (33）holds and $\textstyle \sum _ { n } | A _ { n } | ^ { 2 }$ is convergent in (32), then

$$
\sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } \vert A _ { n } \vert ^ { 2 } \leq \mathfrak { M } \{ \vert f ( x ) \vert ^ { 2 } \} .
$$

As we shall see later,

$$
\sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { N } \vert A _ { n } \vert ^ { 2 } = \mathfrak { M } \{ \vert f ( x ) \vert ^ { 2 } \} .
$$

This is called Parseval's equation. The theorem on a simply periodic function given in Section 2.2 can be applied also for an almost periodic function.

To prove that an almost periodic function can be uniformly approximated by a Fourier series we must first show that an almost periodic function can be uniquely determined by its Fourier series. This is called the uniqueness theorem.To prove the uniqueness theorem it is enough to show that all the coeffcients of the Fourier series belonging to the difference $f ( x ) = f _ { 1 } ( x ) - f _ { 2 } ( x )$ of two different almost periodic functions $f _ { 1 } ( x )$ and $f _ { 2 } ( x )$ are zero; that is,

$$
a ( \lambda ) = \mathfrak { M } \{ f ( x ) e ^ { - i \lambda _ { n } x } \} = 0 .
$$

This is equivalent to Parseval's equation. In fact, if the equation holds, then $ \mathfrak { M } \{ | f ( x ) | ^ { 2 } \} = 0$ and $\begin{array} { r } { f ( { \boldsymbol x } ) \equiv 0 } \end{array}$ ，and conversely if

$$
f ( x ) \sim \sum _ { n } A _ { n } e ^ { i \Lambda _ { n } x } ,
$$

then by writing $f ( x ) e ^ { - i \lambda x }$ instead of $f ( x )$ in (30),we obtain, for the function $g ( x )$ of (29),

$$
\mathfrak { M } \{ f ( x + t ) e ^ { - i \lambda ( x + t ) } \bar { f } ( t ) e ^ { i \lambda t } \} = g ( x ) e ^ { - i \lambda x }
$$

and

$$
{ \mathfrak { M } } \{ g ( x ) e ^ { - i \lambda x } \} = | { \mathfrak { M } } \{ f ( x ) e ^ { - i \lambda x } \} | ^ { 2 } ;
$$

hence

$$
g ( x ) = \sum _ { n } | A _ { n } | ^ { 2 } e ^ { i \Lambda _ { n } x } ;
$$

that is

$$
\quad g ( 0 ) = \operatorname* { \mathfrak { M } } _ { t } \{ f ( t ) { \bar { f } } ( t ) \} = \operatorname* { \mathfrak { M } } _ { t } \{ | f ( t ) | ^ { 2 } \} = \sum _ { n } | A _ { n } | ^ { 2 } ,
$$

which is Parseval's equation.

In order to prove ${ \mathfrak { M } } \{ | f ( x ) | ^ { 2 } \} = 0$ ，Vallée Poussin (1916； see also Bohr,1932; Besicovitch,1932; Favard,1933),constructed

$$
G _ { T } ( x ) = \frac { 1 } { T } { \int } _ { 0 } ^ { T } F _ { T } ( x + t ) \bar { F } _ { T } ( t ) ~ d t
$$

with an almost periodic function

$$
F _ { T } ( x ) = \sum _ { n = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \alpha _ { n } e ^ { 2 \pi i n x / T }
$$

and let $G _ { T } ( \pmb { x } )$ be approximated by $g ( x )$ in $0 < x < T .$ For the assumption ${ \pmb a } ( \lambda ) = 0$ he substituted the assumption that the upper bound of $\left| \alpha _ { n } \right|$ for $T \to \infty$ is zero in $- \infty < n < + \infty$ and proved from $\mathfrak { M } \{ | g ( x ) | ^ { 2 } \} = 0$ that $g ( { \pmb x } ) \equiv 0$ ,and—see(35)—from $g ( 0 ) \equiv 0$ that ${ \mathfrak { M } } _ { t } \{ | f ( t ) | ^ { 2 } \} = 0$ ，that is, $\begin{array} { r } { f ( { \pmb x } ) \equiv 0 } \end{array}$ This leads to the following

The Uniqueness Theorem:An almost periodic function is determined uniquely by its Fourier series.

A simply periodic function is a special case of an almost periodic function.Hence, the uniqueness theorem is true for a simply periodic function,and also for the quasi-periodic function of Bohl,as it is also a special case of an almost periodic function.A similar situation arises for the approximation theorem we are going to prove.An almost periodic function thus belongs to a very wide class of functions.

# 2.13BASE FIELD

Consider a denumerable sequence of real numbers $\Lambda _ { 1 }$ ， $\Lambda _ { 2 } .$ .... We say that $\mathbf { B } = \{ \beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } , . . . \}$ is the base if

(i) $\beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } , \ldots$ are linearly independent, that is, there is no linear homogeneous relation

$$
R _ { 1 } \beta _ { 1 } + R _ { 2 } \beta _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + R _ { m } \beta _ { m } = 0
$$

with rational coefficients $R _ { 1 } , R _ { 2 } , . . . , R _ { m }$ for any integer $_ m$ ,and

(ii) any $\Lambda _ { n }$ can be written uniquely in the form

$$
\Lambda _ { n } = r _ { \bar { \imath } } \beta _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } \beta _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + r _ { m } \beta _ { m } \qquad ( n = 1 , 2 , \ldots )
$$

with rational numbers $r _ { 1 } , r _ { 2 } , \ldots , r _ { m }$ for a sufficiently large m. The numbers $\Lambda _ { 1 } , \Lambda _ { 2 } , \ldots$ belong to this base field, because they form a feld as shown by (36). For constructing the base of an arbitrary sequence $\Lambda _ { 1 } , \Lambda _ { 2 } , . . . ,$ we take at first a nonzero $\Lambda _ { n _ { 1 } }$ and let it be $\beta _ { 1 }$ . Let $\Lambda _ { n }$ such that $r _ { 1 } \beta _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } \Lambda _ { n } = 0$ be $\Lambda _ { n _ { 1 } + 1 } , \Lambda _ { n _ { \bot } + 2 } , . . . .$ We take as $\beta _ { 2 }$ such $\Lambda _ { n _ { 2 } }$ as do not satisfy this relation.Let $\Lambda _ { n }$ such that $r _ { 1 } \beta _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } \beta _ { 2 } + r _ { 3 } \Lambda _ { n } = 0$ be $\Lambda _ { n _ { 2 } + 1 } , \Lambda _ { n _ { 2 } + 2 } , . . . .$ We repeat this process. If it comes to an end after a finite number of steps, $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ ,then

$$
\{ \beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } , . . . , \beta _ { p } \} = \{ \Lambda _ { n _ { 1 } } , \Lambda _ { n _ { 2 } } , . . . , \Lambda _ { n _ { p } } \}
$$

is the base. It may be necessary to repeat this process a denumerable number of times. Then the sequence $\mathbf { B } = \{ \beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } , . . . \}$ is the base. Hence from any sequence $\Lambda _ { 1 }$ ， $\Lambda _ { 2 3 } \ldots$ we can construct the base $\mathbf { B } = \{ \beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } , . . . \}$ in general.

Suppose that the Fourier exponents $\Lambda _ { n }$ are expressed by the base B in the form (36) with rational coefficients. Assuming that $0 \leq k _ { n } ^ { ( q ) } \leq 1$ for all $_ { n }$ and $q$ ,and $k _ { n } ^ { ( q ) } \to 1$ for $q \to \infty$ with a fixed $_ { n }$ ,we form the finite sum

$$
s _ { q } ( x ) = \sum _ { n } k _ { n } ^ { ( q ) } A _ { n } e ^ { i \Lambda _ { n } x } \qquad ( q = 1 , 2 , . . . ) .
$$

Then, by Parseval's equation,

$$
{ \mathfrak { M } } \{ | f ( x ) - s _ { q } ( x ) | ^ { 2 } \} = \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } { ( 1 - k _ { n } ^ { ( q ) } ) ^ { 2 } | A _ { n } | ^ { 2 } } .
$$

Hence we can prove that the finite sum (37) converges to zero for $q \to \infty$ ,and $s _ { q } ( x )$ converges to $f ( x )$ in the mean.

With Fejer's nucleus

$$
K _ { n } ( t ) = \sum _ { \nu = - n } ^ { n } \Big ( 1 - \frac { | \nu | } { n } \Big ) e ^ { - i \nu t } ,
$$

Bochner （1927） constructed

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { K ^ { ( q ) } ( t ) = \prod _ { \nu = L } ^ { q } K _ { P } \Big ( \frac { \beta _ { \nu } } { Q } t \Big ) } } } \\ { { = \displaystyle { \sum _ { \nu _ { 1 } = - P } ^ { P } \cdots \sum _ { \nu _ { q } = - P } ^ { P } \left( 1 - \frac { | \nu _ { 1 } | } { P } \right) \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot \left( 1 - \frac { | \nu _ { q } | } { P } \right) } } } \\ { { \mathrm { } \qquad \quad \times \exp \left[ - i t \Big ( \frac { \nu _ { 1 } } { Q } \beta _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \frac { \nu _ { q } } { Q } \beta _ { 1 } \Big ) \right] } } \end{array}
$$

where $q$ is an integer and $Q = q ! , P = q Q .$ ,and considered

$$
K ^ { \left( q \right) } ( t ) = \sum _ { n } k _ { n } ^ { \left( q \right) } e ^ { - i \Delta _ { n } t } .
$$

Such a $k _ { n } ^ { ( q ) }$ satisfies the above assumption that $0 \leq k _ { n } ^ { ( q ) } \leq 1$ for all $_ { n }$ and $q$ and $k _ { n } ^ { ( q ) } \to 1$ for $q \to \infty$ for a fixed $\pmb { n }$ .Bochner proved that $f ( x )$ is a majorant sequence of the sequence of functions $s _ { q } ( \pmb { x } )$ of（37）with such $k _ { n } ^ { ( q ) }$ ; in other words, the translation number $\boldsymbol { \tau }$ of $f ( x )$ , belonging to any $\epsilon > 0$ , equals the translation number of $s _ { q } ( x )$ , belonging to $\epsilon$ In general, if a sequence of functions $f _ { n } ( x )$ $( n = 1 , 2 , \ldots )$ converges to its majorant function $f ( x )$ in the mean for $n \to \infty$ ,that is,if

$$
\mathfrak { M } \{ | f _ { n } ( x ) - f ( x ) | ^ { 2 } \} \to 0 ,
$$

then the upper bound of $| f _ { n } ( x ) - f ( x ) |$ in $- \infty < x < + \infty$ converges to zero for $n  \infty$ .Hence $f _ { n } ( x )$ converges uniformly to $f ( x )$ . Thus it can be proved that the sequence of function $s _ { q } ( x )$ converges uniformly to $f ( x )$ in the mean for $q \to \infty$ .The proof is based on the formula

$$
\begin{array} { r } { s _ { q } ( x ) = \underset { t } { \mathfrak { M } } \{ f ( x + t ) K ^ { ( q ) } ( t ) \} , } \end{array}
$$

and on the property that $K ^ { \left( q \right) } ( t ) \geq 0$ and $ \mathfrak { M } _ { t } \{ K ^ { ( q ) } ( t ) \} = 1$ This leads to the following

Approximation Theorem: An almost periodic function can be uniformly approximated in $- \infty < x < + \infty$ by the finite sum

$$
s ( x ) = \sum _ { n = - N } ^ { N } a _ { n } e ^ { i \lambda _ { n } x } ;
$$

that is, there exists $s ( x )$ such that for a given $\epsilon > 0$ the inequality $| f ( { \pmb x } ) - s ( { \pmb x } ) | \le \epsilon$ holds for all values of $_ x$

As $s ( x )$ itself is an almost periodic function, its limit function is also almost periodic.Hence, the closed set $H \{ s ( { \pmb x } ) \}$ of the class of all functions $s ( x )$ coincides with almost periodic functions.

Thus we have summarized Bohr's theory of almost periodic functions in the revised form of Vallée Poussn and Bochner; we have omitted the proof in order not to waste space on problems not directly concerned with celestial mechanics.

# 2.14QUASI-PERIODIC FUNCTIONS AND ALMOST PERIODIC FUNCTIONS

We now consider the connection between almost periodic functions and Bohl's quasi-periodic functions (Bohr， 1925). Let $\mu _ { 1 } , \mu _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mu _ { N }$ be nonzero real numbers.There is a segment of length $L$ containing in it the solution $t$ of $N$ Diophantine inequalities (Minkowski, 1907； Koksma, 1936)

$$
| \mu _ { i } t | < \delta \pmod { 2 \pi } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , N )
$$

in the interval $\alpha < t < \beta$ for a given $\delta > 0$ ，Suppose that $\lambda$ is a real number that cannot be written in the form $n _ { 1 } \mu _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + n _ { N } \mu _ { N }$ with integer coefficients $n _ { i }$ . Then，by Kronecker's theorem of Section 2.8, there is a value of $t$ in the segment of length $L$ that satisfies

$$
| \mu _ { i } t | < \delta { \pmod { 2 \pi } } , \qquad | \lambda t - \pi | < { \frac { \pi } { 2 } } { \pmod { 2 \pi } }
$$

for a given $\delta > 0$ . If there exists a δsuch that the value of $\tau$ satisfying

$$
| \tau \mu _ { i } | < \delta \pmod { 2 \pi } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , N )
$$

for a given $\epsilon > 0$ is the translation number of $f ( x )$ ,belonging to $\pmb \epsilon$ ,then $f ( { \pmb x } )$ is an almost periodic function with the exponents $\mu _ { 1 } , \mu _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mu _ { N }$ .Such a function is included in the class of almost periodic functions

$$
f ( x ) \sim \sum _ { n } A _ { n } e ^ { i \Lambda _ { n } x } ,
$$

with all such exponents $\Lambda _ { i }$ that can be written in the form

$$
\Lambda _ { i } = g _ { i 1 } \mu _ { 1 } + g _ { i 2 } \mu _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + g _ { i N } \mu _ { N }
$$

with integer coefficients $g _ { i j }$ .Conversely, if the exponents $\Lambda _ { i }$ of an almost periodic function are written in the form (38) with integer coefficients, then the function is multiply periodic with periods $\mu _ { 1 } , \mu _ { 2 } , \ldots , \mu _ { N }$ In particular,if there is only one term in the base,then the function is simply periodic.

Take a base $\mathbf { B } = \{ \beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } , . . . \}$ and express $\textstyle \sum _ { n } A _ { n } e ^ { i \Lambda _ { n } x }$ in the form

$$
\sum _ { n } A _ { n } \exp { [ i ( r _ { n 1 } \beta _ { 1 } x + \cdot \cdot \cdot + r _ { n } q _ { n } \beta _ { q _ { n } } x ) ] } .
$$

Write $e ^ { i \beta _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } } , e ^ { i \beta _ { 2 } x _ { 2 } } , . . .$ in place of $e ^ { i \beta _ { 1 } x } , e ^ { i \beta _ { 2 } x } , \ldots ,$ ，respectively. Let $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots ,$ be denumerably infinite linearly independent real numbers, and construct

$$
\sum _ { n } A _ { n } \exp { [ { i ( r _ { n 1 } \beta _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) } ] } e ^ { i \Lambda _ { n } x } = \sum _ { n } \mathrm { B } _ { n } ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . ) e ^ { i \Lambda _ { n } x } .
$$

If we put $x _ { 1 } = x _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = k _ { : }$ ，then this expression becomes $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { n } A _ { n } e ^ { i \Lambda _ { n } ( { \pmb x } + { \pmb k } ) } } \end{array}$ a Fourier series of an almost periodic function $f ( x + k )$

Write

$$
r _ { n 1 } \beta _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + r _ { n q _ { n } } \beta _ { q _ { n } } x _ { q _ { n } } = \theta _ { n } .
$$

According to Kronecker's theorem there exists a value of $k$ that satisfies $\left| e ^ { i \theta _ { n } } - e ^ { i \Lambda _ { n } x } \right| < \hat { \delta } ( n = 1 , 2 , . . . , N )$ for a given $\boldsymbol { N }$ and $\delta > 0$ Hence, if we choose the sequence $k _ { 1 } , k _ { 2 } , \ldots$ so that it satisfies

$$
\mathrm { l i m } e ^ { i \Lambda _ { n } k _ { n } } = e ^ { i \theta _ { n } } ,
$$

then

$$
\mathfrak { M } \{ | f ( x + k _ { p } ) - f ( x + k _ { q } ) | ^ { 2 } \}
$$

converges to zero for $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ ， $q \to \infty$ .Hence, $f ( x + k _ { 1 } ) , f ( x + k _ { 2 } ) , . . .$ converge to the limit function $\phi ( x )$ uniformly for all values of $_ x$ Since

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \mathfrak { M } \{ \phi ( x ) e ^ { - i \lambda x } \} = \underset { m \to \infty } { \operatorname* { l i m } } \mathfrak { M } \{ f ( x + k _ { m } ) e ^ { - i \lambda x } \} , } & { } \\ { = \underset { m \to \infty } { \operatorname* { l i m } } A _ { n } e ^ { i \Delta _ { n } k _ { m } } = \underset { 0 } { \binom { A _ { n } e ^ { i \theta _ { n } } } { \Delta } } } & { \mathrm { ~ } ( \lambda \ne \Lambda _ { n } ) , } \end{array}
$$

therefore, the function $\phi ( x )$ has (39)as its Fourier series.Hence, (39) is the Fourier series of an almost periodic function ${ \phi } ( \pmb { x } ) = F ( \pmb { x } _ { 1 } , \pmb { x } _ { 2 } , \ldots ) .$ ， whatever we may choose for $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \dotsc$ It can be shown that this function $F ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \dots )$ is uniformly continuous in the space of denumerable dimensions with coordinates $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \dotsc$

If a function $G ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \dots )$ of a denumerable number of variables is uniformly approximated by a continuous multiply periodic function of as many number of variables, that is,if there exists for a given $\epsilon > 0$ a continuous multiply periodic function $P ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots )$ that satisfies

$$
| G ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots ) - P ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots ) | < \epsilon
$$

in the whole space, then $G ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots )$ is called a limit periodic function.

A limit periodic function is uniformly continuous in the whole space, similarly to the function $P ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots )$ . Let

$$
x _ { 1 } = c _ { 1 } x + d _ { 1 } , \qquad x _ { 2 } = c _ { 2 } x + d _ { 2 } , . . .
$$

in $G ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots )$ ; then on the straight line the function

$$
h ( \pmb { x } ) = G ( c _ { 1 } \pmb { x } + d _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } \pmb { x } + d _ { 2 } , . . . )
$$

is almost periodic. Indeed, as the function (40) is uniformly continuous, we have, for

$$
| x _ { m } ^ { \prime } - x _ { m } ^ { \prime \prime } | < \delta \quad ( \mathrm { m o d } r _ { q _ { m } } ) \qquad ( m = 1 , 2 , . . . , N )
$$

with a rational number $r _ { \perp }$ , the inequality

$$
| G ( x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , . . . ) - G ( x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime \prime } , x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } , . . . ) | < \epsilon .
$$

But we know from Kronecker's theorem of Section 2.8 that

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { { | [ c _ { m } ( x + \tau ) - d _ { m } ] - [ c _ { m } x + d _ { m } ] | = | c _ { m } \tau | < \delta } } & { { ( \mathrm { m o d } \ r _ { q _ { m } } ) } } \\ { { } } & { { ( m = 1 , 2 , . . . , l } } \end{array}
$$

where $\pmb { \tau }$ is the translation number. We can also prove the existence of the mean value of $G ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \dots )$ ：

$$
\mathfrak { M } _ { ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \cdot \cdot \cdot ) } \{ G ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \cdot \cdot \cdot ) \} ;
$$

also the approximation theorem that the limit periodic function $G ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots )$ can be as nearly as possible approximated by the fnite sum of Fourier series terms by taking $m$ sufficiently large, that is,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { G ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots ) \sim \displaystyle \sum _ { n _ { 1 } , n _ { 2 } , \ldots , n _ { N } } c _ { n _ { 1 } n _ { 2 } \ldots n _ { N } } \exp { [ i ( n _ { 1 } \beta _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + n _ { N } \beta _ { N } x _ { N } ) ] } , } } \\ { { \medskip c _ { n _ { 1 } n _ { 2 } . . . n _ { N } } = \displaystyle \operatorname* { s u p } _ { ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \cdot \cdot \cdot ) } \{ G ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots ) \exp { [ - i ( n _ { 1 } \beta _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + n _ { N } \beta _ { N } x _ { N } ) ] } , } } \\ { { \medskip \beta _ { \mathrm { i } } = \displaystyle \frac { 2 \pi } { q _ { i } } . } } \end{array}
$$

This is an extension of Weierstrass's theorem of Section 2.6. In particular, in the case of multiply periodic functions the mean value is replaced by

$$
\frac { 1 } { \beta _ { \ i } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \ d p _ { i } } P ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots ) \ d x _ { i } ;
$$

this forms the foundation of Schuster's periodogram analysis (Brunt, 1917; Stumpff, l937),which has been often employed in astronomy for discovering the periodicities of the sunspot cycles, of the latitude variation, and of solar-terrestrial phenomena.

Thus the relation between a multiply periodic function and an almost periodic function has been generalized to the relation between a limit periodic function and an almost periodic function.

If we can extract a sequence that uniformly converges to a limit periodic function in $- \infty < x < + \infty$ from a sequence

$$
f ( x + h _ { 1 } ) , f ( x + h _ { 2 } ) , . . . , f ( x + h _ { n } ) , . . .
$$

constructed by a continuous function $f ( x )$ in $- \infty < x < + \infty$ and a sequence of arbitrary real numbers $h _ { 1 } , h _ { 2 } , \ldots$ ，then $f ( x )$ is called a normal function according to Bochner (1925,1927, 1933；Favard, 1928, 1933).Bochner called the limit function a super-function (Uberfunktion). An almost periodic function is a normal function and conversely a normal function is almost periodic.It resembles the normal family of functions of Montel (l927) in the theory of analytic functions. The idea of normal functions is useful in the theory of linear differential equations with almost periodic coefficients (see Chapter l2). Such types of equations are generalizations of the differential equations appearing in celestial mechanics.

When a sequence of almost periodic functions

$$
f ( x + k ) \qquad ( - \infty < k < + \infty ) ,
$$

contains its limit function,it is called a closed set offunctions (abgeschlossene Hulle) according to Bohr (1926),and written $H \{ f ( x + k ) \}$ $\operatorname { I f } g ( x )$ belongs to the closed set $H \{ f ( x + k ) \}$ , then it can be shown that $H \{ f ( x + k ) \}$ and $H \{ g ( x + k ) \}$ are identical. If

$$
f ( x ) \sim \sum _ { n } A _ { n } e ^ { i \Lambda _ { n } x } ,
$$

then any function $\phi ( x )$ in $H$ is written

$$
\phi ( x ) \sim \sum _ { n } A _ { n } e ^ { i \Theta _ { n } } e ^ { i \Lambda _ { n } x } .
$$

In order that this Fourier series shall determine a function belonging to the closed set of functions $H \{ f ( { \pmb x } + { \pmb k } ) \}$ , it is necessary and sufficient that the points $( \Theta _ { 1 } , \Theta _ { 2 } , \ldots )$ $( { \bmod { 2 \pi } } )$ in a space of a denumerable number of dimensions should be the accumulation points of the point set formed of the points $( \Lambda _ { 1 } k , \Lambda _ { 2 } k , . . . )$ , that is, that we should be able to determine $k _ { 1 } , k _ { 2 } , \ldots$ such that the Diophantine equations

$$
\operatorname* { l i m } _ { n \to \infty } \Lambda _ { n } k _ { m } = \Theta _ { n } { \pmod { 2 \pi } } \qquad ( m = 1 , 2 , \ldots )
$$

are satisfied.

An almost periodic function,which can be written in the form

$$
\sum _ { n } A _ { n } \exp { [ i ( r _ { n 1 } \beta _ { 1 } k _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + r _ { n q _ { n } } \beta _ { q _ { n } } k _ { q _ { n } } ) ] } e ^ { i \Lambda _ { n } x }
$$

with any sequence of variables $k _ { 1 } , k _ { 2 } , \ldots$ and of rational numbers $r _ { i j }$ is said to belong to the closed set of functions $K \{ f ( x ) \}$ .The closed set of functions $\kappa$ depends only $\mathsf { o n f } ( x )$ but does not depend on any particular value of the base $\{ \beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } , . . . . \}$ .The corresponding limit periodic functions satisfy in the whole space of a denumerable number of dimensions the equations

$$
G ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . ) = F ( x _ { 1 } + k _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } + k _ { 2 } , . . . ) ;
$$

and the closed set of functions $\kappa$ consists of all functions written in the form $g ( x ) = F ( x + k _ { 1 } , x + k _ { 2 } , . . . )$ . If these two closed sets of functions $\kappa$ and $H$ have a function $f ( { \pmb x } )$ in common, then $\pmb { K }$ is contained in $H .$ and $H$ is a closed set of functions of $\kappa$ . In order that $\cal { K } \{ f ( x ) \}$ and $H \{ f ( { \pmb x } + { \pmb k } ) \}$ be coincident,it is necessary and sufficient that $\Lambda _ { n }$ should be the base of integer coefficients, that is,all $\Lambda _ { n }$ should be expressed by linear homogeneous equations with integer coefficients with respect to the base $\{ \beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } , . . . . \}$ Thus $\kappa$ is the closed set of almost periodic functions and $\boldsymbol { H }$ is the closed set of more general almost periodic functions. In this way the relation between a quasi-periodic function and an almost periodic function becomes clearer.

Bochner founded the theory of almost periodic functions on his normal functions. The idea of almost periodic functions has been generalized by Stepanoff-to square summable functions-and amended byBesicovitch. Weyl discussd almost periodic functions on the basis of group theory. Weare concerned with uniformly almost periodic functions,as has been studied by Bohr and Bochner (Bohr,1925 and l932；Bochner,1927; Besicovitch,1932；Maak,1950).

# RELATIVISTIC ONE-BODY PROBLEM

# 2.15TRANSFORMATION OF THE PROBLEM

The relativistic one-body problem,that is, the motion of a particle with an infinitesimal mass in the relativistic gravitational field of Schwarzschild,can be solved as an example of quasi-periodic motions. The relativistic many-body problem,especially the two-body problem, can be approximately treated as a one-body problem by taking the center of mass as origin and replacing the mass in the one-body problem by the sum of the two masses,as has been shown by Eddington and Clark (1938)，Einstein,Infeld,and Hoffmann (l938)，Einstein and Infeld (1940),and Robertson (l938).Hence, the behavior of the motion can be outlined by solving the one-body problem.The following treatment is due to the author (Hagihara, 1931).

In order to apply the theory of Hamilton-Jacobi to this problem we consider the following auxiliary

Theorem: The variational principle

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle { \delta \int d s = 0 , } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
d s ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { \alpha , \beta } g _ { \alpha \beta } d x _ { \alpha } d x _ { \beta } \qquad ( \alpha , \beta = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

can be replaced by

$$
\ S \int T d \sigma = 0 ,
$$

where

$$
T = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { \alpha , \beta } g _ { \alpha \beta } \frac { d x _ { \alpha } } { d \sigma } \frac { d x _ { \beta } } { d \sigma } \qquad ( \alpha , \beta = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Here $x _ { i }$ are at first supposed to be all independent; then the constant $C ^ { 2 }$ is determined by

$$
\sum _ { \alpha , \beta } g _ { \alpha \beta } { \frac { d x _ { \alpha } } { d \sigma } } { \frac { d x _ { \beta } } { d \sigma } } = C ^ { 2 } \qquad ( \alpha , \beta = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

as a function of the integration constants in the second variational statement (45); finally $\pmb { \sigma }$ is eliminated by

$$
s = C \sigma .
$$

Since (46) is the expression for twice the kinetic energy if $\pmb { \sigma }$ is replaced by $t ,$ the motion of the dynamical system according to the second variational statement (45) is given by a Hamilton-Jacobi equation of the form

$$
\frac { \partial W } { \partial \boldsymbol { \sigma } } + \ \Theta \Big ( x _ { i } , \frac { \partial W } { \partial x _ { i } } \Big ) = 0 ,
$$

where the momenta are defined by

$$
{ \frac { \partial T ( x _ { i } , d x _ { i } / d \sigma ) } { \partial ( d x _ { j } / d \sigma ) } } = \rlap { / } p _ { j } \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

The characteristic of Cauchy for this equation,which will be defined in Chapter 4, is determined by

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { \partial \Theta / \partial p _ { i } } = \frac { - d p _ { j } } { \partial \Theta / \partial x _ { j } + ( \partial \Theta / \partial p _ { j } ) p _ { j } } = \frac { d W } { \sum _ { k } ( \partial \Theta / \partial p _ { k } ) p _ { k } } = d \sigma
$$

$$
( i , j , k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

or by the Hamiltonian canonical equations

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d \sigma } = \frac { \partial \Theta } { \partial p _ { i } } , \frac { d p _ { i } } { d \sigma } = - \frac { \partial \Theta } { \partial x _ { i } } ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

In particular, if $_ { T }$ is in the form (46),then the Hamilton-Jacobi equation (49） becomes

$$
{ \frac { \partial W } { \partial \sigma } } + { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \sum _ { \alpha , \beta } g ^ { \alpha \beta } { \frac { \partial W } { \partial x _ { \alpha } } } { \frac { \partial W } { \partial x _ { \beta } } } = 0 \qquad ( \alpha , \beta = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

and the Cauchy characteristic is

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } x _ { i } } { d \sigma ^ { 2 } } + \sum _ { \alpha , \beta } \left\{ ^ { \alpha } { } _ { i } ^ { \beta } \right\} \frac { d x _ { \alpha } } { d \sigma } \frac { d x _ { \beta } } { d \sigma } = 0 ~ ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

where $g ^ { \alpha \beta }$ is contragradient to $g _ { \alpha \beta }$ and the determinant $\| g _ { \alpha \beta } \|$ is assumed :o be nonzero. The Christoffel symbol $\{ \alpha _ { i } \beta \}$ is defined in Section 1.4.

Equation (48) is now written

$$
2 T - C ^ { 2 } = 0 .
$$

When there is a constraint expressed by (51), the variational problem is further replaced (Bolza,1909) by

$$
\delta \int F d \sigma = 0 ,
$$

with

$$
F = T + \lambda ( 2 T - C ^ { 2 } ) = ( 1 + 2 \lambda ) T - \lambda C ^ { 2 } ,
$$

where $\lambda$ is the Lagrangian indeterminate multiplier. The corresponding Euler-Lagrange equation is

$$
\frac { \partial T } { \partial x _ { i } } - \frac { d } { d \sigma } \left[ \frac { \partial T } { \partial ( d x _ { i } / d \sigma ) } \right] = 0 ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

with the restriction (51). Equation (52) admits an integral:

$$
T - \sum _ { i } { \frac { \partial T } { \partial ( d x _ { i } / d \sigma ) } } { \frac { d x _ { i } } { d \sigma } } = { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t . } }
$$

Since $_ { T }$ is a homogeneous quadratic with respect to $d x _ { i } / d \sigma$ ， $_ { T }$ is constant. If we put this constant equal to $C ^ { 2 } / 2$ ,then (47) is satisfied.On the other hand,the solution of (43) is known to be (5O).Hence, the auxiliary theorem has been proved. Thus we need only consider the second variational principle (45),instead of the first one (43),and solve the Hamilton-Jacobi partial differential equation (49) (Synge,l928;Infeld,1931).

For the relativistic trajectory of a light ray we take $d s = 0$ ; that is, $C ^ { 2 } = 0$ in the preceding formulas.

# 2.16RELATIVISTIC FIELD OF SCHWARZSCHILD

We take a particle or the center of a spherically symmetrical celestial body as origin and adopt the time and space as measured by an observer located at the origin.Then the relativistic gravitational field for the

one-body problem obtained by Schwarzschild (Schwarzschild， 1916; Weyl,1919,1919a; Einstein,1914,1915,1916,19i6a; de Donder,1930; Haag,1931） is

$$
d s ^ { 2 } = c _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ( 1 - \alpha / r ) d t ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 1 - \alpha / r } d r ^ { 2 } - r ^ { 2 } d \phi ^ { 2 } - r ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi d \theta ^ { 2 } ,
$$

where $r , \phi , \theta$ are the polar coordinates for the observer, $c _ { 0 }$ is the light velocity in vacuum at infinity,and $m = \alpha / 2$ is the mass of the body measured at an infinite distance in the coordinate system in which the particle is at rest.

Combining equations (49) and (53), we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle \frac { \partial W } { \partial \sigma } + \frac { 1 } { 2 c _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ( 1 - \alpha / r ) } \left( \frac { \partial W } { \partial t } \right) ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( 1 - \frac { \alpha } { r } \right) \left( \frac { \partial W } { \partial r } \right) ^ { 2 } } \\ { - \frac { 1 } { 2 r ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } \left( \frac { \partial W } { \partial \theta } \right) ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 r ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { \partial W } { \partial \phi } \right) ^ { 2 } = 0 , } \end{array}
$$

and the Cauchy characteristic ofthis equation is expresed by

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { \partial \psi } { \partial z } = - ( 1 - \omega ) \frac { \partial \hat { W } } { \partial z } , } \\ & { \frac { \partial \hat { W } } { \partial z } = - \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial \hat { W } } { \partial z } , } \\ & { \frac { \partial \hat { W } } { \partial z } = - \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } , } \\ & { \frac { \partial \hat { W } } { \partial z } = - \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } \frac { \partial \hat { W } } { \partial \theta } , } \\ & { \frac { \partial \hat { W } } { \partial z } \left( \frac { \partial \hat { W } } { \partial z } \right) = 0 , } \\ & { \frac { \partial } { \partial z } \left( \frac { \partial \hat { W } } { \partial z } \right) = \frac { \mathrm { e r f } ^ { 2 } } { 2 r \left( \frac { \partial \hat { W } } { \partial z } \right) ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { \partial \hat { W } } { \partial t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \frac { c } { 2 r ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { \partial \hat { W } } { \partial z } \right) ^ { 2 } } \\ & { - \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 3 } } \left( \frac { \partial \hat { W } } { \partial z } \right) ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 3 } \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } \frac { \left( \hat { W } \right) ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 3 } } , } \\ & { \frac { \partial } { \partial z } \left( \frac { \partial \hat { W } } { \partial z } \right) _ { \gamma = - \mathrm { e x c o s } } - \frac { c \sin \theta } { r ^ { 3 } } \frac { \partial \hat { W } } { \partial z } . } \end{array}
$$$$
\frac { d } { d \sigma } \left( \frac { \partial { \cal W } } { \partial \phi } \right) = - \frac { \cos \phi } { r ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 3 } \phi } \left( \frac { \partial { \cal W } } { \partial \theta } \right) ^ { 2 } ,
$$

$$
{ \frac { d } { d \sigma } } \left( { \frac { \partial W } { \partial \theta } } \right) = 0 .
$$

The variables $\theta$ and $t$ are ignorable coordinates and we can write

$$
W = - K \sigma + f t + g \theta + V ( r , \phi )
$$

with the integration constants $K , f , g .$ The Hamilton-Jacobi equation for $V ( r , \phi )$ is

$$
\frac { \ d \ V } { \ d z } + \frac { f ^ { 2 } } { 2 c _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ( 1 - \alpha / r ) } - \frac { g ^ { 2 } } { 2 r ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( 1 - \frac { \alpha } { r } \right) \left( \frac { \partial V } { \partial r } \right) ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 r ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { \partial V } { \partial \phi } \right) ^ { 2 } = 0
$$

and the Cauchy characteristic is

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { d r } { d \sigma } = - \left( 1 - \frac { \alpha } { r } \right) \frac { \partial V } { \partial r } , } \\ & { \frac { d \phi } { d \sigma } = - \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial V } { \partial \phi } , } \\ & { \frac { d } { d \sigma } \left( \frac { \partial V } { \partial r } \right) = \frac { ( \alpha / r ^ { 2 } ) f ^ { 2 } } { 2 c _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ( 1 - \alpha / r ) ^ { 2 } } - \frac { g ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 3 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } + \frac { \alpha } { 2 r ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { \partial V } { \partial r } \right) ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 3 } } \left( \frac { \partial V } { \partial \phi } \right) ^ { 2 } , } \\ & { \frac { d } { d \sigma } \left( \frac { \partial V } { \partial \phi } \right) = - \frac { \cos \phi } { r ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 3 } \phi } g ^ { 2 } . } \end{array}
$$

To obtain $V$ we can put $g = 0$ ,because we know that there exists an integral $\theta =$ constant and that the trajectory is always on the plane $\theta =$ constant, which we take as the plane $\theta = 0$ Then, from (55),

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { r ^ { 2 } { \frac { d \phi } { d \sigma } } = A C , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { \frac { d } { d \sigma } \left( { \frac { d r / d \sigma } { 1 ~ - ~ \alpha / r } } \right) = { \frac { - ( \alpha / r ^ { 2 } ) f ^ { 2 } } { 2 c _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ( 1 ~ - ~ \alpha / r ) ^ { 2 } } } - { \frac { \alpha } { 2 r ^ { 2 } } } { \frac { ( d r / d \sigma ) ^ { 2 } } { ( 1 ~ - ~ \alpha / r ) ^ { 2 } } } + { \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 3 } } } A ^ { 2 } C ^ { 2 } , } } } \end{array}
$$

and, from (54b),

$$
\frac { d t } { d r } = \frac { f } { c _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ( 1 - \alpha / r ) } ,
$$

where $A$ is an integration constant. From (48), (53), (56), and (57),

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \left( \frac { d r } { d s } \right) ^ { 2 } = - \left( 1 - \frac { \alpha } { r } \right) \left( 1 + \frac { A } { r ^ { 2 } } \right) + \frac { f ^ { 2 } } { c _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } C ^ { 2 } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle r ^ { 2 } \frac { d \phi } { d s } = A , } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\left( { \frac { A } { r ^ { 2 } } } { \frac { d r } { d \phi } } \right) ^ { 2 } + { \frac { A ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } } = { \frac { f ^ { 2 } } { c _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } C ^ { 2 } } } - 1 + { \frac { \alpha } { r } } + { \frac { \alpha } { r } } { \frac { A ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } } \cdotp
$$

For a light ray we put $A = \infty$ in these formulas. Equations (56) and (57）are of the same form as those usually obtained in relativistic mechanics.If we put $\begin{array} { r } { f / ( c _ { 0 } C ) = c _ { : } } \end{array}$ ,then the formulas reduce to Eddington's (Eddington,1923;cf. Einstein, 1915,1916; de Sitter,1916,1917).

The above deduction fails for $\| g _ { \alpha \beta } \| = 0 .$ .The value of the determinant is $- c _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } r ^ { 4 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi .$ It vanishes when $\phi = 0$ or $\pi$ . This difficulty can easily be avoided by taking suitable coordinate axes.Except in the rare case when the two bodies collide in a finite time, $\boldsymbol { r }$ never becomes zero.Hence, we can suppose that the determinant never vanishes.

# 2.17THE COMPLETE INTEGRAL

We start with (54a) and, by puting

$$
W = - K \sigma + V _ { 1 } ( t ) + V _ { 2 } ( r ) + V _ { 3 } ( \phi ) + V _ { 4 } ( \theta ) ,
$$

follow the method of the separation of variables treated in Section 1.16. Then, with the new integration constants $h _ { 1 } , h _ { 2 } , h _ { 3 }$ ，

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { V _ { 1 } = c _ { 0 } h _ { 3 } t , } } \\ { { V _ { 2 } = \displaystyle \int \sqrt { \frac { 2 } { 1 - \alpha / r } \Big [ 1 - \frac { h _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { 2 r ^ { 2 } } + \frac { h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } { 2 ( 1 - \alpha / r ) } - K \Big ] } \ d r , } } \\ { { V _ { 3 } = \displaystyle \int \sqrt { h _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - \frac { h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } { \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } } \ d \phi , } } \\ { { V _ { 4 } = \displaystyle - h _ { 2 } \theta . } } \end{array}
$$

If the canonically conjugate constants $\beta _ { 0 } , \beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 2 } , \beta _ { 3 }$ are put in place of $K , h _ { 1 } , h _ { 2 } , h _ { 3 } ,$ then the solution,by the method ofJacobi (Section 1.10), is

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l } & { \displaystyle \frac { \partial W } { \partial K } = \beta _ { 0 } , \quad } & { y _ { 0 } = \frac { \partial W } { \partial x _ { 0 } } , } \\ & { \displaystyle \frac { \partial W } { \partial h _ { i } } = \beta _ { i } , \quad } & { y _ { i } = \frac { \partial W } { \partial x _ { i } } , \quad } & { ( i = 1 , 2 , 3 ) , } \end{array}
$$

or, by the auxiliary theorem of Section 2.15,

$$
1 = c _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } \Big ( 1 - { \frac { \alpha } { r } } \Big ) \Big ( { \frac { d t } { d \sigma } } \Big ) - { \frac { 1 } { 1 - \alpha / r } } \Big ( { \frac { d r } { d \sigma } } \Big ) ^ { 2 } - r ^ { 2 } \Big ( { \frac { d \phi } { d \sigma } } \Big ) ^ { 2 } - r ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi \Big ( { \frac { d \theta } { d \sigma } } \Big ) ^ { 2 } .
$$

Hence,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \beta _ { 0 } = - \sigma - \int \frac { d r } { \sqrt { R ( r ) } } , } \\ { \displaystyle \beta _ { 1 } = - \int \frac { h _ { 1 } d r } { r ^ { 2 } \sqrt { R ( r ) } } + \int \frac { h _ { 1 } } { \sqrt { \Phi ( \phi ) } } d \phi , } \\ { \displaystyle \beta _ { 2 } = - \int \frac { h _ { 2 } d \phi } { \sin ^ { 2 } \phi \sqrt { \Phi ( \phi ) } } - \theta , } \\ { \displaystyle \beta _ { 3 } = \int \frac { h _ { 1 } r d r } { ( r - \alpha ) \sqrt { R ( r ) } } + c _ { 0 } t , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
R ( r ) = ( h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - 2 K ) + \frac { 2 \alpha K } { r } - \frac { h _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \alpha h _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 3 } } ,
$$

and

$$
\Phi ( \phi ) = h _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } - \frac { h _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } \cdot
$$

Let

$$
u = \frac { \alpha } { r } , ~ U ( u ) = \frac { \alpha ^ { 2 } ( h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - 2 K ) } { h _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 2 K \alpha ^ { 2 } } { h _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } u - u ^ { 2 } + u ^ { 3 } ;
$$

then

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \quad \frac { h _ { 1 } d \sigma } { \alpha ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d u } { u ^ { 2 } \sqrt { U ( u ) } } , } \\ & { \quad \quad \frac { h _ { 1 } d \phi } { \sqrt { \Phi ( \phi ) } } = - \frac { d u } { \sqrt { U ( u ) } } , } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad d \theta = - \frac { h _ { 2 } } { \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } \frac { d \phi } { \sqrt { \Phi ( \phi ) } } , } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad c _ { 0 } d t = \frac { \alpha ^ { 2 } h _ { 3 } } { h _ { 1 } } \frac { d u } { ( 1 - u ) u ^ { 2 } \sqrt { U ( u ) } } } \end{array}
$$

and our condition takes the form

$$
d \sigma ^ { 2 } = c _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ( 1 - u ) d t ^ { 2 } + { \frac { \alpha ^ { 2 } } { u ^ { 2 } } } d \phi ^ { 2 } + { \frac { \alpha ^ { 2 } } { u ^ { 2 } } } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi d \theta ^ { 2 } + { \frac { 1 } { 1 - u } } { \frac { \alpha ^ { 2 } } { u ^ { 4 } } } d u ^ { 2 } = 0 ,
$$

or, $\begin{array} { r } { K = \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \end{array}$ . Hence, $\pmb { \sigma }$ is determined by

$$
d \sigma ^ { 2 } = c _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ( 1 - \alpha / r ) d t ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 1 - \alpha / r } d r ^ { 2 } - r ^ { 2 } d \phi ^ { 2 } - r ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi d \theta ^ { 2 } .
$$

Thus we write $s$ in place of $\sigma$ and take $s = 0$ for $\sigma = 0$ .Now

$$
U ( u ) = u ^ { 3 } - u ^ { 2 } + \frac { \alpha ^ { 2 } } { h _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } u + \frac { \alpha ^ { 2 } ( h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - 1 ) } { h _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } .
$$

For a light ray the trajectory is determined by $d s = 0$ ,or by $\kappa = 0$ In the above treatment, $\begin{array} { r } { K = \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \end{array}$ . Thus the two cases are contradictory. Hence the trajectory of a light ray nowhere coincides with the trajectory of a particle of infinitesimal mass.

Now let cos $I = h _ { 2 } / h _ { 1 ; }$ where $\boldsymbol { I }$ denotes the inclination of the orbital plane with reference to the coordinate plane.Let cos $\phi = \sin I \sin \psi ;$ where $\phi$ denotes the colatitude and $\psi$ the argument of latitude. Since

$$
\frac { d \phi } { \sqrt { \Phi ( \phi ) } } = - \frac { d { \psi } } { h _ { 1 } } ,
$$

therefore,

$$
\frac { d u } { \sqrt { U ( u ) } } = d \psi .
$$

We transform the third equation of (65) into the form

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } & { d \theta = { \frac { \cos I } { \cos ^ { 2 } \phi } } d \psi = { \frac { \cos I } { 2 } } \left( { \frac { d \psi } { 1 \ - \sin I \sin \psi } } + { \frac { d \psi } { 1 \ + \sin I \sin \psi } } \right) } \\ & { \quad = d \cdot \tan ^ { - 1 } \ ( \tan \psi \cos I ) . } \end{array} }
$$

That is,

$$
\tan \left( \theta + \beta _ { 2 } \right) = \tan \psi \cos I .
$$

This is a fundamental formula in spherical trigonometry; $\theta + \beta _ { 2 }$ denotes the longitude counted from the ascending node of the orbit.We obtain finally

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { \displaystyle h _ { 1 } } { \displaystyle \alpha ^ { 2 } } d s = \frac { d u } { u ^ { 2 } \sqrt { U ( u ) } } , } \\ & { \quad \quad 0 = \frac { d u } { \displaystyle \sqrt { U ( u ) } } - d \psi , } \\ & { \quad \quad 0 = \frac { d u } { \displaystyle ( 1 - u ) u ^ { 2 } \sqrt { U ( u ) } } - \frac { h _ { 1 } c _ { 0 } } { \displaystyle \alpha ^ { 2 } h _ { 3 } } d t . } \end{array}
$$

This is a special case of the inversion problem in the theory of algebraic functions.The second formula of (66) is the Abelian integral of the frst kind,and the first and the third formulas are the Abelian integrals of the third kind; they have logarithmic singularities (see Section 2.20).

# 2.18SOLUTION BY ELLIPTIC FUNCTIONS

Denote the three roots of $U ( u ) = 0$ by $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , u _ { 3 }$

$$
u _ { 1 } + u _ { 2 } + u _ { 3 } = 1 ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { u _ { 2 } u _ { 3 } + u _ { 3 } u _ { 1 } + u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } = \frac { \alpha ^ { 2 } } { h _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } u _ { 3 } = - \frac { \alpha ^ { 2 } ( h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - 1 ) } { h _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } . } } \end{array}
$$

Let

$$
x = u - \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } , \qquad e _ { i } = u _ { i } - \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , 3 ) ;
$$

then

$$
d \psi = { \frac { d x } { \sqrt { ( x - e _ { 1 } ) ( x - e _ { 2 } ) ( x - e _ { 3 } ) } } } \cdotp
$$

Using Weierstrass's $\wp$ function gives (Appell and Lacour, 1922)

$$
x = \wp \left( { \frac { \psi + \beta _ { 1 } } { 2 } } \right) , \qquad u = { \frac { \alpha } { r } } = \wp \left( { \frac { \psi + \beta _ { 1 } } { 2 } } \right) + { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } .
$$

Hence,(66） becomes

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { \hat { h } _ { 1 } } { \alpha ^ { 2 } } d s = \frac { d \psi } { \left[ \wp \left( \frac { \psi } { \sqrt { 2 } } + \hat { \beta } _ { 1 } \right) + \frac { 1 } { 3 } \right] ^ { 2 } } \cdot } \\ & { \quad = \frac { d x } { ( x + \frac { 1 } { 3 } ) ^ { 2 } \sqrt { ( x - \varepsilon _ { 1 } ) ( x - \varepsilon _ { 2 } ) ( x - \varepsilon _ { 3 } ) } } , } \\ & { \frac { \hat { h } _ { 1 } \varepsilon _ { 0 } } { \alpha ^ { 2 } \hat { h } _ { 3 } } d t = \frac { d \psi } { \left[ \frac { 2 } { 3 } - \wp \left( \frac { \psi } { \sqrt { 3 } } + \hat { \beta } _ { 1 } \right) \right] \left[ \wp \left( \frac { \psi } { \sqrt { 2 } } + \hat { \beta } _ { 1 } \right) + \frac { 1 } { 3 } \right] ^ { 2 } } } \\ & { \quad = \frac { d x } { ( \frac { 2 } { 3 } - x ) ( x + \frac { 1 } { 3 } ) ^ { 2 } \sqrt { ( x - \varepsilon _ { 1 } ) ( x - \varepsilon _ { 2 } ) ( x - \varepsilon _ { 3 } ) } } . } \end{array}
$$

From the formula of eliptic functions we have

$$
\zeta ( x + y ) - \zeta ( x - y ) - 2 \zeta ( y ) = - \frac { \wp ^ { \prime } ( y ) } { \wp ( x ) - \wp ( y ) } .
$$

Differentiation gives

$$
\wp ( x + y ) + \wp ( x - y ) - 2 \wp ( y ) = { \frac { [ \wp ^ { \prime } ( y ) ] ^ { 2 } } { [ \wp ( x ) - \wp ( y ) ] ^ { 2 } } } + { \frac { \wp ^ { \prime \prime } ( y ) } { \wp ( x ) - \wp ( y ) } }
$$

Write $\wp ( y ) = - \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 3 } }$ and denote the invariants of eliptic functions by $g _ { 2 }$ $g _ { 3 } , \Delta$ ; i.e.,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { g _ { 2 } = - 4 ( e _ { 1 } e _ { 2 } + e _ { 2 } e _ { 3 } + e _ { 3 } e _ { 1 } ) = 4 \Big ( \displaystyle \frac 1 3 - \frac { \alpha ^ { 2 } } { h _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } \Big ) , } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { g _ { 3 } = 4 e _ { 1 } e _ { 2 } e _ { 3 } = 4 \Big ( \displaystyle \frac 2 { 2 7 } + \frac 2 3 \frac { \alpha ^ { 2 } } { h _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } - \frac { \alpha ^ { 2 } h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } { h _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } \Big ) , } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \Delta = g _ { 2 } ^ { 3 } - 2 7 g _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } . } } \end{array}
$$

Then

$$
\begin{array} { c l c r } { { \dot { \wp } ^ { \prime } ( y ) ] ^ { 2 } = 4 [ \wp ( y ) - e _ { 1 } ] [ \wp ( y ) - e _ { 2 } ] [ \wp ( y ) - e _ { 3 } ] = \frac { 4 \alpha ^ { 2 } ( h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - 1 ) } { h _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } \\ { { \wp ^ { \prime \prime } ( y ) = 6 [ \wp ( y ) ] ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } g _ { 2 } = \frac { 2 \alpha ^ { 2 } } { h _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } . } } \end{array}
$$

Furthermore,by the formulas

$$
\frac { d } { d x } \zeta ( x ) = - \wp ( x ) , \frac { d } { d x } \log \sigma ( x ) = \zeta ( x ) ,

\begin{array} { r l } & { = \frac { 1 } { [ \theta ^ { \prime \prime } ( y ) ] ^ { 2 } } } \\ & { \qquad \times \left\{ - \zeta \Big ( \frac { \psi + \beta _ { 1 } } { 2 } + y \Big ) - \zeta \Big ( \frac { \psi + \beta _ { 1 } } { 2 } - y \Big ) - ( \psi + \beta _ { 1 } ) \wp ( y ) \right. } \\ & { \qquad \quad + \left. \frac { \theta ^ { \prime \prime } ( y ) } { \theta ^ { \prime } ( y ) } \left[ \log \frac { \sigma \Big ( \frac { \psi + \beta _ { 1 } } { 2 } + y \Big ) } { \sigma \Big ( \frac { \psi + \beta _ { 1 } } { 2 } - y \Big ) } - ( \psi + \beta _ { 1 } ) \zeta ( y ) \right] \right\} . } \end{array}

Write $\wp ( z ) = { \frac { 2 } { 3 } }$ ; this gives $[ \wp ^ { \prime } ( z ) ] ^ { 2 } = 4 \alpha ^ { 2 } h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } / h _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$ . Integrating the second equation of (67） gives
