\begin{array} { l l l } { \displaystyle \frac { { h _ { 1 } } } { \omega ^ { * } { h _ { 3 } } } \left( { { \omega , \xi + { \beta _ { 3 } } } } \right) = \frac { 1 } { \beta ^ { \prime } \left( z \right) } \Bigg \{ ( { \psi + { \beta _ { 1 } } } ) \zeta ( z ) - \log { \frac { { \sigma \left( { \frac { { \psi + { \beta _ { 1 } } } } { 2 } } + { { \beta _ { 2 } } } \right) } } { \sigma \left( { \frac { { \psi + { \beta _ { 1 } } } } { 2 } } - z \right) } } \Bigg \} } \\ { \displaystyle } \\ { \displaystyle } \\ { \displaystyle } \\ { \qquad - \frac { 1 } { \beta ^ { \prime } \left( y \right) } \Bigg \{ \left( { \psi + { \beta _ { 1 } } } \right) \zeta ( y ) - \log { \frac { { \sigma \left( { \frac { { \psi + { \beta _ { 1 } } } } { 2 } } + { { \beta _ { 1 } } } \right) } } { \sigma \left( { \frac { { \psi + { \beta _ { 1 } } } } { 2 } } - y \right) } } \Bigg \} } \\ { \displaystyle } \\ { \displaystyle } \\ { \qquad \times \left\{ 1 - \frac { { \beta ^ { \prime } \left( y \right) } } { \left[ { \beta ^ { \prime } \left( y \right) } \right] ^ { 2 } } \right\} } \\ { \displaystyle } \\ { \displaystyle } { \qquad + \frac { 1 } { \left[ \beta ^ { \prime } \left( y \right) \right] ^ { 2 } } \left\{ \zeta \left( { \frac { { \psi + { \beta _ { 1 } } } } { 2 } } + y \right) + \zeta \left( { \frac { { \psi + { \beta _ { 1 } } } } { 2 } } - y \right) \right. } \end{array}

Equations (7O)and (71) show that the first and the third equations of (66) are Abelian integrals of the third kind.

By the formulas of elliptic functions (Halphen, l886),

$$

we transform (67) and (68) into the following series form:

$$
\frac { h _ { 1 } } { 2 \alpha ^ { 2 } } [ \wp ^ { \prime } ( y ) ] ^ { 2 } ( s + \beta _ { 0 } )

$$

u - \frac { 1 } { 3 } = - \frac { \eta } { \omega } + \left( \frac { \eta } { 2 \omega } \right) \frac { 2 } { 1 - \cos \frac { \pi } { 2 \omega } \left( \psi + \beta _ { 1 } \right) }

- 2 \Bigl ( \frac { \pi } { \omega } \Bigr ) ^ { 2 } \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \frac { n q ^ { 2 n } } { 1 - q ^ { 2 n } } \cos \frac { n \pi } { 2 \omega } ( \psi + \beta _ { 1 } ) .

The radius vector, longitude, and latitude are computed from these formulas and the relations

$$
r = { \frac { \alpha } { u } } ,
$$

$$
( \theta + \beta _ { 2 } ) = \tan \psi \cos I ,

$$
\displaystyle { \cos \phi = \sin I \sin \psi } .

# 2.19DISCUSSION OF THE MOTION

From the properties of Weierstrass's $\sigma$ -functions it is evident that $s$ and $t$ have singularities at

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { \displaystyle \frac { \psi + \beta _ { 1 } } { 2 } \pm y \equiv 0 } & { ( \mathrm { m o d } 2 \omega , 2 \omega ^ { \prime } ) , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { \psi + \beta _ { 1 } } { 2 } \pm z \equiv 0 } & { ( \mathrm { m o d } 2 \omega , 2 \omega ^ { \prime } ) . } \end{array}
$$

Thus our integrals are of the third kind in Riemann's theory of algebraic functions (Section 2.20). The radius vector $r$ has singularities at $u = 0$ ， i.e.,

$$
\wp \left( \frac { \psi + \beta _ { 1 } } { 2 } \right) + \frac { 1 } { 3 } = 0 , \mathrm { o r } \wp \left( \frac { \psi + \beta _ { 1 } } { 2 } \right) = \wp ( y ) ;
$$

and these singularities coincide with (A).Hence,when the three roots $e _ { 1 } , e _ { 2 } , e _ { 3 }$ of the cubic equation $U ( u ) = 0$ are all different, these are the only singularities.

For $\Delta > 0$ the three roots are all real and $e _ { 3 } < e _ { 2 } < e _ { 1 }$ .We classify two cases:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } > 1 , \quad \mathrm { t h e n } \quad e _ { 3 } < - \frac { 1 } { 3 } < e _ { 2 } < e _ { 1 } < \frac { 2 } { 3 } , } & { } \\ { h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } < 1 , \quad \mathrm { t h e n } \quad - \frac { 1 } { 3 } < e _ { 3 } < e _ { 2 } < e _ { 1 } < \frac { 2 } { 3 } . } & { } \end{array}
$$

For $\Delta < 0$ ,only one root is real and we have two cases:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } > 1 , } & { \mathrm { t h e n } \quad e _ { 3 } < - \frac { 1 } { 3 } < \frac { 2 } { 3 } , } \\ { h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } < 1 , } & { \mathrm { t h e n } \quad - \frac { 1 } { 3 } < e _ { k } < \frac { 1 } { 3 } \qquad ( k = 1 \mathrm { o r } 3 ) . } \end{array}
$$

We suppose that $_ x$ can take any real value extending to infinity.

Case I:

$$
\wp ^ { \prime } ( y ) = { \frac { 2 \alpha } { h _ { 1 } } } \sqrt { h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - 1 } , \qquad \wp ^ { \prime } ( z ) = - { \frac { 2 \alpha h _ { 3 } } { h _ { 1 } } } , \qquad \wp ^ { \prime } ( y ) = { \frac { 2 \alpha ^ { 2 } } { h _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } } .
$$

Separate the real and imaginary parts:

$$
y = y ^ { \prime } + \omega ^ { \prime } , \qquad \beta _ { 0 } = \beta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } + i \beta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime } , \qquad \beta _ { 3 } = \beta _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } + i \beta _ { 3 } ^ { \prime \prime } .
$$

Then

$$
= \left\{ \frac { 1 } { 3 } - \frac { \eta } { \omega } - \frac { 2 \alpha \pi } { \omega h _ { 1 } \sqrt { h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - 1 } } \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \frac { q ^ { n } } { 1 - q ^ { 2 n } } \sin \frac { n \pi y ^ { \prime } } { \omega } \right\} ( \psi + \beta _ { 1 } )
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { \displaystyle - \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \left\{ \frac { 4 \pi } { \omega } \cos \frac { n \pi y ^ { \prime } } { \omega } - \frac { 4 \alpha } { h _ { 1 } \sqrt { h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - 1 } } \frac { \sin \frac { 2 \pi y ^ { \prime } } { \omega } } { n } \right\} } & { } & \\ { \displaystyle } & { } & { \times \frac { q ^ { n } } { 1 - q ^ { 2 n } } \sin \frac { n \pi } { 2 \omega } ( \psi + \beta _ { 1 } ) , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \quad - \displaystyle \{ \frac { 1 } { k _ { \delta } } \left[ \zeta ^ { ( 2 ) } - \frac { \bar { \nu } _ { \xi } ^ { 2 } } { 8 \omega } \right] - \frac { 2 \mathrm { R e } - 3 \mathrm { \Lambda } ^ { 3 } } { 2 \mathrm { R e } - 1 1 \mathrm { R e } ^ { 3 \omega } } \frac { 2 \pi \left( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \bar { \nu } _ { \xi } ^ { ( 4 ) } } { 4 \omega ^ { 2 } } + \mathrm { i } \frac { 8 \pi \mathrm { R e } ^ { 3 \bar { \nu } _ { \xi } } } { 4 \omega } \right) } { \cos { - \frac { \bar { \nu } _ { \xi } ^ { ( 4 ) } } { 8 \omega } } } } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \\ & & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \frac { \bar { \nu } _ { \xi } } { 2 \omega } \left( \frac { \bar { \nu } _ { \xi } } { 8 \omega } - 1 \right) \left( \frac { \bar { \nu } _ { \xi } } { 8 \omega } - \frac { \bar { \nu } _ { \xi } ^ { ( 4 ) } } { 8 \omega } \right) \left( \frac { \bar { \nu } _ { \xi } } { 8 \omega } + \frac { \bar { \nu } _ { \xi } ^ { ( 4 ) } } { 8 \omega } \right) } \\ &  \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad + \frac { 1 } { k _ { \delta } } \omega \frac { \mathrm { i n i n } \frac { \mathrm { R e } } { 2 \omega } } { 4 \omega } \frac { \left( \bar { \nu } _ { \xi } + \frac { \bar { \nu } _ { \xi } } { 2 \omega } + \frac { \bar { \nu } _ { \xi } ^ { ( 4 ) } } { 2 \omega } - \frac { \bar { \nu } _ { \xi } ^ { ( 4 ) } } { 4 \omega } \right) } { \cos { \frac { \bar { \nu } _ { \xi } ^ { ( 4 ) } } { 4 \omega } } } \\ &  \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \end{array}
$$

$$
u - \frac { 1 } { 3 } = - \frac { \eta } { \omega } + \left( \frac { \pi } { 2 \omega } \right) ^ { 2 } \frac { 2 } { 1 - \cos { \frac { \pi } { 2 \omega } \left( \psi + \beta _ { 1 } \right) } }
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { - \displaystyle 2 \left( \frac { \pi } { \omega } \right) ^ { 2 } \displaystyle \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \frac { n q ^ { 2 n } } { 1 - q ^ { 2 n } } \cos \frac { n \pi } { \omega } \left( \psi + \beta _ { 1 } \right) , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { 2 ( h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - 1 ) } { h _ { 1 } } \beta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \dot { \imath } = \displaystyle \frac { \alpha \pi } { h _ { 1 } \sqrt { h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - 1 } } \dot { \imath } } , }  \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { 2 } { \alpha h _ { 3 } } \beta _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } \dot { \imath } = \displaystyle - \frac { 2 h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - 3 } { 2 ( h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - 1 ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \pi \dot { \imath } . } }  \end{array}
$$

The series for $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ has no singularity, but the series for $\boldsymbol { u }$ does have singularities corresponding to collisions at the origin.The series for $t$ has singularities at

$$
\psi _ { 1 } \equiv 2 z - \beta _ { 1 } { \pmod { 4 \omega } } , \qquad { \mathrm { f o r ~ } } t \to \infty ,
$$

and

$$
\psi _ { 2 } \equiv - 2 z - \beta _ { 1 } { \pmod { 4 \omega } } , \mathrm { f o r } t \to - \infty .
$$

There are two kinds of trajectories in this case: (i） the trajectories in $\textstyle { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } < x \leq \infty$ ，i.e.， $0 \leq r < \alpha$ ；and (ii） those in $e _ { 1 } < x < { \frac { 2 } { 3 } }$ ，i.e., $\alpha < r \leq \frac { \alpha } { e _ { 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 3 } } ;$ Thefirst kindisanejectionobitfromtheorigin; either it asymptotically approaches $r = \alpha$ ,or it starts asymptotically from $r = \alpha$ in the infinite past and collides at the origin.The second kind starts asymptoticallyfrom $r = \alpha$ and reaches a maximum at $r = \frac { \alpha } { e _ { 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 3 } }$ and tends to $r = \alpha$ asymptotically again.However, there is at $r = \alpha$ a spherical surface through which any light ray cannot pass even in an infinite time,because the coefficient of $d t ^ { 2 }$ in the expression $d s ^ { 2 }$ of Schwarzschild becomes infinite at $r = \alpha$ ，This spherical surface $r = \alpha$ cannot lie outside the space occupied by the material,even if this has the maximum density known to present-day physics. For the surface $r = \alpha$ to lie on the surface of a celestial body, the mean density of the body must be of the order $1 0 ^ { 1 7 }$ cgs.Stoner (1929,1930) and Pilowski (1928) have deduced the density of a sphere composed of relativistically degenerate gas to be $1 0 ^ { 1 3 }$ . Andersen (1929,1930) showed that density above $1 0 ^ { 1 7 }$ cannot be attained at the center of a celestial body on the basis of relativistic cosmology.Oppenheimer and Volkoff (1939)

estimated the density of a neutron star as $1 0 ^ { 1 5 }$ .Hence,we need not consider the sphere $r = \alpha$ for any physically realizable matter. We call the motion outside this sphere $r = \alpha$ pseudo-elliptic.

Case II: The situation is similar to Case I and the motion is stil pseudo-elliptic.

Case II: The motion, which approaches the two branches of asymptotes at infinity, includes $r = \infty$ .We call such motion pseudo-hyperbolic. In particular, if $\begin{array} { r } { \ell _ { 3 } = \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \end{array}$ ,the motion is pseudo-parabolic.

Case IV: Permissible motions are pseudo-elliptic.

Next, suppose that the values of $_ x$ do not extend to infinity, but that they are bounded between two finite limits.

Case I:

$$
\begin{array} { r } { e _ { 3 } < - \frac { 1 } { 3 } < e _ { 2 } < e _ { 1 } < \frac { 2 } { 3 } , \qquad e _ { 3 } < x < e _ { 2 } . } \end{array}
$$

The trajectory has two asymptotes and lies between $r = \alpha / ( \ell _ { 2 } + { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } )$ and $\infty$ . We call it quasi-hyperbolic. In particular, if $\begin{array} { r } { \ell _ { 3 } = - \frac { 1 } { 3 } } \end{array}$ , it is quasiparabolic.The point for the minimum $r _ { : }$ ,that is,the pericenter,rotates in this relativistic trajectory，but in the corresponding Newtonian tra-jectories the pericenter is always at rest.

Case II :

$$
- \frac { 1 } { 3 } < e _ { 3 } < e _ { 2 } < e _ { 1 } < \frac { 2 } { 3 } , e _ { 3 } < x < e _ { 2 } .
$$

Let

$$
\psi = \psi ^ { \prime } + 2 \omega ^ { \prime } , \qquad y = i y ^ { \prime } , \qquad \beta _ { 0 } = \beta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } + i \beta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime } , \qquad \beta _ { 3 } = \beta _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } + \beta _ { 3 } ^ { \prime \prime } ,
$$

then

$$
\wp ^ { \prime } ( y ) = - i { \frac { 2 \alpha } { h _ { 1 } } } { \sqrt { 1 - h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } } , \qquad \wp ^ { \prime } ( z ) = - { \frac { 2 \alpha h _ { 3 } } { h _ { 1 } } } , \qquad \wp ^ { \prime } ( y ) = { \frac { 2 \alpha ^ { 2 } } { h _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } } ,
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - 1 ) } { h _ { 1 } } ( s + \beta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } ) = \biggl \{ \frac { 1 } { 3 } - \frac { \eta } { \omega } - \frac { \alpha } { h _ { 1 } \sqrt { 1 - h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } } \biggl [ \frac { \eta y ^ { \prime } } { \omega } + \zeta ( y ^ { \prime } ) \biggr ] \biggr \} ( \psi ^ { \prime } + \beta _ { 1 } ) } \\ { - \displaystyle \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \biggl \{ \frac { 4 \pi } { \omega } \cosh \frac { \pi \eta ^ { \prime } } { \omega } + \frac { 4 \alpha } { h _ { 1 } \sqrt { 1 - h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } } \frac { \sinh \frac { \pi \eta ^ { \prime } } { \omega } } { n } \biggr \} } \\ { \times \frac { q ^ { n } } { 1 - \frac { \eta ^ { \prime } } { \omega ^ { 2 n } } \sin \frac { \pi \pi } { \omega - \left( \psi ^ { \prime } + \beta _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \right) } } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { 2 } { \alpha h _ { 3 } } \left( c _ { 0 } t + \beta _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } \right) = \left\{ - \frac { 1 } { h _ { 3 } } \left[ \zeta ( z ) - \frac { \eta z } { \omega } \right] + \frac { 2 h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - 3 } { 2 ( 1 - h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \left[ \bar { \zeta } ( y ^ { \prime } ) + \frac { \eta y ^ { \prime } } { \omega } \right] \right. } \\ { \left. + \frac { h _ { 1 } } { 2 \alpha ( h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - 1 ) } \left( \frac { \eta } { \omega } - \frac { 1 } { 3 } \right) \right\} ( \psi ^ { \prime } + \beta _ { 1 } ) } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle + \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \{ \displaystyle \frac { 4 } { h _ { 1 } } \displaystyle \frac { \sin \displaystyle \frac { n \pi z } { \omega } } { n } - \displaystyle \frac { 2 ( h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - 3 ) } { ( 1 - h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \displaystyle \frac { \sinh \displaystyle \frac { n \pi y ^ { \prime } } { \omega } } { n }  } } \\ { { \displaystyle +  \frac { 2 \pi h _ { 1 } } { \alpha ( h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - 1 ) \omega } \cosh \displaystyle \frac { n \pi y ^ { \prime } } { \omega } \} } } \\ { { \displaystyle \times  \frac { q ^ { n } } { 1 - q ^ { 2 n } } \sin \displaystyle \frac { n \pi } { 2 \omega } ( \psi ^ { \prime } + \beta _ { 1 } ) , } } \end{array}
$$

$$
u - \frac { 1 } { 3 } = - \frac { \eta } { \omega } - 2 \Big ( \frac { \pi } { \omega } \Big ) ^ { 2 } \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \frac { n q ^ { n } } { 1 - q ^ { 2 n } } \cos { \frac { n \pi } { 2 \omega } } ( \psi ^ { \prime } + \beta _ { 1 } ) .
$$

There is no singularity in this motion. The motion has only points of libration， such as discussed in Section 2.4,and $e _ { 3 } \leq x \leqq e _ { 2 } ,$ or $\alpha / ( e _ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 3 } ) \le r \le \alpha / ( e _ { 3 } + \frac { 1 } { 3 } )$ . The period of the motion with respect to $\psi$ is $_ { 4 \omega }$ but the period with respect to $t$ is

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \{ - \frac { 1 } { h _ { 3 } } [ \zeta ( z ) - \frac { \eta z } { \omega } ] + \frac { 2 h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - 3 } { 2 ( 1 - h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } [ \bar { \zeta } ( y ^ { \prime } ) + \frac { y ^ { \prime } \eta } { \omega } ]  } } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \qquad +  \frac { h _ { 1 } } { 2 \alpha ( h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - 1 ) } \bigg ( \frac { \eta } { \omega } - \frac { 1 } { 3 } \bigg ) \} . } \end{array}
$$

The points of maximum and minimum $\boldsymbol { r }$ rotate with a constant rate. This trajectory is called quasi-elliptic. When $4 \omega < 2 \pi ,$ ，the motion is the same in form as those of Einstein,Schwarzschild,and de Sitter.In general, the period for $\psi$ does not equal the period for $r _ { \mathrm { i } }$ ，the former being $4 \omega$ and the latter being $2 \pi$ .When the ratio of these two periods is an irrational number, the motion is quasi-elliptic and the trajectory fills up everywhere densely the ring region bounded by the two circles, respectively,with the maximum and the minimum $r$ as the radii. It is what Poincaré called quasi-ergodic.Hence, it is stable in Poisson's sense (Poincaré,l885,l899；Hagihara,1927)，which will be discussed in a later chapter. The motion of the pericenter, the point of the minimum ${ \boldsymbol { r } } ,$ is approximately represented by Einstein's formula

$$
\frac { 2 4 \pi ^ { 3 } a ^ { 2 } } { c _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } T _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } ,
$$

if $\pi / 2 \omega$ is approximately equal to 1. For a general value of $\pi / 2 \omega$ the period can be written

$$
T _ { 0 } = { \frac { 2 \ { \sqrt { 6 } } \pi \mathbf { a } } { c _ { 0 } \left[ 1 \ - \ \left( { \frac { \pi } { 2 \omega } } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] ^ { 1 / 2 } } } { \left\{ 1 + { \frac { \mathbf { e } ^ { 2 } } { 1 7 2 8 } } { \frac { \Psi \left( { \frac { 2 \omega } { 2 \omega } } \right) } { \left( { \frac { \pi } { 2 \omega } } \right) ^ { 2 } \left[ 2 \ + \ \left( { \frac { \pi } { 2 \omega } } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] \left[ 1 \ + \ 2 \left( { \frac { \pi } { 2 \omega } } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] } } \right. }
$$

where

$$
\Psi \Bigl ( \frac { \pi } { 2 \omega } \Bigr ) = 1 6 8 5 + 6 5 1 0 \Bigl ( \frac { \pi } { 2 \omega } \Bigr ) ^ { 2 } + 9 3 6 9 \Bigl ( \frac { \pi } { 2 \omega } \Bigr ) ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

The third law of Kepler takes the form

$$
{ \bf \tau } _ { 0 } ^ { \mathrm { * } 2 } = { \frac { 8 \pi ^ { 2 } } { c _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } } { \frac { { \bf a } ^ { 3 } } { \alpha } } \left\{ 1 + { \frac { \alpha ^ { 2 } { \bf e } ^ { 2 } } { 1 9 2 { \bf a } ^ { 2 } } } { \frac { \left( { \frac { \pi } { 2 \omega } } \right) ^ { 2 } \Psi \left( { \frac { \pi } { 2 \omega } } \right) } { \left[ 1 - \left( { \frac { \pi } { 2 \omega } } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] \left[ 2 + \left( { \frac { \pi } { 2 \omega } } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] \left[ 1 + 2 \left( { \frac { \pi } { 2 \omega } } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] ^ { 2 } } } \right\}
$$

where a and e correspond,respectively, to the semimajor axis and the eccentricity of the orbit in the Keplerian motion and are similar to those in Gyldén's absolute orbit, which willbe described later.Hagihara (1931) has extended the notion of Bessel functions to an infinite number of arguments and obtained the general solution of the relativistic trajectories in the form of infinite series. Similarly, for the Newtonian two-body problem, Hagihara obtained several theorems based on the expansion for a quasi-elliptic motion in the relativistic one-body problem (Section 5.13).

If two of the three roots $\boldsymbol { e } _ { 1 } , \ \boldsymbol { e } _ { 2 } , \ \boldsymbol { e } _ { 3 }$ coincide, then asymptotic motions appear,as in the limiting cases of libration discussed in Section 2.4, though more complicated as the motion is in two dimensions.It is known that elliptic functions degenerate to trigonometric or hyperbolic functions when two of $e _ { i }$ coincide.If one root in the real domain coincides with another root, then the motion makes a spiral approach to the circle corresponding to these coincident roots.As the motion is asymptotic to the circle $r = \alpha$ ，the motion is called pseudo-spiral. If the two roots bounding the real domain coincide, then the motion is circular and is called pseudo-circular.If two of the $e _ { i }$ coincide in Case I, then the circle with the coincident roots as the radius is an asymptotic orbit for $t \to - \infty$ and for $t \to + \infty$ . The circle itself being a pseudo-circle,the motion is what Poincaré (1899) called doubly asymptotic and the circle what he called the limit cycle (Poincaré,l885)； this will be described in a later chapter.

Summarizing :

If the three roots are all different, then the motions and their respective conditions are:

quasi-elliptic quasi-parabolic quasi-hyperbolic pseudo-elliptic pseudo-parabolic pseudo-hyperbolic

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { \alpha } { \varepsilon _ { 2 } + \frac 1 3 } \leq \tau \leq \frac { \alpha } { \varepsilon _ { 3 } } } \\ & { } \\ & { \frac { \alpha } { \varepsilon _ { 2 } + \frac 1 3 } \leq \tau \leq \infty } \\ & { \frac { \alpha } { \varepsilon _ { 2 } + \frac 1 3 } \leq \tau < \infty } \\ & { \qquad \quad \varepsilon _ { 2 } + \frac 1 3 \leq \tau < \infty } \\ & { \qquad \alpha < \tau \leq \frac { \alpha } { \varepsilon _ { 1 } + \frac 1 3 } } \\ & { \qquad \quad \alpha < \tau < \infty } \\ & { \qquad \alpha < \tau \leq \infty . } \end{array}
$$

If two of the three roots coincide, then:

$$
\frac { \alpha } { e _ { A } + \frac { 1 } { 3 } } < r < \frac { \alpha } { e _ { 3 } + \frac { 1 } { 3 } } , \qquad e _ { A } = e _ { 1 } = e _ { 2 }
$$

(doubly asymptotic motion, Figure 3)

quasi-parabolic spiral

quasi-hyperbolic spiral

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \frac { \alpha } { e _ { A } + \frac { 1 } { 3 } } } < r \equiv \infty } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { \alpha } { e _ { A } + \frac { 1 } { 3 } } } < r \equiv \infty } } \end{array}
$$

pseudo-spiral <eB+eB=e=e(Figure4)

pseudo-circular

quasi-circular

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { r = 3 \alpha , \qquad e _ { 1 } = e _ { 2 } = e _ { 3 } = 0 } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { r = { \frac { \alpha } { e _ { B } + { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } } } \cdot { \qquad e _ { 2 } } = { \frac { \alpha } { e _ { 3 } } } = 0 } } \end{array}
$$

A more detailed discusion is found in Hagihara's paper (1931).

# 2.20 ALGEBRAIC INTEGRALS

The function $y$ defined by a rational polynomial

$$
f ( y , x ) = A _ { 0 } y ^ { n } + A _ { 1 } y ^ { n - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { n - 1 } y + A _ { n } = 0
$$

with functions $A _ { 0 } , A _ { 1 } , . . . , A _ { n }$ of $_ x$ as coefficients is called an algebraic function of $_ x$ .Consider an integral called an Abelian integral

$$
\int _ { a } ^ { x } R ( y , x ) \ d x
$$

of a rational algebraic function $R ( y , x )$ of $_ x$ and $y$ integrated along a path from a point $^ { a }$ to another point $_ x$ of a complex plane (Appell and Goursat,1895；Forsyth,1893；Stahl,1896；Baker,1897,1907；Stahl and Noether，1911；Picard，1905，1931；Osgood,1932；Bliss，1933; Conforto, l956). As a simple example, consider the hyperelliptic case

$$
y ^ { 2 } = f ( x ) , ~ f ( x ) = \lambda _ { 0 } + \lambda _ { 1 } x + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \lambda _ { 6 } x ^ { 6 } .
$$

If $_ x$ is near $^ a$ and $^ { a }$ is not a root of (73a),then we can solve (73a) by substituting $x = a + t , y = P ( t )$ .If $P ( t )$ is a power series arranged in positive powers of $t$ and $^ { a }$ is a branch point where $_ x$ and $y$ are both finite,then we solve (73a) by putting $x = a + t ^ { 2 } , y = P ( t )$ .We examine the behavior at the point at infinity by putting $x = t ^ { - 1 }$ ， $y = t ^ { - 3 } P ( t )$ A rational function $R ( y , x )$ of $_ x$ and $y$ can be written generally in the form $R ( y , x ) = t ^ { - m } Q ( t )$ near a branch point, where $m$ is a positive or negative integer or zero,and $Q ( t )$ does not vanish at $t = 0$ If $m < 0$ ， then $R ( y , x )$ has a zero point of order $- m$ at $t = 0$ .Then the Abelian integral （74） can be written $t ^ { - m } Q ( t ) + C \log t$ with a constant $C .$ If $C$ is zero everywhere and $m < 0$ , the integral is called an integral of the first kind.If $C = 0$ but $m > 0$ at any finite point, theintegral possesses polesthat is,a finite number of algebraic infinity points-and is called an integral of the second kind.A function that possesses only poles is said to be meromorphic. It can be shown that there exist only a finite number of points at which $m > 0$ or $C \neq 0$ ,and that the sum of the values of $C$ for various points is zero. If $m \geq 0$ and $C$ is nonzero at two or more points, then the integral is called an integral of the third kind.

![](images/d9deb3b5ea1915f88c39c5c2122a0d57d2b11d19f1c865b4d6555dd7a4bcc32f.jpg)  
FIGURE 5

Integrals of the first and the second kinds are not uniform, that is, they are not single-valued but multiform. To make the integral uniform (uniformization),we cut the Riemann surface along closed curves in the definite sense of description. Such a curve is called a cross cut. Figure 5 shows as an example the hyperelliptic case defined by (73a).Let the points $a , c ; a _ { 1 } , c _ { 1 } ; a _ { 2 } , c _ { 2 }$ be the branch points.Draw the curve segments connecting $^ { a }$ and $c$ $a _ { 1 }$ and $c _ { 1 } , \ a _ { 2 }$ and $c _ { 2 }$ .These are called the branch cuts (Verzweigungschnitt). Two sheets of the Riemann surface intersect along these curve segments and the upper and the lower sheets interchange along these segments. In general, $_ m$ sheets branch out and interchange one after the other if $y ^ { m } = x$ 、Draw the closed curves $A _ { 1 }$ and $A _ { 2 }$ ,crossing the segments ac and $a _ { 1 } c _ { 1 }$ ， and ac and $a _ { 2 } c _ { 2 }$ ，respectively，and the closed curves $B _ { 1 }$ and $B _ { 2 }$ enclosing $a _ { 1 } c _ { 1 }$ and $\boldsymbol { a } _ { 2 } \boldsymbol { c } _ { 2 }$ ， respectively. After crossing the branch cut $_ { a c }$ ， $a _ { 1 } c _ { 1 }$ ,or $a _ { 2 } c _ { 2 }$ , the curves $A _ { 1 }$ and $A _ { 2 }$ pass to the other sheet of the Riemann surface in order to complete the circuits.The cross cuts are $A _ { 1 } , A _ { 2 } , B _ { 1 } , B _ { 2 }$ in this case.

Let $A _ { 1 }$ be the circuit round along the closed curve $A _ { 1 }$ in the positive direction, and $A _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 }$ be the circuit along $A _ { 1 }$ in the negative direction. The path $A _ { 1 } B _ { 1 } A _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } B _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 }$ forms the boundary of the Riemann surface cut along the cross cuts.Similarly, form the path $A _ { 2 } B _ { 2 } A _ { 2 } ^ { - 1 } B _ { 2 } ^ { - 1 }$ . Along either of these two nonintersecting boundaries $A _ { 1 } B _ { 1 } A _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } B _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 }$ and $A _ { 2 } B _ { 2 } A _ { 2 } ^ { - 1 } B _ { 2 } ^ { - 1 }$ ， the integrals (74) of the first and the second kinds are uniform.The value of the integral at a point $P$ just outside $A _ { 1 }$ differs by $\Omega _ { 1 }$ from its value at a point $P ^ { \prime }$ opposite to $P$ and just inside $A _ { 1 }$ . This value $\Omega _ { 1 }$ is the same all along the curve $A _ { 1 }$ . Similarly the value of integral (74) just outside $B _ { 1 }$ differs by $\Omega _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ from its value at a point opposite and just inside $B _ { 1 }$ .Similar differences $\Omega _ { 2 }$ and $\Omega _ { 2 } ^ { \prime }$ are obtained for $A _ { 2 }$ and $B _ { 2 }$ The four values $\Omega _ { 1 } , \ \Omega _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ \Omega _ { 2 } , \ \Omega _ { 2 } ^ { \prime }$ are called the periods of the integral. Before the cut is made, the value of integral (74) is expressed by

$$
\int _ { a } ^ { x } R ( y , x ) \ : d x + h _ { 1 } \Omega _ { 1 } + h _ { 2 } \Omega _ { 2 } + h _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + h _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ,
$$

with integers $h _ { 1 } , h _ { 2 } , h _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , h _ { 2 } ^ { \prime }$

Since a term $C \log t$ appears in the expansion of an integral of the third kind, the value of the integral increases by $2 \pi i C$ by going along a circuit round the point $t = 0$ If there are two logarithmic singularities with $C$ 's of opposite sign, then the integral can be made uniform by drawing cross cuts connecting the two singular points without crosing any of the foregoing cross cuts.It can be seen that the sum of the values of $C$ is zero.

If a closed curve drawn on a surface can be contracted to a single point—without passing through any branch point-remaining always on the surface, then the surface is said to be simply connected. A Riemann surface can be made to consist of $\pmb { n }$ separate simply connected sheets by means of $q$ cross cuts. The difference $q - n$ is fixed for a given Riemann surface. The integer defined by $N = q - n + 2$ is called the order of connectivity；and the surface, $N .$ -ply connected.The surface of a sphere is simply connected,as $q = 0$ ， $n = 1$ ， $N = 1$ . The surface of an open cylinder is made to be simply connected by cutting along a generator, so that $q = n = 1$ ， $N = 2$ ; thus the uncut surface is doubly connected. The surface of a torus is triply connected,as $q = 2$ ， $n = 1$ ， $N = 3$ A plane area with $_ m$ contours is $_ m$ -ply connected. In general, a surface of the order of connectivity $N$ can be transformed into a simply connected surface by means of $N - 1$ cuts.The number of the boundaries of a multiply connected surface increases or decreases by 1 by drawing a cross cut.

![](images/f5e8a5c981314dd99376863b10fc20d51c4d8242b8889d7d00e2f3e554dda401.jpg)  
FIGURE 6

The boundary of a simply connected surface-or of a surface made simply connected by a suitable number of cross cuts-consists of one closed curve.The number of different boundaries of an $N .$ -ply connected surface is equal to $N$ less zero or an even integer.Hence the order of connectivity of a closed surface with only one boundary is equal to an odd integer. Write $N = 2 p + 1$ . The integer $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ is called the genus (Geschlecht） of the surface. In the case of a spherical surface the genus ${ \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } = 0 .$ In the elliptic case $y ^ { 2 } = 4 x ^ { 3 } - g _ { 2 } x - g _ { 3 } .$ , as a special case of (73), $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } = 1$ , and the surface is a torus (Figure 6). In the hyperelliptic case represented by (73a), $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } = 2$

If we take the upper and the lower limits of the integral (74) at two points on the opposite sides of a cross cut on a surface of genus $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ ，then the value of the integral is constant along the cross cut until another cross cut is met; let this value be $\Omega$ .The number of the different values of $\Omega$ equals the order of connectivity $N .$ The value of the integral before the cross cuts are made can be written

$$
\int _ { a } ^ { x } R ( y , x ) \ : d x + h _ { 1 } \Omega _ { 1 } + h _ { 2 } \Omega _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + h _ { N } \Omega _ { N } ,
$$

where $h _ { 1 } , h _ { 2 } , \ldots , h _ { N }$ are positive or negative integers or zero.

A pair of corresponding values of $_ { x }$ and $\pmb { y }$ is called a place.Different values of $y$ may be related by (73) to the same value of $_ x$ ； they are located on different sheets of the Riemann surface.We call a place the corresponding points on different sheets having the same values of $_ x$ and $\pmb { y }$ There are $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ linearly independent integrals for an integral of the first kind (which is finite everywhere).Let a value of the integral on the

Riemann surface be $W$ ； then the other values are expressed by $\begin{array} { r } { W + \sum _ { r = 1 } ^ { 2 \mathcal { P } } m _ { \tau } \omega _ { \tau } } \end{array}$ with integral values of $m _ { \tau }$ .There are $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } + \mathbb { 1 }$ linearly independent integrals for an integral of the second kind (which has poles but no logarithmic singularity).All its values are expressed in the form $\begin{array} { r } { \Gamma + \sum _ { r = 1 } ^ { 2 \mathcal { P } } n _ { r } \epsilon _ { r } } \end{array}$ with integers $n _ { \tau }$ . Suppose that an integral of the third kind has $\mu$ logarithmic singularities in addition to poles, then the general value of the integral is represented by $\begin{array} { r } { \Pi + \sum _ { \tau = 1 } ^ { 2 p } m _ { \tau } \varpi _ { \tau } + 2 \pi i \sum _ { q = 1 } ^ { \mu } m _ { q } ^ { \prime } C _ { q } , } \end{array}$ where $C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + C _ { \mu } = 0$ .The constants $\omega _ { \tau } , \epsilon _ { \tau } ,$ and $\varpi _ { \tau }$ are called the periods with regard to the rth cross cut.

Since there are $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { p } }$ linearly independent functions for an integral of the first kind,any function of the first kind equals the sum of $\scriptstyle \sum _ { T = 1 } ^ { p } c _ { T } w _ { \tau }$ and a constant,where $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , \ldots , c _ { p }$ are constants and $w _ { r }$ are the $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ linearly independent functions.Denote the periods along these $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ cross cuts by $\omega _ { \tau 1 }$ ,ωr2,..., $\omega _ { \tau p }$ $( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , p )$ , respectively.It can be shown that the determinant $\left. \omega _ { \tau s } \right.$ is not zero. Hence, by suitably choosing $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , \ldots , c _ { p } .$ we can find linear combinations $W _ { 1 } , W _ { 2 } , . . . , W _ { p }$ of $\omega _ { 1 } , \omega _ { 2 } , \ldots , \omega _ { p }$ such that the periods are transformed as shown in Table l.

This form of periods is called the normal form and the integrals $W _ { \tau }$ the normal integrals. The constants $a _ { \tau s }$ are not independent. First, $a _ { \tau s } = a _ { s r }$ Furthermore, if we put $a _ { r s } = \rho _ { r s } + i \sigma _ { r s } ,$ then $\sigma _ { \tau s } = \sigma _ { s \tau }$ $\rho _ { \tau s } = \rho _ { s \tau }$ and, as can be proved, $\rho _ { 1 1 } c _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + 2 \rho _ { 1 2 } c _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } + \rho _ { 2 2 } c _ { 2 2 } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \rho _ { p p } c _ { p } ^ { 2 }$ is negative unless all $c _ { i }$ are zero at the same time.

Functions of the second kind are determined by the poles and the real parts of the periods.It can be shown that the periods can be so arranged that they are zero along the first $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ cross cuts $A _ { 1 } , A _ { 2 } , \dotsc , A _ { p }$ but are algebraic functions at the poles along the remaining $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ cross cuts $B _ { 1 } , B _ { 2 } , \ldots , B _ { p }$ ； that is, the periods have the values $2 d W _ { \tau } / d x$ at simple poles,where $d W _ { r } / d x$ are algebraic functions. Suppose that the function $\Gamma _ { a } ( x ; c )$ has period zero along the cross cuts $A _ { 1 } , A _ { 2 } , \dotsc , A _ { p }$ of type $A$ on the Riemann surface cut by $2 \hbar$ cross cuts. Let the zero places of this function be $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , \ldots , c _ { p } ,$ and the places of simple poles be $^ { a }$ These values are determined by the values of $_ x$ and $y$ satisfying (73). The places with these pairs of values $( x , y )$ are on each sheet of the Riemann surface. Let the periods with regard to the cross cuts $B _ { 1 } , B _ { 2 } , \ldots , B _ { p }$ of type $B$ be $\Omega _ { 1 } , \Omega _ { 2 } , . . . . , \Omega _ { p }$ Such a function $\Gamma _ { a } ( x ; c )$ is a Riemann's normal integral of the second kind.

An integral of the third kind is uniform everywhere on the cut Riemann surface. It has periods along the cross cuts $B _ { 1 } , B _ { 2 } , \ldots , B _ { p }$ of type $B$ ， but no period along the cross cuts $A _ { 1 } , A _ { 2 } , \ldots , A _ { p }$ of type $A$ .Denote by $\Pi _ { x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } } ( x ; c )$ a function that is finite and continuous everywhere except for the logarithmic singularities at $( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } )$ and $( x _ { 2 } , y _ { 2 } )$ . It is proved that $\Pi _ { x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } } ( x ; c ) = \Pi _ { x c } ( x _ { 1 } ; x _ { 2 } )$ . This is a Riemann's normal integral of the third kind.A general integral of the third kind is expressed by

$$
\Pi _ { x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } } ( x ; c ) + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { p } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { p } A _ { i j } W _ { i } ( x ; c ) W _ { j } ( x _ { 1 } ; x _ { 2 } ) , A _ { i j } = A _ { j i } ,
$$

The periods are

$$
0 , 0 , . . . , 0 , 2 \pi i W _ { 1 } ( x _ { 1 } ; x _ { 2 } ) , . . . , 2 \pi i W _ { p } ( x _ { 1 } ; x _ { 2 } ) .
$$

Contd ieghoringpoints $\pmb { x } _ { 1 }$ and $\pmb { x } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ and put $x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } - x _ { 1 } = t _ { x _ { 1 } } ^ { m + 1 }$

$$
[ \Pi _ { x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } x _ { 2 } } ( x ; c ) - \Pi _ { x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } } ( x ; c ) ] / t _ { x _ { 1 } }
$$

for $t _ { x _ { 1 } } \to 0$ as $D _ { t _ { x _ { 1 } } } \Pi _ { x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } } ( x ; c )$ . Then we have

$$
D _ { t _ { x _ { 1 } } } \Pi _ { x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } } ( x ; c ) = \Gamma _ { x _ { 1 } } ( x ; c ) .
$$

Thus an integral of the second kind is obtained from the derivative of an integral of the third kind.

The places on various branch sheets corresponding to a point $x = a$ are denoted by

$$
x - a = t _ { l } ^ { m _ { 1 } + 1 } , . . . , x - a = t _ { k } ^ { m _ { k } + 1 } .
$$

By the dimension of a function $F$ is meant the order of infinity of $\boldsymbol { F }$ at $x = a$ ； or,more precisely, the smallest positive integer $\sigma + 1$ for which $( x \mathrm { ~ - ~ } a ) ^ { \sigma + 1 } F$ is finite at $x = a$ on all the sheets.Suppose that rational functions $h _ { 1 } , h _ { 2 } , . . . , h _ { n - 1 }$ become infinite only at $x = a$ ，and that (x-a)o1+1h,..., $( x \mathrm { ~ - ~ } a ) ^ { \sigma _ { n - 1 } + 1 } h _ { n - 1 }$ are finite at $x = a$ and become infinite only at $x = \infty$ . It can be shown that the dimension of $g _ { i } \equiv ( x - a ) ^ { \sigma _ { \mathrm { i } } + 1 } h _ { i }$ is equal to the dimension of $h _ { i }$ Let $( x , 1 ) _ { l }$ be a polynomial of degree $\mathbf { \xi } _ { l }$ in $\pmb { x }$ An arbitrary integral function-that is,a function $F$ with no singularity other than that at $x = \infty$ — can be written

$$
F = ( 1 , x ) _ { \mu } + ( 1 , x ) _ { \mu _ { 1 } } g _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + ( 1 , x ) _ { \mu _ { i } - 1 } g _ { n - 1 } .
$$

The sum of the dimensions of $g _ { 1 } , g _ { 2 } , . . . , g _ { n - 1 }$ is proved to be equal to ${ \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } + { \pmb { \mathscr { n } } } - { \bf 1 }$ . There exists an integral polynomial in $x , \beta _ { 1 } , . . . , \beta _ { n - 1 }$ for which $\beta _ { i } y ^ { i }$ are integral functions. By representing such a polynomial, in the same manner as $F _ { : }$ ,as a function of linearly independent functions $g _ { 1 } , g _ { 2 } , . . . , g _ { n - 1 }$ ，we obtain in general

$$
g _ { i } ( y , x ) \equiv ( \mu _ { i , n - 1 } y ^ { n - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \mu _ { i , 1 } y + \mu _ { i } ) / D _ { i } ,
$$

vhere $\mu _ { i , n - 1 } , \ldots , \mu _ { i , 1 } , \mu _ { i } , D _ { i }$ are integral polynomials in $_ { x }$

Let the equation (73) defining the given Riemann surface $\begin{array} { r } { \mathbf { b e } f ( y , x ) = 0 . } \end{array}$ We write this in the form $\chi _ { n } ( y , x ) = 0 \quad$ ,in which

$$
\chi _ { i } ( y , x ) \equiv A _ { 0 } y ^ { i } + A _ { 1 } y ^ { i - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { i - 1 } y + A _ { i } .
$$

Furthermore, by equating various powers of $y$ on both sides, we define the functions $\phi _ { 0 } , \phi _ { 1 } , . . . , \phi _ { n - 1 }$ ，such that

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \phi _ { 0 } ( y ^ { \prime } , x ) + \phi _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ( y ^ { \prime } , x ) g _ { 1 } ( y , x ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \phi _ { n - 1 } ( y ^ { \prime } , x ) g _ { n - 1 } ( y , x ) } } \\ { { \mathrm { ~ } } } \\ { { \mathrm { ~ } = [ f ( y ^ { \prime } , x ) - f ( y , x ) ] / ( y ^ { \prime } - y ) } } \\ { { \mathrm { ~ } = y ^ { n - 1 } \chi _ { 0 } + y ^ { n - 2 } \chi _ { 1 } ( y ^ { \prime } , x ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + y \chi _ { n - 2 } ( y ^ { \prime } , x ) + \chi _ { n - 1 } ( y , x ) } } \end{array}
$$

Then an integral of the third kind can be written

$$
P _ { x _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } } ( x ; c ) = \int _ { c } ^ { x } d x [ ( x , x _ { 1 } ) - ( x , x _ { 2 } ) ] ,
$$

where

$$
( x , \xi ) = { \frac { \phi _ { 0 } ( y , x ) + \phi _ { 1 } ( y , x ) g _ { 1 } ( y , \xi ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \phi _ { n - 1 } ( y , x ) g _ { n - 1 } ( y , \xi ) } { ( x - \xi ) f ^ { \prime } ( y ) } } .
$$

This expression for an integral of the third kind with logarithmic singularities at $( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } )$ and $( x _ { 2 } , y _ { 2 } )$ is written in terms of the coefficients of (73).

Let $x = a$ be a zero point on the Riemann surface, and denote the linearly independent integrals of the first kind by $u _ { 1 } ( x ; a ) , \ldots , u _ { p } ( x ; a )$ · Suppose that the derivatives $D _ { t } u _ { 1 } ( x ; a ) , \dotsc , D _ { t } u _ { p } ( x ; a )$ are finite everywhere on the Riemann surface. Suppose, further, that we choose $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ fixed places $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , \ldots , c _ { p }$ on the Riemann surface such that the linear expression

$$
\lambda _ { 1 } D _ { t } u _ { 1 } ( { \pmb x } ; { \pmb a } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \lambda _ { p } D _ { t } u _ { p } ( { \pmb x } ; { \pmb a } )
$$

with constant coefficients $\lambda _ { 1 } , ~ \lambda _ { 2 } , . . . , ~ \lambda _ { p }$ does not vanish at any place $c _ { 1 } , ~ c _ { 2 } , \ldots , ~ c _ { p } ;$ ，but that one of such linear expressons does vanish for a pair of values of $\lambda$ Denote by $\omega _ { i } ( x )$ a linear expression that vanishes at all places $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , \ldots , c _ { p }$ except a place $c _ { i }$ ，where it equals 1. Write $\mu _ { i } ( x )$ in place of $D _ { t } u _ { i } ( x ; a )$ ,and let $\nu _ { i j }$ be the minor of the determinant formed of $\mu _ { j } ( c _ { i } )$ , then

$$
\omega _ { i } ( x ) = \nu _ { i 1 } \mu _ { 1 } ( x ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \nu _ { i p } \mu _ { p } ( x ) ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , p ) .
$$

The general integral of the first kind is writen with this notation:

$$
V _ { i } ( x ; a ) = \int _ { a } ^ { x } \omega _ { i } ( t ) d t _ { x } ,
$$

where $t _ { x }$ is defined as in (73b). Let

$$
\Gamma _ { z } ( x ; a ) - \left[ ( z , x ) - ( z , a ) \right] \frac { d z } { d ^ { * } } \equiv H _ { z } ( x ; a ) .
$$

Then

$$
H _ { z } ( x ; a ) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { p } \omega _ { i } ( z ) H _ { c _ { i } } ( x ; a ) ,
$$

where $\Gamma _ { z } ( x ; a ) / ( d z / d t )$ is a rational function of $_ z$ of the form

$$
\Gamma _ { z } ( x ; a ) = [ ( z , x ) - ( z , a ) ] \frac { d z } { d t } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { p } \omega _ { i } ( z ) H _ { c _ { i } } ( x ; a ) .
$$

Integrate with respect to $_ z$ ; the function

$$
\Pi _ { z , c } ( x ; a ) = P _ { x , a } ( z ; c ) + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { p } V _ { i } ( z ; c ) H _ { c _ { i } } ( x ; a )
$$

is an integral of the third kind, where $\pmb { c }$ is an arbitrary place. The general form of an integral of the second kind is

$$
\Gamma _ { z } ( x ; a ) = D _ { z } \Pi _ { z , c } ( x ; a ) = E _ { z } ( x ; a ) + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { p } V _ { i } ( x ; a ) D _ { z } H _ { c _ { i } } ( z ; c ) ,
$$

where $E _ { z } ( x ; a ) / ( d z / d t )$ is a rational function of $_ z$ .Denote by $\pmb { \kappa }$ an arbitrary symmetric matrix whose elements are $\kappa _ { i j }$ ，and consider $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ functions $L _ { i } ( x ; a )$ defined by

$$
L _ { i } ( x ; a ) = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { p } \nu _ { j i } H _ { c _ { j } } ( x ; a ) - 2 \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { p } \kappa _ { i j } u _ { j } ( x ; a ) .
$$

This form is called the fundamental integral of the second kind associated with the integrals $u _ { 1 } ( x ; a ) , \dotsc , u _ { p } ( x ; a )$ . Multiply $L _ { i } ( x ; a )$ by $\mu _ { i } ( z )$ and sum over $i$ then

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { p } \mu _ { i } ( z ) L _ { i } ( x ; a ) = H _ { z } ( x ; a ) - 2 \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { p } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { p } \kappa _ { i j } u _ { i } ( x ; a ) \mu _ { j } ( z ) ,
$$

which is independent of the places $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , \ldots , c _ { p }$ 、Substituting these $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ independent places for $_ z$ successively,we obtain $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ functions $L _ { i } ( x ; a )$ . In terms of these functions, the integrals of the second kind are represented by

$$
\Gamma _ { z } ( x ; a ) = \left[ ( z , x ) - ( z , a ) \right] \frac { d z } { d t } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { p } \mu _ { i } ( z ) L _ { i } ( x ; a )
$$

$$
+ \ 2 \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { p } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { p } \kappa _ { i j } u _ { i } ( \boldsymbol { x } ; \boldsymbol { a } ) u _ { j } ( \boldsymbol { z } ) ,
$$

and the integrals of the third kind by

$$
\Pi _ { z , c } ( x ; a ) = P _ { z , c } ( x ; a ) + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { p } u _ { i } ( x ; a ) L _ { i } ( z ; c )
$$

$$
+ \ 2 \sum \sum \kappa _ { i j } u _ { i } ( x ; a ) u _ { j } ( z ; c ) .
$$

Let $w _ { 1 } , w _ { 2 } , \ldots , w _ { p }$ be $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ independent functions of the normal form of the first kind on the Riemann surface of genus $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ Consider $q$ ${ \dot { \mathbf { \Gamma } } } ( { \boldsymbol { q } } \leq { \mathbf { \gamma } } _ { p } )$ independent variables $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \dotsc , x _ { q }$ and construct the functions

$$
v _ { r } = w _ { r } ( x _ { 1 } ) + w _ { r } ( x _ { 2 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + w _ { r } ( x _ { q } ) ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , q ) .
$$

The functions $v _ { r }$ are multiform, that is,many-valued, with constant periods $\omega _ { r 1 } , \omega _ { r 2 } , . . . , \omega _ { r , 2 p }$ which are the same as those of $w _ { \tau }$ Since ${ w _ { r } } ( x _ { m } )$ differs from $v _ { r }$ by integral multiples of the periods,we can write $v _ { r }$ in the general form

$$
v _ { r } + m _ { 1 } \omega _ { r , 1 } + m _ { 2 } \omega _ { r , 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + m _ { 2 p } \omega _ { r , 2 p } \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , p ) ,
$$

where the integers $m _ { s }$ $( s = 1 , 2 , . . . , 2 p )$ are the same for the $q$ functions $v _ { r }$ If $q = \phi$ ，then $r _ { 1 } , r _ { 2 } , . . . , r _ { p }$ reduce to uniform functions of the independent variables $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \dotsc , x _ { p }$ when suitable integral multiples of the periods are added or subtracted. Conversely, $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \dotsc , x _ { p } ,$ , regarded as functions of $\ v _ { 1 } , \ v _ { 2 } , \ \dotsc , \ v _ { p }$ possess $2  { p }$ pairs of periods $\omega _ { 1 s } , \omega _ { 2 s } , . . . , \omega _ { p s }$ $( s = 1 , 2 , . . . , 2 p )$ . This procedure is called the inversion (Umkehrung） of Abelian integrals.

The fundamental theorem in the theory of algebraic functions (which Schwarz and Klein have proved in an elegant manner by referring to potential theory) states that there exists a function which is finite at all places on the Riemann surface and is uniform and continuous except along the cross cuts drawn to make the surface simply connected, and for which the real parts of the periods along the cross cuts are of the given constant values; and that there exists a function having the singularities of the given character at the given places on the Riemann surface and having the above-described properties at any other places. According to this fundamental existenc theorem,the functions with the properties in question exist; in particular,functions exist with the given poles,whose periods possess all zero real parts.We need the condition that the imaginary parts of the periods shall also be zero. Consider, for example,poles of the first order $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , \ldots , c _ { m }$ .Such functions are algebraic and finite,uniform and continuous,at all places except the poles. Let $Z _ { r }$ be a function of the second kind with the place $c _ { r }$ as the only pole. The condition for the vanishing of the periods along the cross cuts of types $A _ { 1 }$ and $A _ { 2 }$ is

$$
y = \beta _ { 1 } Z _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \beta _ { m } Z _ { m } + \beta _ { 0 } .
$$

The condition for the vanishing of the periods along the cross cuts of types $B _ { 1 }$ and $B _ { 2 }$ is

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \beta _ { 1 } \phi _ { r } ( c _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \beta _ { m } \phi _ { r } ( c _ { m } ) = 0 ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , p ) } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \phi _ { r } ( x ) = \displaystyle \frac { d W _ { r } } { d x } . } } \end{array}
$$

We must determine the $m + 1$ constants $\beta$ from these $m + 1$ equations. If $q$ functions $\phi _ { \tau }$ in the last $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ equations vanish at all places $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , \ldots , c _ { m } ,$ then we are left with $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { p } } - \boldsymbol { \mathscr { q } }$ functions $\phi _ { \tau }$ . Since they contain $m$ constants $\beta$ we can express $\phi - q$ constants $\beta$ in terms of the remaining $m - p + q$ constants $\beta$ .If the additive constant $\beta _ { 0 }$ is taken into account, the function $y$ contains $m - \phi + q - 1$ arbitrary constants and is a linear combination of arbitrary multiples of $m \_ - \ p + q$ functions,each with $\begin{array} { r } { p \mathrm { ~ - ~ } q \mathrm { ~ + ~ } 1 } \end{array}$ poles of the first order, ${ \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } - { \pmb q }$ of which are common to all functions in the linear combination.This is the Riemann-Roch theorem and has important applications in algebraic geometry (Baker, l897；Coble, l929；van der Waerden,1939;Hirzebruch,1956).

Since $\phi _ { \tau } ( z ) = d W _ { \tau } / d x$ is the derivative of an integral of the first kind, it has neither any pole nor any logarithmic singularity except the branch points. It has a finite number of zero points and is finite and continuous at any other place.Hence it should be a polynomial in $_ x$ The $\phi - 1$ ratios of the coefficients $C$ in their linear combination

$$
\phi ( x ) = C _ { 1 } \phi _ { 1 } ( x ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + C _ { p } \phi _ { p } ( x )
$$

can be determined so that $\phi ( x )$ vanishes at the arbitrary $\phi - 1$ places; accordingly they can be determined by the $\phi - 1$ zero points except for a constant factor. We call this the $\phi$ polynomial.


<!-- chunk 0004: pages 211-280 -->
Let $y$ be an algebraic function defined by the ireducible equation

$$
f ( y , x ) = y ^ { n } + A _ { 1 } y ^ { n - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { n - 1 } y + A _ { n } = 0 ,
$$

where $A _ { 1 } , . . . , A _ { n }$ are polynomials in $_ x$ Let $R ( y , x )$ be a rational function of $_ x$ and $y$ ,and the periods along the cross cuts $A _ { i }$ and $B _ { i }$ be respectively $\Omega _ { i }$ and $\Omega _ { i } ^ { \prime } ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , p )$ . Further denote by $\theta ( y , x )$ a rational polynomial on the Riemann surface $f ( y , x ) = 0$ and let the zero points be the places $P _ { i } ( y _ { i } , x _ { i } )$ and the poles be the places $P _ { i } ^ { \prime } ( y _ { i } ^ { \prime } , x _ { i } ^ { \prime } )$ . Any one of these places does not coincide with a place of any kind of singularity of Abel's integral $\int R ( y , x ) d x$ Let $C$ be a simple closed curve on the Riemann surface which includes all places of singularities of this integral but contains neither the places of the zero points nor of the poles of $\theta ( y , x )$ . Abel's theorem states that

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { q } \int _ { ( x _ { 0 } , y _ { 0 } ) } ^ { ( x _ { i } , y _ { i } ) } R ( y , x ) \ d x - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { q } \int _ { ( x _ { 0 } , y _ { 0 } ) } ^ { ( x _ { i } ^ { ' } , y _ { i } ^ { ' } ) } R ( y , x ) \ d x
$$

is equal to the sum of the residues of $R \log \theta$ inside $C$ added to

$$
\sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { p } ( h _ { k } \Omega _ { k } + h _ { k } ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { k } ^ { \prime } ) ,
$$

where $h _ { k }$ and $\mathcal { h } _ { k } ^ { \prime }$ are integers.

In particular, if Abel's integral is respectively of the first, the second, and the third kinds,then this relation is written

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { q } W ( y _ { i } , x _ { i } ) \ - \ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { q } W ( y _ { i } ^ { \prime } , x _ { i } ^ { \prime } ) \ = \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { p } ( h _ { k } \Omega _ { k } + h _ { k } ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { k } ^ { \prime } ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { q } E _ { v } ( y _ { i } , x _ { i } ; P ) \ - \ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { q } E _ { v } ( y _ { i } , x _ { i } ; P ^ { \prime } ) } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad = \ - \Big | \frac { d \boldsymbol { v } ^ { + 1 } \log \theta } { d t ^ { v + 1 } } \Big | \ + \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { p } ( h _ { k } \Omega _ { k } + h _ { k } ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { k } ^ { \prime } ) , } } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { q } \Pi ( y _ { i } , x _ { i } ; P _ { 1 } , P _ { 2 } ) \ - \ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { q } \Pi ( y _ { i } ^ { \prime } , x _ { i } ^ { \prime } ; P _ { 1 } , P _ { 2 } ) } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad = \ \log \frac { \theta ( P _ { 1 } ) } { \theta ( P _ { 2 } ) } + \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { p } ( h _ { k } \Omega _ { k } + h _ { k } ^ { \prime } \Omega _ { k } ^ { \prime } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Here it is supposed, as an example, that the integral of the second kind contains the poles at the place $P$ of the Riemann surface and can be expanded in the normal form $\nu ! t ^ { - v - 1 } + c _ { 0 } + c _ { 1 } t + \cdot \cdot \cdot$ in the neighborhood of the poles. The integral of the third kind is supposed to have singularities at two places $P _ { 1 }$ and $P _ { 2 }$ with expansions of the form $\log t + c _ { 0 } + c _ { 1 } t + \cdot \cdot \cdot$ at $P _ { 1 }$ and $- \mathrm { l o g } t + c _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } + c _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } t + \cdot \cdot \cdot$ at $P _ { 2 }$ ·

# APPLICATION TO AN EARTH SATELLITE

# 2.21EARTH POTENTIAL

The motion of an artificial satellite around the earth, whose dynamical model is taken as an oblate spheroid, can be discussed under suitable assumptions by Hamilton-Jacobi separation of variables.

The gravitational potential $V$ outside an axially symmetric oblate earth of mass $M$ (the symmetry axis being the polar axis) is expressed by

$$
V ( r , \theta ) = - { \frac { \mu } { r } } \Bigl [ 1 - \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } J _ { n } \Bigl ( { \frac { R } { r } } \Bigr ) ^ { n } P _ { n } ( \sin \theta ) \Bigr ] ,
$$

where $R$ is the equatorial axis of the earth, $\pmb { r }$ is the radius vector, $\theta$ is the latitude of the satellite, $\mu = G M$ （ $G$ is the gravitational constant),and $J _ { n }$ is the coefficient of oblateness introduced by Merson and King-Hele (1958) (see Hagihara,l962).If the earth is symmetrical with respect to the equator, then all odd harmonics vanish: $J _ { 1 } = J _ { 3 } = J _ { 5 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = 0$ The coefficients $J _ { 2 }$ and $J _ { 4 }$ are given by

$$
J _ { 2 } = { \frac { 2 } { 3 } } J = { \frac { I _ { p } - I _ { t } } { M R ^ { 2 } } } , J _ { 4 } = - { \frac { 8 D } { 3 5 } } ,
$$

where $J$ and $D$ were introduced by Jeffreys (l952） and $I _ { p }$ and $I _ { t }$ are the moments of inertia of the earth about its polar and transverse axes, respectively, through the earth center. Recently, asymmetry with respect to the equatorial plane has been observed.If the origin is taken at the earth center of mass on the polar axis, then $J _ { 1 }$ vanishes; $J _ { 3 } , J _ { 5 } , \ldots$ do not vanish but are very small. On the other hand, if the origin is taken not coincident with the center of mass but with the displacement to the north of the center of mass from the origin, then ${ { J } _ { 1 } } \ne 0$ ,and $J _ { 3 } , J _ { 5 } , \ldots$ still do not vanish.

Vinti （1959） derived a solution $V _ { L }$ of Laplace's equation in oblate spheroidal coordinates leading to exact separability of the Hamilton-

Jacobi equation. It contains three adjustable constants $b _ { 0 } , \ b _ { \mathbf 1 }$ and $\pmb { c }$ which may be so chosen that the expansion of $V _ { L }$ is

$$
\begin{array}{c} V _ { L } = \displaystyle - \frac { \mu } { r } [ 1 - J _ { 2 } \Big ( \frac { R } { r } \Big ) ^ { 8 } P _ { 2 } ( \sin \theta ) + J _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \Big ( \frac { R } { r } \Big ) ^ { 4 } P _ { 4 } ( \sin \theta )   \\ { \displaystyle -  J _ { 2 } ^ { 3 } \Big ( \frac { R } { r } \Big ) ^ { 6 } P _ { 6 } ( \sin \theta ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot - J _ { 1 } \Big ( \frac { R } { r } \Big ) P _ { 1 } ( \sin \theta )  } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad \Big . +  J _ { 1 } J _ { 2 } \Big ( \frac { R } { r } \Big ) ^ { 3 } P _ { 3 } ( \sin \theta ) - J _ { 1 } J _ { 2 } \Big ( \frac { R } { r } \Big ) ^ { 5 } P _ { 5 } ( \sin \theta ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot \Big ] , } \end{array}
$$

where $J _ { 2 }$ is given by (77), $J _ { 1 } = - \delta / R$ and $J _ { \textrm { 4 } } = \textrm { -- } J _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }$ ， $J _ { 6 } = J _ { 2 } ^ { 3 } , . . . ,$ J $\mathbf { \psi } _ { \mathrm { { ' } } } = ( - 1 ) ^ { n + 1 } J _ { 2 } ^ { n } ; J _ { 3 } = \mathbf { \psi } - J _ { 1 } J _ { 2 } , J _ { 5 } = J _ { 1 } J _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } , \dots , J _ { 2 n + 1 } = ( - 1 ) ^ { n } J _ { 1 } J _ { 2 } ^ { n }$ ·

Kislik (1960,196l）assumed a potential of the form

$$
V _ { k } = - { \frac { \mu } { d } } { \frac { \sqrt { q _ { 1 } } } { q _ { 1 } - q _ { 2 } } } ,
$$

which is ofLiouville's type of Section 1.15 with the curvilinear coordinates

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { q _ { 1 } = \displaystyle \frac 1 2 \left[ \left( \frac { r ^ { 2 } } { d ^ { 2 } } - 1 \right) + \sqrt { \left( \frac { r ^ { 2 } } { d ^ { 2 } } - 1 \right) ^ { 2 } + 4 \frac { r ^ { 2 } } { d ^ { 2 } } \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } \right] , \hfill } } \\ { { \hfill } } \\ { { q _ { 2 } = \displaystyle \frac 1 2 \left[ \left( \frac { r ^ { 2 } } { d ^ { 2 } } - 1 \right) - \sqrt { \left( \frac { r ^ { 2 } } { d ^ { 2 } } - 1 \right) ^ { 2 } + 4 \frac { r ^ { 2 } } { d ^ { 2 } } \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } \right] \hfill } } \\ { { \hfill } } \\ { { \displaystyle ( - 1 \leq q _ { 2 } \leq 0 \leq q _ { 1 } < + \infty ) , \hfill } } \end{array}
$$

with a disposable constant $d .$ If we let $d = \sqrt { J _ { 2 } } ,$ then this potential $V _ { \kappa }$ coincides with (76) up to the term $J _ { 2 }$ and

$$
V _ { k } - V ( r , \theta ) = - \frac { \mu } { r ^ { 5 } } ( J _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - J _ { 4 } ) P _ { 4 } ( \sin \theta ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

The corresponding Hamilton-Jacobi equation

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \frac { 2 } { d ^ { 2 } } \left[ \frac { q _ { 1 } ( 1 + q _ { 1 } ) } { q _ { 1 } - q _ { 2 } } \left( \frac { \partial S } { \partial q _ { 1 } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \frac { q _ { 2 } ( 1 + q _ { 2 } ) } { q _ { 2 } - q _ { 1 } } \left( \frac { \partial S } { \partial q _ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } \right. } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad \left. + \frac { 1 } { 4 ( 1 + q _ { 1 } ) ( 1 + q _ { 2 } ) } \left( \frac { \partial S } { \partial q _ { 3 } } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] - \frac { \mu } { d } \frac { \sqrt { q _ { 1 } } } { q _ { 1 } - q _ { 2 } } = C } } \end{array}
$$

can be integrated by the separation of variables. Aksenov, Grebenikov, and Demin (l961，1963） solved the problem by Zhongolovich's simplified potential (Zhongolovich, 1957) :

$$
V = \displaystyle - \frac { \mu } { r } \left[ 1 + \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } ( - 1 ) ^ { n } \Big ( \frac { R } { r } \Big ) ^ { 2 n } P _ { 2 n } ( \sin \theta ) \right] ,
$$

using the same method.By including the terms with $n = 1$ (see Gylden's theory,Section 10.8)，Aksenov,Grebenikov,and Demin (l963a） discussed the stability of particular solutions,such as a polar ellptic orbit, a circular equatorial orbit,and periplegmatic orbits with moving perigees on certain ellipsoids.By using the coordinates

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { r = R \cosh v \sin u , \qquad z = - R \sinh v \cos u , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { d \tau = \displaystyle { \frac { d t } { \sinh ^ { 2 } v + \cos ^ { 2 } u } } , } } \end{array}
$$

we may write the equations of motion in the form

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } v } { d \tau ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \mu } { R ^ { 3 } } \cosh v - h \sinh 2 v - \frac { c _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \sinh v } { \cosh ^ { 3 } v } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } u } { d \tau ^ { 2 } } = h \sin 2 u - \frac { c _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \cos u } { \sin ^ { 3 } u } , } } \end{array}
$$

where $h$ and $c _ { 1 }$ are constants.

While Sterne (1957, 1958) referred to the separable disturbing potential

$$
3 k ( \sin ^ { 2 } \theta - { \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } \sin ^ { 2 } I ) \Big ( \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 1 } { a r ^ { 2 } } \Big )
$$

with constants $k$ and $^ { a }$ and with the orbital inclination $I _ { \ast }$ Garfinkel (1958, 1959) has based his intermediary orbit on the separable potential

$$
V _ { 0 } = - \frac { 1 } { r } + 6 k \biggl [ \frac { c _ { 1 } ( \sin ^ { 2 } \theta - c _ { 2 } ) } { 2 r ^ { 2 } } + \frac { c _ { 3 } } { r } + \frac { c _ { 4 } } { r ^ { 3 } } \biggr ] ,
$$

with

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { c _ { 1 } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \phi } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } , ~ } } & { { c _ { 2 } = \cos ^ { 2 } I , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } \\ { { c _ { 3 } = \displaystyle \frac { ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } { 2 \phi ^ { 2 } } P _ { 2 } ( \cos I ) , ~ c _ { 4 } = 0 , } } \end{array}
$$

where ${ \boldsymbol { p } } , { \boldsymbol { a } } ,$ and $\pmb { \ell }$ are the parameter, semimajor axis, and eccentricity, respectively, of the elliptic orbit. This potential gives the simplest possible orbit free from all secular parts by incorporating a major portion of the second spherical harmonics and leads to a closed solution with no secular variation of order $k .$ 、Barrar (l96l） simplified the solution of Vinti by reducing the four elliptic integrals of Vinti to two elliptic integrals as in Garfinkel's solution.Garfinkel (1964） further improved the constants $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , c _ { 3 }$ with the modification $c _ { 1 } + \delta _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } + \delta _ { 2 } , c _ { 3 } + \delta _ { 3 } .$ where $\delta _ { 1 } , \delta _ { 2 } , \delta _ { 3 }$ are determined so as to incorporate in the intermediary orbit the secular variations of the second order. In his later work, Garfinkel used instead of the Hamilton-Jacobi method the method of von Zeipel, which will be treated in Chapter 9.

On the other hand,Callender (l96l) showed the motion of an artificial close satellite to be quasi-periodic by obtaining the bounds of the variation of the radial distance and the variation of the inclination.

Vinti's work (1959) is described in the following section.

# 2.22OBLATE SPHEROIDAL COORDINATES

Take oblate spheroidal coordinates $\xi , \eta _ { ; }$ and $\phi$ such that

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l } & { x = c [ ( \xi ^ { 2 } + 1 ) ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } ) ] ^ { 1 / 2 } \cos \phi } & & { ( 0 < \xi < \infty ) , } \\ & { y = c [ ( \xi ^ { 2 } + 1 ) ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } ) ] ^ { 1 / 2 } \sin \phi } & & { ( - 1 \le \eta \le 1 ) , } \\ & { z = c \xi \eta , } \end{array}
$$

where $c$ is a constant to be determined later. The surface $\xi =$ constant is an oblate spheroid. Its sections perpendicular to the $z$ axis are circles and its meridian sections are confocal ellipses of semiaxes $c ( \xi ^ { 2 } + 1 ) ^ { 1 / 2 }$ and $c \xi$ ，the focal separation being $2 c$ ，The surface $\eta =$ constant is a hyperboloid of one sheet. The element of arc $d s ^ { 2 }$ is given by

$$
\begin{array} { r c l } { { } } & { { } } & { { d s ^ { 2 } = h _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } d \xi ^ { 2 } + h _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } d \eta ^ { 2 } + h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } d \phi ^ { 2 } , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { h _ { 1 } = c ( \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } ( \xi ^ { 2 } + 1 ) ^ { - 1 / 2 } , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { h _ { 2 } = c ( \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } ) ^ { - 1 / 2 } , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { h _ { 3 } = c ( \xi ^ { 2 } + 1 ) ^ { 1 / 2 } ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } . } } \end{array}
$$

Thus the kinetic energy is expressed by

$$
T = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( h _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \dot { \xi } ^ { 2 } + h _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \dot { \eta } ^ { 2 } + h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } \dot { \phi } ^ { 2 } ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Big ( \frac { \dot { p } _ { \xi } ^ { 2 } } { h _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \dot { p } _ { \eta } ^ { 2 } } { h _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \dot { p } _ { \phi } ^ { 2 } } { \dot { h } _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } \Big ) ,
$$

where

$$
{ p } _ { \xi } = h _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } { \dot { \xi } } = \frac { \partial S } { \partial \xi } , \qquad { \boldsymbol { \dot { p } } } _ { \eta } = h _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } { \dot { \eta } } = \frac { \partial S } { \partial \eta } , \qquad { \boldsymbol { \dot { p } } } _ { \phi } = h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } { \dot { \phi } } = \frac { \partial S } { \partial \phi } .
$$

The Hamiltonian takes the form

$$
H = \frac { 1 } { 2 c ^ { 2 } } \left[ \frac { \xi ^ { 2 } + 1 } { \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } } \rlap / p _ { \xi } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } } { \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } } \rlap / p _ { \eta } ^ { 2 } + \frac { \rlap / p _ { \phi } ^ { 2 } } { ( \xi ^ { 2 } + 1 ) ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } ) } \right] + V ( \xi , \eta )
$$

and the Hamilton-Jacobi equation is

$$
( \xi ^ { 2 } + 1 ) \Big ( \frac { \partial S } { \partial \xi } \Big ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } ) \Big ( \frac { \partial S } { \partial \eta } \Big ) ^ { 2 } + \frac { \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } } { ( \xi ^ { 2 } + 1 ) ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } ) } \Big ( \frac { \partial S } { \partial \phi } \Big ) ^ { 2 }
$$

$$
+ ~ 2 c ^ { 2 } ( \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } ) V ( \xi , \eta ) = 2 c ^ { 2 } ( \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } ) \alpha _ { 1 } ,
$$

where ${ \pmb { \alpha } } _ { \mathbf { 1 } }$ is the energy constant.

To separate the variables, write

$$
S = S _ { 1 } ( \xi ) + S _ { 2 } ( \eta ) + S _ { 3 } ( \phi ) .
$$

Then (80) takes the form

$$
( \xi ^ { 2 } + 1 ) \Big ( \frac { \partial S _ { 1 } } { \partial \xi } \Big ) ^ { 2 } + ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } ) \Big ( \frac { \partial S _ { 2 } } { \partial \eta } \Big ) ^ { 2 } + \Big ( \frac { 1 } { 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 1 } { \xi ^ { 2 } + 1 } \Big ) \Big ( \frac { \partial S _ { 3 } } { \partial \phi } \Big ) ^ { 2 }
$$

$$
+ ~ 2 c ^ { 2 } ( \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } ) V ( \xi , \eta ) = 2 c ^ { 2 } ( \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } ) \alpha _ { 1 } .
$$

At first $\partial S _ { 3 } / \partial \phi = \alpha _ { 3 } = { \mathfrak { c } }$ onstant. The elliptic coordinates $\xi$ and $\eta$ can be separated if and only if $V ( \xi , \eta )$ is of the form

$$
V ( \xi , \eta ) = \frac { 1 } { \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } } [ f ( \xi ) + g ( \eta ) ] .
$$

Inserting this in the Laplace equation

$$
\frac { \partial } { \partial \xi } \left[ ( \xi ^ { 2 } + 1 ) \frac { \partial V } { \partial \xi } \right] + \frac { \partial } { \partial \eta } \left[ \left( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } \right) \frac { \partial V } { \partial \eta } \right] = 0
$$

shows that $f ( \xi )$ and $g ( \eta )$ must satisfy the equation

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \xi ^ { 2 } ( \xi ^ { 2 } + 1 ) f ^ { \prime \prime } ( \xi ) - 2 \xi ( \xi ^ { 2 } + 2 ) f ^ { \prime } ( \xi ) + 2 ( \xi ^ { 2 } + 2 ) f ( \xi ) } \\ & { \qquad + \eta ^ { 2 } ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } ) g ^ { \prime \prime } ( \eta ) + 2 \eta ( \eta ^ { 2 } - 2 ) g ^ { \prime } ( \eta ) } \\ & { \qquad - 2 ( \eta ^ { 2 } - 2 ) g ( \eta ) + M ( \xi , \eta ) = 0 , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { M ( \xi , \eta ) = \eta ^ { 2 } F ( \xi ) - \xi ^ { 2 } G ( \eta ) , } \\ & { \quad F ( \xi ) = ( \xi ^ { 2 } + 1 ) f ^ { \prime \prime } ( \xi ) + 2 \xi f ^ { \prime } ( \xi ) - 2 f ( \xi ) , } \\ & { \quad G ( \eta ) = ( \eta ^ { 2 } - 1 ) g ^ { \prime \prime } ( \eta ) + 2 \eta g ^ { \prime } ( \eta ) - 2 g ( \eta ) . } \end{array}
$$

A solution of the Laplace equation (83) of form (82) can exist only if $M ( \xi , \eta )$ is separable. The necessary and suffcient condition that $M ( \xi , \eta )$ be separable is that

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { F ( \xi ) = 4 B + 4 C \xi ^ { 2 } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { G ( \eta ) = - 4 A + 4 C \eta ^ { 2 } , } } \end{array}
$$

where $A , B$ and $C$ are constants.

The condition is evidently sufficient.To show that the condition is also necessary when $M ( \xi , \eta )$ is separable, note that separability means that

$$
\eta ^ { 2 } F ( \xi ) - \xi ^ { 2 } G ( \eta ) = \psi ( \eta ) - \phi ( \xi ) ,
$$

which, if $\eta$ or $\xi$ are equated to zero, gives either

or

$$
\begin{array} { l l l } { { \phi ( \xi ) = \psi ( 0 ) + G ( 0 ) \cdot \xi ^ { 2 } } } & { { { } } } & { { ( \eta = 0 ) , } } \\ { { { } } } & { { { } } } & { { { } } } \\ { { \psi ( \eta ) = \phi ( 0 ) + F ( 0 ) \cdot \eta ^ { 2 } } } & { { { } } } & { { ( \xi = 0 ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Substituting the later two equations gives

$$
\eta ^ { 2 } [ F ( \xi ) - F ( 0 ) ] - \xi ^ { 2 } [ G ( \eta ) - G ( 0 ) ] = \phi ( 0 ) - \psi ( 0 ) .
$$

Let $\xi = 0$ or $\eta = 0$ .Then, $\phi ( 0 ) = \psi ( 0 )$ . Hence,

$$
\frac { 1 } { \xi ^ { 2 } } \left[ F ( \xi ) - F ( 0 ) \right] = \frac { 1 } { \eta ^ { 2 } } \left[ G ( \eta ) - G ( 0 ) \right] = 4 C ,
$$

where $C$ is a constant. Thus we have

$$
{ \cal F } ( \xi ) = { \cal F } ( 0 ) + 4 C \xi ^ { 2 } , \qquad { \cal G } ( \eta ) = { \cal G } ( 0 ) + 4 C \eta ^ { 2 } .
$$

The necessary condition is proved.

Comparing (85) with (86) we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { ( \xi ^ { 2 } + 1 ) f ^ { \prime \prime } + 2 \xi f ^ { \prime } - 2 f - 4 C \xi ^ { 2 } - 4 B = 0 , } \\ & { } & { ( \eta ^ { 2 } - 1 ) g ^ { \prime \prime } + 2 \eta g ^ { \prime } - 2 g - 4 C \eta ^ { 2 } + 4 A = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

Again, from (85) and (86),

$$
M ( \xi , \eta ) = 4 A \xi ^ { 2 } + 4 B \eta ^ { 2 } .
$$

Substituting these in (84) gives the following equation, which $f$ and $g$ should satisfy with a constant $\kappa$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { { \xi ^ { 2 } ( \xi ^ { 2 } + 1 ) f ^ { \prime \prime } ( \xi ) - 2 \xi ( \xi ^ { 2 } + 2 ) f ^ { \prime } ( \xi ) + 2 ( \xi ^ { 2 } + 2 ) f ( \xi ) + 4 A \xi ^ { 2 } } } & { { } } \\ { { } } & { { = \eta ^ { 2 } ( \eta ^ { 2 } - 1 ) g ^ { \prime \prime } ( \eta ) - 2 \eta ( \eta ^ { 2 } - 2 ) g ^ { \prime } ( \eta ) + 2 ( \eta ^ { 2 } - 2 ) g ( \eta ) - { \cal O } ( A ^ { 2 } ) , } } \\ { { } } & { { \equiv 4 K . } } \end{array}
$$

Multiply the first equation of (87) by $\xi ^ { 2 }$ and subtract it from the first equation of (88). Next multiply the second equation of (87) by $\eta ^ { 2 }$ and subtract it from the second equation of (88). Then

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \xi f ^ { \prime } - f = ( \xi ^ { 2 } + 1 ) ^ { - 1 } [ C \xi ^ { 4 } + ( A + B ) \xi ^ { 2 } - K ] , } \\ & { } \\ & { \eta g ^ { \prime } - g = ( \eta ^ { 2 } - 1 ) ^ { - 1 } [ C \eta ^ { 4 } - ( A + B ) \eta ^ { 2 } - K ] . } \end{array}
$$

The most general solution of these equations is

$$
g ( \eta ) = - K - b _ { 1 } \eta + C \eta ^ { 2 } + \frac { _ { 1 } } { ^ { 2 } } ( A + B - C + K ) \eta \log \frac { 1 + \eta } { 1 - \eta } ,
$$

where $b _ { \mathbf { 0 } }$ and $b _ { 1 }$ are the integration constants. Putting these expressions in (87) gives $B = A$ Hence,

$$
f ( \xi ) = K + b _ { 0 } \xi + C \xi ^ { 2 } + ( 2 A - C + K ) \xi \tan ^ { - 1 } \xi ,
$$

$$
g ( \eta ) = - K - b _ { 1 } \eta + C \eta ^ { 2 } + \frac { _ { 1 } } { ^ 2 } ( 2 A - C + K ) \eta \log \frac { 1 + \eta } { 1 - \eta } .
$$

Thus we have shown that all axially symmetric solutions of the form (82） of the Laplace equation in oblate spheroidal coordinates are expressible by

$$
\dot { \left( \xi , \eta \right) } = C + \frac { 1 } { \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } } \left[ b _ { 0 } \xi - b _ { 1 } \eta + b _ { 2 } \Big ( 2 \xi \tan ^ { - 1 } \xi + \eta \log \frac { 1 + \eta } { 1 - \eta } \Big ) \right] .
$$

where $\dot { \bar { z } } ( 2 A - C + K ) = b _ { 2 }$ If $V$ is zero at infinity, then we must take $C = 0$ and we are left with three constants $b _ { 0 } , b _ { 1 } ,$ and $b _ { 2 }$ .Furthermore, since the polar axis $\eta = \pm 1$ cannot be a singularity, we must have $b _ { 2 } = 0$ .Hence,a solution with two constants $b _ { 0 }$ and $b _ { 1 }$ of the Laplace equation that makes the Hamilton-Jacobi equation separable must be of the form

$$
V = \frac { 1 } { \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } } ( b _ { 0 } \xi - b _ { 1 } \eta ) = b _ { 0 } - \Re ( \xi + i \eta ) ^ { - 1 } + b _ { 1 } \Im ( \xi + i \eta ) ^ { - 1 } .
$$

Now, from transformation formulas (89),

$$
( \xi + \dot { \iota } \eta ) ^ { - 1 } = c r ^ { - 1 } ( 1 - 2 h s + h ^ { 2 } ) ^ { - 1 / 2 } , \qquad s = \sin \theta , \qquad h = - \frac { \dot { \iota } c } { r } .
$$

If we choose $c \ll r _ { : }$ then $| h | < 1$ and $( 1 - 2 h s + h ^ { 2 } ) ^ { - 1 / 2 }$ is the generating function for the Legendre polynomials $P _ { n } ( s )$ . Hence,

$$
( \xi + i \eta ) ^ { - 1 } = \frac { c } { r } \sum _ { n = 0 } ^ { \infty } h ^ { n } P _ { n } ( s ) = \frac { c } { r } \sum _ { n = 0 } ^ { \infty } ( - i ) ^ { n } \frac { c ^ { n } } { r ^ { n } } P _ { n } ( s ) ,
$$

from which,

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \frac { \xi } { \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } } = \Re \Big ( \frac { 1 } { \xi + i \eta } \Big ) = \frac { c } { r } [ 1 - \frac { c ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } P _ { 2 } ( \sin \theta ) + \frac { c ^ { 4 } } { r ^ { 4 } } P _ { 4 } ( \sin \theta ) - \cdot \cdot \cdot ] } } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \frac { \eta } { \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } } = - \Re \Big ( \frac { 1 } { \xi + i \eta } \Big ) } \\ & { } & { = \frac { c } { r } [ \frac { c } { r } P _ { 1 } ( \sin \theta ) - \frac { c ^ { 3 } } { r ^ { 3 } } P _ { 3 } ( \sin \theta ) + \frac { c ^ { 5 } } { r ^ { 5 } } P _ { 5 } ( \sin \theta ) - \cdot \cdot \cdot ] . } \end{array}
$$

Combining this with (89) gives the potential in the form

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { V = b _ { 0 } { \cdot } { \frac { c } { r } } \left[ 1 - { \frac { c ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } } P _ { 2 } ( \sin \theta ) + { \frac { c ^ { 4 } } { r ^ { 4 } } } P _ { 4 } ( \sin \theta ) - \cdot \cdot \cdot \right] } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { - b _ { 1 } { \frac { c } { r } } \left[ { \frac { c } { r } } P _ { 1 } ( \sin \theta ) - { \frac { c ^ { 3 } } { r ^ { 3 } } } P _ { 3 } ( \sin \theta ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot \right] . } } \end{array}
$$

Comparing (90) with (76) shows that

$$
b _ { 0 } c = \mathbf { \nabla } - { \mu } , \qquad c ^ { 2 } = R ^ { 2 } J _ { 2 } , \qquad b _ { 1 } c ^ { 2 } = \mathbf { \nabla } - { \mu } R J _ { 1 } = { \mu } { \delta } .
$$

These are the flexible constants and the other $J _ { n }$ can be expressed in terms of them. From (89),

$$
V = - { \frac { \mu } { c } } { \frac { 1 } { \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } } } \left( \xi + \eta { \frac { \delta } { c } } \right) .
$$

This Vinti's potential can be written in form (76) with the limitation that

$$
J _ { 2 m } = ( - 1 ) ^ { m + 1 } J _ { 2 } ^ { m } , \quad J _ { 2 m + 1 } = ( - 1 ) ^ { m } J _ { 1 } J _ { 2 } ^ { m } \quad ( m = 0 , 1 , 2 , \ldots ) .
$$

Substitute in (80) the expression (91) and the relation $\partial S _ { 3 } / \partial \phi = \alpha _ { 3 }$ obtained earlier; then

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { ( \xi ^ { 2 } + 1 ) \Big ( \displaystyle \frac { \partial S _ { 1 } } { \partial \xi } \Big ) ^ { 2 } - \displaystyle \frac { \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } { \xi ^ { 2 } + 1 } - 2 \mu c \xi - 2 \alpha _ { 1 } c ^ { 2 } \xi ^ { 2 } } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { = - ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } ) \Big ( \displaystyle \frac { \partial S _ { 2 } } { \partial \eta } \Big ) ^ { 2 } - \displaystyle \frac { \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } { 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } } + 2 \mu \delta \eta + 2 \alpha _ { 1 } c ^ { 2 } \eta } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { = - \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } . } } \end{array}
$$

Here it is supposed that the plane $\eta = 0$ is intersected by the orbit and, hence, $- \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \ \leqq \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 }$ Thus,

$$
\frac { \partial S _ { 1 } } { \partial \xi } = \pm \frac { 1 } { \xi ^ { 2 } + 1 } [ \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + ( - \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + 2 \mu c \xi + 2 \alpha _ { 1 } c ^ { 2 } \xi ^ { 2 } ) ( \xi ^ { 2 } + 1 ) ] ^ { 1 / 2 } ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \overline { { { \partial \xi } } } = \pm \frac { \ d } { \ d } \overline { { { \xi } } } ^ { 2 } + 1 \ln ^ { ( \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { } } + ( - \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { } + 2 \mu c \xi + \angle \alpha _ { 1 } c ^ { } \xi ^ { - } ) ( \xi ^ { - } + 1 ) { ] } ^ { \dag \prime } { } ^ { , } } } \\ { { \displaystyle } } \\ { { \frac { \partial S _ { 2 } } { \partial \eta } = \pm \frac { 1 } { 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } } \big [ - \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + \big ( \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + 2 \mu \delta \eta + 2 \alpha _ { 1 } c ^ { 2 } \eta ^ { 2 } \big ) \big ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } \big ) \big ] ^ { 1 / 2 } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle } } \\ { { \frac { \partial S _ { 3 } } { \partial \big } _ { - \alpha _ { - } } } } \end{array}
$$$$
\frac { \partial S _ { 3 } } { \partial \phi } = \alpha _ { 3 } .
$$

If we let $\rho = c \xi$ ， $\rho _ { 1 } = c \xi _ { 1 }$ ，and $\eta = \sigma ;$ ，the complete integral of the Hamilton-Jacobi equation becomes

$$
S = \alpha _ { 3 } \phi \pm \int _ { \rho _ { 1 } } ^ { \rho } \frac { 1 } { \rho ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } } \left[ c ^ { 2 } \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + \frac { } { } ( - \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + 2 \mu \rho + 2 \alpha _ { 1 } \rho ^ { 2 } ) ( \rho ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } ) \right] ^ { 1 / 2 }
$$

$$
\pm \int _ { 0 } ^ { \sigma } \frac { 1 } { 1 - \sigma ^ { 2 } } \left[ - \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + ( \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + 2 \mu \delta \sigma + 2 \alpha _ { 1 } c ^ { 2 } \sigma ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 - \sigma ^ { 2 } ) \right] ^ { 1 / 2 } d \sigma .
$$

The general solution of Hamilton's equations is thus

$$
\frac { \partial S } { \partial \alpha _ { 1 } } = t + \beta _ { 1 } , \qquad \frac { \partial S } { \partial \alpha _ { 2 } } = \beta _ { 2 } , \qquad \frac { \partial S } { \partial \alpha _ { 3 } } = \beta _ { 3 } ,
$$

or

$$
\pm \int _ { \rho _ { 1 } } ^ { \rho } \rho ^ { 2 } [ c ^ { 2 } \alpha _ { 3 } + ( - \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + 2 \mu \rho + 2 \alpha _ { 1 } \rho ^ { 2 } ) ( \rho ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } ) ] ^ { - 1 / 2 } d \rho
$$

$$
\pm \ \epsilon ^ { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \sigma } \sigma ^ { 2 } [ - \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } \ + \ ( \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \ + \ 2 \mu \delta \sigma + 2 \alpha _ { 1 } c ^ { 2 } \sigma ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 \ - \ \sigma ^ { 2 } ) ] ^ { - 1 / 2 } \ d \sigma = t \ + \ \beta
$$

$$
\mp \alpha _ { 2 } \int _ { \rho _ { 1 } } ^ { \rho } [ c ^ { 2 } \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + ( - \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + 2 \mu \rho + 2 \alpha _ { 1 } \rho ^ { 2 } ) ( \rho ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } ) ] ^ { - 1 / 2 } d \rho
$$

$$
\pm \ \alpha _ { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \sigma } { \left[ - \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + ( \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + 2 \mu \ \delta \sigma + 2 \alpha _ { 1 } c ^ { 2 } \sigma ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 - \sigma ^ { 2 } ) \right] ^ { - 1 / 2 } d \sigma } = \beta _ { 2 } ,
$$

$$
\pm ~ c ^ { 2 } \alpha _ { 3 } \int _ { \rho _ { 1 } } ^ { \rho } \frac { 1 } { \rho ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } } \left[ c ^ { 2 } \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + ( - \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + 2 \mu \rho + 2 \alpha _ { 1 } \rho ^ { 2 } ) ( \rho ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } ) \right] ^ { - 1 / 2 } d \rho
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \mp ~ \alpha _ { 3 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \sigma } \frac { 1 } { 1 - \sigma ^ { 2 } } \left[ - \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } + ( \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + 2 \mu ~ \delta \sigma \right. } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad + ~ \left. 2 \alpha _ { 1 } c ^ { 2 } \sigma ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 - \sigma ^ { 2 } ) \right] ^ { - 1 / 2 } d \sigma = \beta _ { 3 } . } \end{array}
$$

Vinti (l966a) considered further the third harmonic term.In the oblate spheroidal coordinates

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { x + \sqrt { - 1 } y = r \cos \theta \exp ( \sqrt { - 1 } \phi ) } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } = [ ( \rho ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } ) ] ^ { 1 / 2 } \exp ( \sqrt { - 1 } \phi ) , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { z = r \sin \theta = - \delta + \rho \eta , } } \end{array}
$$

Vinti takes

$$
\begin{array} { r } { c ^ { 2 } = r _ { e } ^ { 2 } J _ { 2 } ( 1 - \frac { 1 } { 4 } J _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } J _ { 2 } ^ { - 3 } ) , \qquad \& = - \frac { 1 } { 2 } r _ { e } J _ { 2 } ^ { - 1 } J _ { 3 } , } \end{array}
$$

where $r _ { e }$ is the equatorial radius. Then the gravitational potential

$$
V = \ - \mu ( \rho ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } \eta ^ { 2 } ) ^ { - 1 } ( \rho + \eta \delta )
$$
