helps to make the problem separable. According to Vinti, it gives about two-thirds of the fourth harmonic and negligible values, of order $J _ { 2 } ^ { 3 }$ or smaler,for the higher harmonics. The inversion formulas for the eliptic integrals become very complicated,but Vinti succeeded in obtaining the solution.Vinti (l966b） studied the effect of a constant force on a Keplerian orbit-in an analogous manner to the theory of the Stark effect in atomic physics - using parabolic coordinates and applying them to the effect of solar radiation pressure on the motion of an artificial satellite.

# 2.23INTEGRATION BY ELLIPTIC INTEGRALS

Izsak (l96O) has worked out Vinti's idea in a complete manner and has taken the solution of Vinti's equations as the intermediary orbit of the motion,by including second-order terms.The solution has been obtained in a complete form,described in Section 2.7; the integrals have been transformed by a linear fractional transformation in the complex domain，referring to Weierstrass's theory of elliptic functions. The solution is expressed in Fourier series and the integration constants as functions of the Keplerian elements.Vinti (196l,196la,1962,1963) tried to simplify Izsak's procedure by expressing the solution in terms of the complete elliptic integrals,avoiding the complicated elliptic functions; that is,as the sum of integrals containing the quadrics as in (95),which are evaluated by convergent Fourier series after the independent variable is transformed into the eccentric anomaly for uniformization.

Following Izsak (1960)， assume that $\delta = 0$ (the same as assuming that $J _ { 1 } = 0 _ { . }$ ）and let

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { P ( \rho ) = 2 \alpha _ { 1 } \rho ^ { 4 } + 2 \mu \rho ^ { 3 } - ( \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - 2 \alpha _ { 1 } c ^ { 2 } ) \rho ^ { 2 } + 2 \mu c ^ { 2 } \rho - ( \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } ) , } } \\ { { Q ( \sigma ) = - 2 \alpha _ { 1 } c ^ { 2 } \sigma ^ { 4 } + ( 2 \alpha _ { 1 } c ^ { 2 } - \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } ) \sigma ^ { 2 } + ( \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

In equations (96), take the upper signs for $\pm$ or $\mp$ , then

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { \partial S } { \partial \alpha _ { 1 } } = \int _ { \rho _ { 1 } } ^ { \rho } \frac { \rho ^ { 2 } d \rho } { \sqrt { P ( \rho ) } } + c ^ { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \sigma } \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } d \sigma } { \sqrt { Q ( \sigma ) } } = t + \beta _ { 1 } , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { \partial S } { \partial \alpha _ { 2 } } = - \alpha _ { 2 } \int _ { \rho _ { 1 } } ^ { \rho } \frac { d \rho } { \sqrt { P ( \rho ) } } + \alpha _ { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \sigma } \frac { d \sigma } { \sqrt { Q ( \sigma ) } } = \beta _ { 2 } , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { \partial S } { \partial \alpha _ { 3 } } = \phi + c ^ { 2 } \alpha _ { 3 } \int _ { \rho _ { 1 } } ^ { \rho } \frac { d \rho } { ( \rho ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } ) \sqrt { P ( \rho ) } } - \alpha _ { 3 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \sigma } \frac { d \sigma } { ( 1 - \sigma ^ { 2 } ) \sqrt { Q ( \sigma ) } } = \beta _ { 1 } } \end{array}
$$

Introduce $v$ with a constant $\Gamma$ (as yet indeterminate) :

$$
\Gamma \int _ { \rho _ { 1 } } ^ { \rho } \frac { d \rho } { \sqrt { P ( \rho ) } } = v ;
$$

then the second equation of (97) takes the form

$$
\alpha _ { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \sigma } { \frac { d \sigma } { \sqrt { Q ( \sigma ) } } } = { \frac { \alpha _ { 2 } } { \Gamma } } v + \beta _ { 2 } .
$$

Suppose that $v = 0$ for $\rho = \rho _ { 1 }$ and $v = v _ { 0 } = - \Gamma \beta _ { 2 } / \alpha _ { 2 }$ for $\sigma = 0$ .The variables $\rho$ and $\sigma$ are obtained in terms of $v$ from these formulas (98) and (99). Then the first and third equations of (97) become

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \phi - \beta _ { 3 } = \alpha _ { 3 } \int _ { v _ { 0 } } ^ { v } \frac { d v } { 1 - \sigma ^ { 2 } } - \frac { c ^ { 2 } \alpha _ { 3 } } { \Gamma } \int _ { 0 } ^ { v } \frac { d v } { \rho ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } } , } } \\ { { t + \beta _ { 1 } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \Gamma } \int _ { 0 } ^ { v } \rho ^ { 2 } d v + \frac { c ^ { 2 } } { \Gamma } \int _ { v _ { 0 } } ^ { v } \sigma ^ { 2 } d v . } } \end{array}
$$

Izsak (l96O) introduced a linear transformation with real coefficients of the complex $\rho$ -plane (Section 2.4)

$$
\rho = \frac { \phi + d _ { * } \xi } { 1 + e _ { * } \xi } ,
$$

such that the roots $\rho _ { 1 } , \rho _ { 2 } , \rho _ { 3 } , \rho _ { 4 }$ of $P ( \rho ) = 0$ are mapped into the points $\xi _ { 1 } = 1$ ， $\xi _ { 2 } = - 1$ ， $\xi _ { 3 } = + \sqrt { - 1 } / m , \xi _ { 4 } = - \sqrt { - 1 } / m$ ,respectively,of the $\xi$ -plane. The constant $m$ is considered real or purely imaginary according as $\rho _ { 3 }$ and $\rho _ { 4 }$ are conjugate complex or real, and to be determined by

$$
{ \frac { \rho _ { 1 } - \rho _ { 3 } } { \rho _ { 1 } - \rho _ { 4 } } } { \cdot } { \frac { \rho _ { 2 } - \rho _ { 3 } } { \rho _ { 2 } - \rho _ { 4 } } } = { \frac { \xi _ { 1 } - \xi _ { 3 } } { \xi _ { 1 } - \xi _ { 4 } } } { \cdot } { \frac { \xi _ { 2 } - \xi _ { 3 } } { \xi _ { 2 } - \xi _ { 4 } } } = \Big ( { \frac { 1 + \sqrt { - 1 } \ m } { 1 - \sqrt { - 1 } \ m } } \Big ) ^ { 2 } .
$$

In analogy to a Keplerian motion (see Sections 5.16-5.20), let

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \rho _ { 1 } = a ( 1 - e ) , } & { { } \quad \rho _ { 2 } = a ( 1 + e ) , } \\ { \rho _ { 3 } = a ( \kappa - \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda ) , } & { { } \quad \rho _ { 4 } = a ( \kappa + \sqrt { - 1 } \lambda ) . } \end{array}
$$

The relations

$$
\rho _ { j } = \frac { \hbar + d _ { * } \xi _ { j } } { 1 + e _ { * } \xi _ { j } } \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 ) ,
$$

with the notation (103) give

$$
\dot { p } = \frac { \rho _ { 1 } + \rho _ { 2 } } { 2 } - \frac { \rho _ { 2 } - \rho _ { 1 } } { 2 } e _ { * } = a ( 1 - e e _ { * } ) ,
$$

$$
d _ { * } = \frac { \rho _ { 1 } + \rho _ { 2 } } { 2 } e _ { * } - \frac { \rho _ { 2 } - \rho _ { 1 } } { 2 } = a ( e _ { * } - e ) ,
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \frac { m } { 1 \ - \ m ^ { 2 } } = \displaystyle \frac { e \lambda } { ( 1 \ - \ \kappa ) ^ { 2 } \ - \ ( e ^ { 2 } \ - \ \lambda ^ { 2 } ) } , } } \\ { { \frac { e _ { * } } { 1 \ + \ e _ { * } ^ { 2 } } = \displaystyle \frac { ( 1 \ - \ \kappa ) e } { ( 1 \ - \ \kappa ) ^ { 2 } \ + \ ( e ^ { 2 } \ + \ \lambda ^ { 2 } ) } , } } \end{array}
$$

where the values of $_ m$ and $^ { e _ { * } }$ are taken so that they vanish for $e = 0$ By applying (103) to (98) we obtain, after some reduction,

$$
- \frac { \Gamma } { A _ { * } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \xi } \frac { d \xi } { ( 1 - \xi ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 + m ^ { 2 } \xi ^ { 2 } ) } = v ,
$$

$$
A _ { * } = \sqrt { \frac { - 2 \alpha _ { 1 } \Delta ^ { 2 } } { ( 1 - \ell _ { * } ^ { 2 } ) ( \ell _ { * } ^ { 2 } + m ^ { 2 } ) } } \qquad \Delta = \rlap / p _ { * } - \ d _ { * } = a e ( 1 - \ell _ { * } ^ { 2 } ) .
$$

Let

$$
\xi = \sqrt { 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } } , A = \sqrt { \frac { - 2 \alpha _ { 1 } \Delta ^ { 2 } ( 1 + m ^ { 2 } ) } { ( 1 - e _ { * } ^ { 2 } ) ( e _ { * } ^ { 2 } + m ^ { 2 } ) } } ;
$$

then

$$
\frac { \Gamma } { A } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \eta } \frac { d \eta } { \sqrt { ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 - k ^ { 2 } \eta ^ { 2 } ) } } = v ,
$$

and

$$
\eta = \mathrm { s n } \bigg ( \frac { A v } { \Gamma } \bigg / k ^ { 2 } \bigg ) , \qquad \xi = \mathrm { c n } \bigg ( \frac { A v } { \Gamma } \bigg / k ^ { 2 } \bigg ) ,
$$

$$
k = { \frac { m } { \sqrt { 1 + m ^ { 2 } } } } \cdotp
$$

Try to determine $\Gamma$ so that the period of $\pmb { \xi } ( \upsilon )$ is equal to $2 \pi$ Since the real quarter-period of cn $\left( z / k ^ { 2 } \right)$ is known to be

$$
K = { \frac { \pi } { 2 } } \left[ 1 + { \binom { 1 } { 2 } } ^ { 2 } k ^ { 2 } + { \binom { 1 \cdot 3 } { 2 \cdot 4 } } ^ { 2 } k ^ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \right] ,
$$

therefore

$$
\xi = \mathrm { c n } \left[ \left( { \frac { 2 K } { \pi } } v \right) \bigg / k ^ { 2 } \right] , \Gamma = { \frac { \pi } { 2 k } } A .
$$

To transform the integral (99), write

$$
\begin{array} { r } { Q ( \sigma ) = ( \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } ) \Big ( 1 - \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } } { \sigma _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } \Big ) \Big ( 1 - \frac { \sigma ^ { 2 } } { \sigma _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } \Big ) , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { \sigma } { \sigma _ { 1 } } = \zeta , \qquad \frac { \sigma _ { 1 } } { \sigma _ { 2 } } = l ; } \end{array}
$$

then (99) becomes

$$
\frac { \alpha _ { 2 } \sigma _ { 1 } } { \sqrt { \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \zeta } \frac { d \zeta } { \sqrt { ( 1 - \zeta ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 - l ^ { 2 } \zeta ^ { 2 } ) } } = \frac { \alpha _ { 2 } } { \Gamma } v + \beta _ { 2 } ,
$$

or

$$
\sigma = \sigma _ { 1 } \sin \bigg [ \bigg ( \frac { \sqrt { \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } } { \Gamma \sigma _ { 1 } } \left( v - v _ { 0 } \right) \bigg ) \bigg / l ^ { 2 } \bigg ] , \qquad v _ { 0 } = - \frac { \Gamma \beta _ { 2 } } { \alpha _ { 2 } } .
$$

Let

$$
{ \cal L } = \frac { \pi } { 2 } [ 1 + \frac { } { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } l ^ { 2 } + ( \frac { 1 \cdot 3 } { 2 \cdot 4 } ) ^ { 2 } l ^ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ] ,
$$

$$
{ \frac { \ d ^ { 2 } L } { \pi } } ( v + \varpi ) = { \frac { \sqrt { \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } } { \Gamma s } } ( v - v _ { 0 } ) , \qquad \sigma _ { 1 } = s = \sin I ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \varpi = \displaystyle { \epsilon v + \omega } , \epsilon = \frac { \pi } { 2 L } \frac { \sqrt { \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } } { \Gamma s } - 1 , } } \\ { { \omega = \displaystyle { - \frac { \pi } { 2 L } \frac { \sqrt { \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } } { \Gamma s } v _ { 0 } = \frac { \pi } { 2 L } \frac { \sqrt { \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } } { \alpha _ { 2 } s } \beta _ { 2 } , } } } \end{array}
$$

where $\boldsymbol { \mathit { I } }$ is the inclination of the orbital plane and $\epsilon$ is the motion of the perigee.

Thus we have obtained

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle { \rho = \frac { \dot { p } + d _ { * } \mathrm { c n } \left[ \left( \frac { 2 K } { \pi } v \right) \Big / k ^ { 2 } \right] } { 1 + e _ { * } \mathrm { c n } \left[ \left( \frac { 2 K } { \pi } v \right) \Big / k ^ { 2 } \right] } , } } \\ { \displaystyle { \sigma = s \mathrm { s n } \left[ \left( \frac { 2 L } { \pi } \left( v + \frac { } { } \right) \right) \Big / l ^ { 2 } \right] . } } \end{array}
$$

These formulas correspond to those for a Keplerian motion, in which the radius vector $\boldsymbol { r }$ and the declination δ are given by

$$
r = { \frac { \phi } { 1 + e \cos v } } , \qquad \sin \delta = s \sin ( v + \omega ) .
$$

The quantities $\pmb { \kappa }$ and $\lambda$ can be obtained from (104), (l05),and (106) in terms of $a , \ e _ { \mathrm { : } }$ and $\pmb { S } ,$ 、The coefficients and the roots of $P ( \rho ) = 0$ are related to those of $Q ( \sigma ) = 0$ as follows :

$$
2 a ( 1 + \kappa ) = - \frac { \mu } { \alpha _ { 1 } } ,
$$

$$
a ^ { 2 } [ ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) + 4 \kappa + ( \kappa ^ { 2 } + \lambda ^ { 2 } ) ] = - \frac { \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - 2 c ^ { 2 } \alpha _ { 1 } } { 2 \alpha _ { 1 } } = c ^ { 2 } \Big ( s ^ { 2 } + \frac { s ^ { 2 } } { \ell ^ { 2 } } \Big ) ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { 2 a ^ { 3 } [ ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) \kappa + ( \kappa ^ { 2 } + \lambda ^ { 2 } ) ] = - { \frac { c ^ { 2 } \mu } { \alpha _ { 1 } } } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { \alpha ^ { 4 } ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ( \kappa ^ { 2 } + \lambda ^ { 2 } ) = - { \frac { c ^ { 2 } ( \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - \kappa _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } ) } { 2 \alpha _ { 1 } } } = c ^ { 4 } s ^ { 2 } \Big ( { \frac { s ^ { 2 } } { l ^ { 2 } } } \Big ) . } } } \end{array}
$$

Let $\nu = c / a$ ; then

$$
\kappa = \frac { \nu ^ { 2 } ( 1 - s ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } - \nu ^ { 2 } s ^ { 2 } ) } { ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } - \nu ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } - \nu ^ { 2 } s ^ { 2 } ) + 4 \nu ^ { 2 } s ^ { 2 } } ,
$$

$$
\kappa ^ { 2 } + \lambda ^ { 2 } = \frac { \nu ^ { 2 } s ^ { 2 } [ ( 1 - \ e ^ { 2 } - \nu ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 - \ e ^ { 2 } - \nu ^ { 2 } s ^ { 2 } ) + 4 \nu ^ { 2 } ] } { ( 1 - \ e ^ { 2 } - \nu ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 - \ e ^ { 2 } - \nu ^ { 2 } s ^ { 2 } ) + 4 \nu ^ { 2 } s ^ { 2 } } ,
$$

or, from expansion in powers of $\nu ^ { 2 }$ (which is of the order $1 0 ^ { - 3 }$ for artificial satellites with small eccentricities),

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \kappa = \displaystyle \frac { \nu ^ { 2 } ( 1 - s ^ { 2 } ) } { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } + \displaystyle \frac { \nu ^ { 4 } ( 1 - s ^ { 2 } ) } { ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 } } ( 1 - 4 s ^ { 2 } - e ^ { 2 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } } \\ { { \nonumber } } \\ { { \lambda ^ { 2 } = \nu ^ { 2 } s ^ { 2 } - \displaystyle \frac { \nu ^ { 4 } } { ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } ( 1 - s ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 - 5 s ^ { 2 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot . . } } \end{array}
$$

For a very low inclination, $\nu$ becomes imaginary so that $\rho _ { 3 }$ and $\rho _ { 4 }$ are real. The value of $\boldsymbol { \mathit { I } }$ for $\lambda = 0$ is given approximately by $s ^ { 2 } = \nu ^ { 2 } / ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 }$ The last equation of (109) and equation (lll） give

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { l ^ { 2 } = \displaystyle \frac { \nu ^ { 2 } s ^ { 2 } } { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } \displaystyle \frac { ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } - \nu ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } - \nu ^ { 2 } s ^ { 2 } ) + 4 \nu ^ { 2 } s ^ { 2 } } { ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } - \nu ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } - \nu ^ { 2 } s ^ { 2 } ) + 4 \nu ^ { 2 } } } } \\ { { = \displaystyle \frac { \nu ^ { 2 } s ^ { 2 } } { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } \displaystyle \frac { 4 \nu ^ { 4 } s ^ { 2 } } { ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 } } ( 1 - s ^ { 2 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot . } } \end{array}
$$

From (104), (105),(106), (110),and (111), Izsak obtained

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { e _ { * } = e \Bigg \{ 1 + \frac { \nu ^ { 2 } } { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } ( 1 - 2 s ^ { 2 } ) } } \\ { { + \frac { \nu ^ { 4 } } { ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 } } [ ( 3 - 1 6 s ^ { 2 } + 1 4 s ^ { 4 } ) - 2 ( 1 - s ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } e ^ { 2 } ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot \Bigg \} , } } \end{array}
$$

$$
k ^ { 2 } = \frac { \nu ^ { 2 } e ^ { 2 } } { ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } s ^ { 2 } - \frac { \nu ^ { 4 } e ^ { 2 } } { ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 4 } } \left[ ( 1 - 1 0 s ^ { 2 } + 1 1 s ^ { 4 } ) + s ^ { 4 } e ^ { 2 } \right] + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

$$
\times \ [ ( 2 8 8 - 1 2 9 6 s ^ { 2 } + 1 0 3 5 s ^ { 4 } ) - ( 1 4 4 + 2 8 8 s ^ { 2 } - 5 1 0 s ^ { 4 } ) e ^ { 2 } ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot
$$

Substituting (108) in (100) gives the first integral in the form

$$
\frac { \alpha _ { 3 } } { \Gamma ( 1 + \epsilon ) } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \upsilon } \frac { d w } { 1 - s ^ { 2 } \mathrm { s n } ^ { 2 } \left[ \left( \frac { 2 L } { \pi } w \right) \Big / l ^ { 2 } \right] } ,
$$

where

$$
w = v + \varpi = ( 1 + \epsilon ) v + \omega .
$$

The integrand is doubly periodic with periods $2 L$ and $2 \sqrt { - 1 } L ^ { \prime }$ ,and has two simple poles $z _ { 1 }$ and $z _ { 2 }$ .For $l < s < 1$ these poles are

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { z _ { 1 } = L + \sqrt { - 1 } y , } } \\ { { \ z _ { 2 } = L + 2 \sqrt { - 1 } L ^ { \prime } - \sqrt { - 1 } y , \ ~ ( 0 < y < L ^ { \prime } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

The residues at these poles computed by Izsak (1960) are respectively

$$
\frac { - \sqrt { - 1 } / 2 } { \sqrt { 1 - s ^ { 2 } } \sqrt { 1 - l ^ { 2 } / s ^ { 2 } } } , ~ \frac { \sqrt { - 1 } / 2 } { \sqrt { 1 - s ^ { 2 } } \sqrt { 1 - l ^ { 2 } / s ^ { 2 } } } .
$$

The integrand of (113) is written

$$
\frac { 1 } { 1 - s ^ { 2 } \ : \mathrm { s n } ^ { 2 } \ : \biggl [ \displaystyle \frac { 2 L } { \pi } \ : w \biggr ) \ : \biggl / l ^ { 2 } \biggr ] } = a _ { 0 } + 2 \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } a _ { 2 n } \cos 2 n w ,
$$

where

$$
a _ { 2 n } = \frac { 1 } { \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { \exp { ( 2 \sqrt { - 1 } \pi w ) } } { 1 - s ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } { \left[ \left( \frac { 2 L } { \pi } w \right) \middle / l ^ { 2 } \right] } } d w .
$$

The integral over the entire period-parallelogram gives

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \oint _ { 0 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } + \int _ { \pi } ^ { \pi + { \pi } \sqrt { - 1 } L ^ { \prime } / L } + \int _ { \pi + \pi \sqrt { - 1 } L ^ { \prime } / L } ^ { \pi \sqrt { - 1 } L ^ { \prime } / L } + \int _ { \pi \sqrt { - 1 } L ^ { \prime } / L } ^ { 0 } = \left( 1 - q ^ { 2 n } \right) \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } } } \\ { { \mathrm { ~ } = \pi \left( 1 - q ^ { 2 n } \right) a _ { 2 n } , } } \\ { { \mathrm { ~ } q = \exp \left( - \frac { \pi L ^ { \prime } } { L } \right) . } } \end{array}
$$

Again, by computing the residues for the singularity of exp $( 2 { \sqrt { - 1 } } \ n w )$ at infinity, Izsak obtained

$$
a _ { 2 n } = \frac { \pi } { 2 L } \frac { ( - 1 ) ^ { n } } { \sqrt { 1 - s ^ { 2 } } \sqrt { 1 - ( l ^ { 2 } / s ^ { 2 } ) } } \frac { \tau ^ { 2 n } - ( q / \tau ) ^ { 2 n } } { 1 - q ^ { 2 n } } ,
$$

$$
\tau = \exp { \left( - \frac { \pi } { 2 L } y \right) } ,
$$

and

$$
a _ { 0 } = 1 - 2 \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } a _ { 2 n } .
$$

Let

$$
\mathrm { s n } \left( { \frac { 2 L } { \pi } } w \Big / l ^ { 2 } \right) = \mathrm { s i n } \psi ,
$$

so that

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \mathrm { c n } \left( \frac { 2 L } { \pi } w \Big / l ^ { 2 } \right) = \cos \psi , } \\ { \displaystyle \mathrm { d n } \left( \frac { 2 L } { \pi } w \Big / l ^ { 2 } \right) = \sqrt { 1 - 2 ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \psi } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
d w = { \frac { \pi } { 2 L } } { \frac { d \psi } { \sqrt { 1 - l ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \psi } } } ;
$$

then (l13) is transformed into

$$
\frac { \alpha _ { 3 } } { \Gamma ( 1 + \epsilon ) } \frac { \pi } { 2 L } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \psi } \frac { d \psi } { ( 1 - s ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \psi ) \sqrt { 1 - l ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \psi } } .
$$

Inverting this integral gives

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle { \psi = \mathrm { a m } \left[ \left( \frac { 2 L } { \pi } \ : \right) \middle / L ^ { 2 } \right] = w + 2 \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \frac { q ^ { n } \sin 2 n \omega } { n ( 1 + \ : q ^ { 2 n } ) } , } } \\ { \displaystyle { q = \exp \left( - \frac { \pi L ^ { \prime } } { L } \right) = \frac { l ^ { 2 } } { 1 6 } \left( 1 + \frac { l ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + \frac { 2 1 l ^ { 4 } } { 6 4 } + \cdots \right) , } } \\ { \displaystyle { \psi = w + \frac { l ^ { 3 } } { 8 } \left( 1 + \frac { l ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) \sin 2 w + \frac { l ^ { 4 } } { 2 5 6 } \sin 4 w + \cdots , } } \\ { \displaystyle { w = \psi - \frac { l ^ { 2 } } { 8 } \left( 1 + \frac { l ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) \sin 2 \psi + \frac { 3 l ^ { 4 } } { 2 5 6 } \sin 4 \psi + \cdots } . } \end{array}
$$

Thus (114） becomes

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \int _ { 0 } ^ { \psi } \frac { \sqrt { 1 - s ^ { 2 } } \sqrt { 1 - ( l ^ { 2 } / s ^ { 2 } ) } } { ( 1 - s ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \psi ) \sqrt { 1 - l ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \psi } } d \psi } } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \psi } \frac { \sqrt { 1 - s ^ { 2 } } d \psi } { 1 - s ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \psi } - \Big ( \frac { l ^ { 2 } } { 2 s ^ { 2 } } + \frac { l ^ { 4 } } { 1 6 s ^ { 4 } } ( 2 + 3 s ^ { 2 } ) \Big ) \sqrt { 1 - \frac { \cos ^ { 2 } \psi } { \cos ^ { 2 } \psi } } } \\ & { } & { + \frac { 3 l ^ { 4 } } { 3 2 s ^ { 2 } } \sqrt { 1 - s ^ { 2 } } \sin 2 \psi + \cdot \cdot \cdot . . } \end{array}
$$

The first integral on the right-hand side is familiar in Keplerian motion and equals tan $^ { - 1 }$ $( \sqrt { 1 - s ^ { 2 } } \tan \psi )$ ·

For the second integral of (100) write

$$
\mathrm { s n } \left( { \frac { 2 K } { \pi } } v { \Big / } k ^ { 2 } \right) = \mathrm { s i n } \phi ;
$$

then

$$
\int _ { 0 } ^ { v } \frac { c ^ { 2 } \ : d v } { \rho ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } } = \frac { \pi } { 2 K } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \phi } \frac { c ^ { 2 } } { \rho ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } } \frac { d \phi } { \sqrt { 1 - k ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } } ,
$$

where

$$
\rho = a \frac { ( 1 - e e _ { * } ) + ( e _ { * } - e ) \cos \phi } { 1 + e _ { * } \cos \phi } .
$$

Furthermore, letting

$$
{ \begin{array} { l } { \nu _ { 1 } = { \frac { \nu ^ { 2 } } { ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } } , \qquad k _ { 1 } = { \frac { k ^ { 2 } } { e ^ { 2 } } } = { \frac { \nu ^ { 2 } } { ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } } s ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot = \nu _ { 1 } s ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \\ { { \widehat { \delta } } _ { 1 } = { \frac { e _ { * } - e } { ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) e } } = { \frac { \nu ^ { 2 } } { ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 } } } ( 1 - 2 s ^ { 2 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot = \nu _ { 1 } ( 1 - 2 s ^ { 2 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot } \end{array} }
$$

Izsak obtained

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle { \int _ { 0 } ^ { \phi } \frac { c ^ { 2 } d \phi } { ( \rho ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } ) \sqrt { 1 - k ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } } } } } \\ { { = \nu _ { 1 } \Big [ ( 1 - \nu _ { 1 } ) + \Big ( \frac 1 2 - 3 \nu _ { 1 } + \delta _ { 1 } + \frac { k _ { 1 } } 4 \Big ) e ^ { 2 } - \Big ( \frac { 3 \nu _ { 1 } } 8 - \frac { k _ { 1 } } { 1 6 } \Big ) } } \\ { { + \nu _ { 1 } \Big [ ( 2 - 4 \nu _ { 1 } ) - \Big ( 3 \nu _ { 1 } - \frac { \delta _ { 1 } } 2 - \frac { k _ { 1 } } 4 \Big ) e ^ { 2 } \Big ] e \sin \phi + \cdot \cdot \cdot . . } } \end{array}
$$

Likewise the integrals in (10l) can be expanded:

$$
\int _ { 0 } ^ { v } \rho ^ { 2 } d w = \frac { \pi } { 2 K } a ^ { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \phi } \frac { ( \rho / a ) ^ { 2 } } { \sqrt { 1 - k ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } } d \phi
$$

with

$$
\begin{array} { r l } {  { \frac { ( \rho / a ) ^ { 2 } d \phi } { \sqrt { 1 - k ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } } = \sqrt { 1 - \varepsilon _ { * } ^ { 2 } } \Big [ ( 1 - \delta _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } ) + \frac { k _ { 2 } } { 2 } \Big ( 1 - 4 \delta _ { 2 } - \frac { 9 k _ { 2 } } { 4 } \Big ) \Big ] E } } \\ & { - \sqrt { 1 - \varepsilon _ { * } ^ { 2 } } \Big [ ( 1 - \delta _ { 2 } ) - \frac { k _ { 2 } } { 2 } \Big ( 1 - \delta _ { 2 } - \frac { 3 k _ { 2 } } { 4 } \Big ) \Big ] \varepsilon \sin } \\ & { - [ \frac { k _ { 2 } } { 2 } ( 1 - \varepsilon _ { * } ^ { 2 } ) - \delta _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - k _ { 2 } \Big ( \delta _ { 2 } + \frac { 9 k _ { 2 } } { 1 6 } \Big ) ( 2 - \varepsilon _ { * } ^ { 2 } ) ] } \\ & { - k _ { 2 } \Big ( \delta _ { 2 } + \frac { 3 k _ { 2 } } { 4 } \Big ) e \sin \phi + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
e = e _ { * } ( 1 - \delta _ { 2 } ) , \cos E = \frac { e _ { * } + \cos \phi } { 1 + e _ { * } \cos \phi } , \sin E = \frac { \sqrt { 1 - e _ { * } ^ { 2 } } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } { 1 + e _ { * } \cos \phi } ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \delta _ { 2 } = \displaystyle \frac { \ell _ { * } - \ell } { \ell _ { * } } } } \\ { { } } \\ { { = \displaystyle \frac { \nu ^ { 2 } } { 1 - s ^ { 2 } } \left( 1 - 2 s ^ { 2 } \right) + \displaystyle \frac { \nu ^ { 4 } } { \left( 1 - e ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 } } \left[ \left( 2 - 1 2 s ^ { 2 } + 1 0 s ^ { 4 } \right) - \left( 1 - 2 s ^ { 2 } + 1 2 s ^ { 2 } \right) \right] \left( \frac { \ell _ { * } - \ell _ { * } } { \ell _ { * } - s ^ { 2 } } \right) } } \\ { { + \cdot \cdot , } } \\ { { k _ { 2 } = \displaystyle \frac { k ^ { 2 } ( 1 - e _ { * } ^ { 2 } ) } { \ell _ { * } ^ { 2 } } } } \\ { { = \displaystyle \frac { \nu ^ { 2 } } { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } s ^ { 2 } - \displaystyle \frac { \nu ^ { 4 } } { \left( 1 - e ^ { 2 } \right) ^ { 3 } } \left[ \left( 1 - 8 s ^ { 2 } + 7 s ^ { 4 } \right) + s ^ { 4 } e ^ { 2 } \right] + \cdot \cdot \cdot . } } \end{array}
$$

Now (l15) can be written in the form

$$
\rho = a ( 1 - e \cos E ) .
$$

This is familiar in a Keplerian motion where $\rho$ and $E$ denote the radius vector and the eccentric anomaly, respectively.

Thus we have obtained

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \phi - \beta _ { 3 } = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \psi } \frac { \sqrt { 1 - s ^ { 2 } } \sqrt { 1 - ( l / s ) ^ { 2 } } d \psi } { ( 1 - s ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \psi ) \sqrt { 1 - l ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \psi } } } } } \\ { { \displaystyle { - \frac { \alpha _ { 3 } } { A } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \phi } \frac { c ^ { 2 } d \phi } { ( \rho ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } ) \sqrt { 1 - k ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { t + \beta _ { 1 } = \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { A } \left[ \int _ { 0 } ^ { \phi } \frac { ( \rho / a ) ^ { 2 } d \phi } { \sqrt { 1 - k ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } } + \frac { K / L } { 1 + \epsilon } \nu ^ { 2 } s ^ { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \psi } \frac { \sin ^ { 2 } \psi d \psi } { \sqrt { 1 - l ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \psi } } \right] } . } } \end{array}
$$

where the expansions are substituted for the integrals. The constants are given by

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \hat { n } \displaystyle \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { A } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - e _ { * } ^ { 2 } } } \displaystyle \frac { \sqrt { 1 + k _ { 2 } } } { ( 1 + \kappa ) ( 1 - \delta _ { 2 } ) } , } } \\ { { \hat { n } = \sqrt { \displaystyle \frac { \mu } { a ^ { 3 } } } \Bigg \{ 1 - \displaystyle \frac { 3 \nu ^ { 2 } ( 1 - s ^ { 2 } ) } { 2 ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } + \displaystyle \frac { 3 \nu ^ { 4 } ( 1 - s ^ { 2 } ) } { 8 ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 } } } } \\ { { \times [ ( 1 + 1 1 s ^ { 2 } ) - ( 1 - 5 s ^ { 2 } ) e ^ { 2 } ] - \cdot \cdot \cdot \Bigg \} , } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { \displaystyle + k _ { 2 } } { \displaystyle ( 1 - \delta _ { z } ) } = 1 - \frac { \gamma ^ { 2 } s ^ { 2 } } { 2 ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } + \frac { \nu ^ { 4 } } { 8 ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 } } } \\ & { \qquad \times [ ( 1 2 - 4 8 s ^ { 2 } + 3 9 s ^ { 4 } ) - ( 8 - 1 6 s ^ { 2 } + 7 s ^ { 4 } ) e ^ { 2 } ] + \cdots , } \\ & { \qquad \frac { \alpha _ { 3 } } { A } = \sqrt { 1 - s ^ { 2 } } \bigg \{ 1 + \frac { \nu ^ { 2 } } { 2 ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } } \\ & { \qquad \quad \times [ ( 5 - 7 s ^ { 2 } ) + ( 1 - s ^ { 2 } ) e ^ { 4 } + \cdots ] \bigg \} , } \\ & { \frac { K / L } { \displaystyle 1 + e } = 1 - \frac { \nu ^ { 2 } } { 2 ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } [ ( 6 - 7 s ^ { 2 } ) - s ^ { 2 } e ^ { 2 } ] + \cdots . } \end{array}
$$

The final expressions for the radius vector and the declination are

$$
\begin{array} { l } { r = \sqrt { ( 1 - e \cos E ) ^ { 2 } + \nu ^ { 2 } ( 1 - s ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \psi ) } , } \\ { \sin \vartheta = \displaystyle \frac { s \sin \psi } { \sqrt { 1 + \nu ^ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { 1 - s ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \psi } { ( 1 - e \cos E ) ^ { 2 } } } } . } \end{array}
$$

The orbital elements are $a , e , s = \sin I , \beta _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 3 } ,$ and $\omega$ . Other important constants are $e _ { * } , k ^ { 2 } , \epsilon ,$ given by (112). The mean motion in the anomaly is $( 1 + \epsilon )$ times the mean motion in space. The motion of the node is represented by

$$
- \frac { 3 \nu ^ { 2 } \sqrt { 1 - s ^ { 2 } } } { 2 ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 3 \nu ^ { 4 } \sqrt { 1 - s ^ { 2 } } } { 1 6 ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 4 } } \left[ ( 1 8 - 1 3 s ^ { 2 } ) + 2 4 s ^ { 2 } e ^ { 2 } \right] + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

Allen and Knolle (l964,l965） derived differential corrections for the Izsak solution, and showed that the Izsak intermediary orbit specified by only six parameters was accurate enough,as compared with the rotatingprecessing ellipse which requires eight parameters. Borchers (l963), by extending the result of Izsak on the second-order solution of Vinti's problem,has presented a procedure for computing the coordinates and velocity of a satellite from the initial values of the position coordinates and velocity components.Borchers also derived the exact expression for the velocity in Vinti's intermediary orbit,using Izsak's orbital elements, for comparison with other methods of perturbation by means of the so-called Newton-Raphson iteration method (Scarborough, 1958). One drawback of the ordinary perturbation methods is that they necessitate frequent multiplications of Fourier series, inevitably accompanied by the question of convergence or divergence of the expansions.

# 2.24REDUCTION TO THE TWO-FIXED-CENTER PROBLEM

Move the coordinate origin (0, 0, O） to $( 0 , 0 , { \sqrt { 2 k } } )$ and write the new rectangular coordinates by $( x , y , z ^ { \prime } )$ , where $z ^ { \prime } = z + { \sqrt { 2 k } }$

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { z ^ { \prime } = r _ { 2 } \sin \theta ^ { \prime } , } & { { } } & { r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } = x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + z ^ { \prime 2 } , } \\ { z = r \sin \theta , } & { { } } & { r ^ { 2 } = x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } . } \end{array}
$$

Then it can be shown (Barrar,l96l） that

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle - \frac { 1 } { r _ { 2 } } + \frac { \sqrt { 2 k } P _ { 1 } ( \sin \theta ^ { \prime } ) } { r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { 1 } { r } + \frac { 2 k P _ { 2 } ( \sin \theta ) } { r ^ { 3 } } } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad + \sum _ { n = 3 } ^ { \infty } ( n - 1 ) ( 2 k ) ^ { n / 2 } \frac { P _ { n } ( \sin \theta ) } { r ^ { n + 1 } } . } \end{array}
$$

Since

$$
\frac { 1 } { r _ { 2 } } = \sum _ { n = 0 } ^ { \infty } c ^ { n } \frac { P _ { n } ( \sin \theta ) } { r ^ { n + 1 } } , \qquad \frac { P _ { n } ( \sin \theta ) } { r ^ { n + 1 } } = \frac { ( - 1 ) ^ { n } } { n ! } \frac { \partial ^ { n } } { \partial z ^ { n } } \Big ( \frac { 1 } { r } \Big ) ,
$$

therefore

$$
\frac { 1 } { r _ { 2 } } = \sum _ { n = 0 } ^ { \infty } c ^ { n } \frac { ( - 1 ) ^ { n } } { n ! } \frac { \partial ^ { n } } { \partial z ^ { n } } \left( \frac { 1 } { r } \right) \cdot
$$

Partial differentiation of both sides with respect to $_ z$ and $z ^ { \prime }$ , respectively, gives

$$
\begin{array} { r c l } { { \displaystyle \frac { P _ { 1 } ( \sin \theta ^ { \prime } ) } { r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { \partial } { \partial z ^ { \prime } } \left( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 2 } } \right) = \sum _ { n = 0 } ^ { \infty } c ^ { n } \frac { ( - 1 ) ^ { n + 1 } } { n ! } \frac { \partial ^ { n + 1 } } { \partial z ^ { n + 1 } } \left( \frac { 1 } { r } \right) } } \\ { { \displaystyle = \sum _ { n = 0 } ^ { \infty } c ^ { n } ( n + 1 ) \frac { P _ { n + 1 } ( \sin \theta ) } { r ^ { n + 2 } } . } } \end{array}
$$

This potential is written in the form

$$
- \frac { \mu } { r } \left[ 1 - \frac { 2 k P _ { 2 } ( \sin \theta ) } { r ^ { 2 } } - k ^ { 3 / 2 } O \Big ( \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 3 } } \Big ) \right] ,
$$

of which the frst two terms coincide with those of Vinti's potential

$$
- { \frac { \mu } { r } } \sum _ { n = 0 } ^ { \infty } ~ ( - 1 ) ^ { n } ( 2 k ) ^ { n } { \frac { P _ { 2 n } ( \sin \theta ) } { r ^ { 2 n } } } ,
$$

but differ from those of the exact potential by $k ^ { 1 / 2 } / r$ times the second term. The equations of motion can be integrated with this potential by the separation of variables in the Hamilton-Jacobi partial differential equation (Barrar, 1961).

Consider two fixed centers with mass $M _ { 1 }$ and $M _ { 2 }$ at respective distances $a _ { 1 } = a M _ { 2 } / M$ and $a _ { 2 } = - a M _ { \mathrm { 1 } } / M$ $\small ( M = M _ { 1 } + M _ { 2 } )$ from the center of mass of these two centers (Aksenov,Grebenikov,Demin,1963b; Marchal, 1964,1966). The problem of two fixed centers can be solved by the separation of variables (Charlier, l906).Let

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { V = - f \Bigl ( \frac { M _ { 1 } } { r _ { 1 } } + \frac { M _ { 2 } } { r _ { 2 } } \Bigr ) , } } & { { \quad r ^ { 2 } = x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } , } } \\ { { \nonumber } } & { { } } \\ { { r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + ( z - a _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } , } } & { { \quad r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } = x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + ( z - a _ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } . } } \end{array}
$$

Then

$$
\frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } = \frac { 1 } { r } \sum _ { n = 0 } ^ { \infty } \bigg ( \frac { a _ { 1 } } { r } \bigg ) ^ { n } P _ { n } \bigg ( \frac { z } { r } \bigg ) , \qquad \frac { 1 } { r _ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { r } \sum _ { n = 0 } ^ { \infty } \bigg ( \frac { a _ { 2 } } { r } \bigg ) ^ { n } P _ { n } \bigg ( \frac { z } { r } \bigg ) .
$$

Hence

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { V = - \displaystyle \frac { f M } { r } \left[ 1 + \sum _ { n = 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { \gamma _ { n } } { r ^ { n } } P _ { n } \Big ( \frac { z } { r } \Big ) \right] , } } \\ { { \gamma _ { 1 } = 0 , \gamma _ { n } = \displaystyle \frac { M _ { 1 } a _ { 1 } ^ { n } + M _ { 2 } a _ { 2 } ^ { n } } { M } ~ ( n > 1 ) . } } \end{array}
$$

In order that $V$ be real it is necessary that $M$ and $\gamma _ { n } , n = 2 , 3 , . . . ,$ be real; besides, $\gamma _ { 1 }$ must be zero, since the center of mass is taken as origin; that is,

$$
\gamma _ { 1 } = 0 , \qquad \Im ( \gamma _ { n } ) = \Im ( M ) = 0 , \qquad n = 2 , 3 , \ldots .
$$

Following Yarov-Yarovoy (1962), let

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { \hat { l } _ { 1 } = \rho _ { 1 } ( \cos \psi _ { 1 } + \sqrt { - 1 } \sin \psi _ { 1 } ) , \quad } & { M _ { 2 } = \rho _ { 2 } ( \cos \psi _ { 2 } + \sqrt { - 1 } \sin \psi _ { 1 } ) , } \\ & { } & \\ & { z _ { 1 } = R _ { 1 } ( \cos \phi _ { 1 } + \sqrt { - 1 } \sin \phi _ { 1 } ) , \quad } & { a _ { 2 } = R _ { 2 } ( \cos \phi _ { 2 } + \sqrt { - 1 } \sin \phi _ { 1 } ) . } \end{array}
$$

Then the frst condition of (117) gives

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \rho _ { 1 } R _ { 1 } \cos { ( \psi _ { 1 } + \phi _ { 1 } ) } + \rho _ { 2 } R _ { 2 } \cos { ( \psi _ { 2 } + \phi _ { 2 } ) } = 0 , } \\ { \rho _ { 1 } R _ { 1 } \sin { ( \psi _ { 1 } + \phi _ { 1 } ) } + \rho _ { 2 } R _ { 2 } \sin { ( \psi _ { 2 } + \phi _ { 2 } ) } = 0 , } \end{array}
$$

that is,

$$
( \psi _ { 1 } + \phi _ { 1 } ) = \tan { ( \psi _ { 2 } + \phi _ { 2 } ) } ,
$$

or

$$
\psi _ { 1 } + \phi _ { 1 } = \psi _ { 2 } + \phi _ { 2 } + s \pi ,
$$

where $s$ is an integer. If cos $( \psi _ { 1 } + \phi _ { 1 } ) \neq 0$ ,then we should have

$$
\frac { \rho _ { 2 } } { \rho _ { 1 } } = \frac { R _ { 1 } } { R _ { 2 } } .
$$

The second condition of (117) gives

$$
\sin { ( \psi _ { 1 } + n \phi _ { 1 } ) } + { \frac { \rho _ { 2 } } { \rho _ { 1 } } } \bigg ( { \frac { R _ { 2 } } { R _ { 1 } } } \bigg ) ^ { n } \sin { ( \psi _ { 2 } + n \phi _ { 2 } ) } = 0 ,
$$

or,by (118),

$$
\sin { ( \psi _ { 1 } + n \phi _ { 1 } ) } + \left( { \frac { R _ { 2 } } { R _ { 1 } } } \right) ^ { n - 1 } \sin { ( \psi _ { 2 } + n \phi _ { 2 } ) } = 0 .
$$

Suppose that $\phi _ { 1 } \neq 0 , \pi , \psi _ { 1 } \neq 0 , \pi , \phi _ { 2 } \neq 0 , \pi , \psi _ { 2 } \neq 0 , \pi ;$ ： then the only solution of (119) that holds for all values of $n$ is $R _ { 1 } = R _ { 2 }$ Then (119) becomes

$$
\tan \left( \psi _ { 1 } + \phi _ { 1 } \right) = \cot \left[ \frac { ( n - 1 ) } { 2 } \left( \psi _ { 1 } + \phi _ { 1 } \right) \right] .
$$

The only solution that holds for all values of $_ { n }$ is $\phi _ { 1 } + \psi _ { 1 } = 0$ ， $2 \pi$ Hence

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \psi _ { 1 } + \phi _ { 1 } = \pi / 2 + s _ { 1 } \pi , } } \\ { { \nonumber } } \\ { { \psi _ { 2 } + \phi _ { 2 } = \pi / 2 + s _ { 2 } \pi \qquad ( s _ { 1 } , s _ { 2 } = 0 , 1 , 2 , \ldots ) . } } \end{array}
$$

From these,

$$
\psi _ { 1 } + \psi _ { 2 } = s _ { 3 } \pi \qquad ( s _ { 3 } = 0 , 1 , 2 , . . . ) .
$$

These relations show that $M _ { 1 }$ and $M _ { 2 }$ on one hand, and $a _ { 1 }$ and $a _ { 2 }$ on the other,are pairs of conjugate complex quantities.

Next suppose that $\phi _ { 1 } = 0 , \pi , \psi _ { 1 } = 0 , \pi , \phi _ { 2 } = 0 , \pi , \psi _ { 2 } = 0 , \pi .$ Then $M _ { 1 }$ ， $M _ { 2 }$ and $a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 }$ should be all real.

Thus there are only two cases in which $U$ is real. In one case, $M _ { 1 }$ and $M _ { 2 } , a _ { 1 }$ and $a _ { 2 }$ are conjugate complex; in the other case, $M _ { 1 } , M _ { 2 } , a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 }$ are all real. The third condition of (l17) is naturally satisfied.

In the first case,

$$
\begin{array} { c c } { { { \cal M } _ { 1 } = \displaystyle \frac { M } { 2 } ( 1 + \sqrt { - 1 } \sigma ) , \quad } } & { { { \cal M } _ { 2 } = \displaystyle \frac { M } { 2 } ( 1 - \sqrt { - 1 } \sigma ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle } } & { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { a _ { 1 } = c ( \sigma + \sqrt { - 1 } ) , \quad } } & { { a _ { 2 } = c ( \sigma - \sqrt { - 1 } ) , } } \end{array}
$$

and the potential is represented by

$$
V = - \frac { \mu } 2 \biggl ( \frac { 1 + \sqrt { - 1 } \sigma } { r _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 - \sqrt { - 1 } \sigma } { r _ { 2 } } \biggr ) , \qquad \mu = f M ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + [ z - c ( \sigma + \sqrt { - 1 } ) ] ^ { 2 } , } } \\ { { \ r _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } = x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + [ z - c ( \sigma - \sqrt { - 1 } ) ] ^ { 2 } , } } \end{array}
$$

or

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { V = - \frac { \mu } { r } \left[ 1 + \sum _ { n = 2 } ^ { \infty } \frac { \gamma _ { n } } { r ^ { n } } P _ { n } \bigg ( \frac { z } { r } \bigg ) \right] } , } } \\ { { } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \gamma _ { n } = \frac { c ^ { n } } { 2 } \left[ ( 1 + \sqrt { - 1 } \ \sigma ) ( \sigma + \sqrt { - 1 } ) ^ { n } + ( 1 - \sqrt { - 1 } \ \sigma ) ( \sigma - \sqrt { - 1 } ) ^ { n } \right] } . } } \end{array}
$$

This potential coincides as far as the third term with the exact potential (76),which (if we put $z / r$ for sin $\theta$ is

$$
V = \ - { \frac { \mu } { r } } \left[ 1 - \sum _ { n = 2 } ^ { \infty } J _ { n } \Big ( { \frac { R } { r } } \Big ) ^ { n } P _ { n } \Big ( { \frac { z } { r } } \Big ) \right] ,
$$

if $M$ is the earth's mass and $\pmb { \sigma }$ and $c$ are so chosen that the second and third terms of these two expansions should be equal, that is, if

$$
c ^ { 2 } ( 1 + \sigma ^ { 2 } ) = J _ { 2 } , 2 \sigma c ^ { 3 } ( 1 + \sigma ^ { 2 } ) = J _ { 3 } ,
$$

or if

$$
c = R \sqrt { J _ { 2 } - ( J _ { 3 } / 2 J _ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } , \qquad \sigma = - \frac { J _ { 3 } } { 2 J _ { 2 } } \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { J _ { 2 } - ( J _ { 3 } / 2 J _ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } } ,
$$

$$
\gamma _ { 4 } = c ^ { 4 } ( 1 + \sigma ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 - 3 \sigma ^ { 2 } ) .
$$

In the second case,

$$
\begin{array} { c c } { { M _ { 1 } = M ( 1 - \gamma ) , ~ } } & { { M _ { 2 } = M \gamma , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } \\ { { a _ { 1 } = a \gamma , ~ } } & { { a _ { 2 } = - a ( 1 - \gamma ) , } } \end{array}
$$

and the potential is

$$
\begin{array}{c} \mathrm { ~  ~ \gamma ~ } = \mathrm { ~ \displaystyle - f { \cal M } } \biggl \{ \frac { 1 - \gamma } { \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + ( z - a \gamma ) ^ { 2 } } } + \frac { \gamma } { \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + [ z - a ( 1 - \gamma ) ] ^ { 2 } } } \biggr . \biggr .  \end{array}
$$

or

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle V = - \frac { \mu } { r } \left[ 1 + \sum _ { n = 2 } ^ { \infty } \frac { \bar { \gamma } _ { n } } { r ^ { n } } P _ { n } \Big ( \frac { z } { r } \Big ) \right] \mathrm { , } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \bar { \gamma } _ { n } = a ^ { n } \gamma ( 1 - \gamma ) [ \gamma ^ { n - 1 } + ( - 1 ) ^ { n } ( 1 - \gamma ) ^ { n - 1 } ] . } } \end{array}
$$

The second term coincides with the second term of the exact potential if

$$
\bar { \gamma } _ { 2 } = - J _ { 2 } R ^ { 2 } .
$$

It is not possible to make the third terms coincide.

Barrar's potential is obtained by expanding

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { V = \displaystyle - \mu \biggl [ \frac { 1 - \gamma } { \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + ( z - \delta + \delta - a _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } } } } } \\ { { + \displaystyle \frac { \gamma } { \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + ( z - \delta + \delta - a _ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } } } } \end{array}
$$

in the form

$$
\begin{array} { l } { V = \displaystyle - \frac { \mu } { \rho } \Big [ 1 - \frac { \delta ( z - \delta ) } { \rho ^ { 2 } } \Big ] , } \\ { \rho = \displaystyle \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + ( z - \delta ) ^ { 2 } } } \\ { \delta = a \displaystyle \sqrt { \gamma ( 1 - \gamma ) } = R \displaystyle \sqrt { J _ { 2 } } . } \end{array}
$$

with

Vinti's potential is obtained if the coordinate origin is displaced by co along the rotation axis of the oblate spheroid. Let

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { r _ { 1 } = \displaystyle { \sqrt { \rho ^ { 2 } - 2 ( \sqrt { - 1 } ~ c ) \zeta + ( \sqrt { - 1 } ~ c ) ^ { 2 } } } , \qquad } } \\ { { r _ { 2 } = \displaystyle { \sqrt { \rho ^ { 2 } - 2 ( - \sqrt { - 1 } ~ c ) \zeta + ( \sqrt { - 1 } ~ \zeta ) ^ { 2 } } } } } \\ { { \rho = \displaystyle { \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + ( z - c \sigma ) ^ { 2 } } } , \qquad \zeta = z - c \sigma , } } \end{array}
$$

and expand $1 / r _ { 1 }$ and $1 / r _ { 2 }$ in powers of ${ \sqrt { - 1 } } c / \rho$ ; then,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle V = - \frac { \mu } { \rho } \left[ 1 + \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } ( - 1 ) ^ { n } c ^ { 2 n } \left( \frac 1 { \rho } \right) ^ { 2 n } P _ { 2 n } \Big ( \frac \zeta \rho \Big ) \right. } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad \left. - \ c \sigma \sum _ { n = 0 } ^ { \infty } ( - 1 ) ^ { n } c ^ { 2 n } \Big ( \frac 1 { \rho } \Big ) ^ { 2 n + 1 } P _ { 2 n + 1 } \Big ( \frac \zeta \rho \Big ) \right] , } } \end{array}
$$

which is Vinti's potential. Thus the physical significance of Vinti's potential is the translation of the origin of the spheroidal coordinates by a distance $\sigma = - J _ { 3 } / 2 J _ { 2 }$ times the equatorial radius. This distance, amounting to $7 ~ \mathrm { k m }$ in the case of the earth,is larger than any change produced by $J _ { 3 }$ . According to Vinti (l966),it is related to the longperiod terms of perturbation theory and turns out to be equal to the displacement by $J _ { 3 }$ of the plane of symmetry of those exactly elliptical polar orbits that are possible solutions with the spheroidal model.

The integration can be carried out by the transformation

$$
z = c \sigma + c \sinh v \cos u , \qquad d t = I d \tau = ( \sinh ^ { 2 } v + \cos ^ { 2 } u ) d \tau .
$$

The kinetic energy $T$ and the potential $V$ take the form

$$
T = { \frac { c ^ { 2 } } { 2 } } \left\{ \left[ \left( { \frac { d u } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( { \frac { d v } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] I + \left( { \frac { d w } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } \cosh ^ { 2 } v \sin ^ { 2 } u \right\} ,
$$

or

$$
T = \frac { 1 } { 2 c ^ { 2 } } \left[ \frac { 1 } { \cal \bar { I } } \left( \phi _ { u } ^ { 2 } + \phi _ { v } ^ { 2 } \right) + \frac { \phi _ { w } ^ { 2 } } { \cosh ^ { 2 } v \sin ^ { 2 } u } \right] ,
$$

and

$$
V = - { \frac { \mu } { c } } \frac { \sinh v - \sigma \cos u } { \sinh ^ { 2 } v + \cos ^ { 2 } u } .
$$

The corresponding Hamilton-Jacobi equation is

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \left( \frac { \partial S } { \partial u } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { \partial S } { \partial v } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { 1 } { \sin ^ { 2 } u } - \frac { 1 } { \cosh ^ { 2 } v } \right) \left( \frac { \partial S } { \partial w } \right) ^ { 2 } } } \\ { { - \left. 2 \mu c ( \sinh v - \sigma \cos u ) = 2 c ^ { 2 } h ( \sinh ^ { 2 } v + \cos ^ { 2 } v + \sigma \cos v + 1 ) \right. } } \end{array}
$$

Separating the variables gives

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \left( \frac { \partial S } { \partial u } \right) ^ { 2 } = c _ { 2 } - \frac { c _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { \sin ^ { 2 } u } - 2 \mu c \sigma \cos u + 2 c ^ { 2 } h \cos ^ { 2 } u , } } \\ { ~ } \\ { { \displaystyle \left( \frac { \partial S } { \partial v } \right) ^ { 2 } = - c _ { 2 } + \frac { c _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { \cosh ^ { 2 } v } + 2 \mu c \sinh v + 2 c ^ { 2 } h \sinh ^ { 2 } v , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \left( \frac { \partial S } { \partial w } \right) ^ { 2 } = c _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } , } \end{array}
$$

where $h , c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 }$ are integration constants. The integration can be carried out by using elliptic integrals.

Timoshkova (1968) wrote

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle = \frac { k ^ { 2 } M } { r } \left[ 1 + \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { \infty } \frac { J _ { j } } { r ^ { j } } P _ { j } \Big ( \frac { z } { r } \Big ) \right] , } \\ { = \frac { c ^ { j } } { 2 } [ ( 1 + \sqrt { - 1 } \sigma ) ( \sigma + \sqrt { - 1 } ) ^ { j } + ( 1 - \sqrt { - 1 } \sigma ) ( \sigma - \sqrt { - 1 } ) ^ { j } ] , } \end{array}
$$

$$
c = R \Big [ - J _ { 2 } - \Big ( \frac { J _ { 3 } } { 2 J _ { 2 } } \Big ) ^ { 2 } \Big ] ^ { 1 / 2 } , \qquad \sigma = \frac { J _ { 3 } } { 2 J _ { 2 } } \Big [ - J _ { 2 } - \Big ( \frac { J _ { 3 } } { 2 J _ { 2 } } \Big ) ^ { 2 } \Big ] ^ { - 1 / 2 } ,
$$

and transformed the coordinates

$$
\begin{array} { l } { x = \sqrt { ( \xi ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } ) } \cdot \cos \varpi , \qquad y = \sqrt { ( \xi ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 - \eta ^ { 2 } } } \\ { \quad z = c \sigma + \xi \eta \qquad ( - 1 \le \eta \le 1 , \xi > 0 , \xi ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } > R _ { \mathtt { p o l e } } ^ { 2 } ) , } \end{array}
$$

and the time

$$
d t = ( \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } ) d \tau .
$$

Andrade (l890） solved the two-fixed-center problem， that is,the motion of a particle attracted by two fixed centers, in complete detail by referring to elliptic functions.Recently the discussion has been revived by Sémirot (1949)，Deprit (l962)，Alekseev (1965)，Degtjarow and Ewdokimowa (l966),and Marchal (l966); some have classified various kinds of possible real motions in three dimensions.
