When three finite masses form the equilibrium figures of Lagrange or Euler,the position of the fourth body for equilibrium is discussed as the $( 3 + 1 )$ -body problem. We have already treated it analytically; it will be discussed algebraically in a later chapter with the use of elliptic functions.

When all $\pmb { n }$ masses are equal, that is, $m _ { 1 } = m _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = m _ { n } = 1 / n ,$ there can occur a homographic motion of each mass at each vertex of a regular $\pmb { n }$ -sided polygon.If the radius of the circumscribed circle be taken as the unit of measurement, then (l2) is satisfed by

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle x _ { i } = \cos \displaystyle \frac { 2 i \pi } { n } , \qquad y _ { i } = \sin \displaystyle \frac { 2 i \pi } { n } \qquad ( i = 0 , 1 , . . . , n - 1 ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle r _ { i j } = 2 \sin \left( i - j \right) \displaystyle \frac { \pi } { n } \qquad ( i > j ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle k ^ { 2 } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 4 n } \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \sin \left( i \pi / n \right) } . } } \end{array}
$$

By supposing that $m _ { n + 1 } = 0$ , we can discuss the $( n + 1 )$ -body problem in this case.

Summarizing,we see that a homographic motion of all particles in one and the same plane is at any instant a plane motion in that same plane and can occur only when the total angular momentum is not zero. If each of the masses describes a circular motion around the center of mass, the particles form an equilibrium configuration of a central figure referred to the rotating coordinate axes.If each mass describes an elliptic motion, the particles form similar configurations at each instant referred to the rotating coordinate axes.

![](images/1e45ffb6c1ac5623b26bd70c48b54c1743056ffa401a19540f34ea37d70c44e5.jpg)  
FIGURE 8

# SPACE HOMOGRAPHIC MOTION

3.6CONDITION FOR SPACE CONFIGURATIONS

Suppose that $\pmb { n }$ bodies are not in one plane at a certain instant.Let the instantaneous rotation axis be the $\zeta .$ axis; then $\omega _ { 1 } = \omega _ { 2 } = 0$ ， $\omega _ { 3 } \neq 0$ and equations (2) of Section 3.l are now

$$
\begin{array} { r } { x _ { \mathrm { * } } \Big ( \lambda \frac { d ^ { 2 } \lambda } { d t ^ { 2 } } - \lambda ^ { 2 } \omega _ { \mathrm { * } } ^ { 2 } \Big ) - y _ { \mathrm { * } } \frac { d } { d t } \left( \lambda ^ { 2 } \omega _ { \mathrm { * } } \right) = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \lambda } \sum _ { j } ^ { \prime } m _ { j } \frac { x _ { j } - x _ { i } } { r _ { i j } ^ { 3 } } , } \\ { x _ { \mathrm { * } } \frac { d } { d t } \left( \lambda ^ { 2 } \omega _ { \mathrm { * } } \right) + y _ { \mathrm { * } } \Big ( \lambda \frac { d ^ { 2 } \lambda } { d t ^ { 2 } } - \lambda ^ { 2 } \omega _ { \mathrm { * } } ^ { 2 } \Big ) = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \lambda } \sum _ { j } ^ { \prime } m _ { j } \frac { y _ { j } - y _ { i } } { r _ { i j } ^ { 3 } } , } \\ { z _ { \mathrm { * } } \lambda \frac { d ^ { 2 } \lambda } { d t ^ { 2 } } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \lambda } \sum _ { j } ^ { \prime } m _ { j } \frac { z _ { j } - z _ { i } } { r _ { i j } ^ { 3 } } . } \end{array}
$$

Multiply the first equation by $- { m _ { i } y _ { i } }$ , and the second equation by $m _ { i } x _ { i }$ and add; then the sum of the right-hand members is zero,and

$$
\sum _ { i } m _ { i } ( x _ { i } ^ { 2 } + y _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) { \frac { d } { d t } } ( \lambda ^ { 2 } \omega _ { 3 } ) = 0 .
$$

Hence,

$$
\lambda ^ { 2 } \omega _ { 3 } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } .
$$

Similarly to the plane homographic motion, there is a relation

$$
\lambda { \frac { d ^ { 2 } \lambda } { d t ^ { 2 } } } - \lambda ^ { 2 } \omega _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } = { \frac { 1 } { \lambda } } \times \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ;
$$

then the third equation of (28) gives

$$
\lambda ^ { 3 } \omega _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } .
$$

From (29) and (30), either $\lambda =$ constant, or $\omega _ { 3 } = 0$ In the first case $\omega _ { 3 }$ is also constant; the third equation of (28) gives

$$
\sum _ { j } ^ { \prime } m _ { j } \frac { z _ { j } - z _ { i } } { r _ { i j } ^ { 3 } } = 0 \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Multiply each of these equations by $m _ { i } z _ { i }$ and sum over $_ i$ ; then

$$
\sum _ { i } \sum _ { j } \frac { m _ { i } m _ { j } } { r _ { i j } } = 0 ,
$$

which is impossible. Hence $\omega _ { 3 } = 0$ . In the second case, in addition.

$$
\lambda ^ { 2 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } \lambda } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - K ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } .
$$

Each particle performs a Keplerian motion on a straight line through the center of mass and fixed in space, that is, the motion is homothetic with $n \geq 4 .$ .The total angular momentum is zero. The configuration of the masses at each instant remains always similar and similarly situated with regard to the center of mass.

To summarize the line and the plane homographic motions: the homographic motion with zero total angular momentum is a homothetic motion on fixed straight lines through the center of mass,each mass at a vertex of a similar and similarly situated polyhedron at each instant, and the homographic motion with nonvanishing total angular momentum is either the plane or the line homographic motion,each mass lying on a straight line or forming a central figure.Homographic motions in which all the masses are on one and the same plane at any instant are plane homographic motions.Homographic motions in which not all of the masses are on one and the same plane at an instant are homothetic motions on fixed straight lines through the center of mass；the masses form a similar and similarly situated polyhedron at each instant,and the total angular momentum is zero. Such a motion is a special case of the line homographic motion in which the total angular momentum is zero. Central figures can occur only when the total angular momentum is not zero.

The condition for space homographic motion is

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { { \sum _ { j } } ^ { \prime } m _ { j } { \frac { x _ { j } - x _ { i } } { r _ { i j } ^ { 3 } } } + K ^ { 2 } x _ { i } = 0 } } \\ { { { \sum _ { j } } ^ { \prime } m _ { j } { \frac { y _ { j } - y _ { i } } { r _ { i j } ^ { 3 } } } + K ^ { 2 } y _ { i } = 0 \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) . } } \\ { { { \sum _ { j } } ^ { \prime } m _ { j } { \frac { z _ { j } - z _ { i } } { r _ { i j } ^ { 3 } } } + K ^ { 2 } z _ { i } = 0 } } \end{array}
$$

With the same definition of $X _ { i } , ~ Y _ { i } , ~ Z _ { i }$ as in Section 3.2

$$
\sum _ { i } m _ { i } x _ { i } = \sum _ { i } m _ { i } y _ { i } = \sum _ { i } m _ { i } z _ { i } = 0 ,
$$

$$
\sum _ { i } m _ { i } ( y _ { i } X _ { i } - x _ { i } Y _ { i } ) = \sum _ { i } m _ { i } ( z _ { i } Y _ { i } - y _ { i } Z _ { i } ) = \sum _ { i } m _ { i } ( x _ { i } Z _ { i } - z _ { i } X _ { i } ) = 0 .
$$

Hence, (31) gives only $( 3 n - 6 )$ independent equations. From these,

$$
{ \sum _ { k } } ^ { m } m _ { k } ( i , j , h , k ) \bigl ( r _ { i k } ^ { - 3 } - r _ { j k } ^ { - 3 } \bigr ) = 0 \qquad ( i , j , h , k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

where $\sum ^ { m }$ denotes the sum over $k$ equal to neither $_ i$ nor $j _ { \mathrm { { i } } }$ nor $h$ and $( i , j , h , k )$ denotes six times the volume of the tetrahedron formed by $m _ { i } , m _ { j } , m _ { h } , m _ { k }$ .From (31） we see that

$$
U = \sum _ { i } \sum _ { j } { \frac { m _ { i } m _ { j } } { r _ { i j } } } + { \frac { K ^ { 2 } } { 2 M } } \sum _ { i } \sum _ { j } { m _ { i } m _ { j } r _ { i j } ^ { 2 } }
$$

should be an extremum.Hence we can prove Laplace's theorem of Section 3.4 for such space homographic motions. The theorem is also true for line homographic motions.

Lehmann-Filhes （l89l）and Bilimovitch （l911） proved that the condition is satisfied for $n = 4$ when al mutual distances $\boldsymbol { r } _ { i j }$ are equal, that is, when all masses form a regular tetrahedron. Recently Brumberg (1958) has again shown the existence of such a configuration.

For $n = 5$ , form (32) and put $r _ { 1 3 } = r _ { 1 4 } = a _ { ; }$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { m _ { 1 } ( 2 3 4 1 ) ( r _ { 1 2 } ^ { - 3 } - r _ { 1 3 } ^ { - 3 } ) + m _ { 5 } ( 2 3 4 5 ) ( r _ { 2 5 } ^ { - 3 } - r _ { 3 5 } ^ { - 3 } ) = 0 , } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { m _ { 1 } ( 2 3 5 1 ) ( r _ { 1 2 } ^ { - 3 } - r _ { 1 3 } ^ { - 3 } ) + m _ { 4 } ( 2 3 5 4 ) ( r _ { 2 4 } ^ { - 3 } - r _ { 3 4 } ^ { - 3 } ) = 0 . } } \end{array}
$$

From these, either $( 2 3 4 5 ) = 0$ or $r _ { 2 4 } = r _ { 3 4 } = b$ ， $r _ { 2 5 } = r _ { 3 5 } = c$ In the first case, the four points 2,3,4,5,are all in one and the same plane and $r _ { 1 2 } = r _ { 1 3 } = r _ { 1 4 } = r _ { 1 5 } = a$ In the second case, either $m _ { 2 } = m _ { 3 }$ or $a = b = c$ ; in the latter event, either $( 1 2 4 5 ) = ( 1 3 4 5 ) = 0$ or

$$
r _ { 1 4 } = r _ { 1 5 } = r _ { 4 5 } .
$$

This last condition is satisfied either for $m _ { 2 } = m _ { 3 }$ or for ${ m _ { 1 } = m _ { 4 } = m _ { 5 } }$ Such a configuration has been obtained by Brehm (1908).

If the four masses form a regular tetrahedron at one instant and approach to or recede from the center of masses with velocities proportional to their respective distances from the center of masses,then the

condition (33) is satisfied, as in the case of plane homographic motion.   
This theorem was proved by Lehmann-Filhes (189l) and Dziobek (1900).

Bilimovitch (1927,1929) proved the existence of homographic motions for these two cases: (i) when two sets of equal masses are situated at the vertices of the corresponding two similar and similarly situated regular polyhedrons,and (ii) when one set of equal masses are at the vertices of a regular polyhedron and the other set of equal masses are at the vertices of its conjugate regular polyhedron, that is,a polyhedron obtained by joining the middle points of the surfaces of the original polyhedron (for example,a regular tetrahedron and a regular octahedron,a regular hexahedron and a regular octahedron).Furthermore, the existence of homographic motions was proved for two regular polyhedrons, the second being obtained by joining the middle points of the edges of the original polyhedron (for example,a regular tetrahedron and a regular octahedron). These are not equilibrium configurations.

There seem to be no existing celestial bodies with such space homo-graphic motions.But it has been proved that,in the immediate neighborhood of simultaneous collisions of all $_ { n }$ bodies at a point,or at infinity when all $\pmb { n }$ bodies recede from the finite region,the $\pmb { n }$ bodies tend indefinitely to one of such homographic configurations.In such cases the total angular momentum is zero,as will be proved later. Sundman proved that in two-body collisions the motion can be analytically continued after the collisions,but in collisions of three or more bodies the motion may become complex if the masses and the initial conditions do not satisfy certain restrictions,and the motion cannot be analytically continued after the collisions. Such studies have been carried out by Sundman，Block，Freundlich,Chazy，Merman，and others,as will be discussed in a later chapter.

# ISOSCELES TRIANGULAR SOLUTIONS

# 3.7REGULAR AND IRREGULAR SINGULARITIES

Suppose that the coefficients ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { 0 } ( x ) , { \mathfrak { p } } _ { 1 } ( x ) , \ldots \ldots , { \mathfrak { p } } _ { n } ( x )$ of the differential equation

$$
p _ { 0 } ( x ) { \frac { d _ { n } y } { d x ^ { n } } } + p _ { 1 } ( x ) { \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } y } { d x ^ { n - 1 } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + p _ { n } ( x ) y = 0
$$

are uniform analytic functions with only isolated singularities in the complex plane.The equation is said to be analytic if the coefficients are analytic.A point $x = \xi$ where all coefficients are holomorphic, that is, single-valued and continuous, is called an ordinary point; all other points are called singular. At an ordinary point, the coefficients are expanded in the form

$$
{ p } _ { i } ( x ) = \sum _ { k = 0 } ^ { \infty } { p } _ { i } ^ { ( k ) } ( x - \xi ) ^ { k } \qquad ( i = 0 , 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

convergent for $| x - \xi | < d ( \xi )$ ，where $d ( \xi )$ is the shortest distance between the point $x = \xi$ and the nearest singular point. At an ordinary point $x = \xi _ { i }$ ，the analytic linear differential equation (34) is satisfied by the power series

$$
y ( x ) = \phi ( x ) = \sum _ { k = 0 } ^ { \infty } c ^ { ( k ) } ( x - \xi ) ^ { k } ,
$$

which is uniquely determined by the initial condition. This series is convergent for $| x - \xi | < d ( \xi )$ . Every solution $y = \phi ( { \pmb x } )$ is analytic. Its only singularities in the finite part of the complex plane are due to the singularities of the coefficients.

The necessary and sufficient condition that an isolated singularity of equation (34) of canonical form can be regular is that it should be at most a pole of order $\pmb { r }$ of the coefficient ${ p } _ { \tau } ( x )$ of $d ^ { n - r } y / d x ^ { n - r }$ in the canonical form in which the highest order coefficient is $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { 0 } ( \pmb { x } ) \equiv 1$ .This is called Fuchs's condition (Hefter，1894；Schlesinger，1908,.1922; Ince,l927；Poole,1936)．A differential equation satisfying Fuchs's condition is said to be of Fuchsian type.

At an ordinary point $x = \xi$ we can construct $_ { n }$ independent solutions, with arbitrary initial values that are not linearly connected,such that any other solution can be written

$$
y = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } c _ { i } \phi _ { i } ( x ) ,
$$

with constants $c _ { i }$ 、Any such system of $\pmb { n }$ solutions is called a fundamental system of solutions. The solutions $\phi _ { 1 } ( x ) , . . . , \phi _ { n } ( x )$ are said to be linearly connected if there exist constants $c _ { 1 } , . . . , c _ { n } $ ，not all zero, such that

$$
\sum _ { i \mathop { = } 1 } ^ { n } c _ { i } \phi _ { i } ( x ) \equiv 0 .
$$

If there are $\pmb { n }$ functions that are differentiable $n - 1$ times, then,

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } c _ { i } { \frac { d ^ { j - 1 } \phi _ { i } ( x ) } { d x ^ { j - 1 } } } = 0 \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Eliminating $c _ { 1 } , \ldots , c _ { n } ,$ we obtain

$$
W ( \phi _ { 1 } , . . . , \phi _ { n } ) \equiv \left| \begin{array} { c c c c } { { \phi _ { 1 } } } & { { \phi _ { 2 } } } & { { \phi _ { n } } } \\ { { \frac { d \phi _ { 1 } } { d x } } } & { { \frac { d \phi _ { 2 } } { d x } } } & { { \frac { d \phi _ { n } } { d x } } } \\ { { \vdots } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { { \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } \phi _ { 1 } } { d x ^ { n - 1 } } \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } \phi _ { 2 } } { d x ^ { n - 1 } } } } & { { \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } \phi _ { n } } { d x ^ { n - 1 } } } } \end{array} \right| = 0 .
$$

This is a Wronskian and is a necessary condition for the functions $\phi _ { 1 } , . . . , \phi _ { n }$ to be linearly connected.If $\pmb { n }$ functions $\phi _ { 1 } ( x ) , . . . , \phi _ { n } ( x )$ are differentiable $n - 1$ times and if $W ( \phi _ { 1 } , . . . , \phi _ { n } ) \equiv 0$ but

$$
W ( \phi _ { 1 } , . . . , \phi _ { n - 1 } ) \neq 0 ,
$$

then the functions are connected by a linear relation with constant coefficients.Let $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { 0 } ( \pmb { x } ) \equiv 1$ ; then

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { d W } { d x } = \displaystyle \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left[ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial W } { \partial \phi _ { i } ^ { ( j - 1 ) } } \phi _ { i } ^ { ( j ) } ( x ) \right] = \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial W } { \partial \phi _ { i } ^ { ( n - 1 ) } } \phi _ { i } ^ { ( n ) } ( x ) } \\ { = \displaystyle - \phi _ { 1 } ( x ) \left[ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial W } { \partial \phi _ { i } ^ { ( n - 1 ) } } \phi _ { i } ^ { ( n - 1 ) } ( x ) \right] = - \phi _ { 1 } ( x ) \cdot W , } \end{array}
$$

where $\phi _ { i } ^ { ( j ) }$ denotes the jth derivative of $\phi _ { i }$ Hence,

$$
W ( x ) = W ( \xi ) \exp \left[ - \int _ { \xi } ^ { x } \beta _ { 1 } ( t ) d t \right] .
$$

This relation is due to Abel and Liouville. If ${ \pmb \beta } _ { 1 } ( { \pmb x } )$ is continuous, then $W ( \phi _ { 1 } , . . . , \phi _ { n } )$ vanishes either identically or not at all. The necessary and sufficient condition for $\pmb { n }$ solutions $\phi _ { 1 } ( x ) , . . . , \phi _ { n } ( x )$ to form a fundamental system of solutions of (34) with $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { 0 } ( \pmb { x } ) \equiv 1$ is that their Wronskian should not vanish at an arbitrary chosen point $\pmb { x } = \pmb { \xi }$ ·

Consider a linear transformation

$$
\Phi _ { i } ( x ) = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i j } \phi _ { j } ( x ) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

corresponding to any selected circuit $\Gamma$ on the complex plane. Consider also

$$
y = c _ { 1 } \phi _ { 1 } ( x ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + c _ { n } \phi _ { n } ( x )
$$

such that

$$
\Bigl [ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } c _ { i } \Phi _ { i } ( x ) \Bigr ] = \lambda \Bigl [ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } c _ { i } \phi _ { i } ( x ) \Bigr ] .
$$

Since $\phi _ { 1 } ( x ) , . . . , \phi _ { n } ( x )$ are linearly independent,

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } c _ { i } a _ { i j } = \lambda c _ { j } \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

If this system of equations for $c _ { 1 } , \ldots , c _ { n }$ is compatible， then $\lambda$ must satisfy

$$
\Delta _ { n } ( \lambda ) = \left| \begin{array} { c c c c c } { { a _ { 1 1 } - \lambda } } & { { a _ { 2 1 } } } & { { } } & { { } } & { { a _ { n 1 } } } \\ { { a _ { 1 2 } } } & { { a _ { 2 2 } - \lambda } } & { { } } & { { a _ { n 2 } } } \\ { { \vdots } } & { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { { a _ { 1 n } } } & { { a _ { 2 n } } } & { { } } & { { a _ { n n } - \lambda } } \end{array} \right| = 0 ,
$$

which is called the characteristic equation for the matrix $( a _ { i j } )$ . No root can be zero, since $\left\| a _ { i j } \right\| \neq 0$ .Each root has at least one linear form (36). When the characteristic equation admits $\pmb { n }$ distinct roots,then there are $\pmb { n }$ linearly independent combinations of $\phi _ { 1 } , . . . , \phi _ { n } ;$ ，which form a new fundamental system obeying the canonical transformation $Y _ { i } = \lambda _ { i } y _ { i }$ $( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ . The root $\lambda$ should be independent of the choice of the original fundamental system $\phi _ { 1 } ( x ) , . . . , \phi _ { n } ( x )$ . For a canonical solution, $y _ { i }$ is provided with the same multiplier $\lambda _ { j }$ ， whether it is expressed in terms of $\phi _ { 1 } ( x ) , . . . , \phi _ { n } ( x )$ or of some other fundamental system

$$
\psi _ { 1 } ( x ) , . . . , \psi _ { n } ( x )
$$

admiting the transformation with constant coefficients $b _ { i j }$ ：

$$
\Psi _ { i } ( x ) = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } b _ { i j } \psi _ { j } ( x ) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Suppose that $\boldsymbol { x } = 0$ is a singularity where Fuchs's condition is satisfied. Consider the normal form

$$
x ^ { n } { \frac { d ^ { n } y } { d x ^ { n } } } + P _ { 1 } ( x ) x ^ { n - 1 } { \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } y } { d x ^ { n - 1 } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + P _ { n } ( x ) y = 0 ,
$$

where $P _ { 1 } ( x ) , . . . , P _ { n } ( x )$ are holomorphic at $x = 0$ .Let $\delta \equiv { \boldsymbol { x } } ( d / d \boldsymbol { x } )$ ; then

$$
x ^ { m } \frac { d ^ { m } } { d x ^ { m } } = \delta ( \delta - 1 ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( \delta - m + 1 ) ,
$$

so that (38) becomes

$$
F ( x , \delta ) y \equiv \delta ^ { n } y + Q _ { 1 } ( x ) \delta ^ { n - 1 } y + \cdot \cdot \cdot + Q _ { n } ( x ) y = 0 .
$$

The coefficients $Q _ { 1 } , . . . , Q _ { n }$ are linear functions of $P _ { 1 } , . . . , P _ { n }$ .Let

$$
Q _ { i } ( x ) = \sum _ { j = 0 } ^ { \infty } Q _ { i j } x ^ { j } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { F _ { 0 } ( \delta ) = \delta ^ { n } + Q _ { 1 0 } \delta ^ { n - 1 } + Q _ { 2 0 } \delta ^ { n - 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + Q _ { n 0 } , } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { F _ { j } ( \delta ) = Q _ { 1 j } \delta ^ { n - 1 } + Q _ { 2 j } \delta ^ { n - 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + Q _ { n j } ; } } \end{array}
$$

then (38a) becomes

$$
F ( x , \delta ) y = \sum _ { j = 0 } ^ { \infty } x ^ { j } F _ { j } ( \delta ) y = 0 .
$$

Substituting a solution

$$
y = x ^ { \rho } ( c _ { 0 } + c _ { 1 } x + c _ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot )
$$

gives

$$
\begin{array} { c l } { { { \displaystyle F ( x , \delta ) y = \sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { \infty } \sum _ { j = 0 } ^ { \infty } x ^ { j } F _ { j } ( \delta ) c _ { i } x ^ { \rho + i } = \sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { \infty } \sum _ { j = 0 } ^ { \infty } x ^ { \rho + i + j } F _ { j } ( \rho + i ) c _ { i } } } } \\ { { { { } } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } = \sum _ { k = 0 } ^ { \infty } x ^ { \rho + k } \left[ \sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { k } F _ { k - i } ( \rho + i ) c _ { i } \right] = 0 . } } \end{array}
$$

This vanishes identically ifeach coefficient is zero,orif $c _ { 0 } , c _ { 1 } , \ldots$ satisfy

$$
\sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { k } F _ { k - i } ( \rho + i ) c _ { i } = 0 \qquad ( k = 0 , 1 , 2 , \ldots ) .
$$

This is called the indicial equation or the fundamental characteristic equation,and the roots $\pmb { \rho }$ are the exponents of the given singularity. If the solution in the form (39) contains only a finite number or none of negative powers of $_ { x }$ at a singular point $( x = 0$ in the example),the singularity is called regular. Otherwise it is irregular.

The number of linearly independent regular solutions (39） cannot exceed the number of distinct roots of the indicial equation.One distinct power series (39) at most is associated with each distinct exponent.

If $F _ { 0 } ( \rho ) = 0 _ { : }$ $F _ { 0 } ( \rho + k ) \neq 0$ ， $( k = 1 , 2 , \ldots )$ ， then we may choose $c _ { 0 } \neq 0$ ； all subsequent coefficients $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , \ldots$ are uniquely determined. If several exponents differ by integers, then (4O) may or may not be compatible and the number of distinct series (39) may be smaller than the number of distinct roots. If the indicial equation has multiple roots, then the deficiency of regular solutions of the type (39) is supplied by solutions involving logarithms.

Let

$$
\lambda = \exp \left( 2 \pi i \rho \right)
$$

and

$$
y = x ^ { \rho } ( u _ { 0 } + u _ { 1 } x + u _ { 2 } x ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) ,
$$

where $u _ { 0 } , u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots$ are polynomials in $\log x$ of degree lower than $\pmb { n }$ Then

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { F ( x , \delta ) y = \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { \infty } \sum _ { j = 0 } ^ { \infty } x ^ { j } F _ { j } ( \delta ) x ^ { \rho + i } u _ { i } = \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { \infty } \sum _ { j = 0 } ^ { \infty } x ^ { \rho + i + j } F _ { j } ( \delta + \rho + i ) u _ { i } } } \\ { { = \displaystyle \sum _ { k = 0 } ^ { \infty } x ^ { \rho + k } \displaystyle \left[ \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { k } F _ { k - i } ( \delta + \rho + i ) u _ { i } \right] , } } \end{array}
$$

which vanishes identically if $u _ { 0 } , u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots$ satisfy

$$
\sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { k } F _ { k - i } ( \delta + \rho + i ) u _ { i } = 0 \qquad ( k = 0 , 1 , 2 , \ldots ) .
$$

This is a system of linear differential equations with constant coefficients in the independent variable $t = \log x _ { 1 }$

The equation for $k = 0$ is written

$$
F _ { 0 } ( \delta + \rho ) u _ { 0 } \equiv F _ { 0 } ( \rho ) u _ { 0 } + \frac { 1 } { 1 ! } F _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } ( \rho ) \delta u _ { 0 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 ! } F _ { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime } ( \rho ) \delta ^ { 2 } u _ { 0 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot = 0 .
$$

If ${ \boldsymbol { \mathscr { u } } } _ { 0 }$ is a polynomial in $t$ not identically zero, then this expression is a polynomial of the same degree unless $F _ { 0 } ( \rho ) = 0$ For a solution, $\pmb { \rho }$ must satisfy the indicial equation. If we identify $\pmb { \rho }$ with the lowest root of the set $\rho = \rho _ { 1 }$ ，then the series belonging to the higher roots appears. Since $F _ { 0 } ( \delta + \rho _ { 1 } ) u _ { 0 } = G _ { 1 } ( \delta ) \delta ^ { \kappa _ { 1 } } u _ { 0 } ,$ ，where the polynomial $G _ { 1 } ( 0 ) \neq 0$ ，the polynomial $u _ { 0 }$ must satisfy $\delta ^ { \kappa _ { 1 } } u _ { 0 } = 0$ .The structure of the solution is determined by the first $N + 1$ relations, where $N$ is the difference between the highest and the lowest exponents of the set.

For the point at infinity, put $x = 1 / z , \ : \delta ^ { \prime } = z ( d / d z ) = - x ( d / d x ) - \delta$ in (38a); then

$$
\delta ^ { \prime } { } ^ { n } y - Q _ { 1 } ( 1 / z ) \delta ^ { \prime } { } ^ { n - 1 } y + \cdot \cdot \cdot + ( - 1 ) ^ { n } Q _ { n } ( 1 / z ) y = 0 .
$$


<!-- chunk 0005: pages 281-350 -->
The necessary and sufficient condition in order that $z = 0$ ,i.e. $x = \infty$ ， shall be regular, is that $Q _ { 1 } ( \infty ) , Q _ { 2 } ( \infty ) , \ldots .$ be finite. In the canonical form (34) with $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { 0 } ( \pmb { x } ) = 1$ ， this means that $x p _ { 1 } ( x ) , x ^ { 2 } p _ { 2 } ( x ) , . . . , x ^ { n } p _ { n } ( x )$ should remain finite for $x \to \infty$

Let

$$
\psi ( x ) \equiv ( x - a _ { 1 } ) ( x - a _ { 2 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( x - a _ { m } ) ,
$$

where $a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots , a _ { m }$ are the singularities at finite distances from the origin. The necessary and sufficient condition for these to be regular is that $\psi ^ { r } \pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { r } ( \pmb { x } ) ~ ( r = 1 , 2 , . ~ . ~ . , n )$ should be holomorphic for all finite values of $_ x$ . These expressions are then integral functions of $_ { x }$ But,since $x ^ { r } p _ { r } ( x ) \ ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ are finite at infinity,

$$
[ \psi ( x ) ] ^ { \tau } \dot { p } _ { r } ( x ) = \Pi _ { \scriptscriptstyle ( m - 1 ) r } ( x ) \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

where $\Pi _ { \alpha } ( x )$ means a polynomial of degree $\propto$

The equation can then be written

$$
\psi ^ { n } { \frac { d ^ { n } y } { d x ^ { n } } } + \Pi _ { m - 1 } \psi ^ { n - 1 } { \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } y } { d x ^ { n - 1 } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \Pi _ { m n - n } y = 0
$$

with polynomial coeffcients. If $n = 1$ , that is,if the equation is linear, then

$$
\quad p _ { 1 } ( x ) \equiv { \frac { \Pi _ { m - 1 } ( x ) } { \psi ( x ) } } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { \frac { A _ { i } } { ( x - a _ { i } ) } } ,
$$

and the indicial equation at $\textstyle x = a _ { i }$ is

$$
\rho ( \rho - 1 ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( \rho - n + 1 ) + A _ { i } \rho ( \rho - 1 ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( \rho - n + 2 ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot = 0 ,
$$

so that the sum of the exponents at $\textstyle x = a _ { i }$ is $[ n ( n - 1 ) / 2 - A _ { i } ]$ . The indicial equation at infinity is

$$
+ 1 ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( \rho + n - 1 ) - \biggl ( \sum _ { i } { \cal A } _ { i } \biggr ) \rho ( \rho + 1 ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( \rho + n - 2 ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot = 0 .
$$

Hence the sum of the exponents at infinity is $\textstyle \sum _ { i } A _ { i } - n ( n - 1 ) / 2$ Thus the sum of all $( m + 1 ) n$ exponents is

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m + 1 } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \rho _ { i j } = ( m - 1 ) n ( n - 1 ) / 2 ,
$$

where $\rho _ { i j }$ denotes the jth rootat the singularity $\textstyle x = a _ { i }$

The most general second-order equation of the Fuchsian type is

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \Pi _ { m - 1 } } { \psi } \frac { d y } { d x } + \frac { \Pi _ { 2 m - 2 } } { \psi ^ { 2 } } y = 0 ,
$$

or

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } } + \biggl [ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } { \frac { A _ { i } } { ( x - a _ { i } ) } } \biggr ] { \frac { d y } { d x } } + \biggl [ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } { \frac { B _ { i } } { ( x - a _ { i } ) ^ { 2 } } } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } { \frac { C _ { i } } { ( x - a _ { i } ) } } \biggr ] y = 0 ,
$$

where

$$
\sum _ { i } C _ { i } = 0 .
$$

At ${ \boldsymbol { x } } = a _ { i }$ the indicial equation is

$$
\rho ( \rho - 1 ) + A _ { i } \rho + B _ { i } = 0 .
$$

Let the roots be ${ \boldsymbol { \rho } } = \rho _ { i }$ and $\rho = \rho _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ ; then the equation is of the canonical form

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \bigg [ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \bigg ( \frac { 1 - \rho _ { i } - \rho _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { x - a _ { i } } \bigg ) \bigg ] \frac { d y } { d x } + \bigg [ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \frac { \rho _ { i } \rho _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { ( x - a _ { i } ) ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \Pi _ { 2 m - 2 } } { \psi } \bigg ] y = 0 .
$$

The indicial equation at infinity is

$$
\rho ( \rho + 1 ) - \Bigl ( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } A _ { i } \Bigr ) \rho + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } ( B _ { i } + C _ { i } a _ { i } ) = 0 .
$$

Fuchs's condition is

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } ( 1 - \rho _ { i } - \rho _ { i } ^ { \prime } ) + ( 1 - \rho _ { \infty } - \rho _ { \infty } ^ { \prime } ) = 2 .
$$

The equations of Gauss, Lamé, Legendre, and Bessel, that appear in mathematical physics, are special cases of such Fuchsian-type equations. (Klein,1894,1933; Hagihara, 1970).

In order to solve the nonhomogeneous equation

$$
\frac { d ^ { n } y } { d x ^ { n } } + \beta _ { 1 } ( x ) \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } y } { d x ^ { n - 1 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \beta _ { n } ( x ) y = f ( x ) ,
$$

let

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { j - 1 } y } { d x ^ { j - 1 } } } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } u _ { i } { \frac { d ^ { j - 1 } \phi _ { i } ( x ) } { d x ^ { j - 1 } } } \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

This equation determines uniquely the functions $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { n } ;$ since $W ( \phi _ { 1 } , . . . , \phi _ { n } ) \neq 0 .$ . We also have

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { 0 = \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \displaystyle \frac { d u _ { i } } { d x } \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { j - 1 } \phi _ { i } ( x ) } { d x ^ { j - 1 } } ~ ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - 1 ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { n } y } { d x ^ { n } } = \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left[ u _ { i } \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { n } \phi _ { i } ( x ) } { d x ^ { n } } + \displaystyle \frac { d u _ { i } } { d x } \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } \phi _ { i } ( x ) } { d x ^ { n - 1 } } \right] . } } \end{array}
$$

Substituting these equations in (34) with $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { 0 } ( \pmb { x } ) = 1$ , we obtain

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { \frac { d u _ { i } } { d x } } { \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } \phi _ { i } ( x ) } { d x ^ { n - 1 } } } = f ( x ) .
$$

Hence

$$
\frac { d u _ { i } } { d x } = f ( x ) \frac { 1 } { W ( x ) } \frac { \partial W ( x ) } { \partial \phi _ { i } { } ^ { ( n - 1 ) } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

where $W ( { \pmb x } )$ denotes the Wronskian in (35a). If the principal solution at $x = \xi ,$ say $y = \Phi ( x ; \xi )$ ,is determined by the condition

$$
\frac { d ^ { j - 1 } y } { d x ^ { j - 1 } } = 0 \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

for $\begin{array} { r } { \pmb { x } = \pmb { \xi } , } \end{array}$ ，then $u _ { 1 } = u _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = u _ { n } = 0$ for $\pmb { x } = \pmb { \xi }$ .Hence

$$
u _ { i } = \int _ { \xi } ^ { x } \frac { f ( t ) } { W ( t ) } \frac { \partial W ( t ) } { \partial \phi _ { i } ^ { ( n - 1 ) } } d t ,
$$

$$
\Phi ( x ; \xi ) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \phi _ { i } ( x ) \int _ { \xi } ^ { x } { \frac { f ( t ) } { W ( t ) } } { \frac { \partial W ( t ) } { \partial \phi _ { i } ^ { ( n - 1 ) } } } d t .
$$

If $\pmb { t }$ is a constant, then

$$
\phi ( x ; t ) \equiv \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { \frac { \phi _ { i } ( x ) } { W ( t ) } } { \frac { \partial W ( t ) } { \partial \phi _ { i } ( \^ { n - 1 ) } } }
$$

gives the solution of the homogeneous equation determined by the initial condition

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { j - 1 } y } { d x ^ { j - 1 } } } = 0 \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - 1 ) , \qquad { \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } y } { d x ^ { n - 1 } } } = 1
$$

for $x = t .$ With this definition of $\phi ( { \pmb x } ; t )$ ,the formula for the principal solution is

$$
\Phi ( x ; \xi ) = \int _ { \xi } ^ { x } \phi ( x ; t ) f ( t ) ~ d t .
$$

Let

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle F y \equiv \phi _ { 0 } \frac { d ^ { n } y } { d x ^ { n } } + \phi _ { 1 } ( x ) \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } y } { d x ^ { n - 1 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \phi _ { n } ( x ) y , } } \\ { { \displaystyle F ^ { * } z \equiv ( - 1 ) ^ { n } \frac { d ^ { n } ( \phi _ { 0 } z ) } { d x ^ { n } } + ( - 1 ) ^ { n - 1 } \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } ( \phi _ { 1 } z ) } { d x ^ { n - 1 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \phi _ { n } z . } } \end{array}
$$

The operators ${ \pmb F }$ and $F ^ { * }$ are said to be adjoint to each other. We have an identity

$$
\begin{array} { r } { z F y - y F ^ { * } z \equiv D \Psi ( y , z ) , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\Psi ( y , z ) \equiv \hbar \ / z \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } y } { d x ^ { n - 1 } } + \biggl [ \hbar \ / z - \frac { d } { d x } ( \not p _ { 0 } z ) \biggr ] \frac { d ^ { n - 2 } y } { d x ^ { n - 2 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot
$$

$$
+ \left[ \beta _ { n - 1 } z - { \frac { d } { d x } } \left( \not p _ { n - 2 } z \right) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + ( - 1 ) ^ { n - 1 } { \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } \left( \not p _ { 0 } z \right) } { d x ^ { n - 1 } } } \right] y
$$

is called the bilinear concomitant.

# 3.8EXISTENCE OF THE SOLUTIONS

So far we have discussed the particular solution of the three-body problem in which the masses are all quite arbitrary.Now we consider a particular solution obtained by restricting the masses，and examine whether there is a solution where the masses form an isosceles triangle. Fransen (1895) discussed three kinds of isosceles-triangular solutions : one with an axis of symmetry, one with a plane of symmetry,and one with both an axis of symmetry and a plane of symmetry- the axis of symmetry being on the plane of symmetry. These are particular cases for equal masses in Euler's solution and in Lagrange's equilateraltriangular solution.Pavanini (l907）and MacMillan （i9l3） calculated the solution for the case with an axis of symmetry, that is,when two of the bodies move in circular orbits on a plane perpendicular to the axis of symmetry and the third body (with infinitesimal mass) moves along the axis of symmetry. Buchanan (l920) obtained periodic solutions and asymptotic solutions in their neighborhood when the two bodies with equal masses move in circular orbits and in elliptic orbits,according to Poincaré's theory of periodic solutions.Wilczynski (l9l3） applied projective differential geometry (Wilczynski, l906；Fubini and Cech, 1926) to a generalization ot Euler's and Lagrange's solutions. Supposing the center of mass of the three bodies to be at rest, he considered the properties of the trajectories of each of the three bodies. Two of these trajectories lie upon the ruled surface described by the straight line joining two of the three bodies,and all three trajectories lie upon the conical surface enveloping the instantaneous orbital planes of the three bodies (Salmon,l9l2).If the velocities of the three bodies are in the plane of the three bodies at each instant, then the orbits are all plane curves and the surface in question coincides with the common plane of the three orbital planes. Wilczynski proved that, except when at least one of the three bodies has a plane curve as its orbit, the case where one of the ruled surfaces is a developable surface or a conical surface occurs only when two sides of the triangle formed by the three bodies are equal. Furthermore, the case where the curve described by one of the three bodies is asymptotic to one of the ruled surfaces occurs only for an isosceles triangle. Seeking the condition under which the ruled surface describes a conical surface, he showed that the vertex of the conical surface recedes to infinity and the surface becomes a cylinder. Then two sides of the triangle become equal and the base of the triangle describes a cylindrical surface which is the ruled surface in question. The ruled surface may sometimes degenerate into a plane or a straight line. From this it is evident that, if two sides of the triangle are equal, then the mass at the vertex of the triangle describes a plane curve passing through the center of mass.This is the case with a plane of symmetry.In other cases where one of the masses describes a curve asymptotic to the ruled surface, the two sides of the triangle meeting at the center of mass should be equal. This is the case with an axis of symmetry. Thus Wilczynski proved the existence of particular solutions for an isosceles triangle. Wilczynski (1913)and MacMillan (l9l3),by studying the zero points and the singular points of the functions appearing in the discusson on the basis of function theory， proved that there is no solution of the three-body problem other than Lagrange's equilateral-triangular solution if two sides of the triangle formed by the three bodies are equal and if at the same time the masses situated at the two ends of the base of the triangle are not equal. We base our discussion of this property on Fuchs's theory oflinear differential equations according to Chazy (1921). This particular solution is important in discussing the collisions of two bodies in the three-body problem,that is, in discussing under what conditions the third mutual distance may become infinitely small without being equal to zero if the other two mutual distances of the three bodies are always larger than a finite length.

Let the two masses on the base of the triangle be $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ and the third mass be $m _ { 3 }$ . Let the rectangular coordinates of $m _ { 2 }$ relative to $m _ { 1 }$ be $x , y$ $z$ and the coordinates of $m _ { 3 }$ relative to the center of masses of $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ be $x _ { 1 } , \ y _ { 1 } ,$ $z _ { 1 }$ . Define $r$ and $r _ { 1 }$ such that $\textstyle r ^ { 2 } = x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 }$ ， $r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + z _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$ Let $R$ be the equal distances of $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ from $m _ { 3 }$ The equations of motion in the Jacobian canonical coordinate system are

$$
\begin{array} { l l l } { { \displaystyle { \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - A x , \quad } } } & { { \displaystyle { \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - A y , \quad } } } & { { \displaystyle { \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - A z , } } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { d ^ { 2 } x _ { 1 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - A _ { 1 } x _ { 1 } , \quad } } } & { { \displaystyle { \frac { d ^ { 2 } y _ { 1 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - A _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } , \quad } } } & { { \displaystyle { \frac { d ^ { 2 } z _ { 1 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - A _ { 1 } z _ { 1 } , } } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
A = { \frac { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } } { r ^ { 3 } } } + { \frac { m _ { 3 } } { R ^ { 3 } } } , \qquad A _ { 1 } = { \frac { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } + m _ { 3 } } { R ^ { 3 } } } .
$$

The integrals of angular momentum are

$$
\begin{array} { r } { y \frac { d z } { d t } - z \frac { d y } { d t } = a , \qquad z \frac { d x } { d t } - x \frac { d z } { d t } = b , \qquad x \frac { d y } { d t } - y \frac { d x } { d t } = c , } \\ { y _ { 1 } \frac { d z _ { 1 } } { d t } - z _ { 1 } \frac { d y _ { 1 } } { d t } = a _ { 1 } , \qquad z _ { 1 } \frac { d x _ { 1 } } { d t } - x _ { 1 } \frac { d z _ { 1 } } { d t } = b _ { 1 } , \quad x _ { 1 } \frac { d y _ { 1 } } { d t } - y _ { 1 } \frac { d x _ { 1 } } { d t } = c _ { 1 } } \end{array}
$$

hence，each mass moves in its own plane.By our assumption， the distances of $m _ { 3 }$ from $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ are equal.

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \left( x _ { 1 } - \frac { m _ { 1 } } { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } } x \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( y _ { 1 } - \frac { m _ { 1 } } { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } } y \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( z _ { 1 } - \frac { m _ { 1 } } { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } } z \right) ^ { 2 } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { = \left( x _ { 1 } + \frac { m _ { 2 } } { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } } x \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( y _ { 1 } + \frac { m _ { 2 } } { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } } y \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( z _ { 1 } + \frac { m _ { 2 } } { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } } z \right) ^ { 2 } } } \end{array}
$$

or

$$
x x _ { 1 } + y y _ { 1 } + z z _ { 1 } = { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { \frac { m _ { 1 } - m _ { 2 } } { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } } } r ^ { 2 } .
$$

If $m _ { 1 } = m _ { 2 } $ ，then the right-hand member is zero.If

$$
A - A _ { 1 } = ( m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } ) \Big ( \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 3 } } - \frac { 1 } { R ^ { 3 } } \Big ) ,
$$

then the three masses form an equilateral triangle of Lagrange.

From (42),

$$
- \left( { \frac { d x } { d t } } x _ { 1 } + { \frac { d y } { d t } } y _ { 1 } + { \frac { d z } { d t } } z _ { 1 } \right) \left( x { \frac { d x _ { 1 } } { d t } } + y { \frac { d y _ { 1 } } { d t } } + z { \frac { d z _ { 1 } } { d t } } \right)
$$

Furthermore, from (4l) and (43),

$$
\dot { p } + q = { \frac { m _ { 1 } - m _ { 2 } } { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } } } r { \frac { d r } { d t } } , \qquad { \frac { d \phi } { d t } } - { \frac { d q } { d t } } = { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { \frac { m _ { 1 } - m _ { 2 } } { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } } } r ^ { 2 } ,
$$

where

$$
\dot { p } = \frac { d x } { d t } x _ { 1 } + \frac { d y } { d t } y _ { 1 } + \frac { d z } { d t } z _ { 1 } , \qquad q = x \frac { d x _ { 1 } } { d t } + y \frac { d y _ { 1 } } { d t } + z \frac { d z _ { 1 } } { d t } .
$$

Expand

$$
{ \frac { d { \boldsymbol { p } } } { d t } } + { \frac { d { \boldsymbol { q } } } { d t } }
$$

and substitute (4l) and (44); then

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \frac { 1 } { 4 } ( \frac { m _ { 1 } - m _ { 2 } } { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } } ) ^ { 2 } [ r \frac { d ^ { 2 } r } { d t ^ { 2 } } + ( \frac { d r } { d t } ) ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( A + A _ { 1 } ) r ^ { 2 } ] - p q } } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { = a a _ { 1 } + b b _ { 1 } + c c _ { 1 } . } \end{array}
$$

Differentiate $( d r / d t ) ^ { 2 }$ and combine the result with (42) and the identity

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \left( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } + z ^ { 2 } \right) \left[ \left( \frac { d x } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d y } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d z } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] } \\ & { \qquad = \left( x \frac { d x } { d t } + y \frac { d y } { d t } + z \frac { d z } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( y \frac { d z } { d t } - z \right. } \\ & { \qquad \quad \left. + \left( z \frac { d x } { d t } - x \frac { d z } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( x \frac { d y } { d t } - y \frac { d x } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } \right. } \end{array}
$$

then,

$$
r \frac { d ^ { 2 } r } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - A r ^ { 2 } + ( a ^ { 2 } + b ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } ) \bigg ( \frac { d r } { d t } \bigg ) ^ { - 2 } .
$$

Substituting these in (46) gives

$$
\frac 1 8 \left( \frac { m _ { 1 } - m _ { 2 } } { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } ( A _ { 1 } - A ) r ^ { 4 } + \frac 1 4 \left( \rlap / p - q \right) ^ { 2 } + \frac 1 4 \left( \frac { m _ { 1 } - m _ { 2 } } { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } ( a ^ { 2 } + b ^ { 2 } + c
$$

Put

$$
R ^ { 2 } = r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \frac { m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } } { ( m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } r ^ { 2 } ;
$$

then (43) and (43a) give the equation corresponding to (47):

$$
r _ { 1 } \frac { d ^ { 2 } r _ { 1 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - A _ { 1 } r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } ( a _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) .
$$

When we substitute $A$ and $A _ { 1 }$ from (4l),we obtain the relations (45), (47), (48), (49),(50) among the unknown quantities $\ p - \ q , r , R , r _ { 1 }$

First suppose that $m _ { 1 } \neq m _ { 2 }$ . There are now five relations among four quantities. We propose that these relations can only be satisfied by Lagrange's solution. For proof, apply Fuchs's theory on the singularities of a linear differential equation. First, take $u = - \int r ^ { - 2 } d t$ as the independent variable instead of $t .$ .This is a regularizing transformation, to be discussed in a later chapter； we only note here that $r = 0$ if $x = y = z = 0 { \mathrm { . } }$ ,that is, if the two $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ bodies collide.This case is trivial; it reduces to the two-body problem.Put

$$
{ \dot { p } } - q = { \frac { m _ { 1 } - m _ { 2 } } { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } } } v .
$$

From (45) and (48),

$$
\frac { d v } { d u } + v ^ { 2 } + ( a ^ { 2 } + b ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } ) - 4 \bigg ( \frac { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } } { m _ { 1 } - m _ { 2 } } \bigg ) ^ { 2 } ( a a _ { 1 } + b b _ { 1 } + c c _ { 1 } ) = 0 .
$$

Then (45) and (47) are transformed to

$$
\frac { 1 } { R ^ { 3 } } = \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 3 } } + \frac { 2 } { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } } \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 4 } } \frac { d v } { d u } ,
$$

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } } { d u ^ { 2 } } \frac { 1 } { r } = A r ^ { 3 } - \frac { 1 } { r } ( a ^ { 2 } + b ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } ) .
$$

Referring to (52) gives the following linear differential equation for $1 / r$ ：

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { 2 } } { d u ^ { 2 } } } { \frac { 1 } { r } } + \left( - { \frac { 2 m _ { 3 } } { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } } } { \frac { d v } { d u } } + a ^ { 2 } + b ^ { 2 } + c ^ { 2 } \right) { \frac { 1 } { r } } = m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } + m _ { 3 } .
$$

Equation (50) takes the form

$$
{ \frac { d } { d u } } \left( { \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } } } { \frac { d r _ { 1 } } { d u } } \right) + A _ { 1 } r ^ { 2 } r _ { 1 } - \left( a _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \right) { \frac { r ^ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } } } = 0 ,
$$

or

$$
{ \frac { d } { d u } } \left( { \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } } } { \frac { d r _ { 1 } } { d u } } \right) + \left( m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } + m _ { 3 } \right) { \frac { r ^ { 2 } r _ { 1 } } { R ^ { 3 } } } - \left( a _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \right) { \frac { r ^ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } } } = 0 .
$$

Thus it is sufficient to prove that the five relations (49), (5l), (52), (54), and (56) among four functions $v , r , R$ ,and $\boldsymbol { r } _ { 1 }$ ,all considered as functions of $\pmb { u }$ ， cannot be satisfied simultaneously except in the Lagrange case.

Suppose that $\boldsymbol { u }$ is a complex variable. If these five equations admit a common solution as u moves on the real axis of the complex plane of $\boldsymbol { u }$ ， then the same five equations are satisfed in the whole complex plane of $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { u } }$ by the principle of analytical continuation. Hence,we consider the common solution in the whole plane of u. At first we see that (51) contains only $v$ .If the solution be a constant or zero, then $1 / r ^ { 3 } - 1 / R ^ { 3 }$ is also zero, by (52), and the case reduces to Lagrange's. If $\boldsymbol { v } ( \boldsymbol { u } )$ is neither a constant nor zero, then by (51） we can continue analytically to the whole plane of $\boldsymbol { u }$ and $v ( u )$ has either a simple pole with residue l or an infinite number of such simple poles.Referring to (54） shows that $1 / r$ can have no singularity other than the singularities of $v$ and can be analytically continued to the whole plane of $\boldsymbol { u }$ .From (52) and (49),we see that $R$ and $r _ { 1 }$ can also be analytically continued to the whole plane of $\boldsymbol { u }$

For the purpose of studying the behavior of $v , r , R , r _ { 1 }$ in the neighborhood of the poles of $v ( u )$ , take a pole of $v$ as the origin $u = 0$ By (51), the expansion of $\boldsymbol { u }$ in the neighborhood of $u = 0$ is of the form

$$
v ( u ) = \frac { 1 } { u } + \alpha _ { 1 } u + \alpha _ { 3 } u ^ { 3 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

Hence,substitution in (54) gives

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { 2 } } { d u ^ { 2 } } } { \frac { 1 } { r } } + \left( { \frac { 2 m _ { 3 } } { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } } } { \frac { 1 } { u ^ { 2 } } } + \beta _ { 0 } + \beta _ { 2 } u ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \right) { \frac { 1 } { r } } = m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } + m _ { 3 } ,
$$

where $\beta _ { 0 } , \beta _ { 2 } , \ldots$ are constants. This is a linear differential equation of Fuchsian type. The fundamental characteristic equation,or indicial equation,in the Fuchsian theory in the neighborhood of a singularity is obtained by replacing $1 / r$ by $u ^ { \lambda } ( 1 + r _ { 1 } u + \cdot \cdot \cdot )$ with constants $r _ { 1 } , . . . ,$ and comparing the coefficients of $u ^ { \lambda - 2 }$ by the methods of Section 3.7, that is,

$$
\lambda ( \lambda - 1 ) + \frac { 2 m _ { 3 } } { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } } = 0 .
$$

Denote the two roots of this equation by $\lambda _ { 1 }$ and $\lambda _ { 2 }$ But (57) has a particular solution $1 / r = f ( u )$ ,which is regular even at $u = 0$ and can be expanded in an ascending power series of $\boldsymbol { u }$ beginning with the first term $( m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } ) u ^ { 2 } / 2$

First suppose that $2 m _ { 3 } / ( m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } ) < 1 / 4$ ； then $\lambda _ { 1 }$ and $\lambda _ { 2 }$ are both real and distinct, and $\textstyle 0 < \lambda _ { 1 } < { \frac { 1 } { 2 } }$ $\ L _ { \bar { 2 } } ^ { 1 } < \lambda _ { 2 } < 1$ . Hence, the general solution of (57) in the neighborhood of $u = 0$ is of the form

$$
\frac { 1 } { r } = C _ { 1 } u ^ { \lambda _ { 1 } } \phi _ { 1 } ( u ) + C _ { 2 } u ^ { \lambda _ { 2 } } \phi _ { 2 } ( u ) + f ( u ) ,
$$

where $C _ { 1 }$ and $C _ { 2 }$ are the integration constants and $\phi _ { 1 }$ and $\phi _ { 2 }$ are integral power series each with the constant term equal to 1.

Next suppose that $2 m _ { 3 } / ( m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } ) > \frac { 1 } { 4 }$ ；then $\lambda _ { 1 }$ and $\lambda _ { 2 }$ are both conjugate complex with real parts $\begin{array} { l } { { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } \end{array}$ .Hence, the general solution of (57) is of the form

$$
\frac { 1 } { r } = \cdot u ^ { 1 / 2 } \{ C _ { 1 } u ^ { i \mu } \phi _ { 1 } ( u ) + C _ { 2 } u ^ { - i \mu } \phi _ { 2 } ( u ) \} + f ( u ) ,
$$

where $\mu$ is a positive constant depending only on the masses.

If $2 m _ { 3 } / ( m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } ) = \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 4 } }$ then $\lambda _ { 1 } = \lambda _ { 2 } = \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } }$ and the general solution of (57) is of the form

$$
\frac { 1 } { r } = u ^ { 1 / 2 } ( C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } \log u ) \phi _ { 1 } ( u ) + C _ { 2 } u ^ { 1 / 2 } \phi _ { 2 } ( u ) + f ( u ) ,
$$

where the constant term of $\phi _ { 2 }$ is zero.

Assume now that $1 / r$ is expressed by a regular integral $f ( u )$ . Corre-sponding to this solution, the functions t $\dot { , } 1 / R ^ { 2 } , R$ and $r _ { 1 }$ , being analytically continued to the same contour of integration,are then expanded in ascending power series of $\boldsymbol { u }$ in the neighborhood of $u = 0$ .The first terms of these expansions, that is, the principal values,will be,according to (49) and (52), $u ^ { 6 + n }$ for $1 / R ^ { 3 }$ ， $u ^ { - 2 - n / 3 }$ for $R$ ， $u ^ { - 2 }$ for $r _ { : }$ and $u ^ { - 2 - n / 3 }$ for $r _ { 1 : }$ ，where $_ { n }$ is an even positive integer.If we substitute these expansions for $r , R$ ,and $r _ { 1 }$ in (56),it becomes an identity. But the orders of the pole at $u = 0$ in these three expansions are $- n / 3 , 2 n / 3$ ,and $2 + n$ ,respectively. Hence,none of them can be an identity for any choice of the even positive integer $n$ Hence, $1 / r$ cannot be expressed by $f ( u )$ ， contrary to our assumption.

Next assume that $1 / r$ as a function of $u$ is expanded in the form (58), with particular values of $C _ { 1 }$ and $C _ { 2 }$ ，of which at least one is different from zero,or in the form (60) with $C _ { 2 } = 0$ ， $C _ { 1 } \neq 0$ As before, $1 / r , \ r ,$ $1 / R ^ { 3 } , R , r _ { 1 }$ can be expanded in ascending power series of $\boldsymbol { u }$ in the neighborhood of $u = 0$ ，where the exponents may be irrational. The first terms of these expansions for $1 / r$ and $\boldsymbol { r }$ are $C u ^ { \lambda }$ and $C ^ { - 1 } u ^ { - \lambda }$ ,respectively. Here it is supposed that $C$ is a constant different from zero. $\lambda$ is equal to $\lambda _ { 1 }$ or $\lambda _ { 2 }$ ,or equal to $\scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } }$ .According to (52） the principal value of $1 / R ^ { 3 }$ is

$$
- \frac { 2 C ^ { 4 } } { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } } u ^ { 4 \lambda - 2 } ,
$$

and that of $R$ is

$$
\left( - \frac { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } } { 2 C ^ { 4 } } u ^ { 2 - 4 \lambda } \right) ^ { 1 / 3 } .
$$

Thus in the neighborhood of $u = 0$ the function $r$ is more important than $R$ .By (49),the principal value of $\boldsymbol { r } _ { 1 }$ is equal to that of $k r$ where

$$
k = \frac { \sqrt { - m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } } } { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } } .
$$

By continuing such arguments we see that (55） cannot be an identity unless $C = 0$ .Thus we are led again to a contradiction.

Finally,assume that the expansion of $1 / r$ in the neighborhood of $u = 0$ is of the form (59),where at least one of $C _ { 1 }$ and $C _ { 2 }$ differs from zero,or of the form (60) with $C _ { 2 } \neq 0$ Then $1 / r , r , 1 / R ^ { 3 } , R .$ and $r _ { 1 }$ are expanded in ascending fractional and integral powers of $\pmb { u }$ ; the coefficients (except the coefficient of $\log { u }$ if it is contained） are bounded as $\pmb { u }$ approaches $u = 0$ along a suitable path of integration. Hence, it is sufficient that $C _ { 1 } u ^ { i \mu } + C _ { 2 } u ^ { - i \mu }$ in (59),or $C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } \log u$ in (60), is larger than a positive number in absolute value.The condition is satisfied for $C _ { 1 } + C _ { 2 } \log u$ along any path of integration passing through $u = 0$ ， because the expression tends to infinity. On the other hand, the expression $C _ { 1 } u ^ { i \mu } + C _ { 2 } u ^ { - i \mu } { } _ { ; }$ ,if none of $C _ { 1 }$ and $C _ { 2 }$ is zero, has an infinite number of zero points on a semi-infinite line starting from $u = 0$ ，with their distances from $u = 0$ in geometrical progression. The required condition is satisfied if $\pmb { u }$ does not enter the inside of the angle formed by two semiinfinite lines starting from $u = 0$ If either $C _ { 1 }$ or $C _ { 2 }$ is zero, then it is sufficient that the path of integration does not rotate an infinite number of times around $u = 0$ . In any case, the principal value of the expansion of $r _ { 1 }$ is equal to that of $k r$ ; by substituting on the left-hand side of (55), we see that it cannot be an identity unless both $C _ { 1 }$ and $C _ { 2 }$ are zero. Hence, this last assumption, too, should be rejected.

Thus we have proved the theorem of Wilczynski and MacMillan that, if $m _ { 1 } \neq m _ { 2 }$ ， then the isosceles triangle in question is the equilateral triangle of Lagrange.

# 3.9NATURE OF THE ISOSCELES-TRIANGULAR SOLUTIONS

Suppose that $m _ { 1 } = m _ { 2 }$ . Equations (45) and (48) are now

$$
\dot { p } + q = 0 \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \frac { 1 } { 4 } ( \dot { p } - q ) ^ { 2 } = a a _ { 1 } + b b _ { 1 } + c c _ { 1 } ,
$$

where $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ and $q$ are now constants. Putting $m _ { 1 } = m _ { 2 }$ in (42)or (43) and in the definition of $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ gives

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { a x + b y + c z = 0 , x x _ { 1 } + y y _ { 1 } + z z _ { 1 } = 0 , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \frac { d x _ { 1 } } { d t } x + \frac { d y _ { 1 } } { d t } y + \frac { d z _ { 1 } } { d t } z = 0 . } } \end{array}
$$

Solving these three equations, we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r } { ( a a _ { 1 } + b b _ { 1 } + c c _ { 1 } ) x = p ( b z _ { 1 } - c y _ { 1 } ) , } \\ { ( a a _ { 1 } + b b _ { 1 } + c c _ { 1 } ) y = p ( c x _ { 1 } - a z _ { 1 } ) , } \\ { ( a a _ { 1 } + b b _ { 1 } + c c _ { 1 } ) z = p ( a y _ { 1 } - b x _ { 1 } ) . } \end{array}
$$

Equations (63） give a unique solution if $a a _ { 1 } + b b _ { 1 } + c c _ { 1 } \neq 0$ If we differentiate each of (63) twice with respect to $t$ , then the left-hand side is multiplied by $^ { - A }$ ,and the right-hand side by $- A _ { 1 }$ and $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ is a constant by (6l).Hence both sides of (63） should be identically zero if $A \neq 0$ (We have excluded the trivial case in which all of $x , y ;$ and $z$ are zero.) Since at least one of $x , y .$ or $_ z$ is not zero, $a a _ { 1 } + b b _ { 1 } + c c _ { 1 } = 0$ .Therefore by the second equation of (61), $\begin{array} { r } { p = q = 0 } \end{array}$ .Thus (62） reduces to

$$
x x _ { 1 } + y y _ { 1 } + z z _ { 1 } = 0 , ~ x { \frac { d x _ { 1 } } { d t } } + y { \frac { d y _ { 1 } } { d t } } + z { \frac { d z _ { 1 } } { d t } } = 0 .
$$

For our discussion we classify four cases:

First suppose that (64) is satisfied for $x _ { 1 } = y _ { 1 } = z _ { 1 } = 0$ by being reduced to an identity for $x , y , z .$ 、 In this case the three masses are on a straight line and $m _ { 3 }$ is situated at the middle point of $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ . This is a special case of Euler's straight-line equilibrium solution.

Next suppose that the coefficients of $x , y ,$ ，z are in proportion.Then $a _ { 1 } = b _ { 1 } = c _ { 1 } = 0$ ，by (42).The position vector $( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } , z _ { 1 } )$ has a fixed direction and position vector $( x , y , z )$ is perpendicular to that direction by the first equation of (64). The motion has an axis of symmetry; $m _ { 3 }$ moves on the axis of symmetry, and $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ move symmetrically with respect to this axis.

Next suppose that $a _ { 1 } , \ b _ { 1 } , \ c _ { 1 }$ are not zero. Then $\begin{array} { r } { x \colon a _ { 1 } = y \colon b _ { 1 } = z \colon c _ { 1 } , } \end{array}$ from (64)； position vector $( x , y , z )$ has a fixed direction and the vector $( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } , z _ { 1 } )$ is perpendicular to it. The motion has a plane of symmetry; $m _ { 3 }$ moves on the plane of symmetry, and $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ move symmetrically with respect to this plane.

Last, suppose that the two preceding cases coincide, that is,position vectors $( x , y , z )$ and $( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } , z _ { 1 } )$ have their own fixed directions and are mutually perpendicular. The motion is a plane motion and has an axis of symmetry lying on that plane; $m _ { 3 }$ moves on the axis of symmetry.

Let us consider the last three of these four cases in greater detail: I, with an axis of symmetry; II,with a plane of symmetry;and III, with an axis of symmetry together with a plane of symmetry. (These three cases correspond respectively to Fransen's three kinds of solution - see beginning of Section 3.8.

I. Consider an isosceles triangle with an axis of symmetry. Take the origin $O$ at the center of masses $m _ { 1 } = m _ { 2 } = m$ ,and the $O z$ -axis along the axis of symmetry. Then $z = 0$ ， $x _ { 1 } = y _ { 1 } = 0$ in (4l）and the coordinates of $m _ { 1 } , m _ { 2 }$ and $m _ { 3 }$ are, respectively, $( - x , - y , 0 )$ ， $( x , y , 0 )$ ,and $( 0 , 0 , z _ { 1 } )$ ； and $r = \sqrt { x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } }$ . The equations of motion are

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - \left[ \frac { m } { 4 r ^ { 3 } } + \frac { m _ { 3 } } { ( r ^ { 2 } + z _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right] x , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - \left[ \frac { m } { 4 r ^ { 3 } } + \frac { m _ { 3 } } { ( r ^ { 2 } + z _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right] y , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } z _ { 1 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { ( 2 m + m _ { 3 } ) z _ { 1 } } { ( r ^ { 2 } + z _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } . } } \end{array}
$$

Let $x = r$ cos $\theta , y = r \sin \theta$ ； then the integral of (65), with the constants of integration $C$ and $h$ , is given by

$$
\left( { \frac { d r } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } + { \frac { m _ { 3 } } { 2 m + m _ { 3 } } } \left( { \frac { d z _ { 1 } } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } = - { \frac { C ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } } + { \frac { m } { 2 r } } + { \frac { 2 m _ { 3 } } { \sqrt { r ^ { 2 } + z _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } } } + h ,
$$

$$
r ^ { 2 } { \frac { d \theta } { d t } } = C .
$$

If $C = 0$ ， then the motion reduces to case III. Suppose that $C \neq 0$ Since the right-hand side of (63) is positive or zero, then

$$
- \frac { C ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } + \frac { m + 4 m _ { 3 } } { 2 r } + h \ge 0 .
$$

Hence, if $h \geq 0$ ， $\boldsymbol { r }$ cannot be smaller than a positive root of this equation for $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { r } }$ If $h < 0$ ， $\boldsymbol { r }$ is in the interval between the two roots.Let the positive root or the smaller of the two roots be $l$ ； then the three mutual distances are less than or equal to $l .$ There is no collision.Hence, the motion can be traced from $t = - \infty$ to $t = + \infty$ .For $h < 0$ the mass $m _ { 3 }$ oscillates on the $O z$ -axis,its motion being symmetric about the origin $o$ .The areal velocity for the motion of either $m _ { 1 }$ or $m _ { 2 }$ is invariant,but the motion is not Keplerian,because the second term of the right-hand member in the first or second equation of (65) is a perturbation.

If the motion of $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ is a revolution in the plane $( x , y )$ around $o$ with the same period as the motion of $m _ { 3 }$ ,then the motion is confined to periodic orbits.For $h \geq 0$ ，the mass $m _ { 3 }$ recedes to infinity,and consequently the motion of $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ tends asymptotically to a Keplerian motion in the $( x , y )$ -plane around $o$ For $m _ { 3 } \to 0$ the motion of $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ also tends to the Keplerian. If we look at these motions from the center of mass of the three bodies, then the origin $o$ together with the plane Oxy moves in the direction of $O z$ with respect to the center of mass.The straight line joining $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ describes a ruled surface in space. Its asymptote is the axis of symmetry $O z$ on which $m _ { 3 }$ moves. This is the case of Wilczynski's second category.

II. Consider the case with a plane of symmetry. Take the plane of symmetry as the Oxy plane; then $x = y = 0$ ， $z _ { 1 } = 0$ .The coordinates of $m _ { 1 } , \ m _ { 2 } ,$ and $m _ { 3 }$ are respectively, $( 0 , 0 , - z )$ ， $( 0 , 0 , z )$ ，and $( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } , 0 )$ Suppose that $z \not \equiv 0$ The equations of motion, if we write $r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$ ， are

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } z } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - \left[ \frac { m } { 4 z ^ { 3 } } + \frac { m _ { 3 } } { ( z ^ { 2 } + r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right] z , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } x _ { 1 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { ( 2 m + m _ { 3 } ) x _ { 1 } } { ( z ^ { 2 } + r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } y _ { 1 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { ( 2 m + m _ { 3 } ) y _ { 1 } } { ( z ^ { 2 } + r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } . } \end{array}
$$

Put $x _ { 1 } = r _ { 1 }$ cos 01 $, y _ { 1 } = r _ { 1 }$ sin $\theta _ { 1 }$ ; then

$$
\left( { \frac { d z } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } + { \frac { m _ { 3 } } { 2 m + m _ { 3 } } } \left[ \left( { \frac { d r _ { 1 } } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } + { \frac { C ^ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } } \right] = { \frac { m } { 2 z } } + { \frac { 2 m _ { 3 } } { \sqrt { z ^ { 2 } + r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } } } + h ,
$$

$$
r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } { \frac { d \theta _ { 1 } } { d t } } = C .
$$

For $C = 0$ the case reduces to case II (Wilczynski's third category). Suppose that $C \neq 0$ .As we have taken $z \not \equiv 0$ ,we have $d ^ { 2 } z / d t ^ { 2 } < 0$ and $d ^ { 2 } z / d t ^ { 2 } = - \infty$ at $z = 0$ Hence, $_ z$ cannot take any minimum value other than O. The masses $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ collide at the origin $o$ . But, as $C \neq 0$ ，the mass $m _ { 3 }$ revolves on the plane of symmetry xy around $o$ ， and the areal velocity is invariant. The motion tends asymptotically to a Keplerian motion for $z  \infty$

The center of mass $G$ of the three bodies lies always in the xy-plane. Seen from $G$ ，the straight line joining $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ describesacylindrical surface with the straight line passing through $G$ and parallel to the $O z$ -axis as the axis of the cylinder.This is the ruled surface ofWilczynski, and the motion is of his second category. The trajectories of $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ lie on the $_ z$ -axis and are mirror images of one another; $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ collide at $o$ in a finite time $t _ { 1 }$ or $- t _ { 1 }$ . But, as will be shown in a later chapter, the motion, according to Sundman,can be analytically continued after any two-body collision.In this sense the motion of $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ can be traced from $t = - \infty$ to $t = + \infty$ For $m _ { 3 } \to 0$ the motion tends to a straight-line Keplerian motion.

III. Consider the case of plane motion with an axis of symmetry and a plane of symmetry; the axis lies on the plane.We take the axis of symmetry as the $_ x$ -axis and the plane of symmetry as the plane $z = 0$ The coordinates of $m _ { 1 } , m _ { 2 }$ and $m _ { 3 }$ are, respectively, $( 0 , - y , 0 )$ ， $( 0 , y , 0 )$ ， $( x _ { 1 } , 0 , 0 )$ ,with $y \not \equiv 0$ .The equations of motion are

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - \left[ \frac { m } { 4 y ^ { 3 } } + \frac { m _ { 3 } } { ( x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } \right] y , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d ^ { 2 } x _ { 1 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { ( 2 m + m _ { 3 } ) x _ { 1 } } { ( x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } . } \end{array}
$$

As $y$ has no minimum other than O, the masses $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ collide at $o$ in a finite time $t _ { 1 }$ or $- t _ { 1 }$ .If owing to the initial conditions $\boldsymbol { x } _ { 1 }$ and $_ y$ do not become zero simultaneously, then only $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ collide and the motion can be analytically continued beyond the two-body collision, as in case II. The angular momentum is zero and the energy integral is

$$
\left( { \frac { d y } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } + { \frac { m _ { 3 } } { 2 m + m _ { 3 } } } \left( { \frac { d x _ { 1 } } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } = { \frac { m } { 2 y } } + { \frac { 2 m _ { 3 } } { \sqrt { y ^ { 2 } + x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } } } + h .
$$

For $h > 0$ either all of the three masses $m _ { 1 } , m _ { 2 } , m _ { 3 }$ or two masses $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ ,or only the single mass $m _ { 3 }$ can recede to infinity. If we continue analytically after the two-body collision of $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ ,then there may be periodic solutions as in case II. There may occur a three-body collision at $o$ . We do not touch on the question as to whether or not repeated two-body collisions can cause a three-body collision.After a three-body collision, the analytical continuation is impossible except for some special values of the masses and the initial conditions,as will be shown in a later chapter.The possibility of colisions for the periodic orbits in cases I and II will be discussed in a later chapter.

We now discuss the motion of an infinitesimal mass $m _ { 3 }$ in case I- the case with the axis of symmetry.We suppose that $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ move in a common circular orbit and that $m _ { 3 }$ moves on the straight line passing normally through the center of this circle.This is a special case of the restricted three-body problem. Denote the radius of the circle by $^ { a }$ ， and the integration constant by $\gamma$ ; then,from (65) and (66),

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } z _ { 1 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { 2 m z _ { 1 } } { ( a ^ { 2 } + z _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } }
$$

or

$$
\left( \frac { d z _ { 1 } } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } = \frac { 4 m } { \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } + z _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } } - 4 \gamma .
$$

For $\gamma < 0$ ， the variable $z _ { 1 }$ reaches $\infty$ and $d z _ { 1 } / d t = 2 \sqrt { - \gamma }$ at $z _ { 1 } = \infty$ For $\gamma > 0$ ,the variable $z _ { 1 }$ is at a standstill, $d z _ { 1 } / d t = 0$ at

$$
z _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = \zeta ^ { 2 } = \frac { m ^ { 2 } } { \gamma ^ { 2 } } - a ^ { 2 } ,
$$

and $z _ { 1 }$ oscillates between $- \zeta \leqq z _ { 1 } \lesseqqgtr \zeta$ through $z _ { 1 } = 0$ .This isa periodic solution. But, unless $m / \gamma \\notin a$ ，the velocity $d z _ { 1 } / d t$ becomes imaginary and the motion is imaginary. In the case $m / \gamma = a$ , the moving point is always at rest at the origin; this is the case of Euler.

Let $1 / w = \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } + z _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } }$ ; then w vanishes only when $z _ { 1 }$ becomes infinite. The variable ${ \pmb w }$ does not vanish for $\gamma > 0$ ,and it never becomes infinite. Then the energy integral is

$$
\left( { \frac { d w } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } = 4 w ^ { 4 } ( 1 - a ^ { 2 } w ^ { 2 } ) ( m w - \gamma ) .
$$

We take $\pmb { u }$ (defined by $w ^ { 2 } d t = d u )$ as the new independent variable instead of $t .$ Then

$$
\left( \frac { d w } { d u } \right) ^ { 2 } = 4 ( 1 - a ^ { 2 } w ^ { 2 } ) ( m w - \gamma ) .
$$

This is transformed to

$$
\left( \frac { d v } { d u } \right) ^ { 2 } = 4 v ^ { 3 } - g _ { 2 } v - g _ { 3 } ,
$$

where

$$
w = - \frac { \upsilon } { ( a ^ { 2 } m ) ^ { 1 / 3 } } + \frac { \gamma } { 3 m } \cdotp
$$

Denote the three roots of this equation by $e _ { 1 } > e _ { 2 } > e _ { 3 }$ .The solution can be written by Weierstrasss elliptic function $v = \wp ( u )$ and

$$
d t = [ - ( a ^ { 2 } m ) ^ { - 1 / 3 } \wp ( u ) + \gamma m ^ { - 1 } ] ^ { - 2 } d u .
$$

$\mathrm { I f } \gamma > 0 , \gamma / m < 1 / a$ ；then

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { ( a ^ { 2 } m ) ^ { - 1 / 3 } e _ { 1 } = \displaystyle \frac { \gamma } { 3 m } + \frac { 1 } { a } < 0 , \qquad ( a ^ { 2 } m ) ^ { - 1 / 3 } e _ { 2 } = - \displaystyle \frac { 2 \gamma } { 3 m } < 0 , } } \\ { { } } \\ { { ( a ^ { 2 } m ) ^ { - 1 / 3 } e _ { 3 } = \displaystyle \frac { \gamma } { 3 m } - \frac { 1 } { a } < 0 . } } \end{array}
$$

The corresponding values of ${ \pmb w }$ are $w _ { 1 } = - 1 / a _ { \mathrm { : } }$ $\ w _ { 2 } = \gamma / m$ ， $ \boldsymbol { w } _ { 3 } = 1 / a$ ； $w _ { 1 } < w _ { 2 } < w _ { 3 }$ .Let the periods of the elliptic functions be $2 \omega$ and $2 \omega ^ { \prime }$ ； then as $\boldsymbol { u }$ varies from $\omega + \omega ^ { \prime }$ to $\omega ^ { \prime }$ , the function $\wp ( u )$ decreases from $\boldsymbol { e } _ { 2 }$ to $\boldsymbol { e } _ { 3 }$ and $\wp ^ { \prime } ( u )$ is real and positive,w increases from $\gamma / m$ to $1 / a$ ,and $\sqrt { a ^ { 2 } + z _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } }$ decreases from $m / \gamma$ to $^ a$ 、Since there is no zero point of $\boldsymbol { \omega }$ ， that is,no point at which $z _ { 1 } = \infty$ ， the relation between dt and du is regular. The variable $\boldsymbol { u }$ is also regular for finite values of $t .$ Hence ${ \pmb w }$ ， and accordingly $z _ { 1 }$ , is regular. The quantity $\sqrt { a ^ { 2 } + z _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } }$ oscillates between $m / \gamma$ and $^ a$ ； it corresponds to a periodic solution. The coordinate $z _ { 1 }$ oscillates between $\pm { \sqrt { ( m / \gamma ) ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } }$ ，situated symmetrically about $O$

If $\gamma > 0$ ， $\gamma / m > 1 / a$ ； then $\boldsymbol { e } _ { 2 }$ and $\boldsymbol { e } _ { 3 }$ interchange. But the motion is imaginary in this case.

$\mathrm { I f } \ \gamma < 0$ , then the values of w corresponding to $e _ { 1 } > e _ { 2 } > e _ { 3 }$ are either $w _ { 1 } = - 1 / a _ { \scriptscriptstyle \mathrm { \perp } }$ $ w _ { 2 } = \gamma / m$ ， $ \ w _ { 3 } = 1 / a$ or $ w _ { 1 } = \gamma / m$ ， $\displaystyle w _ { 2 } = - 1 / a$ ， $ \ w _ { 3 } = 1 / a$ ； $w _ { 1 } < w _ { 2 } < w _ { 3 }$ As $1 / a$ is the only positive root among the three, there is a zero point for ${ \pmb w }$ between ${ \boldsymbol { w } } _ { 2 }$ and $\omega _ { 3 }$ $t$ cannot be related to $\boldsymbol { u }$ regularly at $w = 0$ in the transformation $w ^ { 2 } d t = d u$ In fact, $z _ { 1 } = \infty$ at $w = 0$ Hence,the real motion occurs only between $w = 0$ and ${ \boldsymbol { w } } = { \boldsymbol { w } } _ { 3 }$ ,that is, between $v = ( \gamma / 3 m ) ( a ^ { 2 } m ) ^ { 1 / 3 }$ and $e _ { 3 }$ .At $w = 0$ ，that is, at $z _ { 1 } = \infty$ ，the motion recedes to infinity.

The relation between $t$ and $\boldsymbol { u }$ is obtained from the formulas for elliptic functions

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \frac { d } { d x } \zeta ( x ) = - \wp ( x ) , } } \\ { { } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d } { d x } \log \sigma ( x ) = \zeta ( x ) , } } \\ { { } } \\ { { \zeta ( x + y ) - \zeta ( x - y ) - 2 \zeta ( y ) = - \frac { \wp ^ { \prime } ( y ) } { \wp ( x ) - \wp ( y ) } , } } \\ { { } } \\ { { { } } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\wp ( x + y ) + \wp ( x - y ) - 2 \wp ( y ) = { \frac { [ \wp ^ { \prime } ( y ) ] ^ { 2 } } { [ \wp ( x ) - \wp ( y ) ] ^ { 2 } } } + { \frac { \wp ^ { \prime \prime } ( y ) } { \wp ( x ) - \wp ( y ) ^ { \prime } } }
$$

that is,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { [ \wp ^ { \prime } ( y ) ] ^ { \mathrm { 2 } } \displaystyle \int \displaystyle \frac { d x } { [ \wp ( x ) - \wp ( y ) ] ^ { \mathrm { 2 } } } = - \zeta ( x + y ) - \zeta ( x - y ) - 2 x \wp ( y ) } \\ { + \displaystyle \frac { \wp ^ { \prime \prime } ( y ) } { \wp ^ { \prime } ( y ) } \left[ \log \displaystyle \frac { \sigma ( x + y ) } { \sigma ( x - y ) } - 2 x \zeta ( y ) \right] . } \end{array}
$$

Let $\wp ( \alpha ) = \gamma a ^ { 2 / 3 } m ^ { - 2 / 3 } / 3$ ; then,

$$
d t = a ^ { 4 / 3 } m ^ { 2 / 3 } [ \wp ( u ) - \wp ( \alpha ) ] ^ { - 2 } d u ;
$$

or,if we integrate withtheintegration constant $t _ { 0 }$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \Big [ \frac { \wp ^ { \prime } ( \alpha ) } { a ^ { 2 / 3 } m ^ { 1 / 3 } } \Big ] ^ { 2 } ( t - t _ { 0 } ) = - \zeta ( u + \alpha ) - \zeta ( u - \alpha ) - 2 u \wp ( \alpha ) } \\ { + \frac { \wp ^ { \prime } ( \alpha ) } { \wp ^ { \prime \prime } ( \alpha ) } \left[ \log \frac { \sigma ( u + \alpha ) } { \sigma ( u - \alpha ) } - 2 u \zeta ( \alpha ) \right] . } \end{array}
$$

Write $q = e ^ { - \pi \omega ^ { \prime } / i \omega }$ ; then, using the formulas of eliptic functions,

$$
\wp ( w ) = - { \frac { \eta } { \omega } } + \left( { \frac { \pi } { 2 \omega } } \right) ^ { 2 } { \frac { 1 } { \sin ^ { 2 } { \frac { \pi w } { 2 \omega } } } } - 2 { \left( { \frac { \pi } { \omega } } \right) } ^ { 2 } \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } { \frac { n q ^ { 2 n } } { 1 - q ^ { 2 n } } } \cos { \frac { n \pi w } { \omega } } ,
$$

$$
\zeta ( w ) = \frac { \eta w } { \omega } + \frac { \pi } { 2 \omega } \cot \frac { \pi w } { 2 \omega } + \frac { 2 \pi } { \omega } \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \frac { q ^ { 2 n } } { 1 - q ^ { 2 n } } \sin \frac { n \pi w } { \omega } ,
$$

$$
\log \sigma ( w ) = \frac { \eta w ^ { 2 } } { 2 \omega } + \log \left( \frac { 2 \omega } { \pi } \sin \frac { \pi w } { 2 \omega } \right) + 2 \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \frac { q ^ { 2 n } } { 1 - q ^ { 2 n } } \frac { 1 - \cos \frac { \pi w } { \omega } } { n } ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { w = \displaystyle \frac { \gamma } { 3 m } - a ^ { - 2 / 3 } m ^ { - 1 / 3 } \biggl [ - \frac { \eta } { \omega } + \biggl ( \frac { \pi } { 2 \omega } \biggr ) ^ { 2 } \biggl ( \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { \pi u } { 2 \omega } \biggr ) ^ { - 2 } } } \\ { { - 2 \biggl ( \frac { \pi } { \omega } \biggr ) ^ { 2 } \displaystyle \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \frac { n q ^ { 2 n } } { 1 - q ^ { 2 n } } \cos \frac { n \pi u } { \omega } \biggr ] , } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\biggl [ \frac { \wp ^ { \prime } ( \alpha ) } { a ^ { 2 / 3 } m ^ { 1 / 3 } } \biggr ] ^ { 2 } \cdot ( t - t _ { 0 } )
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { - \frac { \wp ^ { \prime \prime } ( \alpha ) } { \wp ^ { \prime } ( \alpha ) } \biggl \{ \frac { 2 \alpha u } { \omega } - 4 u \zeta ( \alpha ) + \log [ \frac { 2 \omega } { \pi } \sin \frac { \pi ( u + \alpha ) } { 2 \omega } ] } }  \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \qquad - \log [ \frac { 2 \omega } { \pi } \sin \frac { \pi ( u - \alpha ) } { 2 \omega } ] } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \qquad + 4 \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \frac { q ^ { 2 n } } { 1 - q ^ { 2 n } } \frac { 1 } { n } \sin \frac { n \pi u } { \omega } \sin \frac { n \pi \alpha } { \omega } \biggr \} . } \end{array}
$$

As we see from these expansions, the variable w has no singularity, as $\pmb { u }$ varies from $\omega + \omega ^ { \prime }$ to $\omega ^ { \prime }$ .But $t \mathrm { ~ - ~ } t _ { 0 }$ has a singularity, indeed a logarithmic singularity, at $u = \alpha$ ； this corresponds to $w = 0$ ,that is,to $z _ { 1 } = \infty$ For $\gamma > 0$ this singularity does not lie in the interval between $\boldsymbol { v } = \boldsymbol { e } _ { 2 }$ and $\upsilon = e _ { 3 }$ . Hence, the growth of $t \mathrm { ~ - ~ } t _ { 0 }$ consists of a uniform component, superposed on periodic oscillations in $\boldsymbol { u }$ of period $4 \omega$ .The variable $\boldsymbol { \omega }$ ， and accordingly $z _ { 1 }$ ,oscillates with period $_ { 4 \omega }$ .Thus, if $\gamma > 0$ ， $\gamma / m < 1 / a$ ， then there exist period solutions of period $_ { 4 \omega }$ . The period can be computed by

$$
\sqrt { \frac { 2 \omega } { \pi } } = \frac { 2 } { ( e _ { 1 } - e _ { 3 } ) ^ { 1 / 4 } + ( e _ { 1 } - e _ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 4 } } ( 1 + 2 h ^ { 4 } + 2 h ^ { 1 6 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) ,
$$

where

$$
\iota = { \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } l + 2 ( { \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } l ) ^ { 5 } + 1 5 ( { \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } l ) ^ { 9 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , \qquad l = \frac { ( e _ { 1 } - e _ { 3 } ) ^ { 1 / 4 } - ( e _ { 1 } - e _ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 4 } } { ( e _ { 1 } - e _ { 3 } ) ^ { 1 / 4 } + ( e _ { 1 } - e _ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 4 } }
$$

The period with regard to $t$ is

$$
4 \omega \biggl [ \frac { \wp ^ { \prime } ( \alpha ) } { \alpha ^ { 2 / 3 } m ^ { 1 / 3 } } \biggr ] ^ { 2 } \biggl \{ - \frac { 2 \eta } { \omega } + \frac { \wp ^ { \prime \prime } ( \alpha ) } { \wp ^ { \prime } ( \alpha ) } \left[ \frac { 2 \eta \alpha } { \omega } - 4 \zeta ( \alpha ) \right] \biggr \} .
$$

Compare this with the two-body problem; we see that the case $\gamma > 0$ corresponds to elliptic motions and the case $\gamma < 0$ to hyperbolic motions.

When $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ move in elliptic orbits, we may be able to make a similar transformation but still cannot carry the computation through. Buchanan (l92O） computed the periodic solutions in the sense of Poincaré, but we postpone consideration of this to a later chapter, along with the other classes of isosceles-triangular solutions,and the discussion of their collisions.

Various attempts have been made to attack the three-body problem by introducing new ideas. Sokolov (1945) introduced a new independent variable in the plane problem and derived the known configurations of Lagrange and Euler (Sections 3.2, 3.7). Pylarinos (194l) introduced a rigid triangle associated with the triangle formed by the three bodies. Egervary,according to Meffroy, used the analogy of the motion of a top. Recently Meffroy (l962,1968) studied the circular cone with one of the three bodies as vertex and a fixed direction in space as axis.Let the vertex angle of the cone be fixed at $\pi - 2 \theta$ The plane $P$ containing the three masses rolls around this cone, the polar coordinates of the plane being $\pi / 2 - \theta$ and $\phi$ .The angle $\theta$ is fixed because of the areal velocity integrals and the energy integral. If the plane $P$ rotates around a fixed direction on the plane $P$ which passes through one of the three masses, then either the motion is Lagrange's or in the limit, two of the three bodies collide.
