In a special case $\theta = \pi / 2$ ， we determine the relative motion from a differential equation of the third order,

$$
\lambda = \bigg \{ 2 \frac { d \phi } { d t } \frac { d ^ { 3 } \phi } { d t ^ { 3 } } - 3 \Big ( \frac { d \phi } { d t } \Big ) ^ { 2 } + 4 \Big ( \frac { d \phi } { d t } \Big ) ^ { 4 } \bigg \} \bigg / \bigg \{ 4 k ^ { 2 } \Big ( \frac { d \phi } { d t } \Big ) ^ { 7 / 2 } \bigg \} ,
$$

by putting $\lambda = 0$ or $\lambda = \infty$ as is appropriate. Otherwise the plane $P$ cannot rotate around any direction fixed on $P$ that passes through one of the bodies.If one of the bodies is of mass zero, then the differential equations are reduced to a complicated one of second order.

# Part II Transformation Theory

# Continuous Groups of Transformations

# INFINITESIMAL TRANSFORMATIONS

# 4.ITHE FIRST INTEGRALS

Consider a system of differential equations

$$
{ \frac { d x _ { 1 } } { X _ { 1 } } } = { \frac { d x _ { 2 } } { X _ { 2 } } } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = { \frac { d x _ { n } } { X _ { n } } } = d t .
$$

Under certain conditions which must be satisfed by the functions $X _ { i }$ ， this systemadmits thefrst integrals, which are regular in $t , x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ,$

$$
f _ { i } ( t , x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) = c _ { i } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

with integration constants $c _ { i }$ . These integrals are independent if the Jacobian $\partial ( f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , . . . , f _ { n } ) / \partial ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } )$ does not vanish. Solving (2) in that case gives general integrals

$$
x _ { i } = \phi _ { i } ( t , c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , \ldots , c _ { n } ) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

Iffis an integral of (1), then $f$ satisfies a partial differential equation of the first order. We consider $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ as the coordinates for an integral curve in an $\pmb { n }$ -dimensional space,which are represented as functions of a parameter $t .$ Since the function $f$ becomes a constant when the variables $x _ { i }$ in $f$ are expressed as functions of $t$ we have $d f = 0$ .In other words, the function $f$ is invariant under a transformation

$$
t ^ { \prime } = t + d t , \qquad x _ { i } ^ { \prime } = x _ { i } + X _ { i } d t \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

as the transformed point moves along the integral curve. This transformation (4) is called an infinitesimal transformation, which was studied by

Lie（Lie,1888，1890，1891，1893，1893a，1896；Kowalewski,1931;Eisenhardt,l933；Campbell, 1903；Engel and Faber,1932；Hoheisel,1928;Cartan,1933,1937;Vivanti, 1918;Bianchi, 1918;Potron,1936;Cerf,1929;Buhl,1928;Caratheodory,1933;Pontrjagin,1939;Chevalley1946,195l).We say then that the system of differential equations (l)admits an infinitesimal transformation (4),or,replacing $d x _ { i }$ in df by $X _ { i }$ dtaccording to (l),we have

$$
X ( f ) = { \frac { \partial f } { \partial t } } + X _ { 1 } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { 1 } } } + X _ { 2 } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { 2 } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + X _ { n } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { n } } } = 0 .
$$

$X ( f )$ is the symbol for an infinitesimal transformation;an infinitesimal transformation (5) is said to generate (2).Any first integral (2) satisfies (5).Conversely, any function $f$ satisfying (5) is a first integral of (l), because substituting (l） for $X _ { i }$ of (5） gives $d f = 0$ Any function $F$ of $f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , \ldots , f _ { n }$ satisfies (5). If we eliminate the coefficients $1 , X _ { 1 } , . . . , X _ { n }$ from these equations,we obtain the Jacobian

$$
\partial ( F , f _ { 1 } , . . . , f _ { n } ) / \partial ( t , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) = 0 ,
$$

which shows that $F$ is a function 0 $f _ { 1 } , \ldots , f _ { n }$

Thus an integral of (5) is an integral of (1),and vice versa.

Next, consider the problem in the reverse order.Does any solution of a partial differential equation of the first order become an integral of an ordinary differential equation? Consider a partial differential equation

$$
F ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } , z , \phi _ { 1 } , . . . , \phi _ { n } ) = 0 ,
$$

where $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { \mathrm { : } } = \partial z / \partial \pmb { x } _ { i }$ . Suppose that we know an arbitrary solution of this equation $z = \Phi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } )$ .Letting $X _ { i } = \partial F / \partial x _ { i }$ $P _ { k } = \partial { F } / \partial { p _ { k } } .$ $Z = \partial F / \partial z _ { ; }$ ， we construct

$$
{ \frac { d x _ { 1 } } { P _ { 1 } } } = { \frac { d x _ { 2 } } { P _ { 2 } } } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = { \frac { d x _ { n } } { P _ { n } } } .
$$

The $P _ { i }$ are known functions of $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } .$ . As these equations constitute $n - 1$ relations among $\scriptstyle n$ variables $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ;$ ，they represent a curve on an $n$ -dimensional hypersurface $z = \Phi$ embedded in an $( n + 1 )$ - dimensional space.The given differential equations represent relations among infinitesimals. By giving arbitrary values to the integration constants we obtain a family of curves on that hypersurface.Through any point of an integral hypersurface $z = \Phi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } )$ there passes one and only one curve belonging to this family. Let a set $\left( \pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { 1 } , \pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { 2 } , . . . . , \pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { n } \right)$ correspond to each point $z = \Phi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } )$ ; that is, if we restrict the direction to be ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { 1 } , { \mathfrak { p } } _ { 2 } , \dotsc , { \mathfrak { p } } _ { n }$ from that point, we obtain $\infty ^ { 1 }$ elements.

Any set of $2 n + 1$ values for $z , \ x _ { i } , \ p _ { i }$ is called an element. The curve obtained in this manner is called the characteristic curve of Cauchy.

If we do not know the function $\Phi$ , we proceed as follows. Suppose that $z , \ x _ { i } , \ p _ { i }$ satisfies $F = 0$ along the characteristic curve starting at the elements $\left( z ^ { 0 } , x _ { i } ^ { 0 } , \phi _ { i } ^ { 0 } \right)$ , that is, if we set $\begin{array} { r } { \ p _ { i k } = \partial ^ { 2 } z / \partial x _ { i } \partial x _ { k } . } \end{array}$ then

$$
d z = \hbar _ { 1 } d x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \hbar _ { n } d x _ { n } , \ d y _ { i } = \hbar _ { i 1 } d x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \hbar _ { i n } d x _ { n } .
$$

Partial diferentiation of $F = 0$ with respect to $x _ { i }$ gives

$$
X _ { i } + p _ { i } Z + P _ { 1 } p _ { i 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + P _ { n } p _ { i n } = 0 .
$$

Or, if this equation is written in the form

$$
{ \frac { d x _ { 1 } } { P _ { 1 } } } = { \frac { d x _ { 2 } } { P _ { 2 } } } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = { \frac { d x _ { n } } { P _ { n } } } = d u ,
$$

we obtain

$$
( X _ { i } + \not p _ { i } Z ) ~ d u + \not p _ { i 1 } d x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \not p _ { i n } d x _ { n } = 0 ,
$$

that is,

$$
( X _ { i } + p _ { i } Z ) ~ d u + d p _ { i } = 0 .
$$

Hence

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { P _ { i } } = \frac { d z } { P _ { 1 } \phi _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + P _ { n } \phi _ { n } } = \frac { - d p _ { k } } { X _ { k } + p _ { k } Z } = d u \qquad ( i , k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

This equation does not depend on $\Phi$ . We can solve these ordinary differential equations for $z , x _ { i } , p _ { i }$ if we know $z ^ { 0 } , x _ { i } ^ { 0 } , \phi _ { i } ^ { 0 }$

When the elements $z , \ x _ { i } , \ p _ { i }$ are connected by relations of the form $\begin{array} { r } { d z = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \beta _ { i } d x _ { i } } \end{array}$ ， they are called， following Lie， a family of elements (Element-uerein). This is an $\pmb { n }$ -dimensional continuum. According to Lie, the method of Lagrange and Charpit for solving a partial differential equation is equivalent to that of finding the family of elements of the highest order.Lie called it a family of integrals.Let a family of complete integrals, that is,an integral with $\pmb { n }$ arbitrary constants $\alpha _ { i }$ of a partial differential equation (6), be

$$
V ( z , x _ { i } , \alpha _ { i } ) = 0 .
$$

Since $z$ should satisfy the partial differential equation with any set of constants $\alpha _ { i }$ ，therefore

$$
{ \frac { \partial V } { \partial x _ { i } } } + { / { i } } { \frac { \partial V } { \partial z } } = 0 \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

If we eliminate $\alpha _ { i }$ from the $( n + 1 )$ equations (8) and (9),we obtain the given partial differential equation.Hence,our problem is to find a function $_ z$ of $x _ { i }$ and $\alpha _ { i }$ that satisfies these equations, that is,

$$
z = f _ { 0 } ( x _ { i } ) , \qquad \alpha _ { i } = f _ { i } ( x _ { j } ) .
$$

Differentiate the completc integral with regard to $x _ { i }$ and refer to (9); then

$$
\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial V } { \partial \alpha _ { j } } \frac { \partial \alpha _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } = 0 \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Solving (8) and (9) is equivalent to solving (8) and (1O). In order to satisfy (8) and (9), put

$$
V = 0 , \frac { \partial V } { \partial \alpha _ { i } } = 0 ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

In this case $z = f _ { 0 } ( x _ { i } )$ is called a singular integral. If the $\partial V / \partial \alpha _ { i }$ are not all zero, then （l0） gives

$$
{ \frac { \partial ( \alpha _ { 1 } , . . . , \alpha _ { n } ) } { \partial ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) } } = 0 .
$$

This shows that there is a relation among $\alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n }$ . Let the relation be $\phi = 0$ .The differentials $d \alpha _ { 1 } , d \alpha _ { 2 } , . . . , d \alpha _ { n }$ satisfy

$$
d \phi = 0 .
$$

Then

$$
{ \frac { \partial V / \partial \alpha _ { 1 } } { \partial \phi / \partial \alpha _ { 1 } } } = { \frac { \partial V / \partial \alpha _ { 2 } } { \partial \phi / \partial \alpha _ { 2 } } } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = { \frac { \partial V / \partial \alpha _ { n } } { \partial \phi / \partial \alpha _ { n } } } = \lambda .
$$

Equations (8), (11), and (12) are $n + 2$ equations with z, $\alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n } ,$ $\lambda$ as the unknown quantities. Eliminate $\lambda$ $, \alpha _ { 1 } , . . . , \alpha _ { n }$ from these equations; then we obtain a relation that gives an integral $_ z$ containing an arbitrary function $\phi$ This is called the general integral.

The equations (l） are of the first order with regard to each of the $_ n$ unknown functions. They are said to be of order $\pmb { n }$ ,as they are equivalent to a differential equation of order $\boldsymbol { n }$ for a single unknown function.

In fact, first suppose that $n = 2$ .Combining

$$
\frac { d x _ { 1 } } { d t } = X _ { 1 } ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } ) \mathrm { a n d } \frac { d x _ { 2 } } { d t } = X _ { 2 } ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } ) ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\frac { d x _ { 2 } } { d t } = f \biggl ( x _ { 1 } , \frac { d x _ { 1 } } { d t } \biggl ) .
$$

If we put $x _ { 2 } = d x _ { 1 } / d t$ ，we obtain a differential equation of the second order with respect to $\pmb { x } _ { 1 }$

Next suppose that this theorem is true when the number of equations is equal to $n - 1$ ; we shall prove by mathematical induction that it is true when the number of equations is $_ { n }$ From the first $n - 1$ equations, we obtain

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = x _ { i + 1 } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - 2 ) ,
$$

where $x _ { n }$ is taken as a parameter. Solving these $n - 2$ equations gives $x _ { n }$ as a function of $x _ { 1 }$ and its $n - 1$ derivatives up to the order $n - 1$ Substitute these in the nth equation of (1);this gives $d x _ { n } / d t$ in terms of $\pmb { x _ { 1 } }$ and its $n - 1$ derivatives. Put $x _ { n } = d x _ { n - 1 } / d t$ ； this gives a differential equation of the nth order for $x _ { 1 }$ . Conversely, a differential equation of the nth order

$$
{ \frac { d ^ { n } x } { d t ^ { n } } } = F \left( { \frac { d ^ { n - 1 } x } { d t ^ { n - 1 } } } , \cdot \cdot \cdot , { \frac { d x } { d t } } , x , t \right)
$$

can be transformed into

$$
{ \frac { d x _ { 1 } } { x _ { 2 } } } = { \frac { d x _ { 2 } } { x _ { 3 } } } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = { \frac { d x _ { n - 1 } } { x _ { n } } } = { \frac { d x _ { n } } { F ( x _ { n } , x _ { n - 1 } , \cdot \cdot \cdot , x , t ) } } = d t
$$

if we let $= x _ { 1 } , ~ d x _ { i } / d t = x _ { i + 1 } ~ ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - 1 )$ .Hence,(1）is equivalent to a differential equation of the nth order with respect to a single variable $x _ { 1 }$ ; it is of order $_ { n }$

If (1） has $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ $\left( { \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } \lessgtr \pmb { n } \right)$ independent integrals, then the order of the differential equation is reduced to $n - \phi$ .Suppose the $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ integrals to be

$$
f _ { 1 } = C _ { 1 } , ~ f _ { 2 } = C _ { 2 } , . . . , ~ f _ { p } = C _ { p } .
$$

We express $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \dotsc , x _ { p }$ in terms of $t$ and $x _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ . Denote the result of substituting these values of $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \dotsc , x _ { p }$ in $X _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , X _ { n }$ by $[ X _ { p + 1 } ] , . . . , [ X _ { n } ]$ . Then $[ X _ { p + 1 } ] , . . . , [ X _ { n } ]$ are functions of

$$
t , x _ { p + 1 } , . . . , x _ { n }
$$

only.From this arises a system of differential equationsof order $n - { \boldsymbol { p } }$

$$
{ \frac { d x _ { p + 1 } } { [ X _ { p + 1 } ] } } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = { \frac { d x _ { n } } { [ X _ { n } ] } } = d t .
$$

Thus, if we can find $\pmb { n }$ independent integrals,we can reduce the order of the differential equation to zero,and the equations will no longer contain any derivative; that is,we can solve the equations without integration.If we are able to do this, we say that the equations have been solved by quadrature. To find the $_ { n }$ independent integrals, we use Mayer's important method of solving a differential equation of order $\pmb { n }$ The differential equations for the motion of masses in the general three-body problem are of order 18. The equations would be solved if we could find 18 independent integrals.As will be shown later,only l2 independent integrals are known at present. A certain type of integral has even been proved nonexistent.Solving the general three-body problem evidently calls for some transcendental process.

# 4.2INFINITESIMAL TRANSFORMATIONS

If there is a relation giving the third element from two other elements in a set $G$ of objects, such as $A B = C ;$ ,if the relation obeys an associative law $( A B ) C = A ( B C )$ ,and if $G$ contains a unit element $\boldsymbol { I }$ independent of any element of $G$ such that $I A = A I = A$ and also an inverse element such as $A ^ { - 1 } A = I ,$ then $G$ is called a group. If $A B = B A$ ，then it is a commutative group or an Abelian group.For an Abelian group we sometimes use $A + B$ instead of $A B$ and call it an additive group,or a modulus (Van der Waerden,1955,1959).

Let $\epsilon$ be an infinitesimal quantity independent of $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ .A transformation of the form

$$
x _ { i } ^ { \prime } = x _ { i } + \alpha _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) \epsilon \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

is called an infinitesimal transformation and is writen $\Delta x _ { i } = \alpha _ { i } \epsilon$ If $f$ is a function of $x _ { i }$ ,then,

$$
f ( x _ { i } ^ { \prime } ) = f ( x _ { i } + \alpha _ { i } \epsilon ) = f ( x _ { i } ) + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { i } } } \Delta x _ { i } ,
$$

or

$$
\Delta f = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { i } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { i } } } \epsilon .
$$

Such a transformation forms a group.Two infinitesimal transformations represented by $\epsilon _ { 1 }$ and $\epsilon _ { 2 }$ operated successively are equivalent to a single transformation belonging to the group; $\epsilon = 0$ is an identity transformation; this group also contains an inverse transformation corresponding to $- \epsilon .$ . This is a one-parameter group. We denote an infinitesimal transformation by an operator $A$ ：

$$
A = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { i } { \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { i } } } \cdot
$$

This means

$$
A ( f ) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { i } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { i } } } .
$$

Let $\pmb { c }$ be a constant and ${ \pmb F }$ be a function $\mathbf { o f } f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , \ldots . . . , f _ { m } .$ then

$$
A ( f _ { 1 } + f _ { 2 } ) = A ( f _ { 1 } ) + A ( f _ { 2 } ) , A ( c f ) = c A ( f ) ,
$$

$$
A ( f _ { 1 } f _ { 2 } ) = f _ { 1 } A ( f _ { 2 } ) + f _ { 2 } A ( f _ { 1 } ) , A ( F ( f _ { 1 } , . . . , f _ { m } ) ) = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { m } \frac { \partial F } { \partial f _ { j } } A ( f _ { j } ) .
$$

Thus $A$ is a linear operator. Furthermore, let

$$
B = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \beta _ { i } { \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { i } } } ;
$$

then the compound operator

$$
( A , B ) \equiv A B - B A = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( A { \beta } _ { i } - B { \alpha } _ { i } \right) \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { i } }
$$

is called a commutator (Klammerausdruck). Since it can be shown that

$$
( A , B ) f = A ( B f ) - B ( A f ) ,
$$

this gives

$$
( A , B ) = - ( B , A ) , \qquad ( A , A ) = 0 .
$$

Hence,a commutator is an infinitesimal transformation. Since it contains the inverse transformation that can be obtained by putting $- \epsilon$ in place of $\epsilon$ ， such infinitesimal transformations form a group.Lie called this group of infinitesimal transformations a continuous transformation group, as $\epsilon$ can vary continuously.

If there is no independent relation of the form

$$
\sum _ { r = 1 } ^ { m } \lambda _ { r } A _ { r } f \equiv 0
$$

among the $\pmb { m }$ infinitesimal transformations

$$
A _ { r } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \alpha _ { i } ^ { ( r ) } \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { i } } \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , m )
$$

with constants $\lambda _ { i }$ ,then the $A _ { \tau } f$ are said to be linearly independent infinitesimal transformations, $m$ in number.A solution common to all partial differential equations

$$
A _ { r } f = 0 \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , m )
$$

satisfies

$$
( A _ { r } , A _ { s } ) f = 0 \qquad ( r , s = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

Hence, $( A _ { r } , A _ { s } ) f = 0$ is a consequence of

$$
A _ { 1 } f = 0 , A _ { 2 } f = 0 , . . . , A _ { m } f = 0 .
$$

In other words,

$$
( A _ { r } , A _ { s } ) f = \sum _ { t = 1 } ^ { m } c _ { r s t } ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) A _ { t } f .
$$

In this case,Clebsch called the system $A _ { 1 } f = 0$ ， $A _ { 2 } f = 0 , . . . , A _ { m } f = 0$ an m-parameter complete system.Lie showed that $c _ { r s t }$ is a constant; it is called the structure constant. This is the second fundamental theorem on the Lie groups (Section 4.26).Lie (1888,1890,1893,1893a,1896) has made a detailed investigation of the continuous transformation groups,and his work has been applied to the theory of differential equations and to geometry.

In order to solve a partial differential equation of the first order

$$
{ \frac { \partial f } { \partial t } } + X _ { 1 } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { 1 } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + X _ { n } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { n } } } = 0 , \qquad X _ { i } = X _ { i } ( t , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) ,
$$

for $f ( t , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ ， it is necessary to find $\pmb { n }$ independent functions $f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , \ldots , f _ { n } ;$ ,that fulfill the identities

$$
\frac { \partial f _ { i } } { \partial t } + X _ { 1 } \frac { \partial f _ { i } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + X _ { n } \frac { \partial f _ { i } } { \partial x _ { n } } \equiv 0 ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

The most general solution is $F ( f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , \ldots , f _ { n } )$ with an arbitrary function ${ \pmb F } _ { \pmb { \bot } }$

If we let each of these $\pmb { n }$ functions $f _ { i }$ equal a constant, then each of these equations is an integral of the system of ordinary differential equations

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = X _ { i } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

By an infinitesimal transformation

$$
t ^ { \prime } = t + \Delta t , \qquad x _ { i } ^ { \prime } = x _ { i } + X _ { i } \Delta t \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

such a function $f _ { i }$ is unaltered, that is, because of (14),

$$
\begin{array} { l } { f _ { i } ( t ^ { \prime } , x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \dots , x _ { n } ^ { \prime } ) = f _ { i } ( t , x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { n } ) + \displaystyle \left( \frac { \partial f _ { i } } { \partial t } + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { j } \frac { \partial f _ { i } } { \partial x _ { j } } \right) \Delta t } \\ { = f _ { i } ( t , x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { n } ) . } \end{array}
$$

Hence,an integral of the system of ordinary differential equations is invariant with respect to an infinitesimal transformation admitted by the system. $\Delta t$ is what we have denoted by $\epsilon$

If a system of ordinary differential equations (15) admits an infinitesimal transformation Afand ifit has an integral $y =$ constant, then $A y = 0$ · Actually, $y$ has the same value at each point of the integral curve.At the point transformed from a point on this curve by this infinitesimal transformation, the value of $y$ increases by $A ( y ) \Delta t$ ，Along the integral curve the quantity $y$ must have the same value whatever $\Delta t$ is. Hence, $A y = 0$

Conversely, if the results of operating with $A$ on the $_ { n }$ independent integrals $y _ { i }$ are all zero, then the system ofdifferential equations admits an infinitesimal transformation $A f .$ At the point transformed from a point on the integral curve, the independent integrals $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ take the values $y _ { 1 } + A ( y _ { 1 } ) \Delta t , y _ { 2 } + A ( y _ { 2 } ) \Delta t , . . . , y _ { n } + A ( y _ { n } ) \Delta t .$ ，respectively. Since they are also the integrals, they are equal to constants and do not depend on the values initially taken; in other words, the points obtained by the infinitesimal transformation form an integral curve.Hence, the differential equations admit an infinitesimal transformation $A f .$ Thus, if there exists a function $f$ for which $A f$ becomes zero, then $f$ is an integral. This is the property that we apply to obtain the integrals of the $_ n$ -body problem in the following section.

When an infinitesimal transformation is a contact transformation, it is an infinitesimal contact transformation and naturally forms a group. Let

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { \Delta q _ { i } = \phi _ { i } ( q , \boldsymbol { \phi } ) \Delta t , } & { \quad \Delta \phi _ { i } = \psi _ { i } ( q , \boldsymbol { \phi } ) \Delta t , } & \\ { q _ { i } ^ { \prime } = q _ { i } + \Delta q _ { i } , } & { \quad \hbar p _ { i } ^ { \prime } = \hbar _ { i } + \Delta p _ { i } . } \end{array}
$$

Then

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { \bigl ( } \beta _ { i } ^ { \prime } d q _ { i } ^ { \prime } - \beta _ { i } d q _ { i } { \bigr ) } = d W ( q , \beta )
$$

is a perfect differential, since it is a contact transformation. Thus

$$
\Delta t \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } ( \psi _ { i } d q _ { i } + \dot { p _ { i } } d \phi _ { i } ) = d W ,
$$

and

$$
\sum _ { i \mathop { = } 1 } ^ { n } ( \psi _ { i } d q _ { i } + \phi _ { i } d \phi _ { i } ) = d U ,
$$

where

$$
d W = U ( q , p ) \Delta t .
$$

Hence

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { ( \psi _ { i } d q _ { i } - \phi _ { i } d p _ { i } ) } = d \bigg ( U - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } / p _ { i } \phi _ { i } \bigg ) = - d H ( q , p ) .
$$

Thus the most general infinitesimal contact transformation is expressed by

$$
q _ { i } ^ { \prime } = q _ { i } + { \frac { \partial H } { \partial p _ { i } } } \Delta t , \qquad { \boldsymbol { \dot { p } } } _ { i } ^ { \prime } = { \boldsymbol { \dot { p } } } _ { i } - { \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { i } } } \Delta t , \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

where $_ H$ is an arbitrary function of $q _ { i }$ and $\mathbf { \nabla } \beta _ { i }$ and $\Delta t$ is an increment independent of $q _ { i } , \mathbf { \nabla } \phi _ { i }$ . This infinitesimal transformation is admitted by a system of differential equations

$$
\frac { d q _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial p _ { i } } , \frac { d \phi _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { i } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Hence, the study of a Hamiltonian system of canonical differential equations is reduced to the study of this infinitesimal contact transformation. By repeating such infinitesimal transformations we can finally obtain the whole trajectory, if we start at the initial values of $q _ { i } , \mathbf { \phi } _ { j }$ of the dynamical system.

Denote two independent infinitesimal increments of an infinitesimal contact transformation by δand $\Delta$ .We have proved in Section 1.8 that a bilinear covariant is invariant under a contact transformation, that is,

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } ( \Delta q _ { i } \delta \phi _ { i } - \delta q _ { i } \Delta \phi _ { i } ) = \mathrm { i n v a r i a n t } ,
$$

or

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \Delta q _ { i } \delta \phi _ { i } - \delta q _ { i } \Delta \phi _ { i } \right) = 0 .
$$

Let $u _ { 1 } = a _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } = a _ { 2 } , . . . , u _ { 2 n } = a _ { 2 n }$ be the integrals in question and $u _ { r }$

be a function of $q _ { i } , p _ { i }$ t.Let $\Delta$ be an increment due to the variation of $a _ { \tau ; \ l }$ and δ be an increment due to the variation of $a _ { s }$ .Then

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \Big ( \frac { \partial q _ { i } } { \partial a _ { r } } \frac { \partial / p _ { i } } { \partial a _ { s } } - \frac { \partial q _ { i } } { \partial a _ { s } } \frac { \partial / p _ { i } } { \partial a _ { r } } \Big ) = 0 ,
$$

or, by definition of the Lagrangian brackets,

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \left[ a _ { r } , a _ { s } \right] = 0 \qquad ( r , s = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Hence, the Lagrangian brackets are invariant during the motion. This is called Lagrange's theorem. We have proved in Section 1.9 that the Lagrangian and Poisson brackets are reciprocal. Hence, the Lagrangian brackets and Poisson brackets formed of $2 n$ independent constants of the integrals are both invariant by any infinitesimal contact transformation, that is,have the same values throughout the whole domain of the motion expressed by a Hamiltonian system of canonical equations.

# 4.3POISSON's THEOREM

We have seen that the function $f ,$ for which the ordinary differential equation (l5） has an integral $f ( x _ { i } , t ) =$ constant,satisfies the first-order partial differential equation (l4).Let $F ( x _ { i } , t ) =$ constant be an integral of a Hamiltonian system of differential equations (l7); then (l4) can be written in the form

$$
\frac { \partial F } { \partial t } + ( H , F ) = 0 ,
$$

where

$$
( H , F ) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \frac { \partial H } { \partial \boldsymbol { \dot { p } _ { i } } } \frac { \partial F } { \partial q _ { i } } - \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { i } } \frac { \partial F } { \partial \boldsymbol { \dot { p } _ { i } } } \right)
$$

is a Poisson bracket.In other words,an integral $F =$ constant of a system of canonical equations should satisfy equation (l8). Conversely, as we have proved, a solution, $F =$ constant, of a partial differential equation (18) is an integral of the Hamiltonian system of canonical equations (17). We call this Poisson's first theorem.

From this theorem with a Poisson operator

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \frac { \partial H } { \partial \dot { p } _ { i } } \frac { \partial } { \partial q _ { i } } - \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { i } } \frac { \partial } { \partial \dot { p } _ { i } } \right)
$$

we desire

$$
\begin{array} { r } { ( H , f _ { 1 } + f _ { 2 } ) = ( H , f _ { 1 } ) + ( H , f _ { 2 } ) , \qquad ( H , c f ) = c ( H , f ) , } \\ { ( H , f _ { 1 } f _ { 2 } ) = f _ { 1 } ( H , f _ { 2 } ) + f _ { 2 } ( H , f _ { 1 } ) . \qquad } \end{array}
$$

Generally,

$$
( H , F ) = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left( H , f _ { j } \right) { \frac { \partial F } { \partial f _ { j } } } .
$$

Hence, a Poisson operator is a linear operator and a special type of commutator.

Here we have an important identity

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \left( u , \left( v , w \right) \right) + \left( v , \left( w , u \right) \right) + \left( w , \left( u , v \right) \right) \equiv 0 . } \end{array}
$$

This is called Poisson's identity.

By our' construction，the left-hand member of (l9） is the sum of products of three derivatives of $u , \ v , \ w$ 、Two factors come from the first-order derivatives of two of $u , v , w$ ; the third factor is a second-order derivative of the remaining third variable.If we can show that the sum does not contain any second-order derivative, then the theorem will be proved.Consider two Poisson operators $A f = ( v , f )$ and $B f = ( w , f )$ operating on $f .$ Then the left-hand side of (19)becomes

$$
( u , ( v , w ) ) + A B u - B A u = ( u , ( v , w ) ) + ( A , B ) u ,
$$

where $( A , B )$ is a commutator operator.If the sum contains a secondorder derivative of $\boldsymbol { u }$ ，then such a derivative should be contained in $( A , B ) u$ ，because $( u , ( v , w ) )$ contains only first-order derivatives of $\boldsymbol { u }$ But by (13), $( A , B )$ contains only first-order derivatives.

Next consider Poisson's second theorem. $\operatorname { I f } f _ { 1 }$ and $f _ { 2 }$ are two independent solutions of canonical equations (l7), then a Poisson bracket $( f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } )$ is also an independent solution of (17).In other words, if

$$
\frac { \partial f _ { 1 } } { \partial t } + ( H , f _ { 1 } ) = 0 , \frac { \partial f _ { 2 } } { \partial t } + ( H , f _ { 2 } ) = 0 ,
$$

then

$$
\frac { \partial ( f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } ) } { \partial t } + ( H , ( f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } ) ) = 0 .
$$

By Poisson’s identity (19),

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \left( f _ { 1 } , ( f _ { 2 } , H ) \right) + \left( f _ { 2 } , ( H , f _ { 1 } ) \right) + \left( H , ( f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } ) \right) \equiv 0 . } \end{array}
$$

From our assumption, we obtain

$$
\left( f _ { 1 } , \frac { \partial f _ { 2 } } { \partial t } \right) - \left( f _ { 2 } , \frac { \partial f _ { 1 } } { \partial t } \right) + \left( H , \left( f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } \right) \right) \equiv 0 .
$$

But we know that

$$
\frac { \partial } { \partial t } \left( f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } \right) = \left( f _ { 1 } , \frac { \partial f _ { 2 } } { \partial t } \right) - \left( f _ { 2 } , \frac { \partial f _ { 1 } } { \partial t } \right) \cdot
$$

This proves the theorem.

# LIE'S THEOREM

# 4·4ELIMINATION OF TIME

Suppose that in the canonical equations

$$
\frac { \partial q _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial p _ { i } } , \frac { \partial p _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { i } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

$H$ does not contain $m$ coordinates $q _ { i }$ . Then these equations have the integrals

$$
{ p } _ { r } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } = { p } _ { r } ^ { \left( 0 \right) } ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

and the equations split into two sets of equations: the first set is

$$
\frac { d q _ { m + j } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial p _ { m + j } } , \frac { d \phi _ { m + j } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { m + j } } \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - m ) ,
$$

and the second set is

$$
\frac { d q _ { r } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial / p _ { r } } \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

In the first set of equations, $H$ does not contain $q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , \ldots , q _ { m }$ This set can be solved as $2 ( n - m )$ equations by putting $\phi _ { r } = \phi _ { r } ^ { \left( 0 \right) }$ ，that is, a system of equations of order $2 ( n - m )$ . The general integral contains $2 ( n - m )$ constants. Substitute this solution in the second set of equations and solve for $q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , \ldots , q _ { m }$ There are $_ m$ integration constants $q _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , q _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , q _ { m } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ ,making $2 n$ integration constants altogether. Hence, if $\boldsymbol { H }$ does not contain the $q _ { i } , m$ in number, the order of differential equations (20) is reduced by $2 m$

Next suppose that $H$ does not contain $t$ explicitly, then the integral

$$
H = h = { \tt c o n s t a n t } ,
$$

is obtained, which is the energy integral. In this case we can reduce the order of equations (2O) by two.In fact,if we put aside a solution such as that in which $q _ { i }$ and $\mathbf { \nabla } \beta _ { \mathrm { i } }$ are all constant, then each pair $q _ { i } , \mathbf { \phi } _ { j } $ de-pends on t.Let one of the latter pairs be $q _ { n } , \ b _ { n }$ Since $d q _ { n } / d t = 0$ ,therefore $\partial H / \partial { \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { n } \neq 0$ . Now we can solve $H = h$ for ${ \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { \pmb { \mathscr { n } } }$ ，

$$
{ \mathfrak { p } } _ { n } = K ( q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { n } , { \mathfrak { p } } _ { 1 } , . . . , { \mathfrak { p } } _ { n - 1 } , { \mathfrak { h } } ) .
$$

Divide each of the first $2 ( n - 1 )$ equations of (20) side by side by $d q _ { n } / d t = \partial H / \partial p _ { n }$ .Let $\boldsymbol { u }$ be any one of $q _ { 1 } , \dotsc , q _ { n - 1 } , \dotsc , p _ { 1 } , \dotsc , p _ { n - 1 }$ . Since

$$
\frac { \partial K } { \partial u } = - \frac { \partial H / \partial u } { \partial H / \partial { \boldsymbol { \rho } _ { n } } }
$$

we obtain

$$
\frac { d q _ { i } } { d q _ { n } } = \ - \frac { \partial K } { \partial / } , \frac { d / _ { i } } { d q _ { n } } = \frac { \partial K } { \partial q _ { i } } ~ ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - 1 ) .
$$

The function $\kappa$ does not contain ${ \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { \pmb { \mathscr { n } } }$ .Hence the order of differential equations is reduced to $2 ( n - 1 )$ . After solving these equations we obtain $t$ from

$$
d t = \frac { d q _ { n } } { \partial H / \partial { p _ { n } } }
$$

without solving any differential equation.We call this procedure the elimination of time.

# 4.5INVOLUTION

If among the conjugate variables $q _ { i } , \ p _ { i } \ ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ ，of (20), there exist $m \ ( m < n )$ ,relations

$$
f _ { r } ( q , \beta ) = 0 \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

from which we can derive Poisson’s equations

$$
( f _ { \tau } , f _ { s } ) = 0 ( r , s = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

and if (23) can be solved for $_ m$ variables $\mathbf { \nabla } \phi _ { i }$ , forexample, for $\rho _ { 1 } , \rho _ { 2 } , . . . . , \rho _ { m } ;$ that is, if in involution. $\begin{array} { l } { \partial ( f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , . . . , f _ { m } ) / \partial ( \phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } , . . . , \phi _ { m } ) \neq 0 } \\ { n . } \end{array}$ ，then (23) is said to be

If (23) is solved for ${ \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { 1 } , { \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { 2 } , \dots { \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { m }$ and is reduced to the form

$\begin{array} { r } { p _ { i } = \phi _ { i } ( p _ { m + 1 } , \ldots , p _ { n } , q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , \ldots , q _ { n } ) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , m ) , } \end{array}$ then from (24) we can derive

$$
( \phi _ { i } - \phi _ { i } , \phi _ { i } - \phi _ { j } ) = 0 \qquad ( i , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

In other words, (25) is also in involution.This theorem is called Jacobi's lemma.

Put $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { u } }$ for any one of the variables $q _ { i } , \ p _ { i }$ that is not ${ \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { 1 } , { \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { 2 } , \dots { \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { m }$ From (23),

$$
\frac { \partial f _ { r } } { \partial u } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \frac { \partial f _ { r } } { \partial \rho _ { i } } \frac { \partial \phi _ { i } } { \partial u } = 0 \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

Hence,

$$
{ \frac { \partial f _ { r } } { \partial u } } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } { \frac { \partial f _ { r } } { \partial \dot { p } _ { i } } } { \frac { \partial ( \dot { p } _ { i } - \dot { \phi } _ { i } ) } { \partial u } } \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

If $\boldsymbol { u }$ is any one of ${ \bf { \dot { p } } } _ { 1 } , p _ { 2 } , . . . . , p _ { m } ,$ then it is an identity. Thus,

$$
{ \frac { \partial f _ { r } } { \partial q _ { k } } } { \frac { \partial f _ { s } } { \partial \dot { p } _ { k } } } = \sum _ { i , j } ^ { 1 , \cdots , m } { \frac { \partial f _ { r } } { \partial \dot { p } _ { i } } } { \frac { \partial f _ { s } } { \partial \dot { p } _ { j } } } { \frac { \partial ( \dot { p } _ { i } - \dot { \phi } _ { i } ) } { \partial q _ { k } } } { \frac { \partial ( \dot { p } _ { j } - \dot { \phi } _ { j } ) } { \partial \dot { p } _ { k } } } \cdot
$$

From this,

$$
( f _ { r } , f _ { s } ) = \sum _ { i , j } ^ { 1 , \cdots , m } \frac { \partial f _ { r } } { \partial \rho _ { i } } \frac { \partial f _ { s } } { \partial q _ { j } } ( \rho _ { i } - \phi _ { i } , \rho _ { j } - \phi _ { j } ) .
$$

From our assumption (24), we obtain

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \frac { \partial f _ { \tau } } { \partial \dot { p } _ { j } } \left[ \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { m } \frac { \partial f _ { s } } { \partial \dot { p } _ { i } } \left( \dot { p } _ { i } - \phi _ { i } , \dot { p } _ { j } - \phi _ { j } \right) \right] = 0 \qquad ( r , s = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

Consider these equations as a system of $m$ simultaneous linear equations for the $_ m$ unknown functions in the parentheses. Then

$$
\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { m } \frac { \partial f _ { s } } { \partial \dot { p } _ { j } } \left( \dot { p } _ { i } - \phi _ { i } , \dot { p } _ { j } - \phi _ { j } \right) = 0 \qquad ( \dot { i } , s = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

because $\partial ( f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , . . . , f _ { m } ) / \partial ( \substack { \scriptstyle \beta _ { 1 } , \ b \beta _ { 2 } , . . . , \ b \beta _ { m } } ) \neq 0 ,$ . By a similar argument,

$$
\big ( / p _ { i } - \phi _ { i } , p _ { j } - \phi _ { j } \big ) = 0 \qquad ( i , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

and the lemma is proved.

Einstein in a l929 paper applied the idea of involution to the unified field theory of general relativity (Cartan, 1931).

# 4.6INVARIANTIVE RELATIONS

We say that

$$
f ( t , x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) = 0
$$

is an invariantive relation with regard to

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = X _ { i } ( t , x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

when the relation (27） satisfying the differential equations (28） for a particular value of $t$ causes it to satisfy them for all values of $t .$ An

invariantive relation does not contain any integration constants (unlike integrals of the equations).

$\operatorname { I f } f = 0$ is an invariantive relation with regard to (28), then

$$
{ \frac { d f } { d t } } = { \frac { \partial f } { \partial t } } + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } X _ { j } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { j } } } = 0 ,
$$

or,if $\lambda$ denotes a function of $t$ $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ;$

$$
\frac { d f } { d t } = \lambda f .
$$

Equation (30) is the necessary and suffcient condition for (27) to be an invariantive relation with regard to (28). Indeed, $d f / d t$ is a function of $t , ~ x _ { i }$ If we substitute for $x _ { n }$ its value obtained from $f = 0$ ，then df/dt becomes a function $G ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n - 1 } , f , t )$ of $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n - 1 } , f , t . \ \mathrm { E x } .$ panding this function with respect to $f$ gives

$$
{ \frac { d f } { d t } } = G ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n - 1 } , 0 , t ) + \left( { \frac { \partial G } { \partial f } } \right) _ { f = 0 } f + \cdot \cdot \cdot . .
$$

If f= 0,)then df $\boldsymbol { \mathit { \check { d } } } t = 0$ Hence, $G ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n - 1 } , 0 , t ) = 0$ Denote the remaining terms by $\lambda$ ; this gives (30). Conversely, if $f = 0$ in (30), then ${ d f } / { d t } = 0$

In general, if there are $m + 1$ independent relations

$$
\begin{array} { r } { f _ { r } ( t , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) = 0 \qquad ( r = 0 , 1 , . . . , m ) , } \end{array}
$$

the necessary and sufficient condition for (31） to be invariantive with regard to (28) is that

$$
\frac { d f _ { r } } { d t } = \sum _ { s = 0 } ^ { m } \lambda _ { r s } f _ { s } \qquad ( r = 0 , 1 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

where $\lambda _ { \tau s }$ are functions of $t$ $, x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ and $d / d t$ has the meaning given in (29).

Now suppose that we know the solution ${ \pmb x } _ { i } ( t )$ of (28) and that ${ \pmb x } _ { i } ( t ) + \delta { \pmb x } _ { i }$ also satisfies (28). $\delta \boldsymbol { x } _ { \tau }$ is a function of $t$ and δ obeys (27).We consider $\delta$ to be simultaneous displacements, that is, $\delta t = 0$ Then

$$
{ \frac { d \delta x _ { i } } { d t } } = \delta X _ { i } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } { \frac { \partial X _ { i } } { \partial x _ { j } } } \delta x _ { j }
$$

represents the difference between the varied motion with a modified initial condition and the original motion as a function of $t .$ Suppose that the variables $x _ { i }$ in ${ \partial X _ { i } } / { \partial x _ { j } }$ are replaced by the known functions ${ x _ { i } } ( t ) .$ of ${ \pmb t } .$

Poincaré calld (33) the variational equation (equation aux variations) for (28). From (33),

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \delta x _ { i } = \delta \frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } .
$$

This expresses the commutativity of δ and $d / d t$ 、More generally, the relation

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \delta f = \delta \frac { d f } { d t }
$$

holds true for any function $f \circ \mathrm { f } \ t , x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n }$ .δ represents the variation obeying (28) and $d / d t$ has the meaning given in (29).

Consider the variation

$$
\delta f = 0
$$

together with the invariantive relation (27) with regard to the canonical equations (20). Since the initial displacement $\delta { x } _ { i }$ of (33) is arbitrary, (34) is reduced to

$$
\frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { i } } = 0 \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

If the combination of (35) $\mathbf { a n d } f = 0$ gives compatible relations among the $\pmb { n }$ variables $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ,$ ,then (35) is an invariantive relation. Combining (30）with

$$
\delta f = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { j } } \delta x _ { j } \quad \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad \quad \frac { d } { d t } \delta f = \delta \frac { d f } { d t } ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \frac { d } { d t } \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { j } } \right) \delta x _ { j } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { i } } \left( \lambda \delta x _ { i } - \frac { d \delta x _ { i } } { d t } \right) + f \delta \lambda .
$$

Referring to (27) and (35) and noting that $\delta { x } _ { j }$ is arbitrary, we see that

$$
{ \frac { d } { d t } } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { j } } } = 0 \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

Hence, $\partial f / \partial x _ { j } = 0$ is an invariantive relation.The relations (35) obtained from (34) are called the stationarity condition.

More generally, $m + 1$ independent invariantive relations of form (31) with regard to differential equations (28) should satisfy the relations of form (32). Assume that the system of differential equations representing the stationarity condition that one of the invariantive relations

$$
\delta f _ { 0 } = 0
$$

should be stationary for all possible virtual displacements satisfies not only $f = 0$ but also

$$
f _ { 1 } = 0 , f _ { 2 } = 0 , . . . , f _ { m } = 0 .
$$

Then we can express (37) in terms of $x _ { m + 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ by solving them with respect to $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { m }$ Use $f _ { 1 } , \ldots , f _ { m }$ as new variables in place of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { m }$ .Then (28) can be written

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { \displaystyle \frac { d f _ { r } } { d t } = \Xi _ { r } } & { ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = \Xi _ { i } } & { ( i = m + 1 , . . . , n ) . } \end{array}
$$

The function $\Xi$ is the result of substituting $f _ { 1 } , \ldots , f _ { m }$ for $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { m }$ respectively, in $X ,$ and is a function ( $\mathrm { \sf f } f _ { 1 } , . . . , f _ { m } , x _ { m + 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } , t .$

From the first equation of (32) and the last $n - m$ equations of (38) we obtain

$$
\frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial t } + \sum _ { r = 1 } ^ { m } \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial f _ { r } } \frac { d f _ { r } } { d t } + \sum _ { i = m + 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } \Xi _ { i } = \sum _ { s = 0 } ^ { m } \lambda _ { 0 s } f _ { s } .
$$

Putting $f _ { 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = f _ { m } = 0$ in (32) gives

$$
\overline { { \left( \frac { d f _ { r } } { d t } \right) } } = \bar { \lambda } _ { r 0 } \bar { f } _ { 0 } \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

where, in general, $\bar { F }$ is the result of substituting $f _ { 1 } , \ldots , f _ { m }$ for $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { m } ;$ respectivelyin $F ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , t )$ according to (37). Again， since $t , x _ { m + 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ do not depend $\mathsf { o n f } _ { 1 } , \ldots . . . , f _ { m } ;$ ，therefore

$$
\overline { { \left( \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial t } \right) } } = \frac { \partial \bar { f } _ { 0 } } { \partial t } , \qquad \overline { { \left( \frac { \partial \bar { f } _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { r } } \right) } } = \frac { \partial \bar { f } _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { r } } \qquad ( r = m + 1 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

and

$$
\frac { \partial \bar { f } _ { 0 } } { \partial t } + \sum _ { i = m + 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial \bar { f } _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } \Xi _ { i } = \left[ \bar { \lambda } _ { 0 0 } - \sum _ { r = 1 } ^ { m } \overline { { \left( \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial \bar { f } _ { r } } \right) } } \bar { \lambda } _ { r 0 } \right] \bar { f } _ { 0 } .
$$

The left-hand member of this equation is a perfect differential of $\bar { f } _ { 0 }$ and the right-hand side is of the form $\lambda \bar { f } _ { 0 }$ . Since the equation is of the form $d \bar { f } _ { 0 } / d t = \lambda \bar { f } _ { 0 }$ ，the equation $\bar { f } _ { 0 } = 0$ is an invariantive relation with regard to

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = \Xi _ { i } \qquad ( i = m + 1 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Thus,if there exists an invariantive relation (37), then there exists also an invariantive relation $f = 0$ ,in the form of the variational equation (36), with regard to equations (38) which were obtained by eliminating some of the variables.

We now apply this theorem to the canonical equations (20). Suppose that $H$ does not contain $t _ { : }$ ,and that there exist $_ m$ invariantive relations

$$
f _ { r } ( q , \pmb { \mathscr { p } } ) = 0 \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

which do not contain $t _ { : }$ ，are independent of $H =$ constant, and are mutually in involution. According to Jacobi's lemma the equations

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \begin{array} { r l } { \mathfrak { h } \mathbin { - } \phi _ { i } \bigl ( \hbar \mathfrak { p } _ { + 1 } , \cdot \cdot \cdot , \mathfrak { p } _ { n } , q _ { 1 } , \cdot \cdot \cdot , q _ { n } \bigr ) = 0 } & { { } \quad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) , } \end{array} } \end{array}
$$

obtained by solving (39) are also in involution.Write

$$
\left\{ u , v \right\} = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n - m } \bigg ( \frac { \partial u } { \partial / p _ { m + j } } \frac { \partial v } { \partial q _ { m + j } } - \frac { \partial u } { \partial q _ { m + j } } \frac { \partial v } { \partial / p _ { m + j } } \bigg ) .
$$

Then the condition for involution $( \ p _ { i } - \phi _ { i } , \ p _ { j } - \phi _ { j } ) = 0$ is written

$$
\frac { \partial \phi _ { i } } { \partial q _ { j } } - \frac { \partial \phi _ { j } } { \partial q _ { i } } + \{ \phi _ { i } , \phi _ { j } \} = 0 ~ ( i , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

because the right-hand side of (41） does not depend on ${ \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { j }$ $( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , m )$ , according to (40).

We now express the fact that (4O) are invariantive relations with regard to (20): differentiate (40) with respect to $t$ and refer to (20); this gives

$$
{ \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { i } } } + \{ H , \phi _ { i } \} + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { m } { \frac { \partial H } { \partial \phi _ { j } } } { \frac { \partial \phi _ { j } } { \partial q _ { i } } } = 0 \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

Let $\bar { H }$ be the result of substituting (40） in $H$ ； then the differentialcoefficients of $\overleftarrow { H } ( \mathfrak { p } _ { m + 1 } , \dots , \mathfrak { p } _ { n } , q _ { 1 } , \dots , q _ { n } )$ are

$$
\frac { \partial \overline { { H } } } { \partial \mathfrak { p } _ { m + j } } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial \mathfrak { p } _ { m + j } } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \frac { \partial H } { \partial \mathfrak { p } _ { i } } \frac { \partial \phi _ { i } } { \partial \mathfrak { p } _ { m + j } } ,
$$

$$
{ \frac { \partial \overline { { { \cal H } } } } { \partial q _ { m + j } } } = { \frac { \partial { \cal H } } { \partial q _ { m + j } } } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \frac { \partial { \cal H } } { \partial / _ { i } } { \frac { \partial \phi _ { i } } { \partial q _ { m + j } } } ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - m ) ,
$$

$$
{ \frac { \partial \overline { { { \cal H } } } } { \partial q _ { r } } } = { \frac { \partial { \cal H } } { \partial q _ { r } } } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } { \frac { \partial { \cal H } } { \partial \beta _ { i } } } { \frac { \partial \phi _ { i } } { \partial q _ { r } } } \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

From (43),

$$
\{ \overleftrightarrow { H } , \phi _ { r } \} = \{ H , \phi _ { r } \} - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \frac { \partial H } { \partial / } \{ \phi _ { r } , \phi _ { i } \} \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

Adding (44) and this equation side by side gives

$$
{ \frac { \partial \overline { { H } } } { \partial q _ { r } } } + \{ \overline { { H } } , \phi _ { r } \} = { \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { r } } } + \{ H , \phi _ { r } \} + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } { \frac { \partial H } { \partial \phi _ { i } } } \biggl ( { \frac { \partial \phi _ { i } } { \partial q _ { r } } } - \{ \phi _ { r } , \phi _ { i } \} \biggr )
$$

Finally,referring to (4l) and (42), we obtain

$$
\frac { \partial \widehat { H } } { \partial q _ { r } } + \{ \widehat { H } , \phi _ { r } \} = 0 ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

Now, as $H$ is an integral of (2O), the stationarity condition for $H ,$ that is, $\delta H = 0$ ，is satisfied for all possible displacements fulfilling the conditions (45).As we have proved, the condition is equivalent to $\delta \bar { \cal H } = 0 .$ or to

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \displaystyle \frac { \partial \widehat { H } } { \partial \beta _ { m + j } } = 0 , \quad } & { \displaystyle \frac { \partial \widehat { H } } { \partial q _ { m + j } } = 0 \quad \quad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - m ) , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { \partial \widehat { H } } { \partial q _ { r } } = 0 \quad } & { ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) . } \end{array}
$$

But the last $\pmb { m }$ equations can be derived from the first $n - m$ equations by means of (4O). Hence, even if there exist $_ m$ relations of the form (29) or (40),the stationarity condition $\delta H = 0$ gives only $2 ( n - m )$ relations. This is called Levi-Civita's theorem.This discussion is based on those particular solutions of differential equations that satisfy the stationarity condition.We shall now prove that even in the general solution the order of canonical differential equations is reduced by $2 m$ when $_ m$ integrals are mutually in involution.

# 4.7LIOUVILLE's THEOREM

If a canonical system of 2nth-order equations admits $\pmb { n }$ integrals that are independent, in involution,and solvable for $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ ，then the differential equations can be solved by quadrature alone. This is called Liouville's theorem.

Suppose that we know $\pmb { n }$ mutually independent integrals

$$
f _ { r } ( q , \beta , t ) = c _ { r } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

in involution and solvable for $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ . It is sufficient to prove by the Hamilton-Jacobi theorem that we can obtain a complete integral $W ( q , c , t )$ of

$$
{ \frac { \partial W } { \partial t } } + H = 0
$$

by mere quadrature. Since $\mathcal { f } _ { \tau }$ are integrals of the canonical equations, then

$$
{ \frac { \partial f _ { r } } { \partial t } } + ( H , f _ { r } ) = 0 \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

by Poisson's first theorem and, moreover,

$$
( f _ { r } , f _ { s } ) = 0 \qquad ( r , s = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Take a new pair of variables $q _ { 0 } = t$ and $\scriptstyle { \hat { p } } _ { 0 }$ besides ${ q _ { i } , p _ { i } } ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ ， and suppose that $\scriptstyle { \hat { p } } _ { 0 }$ is an auxiliary parameter contained neither in $H$ nor in $f .$ Denote these $2 ( n + 1 )$ canonical variables by $u , \ v$ and the Poisson brackets for these variables by $( ( u , v ) )$ . Then,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \displaystyle ( \left( \boldsymbol { u } , \boldsymbol { v } \right) \big ) = \frac { \partial \boldsymbol { u } } { \partial \boldsymbol { \hat { p } _ { 0 } } } \frac { \partial \boldsymbol { v } } { \partial t } - \frac { \partial \boldsymbol { u } } { \partial t } \frac { \partial \boldsymbol { v } } { \partial \boldsymbol { \hat { p } _ { 0 } } } + ( \boldsymbol { u } , \boldsymbol { v } ) . } \end{array}
$$

When neither $\boldsymbol { u }$ nor $v$ contains $\scriptstyle { \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { 0 }$ , then $( ( u , v ) ) = ( u , v )$ and

$$
\left( ( { \cdot p _ { 0 } + H , f _ { r } } ) \right) = \frac { \partial f _ { r } } { \partial t } + ( H , f _ { r } ) \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Hence,conditions (46), (47） become

$$
\left( \left( p _ { 0 } + H , f _ { r } \right) \right) = 0 , \left( \left( f _ { r } , f _ { s } \right) \right) = 0 \left( r , s = 1 , 2 , . . . , n \right) .
$$

These equations show that $p _ { 0 } + H { \mathrm { a n d } } f _ { r }$ are in involution for the $n + 1$ pairs of $q$ and $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { p } }$ . Since we can solve equations

$$
f _ { r } = c _ { r } , ~ p _ { 0 } + H = 0 ~ ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

for $\ p _ { 0 } , p _ { 1 } , . . . . , p _ { n } ,$ we can express $\scriptstyle { \hat { p } } _ { 0 }$ as a function of $q _ { i } , ~ c _ { i } , ~ t$ by the second equation. Let them be expressed by

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { { \dot { p } _ { r } - \phi _ { r } ( q , c , t ) = 0 } } & { { { } ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) , } } \\ { { \dot { p } _ { 0 } - \phi _ { 0 } ( q , c , t ) = 0 . } } & { { { } } } \end{array}
$$

Hence, $\phi _ { 0 }$ is the result of substituting $\phi _ { \tau }$ for $\scriptstyle { \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { \pmb { \tau } }$ in $H .$ From Jacobi's lemma,we obtain

$$
\big ( \big ( \boldsymbol { \rho } _ { r } - \boldsymbol { \phi } _ { r } , \boldsymbol { \phi } _ { s } - \boldsymbol { \phi } _ { s } \big ) \big ) = 0 \qquad ( \boldsymbol { r } , s = 0 , 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

The functions $\phi$ do not contain $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ in these equations, therefore

$$
\frac { \partial \phi _ { r } } { \partial q _ { s } } = \frac { \partial \phi _ { s } } { \partial q _ { r } } \qquad ( r , s = 0 , 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

This shows that there exists a function $W$ such that

$$
\frac { \partial W } { \partial t } = \phi _ { 0 } , \frac { \partial W } { \partial q _ { r } } = \phi _ { r } \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Hence we can obtain $W$ from these equations by mere quadrature and Liouville's theorem is proved.

# 4.8LIE's THEOREM

If in a canonical system of 2nth-order differential equations we know $m$ integrals that are independent,in involution,and solvable for $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { p } }$ ,then we can reduce the order of the equations to $2 ( n - m )$ . This is called Lie's theorem.

Define $q _ { 0 } = t$ and $\pmb { \hat { p } _ { 0 } }$ as in the proof of Liouville's theorem; this gives integrals

$$
p _ { 0 } + H = 0 ,
$$

$$
f _ { r } ( q , \beta , t ) \ : - \ : c _ { r } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

that are independent, in involution, and solvable for $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ .Hence they can be written

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { { \dot { p } _ { r } - \phi _ { r } ( q , c , t ) = 0 } } & { { { } ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) , } } \\ { { \dot { p } _ { 0 } - \phi _ { 0 } ( q , c , t ) = 0 , } } & { { { } } } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
( ( \rho _ { r } - \phi _ { r } , \phi _ { s } - \phi _ { s } ) ) = 0 \qquad ( r , s = 0 , 1 , . . . , m ) .
$$

Also, the $n + 1$ integrals (49) are mutually in involution.As in Liouville's theorem, the canonical equations (2O) can be written in one of the forms

$$
\frac { \partial W } { \partial t } + H = 0 , ~ \ p _ { 0 } + H = 0 ,
$$

$$
{ \frac { \partial W } { \partial q _ { 0 } } } + H { \Big ( } q _ { 0 } , q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { n } , { \frac { \partial W } { \partial q _ { 1 } } } , . . . , { \frac { \partial W } { \partial q _ { n } } } { \Big ) } = 0 .
$$

The $\pmb { m }$ known integrals are written in the form

$$
\frac { \partial W } { \partial q _ { r } } - \phi _ { r } ( q _ { 0 } , q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { n } , c _ { 1 } , . . . , c _ { m } ) = 0 \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

Thus the canonical equations (2O) can be replaced by this system of $m + 1$ partial differential equations of $n + 1$ variables.Hence, to have integrals that are independent, in involution,and solvable for $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ ina partial differential equation of $n + 1$ variables, is equivalent to having a system of $m + 1$ partial differential equations for $n + 1$ variables that are in involution.It is sufficient to prove that this system of $m + 1$ partial differential equations can be reduced to a partial differential equation of $n + 1 - m$ variables.

Let the system of $m + 1$ partial differential equations be

$$
{ \frac { \partial W } { \partial q _ { i } } } = \phi _ { i } \Bigl ( q _ { 0 } , q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { n } , { \frac { \partial W } { \partial q _ { m + 1 } } } , . . . , { \frac { \partial W } { \partial q _ { n } } } \Bigr ) \qquad ( i = 0 , 1 , . . . , m ) .
$$

This is obtained by substituting (49) in

$$
{ \boldsymbol { \phi } } _ { r } = { \frac { \partial W } { \partial q _ { r } } } \qquad ( r = 0 , 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

and eliminating $\pmb { \hat { p } } _ { 0 } , \pmb { \hat { p } } _ { 1 } , \dots \pmb { \hat { p } } _ { m }$ It also satisfies (50). To integrate (51): according to Jacobi's lemma,find integrals (not containing ${ p _ { 1 } , p _ { 2 } , . . . , p _ { m } } )$ of the equations

$$
( \phi _ { i } - \phi _ { i } , f ) = 0 \qquad ( i = 0 , 1 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

or of

$$
\frac { \partial f } { \partial q _ { i } } + \sum _ { j = m + 1 } ^ { n } \left( \frac { \partial \phi _ { i } } { \partial q _ { j } } \frac { \partial f } { \partial \dot { p } _ { j } } - \frac { \partial \phi _ { i } } { \partial \dot { p } _ { j } } \frac { \partial f } { \partial q _ { j } } \right) = 0 \qquad ( i = 0 , 1 , . . . , m ) .
$$

For that purpose, take $\pmb { x } _ { 0 } , \pmb { x } _ { 1 } , \dots , \pmb { x } _ { m }$ as new variables after carrying out Mayer's transformation

$$
q _ { 0 } = q _ { o } ^ { ( 0 ) } + x _ { 0 } , \qquad q _ { 1 } = q _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } + x _ { 0 } x _ { 1 } , \qquad \ldots , \qquad q _ { m } = q _ { m } ^ { ( 0 ) } + x _ { 0 } x _ { m } .
$$

Then (52) can be written

$$
{ \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { i } } } + \sum _ { j = m + 1 } ^ { n } \left( { \frac { \partial \Phi _ { i } } { \partial q _ { j } } } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial \dot { p } _ { j } } } - { \frac { \partial \Phi _ { i } } { \partial \dot { p } _ { j } } } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial q _ { j } } } \right) = 0 \qquad ( i = 0 , 1 , . . . , m ) .
$$

Here $\Phi _ { i }$ $\mathbf { \sigma } _ { i } ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m )$ and $\Phi _ { 0 }$ are the results of performing Mayer's transformation on $\phi _ { i } ( i = 1 , . . . , m )$ and on

$$
\phi _ { 0 } + x _ { 1 } \phi _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + x _ { m } \phi _ { m } ,
$$

respectively. We have

$$
\frac { \partial W } { \partial q _ { 0 } } = \Phi _ { 0 } , \frac { \partial W } { \partial q _ { r } } = x _ { 0 } \Phi _ { r } \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , m )
$$

after Mayer's transformation on (51). Thus the relation between (51) and (52) corresponds to the relation between (53) and (54). Hence, integrating either both or the first equation (54) is equivalent to integrating a linear differential equation

$$
( \ p _ { 0 } - \Phi _ { 0 } , f ) = 0 ,
$$

or integrating the first equation of (53).

Our next step is to prove that, if we can integrate an equation of $n + 1 - m$ variables

$$
\frac { \partial W } { \partial q _ { 0 } } - \Phi _ { 0 } = 0 ,
$$

then we can obtain a complete integral of (54). This equation can be written

$$
{ \frac { \partial W } { \partial x _ { 0 } } } = \Phi _ { 0 } { \Big ( } x _ { 0 } , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { m } , q _ { m + 1 } , \ldots , q _ { n } , { \frac { \partial W } { \partial q _ { m + 1 } } } , \ldots , { \frac { \partial W } { \partial q _ { n } } } { \Big ) } .
$$

By the Hamilton-Jacobi theorem, this is equivalent to integrating

$$
{ \frac { d q _ { i } } { d x _ { 0 } } } = \ - { \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial p _ { i } } } , { \frac { d \phi _ { i } } { d x _ { 0 } } } = { \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial q _ { i } } } \qquad ( i = m + 1 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Then integrals are obtained in the form

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { q _ { i } = q _ { i } ( x _ { 0 } , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { m } , a _ { m + 1 } , . . . , a _ { n } , b _ { m + 1 } , . . . , b _ { n } ) , } \\ & { } & { \dot { p } _ { i } = \dot { p } _ { i } ( x _ { 0 } , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { m } , a _ { m + 1 } , . . . , a _ { n } , b _ { m + 1 } , . . . , b _ { n } ) , } \\ & { } & { ( i = m + } \end{array}
$$

Here the values of $\ p _ { m + 1 } , \ldots , \ p _ { n } , \ q _ { m + 1 } , \ldots , \ q _ { n }$ for $x _ { 0 } = 0$ are denoted by $a _ { m + 1 } , \ldots , a _ { n } , b _ { m + 1 } , \ldots , b _ { n } ,$ respectively.Then

$$
W = \sum _ { i = m + 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i } b _ { i } + \int _ { 0 } ^ { x _ { 0 } } \left( \Phi _ { 0 } - \sum _ { i = m + 1 } ^ { n } \mathcal { P } _ { i } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial \dot { p } _ { i } } \right) d x _ { 0 }
$$

is an integral of $\partial W / \partial x _ { 0 } = \Phi _ { 0 }$ that becomes

$$
a _ { m + 1 } q _ { m + 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + a _ { n } q _ { n } \qquad \mathrm { f o r } \quad x _ { 0 } = 0 .
$$

Since the solution is

$$
{ \boldsymbol { \hat { p } _ { i } } } = { \frac { \partial W } { \partial q _ { i } } } , \qquad { \boldsymbol { b _ { i } } } = { \frac { \partial W } { \partial a _ { i } } } , \qquad ( { \boldsymbol { i } } = m + 1 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

we have

$$
\sum _ { i = m + 1 } ^ { n } { \frac { \partial W } { \partial q _ { i } } } d q _ { i } = \sum _ { i = m + 1 } ^ { n } \beta _ { i } d q _ { i } = \sum _ { i = m + 1 } ^ { n } \left( - \beta _ { i } { \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial \dot { p } _ { i } } } \right) d x _ { 0 } .
$$

Furthermore, $W$ contains $\scriptstyle x _ { 0 }$ and $\partial W / \partial x _ { 0 } = \Phi _ { 0 }$ Hence,

$$
W = \int _ { 0 } ^ { x _ { 0 } } \Phi _ { 0 } d x _ { 0 } .
$$

Adding these two equations gives (57).

Substitute in $W$ the values of $b _ { m + 1 } , \ldots , b _ { n }$ obtained from (56), giving $\overline { { W } } .$ We now prove that $\overline { W }$ satisfies (51), if put in the place of $W .$

Substitute the values from (56) for $q _ { m + 1 } , \ldots , q _ { n }$ in $W .$ . This gives $\overline { W }$ ， according to our definition. Consequently,

$$
\frac { \partial W } { \partial x _ { i } } = \frac { \partial \overline { { W } } } { \partial x _ { i } } + \sum _ { j = m + 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial \overline { { W } } } { \partial q _ { j } } \frac { \partial q _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } .
$$

On the other hand, $W$ is a function of

$$
x _ { 0 } , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { m } , a _ { m + 1 } , . . . , a _ { n } , b _ { m + 1 } , . . . , b _ { n } .
$$

Hence,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle \frac { \partial W } { \partial x _ { i } } = \int _ { 0 } ^ { x _ { 0 } } \left\{ \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } + \sum _ { j = m + 1 } ^ { n } \left[ \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial q _ { j } } \frac { \partial q _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } - \hbar _ { j } \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { i } } \left( \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial p _ { j } } \right) \right] \right\} d x _ { 0 } } \\ { \displaystyle = \int _ { 0 } ^ { x _ { 0 } } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } d x _ { 0 } + \int _ { 0 } ^ { x _ { 0 } } \sum _ { j = m + 1 } ^ { n } \left( \frac { d p _ { j } } { d x _ { 0 } } \frac { \partial q _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } + \hbar _ { j } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } q _ { j } } { \partial x _ { 0 } \partial x _ { i } } \right) d x _ { 0 } } \\ { \displaystyle = \int _ { 0 } ^ { x _ { 0 } } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } d x _ { 0 } + \sum _ { j = m + 1 } ^ { n } \left[ \hat { f } _ { j } \frac { \partial q _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { x _ { 0 } } . } \end{array}
$$

Since $q _ { j } = b _ { j }$ for $x _ { 0 } = 0$ , therefore

$$
{ \frac { \partial \overline { { W } } } { \partial x _ { i } } } = \int _ { 0 } ^ { x _ { 0 } } { \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } } d x _ { 0 } + \sum _ { j = m + 1 } ^ { n } \left( \rlap / p _ { j } - \frac { \partial \overline { { W } } } { \partial q _ { j } } \right) { \frac { \partial q _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } } .
$$

But, as $\overline { W }$ is anintegralofthefirstequationof(54),wehave $\partial \overrightarrow { W } / \partial q _ { j } = p _ { j } ,$ Thus

$$
{ \frac { \partial \overline { { W } } } { \partial x _ { i } } } = \int _ { 0 } ^ { x _ { 0 } } { \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } } d x _ { 0 } .
$$

Now, since (54) is in involution,

$$
( ( \pounds _ { 0 } - \Phi _ { 0 } , \pounds _ { i } - x _ { 0 } \Phi _ { i } ) ) = 0 , \qquad x _ { 0 } \Phi _ { i } = \phi _ { i } ,
$$

that is,

$$
\frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } - \frac { \partial \phi _ { i } } { \partial x _ { 0 } } + \sum _ { j = m + 1 } ^ { n } \left( \frac { \partial \phi _ { i } } { \partial q _ { j } } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial p _ { j } } - \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial q _ { j } } \frac { \partial \phi _ { i } } { \partial p _ { j } } \right) = 0 .
$$

Substituting $q _ { j } , \ p _ { j }$ from (56), which are themselves integrals of (55), gives

$$
\frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } = \frac { \partial \phi _ { i } } { \partial x _ { 0 } } + \sum _ { j = m + 1 } ^ { n } \left( \frac { \partial \phi _ { i } } { \partial q _ { j } } \frac { d q _ { j } } { d x _ { 0 } } + \frac { \partial \phi _ { i } } { \partial t _ { j } } \frac { d p _ { j } } { d x _ { 0 } } \right) = \frac { d \phi _ { i } } { d x _ { 0 } }
$$

and

$$
{ \frac { \partial { \overline { { W } } } } { \partial x _ { i } } } = \int _ { 0 } ^ { x _ { 0 } } { \frac { d \phi _ { i } } { d x _ { 0 } } } d x _ { 0 } = [ \phi _ { i } ] _ { 0 } ^ { x _ { 0 } } = x _ { 0 } \Phi _ { i } .
$$

Hence, $\overline { W }$ satisfes (54).

Thus we have proved that, if we can integrate the single first equation of (54)-which is nothing but a partial differential equation of $n + 1 - m$ variables - then we can integrate the entire system of partial differential equations (54). Equations (54) are equivalent to the system of partial differential equations (51). Hence, if we integrate a single partial differential equation of $n + 1 - m$ variables, or (going back to our original variables before we have adopted $q _ { 0 } , p _ { 0 } )$ if we integrate a Hamilton-Jacobi partial differential equation of $n - m$ variables, then our problem is completely solved. This is the case of canonical equations of $2 ( n - m )$ canonically conjugate variables.Thus, the order of the given differential equations is reduced to $2 ( n - m )$

Van Kampen and Wintner (1938) proved by their method of parametrization that the order of the differential equations can be reduced even if there exist $m$ invariantive relations instead of $m$ integrals (Section 4.14).

# FUNCTION GROUPS

# 4.9COMPLETE SYSTEMS

Suppose that $a _ { i } ^ { ( j ) }$ in the linear differential operators

$$
A _ { j } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i } ^ { ( j ) } { \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { i } } }
$$

are functions of $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \dotsc , x _ { n }$ and that there is no linear homogeneous relation of the form

$$
\lambda _ { 1 } A _ { 1 } f + \lambda _ { 2 } A _ { 2 } f + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \lambda _ { m } A _ { m } f = 0
$$

among the operators

$$
A _ { 1 } f _ { 3 } A _ { 2 } f _ { 3 } . . . , A _ { m } f
$$

with nonvanishing functions $\lambda$ of $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ Then $A _ { 1 } f , A _ { 2 } f , . . . , A _ { m } f$ are said to be independent. Let $X _ { i }$ be $m$ independent linear operators and $f$ be an integral of a system of partial differential equations of $_ { n }$ variables $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ ：

$$
X _ { i } f = 0 \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

Then, since both $X _ { \alpha } f = 0$ and $X _ { \beta } f = 0 \ ( \alpha , \beta = 1 , 2 , . . . , m )$ , we have $X _ { \alpha } X _ { \beta } f = 0 _ { ; }$ $X _ { \beta } X _ { \alpha } f = 0$ ; hence

$$
( X _ { \alpha } , X _ { \beta } ) f = ( X _ { \alpha } X _ { \beta } - X _ { \beta } X _ { \alpha } ) f = 0 .
$$

In general, a commutator $( X _ { \alpha } , X _ { \beta } )$ can be expressed linearly in terms of $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , . . . , X _ { m }$ ；that is,it is a linear combination of $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , . . . , X _ { m }$ Suppose that we acquire $s$ new independent operators by the operations $( X _ { \alpha } , X _ { \beta } )$ , making altogether $m + s$ operators $X _ { i }$ .If $m + s = n$ ,then there is no integral other than $f =$ constant, because this is a system of $n$ simultaneous linear homogeneous partial differential equations of $_ { n }$ independent variables. If $m + s < n$ ，we carry out a similar operation again.Thus, either we obtain $_ { n }$ independent operators $X _ { i }$ and come to the above case, or we obtain $r \prec n$ independent operators. In the latter case we repeat the operations. Finally, we come to the stage where all combinations $( X _ { i } , X _ { j } ) f$ are linear combinations of $X _ { 1 } f , X _ { 2 } f , \dotsc , X _ { r } f .$ Clebsch calls such a system of partial differential equations a complete system. The case for $r = n$ is special. Equations (5l） form a complete system.Thus the integration of a system of linear simultaneous partial differential equations $X _ { 1 } f = 0$ ， $X _ { 2 } f = 0 , . . . , X _ { m } f = 0$ is reduced to the integration of a complete system.

We now announce four propositions.

I. Any transformation of the independent variables in a complete system leads to a complete system.

When we make a transformation $x _ { i } = \phi _ { i } ( y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { n } )$ to (58), then

$$
X f = Y f = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } b _ { i } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial y _ { i } } } ,
$$

where $b _ { i }$ are functions of $\dot { y } _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \dotsc , y _ { n }$ From $X _ { i } f = 0$ we obtain $Y _ { j } f = 0$ Since $X _ { i } f = Y _ { i } f ,$ and

$$
( X _ { i } , X _ { j } ) f = \lambda _ { 1 } X _ { 1 } f + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \lambda _ { r } X _ { r } f ,
$$

therefore,

$$
( Y _ { i } , Y _ { j } ) f = \lambda _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } Y _ { 1 } f + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \lambda _ { \tau } ^ { \prime } Y _ { \tau } f .
$$

The coefficients $\lambda _ { j } ^ { \prime }$ are the result of substituting $y$ for $_ x$ in $\lambda _ { j }$ .Hence, the operators $Y _ { j } f$ also form a complete system.

II. If $Z _ { 1 } , Z _ { 2 } , . . . , Z _ { r }$ are linear combinations of $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , . . . , X _ { r } ,$ and, conversely, if $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , . . . , X _ { r }$ are linear combinations of $Z _ { 1 } , Z _ { 2 } , . . . , Z _ { r } ,$ then the system

$$
Z _ { 1 } f = 0 , Z _ { 2 } f = 0 , . . . , Z _ { r } f = 0
$$

is said to be equivalent to the system

$$
X _ { 1 } f = 0 , X _ { 2 } f = 0 , . . . , X _ { r } f = 0 .
$$

A system of partial differential equations that is equivalent to a complete system is itself a complete system.A complete system can be replaced by an infinite number of complete systems that are equivalent to the first complete system.A complete system among these,which is such that all combinations $( X _ { i } , X _ { j } ) = 0$ $( i , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , r )$ , is called a Jacobian system. An infinitesimal transformation group in such a case is commutative, that is,an Abelian group,and forms a subgroup of the original infinitesimal transformation group.

III.Any complete system is equivalent to a Jacobian system.We solve a complete system $X _ { 1 } f = 0 , X _ { 2 } f = 0 , . . . , X _ { r } f = 0$ with respect to $\partial f / \partial { x } _ { i }$ and denote the result by

$$
Z _ { i } f = \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { i } } + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n - r } b _ { i j } \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { r + j } } = 0 \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

This is a complete system equivalent to the original system. As $( Z _ { i } , Z _ { j } ) f$ contains only

$$
\frac { \partial f } { \partial \pmb { x } _ { r + 1 } } , \dots , \frac { \partial f } { \partial \pmb { x } _ { n } } ,
$$

then $( Z _ { i } , Z _ { j } ) f = 0$ if $( Z _ { i } , Z _ { j } ) f$ is to be a linear combination of $Z _ { 1 } f , Z _ { 2 } f , . . . , Z _ { \tau } f .$ Thus we obtain a Jacobian system.

IV.A complete system of $r$ partial differential equations of $\pmb { n }$ inde-pendent variables is reduced,if we know an integral of the system, to a complete system of $r - 1$ partial differential equations of $n - 1$ independent variables.

Suppose that we can integrate $X _ { 1 } f = 0$ and choose $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ so that its $n - 1$ integrals are $y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ By this transformation the first equation $X _ { 1 } f = 0$ of the system $X _ { 1 } f = 0 , . . . , X _ { r } f = 0$ becomes $\partial f / \partial y _ { 1 } = 0$ .Solve the remaining $r - 1$ equations with respect to $\partial f / \partial y _ { 2 } , . . . , \partial f / \partial y _ { r }$ and let the solution be

$$
Y _ { 1 } f = { \frac { \partial f } { \partial y _ { 1 } } } = 0 ,
$$

$$
Y _ { i } f = { \frac { \partial f } { \partial y _ { i } } } + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n - r } c _ { i j } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial y _ { r + j } } } = 0 \qquad ( i = 2 , 3 , . . . , r ) .
$$

This is a Jacobian system,as it is of the form just given. Because of the relation

$$
( Y _ { 1 } , Y _ { i } ) f = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n - r } { \frac { \partial c _ { i j } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial y _ { r + j } } } \equiv 0 ,
$$

the $c _ { i j }$ do not depend on $y _ { 1 }$ .Furthermore, $( Y _ { i } , Y _ { j } ) f = 0$ for $i > 1 , j > 1$ Hence, $r - 1$ equations

$$
Y _ { 2 } f = 0 , . . . , Y _ { \tau } f = 0
$$

form a system of $r - 1$ partial differential equations of $n - 1$ independent variables.

By repeating this process we can reduce the given complete system to a partial differential equation of $n - r + 1$ variables. This gives us the following theorem: a complete system of $r$ partial differential equations of $\pmb { n }$ independent variables admits $n - r$ independent integrals；the general integral is an arbitrary function of these integrals.

If we call the integration of a system consisting of $\pmb { n }$ ordinary differential equations an operation, then we need $n - r , n - r - 1 , . . . , 2 , 1$ opera-tions in the above case.The last operation is a mere quadrature.When we know $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ independent integrals,we need $n - \phi - \bar { r } , n - \beta - r - 1 , . . .$ operations. In particular,if the given equations are canonical with $_ { n }$ pairs of conjugate variables $q _ { i } , p _ { i }$ ,and if we know $\pmb { r }$ independent integrals that are in involution, then it is sufficient to carry out only $2 ( n - r { \overset { \cdot } { + } } 1 )$ operations, according to Lie's theorem. A method of solving a Hamilton-Jacobi equation

$$
H _ { 0 } ( q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { n } , \not p _ { 1 } , . . . , \not p _ { n } ) = a _ { 0 }
$$

is:first find a solution $H _ { 1 }$ that is independent of $H _ { 0 }$ and satisfies $( H _ { 0 } , H _ { 1 } ) = 0$ We need $2 n - 1$ operations for this purpose,as we already know an integral $H _ { 0 } = a _ { 0 }$ Next,find a solution $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { H } }$ of a complete system $( H _ { 0 } , H ) = 0 _ { : }$ $( H _ { 1 } , H _ { 0 } ) = 0 { } _ { ; }$ ， $( H _ { 1 } , H ) = 0$ We need

$$
2 ( n - 1 ) - 1 = 2 n - 3
$$

operations for this purpose. Thus the number of operations is reduced by two.

# 4.10FUNCTION GROUPS

Let $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r }$ be $r$ independent functions of $2 n$ variables

$$
q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , . . . , q _ { n } , \flat _ { 1 } , \flat _ { 2 } , . . . , \flat _ { n } .
$$

If all the Poisson brackets $( u _ { i } , u _ { j } )$ are expressed in terms of $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r }$ and nothing else, that is, if

$$
( u _ { i } , u _ { j } ) = f _ { i j } ( u _ { 1 } , . . . , u _ { r } ) \qquad ( i , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) ,
$$

then $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r }$ are said to form a function group. The functions $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r }$ are called its base, and $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { r } }$ the order of the function group. It is an $\boldsymbol { r }$ parameter group. In particular, if $f _ { i j } = 0$ ， then this function group is in involution, that is, it is an Abelian group (Lie, l929, 1888, 1890,1893,1893a,1896;Goursat,1921).

When $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r }$ form a function group, any function

$$
F ( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r } )
$$

belongs to that group. When $F ( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r } )$ and $\Phi ( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r } )$ belong to the same group, then $( F , \Phi )$ also belongs to the same group. This is because

$$
