\begin{array} { l } { ( F , \Phi ) = \displaystyle \sum _ { i , j } \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { i } } \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial u _ { j } } ( u _ { i } , u _ { j } ) } \\ { = \displaystyle \sum _ { i , j } \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { i } } \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial u _ { j } } f _ { i j } ( u _ { 1 } , . . . , u _ { r } ) . } \end{array}
$$

When we take $r$ independent variables $v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , \ldots , v _ { r }$ of $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r } ,$ then $( v _ { i } , v _ { j } )$ can be expressed in terms of $v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , \ldots , v _ { r } .$ These two function groups $( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r } )$ and $( v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , \ldots , v _ { r } )$ are considered two different forms of the same function group. Each of them is a base of that group.

f $r$ independent functions $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r }$ of $2 n$ variables

$$
q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , . . . , q _ { n } , \not p _ { 1 } , \not p _ { 2 } , . . . , \not p _ { n }
$$

form a function group of order $r _ { \parallel }$ ，then $r$ linear expressions

$$
( u _ { 1 } , f ) = 0 , ( u _ { 2 } , f ) = 0 , . . . , ( u _ { r } , f ) = 0
$$

form a complete system.

In proving this theorem, it is seen at once that $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r }$ are independent if the $r$ equations (6la) are independent-that is, if not all of the minor determinants of order $\boldsymbol { r }$ formed from the determinant

$\| \partial u _ { i } / \partial q _ { j } \|$ are zero, that is, if the rank of the determinant is $\boldsymbol { r }$ Let $( u _ { i } , f ) = A _ { i } f .$ From Jacobi's identity,

$$
( ( u _ { i } , u _ { j } ) , f ) \ : + \ : ( ( u _ { j } , f ) , u _ { i } ) \ : + \ : ( ( f , u _ { i } ) , u _ { j } ) \equiv 0 ,
$$

we obtain

$$
( A _ { i } , A _ { j } ) f \equiv A _ { i } ( A _ { j } f ) - A _ { j } ( A _ { i } f ) = ( ( u _ { i } , u _ { j } ) , f ) .
$$

Writing $( u _ { i } , u _ { j } ) = { f _ { i j } } ( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , . ~ . ~ . , u _ { \tau } )$ , we obtain

$$
A _ { j } ( A _ { i } f ) - A _ { i } ( A _ { j } f ) = \frac { \partial f _ { i j } } { \partial u _ { 1 } } A _ { 1 } f + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \frac { \partial f _ { i j } } { \partial u _ { r } } A _ { r } f .
$$

Hence, the operators $A _ { \tau } f$ form a complete system.

Conversely, if the functions $\phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } , . . . . , \phi _ { r }$ of $q _ { i } , p _ { i }$ are independent, it is necessary and sufficient that $\phi _ { i }$ should form a function group of order $\pmb { r }$ in order that

$$
( \phi _ { 1 } , f ) = 0 , . . . , ( \phi _ { r } , f ) = 0
$$

form a complete system.

From the above identity, $( ( \phi _ { i } , \phi _ { j } ) , f ) = 0$ If the system is complete, then this formula should be derivable from the original system of equations.This happens only when the Poisson brackets $( \phi _ { i } , \phi _ { j } )$ are functions of $\phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } , . . . , \phi _ { r }$ .This proves the converse theorem.

Now the system of equations

$$
( u _ { 1 } , f ) = 0 , ( u _ { 2 } , f ) = 0 , . . . , ( u _ { r } , f ) = 0
$$

admits $2 n - r$ independent integrals $v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , \dotsc , v _ { 2 n - r }$ according to the theorem stated at the end of Section 4.9,and other integrals can be expressed in terms of these $2 n - r$ integrals.Poisson's theorem states that $( v _ { i } , v _ { j } )$ are also integrals of this system and accordingly are expressed in terms of $v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , \dotsc , v _ { 2 n - r } .$ Hence, $v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , \dotsc , v _ { 2 n - r }$ form a function group of order $2 n - r$ ，Each function of this group is in involution with any function of the original function group $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r }$ The new group is called the polar group of the original function group.

The system

$$
( v _ { 1 } , f ) = 0 , ( v _ { 2 } , f ) = 0 , \ldots , ( v _ { 2 n - r } , f ) = 0
$$

is a complete system with $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r }$ as the $\boldsymbol { r }$ independent integrals; all other integrals are expressed in terms of $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r } .$ Thus,

$$
( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , . . . , u _ { r } ) \qquad \mathrm { a n d } \qquad ( v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , . . . , v _ { 2 n - r } )
$$

are in reciprocal relationship. Hence, a polar group is also a reciprocal group.

If any function $U$ belonging to a function group $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r }$ isin involution with any other function in the same function group, then $U$ is called a distinguished function (ausgezeichnete Funktion).A distinguished function is a function common to a function group and its polar group. Denote the polar group of $( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r } )$ by $( v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , . . . , v _ { 2 n - r } )$ and the distinguished function by $F ( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , . . . , q _ { n } , \phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } , . . . , \phi _ { n } )$ ； then

$$
\begin{array} { l } { F ( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , . . . , q _ { n } , \phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } , . . . , \phi _ { n } ) = U ( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , . . . , u _ { \tau } ) } \\ { \qquad \quad \qquad \quad \qquad \qquad \qquad \quad } \\ { \qquad = \ : V ( v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , . . . , v _ { 2 n - \tau } ) . } \end{array}
$$

If there are $m$ distinguished functions,then

$$
U _ { \mathrm { i } } ( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , . . . , u _ { r } ) = V _ { \mathrm { i } } ( v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , . . . , v _ { 2 n - r } ) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

Let $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , . . . , u _ { r } , v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , . . . , v _ { 2 n - r }$ be a group $G$ ，The functions $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r }$ and $v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } , \dotsc , v _ { 2 n - r }$ form respectively the subgroups $U$ and $V$ of $G$ A distinguished function belongs to a subgroup $\pmb { A }$ of each of $\boldsymbol { U }$ and $V .$ ，The Poisson brackets $( u _ { i } , u _ { j } )$ form a coset (Nebengruppe). As $v U _ { : }$ ，that is, $( u _ { i } , v _ { j } )$ ,and $U v$ ,that is, $( v _ { i } , u _ { j } )$ , are both zero, the right coset coincides with the left coset and we have $U A = A U$ ； in other words this is an Abelian group. As $U ^ { - 1 } A U = A$ ,the group is also self-conjugate. Moreover, $V ^ { - 1 } A V = A .$ We have $G ^ { - 1 } A G = A$ ; that is, $\pmb { A }$ is invariant. Hence, $\pmb { A }$ is an invariant subgroup of $G$

We can determine the distinguished functions belonging to a function group of order $\pmb { r }$ in the following manner.By definition, the number of distinguished functions belonging to the given function group equals the number of such independent integrals of the complete system (62) that are functions of $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r }$ only.

Write

$$
f = U ( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , . . . , u _ { r } ) \qquad ( u _ { i } , f ) = A _ { i } f .
$$

Then, (62) yields $\pmb { r }$ equations for $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r }$ ：

$$
A _ { i } U = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { r } \frac { \partial U } { \partial u _ { j } } \left( u _ { i } , u _ { j } \right) = 0 \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) ,
$$

because $( u _ { i } , u _ { j } )$ can be expressed in terms of $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r } ,$ according to our assumption. Suppose that these $r$ equations are reduced to $r \mathrm { ~ - ~ } m$ independent equations

$$
A _ { 1 } U = 0 , A _ { 2 } U = 0 , . . . , A _ { r - m } U = 0 .
$$

This is a complete system,because,by puting $( u _ { i } , u _ { j } ) = { f _ { i j } } ( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , . ~ . ~ . , u _ { r } )$ ， we obtain

$$
A _ { i } A _ { j } U - A _ { j } A _ { i } U = { \frac { \partial f _ { i j } } { \partial u _ { 1 } } } A _ { 1 } U + \cdot \cdot \cdot + { \frac { \partial f _ { i j } } { \partial u _ { r } } } A _ { r } U
$$

from Jacobi's identity. Since this is a complete system of $r \mathrm { ~ - ~ } m$ equations for $r$ variables,it should have $\pmb { m }$ integrals. Hence the number of the distinguished functions is equal to the number of linearly independent integrals of (65).Let the order of the first nonzero minor of the determinant $\Delta = \parallel ( u _ { i } , u _ { j } ) \parallel ( i , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , r )$ be $r \mathrm { ~ - ~ } m$ .Then $_ m$ is the number of distinguished functions. Take $r \mathrm { ~ - ~ } m$ linearly independent equations (66) out of the system (65),and look for $m$ integrals of (66).As this determinant is skew-symmetric,it is zero if its order is odd.Hence there must be at least one distinguished function in this case.

# 4.II CANONICAL FORM OF A FUNCTION GROUP

If a function group is in involution, then it is in involution in whatever form it is written.A group in involution is the simplest form of a function group and can be considered to be of a canonical form.

Consider a function group that is not in involution. A function group of order $r$ that is not in involution cannot contain more than $\ r \mathrm { ~ - ~ } 2$ independent distinguished functions. Suppose that there were $r - 1$ independent distinguished functions. Let them be denoted by $U _ { 1 } , U _ { 2 } , . . . , U _ { r - 1 }$ ; and the remaining functions belonging to the group be denoted by $V .$ .Then,we should have

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { ( U _ { 1 } , V ) = 0 , ( U _ { 2 } , V ) = 0 , . . . , ( U _ { r - 1 } , V ) = 0 , } } \\ { { ( U _ { i } , U _ { j } ) = 0 ( i , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , r - 1 ) , } } \end{array}
$$

that is, this function group would be in involution,contrary to our assumption. Hence, there cannot be more than $r - 2$ independent distinguished functions.

Consider a function group $( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r } )$ that is not in involution, and suppose that $u _ { 1 }$ is not a distinguished function of this group.Then at least one of $( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } ) , \dotsc ,$ $( u _ { 1 } , u _ { \tau } )$ is not zero. We can find such a function $F ( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r } )$ ，belonging to this function group， that satisfies $( u _ { 1 } , F ) = 1$ .Let sucha function $\pmb { F }$ be $\boldsymbol { u } _ { 2 }$ ,that is,a function $F$ that satisfies the partial differential equations

$$
\left( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } \right) \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { 2 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \left( u _ { 1 } , u _ { r } \right) \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { r } } = 1 ,
$$

and is a function of $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r } ,$ Then $( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } ) = 1$

Next, the two equations $( u _ { 1 } , F ) = 0$ $( u _ { 2 } , F ) = 0$ or

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle { \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { 2 } } + \left( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 3 } \right) \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { 3 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \left( u _ { 1 } , u _ { r } \right) \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { r } } = 0 , } } \\ { \displaystyle { - \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { 1 } } + \left( u _ { 2 } , u _ { 3 } \right) \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { 3 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \left( u _ { 2 } , u _ { r } \right) \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { r } } = 0 , } } \end{array}
$$

are independent and form a complete system. By Jacobi's identity, we may write

$$
( u _ { 1 } , ( u _ { 2 } , F ) ) - ( u _ { 2 } , ( u _ { 1 } , F ) ) = ( ( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } ) , F ) = ( 1 , F ) \equiv 0 .
$$

The coefficients $( u _ { i } , u _ { j } )$ of this complete system are functions of $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r } ,$ Let the $r \mathrm { ~ - ~ } 2$ independent integrals of these partial differential equations be $u _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , u _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , u _ { r - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ .These functions form a function group of order $r \mathrm { ~ - ~ } 2$ .The functions $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , u _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , u _ { r - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ are independent. If we assume that there exists a relation among them,then it should contain either $u _ { 1 }$ or $u _ { 2 }$ ，because otherwise it would be contrary to our hypothesis that $u _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , u _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , u _ { \tau - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ are independent integrals of (67a). Now suppose that the relation contains $u _ { 1 }$ ,and let it be

$$
\psi ( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , u _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , u _ { r - 2 } ^ { \prime } ) = 0 .
$$

Then

$$
( u _ { 2 } , \psi ) = \frac { \partial \psi } { \partial u _ { 1 } } \left( u _ { 2 } , u _ { 1 } \right) + \frac { \partial \psi } { \partial u _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } } \left( u _ { 2 } , u _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \right) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \frac { \partial \psi } { \partial u _ { r - 2 } ^ { \prime } } \left( u _ { 2 } , u _ { r - 2 } ^ { \prime } \right) = 0 .
$$

As $u _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , u _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , u _ { r - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ are solutions of (67), therefore

$$
( u _ { 2 } , u _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ) = \cdot \cdot \cdot = ( u _ { 2 } , u _ { r - 2 } ^ { \prime } ) = 0 .
$$

We have taken $( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } ) = 1$ .Hence $( u _ { 2 } , \psi ) = - \partial \psi / \partial u _ { 1 } = 0$ This contradicts our hypothesis.Therefore $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , u _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , u _ { r - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ are independent.

Thus, if $( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { r } )$ are not in involution, first take $( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } ) = 1$ ； another set $u _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , u _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , u _ { r - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ is now in involution with $u _ { 1 }$ and $u _ { 2 }$ If these $u _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , u _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , u _ { r - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ are in involution, write $u _ { 2 } = Q _ { 1 }$ ， $u _ { 1 } = P _ { 1 }$ ; the new set $Q _ { 1 } , P _ { 1 } , u _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , u _ { r - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ satisfies

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { ( P _ { 1 } , Q _ { 1 } ) = 0 , ( P _ { 1 } , u _ { i } ^ { \prime } ) = 0 , ( Q _ { 1 } , u _ { i } ^ { \prime } ) = 0 , ( u _ { i } ^ { \prime } , u _ { j } ^ { \prime } ) = 0 } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { ( i , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , r - 2 ) } } \end{array}
$$

If $u _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , u _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , u _ { r - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ are not in involution, repeat this operation； this gives the set $P _ { \ L ^ { 2 } } \ : Q _ { \ L ^ { 2 } } , u _ { \ L ^ { 3 } } ^ { \prime \prime } , \dots , u _ { \tau - \ L ^ { 4 } } ^ { \prime \prime }$ , which has the same property. Repeating this process leads to the following

Theorem : Any function group can be put in the form

$$
Q _ { 1 } , P _ { 1 } , . . . , Q _ { q } , P _ { q } , Q _ { q + 1 } , . . . , Q _ { q + m } ,
$$

such that

$$
( P _ { i } , Q _ { i } ) = 1 \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , q ) ,
$$

and the other Poisson brackets all vanish.In such a case the distinguished functions are functions of $Q _ { q + 1 } , . . . , Q _ { q + m }$ only.

The second part of this theorem is proved as follows : Let a distinguished function be $\Pi ( Q _ { 1 } , . . . , Q _ { q + m } , P _ { 1 } , . . . , P _ { q } )$ . Then the equations for determining the distinguished functions are

$$
( u _ { i } , \Pi ) = \frac { \partial \Pi } { \partial u _ { 1 } } ( u _ { i } , u _ { 1 } ) + \frac { \partial \Pi } { \partial u _ { 2 } } ( u _ { i } , u _ { 2 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \frac { \partial \Pi } { \partial u _ { r } } ( u _ { i } , u _ { r } ) = 0
$$

and they can be written

$$
( Q _ { i } , \Pi ) = \frac { \partial \Pi } { \partial P _ { i } } = 0 , \qquad ( P _ { i } , \Pi ) = \frac { \partial \Pi } { \partial Q _ { i } } = 0 \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , q ) .
$$

Thus the distinguished functions are functions of $Q _ { q + 1 } , . . . , Q _ { q + m }$ only. As they are in involution with all $P _ { i } , Q _ { i } ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , q )$ , the functions $Q _ { q + 1 } , . . . , Q _ { q + m }$ are themselves the distinguished functions, $_ m$ in number. Hence,the difference between the number $2 q + m$ of the functions belonging to a function group of order $2 q + m$ and the number $\pmb { m }$ of the distinguished functions is $2 q$ and therefore always even.

Suppose that the functions in the system

$$
f _ { 1 } = c _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } = c _ { 2 } , . . . , f _ { q } = c _ { q }
$$

of $\pmb { n }$ variables are mutually in involution, and that we know $r \mathrm { ~ - ~ } q$ independent integrals $f _ { q + 1 } , \ldots , f _ { r }$ of a complete system

$$
( f _ { 1 } , f ) = 0 , ( f _ { 2 } , f ) = 0 , . . . , ( f _ { q } , f ) = 0 .
$$

Suppose that we cannot obtain any new integral from the Poisson brackets formed $\cot f _ { q + 1 } , \ldots , f _ { r }$ .These functions $f _ { 1 } , \ldots , f _ { r }$ form a function group. If $r = 2 n - q .$ ，then we can integrate the system by quadrature. Suppose that $r < 2 n - q .$ 、Suppose that there are $\pmb { m }$ distinguished functions that are independent of the $q$ distinguished functions $f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , \ldots , f _ { q }$ among $f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , \ldots , f _ { r }$ .Then， $r = q + m + 2 \nu$ As this function group contains a function group of order $q + m + \nu$ which is in involution, we must integrate a system of $q + m + \nu$ equations in involution to determine these functions. This is done in

$2 n - 2 q - 2 m - 2 \nu$ $2 n - 2 q - 2 m - 2 \nu - 2 , . . . , 2$ operations. This method of integration is due to Jacobi-Mayer.

Without leading to the integration of a system of differential equations in involution we may consider $X _ { j } f = 0 \ ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , q )$ , such that

$$
( X _ { i } X _ { j } \ - \ X _ { j } X _ { i } ) f = \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { j } \lambda _ { i j } ^ { k } X _ { k } f \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , j - 1 ; j = 1 , 2 , \ldots .
$$

A collection of such $X _ { j } f = 0 \ ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , q )$ ， is called a successively complete system and is used in Pfeiffer's (l933) method of integration. A generalization of Jacobi's method has been treated by de Donder (1927) and Saltykov (1934, 1935).

Now we prove the following important

Theorem: The number of independent functions in involution in a given function group is $( r + m ) / 2$ ，where $_ r$ is the order of the function group and $_ m$ is the number of the distinguished functions.

When reduced to a canonical form according to the process just described, any function group of order $\boldsymbol { r }$ can be written in the form

$$
P _ { 1 } , . . . , P _ { q } , \quad Q _ { 1 } , . . . , Q _ { q } , \quad Q _ { q + 1 } , . . . , Q _ { q + m } \qquad ( 2 q + m = r ) ,
$$

such that $( P _ { i } , Q _ { i } ) = 1 ~ ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , q )$ ； the other Poisson brackets all vanish.Hence, $q + m = ( r + m ) / 2$ functions

$$
Q _ { 1 } , . . . , Q _ { q } ; \quad Q _ { q + 1 } , . . . , Q _ { q + m }
$$

are in involution. We proceed to prove that there cannot be more than $q + m$ functions in involution.

Assume tentatively that there are $\nu > q$ functions.Let them be $Q _ { 1 } , . . . , Q _ { v }$ . Then we should be able to determine independent functions $P _ { q + 1 } , . . . , P _ { q + m }$ ， $Q _ { q + m + 1 } , . . . , Q _ { n }$ other than those in (70), so that $P _ { 1 } , . . . , P _ { n } , Q _ { 1 } , . . . , Q _ { n }$ would form a canonical function group of order $2 n$ . If we eliminate one of the distinguished functions $Q _ { q + 1 } , . . . , Q _ { q + m }$ (for example, $Q _ { q + 1 } )$ ，the polar group of a function group of order $2 q + m - 1$

$$
P _ { 1 } , . . . , P _ { q } , \quad Q _ { 1 } , . . . , Q _ { q } , ^ { * } , Q _ { q + 2 } , . . . , Q _ { q + m }
$$

in which the asterisk denotes the absence of $Q _ { q + 1 } ,$ will have the form

$$
Q _ { q + 1 } , U _ { 1 } , U _ { 2 } , . . . , U _ { 2 n - 2 q - m } ,
$$

because $Q _ { q + 1 }$ is in involution with the $2 q + m - 1$ functions of (71). Hence, in the same manner in which we constructed a canonical function group,we can determine $P _ { q + 1 }$ so that it satisfies

$$
( P _ { q + 1 } , Q _ { q + 1 } ) = 1 .
$$

The functions $P _ { 1 } , . . . , P _ { q } , P _ { q + 1 } , Q _ { 1 } , . . . , Q _ { q + m }$ form a canonical function group and contain the functions (7O).The group's distinguished functions are $Q _ { q + 2 } , . . . , Q _ { q + m }$ ； they are mutually independent. By repeating similar processes we obtain

$$
P _ { 1 } , . . . , P _ { q + m } , \qquad Q _ { 1 } , . . . , Q _ { q + m } ,
$$

but these do not contain distinguished functions. We add to these functions the polar group of $2 ( n - q - m )$ functions belonging to (72). As the function group (72) does not contain distinguished functions, its polar group does not contain any distinguished function. Hence, by treating this polar function group similarly, we obtain both a canonical function group of order $2 n$ ,and, from (72), the polar group belonging to it. The canonical function group that we obtain is

$$
P _ { 1 } , . . . , P _ { q } , \ P _ { q + 1 } , . . . , P _ { n } , \ Q _ { 1 } , . . . , Q _ { q } , \ Q _ { q + 1 } , . . . , Q _ { q + m } , . . . , Q _ { n } .
$$

Thus, $n - q \mathrm { ~ - ~ } m$ functions $Q _ { q + m + 1 } , . . . , Q _ { n }$ are mutually in involution and also in involution with the functions $P _ { 1 } , . . . , P _ { q } , Q _ { 1 } , . . . , Q _ { q + m }$ of (70).Hence, they are in involution with the functions $\phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } , . . . , \phi _ { q }$ contained in (70). By hypothesis, $\phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } , . . . . , \phi _ { v }$ are mutually in involu-tion. Hence， we have altogether a function group consisting of $n - q - m + \nu$ functions:

$$
Q _ { q + m + 1 } , . . . , Q _ { n } , \qquad \phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } , . . . , \phi _ { v } ,
$$

which are mutually in involution. But the order of the polar group of a given function group of order $\boldsymbol { r }$ is $2 n - r$ If $r$ functions of a function group of order $\pmb { r }$ are all mutually in involution, then a complete system

$$
( u _ { 1 } , f ) = 0 , . . . , ( u _ { r } , f ) = 0
$$

admits $r$ independent integrals $u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { r }$ As these functions of the polar group should satisfy these $\pmb { r }$ equations, the polar group must contain these $r$ functions. Hence, $r \leq 2 n - r ,$ that is, $r \leq n$

Going back to our function group (74), we have

$$
n - q - m + \nu \leq n .
$$

Hence

$$
\nu \leq q + m = { \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } ( r + m ) ,
$$

since $n - q - m + \nu$ functions of $\pmb { u }$ are mutually in involution. Thus, we arrive at the conclusion that the number of functions mutually in involution among functions (70) is $( r + m ) / 2$ ·

In the course of our proof it is seen that a general function group $Q _ { 1 } , . . . , Q _ { n } , P _ { 1 } , . . . , P _ { n }$ of order $2 n$ , that are not in involution and, hence, have no distinguished function,are functions of our original independent variables $q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { n } , p _ { 1 } , . . . , p _ { n }$ and are such that

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { ( P _ { i } , Q _ { j } ) = \delta _ { i j } , } } \\ { { ( P _ { i } , P _ { j } ) = ( Q _ { i } , Q _ { j } ) = 0 \qquad ( i , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Hence,by the theorem in Section 1.9, the transformation from $( q _ { i } , p _ { i } )$ to $( Q _ { i } , P _ { i } )$ is a contact transformation.

# INTEGRALS OF THE $^ { \pmb { \mathscr { n } } }$ -BODY PROBLEM

# 4.12EULERIAN INTEGRALS

Consider $\pmb { n }$ particles moving in an Euclidean three-dimensional space according to Newton's gravitational law. Following Poincaré (1897, 1905）we denote the coordinates of the first particle by $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , x _ { 3 }$ and its mass by $m _ { 1 } = m _ { 2 } = m _ { 3 }$ ，the second particle by ${ \mathfrak { x } } _ { 4 } , { \mathfrak { x } } _ { 5 } , { \mathfrak { x } } _ { 6 }$ and its mass by $m _ { 4 } = m _ { 5 } = m _ { 6 } .$ ,and so on, finally the coordinates of the jth particle by $x _ { 3 j - 2 } , x _ { 3 j - 1 } , x _ { 3 j }$ and its mass by $m _ { 3 j - 2 } = m _ { 3 j - 1 } = m _ { 3 j } ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ · The corresponding momenta are

$$
y _ { i } = m _ { i } \frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n ) .
$$

The total kinetic energy of the system is

$$
T = { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } m _ { i } \Bigl ( { \frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } } \Bigr ) ^ { 2 } = { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } { \frac { y _ { i } ^ { 2 } } { m _ { i } } } ,
$$

and the force function is

$$
U = \sum _ { i , j } \frac { m _ { 3 i } m _ { 3 j } } { \Delta _ { 3 i , 3 j } } \qquad ( i , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

where $\Delta _ { 3 i , 3 j }$ is the mutual distance between the ith and jth particles. Here the units are so chosen that the constant of universal gravitation equals 1.

Let

$$
H = T - U ,
$$

and the equations of motion of the dynamical system be

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial y _ { i } } , \qquad \frac { d y _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { i } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n ) .
$$

The order of this system of equations is $6 n$

Now, since the function $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { \Pi } } ^ { H }$ of (77） does not contain $t$ explicitly, the differential equations remain unaltered when we make the transformation $t ^ { \prime } = t + \epsilon$ 、Hence,(78) admits the infinitesimal transformation

$$
A _ { 0 } f = { \frac { \partial f } { \partial t } } .
$$

Corresponding to this transformation, we have $d H / d t = \partial H / \partial t = 0 _ {  }$ which gives the integral $H =$ constant, the energy integral.

Differential equations (78) remain unaltered when we translate the coordinate origin $x _ { 3 j - 2 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 3 j - 2 } + \epsilon ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ in the $\pmb { x } _ { 1 }$ direction. The force function $U$ depends only on the distances between the particles, and the kinetic energy $_ T$ depends only on the momenta.Hence,the system (78) admits the infinitesimal transformation

$$
A _ { 1 } f = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { 3 j - 2 } } } \cdotp
$$

Likewise,(78) admits

$$
A _ { 2 } f = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { 3 j - 1 } } } , \qquad A _ { 3 } f = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { 3 j } } } .
$$

Now the result of applying to the integral $H =$ constant the infinitesimal transformations admitted by the system (78) is also an integral. Thus

$$
A _ { 1 } H = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } { \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { 3 j - 2 } } } = 0 .
$$

This last transformation has been set equal to zero because $T$ does not contain the coordinates and $\boldsymbol { U }$ depends only on the mutual distances.

$$
\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } { \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { 3 j - 2 } } } = 0 .
$$

Hence, we have from (78) the equation

$$
\sum _ { j \mathop { = } 1 } ^ { n } \frac { d y _ { 3 j - 2 } } { d t } = 0 ,
$$

which gives the integral

$$
H _ { 1 } = - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 3 j - 2 } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } .
$$

Similarly, we have the integrals

$$
H _ { 2 } = - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 3 j - 1 } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ,
$$

$$
H _ { 3 } = - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 3 j } = { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } } ,
$$

or

$$
\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 j - 2 } \frac { d x _ { 3 j - 2 } } { d t } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } , \qquad \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 j - 1 } \frac { d x _ { 3 j - 1 } } { d t } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ,
$$

$$
\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 j } { \frac { d x _ { 3 j } } { d t } } = { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } } .
$$

These three integrals are what we call the frst integrals of the center of mass.

Since $U$ depends only on the differences of coordinates and $_ { T }$ on the momenta,(78） remains unaltered when we rotate the coordinate axes.Let the rotation be

$$
\begin{array} { r } { x _ { 3 j - 1 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 3 j - 1 } \cos \epsilon + x _ { 3 j } \sin \epsilon \approx x _ { 3 j - 1 } + x _ { 3 j } \epsilon , \quad } \\ { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \times _ { 3 j } ^ { \prime } = - x _ { 3 j - 1 } \sin \epsilon + x _ { 3 j } \cos \epsilon \approx x _ { 3 j - 1 } \epsilon + x _ { 3 j } , } \end{array}
$$

Then

$$
f ( x _ { 3 j - 1 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 3 j } ^ { \prime } ) - f ( x _ { 3 j - 1 } , x _ { 3 j } ) = \epsilon x _ { 3 j } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { 3 j - 1 } } } - \epsilon x _ { 3 j - 1 } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { 3 j } } } .
$$

Equations (78) admit the infinitesimal transformation

$$
A _ { 4 } f = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } { \left( - x _ { 3 j } \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { 3 j - 1 } } + x _ { 3 j - 1 } \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { 3 j } } \right) } .
$$

Similarly, they admit

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \cal A } _ { 5 } f = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \Big ( - x _ { 3 j - 2 } \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { 3 j } } + x _ { 3 j } \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { 3 j - 2 } } \Big ) } , } \\ { { \displaystyle { \cal A } _ { 6 } f = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \Big ( - x _ { 3 j - 1 } \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { 3 j - 2 } } + x _ { 3 j - 2 } \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { 3 j - 1 } } \Big ) . } } \end{array}
$$

When we apply these transformations to the integral $H =$ constant, then, from the expression $\partial H / \partial x _ { i }$ in (78),we obtain

$$
\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { 3 j } \frac { d y _ { 3 j - 1 } } { d t } - x _ { 3 j - 1 } \frac { d y _ { 3 j } } { d t } \right) = 0 ,
$$

that is,

$$
H _ { 4 } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } ( x _ { 3 j } y _ { 3 j - 1 } - x _ { 3 j - 1 } y _ { 3 j } ) = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } . \nonumber
$$

Similarly,

$$
H _ { 5 } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 3 j } - x _ { 3 j } y _ { 3 j - 2 } \right) = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ,
$$

and

$$
H _ { 6 } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left( x _ { 3 j - 1 } y _ { 3 j - 2 } - x _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 3 j - 1 } \right) = { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } } .
$$

These are the integrals of the momenta of momentum, or the integrals of angular momentum, or the integrals of areal velocity.

Finally, we apply the transformation

$$
x _ { 3 j - 2 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 3 j - 2 } + t \epsilon \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Then $U$ remains unaltered,as it depends only on the coordinate differences. Differentiating this and multiplying the result by $m _ { 3 j - 2 }$ gives

$$
y _ { 3 j - 2 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 3 j - 2 } + \mathop { m _ { 3 j - 2 } } \epsilon \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

The kinetic energy $_ { T }$ increases by the amount

$$
\sum _ { j \mathop { = } 1 } ^ { n } \frac { 1 } { 2 m _ { 3 j - 2 } } 2 m _ { 3 j - 2 } \epsilon y _ { 3 j - 2 } ,
$$

if we neglect quantities of order $\epsilon ^ { 2 }$ . The derivative $d x _ { 3 j - 2 } / d t$ increases by $\epsilon .$ Hence, the equation

$$
\frac { d x _ { 3 j - 2 } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial y _ { 3 j - 2 } }
$$

remains unaltered by this transformation. Similarly, $\partial H / \partial x _ { 3 j - 2 }$ remains unaltered, $d y _ { 3 j - 2 } / d t$ remains unaltered, and

$$
\frac { d y _ { 3 j - 2 } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { 3 j - 2 } }
$$

remains unaltered. Thus equations (78) admit the infinitesimal transformation

Similarly, they admit

$$
A _ { 7 } f = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } { \bigg ( } m _ { 3 j - 2 } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial y _ { 3 j - 2 } } } + { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { 3 j - 2 } } } { \bigg ) } .
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \cal A } _ { 8 } f = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left( m _ { 3 j - 1 } \frac { \partial f } { \partial y _ { 3 j - 1 } } + t \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { 3 j - 1 } } \right) } , } \\ { { \displaystyle { \cal A } _ { 9 } f = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left( m _ { 3 j } \frac { \partial f } { \partial y _ { 3 j } } + t \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { 3 j } } \right) } . } \end{array}
$$

Apply the transformation $A _ { 7 } f$ to $H =$ constant. By (75), we have

$$
\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 3 j - 2 } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } = \alpha _ { 1 } , \qquad 
$$

as $H$ depends only on the coordinate differences and $y$ is contained only in $T .$ Further integration gives

that is,

$$
\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 j - 2 } x _ { 3 j - 2 } = \alpha _ { 1 } t + \beta _ { 1 } , \qquad \beta _ { 1 } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ,
$$

$$
H _ { 7 } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } ( m _ { 3 j - 2 } x _ { 3 j - 2 } - y _ { 3 j - 2 } t ) = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } .
$$

Similarly,

$$
H _ { 8 } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } ( m _ { 3 j - 1 } x _ { 3 j - 1 } - y _ { 3 j - 1 } t ) = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ,
$$

and

$$
H _ { 9 } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \left( m _ { 3 j } x _ { 3 j } - y _ { 3 j } t \right) = { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } } .
$$

These are the second integrals of the center of mass.

Thus,we have obtained ten integrals, that is,one integral of the energy, $H ,$ six integrals of the center of mass, $H _ { 1 } , H _ { 2 } , H _ { 3 } , H _ { 7 } , H _ { 8 } , H _ { 9 } .$ and three integrals of angular momentum, $H _ { 4 }$ ， $H _ { 5 }$ ， $H _ { 6 }$ . The corresponding infinitesimal transformations are $A _ { 0 } f , \ A _ { 1 } f , \ A _ { 2 } f , \ A _ { 3 } f , \ A _ { 7 } f , \ A _ { 8 } f ,$ $A _ { 9 } f , A _ { 4 } f , A _ { 5 } f , A _ { 6 } f ;$ ； they constitute a function group of ten members,as will be shown immediately. These integrals are sometimes called the classical Eulerian integrals, or -from a transformation-group viewpoint - a function group of Galilei of order ten. It is to be noted that these ten integrals are all algebraic with respect to the coordinates and the momenta (Saltykov, 1950).

# 4.13DISTINGUISHED FUNCTIONS

TABLE 1

Table 1 shows the Poisson brackets

$$
( f , g ) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } \left( { \frac { \partial f } { \partial { x _ { i } } } } { \frac { \partial g } { \partial { y _ { i } } } } - { \frac { \partial f } { \partial { y _ { i } } } } { \frac { \partial g } { \partial { x _ { i } } } } \right)
$$

formed from the ten classical integrals of Euler,where

$$
M = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 j - 2 } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 j - 1 } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 j }
$$

is the total mass of the system. As it is inconvenient to have $t$ explicitly in the integrals, we use

$$
H _ { 7 } ^ { \prime } = H _ { 3 } H _ { 8 } - H _ { 2 } H _ { 9 } , H _ { 8 } ^ { \prime } = H _ { 1 } H _ { 9 } - H _ { 3 } H _ { 7 } , H _ { 9 } ^ { \prime } = H _ { 2 } H _ { 7 } - H _ { 8 }
$$

instead of $H _ { 7 } , H _ { 8 } , H _ { 9 }$ . This leads to the identity

$$
H _ { 1 } H _ { 7 } ^ { \prime } + H _ { 2 } H _ { 8 } ^ { \prime } + H _ { 3 } H _ { 9 } ^ { \prime } \equiv 0 .
$$

Hence, nine of the ten integrals $H , H _ { 1 } , H _ { 2 } , H _ { 3 } , H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , H _ { 7 } ^ { \prime } , H _ { 8 } ^ { \prime } , H _ { 9 } ^ { \prime }$ are independent and form a ninth-order function group. Table l lists the structure constants for the Poisson operators; in other words, it is a representation ofa function group, that is,it is equivalent to a representation of this group by the matrix of a linear homogeneous transformation.

Now we form the ninth-order determinant. Any combination with $\boldsymbol { H }$ in the Poisson brackets is zero,as $\boldsymbol { H }$ is in involution with any of the integrals.The first nonzero minor of this determinant is of order six; that is,the order of this determinant is six.Put $\boldsymbol { r } = 9$ ， $m = 3$ in the theorem of Section 4.1l. Because of identity (79),the number of distinguished functions is three and the number of functions mutually in involution is $( r + m ) / 2 = 6 . \ : H$ is included among these functions.For the moment, we put aside any $H$ which is in involution with any other function.As the Poisson brackets among the three functions $H _ { 1 } , H _ { 2 } , H _ { 3 }$ are all zero, these three functions constitute a function group of order three that are mutually in involution.Denote a function in involution with $H _ { 1 } , H _ { 2 } , H _ { 3 }$ and $H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 }$ by $\Phi ( H _ { 1 } , H _ { 2 } , H _ { 3 } , H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } )$ and look for such a function that satisfies the requirement

$$
( H _ { 1 } , \Phi ) = 0 , \qquad ( H _ { 2 } , \Phi ) = 0 , \qquad ( H _ { 3 } , \Phi ) = 0 .
$$

Take $F ( f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , \ldots . . , f _ { m } )$ and put it in the definition of a Poisson bracket; then,

$$
( K , F ) = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { m } \left( K , f _ { j } \right) { \frac { \partial F } { \partial f _ { j } } } \cdot
$$

We substitute each $H$ in succession for one of $\kappa$ and $f _ { j }$ and, bearing in mind the identity

$$
H _ { 1 } ( H _ { 1 } , \Phi ) + H _ { 2 } ( H _ { 2 } , \Phi ) + H _ { 3 } ( H _ { 3 } , \Phi ) \equiv 0
$$

among these equations, we see that only two of the Poisson brackets are independent. We thus have two independent equations

$$
H _ { 3 } \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial H _ { 5 } } - H _ { 2 } \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial H _ { 6 } } = 0 , \qquad - H _ { 3 } \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial H _ { 4 } } + H _ { 1 } \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial H _ { 6 } } = 0 .
$$

Integrating these equations, we obtain

$$
\Phi = H _ { 1 } H _ { 4 } + H _ { 2 } H _ { 5 } + H _ { 3 } H _ { 6 } .
$$

Thus we obtain five functions $H , H _ { 1 } , H _ { 2 } , H _ { 3 } , \Phi$ that are mutually in involution (Lie,l874,1929; Engel,1916,1917; Engel and Faber, 1932; Cartan, 1922; Goursat, 1921, 1923).

Next we seek a function $\Pi$ of ten integrals $H$ that is in involution with $H , H _ { 1 } , H _ { 2 } , H _ { 3 } , \Phi .$ The required equations are

$$
( H , \Pi ) = ( H _ { 1 } , \Pi ) = ( H _ { 2 } , \Pi ) = ( H _ { 3 } , \Pi ) = ( \Phi , \Pi ) = 0 .
$$

Such a function is found to be

$$
\Pi = ( M H _ { 4 } - H _ { 7 } ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } + ( M H _ { 5 } - H _ { 8 } ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } + ( M H _ { 6 } - H _ { 9 } ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } .
$$


<!-- chunk 0006: pages 351-420 -->
Thus we have six functions

$$
H , H _ { 1 } , H _ { 2 } , H _ { 3 } , \Phi , \Pi
$$

that are mutually in involution.

A distinguished function should be in involution with any function belonging to the same group. Let $f$ be such a function. It should satisfy

$$
( H _ { 4 } , f ) = ( H _ { 5 } , f ) = ( H _ { 6 } , f ) = ( H _ { 7 } ^ { \prime } , f ) = ( H _ { 8 } ^ { \prime } , f ) = ( H _ { 9 } ^ { \prime } , f ) = 0 .
$$

We have two functions

$$
\begin{array} { l } { f = \Psi = \frac 1 2 ( H _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } ) \qquad \mathrm { a n d } } \\ { \qquad \quad } \\ { f = \Pi = ( M H _ { 4 } - H _ { 7 } ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } + ( M H _ { 5 } - H _ { 8 } ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } + ( M H _ { 6 } - H _ { 9 } ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } } \end{array}
$$

satisfying these equations. The two functions $\Psi$ and $\Pi$ together with $H$ are the required three distinguished functions.Hence, the six functions mutually in involution are $H , H _ { 1 } , H _ { 2 } , H _ { 3 } , \Psi , \Pi$ (Hagihara, 1938).

On the other hand,according to Lie's theorem, the order of a system of differential equations of order $2 m$ is reduced (when there are $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { p } }$ integrals mutually in involution） to order $2 ( m - { p } )$ .Hence, the order of the differential equations of the $_ n$ -body problem is reduced to $6 n - 1 2$ ， because there exist six integrals mutually in involution.Note that the number of the known Eulerian integrals is ten.This difference between twelve and ten is explained by the existence of two other integrals: the integral for the elimination of time (Section 4.4),and the integral for the elimination of nodes discovered by Jacobi (l842,1844；see also Section 5.4).

In the plane $_ { n }$ -body problem,the order of the differential equations is $_ { 4 n }$ .There are six corresponding classical integrals:

$$
H = T - U , \qquad H _ { 1 } = - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 2 j - 1 } , \qquad H _ { 2 } = - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 2 j } ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle H _ { 6 } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \big ( x _ { 2 j } y _ { 2 j - 1 } - x _ { 2 j - 1 } y _ { 2 j } \big ) , } } \\ { ~ } \\ { { \displaystyle H _ { 7 } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \big ( m _ { 2 j - 1 } x _ { 2 j - 1 } - y _ { 2 j - 1 } t \big ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle H _ { 8 } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \big ( m _ { 2 j } x _ { 2 j } - y _ { 2 j } t \big ) . } } \end{array}
$$

The function group is of order $r = 6$ .Table 2 gives the matrix repre-sentation of this function group.

TABLE 2   

<table><tr><td></td><td>H</td><td>H</td><td>H</td><td>H6</td><td>H</td><td>H</td></tr><tr><td>H</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>0</td></tr><tr><td>H</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>-H2</td><td>-M</td><td>0</td></tr><tr><td>H2</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>H</td><td>0</td><td>-M</td></tr><tr><td>H6</td><td>0</td><td>H</td><td>-H</td><td>0</td><td>H</td><td>-H7</td></tr><tr><td>H</td><td>0</td><td>M</td><td>0</td><td>-Hg</td><td>0</td><td>0</td></tr><tr><td>H</td><td>0</td><td>0</td><td>M</td><td>H</td><td>0</td><td>0</td></tr></table>

The order of the determinant formed of this matrix is four; therefore $m = 2$ . That is, there are four independent functions mutually in involution and two distinguished functions.The functions mutually in involution are $H , H _ { 1 } , H _ { 2 } , X _ { 3 }$ ； and the distinguished functions are $H$ and $X _ { i }$ where

$$
X = M H _ { 6 } - H _ { 2 } H _ { 7 } + H _ { 1 } H _ { 8 } .
$$

Thus,the order of the differential equations is reduced to $4 n - 8$ The order is reduced by eight, but there are only six known classical integrals；this difference is due to two integrals:the integral for the elimination of time,and the integral expressing the fact that the longi-tude of perihelion appears only in the difference of longitudes (Hagihara, 1944).

It is known that with cyclic coordinates,a Hamiltonian system possesses an integral common to the whole class of potential functions. Schmeidler (l963) obtained a general criterion for the existence of such an integral, on the basis of Lie's idea of transformation groups and function groups.He applied this criterion to problems of stellar dynamics and showed that the integral could not exist (see Chapter 14).

# 4.14PARAMETRIZATION

The classical reduction of the differential equations for a dynamical system described in the foregoing sections can be carried out owing to the existence of a function group of the first integrals (Section 4.10). By using the Hamiltonian matrices (Sections l.18-l.22) van Kampen and Wintner (l938） treated a more general problem—in which known functions or hypersurfaces in the phase space cannot be represented by first integrals -by substituting invariantive relations for the first integrals (Section 4.6).In particular, the known hypersurfaces need not constitute a continuous family but may be isolated.The restricted three-body problem can be reduced by substituting for these first integrals certain combinations ofcomplicated invariantive-relations systems.This substitution is based on a distinct idea of the parametrization of an invariantiverelations system.The parametrization is symmetric with respect to the coordinates and momenta. Thus the reduction has a formal advantage when an invariantive relation is a first integral; it can be carried out by canonical transformations,as has been shown in Sections l.18-1.22.

A matrix with $k$ rows and $\iota$ columns is called a $( k , l )$ -matrix.The product $\boldsymbol { C D }$ ofa $( k , l )$ -matrix $C$ and an $( l , m )$ -matrix $_ D$ is a $( k , m )$ -matrix. The transposed matrix of a $( k , l )$ -matrix is an $( l , k )$ -matrix. A $( k , 1 ) \cdot$ matrix is a $k \mathrm { . }$ -vector.A $( k , l )$ -matrix with all its $k l$ elements zero is denoted by $0 _ { l } ^ { k }$ .A zero $k .$ -vector $0 _ { 1 } ^ { k }$ is written as $0 ^ { k }$ .Total differentiation with respect to $t$ is denoted by a dot; partial differentiation with respect to $t$ is denoted by a subscript $t .$ If a scalar $c = c ( Z , t )$ is a function of $t$ and a $k$ -vector $Z = Z ( t )$ , then

$$
\dot { c } = c _ { t } + \dot { Z } ^ { \prime } \mathrm { g r a d } _ { z } c ,
$$

where the prime denotes the transposed matrix.

Consider

$$
\begin{array}{c} G = { \binom { 0 _ { n } ^ { n } } { E ^ { n } } } \quad - { E ^ { n } }  \\ { { E ^ { n } } } \quad { 0 _ { n } ^ { n } }  \end{array} ) , \quad \quad G = - G ^ { \prime } = - G ^ { - 1 } ,
$$

where $E ^ { n }$ is a unit matrix with $\boldsymbol { n }$ rows and columns,and a canonical dynamical system with $_ { n }$ degrees of freedom

$$
G \dot { X } = \operatorname { g r a d } _ { X } h , \qquad h = h ( q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { n } , \phi _ { 1 } , . . . , \phi _ { n } , t ) ,
$$

where

$$
X = ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } , x _ { n + 1 } , . . . , x _ { 2 n } ) = { \binom { Q } { P } } ,
$$

$$
Q = { \binom { q _ { 1 } } { \vdots } } = { \binom { x _ { 1 } } { \vdots } } , \qquad P = { \binom { \hbar _ { 1 } } { \vdots } } = { \binom { x _ { n + 1 } } { \vdots } } .
$$

Suppose that $\textstyle K ( X , t )$ isa $( 2 n - m )$ -vector，where $m \leq 2 n$ ，in the $( 2 n + 1 )$ -dimensional $( X , t )$ -domain; then $2 n - m$ is called the rank of $\operatorname { g r a d } _ { X } K$ .Then

$$
K = K ( X , t ) = 0 ^ { 2 n - m }
$$

determines a nonempty set $\Lambda ( t )$ in the $2 n$ -dimensional $X$ -space for a fixed $t .$ Thus $\Lambda ( t )$ is a locally $_ m$ -dimensional manifold for a fixed $t .$

We choose a $2 n$ -vector $F = F ( Y , t )$ dependent on $t _ { : }$ and an $m$ -vector $Y = ( y _ { 1 } , \dots y _ { m } )$ , such that

$$
X = F ( Y , t )
$$

is a local parametrization of the manifold $\Lambda ( t )$ and $m$ is the rank of a $( 2 n , m )$ -matrix

$$
J = J ( Y , t ) = \operatorname { g r a d } _ { Y } F .
$$

A curve

$$
X ( t ) = F ( Y ( t ) , t )
$$

in the $2 n$ dimensional phase space obeys the relation

$$
{ \dot { x } } = F _ { t } + J { \dot { Y } }
$$

(where $J = J ( Y , t ) = \operatorname { g r a d } _ { Y } { \mathcal { F } }$ is the Jacobian),as an identity in t. Thus, if every solution $X ( t )$ of (80） that lies on $\Lambda ( t )$ for a single $t$ lies on $\Lambda ( t )$ for euery $t$ ,then the $2 n - m$ relation (82) is called an invariantive system of the differential equations (80).The parametrization (83) of (80） is called an invariantive system of (80).An invariantive system does not determine $F = F ( Y , t )$ uniquely, since $m =$ rank of the Jacobian $J$ is the only restriction imposed on the choice of the parametrization (83).

If a given solution $X ( t )$ satisfies (82),then there exists just one path $Y = Y ( t )$ ， such that (83a) is satisfied in the space of the parameter vector $Y .$ 、This unique $Y ( t )$ satisfies

$$
G J { \dot { Y } } = - G F _ { t } + \operatorname { g r a d } _ { x } h
$$

identicall when (83) is substituted. Hence a unique vector $L = L ( Y , t )$ in the $( m + 1 )$ -dimensional $( Y , t )$ -space-defined so that

$$
\mathbf { \nabla } \cdot G J L = \mathbf { \nabla } - G F _ { t } + \mathbf { g r a d } _ { x } \ : h
$$

is satisfied identically by (83)- belongs to this parametrization. Thus $Y ( t )$ and $L ( Y , t )$ are uniquely determined,and

$$
\dot { Y } = L ( Y , t ) .
$$

If this system of equations has a solution $Y ( t )$ such as (83a),then and only then is $X ( t )$ the solution of the given system of equations satisfying the invariantive system (82).

# 4.15SEPARATING PARAMETRIZATION

Define an $\pmb { m }$ -vector $\pmb { R }$ and a scalar $_ r$ by

$$
\begin{array} { r } { R = R ( Y , t ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } J ^ { \prime } G F , \qquad r = r ( Y , t ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } F _ { t } ^ { \prime } G F ; } \end{array}
$$

then it can be proved (van Kampen and Wintner, l938) that a matrix function $J ^ { \prime } G J$ in $( Y , t )$ is represented in the $( Y , t )$ -domain by an alternating derivative,that is, the curl

$$
J ^ { \prime } G J = ( \mathrm { g r a d } _ { Y } R ) - ( \mathrm { g r a d } _ { Y } R ) ^ { \prime } ,
$$

and a vector function $J G F _ { t }$ in $( Y , t )$ is represented by

$$
J ^ { \prime } G F _ { t } = R _ { t } - \mathrm { g r a d } _ { Y } r .
$$

Since the Jacobian $\boldsymbol { J }$ isa $( 2 n , m )$ -matrix, $J ^ { \prime } G J$ is a skew-symmetric $( m , m )$ -matrix, and $2 l$ is the rank of $J ^ { \prime } G J$ for an integer $\boldsymbol { l }$ such that $2 l \le m$ . This integer $\boldsymbol { l }$ is determined uniquely by the invariantive system (82),because the matrix $N = N ( X , t ) = \operatorname* { g r a d } _ { X } K$ is a $( 2 n - m , 2 n )$ -matrix, and $N G N ^ { \prime }$ is a skew-symmetric $( 2 n - m , 2 n - m )$ -matrix.

The $m$ -vector $Y = ( y _ { 1 } , \dots y _ { m } )$ can be split into a $2 l .$ -vector $\mathbf { \nabla } \mathbf { Y } ^ { 0 }$ and an $( m - 2 l )$ -vector $\gamma *$ such that

$$
( y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { m } ) = Y = { \binom { Y ^ { 0 } } { Y ^ { * } } } ,
$$

$$
Y ^ { 0 } = ( y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 2 l } ) , \qquad Y ^ { * } = ( y _ { 2 l + 1 } , . . . , y _ { m } ) .
$$

If there exists a (2l,2l)-matrix $S ^ { 0 } = S ^ { \circ } ( Y , t )$ such that the $( m , m )$ matrix $J ^ { \prime } G J$ is represented in the form

$$
J ^ { \prime } G J = \binom { S ^ { 0 } } { 0 _ { 2 l } ^ { m - 2 l } } \begin{array} { l l } { { 0 _ { m - 2 l } ^ { 2 l } } } \\ { { 0 _ { 2 l } ^ { m - 2 l } } } \end{array} ,
$$

then the parametrization (83) is called a separating parametrization.

If the parametrization (83) of the invariantive system (82） of the differential equation (80) is a separating parametrization, then the first $2 l$ of the $_ m$ differential equations (85) are of the form

$$
\dot { Y } ^ { \circ } = L ^ { \circ } ( Y ^ { \circ } , t ) ;
$$

that is, these $2 l$ differential equations are separated from the remaining $m - 2 l$ differential equations defining the components $Y ^ { * }$ of the $m -$ vector $Y = ( y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 2 l } , y _ { 2 l + 1 } , . . . , y _ { m } )$ .The differential equations are of the form

$$
\dot { Y } ^ { \ast } = L ^ { \ast } ( Y ^ { \ast } , Y ^ { 0 } , t ) .
$$

When the solution $Y ^ { \circ } ( t )$ of the equations (89) is known, the corresponding function $Y ^ { * } ( t )$ is the solution of

$$
\dot { Y } ^ { * } = L ^ { * } ( Y ^ { * } , t ) ,
$$

where

$$
L ^ { * } ( Y ^ { * } , t ) = L ^ { * } ( Y ^ { * } , Y ^ { 0 } ( t ) , t )
$$

is a known function of $t$ and the $( m - 2 l )$ -vector $Y ^ { * }$ . Thus the equation (85) is split into two separate systems (89) and (90). The $_ m$ -vector $Y ( t )$ determined by $Y ^ { \circ } ( t )$ and $Y ^ { * } ( t )$ gives the solution (83a) of equation (80).In the case of separating parametrization, the $2 l .$ -vector $Y ^ { * }$ is determined by the system of $2 l$ differential equations forming a nondegenerate Pfaffian dynamical system (Section 1.22) of $\boldsymbol { l }$ degrees of freedom in the sense used by Birkhoff (1927).

If $S ^ { \circ }$ exists in (88),then det $S ^ { 0 } \ne 0$ and $S ^ { 0 } = - ( S ^ { 0 } ) ^ { \prime }$ ， since $J ^ { \prime } G J$ is skew-symmetric and $2 l$ is its rank. In such a case $S ^ { \circ }$ can be represented by the alternating derivative

$$
S ^ { 0 } ( Y ^ { 0 } , t ) \equiv S ^ { 0 } = ( \mathrm { g r a d } _ { Y } \circ R ^ { 0 } ) - ( \mathrm { g r a d } _ { Y ^ { 0 } } R ^ { 0 } ) ^ { \prime } ,
$$

and

$$
J ^ { \prime } G F _ { t } = \operatorname { g r a d } _ { Y } h + { \binom { R _ { t } ^ { 0 } } { 0 ^ { m - 2 l } } } \quad \begin{array} { l } { - \operatorname { g r a d } _ { Y } \circ h ^ { 0 } } \\ { 0 ^ { m - 2 l } } \end{array} \biggr ) ,
$$

where

$$
R ^ { 0 } = R ^ { 0 } ( Y ^ { 0 } , t ) , \qquad h ^ { 0 } = h ^ { 0 } ( Y ^ { 0 } , t )
$$

are both $2 l .$ vectors. In the particular case of separating parametrization, the functions $J ^ { \prime } G J$ and $J ^ { \prime } G F _ { t } - \operatorname { g r a d } _ { Y } h$ (which are functions of $t$ and the $_ m$ -vector $Y$ ）are independent of the $( m - 2 l )$ -vector $Y ^ { * }$ of (87).

It can be proved (van Kampen and Wintner, l938) that there always exists a separating parametrization of an invariantive system. Since we know

$$
J ^ { \prime } G J \dot { Y } + J ^ { \prime } G F _ { t } - \mathrm { g r a d } _ { Y } h = 0 ^ { m } ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\left[ \left( \mathrm { g r a d } _ { Y } R \right) - \left( \mathrm { g r a d } _ { Y } R \right) ^ { \prime } \right] \dot { Y } + R _ { t } - \mathrm { g r a d } _ { Y } \left( r + h \right) = 0 ^ { m } ,
$$

where $R$ and $r$ are given by (86) and

$$
h = h ( Y , t ) = h ( F ( Y , t ) , t ) .
$$

Let $\pmb { \omega }$ be an $( m + 1 )$ -ary Pfaffian

$$
\omega = R ^ { \prime } d Y + ( r + h ) d t ;
$$

then the foregoing equation (91） is equivalent to the Euler-Lagrange equations belonging to the variational problem with fixed end points

$$
\delta \int \omega \equiv \delta \int \{ [ R ( Y , t ) ] ^ { \prime } d Y + [ r ( Y , t ) + h ( Y , t ) ] d t \} = 0 .
$$

This can be expressed by

$$
\delta \int \left[ - \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } Y ^ { 0 \prime } G ^ { 0 } d Y ^ { 0 } + h ^ { 0 } ( Y ^ { 0 } , Y ^ { * } , t ) d t \right] = 0 ,
$$

where the Pfaffian $\omega$ is represented in the $( 2 l + 1 )$ -ary normal form

$$
- { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } Y ^ { 0 \prime } G ^ { 0 } d Y ^ { 0 } + h ^ { 0 } d t ,
$$

and

$$
G ^ { 0 } = { \binom { 0 _ { l } ^ { l } } { E _ { l } } } \quad \quad { \boldsymbol { 0 } } _ { l } ^ { l } { \bf \Phi } = - ( G ^ { 0 } ) = - ( G ^ { 0 } ) ^ { - 1 } .
$$

The Euler-Lagrange equation belonging to the new variational problem is

$$
\binom { G ^ { 0 } } { 0 _ { 2 l } ^ { m - 2 l } } \binom { 0 2 l } { m - 2 l } \binom { \dot { Y } ^ { 0 } } { \dot { Y } ^ { * } } - \binom { \mathrm { g r a d } _ { Y ^ { 0 } } h ^ { 0 } } { \mathrm { g r a d } _ { Y ^ { * } } h ^ { 0 } } = 0 ^ { m } .
$$

Hence

$$
J ^ { \prime } G J = \binom { G ^ { 0 } } { 0 _ { 2 l } ^ { m - 2 l } } \ \begin{array} { l } { 0 _ { m - 2 l } ^ { 2 l } } \\ { 0 _ { 2 l } ^ { m - 2 l } } \end{array}
$$

Thus, the condition (88) characterizing the separating parametrization is satisfied.

A canonical parametrization is a separating parametrization for which the corresponding separated system of $2 l$ differential equations-that is, the system (89)-is a canonical dynamical system of $\mathbf { \xi } _ { l }$ degrees of freedom; accordingly the equation

$$
[ ( \mathrm { g r a d } _ { \mathrm { Y } ^ { 0 } } R ^ { 0 } ) - ( \mathrm { g r a d } _ { \mathrm { Y } ^ { 0 } } R ^ { 0 } ) ^ { \prime } ] \dot { Y } ^ { 0 } = - R _ { t } ^ { 0 } + \mathrm { g r a d } _ { \mathrm { Y } ^ { 0 } } h ^ { 0 }
$$

is of the particular form

$$
G ^ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } \dot { Y } ^ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } = \mathrm { g r a d } _ { \scriptscriptstyle Y ^ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } } h ^ { \scriptscriptstyle 0 } ,
$$

where

$$
\mathbf { g r a d } _ { Y ^ { * } } h ^ { 0 } = 0 ^ { m - 2 l } .
$$

Thus,the invariantive system (82) of equations (80) always admits a canonical parametrization. The construction of such canonical parametrization of a given invariantive system depends on the point transformation that transforms the Pfaffian $\omega$ into its normal form (92). This point transformation is determined by the solution of a complete system of partial differential equations (see Section 4.9). In general, the solution can be obtained not by mere quadrature but by successive approximations.

# 4.16FIRST INTEGRALS

A scalar function $g = g ( X , t )$ is a first integral of the system (80), if and only if $g = 0$ is satisfied identically by this system of differential equations. If the equation

$$
K ( X , t ) = C
$$

with a constant $( 2 n - m )$ -vector $C$ is an invariantive system for any $C$ in the $( 2 n - m )$ -dimensional domain, then $\dot { K } = 0 ^ { 2 n - m }$ is an identity because of the system of differential equations.When the invariantive system consists of only first integrals, if the $2 n - m$ first integrals repre-sented by (94), which are mutually independent, form a function group (see Section 4.1O) in the sense of Lie, then this function group contains $n - l$ independent first integrals in involution and nothing more (see Section 4.5；also see van Kampen and Wintner， 1938；Williamson, 1940). Denote by $k$ the rank of $N G N ^ { \prime }$ ，where $N = \operatorname { g r a d } _ { x } K$ It can be proved that,if （94） consists of $2 n - m$ first integrals of the canonical system (80） of $\boldsymbol { n }$ degrees of freedom，and if these first integrals are independent and form a function group, then the maximum number $j$ of the first integrals in involution that belong to the function group is equal to $2 n - m - k$ (see Sections 4.10, 4.11).

Consider the three-body problem in which $\xi _ { i } , \ \eta _ { i } , \ \zeta _ { i } \ ( i = 1 , 2 , 3 )$ are the rectangular coordinates of the mass $\mu _ { i }$ referred to the inertial system, and $\Xi _ { i } , \mathrm { H } _ { i } , Z _ { i }$ are the conjugate momenta $\mu _ { i } \dot { \xi } _ { i } , \mu _ { i } \dot { \eta } _ { i } , \mu _ { i } \dot { \zeta } _ { i }$ The system of differential equations of motion admits, besides the energy integral, nine classical integrals

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \begin{array} { l l l } & { \displaystyle \mathcal { F } _ { 1 } - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 } ( \mathbb { H } \xi _ { i } - Z _ { 0 : i } ) , } & & { \displaystyle \int _ { 0 } - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 } ( Z _ { 1 } \xi _ { i } - \mathbb { E } \xi _ { i } ) , } \\ & { \displaystyle \int _ { \xi _ { 0 } } - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 } ( Z _ { 1 } \eta _ { i } - \mathbb { H } \xi _ { i } ) , } & & { \displaystyle \int _ { \xi _ { 1 } } - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 } \mathbb { E } \xi _ { i } , } \end{array} } \\ & { \begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \mathcal { F } _ { 3 } - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 } ( \xi _ { 1 } , \eta _ { i } - \mathbb { H } , \xi _ { i } ) , } & & { \displaystyle \int _ { \xi _ { 1 } } - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 } \mathbb { E } \xi _ { i } , } \\ & { \displaystyle \int _ { \xi _ { 1 } } - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 } \mathbb { H } _ { \xi _ { i } } , } \end{array} } \\ & { \begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \mathcal { F } _ { 4 } - \sum _ { i = 2 } ^ { 3 } \mathbb { H } , } \\ & { \displaystyle \int _ { \xi _ { 1 } } - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 } ( \mu _ { i } \xi _ { i } - \mu \mathbb { E } ) , } \end{array} } \\ & { \begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \mathcal { F } _ { 2 } - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 } ( \mu _ { i } \xi _ { i } - \mu \mathbb { E } ) , } \\ & { \displaystyle \int _ { \xi _ { 2 } } - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 } ( \mu _ { i } \xi _ { i } - \mu \mathbb { E } ) , } \end{array} \quad \begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \mathcal { F } _ { 6 } - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 } ( \mu _ { i } \eta _ { i } - \mathbb { E } \mathbb { H } \xi _ { i } ) , } \\ { \displaystyle \int _ { \xi _ { 1 } } - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 } ( \mu _ { i } \xi _ { i } - \mu \mathbb { Z } _ { \xi _ { 1 } } ) . } \end{array} } \\ &  \begin{array} { l }  \displaystyle \int _ { \xi _ { 2 } } ( \mu _ { i } \xi _   \end{array} \end{array}
$$

The $( 2 n - m )$ -vector $\kappa$ defines a 9-vector with components $f _ { 1 } , \ldots , f _ { 9 } ,$ Thus $n = 9$ ， $m = 9$ ；hence $l = k$ $j = 9 - k$ ，where $k = 4$ Hence $l = 4$ and $j = 5$ ; these are respectively the degree of freedom and the maximum number of the first integrals in involution contained in the function group generated by $f _ { 1 } , \ldots , f _ { 9 }$ of the separated system (93). The Poisson brackets (see Sections 4.12, 4.13) are represented by

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { ( f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } ) = f _ { 3 } , ~ ( f _ { 2 } , f _ { 4 } ) = - f _ { 6 } , ~ ( f _ { 3 } , f _ { 4 } ) = f _ { 5 } , } } \\ { { ~ ( f _ { 2 } , f _ { 7 } ) = - f _ { 9 } , ~ ( f _ { 3 } , f _ { 7 } ) = f _ { 8 } , ~ ( f _ { 4 } , f _ { 7 } ) = \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 } \mu _ { i } . } } \end{array}
$$

Thus,

$$
\left| \left( f _ { \rho } , f _ { \sigma } \right) \right| = \left( \begin{array} { c c c } { { F _ { 1 } } } & { { F _ { 2 } } } & { { F _ { 3 } } } \\ { { F _ { 2 } } } & { { 0 } } & { { \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 } \mu _ { i } E ^ { 3 } } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { { F _ { 3 } } } & { { - \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 } \mu _ { i } E ^ { 3 } } } & { { O } } \end{array} \right) ,
$$

where

$$
F _ { i } = \left( - f _ { 3 i } \qquad O \qquad f _ { 3 i } \qquad - f _ { 3 i - 1 } \right) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , 3 ) .
$$

In the plane problem, $\zeta _ { i } = Z _ { i } = 0$ $( i = 1 , 2 , 3$ ). Hence we have five integrals $f _ { 3 } , f _ { 4 } , f _ { 5 } , f _ { 7 } , f _ { 8 }$ .Thus, $n = 6$ ， $2 n - m = 5$ and $l = k + 1$ ， $j = 5 - k$

The 5-rowed skew-symmetric matrix is of rank 4;and $k = 2 , l = 3$ ， $j = 3$

# INTEGRAL INVARIANTS

# 4.17INTEGRALS AND INTEGRAL INVARIANTS

Suppose that, in the system of differential equations

$$
{ \frac { d x _ { 1 } } { X _ { 1 } } } = { \frac { d x _ { 2 } } { X _ { 2 } } } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = { \frac { d x _ { n } } { X _ { n } } } = d t ,
$$

the functions $X _ { i }$ ，together with their derivatives,are single-valued and continuous,and do not contain $t$ explicitly. The trajectory $x _ { i } = f _ { i } ( t )$ $( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ in the $\pmb { n }$ dimensional space $( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ represents the solution of (95） that passes through $x _ { i } = x _ { i } ^ { 0 } ( i = 1 , 2 , . ~ . ~ . , n )$ at $t = t _ { 0 }$ . Take a subspace $E _ { p } ^ { ( 0 ) } \ { \mathsf { o f } } \ p$ dimensions in this space and suppose that the trajectory that was in this subspace $E _ { p } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ at $t = t _ { 0 }$ is transferred to a $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ -dimensional subspace $E _ { p }$ at time t. A homogeneous expression of degree $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ with regard to the differentials

$$
\omega = \sum _ { \alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \cdot \cdot \cdot , \alpha _ { p } } A _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \alpha _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \alpha _ { p } } d x _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } d x _ { \alpha _ { 2 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { \alpha _ { p } }
$$

is called a Pfaffian of degree $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ (Section l.7). If the integral $I _ { p } = \int \omega$ taken over this Pfaffian has the same value for any $E _ { p }$ and $E _ { p } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ ,then $I _ { p }$ is called an integral invariant of degree $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ (Section i.2).Here an integral invariant is meant in the sense used by Poincaré.The index $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ can take any value between l and $_ { n }$ (Poincaré,1889,1899;Weber,1900;Goursat, 1908,1912,1915,1921；de Donder,1901,1902,1927；Cartan,1922; Schouten and van der Kulk, 1949).

It is evident from this definition that an integral invariant remains an integral invariant after any transformation of the variables. Let $f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , \ldots , f _ { n - 1 }$ be $n - 1$ independent integrals of

$$
X ( f ) = X _ { 1 } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { 1 } } } + X _ { 2 } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { 2 } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + X _ { n } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { n } } } = 0 .
$$

Then we have $n - 1$ integrals of (95): $f _ { i } = c _ { i } ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - 1 )$ and $t = f _ { n } + c _ { n }$ ，where the $c _ { i }$ are constants and $f _ { i }$ depend only on $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ Take new variables $y _ { 1 } = f _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } = f _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { n - 1 } = f _ { n - 1 }$ ， $z = f _ { n }$ ； then (95) is transformed into the system

$$
{ \frac { d y _ { 1 } } { 0 } } = { \frac { d y _ { 2 } } { 0 } } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = { \frac { d y _ { n - 1 } } { 0 } } = { \frac { d z } { 1 } } = d t ,
$$

whose general integrals are

$$
y _ { 1 } = c _ { 1 } , \qquad y _ { 2 } = c _ { 2 } , . . . , \qquad y _ { n - 1 } = c _ { n - 1 } , \qquad z = t + c _ { n } .
$$

The original Pfaffian $\pmb { \omega }$ is changed to a Pfaffian $\omega$ of $d y _ { 1 } , d y _ { 2 } , . . . , d y _ { n - 1 } ;$ $d z$ ； the coefficients $A$ of the differentials in $\omega$ are now functions of $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { n - 1 }$ ， $_ z$ Hence,an integral invariant $I _ { p } = \int \omega$ is transformed to an integral invariant $\int { \varpi \frac { } { } }$ with regard to (97). If we increase $z$ by $t \mathrm { ~ - ~ } t _ { 0 }$ by keeping $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { n - 1 }$ unaltered in the subspace $E _ { p } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ ，then the points in $E _ { p } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ are displaced to the points in $E _ { p }$ . In order that $\int \varpi$ may not depend on $t$ -that is, in order that $\int \varpi \mathrm { m a y }$ be an integral invariant with regard to (97)-it is necessary and sufficient that $\eqcirc$ should be invariant under the change of variable from $z$ to $z + t$ in $\pmb { \varpi }$ in other words, that the coefficients of $\eqcirc$ should not depend on $_ z$ But the system (97) does not alter its form even if we take $n - 1$ arbitrary independent functions of $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { n - 1 }$ as our new variables. Hence, there must be an infinite number of such integral invariants of degree $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$

As a corollary, consider $f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , . . . , f _ { r } ~ ( r < n )$ ， which are integrals of (95） independent of $t _ { : }$ and a Pfaffian $\omega$ of degree $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ , in which the coefficients of $d f _ { 1 } , d f _ { 2 } , \ldots , d f _ { \tau }$ are functions of $f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , \ldots , f _ { r }$ only. Then $\int \omega$ is an integral invariant of degree $\pmb { r }$ If, for example, $f$ is an integral of (97), then $\int$ df is an integral invariant. Further,suppose that

$$
\int { \left( \sum _ { i , j } B _ { i j } d x _ { i } d x _ { j } \right) ^ { 1 / 2 } }
$$

becomes

$$
\int \Big ( \sum _ { i , j } B _ { i j } ^ { \prime } d y _ { i } d y _ { j } \Big ) ^ { 1 / 2 }
$$

by this transformation and that $B _ { i j } ^ { \prime }$ do not depend on $z$ 、Then this is an integral invariant. Let $\int \omega _ { p }$ and $\int . \omega _ { q }$ be two integral invariants with regard to (95).In the transformation from (95) to (97),the coefficients in these integral invariants do not contain $_ z$ Hence, the situation is the same for the product, and $\int \omega _ { p } \omega _ { q }$ is also an integral invariant.

Denote the differential of (96) formally by

$$
\omega ^ { \prime } = \sum d A _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \alpha _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \alpha _ { p } } d x _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } d x _ { \alpha _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot } d x _ { \alpha _ { p } }
$$

and call it a differential form.Put it in another form

$$
\omega ^ { \prime } = \sum B _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \alpha _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \alpha _ { p } \alpha _ { p + 1 } } d x _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } d x _ { \alpha _ { 2 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { \alpha _ { p } } d x _ { \alpha _ { p + 1 } } .
$$

$\mathbf { I f } \ p$ is even, then

$$
B _ { \alpha _ { \mathrm { i } } \alpha _ { 2 } \cdots \alpha _ { p } } = \frac { \partial A _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \alpha _ { 2 } \cdots \alpha _ { p } } } { \partial x _ { \alpha _ { p + 1 } } } + \frac { \partial A _ { \alpha _ { 2 } \cdots \alpha _ { p + 1 } } } { \partial x _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } } + \cdot \cdot + \frac { \partial A _ { \alpha _ { p + 1 } \alpha _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \alpha _ { p - 1 } } } { \partial x _ { \alpha _ { p } } } ,
$$

and if $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ is odd, then

$$
B _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \alpha _ { p + 1 } } = { \frac { \partial A _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \alpha _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \alpha _ { p } } } { \partial x _ { \alpha _ { p + 1 } } } } - { \frac { \partial A _ { \alpha _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \alpha _ { p + 1 } } } { \partial x _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot - { \frac { \partial A _ { \alpha _ { p + 1 } \alpha _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \alpha _ { p - 1 } } } { \partial x _ { \alpha _ { p } } } } .
$$

In particular, if $\omega$ is a Pfaffian of degree one,

$$
\omega = A _ { 1 } d x _ { 1 } + A _ { 2 } d x _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { n } d x _ { n } ,
$$

then

$$
\omega ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { i , j } \left( \frac { \partial A _ { i } } { \partial x _ { j } } - \frac { \partial A _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } \right) d x _ { i } d x _ { j } .
$$

Exchanging $d x _ { i } \ d x _ { j }$ with $d x _ { j } \ \delta x _ { i } - \ \delta x _ { j } \ d x _ { i }$ gives the bilinear covariant of Section 1.4,

$$
\omega ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { i , j } \Big ( \frac { \partial A _ { i } } { \partial x _ { j } } - \frac { \partial A _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } \Big ) ( d x _ { j } \delta x _ { i } - \delta x _ { j } d x _ { i } ) .
$$

By the above theorem,it is necessary and sufficient that $\partial A _ { i } / \partial x _ { j } - \partial A _ { j } / \partial x _ { i }$ should be independent of $z$ for this expression $\omega ^ { \prime }$ to be an integral invariant.

Let the points on a trajectory, which starts at time $t _ { 0 }$ from the subspace $E _ { \mathcal { P } + 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ , embedded in an $\pmb { n }$ dimensionalspace,occupythesubspace $E _ { p + 1 }$ at time $t .$ The subspaces $E _ { p + 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ and $E _ { p + 1 }$ are bounded by the subspaces $E _ { p } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ and $E _ { p }$ ,respectively.By a generalization ofStokes's theorem, the integrals $\int \omega ^ { \prime }$ extended over $E _ { p + 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ and $E _ { p + 1 }$ are equal to $\int \omega$ extended over $\mathbf { } E _ { p } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ and $E _ { p }$ ，respectively. If $\int \omega$ is an integral invariant, then the integrals $\int \omega$ extended over $E _ { p } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ and $E _ { p }$ have the same value. Hence, the integrals $\int \omega ^ { \prime }$ extended over $E _ { p + 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ and $E _ { p + 1 }$ havethesamevalue;thatis,theyare integral invariants. Thus,a new integral invariant ${ \cal { I } } ^ { \prime } = \int \omega ^ { \prime }$ is derived from an integral invariant with regard to (95).

The integral $\int \omega$ extended over $E _ { p }$ is called an absolute integral invariant. When and only when $E _ { p }$ is a closed space,an integral invariant over it is said to be a relative integral invariant and written as $J .$ .The above integrals $\int \omega ^ { \prime }$ ,extended over the spaces $E _ { p + 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ and $E _ { p + 1 }$ ,respectively,are equal to ω extended over the closed subspaces $E _ { p } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ and $E _ { p }$ bounding $E _ { p + 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ and $E _ { p + 1 } ,$ respectively. Since $\int \omega$ is a relative integral invariant by definition, $\int \omega ^ { \prime }$ is an absolute integral invariant. The necessary and sufficient condition that $\int \omega$ be a relative but not an absolute integral invariant is that $\int \omega ^ { \prime }$ should be an absolute integral invariant. Conversely, if $\int \omega ^ { \prime }$ is an absolute integral invariant, then $\int \omega$ is a relative or an absolute integral invariant.A perfect differential is zero when integrated over a closed space; therefore,an integral of the sum of the integrand $\omega$ of a relative integral invariant and any perfect differential of the same degree extended over a closed space is also a relative integral invariant.

# 4.18CONDITION FOR AN INTEGRAL INVARIANT

Let us find the condition for (96) to be an absolute integral invariant with regard to (95). Denote the integral $\int \omega$ extended over $E _ { p }$ by $I _ { p } ( t )$ It is required that $d I _ { p } / d t = 0$ in order that $\int \omega$ shall be an integral invariant. As $t$ increases by $\delta t$ ，the variable $x _ { i }$ increases to

$$
x _ { i } ^ { \prime } = x _ { i } + X _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) \ \delta t \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

By taking $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ independent variables $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \dotsc , u _ { p }$ we can write

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { I _ { p } ( t ) = \displaystyle { \int \sum { A _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } . . . \alpha _ { p } \frac { \partial { x _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } } } { \partial { u _ { 1 } } } . . . \frac { \partial { x _ { \alpha _ { p } } } } { \partial { u _ { p } } } d u _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d u _ { p } } } , } \\ & { } & { \mathrm { } _ { p } ( t + \delta t ) = \displaystyle { \int \sum { A _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } ^ { \prime } . . . \alpha _ { p } \frac { \partial { x _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } ^ { \prime } } } { \partial { u _ { 1 } } } . . . \frac { \partial { x _ { \alpha _ { p } } ^ { \prime } } } { \partial { u _ { p } } } d u _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot d u _ { p } } . } \end{array}
$$

$A ^ { \prime }$ is the result of substituting $\pmb { x } _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ for $x _ { i }$ in $A _ { i }$ . We also have

$$
{ \cal A } _ { \beta _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \beta _ { p } } ^ { \prime } = { \cal A } _ { \beta _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \beta _ { p } } + X ( { \cal A } _ { \beta _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \beta _ { p } } ) \delta t ,
$$

$$
\frac { \partial x _ { \alpha } ^ { \prime } } { \partial u _ { j } } = \frac { \partial x _ { \alpha } } { \partial u _ { j } } + \ : \delta t \sum _ { i } \frac { \partial X _ { \alpha } } { \partial x _ { i } } \frac { \partial x _ { i } } { \partial u _ { j } } \qquad ( \alpha , j = 1 , . . . , \beta ) ,
$$

where

$$
X ( f ) = X _ { 1 } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { 1 } } } + X _ { 2 } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { 2 } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + X _ { n } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { n } } } .
$$

Hence, the coefficient of

$$
\frac { \partial x _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } } { \partial u _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot \frac { \partial x _ { \alpha _ { p } } } { \partial u _ { p } } \delta t
$$

in $I _ { p } ( t + \delta t )$ is

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \cal X } ( A _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } . . . _ { \alpha _ { p } } ) + \sum _ { i } \Big ( A _ { i \alpha _ { 2 } } . . . _ { \alpha _ { p } } \frac { \partial { \cal X } _ { i } } { \partial x _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } } + A _ { \alpha _ { l i } } . . . _ { \alpha _ { p } } \frac { \partial { \cal X } _ { i } } { \partial x _ { \alpha _ { 2 } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + ~ A _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } . . . _ { \alpha _ { p - 1 } i } \frac { \partial { \cal X } _ { i } } { \partial x _ { \alpha _ { p } } } \Big ) } . } \end{array}
$$

In order that $I _ { p } ( t + \delta t ) - I _ { p } ( t )$ shall be zero, it is necessry and sufficient that the quantity (lOO) be zero for any combination of the indices $\alpha _ { 1 } , \dotsc , \alpha _ { p }$ In order that $\int \omega$ shall be a relative integral invariant with regard to (95), it is necessary that the expression (l00) integrated over a closed space of $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { p } }$ dimensions be zero.

From the integral invariant of order $_ { n }$ ，

$$
I _ { n } = \int M ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) d x _ { 1 } d x _ { 2 } . . . d x _ { n } ,
$$

we form the expression

$$
X ( M ) + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } M { \frac { \partial X _ { i } } { \partial x _ { i } } } = 0 ,
$$

or

$$
{ \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { 1 } } } \left( X _ { 1 } M \right) + { \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { 2 } } } \left( X _ { 2 } M \right) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + { \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { n } } } \left( X _ { n } M \right) = 0 .
$$

Hence,for (lOl） to be an integral invariant with regard to (95） it is necessary and sufficient for $M$ to be the last multiplier of the differential equations (95)． That is, (95) becomes a perfect differential when multiplied by $M$ on the left-hand side (Poincaré,1899).

If

$$
\int M d x _ { 1 } d x _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n } , \qquad \int M ^ { \prime } d x _ { 1 } d x _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n } ,
$$

are two integral invariants, then $M / M ^ { \prime }$ is an integral of (95). For, we obtain

$$
{ \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { 1 } } } \left( X _ { 1 } { \frac { M } { M ^ { \prime } } } \right) + { \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { 2 } } } \left( X _ { 2 } { \frac { M } { M ^ { \prime } } } \right) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + { \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { n } } } \left( X _ { n } { \frac { M } { M ^ { \prime } } } \right) = 0
$$

from

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { 1 } } \left( X _ { 1 } M \right) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { n } } \left( X _ { n } M \right) = 0 , } \\ & { } \\ & { \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { 1 } } \left( X _ { 1 } M ^ { \prime } \right) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { n } } \left( X _ { n } M ^ { \prime } \right) = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

In particular, if $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \partial X _ { i } / \partial x _ { i } = 0 } \end{array}$ ，then letting $M = 1$ gives the ndimensional volume $\int d x _ { 1 } d x _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n }$ as an integral invariant. A dynamical system with such a relation is called a Liouville dynamical system.Since the volume is invariant, the dynamical system is represented by an incompressible fluid motion

$$
\sum _ { i \mathop { = } 1 } ^ { n } { \frac { \partial X _ { i } } { \partial x _ { i } } } = \mathop { \mathrm { d i v } } \mathbf { X } = 0 ,
$$

where $\mathbf { x }$ is a vector with components $X _ { 1 } , X _ { 2 } , . . . , X _ { n }$

When $X _ { i }$ does not contain $t$ in the Pfaffian of order $n - 1$ ， we have

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \Omega _ { n - 1 } = X _ { 1 } d x _ { 2 } d x _ { 3 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n } \pm X _ { 2 } d x _ { 3 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n } d x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \pm X _ { n } d x _ { 1 } d x _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n - 1 } , } } \end{array}
$$

where the plus sign is taken for $\pmb { n }$ odd and the minus sign for $\scriptstyle n$ even. An integral of $X ( f ) = 0$ is also an integral of $\Omega _ { n - 1 } d f = 0 .$ since

$$
\Omega _ { n - 1 } d f = \pm \ X ( f ) \ d x _ { 1 } d x _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n } .
$$

Conversely, an integral of $\Omega _ { n - 1 } d f = 0$ is also an integral of $X ( f ) = 0$ · Suppose that transforming from (95) to (97） gives

$$
\Omega _ { n - 1 } = { \cal K } d y _ { 1 } d y _ { 2 } . \cdot \cdot d y _ { n - 1 } .
$$

This occurs when and only when MK does not depend on $z$ or when $\int M \Omega _ { n - 1 }$ is an integral invariant if $M \Omega _ { n - 1 }$ is a differential； that is, $\int M \Omega _ { n - 1 }$ is an integral invariant if and only if

$$
{ \frac { \partial ( M X _ { 1 } ) } { \partial x _ { 1 } } } + { \frac { \partial ( M X _ { 2 } ) } { \partial x _ { 2 } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + { \frac { \partial ( M X _ { n } ) } { \partial x _ { n } } } = 0 .
$$

Hence，we can obtain an integral invariant $\int M \Omega _ { n - \dot { 1 } }$ from the last multiplier of (95). Here it is assumed that $M$ and $\Omega _ { n - 1 }$ contain neither $_ z$ nor $d z$ after the transformation.

Conversely,by constructing an expression analogous to (loo） from

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \omega _ { n - 1 } = A _ { 1 } d x _ { 2 } d x _ { 3 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n } \pm A _ { 2 } d x _ { 3 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n } d x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { \pm A _ { n } d x _ { 1 } d x _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n - 1 } , } } \\ { { \ } } \end{array}
$$

we obtain

$$
