\begin{array} { l } { { X ( A _ { 1 } ) d x _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n } \pm \cdot \cdot \cdot \pm \ X ( A _ { n } ) d x _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n - 1 } } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { + A _ { 1 } [ d X _ { 2 } d x _ { 3 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n } + d x _ { 2 } d X _ { 3 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ] } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { \pm \ A _ { 2 } [ d X _ { 3 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n } d x _ { 1 } + d x _ { 3 } d X _ { 4 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ] } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { \vdots } } \\ { { \pm \ A _ { n } [ d X _ { 1 } d x _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n - 1 } + d x _ { 1 } d X _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ] , } } \end{array}
$$

where the coefficient of $d x _ { 2 } d x _ { 3 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n }$ , for example, is

$$
X ( A _ { 1 } ) - A ( X _ { 1 } ) + A _ { 1 } H ,
$$

with

$$
A = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } A _ { i } { \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { i } } } , \qquad H = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { \frac { \partial X _ { i } } { \partial x _ { i } } } .
$$

Letting all coefficients equal zero, we see that the necessary and sufficient condition for $\int \omega _ { n - 1 }$ to be an integral invariant is that

$$
X ( A ( f ) ) - A ( X ( f ) ) + H A ( f ) = 0
$$

for any function $f .$

Furthermore,

$$
X ( f ) = 0 , \quad \quad A ( f ) + M f = 0 \quad \quad \mathrm { w i t h } \quad \quad M = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { \frac { \partial A _ { i } } { \partial x _ { i } } }
$$

form a system in involution, since the necessary and sufficient condition is that

$$
X ( A ( f ) + M f ) - A ( X ( f ) ) - M X ( f ) = 0
$$

should be a consequence of $X ( f ) = 0$ ， $A ( f ) + M f = 0$ From (103), this becomes

$$
X ( M ) f - H A ( f ) = [ X ( M ) + H M ] f = 0 .
$$

The relation $X ( M ) + H M = 0$ expresses that $M$ is a last multiplier, since the derived form of $\omega _ { n - 1 }$ is $M d x _ { 1 } d x _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n }$

The system $X ( f ) = 0$ ， $A ( f ) + M f = 0$ is obtained by finding those integrals of $X ( f ) = 0$ that are also the multipliers for $\omega _ { n - 1 }$ . We have

$$
( \omega _ { n - 1 } f ) ^ { \prime } = \omega _ { n - 1 } ^ { \prime } f + \omega _ { n - 1 } d f = [ A ( f ) + M f ] d x _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n } .
$$

We obtain the equations $X ( f ) = 0 { \it \Omega }$ $A ( f ) = 0$ at the same time by finding those integrals of $X ( f ) = 0$ that satisfy also the relation $\omega _ { n - 1 } d f = 0$ .Hence, if the integral $\int \omega _ { n - 1 }$ is an integral invariant, the linear equations $( \omega _ { n - 1 } f ) ^ { \prime } = 0 , X ( \check { f } ) = 0$ form a system in involution, and consequently the equations $\omega _ { n - 1 } d f = 0 \mathrm { _ { ; } }$ ， $X ( f ) = 0$ form a complete system of linear homogeneous equations.

# 4.19INTEGRAL INVARIANTS ALONG TRAJECTORIES

Write equations (95) in the form $d x _ { i } = X _ { i }$ dt $( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ ,where $X _ { i }$ contains $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ but not $t$ .Consider the second system

$$
d x _ { i } = \lambda X _ { i } d t \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

where $\lambda$ is a function of $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ .The integrals $f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , \ldots , f _ { n - 1 }$ of the first system are also the integrals of the second system. Only the nth integral $f _ { n }$ contains t. Hence, the nth integral of the second system contains $\lambda$ .The trajectory has the same form in both systems, but there is a difference in the way it is described in terms of $t .$ If the coefficients in a Pfaffian $\omega$ of order $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ with respect to $d f _ { 1 } , d f _ { 2 } , \ldots , d f _ { n - 1 }$ are functions $\mathrm { o f } f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , \dotsc , f _ { n - 1 }$ only, then $\int \omega$ is an integral invariant whatever $\lambda$ may be. Such an integral invariant is called an integral invariant along the trajectory and denoted by $I _ { p } ^ { l }$ . We have seen that the necessary and sufficient condition for $\int \Omega$ to be an integral invariant in the transformation from (95) to (97) is that the coefficients of $\Omega$ do not depend on $_ z$ Also,whatever the factor $\lambda$ is, $\Omega$ must be independent of $z$ and $d z$ for $\int \Omega$ to be an integral invariant. That is, $\Omega$ must contain neither $z$ nor $d z$ ， for $\int \Omega$ to be an integral invariant along the trajectory.

In order that an integral invariant $\int \Omega$ with regard to (95) shall be also an integral invariant along the trajectory, it is necessary and suffcient that $\int \Omega$ should still be an integral invariant even if we write $\lambda X _ { i }$ for $X _ { i ; \ l }$ ， whatever may be the value of $\lambda$ .Write $\lambda X _ { i }$ for $X _ { i }$ in the condition (100).

In order that this rewritten condition hold foranyfunction $\lambda$ , itis necessary and sufficient to have

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } A _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \alpha _ { p - 1 } i } X _ { i } = 0
$$

for all combinations of $\phi - 1$ indices taken arbitrarily out of the $\pmb { n }$ indices. When we take $d \pmb { x } _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot d \pmb { x } _ { \alpha _ { p - 1 } }$ out of each term of

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { A _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } . . . . _ { \alpha _ { p } - 1 ^ { i } } d x _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } . . . d x _ { \alpha _ { p } - 1 } d x _ { i } , } } \\ { { . } } \end{array}
$$

we obtain a Pfaffian of order one

$$
\omega _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot \alpha _ { p - 1 } } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } A _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \alpha _ { p - 1 } i } d x _ { i } .
$$

By taking all combinations of $\phi - 1$ out of the $_ n$ indices we obtain the Pfaffan of order one associated with

$$
\Omega = \sum A _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \alpha _ { p } } d x _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { \alpha _ { p } } .
$$

When we substitute (95) in these last two expressions, they are reduced to zero by reason of (lO4); that is,such equations are satisfied by a function of one variable satisfying $n - 1$ integrals $f _ { i } = c _ { i } ( i = 1 , 2 , . ~ . ~ . , n - 1 )$ ， of the system (95). Hence, $\omega _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \alpha _ { p - 1 } } = 0$ is a result of

$$
d f _ { 1 } = 0 , . . . , d f _ { n - 1 } = 0 ,
$$

and the Pfaffian of order one associated with $\Omega$ is a linear homogeneous expression with respect to $d f _ { 1 } , . . . , d f _ { n - 1 }$ 、Also,(104） is the condition for an integral invariant along the trajectory,and (lOO） gives the condition for an integral invariant in general. It can be proved,as in the general case, that if $\int \Omega$ is an integral invariant along the trajectory then so is $\int \Omega ^ { \prime }$

By applying Poincaré's method we can obtain an integral invariant of order $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } - 1$ along the trajectory from a general integral invariant of order $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { p } }$ not necessarily along the trajectory. Let an integral invariant in general, not along the trajectory, be

$$
I _ { p } = \int \omega _ { p } = \int \sum A _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \cdots \alpha _ { p } } d x _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { \alpha _ { p } } ,
$$

and put

$$
C _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \alpha _ { p - 1 } } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } A _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \alpha _ { p - 1 } i } X _ { i }
$$

then it does not necessarily follow that all $C _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \cdots \alpha _ { p - 1 } }$ are zero. If we write

$$
\omega _ { p - 1 } = \sum C _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \alpha _ { p - 1 } } d x _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { \alpha _ { p - 1 } } ,
$$

then $\int \omega _ { p - 1 }$ is an integral invariant along the trajectory. That this is an integral invariant follows from the fact that the expression (l00) formed for ωp-1s

$$
X ( C _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \cdots \alpha _ { p - 1 } } ) + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( C _ { i \alpha _ { 2 } \cdots \alpha _ { p - 1 } } { \frac { \partial X _ { i } } { \partial x _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + C _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \cdots \alpha _ { p - 2 ^ { i } } } { \frac { \partial X _ { i } } { \partial x _ { \alpha _ { p - 1 } } } } \right) = 0
$$

is the result of equating the expression (100) formed for $\omega _ { p }$ to zero. It is

$$
X ( C _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \cdots \alpha _ { p - 1 } } ) = \sum _ { i } X _ { i } X ( A _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \cdots \alpha _ { p - 1 } i } ) + \sum _ { i } \sum _ { j } A _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \cdots \alpha _ { p - 1 } i } X _ { j } { \frac { \partial X _ { i } } { \partial x _ { j } } } .
$$

This can be proved if we substitute in $X ( A _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } . . . { \alpha _ { p } } _ { - 1 } i )$ the expression obtained by equating the expression (l00) formed for $\omega _ { p }$ to zero, and if at the same time we substitute in $C _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \cdots \alpha _ { p - 1 } }$ the expression in terms of such $A _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \alpha _ { p - 1 } i }$ Hence, $\int \omega _ { p - 1 }$ is an integral invariant. That it is also an integral invariant along the trajectory can be proved by the fact that the sum in

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } C _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \alpha _ { p - 2 } i } X _ { i } = \sum _ { i } \sum _ { j } A _ { \alpha _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \alpha _ { p - 2 } j i } X _ { i } X _ { j }
$$

becomes zero by exchanging i and $j$ on the right-hand side,and equation (104).is then satisfied.

De Donder(190l)applied the theory ofintegral invariants to the theory of turbulence.

# 4.20 VARIATIONAL EQUATIONS

Let $x _ { i } = x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } ( t ) ~ ( i = 1 , 2 , . ~ . ~ . , n )$ be a particular solution of the system of differential equations

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = X _ { i } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Put $x _ { i } = x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } + \xi _ { i } ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ ， and substitute in (l05)； then we have the differential equations for the variation $\xi _ { i }$ .The system of

differential equations obtained by neglecting higher-order terms with regard to $\xi _ { i }$ ，

$$
\frac { d \xi _ { i } } { d t } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial X _ { i } } { \partial x _ { j } } \bigg | _ { x = x ^ { ( 0 ) } } \xi _ { j } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

are called the variational equations. Let a solution of (105) be

$$
x _ { i } = \phi _ { i } ( t , c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , . . . , c _ { p } ) ,
$$

and call a solution corresponding to $x _ { i } = x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ with

$$
c _ { 1 } = c _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = c _ { p } = 0
$$

the generating solution; then (106) has particular solutions

$$
\xi _ { i } = \frac { \partial \phi _ { i } } { \partial c _ { j } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ; j = 1 , 2 , . . . , p ) .
$$

Thus,when we know solutions of (l05) containing integration constants, we can obtain a particular solution of (lO6). If we know an integral of (105), $F ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) =$ constant, then we have the integrals

$$
F [ \phi _ { 1 } ( t ) , \phi _ { 2 } ( t ) , . . . , \phi _ { n } ( t ) ] = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t }
$$

for (105), and

$$
F [ \phi _ { 1 } ( t ) + \xi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } ( t ) + \xi _ { 2 } , . . . , \phi _ { n } ( t ) + \xi _ { n } ] = \mathrm { c o n s t a n }
$$

for (106). From these two integrals we obtain an integral of (106)

$$
\left. { \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { 1 } } } \right| _ { x = x ^ { ( 0 ) } } \xi _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \left. { \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { n } } } \right| _ { x = x ^ { ( 0 ) } } \xi _ { n } = { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } } ,
$$

where $\partial F / \partial x _ { i } \big | _ { x = x ^ { ( 0 ) } }$ is the result of substituting $x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } = \phi _ { i } ( t )$ after the differentiation.

Poincaré (l899） has found a close connection between an integral of (106) and an integral invariant with regard to (lO5) in general. Let $F ( \xi _ { 1 } , \xi _ { 2 } , . . . , \xi _ { n } ) =$ constant be an integral of (lO6). It is a homogeneous function with respect to $\xi _ { i }$ and depends on $x _ { i } . F$ can be considered linear homogeneous with respect to $\xi _ { i }$ ； if not, we can still construct a linear homogeneous expression by forming a power of expression $F$ with a suitable index. Thus,

$$
\int F ( d x _ { 1 } , d x _ { 2 } , \ldots , d x _ { n } )
$$

is an integral invariant with regard to (lO5). In fact, the integrand is supposed to be infinitely small and linear homogeneous with respect to $d x _ { i }$ Let $F _ { 0 }$ be the infinitely short arc of a curve with the values of $( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ and $( x _ { 1 } + \xi _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } + \xi _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } + \xi _ { n } )$ at $t = 0$ as the coordinates of the two ends, then the integral (lO7） extended over this short arc is nothing but $F ( \xi _ { 1 } , \xi _ { 2 } , . . . , \xi _ { n } )$ . Since this expression is an integral, its values on $F _ { 0 }$ and on a curve $F$ consisting of the points at time $t$ should be equal. If $F _ { 0 }$ and $F$ are both finite, we divide these curves into infinitely short arcs and extend (l07） over the corresponding arcs on each of the curves. Then the integral (lO7） extended over an arc of $\pmb { F }$ equals the integral (l07） extended over the corresponding arc of $F _ { 0 }$ Hence,(lO7) is an integral invariant.

Conversely,if (lO7） is an integral invariant of order one, then $F ( \xi _ { 1 } , \xi _ { 2 } , . . . , \xi _ { n } )$ is an integral of (106). Indeed, the integral (107), being of equal values for $F _ { 0 }$ and for $F$ , has the same value $F ( \xi _ { 1 } , \xi _ { 2 } , . . . , \xi _ { n } )$ when the ends of $F _ { 0 }$ are $x _ { i }$ and $x _ { i } + \xi _ { i }$ .But,as this is an integral invariant, it is a constant. Hence,it is an integral of (106).

Now we extend this notion to the integral invariants of order two.Take two particular solutions $\xi _ { i }$ and $\xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } ~ ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ of the variational equations (lO6).There exists a function $F ( x _ { i } , \xi _ { i } , \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } )$ that is independent of $t$ whatever the two particular solutions are; in other words, $\pmb { F }$ is an integral of the systems of differential equations

$$
\frac { d \xi _ { j } } { d t } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial X _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } \Big | _ { x = x ^ { ( 0 ) } } \xi _ { i } , \frac { d \xi _ { j } ^ { \prime } } { d t } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial X _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } \Big | _ { x = x ^ { ( 0 ) } } \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } ,
$$

for $\xi _ { i }$ and $\xi _ { i : } ^ { \prime }$ , respectively. In particular, if we take $F$ to be of the form

$$
\sum _ { i , j } A _ { i j } ( \xi _ { i } \xi _ { j } ^ { \prime } - \xi _ { j } \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } ) ,
$$

where $A _ { i j }$ are functions of $_ x$ only, then the double integral

$$
I = \int \int \sum A _ { i j } d x _ { i } d x _ { j }
$$

is an integral invariant with regard to (105).

To prove this theorem,construct for $F _ { 0 }$ an infinitely small parallelogram with its vertices at the points $x _ { i } , x _ { i } + \xi _ { i } , x _ { i } + \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } , x _ { i } + \xi _ { i } + \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ at time $t = 0$ For $\pmb { F }$ we construct another infinitely small parallelogram with its vertices at the same coordinates at time $t .$ Then $\boldsymbol { \mathit { I } }$ is equal to

$$
\sum _ { i , j } A _ { i j } ( \xi _ { i } \xi _ { j } ^ { \prime } - \xi _ { j } \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } )
$$

and,being an integral of (l08) by our assumption, should have equal values for $\boldsymbol { F }$ and $F _ { 0 }$ .If $F$ and $F _ { 0 }$ have finite areas,we divide them into infinitely small parts and proceed in the same way as in the above case of integral invariants of order one. The value of $\boldsymbol { \mathit { I } }$ extended over any one of the parts of $F _ { 0 }$ equals the value of $\boldsymbol { \mathit { I } }$ extended over the corresponding part of $F .$ .Thus, its values for $F _ { 0 }$ and $F$ are equal. Hence, $\boldsymbol { \mathit { I } }$ is an integral invariant. The converse of this theorem can be proved in the same way as in the foregoing case.

Integral invariants of order three can be obtained from three particular solutions $\xi _ { i } , \ \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } , \ \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime }$ ， respectively，of the three systems of variational equations

$$
\frac { d \xi _ { j } } { d t } = \left. \sum _ { i } \frac { \partial X _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } \right| _ { x = x ^ { ( 0 ) } } \xi _ { i } , \frac { d \xi _ { j } ^ { \prime } } { d t } = \left. \sum _ { i } \frac { \partial X _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } \right| _ { x = x ^ { ( 0 ) } } \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } ,
$$

$$
\frac { d \xi _ { j } ^ { \prime \prime } } { d t } = \sum _ { i } \frac { \partial X _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } \bigg | _ { x = x ^ { ( 0 ) } } \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime } .
$$

Since these systems admit the integral

$$
\sum _ { i , j , k } A _ { i j k } { \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { \xi _ { i } } & { \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } } & { \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime } } \\ { \xi _ { j } } & { \xi _ { j } ^ { \prime } } & { \xi _ { j } ^ { \prime \prime } } \\ { \xi _ { k } } & { \xi _ { k } ^ { \prime } } & { \xi _ { k } ^ { \prime \prime } } \end{array} \right| } ,
$$

it follows that the integral

$$
\iiint \sum _ { i , j , k } A _ { i j k } d x _ { i } d x _ { j } d x _ { k } ,
$$

where $\boldsymbol { A } _ { i j k }$ are functions of $_ x$ only, is an integral invariant of (105).

In general, let there be $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ $\left( { \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } < { \pmb { \mathscr { n } } } \right)$ integrals of $( 1 0 6 ) \colon \Phi _ { 1 } , \Phi _ { 2 } , . . . , \Phi _ { p } \mathrm { . }$ suppose that they are functions of $_ x$ only. Denote $q$ first-order integral invariants with regard to (l05) by $\int F _ { 1 } ( d x _ { i } ) , \ldots , \int F _ { q } ( d x _ { i } )$ Then $F _ { 1 } ( \xi _ { i } ) , . . . , F _ { q } ( \xi _ { i } )$ are integrals of (l06)， linear homogeneous with respect to $\xi _ { i }$ .Takea function

$$
\Theta ( \Phi _ { 1 } , . . . , \Phi _ { p } , F _ { 1 } , . . . , F _ { q } ) \equiv \Theta [ \Phi _ { k } , F _ { l } ] ,
$$

that is linear homogeneous with respect to $F$ ; then $\Theta [ \Phi _ { k } , F _ { l } ( \xi _ { i } ) ]$ is a new integral of (l06), linear homogeneous with respect to $\xi _ { i }$ Hence, $\int \Theta [ \Phi _ { k } , F _ { l } ( d x _ { i } ) ]$ is a first-order integral invariant with regard to (105).

Similarly, let

$$
\iint F _ { 1 } ( d x _ { i } d x _ { j } ) , . . . , \iint F _ { q } ( d x _ { i } d x _ { j } )
$$

be $q$ second-order integral invariants and $\Theta [ \Phi _ { k } , F _ { l } ]$ be a function of $\Phi$ and $F .$ ， and linear homogeneous with respect to $F _ { \cdot }$ Then

$$
\Theta [ \Phi _ { k } , F _ { l } ( \xi _ { i } \xi _ { j } ^ { \prime } - \xi _ { j } \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } ) ]
$$

is an integral of (106) and

$$
\int \int \Theta [ \Phi _ { k } , F _ { l } ( d x _ { i } d x _ { j } ) ]
$$

is a second-order integral invariant with regard to (105).

More generally, let $\int F _ { 1 }$ and $\int F _ { 2 }$ be integral invariants of order $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ and $q$ ， respectively， and iet $\xi _ { i } ^ { ( 1 ) } , \ldots , \xi _ { i } ^ { ( p + q ) }$ be ${ p + q }$ integrals of (106). Denote by $F _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ the result of substituting for the determinant formed of $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { p } }$ differentials in $F _ { 1 }$ the determinant formed of $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ solutions $\xi _ { i } ^ { ( 1 ) } , \ldots , \xi _ { i } ^ { ( p ) }$ Similarly, denote by $F _ { 2 } ^ { \prime }$ the result of substituting, for $q$ differentials in $F _ { 2 }$ ， $q$ solutions $\xi _ { i } ^ { ( p + 1 ) } , \ldots , \xi _ { i } ^ { ( p + q ) }$ .Then $F _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \cdot F _ { 2 } ^ { \prime }$ is an integral of (106).We make arbitrary permutations of the ${ \pmb q }$ solutions $\xi _ { i } ^ { ( j ) }$ . If we assign a plus or minus sign according to the odd or even number of the permutations and add the result, then the sum is also an integral of (lO6). There are $( \pmb { \mathscr { p } } + \pmb { q } ) ! / ( \pmb { \mathscr { p } } ! \pmb { q } ! )$ such independent permutations. This is a determinant of order $\phi + q$ formed of $\textit { \textbf { p } } + \textit { q }$ solutions $\xi _ { i } ^ { ( 1 ) } , \ldots , \xi _ { i } ^ { ( p + q ) }$ ,and its integral is linear homogeneous. The result is an integral invariant of order ${ p + q }$ ，with regard to (lO5).This is how Poincaré constructed integral invariants of various orders.

Consider, instead of (105), the system of canonical equations

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial y _ { i } } , \frac { d y _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { i } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

The variational equations are now

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \frac { d \xi _ { i } } { d t } = \sum _ { j } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } F } { \partial y _ { i } \partial x _ { j } } \xi _ { j } + \sum _ { j } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } F } { \partial y _ { i } \partial y _ { j } } \eta _ { j } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d \eta _ { i } } { d t } = - \sum _ { j } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } F } { \partial x _ { i } \partial x _ { j } } \xi _ { j } - \sum _ { j } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } F } { \partial x _ { i } \partial y _ { j } } \eta _ { j } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Denote another solution of (110) by $\xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } , \eta _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ ; then,

$$
\sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { n } \frac { d } { d t } \left( \eta _ { i } ^ { \prime } \xi _ { i } - \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } \eta _ { i } \right) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \left( \eta _ { i } ^ { \prime } \frac { d \xi _ { i } } { d t } - \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } \frac { d \eta _ { i } } { d t } - \eta _ { i } \frac { d \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { d t } + \xi _ { i } \frac { d \eta _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { d t } \right) = 0 .
$$

Hence, we have an integral of (llO) in the form

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { \bigl ( } \xi _ { i } \eta _ { i } ^ { \prime } - \eta _ { i } \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } { \bigr ) } = { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t . } }
$$

If $\xi _ { i }$ and $\eta _ { i }$ are integrals of (llO), then so are $d \xi _ { i } / d t$ and $d \eta _ { i } / d t$ The integral invariant obtained in this way,

$$
I _ { 1 } = \int { ( d x _ { 1 } d y _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + d x _ { n } d y _ { n } ) } ,
$$

is of order two and has a very important application.

The integral formed of four sets of particular solutions

$$
\sum _ { i , j } { \left| \begin{array} { l l l l } { \xi _ { i } } & { \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } } & { \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime } } & { \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime } } \\ { \eta _ { i } } & { \eta _ { i } ^ { \prime } } & { \eta _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime } } & { \eta _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime } } \\ { \xi _ { j } } & { \xi _ { j } ^ { \prime } } & { \xi _ { j } ^ { \prime \prime } } & { \xi _ { j } ^ { \prime \prime } } \\ { \eta _ { j } } & { \eta _ { j } ^ { \prime } } & { \eta _ { j } ^ { \prime \prime } } & { \eta _ { j } ^ { \prime \prime } } \end{array} \right| } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t }
$$

is also an integral of (1lO)； the corresponding integral invariant with regard to (109),

$$
I _ { 2 } = \int \sum _ { i , j } d x _ { i } d y _ { i } d x _ { j } d y _ { j } ,
$$

is of order four, where the sum is extended over the $n ( n - 1 ) / 2$ combinations of $_ i$ and $j$ Similarly,

$$
I _ { 3 } = \int \sum _ { i , j , k } d x _ { i } d y _ { i } d x _ { j } d y _ { j } d x _ { k } d y _ { k }
$$

extended over the $n ( n - 1 ) ( n - 2 ) / 6$ combinations of $i , j , k$ is a sixthorder integral invariant.In addition,the equations (lO6） admit the integral expressed by the sum of determinants

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \sum _ { \alpha _ { 1 } , \cdots , \alpha _ { p } } \| \xi _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } \eta _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } \xi _ { \alpha _ { 2 } } \eta _ { \alpha _ { 2 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot \xi _ { \alpha _ { p } } \eta _ { \alpha _ { p } } \| = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } } \\ { \displaystyle ( \alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { p } = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) . } \end{array}
$$

Hence,we can construct $\pmb { n }$ similar integral invariants $I _ { 1 } , I _ { 2 } , . . . , I _ { n }$ of order twice the index; they are not necessarily independent. This is how Poincaré constructed integral invariants of higher orders.

Let an integral of (105) be $\Phi ( x _ { i } , y _ { i } , t ) = $ constant. Then the equation

$$
\sum _ { i } { \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial x _ { i } } } \xi _ { i } + \sum _ { i } { \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial y _ { i } } } \eta _ { i } = { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } }
$$

is an integral of (105). Since, identically,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { i } \frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial x _ { i } } \right) \xi _ { i } + \sum _ { i } \frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial y _ { i } } \right) \eta _ { i } + \sum _ { i } \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial x _ { i } } \left( \sum _ { j } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } F } { \partial y _ { i } \partial x _ { j } } \xi _ { j } + \sum _ { j } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } F } { \partial y _ { i } \partial y _ { j } } \right. } \\ { \left. - \sum _ { i } \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial y _ { i } } \left( \sum _ { j } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } F } { \partial x _ { i } \partial x _ { j } } \xi _ { j } + \sum _ { j } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } F } { \partial x _ { i } \partial y _ { j } } \eta _ { j } \right) : \right. } \end{array}
$$

therefore,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial x _ { i } } \right) = - \sum _ { j } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } F } { \partial y _ { i } \partial x _ { j } } \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial x _ { j } } + \sum _ { j } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } F } { \partial x _ { i } \partial x _ { j } } \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial y _ { j } } , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d } { d t } \left( \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial y _ { i } } \right) = - \sum _ { j } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } F } { \partial y _ { i } \partial y _ { j } } \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial x _ { j } } + \sum _ { j } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } F } { \partial x _ { i } \partial y _ { j } } \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial y _ { j } } . } \end{array}
$$

Hence, $\xi _ { i } = \partial \Phi / \partial y _ { i }$ ， $\eta _ { i } = - \partial \Phi / \partial x _ { i }$ is a solution of (106). Furthermore, if $\Phi =$ constant, $\Phi _ { 1 } =$ constant are both solutions of (105), then, by substituting the expressions for $\xi _ { i } , \eta _ { i }$ just obtained in

$$
\sum _ { i } { \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial x _ { i } } } \xi _ { i } + \sum _ { i } { \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial y _ { i } } } \eta _ { i } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ,
$$

we obtain

$$
( \Phi , \Phi _ { 1 } ) \equiv \sum _ { i } \left( \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial x _ { i } } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 1 } } { \partial y _ { i } } - \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial y _ { i } } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 1 } } { \partial x _ { i } } \right) = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } .
$$

This is Poisson's theorem. Since $\xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } = \partial \Phi _ { 1 } / \partial y _ { i } , \eta _ { i } ^ { \prime } = - \partial \Phi _ { 1 } / \partial x _ { i }$ constitute a particular solution of (l06), there follows the integral

$$
\sum _ { i } \left( \xi _ { i } \eta _ { i } ^ { \prime } - \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } \eta _ { i } \right) = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } .
$$

This is the formula (ll1） which we have just obtained. The integral invariant $I _ { 1 }$ of (112) is merely another form of Poisson's theorem.

Let $\Phi$ $, \Phi _ { 1 } , \Phi _ { 2 } , \Phi _ { 3 }$ be four integrals of (l05).Let their Jacobian with respect to $x _ { i } , ~ x _ { j } , ~ y _ { i } , ~ y _ { j }$ be $\Delta _ { i j }$ The sum $\textstyle \sum _ { i , j } \Delta _ { i j }$ of $\Delta _ { i j }$ with all possible combinations of $i , j$ is also an integral of (lO6),that is,the integral invariant $I _ { 2 }$ .By a similar correspondence we can generalize Poisson's theorem to $I _ { 2 } , I _ { 3 } , . . . , I _ { n }$

4.2IINTEGRAL INVARIANTS OF THE $\pmb { n }$ -BODY PROBLEM

The energy integral of the differential equations for the $\boldsymbol { n }$ -body problem

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial y _ { i } } , \frac { d y _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { i } } ~ ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n )
$$

is

$$
H = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } { \frac { 1 } { 2 m _ { i } } } y _ { i } ^ { 2 } - U = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } .
$$

Let a solution of (113) be

$$
x _ { i } = \phi _ { i } ( t ) , \qquad y _ { i } = m _ { i } \phi _ { i } ^ { \prime } ( t ) .
$$

Then another solution is written with an arbitrary constant $c$ in the form

$$
x _ { i } = \phi _ { i } ( t + c ) , ~ y _ { i } = m _ { i } \phi _ { i } ^ { \prime } ( t + c ) .
$$

A particular solution of the variational equations is

$$
\xi _ { i } = c \phi _ { i } ^ { \prime } ( t ) = c \frac { y _ { i } } { m _ { i } } , \qquad \eta _ { i } = c m _ { i } \phi _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime } ( t ) = c \frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { i } } .
$$

Hence

$$
\xi _ { i } = \frac { y _ { i } } { m _ { i } } , \qquad \eta _ { i } = \frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { i } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n ) ,
$$

constitute a particular solution of the variational equations, and the variational equations admit an integral

$$
\sum _ { i } { \frac { y _ { i } \eta _ { i } } { m _ { i } } } - \sum _ { i } { \frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { i } } } \xi _ { i } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ,
$$

corresponding to (114). The corresponding integral invariant is

$$
\int \omega _ { 0 } = \int \sum _ { i } \left( { \frac { 1 } { m _ { i } } } y _ { i } d y _ { i } - { \frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { i } } } d x _ { i } \right) .
$$

Similarly， the particular solutions of the variational equations corresponding to the first integrals of the center of mass,i.e.,

$$
H _ { 1 } = - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 3 j - 2 } , \qquad H _ { 2 } = - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 3 j - 1 } , \qquad H _ { 3 } = - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 3 j } ,
$$

are

$$
- \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \eta _ { 3 j - 2 } , \qquad - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \eta _ { 3 j - 1 } , \qquad - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \eta _ { 3 j } .
$$

The corresponding integral invariants are

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { { \displaystyle \int \omega _ { 1 } = - \int \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } d y _ { 3 j - 2 } , \quad \quad \displaystyle \int \omega _ { 2 } = - \int \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } d y _ { 3 j - 1 } , } } } \\ { { { \displaystyle \int \omega _ { 3 } = - \int \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } d y _ { 3 j } } . } } \end{array}
$$

Corresponding to the integral of angular momentum

$$
H _ { 4 } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } { \left( { x _ { 3 j } y _ { 3 j - 1 } } - { x _ { 3 j - 1 } y _ { 3 j } } \right) } ,
$$

the particular solution of the variational equations is

$$
\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } ( x _ { 3 j } \eta _ { 3 j - 1 } - y _ { 3 j } \xi _ { 3 j - 1 } - x _ { 3 j - 1 } \eta _ { 3 j } + y _ { 3 j - 1 } \xi _ { 3 j } ) ,
$$

and the corresponding integral invariant is

$$
\int \omega _ { 4 } = \int \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } ( x _ { 3 j } d y _ { 3 j - 1 } - y _ { 3 j } d x _ { 3 j - 1 } - x _ { 3 j - 1 } d y _ { 3 j } + y _ { 3 j - 1 } d x _ { 3 j } ) .
$$

Similarly

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \int \omega _ { 5 } = \int \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \big ( x _ { 3 j - 2 } d y _ { 3 j } - y _ { 3 j - 2 } d x _ { 3 j } - x _ { 3 j } d y _ { 3 j - 2 } + y _ { 3 j } d x _ { 3 j - 2 } \big ) } , }  \\ { { \displaystyle \int \omega _ { 6 } = \int \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \big ( x _ { 3 j - 1 } d y _ { 3 j - 2 } - y _ { 3 j - 1 } d x _ { 3 j - 2 } - x _ { 3 j - 2 } d y _ { 3 j - 1 } + y _ { 3 j - 2 } d x _ { 3 j - 1 } \big ) } . }  \end{array}
$$

We also obtain from (115), putting $i \equiv \alpha$ (mod 3), another particular solution

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { ( t ) \ + \ c _ { \alpha } + k _ { \alpha } t , \qquad y _ { i } = m _ { i } \phi _ { i } ^ { \prime } ( t ) \ + m _ { i } k _ { \alpha } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n ) , } \\ & { \qquad c _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } = c ^ { 2 } , \qquad k _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + k _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + k _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } = k ^ { 2 } . } \end{array}
$$

Supposing the constants $\pmb { c }$ and $k$ to be infinitely small,we obtain the solutions of the variational equations

$$
\xi _ { \alpha } = 1 , \qquad \eta _ { \alpha } = 0 ,
$$

and

$$
\xi _ { \alpha } = t , \qquad \eta _ { \alpha } = m _ { \alpha } \qquad ( \alpha = 3 j - 2 , 3 j - 1 , 3 j ) .
$$

Corresponding to the integrals

$$
\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \eta _ { 3 j - 2 } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } , \qquad \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \eta _ { 3 j - 1 } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } , \qquad \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \eta _ { 3 j } = \mathrm { c o n s }
$$

$$
\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \eta _ { 3 j - 2 } t - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 j - 2 } \xi _ { 3 j - 2 } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ,
$$

$$
\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \eta _ { 3 j - 1 } t - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 j - 1 } \xi _ { 3 j - 1 } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ,
$$

$$
\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } \eta _ { 3 j } t - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 j } \xi _ { 3 j } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ,
$$

of the variational equations, we obtain the respective integral invariants

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \int \omega _ { 7 } = \int \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 j - 2 } \big ( \delta x _ { 3 j - 2 } - t \delta y _ { 3 j - 2 } - y _ { 3 j - 2 } \big ) \big ( \delta t \big ) } , }  \\ { { \displaystyle \int \omega _ { 8 } = \int \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 j - 1 } \big ( \delta x _ { 3 j - 1 } - t \delta y _ { 3 j - 1 } - y _ { 3 j - 1 } \delta t \big ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \int \omega _ { 9 } = \int \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 j } \big ( \delta x _ { 3 j } - t \delta y _ { 3 j } - y _ { 3 j } \delta t \big ) . } } \end{array}
$$

A very interesting integral invariant is obtained when $U$ is homo-geneous, of degree $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ : the Newtonian law of gravitation corresponds to $\begin{array} { r } { \mathbf { \nabla } \phi = - 1 } \end{array}$ . Equations (ll3） remain unaltered when we multiply $\scriptstyle t$ by $\lambda ^ { 2 - p }$ ， $x _ { i }$ by $\lambda ^ { 2 } , y _ { i }$ by $\lambda ^ { p }$ ,where $\lambda$ is a constant. If we take $\lambda$ to be equal to $1 + \epsilon$ (very close to l),then we have a particular solution of the variational equations

$$
\xi _ { i } = 2 \phi _ { i } - ( 2 - \hbar ) t \phi _ { i } ^ { \prime } , \eta _ { i } = \hbar p m _ { i } \phi _ { i } ^ { \prime } - ( 2 - \hbar ) m _ { i } t \phi _ { i } ^ { \prime } ,
$$

or

$$
\xi _ { i } = 2 x _ { i } - ( 2 - \phi ) t \frac { y _ { i } } { m _ { i } } , \qquad \eta _ { i } = \phi y _ { i } - ( 2 - \phi ) t \frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { i } } .
$$

From these we derive the particular solution

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } \left( 2 x _ { i } \eta _ { i } - \rlap / p y _ { i } \xi _ { i } \right) = ( 2 - \rlap / p ) t \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } \left( \frac { y _ { i } \eta _ { i } } { m _ { i } } - \frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { i } } \xi _ { i } \right) } } \\ { { = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } } } \end{array}
$$

of the variational equations.This solution cannot be obtained by differentiating any known integral of (l13). The corresponding integral invariant is

$$
\int { \omega _ { 1 0 } } = \int { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } { { { \left( { 2 { x _ { i } } d { y _ { i } } - \beta { y _ { i } } d { x _ { i } } } \right) } } + { \left( { \dot { p } } - 2 \right) } t } } \int { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } { \left( { \frac { { { y _ { i } } d { y _ { i } } } } { { { m _ { i } } } } - \frac { { \partial U } } { { \partial { x _ { i } } } } d { x _ { i } } } \right) } } .
$$

As this expression contains $t$ explicitly,this integral invariant is different in character from those already found. The second integral is writen

$$
\int d \left[ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } \left( \frac { y _ { i } ^ { 2 } } { 2 m _ { i } } - U \right) \right] ;
$$

there is a perfect differential under the integral sign. The sum is equal to the energy constant,by (114). $\int \omega _ { 1 0 }$ is linear and is an integral extended over an arbitrary arc of a curve.Let the values of the energy integral at the two ends of the arc be $C _ { 0 }$ and $C _ { 1 }$ ，respectively. This arc is what we have called $F _ { 0 }$ in the foregoing discussion； since it is deformed continuously to $F _ { : }$ ， the values of $C _ { 0 }$ and $C _ { 1 }$ do not alter.Hence

$$
\omega _ { 1 0 } = \int \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } { ( 2 x _ { i } d y _ { i } - p y _ { i } d x _ { i } ) - ( p - 2 ) t ( C _ { 1 } - C _ { 0 } ) } .
$$

The first term on the right-hand side varies as $F$ is deformed, but the variation is proportional to $t .$ If $C _ { 0 } = C _ { 1 }$ at both ends of this curve, then $\int \omega _ { 1 0 }$ is a constant. If this curve is closed, then the same situation occurs. Hence, this is a relative integral invariant.But in the case of a closed curve the integral remains unaltered when we put a perfect differential under the integral sign. Adding $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n }$ $( x _ { i } d y _ { i } + y _ { i } d x _ { i } )$ after multiplying by a constant factor, we see that $\int \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } y _ { i } d x _ { i }$ and $\int \sum _ { i } ^ { 3 n } { x _ { i } } d y _ { i }$ are both relative integral invariants. We have shown how we can get an absolute integral invariant of order two from a relative integral invariant of order one.The former invariant in the present case is

$$
I _ { 1 } = \int \int \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } d x _ { i } d y _ { i } ,
$$

which we have already derived. The differential $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } \left( 2 x _ { i } d y _ { i } - \phi y _ { i } d x _ { i } \right) } \end{array}$ isa perfect differential when $\hbar = - 2$ ,that is,when the attractive force is inversely proportional to the cube of the mutual distance (instead of the square as in Newtonian law),and the invariant takes the form

$$
\int \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } ( 2 x _ { i } d y _ { i } - p y _ { i } d x _ { i } ) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } 2 x _ { i } y _ { i } .
$$

Hence, $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } 2 x _ { i } y _ { i }$ is linear with respect to $t .$ ,But this is equal to

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } m _ { i } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } ,
$$

and

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } m _ { i } x _ { i } ^ { 2 }
$$

is quadratic with respect to $t _ { \ast }$ , as was found by Jacobi. In general,

$$
\sum _ { i \mathop { = } 1 } ^ { 3 n } ( 2 x _ { i } d y _ { i } - / { y } y _ { i } d x _ { i } )
$$

is not a perfect differential; it equals

$$
\int \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } ( 2 x _ { i } d y _ { i } + y _ { i } d x _ { i } ) - 3 t ( C _ { 1 } - C _ { 0 } )
$$

in the Newtonian case, $\begin{array} { r } { p = - 1 } \end{array}$ . Even if we form Poisson's brackets using this new integral and the clasical ten integrals, there occurs no additional new integral,as can be seen from a simple computation. Indeed, this integral is not in involution with any known integral except the energy integral $H .$ ，Hence, the order of the differential equations cannot be reduced by such an integral.

# 4.22CARTAN'S EXTERIOR MULTIPLICATION

Cartan (1922,1945) used the notation

$$
\left| \begin{array} { l l } { u _ { i } } & { u _ { j } } \\ { v _ { i } } & { v _ { j } } \end{array} \right| = [ u _ { i } \quad u _ { j } ] , \qquad \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { u _ { 1 } } & { u _ { 2 } } & { u _ { 3 } } \\ { v _ { 1 } } & { v _ { 2 } } & { v _ { 3 } } \\ { w _ { 1 } } & { w _ { 2 } } & { w _ { 3 } } \end{array} \right| = [ u _ { 1 } \quad u _ { 2 } \quad u _ { 3 } ] , \ldots ,
$$

and called these (following Grassmann and Plucker) the exterior multiplication forms.With the linear forms

$$
f ( u ) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { a _ { i } u _ { i } } , \qquad f ^ { \prime } ( u ) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { a _ { i } ^ { \prime } u _ { i } } ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\left[ f ( u ) , f ^ { \prime } ( u ) \right] = \left| \begin{array} { l l } { f ( u ) } & { f ^ { \prime } ( u ) } \\ { f ( v ) } & { f ^ { \prime } ( v ) } \end{array} \right| = \sum _ { i , j } a _ { i } a _ { j } ^ { \prime } \left| \begin{array} { l l } { u _ { i } } & { u _ { j } } \\ { v _ { i } } & { v _ { j } } \end{array} \right| = \sum _ { i , j } a _ { i } a _ { j } ^ { \prime } [ u _ { i } u _ { j } ] ,
$$

if we regard this quantity as a product of two linear forms where the order of the factors in the partial products has not been altered,and such that the product of two identical variables is zero and the product of two different variables changes sign when the order of the factors is reversed. Thus, to any alternating bilinear form corresponds a form of the second degree with a single series of variables but with exterior multiplication; conversely, to any quadratic form with exterior multiplication there corresponds an alternating bilinear form.

For the case of three variables,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \left[ u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } u _ { 3 } \right] = - [ u _ { 2 } u _ { 1 } u _ { 3 } ] = - [ u _ { 1 } u _ { 3 } u _ { 2 } ] = - [ u _ { 3 } u _ { 2 } u _ { 1 } ] } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } = [ u _ { 2 } u _ { 3 } u _ { 1 } ] = [ u _ { 3 } u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } ] . } } \end{array}
$$

In general, an exterior multiplication form of order $\pmb { n }$ is writen

$$
A [ u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot u _ { n } ] .
$$

An exterior multiplication form $[ F \Phi \Psi ]$ can be defined from exterior multiplication forms $F , \Phi , \Psi$ of arbitrary orders, the degree of the product being the sum of the degrees of the factors. The product is zero if the sum of the degrees exceeds $_ n$ .For example, from

$$
F = [ u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } ] + [ u _ { 3 } u _ { 4 } ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot + [ u _ { 2 s - 1 } u _ { 2 s } ]
$$

we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 ! } [ F ^ { 2 } ] = [ u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } u _ { 3 } u _ { 4 } ] + [ u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } u _ { 5 } u _ { 6 } ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot + [ u _ { 2 s - 3 } u _ { 2 s - 2 } u _ { 2 s - 1 } u _ { 2 s } ] } , }  \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 3 ! } [ F ^ { 3 } ] = [ u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } u _ { 3 } u _ { 4 } u _ { 5 } u _ { 6 } ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \frac { 1 } { s ! } [ F ^ { s } ] = [ u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } u _ { 3 } \cdot \cdot \cdot u _ { 2 s - 1 } u _ { 2 s } ] , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \frac { 1 } { + 1 ) ! } [ F ^ { s + 1 } ] = 0 . } } \end{array}
$$

From $\omega _ { \delta } = \textstyle \sum _ { i } a _ { i } \delta x _ { i }$ we derive

$$
{ \omega } _ { \delta } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { i } [ \delta a _ { i } \ \delta x _ { i } ] = \sum _ { i , j } \Big ( \frac { \partial a _ { j } } { \partial x _ { i } } - \frac { \partial a _ { i } } { \partial x _ { j } } \Big ) [ \delta x _ { i } \ \delta x _ { j } ] .
$$

This is called the exterior derivative of the Pfaffian $\omega _ { \delta }$ . From Stokes's formula in the present notation,

$$
\int _ { { \cal C } } \omega _ { \delta } = \int \int _ { s } \omega _ { \delta } ^ { \prime } ,
$$

the necessary and sufficient condition for $\omega ^ { \prime } = 0$ is that $\omega$ should be a perfect differential.

To a bilinear form

$$
\Omega = \sum _ { i , j } a _ { i j } [ \delta x _ { i } \delta x _ { j } ]
$$

corresponds a bilinear alternating form

$$
\sum _ { i , j } a _ { i j } ( \delta x _ { i } \delta x _ { j } ^ { \prime } - \delta x _ { j } \delta x _ { i } ^ { \prime } ) .
$$

Corresponding to a trilinear form

$$
\begin{array} { r }  { { \bf \Phi } ^ { \prime } ( \delta \mathrm {  ~ \delta } \delta ^ { \prime } \mathrm {  ~ \delta } \delta ^ { \prime \prime } ) = \displaystyle \sum _ { i , j } [ \delta a _ { i j } ( \delta ^ { \prime } x _ { i } \mathrm {  ~ \delta } \delta ^ { \prime \prime } x _ { j } - \delta ^ { \prime } x _ { j } \mathrm {  ~ \delta } \delta ^ { \prime \prime } x _ { i } ) - \delta ^ { \prime } a _ { i j } ( \delta x _ { i } \mathrm {  ~ \delta } \delta ^ { \prime \prime } x _ { j } - \delta x _ { j } \mathrm {  ~ \delta } \delta ^ { \prime \prime } x } \\ { + \ \delta ^ { \prime \prime } a _ { i j } ( \delta x _ { i } \mathrm {  ~ \delta } \delta ^ { \prime } x _ { j } - \delta x _ { j } \mathrm {  ~ \delta } \delta ^ { \prime } x _ { i } ) ] , \qquad } \end{array}
$$

there is the trilinear alternating exterior derived form:

$$
\Omega _ { \delta } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { i , j } [ \delta a _ { i j } \ \delta x _ { i } \ \delta x _ { j } ] = \sum _ { i , j , k } \left( { \frac { \partial a _ { i j } } { \partial x _ { k } } } + { \frac { \partial a _ { j k } } { \partial x _ { i } } } + { \frac { \partial a _ { k i } } { \partial x _ { j } } } \right) [ \delta x _ { i } \ \delta x _ { j } \ \delta x _ { k } ] .
$$

This is the exterior differential of $\Omega$ . If an exterior multiplication form of any degree is exteriorly derived, then the degree increases by one.A term $A [ \delta x _ { i } \ \delta x _ { j } \cdot \cdot \cdot \delta x _ { l } ]$ generates a term $[ \delta A \ \delta x _ { i } \ \delta x _ { j } \cdot \cdot \cdot \delta x _ { l } ]$ by exterior derivation.From a constant $_ m$ and an exterior multiplication form $\Omega$ ， we obtain $( m \Omega ) ^ { \prime } = [ d m \cdot \Omega ] + m \Omega ^ { \prime }$ . From two exterior multiplication forms $\Omega$ and $\Pi$ we obtain

$$
[ \Omega \Pi ] ^ { \prime } = [ \Omega ^ { \prime } \Pi ] + [ \Omega \Pi ^ { \prime } ]
$$

if the degree of $\Omega$ is even, and

$$
[ \Omega \Pi ] ^ { \prime } = [ \Omega ^ { \prime } \Pi ] - [ \Omega \Pi ^ { \prime } ]
$$

if the degree is odd. In particular,

$$
[ \Omega ^ { p } ] ^ { \prime } = \pmb { \mathscr { p } } [ \Omega ^ { p - 1 } \Omega ^ { \prime } ]
$$

if the degree of $\Omega$ is even.

The derivative of the exterior derivative $\Omega ^ { \prime }$ of an exterior multiplication form is identically zero.Cartan (l945） called this Poincaré's theorem. The term of $\Omega ^ { \prime }$ corresponding to an arbitrary term $a [ \delta x _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \delta x _ { p } ]$ of $\Omega$ is $[ \delta a \ \delta x _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \delta x _ { p } ]$ . If $^ { a }$ depends only on $x _ { 1 } , \dotsc , x _ { p }$ ，then the last term and its derivatives are zero. If $^ { a }$ does not depend on $x _ { 1 } , \dotsc , x _ { p } ,$ then - by a change of variables so that $^ { a }$ becomes equal to ${ \pmb x } _ { p + 1 }$ - the exterior derivative of a term $[ \delta x _ { p + 1 } \delta x _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \delta x _ { p } ]$ is zero,because,the coefficient of this term being unity, the derivative contributes nothing toward the formation of the exterior derivative.

Conversely, if the derivative of a differential form $\Omega$ is zero, then the form $\Omega$ can be regarded as the derivative of a form $\Pi$ whose degree is one less than the degree of $\Omega$ 、To prove this theorem we refer to the lemma:if the derivative of a form $\Omega$ is zero and this form does not contain the differential $\phantom { } \delta x _ { n }$ ， then the coefficients are all independent of $x _ { n }$ . In fact a term such as $A [ \delta x _ { 1 } \delta x _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \delta x _ { p } ]$ generates in the derivation a term $[ \delta A \ \delta x _ { 1 } \ \delta x _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \delta x _ { p } ]$ ,or,by expansion, several terms one of which is $( \partial A / \partial x _ { n } ) [ \delta x _ { n } \ \delta x _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \delta x _ { p } ]$ . This last term cannot cancel by combining with another term, since no term of $\Omega$ contains $\ S x _ { n }$ .Hence $\partial A / \partial x _ { n } = 0 ,$ because $\Omega ^ { \prime } = 0$

To prove the theorem, let $\Omega _ { 0 }$ be the result of substituting $x _ { n } = x _ { n } ^ { 0 }$ $\delta x _ { n } = 0$ in $\Omega$ Evidently $\Omega _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } = 0$ if $\Omega ^ { \prime } = 0$ .Suppose that the theorem is true for $n - 1$ variables and that it is possible to find a form $\Pi _ { 0 }$ con-structed with the variables $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n - 1 }$ such that $\Pi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } = \Omega _ { 0 }$ .Divide $\Omega$ and $\Pi$ into the parts containing $\phantom { } \delta x _ { n }$ and the parts independent of $\phantom { } \delta x _ { n }$ ， so that

$$
\Omega = \Omega _ { 1 } + [ \delta x _ { n } \Omega _ { 2 } ] , ~ \Pi = \Pi _ { 1 } + [ \delta x _ { n } \Pi _ { 2 } ] .
$$

The term containing $\phantom { } \delta x _ { n }$ in $\Pi ^ { \prime }$ is

$$
\Pi ^ { \prime } = \left[ \delta x _ { n } { \frac { \partial \Pi _ { 1 } } { \partial x _ { n } } } \right] - [ \delta x _ { n } \Pi _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

Choose $\Pi _ { 2 }$ arbitrarily and determine $\Pi _ { 1 }$ by the condition that $( i ) \ \Pi _ { 1 }$ reduces to $\Pi _ { 0 }$ for $x _ { n } = x _ { n } ^ { 0 } .$ and $( i i ) \partial \Pi _ { 1 } / \partial x _ { n } = \Pi _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } + \Omega _ { 2 } ,$ so that $\Pi _ { 1 }$ can be evaluated by quadrature. The form $\Pi ^ { \prime } - \Omega$ does not contain $\ S x _ { n }$ after similar terms are cancelled and reduces to zero if we put $x _ { n } = x _ { n } ^ { 0 }$ in the coefficients.Because of the lemma, all coefficients are independent of $x _ { n }$ .Hence the difference $\Pi ^ { \prime } - \Omega$ should be identically zero.

This theorem shows that if the exterior derivative of a linear form $\Omega$ is zero then $\Omega$ is an exact differential.If $\Omega$ is a quadratic form with three variables

$$
\Omega = P [ \delta y \delta z ] + Q [ \delta z \ \delta x ] + R [ \delta x \ \delta y ] ,
$$

then the condition

$$
\frac { \partial P } { \partial x } + \frac { \partial Q } { \partial y } + \frac { \partial R } { \partial z } = 0
$$

is necessary and sufficient in order that $\Omega$ shall be regarded as the derivative of a linear form,that is,in order that we can find three functions $A , B , C$ satisfying

$$
{ \frac { \partial C } { \partial y } } - { \frac { \partial B } { \partial z } } = P , { \frac { \partial A } { \partial z } } - { \frac { \partial C } { \partial x } } = Q , { \frac { \partial B } { \partial x } } - { \frac { \partial A } { \partial y } } = R .
$$

By the rank of a quadratic form we mean the number of quadratic terms necessary to express the quadratic form as their sum.To find the rank of a general quadratic form we need only see how many independent equations the actual equations would reduce to, if we equated all the partial derivatives of the quadratic form to zero. Similarly the rank is defined for an exterior multiplication form $\begin{array} { r } { F = \sum _ { i , j } a _ { i j } [ u _ { i } u _ { j } ] } \end{array}$ The equations obtained by equating all its partial derivatives to zero are called the associated system.Note that

$$
\frac { \partial [ u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } ] } { \partial u _ { 1 } } = u _ { 2 } , \quad \quad \frac { \partial [ u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } ] } { \partial u _ { 2 } } = - u _ { 1 } , \quad \quad \frac { \partial [ u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } ] } { \partial u _ { i } } = 0
$$

If $a _ { 1 2 } \neq 0$ in the coefficients of $F ( u )$ , then

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { a _ { 1 2 } } \left[ \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { 2 } } \right] = \frac { 1 } { a _ { 1 2 } } \left[ ( a _ { 1 2 } u _ { 2 } + a _ { 1 3 } u _ { 3 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + a _ { 1 n } u _ { n } ) \right. } } \\ { { \displaystyle \phantom { \frac { 1 } { a _ { 1 2 } } } \times \left. ( a _ { 2 1 } u _ { 1 } + a _ { 2 3 } u _ { 3 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + a _ { 2 n } u _ { n } ) \right] } } \end{array}
$$

has the same coefficients as $F$ in the terms combining $\pmb { u } _ { 1 }$ or ${ \boldsymbol { u } } _ { 2 }$ , so that

$$
F ( u ) \ - \frac { 1 } { a _ { 1 2 } } \left[ \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { 2 } } \right] \equiv F ^ { \prime } ( u )
$$

contains only $u _ { 3 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ If in addition $a _ { 3 4 } ^ { \prime } \neq 0$ ,then

$$
F ^ { \prime } ( u ) - \frac { 1 } { a _ { 3 4 } ^ { \prime } } \left[ \frac { \partial F ^ { \prime } } { \partial u _ { 3 } } \frac { \partial F ^ { \prime } } { \partial u _ { 4 } } \right] = F ^ { \prime \prime } ( u )
$$

contains only $u _ { 5 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ . Continuing this process, we obtain

$$
F ( u ) = \frac { 1 } { a _ { 1 2 } } \left[ \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { 2 } } \right] + \frac { 1 } { a _ { 3 4 } ^ { \prime } } \left[ \frac { \partial F ^ { \prime } } { \partial u _ { 3 } } \frac { \partial F ^ { \prime } } { \partial u _ { 4 } } \right] + \frac { 1 } { a _ { 5 6 } ^ { \prime \prime } } \left[ \frac { \partial F ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial u _ { 5 } } \frac { \partial F ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial u _ { 6 } } \right] + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

or

$$
F ( U ) = [ U _ { 1 } U _ { 2 } ] + [ U _ { 3 } U _ { 4 } ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot + [ U _ { 2 s - 1 } U _ { 2 s } ] ( 2 s \equiv n ) ,
$$

where

$$
\frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { 1 } } = U _ { 1 } , \qquad \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { 2 } } = a _ { 1 2 } U _ { 2 } , \qquad \frac { \partial F ^ { \prime } } { \partial u _ { 3 } } = U _ { 3 } , \qquad \frac { \partial F ^ { \prime } } { \partial u _ { 4 } } = a _ { 3 4 } ^ { \prime } U _ { 4 } ,
$$

$$
{ \frac { \partial F ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial u _ { 5 } } } = U _ { 5 } , ~ { \frac { \partial F ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial u _ { 6 } } } = a _ { 5 6 } ^ { \prime \prime } U _ { 6 } , . ~ . ~ . .
$$

This is called the canonical form of $F .$ 、We see that the rank of $F$ should always be even. The associated system is $U _ { 1 } = U _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = U _ { 2 s } = 0$

If a given exterior multiplication form $\Omega$ is invariant with regard to the system of differential equations

$$
{ \frac { d x _ { 1 } } { X _ { 1 } } } = { \frac { d x _ { 2 } } { X _ { 2 } } } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = { \frac { d x _ { n } } { X _ { n } } } ,
$$

then $\Omega$ can be expressed by $n - 1$ independent integrals $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { n - 1 }$ and their differentials,by the theorem of Section 4.17.The situation is the same for the exterior derivative $\Omega ^ { \prime }$ .The system of linear differential equations associated with two exterior multiplication forms $\Omega$ and $\Omega ^ { \prime }$ is a consequence of the equations

$$
d y _ { 1 } = 0 , d y _ { 2 } = 0 , . . . , d y _ { n - 1 } = 0 ,
$$

and hence a consequence of (l16). In other words, in order that (116) shall admit $\Omega ( \delta )$ as an invariant form, it is necessary that the system associated with $\Omega$ and $\Omega ^ { \prime }$ be satisfied when the variables $\delta x _ { 1 } , . . . , \delta x _ { n }$ are replaced by $X _ { 1 } , . . . , X _ { n }$ ，respectively. The system of equations expressed by perfect differentials in the form (1l7) is called the characteristic system with respect to (116).

Conversely,suppose that the above condition for perfect differentials is satisfied. Since the system associated with $\Omega ( d )$ is satisfied when (116) is taken into account, it is satisfied when the equivalent system (li7) is taken into account.Hence $\Omega ( d )$ , considered as an exterior multiplication form of $d { \pmb x } _ { i }$ ,can be expressed uniquely by $d y _ { 1 } , . . . , d y _ { n - 1 } .$ ，the coefficients being functions of $_ x$ that can be expressed in terms of $y _ { 1 } , \dotsc , y _ { n - 1 }$ and $x _ { n } ,$ if $X _ { n } \neq 0$ Thus

$$
\Omega = \textstyle \sum { A _ { i _ { 1 } } . . . _ { i _ { p } } [ d y _ { i _ { 1 } } . . . d y _ { i _ { p } } ] } .
$$

Only the term in $[ d x _ { n } d y _ { i _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot d y _ { i _ { p } } ]$ has the coefficient $\partial A _ { i _ { 1 } } . . . _ { i _ { p } } / \partial x _ { n }$ By hypothesis $\Omega ^ { \prime }$ should be expressed by means of the variables $d y _ { i }$ only. Hence

$$
\frac { \partial A _ { i _ { 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot i _ { p } } { \partial x _ { n } } = 0 ,
$$

that is, $\Omega$ is expressed by the first integrals of the given system and their differentials.This is an invariant form.Thus the equations of the characteristic system of $\Omega$ reduce to the equations of the associated system of $\Omega$ joined with the equations of the associated system of $\Omega ^ { \prime }$

In particular, if $\Omega$ is a perfect differential, that is,if $\Omega ^ { \prime } = 0$ ,then the characteristic equation of a differential form $\Omega$ coincides with the associated system of form $\Omega$

The characteristic equation of a relative integral invariant $\int \Omega$ coincides with the associated system of the derived form $\Omega ^ { \prime }$ ,because a relative invariant leads to an absolute invariant $\int \Omega ^ { \prime }$ ，where $\Omega ^ { \prime }$ isa perfect differential. From the linear integral invariant in dynamics

$$
\int \omega _ { \delta } = \int \sum _ { i } \beta _ { i } \delta q _ { i } - H \delta t ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\omega _ { \delta } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { i } \left[ \delta p _ { i } \delta q _ { i } \right] - [ \delta H \delta t ] .
$$

The associated system of form $\omega ^ { \prime }$ is

$$
\frac { \partial \omega ^ { \prime } } { \partial ( \delta q _ { i } ) } = 0 , \frac { \partial \omega ^ { \prime } } { \partial ( \delta p _ { i } ) } = 0 , \frac { \partial \omega ^ { \prime } } { \partial ( \delta t ) } = 0 ;
$$

that is, replacing δ by $d$

$$
- \ d p _ { i } \ - \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { i } } d t = 0 , \qquad d q _ { i } - \frac { \partial H } { \partial p _ { i } } d t = 0 , \qquad d H - \frac { \partial H } { \partial t } d t = 0 .
$$

This is the result already obtained in Chapter 1.

An electromagnetic field defined by the exterior form

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \Omega = H _ { x } [ \delta y \ \delta z ] + H _ { y } [ \delta z \ \delta x ] + H _ { z } [ \delta x \ \delta y ] + E _ { x } [ \delta x \ \delta t ] } \\ & { ~ + E _ { y } [ \delta y \ \delta t ] + E _ { z } [ \delta z \ \delta t ] } \end{array}
$$

satisfies the condition that $\Omega ^ { \prime }$ is zero. Thus,

$$
^ { ' } = \bigg ( { \frac { \partial H _ { x } } { \partial x } } + { \frac { \partial H _ { y } } { \partial y } } + { \frac { \partial H _ { z } } { \partial z } } \bigg ) [ \delta x \ \delta y \ \delta z ] + \bigg ( { \frac { \partial H _ { x } } { \partial t } } + { \frac { \partial E _ { z } } { \partial y } } - { \frac { \partial E _ { y } } { \partial z } } \bigg ) [ \delta y \ \delta z \ \delta t ]
$$

$$
+ \left( { \frac { \partial H _ { y } } { \partial t } } + { \frac { \partial E _ { x } } { \partial z } } - { \frac { \partial E _ { z } } { \partial x } } \right) [ \delta z \ \delta x \ \delta t ] + \left( { \frac { \partial H _ { z } } { \partial t } } + { \frac { \partial E _ { y } } { \partial x } } - { \frac { \partial E _ { x } } { \partial y } } \right) [ \delta x \ \delta y \ \delta t ] .
$$

Equating to zero the four coefficients of $\Omega ^ { \prime }$ ,we obtain the classical four equations of Maxwell

$$
\operatorname { d i v } H = 0 , \frac { \partial H } { \partial t } + \operatorname { c u r l } E = 0 ,
$$

If the exterior differential of a form $\pmb { \omega }$ of degree $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ is zero,then the integral $\int \omega$ extended over the boundary of an arbitrary domain of $( \pmb { \mathscr { p } } + 1 )$ dimensions is zero.In this case the form is said to be closed. Let $\theta _ { \alpha } \equiv A _ { \alpha _ { i } } d x _ { i } = 0 ( \alpha = 1 , 2 , . . . , r )$ be a differential system of linear equations where $\theta _ { \alpha }$ are supposed to be independent. The set of exterior forms

$$
\Phi = [ F _ { 1 } \phi _ { 1 } ] + [ F _ { 2 } \phi _ { 2 } ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot + [ F _ { h } \phi _ { h } ]
$$

-where $\phi _ { i }$ are homogeneous exterior forms under the condition that the sum of the degrees of two factors in any term $[ F _ { i } \phi _ { i } ]$ is the same for all terms on the right-hand side- is called the ring (Van der Waerden, 1955) determined by the $h$ homogeneous exterior forms $F _ { 1 } , F _ { 2 } , \ldots , F _ { h }$ of $\boldsymbol { n }$ variables. Any form $\Phi$ of the ring determined by $h$ given forms

$$
F _ { 1 } , F _ { 2 } , . . . , F _ { h }
$$

vanishes for any solution of the system $F _ { 1 } = 0 , F _ { 2 } = 0 , . . . , F _ { h } = 0 $ But in order that an exterior form $\Phi$ may vanish for all solutions of the system of linear independent equations $F _ { 1 } = 0 , F _ { 2 } = 0 , . . . , F _ { h } = 0$ itis necessary and sufficient that $\Phi$ belongs to the ring determined by the forms $F _ { i }$ The necessary and sufficient condition for $\Phi$ to belong to the ring is that the form $[ F _ { 1 } F _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot F _ { h } \Phi ]$ be zero.

In order that a system of $r$ linear differential equations shall be completely integrable,it is necessary and sufficient that in the neighborhood of any point in space the exact differential $d \theta _ { \alpha }$ belong to the ring determined by the forms $\theta _ { \alpha }$ .A differential system is said to be closed if the exterior differentials $d \theta _ { \alpha }$ belong to the ring determined by $\theta _ { \alpha }$ The necessary and sufficient condition for the complete integrability of the system

$$
\theta _ { \alpha } \equiv A _ { \alpha _ { i } } d x _ { i } = 0 \qquad ( \alpha = 1 , 2 , . . . , r )
$$

is that

$$
[ \theta _ { 1 } \theta _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot \theta _ { r } d \theta _ { \alpha } ] = 0 \qquad ( \alpha = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

Attempts to solve differential systems lead to the study of the solutions of closed differential systems.

The exterior product of two exterior invariant forms is evidently an invariant form.Hence, if an invariant exterior form $\Omega$ is known, we can derive a series of other invariant forms, that is, $\Omega ^ { \acute { \prime } }$ and all forms deducible from $\Omega$ and $\Omega ^ { \prime }$ ,by exterior multiplication.

If $\Omega$ is an absolute invariant form of an even degree, then two series of absolute invariant forms

$$
[ \Omega ^ { p } ] , \qquad [ \Omega ^ { p - 1 } \Omega ^ { \prime } ] \qquad ( \rlap { / } { p } = 1 , 2 , . . . ) ,
$$

can be deduced. The derivative of a form of the first series is equal to a form of the second series. The derivative of a form of the second series is zero. If $\Omega$ is an absolute invariant of an odd degree, then we obtain two series

$$
[ \Omega ^ { \prime p } ] , \qquad [ \Omega \Omega ^ { \prime p - 1 } ] \qquad ( \not p = 1 , 2 , \ldots ) .
$$

The derivative of a form of the frst series is zero,and the derivative of a form of the second series is equal to a form of the first series.

Consider a relative integral invariant $\int \Omega$ and suppose that $\Omega$ is of an even degree.Then only one new invariant, that is, the absolute invarint $\int \Omega ^ { \prime }$ ,can be derived.If $\Omega$ is of an odd degree, then we obtain a series of relative integral invariants $\int \Omega \Omega ^ { \prime } ^ { p - 1 }$ and a series of absolute integral invariants $\int \Omega ^ { \prime } \ d \mathbf { p }$ .The relative invariant $\int \Omega \Omega ^ { \prime } { } ^ { p - 1 }$ leads to the absolute invariant $\int ^ { \infty } \Omega ^ { \prime } ^ { p }$ by derivation.

For example, take the relative invariant of dynamics

$$
\int \omega = \int \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \dot { p } _ { i } \delta q _ { i } - H \delta t .
$$

The relative integral invariants derivable from this invariant are $\int _ { . } \omega \omega ^ { \prime p - 1 } \left( \phi = 1 , 2 , . . . , n \right)$ ，and the absolute integral invariants are $\int \omega ^ { \prime p } \ \left( \boldsymbol { \phi } = 1 , 2 , . . . , n \right)$ . Hence there exists an invariant, relative or absolute,of any given degree, lower than or equal to $2 n$

Consider a form $\pmb { F }$ of degree $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ .Denote by $\xi , \xi ^ { \prime } , \ldots , \xi ^ { ( n - p + 1 ) }$ the linear forms with indeterminate coefficients

$$
\xi = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \xi _ { i } u _ { i } , \xi ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } u _ { i } ,
$$

The exterior product $[ F \xi \xi ^ { \prime } \cdot \cdot \cdot \xi ^ { ( n - p + 1 ) } ]$ is of degree $\pmb { n }$ and hence of the form $\Phi [ u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot u _ { n } ]$ The coefficient $\Phi$ is linear with respect to each series of coefficients $\xi$ and is alternating. It corresponds to an exterior form of degree $n - \phi$ with respect to the variables $\xi _ { 1 } , \xi _ { 2 } , . . . , \xi _ { n }$ This is defined as the adjoint form of $\pmb { F }$

If we apply a linear substitution to the variables $\pmb { u }$ and a linear substitution to the variables $\xi$ so that the expression

$$
\xi _ { 1 } u _ { 1 } + \xi _ { 2 } u _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \xi _ { n } u _ { n }
$$

is conserved, then the expression $\Phi [ u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot u _ { n } ]$ is conserved. In other words, the adjoint form is multiplied by the determinant of the linear substitution applied to the variables $\boldsymbol { u }$

An exterior form of degree $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ is called a monomial if it can be expressed as an exterior product of $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { p } }$ linear forms.Consider the system of linear equations obtained by equating to zero all partial derivatives of order $\phi - 1$ of that exterior form, that is, the associated system.If a linear substitution with nonvanishing determinant is applied to the form $F ( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { p } )$ to transform it into the form $\Phi ( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , . . . , u _ { p } )$ , then the same process transforms the associated system of $\pmb { F }$ into the associated system of $\Phi$ . If an exterior monomial form is linearly transformed so that it contains only a single term $a [ u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot u _ { p } ]$ ， then the associated system consists of $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ independent equations $u _ { 1 } = 0 , u _ { 2 } = 0 , . . . , u _ { p } = 0 .$ Conversely, if the associated system of a form ${ \pmb F }$ of degree $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ is reduced to $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ independent equations, then the associated system can take the form ${ \bf \chi } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = 0 , u _ { 2 } = 0 , . . . , u _ { p } = 0$ by a linear substitution,but none of the nonvanishing coefficients of the form can contain indices other than $1 , 2 , \ldots , \boldsymbol { \mathscr { p } }$ 、Hence the form should be $a [ u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot u _ { p } ]$ ，a monomial. The necessary and sufficient condition for an exterior form of degree $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ to be a monomial is that the associated system be of rank $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$

The adjoint form of $F = F _ { 1 } + F _ { 2 }$ is the sum of the adjoint forms $\Phi _ { 1 }$ and $\Phi _ { 2 }$ .The adjoint form of $a F$ is $a \Phi$ ,where $^ { a }$ is a numerical constant. To calculate the adjoint form it is suffcient to calculate the adjoint form of a monomial such as $F = [ u _ { \alpha _ { 1 } } u _ { \alpha _ { 2 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot u _ { \alpha _ { p } } ]$ . Then $\Phi = [ \xi _ { \alpha _ { p + 1 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot \xi _ { \alpha _ { n } } ] _ { \mathrm { \ell } }$ ， where the indices $\alpha _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n }$ are those indices $1 , 2 , \ldots , n$ that do not appear in the sequence $\alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { p } ,$ ,and these indices are arranged in an order such that the total sequence $\alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { p } , \alpha _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { n }$ is even.

If $F$ is of degree $n - 1$ ,then the adjoint form is of the first degree.We can suppose that the adjoint form is $\xi _ { n }$ so that $\pmb { F }$ can be put in the form $F = [ u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot u _ { n - 1 } ]$ If $\boldsymbol { F }$ is of degree $n - 2$ ，then $\Phi$ is of the second degree, and is given by $\Phi = [ \xi _ { 1 } \xi _ { 2 } ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot + [ \xi _ { 2 s - 1 } \xi _ { 2 s } ]$

$$
\{ u _ { 3 } u _ { 4 } u _ { 5 } \cdot \cdot \cdot u _ { n } \} + [ u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } u _ { 5 } u _ { 6 } \cdot \cdot \cdot u _ { n } ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot + [ u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot u _ { 2 s - 2 } u _ { 2 s + 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot u _ { n } ] .
$$

Suppose that $f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , \ldots . . . , f _ { h }$ are $h$ independent linear forms. Let $\pmb { F }$ be an arbitrary exterior form.In order that $F$ may vanish when there are relations $f _ { 1 } = 0 , f _ { 2 } = 0 , . . . , f _ { h } = 0$ among the variables, it is necessary and sufficient that $[ F f _ { 1 } f _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot f _ { h } ] = 0 .$ For,if any term of $F$ contains at least one of the variables $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { h }$ as a factor, then the product $\left[ F u _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot u _ { h } \right]$ is zero.Conversely,if this product is zero,then an arbitrary term of $F$ must contain at least one of the variables $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { h }$ ，otherwise the multiplication of the term by $\left[ u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot u _ { h } \right]$ will give a nonzero product that we cannot cancel by combining it with any other product.

Consider an exterior quadratic form $\pmb { F }$ in $_ n$ variables $u _ { 1 } , . . . , u _ { n }$ Suppose that the variables are connected by a linear relation

$$
f \equiv a _ { 1 } u _ { 1 } + a _ { 2 } u _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + a _ { n } u _ { n } = 0 .
$$

The form $F _ { : }$ where $u _ { n }$ is expressed in terms of $u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n - 1 }$ by means of this linear relation, is of a certain rank corresponding to the number of linearly independent equations of the associated system, namely

$$
{ \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { 1 } } } - { \frac { a _ { 1 } } { a _ { n } } } { \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { n } } } = 0 , . . . , { \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { n - 1 } } } - { \frac { a _ { n - 1 } } { a _ { n } } } { \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { n } } } = 0 , \quad f = 0 .
$$

If there are $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ linearly independent relations

$$
f _ { 1 } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i } u _ { i } = 0 , \mathcal { f } _ { 2 } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i } ^ { \prime } u _ { i } = 0 , . . . , \mathcal { f } _ { p } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } a _ { i } ^ { ( p - 1 ) } u _ { i } =
$$

then the associated system of $F$ is

$$
{ \left| \begin{array} { l l l l l } { { \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { 1 } } } } & { { \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { 2 } } } } & { { } } & { { \frac { \partial F } { \partial u _ { n } } } } \\ { a _ { 1 } } & { a _ { 2 } } & { { } } & { a _ { n } } \\ { a _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } } & { { a _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } } } & { { } } & { a _ { n } ^ { \prime } } \\ { \vdots } & { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { a _ { 1 } ^ { ( p - 1 ) } } & { { a _ { 2 } ^ { ( p - 1 ) } } } & { { } } & { { a _ { n } ^ { ( p - 1 ) } } } \end{array} \right| } = 0 , ~ f _ { 1 } = 0 , ~ f _ { 2 } = 0 , \ldots , ~ f _ { p } = 0
$$

The rank $2 s ^ { \prime }$ of the form $F _ { : }$ ，when the variables are connected by the $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ linearly independent relations, is twice the largest exponent for which the form $[ f _ { 1 } f _ { 2 } . \cdot \cdot f _ { p } F ^ { s ^ { \prime } } ]$ is not zero.

If we define the bilinear alternating form

$$
\Phi ( \xi , \xi ^ { \prime } ) = \sum _ { i , j } a _ { i j } \xi _ { i } \xi _ { j } ^ { \prime }
$$

by the equation

$$
s [ F ^ { s - 1 } ( \xi _ { 1 } u _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \xi _ { n } u _ { n } ) ( \xi _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } u _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \xi _ { n } ^ { \prime } u _ { n } ) ] = \Phi ( \xi , \xi ^ { \prime } ) [ F ^ { s } ] ,
$$

then the exterior quadratic form $\begin{array} { r } { \Phi ( \xi ) = \sum _ { i , j } a _ { i j } [ \xi _ { i } \xi _ { j } ] } \end{array}$ is equal to the adjoint form of $F ^ { s - 1 } / ( s - 1 ) !$ ,multiplied by a constant factor.It is an absolute covariant of $\pmb { F }$ in the sense that if we apply a linear substitution to the variables $u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { n }$ and a linear substitution to the variables $\xi _ { 1 } , . . . , \xi _ { n }$ such that $\xi _ { 1 } u _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \xi _ { n } u _ { n }$ is conserved,and if $\pmb { F } ( \pmb { u } )$

and $\Phi ( \xi )$ become respectively equal to $\bar { F } ( \bar { u } )$ and $\overline { { \Phi } } ( \xi )$ through these two substitutions, then

$$
s [ \bar { F } ^ { s - 1 } \bigl ( \bar { \xi } _ { 1 } \bar { u } _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \bar { \xi } _ { n } \bar { u } _ { n } \bigr ) \bigl ( \bar { \xi } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \bar { u } _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \bar { \xi } _ { n } ^ { \prime } \bar { u } _ { n } \bigr ) ] = \overline { { { \Phi } } } ( \bar { \xi } \bar { \xi } ^ { \prime } ) [ \bar { F } ^ { s } ] .
$$

In particular, if $\pmb { F }$ is of the canonical form

$$
{ \cal F } = [ u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot + [ u _ { 2 s - 1 } u _ { 2 s } ] ,
$$

then

$$
\Phi = [ \xi _ { 1 } \xi _ { 2 } ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot + [ \xi _ { 2 s - 1 } \xi _ { 2 s } ] .
$$

This leads to the general identity

$$
\biggl [ \frac { F ^ { s - p } } { ( s - p ) ! } ( \xi _ { 1 } u _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \xi _ { n } u _ { n } ) ( \xi _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } u _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \xi _ { n } ^ { \prime } u _ { n } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot +
$$

$$
\left( \xi _ { 1 } ^ { ( 2 p - 1 ) } u _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \xi _ { n } ^ { ( 2 p - 1 ) } u _ { n } \right) \bigg ] = \frac { \Phi ^ { ( p ) } ( \xi , \xi ^ { \prime } , \cdot \cdot , \xi ^ { ( 2 p - 1 ) } ) } { \rlap / p ! } \bigg [ \frac { F ^ { s } } { s ! } \bigg ] ,
$$

where the exterior form of degree $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ corresponding to the multilinear alternating form $\Phi ^ { ( p ) }$ is equal to $[ \Phi ^ { ( p ) } ( \xi ) ]$ .If $\pmb { \hat { p } } = 2$ , then,by taking the terms in $[ \xi _ { i } \xi _ { j } ^ { \prime } \xi _ { k } ^ { \prime \prime } \xi _ { l } ^ { \prime \prime \prime } ]$ , we obtain

$$
\left[ \frac { F ^ { s - 2 } } { ( s - 2 ) ! } u _ { i } u _ { j } u _ { k } u _ { l } \right] = ( a _ { i j } a _ { k l } + a _ { i k } a _ { l j } + a _ { i l } a _ { j k } ) \left[ \frac { F ^ { s } } { s ! } \right] ,
$$

where $\alpha _ { i j }$ are defined by

$$
\left[ \frac { F ^ { s - 1 } } { ( s - 1 ) ! } u _ { i } u _ { j } \right] = a _ { i j } \Bigl [ \frac { F ^ { s } } { s ! } \Bigr ] .
$$

Consider a form of degree $2 s - 1$ ：

$$
\left[ \frac { F ^ { s - 2 } } { ( s - 2 ) ! } u _ { i } u _ { j } u _ { k } \right] - \left[ \frac { F ^ { s - 1 } } { ( s - 1 ) ! } ( a _ { i j } u _ { k } + a _ { j k } u _ { i } + a _ { k i } u _ { j } ) \right] .
$$

Multiply exteriorly by any one of the variables $u _ { 1 } , \dotsc , u _ { 2 s } ,$ ， for example by ${ \boldsymbol { \mu } } _ { l }$ ；then,by (ll9)，the product is zero.Hence the form itself is identically zero. Since $u _ { i } , u _ { j } , u _ { k }$ can be replaced by any three linear forms of the variables, we obtain the following

Theorem : Consider linear forms $f _ { 1 } , \ldots , f _ { p }$ and put

$$
\Big [ \frac { F ^ { s - 1 } } { ( s - 1 ) ! } f _ { i } f _ { j } \Big ] = a _ { i j } \Big [ \frac { F ^ { s } } { s ! } \Big ] ( i , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , p ) ,
$$

then,

$$
\left[ \frac { F ^ { s - 2 } } { ( s - 2 ) ! } f _ { i } f _ { j } f _ { k } \right] = \left[ \frac { F ^ { s - 1 } } { ( s - 1 ) ! } ( a _ { i j } f _ { k } + a _ { j k } f _ { i } + a _ { k i } f _ { j } ) \right] .
$$

Consider $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { p } }$ linearly independent relations $f _ { 1 } = 0 , f _ { 2 } = 0 , . . . , f _ { p } = 0 .$ We can suppose that they are reduced to the forms

$$
u _ { 1 } = 0 , u _ { 2 } = 0 , . . . , u _ { p } = 0 ,
$$

on the single condition that the frst $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ variables $u _ { 1 } , . . . , u _ { p }$ can be exchanged among themselves. Thus an arbitrary linear substitution can be applied to the variables $\xi$ on the single condition that the last $2 s - \pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ variables $\xi _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , \xi _ { 2 s }$ can be exchanged among themselves. Put

$$
\left[ { \frac { F ^ { s - 1 } } { ( s - 1 ) ! } } u _ { i } u _ { j } \right] = a _ { i j } \Bigl [ { \frac { F ^ { s } } { s ! } } \Bigr ] \qquad ( i , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , \rlap / p )
$$

and drop the terms in $\xi _ { p + 1 } , . . . , \xi _ { 2 s }$ in $\Phi$ ; then,

$$
\overline { { \Phi } } = \sum _ { i , j = 1 } ^ { p } a _ { i j } [ \xi _ { i } \xi _ { j } ] .
$$

Let $2 q$ be the rank of the form $\overline { { \Phi } }$ .By suitable linear substitution on $\xi _ { 1 } , . . . , \xi _ { p } .$ ，we can reduce $\overline { { \Phi } }$ to

$$
\Phi = [ \xi _ { 1 } \xi _ { 2 } ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot + [ \xi _ { 2 q - 1 } \xi _ { 2 q } ] .
$$

Replacing $\xi _ { 1 } , . . . , \xi _ { p }$ by linear combinations of $\xi _ { p + 1 } , . . . , \xi _ { 2 s } ,$ we obtain $\Phi$ in the form

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \Phi = [ \xi _ { 1 } \xi _ { 2 } ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot + [ \xi _ { 2 q - 1 } \xi _ { 2 q } ] + [ \xi _ { 2 q + 1 } \xi _ { p + 1 } ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot + [ \xi _ { p } \xi _ { 2 p - 2 q } ] } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \mathrm { } + [ \xi _ { 2 p - 2 q + 1 } \xi _ { 2 p - 2 q + 2 } ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot + [ \xi _ { 2 s - 1 } \xi _ { 2 s } ] . } } \end{array}
$$

Then ${ \pmb F }$ takes the form

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { F = [ u _ { 1 } u _ { 2 } ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot + [ u _ { 2 q - 1 } u _ { 2 q } ] + [ u _ { 2 q + 1 } u _ { p + 1 } ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot + [ u _ { p } u _ { 2 p - 2 q } ] } } \\ { { \ \qquad + \cdot \cdot \cdot + [ u _ { 2 s - 1 } u _ { 2 s } ] . } } \end{array}
$$

Hence,if there are $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ relations $u _ { 1 } = 0 , u _ { 2 } = 0 , . . . , u _ { p } = 0 $ ，then the rank of $F$ is reduced by $2 p - 2 q$ 、From this we obtain the following

Theorem: Consider linearly independent forms $f _ { 1 } , f _ { 2 } , \ldots , f _ { p }$ and $\textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } p ( p - 1 )$ quantities $a _ { i j }$ defined by

$$
\left[ \frac { F ^ { s - 1 } } { ( s - 1 ) ! } f _ { i } f _ { j } \right] = a _ { i j } \Bigl [ \frac { F ^ { s } } { s ! } \Bigr ] ,
$$

and the exterior quadratic form of $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ variables $\xi _ { 1 } , . . . , \xi _ { p }$

$$
\Phi ( \xi ) = \sum _ { i , j = 1 } ^ { p } a _ { i j } [ \xi _ { i } \xi _ { j } ] .
$$

If the rank of this form is $2 q$ ,then the rank of the form $\pmb { F }$ is reduced by $2 p - 2 q$ when the variables are connected by $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ relations

$$
f _ { 1 } = 0 , f _ { 2 } = 0 , . . . , f _ { p } = 0 .
$$
