If,in addition, we apply to the $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ given linear forms a linear substitution such that $\Phi$ reduces to the canonical form

$$
\Phi = [ \xi _ { 1 } \xi _ { 2 } ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot + [ \xi _ { 2 q - 1 } \xi _ { 2 q } ] ,
$$

then the form $\pmb { F }$ reduces to the canonical form

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { F = [ f _ { 1 } f _ { 2 } ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot + [ f _ { 2 q - 1 } f _ { 2 q } ] + [ f _ { 2 q + 1 } f _ { p + 1 } ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot + [ f _ { p } f _ { 2 p - 2 q } ] } } \\ { { \ \qquad + \cdot \cdot \cdot + [ f _ { 2 s - 1 } f _ { 2 s } ] , } } \end{array}
$$

where $f _ { p + 1 } , \ldots , f _ { 2 s }$ denote the new linear forms conveniently chosen so that they are independent of one another and of the given forms.

Suppose that a system of differential equations

$$
\frac { d x _ { 1 } } { d t } = X _ { 1 } , . . . , \frac { d x _ { n } } { d t } = X _ { n }
$$

admits an integral invariant of the maximum degree $\pmb { n }$

$$
\Omega = M [ ( \delta x _ { 1 } - X _ { 1 } \delta t ) ( \delta x _ { 2 } - X _ { 2 } \delta t ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( \delta x _ { n } - X _ { n } \delta t ) ] .
$$

The required coidition is that $\Omega ^ { \prime } = 0$ ,that is,

$$
\frac { \partial M } { \partial t } + \frac { \partial ( M X _ { 1 } ) } { \partial x _ { 1 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \frac { \partial ( M X _ { n } ) } { \partial x _ { n } } = 0 .
$$

The coefficient $M$ is called the Jacobi last multiplier (Section 4.18). This condition indicates that the form $\Omega$ can be expressed by means of $\pmb { n }$ independent first integrals $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ of (123) and their differentials; that is,

$$
M [ ( \delta x _ { 1 } - X _ { 1 } \delta t ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( \delta x _ { n } - X _ { n } \delta t ) ] = H ( y _ { 1 } , \cdot \cdot \cdot , y _ { n } ) [ \delta y _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \delta y _ { n } ] .
$$

Let $\omega _ { 1 } = 0 , . . . , \omega _ { r } = 0$ be a system of Pfaffians completely integrable, of which we know an invariant form of the maximum degree $r _ { : }$ i.e., $\Omega = M [ \omega _ { 1 } \omega _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \omega _ { \tau } ]$ . If we know $r - 1$ first integrals $y _ { 1 } , \dotsc , y _ { r - 1 }$ of the system， then, by equating $y _ { 1 } , \dotsc , y _ { r - 1 }$ each to an arbitrary constant, we can replace the given system by a single system $\omega _ { \tau } = 0$ , and obtain

$$
\Omega = N [ \delta y _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \delta y _ { r - 1 } \omega _ { r } ] .
$$

The coefficient $N$ can be calculated from $M .$ Thus $N \omega _ { \tau }$ is an invariant form for a single equation $\omega _ { r } = 0$ ; that is, $N \omega _ { \tau }$ is a perfect differential, and the integration can be carried out by quadrature.

If this Pfaffan system admits an infinitesimal transformation

$$
A f = \xi _ { 1 } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { 1 } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \xi _ { n } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { n } } } ,
$$

then we can deduce from an invariant differential form $\Omega$ another invariant form $A ( \Omega )$ .Let $\Omega = [ \omega _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \omega _ { r } ]$ . We construct

$$
\omega _ { 1 } ( A ) , \omega _ { 2 } ( A ) , . . . , \omega _ { \tau } ( A )
$$

and suppose that $\omega _ { 1 } ( A ) = 1$ ， $\omega _ { 2 } ( A ) = \cdot \cdot \cdot = \omega _ { r } ( A ) = 0$ ，are the results of a suitable transformation. Suppose that $\Omega$ is of the third degree,and consider the corresponding trilinear differential form $\Omega ( \delta , \delta ^ { \prime } , \delta ^ { \prime \prime } )$ Replace δ by the symbol $A$ for the infinitesimal transformation； then, we obtain $\Omega ( A , \delta ^ { \prime } , \delta ^ { \prime \prime } )$ - with two series of differentials $\delta ^ { \prime }$ and $\delta ^ { \prime \prime }$ 丨to which corresponds an exterior quadratic form $\Omega ( A , \delta )$ . If $\Omega$ is expressed in terms of the first integrals $y _ { i }$ and their differentials,then $\Omega ( A , \delta )$ is expressed in terms of $y _ { i }$ and $\delta { y } _ { i }$ . Thus, from any invariant form we can derive another invariant form whose degree is diminished by one:

$$
\Omega ( A , \delta ) = \xi _ { 1 } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial ( \delta x _ { 1 } ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \xi _ { n } \frac { \partial \Omega } { \partial ( \delta x _ { n } ) } \cdot
$$

Hence, if we know an infinitesimal transformation, we can deduce from the invariant form $\Omega ( \delta )$ a new invariant form $\Omega ( A , \delta )$ by means of this formula.

Let $2 n$ be the rank of the exterior derivative $\omega ^ { \prime }$ ,and $f$ and $g$ be two first integrals of its characteristic system.The two differential forms $[ \omega ^ { \prime n - 1 } \ d f d g ]$ and $[ \omega ^ { \prime n } ]$ are invariants of the maximum degree $2 n$ Hence these two forms differ only by a factor that is a first integral. Put

$$
\frac { 1 } { ( n - 1 ) ! } [ \omega ^ { \prime } { } ^ { n - 1 } d f d g ] = \frac { 1 } { n ! } ( f , g ) [ \omega ^ { \prime } { } ^ { n } ] ,
$$

where $( f , g )$ is a Poisson bracket and is an alternating bilinear form with respect to the partial derivatives of $f$ and $g$

If the condition $( f , g ) = 0$ is satisfed, then the rank of $\omega ^ { \prime }$ is $2 n - 4$ In this case we say that $f$ and $g$ are in involution. If this condition is not satisfied,then,by the definition of $( f , g )$ , the form $\omega ^ { \prime } - [ d f d g ] / ( f , g )$ is of rank $2 n - 2$ ,and its nth power is

$$
[ \omega ^ { \prime \prime } ] - \frac { n } { ( f , g ) } [ \omega ^ { \prime \prime - 1 } d f d g ] = 0 .
$$

If （124） is applied to $\begin{array} { r } { \omega ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { i } \left[ \delta \beta _ { i } \delta q _ { i } \right] - \left[ \delta H \delta t \right] } \end{array}$ in dynamics, and if the terms $[ \delta p _ { 1 } \delta q _ { 1 } \delta p _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \delta q _ { n } ]$ are equated on both sides, then we obtain

$$
( f , g ) = \sum _ { i } \bigg ( \frac { \partial f } { \partial \phi _ { j } } \frac { \partial g } { \partial q _ { i } } - \frac { \partial f } { \partial q _ { j } } \frac { \partial g } { \partial \phi _ { i } } \bigg ) ,
$$

as in Chapter 1.

From (120) we obtain the identity

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { ( n - 1 ) ! } \left[ \omega ^ { \prime n - 1 } \{ ( f , g ) d h + ( g , h ) d f + ( h , f ) d g \} \right] } } \\ { \displaystyle { \ } } \\ { \displaystyle { \ = \frac { 1 } { ( n - 2 ) ! } \left[ \omega ^ { \prime n - 2 } d f d g d h \right] } . } \end{array}
$$

By exterior derivation,and noting that the exterior derivative of the right-hand member is zero, we see that

$$
[ \omega ^ { \prime \prime - 1 } d ( f , g ) d h ] + [ \omega ^ { \prime \prime - 1 } d ( g , h ) d f ] + [ \omega ^ { \prime \prime - 1 } d ( h , f ) d g ] = 0 .
$$

This is Jacobi's identity (Section 4.3).

If there are two linear invariant forms $\varpi _ { 1 }$ and $\varpi _ { 2 }$ , then the quantity $\pmb { \alpha }$ defined by

$$
n [ \omega ^ { \prime n - 1 } \varpi _ { 1 } \varpi _ { 2 } ] = \alpha [ \omega ^ { \prime n } ]
$$

is a first integral. It reduces to $( y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } )$ if $\varpi _ { 1 }$ and $\varpi _ { 2 }$ are differentials of two first integrals $y _ { 1 }$ and $y _ { 2 }$

Suppose that the characteristic equations for $\omega ^ { \prime }$ admit two infinitesimal transformations $A _ { 1 } f$ and $A _ { 2 } f .$ Then

$$
\varpi _ { 1 } = \omega ^ { \prime } ( A _ { 1 } , \ S ) , \qquad \varpi _ { 2 } = \omega ^ { \prime } ( A _ { 2 } , \ S ) .
$$

Apply to both sides of (l25) the operation that carries an invariant form $\Omega ( \delta )$ to the form $\Omega ( A _ { 1 } , \delta )$ . Then,

$$
n ( n - 1 ) [ \omega ^ { \prime n - 2 } \varpi _ { 1 } \varpi _ { 2 } ] - n [ \omega ^ { \prime n - 1 } \varpi _ { 1 } ] \varpi _ { 2 } ( A _ { 1 } ) = n \alpha [ \omega ^ { \prime n - 1 } \varpi _ { 1 } ] .
$$

Since $[ \omega ^ { \prime n - 1 } \varpi _ { 1 } ]$ is not zero,

$$
\alpha = - \varpi _ { 2 } ( A _ { 1 } ) = \omega ^ { \prime } ( A _ { 1 } , A _ { 2 } ) = \varpi _ { 1 } ( A _ { 2 } ) .
$$

Thus, if we apply the generalized theorem of Poisson-Jacobi to the two invariant forms $\omega ^ { \prime } ( A _ { 1 } , \delta )$ and $\omega ^ { \prime } ( A _ { 2 } , \delta )$ , we can derive the first integral $\omega ^ { \prime } ( A _ { 1 } , A _ { 2 } )$ by twice performing on $\omega ^ { \prime }$ the operation corresponding to the infinitesimal transformations $A _ { 1 } f$ and $A _ { 2 } f .$

After these general considerations we now apply the theory to the reduction of the differential equations for the $\pmb { n }$ -body problem.

Suppose that we reduce the equations to the order $6 n - 6$ by using the six integrals for the center of mass.The equations then admit four infinitesimal transformations $A _ { i } f ( i = 0 , 4 , 5 , 6 )$ . Corresponding to these transformations are four integrals $H _ { i }$ $( i = 0 , 4 , 5 , 6 )$ .The infinitesimal transformation corresponding to the integral invariant ${ \boldsymbol { \omega } } _ { 1 0 }$ (Section 4.21) is

$$
A _ { 1 0 } f = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } \left[ x _ { i } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { i } } } - { \frac { \dot { p } } { 2 } } y _ { i } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial y _ { i } } } \right] + \left[ 1 + { \frac { \dot { p } } { 2 } } \right] t { \frac { \partial f } { \partial t } } .
$$

The corresponding linear invariants $\omega _ { i } = \omega ^ { \prime } ( A _ { i } , \delta )$ are

$$
\omega _ { 0 } = \delta H _ { 0 } , \qquad \omega _ { 4 } = \delta H _ { 4 } , \qquad \omega _ { 5 } = \delta H _ { 5 } , \qquad \omega _ { 6 } = \delta H _ { 6 } ,
$$

$$
\omega _ { 1 0 } = \mathrm { ~ - ~ } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } m _ { i } \Bigl ( x _ { i } ~ \delta y _ { i } + \frac { \dot { p } } { 2 } y _ { i } ~ \delta x _ { i } \Bigr ) + \Bigl ( 1 + \frac { \dot { p } } { 2 } \Bigr ) t ~ \delta H _ { 0 } + p H _ { 0 } ~ \delta t .
$$

We have

$$
\omega _ { 1 0 } ^ { \prime } = A _ { 1 0 } ( \omega ^ { \prime } ) = \Big ( 1 - \frac { \rlap / p } { 2 } \Big ) \omega ^ { \prime } .
$$

If we compute $a _ { i j } = \omega ^ { \prime } ( A _ { i } , A _ { j } )$ by (125) and (126), then we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \Phi ( \xi ) = \displaystyle \rlap / { / } H _ { 0 } [ \xi _ { 1 0 } \xi _ { 0 } ] + \frac { \beta - 2 } { 2 } [ \xi _ { 1 0 } ( H _ { 4 } \xi _ { 4 } + H _ { 5 } \xi _ { 5 } + H _ { 6 } \xi _ { 6 } ) ] } \\ & { \qquad + \ H _ { 4 } [ \xi _ { 5 } \xi _ { 6 } ] + H _ { 5 } [ \xi _ { 6 } \xi _ { 4 } ] + H _ { 6 } [ \xi _ { 4 } \xi _ { 5 } ] . } \end{array}
$$

The rank is four. Let

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \xi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } = \hbar H _ { 0 } \xi _ { 0 } + \frac { \displaystyle \dot { p } - \displaystyle 2 } { \displaystyle 2 } ( H _ { 4 } \xi _ { 4 } + H _ { 5 } \xi _ { 5 } + H _ { 6 } \xi _ { 6 } ) . } \\ & { \xi _ { 4 } ^ { \prime } = \alpha _ { 1 } \xi _ { 4 } + \alpha _ { 2 } \xi _ { 5 } + \alpha _ { 3 } \xi _ { 6 } , \qquad \xi _ { 5 } ^ { \prime } = \beta _ { 1 } \xi _ { 4 } + \beta _ { 2 } \xi _ { 5 } + \beta _ { 3 } \xi _ { 6 } , } \\ & { \xi _ { 6 } ^ { \prime } = \xi _ { 0 } , \qquad \xi _ { 1 0 } ^ { \prime } = \xi _ { 1 0 } . } \end{array}
$$

Then we obtain the canonical form

$$
\Phi = [ \xi _ { 1 0 } ^ { \prime } \xi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } ] + [ \xi _ { 4 } ^ { \prime } \xi _ { 5 } ^ { \prime } ] ,
$$

where $\propto$ and $\beta$ are chosen so that

$$
\alpha _ { 2 } \beta _ { 3 } - \alpha _ { 3 } \beta _ { 2 } = H _ { 4 } , \qquad \alpha _ { 3 } \beta _ { 1 } - \alpha _ { 1 } \beta _ { 3 } = H _ { 5 } , \qquad \alpha _ { 1 } \beta _ { 2 } - \alpha _ { 2 } \beta _ { 1 } = H _ { 6 } ,
$$

and with further conditions

$$
\alpha _ { 1 } \beta _ { 1 } + \alpha _ { 2 } \beta _ { 2 } + \alpha _ { 3 } \beta _ { 3 } = 0 ,
$$

$$
\alpha _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + \alpha _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } = \beta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \beta _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + \beta _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } = \sqrt { H _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 5 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 6 } ^ { 2 } } .
$$

If we define five linear forms $\varpi _ { 0 } , \varpi _ { 4 } , \varpi _ { 5 } , \varpi _ { 6 } , \varpi _ { 1 0 } ,$ by

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \xi _ { 0 } \omega _ { 0 } + \xi _ { 4 } \omega _ { 4 } + \xi _ { 5 } \omega _ { 5 } + \xi _ { 6 } \omega _ { 6 } + \xi _ { 1 0 } \omega _ { 1 0 } } \\ & { \qquad = \xi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \varpi _ { 0 } + \xi _ { 4 } ^ { \prime } \varpi _ { 4 } + \xi _ { 5 } ^ { \prime } \varpi _ { 5 } + \xi _ { 6 } ^ { \prime } \varpi _ { 6 } + \xi _ { 1 0 } ^ { \prime } \varpi _ { 1 0 } , } \end{array}
$$

then we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \varpi _ { 0 } = \frac { 2 } { \dot { p } - 2 } \frac { 2 } { 2 } \frac { H _ { 4 } d H _ { 4 } + H _ { 5 } d H _ { 5 } + H _ { 6 } d H _ { 6 } } { H _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 5 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 6 } ^ { 2 } } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \varpi _ { 4 } = \frac { \alpha _ { 1 } d H _ { 4 } + \alpha _ { 2 } d H _ { 5 } + \alpha _ { 3 } d H _ { 6 } } { \sqrt { H _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 5 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 6 } ^ { 2 } } } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \varpi _ { 5 } = \frac { \beta _ { 3 } d H _ { 4 } + \beta _ { 2 } d H _ { 5 } + \beta _ { 3 } d H _ { 6 } } { \sqrt { H _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 5 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 6 } ^ { 2 } } } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \varpi _ { 6 } = d H _ { 6 } - \frac { 2 \beta H _ { 0 } } { \beta - 2 } \frac { H _ { 4 } d H _ { 4 } + H _ { 5 } d H _ { 5 } + H _ { 6 } d H _ { 6 } } { H _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 5 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 6 } ^ { 2 } } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \varpi _ { 6 } = d H _ { 0 } - \frac { 2 \beta H _ { 0 } } { \beta - 2 } \frac { H _ { 4 } d H _ { 4 } + H _ { 5 } d H _ { 5 } + H _ { 6 } d H _ { 6 } } { H _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 5 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 6 } ^ { 2 } } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \varpi _ { 1 0 } = \omega _ { 1 0 } } } } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\omega ^ { \prime } = [ \varpi _ { 1 0 } \varpi _ { 0 } ] + [ \varpi _ { 4 } \varpi _ { 5 } ] + [ \omega _ { 1 } \varpi _ { 6 } ] + [ \omega _ { 2 } \omega _ { 3 } ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

Hence

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { \omega ^ { \prime } = \displaystyle \frac { 2 } { \beta - 2 } \left[ \omega _ { 1 0 } \frac { H _ { 4 } d H _ { 4 } + H _ { 5 } d H _ { 5 } + H _ { 6 } d H _ { 6 } } { H _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 5 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 6 } ^ { 2 } } \right] } & { } & \\ { \cdot + \frac { H _ { 4 } [ d H _ { 5 } d H _ { 6 } ] + H _ { 5 } [ d H _ { 6 } d H _ { 4 } ] + H _ { 6 } [ d H _ { 4 } d H _ { 5 } ] } { H _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 5 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 6 } ^ { 2 } } + \Omega , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \Omega = \displaystyle \left[ \omega _ { \mathrm { 1 } } \Big ( d H _ { \mathrm { 0 } } - \frac { 2 \beta H _ { \mathrm { 0 } } } { \beta - 2 } \frac { H _ { \mathrm { 4 } } d H _ { \mathrm { 4 } } + H _ { \mathrm { 5 } } d H _ { \mathrm { 5 } } + H _ { \mathrm { 6 } } d H _ { \mathrm { 6 } } } { H _ { \mathrm { 4 } } ^ { \mathrm { 2 } } + H _ { \mathrm { 5 } } ^ { \mathrm { 2 } } + H _ { \mathrm { 6 } } ^ { \mathrm { 2 } } } \Big ) \right] } } \\ { { + \left( \omega _ { \mathrm { 2 } } \omega _ { \mathrm { 3 } } \right) + \cdot \cdot \cdot . } } \end{array}
$$

By the theorem relative to (l21), the rank of $\Phi$ is four. Since there are five integral invariants, the rank of $\omega ^ { \prime }$ is reduced by $2 \times 5 - 4 = 6$ and the order of the differential equations is reduced from $6 n - 6$ to $6 n - 1 2$ ; that is, we obtain the integrals by puting each of $H _ { 0 } , H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 }$ $H _ { 6 }$ equal to arbitrary constants. However, the corresponding characteristic system contains arbitrary parameters.

There is a proces for reducing the number of the degree offreedom by avoiding the introduction of arbitrary parameters. Equate the exterior derivative of the right-hand member of (l27) to zero; then we obtain

$$
\Omega ^ { \prime } = \biggl [ \frac { H _ { 4 } d H _ { 4 } + H _ { 5 } d H _ { 5 } + H _ { 6 } d H _ { 6 } } { H _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 5 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 6 } ^ { 2 } } \Omega \biggr ] ,
$$

by referring to $\omega _ { 1 0 } ^ { \prime } = \frac { \ d H _ { 1 } } { \ d z ^ { 2 } } ( 2 - \hbar \omega ^ { \prime } )$ .This shows that the exterior derivative of a quadratic form

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { H _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 5 } ^ { 2 } + ~ H _ { 6 } ^ { 2 } } } } \Omega = \frac { 2 } { 2 ~ - ~ \hat { p } } \left( \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { H _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 5 } ^ { 2 } + ~ H _ { 6 } ^ { 2 } } } \omega _ { 1 0 } \right) ^ { \prime } } } \\ { { - \frac { H _ { 4 } [ d H _ { 5 } d H _ { 6 } ] + H _ { 5 } [ d H _ { 6 } d H _ { 4 } ] + H _ { 6 } [ d H _ { 4 } d H _ { 5 } ] } { ( H _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 5 } ^ { 2 } + ~ H _ { 6 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } ~ , } } \end{array}
$$

is zero.The first term on the right-hand side is zero because it is an exact exterior derivative.For the second term,consider a vector $O S$ of length $\gamma = ( H _ { 4 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 5 } ^ { 2 } + H _ { 6 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 }$ . This is the angular momentum of the whole system with respect to the center of mass.Let $d \sigma$ be the surface element described by the point $S$ and $\alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \alpha _ { 3 }$ be the direction cosines of the normal to this surface element. Then

$$
[ d H _ { 5 } d H _ { 6 } ] = \alpha _ { 1 } d \sigma , \qquad [ d H _ { 6 } d H _ { 4 } ] = \alpha _ { 2 } d \sigma , \qquad [ d H _ { 4 } d H _ { 5 } ] = \alpha _ { 3 } d \sigma .
$$

The second term on the right-hand side of (127a) becomes

$$
- { \frac { \alpha _ { 1 } H _ { 4 } + \alpha _ { 2 } H _ { 5 } + \alpha _ { 3 } H _ { 6 } } { \gamma ^ { 3 } } } d \sigma = - { \frac { \cos \psi d \sigma } { \gamma } } ,
$$

where $\psi$ is the angle between the normal and the vector $O S .$ The righthand member of (l27b) equals the solid angle subtended by $d \sigma$ at the center of mass. Denote by $\theta$ and $\phi$ the colatitude and the longitude of $S$ ； then this right-hand member becomes $- \sin \theta [ d \theta d \phi ]$ ,that is,the exterior derivative of cos $\theta \ : d \phi$ .Let

$$
\varpi = \frac { 2 } { 2 - \beta } \frac { \omega _ { 1 0 } } { \gamma } + \cos { \theta } d \phi ;
$$

then the characteristic equation of the relative integral invariant $\int \varpi$ is

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { H _ { 0 } - { \displaystyle \frac { 2 \phi } { \beta - 2 } } H _ { 0 } { \displaystyle \frac { d \gamma } { \gamma } } = 0 , \omega _ { 4 } = 0 , \omega _ { 5 } = 0 , \omega _ { 6 } = 0 , } } \\ { { \omega _ { 1 } = 0 , . . . , \omega _ { 7 } = 0 , . . . , \omega _ { 1 0 } = 0 . } } \end{array}
$$

# ADIABATIC INVARIANTS

# 4.23ADIABATIC INVARIANTS

Ehrenfest (l9l7) and Burgers (l9l7) showed that the mean value of the variation of the phase integral (Section 2.7)

$$
\scriptstyle J = \oint \ p \ d q
$$

becomes zero in multiply-periodic motions of atomic systems in classical quantum theory, when the system is submitted to slowly varying perturbations.This invariance of the phase integral for infinitely slow variation has been called the adiabatic invariance by these two workers, and also by Born (l926).Sommerfeld (l922） assumed that the time variation of the mechanical system is nonsystematic,rather than assuming zero time-average.But a slow variation does not necessarily satisfy this assumption. Kneser (l924) supposed that the mechanical system contains a slowly varying parameter $^ { a }$ ,such that $a = f ( t )$ for $0 < t < T _ { : }$ and,by extending the interval to $0 < t < l T _ { : }$ ，proved that the total variation of the phase integral $J ( t = l T ) - J ( t = 0 )$ tends to zero as $l  \infty$ , for the case of one degree of freedom. The proof of Fermi and Persico (1926) is not satisfactory.Born and Fock (l928), using Schrodinger's equation in quantum mechanics as a basis, estimated the order for the deviation from invariance which tends to zero asymptotically for $T \to \infty$

Let the eigenfunctions of the operator $H ( s )$ be $\phi _ { 1 } ( q , s ) , \phi _ { 2 } ( q , s ) , . . . ,$ which satisfy

$$
H ( s ) \phi _ { n } ( q , s ) = W _ { n } ( s ) \phi _ { n } ( q , s ) \qquad ( n = 1 , 2 , \ldots ) .
$$

The Schrodinger equation

$$
H \psi _ { n } + \frac { h } { 2 \pi i } \frac { \partial \psi _ { n } } { \partial t } = 0 \qquad ( n = 1 , 2 , . . . )
$$

is satisfied by the complete, normalized orthogonal system of functions $\psi _ { 1 } ( q , t ) , \psi _ { 2 } ( q , t ) , . . . ,$ with the initial condition

$$
\psi _ { n } ( q , 0 ) = \phi _ { n } ( q , 0 ) ( n = 1 , 2 , . . . ) .
$$

We have

$$
\dot { q } = \frac { 2 \pi i } { h } ( H q - q H ) , ~ \dot { p } = \frac { 2 \pi i } { h } ( H \rlap / p - \rlap / p H ) .
$$

These equations are satisfied by

$$
q = U ^ { \dagger } q ^ { 0 } U , \qquad \hbar = U ^ { \dagger } p ^ { 0 } U , \qquad H = U ^ { \dagger } H ^ { 0 } U ,
$$

with unitary matrix $U _ { : }$ ，where $U ^ { \dagger }$ denotes the transposed conjugate matrix of $U _ { : }$ and $H ^ { 0 }$ ， $q ^ { 0 } , p ^ { 0 }$ are the initial values of $H , q , \phi$ ,respectively. $U$ should satisfy

$$
\frac { h } { 2 \pi i } \dot { U } + H ^ { \circ } U = 0 .
$$

The solution is of the form $U = V Y$ with unitary matrices $V$ and Y. Let $s = t / T$ ； then,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { d Y } { d s } = - \frac { 2 \pi i T } { h } W Y + i Q Y , } \\ { Q = - i \frac { d V ^ { \dagger } } { d s } V = i V ^ { \dagger } \frac { d V } { d s } . } \end{array}
$$

Referring to the matrix elements

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { U _ { m n } = \displaystyle \int \phi _ { m } ^ { * } ( q , 0 ) \psi _ { n } ( q , t ) \rho d q , \quad } } & { { \quad V _ { m n } = \displaystyle \int \phi _ { m } ^ { * } ( q , 0 ) \phi _ { n } ( q , s ) \rho d q , } } \\ { { { } } } & { { { } } } \\ { { { \displaystyle Y _ { m n } = \int \phi _ { m } ^ { * } ( q , s ) \psi _ { n } ( q , t ) \rho d q } , } } & { { } } \end{array}
$$

where a superscript \* means the conjugate complex and $\rho$ denotes the density function in $q$ -configuration space, we obtain

$$
\frac { d Y _ { m n } } { d s } = { } - \frac { 2 \pi i T } { h } W _ { m } Y _ { m n } + i \sum _ { k } Q _ { m k } Y _ { k n } .
$$

Let

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \omega _ { k } ( s ) = \frac { 2 \pi } { h } \int _ { 0 } ^ { s } W _ { k } ( s ) \ d s , } \\ { \displaystyle c _ { m n } ( s ) = Y _ { m n } e ^ { i T \omega _ { m } } , \qquad c _ { m n } ( 0 ) = \delta _ { m n } . } \end{array}
$$

Assume that $d ( \omega _ { m } - \omega _ { n } ) / d s = 2 \pi \nu _ { m n } ( s )$ admits only a finite number of zero points of the rth order such that

$$
\frac { 1 } { | 2 \pi \nu _ { m n } ( s ) | } < \frac { A } { | s - s _ { 0 } | ^ { r } }
$$

with a finite positive constant $A$ in the neighborhood of a zero point ${ \mathfrak { s } } _ { 0 } .$ that $| Q _ { m n } ( s ) | \leqq M _ { m n }$ with a fnite positive constant matrix $M _ { m n }$ ，and that the real and the imaginary parts of $Q _ { m n } ( s ) / \nu _ { m n } ( s )$ are piecewise monotonic with a finite number of discontinuity points. Then Born and Fock proved that $c _ { m n } - \delta _ { m n }$ is of order $T ^ { - 1 / ( r + 1 ) }$ for a finite $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ and an infinite $_ { T }$ such that

$$
c _ { m n } = \delta _ { m n } + { \cal O } \big ( T ^ { - 1 / ( r + 1 ) } \big ) .
$$

Suppose，with Levi-Civita (l928)，that the system of differentiah equations

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = X _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } , a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , . . . , a _ { r } ) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

containing the parameters $a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots , a _ { r } ;$ but not $t$ explicitly, admits the integrals

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { f _ { j } ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } , a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , . . . , a _ { r } ) = c _ { j } \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - 1 ) , } \\ & { } \\ & { f _ { n } ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } , a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , . . . , a _ { r } ) = t - t _ { 0 } , } \end{array}
$$

where $a _ { \rho }$ are all constant. An integral curve of (128) is the trajectory of a point moving in the $\pmb { n }$ dimensional space $R _ { n }$ ， and the trajectory is represented by the intersection of the $n - 1$ hypersurfaces,that is,the $( n - 1 )$ -dimensional continuum represented by the first set of $n - 1$ equations of (129) embedded in $R _ { n }$ ·

When the parameter $a _ { \rho }$ varies as a function of $t$ ，the variables

$$
t _ { 0 } , c _ { 1 } , . . . , c _ { n - 1 }
$$

of (129) are functions of $t$ . By the principle of the variation of constants we must integrate

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { \displaystyle \frac { d c _ { j } } { d t } = \sum _ { \rho = 1 } ^ { r } \frac { \partial f _ { j } } { \partial a _ { \rho } } \frac { d a _ { \rho } } { d t } } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d t _ { 0 } } { d t } = - \sum _ { \rho = 1 } ^ { r } \frac { \partial f _ { n } } { \partial a _ { \rho } } \frac { d a _ { \rho } } { d t } } & { ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - 1 ) . } \end{array}
$$

For the coefficients of the time derivatives of $a _ { \rho }$ we substitute the solution of (128), and the result is of the form

$$
\frac { \partial f _ { i } } { \partial a _ { \rho } } = \phi _ { i \rho } ( t - t _ { 0 } , c _ { 1 } , . . . , c _ { n - 1 } , a _ { 1 } , . . . , a _ { r } )
$$

$$
( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ; \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) ;
$$

also,(130) is transformed to

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { d c _ { j } = \displaystyle \sum _ { \rho = 1 } ^ { r } \phi _ { i \rho } d a _ { \rho } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { d t _ { 0 } = \displaystyle - \sum _ { \rho = 1 } ^ { r } \phi _ { n \rho } d a _ { \rho } ~ ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - 1 ) . } } \end{array}
$$

If we know $a _ { \rho } ( t )$ ,then,when (130) is solved, $c _ { j } ( t )$ should be a functional of $a _ { \rho } ( t )$ . Let the domain of $t$ be $T .$ ，and the two ends be $t _ { 0 } + T _ { 0 }$ and $t _ { 0 } + T _ { 1 }$ .Take a distribution function

$$
F ( t - t _ { 0 } , c _ { 1 } , . . . , c _ { n - 1 } , a _ { 1 } , . . . , a _ { r } )
$$

and consider the mean value of an arbitrary function

$$
J ( t - t _ { 0 } , c _ { 1 } , . . . , c _ { n - 1 } , a _ { 1 } , . . . , a _ { r } ) colon
$$

$$
\bar { J } = \int _ { t _ { 0 } + T _ { 0 } } ^ { t _ { 0 } + T _ { 1 } } J F d t \biggl / \int _ { t _ { 0 } + T _ { 0 } } ^ { t _ { 0 } + T _ { 1 } } F d t ,
$$

where the integration is to be carried out with regard to $t$ appearing explicitly in the integrands.By applying this operation to (l31) we obtain

$$
d c _ { j } = \sum _ { \rho = 1 } ^ { r } \bar { \phi } _ { j \rho } d a _ { \rho } , d t _ { 0 } = - \sum _ { \rho = 1 } ^ { r } \bar { \phi } _ { n \rho } d a _ { \rho } ;
$$

the formulas still contain $t _ { 0 } , c _ { 1 } , . . . , c _ { n - 1 } , a _ { 1 } , . . . , a _ { r } ,$ but the parameters $a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { r }$ are now considered independent variables. This happens when the parameters $a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { r }$ vary slowly. An invariant expression of the system of differential equations of the form (l33）which were obtained by taking the mean values is called an adiabatic invariant (Levi-Civita, 1928,1928a; Kneser,1924; Graf,1936; Mineur,1937; Mattioli, 1932; Geppert, 1928,1929;Lenard,1959).In order that

$$
J ( t _ { 0 } , c _ { 1 } , . . . , c _ { n - 1 } , a _ { 1 } , . . . , a _ { r } )
$$

shall be an adiabatic invariant, it must satisfy the condition

$$
\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \frac { \partial J } { \partial c _ { j } } \bar { \phi } _ { j \rho } - \frac { \partial J } { \partial t _ { 0 } } \bar { \phi } _ { n \rho } + \frac { \partial J } { \partial a _ { \rho } } = 0 \qquad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

If一 referring to (l34)- we can show that the differential coefficients of all orders with respect to $t$ of the mean values defined by (132) are zero,then the function is said to be invariant in the mean with regard to (128),where the word “in the mean" is taken in the sense of Section 2.2. According to the definition commonly accepted by physicists an adiabatic invariant is a function of the integration constants and the parameters of (l28) that is invariant in the mean for all values of

$$
d a _ { \rho } / d t \qquad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

From such a definition, we obtain,

$$
\frac { d J } { d t } = \sum _ { \rho = 1 } ^ { r } \bigg ( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \frac { \partial J } { \partial c _ { i } } \phi _ { i \rho } - \frac { \partial J } { \partial t _ { 0 } } \phi _ { n \rho } + \frac { \partial J } { \partial a _ { \rho } } \bigg ) \frac { d a _ { \rho } } { d t } .
$$

As $J$ does not depend on $t$ explicitly, this definition requires the validity of (134). This notion was conceived by Ehrenfest (19l7) and developed by Burgers (l9l7). It deals with invariance in the mean rather than ordinary invariance. An external electromagnetic field, for example, in quantum mechanics varies very slowly and slightly compared to variations within the atom itself, so we can quantize with respect to action variables which are considered to be adiabatically invariant.

The mean value defined by (l32) depends on $t _ { 0 }$ . Now suppose that it does not. Then the domain $T _ { \ast }$ also,does not depend on $t _ { 0 }$ .We consider with Levi-Civita (l928) the mean value taken over the infinite space

$$
[ J ] = \operatorname* { l i m } _ { \tau \to \infty } \Bigg \{ \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t _ { 0 } + T } J F d t \Bigg / \int _ { t _ { 0 } } ^ { t _ { 0 } + T } F d t \Bigg \} .
$$

The definition of adiabatic invariants is narrower,and the adiabatic invariant $\boldsymbol { J }$ must identically satisfy the condition

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \frac { \partial J } { \partial c _ { i } } \left[ \phi _ { i \rho } \right] + \frac { \partial J } { \partial a _ { \rho } } = 0 \qquad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

As any function of one or more than one adiabatic invariants is also an adiabatic invariant,our problem is to find independent particular solutions of the equations (l36).The number of such solutions is at most $n - 1$ and they express the relation between the parameters and

$$
c _ { 1 } , \ldots , c _ { n - 1 } .
$$

In particular, an integral $f _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) = c _ { i }$ independent of the parameters is an adiabatic invariant.

The first $n - 1$ integrals (l29） determine the trajectory $s$ in the $\pmb { n }$ -dimensional space.Suppose that $f _ { i }$ is single-valued or at least a finitenumber-valued function along $S _ { i }$ ,and suppose in addition that,if the domain $_ { T }$ of $t$ over which to take the mean extends to infinity $f _ { i }$ has the property of ergodicity - because we have no information as to its behavior in the infinite domain. That is,almost all trajectories defined by (128) fill up densely a certain continuum $\Phi$ .The words “ almost all" are used (in the sense of point-set theory) to mean that we exclude such values of the integration constants as the Lebesgue measure zero (Sections 2.9,2.l9).As we shall see later,Poincaré and Carathéodory proved that such a situation occurs when the trajectories are in a domain of $R _ { n }$ with finite measure,and the last multiplier $M$ for (128) is either positive or zero (at most) at points of measure zero in that domain. This is called the quasi-ergodic theorem (see Dragoni, 1934,1934a).

If we make this additional assumption, then the trajectory fills up densely the $( n - m )$ -dimensional continuum $\Phi$ if $_ m$ of the $\pmb { n }$ equations (l29) are single-valued (or, at most, finite-number-valued) along the whole trajectory and the remaining $n - m$ equations are infinite-numbervalued. For convenience we assume that $\Phi$ is a closed continuum. The number $_ { n }$ is called the primitivity order.When $m = 0$ ,the space $\Phi$ is called primitive. By this assumption of quasi-ergodicity in topology，we can replace the operations extending over $t$ of (135) by the operations with respect to the space $\Phi$

Consider the case $m = 1$ . There is only one integral,

$$
f ( x _ { 1 } , . ~ . ~ . , x _ { n } , a _ { 1 } , . ~ . ~ . , a _ { \tau } ) = c ,
$$

and it determines an $( n - 1 )$ -dimensional hypersurface $\Phi$ . Assume that ${ \pmb F } =$ constant. Suppose that an element $d \Phi _ { 0 }$ of $\Phi$ at $t = t _ { 0 }$ is displaced to $d \Phi _ { 1 }$ at $t = t _ { 1 }$ ,and define a single-valued density function $\pmb { \kappa }$ such that $\kappa _ { 0 } d \Phi _ { 0 } = \kappa _ { 1 } d \Phi _ { 1 }$ . The quantity $\kappa _ { 0 }$ is the value of $\pmb { \kappa }$ where $t = t _ { 0 } { \mathrm { : } }$ and $\kappa _ { 1 }$ is the value where $t = t _ { 1 }$ .There is one and only one function $\pmb { \kappa }$ with this property. For, if there were another single-valued function $\lambda$ with this property， then $\kappa d \Phi$ and $\lambda d \Phi$ would be invariant because of (l28). $\mathrm { o } \mathfrak { n }$ the assumption that $f$ is a finite-number-valued function, the ratio $\kappa { : } \lambda$ ,being an integral of (l28),should be a function of $f ,$ that is, should be equal to a constant on the hypersurface $\Phi$ .Hence, there is only one such $\pmb { \kappa }$

Now consider besides $f = c$ another hypersurface $f = c + d c$ and call it $\Phi ^ { \prime }$ .Let $P ^ { \prime }$ be the point of intersection of the normal to $\Phi$ at a point $P$ of $\Phi$ with the hypersurface $\Phi ^ { \prime }$ . The direction cosines of the normal are

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \Gamma _ { 1 n } } \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { i } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) , } \\ { \displaystyle } \\ { \Gamma _ { 1 n } ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \Big ( \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { i } } \Big ) ^ { 2 } . } \end{array}
$$

If we denote a line element of the normal by $d ^ { \prime } n$ ,then $\Gamma _ { 1 n } d ^ { \prime } n = d c _ { 3 }$ and the components of $d ^ { \prime } n$ are

$$
d ^ { \prime } x _ { i } = { \frac { 1 } { \Gamma _ { 1 n } ^ { 2 } } } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { i } } } d c \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Let the volume element situated between $\Phi$ and $\Phi ^ { \prime }$ be $d \tau = d \Phi d ^ { \prime } n$ · As the trajectories are on this hypersurface, by (l28), then

$$
d \tau = d \Phi _ { 1 } d ^ { \prime } n _ { 1 } .
$$

Hence,

$$
\kappa d \Phi d c = \kappa \Gamma _ { 1 n } d \tau = \kappa _ { 1 } d \Phi _ { 1 } d c = [ \kappa \Gamma _ { 1 n } ] _ { 1 } d \tau _ { 1 } ,
$$

that is, $M d \tau = M _ { 1 } d \tau _ { 1 } ,$ where $M = \kappa  { \Gamma _ { 1 n } }$ . In other words, $\int M d \tau$ is an integral invariant with regard to (l28). Hence, $M$ is the last multiplier by the theorem in Section 4.18 due to Poincaré and must satisfy

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { \frac { \partial M } { \partial x _ { i } } } X _ { i } + M \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } { \frac { \partial X _ { i } } { \partial x _ { i } } } = 0 .
$$

Hence, if $M$ is the last multiplierof (128), then $\kappa = M / \Gamma _ { 1 n }$

Assume here that the solution of (l37） is finite-number-valued and does not become zero on $\Phi$ except at a finite number of points and that its sign is positive; then, instead of (l35),we have

$$
[ J ] = \int _ { \Phi } J { \frac { M } { \Gamma _ { 1 n } } } d \Phi \biggl / \int _ { \Phi } { \frac { M } { \Gamma _ { 1 n } } } d \Phi .
$$

If $F \not \equiv 1$ , then

$$
[ J ] = \int _ { \Phi } J F \frac { M } { \Gamma _ { 1 n } } d \Phi \biggl / \int _ { \Phi } F \frac { M } { \Gamma _ { 1 n } } d \Phi .
$$

Hence, the adiabatic invariant (l36) can be writen

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { \partial J } { \partial c } \displaystyle \int _ { \Phi } \frac { \partial f } { \partial a _ { o } } F \frac { M } { \Gamma _ { 1 n } } d \Phi + \frac { \partial J } { \partial a _ { o } } \displaystyle \int _ { \Phi } F \frac { M } { \Gamma _ { 1 n } } d \Phi = 0 } \\ { ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) . } \end{array}
$$

Geppert (1928,l929),by studying the condition for integrability of this equation,derived the necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of adiabatic invariants in a dynamical system of primitivity order $m = 1$ to be

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { [ \frac { \partial f } { \partial a _ { \rho } } \frac { \partial \log M F } { \partial a _ { \lambda } } ] - [ \frac { \partial f } { \partial a _ { \rho } } ] [ \frac { \partial \log M F } { \partial a _ { \lambda } } ] } } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { = [ \frac { \partial f } { \partial a _ { \lambda } } \frac { \partial \log M F } { \partial a _ { \rho } } ] - [ \frac { \partial f } { \partial a _ { \lambda } } ] [ \frac { \partial \log M F } { \partial a _ { \rho } } ] } \\ & { } & { ~ ( \lambda , \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) . } \end{array}
$$

In particular, if $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \partial X _ { i } / \partial x _ { i } = 1 } \end{array}$ ， the dynamical system is called Liouville's； the $\pmb { n } .$ dimensional volume $V = \int d x _ { 1 } , \ldots , d x _ { n }$ is now an integral invariant of the system of differential equations. It occurs in the motion of an incompressible fluid.In this case $M =$ constant and $F = 1$ · Such a dynamical system has been studied by Gibbs and Hertz in analytical dynamics and statistical mechanics.We can prove that an adiabatic invariant exists by taking the mean value. In general, when MF does not depend on the parameters $a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { r }$ we call it the general Liouville case； there then exists an adiabatic invariant.Hence, there exists an integral invariant

$$
V = \int _ { \Phi } M F d x _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n }
$$

extended over the inside of the hypersurface $\Phi$ . In the general Liouville case,because of (l40),equations (l38) can be written in the form

$$
\frac { \partial J } { \partial c } \frac { \partial V } { \partial a _ { \rho } } - \frac { \partial J } { \partial a _ { \rho } } \frac { \partial V } { \partial c } = 0 \qquad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

Thus

$$
J = V = \int M F d x _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n }
$$

is an adiabatic invariant in the general Liouville case.

For a general value of $m$ , if there exists an independent integral

$$
f _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ; a _ { 1 } , . . . , a _ { r } ) = c _ { i } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

then,by a similar argument, the density function $\kappa$ is $M / \Gamma _ { m n }$ ，where $\Gamma _ { m n } ^ { 2 }$ is the square of the matrix $( \partial ( f _ { 1 } , . . . , f _ { m } ) / \partial ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { m } ) )$ ; and the differential equations for the adiabatic invariants corresponding to (138) are written

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { m } \frac { \partial J } { \partial c _ { i } } \int _ { \Phi } \frac { \partial f _ { i } } { \partial a _ { \rho } } F \frac M { \Gamma _ { m n } } d \Phi + \frac { \partial J } { \partial a _ { \rho } } \int _ { \Phi } \frac M { \Gamma _ { m n } } d \Phi = 0 } \\ { ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) . } \end{array}
$$

Now consider the Hamiltonian canonical equations

$$
\frac { d q _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial p _ { i } } , \frac { d \phi _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial q _ { i } } ~ ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

where $H$ does not contain $t$ explicitly. There exists the integral $H = h$ Assume that there exists no integral other than $H = h$ and that the hypersurface $H = h$ is closed and that the dynamical system is quasiergodic (as is evident from topological transformations of the motion of molecules in a cube - see Section 2.9).Then the integral $\int F M d x _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot d x _ { n }$ extended over the volume in the phase space $\Phi _ { 2 n }$ bounded by $H = h$ is an adiabatic invariant；in particular, if $F =$ constant in a Liouville dynamical system, then the volumeis an integralinvariant.If,inaddition, (142) has $m$ integrals $f _ { i } = c _ { i }$ mutually in involution, then

$$
( f _ { \alpha } , f _ { \beta } ) = 0 ( \alpha , \beta = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

and

$$
( f _ { \alpha } , H ) = 0 \qquad ( \alpha = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

Let the $_ m$ solutions of ${ \dot { f } } _ { i } = c _ { i }$ with respect to the $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { \alpha }$ be

$$
\qquad p _ { \alpha } = p _ { \alpha } ( { p } _ { m + 1 } , \ldots , { p } _ { n } , q _ { 1 } , \ldots , q _ { n } , c _ { 1 } , \ldots , c _ { m } ) \qquad ( \alpha = 1 , 2 , \ldots , m )
$$

and the result of the substitution of these equations in $\boldsymbol { H }$ be $\mathfrak { S }$ By Lie's theorem (Section 4.8), the canonical equations (l42) are reduced to the differential equations of order $2 ( n - m )$ ：

$$
\frac { d q _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial \underline { { \sf { S } } } } { \partial \underline { { \theta } } _ { i } } , \frac { d \underline { { \phi } } _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial \underline { { \sf { S } } } } { \partial q _ { i } } ( i = m + 1 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

and $q _ { \alpha }$ are obtained from

$$
{ \frac { d q _ { \alpha } } { d t } } = { \frac { \partial \Im } { \partial / _ { \alpha } } } \qquad ( \alpha = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

We call such a $2 ( n - m )$ -dimensional space the reduced phase space. If the reduced phase space is closed and the dynamical system is Liouville's, then the volume $W ( h , c _ { 1 } , \ldots , c _ { m } )$ of this phase space is an integral invariant. If $H$ and $f _ { \alpha }$ both contain the parameters $a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { r } ,$ and hence, $\mathfrak { H }$ contains $2 m + r$ parameters $q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { m } ; c _ { 1 } , . . . , c _ { m } ; a _ { 1 } , . . . , a _ { r } ,$ all of which vary slowly, and if the trajectory is quasi-ergodic in the $2 ( n - m )$ 1 dimensional closed space, then the volume $W ( h , c _ { 1 } , \ldots , c _ { r } )$ of the reduced phase space is an adiabatic invariant. Since $H = h$ ，we rewrite it $f _ { 0 } = c _ { 0 }$ ; then

$$
( f _ { \alpha } , f _ { \beta } ) = 0 ( \alpha , \beta = 0 , 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

Let the result of substitution of these $\pmb { m }$ integrals for $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { m }$ in the $m + 1$ functions $f _ { \alpha }$ be $F _ { \alpha }$ ; then,

$$
{ \frac { d q _ { i } } { d t } } = { \frac { \partial F _ { \alpha } } { \partial p _ { i } } } , { \frac { d p _ { i } } { d t } } = - { \frac { \partial F _ { \alpha } } { \partial q _ { i } } } \qquad ( i = m + 1 , . . . , n ; \alpha = 0 , 1 , . . . , m )
$$

All these equations give the same trajectory. There exist adiabatic invariants $W _ { 1 } , . . . , W _ { m }$ ，which are the respective $2 ( n - m )$ -dimensional volumes corresponding to $F _ { 1 } , . . . , F _ { m }$ taken for $F _ { 0 }$ or $\mathfrak { S }$ in the above discussion.Hence, such a dynamical system given by canonical equations of primitivity order $m + 1$ has $m + 1$ adiabatic invariants,if the $_ m$ independent integrals are mutually in involution.

A special case $n = m + 1$ contains the case in which the solution can be obtained by separation of the variables according to Stäckel's theorem. Suppose that the functions $\phi _ { \alpha \beta }$ (which appeared in our proof of Stäckel's theorem in Section 1.16） are functions of $q _ { \beta }$ only.Then we have $\pmb { n }$ integrals

$$
F _ { \alpha } = \sum _ { \beta = 0 } ^ { n - 1 } \phi ^ { \alpha \beta } \bigl ( { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \beta _ { \beta } ^ { 2 } - U _ { \beta } \bigr ) \qquad ( \alpha = 0 , 1 , . . . , n - 1 ) ,
$$

where $U _ { \beta }$ is a function of a single variable $q _ { \beta }$ only,and this function $F _ { \alpha }$ is a function of a single variable $q _ { \alpha }$ only.Hence the $F _ { \alpha }$ are mutually in involution. The reduced phase space is now a plane, and there exist $\pmb { n }$ adiabatic invariants

$$
W _ { i } = \int / p _ { i } d q _ { i } \qquad ( i = 0 , 1 , . . . , n - 1 ) .
$$

This proves that the action in quantum mechanics is an adiabatic invariant (Section 2.7). The proof does not necessitate our assuming that the periods of the component oscillations are mutually in irrational ratios as in the case studied by Burgers (l9l7).Thus we have proved the fundamental theorem of quantum mechanics. We have assumed quasiergodicity in our proof; this assumed property is valid in any Liouville dynamical system,as will be shown in a later chapter on the fundamentals of the statistical mechanics for Boltzmann's theory.We have proved that the coordinates and the momenta in Stäckel's case are expressed by quasi-periodic functions (Section 2.lO).Hence, the motion in this case is quasi-periodic.

Lenard (l959） has shown that a positive constant $M > 0$ can be found such that,for a change $\Delta Q$ of anadiabaticinvariant $Q , | \Delta Q | < \epsilon ^ { n } M$ for all small values of $\epsilon$ ; he states that the adiabatic invariant is of the nth order. There are many such invariants that are known to be constant to a low order but are actually so to all orders. Kulsrud (1957) proved this proposition for the harmonic oscilltor and Lenard (i959) for nonlinear oscillators.

The theory of adiabatic invariants has been applied to the fall of meteoric swarms or cosmic dust,and to the motion of celestial bodies of varying mass as affected by the ejection or accretion of their masses (Levi-Civita,1934).

# 4.24ADIABATIC APPROXIMATION

A function $\textstyle J ( p , q , \lambda )$ was called by Arnold (1963) an adiabatic invariant of a dynamical system with the Hamiltonian

$$
H ( p , q , \lambda ) \qquad ( p = p _ { 1 } , p _ { 2 } , . . . , p _ { n } ; q = q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , . . . , q _ { n } ) ,
$$

on the following condition: for any $\kappa > 0$ there exists a $\mu _ { 0 } > 0$ ，such that, if $0 < \mu < \mu _ { 0 } ;$ ，then for any $t$ in the interval $0 < t < 1 / \mu$ there exists the inequality $| J ( t ) - J ( 0 ) | < \kappa$

The linear oscillatory system occupies an exceptional position: the frequency of its oscillation does not depend on the amplitude,and there are well-defined resonant frequencies. In nonlinear systems the frequency changes with amplitude, and oscillations do not grow without limit as in the linear case； that is, the condition of resonance in the nonlinear case changes as the amplitude changes.

Consider a nonlinear oscillatory system with one degree of freedom with the analytic Hamiltonian function $H ( p , q , \lambda )$ depending on slow time $\lambda = \mu t$ periodically, that is, $H ( p , q , \lambda + 2 \pi ) = H ( p , q , \lambda )$ . In the zero-th approximation, $\lambda =$ constant and the system is autonomous, i.e., independent of an explicit appearance of $t .$ Also, the system is integrable. Periodic motions of the system can be described by the action $I$ and angle w. We have

$$
{ \boldsymbol { \beta } } = { \frac { \partial { \boldsymbol { S } } } { \partial { \boldsymbol { q } } } } , \qquad { \boldsymbol { w } } = { \frac { \partial { \boldsymbol { S } } } { \partial { \boldsymbol { I } } } } ,
$$

with the generating function

$$
S ( I , q ) = \int _ { H = h } ^ { q } \ d h \ d h \phi d q ,
$$

where $H _ { 0 } ( I ) = h$ is the inverse function of

$$
I ( h ) = { \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } } \int _ { H = h } \beta d q ,
$$

and $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ is the value derived from $H ( p , q ) = h$ .The variation of $\boldsymbol { I }$ and w with time is given by

$$
\dot { w } = \omega ( I ) = \frac { \partial H _ { 0 } } { \partial I } , I = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } .
$$

This approximation is valid during the time $t \sim 1$

The transformation $\begin{array} { r } { p , q  I , } \end{array}$ w being canonical, the change of $\boldsymbol { I }$ and ${ \boldsymbol w }$ is determined by the canonical equations with the Hamiltonian

$$
H ( I , w , \lambda ) = H _ { 0 } ( I ) + \mu H _ { 1 } ( I , w , \lambda ) ,
$$

where $H _ { 1 } = \partial S / \partial \lambda$ is a single-valued function of period $2 \pi$ with respect to w and $\lambda$ In the space of $\dot { p } , q , \lambda$ the equation $\boldsymbol { I } =$ constant determines a two-dimensional torus and the angular variables ${ \boldsymbol w }$ and $\lambda$ are the latitude and longitude on this torus (Figure l).In the conservative approximation $\mu = 0$ ,every point of the torus moves along the meridian $\lambda =$ constant with angular velocity $\omega ( I , \lambda )$ depending on the longitude. For $\mu \neq 0$ a slow motion $\lambda = \mu$ is added across the meridian; the motion now has two frequencies. But in the approximation the phase point remains on the invariant torus $\boldsymbol { I } =$ constant.This approximation is called adiabatic. The true motion is near the adiabatic approximation during $t \sim 1 / \mu$

![](images/56d62c7240a3dceb69548e793383a78dcdca73d5be171a4804aabd8b8ca77896.jpg)  
FIGURE I

In the linear system, $\omega ( I ) =$ constant, $H _ { 0 } ( I ) = I \omega$ In the nonlinear system,

$$
\bar { \omega } ( I ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } \oint \omega ( I , \lambda ) d \lambda = \frac { d \bar { H } _ { 0 } } { d I } , \qquad \bar { H } _ { 0 } ( I ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } \oint H _ { 0 } ( I , \lambda ) d \lambda .
$$

On the assumption that

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } \bar { H } _ { 0 } } { d I ^ { 2 } } = \frac { d \bar { \omega } } { d I } \neq 0
$$

we can prove the perpetual adiabatic invariance of $\boldsymbol { \mathit { I } }$ (Arnold, 1963).

In the case of the perturbed Hamiltonian (143) we obtain many invariant tori, real but not approximate, near the tori $\pmb { I } =$ constant, for small $\pmb { \mu }$ .They are two-dimensional and divide the three-dimensional phase space ${ p , q , \lambda }$ into thin layers.It can be proved that, if the initial point $( \ p _ { 0 } , q _ { 0 } , \lambda )$ lies between two such tori $T _ { 1 }$ and $T _ { 2 }$ ，then the entire trajectory ${ \mathfrak { p } } ( t ) , q ( t ) , \lambda ( t )$ lies wholly between $T _ { 1 }$ and $T _ { 2 }$ ; more precisely, we can find a constant $\mu _ { 0 } > 0$ for any $\kappa > 0$ such that, if $| \mu | < \mu _ { 0 } ;$ then any point $( \ p _ { 0 } , q _ { 0 } , \lambda _ { 0 } )$ lies between two invariant tori $T _ { 1 }$ and $T _ { 2 } ,$ and

$$
| I ( \phi _ { 1 } , q _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 1 } ) - I ( \phi _ { 2 } , q _ { 2 } , \lambda _ { 2 } ) | < \kappa
$$

for $\not p _ { 1 } , q _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 1 }$ belonging to $T _ { 1 }$ and $\phi _ { 2 } , q _ { 2 } ,$ $\lambda _ { 2 }$ belonging to $T _ { 2 }$

Let us introduce a new time, $T = w$ .Then the integral curves of the Hamiltonian system in space $I , w , \lambda$ are invariantly connected with the differential form

$$
I d w - H ( I , w , \mu t ) d t = - { \frac { 1 } { \mu } } ( H d \lambda - \mu I d w ) .
$$

Consider $H , \lambda ,$ w instead of $I , w ,$ $t$ as the independent variables. Solving (143) with respect to $I ,$ we obtain

$$
 I ( H , \lambda , w ) = I _ { 0 } ( H , \lambda ) + \mu I _ { 1 } ( H , \lambda , w ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

Put

$$
{ \boldsymbol { \dot { p } } } ^ { \prime } = { \boldsymbol { H } } , \qquad { \boldsymbol { q } } ^ { \prime } = \lambda , \qquad { \boldsymbol { T } } = { \boldsymbol { w } } , \qquad { \boldsymbol { K } } = \mu { \boldsymbol { I } } .
$$

Then,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { K ( \phi ^ { \prime } , q ^ { \prime } , T ) = \mu I _ { 0 } ( \phi ^ { \prime } , q ^ { \prime } ) + \mu ^ { 2 } I _ { 1 } ( \phi ^ { \prime } , q ^ { \prime } , T ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { H d \lambda - \mu I d w = \phi ^ { \prime } d q ^ { \prime } - K ( \phi ^ { \prime } , q ^ { \prime } , T ) d T , } } \end{array}
$$

so that the systems with the Hamiltonians (143) and (145) are equivalent. The variables $\pmb { P }$ and $Q$ are introduced by the canonical transformation

$$
{ \boldsymbol { \rlap / p } } ^ { \prime } = { \frac { \partial S } { \partial { \boldsymbol { q } } ^ { \prime } } } , \qquad { \boldsymbol { Q } } = { \frac { \partial S } { \partial { \boldsymbol { P } } } } ,
$$

with the generating function

$$
S ( P , q ^ { \prime } ) = \int ^ { q ^ { \prime } } H _ { 0 } ( I , \lambda ) \ d \lambda .
$$

The Hamiltonian is transformed to

$$
k ( P , Q , T ) = \mu k _ { 0 } ( P ) + \mu ^ { 2 } k _ { 1 } ( P , Q , T ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

where $I = k _ { 0 } ( P )$ is a function inverse to $H _ { 0 } ( I )$

Hence,if the frequency $\omega ( I , \lambda ) = \partial H _ { 0 } / \partial I$ does not vanish, there exist analytic functions $P , \ Q , \ T$ of the variables $I , \ w , \ \lambda$ independent of $\pmb { \mu } ,$ such that (i) $P , Q \mathrm { ~ - ~ } \lambda , T \mathrm { ~ - ~ } w$ are periodic with period $2 \pi$ with respect to ${ \pmb w }$ and $\lambda$ ； (ii) the canonical equations with the Hamiltonian (143) are equivalent to the equations

$$
\frac { d P } { d T } = - \frac { \partial k } { \partial Q } , \frac { d Q } { d T } = \frac { \partial k } { \partial P } ,
$$

with the Hamiltonian (146) which is of period $2 \pi$ with respect to both $Q$ and $_ { T }$ and analytic in the complex neighborhood of the toroidal layer $\left| P - I _ { 0 } \right| \leqslant r$ ；and (iii） the function $k _ { 0 } ( P )$ of (146） is inverse to $\textstyle { \overline { { H } } } _ { 0 } ( I )$ ， so that $\overrightarrow { H } _ { 0 } ( k _ { 0 } ( P ) ) = P$

From this theorem we can show the perpetual adiabatic invariance of action.

Theorem: Let an oscillatory system be described by the analytic Hamiltonian (l43),in terms of action-angle variables. Suppose that $\partial H _ { 0 } / \partial I \neq 0$ and $\partial ^ { 2 } H _ { 0 } / \partial I ^ { 2 } \neq 0$ in the region $\left| I - I _ { 0 } \right| \leqslant r$ everywhere. If for any $\kappa > 0$ we can find a constant $\mu _ { 0 } > 0$ such that $| \mu | < \mu _ { 0 }$ and $\left| I ( 0 ) - I _ { 0 } \right| \le r - \kappa _ { : }$ then $| I ( t ) - I ( 0 ) | < \kappa$ will hold for

$$
- \infty < t < + \infty .
$$

In fact, the system with the Hamiltonian (l46) can be reduced to a set of canonical equations with two degrees of freedom

$$
{ \frac { d P } { d \tau } } = \tau { \frac { \partial k } { \partial Q } } , { \frac { d R } { d \tau } } = - { \frac { \partial k } { \partial T } } , { \frac { d Q } { d \tau } } = { \frac { \partial k } { \partial P } } , { \frac { d T } { d \tau } } = 1 ,
$$

with'the Hamiltonian

$$
R + k ( P , Q , T ) = R + \mu k _ { 0 } ( P ) + \mu ^ { 2 } k _ { 1 } ( P , Q , T ) \cdot \cdot \cdot _ { \cdotp } 
$$

The Hamiltonian is now of the degenerateform

$$
H ( p , q ) = H _ { 0 } ( p _ { 0 } ) + \mu H _ { 1 } ( p , q ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

where $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } = ( \pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { 0 } , \pmb { \mathscr { p } } _ { 1 } )$ with a vector $\scriptstyle { \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { 0 }$ of $n _ { 0 }$ dimensions (corresponding to the fast-varying coordinates $q _ { 0 , }$ ）and a vector ${ \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { 1 }$ of $\pmb { n } _ { 1 }$ dimensions, $\boldsymbol { n } = \boldsymbol { n } _ { 0 } + \boldsymbol { n } _ { 1 }$ (corresponding to the slow-varying coordinates $q _ { 1 , }$ ).The solution with this Hamiltonian (l47) in the zero-th approximation is

$$
\begin{array} { l l r } { { \dot { \boldsymbol { { p } } } _ { 0 } = 0 , \quad } } & { { \dot { \boldsymbol { { q } } } _ { 0 } = \omega _ { 0 } ( \boldsymbol { { p } } _ { 0 } ) = \displaystyle \frac { \partial H _ { 0 } } { \partial { p _ { 0 } } } , } } \\ { { \dot { \boldsymbol { { p } } } _ { 1 } = 0 , \quad } } & { { \dot { \boldsymbol { { q } } } _ { 1 } = \mu \xi _ { 1 } ( \boldsymbol { { p } } ) = \mu \displaystyle \frac { \partial \overline { { { H } } } _ { 1 } } { \partial { p _ { 1 } } } , } } \end{array}
$$

where $\omega _ { 1 } = \mu \xi _ { 1 }$ for the slow frequencies.

Assume that the secular part of the perturbation averaged over the fast motion in the coordinates $q _ { 0 }$ ，

$$
\overline { { { H } } } _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { ( 2 \pi ) ^ { n _ { 0 } } } \oint H _ { 1 } d q _ { 0 } ,
$$

is independent of the phase $q _ { 1 }$ of the slow motion $\overline { { H } } _ { 1 } = \overline { { H } } _ { 1 } ( \mathfrak { p } _ { 1 } )$ ,and that the determinants of the frequencies with respect to momenta $\scriptstyle { \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { 0 }$ and $\pmb { \mathscr { p } _ { 1 } }$ do not become identically zero; that is,

$$
\left\| \frac { \partial \omega _ { 0 } } { \partial { \boldsymbol { \beta } } _ { 0 } } \right\| = \left\| \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } { \boldsymbol { H } } _ { 0 } } { \partial \boldsymbol { \beta } _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \right\| \not \equiv 0 , \qquad \left\| \frac { \partial \xi _ { 1 } } { \partial \boldsymbol { \beta } _ { 1 } } \right\| = \left\| \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \boldsymbol { \bar { H } } _ { 1 } } { \partial \boldsymbol { \beta } _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } \right\| \not \equiv 0 .
$$

Arnold (1963) has proved that for sufficiently small $\left| \mu \right|$ for the majority of initial conditions - that is, all except for sets of Lebesgue measure zero 一 the motion determined by the canonical equations with the

Hamiltonian (l47） is conditionally periodic (Section 2.7） and，for $- \infty < t < + \infty$ ,is near the conditionally periodic motion (l48).This is, according to Arnold, because almost every vector

$$
\omega = ( \omega _ { 1 } , \omega _ { 2 } , . . . , \omega _ { n } )
$$

satisfies the inequalities

$$
| ( \omega , k ) | \geq K | k | ^ { - \nu } \qquad ( | k | = | k _ { 1 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | k _ { n } | ; \nu = n + 1 )
$$

for all integers $k \neq 0$ for some $K ( \omega ) > 0$ ,as will be discussed in a later chapter.

Thus we come to the following conclusion: If the function (l46） is analytic for $| \mu | < \bar { \mu }$ in the region

$$
| \Im Q , T | \le \rho , \qquad | P - P _ { 0 } | \le r
$$

and in this region

$$
\left| k _ { 0 } \right| \le M , \qquad \left| k _ { 1 } + ( \mu ) \cdot \cdot \cdot \right| \le M , \qquad \left| \frac { d ^ { 2 } k _ { 0 } } { d P _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \right| \ge \theta > 0 ,
$$

then for any $\kappa > 0$ we can find a constant $\mu _ { 0 } ( \kappa , \bar { \mu } , r , \rho , M , \theta ) > 0$ with the property that if $| \mu | < \mu _ { 0 } ;$ ，then the real toroidal layer $F _ { : }$ ，where $\left| P - P _ { 0 } \right| \le r ,$ is filled with invariant tori with accuracy up to the remainder of measure smaller than $\kappa m ( F )$ , and each of these invariant tori is located at a distance less than $\kappa$ from some torus $P =$ constant. Hence the theorem on the perpetual adiabatic invariance of action is proved.

We confine ourselves for the moment to a conservative dynamical system with two degrees of freedom $X ,$ ，Y.Assume that the change of the coordinate $X$ only slightly affects the state of the system; then the system can be regarded as having approximately one degree of freedom but depending on the slowly varying parameter $\mu X .$ .The action variable $I _ { Y }$ takes the part of an adiabatic invariant.

Lemma : Let $X , ~ Y , ~ P _ { x } , ~ P _ { \mathrm { { y } } }$ be canonically conjugate variables,and consider the Hamiltonian $H ( \mu X , Y , P _ { X } , P _ { Y } )$ .If $\mu$ is small, then the state of the system changes little during the variation of $\pmb { X }$ by a first-order quantity. If we fix $\pmb { X }$ and $P _ { x }$ ，then the Hamiltonian is of the form $H _ { \mathrm { Y } } ( Y , P _ { \mathrm { Y } } )$ , and the action variable is $I _ { \mathbf { Y } } ( Y , P _ { \mathbf { Y } } )$ . The value of $I _ { \tt Y }$ depends also on $\mu X$ and $P _ { x }$ 、For the Hamiltonian $H ( \mu X , Y ; P _ { X } , P _ { Y } )$ , the action $I _ { Y }$ is an adiabatic invariant in the sense that the change of

$$
I _ { Y } [ \mu X ( t ) , Y ( t ) , P _ { X } ( t ) , P _ { Y } ( t ) ]
$$

during the time $t \sim 1 / \mu$ is small,and of order $\mu$ . This Hamiltonian can be written $H = H _ { x } ( \mu X , P _ { X } , I _ { Y } )$ . For an approximation the system is described by the Hamiltonian $H _ { x } ( \mu X , P _ { x } )$ . If it is periodic with one degree of freedom, then we introduce the action variable $I _ { x } \sim 1 / \mu$ .In the adiabatic approximation, the motion consists of fast oscillations of $Y$ with frequence $\omega _ { Y }$ and slow oscillations of $X$ with frequency ${ \pmb { \omega } } _ { { \pmb { X } } }$ of order $\mu$ ,in which $I _ { x }$ and $\mu I _ { x }$ are conserved.

Suppose that the Hamiltonian is reduced to the form (145).That $I _ { Y }$ and $\mu I _ { x }$ are perpetual adiabatic invariants is easily concluded from the existence of two-dimensional invariant tori, since these tori divide the three-dimensional energy level $H = h$

Let the Hamiltonian function $H ( x , Y , P _ { x } , P _ { y } )$ ，where $x = \mu X ,$ be analytic and,for a fixed $_ x$ , let the momenta $P _ { x }$ determine the oscillatory system with the variables $I _ { \mathtt { Y } } ( x , P _ { x } , h )$ and $w _ { \mathrm { Y } } ( x , Y , P _ { x } , P _ { \mathrm { Y } } )$ . Then there exists an analytic substitution, changing $x , Y , P _ { x } , P _ { y }$ to

$$
x ^ { \prime } , w ^ { \prime } , P ^ { \prime } , I ^ { \prime }
$$

such that: (i) the functions o $\mathrm { f } \ x , \ Y , P _ { x } , P _ { \mathrm { y } }$ admit period $2 \pi$ with regard to ${ \boldsymbol w }$ ，and for $\mu \to 0$ the variables $x ^ { \prime } , \ w ^ { \prime } , \ P ^ { \prime } , \ I ^ { \prime }$ are transformed to $x , w _ { \mathrm { Y } } , P _ { x } , I _ { \mathrm { Y } }$ ； (ii) along the integral curves of the canonical equations

$$
\frac { d P _ { X } } { d t } = \triangledown - \frac { \partial H } { \partial X } , \frac { d P _ { Y } } { d t } = \triangledown - \frac { \partial H } { \partial Y } , \frac { d X } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial P _ { X } } , \frac { d Y } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial P _ { Y } } ,
$$

the canonical equations

$$
{ \frac { d P ^ { \prime } } { d w ^ { \prime } } } = - { \frac { \partial K } { \partial x ^ { \prime } } } , \qquad { \frac { d x ^ { \prime } } { d w ^ { \prime } } } = { \frac { \partial K } { \partial P ^ { \prime } } }
$$

are satisfied with the Hamiltonian $K ( P ^ { \prime } , x ^ { \prime } , w ^ { \prime } , h )$ depending on the parameter $h$ ; and (ii) the function $\kappa$ is of the form $K = - \mu I ^ { \prime }$ where

$$
{ \cal I } ^ { \prime } = { \cal I } _ { 0 } ( P ^ { \prime } , x ^ { \prime } , h ) + \mu { \cal I } _ { 1 } ( P ^ { \prime } , x ^ { \prime } , w ^ { \prime } , h ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot
$$

is analytic with period $2 \pi$ with respect to $w ^ { \prime }$ and

$$
I _ { 0 } ( P ^ { \prime } , x ^ { \prime } , h ) = I _ { Y } ( x ^ { \prime } , P ^ { \prime } , h ) .
$$

From $H ( x , Y , P _ { X } , P _ { Y } ) = h _ { ! }$ we have

$$
P _ { \mathrm { Y } } = P _ { \mathrm { Y } } ( x , P _ { X } , Y , h ) .
$$

Assume that $\begin{array} { r } { 2 \pi I _ { \tt Y } ( { \boldsymbol { x } } , { \boldsymbol { P } } _ { \boldsymbol { x } } , h ) = \oint P _ { \tt Y } d X } \end{array}$ ；then this relation determines $h ( x , P _ { x } , I _ { \mathrm { Y } } )$ . The generating function $P ^ { \prime } X + S ( x , P ^ { \prime } , Y , I ^ { \prime } )$ , where

$$
S = \int ^ { \tau } P _ { \mathrm { { r } } } [ x , P ^ { \prime } , Y , h ( x , P ^ { \prime } , I ^ { \prime } ) ] d Y ,
$$

gives the canonical transformation $X , Y , P _ { x } , P _ { y }  X ^ { \prime } , w ^ { \prime } , P ^ { \prime } , I ^ { \prime } ;$ according to the formulas

$$
X ^ { \prime } = X + \frac { \partial S } { \partial P ^ { \prime } } , \qquad w ^ { \prime } = \frac { \partial S } { \partial I ^ { \prime } } , \qquad P _ { X } = P ^ { \prime } + \mu \frac { \partial S } { \partial x } , \qquad P _ { Y } = \frac { \partial S } { \partial Y } .
$$

Assume that $\scriptstyle x ^ { \prime } = \mu X ^ { \prime }$ . Since

$$
H \{ x , Y , P ^ { \prime } , P _ { Y } [ x , P ^ { \prime } , Y , h ( x , P ^ { \prime } , I ^ { \prime } ) ] \} = h ( x , P ^ { \prime } , I ^ { \prime } ) ,
$$

then

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { H \{ x , Y , P _ { X } , P _ { Y } [ x , P ^ { \prime } , Y , h ( x , P ^ { \prime } , I ^ { \prime } ) ] \} = h ( x , P ^ { \prime } , I ^ { \prime } ) } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { \qquad + \ \mu \frac { \partial S } { \partial x } \frac { \partial H } { \partial P _ { X } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot . . } } \end{array}
$$

Substituting the new variables for $\pmb { X }$ and $\pmb { Y } _ { : }$ ，we obtain

$$
H ( x , Y , P _ { X } , P _ { Y } ) = h ( x ^ { \prime } , P ^ { \prime } , I ^ { \prime } ) + \mu \frac { \partial S } { \partial x } \frac { \partial H } { \partial P _ { X } } - \mu \frac { \partial S } { \partial P ^ { \prime } } \frac { \partial H } { \partial x } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

or

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { H ( x , Y , P _ { X } , P _ { Y } ) = H ^ { \prime } ( x ^ { \prime } , P ^ { \prime } , I ^ { \prime } , w ^ { \prime } ) } \\ & { \qquad = h _ { 0 } ( x ^ { \prime } , P ^ { \prime } , I ^ { \prime } ) + \mu h _ { 1 } ( x ^ { \prime } , P ^ { \prime } , I ^ { \prime } , w ^ { \prime } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } \end{array}
$$

where $\mu h _ { 1 } + \cdots$ is analytic, with period $2 \pi$ with respect to $w ^ { \prime }$

Taking $P ^ { \prime } , X ^ { \prime } , - H , t , w ^ { \prime }$ ,instead of $P ^ { \prime } , X ^ { \prime } , I ^ { \prime } , w ^ { \prime } , t$ as the new independent variables,and taking $\boldsymbol { h }$ as a parameter and $w ^ { \prime }$ as time, gives the new Hamiltonian as $I ^ { \prime } ( X ^ { \prime } , P ^ { \prime } , w ^ { \prime } , h )$ , where $\scriptstyle { I ^ { \prime } }$ is determined by

$$
H ^ { \prime } ( \mu X ^ { \prime } , P ^ { \prime } , I ^ { \prime } , w ^ { \prime } ) = h .
$$

The coordinate $t$ is cyclical. By dropping $- \pmb { H }$ and $t$ we obtain a nonautonomous system with one degree of freedom.Multiply $X ^ { \prime }$ and $- \boldsymbol { I ^ { \prime } }$ by the constant $\mu$ ，giving $\mu X ^ { \prime } = x ^ { \prime }$ and $- \mu I ^ { \prime } = K .$ ，The derivatives of $\scriptstyle { \boldsymbol { x } } ^ { \prime }$ and $P ^ { \prime }$ with respect to $w ^ { \prime }$ are determined by the canonical equations (149)with the Hamiltonian $\kappa$

These forms of $\boldsymbol { \mathit { \Pi } } _ { H }$ and $\scriptstyle { H ^ { \prime } }$ show that $\scriptstyle { { \cal { I } } ^ { \prime } }$ is of the form (150); thus the lemma is proved.

According to (150),the Hamiltonian $\kappa$ is of the form (145). By our assumption, the inequalities (l44) are satisfied. Consequently the argument in the proof of perpetual adiabatic invariance can be applied. Hence the existence of the invariant tori and the perpetual adiabatic invariance of $I _ { Y }$ and $\mu I _ { x }$ are proved,on the assumption that the mean value $\overline { { ( \omega _ { \mathbf { Y } } / \omega _ { X } ) } }$ of the ratio $\omega _ { Y } / \omega _ { X }$ depends on $I _ { \tt Y }$ for a fixed total energy $h$

Thus the method of adiabatic approximation is justified in this case of two degrees of freedom.

Arnold applied the idea to the problem of magnetic traps, that is, the motion of a charged particle in a magnetic field.Assume that the instantaneous radius of the helix along which the particle moves is small compared with the distance at which the field changes by a noticeable amount.Arnold has shown that in axially-symmetric magnetic traps the adiabatic invariant $W _ { \perp } / B$ is conserved perpetually， where $B$ is the magnetic field intensity and $W _ { \perp }$ is the kinetic energy of the motion transverse to the field.

Suppose that the magnetic field $\boldsymbol { B }$ is derived from the vector potential $A _ { , }$ ，which has only one component $A _ { \phi } = A ( r , z )$ in polar coordinates. Then the intensity is

$$
B _ { \tau } = - { \frac { \partial A } { \partial z } } , B _ { z } = { \frac { 1 } { r } } { \frac { \partial ( r A ) } { \partial r } } ,
$$

and the lines of force are given by $r A =$ constant and $\phi =$ constant. The Lagrangian function for unit charge and unit mass is

$$
L = { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ( \dot { r } ^ { 2 } + \dot { z } ^ { 2 } + r ^ { 2 } \dot { \phi } ^ { 2 } ) + r A \dot { \phi } ,
$$

and the Hamiltonian is

$$
H = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \rlap { / } p _ { \tau } ^ { 2 } + \rlap { / } p _ { z } ^ { 2 } + \frac { ( \rlap { / } p _ { \phi } - r A ) ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } \right] .
$$

Since $\phi$ is cyclical, the value of $\hbar _ { \phi } = M$ is constant. There remains to be studied the plane motion in the field with potential

$$
U ( r , z ) = \frac { ( M - r A ) ^ { 2 } } { 2 r ^ { 2 } } .
$$

This potential represents a potential ditch with zero bottom along the line of force $r A = M .$ In the neighborhood of this line we have

$$
U ( r , z ) = { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } B ^ { 2 } y ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

where $y$ is the distance to a line of force.

We introduce the coordinates $_ x$ and $y$ by

$$
d r ^ { 2 } + d z ^ { 2 } = [ 1 + y k ( x ) ] ^ { 2 } d x ^ { 2 } + d y ^ { 2 } ,
$$

where $k ( x )$ is the curvature of the line of force at the point $( x , 0 )$ . The new Hamiltonian is

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { H = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left. \frac { \rlap / p _ { x } ^ { 2 } } { [ 1 \ + y k ( x ) ] ^ { 2 } } + \rlap / p _ { y } ^ { 2 } \right. + U , } } \\ { { U ( x , y ) = \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } B ( x ) y ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot . . } } \end{array}
$$

![](images/614368bc3e56be54c3ce116ea375867a35e9cba193a69e3f5beff8c106d30fb7.jpg)  
FIGURE 2

We are interested in the case in which the radius of the Larmor spiral described by the charge around the lines of force is very small compared with the characteristic dimension of the field；that is,we consider the values of $M$ and the total energy $h$ such that $U \leq h$ determines a strip of width $\sim \mu$ around the line of force $r A = M$ (Figure 2). Introduce new variables $X , Y , P _ { x } , P _ { \mathrm { y } }$ by $x = \mu X , y = \mu Y , \dot { p } _ { x } = \mu P _ { x } , \dot { p } _ { y } = \mu P _ { x }$ ；then the Hamiltonian becomes

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle H ^ { \prime } = \frac 1 2 \left\{ \frac { P _ { X } ^ { 2 } } { [ 1 + \mu Y k ( \mu X ) ] ^ { 2 } } + P _ { Y } ^ { 2 } \right\} + U ^ { \prime } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle I ^ { \prime } ( \mu X , Y ) = \frac 1 2 B ^ { 2 } ( \mu X ) Y ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot . } } \end{array}
$$

or

$$
H ^ { \prime } = \frac { P _ { X } ^ { 2 } + P _ { Y } ^ { 2 } + B ^ { 2 } ( x ^ { 2 } ) Y ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + \mu H _ { 1 } ( x , Y , P _ { X } , P _ { Y } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot
$$

of the form similar to the foregoing.

We select the initial values of $y , \dot { x } , \dot { y }$ in such a manner that $H \sim \mu ^ { 2 }$ ， $\mu \ll 1$ ,and $y , { \dot { x } } , { \dot { y } } \sim \mu$ We fix $Y , { \dot { X } } , { \dot { Y } } ,$ choose $y = \mu Y , \dot { x } = \mu \dot { X } , \dot { y } = \mu \dot { Y } ,$ and put $\mu$ equal to zero. If the magnetic field is analytic and $B ( x ) > 0 _ { ; }$ ， $B ( { \pmb x } )  \infty$ for $| x |  \infty$ , then for any $\kappa > 0$ we have $\mu _ { 0 } > 0$ such that, if $| \mu | < \mu _ { 0 }$ ，then $| I _ { \mathrm { Y } } ( t ) - I _ { \mathrm { Y } } ( 0 ) | < \kappa$ for all $- \infty < t < + \infty$ ，where $I _ { Y } = ( P _ { Y } ^ { 2 } + B ^ { 2 } Y ^ { 2 } ) / ( 2 B )$ Hence,a particle forwhich ${ { I } _ { Y } } \ne 0$ is locked in a bounded region if the field $B$ grows indefinitely as $x \to \infty$ .This is the phenomenon of the trap with magnetic mirrors.We see that when $Y = 0$ ，then $\dot { \phi } = 0$ and ${ \dot { y } } ^ { 2 } / 2 = W _ { \perp } ,$ so that $\mu ^ { 2 } I _ { \mathrm { Y } } = W _ { \perp } / B$ .Hence,in an axially-symmetric magnetic trap,the magnetic moment $W _ { \perp } / B$ isa perpetual adiabatic invariant.

This picture of the magnetic trap applies to the motion of charged particles around the geomagnetic lines of force in the Van Allen belt, or that of charged particles along the magnetic lines of force in the solar chromosphere between pairs of sunspots.

The generalization of the theorem to multiparametric problems can be made on the assumption that

$$
| ( k , \xi ) | > K | k | ^ { - \upsilon } , \qquad | k | > 0 , \qquad \nu = n + 1 , \qquad K > 0 .
$$

The extension to the case of many degrees of freedom has yet to be accomplished.

# 4.25EXISTENCE OF ADIABATIC INVARIANTS

Kasuga (l961) has given a precise formulation of the adiabatic theorem in the general case and proved it by the operator method in the classical mechanics of von Neumann (1932) and Koopman (1931).

According to Kasuga a quantity $I ( p , q , s )$ , which is an integral of

$$
\frac { d \phi } { d t } = \mathrm { ~ - } \frac { \partial H } { \partial q } , \frac { d q } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial \phi } , H = H ( \phi , q , s ) ,
$$

where $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { p } } = ( \mathscr { p } _ { 1 } , \mathscr { p } _ { 2 } , . . . , \mathscr { p } _ { n } ) , \mathscr { q } = ( q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , . . . , q _ { n } )$ ，for every fixed $s ,$ $( 0 \leq s \leq 1 ) ,$ ,is called an adiabatic invariant of (153）if $I ( p , q , t / \lambda )$ is conserved, asymptotically for $\lambda \to + \infty$ ，along almost all trajectories of the system

$$
\frac { d \phi } { d t } = \ - \frac { \partial H } { \partial q } , \frac { d q } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial \phi } , H = H ( \phi , q , t / \lambda ) ;
$$

that is (if we let $t ^ { \prime } = t / \lambda )$ , of the system

$$
\frac { d \phi } { d t ^ { \prime } } = - \lambda \frac { \partial H } { \partial q } , \frac { d q } { d t ^ { \prime } } = \lambda \frac { \partial H } { \partial \substack { \partial p } } , H = H ( p , q , t ^ { \prime } ) ,
$$

in the whole interval of time $0 \leq t \leq \lambda$ ,that is, $0 \leq t ^ { \prime } \leq 1$ .The fundamental case of the adiabatic theorem in celestial mechanics is the case in which (l53) admits no single-valued Lebesgue measurable integral other than the energy integral for almost all $s , 0 \leq s \leqq 1$ .In this case the phase volume

$$
\bar { \mathfrak { S } } ( \phi , q , s ) = \iint d \phi d q ,
$$

where the integral extended over the domain $I _ { E , s }$ in the phase space enclosed by the energy surface $S _ { E , s } = \{ ( \phi , q ) \mid E ( \phi , q , s ) = E \}$ of (153) passing through $( \pmb { \mathscr { p } } , \pmb { q } )$ , is the only adiabatic invariant.

Kasuga first determined the region $\pmb { D }$ in a $( 2 n + 1 )$ -dimensional space $R ^ { 2 n + 1 }$ such that the adiabatic theorem can be stated,and studied the properties of the phase volume function $\textstyle { \bar { \mathfrak { I } } } ( p , q , s )$ in $D$

Let $B$ be a subset of a Euclidian space or a subset of a Hilbert space (von Neumann 1929； Stone, 1932;Hille,l948)； then the closure of $\boldsymbol { B }$ in the space is denoted by $\bar { B }$ ,and the set of all inner points of $\pmb { B }$ by $B ^ { 0 }$ · A subset of $B$ is called relatively open in $B$ if it can be considered as an intersection of an open set in the Euclidian space with $B$ .We denote by $C ^ { m } ( B )$ the set of all $_ m$ -times continuously differentiable functions on $B$ 一 noting that $\bar { B } ^ { 0 } \supset B$ — and by $C _ { 0 } ^ { m } ( B )$ the set of all $_ { m } .$ -times continuously differentiable functions on $\boldsymbol { B }$ vanishing outside compact sets contained in $B$ By $\pmb { K }$ we denote the point set

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { \{ ( { \boldsymbol { \phi } } , { \boldsymbol { q } } , { \boldsymbol { s } } ) ~ | ~ - \infty ~ < { \boldsymbol { p } } _ { i } < + \infty , - \infty < { \boldsymbol { q } } _ { i } < + \infty , i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n , } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \qquad \quad \alpha \leq { \boldsymbol { s } } \leq b \} } \end{array}
$$

in $R ^ { 2 n + 1 }$ with two fixed numbers $^ { a }$ and $b , a < b$

on $G$ and the following condition:For each $s , a \leqq s \leqq b .$ ，there is an open interval

$$
\Gamma _ { s } = \{ E | E _ { s } < E < E _ { s } ^ { \prime } \} , \qquad - \infty \leq E _ { s } < E _ { s } ^ { \prime } = + \infty ,

on the $E$ -line such that the set

in the open set $G _ { s } = \{ ( p , q ) \mid ( p , q , s ) \in G \}$ is nonempty and is a closed $( 2 n - 1 )$ -dimensional manifold of $R ^ { 2 n }$ enclosing a domain $I _ { E , s }$ in $R ^ { 2 n }$ for each $E \in \Gamma _ { s }$ and $G _ { s } = \mathbf { U } _ { E \in \Gamma _ { s } }$ (the neighborhood of $S _ { E , s }$ for which $E \in \Gamma _ { s . }$ ).

\Im ( E , s ) = \int _ { I _ { E , s } } d / s d q \in C ^ { \prime } ( \Lambda ) , \qquad \hat { \Im } ( / s , q , s ) = \Im ( H ( p , q , s ) , s ) ,

For any integrable function $f ( \pmb { \mathscr { p } } , \pmb { q } )$ on $I ( s )$ ，the function $f ( \pmb { \mathscr { p } } , \pmb { q } )$ is integrable on the measure space $( S ( J , s ) , m _ { J , s } )$ for almost all $J \left( J _ { 1 } ^ { * } < J < J _ { 2 } ^ { * } \right)$ ,and

for $J _ { 1 } ^ { * } < J < J _ { 2 } ^ { * }$ and $a \leq s \leq b$

He then studied the flows defined by (l53) and the infinitesimal generator $i A$ of the unitary group $\{ U _ { t } \mid - \infty < t < + \infty \}$ associated with one of the flows,and proved the adiabatic theorem for a multidimensional and multiparametric case.

Let $( X , m )$ be a measure space where $_ m$ is a finite, separable,and complete measure (Section 2.1） defined on a Borel field in $X .$ Aoneparameter group $\{ \mathfrak { T } _ { t } \ | \ - \infty < t < \ + \infty \}$ of a one-to-one mapping ${ \mathfrak { T } } _ { t }$ of $X$ onto $\boldsymbol { \chi }$ is called a flow on $( X , m )$ . A measurable function $f ( P )$ on $( X , m )$ is called an invariant function of a fow $\{ \mathfrak { T } _ { t } \}$ on $( X , m )$ if

$$
f ( \mathfrak { T } _ { t } ( P ) ) = f ( P )
$$

almost everywhere on $( X , m )$ for every fixed $\scriptstyle t$ 、It is called a strictly invariant function of a flow $\{ \mathfrak { T } _ { t } \}$ on $( X , m )$ if it is defined everywhere on $X$ and if $f ( \mathfrak { T } _ { t } ( P ) ) = f ( P )$ for all $( P , t )$ such that $P \in X _ { \mathrm { { i } } }$ $- \infty < t < + \infty$ A measure-preserving and measurable flow $\{ \mathfrak { T } _ { t } \}$ on $( X , m )$ is ergodic in the sense used by von Neumann (l932) if and only if all its invariant functions are equivalent, that is, coincident almost everywhere, to constants on $( X , m )$ . If a flow $\{ \mathfrak { T } _ { t } \}$ on $( X , m )$ is measure-preserving and measurable, then we can associate with it a one-parameter group $\{ \mathfrak { U } _ { t } \ | \ - \infty < t < \ + \infty \}$ of unitary transformations (Section 1.18) ${ \mathfrak { U } } _ { t }$ on $L ^ { 2 } ( X , m )$ by

$$
\mathfrak { U } _ { t } f ( P ) = f ( \mathfrak { T } _ { t } ( P ) ) , \qquad f \in L ^ { 2 } ( X , m ) , \qquad P \in X ;
$$

and ${ \mathfrak { U } } _ { t }$ is a continuous function of $t$ in the strong topology of ${ \mathfrak { U } } _ { t }$ . (See a later chapter on the ergodic theorem.)

If $X$ is a Lebesgue measurable subset of a Euclidian space $R ^ { \tau }$ ,and $\pmb { m }$ is the Lebesgue measure of $R ^ { \tau }$ defined for all Lebesgue measurable subsets of $X ,$ a flow on $( X , m )$ is called a flow on $X .$

in the open set $I ( s )$ for a fixed $s$ $\mathit { \Pi } ^ { \prime } a \ \leq \ s \ \leq \ b \ \mathrm { , }$ ）with the initial conditions

if $( \phi ^ { 0 } , q ^ { 0 } ) \in S ( J , s )$

$$

# LIE GROUPS
