# 4.26LIE'S FUNDAMENTAL THEOREMS

Consider a set of independent functions

$$
f _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } ; a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , . . . , a _ { r } ) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

of $\pmb { n }$ independent variables $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ containing $_ r$ parameters $a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots , a _ { r } ;$ ，continuous of class $C ^ { 2 }$ in $_ x$ and in $\pmb { a }$ By assumption, the Jacobian of $f _ { i }$ with respect to $_ { x }$ is not zero. If there is no relation of the form

$$
\sum _ { \sigma = 1 } ^ { r } \phi _ { \sigma } ( a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , . . . , a _ { r } ) { \frac { \partial f _ { i } } { \partial a _ { \sigma } } } = 0 ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

with nonzero functions $\phi _ { \sigma } ( a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , . . . , a _ { \tau } )$ ， then and only then are $a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots , a _ { \tau }$ said to be the essential parameters. Any transformation by an $\pmb { r }$ parameter group

$$
x _ { i } ^ { \prime } = f _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } ; a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , . . . , a _ { \tau } ) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )
$$

transforms a point $P ( x _ { 1 } , . . . . , x _ { n } )$ of an $\pmb { n }$ dimensional manifold to a point $P ^ { \prime } ( x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , x _ { n } ^ { \prime } )$ ；that is, $T _ { a } { \pmb x } = { \pmb x } ^ { \prime }$ ， $T _ { a } ^ { - 1 } x ^ { \prime } = x$ ，The composite transformation $T _ { b } T _ { a } = T _ { c }$ is determined uniquely; thus,

$$
T _ { c } ( T _ { b } T _ { a } ) = ( T _ { c } T _ { b } ) T _ { a } .
$$

A system with these properties is called a semi-group. If it contains an identity such as $T _ { a } I = I T _ { a } = T _ { a }$ and an inverse element such as $T _ { a } ^ { - 1 } T _ { a } = T _ { a } T _ { a } ^ { - 1 } = I ,$ then it is called a group. From (l55) we obtain

$$
x _ { j } = f _ { j } ^ { \prime } ( x _ { i } ^ { \prime } , . . . , x _ { n } ^ { \prime } , a _ { 1 } , . . . , a _ { r } ) \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

The transformations (155) define a continuous transformation group $_ { T }$ (Lie,1888,1890,1891, 1893,1893a,1896).

From (155),

$$
\hat { \delta } x _ { i } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \rho } f _ { i \rho } ( x , a ) \ \hat { \delta } a _ { \rho } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

or

$$
\delta x _ { i } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \rho } f _ { i \rho } \{ x ^ { \prime } , a \} \delta a _ { \rho } ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

This is an infinitesimal transformation $T _ { a } ^ { - 1 } T _ { a + \delta a }$ According to Lie it is written

$$
\sum _ { \rho } \left( \sum _ { i } { f _ { i \rho } \{ x ^ { \prime } , a \} } \frac { { \partial { f ^ { \prime } } } } { { \partial { x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } } } \right) \frac { { \delta { a _ { \rho } } } } { { \delta t } } .
$$

We write the relation $T _ { \flat } T _ { a } = T _ { c }$ in the form

$$
c _ { \rho } = \psi _ { \rho } ( a , b ) \qquad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

Since $T _ { b } T _ { a + \delta a } = T _ { c + \delta c : }$ we have

$$
T _ { c } ^ { - 1 } T _ { c + \delta c } = T _ { a } ^ { - 1 } T _ { b } ^ { - 1 } T _ { b } T _ { a + \delta a } = T _ { a } ^ { - 1 } T _ { a + \delta a } .
$$

Thus, $T _ { a } ^ { - 1 } T _ { a + \delta a }$ is invariant for the transformation (157). This leads to the relation

$$
\sum _ { \rho } \left( \sum _ { i } f _ { i \rho } \{ x ^ { \prime } , a \} \frac { \partial f ^ { \prime } } { \partial x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } \right) \frac { \delta a _ { \rho } } { \delta t } = \sum _ { \sigma } \left( \sum _ { i } f _ { i \sigma } \{ x ^ { \prime } , c \} \frac { \partial f ^ { \prime } } { \partial x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } \right) \frac { \delta c _ { \sigma } } { \delta t } .
$$

From (157) we have

$$
\delta c _ { \sigma } = \sum _ { \rho } \phi _ { \sigma \rho } ( b , a ) \ \delta a _ { \rho } = \sum _ { \rho } \phi _ { \sigma \rho } \{ a , c \} \ \delta a _ { \rho } .
$$

Substituting this expression for $\delta c _ { \sigma }$ in (156b） makes the infinitesimal transformation (l56a) become

$$
\sum _ { \rho } \left( \sum _ { i } f _ { i \rho } \{ x ^ { \prime } , a \} \frac { \partial f ^ { \prime } } { \partial x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } \right) \frac { \partial a _ { \rho } } { \partial t } = \sum _ { \sigma } \left[ \left( \sum _ { i } f _ { i \sigma } \{ x ^ { \prime } , c \} \frac { \partial f ^ { \prime } } { \partial x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } \right) \cdot \sum _ { \rho } \phi _ { \sigma \rho } \{ a , c \} \frac { \partial a _ { \rho } } { \partial t } \right]
$$

We fix $c .$ .Then $b$ is given by (157),and the $\mathcal { f } _ { i \sigma } \{ x ^ { \prime } , c \}$ become functions of $\scriptstyle { \boldsymbol { x } } ^ { \prime }$ only. We can put

$$
f _ { i \sigma } \{ x ^ { \prime } , c \} = \xi _ { \sigma i } ( x ^ { \prime } ) , \phi _ { \sigma \rho } \{ a , c \} = \psi _ { \sigma \rho } ( a ) ,
$$

$$
\sum _ { i } \xi _ { \sigma i } ( x ^ { \prime } ) \frac { \partial f ^ { \prime } } { \partial x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } = X _ { \sigma } ^ { \prime } f ^ { \prime } .
$$

This gives an important relation:

$$
\sum _ { \rho } \left( \sum _ { i } f _ { i \rho } \{ x ^ { \prime } , a \} \frac { \partial f ^ { \prime } } { \partial x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } \right) \frac { \partial a _ { \rho } } { \partial t } = \sum _ { \sigma } \left( \sum _ { \rho } \psi _ { \sigma \rho } ( a ) \frac { \delta a _ { \rho } } { \delta t } \right) X _ { \sigma } ^ { \prime } f ^ { \prime } .
$$

These infinitesimal transformations $X _ { 1 } f _ { 3 } \ldots , X _ { r } f$ constitute a ring with the operations of addition and multiplication,which is called an infinitesimal ring.

We have $( \pmb { x } ^ { \prime } ) = ( \pmb { x } ) S _ { t } , ( \pmb { x } ) = ( \pmb { x } ^ { \prime } ) S _ { t } ^ { - 1 }$ ; accordingly

$$
( x ^ { \prime } + \delta x ^ { \prime } ) = ( x ^ { \prime } ) S _ { t } ^ { - 1 } S _ { t + \delta t } .
$$

Hence, the infinitesimal transformation $T _ { a } ^ { - 1 } T _ { a + \delta a }$ can be formed from the basic transformations $X _ { 1 } f _ { 3 } \ldots , X _ { \tau } f ,$ linearly with the coefficients

$$
\sum _ { \tau } \psi _ { 1 \tau } ( a ) \ \delta a _ { \tau } , . . . , \sum _ { \tau } \psi _ { r \tau } ( a ) \ \delta a _ { \tau } .
$$

Write (157) in the form $a _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } = \psi _ { \rho } ( a , b )$ and let $T _ { b } T _ { a } = T _ { a ^ { \prime } }$ .If $T _ { c } T _ { a ^ { \prime } } = T _ { a ^ { \prime \prime } ; }$ then $T _ { c } T _ { b } T _ { a } = T _ { a ^ { \prime \prime } }$ But since $T _ { c } T _ { b } = T _ { d }$ ，therefore, $T _ { d } T _ { a } = T _ { a ^ { \prime \prime } } $ This is called a left-hand group. It is the second parameter group of Lie. Write (157) in the form $a _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } = \psi _ { \rho } ( b , a )$ and let $T _ { a } T _ { b } = T _ { a ^ { \prime } }$ .If $T _ { a ^ { \prime } } T _ { c } = T _ { a ^ { \prime \prime } }$ then $T _ { a } T _ { b } T _ { c } = T _ { a ^ { \prime \prime } }$ Since $T _ { b } T _ { c } = T _ { d } { : }$ ，therefore $T _ { a } T _ { d } = T _ { a ^ { \prime \prime } }$ This is Lie's first parameter group; it is a right-hand group. The parameter $\pmb { b }$ is essential in these groups. Cartan (1933, l937) introduced the notion of equipollence by means of a geometrical illustration. Changing $T _ { a } ^ { - 1 } T _ { a + \delta a }$ to $T _ { a ^ { \prime } } ^ { - 1 } T _ { a ^ { \prime } + \delta a ^ { \prime } }$ by means of (158), we obtain

$$
\sum _ { \rho } \left( \sum _ { \tau } \psi _ { \rho \tau } ( a ) \ \delta a _ { \tau } \right) X _ { \rho } f = \sum _ { \rho } \left( \sum _ { \tau } \psi _ { \rho \tau } ( a ^ { \prime } ) \ \delta a _ { \tau } ^ { \prime } \right) X _ { \rho } f
$$

for the second parameter group. Since the basic infinitesimal transformations are linearly independent, therefore

$$
\sum _ { \tau } \psi _ { \rho \tau } ( a ) \ \delta a _ { \tau } = \sum _ { \tau } \psi _ { \rho \tau } ( a ^ { \prime } ) \ \delta a _ { \tau } ^ { \prime } \qquad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

That is, the $\pmb { r }$ Pfaffians $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { \tau } \psi _ { \rho \tau } ( a ) \ \delta a _ { \tau } } \end{array}$ are invariant for the second parameter group.

Construct an $\pmb { r }$ -dimensional parameter space with coordinates $a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots , a _ { r } .$ Put $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { \tau } \psi _ { \rho ^ { \prime } \tau } ( a ) \ \delta a _ { \tau } = \epsilon _ { \rho \rho ^ { \prime } } \ \delta t . } \end{array}$ ，where $\epsilon _ { \rho \rho } = 1$ ， $\epsilon _ { \rho \rho ^ { \prime } } = 0$ $\left( \rho \neq \rho ^ { \prime } \right)$ . Such a vector feld is invariant for the second parameter group；it is the field of the infinitesimal increment defined by the infinitesimal transformations

$$
A _ { \rho } f = \sum _ { \tau } \alpha _ { \rho \tau } ( a ) \frac { \partial f } { \partial a _ { \tau } } \qquad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) ,
$$

with the determinant $\| \psi _ { \sigma \tau } \| \neq 0$ ，where $\alpha _ { \rho \tau } ( a )$ is the minor of this determinant. Since any infinitesimal transformation $\ b { A f }$ is formed linearly with constant coefficients from $A _ { 1 } f _ { 3 } \ldots , A _ { r } f ,$ we obtain

$$
( A _ { \sigma } , A _ { \rho } ) f = \sum _ { \tau } c _ { \sigma \rho \tau } A _ { \tau } f \qquad ( \sigma , \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r )
$$

with the commutator $( A _ { \sigma } , A _ { \rho } )$ (Section 4.2).

If the second parameter group $T _ { c } T _ { a ^ { \prime } } = T _ { a ^ { \prime \prime } }$ is applied to the first parameter group $T _ { a } T _ { b } = T _ { a ^ { \prime } }$ ，then $T _ { c } T _ { a } T _ { b } = T _ { a ^ { \prime \prime } }$ Let $T _ { c } T _ { a } = T _ { a _ { 1 } ; }$ $T _ { a _ { 1 } } T _ { b } = T _ { a _ { 2 } }$ ；then $T _ { c } T _ { a } T _ { b } = T _ { a _ { 2 } }$ 、Hence, if transformations from the first and the second parameter groups are denoted by $\scriptstyle A _ { b }$ and $B _ { c }$ ， respectively, then $A _ { b } B _ { c } = B _ { c } A _ { b }$ .Thus, $B _ { c } = A _ { b } ^ { - 1 } B _ { c } A _ { b } ,$ $A _ { b } = B _ { c } ^ { - 1 } A _ { b } B _ { c }$ ； that is, any transformation of one of the parameter groups is invariant for transformations of the other parameter group.Hence, $A _ { b } ^ { - 1 } A _ { b + \delta b }$ is invariant for $B _ { c }$ .We see that the infinitesimal transformations of the first parameter group are formed by linear combinations with constant coefficients from $A _ { 1 } f _ { 3 } \ldots , A _ { r } f .$ Let an infinitesimal transformation of the second parameter group be $B f ,$ and let $B f$ operate on $A _ { \rho } f _ { \mathrm { : } }$ ；then we obtain $A _ { \rho } f + \ ( A _ { \rho } , B ) \delta t$ .Since this is invariant for the second parameter group, therefore $( A _ { \rho } , B _ { \sigma } ) = 0$ $( \rho , \sigma = 1 , 2 , . . . , r )$ ·

Let $A _ { \rho } f$ be a basic infinitesimal transformation of the first parameter group, and let $\delta _ { \rho }$ be the displacement due to $A _ { \rho } f _ { ; }$ ；then,by(159) and (160),

$$
( A _ { \sigma } , A _ { \tau } ) = \sum _ { \rho } \left( \frac { \hat { \delta } _ { \sigma } \alpha _ { \tau \rho } } { \delta t } - \frac { \hat { \delta } _ { \tau } \alpha _ { \sigma \rho } } { \delta t } \right) \frac { \delta f } { \delta \alpha _ { \rho } } = \sum _ { \rho } \sum _ { \kappa } c _ { \sigma \tau \kappa } \alpha _ { \kappa \rho } \frac { \partial f } { \partial \alpha _ { \rho } } .
$$

Multiply by $\psi _ { \lambda \rho }$ and sum over $\pmb { \rho }$ ； then,remembering that the matrix $\pmb { \alpha }$ is the reciprocal of the matrix $\psi$ ,we obtain

$$
\sum _ { \rho } \left( \psi _ { \lambda \rho } \frac { \hat { \delta } _ { \sigma } \alpha _ { \tau \rho } } { \delta t } - \psi _ { \lambda \rho } \frac { \hat { \delta } _ { \tau } \alpha _ { \sigma \rho } } { \delta t } \right) = c _ { \sigma \tau \lambda } .
$$

With $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { \rho } \psi _ { \lambda \rho } \alpha _ { \tau \rho } = \epsilon _ { \lambda \tau } , \sum _ { \rho } \psi _ { \lambda \rho } \alpha _ { \sigma \rho } = \epsilon _ { \lambda \sigma } . } \end{array}$ we have

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \displaystyle \sum _ { \rho } { \left( \alpha _ { \sigma \rho } \frac { \delta _ { \tau } \psi _ { \lambda \rho } } { \delta t } - \alpha _ { \tau \rho } \frac { \delta _ { \sigma } \psi _ { \lambda \rho } } { \delta t } \right) } = \displaystyle \sum _ { \rho } { \sum _ { \kappa } { \left( \alpha _ { \sigma \rho } \alpha _ { \tau \kappa } \frac { \partial \psi _ { \lambda \rho } } { \partial a _ { \kappa } } - \alpha _ { \tau \rho } \alpha _ { \sigma \kappa } \frac { \partial \psi _ { \lambda \rho } } { \partial a _ { \kappa } } \right) } } } \\ { = c _ { \sigma \tau \lambda } = \displaystyle \sum _ { \rho } { \sum _ { \kappa } { \alpha _ { \sigma \rho } \alpha _ { \tau \kappa } \left( \frac { \partial \psi _ { \lambda \rho } } { \partial a _ { \kappa } } - \frac { \partial \psi _ { \lambda \kappa } } { \partial a _ { \rho } } \right) } } . } \end{array}
$$

Hence

$$
\frac { \partial \psi _ { \lambda \rho } } { \partial a _ { \sigma } } - \frac { \partial \psi _ { \lambda \sigma } } { \partial a _ { \rho } } = \sum _ { \kappa } \sum _ { \tau } c _ { \kappa \tau \lambda } \psi _ { \kappa \rho } \psi _ { \tau \sigma } .
$$

This is called Maurer's relation.

If the group contains at least a transformation (l55） that maps a point $( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } )$ of the $\pmb { n }$ dimensional manifold to any other point $( x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , x _ { n } ^ { \prime } )$ of that manifold, then the group is said to be transitive. When there are exactly $_ { n }$ independent equations among

$$
X _ { 1 } f = 0 , . . . , X _ { r } f = 0 ,
$$

then the transformation of the point $( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } )$ is transitive.Otherwise it is intransitive.If $n = r _ { \mathrm { { i } } }$ ，then the system of $_ { x }$ can be transformed to the system of $^ { a }$ under certain conditions. Such a group is said to be simply transitive.

By combining $\begin{array} { r } { P _ { \rho } = \sum _ { \tau } \psi _ { \rho \tau } ( a ) \delta a _ { \tau } ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) } \end{array}$ and the basic infinitesimal transformations

$$
X _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } f ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { i } \xi _ { \rho i } ( x ^ { \prime } ) \frac { \partial f ^ { \prime } } { \partial x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } ,
$$

we have seen that the transformation $T _ { a } ^ { - 1 } T _ { a + \delta a }$ satisfies

$$
\delta x _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime } = \sum _ { \rho } { P _ { \rho } \xi _ { \rho i } ( x ^ { \prime } ) } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

If $x ^ { \prime }$ is regarded as a function of $^ { a }$ ,then it should satisfy

$$
d x _ { i } ^ { \prime } - \sum _ { \rho , \sigma } \psi _ { \sigma \rho } ( a ) \xi _ { \sigma i } ( x ^ { \prime } ) d a _ { \rho } = 0 \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

or

$$
\frac { \partial x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { \partial a _ { \rho } } = \sum _ { \sigma } \psi _ { \sigma \rho } ( a ) \xi _ { \sigma i } ( x ^ { \prime } ) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ; \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

Hence,a transformation of an $\boldsymbol { r }$ -parameter group should satisfy this system (163) of partial differential equations. This is the first fundamental theorem of $\pmb { L i e }$

Substituting (l62) in the relation

$$
d F = \sum _ { i } { \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } } d x _ { i } ^ { \prime } + \sum _ { \rho } { \frac { \partial F } { \partial a _ { \rho } } } d a _ { \rho }
$$

we obtain

$$
\frac { \partial F } { \partial a _ { \rho } } + \sum _ { i , \sigma } \psi _ { \sigma \rho } ( a ) \xi _ { \sigma i } ( x ^ { \prime } ) \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } = 0 ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) ,
$$

or, with the definition of the matrix $\pmb { \alpha }$

$$
\sum _ { i } \xi _ { \rho i } ( x ^ { \prime } ) \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } + \sum _ { \sigma } \alpha _ { \rho \sigma } ( a ) \frac { \partial F } { \partial a _ { \sigma } } = 0 \qquad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

Since $x _ { i } ^ { \prime } = f _ { i } ( x , a )$ satisfies (l62） with the integration constants $^ { a }$ ，the relation $x _ { i } = f _ { i } ^ { \prime } ( x ^ { \prime } , a )$ satisfies (165).As these $_ { n }$ functions $f _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ are independent with regard to $\scriptstyle { x ^ { \prime } }$ ，the equations （l65）admit $_ { n }$ independent integrals $f _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ .There are $r$ independent equations for $n + r$ variables in the form (l65).Hence,(l65) is a complete system and can be integrated completely.

Conversely,it can be shown that if the transformation (155) containing the identical elements $a _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } , \ldots , a _ { r } ^ { 0 }$ of the parameters satisfies the Pfaffian system (l62）which is completely integrable with respect to $x ^ { \prime }$ and $^ { a }$ ， such that $\| \psi _ { \sigma \rho } ( a ^ { 0 } ) \| \neq 0$ ， then the transformation gives a transformation group.

According to (162） the transformation $T _ { a } ^ { - 1 } T _ { a + \delta a }$ coincides with the infinitesimal transformation $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { \sigma } P _ { \sigma } ( a ) X _ { \sigma } ^ { \prime } } \end{array}$ Let $( a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { r } )$ represent a point $\pmb { A }$ and $( a _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } , \ldots , a _ { r } ^ { 0 } )$ a point $A ^ { 0 }$ of the parameter space.Divide the arc $A A ^ { 0 }$ joining $A ^ { 0 }$ to $\pmb { A }$ into segments by the points $A ^ { 1 } , A ^ { 2 } , . . . , A ^ { n - 1 }$ on the arc.Then $T _ { A ^ { 0 } } ^ { - 1 } T _ { A }$ is divided into factors

$$
T _ { A ^ { 0 } } ^ { - 1 } T _ { A ^ { 1 } } , T _ { A ^ { 1 } } ^ { - 1 } T _ { A ^ { 2 } } , . . . , T _ { A ^ { n } - 1 } ^ { - 1 } T _ { A ^ { n } } .
$$

Each of these factors can become an infinitesimal transformation,if we increase the number of these segments indefinitely. If the arc is written

$$
P _ { \sigma } ( a ) = c _ { \sigma } d t \qquad ( \sigma = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) ,
$$

then $T _ { A ^ { 0 } } ^ { - 1 } T _ { A }$ is said to be generated (erzeugt） by the infinitesimal transformation $\textstyle \sum _ { \sigma } c _ { \sigma } X _ { \sigma }$ .Referring to the definition of $P _ { \rho }$ ， we obtain， from (166),

$$
\frac { d a _ { o } } { d t } = \sum _ { \sigma } c _ { \sigma } \alpha _ { \sigma \rho } ( a ) \qquad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

The integral curve of these equations is a trajectory that admits the infinitesimal transformation

$$
\sum _ { \rho } \sum _ { \sigma } c _ { \sigma } a _ { \sigma \rho } ( a ) \ : \frac { \partial f } { \partial a _ { \rho } } = \sum _ { \sigma } c _ { \sigma } A _ { \sigma } f ,
$$

and passes through $A ^ { 0 }$ . Thus $A ^ { 0 }$ is transformed to $A$ by the transformation $\textstyle \sum _ { \sigma } c _ { \sigma } A _ { \sigma }$ operating in the parameter space. At the same time the infinitesimal transformation $\textstyle \sum _ { \sigma } c _ { \sigma } X _ { \sigma }$ should operate in the $_ { x }$ -manifold, where

$$
X _ { \sigma } f = \sum _ { i } \xi _ { \sigma i } ( x ) { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { i } } } , \qquad \delta x _ { i } = \delta t \cdot \sum _ { \rho } c _ { \rho } \xi _ { \rho i } ( x ) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , n ) .
$$

Formula (163) shows that

$$
\sum _ { \rho } \psi _ { i \rho } ( a ) \ \delta a _ { \rho } = c _ { i } \ \delta t ,
$$

which is derived from (155).

Suppose that the points $A ^ { \circ } , B ^ { \circ } , C ^ { \circ } , \ldots .$ are transformed respectively to $A , B , C , \ldots$ by $\textstyle \sum _ { \sigma } c _ { \sigma } A _ { \sigma }$ for the same variation of $t .$ 、Then the transformations

$$
T _ { A ^ { 0 } } ^ { - 1 } T _ { A } , \qquad T _ { B ^ { 0 } } ^ { - 1 } T _ { B } . \qquad T _ { C ^ { 0 } } ^ { - 1 } T _ { C } , . . .
$$

all coincide and generate the infinitesimal transformation $\textstyle \sum _ { \sigma } c _ { \sigma } X _ { \sigma }$ Denote by $A ^ { \circ } , B ^ { \circ } , C ^ { \circ } , \ldots$ ，the successive images of the point $( a ^ { 0 } )$ corre-sponding to the identical element, and by $A , B , C , \ldots$ the corresponding images of the point $( a )$ . Then $T _ { a ^ { 0 } } ^ { - 1 } T _ { a }$ or $T _ { a }$ should be among the transformations (168).Since $T _ { A ^ { 0 } } { } ^ { - 1 } T _ { A } = T _ { a } ,$ therefore $T _ { A } = T _ { A ^ { 0 } } T _ { a }$ Thus, from two transformations $T _ { A ^ { 0 } }$ and $T _ { a }$ belonging to (l55） we obtain the transformation $T _ { A }$ belonging to (155). The point $\boldsymbol { A ^ { 0 } }$ is an arbitrary point near the point $( a ^ { 0 } )$ ,and $( a )$ is obtained from $( a ^ { 0 } )$ by the transformation $\textstyle \sum _ { \sigma } c _ { \sigma } A _ { \sigma }$ Hence, by choosing $c _ { \sigma }$ suitably, we can reach any point $( a )$ in the neighborhood of $( a ^ { 0 } )$ .For,integrating (167) from the point $( a ^ { 0 } )$ gives

$$
a _ { \rho } = a _ { p } ^ { 0 } + \sum _ { \sigma } t c _ { \sigma } \alpha _ { \sigma \rho } ( a ^ { 0 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot \qquad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) ,
$$

Thus $( a _ { \rho } )$ is a function of $\lambda _ { \sigma } = t c _ { \sigma }$ The Jacobian of $a _ { \rho }$ with respect to $\lambda _ { \sigma }$ at $\lambda _ { \sigma } = 0$ is $\| \alpha _ { \sigma \rho } ( a ^ { 0 } ) \|$ and does not vanish. By solving these series we obtain

$$
t c _ { \sigma } = \sum _ { \rho } \psi _ { \sigma \rho } ( a ^ { 0 } ) ( a _ { \rho } - a _ { \rho } ^ { 0 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot \quad \quad ( \sigma = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

Hence $T _ { a }$ is generated by the infinitesimal transformation $\textstyle \sum _ { \sigma } c _ { \sigma } X _ { \sigma }$ if $( a )$ is in the neighborhood of $( a ^ { 0 } )$

Since by definition $\begin{array} { r } { \delta f ^ { \prime } = X ^ { \prime } f ^ { \prime } \delta t , X ^ { \prime } f ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { i } \xi _ { \sigma i } ( x ^ { \prime } ) \ \partial f ^ { \prime } / \partial x . } \end{array}$ ，therefore $d f ^ { \prime } / d t = X ^ { \prime } f ^ { \prime }$ ， $d ^ { 2 } f ^ { \prime } / d t ^ { 2 } = X ^ { \prime } X ^ { \prime } f ^ { \prime } = X ^ { \prime 2 } f ^ { \prime }$ ,.... Denote the values of $f ^ { \prime } , d f ^ { \prime } / d t , d ^ { 2 } f ^ { \prime } / d t ^ { 2 } , . . .$ at $t = 0 \ b y f , X f , X ^ { 2 } f , . . . ,$ respectively; then

$$
f ^ { \prime } = f + \frac { t } { 1 ! } X f + \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 ! } X ^ { 2 } f + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

If the infinitesimal transformations $X _ { 1 } f _ { 3 } \ldots , X _ { r } f$ belonging to the foregoing group satisfy the conditions of Lie's theorem， then any transformation belonging to the group in the neighborhood of the identical element is generated by

$$
X f = e _ { 1 } X _ { 1 } f + \cdot \cdot \cdot + e _ { r } X _ { r } f .
$$

Let $t = 1$ ; then such a transformation is writen

$$
f ( x ^ { \prime } ) = f ( x ) + { \frac { 1 } { 1 ! } } \sum _ { \rho } e _ { \rho } X _ { \rho } f + { \frac { 1 } { 2 ! } } \sum _ { \rho , \sigma } e _ { \rho } e _ { \sigma } X _ { \rho } X _ { \sigma } f + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

The $\pmb { r }$ constants $e _ { 1 } , \ldots , e _ { \tau }$ are called the canonical parameters. If $f ( x ^ { \prime } )$ satisfies

$$
\frac { d f ( x ^ { \prime } ) } { d t } = \sum _ { \rho } \lambda _ { \rho } X _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } f ( x ^ { \prime } ) ,
$$

without $\delta t$ being put equal to zero, then the infinitesimal transformation $( \lambda _ { 1 } X _ { 1 } f + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \lambda _ { \tau } X _ { \tau } f ) \delta t$ generates a finite transformation

$$
f ( x ^ { \prime } ) = f ( x ) + \frac { 1 } { 1 ! } t \sum _ { \rho } \lambda _ { \rho } X _ { \rho } f + \frac { 1 } { 2 ! } t ^ { 2 } \sum _ { \rho , \sigma } \lambda _ { \rho } \lambda _ { \sigma } X _ { \rho } X _ { \sigma } f + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

Here $X _ { 1 } f , . . . , X _ { \tau } f$ are called the generators of the infinitesimal transformations.

The transformation (155) is represented in general by

$$
X _ { \rho } f = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \xi _ { \rho i } ( x ) { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { i } } } \quad \quad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

Integrating the differential equations

$$
{ \frac { d x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } } { \displaystyle { \sum _ { \rho = 1 } ^ { r } \lambda _ { \rho } \xi _ { \rho 1 } ( x ^ { \prime } ) } } } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = { \frac { d x _ { n } ^ { \prime } } { \displaystyle { \sum _ { \rho = 1 } ^ { r } \lambda _ { \rho } \xi _ { \rho n } ( x ^ { \prime } ) } } } = d t
$$

from $x _ { i } ^ { \prime } = x _ { i }$ for $t = 0$ ,we obtain

$$
x _ { i } ^ { \prime } = \phi _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } , \lambda _ { 1 } t , . . . , \lambda _ { r } t ) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

This is in the required canonical form,and is written

$$
x _ { i } ^ { \prime } = \phi _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } , u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { r } ) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , r ) ,
$$

where $\lambda _ { \rho } t = u _ { \rho }$

The equations (165) can be put in the form $X _ { \sigma } ^ { \prime } F + A _ { \sigma } F = 0 \ :$ where

$$
\sum _ { \rho } \alpha _ { \sigma \rho } ( a ) \frac { \partial { \cal F } } { \partial x _ { \rho } } = A _ { \sigma } { \cal F } ,
$$

and are satisfied by the transformation (155). $A _ { \sigma } F$ is the first parameter group. Since this group is a complete system (Section 4.9),the commutator $( X _ { \sigma } ^ { \prime } F + A _ { \sigma } F , X _ { \tau } ^ { \prime } F + A _ { \tau } F )$ is a linear combination of

$$
X _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } F + A _ { \rho } F \qquad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) ,
$$

that is,

$$
( X _ { \sigma } ^ { \prime } F + A _ { \sigma } F , X _ { \tau } ^ { \prime } F + A _ { \tau } F ) = \sum _ { \rho } \phi _ { \sigma \tau \rho } ( X _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } F + A _ { \rho } F ) .
$$

Since

$$
( X _ { \sigma } ^ { \prime } F + A _ { \sigma } F , X _ { \tau } ^ { \prime } F + A _ { \tau } F ) = ( X _ { \sigma } ^ { \prime } F , X _ { \tau } ^ { \prime } F ) + ( A _ { \sigma } F , A _ { \tau } F ) ,
$$

the commutator is divided into

$$
( X _ { \sigma } ^ { \prime } , X _ { \tau } ^ { \prime } ) = \sum _ { \rho } \phi _ { \sigma \tau \rho } X _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } F , \qquad ( A _ { \sigma } , A _ { \tau } ) = \sum _ { \rho } \phi _ { \sigma \tau \rho } A _ { \rho } F \eqno ( \sigma , \tau _ { - } ) ,
$$

Since $\| \alpha _ { \sigma \rho } ( a ) \| \neq 0$ ，the second relation determines $\phi _ { \sigma \tau \rho }$ uniquely as functions of $^ a$ Differentiating the first relation with respect to $a _ { \lambda }$ we obtain

$$
\sum _ { \rho } \frac { \partial \phi _ { \sigma \tau \rho } } { \partial a _ { \lambda } } X _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } F = 0 .
$$

Since the $X ^ { \prime } F$ are linearly independent, we should have

$$
\frac { \partial \phi _ { \sigma \tau \rho } } { \partial a _ { \lambda } } = 0 , \mathrm { t h a t i s , } \phi _ { \sigma \tau \rho } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t . }
$$

Thus

$$
( X _ { \sigma } , X _ { \tau } ) f = \sum _ { \rho } c _ { \sigma \tau \rho } X _ { \rho } f \qquad ( \sigma , \tau = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

This is the second fundamental theorem of $\ L i e ; c _ { \sigma \tau \rho }$ are the structure constants. Conversely, if $r$ independent infinitesimal transformations

$$
X _ { 1 } f _ { 3 } \ldots , X _ { r } f
$$

satisfy (l74)， then these transformations belong to the $r$ -parameter group, and any transformation of this group in the neighborhood of the identical element is generated by $\textstyle \sum _ { \rho } c _ { \rho } X _ { \rho } f .$

There are relations among the $r ^ { 3 }$ structure constants. Since

$$
( X _ { \sigma } , X _ { \tau } ) + ( X _ { \tau } , X _ { \sigma } ) = 0 ,
$$

we have

$$
\sum _ { \rho } { ( c _ { \sigma \tau \rho } + c _ { \tau \sigma \rho } ) X _ { \rho } f } = 0 .
$$

Since $X _ { \rho } f$ are linearly independent, we also have

$$
c _ { \sigma \tau \rho } + c _ { \tau \sigma \rho } = 0 .
$$

Substituting $X _ { \sigma } f , X _ { \tau } f , X _ { \rho } f$ in Poisson's identity (Section 4.3), we obtain

$$
( ( X _ { \sigma } , X _ { \tau } ) , X _ { \rho } ) \ + \ ( ( X _ { \tau } , X _ { \rho } ) , X _ { \sigma } ) \ + \ ( ( X _ { \rho } , X _ { \sigma } ) , X _ { \tau } ) \equiv 0 ,
$$

or

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle  \sum _ { \lambda } \sum _ { \kappa } [ ( c _ { \sigma \tau \lambda } c _ { \lambda \rho \kappa } + c _ { \tau \rho \lambda } c _ { \lambda \sigma \kappa } + c _ { \rho \sigma \lambda } c _ { \lambda \tau \kappa } ) ] \equiv 0 } \\ { \displaystyle ( \rho , \sigma , \tau = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) . } \end{array}
$$

Equations (l75） represent a plane manifold in the $r ^ { 3 }$ -dimensional space,while the equations (l76) represent a quadratic manifold. Each point on the intersection $\mathfrak { M }$ of these two manifolds corresponds to the structure constant of the group. Substituting $\bar { X } _ { 1 } f , . . . , \bar { X } _ { r } f \mathrm { f o r }$

$$
X _ { 1 } f , . . . , X _ { r } f
$$

by means of

$$
\bar { X } _ { \rho } f = a _ { \rho 1 } X _ { 1 } f + \cdot \cdot \cdot + a _ { \rho r } X _ { r } f \qquad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) ,
$$

we obtain

$$
X _ { \sigma } f = \bar { a } _ { 1 \sigma } \bar { X } _ { 1 } f + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \bar { a } _ { r \sigma } \bar { X } _ { r } f \qquad ( \sigma = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) ,
$$

since the $X _ { \rho } f$ are mutually independent. From the relations

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { ( \displaystyle \bar { X } _ { \tau } , \displaystyle \bar { X } _ { \kappa } ) f = \sum _ { \lambda , \mu , \nu } a _ { \tau \lambda } a _ { \kappa \mu } c _ { \lambda \mu \nu } \displaystyle \bar { X } _ { \nu } f } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } = \displaystyle \sum _ { \lambda , \mu , \nu , \rho } a _ { \tau \lambda } a _ { \kappa \mu } \bar { a } _ { \rho \nu } c _ { \lambda \mu \nu } \displaystyle \bar { X } _ { \rho } f = \sum _ { \rho } \bar { c } _ { \tau \kappa \rho } \displaystyle \bar { X } _ { \rho } f , } } \end{array}
$$

we obtain

$$
\bar { c } _ { \tau \kappa \rho } = \sum _ { \lambda , \mu , \nu } a _ { \tau \lambda } a _ { \kappa \mu } \bar { a } _ { \rho \nu } c _ { \lambda \mu \nu } \qquad ( \rho , \tau , \kappa = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

Thus to each linear transformation (l77） there corresponds a linear transformation of $c _ { \tau \kappa \rho }$ of (178). Conversely,by solving (178) we obtain

$$
c _ { \tau \kappa \rho } = \sum _ { \lambda , \mu , \nu } \bar { a } _ { \lambda \tau } a _ { \mu \kappa } \bar { a } _ { \nu \rho } c _ { \lambda \mu \nu } \qquad ( \rho , \tau , \kappa = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

Since (177) constitute a group, the transformations (l78) also con-stitute a group. This group of $c _ { \tau \kappa \rho }$ is said to be induced by the group $X _ { \sigma } f .$ The constants $\bar { c } _ { \tau \kappa \rho }$ and ${ c } _ { \lambda \mu \nu }$ related through (178) are not essentially different.A point of $\mathfrak { M }$ is simply transformed to another·point of $\mathfrak { M }$ Two groups with the same structure constants are said to be equivalent.

For proving the converse of this theorem- that is, that there exists an $\pmb { r }$ -parameter group (174) for the $r ^ { 3 }$ constants satisfying (175) and (176)- it is sufficient to show the existence of $\psi _ { \sigma \rho } ( a )$ ， such that $\| \psi _ { \sigma \rho } ( a ) \| \neq 0$ satisfies Maurer's relations (161)

$$
\frac { \partial \psi _ { \rho \sigma } } { \partial a _ { \tau } } - \frac { \partial \psi _ { \rho \tau } } { \partial a _ { \sigma } } = \sum _ { \lambda , \kappa } c _ { \lambda \kappa \rho } \psi _ { \lambda \sigma } \psi _ { \kappa \tau } \qquad ( \rho , \sigma , \tau = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

This relation is merely the integrability condition for the Pfaffan (162), which can be written, similarly to (158a),

$$
d f ( x ^ { \prime } ) = \sum _ { \rho } P _ { \rho } X _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } f ( x ^ { \prime } ) , \qquad P _ { \rho } = \sum _ { \sigma } \psi _ { \rho \sigma } ( a ) \ d a _ { \sigma } .
$$

Let $a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { r }$ be the canonical parameters. The finite transformations of the corresponding group are obtained by the infinitesimal transformation $\textstyle ( \sum _ { i } a _ { i } X _ { i } f ) \delta t$ from $t = 0$ to $t = 1$ . If we continue it from $t = 1$ to $t = 1 + \delta t$ ，then we obtain the finite transformations with the canonical parameters $a _ { 1 } ( 1 + \delta t ) , \dotsc , a _ { r } ( 1 + \delta t )$ .From(180) we obtain

$$
\delta f ( x ^ { \prime } ) = \sum _ { \rho , \sigma } \psi _ { \rho \sigma } ( a ) a _ { \sigma } X _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } f ( x ^ { \prime } ) \delta t .
$$

On the other hand,

$$
\delta f ( x ^ { \prime } ) = \sum _ { \rho } a _ { \rho } X _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } f ( x ^ { \prime } ) \delta t .
$$

Hence

$$
\sum _ { \sigma } \psi _ { \rho \sigma } ( a ) a _ { \sigma } = a _ { \rho } \qquad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

Multiply Maurer's relation by $a _ { \tau }$ and sum over $\pmb { \tau }$ ； then, referring to (181),we obtain

$$
\sum _ { \tau } \frac { \partial \psi _ { \rho \sigma } } { \partial a _ { \tau } } a _ { \tau } - \sum _ { \tau } \frac { \partial \psi _ { \rho \tau } } { \partial a _ { \sigma } } a _ { \tau } = \sum _ { \lambda , \kappa } c _ { \lambda \kappa \rho } \psi _ { \lambda \sigma } a _ { \kappa } .
$$

Write

$$
U f = a _ { 1 } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial a _ { 1 } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + a _ { r } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial a _ { r } } } ;
$$

then, from (181),

$$
U \psi _ { \rho \sigma } = \epsilon _ { \rho \sigma } - \psi _ { \rho \sigma } + \sum _ { \lambda , \kappa } c _ { \lambda \kappa \rho } \psi _ { \lambda \sigma } a _ { \kappa } \qquad ( \rho , \sigma = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

Put $a _ { \rho } = \lambda _ { \rho } t$ ; then (182） becomes

$$
\frac { \partial ( t \psi _ { \rho \sigma } ) } { \partial t } = \epsilon _ { \rho \sigma } + \sum _ { \lambda , \kappa } c _ { \lambda \kappa \rho } ( t \psi _ { \lambda \sigma } ) \lambda _ { \kappa } ,
$$

since

$$
\frac { \partial \psi _ { \rho \sigma } } { \partial t } = \sum _ { \tau } \frac { \partial \psi _ { \rho \sigma } } { \partial a _ { \tau } } \lambda _ { \tau } = t ^ { - 1 } U \psi _ { \rho \sigma } .
$$

Denote by $L$ the matrix $( l _ { \rho \lambda } )$ , such that $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { \kappa } c _ { \lambda \kappa \rho } \lambda _ { \kappa } = l _ { \rho \lambda } , } \end{array}$ and by $\Psi = t \psi$ the matrix $( \Psi _ { \rho \sigma } ) = t ( \psi _ { \rho \sigma } )$ ; then the $r ^ { 3 }$ equations become

$$
\frac { \partial \Psi } { \partial t } = L \Psi + I ,
$$

where $\Psi$ reduces to a null matrix for $t = 0$ If $\lambda _ { 1 } , . . . , \lambda _ { \tau }$ are given, then this matrix equation determines $\Psi$ with the initial condition that $\Psi = 0$ for $t = 0$ in the form

$$
\Psi = t I + \frac { 1 } { 2 ! } t ^ { 2 } L + \frac { 1 } { 3 ! } t ^ { 3 } L ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

Hence,

$$
\psi = I + \frac { t } { 2 ! } L + \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 3 ! } L ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

If we write $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { \kappa } c _ { \sigma \kappa \rho } a _ { \kappa } = A _ { \rho \sigma } } \end{array}$ and denote the matrix of $A _ { \rho \sigma } = t l _ { \rho \sigma }$ by $\pmb { A }$ ， then the $\psi _ { \rho \sigma }$ are given as functions of $^ { a }$ by means of

$$
\psi = I + \frac { 1 } { 1 ! } A + \frac { 1 } { 2 ! } A ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , A ^ { m } = t ^ { m } L ^ { m } .
$$

It can be shown that this matrix $\psi$ satisfies Maurer's relation (161).

Denote the matrices of $\psi _ { \rho \sigma }$ and $\alpha _ { \rho \sigma }$ by $\psi$ and $\pmb { \alpha }$ ，respectively. By definition, $\psi _ { \alpha } = I .$ Then (l83) is written in the form

$$
\psi = \frac { e ^ { t L } - 1 } { t L }
$$

and the transposed reciprocal in the form

$$
\alpha ^ { \prime } = \frac { t L } { e ^ { t L } - 1 }
$$

in the neighborhood of $t = 0$ .With Bernoulli's numbers $B _ { 1 } , B _ { 2 } , \ldots ,$ it is known that

$$
\frac { x } { e ^ { x } - 1 } = 1 - \frac { x } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 ! } B _ { 1 } x ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 4 ! } B _ { 2 } x ^ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

Thus

$$
\alpha ^ { \prime } = I - \frac { 1 } { 2 } A + \frac { 1 } { 2 ! } B _ { 1 } A ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 4 ! } B _ { 2 } A ^ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

Since the elements of the transposed matrix are $\begin{array} { r } { A _ { \rho \sigma } ^ { \prime } = - \sum _ { \tau } a _ { \tau } c _ { \tau \rho \sigma } } \end{array}$ we have

$$
\alpha = I - \frac { 1 } { 2 } A ^ { \prime } + \frac { 1 } { 2 ! } B _ { 1 } A ^ { \prime 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 4 ! } B _ { 2 } A ^ { \prime 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

or, by writing the matrix of $c _ { \tau \rho \sigma }$ as $C _ { \tau }$ ，

$$
\alpha = I + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { \tau } a _ { \tau } C _ { \tau } + \frac { 1 } { 2 ! } B _ { 1 } \Bigl ( \sum _ { \tau } a _ { \tau } C _ { \tau } \Bigr ) ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 4 ! } B _ { 2 } \Bigl ( \sum _ { \tau } a _ { \tau } C _ { \tau } \Bigr ) ^ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

where

$$
A ^ { \prime } = - \sum _ { \tau } a _ { \tau } C _ { \tau } , \qquad C _ { \tau } = ( c _ { \tau \rho \sigma } ) ,
$$

with fixed $\pmb { \tau }$ .Thus,the infinitesimal transformations $A _ { 1 } f _ { 3 } \ldots , A _ { r } f$ are determined from the matrix $\pmb { \alpha }$

This proof is due to Schur (l89l).This is the foundation of Baker's method of solving a system of linear differential equations with variable coefficients (Baker,1902,1904;Hagihara,1940),which will be applied in Chapter l2 (Volume 3） to several problems in celestial mechanics. It is to be noticed that the series (l84) is convergent if $\pmb { A }$ ,and accordingly $t _ { : }$ ， is sufficiently small. Since the elements $\psi _ { \rho \sigma }$ are convergent series arranged in powers of $a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { r }$ ,so are the elements $\alpha _ { \rho \sigma }$ of the reciprocal matrix.

Lie's series are applied by Grobner (l96O) to the numerical solution of differential equations,and by Knapp (l964） to the computation of perturbation (Section 10.44).

# 4.27ADJOINT GROUPS

A finite continuous group containing at least one transformation that transforms every point of a region in a space $R _ { n } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } )$ to another point of the space is said to be transitive. If for a given set of values of $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } , x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , x _ { n } ^ { \prime }$ there is at least one set of values of $a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { r }$ satisfying (l55)，then the transformation group (l55） is transitive. Otherwise it is intransitive.Hence,a transitive group should contain at least $\pmb { n }$ essential parameters.For $n = r$ there exists in general a transformation that transforms one arbitrary point to another arbitrary point. Thus, there is no transformation that makes a general point invariant, except the identity transformation. Such a group is called simply transitive. (For the same group definitions in another context, see discussion preceding the first fundamental theorem of Lie, Section 4.26.) Since the finite group of $r$ independent infinitesimal transformations

$$
X _ { \rho } f = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \xi _ { \rho i } ( x ) \ { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { i } } } \qquad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r )
$$

can be written

$$
x _ { i } ^ { \prime } = x _ { i } + \sum _ { \rho = 1 } ^ { r } e _ { \rho } \xi _ { \rho i } ( x ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

it is sufficient for this group to be transitive that none of the nth order determinants $\| \xi _ { \rho i } \|$ be zero. Denote the rank of the matrix $( \xi _ { \rho i } )$ by $m \textless n$ ； then $_ m$ transformations out of $X _ { \rho } f$ are independent and the remaining transformations are linear combinations of these. These $\pmb { m }$ transformations form a complete system (Section 4.9).Hence the system admits $n - m$ independent solutions $\Omega _ { 1 } ( { \pmb x } ) , . . . , \Omega _ { n - m } ( { \pmb x } )$ .Thus there are $n - m$ relations between $_ { x }$ and $\scriptstyle { \pmb x } ^ { \prime }$ ; that is,

$$
\Omega _ { s } ( x ^ { \prime } ) = \Omega _ { s } ( x ) \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - m ) .
$$

Hence,in order for the group to be transitive,it is necessary and sufficient that $m$ of $X _ { 1 } f = 0 , . . . , X _ { r } f = 0$ be independent. If the group is transitive it contains no invariant.If it is intransitive, then the solutions common to the $r$ transformations $X _ { \rho } f = 0$ are the only invariants, and

$$
\Omega _ { 1 } ( x ) = C _ { 1 } , . . . , \Omega _ { n - m } ( x ) = C _ { n - m }
$$

divide the $\pmb { n }$ dimensional space into $_ m$ -dimensional regions $M _ { m }$ that are invariant for $X _ { 1 } f = 0 , . . . , X _ { \tau } f = 0$ ，This invariant manifold $M _ { m }$ can be determined by eliminating the given transformation group from the finite equations (155).

Since a point of the space $R _ { n }$ can belong to one and only one of the $\infty ^ { n - m }$ manifolds (186),we say that any manifold decomposes (zerlegen) the space $R _ { n }$ ； this decomposition is invariant for any transformation of $X _ { 1 } f = 0 , . . . , X _ { \tau } f = 0 \quad$ ，Even a transitive group can decompose $R _ { n }$ into $\infty ^ { n - m }$ manifolds of the form (186).Such manifolds are invariant for the transformation group as a whole, but each individual manifold may be interchanged by a single transformation of the group. If a group decomposes $R _ { n }$ invariantly into $\infty ^ { n - m }$ $m$ -dimensional manifolds $M _ { m }$ in this manner, then the group is called imprimitive. Otherwise it is primitive. An intransitive group is imprimitive.A primitive group is transitive.

The condition for imprimitivity is that the complete system of $_ m$ independent equations $Y _ { 1 } f = 0 , . . . , Y _ { m } f = 0$ admits all transformations of the group. This system of equations defines an $_ m$ -dimensional manifold $M _ { m }$ .If the system admits all transformations of the group, then $M _ { m }$ is invariant for those transformations. The integer $\pmb { m }$ is called the order of imprimitivity (Section 4.23).The necessary and sufficient condition for the order of imprimitivity to be $_ m$ is that the complete system of equations $X _ { 1 } f = 0 , . . . , X _ { m } f = 0$ admits $n - m$ independent solutions.

Consider in $m$ independent infinitesimal transformations

$$
X _ { \rho } f = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \xi _ { \rho i } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { i } } } \qquad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

a set of $\infty ^ { n - m }$ infinitesimal transformations represented by

$$
e _ { 1 } X _ { 1 } f + \cdot \cdot \cdot + e _ { m } X _ { m } f
$$

with $_ m$ arbitrary constants $e _ { 1 } , \ldots , e _ { m }$ . The transformed set can be written

$$
\sum _ { \rho = 1 } ^ { m } e _ { \rho } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \xi _ { \rho i } ( x ) \ \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { i } } = \sum _ { \rho = 1 } ^ { m } e _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \xi _ { \rho i } ( x ^ { \prime } ) \ \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } .
$$

Hence, if

$$
\sum _ { \rho = 1 } ^ { m } e _ { \rho } X _ { \rho } f = \sum _ { \sigma = 1 } ^ { m } e _ { \sigma } ^ { \prime } X _ { \sigma } ^ { \prime } f ,
$$

then the set of transformations $\scriptstyle \sum _ { \rho = 1 } ^ { m } e _ { \rho } X _ { \rho } f$ is invariant for the transformation of $_ { x }$ to $\scriptstyle { \pmb x } ^ { \prime }$ ; that is, $S T _ { e } S ^ { - 1 } = T _ { e ^ { \prime } ; }$ or $S T _ { e } = T _ { e ^ { \prime } } S .$

Let $Y f$ be a one-parameter group for the transformation of the parameter $t$ -from $_ x$ to $x _ { i } ^ { \prime } = f _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } , t )$ . Then $x _ { i } ^ { \prime } = x _ { i } + t Y + \cdot \cdot \cdot _ { ; }$ and $X _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } f = X _ { \rho } f + t ( Y , X _ { \rho } ) f + \cdot \cdot \cdot$ . In order that this transformation may belong to the set of those transformations, we should have

Hence

$$
( Y , X _ { \rho } ) f = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { m } g _ { \rho j } X _ { j } f \qquad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

$$
\frac { \partial ( X _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } f ) } { \partial t } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { m } g _ { \rho j } X _ { j } ^ { \prime } f \qquad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

Since

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \frac { \partial } { \partial t } \sum _ { \rho } e _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } X _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } f = \sum _ { \rho } \frac { d e _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } } { d t } X _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } f + \sum _ { \rho } e _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } \sum _ { j } g _ { \rho j } X _ { j } ^ { \prime } f } } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { = \sum _ { \rho } \{ \frac { d e _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } } { d t } + \sum _ { j } g _ { j \rho } e _ { j } ^ { \prime } \} X _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } f , } \end{array}
$$

therefore

$$
\frac { d e _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } } { d t } + \sum _ { j } g _ { j \rho } e _ { j } ^ { \prime } = 0 .
$$

The values of $\boldsymbol { e } _ { \rho }$ are determined by integrating (188） from $e _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } = e _ { \rho }$ at $t = 0$

If $X _ { 1 } f _ { 3 } \ldots , X _ { r } f$ satisfies (188)，that is,if $\textstyle \sum _ { \rho } e _ { \rho } X _ { \rho } f$ constitutes an $\boldsymbol { r }$ -parameter group and all transformations of this set of transformation groups make the one-parameter group $Y f$ invariant, then

$$
( Y , X _ { \rho } ) f = \sum _ { \sigma = 1 } ^ { r } g _ { \rho \sigma } X _ { \sigma } f \qquad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

The coefficients $g _ { \rho \sigma }$ are given by (188) in terms of $\boldsymbol { e } _ { \rho }$ Then $r + 1$ infinitesimal transformations $X _ { 1 } f , . . . , X _ { r } f ,$ Yf constitute an $( r + 1 )$ parameter group,and the $\boldsymbol { r }$ transformations $X _ { 1 } f , . . . , X _ { \tau } f$ constitute its $\pmb { r }$ parameter subgroup. Let $x _ { i } ^ { \prime } = \psi _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ; a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { r + 1 } )$ be the finite transformations of the $( r + 1 )$ -parameter group; then the set of transformations $\textstyle \sum _ { \rho } e _ { \rho } X _ { \rho } f$ is invariant for the change of $_ { x }$ to $\mathbf { \Delta } _ { \mathbf { \boldsymbol { x } } ^ { \prime } }$

A similar situation exists for infinitesimal transformations $Y _ { 1 } f _ { 3 } \ldots , Y _ { m } f$ instead ofa single Yf. If $X _ { 1 } f , . . . , X _ { \tau } f$ are invariant for all transformations of an $( r + m )$ -parameter group, then they constitute an invariant subgroup (Lie,l888). Then (189） holds for all such $Y _ { i } f .$ Hence,if $X _ { 1 } f , . . . , X _ { \tau } f$ are contained in $X _ { 1 } f , . . . , X _ { r } f , Y _ { 1 } f , . . . , Y _ { m } f ,$ and all commutators $( Y _ { i } , Y _ { k } ) f$ are linear combinations of $X _ { 1 } f , . . . , X _ { r } f$ with constant coefficients, then $X _ { 1 } f , . . . , X _ { r } f$ constitute an invariant subgroup. In particular, if the transformation group $( X _ { \sigma } , X _ { \rho } )$ is an $\boldsymbol { r }$ -parameter group,then the group is the same as the group $X _ { 1 } f , . . . , X _ { r } f .$ If it contains less than $\pmb { r }$ parameters, then the group is an invariant subgroup of $X _ { 1 } f , \ldots , X _ { r } f .$ It can be shown that the group $X _ { 1 } f , . . . , X _ { r } f$ constitutes an invariant subgroup if mutually independent infinitesimal transformations $\boldsymbol { e } _ { 1 } X _ { 1 } \boldsymbol { f } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \boldsymbol { e } _ { \tau } X _ { \tau } \boldsymbol { f } ,$ also independent of all commutators $( X _ { \rho } , X _ { \sigma } )$ ， are added to $( X _ { \rho } , X _ { \sigma } )$ . Actually, $( X _ { \sigma } , X _ { \rho } ) f$ contains at most $\boldsymbol { r }$ -parameter groups, as it belongs to $X _ { 1 } f _ { 3 } \ldots , X _ { r } f .$ Since

$$
( ( X _ { \sigma } , X _ { \rho } ) , ( X _ { \lambda } , X _ { \mu } ) ) = \sum _ { \tau , \kappa } c _ { \sigma \rho \tau } c _ { \lambda \mu \kappa } ( X _ { \tau } , X _ { \kappa } )
$$

from (182), the transformations $( X _ { \sigma } , X _ { \rho } )$ constitute a group. Since

$$
( X _ { \tau } , ( X _ { \rho } , X _ { \sigma } ) ) = \sum _ { \lambda } c _ { \rho \sigma \lambda } ( X _ { \tau } , X _ { \lambda } ) ,
$$

these transformations are invariant for $X _ { 1 } f _ { 3 } \ldots , X _ { r } f .$ This theorem will be applied in Section 4.28 for simple groups.

If infinitesimal transformations $Y _ { 1 } f _ { 3 } \ldots , Y _ { p } f$ and $Z _ { 1 } f _ { 3 } \ldots , Z _ { q } f$ of two invariant subgroups of the same group $G _ { r }$ have no infinitesimal transformation in common, then the $( Y _ { i } , Z _ { j } )$ should all vanish identically. If they have any common transformation, then the common infinitesimal transformation generates invariant subgroups of $G _ { \tau }$ . Actually， the $( Y _ { i } , Z _ { j } )$ are linear combinations of the infinitesimal transformations of the invariant subgroup with constant coefficients. If they have no transformation in common, then these combinations should be zero. If the $( Y _ { i } , Z _ { j } )$ are all zero, then the $Y _ { i }$ and $Z _ { j }$ are said to be permutable (vertauschbar). This theorem will be applied also in Section 4.28 for semisimple groups.

Let the independent transformations $Y _ { 1 } f _ { 3 } \ldots , Y _ { r } f$ belonging to the group $X _ { 1 } f _ { 3 } \ldots , X _ { \tau } f$ be such that

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { ( Y _ { \sigma } , Y _ { \rho } ) f = \displaystyle \sum _ { \tau } c _ { \sigma \rho \tau } ^ { \prime } Y _ { \tau } f , ( X _ { \sigma } , X _ { \rho } ) f = \displaystyle \sum _ { \tau } c _ { \sigma \rho \tau } X _ { \tau } f , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { \displaystyle Y _ { \sigma } f = \displaystyle \sum _ { \rho } h _ { \sigma \rho } X _ { \rho } f . } } } \end{array}
$$

Then,

$$
( Y _ { \sigma } , Y _ { \rho } ) f = \sum _ { \lambda , \mu , \tau } h _ { \sigma \lambda } h _ { \rho \mu } c _ { \lambda \mu \tau } X _ { \tau } f = \sum _ { \lambda , \tau } h _ { \lambda \tau } c _ { \sigma \rho \lambda } ^ { \prime } X _ { \tau } f .
$$

Hence

$$
c _ { \sigma \rho \tau } ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { D } \sum _ { \lambda } \bigg \{ \frac { \partial D } { \partial h _ { \tau \lambda } } \sum _ { \mu , \nu } h _ { \sigma \mu } h _ { \rho \nu } c _ { \mu \nu \lambda } \bigg \} , \quad \quad D = \| h _ { \rho \sigma } \| .
$$

If arbitrary $Z _ { 1 } f _ { 3 } \ldots , Z _ { r } f$ are such that $\begin{array} { r } { ( Z _ { \sigma } , Z _ { \rho } ) f = \sum _ { \tau } c _ { \sigma \rho \tau } ^ { \prime } Z _ { \tau } f } \end{array}$ with the constants $c _ { \sigma \rho \tau } ^ { \prime }$ satisfying these relations, then $Z _ { \rho } f$ and $X _ { \sigma } f$ are said to be of equal structure (gleichzusammengesetzt). If $Y _ { 1 } f _ { 3 } \ldots , Y _ { r - q } f$ can be chosen so that, for the infinitesimal transformations

$$
Z _ { \rho } f = \sum _ { \sigma = 1 } ^ { r - q } h _ { \rho \sigma } Y _ { \sigma } f \qquad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r )
$$

of the $( r - q )$ -parameter group, none of the minor determinants of the $( r - q ) \operatorname { t h }$ order of $\| h _ { \rho \sigma } \|$ can be made zero, and, at the same time, chosen so that $\begin{array} { r } { ( Z _ { \sigma } , Z _ { \rho } ) = \sum _ { \tau = 1 } ^ { \tau } c _ { \sigma \rho \tau } Z _ { \tau } f } \end{array}$ are satisfied identically, then the group $X _ { 1 } f , \dotsc , X _ { \tau } f$ is said to be isomorphic for $q = 0$ ， and homomorphic for $q > 0$ to $Y _ { 1 } f _ { 3 } \ldots , Y _ { r - q } f .$ Two isomorphic or homomorphic groups are of equal structure, that is, have the same structure constants.

If $\textstyle \sum _ { \rho } c _ { \rho } X _ { \rho } f$ formed of the infinitesimal transformations $X _ { \rho } f$ of an $\pmb { r - }$ parameter group $x _ { i } ^ { \prime } = f _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { n } ; a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { r } )$ can be written in the form (l87) for a transformation of $_ { x }$ to $\scriptstyle { \pmb x } ^ { \prime }$ as new variables, then

$$
\ell _ { \tau } ^ { ' } = \sum _ { \sigma } \rho _ { \tau \sigma } ( a _ { 1 } , . . . , a _ { r } ) e _ { \sigma } \qquad ( \tau = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) ,
$$

where $e ^ { \prime }$ is independent of $_ x$ . Substituting $\pmb { x } _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime } = f _ { i } ( \pmb { x } ^ { \prime } , \pmb { b } )$ gives

$$
\sum _ { \rho } \ e _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } X _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } f = \sum _ { \sigma } e _ { \rho } ^ { \prime \prime } X _ { \rho } ^ { \prime \prime } f , \qquad e _ { \sigma } ^ { \prime \prime } = \sum _ { \tau } \rho _ { \sigma \tau } ( b _ { 1 } , . . . , b _ { r } ) e _ { \tau } ^ { \prime } .
$$

Since $\boldsymbol { x } _ { i } ^ { \prime } = f _ { i } ( \boldsymbol { x } , \boldsymbol { a } )$ form a group, we can write

$$
x _ { i } ^ { \prime \prime } = f _ { i } ( x , c ) , \qquad c _ { \rho } = \phi _ { \rho } ( a _ { 1 } , . . . , a _ { r } , b _ { 1 } , . . . , b _ { r } ) .
$$

Hence,

$$
\sum _ { \rho } e _ { \rho } X _ { \rho } f = \sum _ { \sigma } e _ { \sigma } ^ { \prime \prime } X _ { \sigma } ^ { \prime \prime } f ,
$$

and

$$
\epsilon _ { \sigma } ^ { \prime \prime } = \sum _ { \tau } \rho _ { \sigma \tau } ( c _ { 1 } , . . . , c _ { r } ) e _ { \tau } = \sum _ { \tau } \rho _ { \sigma \tau } ( \phi _ { 1 } ( a , b ) , . . . , \phi _ { r } ( a , b ) ) e _ { \tau } .
$$

Thus $\begin{array} { r } { e _ { \sigma } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \tau } \rho _ { \sigma \tau } ( a ) e _ { \tau } } \end{array}$ form a group, and

$$
\ell _ { \sigma } ^ { \prime \prime } = \sum _ { \tau , \kappa } \rho _ { \sigma \tau } ( b _ { 1 } , . . . , b _ { r } ) \rho _ { \tau \kappa } ( a _ { 1 } , . . . , a _ { r } ) e _ { \kappa } ,
$$

$$
\rho _ { \sigma \tau } ( \phi _ { 1 } ( a , b ) , \ldots , \phi _ { r } ( a , b ) ) \equiv \sum _ { \kappa } \rho _ { \kappa \tau } ( a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { r } ) \rho _ { \sigma \kappa } ( b _ { 1 } , \ldots , b _ { r } ) .
$$

Hence, the group (190) belongs to a group $\pmb { x } _ { i } ^ { \prime } = f _ { i } ( \pmb { x } , \pmb { a } )$ or to a group of infinitesimal transformations $X _ { 1 } f , . . . , X _ { \tau } f .$ This last group is called the adjoint group.

In other words, let $S T _ { e } S ^ { - 1 } = T _ { e ^ { \prime } }$ be the first transformation, and $S _ { 1 } T _ { e } { , } S _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } = T _ { e } { , }$ be the second. Then

$$
T _ { e ^ { \prime \prime } } = S _ { 1 } S T _ { e } S ^ { - 1 } S _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } = S _ { 2 } T _ { e } S _ { 2 } ^ { - 1 } ,
$$

with $S _ { 2 } = S _ { 1 } S$ If $S T _ { e } S ^ { - 1 } = T _ { e ^ { \prime } } , S T _ { e ^ { \prime } } S ^ { - 1 } = T _ { e ^ { \prime \prime } } ;$ then

$$
T _ { e ^ { \prime \prime } } T _ { e ^ { \prime } } = S T _ { e ^ { \prime } } S ^ { - 1 } S T _ { e } S ^ { - 1 } = S T _ { e ^ { \prime } } T _ { e } S ^ { - 1 } ;
$$

that is, $T _ { e }$ is in automorphism. Thus $T _ { e }$ constitutes a group, and every transformation of the adjoint group is homomorphic to the given group.Hence, if a canonical parameter $\boldsymbol { e } _ { \rho }$ admits the transformations of an adjoint group,then the structure constants are invariant. Consequently, if the infinitesimal transformations of an adjoint group are written in the form

$$
E _ { \mu } f = \sum _ { \lambda , \kappa } c _ { \lambda \mu \kappa } e _ { \lambda } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial e _ { \kappa } } } \qquad ( \mu = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) ,
$$

then $\lambda _ { 1 } E _ { 1 } f + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \lambda _ { \tau } E _ { \tau } f$ is a general infinitesimal transformation of the adjoint group and satisfes the relation

$$
( E _ { \mu } , E _ { \nu } ) f = \sum _ { \rho } c _ { \mu \nu \rho } E _ { \rho } f \qquad ( \mu , \nu = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

If $E _ { 1 } f _ { 3 } \ldots , E _ { \tau } f$ are not independent, but $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { \rho } g _ { \rho } E _ { \rho } f \equiv 0 } \end{array}$ ,then, from

$$
\sum _ { \rho } g _ { \rho } \sum _ { \sigma , \tau } c _ { \sigma \rho \tau } e _ { \sigma } \frac { \partial f } { \partial e _ { \tau } } = 0 ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\sum _ { \rho } g _ { \rho } c _ { \sigma \rho \tau } = 0 \qquad ( \sigma , \tau = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

Hence

$$
\Bigl ( X _ { \sigma } f , \sum _ { \rho } g _ { \rho } X _ { \rho } f \Bigr ) = \sum _ { \tau } \Bigl ( \sum _ { \rho } g _ { \rho } c _ { \sigma \rho \tau } \Bigr ) X _ { \tau } f \equiv 0 .
$$

Thus, $\textstyle \sum _ { \rho } g _ { \rho } X _ { \rho } f$ is permutable with all infinitesimal transformations $X _ { \rho } f .$ Conversely, if $X _ { 1 } f , . . . , X _ { r } f$ contain allinfinitesimal transformations that are permutable with all $X _ { \sigma } f$ in $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { \rho } g _ { \rho } X _ { \rho } f , } \end{array}$ then $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { \rho } g _ { \rho } E _ { \rho } f \equiv 0 . } \end{array}$ Such a $\textstyle \sum _ { \rho } g _ { \rho } X _ { \rho } f$ is called the distinguished (ausgezeichnete) infinitesimal transformation of the group.A distinguished infinitesimal transformation is invariant for the transformation $\boldsymbol { x } _ { i } ^ { \prime } = f _ { i } ( \boldsymbol { x } , \boldsymbol { a } )$ ; that is,

$$
\sum _ { \rho } g _ { \rho } X _ { \rho } f = \sum _ { \rho } g _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } X _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } f .
$$

In the corresponding adjoint group, $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { \rho } g _ { \rho } E _ { \rho } f = 0 . } \end{array}$ Hence, if there are just $m$ such distinguished transformations among $X _ { \rho } f$ of the transformation group $G _ { \tau ; \tau }$ then $r \mathrm { ~ - ~ } m$ among $E _ { \sigma } f$ are not independent. The adjoint group has $r \mathrm { ~ - ~ } m$ essential parameters. Thus, since $\textstyle \sum _ { \rho } g _ { \rho } X _ { \rho } f$ is invariant for the group $G _ { r }$ ，this group $G _ { r }$ is imprimitive.Conversely,if $G _ { \tau }$ is primitive in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ,$ then $G _ { r }$ does not contain any distinguished infinitesimal transformation. The adjoint group of an $\boldsymbol { r }$ parameter primitive group contains $\pmb { r }$ essential parameters.Conversely, $G _ { r }$ has a primitive group, that is, an adjoint group.

The transformation (155) with $\boldsymbol { r }$ essential parameters satisfies

$$
\frac { \partial x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { \partial a _ { \rho } } = \sum _ { \sigma } \psi _ { \sigma \rho } ( a ) \xi _ { \sigma i } ( x ^ { \prime } ) .
$$

Since $\| \psi _ { \sigma \rho } \| \neq 0 _ { ; }$ ，these equations can be solved in the form

$$
\xi _ { \sigma i } ( \pmb { x } ^ { \prime } ) = \sum _ { \rho } \alpha _ { \sigma \rho } ( a ) \frac { \partial \pmb { x } _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { \partial a _ { \rho } } ,
$$

and the transformation (155) is generated by $\boldsymbol { r }$ independent infinitesimal transformations

$$
X _ { \rho } f = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \xi _ { \rho i } ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { i } } \quad \quad ( \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

The $\pmb { r }$ infinitesimal transformations of the corresponding parameter group

$$
A _ { \rho } f = \sum _ { \sigma } \alpha _ { \rho \sigma } ( a ) \ : \frac { \partial f } { \partial a _ { \sigma } }
$$

are independent. It has been seen that their structure constants are the same as those of $X _ { \rho } f ;$ that is,if

$$
( X _ { \rho } , X _ { \sigma } ) f = \sum _ { \tau } c _ { \rho \sigma \tau } X _ { \tau } f ,
$$

then

$$
( A _ { \rho } , A _ { \sigma } ) f = \sum _ { \tau } c _ { \rho \sigma \tau } A _ { \tau } f .
$$

We have seen also that the finite transformation

$$
x _ { i } ^ { \prime } = F _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { n } , \lambda _ { 1 } t , . . . , \lambda _ { r } t )
$$

with the canonical form

$$
x _ { i } ^ { \prime } = x _ { i } + \sum _ { \sigma } e _ { \sigma } \xi _ { \sigma i } ( x ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot
$$

of the transformation (155) is obtained by integrating

$$
\frac { d x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } } { \sum _ { \rho } \lambda _ { \rho } \xi _ { \rho 1 } ^ { \prime } } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = \frac { d x _ { n } ^ { \prime } } { \sum _ { \rho } \lambda _ { \rho } \xi _ { \rho n } ^ { \prime } } = d t ,
$$

with the initial condition $x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ^ { \prime } = x _ { n }$ for $t = 0$ Multiply (194) by $\lambda _ { \sigma }$ and sum over $\pmb { \sigma }$ ,then from these differential equations we obtain

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { d t } = \sum _ { \rho , \sigma } \lambda _ { \rho } \alpha _ { \rho \sigma } ( a ) \frac { \partial x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { \partial a _ { \sigma } } .
$$

Hence, regarded as functions of $t _ { : }$ ， the parameters $a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { r }$ must satisfy

$$
\frac { d a _ { \rho } } { d t } = \sum _ { \sigma } \lambda _ { \sigma } \alpha _ { \sigma \rho } ( a ) .
$$

The initial condition is that $^ { a }$ should be $a ^ { 0 }$ ，obtained by putting $\boldsymbol { x } = \boldsymbol { x } ^ { \prime }$ for $t = 0$ in the equations (155). Integrating (l96) with this initial condition,we obtain

$$
a _ { \rho } = \Phi _ { \rho } ( \lambda _ { 1 } t , . . . , \lambda _ { \tau } t ) .
$$

Putting these in (155)，we obtain the required canonical form (195). Write this as

$$
a _ { \rho } = \Phi _ { \rho } ( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { r } ) , \qquad u = \lambda t .
$$

Then the canonical transformation is of the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { x _ { i } ^ { \prime } = f _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { n } ; \Phi _ { 1 } ( u ) , \dots , \Phi _ { r } ( u ) ) . } \end{array}
$$

In other words, the system of equations in $a _ { 1 } , \dotsc , a _ { r } , t$ admits the infinitesimal transformation

$$
A f = \lambda _ { 1 } A _ { 1 } f + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \lambda _ { r } A _ { r } f + \frac { \partial f } { \partial t } ,
$$

and can be integrated with respect to $^ { a }$ with the initial condition that $\ a = a ^ { 0 }$ for $t = 0$ ,where it is assumed that $\| \psi _ { \rho \sigma } \| \neq 0$ for $\scriptstyle a = a ^ { 0 }$

The computation is simplifed if we use the adjoint group (Lie, 1893, 1893a). Substituting ${ \pmb x } _ { i } ^ { \prime } = f _ { i } ( { \pmb x } )$ in

$$
\sum _ { \rho } e _ { \rho } \sum _ { i } \xi _ { \rho i } ( x ) \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { i } } = \sum _ { \rho } e _ { \rho } X _ { \rho } f ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\sum _ { \rho } e _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } X _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } f = \sum _ { \sigma } e _ { \sigma } X _ { \sigma } f .
$$

Accordingly

$$
e _ { \sigma } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \tau } \rho _ { \sigma \tau } ( a ) e _ { \tau }
$$

defines the adjoint group. If the integral (l97) of the system (l96) is known， then the canonical form of the adjoint group is obtained by substituting (l97) in (198). The result is of the form

$$
\boldsymbol { e } _ { \sigma } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \tau } \rho _ { \sigma \tau } ( \Phi _ { 1 } ( \lambda t ) , . . . , \Phi _ { \tau } ( \lambda t ) ) \boldsymbol { e } _ { \tau } .
$$

If the integral (l97） is not known,then we determine the canonical form by solving

$$
\frac { d \boldsymbol { e } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } } { \underset { \rho } { \sum } \lambda _ { \rho } \boldsymbol { e } _ { \rho 1 } ^ { \prime } } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = \frac { d \boldsymbol { e } _ { r } ^ { \prime } } { \underset { \rho } { \sum } \lambda _ { \rho } \boldsymbol { e } _ { \rho r } ^ { \prime } } = d t ,
$$

with the initial condition that $e ^ { \prime } = e$ for $t = 0$ ，because the adjoint group is generated by

$$
E _ { \rho } f = \sum _ { \sigma , \tau } c _ { \sigma \rho \tau } e _ { \sigma } \frac { \partial f } { \partial e _ { \tau } } , \qquad e _ { \rho \sigma } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \tau } c _ { \tau \rho \sigma } e _ { \tau } ^ { \prime }
$$

and satisfies

$$
( E _ { \sigma } , E _ { \rho } ) f = \sum _ { \tau } c _ { \sigma \rho \tau } E _ { \tau } f .
$$

Equations (l99) can be solved by mere algebraic operations, since they have constant coeffcients. Thus the canonical form of the adjoint group is determined in the form

$$
\boldsymbol { e } _ { \mu } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \nu } \sigma _ { \mu \nu } ( \lambda _ { 1 } t , . . . , \lambda _ { \tau } t ) \boldsymbol { e } _ { \nu } ,
$$

where $\sigma _ { \mu \nu }$ contain $\lambda t$ only in the combination $\lambda t = u$ 、This will be identical with (198) if we substitute (197); that is,

$$
\rho _ { \mu \nu } ( a _ { 1 } , . . . , a _ { r } ) = \sigma _ { \mu \nu } ( \lambda _ { 1 } t , . . . , \lambda _ { r } t )
$$

is an identity. This is the integral of (196).It satisfies (197) identically for $a = a ^ { 0 }$ ， $t = 0$ It is when the $\boldsymbol { r }$ parameter group does not contain the distinguished infinitesimal transformation that the equations (199） can be solved algebraically. If the group contains $ { l }$ independent distinguished transformations,that is,if $X _ { 1 } f , . . . , X _ { \tau } f$ contain $\mathbf { \xi } _ { l }$ transformations $\begin{array} { r } { Z f = \sum _ { i } \xi _ { i } ( { \pmb x } ) \ \partial f / \partial { \pmb x } _ { i } } \end{array}$ that are permutable with each of $X _ { 1 } f , \ldots , X _ { r } f ,$ then just $\mathbf { \xi } _ { l }$ independent integrations should be carried out besides algebraic operations (Lie, l927). The adjoint group constitutes, then, an $( r - l )$ -parameter group and obeys the relation (l95). The structure constants $c _ { \rho \sigma \tau }$ should satisfy Lie's third fundamental theorem. Conversely, if $r ^ { 3 }$ structure constants $c _ { \rho \sigma \tau }$ are given, then $X _ { 1 } f , . . . , X _ { r } f$ need not be considered, since the structure constants give the $\boldsymbol { r }$ parameter group.

Baker's matrix method of integrating a system of linear differential equations is founded on this use of the adjoint group (Baker, l902,1904; Hagihara, 1940).

# 4.28SIMPLE AND SEMISIMPLE GROUPS

A group that does not contain any invariant subgroup is called simple. Otherwise it is nonsimple (Lie,1893,i927; Cartan,1930,1933; Chevalley, 1951；see also Weyl, 1923,1923a,1924,1931,1939;van der Waerden, 1955,1959；Speiser,1923；Pontrjagin,1939;Grobner,1960).A simple group contains only one homomorphic subgroup, namely, the identity transformation.

Consider a nonsimple group. We choose the infinitesimal transformations of the group $G _ { r }$ so that $X _ { m + 1 } f _ { 3 } \ldots , X _ { \tau } f$ generate an $( r - m )$ parameter group.Then the structure of $G _ { r }$ is such that

$$
( X _ { \mu } , X _ { \nu } ) f = \sum _ { \sigma = 1 } ^ { m } c _ { \mu \nu \sigma } X _ { \sigma } f + \sum _ { \tau = 1 } ^ { r - m } c _ { \mu , \nu , m + \tau } X _ { m + \tau } f
$$

$$
( X _ { \mu } , X _ { m + k } ) f = \sum _ { \tau = 1 } ^ { r - m } c _ { \mu , m + k , m + \tau } X _ { m + \tau } f ,
$$

$$
( X _ { m + k } , X _ { m + j } ) f = \sum _ { \stackrel { \scriptstyle \tau = 1 } { \tau = 1 } } ^ { \stackrel { \scriptstyle \tau - m } { } } c _ { m + k , m + j , m + \tau } X _ { m + \tau } f
$$

Let the $_ m$ -parameter group homomorphic to $G _ { \tau }$ determined by the invariant subgroup be

$$
( \bar { X } _ { \mu } , \bar { X } _ { v } ) f = \sum _ { \sigma = 1 } ^ { m } c _ { \mu v \sigma } \bar { X } _ { \sigma } f \qquad ( \mu , \nu = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

and suppose that $\overline { { X } } _ { q + 1 } f , . . . , \overline { { X } } _ { m } f$ $( 0 < q < m )$ generate an invariant subgroup composed of other transformations besides the identity transformation. Then $\bar { X } _ { q + 1 } f , . . . , \bar { X } _ { m } f , X _ { m + 1 } f , . . . , X _ { r } f$ generate an $( r - q )$ - parameter invariant subgroup $G _ { \tau - q }$ containing the $( r - m )$ parameter invariant subgroup $G _ { r - m }$ .Hence, in order that the $\pmb { m }$ parameter group $G _ { m }$ ,homomorphic to the given $\pmb { r }$ -parameter group $G _ { \tau }$ $( r > m )$ ， shall be simple,it is necessary and sufficient that it be possible to obtain the structure of $G _ { r }$ so that the invariant subgroup of the highest order $r \mathrm { ~ - ~ } m$ corresponds to the identity transformation.

We can derive from the structure of the $\boldsymbol { r }$ -parameter group the structure of a sequence_of simple groups by choosing the subgroups ${ \overline { { G } } } _ { 1 } , { \overline { { G } } } _ { 2 } , \dots$ of $G _ { r }$ so that $\overline { { G } } _ { 1 }$ is invariant in $G _ { r }$ but is not contained in any larger invariant subgroup of $\overline { { G } } _ { k }$ ,and so that $\overline { { G } } _ { k + 1 }$ is invariant in $\overline { { G } } _ { k }$ but is not contained in any larger invariant subgroup of $\overleftarrow { G } _ { k }$ . Then $\overline { { G } } _ { 1 }$ gives the structure of the simple group that is isomorphic to $G _ { r } ,$ and $\overline { { G } } _ { 2 }$ gives the structure of the simple group that is isomorphic to $\overline { { G } } _ { 1 }$ , and so on. The numbers of the essential parameters of such simple groups in this sequence are $\boldsymbol { r } - \boldsymbol { r } _ { 1 } , \boldsymbol { r } _ { 1 } - \boldsymbol { r } _ { 2 } , \boldsymbol { r } _ { 2 } - \boldsymbol { r } _ { 3 } , \ldots ,$ respectively. These are called the indices of the simple groups; since each index is smaller than the preceding one, the process will end at the stage where $\overline { { G } } _ { m }$ ,for example, consists of only the identity transformation and $\overline { { G } } _ { m - 1 }$ is simple. Thus we get $\pmb { m }$ such subgroups and the number of the essential parameters is $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { r } } .$

This sequence of $G _ { \tau } , \overline { { G } } _ { 1 } , \overline { { G } } _ { 2 } , . . . , \overline { { G } } _ { m }$ of the group $G _ { r }$ is called the composition series (Kompositionsreihe). The sequence of invariant subgroups only is called the normal series (Normalreihe). If no other subgroup can be inserted between any two of the subgroups in a sequence, that is, if no refinement (Verfeinerung) can be made, then the sequence is a composition series.Two normal series are isomorphic and can be refined. This is Schreier's theorem (Schreier,1926,1927).Since $\overline { { G } } _ { 1 }$ is not necessarily the only subgroup in $G _ { r }$ with the same property, there may be more than one composition series - not necessarily of the same property.

Let the differences between the essential parameters of $G _ { r }$ and those of the largest subgroup containing $\overline { { G } } _ { 1 }$ (but contained in $G _ { \tau }$ be $l _ { 1 }$ . In general, let the differences between the essential parameters of $\overrightharpoon { G } _ { k - 1 }$ and those of the largest subgroup containing $\widehat { G } _ { k }$ but contained in $\widehat { G } _ { k - 1 }$ be $l _ { k }$ The numbers $l _ { 1 } , l _ { 2 } , \ldots$ are the same (if we disregard the order) for any composition series $G _ { r } , { \overrightarrow { G } } _ { 1 } , { \overrightarrow { G } } _ { 2 } , \dots .$ This is Lie-Jordan's theorem.

Let $G _ { m }$ be the invariant subgroup of $G _ { \tau }$ ， $m < r ,$ and its basicinfinitesimal transformations be $X _ { 1 } f _ { 3 } \ldots , X _ { m } f = 0 \quad$ . In general,

$$
( \tau = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ; \lambda = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ; \kappa = m + 1 , . . . , r ) .
$$

Substituting in (176), we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { c _ { \rho \sigma \lambda } c _ { \lambda \mu \tau } + c _ { \sigma \mu \lambda } c _ { \lambda \rho \tau } + c _ { \mu \rho \lambda } c _ { \lambda \sigma \tau } = 0 } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { ( \rho , \sigma , \tau , \lambda , \mu = m + 1 , . . . , r ) } } \end{array}
$$

Hence,there exists the group $H _ { r - m }$ of these structure constants, according to Lie's third fundamental theorem. Since these groups are.all isomorphic, each of them is called the factor group of $G _ { r }$ determined by the invariant subgroup $G _ { m }$ ,and is written $F _ { r - m } = G _ { r } / G _ { m }$ .The subgroup $G _ { m }$ is called the normal divisor (Normalteiler).Let its basic infinitesimal transformations be $Y _ { m + 1 } f , \dots . , Y _ { r } f ,$ and add $Y _ { 1 } f = 0 , . . . , Y _ { m } f = 0$ to them； then the groups $G _ { r }$ and $F _ { \tau - m }$ are homomorphic and $G _ { m }$ corresponds to the identity transformation in $F _ { \neg m }$ . Conversely,if $F _ { r - m }$ is homomorphic to $G _ { \tau } ,$ then $F _ { \tau - m }$ is the factor group of $G _ { \tau }$ determined by the invariant subgroup $G _ { r }$ that corresponds to the identity transformation in $F _ { \neg m }$ If $F _ { s }$ is the largest invariant subgroup of $F _ { r - m }$ ,then and only then is the corresponding invariant subgroup $G _ { s + m }$ of $G _ { r }$ the largest. If $F _ { \tau - m }$ is simple, then and only then is $G _ { m }$ the largest invariant subgroup. Hence, the necessary and suffcient condition for the factor group $G _ { r } / G _ { m }$ to be simple is that $G _ { m }$ should be the largest invariant subgroup of $G _ { r }$ If $F _ { \tau - m }$ is not simple, then it contains the composition series

$$
F _ { r - m } , F _ { r - m _ { 1 } } , . . . , F _ { r - m _ { q } } ;
$$

the corresponding subgroups $G _ { r - m _ { 1 } + m } , \ldots , G _ { r - m _ { 1 } + m _ { q } } , G _ { m } , 1$ constitute the composition series. Hence the invariant subgroup of $G _ { r }$ is one of the composition series of $G _ { r }$

Suppose that $G _ { r }$ contains the composition series $G _ { r } , \bar { G } _ { 1 } , \bar { G } _ { 2 } , . . . , \bar { G } _ { \rho } , 1 ,$ and that the order of $\overline { { G } } _ { \alpha }$ is $r _ { \alpha }$ .As has just been seen, $F _ { \alpha } = \overline { { G } } _ { \alpha } / \overline { { G } } _ { \alpha + 1 }$ is simple. Thus there are simple factor groups $F _ { \tau - \tau _ { 1 } } , F _ { \tau _ { 1 } - \tau _ { 2 } } , . . . , F _ { \tau _ { \rho } }$ that are, respectively， of order $r - r _ { 1 } , r _ { 1 } - r _ { 2 } , . . . , r _ { \rho - 1 } - r _ { \rho } , r _ { \rho }$ Let the second composition series of $G _ { r }$ be $G _ { r } , \vec { G } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \vec { G } _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , \vec { G } _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } , 1$ ；then the numbers of elements are equal for these two composition series, according to Lie-Jordan's theorem. Hence,if $H _ { m }$ is the common subgroup of $\overline { { G } } _ { 1 }$ and $\overline { { G } } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ , it must be the largest invariant subgroup of the two groups. If the orders of the subgroups in the second composition series are $\pmb { r } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , \pmb { r } _ { \rho } ^ { \prime }$ ，then $r = r _ { 1 } + r _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } - m$ . Suppose that the basic infinitesimal transformations of $G _ { r }$ are so chosen that the basic infinitesimal transformations of $H _ { m }$ are $X _ { 1 } f _ { 3 } \ldots , X _ { m } f .$ Then $X _ { 1 } f , . . . , X _ { m } f , X _ { m + 1 } f , . . . , X _ { r _ { 3 } } f$ are the base of $\overline { { G } } _ { 1 }$ ; and $X _ { 1 } f , . . . , X _ { m } f , X _ { r _ { 1 } + 1 } f , . . . , X _ { r } f$ are the base of $\overline { { G } } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ .The infinitesimal transformations

$$
X _ { 1 } f , . . . , X _ { m } f , X _ { m + 1 } f , . . . , X _ { r _ { 1 } } f , X _ { r _ { 1 } + 1 } f , . . . , X _ { r } f
$$

are the base of $G _ { r }$ .However, the three indices of the structure constants of the factor group $G _ { r } / \overline { G } _ { 1 }$ are $m + 1 , . . . , r ,$ as has just been seen, and those of the factor group $\overline { { G } } _ { 1 } / H _ { m }$ are $m + 1 , . . . , r _ { 1 }$ The factor groups $\overline { { G } } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } / H _ { m }$ and $G _ { r } / \overline { { G } } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ must have the same structure constants as the former ones.Thus, if $\overline { { G } } _ { 1 }$ and $\overline { { G } } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ are the largest invariant subgroups of $G _ { r }$ with the common invariant subgroup $H _ { m }$ ，then the factor groups $G _ { \tau } / G _ { 1 }$ and $\overline { { G } } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } / H _ { m }$ are isomorphic,and hence $G _ { r } / \overline { { G } } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ and $\overline { { G } } _ { 1 } / H _ { m }$ are also isomorphic.

It can be shown that,if $G _ { r }$ has two composition series,then the simple factor groups of these two composition series are isomorphic if we disregard the order. Indeed, let $H$ be the largest invariant subgroup of $G _ { \tau } ^ { \prime } , \overline { { G } } _ { 1 } .$ $\overline { { G } } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ ,and let the order be $h$ If $H _ { \mathrm { { ; } } }$ ， $H _ { 1 }$ ， $H _ { 2 } , \ldots$ are a composition series of subgroups of $H _ { ; }$ then $G _ { r } , \bar { G } _ { 1 } , H , H _ { 1 } , . . .$ and $G _ { r } , \ : \overline { { G } } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , H , H _ { 1 } , \ldots .$ are two composition series of subgroups of $G _ { r }$ ，These two composition series have the same structure constants, as just stated. Next,

$$
G _ { r } , { \bar { G } _ { 1 } } , { \bar { G } _ { 2 } } , \ldots \qquad \mathrm { a n d } \qquad G _ { r } , { \bar { G } _ { 1 } } , H , H _ { 1 } , \ldots .
$$

give_the same series of simple groups. Similarly, $G _ { \tau } , \overline { { G } } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \overline { { G } } _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , \dots .$ and $G _ { \tau } , \vec { G } _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , H , H _ { 1 } , \ldots$ give the same series of simple groups. Hence, the simple factor groups of the two composition series in question are isomorphic as a whole. This is Holder-Engel’s theorem.

The subgroups of an Abelian group are normal divisors. If the structure is written by means of addition, then the group and the subgroups are called modulus,and the factor group the remainder class modulus (Restklassenmodul).

If the group $G _ { \tau }$ consisting of $X _ { 1 } f , . . . , X _ { \tau } f$ is an $\pmb { r }$ parameter group, then $( X _ { \rho } , X _ { \sigma } ) f$ is the invariant subgroup of $G _ { r }$ ，according to Section 4.27. Hence, in the adjoint group $E _ { r }$ consisting of $E _ { 1 } f _ { 3 } \ldots , E _ { r } f _ { 3 }$ the commutator $( E _ { \rho } , E _ { \sigma } ) f$ generates the invariant subgroup of the adjoint group.This invariant subgroup is called the derived group of $G _ { \tau }$ A derived group of $G _ { \tau }$ includes also a derived group of itself. Such a derived group is called the second derived group. In general, the mth derived group can be defined in a similar manner. All derived groups are invariant for $G _ { r }$ . If the first derived group of $G _ { \tau }$ coincides with itself, then all derived groups coincide. In such a case they are said to be perfect in the sense of point-set theory (Section 2.1). The order of the mth derived group cannot be higher than $m - 1$ . Hence, the order of the qth derived group may coincide with that of the $( q + 1 ) \operatorname { t h }$ .
