Then the qth derived group may or may not consist of only the identity transformation. A group $G _ { \tau }$ whose derived group consists only of the identity transformation is called integrable (auflosbar).A group that has no such derived group is called nonintegrable (unauflosbar).Every subgroup ofan integrable group is integrable.

An $( r - m )$ -parameter group homomorphic to an $\pmb { r }$ -parameter integrable group is itselfintegrable. Hence, the adjoint group of an integrable group is integrable.

The difference between the order of $G _ { r }$ and the order of its derived group is equal to the number of independent invariants of the adjoint group of $G _ { r }$ For,let $\boldsymbol { e } _ { 1 }$ be an invariant of the adjoint group; then

$$
E _ { \mu } e _ { 1 } = \sum _ { \sigma } e _ { \sigma } c _ { \sigma \mu 1 } = 0 .
$$

Conversely,if the derived group is determined by $X _ { m + 1 } f , \ldots , X _ { \tau } f ,$ then $c _ { \sigma \mu \tau } = 0 ( \sigma , \mu = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ; \tau = 1 , 2 , . . . , m )$ , where $e _ { 1 } , e _ { 2 } , \ldots , e _ { m }$ are the invariants of the adjoint group. An integrable group $G _ { \tau }$ of order $r$ contains an invariant subgroup $G _ { \tau - 1 }$ of order $r - 1 ; G _ { \tau - 1 }$ contains an invariant subgroup $G _ { \tau - 2 }$ of order $\ r - 2$ ; in general, an invariant subgroup of order $\boldsymbol { r } \gets \boldsymbol { r } _ { 1 }$ contains an invariant subgroup of order $r - r _ { 1 } - 1 $ 、 Thus the infinitesimal transformations of $G _ { r }$ can be written

$$
( X _ { \sigma } , X _ { \sigma + \rho } ) f = \sum _ { \tau = 1 } ^ { \sigma } c _ { \sigma , \sigma + \rho , \tau } X _ { \tau } f
$$

$$
( \sigma = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ; \rho = 1 , 2 , . . . , r - \sigma ) .
$$

We propose to determine all second-order groups that contain a transformation $\textstyle \sum _ { \tau } e _ { \tau } X _ { \tau } f$ of $G _ { \tau }$ We search for $\lambda$ such that, with two constants $\pmb { \rho }$ and $\pmb { \omega }$ , we may form

$$
\left( \sum _ { \tau } e _ { \tau } X _ { \tau } , \sum _ { \sigma } \lambda _ { \sigma } X _ { \sigma } \right) f = \rho \sum _ { \tau } e _ { \tau } X _ { \tau } f + \omega \sum _ { \sigma } \lambda _ { \sigma } X _ { \sigma } f .
$$

Let $X _ { 1 } f$ be the required group, then, for $\lambda _ { 1 } = 0$ ，

$$
\Big ( X _ { 1 } , \sum _ { \sigma = 2 } ^ { \tau } \lambda _ { \sigma } X _ { \sigma } \Big ) f = \sum _ { \sigma , \tau } \lambda _ { \sigma } c _ { 1 \sigma \tau } X _ { \tau } f = \rho X _ { 1 } f + \omega \sum _ { \tau = 2 } ^ { \tau } \lambda _ { \sigma } X _ { \sigma } f .
$$

Hence, $\lambda$ can be obtained from

$$
\sum _ { \sigma = 2 } ^ { r } c _ { 1 \sigma \tau } \lambda _ { \sigma } = \omega \lambda _ { \tau } ,
$$

with the roots $\omega$ of $\| c _ { 1 \sigma \tau } - \omega \| = 0$ .Then $\pmb { \rho }$ is given by

$$
\rho = \sum _ { \sigma = 2 } ^ { \tau } \lambda _ { \sigma } c _ { 1 \sigma 1 } .
$$

If $\rho = 0$ in particular, that is, if we search for $\textstyle \sum _ { \sigma } \lambda _ { \sigma } X _ { \sigma }$ invariant for $\textstyle \sum _ { \tau } e _ { \tau } X _ { \tau }$ ，then $\omega$ is determined by the characteristic equation or Killing's equation

$$
\Delta ( \omega ) = \left. \sum _ { \tau = 1 } ^ { r } e _ { \tau } c _ { \tau \lambda \kappa } - \omega \right. = 0 .
$$

Write this equation in the form

$$
( - 1 ) ^ { r } \Delta ( \omega ) = \omega ^ { r } - \psi _ { 1 } ( \epsilon ) \omega ^ { r - 1 } + \psi _ { 2 } ( \epsilon ) \omega ^ { r - 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + ( - 1 ) ^ { r - 1 } \psi _ { r - 1 } ( \epsilon
$$

Then $\psi _ { i } ( e )$ is a homogeneous polynomial of degree $_ i$ in $e _ { 1 } , \ldots , e _ { r } ,$ such that

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \psi _ { 1 } ( e ) = \displaystyle \sum _ { \sigma , \tau } e _ { \sigma } c _ { \sigma \tau \tau } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { [ \psi _ { 1 } ( e ) ] ^ { 2 } - 2 \psi _ { 2 } ( e ) = \displaystyle \sum _ { \sigma , \tau , \lambda , \mu } e _ { \sigma } e _ { \tau } c _ { \sigma \lambda \mu } c _ { \tau \mu \lambda } , } } \end{array}
$$

If we transform by $\begin{array} { r } { X _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } f = \sum _ { \sigma } h _ { \rho \sigma } X _ { \sigma } f , } \end{array}$ such that $\begin{array} { r } { e _ { \sigma } = \sum _ { \rho } h _ { \rho \sigma } e _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } , } \end{array}$ $\begin{array} { r } { ( X _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } , X _ { \sigma } ^ { \prime } ) f = \sum _ { \tau } c _ { \rho \sigma \tau } ^ { \prime } X _ { \tau } ^ { \prime } f } \end{array}$ then we obtain

$$
\sum _ { \lambda } h _ { \lambda \sigma } c _ { \rho \tau \lambda } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \mu , \nu } h _ { \rho \mu } h _ { \tau \nu } c _ { \mu \nu \sigma } \qquad ( \sigma , \rho , \tau = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

If the new characteristic equation is $\Delta ^ { \prime } ( \omega )$ , then

$$
\Delta ( \omega ) = \Delta ^ { \prime } ( \omega ) ,
$$

and

$$
\psi _ { i } ^ { \prime } ( e _ { 1 } , . . . , e _ { r } ) = \psi _ { i } \Bigl ( \sum _ { \rho } h _ { \rho 1 } e _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , \sum _ { \rho } h _ { \rho r } e _ { r } ^ { \prime } \Bigr )
$$

It can be shown by the third fundamental theorem of Lie that $\psi _ { 1 } ( e ) , . . . , \psi _ { r - 1 } ( e )$ are invariant for the transformations of the adjoint group.

Killing calld the number of independent functions among

$$
\psi _ { 1 } ( e ) , . . . , \psi _ { r - 1 } ( e )
$$

the rank of $G _ { \tau }$ .The function $\psi _ { i } ( e )$ is the sum of the principal minor determinants of order $r - i$ of the characteristic equation for $\omega = 0$ ,that is,of $\Delta ( 0 )$ . Hence the rank is either equal to or lower than the order of the principal minor of $\Delta ( 0 )$ . It equals at most the number of roots that are identically zero of the characteristic equation. The nonzero roots of the characteristic equation, as functions of $e _ { 1 } , \ldots , e _ { r } $ ，are the invariants of the adjoint group; and the number of the nonzero roots that are functionally independent is the rank of the group, because the number of independent functions $\psi _ { i }$ equals the number of independent nonzero roots.

The root of $\psi _ { 1 } ( e )$ of the characteristic equation is always zero. Since the Poisson bracket of (20l） is of the form $( S , T ) = S T - T S$ and the traces of $s \tau$ and $\boldsymbol { \tau } s$ are equal, the trace of $S T - T S$ is zero.A trace (spur) is the sum of the elements in the principal diagonal of a matrix. Corresponding to a root $\omega ^ { ( \alpha ) }$ of $\omega$ there is always a root $- \omega ^ { ( \alpha ) }$ . For the zero roots of $\omega$ the group $X _ { 1 } f , . . . , X _ { r } f$ is Abelian.

The characteristic equation of an integrable group derived from (200) is written

$$
\Delta ( \omega ) = - \omega \prod _ { \sigma = 1 } ^ { \tau - 1 } \Big ( \sum _ { \tau = 1 } ^ { \tau } e _ { \tau } c _ { \tau \sigma \sigma } - \omega \Big ) ,
$$

and is decomposed into linear factors in $\omega$ ， $e _ { 1 } , \ldots , e _ { \tau }$ . Since $\textstyle \sum _ { \sigma } e _ { \sigma } C _ { \sigma i i }$ $( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , r )$ are the invariants of the adjoint group, they are equal to zero for a derived group. Hence the rank of the derived group of an integrable group is zero.If the rank of a derived group is zero, then and only then is the original group integrable.

Cartan (l933) proved that the necessary and sufficient condition for $G _ { r }$ to be integrable is that the transformations of the derived group make identically zero the coefficient $\psi _ { 2 } ( e )$ of the characteristic equation.The group is integrable if

$$
\sum _ { \tau } c _ { \rho \sigma \tau } { \frac { \partial { \psi } _ { 2 } } { \partial e _ { \tau } } } \equiv 0 \qquad ( \rho , \sigma = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

In other words, the necessary and suffcient condition for integrability is derived by substituting this relationship in the equation for $\psi _ { 2 }$ ,that is,

$$
\sum _ { \lambda , \mu , \nu } c _ { \rho \lambda \mu } c _ { \sigma \mu \nu } c _ { \tau \nu \lambda } = \sum _ { \lambda , \mu , \nu } c _ { \rho \mu \lambda } c _ { \sigma \nu \mu } c _ { \tau \lambda \nu } \qquad ( \rho , \sigma , \tau = 1 , 2 , . . . , r ) .
$$

A group that contains no invariant subgroup other than the identical element is called semisimple. Since a simple group contains no invariant subgroup, it is also semisimple.

In order that a group be semisimple,it is necessary and sufficient that $\partial \psi _ { 2 } / \partial e _ { \tau } = 0$ shall always be associated with $e _ { 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = e _ { r } = 0 $ Hence, $\psi _ { 2 } ( e )$ should be written as the sum of $r$ squares. For a group to be semisimple, it is therefore necessary and suffcient that the discriminant of the quadratic term $\psi _ { 2 } ( e )$ shall not be zero; in other words, the rank of the determinant formed by the coefficients of $e _ { 1 } , \ldots , e _ { r }$ of the quadratic form must be $\boldsymbol { r }$

Suppose that a semisimple group $G _ { r }$ contains an invariant subgroup $g _ { m }$ . Since $\psi _ { 2 }$ is a function of $e _ { 1 } , \ldots , e _ { m }$ only, the equations

$$
{ \frac { \partial \psi _ { 2 } } { \partial e _ { 1 } } } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = { \frac { \partial \psi _ { 2 } } { \partial e _ { m } } } = 0
$$

determine another invariant subgroup $g ^ { \prime }$ or $G _ { r }$ The transformations common to $g _ { m }$ and $g ^ { \prime }$ determine the integrable invariant subgroup of $G _ { r }$ · Hence $e _ { 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = e _ { r } = 0$ should be derived from

$$
{ \frac { \partial \psi _ { 2 } } { \partial e _ { 1 } } } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = { \frac { \partial \psi _ { 2 } } { \partial e _ { m } } } = e _ { m + 1 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = e _ { r } = 0 .
$$

Thus $\partial \psi _ { 2 } / \partial e _ { 1 } , . . . , \partial \psi _ { 2 } / \partial e _ { m }$ are independent of $e _ { 1 } , \ldots , e _ { m }$ . The subgroup $g ^ { \prime }$ contains $r \mathrm { ~ - ~ } m$ infinitesimal transformations $X _ { m + 1 } ^ { \prime } f _ { 3 } \ldots , X _ { r } ^ { \prime } f .$ Hence it is written in the form

$$
\psi _ { 2 } = \phi ( e _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , e _ { m } ^ { \prime } ) + \chi ( e _ { m + 1 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , e _ { r } ^ { \prime } ) ,
$$

where $\phi$ and $\chi$ are both quadratic forms with nonzero discriminants. Thus the two invariant subgroups $g _ { m }$ and $g ^ { \prime }$ are semisimple, and the order of $g ^ { \prime }$ is $r \mathrm { ~ - ~ } m$

Let $( e _ { 1 } , \ldots , e _ { r } )$ be a vector defined in a vector space, and consider the matrix of the coeffcients of the quadratic form

$$
\psi _ { 2 } ( e ) = - { \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } \sum _ { \sigma , \tau , \lambda , \mu } e _ { \sigma } e _ { \tau } c _ { \sigma \lambda \mu } c _ { \tau \mu \lambda } = \sum _ { \sigma , \tau } g _ { \sigma \tau } e _ { \sigma } e _ { \tau } ,
$$

$$
g _ { \sigma \tau } = - { \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } \sum _ { \lambda , \mu } c _ { \sigma \lambda \mu } c _ { \tau \mu \lambda } ,
$$

as the matrix of the linear transformation

$$
e _ { \rho } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \sigma } g _ { \rho \sigma } e _ { \sigma }
$$

from a vector $( e _ { 1 } , \ldots , e _ { r } )$ to a vector $( e _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , e _ { r } ^ { \prime } )$ . The vector $( e _ { 1 } , \ldots , e _ { r } )$ corresponding to a root of the characteristic equation defines the root space (Wur zelraum) $E _ { r }$ .A group is integrable if and only if the quadratic form $\psi _ { 2 } ( e )$ is zero for each vector $( e _ { 1 } , \ldots , e _ { r } )$ defining the derived group. If an arbitrary vector of a group becomes zero for a root of the character-istic equation, then the group is integrable. If two vectors $( e _ { 1 } , \ldots , e _ { r } )$ and $( \epsilon _ { 1 } , \ldots , \epsilon _ { r } )$ are such that $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { \sigma , \tau } g _ { \sigma \tau } e _ { \sigma } \epsilon _ { \tau } = 0 } \end{array}$ for each pair of $\pmb { \sigma }$ and $\boldsymbol { \tau } ,$ then they are said to be orthogonal. For a simple group,any vector of the root space $E _ { 0 }$ corresponding to a zero root of the characteristic equation is orthogonal to any vector of the other root space $E _ { \alpha }$ . In fact,

$$
\sum _ { \sigma , \tau } g _ { \sigma \tau } e _ { \sigma } ^ { ( \alpha ) } \epsilon _ { \tau } = 0
$$

for an $e ^ { ( \alpha ) }$ corresponding to the root space $E _ { \alpha }$ .Denote the vector in $E _ { 0 }$ by $e _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , e _ { r } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ ； then $\textstyle \sum _ { \sigma } e _ { \sigma } ^ { ( 0 ) } X _ { \sigma } f$ constitute a subgroup of $G _ { r }$ Denote it by $U .$ .It can be shown that such a subgroup $U$ is Abelian for a semisimple group（Cartan，1914,1914a,1926,1927,1927a,1927b,1929, 1930,1933,1936,1938；Weyl，1924,1925,1926,1926a,1931，1939; Chevalley, 1946,1951).

# 4.29REPRESENTATION OF A LIE GROUP

A finite and continuous abstract group is called a Lie group if in a sufficiently small neighborhood of the unit element we can find the coordinates or the parameters $a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { r }$ such that-with the given $A$ with parameters $a _ { i }$ as coordinates,and $B$ with parameters $b _ { i }$ as coordinates - the product $C = A B$ with parameters $c _ { 1 } , \ldots , c _ { r }$ is of class $C ^ { 2 }$ and is represented by $c _ { i } = \phi _ { i } ( a , b )$ . The operations on the parameter group are generated by the linearly independent infinitesimal transformations $X _ { 1 } f , \ldots , X _ { r } f .$ They are such that $\begin{array} { r } { ( X _ { i } , X _ { j } ) = \sum _ { s } c _ { i j s } X _ { s } . } \end{array}$ and $c _ { i j s }$ satisfy the requirements of the third fundamental theorem ofLie.In the place of $X$ we take the notation $T$ and regard it as a vector in an $r -$ dimensional space. An arbitrary vector of this space is written $\begin{array} { r } { \bar { A } = \sum _ { \mu } \alpha ^ { \mu } T _ { \mu } . } \end{array}$ ，where the coefficients $\alpha ^ { \mu }$ are the components of vector $\bar { A }$ From $\bar { A }$ we can construct a ring,which by definition does not obey the permutation law. In contrast to a group, we call it the infinitesimal ring of the group.A ring is an aggregate of elements obeying two operations 一 addition and multiplication - ·where the commutative law is not obeyed for multiplication.The distributive law is obeyed by both a ring and a group, that is,if $\begin{array} { r } { \bar { A } = \sum _ { \mu } \alpha ^ { \mu } T _ { \mu } } \end{array}$ ， $\begin{array} { r } { \bar { B } = \sum _ { \mu } \beta ^ { \mu } T _ { \mu } } \end{array}$ ，then $( { \bar { A } } { \bar { B } } ) = { \bar { A } } \times { \bar { B } }$ and

$$
A \ \times \ B = \sum _ { \kappa , \lambda } \alpha ^ { \kappa } \beta ^ { \lambda } ( T _ { \kappa } , T _ { \lambda } ) = \sum _ { \kappa , \lambda , \mu } \alpha ^ { \kappa } \beta ^ { \lambda } c _ { \kappa \lambda \mu } T _ { \mu } ,
$$

$$
A + B = \sum _ { \mu } \alpha ^ { \mu } T _ { \mu } + \sum _ { \mu } \beta ^ { \mu } T _ { \mu } = \sum _ { \mu } \left( \alpha ^ { \mu } + \beta ^ { \mu } \right) T _ { \mu } = \bar { B } + \bar { A } ,
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { c l l } { { ( \bar { A } + \bar { B } ) \times \bar { C } = \bar { A } \times \bar { C } + \bar { B } \times \bar { C } , } } & { { } } \\ { { \bar { A } \times ( \bar { B } + \bar { C } ) = \bar { A } \times \bar { B } + \bar { A } \times \bar { C } , } } & { { } } \\ { { ( k \bar { A } ) \times \bar { B } = k ( \bar { A } \times \bar { B } ) = \bar { A } \times ( k \bar { B } ) } } & { { } } \end{array}
$$

with a scalar $k$ .Such operations are called linear (Van der Waerden, 1955, 1959;Weyl, 1926,1931, 1939).We have $\bar { A } \times \bar { B } = - \bar { B } \times \bar { A }$ instead of the commutative law, and

$$
( { \bar { A } } \times { \bar { B } } ) \times { \bar { C } } + ( { \bar { B } } \times { \bar { C } } ) \times { \bar { A } } + ( { \bar { C } } \times { \bar { A } } ) \times { \bar { B } } \equiv 0
$$

(let us call this the combination law) instead of the associative law.This is determined by the third fundamental theorem of Lie. If these rules are satisfied, then Lie's $\boldsymbol { r }$ -parameter group, that is, an $_ r$ -parameter group germ,belongs to it. By Schreier's (1926,1927) definition: if the (new) combination law in the neighborhood of the unit element $\boldsymbol { \mathit { I } }$ of a local continuous group can be applied only for the elements sufficiently near the center $I _ { s }$ then this part of space is called the group germ (Gruppenkeim). This combination law is the same as the law for the continuous group. The algebra in which this combination law obeys Lie's fundamental theorems, that is, the Lie algebra-an algebra in which we restrict ourselves only in such infinitesimal elements of a group—is called the kernel $( K e r n )$ .Such an algebra determines the group germ on the kernel of such infinitesimal elements.

With a group or a ring, or an algebra $\mathfrak { A }$ in general, it is possible to associate a linear transformation $( a ) \colon x \to x ^ { \prime } = a x$ that operates on the varying element $_ x$ of the algebra $\mathfrak { A }$ (Schur,1905,1906,1924;Frobenius and Schur,1906; Brauer and Schur,1930; von Neumann,1929; Van der Waerden,1933,1935； Chevalley,1951). Then an algebra $\mathfrak { A }$ of order $_ n$ can be regarded either as an aggregate of element $^ a$ corresponding to the transformation $( a )$ , or as an $\pmb { n }$ -dimensional vector space $\Omega$ in which this transformation operates. This homomorphic correspondence $( { \mathfrak { A } } ) : a \to ( a )$ defines the representation (Darstellung); that is, to any element $s$ of a group there corresponds its image $U ( s )$ in the $\pmb { n } \cdot$ -dimensional vector space $\Re = \Re _ { n }$ ，such that $U ( s ) U ( t ) = U ( s t )$ . In this case, $s$ is said to induce (induzieren) the mapping image or simply the image $U ( s )$ in the representation space $\Re$ .If it is isomorphic instead of homomorphic,then it is the true representation (Murnaghan, l938). Introducing the coordinates in $\Re$ we represent the mapping $U ( s )$ bya matrix with a nonzero determinant consisting of $_ { n }$ rows and $\pmb { n }$ columns. If $\pmb { A }$ is a transformation of this coordinate to another, then the image of the mapping $U ( s )$ is represented by the matrix $A U ( s ) A ^ { - 1 }$

Let the correspondence $s \to U ( s )$ be the representation $\mathfrak { H }$ ； then the correspondence $s  A U ( s ) A ^ { - 1 }$ is also the representation $\mathfrak { S }$ .These two representations are equiualent, only the coordinate system being differently chosen. The trace, that is, the sum of the elements on the principal diagonal of the matrix $\pmb { A }$ , is not affected by the choice of such coordinates.

In the representation $\mathfrak { s }$ of a group $G$ ,the trace $\chi ( s )$ of the image $U ( s )$ corresponding to the element $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ of the group depends on ${ \mathfrak { s } } .$ . This is called the character of the representation (Littlewood， 1940). Equivalent representations have the same character. Since

$$
U ( t s t ^ { - 1 } ) = U ( t ) U ( s ) U ( t ^ { - 1 } ) = U ( t ) U ( s ) U ^ { - 1 } ( t ) ,
$$

the images $U ( s )$ and $U ( t s t ^ { - 1 } )$ differ by position but have the same trace, i.e., $\chi ( t s t ^ { - 1 } ) = \chi ( s )$

A representation,if transitive, is called irreducible； otherwise,reducible. A reducible representation may sometimes be regarded as the sum of ireducible representations. The matrix of an irreducible representation does not possess any invariant submatrix.

Let $U _ { \mu }$ correspond uniquely to Lie's infinitesimal transformation $T _ { \mu }$ ， and $\textstyle \sum _ { \mu } \alpha ^ { \mu } U _ { \mu }$ to $\textstyle \sum _ { \mu } \alpha ^ { \mu } T _ { \mu }$ .Put $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { \mu } \alpha ^ { \mu } U _ { \mu } = A _ { ; } } \end{array}$ ， $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { \mu } \beta ^ { \mu } U _ { \mu } = B } \end{array}$ ; then

$$
( A B ) = \sum _ { \kappa , \lambda , \mu } \alpha ^ { \kappa } \beta ^ { \lambda } c _ { \kappa \lambda \mu } U _ { \mu } , \sum _ { \mu } \delta ^ { \mu } U _ { \mu } = A \times B ,
$$

$$
\delta ^ { \mu } = \sum _ { \kappa , \lambda } \alpha ^ { \kappa } \beta ^ { \lambda } c _ { \kappa \lambda \mu } .
$$

Thus， to $\textstyle \sum _ { \mu } \delta ^ { \mu } U _ { \mu }$ corresponds the parameter group $\sum _ { \mu } \delta ^ { \mu } T _ { \mu }$ Thecoefficients $\delta ^ { \mu }$ are the same for both.To

$$
\bar { A } = \sum _ { \mu } \alpha ^ { \mu } T _ { \mu } , \qquad \bar { B } = \sum _ { \mu } \beta ^ { \mu } T _ { \mu }
$$

corresponds the transformation $( { \bar { A } } { \bar { B } } ) \to ( A B )$ .Thus to each vector $\bar { A }$ of an infinitesimal ring corresponds uniquely the infinitesimal transformation $\pmb { A }$ ，such that $( { \bar { A } } { \bar { B } } )$ corresponds to $( A B )$ ，and $\textstyle \sum _ { \mu } \delta ^ { \mu } U _ { \mu }$ to $\textstyle \sum _ { \mu } \delta ^ { \mu } T _ { \mu }$ . If the representation of an infinitesimal ring by means of linear infinitesimal transformations

$$
A ^ { i } = \sum _ { j } a _ { j } ^ { i } x ^ { j } , \qquad B ^ { i } = \sum _ { j } b _ { j } ^ { i } x ^ { j } ,
$$

is such that the matrix $\pmb { A }$ corresponds to $\bar { A }$ and the operator

$$
( A B ) = A B - B A
$$

corresponds to $\begin{array} { r } { \bar { A } \times \bar { B } _ { \mathrm { i } } } \end{array}$ , then the representation of Lie groups by means of linear infinitesimal transformations becomes a problem in algebra.

An adjoint group can be the representation of the original group by means of $C _ { \kappa } = ( c _ { \kappa \lambda } ^ { \mu } )$ ， where the structure constants ${ c _ { \kappa \lambda \mu } }$ of the previous

sections are now denoted by $c _ { \kappa \lambda } ^ { \mu }$ . In order to see this possibility, let $\begin{array} { r } { \displaystyle { \vec { B } } = \sum _ { \mu } \beta ^ { \mu } T _ { \mu } } \end{array}$ be an element of the infinitesimal ring. Then

$$
C _ { \kappa } \bar { B } = \sum _ { \kappa , \mu } c _ { \kappa \lambda } ^ { \mu } \beta ^ { \lambda } T _ { \mu } = T _ { \kappa } \times \bar { B } ;
$$

and this means that the linear transformation $C _ { \kappa }$ generates $T _ { \kappa }$ . Similarly, for an arbitrary infinitesimal transformation $\begin{array} { r } { A _ { a } = \sum _ { \kappa } \alpha ^ { \kappa } C _ { \kappa } } \end{array}$ of an adjoint group,

$$
\mathop { \overline { { A } } _ { a } \bar { B } } = \sum _ { \kappa } \alpha ^ { \kappa } C _ { \kappa } \bar { B } = \sum _ { \kappa } \alpha ^ { \kappa } ( T _ { \kappa } \times \bar { B } ) = \bar { A } \times \bar { B } .
$$

Thus a linear transformation $A _ { \mathfrak { a } }$ means multiplication by $\vec { A } .$ It is known from the correspondence $\bar { A } \times \bar { B }  A _ { a } B _ { a } - B _ { a } A _ { a }$ that $A _ { a }$ of the adjoint group gives the representation of $\bar { A }$ ，By applying this to an arbitrary vector $\bar { C }$ we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { ( \bar { A } \times \bar { B } ) \times \bar { C } = ( A _ { a } B _ { a } - B _ { a } A _ { a } ) \bar { C } = A _ { a } ( B _ { a } \bar { C } ) - B _ { a } ( A _ { a } \bar { C } ) } } \\ { { = \bar { A } \times ( \bar { B } \times \bar { C } ) - \bar { B } \times ( \bar { A } \times \bar { C } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Thus the third fundamental theorem of Lie is obtained.

For a linear infinitesimal transformation, consider a linear operato1

$$
e ^ { \tau A } = I + \tau A + \cdot \cdot \cdot
$$

as in Section 4.26,and write $A = \left( \alpha _ { \mu \nu } \right)$ .Let $\begin{array} { r } { S ( A ) = \sum _ { \tau } \alpha _ { \tau \tau } } \end{array}$ be the trace of $A$ ; then $\left| e ^ { A } \right| = e ^ { S ( A ) }$ For if $e ^ { \tau A }$ is the group of the matrix, then $\left| e ^ { \tau A } \right|$ is the representation of this group. Since $| e ^ { \tau A } | = 1 + \tau S ( A ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,$ then, from Lie's fundamental theorem,

$$
\left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \tau } \left| e ^ { \tau A } \right| \right) _ { 0 } = \left( \frac { \partial } { \partial \tau } e ^ { \tau S ( A ) } \right) _ { 0 } = S ( A ) e ^ { \tau S ( A ) } = S ( A ) .
$$

Hence $S ( A )$ is the infinitesimal transformation of the representation of the group $\left\{ e ^ { \tau A } \right\}$ by means of a determinant. By this property we can judge whether or not the group is Abelian by looking at the infinitesimal ring of that group; if it is Abelian, then we obtain $B e ^ { A \tau } B ^ { - 1 } = e ^ { A \tau }$ from an arbitrary element $\boldsymbol { B }$ of the group and an arbitrary infinitesimal transformation $A$ ； in other words, $e ^ { \ b { A } \tau }$ is invariant for an arbitrary transformation of the adjoint group. It is necessary and sufficient that $\pmb { A }$ be invariant for an arbitrary transformation of the adjoint group; that is, $A$ must be such that $C B - B C = C \times B = 0$ 、This should be satisfied for any two elements ofthe infinitesimal ring,in order that the corresponding group may be Abelian.

Another interesting application is to the composition series. By taking $G _ { i + 1 }$ as the largest normal divisor of $G _ { i }$ ， we construct Lie's subgroups $G , G _ { 1 } , G _ { 2 } , . . . , G _ { r } = I$ with a finite number of steps.If we transfer this subgroup to the infinitesimal ring belonging to $G$ ，the composition series is $g , g _ { 1 } , g _ { 2 } , . . . , g _ { \tau } = 0$ . The element $g _ { i + 1 }$ is the largest ideal among $g _ { i }$ Generally it is known that to a normal divisor in group theory there corresponds an ideal in ring theory; that is,a subset $_ m$ of a ring is called an ideal, if $a - b \in m$ follows from $a \in m$ ， $b \in m$ ； and if $a r \in m$ follows from $a \in m$ with an element $r$ of the ring (Krull, 1935). Such an ideal is called a right-hand ideal. If $r a \in m$ ， then it is a left-hand ideal.Here $a \in m$ means that $^ a$ is an element contained in $m$ . Thus, if the factor group $G _ { i } / G _ { i + 1 } = \overline { { G } } _ { i }$ is Abelian, then $G$ is also Abelian. Conversely, if a group contains a composition series such that all its factor groups $\overline { { G } } _ { i }$ are Abelian, then the group $G$ is said to be integrable (Section 4.28). The symbol $a b a ^ { - 1 } b ^ { - 1 }$ formed from $a \in G$ $b \in G$ represents the commutator of $^ a$ and $b$ . The expression $\begin{array} { r } { c = \prod _ { r = 1 } ^ { k } \left( a _ { r } b _ { r } a _ { r } ^ { - 1 } b _ { r } ^ { - 1 } \right) } \end{array}$ represents an element of the commutator group and is written $G ^ { ( 1 ) }$ . The commutator group $G ^ { ( 1 ) }$ is a normal divisor of $G$ and is indeed the smallest normal divisor with Abelian factor group $G / G ^ { ( 1 ) }$ . It can be shown that the commutator ring $g ^ { ( 1 ) } = g \times g$ of the infinitesimal ring $g$ of $G$ is the smallest ideal with an Abelian remainder class ring in $g$ The group $G$ is integrable if and only if $g ^ { ( s ) } = 0$ for a finite $s$ ,such that $g \supset g ^ { ( 1 ) } \supset g ^ { ( 2 ) } \supset \cdot \cdot \cdot \supset g ^ { ( s ) } = 0$

The largest integrable ideal r in an infinitesimal ring $g$ is called semisimple if $\mathbf { r } = 0$ .The corresponding group is called semisimple (Section 4.28).The largest integrable ideal is called the radical,as in the theory of hypercomplex numbers.Suppose that multiplication obeys the associative law in the hypercomplex number system $\Omega$ ，such that $( u v ) w = u ( v w )$ If a positive integer $\rho$ can be found such as ${ \mathfrak { a } } ^ { \rho } = 0$ for an ideal $\alpha$ ,then $\mathfrak { a }$ is called a null-power ideal in $\Omega$ A null-power ideal in $\Omega$ corresponds to an integrable ideal in $\mathfrak { g }$ .Thus,the largest null-power ideal is a radical r. For $\mathfrak { r } = 0$ this system $\Omega$ is semisimple.

A group $G$ having no normal divisor except itself and the unit element has been called simple (Section 4.28). Similarly,a ring $g$ ,or an ideal $\mathfrak { g }$ or the system Ω, is called simple if it contains no ideal other than itself and zero.A semisimple infinitesimal ring can be expressed as the sum of simple rings that vanish by pairs in multiplication.

Suppose that an integrable group $G$ is represented by a linear transformation in a vector space.It can be proved that there exists a vector $\mathbf { e }$ such that $A \mathbf { e } = \lambda \mathbf { e }$ for all $A$ of the infinitesimal ring of the representation $\mathfrak { G }$ of $G$ (Lie's theorem).

Consider the representation of a semisimple group. Suppose that the representation of an infinitesimal ring $g$ by means of a matrix $A$ is given. Addition of a quantity written as $\beta ( g )$ is called modification.How should $\beta ( g )$ be chosen in order that $\{ A \}$ and $\{ A + I \beta ( g ) \}$ shall both be representations of $g !$ The necessary and sufficient condition is that the quantity $\beta ( g )$ be a representation of the ring by means of numbers. It can be shown that the representation $\mathfrak { G }$ by means of a matrix $A$ can be replaced by its representation by means of another matrix whose traces are all null.Then the theorem can be proved: that the irreducible representation $\mathfrak { G }$ of $g$ is the representation of the factor group $g / h$ and that a null matrix can be made to correspond to each element of $h$

Let $t = \sum _ { i } \mu _ { i } g _ { i }$ be a general element of $g$ ,and let

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \mathsf { f } \left( t , \zeta \right) = | I \zeta - T | . } \end{array}
$$

Let $\boldsymbol { \mathfrak { f } } ^ { * } ( t , \zeta )$ be the result of dropping multiple factors from $\mathfrak { f }$ for a general value of $t .$ Assume that $\tilde { \uparrow } ^ { * }$ contains no double root for $t = t _ { 0 }$ .Let $\mathfrak { r } _ { \alpha }$ be a partial space of the root space for the root $\pmb { \alpha }$ . We say that the element $r = r ^ { ( \alpha ) }$ of $\mathfrak { r } _ { \alpha }$ belongs to the root $\pmb { \alpha }$ ,that is, $( T _ { 0 } - \alpha ) ^ { l } r = 0$ for a sufficiently large l. It can be shown that,if $\mathfrak { r } _ { \alpha }$ belongs to the root $\pmb { \alpha }$ ,and $\mathfrak { r } _ { \beta }$ to the root $\beta$ ,then $\mathfrak { r } _ { \alpha } \times \mathfrak { r } _ { \beta }$ belongs to the root $\alpha + \beta$

Suppose that $\pmb { \alpha }$ and $- \alpha$ are the roots. If $\Lambda = \Lambda ( h )$ is a linear form in $\lambda$ ， then $\Lambda _ { \alpha } ( h _ { \alpha } )$ is written $\Lambda _ { \alpha }$ . Suppose further that the representation of $g$ is given in the correspondence $h  H _ { : }$ ， $e _ { \alpha } \to I _ { \alpha }$ .Then there exists an $\epsilon \neq 0$ such that $H e = \Lambda e$ ; we say that $\pmb { \ell }$ is of weight $\Lambda$ .The only possible weights are the roots of the characteristic equation. If $r$ is of weight $\Lambda$ ,then so is $H r$ and $I _ { \alpha }$ is of weight $\Lambda + \alpha$ If a vector of weight $\Lambda$ is the sum of vectors of other weights, that vector equals zero. If $e _ { \alpha } ^ { * } \subset H _ { \alpha }$ ， $h _ { \alpha } ^ { * } = e _ { \alpha } \times e _ { - \alpha }$ and $\Lambda$ is a representation root, that is, $\Lambda ( h _ { \alpha } ^ { * } ) = \Lambda _ { \alpha } ^ { * }$ ，then $\Lambda _ { \alpha } ^ { * }$ isa rational multiple of $e _ { \alpha } ^ { \ast }$ ·

The ring $g$ is integrable if the equation $\phi ( t ) = 0$ holds for all elements of $g \ \times \ g .$ where $\phi ( t )$ appears in Cartan's criterion for integrability and semisimplicity of $g$ If $g$ is semisimple, then the quadratic form $\phi ( t )$ is nondegenerate. Thus we obtain the important decomposition theorem that any semisimple group is the direct sum of simple groups.

Weyl (1925,1926,1926a) and Peter and Weyl (1927) discussed the representation. Instead of leading by linear transformations to quadratic forms,they led the representation by unitary transformations to Hermitian forms (cf.,Van der Waerden, 1933; Littlewood, 1940). Cartan (1914, 1914a,1926,1927,1927a,1927b,1929,1938) considered the unitary geometry in the Riemannian space instead of referring to the notion of representation.

# Reduction of the n-Body Problem

# TRANSFORMATION OF LAGRANGE

# 5.ITRANSFORMATION OF LAGRANGE

Consider at first the three-body problem and let the positions of the three bodies be $P _ { 1 } , P _ { 2 } , P _ { 3 }$ and their masses be $m _ { 1 } , m _ { 2 } , m _ { 3 }$ Denote the coordinates of $P _ { 3 }$ relative to $P _ { 2 }$ ，the coordinates of $P _ { 1 }$ relative to $P _ { 3 }$ ,and the coordinates of $P _ { 2 }$ relative to $P _ { 1 }$ ,by $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , x _ { 3 } ; x _ { 4 } , x _ { 5 } , x _ { 6 }$ ; and ${ \pmb x } , _ { 7 } { \pmb x } _ { 8 } , { \pmb x } _ { 9 } ,$ respectively.The differential equations of motion of the three bodies in these relative coordinates are

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \left( \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { 3 } m _ { \mu } \right) \frac { x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } } { r _ { \mu } ^ { 3 } } - m _ { \mu } \Bigl ( \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { 3 } \frac { x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } } { r _ { \mu } ^ { 3 } } \Bigr ) = 0 ,
$$

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \left( \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { 3 } m _ { \mu } \right) \frac { x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } } { r _ { \mu } ^ { 3 } } - m _ { \mu } \Bigl ( \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { 3 } \frac { x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } } { r _ { \mu } ^ { 3 } } \Bigr ) = 0 ,
$$

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } x _ { 3 \mu } } { d t ^ { 2 } } + \biggl ( \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { 3 } m _ { \mu } \biggr ) \frac { x _ { 3 \mu } } { r _ { \mu } ^ { 3 } } - m _ { \mu } \biggl ( \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { 3 } \frac { x _ { 3 \mu } } { r _ { \mu } ^ { 3 } } \biggr ) = 0 \qquad ( \mu = 1 , 2 , 3 ) .
$$

$$
r _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } = x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 \mu } ^ { 2 } ,
$$

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { 3 } x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } = \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { 3 } x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } = \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { 3 } x _ { 3 \mu } = 0 .
$$

If the motions of $P _ { 2 }$ and $P _ { 3 }$ relative to $\boldsymbol { P } _ { 1 }$ are known, then the motion of $P _ { 1 }$ relative to $P _ { 2 }$ can be obtained, since the equations (l） have been written in an explicit form.

Let the constants of the angular momentum be $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , c _ { 3 }$ ; then

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { 3 } \frac { 1 } { m _ { \mu } } \left( x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } \frac { d x _ { 3 \mu } } { d t } - x _ { 3 \mu } \frac { d x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } } { d t } \right) = c _ { 1 } } , } \\ { { \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { 3 } \frac { 1 } { m _ { \mu } } \left( x _ { 3 \mu } \frac { d x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } } { d t } - x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } \frac { d x _ { 3 \mu } } { d t } \right) = c _ { 2 } } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { 3 } \frac { 1 } { m _ { \mu } } \left( x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } \frac { d x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } } { d t } - x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } \frac { d x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } } { d t } \right) = c _ { 3 } .
$$

Write

$$
u _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } = \left( \frac { d x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { d x _ { 3 \mu } } { d t } \right) ^ { 2 } \qquad ( \mu = 1 , 2 , 3 ) ;
$$

then, if $\scriptstyle h$ is an arbitrary constant, the energy integral is

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { 3 } \frac { u _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } } { m _ { \mu } } - 2 \Bigl ( \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { 3 } m _ { \mu } \Bigr ) \Bigl ( \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { 3 } \frac { 1 } { m _ { \mu } r _ { \mu } } \Bigr ) = h .
$$

Referring to (3) we see that the solution requires six unknown functions and their derivatives.These functions are determined by six differential equations of order two.However, since four integrals from (5） and (6) are known,eight unknown quantities are required; that is,the order of the differential equations of the three-body problem is reduced to eight from $3 \times 6 = 1 8$

Lagrange tried to determine the sides of the triangle formed by the three bodies and then the position in space of the plane of the triangle (Lagrange, 1873； Tisserand, 1889；Radau, 1868, 1886; Bohlin,1907). Let $\lambda , \mu , \nu$ be a cyclic permutation of 1, 2, 3, and put

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { r _ { \mu } ^ { 3 } } } - { \frac { 1 } { r _ { \nu } ^ { 3 } } } = q _ { \lambda } \qquad ( \lambda , \mu , \nu = 1 , 2 , 3 ) ;
$$

then

$$
q _ { 1 } + q _ { 2 } + q _ { 3 } = 0 , \frac { q _ { 1 } } { r _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } } + \frac { q _ { 2 } } { r _ { 2 } ^ { 3 } } + \frac { q _ { 3 } } { r _ { 3 } ^ { 3 } } = 0 .
$$

from (2) and (3). Differentiate (2) twice with respect to $t$ ,then,because of (5)，

The left-hand members of (25) and (26) are expressed in terms of $r _ { 1 } , r _ { 2 } , r _ { 3 }$ and their first-and second-order derivatives.

Thus we have obtained four fundamental equations (13), (21), (22), and (26). $\not p _ { 1 } , \not p _ { 2 } , \not p _ { 3 } , \ u _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } , \ u _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } , \ u _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } , \ v _ { 1 } , \ v _ { 2 } , \ v _ { 3 } , \Sigma _ { 1 } , \Sigma _ { 2 } , \Sigma _ { 3 }$ are expressed in terms of $r _ { 1 } , r _ { 2 } , r _ { 3 }$ and their derivatives because of (ll), (l2), (l7),and (20). Then(21） expresses $\rho$ in terms of these functions.Hence, the differential equations of the three-body problem reduce to the three equations (l3), (22),and (26) for the three unknown functions $r _ { 1 } , r _ { 2 } , r _ { 3 }$ Equations (13) and (26) are second-order differential equations and contain two constants $h$ and $k$ ； (22）is a third-order differential equation.Hence,the whole set of differential equations is of order 7,and we require 7 integrals. Adding $h$ and $k$ brings the number of integration constants up to nine. Although we can solve this set of differential equations with these constants,we still require three more constants: two representing the position of the plane of the triangle formed by the three bodies,and another representing the orientation of the triangle on that plane.Thus we require twelve integration constants in all, in order to determine the relative motion of the three bodies. There are six more integration constants corresponding to the rectilinear motion in space of the center of mass of the whole system. Thus, by this transformation of Lagrange we can reduce the order of the differential equations from l8 to 7.We can reduce the order still further,as will be explained in this chapter.

Differentiate (5) with respect to $t$ and refer to (1). Then, usig (2), (10),(15),we obtain

$$
\frac { d u _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } } { d t } = 2 ( m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } + m _ { 3 } ) \frac { d } { d t } \frac { 1 } { r _ { \mu } } + m _ { \mu } \Big ( q _ { \mu + 2 } \frac { d \phi _ { \mu + 2 } } { d t } - q _ { \mu + 1 } \frac { d \phi _ { \mu + 1 } } { d t } - q _ { \mu }
$$

Integrate (27) with respect to $t$ and substitute $u _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } , u _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } , u _ { 3 } ^ { 2 }$ in (12) and (27); then

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \frac { { \mathit { l } } ^ { 2 } r _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } } { d t ^ { 2 } } - \frac { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } + m _ { 3 } } { r _ { \mu } } } } \\ { ~ - ~ m _ { \mu } \Big [ \phi _ { \mu + 2 } q _ { \mu + 2 } - \phi _ { \mu + 1 } q _ { \mu + 1 } } \\ { ~ + \int \left( q _ { \mu + 2 } \frac { d \phi _ { \mu + 2 } } { d t } - q _ { \mu + 1 } \frac { d \phi _ { \mu + 1 } } { d t } - q _ { \mu } \rho \right) d t \Big ] ~ { = } ~ 0 . }  \end{array}
$$

The third-order differential equation obtained by differentiating this equation is important to the theory underlying Lindstedt's solution of the three-body problem (Sundman, 1913; Lindstedt, l883, 1884). Lagrange treated the particular solution of the three-body problem that we discussed in Chapter 3 on the basis of this set of seventh-order differential equations.Instead of the mutual distances of the three bodies in the problem of Lagrange, the distances of the three bodies from the center of mass were employed as the unknown functions by Charlier (1899).

# 5.2LEVI-CIVITA's TRANSFORMATION

Now we propose to reduce the order of the differential equations from 18 to 8 by a transformation arising from the geometrical considerations of Levi-Civita (1915；see also Charlier, 1899；Ronchi, 1917).

We take the rectangular coordinates $\xi , \eta$ in the invariable plane of the three bodies $P _ { 1 } , P _ { 2 } , P _ { 3 }$ and the $\zeta$ axis perpendicular to that plane.The center of mass $o$ is taken as the coordinate origin.The instantaneous moving plane containing the three bodies at any instant passes always through the origin $o$ .Denote the positions of the three bodies referred to the moving rectangular coordinates on this instantaneous plane at $t$ by $x _ { 2 v - 1 }$ ， $\scriptstyle x _ { 2 v }$ $( \nu = 1 , 2 , 3 )$ .The $X$ axis is taken to be in the direction of the ascending node of this instantaneous plane on the invariable plane, in such a way that the positive direction is counted from the lower to the upper side of the invariable plane in positive rotation around the $O \xi$ axis. The $z$ axis is normal to this instantaneous moving plane. Let the angular distance of this ascending node from the $O \xi$ axis be $\psi$ ,and the angle between these two planes be $\theta .$ ，Twice the area of the triangle formed by the three bodies is

$$
V = { \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { x _ { 1 } } & { x _ { 2 } } & { 1 } \\ { x _ { 3 } } & { x _ { 4 } } & { 1 } \\ { x _ { 5 } } & { x _ { 6 } } & { 1 } \end{array} \right| } .
$$

Let the masses of the three bodies be $m _ { 2 \nu - 1 } = m _ { 2 v } ( \nu = 1 , 2 , 3 )$ ， $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { 3 } m _ { 2 \nu - 1 } = \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { 3 } m _ { 2 \nu } = m _ { 1 } } \end{array}$ ，and the moments and the products of inertia around $O X$ and $O Y ,$ ，respectively,be

$$
I _ { X } = \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { 3 } m _ { 2 \nu } x _ { 2 \nu } ^ { 2 } , \qquad I _ { Y } = \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { 3 } m _ { 2 \nu - 1 } x _ { 2 \nu - 1 } ^ { 2 } ,
$$

$$
{ \cal J } = \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { 3 } m _ { 2 \nu } x _ { 2 \nu - 1 } x _ { 2 \nu } , \qquad { \cal D } = \left| { \cal I } _ { x } \quad { \cal J } \right| ;
$$

then it can be proved that

$$
m _ { 2 } m _ { 4 } m _ { 6 } V ^ { 2 } = m D > 0 .
$$

The direction cosines of the normal to the invariable plane referred to the coordinate system OXYZ defined by the instantaneous moving plane of the three bodies are O,sin $\theta _ { \cdot }$ cos $\theta _ { \cdot }$ 、Hence, the total kinetic energy of the system is

$$
T = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { 3 } m _ { 2 \nu - 1 } [ ( \dot { x } _ { 2 \nu - 1 } - \dot { \psi } \cos \theta \cdot x _ { 2 \nu } ) ^ { 2 } + ( \dot { x } _ { 2 \nu } + \dot { \psi } \cos \theta \cdot x _ { 2 \nu - 1 } ) ^ { 2 }
$$

and the force function, assuming the gravitational constant to be unity, is

$$
\begin{array} { r } { U = \frac { m _ { 2 } m _ { 4 } } { r _ { 1 2 } } + \frac { m _ { 4 } m _ { 6 } } { r _ { 2 3 } } + \frac { m _ { 6 } m _ { 2 } } { r _ { 3 1 } } . } \end{array}
$$

The canonically conjugate variables to these variables $x _ { 2 \nu - 1 } , ~ x _ { 2 \nu } , ~ \theta , ~ \psi$ are

$$
y _ { 2 \nu - 1 } = \frac { \partial T } { \partial \dot { x } _ { 2 \nu - 1 } } = m _ { 2 \nu - 1 } ( \dot { x } _ { 2 \nu - 1 } - \dot { \psi } \cos \theta \cdot x _ { 2 \nu } ) ,
$$

$$
y _ { 2 \nu } = \frac { \partial T } { \partial \dot { x } _ { 2 \nu } } = m _ { 2 \nu } ( \dot { x } _ { 2 \nu } + \dot { \psi } \cos \theta \cdot x _ { 2 \nu - 1 } ) ,
$$

$$
\Theta = \frac { \partial T } { \partial \dot { \theta } } = I _ { x } \dot { \theta } - J \dot { \psi } \sin \theta ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \Psi = \frac { \partial T } { \partial \dot { \psi } } = \sin \theta ( - J \dot { \theta } + I _ { \tt Y } \dot { \psi } \sin \theta ) + \cos \theta \cdot M , } \\ { \displaystyle } \\ { \displaystyle M = \sum _ { y = 1 } ^ { 3 } \big ( x _ { 2 \nu - 1 } y _ { 2 \nu } - x _ { 2 \nu } y _ { 2 \nu - 1 } \big ) . } \end{array}
$$

Thus,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle T = \mathbf T + f , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \mathbf T = \frac 1 2 \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { 3 } \frac 1 { m _ { 2 \nu } } ( y _ { 2 \nu - 1 } ^ { 2 } + y _ { 2 \nu } ^ { 2 } ) , } } \end{array}
$$

$$
f = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { 3 } m _ { 2 \nu } ( { \dot { \theta } } x _ { 2 \nu } - { \dot { \psi } } \sin { \theta } { \cdot } x _ { 2 \nu - 1 } ) ^ { 2 } .
$$

Substituting $\Theta , \Psi$ ，we obtain the function $f$ in the form

$$
f ( { \dot { \theta } } , { \dot { \psi } } ) = F ( \Theta , \Psi )
$$

and

$$
H = \mathbf { H } + F , \mathbf { H } = \mathbf { T } - \mathbf { \Omega } U .
$$

Thus, the equations of motion are

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l r l } & { \frac { d x _ { 2 \nu - 1 } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial y _ { 2 \nu - 1 } } , } & & { \frac { d x _ { 2 \nu } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial y _ { 2 \nu } } , } & & { \frac { d \theta } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial \Theta } , } & & { \frac { d \psi } { d t } = } \\ & { \frac { d y _ { 2 \nu - 1 } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { 2 \nu - 1 } } , } & & { \frac { d y _ { 2 \nu } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { 2 \nu } } , } & & { \frac { d \Theta } { d t } = - \frac { \partial F } { \partial \theta } , } & & { \frac { d \Psi } { d t } = } \end{array}
$$

( \nu = 1 , 2 , 3 ) .
$$

The total angular momentum $\mathbf { \delta x }$ has, with respect to OXYZ, components

$$
\begin{array} { r } { I _ { x } \dot { \theta } - J \dot { \psi } \sin \theta , \qquad - J \dot { \theta } + I _ { x } \dot { \psi } \sin \theta , \qquad M , } \end{array}
$$

but, on the other hand, the components are 0, $\kappa$ sin $\theta$ ， $\kappa$ cos $\theta$ ，where $| \mathbf { K } | = K .$ Hence

$$
\begin{array} { r } { I _ { x } \dot { \theta } - J \dot { \psi } \sin \theta = 0 , ~ - J \dot { \theta } + I _ { x } \sin \theta = K \sin \theta , ~ M = K \cos \theta . } \end{array}
$$

Substituting the definition of $\Theta , \Psi$ ， we obtain the integrals of (32):

$$
\Theta = 0 , ~ \Psi = M \cos \theta + K \sin ^ { 2 } \theta = K , ~ M = K \cos \theta .
$$

Consequently,

$$
\frac { \partial F } { \partial \theta } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial \psi } = 0 .
$$

Eliminating $\theta$ and $\psi$ from (33), we obtain

$$
\dot { \psi } = \frac { K } { D } I _ { x } , \qquad \dot { \theta } = \frac { K } { D } J \sin \theta .
$$

Since $F _ { : }$ , and accordingly $f ,$ is a homogeneous quadratic form in ${ \dot { \theta } } , { \dot { \psi } }$ by (31), therefore

$$
F = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \frac { \partial f } { \partial \dot { \theta } } \dot { \theta } + \frac { \partial f } { \partial \dot { \psi } } \dot { \psi } \right) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } [ \Theta \dot { \theta } + ( \Psi - M \cos \theta ) \dot { \psi } ] .
$$

From (34) and (35) we obtain

$$
{ \cal F } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { K - M \cos \theta } { D } K I _ { x } ,
$$

or, substituting from the third equation of (34),

$$
\frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { I _ { X } } { D } \left( K ^ { 2 } - M ^ { 2 } \right) = { \bf F } ,
$$

which depends only on $x _ { 2 \nu - 1 } , \ x _ { 2 \nu } , \ y _ { 2 \nu - 1 } , \ y _ { 2 \nu } .$ Hence,the first twelve equations of (32） depend only on these twelve variables, for now $H = \mathbf { H } + \mathbf { F }$ . Thus the problem is reduced to the plane problem in which the inclination $\theta$ is kept constant.

Furthermore, we transform by

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l r l r } { x _ { 2 \nu - 1 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 2 \nu - 1 } - x _ { 5 } , } & { { } } & { x _ { 2 \nu } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 2 \nu } - x _ { 6 } , } & { { } } & { x _ { 5 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 5 } , } & { { } } & { x _ { 6 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 6 } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
y _ { 2 \nu - 1 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 2 \nu - 1 } , \quad y _ { 2 \nu } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 2 \nu } , \quad y _ { 5 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 1 } + y _ { 3 } + y _ { 5 } , \quad y _ { 6 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 2 } + y _ { 4 } + y _ { 5 } ,
$$

The transformation is canonical. $y _ { 5 } ^ { \prime } , \ y _ { 6 } ^ { \prime }$ are the components of the momentum of the center of mass, which we can equate to zero. Then

$$
\frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { 5 } ^ { \prime } } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { 6 } ^ { \prime } } = 0 .
$$

Thus $H$ depends neither on ${ \pmb x } _ { 5 } ^ { \prime }$ nor on ${ \pmb x } _ { 6 } ^ { \prime }$ . Hence, the equations of motion are of the eighth order and of the form

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { \displaystyle { \frac { d x _ { 2 \nu - 1 } ^ { \prime } } { d t } } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial y _ { 2 \nu - 1 } ^ { \prime } } } , } & { { ~ \displaystyle { \frac { d x _ { 2 \nu } ^ { \prime } } { d t } } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial y _ { 2 \nu } ^ { \prime } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { d y _ { 2 \nu - 1 } ^ { \prime } } { d t } } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { 2 \nu - 1 } ^ { \prime } } } , } & { { ~ \displaystyle { \frac { d y _ { 2 \nu } ^ { \prime } } { d t } } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { 2 \nu } ^ { \prime } } } \quad { } ~ ( \nu = 1 , 2 ) . }  \end{array}
$$

After we solve these equations for $x _ { 2 \nu - 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ x _ { 2 \nu } ^ { \prime } , \ y _ { 2 \nu - 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ y _ { 2 \nu } ^ { \prime } ,$ we compute $x _ { 2 \nu - 1 } , \ x _ { 2 \nu } , \ y _ { 2 \nu - 1 } , \ y _ { 2 \nu }$ by the inverse transformation of (37） and then compute $I _ { x } , I _ { y } , J , D$ Then we obtain $\psi$ and $\theta$ from

$$
\frac { d \psi } { d t } = \frac { K } { D } I _ { x } , \frac { d \theta } { d t } = \frac { K } { D } J \sin \theta .
$$

Instead of the transformation (37) we may use the mutual distances of the three bodies according to Lagrange. Let $\phi _ { v }$ be the inclination to the $X$ axis of the side opposite the vertex $\left( x _ { 2 v - 1 } , x _ { 2 v } \right)$ of the triangle formed by the three bodies； then

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \cos \phi _ { v } = \frac { x _ { 2 \lambda - 1 } - x _ { 2 \mu - 1 } } { \sqrt { ( x _ { 2 \lambda - 1 } - x _ { 2 \mu - 1 } ) ^ { 2 } + ( x _ { 2 \lambda } - x _ { 2 \mu } ) ^ { 2 } } } , } \\ { \sin \phi _ { v } = \frac { x _ { 2 \lambda } - x _ { 2 \mu } } { \sqrt { ( x _ { 2 \lambda - 1 } - x _ { 2 \mu - 1 } ) ^ { 2 } + ( x _ { 2 \lambda } - x _ { 2 \mu } ) ^ { 2 } } } , } \end{array}
$$

where $\lambda , \mu , \nu$ are a cyclic permutation of 1, 2, 3. Put $\theta _ { \nu } = \phi _ { \lambda } - \phi _ { \mu }$ ; then $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { v = 1 } ^ { 3 } \theta _ { v } = 0 . } \end{array}$ Introduce

$$
\omega = \frac { 1 } { 3 } \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { 3 } \phi _ { \nu } , \quad r _ { \lambda } \equiv r _ { \mu , \nu } = [ ( x _ { 2 \mu - 1 } - x _ { 2 \nu - 1 } ) ^ { 2 } + ( x _ { 2 \mu } - x _ { 2 \nu } ) ^ { 2 } ] ^ { 1 / 2 } ,
$$

and denote the corresponding momenta by $\Omega , P _ { \lambda } ,$ respectively. Then we can prove that

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { y _ { 2 \lambda - 1 } = P _ { \mu } \cos \phi _ { \mu } - P _ { v } \cos \phi _ { \nu } - \frac { \Omega } { 3 } \Big ( \frac { \sin \phi _ { \mu } } { r _ { \mu } } - \frac { \sin \phi _ { v } } { r _ { v } } \Big ) , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { y _ { 2 \lambda } = P _ { \mu } \sin \phi _ { \mu } - P _ { v } \sin \phi _ { \nu } + \frac { \Omega } { 3 } \Big ( \frac { \cos \phi _ { \mu } } { r _ { \mu } } - \frac { \cos \phi _ { v } } { r _ { v } } \Big ) , } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \mathfrak { s } \theta _ { \lambda } = \frac { \cdot _ { \lambda } } { 2 r _ { \mu } r _ { \nu } } , \qquad \sin \theta _ { \lambda } = \frac { \mathfrak { r } } { r _ { \mu } r _ { \nu } } , } \\ & { } \\ & { 4 V ^ { 2 } = ( r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } + r _ { 3 } ) ( - r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } + r _ { 3 } ) ( r _ { 1 } - r _ { 2 } + r _ { 3 } ) ( r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } - r _ { 3 } ) , } \\ & { } \\ & { \phi _ { \lambda } = \omega + \frac { 1 } { 3 } ( \theta _ { \mu } - \theta _ { \nu } ) . } \end{array}
$$

Hence

$$
\begin{array} { r } { m x _ { 2 \lambda - 1 } = m _ { v } r _ { \mu } \cos \phi _ { \mu } - m _ { \mu } r _ { v } \cos \phi _ { v } , } \\ { m x _ { 2 \lambda } = m _ { v } r _ { \mu } \sin \phi _ { \mu } - m _ { \mu } r _ { v } \sin \phi _ { v } , } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \sum { \frac { 1 } { m _ { \lambda } } } \left( y _ { 2 \nu - 1 } ^ { 2 } + y _ { 2 \nu } ^ { 2 } \right) = { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \sum { \frac { 1 } { m _ { \lambda } } } \left( P _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } + P _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } - 2 P _ { \mu } P _ { \nu } \cos \theta _ { \lambda } \right)
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle + \frac { \Omega } { 3 } \sum \frac { 1 } { m _ { \lambda } } \left( \frac { P _ { \mu } } { r _ { \nu } } - \frac { P _ { \nu } } { r _ { \mu } } \right) \sin \theta _ { \lambda } } } \\ { { \displaystyle + \frac { \Omega ^ { 2 } } { 1 8 } \sum \frac { 1 } { m _ { \lambda } } \left( \frac { 1 } { r _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { r _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 2 \cos \theta _ { \lambda } } { r _ { \mu } r _ { \nu } } \right) } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
I _ { Y } = \frac { m _ { 2 } m _ { 4 } m _ { 6 } } { m } \sum \frac { 1 } { m _ { \lambda } } r _ { \lambda } ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } { [ \omega + \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 3 } } ( \theta _ { v } - \theta _ { \mu } ) ] } .
$$

Then the Hamiltonian function of (32) is

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { H = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { m _ { \lambda } } \left( y _ { 2 \lambda - 1 } ^ { 2 } + y _ { 2 \lambda } ^ { 2 } \right) \mathrm { ~ - ~ } \sum \displaystyle \frac { m _ { \mu } m _ { \nu } } { r _ { \lambda } } } } \\ { { \mathrm { ~ } } } \\ { { \mathrm { ~ } + \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { D } \left[ \Theta ^ { 2 } I _ { x . } - 2 \Theta \displaystyle \frac { M \cos \theta - \Psi } { \sin \theta } J + \frac { ( M \cos \theta - \Psi ) ^ { 2 } } { \sin ^ { 2 } \theta } I _ { \mathrm { { r } } } \right] . } } \end{array}
$$

Since the origin is the center of mass,

$$
\begin{array} { l l l } { { \displaystyle { \frac { d r _ { \lambda } } { d t } } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial P _ { \lambda } } , } } & { { ~ \displaystyle { \frac { d \omega } { d t } } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial \Omega } , } } & { { } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \frac { d P _ { \lambda } } { d t } } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial r _ { \lambda } } , \quad { \displaystyle { \frac { d \Omega } { d t } } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial \omega } } } } & { { ~ ( \lambda = 1 , 2 , 3 ) . } } \end{array}
$$

If we express $H$ in terms of $\omega$ $\phantom { } _ { 0 } , r _ { \lambda } , \Omega , P _ { \lambda }$ by substituting in $\boldsymbol { H }$ from equations (41),(42), (43),and (44),we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { = \displaystyle \sum _ { \bar { 2 } \overline { { { m _ { \lambda } } } } } ( P _ { \mu } ^ { z } + P _ { \nu } ^ { z } - 2 P _ { \mu } P _ { \nu } \cos \theta _ { \lambda } ) } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \quad + \frac { \Omega } { 3 } \sum \left( \frac { P _ { \mu } } { r _ { \nu } } - \frac { P _ { \nu } } { r _ { \mu } } \right) \frac { \sin \theta _ { \lambda } } { m _ { \lambda } } + \frac { \Omega ^ { 2 } } { 1 8 } \sum _ { m _ { \lambda } } \left( \frac { 1 } { r _ { \mu } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 1 } { r _ { \nu } ^ { 2 } } - \frac { 2 \cos \theta _ { \lambda } } { r _ { \mu } r _ { \nu } } \right) } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \quad + \frac { K ^ { 2 } - \Omega ^ { 2 } } { D ^ { 2 } } \sum \frac { r _ { \lambda } ^ { 2 } } { m _ { \lambda } } \sin ^ { 2 } \left[ \omega + \frac { 1 } { 3 } ( \theta _ { \mu } - \theta _ { \nu } ) \right] - \sum \frac { m _ { \mu } m _ { \nu } } { r _ { \lambda } } , } } \end{array}
$$

# $\lambda , \mu , \nu$ are a cyclic permutation of 1, 2, 3
