by (42) and (43). Thus the order of the differential equations (46) is reduced to eight.

Van Kampen and Wintner (1937) and Murnaghan (1936) applied this transformation of Levi-Civita to the plane motion of three bodies.

Lemaitre (1952；see also Deprit and Delie, l961,l963;Deprit and Roels, l962) discovered an elegant symmetric coordinate system for the reduction of the three-body problem.Write

$$
m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } + m _ { 3 } , \qquad \mu m _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } = m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } m _ { 3 } , \qquad \tan \alpha _ { i } = \frac { m _ { i } } { m _ { 0 } } \quad ( i = 1 , 2 , 3 ) ,
$$

$$
m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } m _ { 3 } A ^ { 2 } = ( m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } ) ( m _ { 2 } + m _ { 3 } ) ( m _ { 3 } + m _ { 1 } ) ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c c } { { \chi = \frac { 1 } { 3 } ( \chi _ { 1 } + \chi _ { 2 } + \chi _ { 3 } ) , } } & { { \chi _ { 1 } = \chi + \frac { 1 } { 3 } ( \alpha _ { 3 } - \alpha _ { 2 } ) , } } \\ { { { } } } & { { { } } } \\ { { \chi _ { 2 } = \chi + \frac { 1 } { 3 } ( \alpha _ { 1 } - \alpha _ { 3 } ) , } } & { { \chi _ { 3 } = \chi + \frac { 1 } { 3 } ( \alpha _ { 2 } - \alpha _ { 1 } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

It can be shown that

$$
\sum _ { i } \sin \ b \beta \alpha _ { i } \cos \ b \beta \chi _ { i } \equiv 0 , \qquad \sum _ { i } \sin \ b \beta \alpha _ { i } \sin \ b \beta \chi _ { i } \equiv 0
$$

$$
\sum _ { i } m _ { i } \cos ^ { 2 } \alpha _ { i } \cos ^ { 2 } \chi _ { i } \equiv \frac { \mu } { A ^ { 2 } } , \quad \quad \sum _ { i } m _ { i } \cos ^ { 2 } \alpha _ { i } \sin ^ { 2 } \chi _ { i } \equiv \frac { \mu } { A ^ { 2 } } ,
$$

$$
\sum _ { i } m _ { i } \cos ^ { 2 } \alpha _ { i } \cos \chi _ { i } \sin \chi _ { i } \equiv 0 .
$$

In the plane of the three bodies,Lemaitre introduced

xi $= A r \cos \alpha _ { i } \cos \sigma \cos \chi _ { i } , ~ y _ { i } = A r \cos \alpha _ { i } \sin \sigma \sin \chi _ { i } ~ ( i = 1 , 2 , \frac { \pi } { 4 } )$ 3），so that

$$
\sum _ { i } m _ { i } x _ { i } = 0 , \sum _ { i } m _ { i } y _ { i } = 0 , \sum _ { i } m _ { i } x _ { i } y _ { i } = 0 ;
$$

that is, the xyz coordinate axes coincide with the principal axes of inertia. Let the moments of inertia be

$$
I _ { x x } = \sum _ { i } m _ { i } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } = \mu r ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \sigma , \qquad I _ { y y } = \sum _ { i } m _ { i } y _ { i } ^ { 2 } = \mu r ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } \sigma ,
$$

$$
J = I _ { x x } + I _ { y y } = \mu r ^ { 2 } ,
$$

where $\boldsymbol { r }$ denotes the radius of gyration with respect to the center of mass of the three masses in the plane of the triangle. The area of the triangle $m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } m _ { 3 }$ is

$$
\Delta = \frac { 1 } { 4 } \frac { J } { m _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } \sin 2 \sigma ,
$$

which is a symmetric function of the three masses. Denote the angle of the vector $m _ { j } m _ { k }$ with the $_ x$ axis by $\phi _ { i }$ $_ i \ ( i , j , k = 1 , 2 , 3 )$ ; then

$$
\tan \phi _ { i } = - \tan \sigma \cot \chi _ { i } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , 3 ) .
$$

The angle $x$ gives the position of the triangle with respect to the $_ { x }$ axis in the plane of the three bodies.

Let $\rho _ { i }$ be the distance of $m _ { j }$ and $m _ { k }$ and $\theta _ { i }$ be the angle between the vectors $m _ { i } m _ { k }$ and $m _ { i } m _ { j } .$ Then

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle { \rho _ { i } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 2 } } A r \sin \alpha _ { i } \sqrt { 1 - \cos { 2 \sigma } \cos { 2 \chi _ { i } } } , } } \\ { \displaystyle { \sin \theta _ { i } = \frac { \sin \alpha _ { i } \sin { 2 \sigma } } { \sqrt { 1 - \cos { 2 \sigma } \cos { 2 \chi _ { i } } } \sqrt { 1 - \cos { 2 \sigma } \cos { 2 \chi _ { k } } } } , } } \\ { \displaystyle { \cos { \theta _ { i } } = - \frac { \cos { \alpha _ { i } } + \cos { 2 \sigma } \cos { ( \chi _ { i } + \chi _ { k } ) } } { \sqrt { 1 - \cos { 2 \sigma } \cos { 2 \chi _ { i } } } \sqrt { 1 - \cos { 2 \sigma } \cos { 2 \chi _ { k } } } } . } } \end{array}
$$

Refer the moving $x , y$ ， $_ z$ axes to the $X , ~ Y , ~ Z$ axes fixed in space by means of Euler's angles $\theta , \phi , \psi$ ; there the angular velocity components are

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l } & { \omega _ { x } = \dot { \theta } \cos \phi + \dot { \psi } \sin \theta \sin \phi , } & & { \omega _ { y } = - \dot { \theta } \sin \phi + \dot { \psi } \sin \theta \cos \phi , } \\ & { \omega _ { z } = \dot { \phi } + \dot { \psi } \cos \theta . } \end{array}
$$

Then,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { T = T _ { r } + \omega _ { z } C _ { r } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( I _ { y y } \omega _ { x } ^ { 2 } + I _ { x x } \omega _ { y } ^ { 2 } + J \omega _ { z } ^ { 2 } ) , } \\ { U = k ^ { 2 } \Big ( \frac { m _ { 2 } m _ { 3 } } { \rho _ { 1 } } + \frac { m _ { 3 } m _ { 1 } } { \rho _ { 2 } } + \frac { m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } } { \rho _ { 3 } } \Big ) } \\ { \displaystyle = \frac { k ^ { 2 } \sqrt { 2 } } { { \cal A } r } \sum _ { i } \frac { m _ { i } m _ { k } } { \sin { \alpha _ { i } } \sqrt { 1 - \cos { 2 \sigma } \cos { 2 \chi _ { i } } } } , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \cal T } _ { r } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i } m _ { i } ( \dot { x } _ { i } ^ { 2 } + \dot { y } _ { i } ^ { 2 } ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mu ( \dot { r } ^ { 2 } + r ^ { 2 } \dot { \sigma } ^ { 2 } + r ^ { 2 } \dot { \chi } ^ { 2 } ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle } } \\ { { \displaystyle C _ { r } = \sum _ { i } m _ { i } ( x _ { i } \dot { y } _ { i } - \dot { x } _ { i } y _ { i } ) = \mu r ^ { 2 } \dot { \chi } \sin 2 \sigma . } } \end{array}
$$

Since ${ \cal L } = T + U _ { : }$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle { \dot { \mathcal { P } } _ { \theta } = \frac { \partial L } { \partial \dot { \theta } } = \frac { \partial T } { \partial \omega _ { x } } \cos \phi - \frac { \partial T } { \partial \omega _ { y } } \sin \phi \mathrm { , } } } \\ { \displaystyle { \dot { \mathcal { P } } _ { \phi } = \frac { \partial L } { \partial \dot { \phi } } = \frac { \partial T } { \partial \omega _ { z } } \mathrm { , } } } \\ { \displaystyle { \dot { \mathcal { P } } _ { \psi } = \frac { \partial L } { \partial \dot { \psi } } = \frac { \partial T } { \partial \omega _ { x } } \sin \theta \sin \phi + \frac { \partial T } { \partial \omega _ { y } } \sin \theta \cos \phi + \frac { \partial T } { \partial \omega _ { z } } \cos \theta \mathrm { . } } } \end{array}
$$

We compute the Routh function

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { R = \dot { \theta } \dot { p } _ { \theta } + \dot { \phi } p _ { \phi } + \dot { \psi } p _ { \psi } - L } \\ & { \quad = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( I _ { y y } \omega _ { x } ^ { 2 } + I _ { x x } \omega _ { y } ^ { 2 } + J \omega _ { z } ^ { 2 } ) - T _ { \tau } - U } \\ & { \quad = \frac { 1 } { 2 I _ { y y } } [ b _ { \theta } \cos \phi + ( \dot { p } _ { \psi } - p _ { \phi } \cos \theta ) \cos \epsilon \theta \sin \phi ] ^ { 2 } } \\ & { \quad \quad + \frac { 1 } { 2 I _ { x x } } [ - \dot { p } _ { \theta } \sin \phi + ( \dot { p } _ { \psi } - \dot { p } _ { \phi } \cos \theta ) \cos \epsilon \cos \theta \cos \phi ] ^ { 2 } } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad + \frac { 1 } { 2 J } ( \dot { p } _ { \phi } - C _ { \tau } ) ^ { 2 } - T _ { \tau } - U . } \end{array}
$$

Denote the magnitude of the angular momentum vector $c$ by $\kappa$ ; then we have integrals

$$
{ \dot { p } } _ { \theta } = 0 , \qquad { \dot { p } } _ { \phi } = K \cos \theta , \qquad { \dot { p } } _ { \psi } = K .
$$

The third equation shows that $\psi$ is an ignorable coordinate. The first integral shows that $\theta$ can be replaced by its value ${ \cos ^ { - 1 } \left( \pmb { \rho } _ { \phi } / \pmb { K } \right) }$ in $R$ Thus,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle R = \frac { 1 - \cos 2 \sigma \cos 2 \phi } { J \sin ^ { 2 } 2 \sigma } \left( K ^ { 2 } - \phi _ { \phi } ^ { 2 } \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \dot { p } _ { \phi } ^ { 2 } } { J } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad - \dot { \chi } \phi _ { \phi } \sin 2 \sigma - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \mu \big ( \dot { r } ^ { 2 } + r ^ { 2 } \dot { \sigma } ^ { 2 } + r ^ { 2 } \dot { \chi } ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } 2 \sigma \big ) - U . } } \end{array}
$$

The equations of motion are then reduced to those of the eighth order

$$
\dot { \phi } = { \frac { \partial R } { \partial \dot { p } _ { \phi } } } , \qquad \dot { p } _ { \phi } = - { \frac { \partial R } { \partial \phi } } ,
$$

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \frac { \partial R } { \partial \dot { r } } - \frac { \partial R } { \partial r } = 0 , \frac { d } { d t } \frac { \partial R } { \partial \dot { \sigma } } - \frac { \partial R } { \partial \sigma } = 0 ,
$$

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \frac { \partial R } { \partial \dot { x } } - \frac { \partial R } { \partial \chi } = 0 .
$$

We have the energy integral with a constant $\pmb { h }$ .The third equation for $\pmb { r }$ gives

$$
\ddot { r } + \frac { \dot { r } ^ { 2 } } { r } - \frac { 1 } { \mu r } \left( U + 2 h \right) = 0 ,
$$

from which we obtain the relation due to Lagrange

$$
\ddot { J } = 2 U + 4 h .
$$

The Hamiltonian function is

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { H = \dot { r } \dot { p } _ { r } + \dot { \sigma } \dot { p } _ { \sigma } + \dot { \chi } \dot { p } _ { x } + R } } \\ { { \mathrm { ~ } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 \mu } \dot { p } _ { r } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 J } \left( \dot { p } _ { \sigma } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { \cos ^ { 2 } 2 \sigma } \dot { p } _ { x } ^ { 2 } - 2 \frac { \sin 2 \sigma } { \cos ^ { 2 } 2 \sigma } \dot { p } _ { x } \dot { p } _ { \phi } \right) } } \\ { { \mathrm { ~ } + \frac { 1 } { 2 J \cos ^ { 2 } 2 \sigma } \dot { p } _ { \phi } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 - \cos 2 \sigma \cos 2 \phi } { \sin ^ { 2 } 2 \sigma } \frac { K ^ { 2 } - \dot { p } _ { \phi } ^ { 2 } } { J } - U . } } \end{array}
$$

Put

$$
q _ { 1 } = q \cos \lambda \cos \beta , \qquad q _ { 2 } = q \sin \lambda \cos \beta , \qquad q _ { 3 } = q \sin \beta .
$$

Then

P1 $: q ^ { 2 } ( 1 - \cos ^ { 2 } \lambda \cos ^ { 2 } \beta ) , ~ \rho _ { 2 } = q ^ { 2 } ( 1 - \sin ^ { 2 } \lambda \cos ^ { 2 } \beta ) , ~ \rho _ { 3 } = q ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \beta ,$ and the canonical variables are

$$
\begin{array} { c c c c c c } { { J } } & { { \lambda } } & { { } } & { { \beta } } & { { } } & { { \phi } } \\ { { \phi _ { J } } } & { { \varpi _ { \lambda } } } & { { } } & { { \varpi _ { \beta } } } & { { } } & { { \rho _ { \phi } , } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { w _ { \lambda } = \dot { p } _ { \lambda } - A _ { \lambda } \dot { p } _ { \phi } , \quad \quad w _ { \beta } = \dot { p } _ { \beta } - A _ { \beta } \dot { p } _ { \phi } , } \\ & { A _ { \lambda } = \frac { 2 m _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } q _ { 1 } q _ { 2 } \sin { 2 \sigma } } { J ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } { 2 \sigma } } \Phi , \quad \quad A _ { \beta } = \frac { 2 m _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } q q _ { 3 } \sin { 2 \sigma } } { J ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } { 2 \sigma } \cos { \beta } } \Psi , } \\ & { \Phi = \frac { J } { m _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } ( q ^ { 2 } + q _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } ) - 4 \bigg ( \frac { \rho _ { 1 } } { m _ { 1 } } + \frac { \rho _ { 2 } } { m _ { 2 } } \bigg ) q ^ { 2 } q _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } , } \\ & { \Psi = \frac { J } { m _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } ( q _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } - q _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) + 4 \bigg ( \frac { \rho _ { 1 } } { m _ { 1 } } - \frac { \rho _ { 2 } } { m _ { 2 } } \bigg ) q _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } q _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } , } \end{array}
$$

with the Hamiltonian

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { H ^ { \prime } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( \frac { \varpi _ { \lambda } ^ { 2 } } { \cos ^ { 2 } \beta } + \varpi _ { \beta } ^ { 2 } \right) + 2 m _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } q ^ { 2 } \rho _ { 1 } \rho _ { 2 } \rho _ { 3 } \bigg ( 4 \beta _ { J } ^ { 2 } + \frac { \dot { p } _ { \phi } ^ { 2 } } { J ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { U } { J } - 2 \frac { h } { J } \bigg ) } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { + \frac { 4 m _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } q ^ { 2 } \rho _ { 1 } \rho _ { 2 } \rho _ { 3 } } { J ^ { 2 } } \frac { 1 - \cos { 2 \sigma } \cos { 2 \phi } } { \sin ^ { 2 } 2 \sigma } ( K ^ { 2 } - \dot { p } _ { \phi } ^ { 2 } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

The equations of motion are now

$$
\dot { r } = \frac { 1 } { \mu } \dot { p } _ { r } , \qquad \dot { x } = \frac { 1 } { J \cos ^ { 2 } 2 \sigma } \varpi _ { x } , \qquad \dot { \sigma } = \frac { 1 } { J } \dot { p } _ { \sigma } ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \dot { p } } _ { r } = \frac { 1 } { \mu \nu ^ { 3 } } \left( { \dot { p } } _ { \sigma } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { \cos ^ { 2 } { 2 \sigma } } \varpi ^ { 2 } + { \dot { p } } _ { \sigma } ^ { 2 } \right) } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + \frac { 2 ( 1 - \cos { 2 \sigma } \cos { 2 \phi } ) } { \mu \nu ^ { 3 } \sin ^ { 2 } { 2 \sigma } } ( K ^ { 2 } - p _ { \sigma } ^ { 2 } ) - \frac { U } { r } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \dot { p } } _ { \sigma } = - \frac { 2 \sin { 2 \sigma } } { J \cos ^ { 3 } { 2 \sigma } } \varpi _ { x } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 2 ( K ^ { 2 } - p _ { \sigma } ^ { 2 } ) } { J \sin ^ { 3 } { 2 \sigma } } } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ \times \left[ 2 \cos { 2 \sigma } ( 1 - \cos { 2 \sigma } \cos { 2 \phi } ) - \sin ^ { 2 } { 2 \sigma } \cos { 2 \phi } \right] } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + \frac { 2 \dot { \chi } } { \mu \nu } \rho _ { \sigma } \cos { 2 \sigma } - \frac { 3 } { 2 } k ^ { 2 } A ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2 } \sin { 2 \sigma } \sum _ { i , j , k } \frac { m _ { j } m _ { k } } { \rho _ { \sigma } ^ { 3 } } \sin ^ { 2 } { \alpha } _ { i } \cos { 2 \phi } } . }  \end{array}
$$

$$
\dot { \varpi } _ { \chi } = - 2 \dot { \sigma } \phi _ { \phi } \cos 2 \sigma - \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } k ^ { 2 } A ^ { 2 } r ^ { 2 } \cos 2 \sigma \sum _ { i , j , k } \frac { m _ { j } m _ { k } } { \rho _ { i } ^ { 3 } } \sin ^ { 2 } \alpha _ { i } \sin 2 \chi _ { i } ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \varpi _ { x } = \phi _ { x } - \phi _ { \phi } \sin 2 \sigma , } \end{array}
$$

where the summation is to be extended to three terms obtained by the cyclic permutation of $i , j , k$ among 1, 2, 3.

Lemaitre further introduced $\rho _ { i } = q _ { j } ^ { 2 } + q _ { k } ^ { 2 }$ with cyclic permutation of $i , j , k$ .The new coordinates $q _ { 1 } , q _ { 2 } , q _ { 3 }$ are such that: (i) $q _ { 1 } = q _ { 2 } = q _ { 3 } = 0$ represents the triple simultaneous collision of the three bodies；(ii) $q _ { i } = 0$ represents the straight line of the collinear configuration with $m _ { i }$ situated between $m _ { j }$ and $m _ { k }$ ；(ii） the plane $q _ { j } = q _ { k }$ represents the isosceles configuration with $m _ { j }$ and $m _ { k }$ as base; and (iv) $q _ { 1 } = q _ { 2 } = q _ { 3 }$ represents the equilateral configuration (see Chapter 3).

Put $q ^ { 2 } = q _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + q _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + q _ { 3 } ^ { 2 }$ ; then

$$
2 q ^ { 2 } = \rho _ { 1 } + \rho _ { 2 } + \rho _ { 3 } , \qquad 2 q _ { i } ^ { 2 } = \rho _ { j } + \rho _ { k } - \rho _ { i } , \qquad \Delta = q q _ { 1 } q _ { 2 } q _ { 3 } ,
$$

$$
1 + \cos \theta _ { i } = \frac { 2 q _ { j } ^ { 2 } q _ { k } ^ { 2 } } { \rho _ { j } \rho _ { k } } , \qquad 1 - \cos \theta _ { i } = \frac { 2 q ^ { 2 } q _ { i } ^ { 2 } } { \rho _ { j } \rho _ { k } } ,
$$

$$
\cos { \theta _ { j } } + \cos { \theta _ { k } } = - 2 \frac { q _ { j } ^ { 2 } q _ { k } ^ { 2 } } { \rho _ { j } \rho _ { k } } \frac { \rho _ { j } + \rho _ { k } } { \rho _ { i } } ,
$$

$$
\cos \theta _ { j } - \cos \theta _ { k } = 2 \frac { q ^ { 2 } q _ { i } ^ { 2 } } { \rho _ { j } \rho _ { k } } \frac { \rho _ { j } - \rho _ { k } } { \rho _ { i } } ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { 1 + \cos \theta _ { 1 } + \cos \theta _ { 2 } + \cos \theta _ { 3 } = - 4 \frac { q _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } q _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } q _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } { \rho _ { 1 } \rho _ { 2 } \rho _ { 3 } } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\tan { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \theta _ { i } = { \frac { q q _ { i } } { \rho _ { j } \rho _ { k } } } ,
$$

with cyclic permutation of $i , j , k .$ 、This set of variables was used in the regularization of the three-body problem by Lemaitre (see Section 17.15 dealing with regularizing variables).

Following Murnaghan (1936), put

$$
J = \mu r ^ { 2 } , \qquad x = \frac { q _ { 1 } } { q + q _ { 3 } } , \qquad y = \frac { q _ { 2 } } { q + q _ { 3 } } ,
$$

or

$$
{ \mathrm {  ~ \sigma ~ } } _ { 1 } = q \frac { 2 x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } , \qquad q _ { 2 } = q \frac { 2 y } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } , \qquad q _ { 3 } = q \frac { 1 - x ^ { 2 } - y } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } + y }
$$

$$
\lambda = \tan ^ { - 1 } { \frac { y } { x } } , \qquad \beta = \sin ^ { - 1 } { \frac { 1 - x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } } } ,
$$

and

$$
w = \int ^ { t } \frac { \mu r ^ { 2 } ( \tau ) [ 1 + x ^ { 2 } ( \tau ) + y ^ { 2 } ( \tau ) ] ^ { 2 } d \tau } { 1 6 m _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } q ^ { 2 } ( \tau ) \rho _ { 1 } ( \tau ) \rho _ { 2 } ( \tau ) \rho _ { 3 } ( \tau ) } .
$$

Then the canonical equations with the independent variable $\boldsymbol { w }$ are

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l r l r l } { r } & { { } } & { \pmb { x } } & { { } } & { \pmb { y } } & { { } } & { \pmb { \phi } } \\ { \pmb { \phi _ { r } } } & { { } } & { \pmb { \varpi _ { x } } } & { { } } & { \pmb { \varpi _ { y } } } & { { } } & { \pmb { \phi _ { \phi } } , } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { w _ { x } = \displaystyle { \hat { p } _ { x } - \hat { p } _ { \phi } A _ { x } } , \quad \quad \varpi _ { y } = \displaystyle { \hat { p } _ { y } - \hat { p } _ { \phi } A _ { y } } , } \\ & { \quad } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad A _ { x } = \frac { 2 m _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } q ^ { 2 } x \sin { 2 \sigma } } { { \mathcal { J } ^ { 2 } } ( x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 + x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } { 2 \sigma } } } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \times [ 4 y ^ { 2 } \Phi + ( 1 - x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 + x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } ) \Psi ] , } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad 2 m _ { 0 } ^ { 3 } q ^ { 2 } y \sin { 2 \sigma } } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \times [ 4 x ^ { 2 } \Phi - ( 1 + x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } { 2 \sigma } } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \times [ 4 x ^ { 2 } \Phi - ( 1 - x ^ { 2 } - y ^ { 2 } ) ( 1 + x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } ) \Psi ] , } \end{array}
$$

with

$$
H ^ { \prime } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( \varpi _ { x } ^ { 2 } + \varpi _ { y } ^ { 2 } ) + \frac { 8 m _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } q ^ { 2 } \rho _ { 1 } \rho _ { 2 } \rho _ { 3 } } { J ( 1 + x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { 1 } { \mu } \rlap / p _ { r } ^ { 2 } + \frac { \rho _ { \phi } ^ { 2 } } { J } - 2 ( U + h ) \right)
$$

$$
+ \frac { 1 6 m _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } q ^ { 2 } \rho _ { 1 } \rho _ { 2 } \rho _ { 3 } } { ( 1 + x ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } \frac { 1 - \cos 2 \sigma \cos 2 \phi } { J ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } 2 \sigma } ( K ^ { 2 } - \rlap / p _ { \phi } ^ { 2 } ) .
$$

# TRANSFORMATION OF POINCARE

# 5·3TRANSFORMATION OF POINCARE

Take the differential equations of motion of the $\pmb { n }$ -body problem in the form

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial y _ { i } } , \qquad \frac { d y _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { i } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n ) .
$$

Following Poincaré (1897)， we denote the mass of the $\mu \mathrm { t h }$ body by $\displaystyle { m _ { 3 \mu - 2 } = m _ { 3 \mu - 1 } = m _ { 3 \mu } } _ { ; }$ ,its position coordinates by $x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } , x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } , x _ { 3 \mu } .$ and its momentum components by $y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } , y _ { 3 \mu - 1 } , y _ { 3 \mu }$ If $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { i } x _ { i } ^ { \prime } d y _ { i } ^ { \prime } - \sum _ { i } x _ { i } d y _ { i } } \end{array}$ in the transformation from ${ x _ { i } } , { y _ { i } }$ to $x _ { i } ^ { \prime } , y _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ is a perfect differential, then the equations of motion conserve their canonical form. $\operatorname { I f } x _ { i } ^ { \prime } ( \ = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n )$ are functions of $\boldsymbol { x } _ { j }$ $( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n )$ only,and $y _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ $( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n )$ are functions of $y _ { j }$ $( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n )$ only, then the condition is writen

$$
\sum _ { i } y _ { i } ^ { \prime } x _ { i } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { j } y _ { j } x _ { j } .
$$

We make further assumptions:

(1) $x _ { 3 v - 2 } ^ { \prime } ( \nu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ are functions of $x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } \ ( \mu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ only, $y _ { 3 v - 2 } ^ { \prime } ~ ( \nu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ are functions of $y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ~ ( \mu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ only, $x _ { 3 v - 1 } ^ { \prime } ~ ( \nu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ are functions of $x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } \ ( \mu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ only, $y _ { 3 \nu - 1 } ^ { \prime } ~ ( \nu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ are functions of $y _ { 3 \mu - 1 } ~ ( \mu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ only, $x _ { 3 v } ^ { \prime } \quad ( \nu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ are functions of ${ \pmb x } _ { 3 { \pmb \mu } }$ $( \mu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ only, y'sv $( \nu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ are functions of $y _ { 3 \mu }$ $( \mu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ only.

(ii) The linear homogeneous relations are the same: between   
$x _ { 3 \nu - 2 } ^ { \prime } \ ( \nu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ and $x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } \ ( \mu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ;$ between   
$x _ { 3 \nu - 1 } ^ { \prime } \left( \nu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n \right) \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } \left( \mu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n \right) ;$ between   
$\pmb { x } _ { 3 \nu } ^ { \prime }$ $\ L _ { \nu } \left( \nu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n \right) \mathrm { a n d } x _ { 3 \mu } \left( \mu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n \right) .$ (iii) The relations are the same: between   
$y _ { 3 \nu - 2 } ^ { \prime } ~ ( \nu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ~ ( \mu = 1 , 2 , . . . . , n ) ;$ between   
$y _ { 3 \nu - 1 } ^ { \prime } ~ ( \nu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad y _ { 3 \mu - 1 } ~ ( \mu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ;$ between   
$y _ { 3 v } ^ { \prime } \quad ( \nu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ and y3μ (μ, = 1,2..,n).

Assumption (ii) is a consequence of assumptions (i) and (ii） and equation (48). By assumption (i), equation (48) becomes

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \sum _ { \nu } y _ { 3 \nu - 2 } ^ { \prime } x _ { 3 \nu - 2 } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \mu } y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \sum _ { \nu } y _ { 3 \nu - 1 } ^ { \prime } x _ { 3 \nu - 1 } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \mu } y _ { 3 \mu - 1 } x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \sum _ { \nu } y _ { 3 \nu } ^ { \prime } x _ { 3 \nu } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \mu } y _ { 3 \mu } x _ { 3 \mu } , } } \end{array}
$$

or, by assumption (ii),

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \sum _ { \nu } y _ { 3 \nu - 2 } ^ { \prime } x _ { 3 \nu - 1 } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \mu } y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } } , } \\ { { \displaystyle \sum _ { \nu } y _ { 3 \nu - 1 } ^ { \prime } x _ { 3 \nu - 2 } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \mu } y _ { 3 \mu - 1 } x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } } , } \end{array}
$$

and, in general,

$$
\sum _ { v } y _ { 3 v - \kappa } ^ { \prime } x _ { 3 v - \lambda } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \mu } y _ { 3 \mu - \kappa } x _ { 3 \mu - \lambda } \qquad ( \kappa , \lambda = 0 , 1 , 2 ) .
$$

From these we obtain

$$
\sum _ { \nu } \left( x _ { 3 \nu } ^ { \prime } y _ { 3 \nu - 1 } ^ { \prime } - x _ { 3 \nu - 1 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 3 \nu } ^ { \prime } \right) = \sum _ { \mu } \left( x _ { 3 \mu } y _ { 3 \mu - 1 } - x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } y _ { 3 \mu } \right) ,
$$

$$
\sum _ { \nu } \left( x _ { 3 \nu - 2 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 3 \nu } ^ { \prime } - x _ { 3 \nu } ^ { \prime } y _ { 3 \nu - 2 } ^ { \prime } \right) = \sum _ { \mu } \left( x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } y _ { 3 \mu } - x _ { 3 \mu } y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } \right) ,
$$

$$
\sum _ { \nu } \left( x _ { 3 \nu - 1 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 3 \nu - 2 } ^ { \prime } - x _ { 3 \nu - 2 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 3 \nu - 1 } ^ { \prime } \right) = \sum _ { \mu } \left( x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } - x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } y _ { 3 \mu - 1 } \right) .
$$

Hence, the form of each angular-momentum integral remains unaltered by our transformation.

As our transformation satisfies the conditions of equation (48), we write equations (47) in the form

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial y _ { i } ^ { \prime } } , \qquad \frac { d y _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n ) .
$$

We further assume that

$$
k y _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ,
$$

where $k$ is constant, and that $x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ~ ( \mu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ , depend only on $x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } - x _ { 3 n - 2 }$ $( \mu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - 1 )$ , that is,on the coordinates relative to the nth body. The momentum $y _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ is then a constant multiple of the momentum of the center of mass.We can assume that the center of mass is at rest. Hence, $\boldsymbol { H }$ does not depend on $\pmb { x } _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ as

$$
\frac { d y _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } } .
$$

Similarly, it does not depend on ${ \pmb x } _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime }$ .This happens when $x _ { 3 \nu - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ depends only on the difference $x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } - x _ { 3 n - 2 }$ in the relation between the old and the new variables. Actually, $T$ depends on $y ^ { \prime }$ and not on $x ^ { \prime }$ ， since it depends on $y$ and not on x. $U$ depends on $x _ { 3 \nu - 2 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 3 \nu - 1 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 3 \nu } ^ { \prime }$ and not on $x _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime }$ ,since it depends only on $x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } - x _ { 3 n - 2 } , x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } - x _ { 3 n - 1 }$ ${ x _ { 3 \mu } } \mathrm { ~ - ~ } { x _ { 3 n } }$ Hence, $H$ does not contain ${ x } _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ ， $x _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime }$

We can prove that the assumption (51） is not contradictory. If $\begin{array} { r } { k y _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \nu } y _ { 3 \nu - 2 ; } } \end{array}$ ，then $x _ { 3 y - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ can be shown to depend on $x _ { 3 v - 2 } - x _ { 3 n - 2 } ,$ because,if $x _ { 3 \mu - 2 }$ are all equal to $x _ { 3 n - 2 }$ in $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { \nu } x _ { 3 \nu - 2 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 3 \nu - 2 } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \mu } x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } , } \end{array}$ then the right-hand side is proportional to $\sum { } _ { \nu } y _ { 3 \nu - 2 }$ that is,to $y _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ Hence, $y _ { 3 \nu - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ on the left-hand side must be zero, and $x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ must vanish with $x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } - x _ { 3 n - 2 } .$ Thus $x _ { 3 y - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ is a linear expression of these differences.

It is seen that $H$ does not depend on $x _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime }$ . Hence we can suppose that $y _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime } = 0$ ,and the order of the differential equations (47) is reduced by six.

# 5·4ELIMINATION OF THE NODES

We consider fictitious bodies according to Radau in the following manner (Bertrand,l852).The coordinates and the momenta of the first fictitious body are $x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } , y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , y _ { 3 } ^ { \prime }$ ；its instantaneous orbital plane, that is, the plane containing the origin, the first fictitious body,and the momentum vector, is perpendicular to the vector

$$
{ \mathfrak { v } } _ { 1 } = \big ( x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } - x _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } - x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } - x _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \big ) .
$$

The position coordinates and the momenta of the second fictitious body are $x _ { 4 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 5 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 6 } ^ { \prime } , y _ { 4 } ^ { \prime } , y _ { 5 } ^ { \prime } , y _ { 6 } ^ { \prime }$ ,and its instantaneous orbital plane is perpendicular to the vector $\mathfrak { v } _ { 2 } = ( x _ { 5 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 6 } ^ { \prime } - x _ { 6 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 5 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 6 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 4 } ^ { \prime } - x _ { 4 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 6 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 4 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 5 } ^ { \prime } - x _ { 5 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 4 } ^ { \prime } )$ . In general, we define the vector

$$
\mathfrak { d } _ { \mu } = \bigl ( x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 3 \mu } ^ { \prime } - x _ { 3 \mu } ^ { \prime } y _ { 3 \mu - 1 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 3 \mu } ^ { \prime } y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { \prime } - x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 3 \mu } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 3 \mu - 1 } ^ { \prime } - x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } ^ { \prime } y _ { 3 \mu } ^ { \prime } \bigr )
$$

The sum of vector $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { \mu = 2 } ^ { n - 1 } \mathfrak { v } _ { \mu } = \mathfrak { V } _ { 1 } } \end{array}$ and vector ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { 1 }$ , has the components

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } ( x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } y _ { 3 \mu } - x _ { 3 \mu } y _ { 3 \mu - 1 } ) , \qquad \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } ( x _ { 3 \mu } y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } - x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } y _ { 3 \mu } ) ,
$$

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } ( x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } y _ { 3 \mu - 1 } - x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ) ,
$$

since $y _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime } = 0$ ，and this vector sum equals the total angular momentum vector $\mathfrak { V }$ of the $\pmb { n }$ -body system.As the total angular momentum vector is perpendicular to the invariable plane of the $\pmb { n }$ -body system，the vector $\mathfrak { v } _ { 1 } + \mathfrak { V } _ { 1 }$ is perpendicular to the invariable plane. Consequently $\mathfrak { d } _ { 1 } , \ \mathfrak { V } _ { 1 }$ and $\mathfrak { B }$ are all in the same plane. Hence,the intersection of the two planes perpendicular respectively to the vectors ${ \mathfrak { v } } _ { 1 }$ and ${ \mathfrak { V } } _ { 1 }$ is parallel to the invariable plane.In other words, the intersection of the instantaneous orbital plane of an arbitrary one of the $n - 1$ fictitious bodies and the plane perpendicular to the vector repre-senting the resultant angular momentum of the remaining $n - 2$ fictitious bodies is parallel to the invariable plane of the original $\pmb { n }$ -body system. This is an extension to the $_ { n }$ -body problem of the Jacobi theorem on the elimination of the nodes in the three-body problem.

In particular,in the three-body problem ( $( n = 3 )$ ）the theorem reduces to the following: the direction of the ascending node of the instantaneous orbital plane of the first fictitious body differs by l80 degrees from the direction of the ascending node of the instantaneous orbital plane of the second fictitious body.If the triangle formed by ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { 1 }$ ， ${ \mathfrak { V } } _ { 1 }$ ,and $\mathfrak { L }$ is acute, then the components of the vectors ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { 1 }$ and ${ \mathfrak { V } } _ { 1 }$ in the direction of $\mathfrak { L }$ are of the same direction； that is,the two fictitious bodies revolve either both in the direct sense or both in the retrograde sense.But as the components in the direction perpendicular to $\mathfrak { B }$ are of opposite direction, the inclinations of the instantaneous orbital planes of the two fictitious bodies relative to the invariable plane (which is perpendicular to $\mathfrak { B }$ ） should have opposite signs.If the triangle formed of ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { 1 } , \ { \mathfrak { V } } _ { 1 } .$ and $\mathfrak { L }$ is obtuse, then the components perpendicular to $\mathfrak { B }$ of the vectors ${ \mathfrak { p } } _ { 1 }$ and ${ \mathfrak { V } } _ { 1 }$ are of opposite directions,and one of the two fictitious bodies revolves in the direct sense and the other in the retrograde sense. In either case the ascending nodes in question should difer by l80 degrees in direction, because the intersections of the two orbital planes with the invariable plane should be parallel. This is the Jacobi theorem (Jacobi,1842,1844) on the elimination of the nodes,and it is an integral of the problem.

# 5.5TRANSFORMATION $\propto$

Poincaré has specified his transformation by further assumptions and classified the cases $\alpha , ~ \beta$ and $\gamma$ .The transformation，which was first discovered by Cauchy, is

$$
x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } - x _ { 3 n - 2 } , \qquad x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } - x _ { 3 n - 1 } , \qquad x _ { 3 \mu } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 3 \mu } - x _ { 3 \mu } .
$$

$$
x _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 3 n - 2 } , \qquad x _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 3 n - 1 } , \qquad x _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 3 n } ,
$$

$$
y _ { i } ^ { \prime } = y _ { i } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n - 3 )
$$

$$
y _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } , \qquad y _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 3 \mu - 1 } , \qquad y _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 3 \mu } .
$$

The motion of the $\mu \mathrm { t h }$ fictitious body that we are now considering is a relative motion around the nth body, but its momentum is that due to its absolute motion, and $y _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } , y _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime } , y _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime }$ are the components of the total momentum of the whole $\pmb { n } .$ -body system.

There is another transformation by Poincaré which is not essentially different from this:

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } - x _ { 3 n - 2 } , \qquad x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } - x _ { 3 n - 1 } , } } \\ { { x _ { 3 \mu } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 3 \mu } - x _ { 3 n } \qquad ( \mu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - 1 ) , } } \end{array}
$$

$$
x _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } = { \frac { \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 \mu - 2 } x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } } { \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 \mu - 2 } } } , \qquad x _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime } = { \frac { \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 \mu - 1 } x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } } { \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 \mu - 1 } } } ,
$$

$$
x _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime } = \frac { \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 \mu } x _ { 3 \mu } } { \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 \mu } } ,
$$

$$
y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } - { \frac { m _ { 3 \mu - 2 } y _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } } { \underset { \mu = 1 } { \overset { n } { \sum } } } } , \qquad y _ { 3 \mu - 1 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 3 \mu - 1 } - { \frac { m _ { 3 \mu - 1 } y _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime } } { \underset { \mu = 1 } { \overset { n } { \sum } } } } ,
$$

$$
y _ { 3 \mu } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 3 \mu } - \frac { m _ { 3 \mu } y _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime } } { \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 \mu } } ,
$$

$$
y _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } , \qquad y _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 3 \mu - 1 } , \qquad y _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 3 \mu }
$$

As we may suppose that the center of mass is at rest, we put

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } = 0 .
$$

Then $x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { \prime } , y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { \prime } , y _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ have the same values as in (52). Only ${ \pmb x } _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ is different. The Hamiltonian $\pmb { H }$ depends on $x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } - x _ { 3 n - 2 }$ but does not depend on ${ \pmb x } _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime }$

In order to reduce the order of the differential equations (47)

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial y _ { i } } , \frac { d y _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { i } } ~ ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n ) ,
$$

in conjunction with

$$
H = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } { \frac { y _ { i } ^ { 2 } } { 2 m _ { i } } } - \sum _ { \mu , v } ^ { n } { \frac { m _ { 3 \mu } m _ { 3 v } } { r _ { 3 \mu , 3 v } } } ,
$$

from $6 n$ to $6 n - 6$ ，we make the contact transformation

$$
x _ { i } = \frac { \partial W } { \partial y _ { i } } , \qquad y _ { i } ^ { \prime } = \frac { \partial W } { \partial x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } , \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n ) ,
$$

$$
W = \Big ( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 ^ { n - 3 } } x _ { i } ^ { \prime } y _ { i } \Big ) \ + \ x _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } \ + \ x _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime } \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 3 \mu - 1 } \ + \ x _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime } \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 3 \mu }
$$

Then the transformed equations are

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial y _ { i } ^ { \prime } } , \frac { d y _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n - 3 ) ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { I = \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n - 3 } \frac { y _ { i } ^ { \prime 2 } } { 2 m _ { i } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 m _ { 3 n } } [ ( y _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } - \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } + ( y _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime } - \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } y _ { 3 \mu - 1 } ^ { \prime }  } \\ {  +  ( y _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime } - \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } y _ { 3 \mu } ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } ] - \sum _ { \mu , \nu = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { m _ { 3 \mu } m _ { 3 \mu } } { r _ { 3 \mu , 3 } } } \end{array}
$$

where $x _ { i }$ in $\boldsymbol { r } _ { 3 \mu , 3 \nu }$ are rewritten in terms of the new variables. As $\boldsymbol { H }$ is independent of ${ x } _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ ， $x _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ x _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime }$ ， we obtain the integrals of the total angular momentum $y _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } =$ constant, $y _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime } =$ constant, $y _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime } =$ constant.

We set these constants equal to zero by considering the motion relative to the center of mass; that is, we remove the primes:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } {  { : \frac { \partial H } { \partial y _ { i } } , \quad \frac { \partial y _ { i } } { \partial t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { i } } } \quad } & { ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , 3 n - 3 ) , } \\ & { \stackrel { \mathrm { \scriptsize ~ 3 n - 3 ~ } } { = } \frac { y _ { i } ^ { 2 } } { i - 1 } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } } { 2 m _ { i } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 m _ { 3 n } } \biggl [ \biggl ( \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } \biggr ) ^ { 2 } + \biggl ( \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } y _ { 3 \mu - 1 } \biggr ) ^ { 2 } + \biggl ( \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } y _ { 3 \mu } \biggr ) ^ { 2 } \biggr ] } \\ & { - \underbrace { \sum } _ { \mu , \nu = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { m _ { 3 \mu } m _ { 3 \nu } } { r _ { 3 \mu , 3 \nu } } , } \\ & { r _ { 3 \mu , 3 \nu } ^ { 2 } = ( x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } - x _ { 3 \nu - 2 } ) ^ { 2 } + ( x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } - x _ { 3 \nu - 1 } ) ^ { 2 } + ( x _ { 3 \mu } - x _ { 3 \nu } ) ^ { 2 } } \\ & { \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad \quad } \\ & { r _ { 3 \mu , 3 \nu } ^ { 2 } = x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 \nu + 1 } ^ { 2 } . } \end{array}
$$

# 5.6TRANSFORMATION $\beta$

Put

$$
y _ { i } ^ { \prime } = m _ { i } ^ { \prime } { \frac { d x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { d t } } , \qquad m _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { \prime } = m _ { 3 \mu - 1 } ^ { \prime } = m _ { 3 \mu } ^ { \prime } \qquad ( \mu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ;
$$

then the relation between $y _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ and $y _ { i }$ is the same as the relation between $m _ { i } ^ { \prime } x _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ and $m _ { i } x _ { i }$ ,and (48) takes the form

$$
\sum _ { i } m _ { i } ^ { \prime } x _ { i } ^ { \prime 2 } = \sum _ { i } m _ { i } x _ { i } ^ { 2 } .
$$

This is nothing but the kinetic energy in terms of the new variables

$$
T = \sum _ { i } { \frac { m _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { 2 } } \left( { \frac { d x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } = \sum _ { i } { \frac { 1 } { 2 m _ { i } ^ { \prime } } } y _ { i } ^ { \prime 2 } .
$$

Hence, the expressons not only for the total momentum but also for the kinetic energy are invariant by this transformation $\beta$ ，Moreover, the canonical form of the differential equations is conserved.

We propose to obtain such a transformation. Consider the transformation

$$
x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } = \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { n } c _ { \mu \kappa } x _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } = \sum _ { \kappa = 1 } ^ { n } c _ { \mu \kappa } x _ { 3 \kappa - 1 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 3 \mu } = \sum _ { \kappa = 1 } ^ { n } c _ { \mu \kappa } x _ { 3 \kappa } ^ { \prime } .
$$

There are $n ^ { 2 }$ coefficients ${ \pmb { c } } _ { { \pmb { \mu } } { \pmb { \kappa } } }$ .Condition (48) becomes

$$
\sum _ { \mu } m _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { \prime } x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { \prime 2 } = \sum _ { \mu } m _ { 3 \mu - 2 } x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { 2 } .
$$

Substituting (59) in this expression and equating the coefficients of $x _ { 3 \rho - 2 } ^ { \prime } x _ { 3 \sigma - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ to zero, we obtain $n ( n - 1 )$ relations

$$
\sum _ { \mu } m _ { 3 \mu - 2 } c _ { \mu \rho } c _ { \mu \sigma } = 0 \qquad ( \rho , \sigma = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

From the coefficients of ${ \pmb x } _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ we obtain $\pmb { n }$ relations

$$
\sum _ { \mu } m _ { 3 \mu - 2 } c _ { \mu \kappa } ^ { 2 } = m _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } ^ { \prime } \qquad ( \kappa = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Altogether we have $\scriptstyle { \pmb { n } } ^ { 2 }$ relations among the coefcients ${ c } _ { \mu \kappa }$

Following Jacobi, take the coordinates

$$
x _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 3 \kappa - 1 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 3 \kappa } ^ { \prime } \qquad ( \kappa = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - 1 ) ,
$$

so that the $( \kappa + 1 ) \operatorname { t h }$ body is referred to the center of masss of the first $\kappa$ bodies, that is,

$$
x _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 3 ( \kappa + 1 ) - 2 } - { \biggl ( \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { \kappa } m _ { 3 \mu - 2 } x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } \biggr ) } \bigg / { \biggl ( \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { \kappa } m _ { 3 \mu - 2 } \biggr ) }
$$

$$
x _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } = \mathrm { ~ - \biggl ( \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 \mu - 2 } \it { x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } } \biggr ) \bigg / \biggl ( \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 \mu - 2 } \biggr ) \cdot \nonumber }
$$

To satisfy (61) and (62), put

$$
M _ { \kappa } = \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { \kappa } m _ { 3 \mu - 2 } \qquad ( \kappa = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - 1 ) ;
$$

then solve (63) for ${ x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } }$

$$
x _ { 3 ( \kappa + 1 ) - 2 } = x _ { 1 } + \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { \kappa - 1 } \frac { m _ { 3 \mu - 2 } x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { \prime } } { M _ { \mu } } + x _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } ^ { \prime } .
$$

Next substitute this expression for $x _ { 3 \mu - 2 }$ in the last equation of (63):

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle M _ { n } x _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } = M _ { n } x _ { 1 } + \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } m _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ( M _ { \mu } + m _ { 3 ( \mu + 1 ) - 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + m _ { 3 n - 2 } ) } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad \times \frac { x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { \prime } } { M _ { \mu } } + m _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } x _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } } , } \end{array}
$$

and substitute this $\pmb { x } _ { 1 }$ in (65). Then,

$$
x _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } = x _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } - \sum _ { \mu = \kappa + 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 \mu - 2 } { \frac { x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { \prime } } { M _ { \mu } } } + { \frac { M _ { \kappa - 1 } } { M _ { \kappa } } } x _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } ^ { \prime } .
$$

Comparing this expression with (59), we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l l l } { { c _ { \kappa \mu } = - { \displaystyle \frac { m _ { 3 \mu - 2 } } { M _ { \kappa } } } } } & { { } } & { { ( \mu > \kappa ) , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { { c _ { \kappa \mu } = 0 } } & { { } } & { { ( \mu < \kappa ) , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { c _ { \kappa \mu } = { \displaystyle \frac { M _ { \kappa - 1 } } { M _ { \kappa } } } } } & { { } } & { { ( \mu = \kappa ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Equation (61) is satisfied by these coefficients.Put (67) in (62)； then the right-hand member becomes

$$
\frac { M _ { \kappa - 1 } } { M _ { \kappa } } m _ { 3 \kappa - 2 }
$$

and we have

$$
m _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } ^ { \prime } = m _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } \frac { M _ { \kappa - 1 } } { M _ { \kappa } } \qquad ( \kappa = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) .
$$

Condition (48) is satisfied if we determine $y _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ by (61) and (63). This is an extension of Poincaré's transformation $\beta$ ; the $y _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ are called the relative canonical coordinates of Jacobi.

The expression for the force function $U$ is rather complicated when (66) is used. Taking the center of masses as the origin, we have

$$
x _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime } = 0 .
$$

The center of mass has zero momentum; that is, it is at rest:

$$
y _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime } = 0 .
$$

By this transformation, the order of differential equations (47) is reduced from $6 n$ to $6 n - 6$

$$
y _ { i } = { \frac { \partial W } { \partial x _ { i } } } , \qquad x _ { i } ^ { \prime } = { \frac { \partial W } { \partial y _ { i } ^ { \prime } } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n ) ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { W = \displaystyle \sum _ { x = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \{ y _ { \mathrm { a s } = 2 } ^ { x } [ x _ { \mathrm { a } ( x + 1 ) - 2 } - ( \displaystyle \sum _ { u = 1 } ^ { X } m _ { \mathrm { a s } - 2 } x _ { \mathrm { a } ( u - 2 ) } ) \middle / ( \displaystyle \sum _ { u = 1 } ^ { X } m _ { \mathrm { a s } - 2 } ) ]  } \\ {  + y _ { \mathrm { a s } = 1 } ^ { y } [ x _ { \mathrm { 3 } ( x + 1 ) - 1 } - ( \displaystyle \sum _ { u = 1 } ^ { X } m _ { \mathrm { a s } - 1 } x _ { \mathrm { a } ( u - 1 ) - 1 } ) \middle / ( \displaystyle \sum _ { u = 1 } ^ { X } m _ { \mathrm { a s } - 1 } ) ]  } \\ {  + y _ { \mathrm { a s } } ^ { r } [ x _ { \mathrm { a } ( x + 1 ) - } ( \displaystyle \sum _ { u = 1 } ^ { X } m _ { \mathrm { a s } } x _ { \mathrm { a } u } ) \middle / ( \displaystyle \sum _ { u = 1 } ^ { X } m _ { \mathrm { a s } } ) ] \} } \\ {  + y _ { \mathrm { a s } - 2 } ^ { r } ( \displaystyle \sum _ { u = 1 } ^ { B } m _ { \mathrm { a s } - 2 } x _ { \mathrm { a } u - 2 } ) + y _ { \mathrm { a s } - 1 } ^ { r } ( \displaystyle \sum _ { u = 1 } ^ { B } m _ { \mathrm { a s } - 1 } x _ { \mathrm { a } u - 2 } )  } \\ {  + y _ { \mathrm { a s } } ^ { r } ( \displaystyle \sum _ { u = 1 } ^ { B } m _ { \mathrm { a s } } x _ { \mathrm { a } u } ) . } \end{array}

After this contact transformation, put

$$
y _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime } = 0 ,
$$

and drop the primes. Then,

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial y _ { i } } , \frac { d y _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { i } } , ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n - 3 ) ,
$$

$$
H = \sum _ { \kappa = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \left( \frac { y _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } ^ { 2 } } { 2 m _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } ^ { \prime } } + \frac { y _ { 3 \kappa - 1 } ^ { 2 } } { 2 m _ { 3 \kappa - 1 } ^ { ' } } + \frac { y _ { 3 \kappa } ^ { 2 } } { 2 m _ { 3 \kappa } ^ { ' } } \right) - \sum _ { \mu , \nu = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { m _ { 3 \mu } m _ { 3 \nu } } { r _ { 3 \mu , 3 \nu } } ,
$$

where $\boldsymbol { r } _ { 3 { \mu } , 3 { \nu } }$ is expressed in terms of new coordinates, using (59), (67), and (68),and the primes are dropped.Jacobi and Bertrand obtained the transformation for the case $n = 3$ . It is extended to the general case $\boldsymbol { n }$ in the present discussion.

Put

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { m _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } = \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 \mu - 2 } , \qquad m _ { i } ^ { \prime } = m _ { i } ~ ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n - 3 ) , } } \\ { { } } \\ { { y _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } = \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } = m _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } \displaystyle \frac { d x _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } } { d t } , } } \\ { { } } \\ { { { } \displaystyle 2 { x _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } = \displaystyle \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } m _ { 3 \mu - 2 } x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } } , } } \end{array}

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } - x _ { 3 n - 2 } \qquad ( \mu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - 1 ) , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { y _ { i } ^ { \prime } = m _ { i } ^ { \prime } \displaystyle \frac { d x _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { d t } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n ) . } } \end{array}

The momenta $y _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime }$ and $y _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime }$ are transformed similarly to $y _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } ; x _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime }$ and $\pmb { x } _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime }$ similarly to $x _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } ; x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } ^ { \prime }$ and $\pmb { x _ { 3 \mu } ^ { \prime } }$ similarly to $x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ .The sum of the masses of the system is $m _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } = m _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime } = m _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime }$ ； the coordinates of the center of mass are $x _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime }$ .The coordinates of the $\mu \mathrm { t h }$ body relative to the nth body are $x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { \prime } , \ x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ x _ { 3 \mu } ^ { \prime }$ .The momentum components of the $\mu \mathrm { t h }$ body relative to the nth body are $y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { \prime } , y _ { 3 \mu - 1 } ^ { \prime } , y _ { 3 \mu } ^ { \prime } .$ This transformation $\gamma$ does not satisfy conditions (48) or (49).Hence, the transformed equations are not canonical. Accordingly we cannot eliminate the nodes by this transformation.

We fix the center of mass, transfer the coordinate origin to the center of mass, and put $x _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime } = 0$ ， $y _ { 3 n - 2 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 3 n - 1 } ^ { \prime } = y _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime } = 0$ The transformed equations, after the primes are dropped, are

$$
\underline { { \underline { { \ { \ - 2 } } } } } = \frac { \partial H _ { \mu } } { \partial y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } } , \qquad \frac { d x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H _ { \mu } } { \partial y _ { 3 \mu - 1 } } , \qquad \frac { d x _ { 3 \mu } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H _ { \mu } } { \partial y _ { 3 \mu } } ,
$$

$$
\frac { d y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } } { d t } = \triangledown - \frac { \partial H _ { \mu } } { \partial x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } } , \frac { d y _ { 3 \mu - 1 } } { d t } = \triangledown - \frac { \partial H _ { \mu } } { \partial x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } } , \frac { d y _ { 3 \mu } } { d t } = \triangledown - \frac { \partial H _ { \mu } } { \partial x _ { 3 \mu } } ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c }  { H _ { \mu } = { \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \left( \frac { y _ { 3 \mu - 2 } ^ { 2 } } { m _ { 3 \mu - 2 } } + \frac { y _ { 3 \mu - 1 } ^ { 2 } } { m _ { 3 \mu - 1 } } + \frac { y _ { 3 \mu } ^ { 2 } } { m _ { 3 \mu } } \right) \nonumber - \displaystyle { \frac { m _ { 3 \mu } m _ { 3 n } } { r _ { 3 \mu , 3 n } } - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \frac { m _ { 3 \mu } m _ { 3 \nu } } { r _ { 3 \mu , 3 \nu } } } } } \\ { { + \displaystyle { \sum _ { \nu = 1 \atop ( \mu \neq \nu ) } ^ { n - 1 } \frac { m _ { 3 \mu } m _ { 3 \nu } } { r _ { 3 \mu , 3 \nu } ^ { 3 } } \left( x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } x _ { 3 \nu - 2 } + x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } x _ { 3 \nu - 1 } + x _ { 3 \mu } x _ { 3 \nu } \right) } } } \\ { { \nonumber } } \\ { { ( \mu = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - 1 ) . } } \end{array}
$$

These are the usual equations of motion in the perturbation theory. The Hamiltonian $H$ differs only by the last term, that is,

$$
H _ { \mu } = T - U + \sum _ { \stackrel { \nu = 1 } { ( \mu \neq \nu ) } } ^ { n - 1 } \frac { m _ { 3 \mu } m _ { 3 \nu } } { r _ { 3 \mu , 3 \nu } ^ { 3 } } ( x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } x _ { 3 \nu - 2 } + x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } x _ { 3 \nu - 1 } + x _ { 3 \mu } x _ { 3 \nu } ) ,
$$

where

$$
T = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i = 1 \atop i = 1 } ^ { 3 n - 3 } \frac { 1 } { m _ { i } } y _ { i } ^ { 2 } , \qquad U = et { } { ' } \sum _ { \stackrel { \mu , v } { ( \mu \neq v ) } } \frac { m _ { 3 \mu } m _ { 3 v } } { r _ { 3 \mu , 3 v } } .
$$

The perturbation of the vth body due to presence of the $\mu \mathrm { t h }$ body is

$$
\frac { m _ { 3 v } } { r _ { 3 \mu , 3 v } } - \frac { m _ { 3 v } } { r _ { 3 \mu , 3 v } ^ { 3 } } ( x _ { 3 \mu - 2 } x _ { 3 v - 2 } + x _ { 3 \mu - 1 } x _ { 3 v - 1 } + x _ { 3 \mu } x _ { 3 v } ) ,
$$

and is called the disturbing function or the perturbative function. The first term is called the direct part and the second the indirect part.Such a situation occurs in the case when the mass of the nth body is very large, compared with the masses of the remaining $n - 1$ bodies. The motion of the $\mu \mathrm { t h }$ body around the nth body is disturbed by the perturbative action of the remaining $n - 2$ bodies.The perturbation is included in the perturbative function and is multiplied by the disturbing mass $m _ { 3 v }$ ·

$$

$$
+  n _ { 2 } \Biggl \{ \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { n - 3 } | \begin{array} { c c } { { x _ { 3 \nu } } } & { { x _ { 3 \nu - 2 } } } \\ { { y _ { 3 \nu } } } & { { y _ { 3 \nu - 2 } } } \end{array} | + | \begin{array} { c c } { { 0 } } & { { x _ { 3 n - 8 } } } \\ { { \Gamma } } & { { y _ { 3 n - 8 } } } \end{array} | + | \begin{array} { c c } { { 0 } } & { { x _ { 3 n - 6 } } } \\ { { \Gamma ^ { \prime } } } & { { y _ { 3 n - 6 } } } \end{array} | \Biggr \} .
$$

Thus $\Gamma$ and $\Gamma ^ { \prime }$ are given by the two linear equations:

$$
\Gamma x _ { 3 n - 7 } + \Gamma ^ { \prime } x _ { 3 n - 5 } = y _ { 3 n - 3 } - \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { n - 3 } \bigg | \begin{array} { l l } { { x _ { 3 \nu - 1 } } } & { { x _ { 3 \nu } } } \\ { { y _ { 3 \nu - 1 } } } & { { y _ { 3 \nu } } } \end{array} \bigg | ,
$$

$$
\Gamma x _ { 3 n - 8 } + \Gamma ^ { \prime } x _ { 3 n - 6 } = y _ { 3 n - 4 } \ \mathrm { c o s e c } \ x _ { 3 n - 3 } + \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { n - 3 } \left| { x _ { 3 \nu } } \quad { x _ { 3 \nu - 2 } } \right|
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { { } + \cot x _ { 3 n - 3 } \bigg \{ \displaystyle \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { n - 3 } \bigg | \displaystyle x _ { 3 \nu - 2 } } & { { } x _ { 3 \nu - 1 } \bigg | + \bigg | \displaystyle x _ { 3 n - 8 } } & { { } x _ { 3 n - 7 } \bigg | } \\ { { } } & { { } \qquad \quad + \mathrm { ~ c o t ~ } x _ { 3 n - 3 } \bigg \{ \displaystyle \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { n - 3 } \bigg | \displaystyle x _ { 3 \nu - 2 } } & { { } y _ { 3 \nu - 1 } \bigg | + \bigg | \displaystyle y _ { 3 n - 8 } } & { { } y _ { 3 n - 7 } \bigg | } \\ { { } } & { { } \qquad \quad + \mathrm { ~ } \bigg | \displaystyle x _ { 3 n - 6 } } & { { } x _ { 3 n - 5 } \bigg | \bigg \} . }  \end{array}
$$

These equations do not depend on $x _ { 3 n - 4 }$ . Thus, the Hamiltonian in the new variables is

$$
H = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n - 9 } \frac { y _ { i } ^ { 2 } } { 2 m _ { i } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 m _ { 3 n - 6 } } ( y _ { 3 n - 8 } ^ { 2 } + y _ { 3 n - 7 } ^ { 2 } + \Gamma ^ { 2 } )
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \displaystyle } & { + \frac { 1 } { 2 m _ { 3 n - 3 } } ( g _ { 9 n - 6 } ^ { n } + y _ { 9 n - 5 } ^ { 2 } + \Gamma ^ { n } ) } \\ & { + \frac { 1 } { 2 m _ { 3 n } } \displaystyle \left[ \left( \sum _ { \gamma = 1 } ^ { n - 3 } y _ { 3 n - 6 } + y _ { 9 n - 6 } + y _ { 9 n - 6 } \right) ^ { 2 } \right. } \\ & { \qquad + \left. \left( \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { n - 3 } y _ { 9 n - 1 } + y _ { 3 n - 7 } + y _ { 3 n - 5 } \right) ^ { 2 } \right. } \\ & { \qquad + \left. \left( \sum _ { \gamma = 1 } ^ { n - 3 } y _ { 3 n } + \Gamma + \Gamma \right) ^ { 2 } \right] - \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { n } { \gamma _ { 3 n } m _ { 3 n } } , } \end{array}
$$

where $r _ { 3 \mu , 3 \nu }$ is expressed in terms of $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n - 5 } ,$ As this expression for $\boldsymbol { H }$ does not depend on $x _ { 3 n - 4 }$ in the canonical equations

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial y _ { i } } , \frac { d y _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { i } } ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n - 3 ) ,
$$

the canonical equations have the integral

$$
y _ { 3 n - 4 } = K = \tt c o n s t a n t .
$$

$x _ { 3 n - 4 }$ appears only in $d x _ { 3 n - 4 } / d t = \partial H / \partial y _ { 3 n - 4 }$ .Hence, there is no need to consider $x _ { 3 n - 4 }$ in our discussion.We obtain $x _ { 3 n - 4 }$ by mere quadrature in the last stage. Thus we come to the differential equations of order $6 n - 8$ ：

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial y _ { i } } , \frac { d y _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { i } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , 3 n - 5 , 3 n - 3 ) ,
$$

where we have put $\kappa$ instead of $y _ { 3 n - 4 }$

Now we choose the $o z$ axis parallel to the resultant angular momentum of the $n$ -body system,so that the $X Y$ plane is now the invariable plane.There are two integrals for the angular momentum; that is, the angular momentum around $O X ^ { \prime }$ is zero and the angular momentum around $O Z ^ { \prime }$ is equal to $- \kappa$ cos $x _ { 3 n - 3 }$ ; that is,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { y _ { 3 n - 3 } = 0 , } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { \displaystyle - K \cos x _ { 3 n - 3 } = \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { n - 3 } \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { { \displaystyle x _ { 3 \nu - 2 } } } & { { \displaystyle x _ { 3 \nu - 1 } } } \\ { { \displaystyle y _ { 3 \nu - 2 } } } & { { \displaystyle y _ { 3 \nu - 1 } } } \end{array} \right| + \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { { \displaystyle x _ { 3 n - 8 } } } & { { \displaystyle \hat { x } _ { 3 n - 7 } } } \\ { { \displaystyle y _ { 3 n - 8 } } } & { { \displaystyle y _ { 3 n - 7 } } } \end{array} \right| } } \\ { { \ \displaystyle ~ + \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { { \displaystyle x _ { 3 n - 6 } } } & { { \displaystyle x _ { 3 n - 5 } } } \\ { { \displaystyle y _ { 3 n - 6 } } } & { { \displaystyle y _ { 3 n - 5 } } } \end{array} \right| . } } \end{array}
$$

Let $H ^ { \prime }$ be the result of substituting these values of $x _ { 3 n - 3 }$ and ${ y _ { 3 n - 3 } }$ in $\pmb { H }$ and let one of these variables be $\pmb { S } ,$ Then,

$$
\frac { \partial H ^ { \prime } } { \partial s } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial s } + \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { 3 n - 3 } } \frac { \partial x _ { 3 n - 3 } } { \partial s } .
$$

But, since $y _ { 3 n - 3 } = 0$ and $\partial H / \partial x _ { 3 n - 3 } + d y _ { 3 n - 3 } / d t = 0 _ { \mathrm { ; } }$ ，therefore

$$
\frac { \partial H ^ { \prime } } { \partial s } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial s } \cdot
$$

The reduced differential equations are now

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H ^ { \prime } } { \partial y _ { i } } , \frac { d y _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H ^ { \prime } } { \partial x _ { i } } ~ ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n - 5 ) ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \quad \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \eta _ { 1 } } { \partial t } = } \\ & { = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { 1 } { \partial q _ { 2 } } [ ( \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 } \frac { \partial } { \partial { x } _ { i } } + \frac { \partial } { \partial { x } _ { i } } \ y _ { i + 1 } \ y _ { i + 2 } ) ^ { 2 }  } \\ & { \qquad + \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 } \frac { \partial } { \partial { x } _ { i } } \ y _ { i - 1 } + \frac { \partial } { \partial { x } _ { i } } \ y _ { i - 3 } \ y _ { i } + \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 } ( \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 } \frac { \partial } { \partial { x } _ { i } } - \Gamma + \Gamma ) ^ { 2 } ] } \\ & { = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 3 } } \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 } \frac { \partial } { \partial q _ { 2 } \partial q _ { 3 } } [ ( \partial _ { i } \eta _ { 1 } - 2 - x _ { 1 } , \ldots , 2 ) ^ { 2 }  } \\ & { \qquad +  ( \partial _ { i \Omega _ { \Delta \omega - 1 } } - x _ { 2 } , \ldots , \ldots , ) ^ { 2 }  +   ( \partial _ { i \Omega _ { \Delta \omega - 1 } } - x _ { 3 } , \ldots , \ldots , ) ^ { 3 } ] ^ { 3 - 1 } } \\ & { \qquad -  \partial _ { \Omega \omega - 1 } \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 } \rho _ { 0 } [ ( x _ { i } - x _ { i } , \ldots , x _ { i } , \ldots , x _ { i } , \ldots , x _ { i } , 0 ) ^ { 2 } + ( \partial _ { i } \eta _ { 1 } - \delta _ { i } , \ldots , x _ { i } , 0 ) ^ { 2 } + ( \partial _ { i } \eta _ { 1 } - \delta _ { i } , \ldots , x _ { i } , \ldots , ) ^ { 2 } ] } \\ &  \qquad \quad - \Pi _ { \omega - 1 } \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 } \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { i } } [ ( \partial _ { i } \eta _ { i } - 2 - x _ { i } , \ldots , x _ { i } , 0 ) ^ { 2 } + ( \partial _ { i } \eta _ { 1 } - 2 - x _  \end{array}
$$

By (79), the expressions for $\Gamma$ and $\Gamma ^ { \prime }$ in the new variables become

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \Gamma x _ { 9 n - 7 } + \Gamma ^ { \prime } x _ { 9 n - 5 } = } & { - \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - 3 } \left| { x _ { 9 n - 1 } \atop | y _ { 9 n - 1 } } \right. \ } \\ { \mathrm { T x _ { 9 n - 9 } ~ + ~ \Gamma ^ { \prime } x _ { 9 n - 6 } ~ = ~  { K \operatorname { c o s c } _ { < 0 n - 3 } + \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - 3 } \left| { x _ { 3 n } \atop y _ { 9 n - 9 } } \right| \ll _ { 9 n - 2 } \left| \right|} , } } \\ { \displaystyle } & { \displaystyle = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - 3 } \left| { x _ { 9 n } \atop y _ { 9 n } } \right. \ \ \cdot \ { x _ { 9 n - 2 } }  } \\ &  \displaystyle \phantom { + \ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - 3 } \left| { x _ { 9 n - 1 } \atop y _ { 9 n - 1 } } \right| \ } + \left. { K ^ { 2 } - \left[ \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - 3 } \left| { x _ { 9 n - 2 } \atop y _ { 9 n - 2 } } \right. \ \right. \right. } \\ & { \displaystyle \phantom { + \ \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - 3 } \left| { x _ { 9 n - 6 } \atop y _ { 9 n - 6 } } \right.} + \left| { x _ { 9 n - 6 } \atop y _ { 9 n - 6 } } \right. \ \left. \right. \left. \right| + \left| { x _ { 3 n - 8 } \atop y _ { 9 n - 5 } } \right. \ \  } \\ & { \displaystyle \phantom { + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n - 3 } \left| { x _ { 9 n - 6 } \atop y _ { 9 n - 6 } } \right.} + \left| \right. _ { 9 n - 6 }  \ \left. \ { x _ { 9 n - 5 } } \right| \Bigg \} } \end{array}
$$

Thus the order of the equations of motion has been reduced to $6 n - 1 0$

The next step is to reduce the order to $6 n - 1 2$ , by using the energy integral and the elimination of time.This is the final stage of the reduction — using all the known first integrals - as has been shown in Section 4.10.

# ELLIPTIC MOTIONS

# 5.9MOTION IN CONIC SECTIONS

Consider an $_ { n }$ -body problem where one of the $_ { n }$ bodies has a mass much greater than the mass of any one of the other bodies. We discuss two cases.First, we investigate the motion of one of the bodies of smaller mass around the body of larger mass; the effects of the remaining bodies can then be regarded as small perturbations superposed on the motion in the two-body problem. Second,we will inquire into the case where the distance between two of the $\pmb { n }$ bodies is very small compared with their distances from the other bodies;the motion of these two can then be treated as a two-body problem,and the effect of the other bodies is considered a small superposed perturbation.The first case corresponds to the motion of the planets in the solar system; the second case to the motion ofa satellite (including the moon and artificial earth satellites) around the planet to which the satellite belongs.We start with the rigorous solution of the two-body problem with two finite masses. The solution of the two-body problem is a motion in a conic section, that is,a Keplerian motion along a plane quadratic curve. It must be admitted that Keplerian motion is often not close enough even to serve as a first approximation to the actual complicated motions observed by modern techniques; so that we usually consider a more elaborate motion, as the first approximation. Nevertheless, here we shall begin by considering Keplerian motion, as it has long been the traditional first approximation in elementary theory.

Suppose that a mass $m _ { 1 }$ is at the coordinate origin. The kinetic energy and the force function for the motion of a mass $m _ { 2 }$ around the mass $m _ { 1 }$ expressed in polar coordinates are

$$
T = { \frac { m } { 2 } } \left[ \left( { \frac { d r } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } + r ^ { 2 } \Bigl ( { \frac { d \phi } { d t } } \Bigr ) ^ { 2 } + r ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \phi \Bigl ( { \frac { d \theta } { d t } } \Bigr ) ^ { 2 } \right] ,
$$

$$
U = \frac { \beta } { r } , \qquad \beta = k ^ { 2 } m , \qquad m = m _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } .
$$

The corresponding momenta are

$$
\frac { \partial T } { \partial \dot { r } } = m \frac { d r } { d t } , \qquad \dot { p } _ { \phi } = \frac { \partial T } { \partial \dot { \phi } } = m r ^ { 2 } \Big ( \frac { d \phi } { d t } \Big ) , \qquad \dot { p } _ { \theta } = \frac { \partial T } { \partial \dot { \theta } } = m r ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \phi \frac { d \theta } { d t } .
$$

Hence, if we write $m \beta$ and $m h _ { 1 }$ respectively for $\beta$ and $h _ { 1 }$ ,the Hamilton-Jacobi partial differential equation becomes

$$
\left( { \frac { \partial W } { \partial r } } \right) ^ { 2 } + { \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } } } \left( { \frac { \partial W } { \partial \phi } } \right) ^ { 2 } + { \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } \phi } } \left( { \frac { \partial W } { \partial \theta } } \right) ^ { 2 } = { \frac { 2 \beta } { r } } + 2 h _ { 1 } .
$$

By separation of variables (Section 2.5) we obtain

$$
r ^ { 2 } \Big [ \Big ( \frac { \partial W } { \partial r } \Big ) ^ { 2 } - \frac { 2 \beta } { r } - 2 h _ { 1 } \Big ] = - \Big ( \frac { \partial W } { \partial \phi } \Big ) ^ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { \cos ^ { 2 } \phi } \Big ( \frac { \partial W } { \partial \theta } \Big ) ^ { 2 } .
$$

As the left-hand member depends only on $r$ while the right-hand member does not depend on $r _ { : }$ ，both members must equal a constant in order to be equal. Let the constant be $- h _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }$ ; then,

$$
\cos ^ { 2 } \phi \Big [ \Big ( \frac { \partial W } { \partial \phi } \Big ) ^ { 2 } - h _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \Big ] = - \Big ( \frac { \partial W } { \partial \theta } \Big ) ^ { 2 } ,
$$

$$
\left( \frac { \partial W } { \partial r } \right) ^ { 2 } = \frac { 2 \beta } { r } + 2 h _ { 1 } - \frac { h _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } .
$$

Similarly,by equating both sides of the equation in $\phi$ and $\theta$ to a constant $- h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 }$ ，we obtain

$$
\left( \frac { \partial W } { \partial \phi } \right) ^ { 2 } = h _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - \frac { h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } { \cos ^ { 2 } \phi } , \qquad \left( \frac { \partial W } { \partial \theta } \right) ^ { 2 } = h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } .
$$

Hence, by the method treated in Section 1.12,we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { d t = \displaystyle \frac { d r } { \sqrt { \displaystyle \frac { 2 \beta } { r } + 2 h _ { 1 } - \frac { h _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } } } } \\ { 0 = h _ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { d \phi } { \sqrt { h _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - \frac { h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } { \cos ^ { 2 } \phi } } } - h _ { 2 } \displaystyle \frac { d r } { r ^ { 2 } \sqrt { \displaystyle \frac { 2 \beta } { r } + 2 h _ { 1 } - \frac { h _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } } } } \\ { 0 = d \theta - h _ { 3 } \displaystyle \frac { d \phi } { \cos ^ { 2 } \phi \displaystyle \sqrt { h _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - \frac { h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } { \cos ^ { 2 } \phi } } } , } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
W = \int _ { r _ { 0 } } ^ { r } { \sqrt { { \frac { 2 \beta } { r } } + 2 h _ { 1 } - { \frac { h _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } } } } d r + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \theta } h _ { 3 } d \theta + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \phi } { \sqrt { h _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - { \frac { h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } { \cos ^ { 2 } \phi } } } } d \phi .
$$

Put

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { r } = \rho , \qquad U ( \rho ) = 2 h _ { 1 } + 2 \beta \rho - h _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \rho ^ { 2 } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle h _ { 3 } = h _ { 2 } \cos I , \qquad \sin \phi = \sin I \sin \psi ; } } \end{array}
$$

then, with the integration constants $\gamma _ { 1 } , \gamma _ { 2 } , \gamma _ { 3 }$ ，equations (84) take the form

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \gamma _ { 1 } + t = - \displaystyle \int \frac { d \rho } { \rho ^ { 2 } \sqrt { U ( \rho ) } } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \gamma _ { 2 } = \psi + h _ { 2 } \displaystyle \int \frac { d \rho } { \sqrt { U ( \rho ) } } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \gamma _ { 3 } = \theta - \tan ^ { - 1 } { \left( { \tan \psi \cos { I } } \right) } . } } \end{array}
$$

From the first and the third equations of (84a),we obtain the law of conservation of angular momentum, or the Keplerian second law

$$
r ^ { 2 } \frac { d \psi } { d t } = h _ { 2 } ,
$$

where $h _ { 2 }$ is twice the areal velocity. The third equation of (84a) gives

$$
\tan \psi \cos I = \tan ( \theta - \gamma _ { 3 } ) .
$$

If we take the ecliptic as our reference plane, and the vernal equinox $\boldsymbol { \Upsilon }$ of an epoch as the standard direction- that is, if we take a certain fixed epoch as reference - then $\theta$ is the longitude, $\phi$ is the latitude,and (85) and (86) give their relations in a right-angled spherical triangle.The motion occurs always on the plane having inclination $I$ to the ecliptic and intersecting it at a point of longitude $\gamma _ { 3 }$ . The variation of $r$ and $\psi$ represents the motion. $\psi$ is called the argument of latitude (Figure l).Hence, the problem is reduced to the discussion of

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \gamma _ { 1 } + t = - \int \frac { d \rho } { \rho ^ { 2 } \sqrt { U ( \rho ) } } , } \\ { \displaystyle \gamma _ { 2 } - \psi = h _ { 2 } \int \frac { d \rho } { \sqrt { U ( \rho ) } } . } \end{array}
$$

![](images/9a79ea1b4bdd3dba1ff25877a45ffb273a31ab45d2459d58800b654dc3393a60.jpg)  
FIGURE I

# Consider

$$
U ( \rho ) = 2 h _ { 1 } + 2 \beta \rho - h _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \rho ^ { 2 } .
$$

If $U ( \rho ) = 0$ has no real root, then $U ( \rho ) > 0$ for a real motion. If $U ( \rho ) = 0$ has real roots $\rho _ { 1 } , \rho _ { 2 } \left( \rho _ { 1 } \right) \geq \rho _ { 2 } )$ ,then $U ( \rho ) = h _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } ( \rho _ { 1 } - \rho ) ( \rho - \rho _ { 2 } )$ and $\rho$ varies between $\pmb { \rho _ { 1 } }$ and $\rho _ { 2 }$ . According as $h _ { 1 } > 0 , h _ { 1 } < 0 , h _ { 1 } = 0$ we have $\rho _ { 1 } \rho _ { 2 } < 0$ ， $\rho _ { 1 } \rho _ { 2 } > 0$ ， $\rho _ { 1 } \rho _ { 2 } = 0$

If $\rho _ { 1 } \rho _ { 2 } < 0$ , then one root is positive and the other negative and the motion extends to infinity, as $\rho = 1 / r \geq 0$ But, by (87),it is an asymptotic motion where $\pmb { \rho }$ approaches zero as $t$ approaches infinity. We have also $t = O ( \rho ^ { - 1 } )$ and $r = O ( t )$ . This is a hyperbolic motion.

If $\rho _ { 1 } \rho _ { 2 } > 0$ , then the two roots are real. The motion occurs in

$$
r _ { 1 } \ \triangleq r \ \leqq \ r _ { 2 } ,
$$

where $r _ { 1 } = 1 / \rho _ { 1 } , r _ { 2 } = 1 / \rho _ { 2 } . r _ { 1 }$ is the perihelion distance and $r _ { 2 }$ is the aphelion distance; this is an elliptic motion ora circular motion.

If $\rho _ { 1 } \rho _ { 2 } = 0$ ， then one root is zero. Let $\rho _ { 2 } = 0$ ； then，by the first equation of (87), this is an asymptotic motion where $\rho$ approaches zero. We have $t = O ( \rho ^ { - 3 / 2 } )$ or $t = O ( r ^ { 2 / 3 } )$ . This is a parabolic motion.

Consider in particular a straight-line motion. Suppose that $h _ { 2 } = 0$ The motion occurs on the straight line $\psi = \gamma _ { 2 }$ .From the first equation of (87) we obtain

$$
\left( { \frac { d r } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } = { \frac { 2 \beta } { r } } + 2 h _ { 1 } .
$$

If $h _ { 1 } > 0$ ，then $\pmb { r }$ must monotonically increase or decrease,as the righthand member does not vanish for $h _ { 1 } > 0$ ； that is, the two bodies either recede to infinity or collide.Let $h _ { 1 } = - f < 0 .$ Let variable $\pmb { u }$ be defined by

$$
\left( { \frac { d u } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } = { \frac { \epsilon } { r } } ;
$$

then

$$
\left( \frac { d r } { d u } \right) ^ { 2 } = \frac { 2 f } { \epsilon } \left( \frac { \beta } { f } - r \right) ,
$$

where $\epsilon$ is a positive constant to be determined later. Let $\boldsymbol { u }$ increase monotonically with $t .$ Put $\epsilon = f ^ { 2 } / ( 2 \beta )$ and integrate (90); then

$$
r = \frac { \beta } { f } ( 1 - u ^ { 2 } ) .
$$

Substitution of (91) in (89) gives

$$
u \sqrt { 1 - u ^ { 2 } } + \sin ^ { - 1 } u = \frac { \sqrt { 2 } f ^ { 3 / 2 } } { \beta } ( t - t _ { 0 } ) .
$$

Equations (91） and (92) are the solution for the straight-line case. $u < 0$ for $t \ < \ t _ { 0 }$ . The variable $\boldsymbol { u }$ increases from zero at first,until $r$ attains its maximum, $\beta / f .$ After this, $r$ decreases and then vanishes；that is,the two bodies collide in a finite time $t = t _ { 0 } + \beta \pi / ( 2 \sqrt { 2 } f ^ { 3 / 2 } )$

Let the instant of the collision be $t = 0$ and let $r = \sqrt [ 3 ] { \beta } w$ Then the equation of motion becomes

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } w } { d t ^ { 2 } } = - \frac { 1 } { w ^ { 2 } } \cdotp
$$

Substitute $w = \gamma t ^ { m }$ in this equation; then $m = \textstyle { \frac { 2 } { 3 } }$ . Hence,

$$
w = \sqrt [ 3 ] { \frac { 9 } { 2 } } t ^ { 2 / 3 } = \tau
$$

is a particular solution.In order to obtain the general solution, put

$$
w = \tau + \alpha _ { 2 } \tau ^ { 2 } + \alpha _ { 3 } \tau ^ { 3 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot
$$

in the equation of motion and determine $\alpha _ { 2 } , \alpha _ { 3 } , \ldots .$ ，This gives the general solution

$$
r = \sqrt [ 3 ] { \bar { \beta } } ( \tau + \alpha _ { 2 } \tau ^ { 2 } - { \textstyle \frac { 3 } { 7 } } \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \tau ^ { 2 } + { \textstyle \frac { 2 } { 6 } } { \textstyle \frac { 3 } { 3 } } \alpha _ { 2 } ^ { 3 } \tau ^ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) .
$$

${ \pmb { \alpha } } _ { 2 }$ is one of the integration constants, such that $h _ { 1 } = 5 \beta ^ { 2 / 3 } \alpha _ { 2 }$ .The other integration constant is the time of collision.This treatment is due to Burrau (1894).

An important fact known in this particular case of collision is that the mutual distance $r$ is expressed in the form of an integral power series of $t ^ { 2 / 3 }$ ， $t$ being counted backward from the instant of collision.According to Sundman (l9l3),a similar situation occurs in the collision of two bodies in the three-body problem，as will be discussed in Chapter 22.The differential equation remains unaltered when we change the sign of $t .$ Hence,if $t$ denotes the time interval elapsed since the instant of collision, the solution can likewise be represented by the same integral power series of $t ^ { 2 / 3 }$ .Mathematically, the solution can be analytically continued after the collision. Sundman shows that this is true for the three-body problem. It is also true for the isosceles-triangular solution treated in Section 3.9.

# 5.10ELLIPTIC MOTIONS

An elliptic motion takes place for $\rho _ { 1 } \rho _ { 2 } > 0$ and accordingly for $h _ { 1 } = - f < 0 .$ By Section 2.4,we have

$$
\frac { d r } { d u } = \sqrt { ( r _ { 2 } - r ) ( r - r _ { 1 } ) } ,
$$

$$
\frac { d u } { d t } = \frac { h _ { 2 } } { r } \sqrt { \rho _ { 1 } \rho _ { 2 } } ,
$$

or, by integrating (95),

$$
\frac { 1 } { \rho } = r = \frac { r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } } { 2 } - \frac { r _ { 2 } - r _ { 1 } } { 2 } \cos u .
$$

On the other hand,from (84b) we obtain

$$
r ^ { 2 } { \frac { d { \psi } } { d t } } = h _ { 2 } = { \frac { 1 } { \rho ^ { 2 } } } { \frac { d \psi } { d t } } \cdot
$$

Solve (98) together with $d \rho / d t = - \rho ^ { 2 } h _ { 2 } \sqrt { \left( \rho _ { 1 } - \rho \right) \left( \rho - \rho _ { 2 } \right) }$ and denote the integration constant by $\pi / 2 + \varpi - \Omega$ ; then

$$
\frac { \pi } { 2 } - \ 4 + \varpi - \ \Omega = \sin ^ { - 1 } \left[ \left( { \rho } - \frac { { \rho } _ { 1 } + { \rho } _ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) \middle / \left( \frac { { \rho } _ { 1 } - { \rho } _ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) \right] .
$$

Put

$$
\cdot \left( \rho _ { 1 } + \rho _ { 2 } \right) = \frac 1 2 \left( \frac 1 { r _ { 1 } } + \frac 1 { r _ { 2 } } \right) = \frac 1 \rho , \qquad r _ { 1 } = a ( 1 - e ) , \qquad r _ { 2 } = a ( 1 + e
$$

then, $a { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } = p$ and the integral is

$$
r = \frac { \phi } { 1 + e \cos { ( \psi - \varpi + \Omega ) } } .
$$

The constant $\Omega$ is $\gamma _ { 3 }$ in the former notation. $\boldsymbol { \varpi }$ is the value of $\psi + \Omega$ for $\boldsymbol { r } = \boldsymbol { r } _ { 1 }$ . Note that the counting of the angles is over two different planes, that is, $\Omega$ is counted on the ecliptic and $\psi$ on the orbital plane. $\eqcirc$ is called the longitude of the perihelion, $\Omega$ the longitude of the ascending node. In the case of asteroids and comets, $\omega$ is often used for $\varpi - \Omega$ Equation (99) is the equation for an ellipse and expresses the first law of Kepler; $e$ is the eccentricity, $^ a$ the semimajor axis,and $\boldsymbol { u }$ (equation 97） the eccentric anomaly.

Solving (97), we obtain

$$
r = a ( 1 - e \cos u ) .
$$

Write (96) as $r d u = \sqrt { \overline { { \rho _ { 1 } \rho _ { 2 } } } } h _ { 2 } d t$ . Substitute this in (100); then,

$$
u \mathrm { ~ - ~ } e \sin u = \frac { \sqrt { \rho _ { 1 } \rho _ { 2 } } h _ { 2 } } { a } ( t - t _ { 0 } ) .
$$

From $h _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } = a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) \beta , h _ { 1 } = - \beta / 2 a$ ，we obtain $\sqrt { \rho _ { 1 } \rho _ { 2 } } h _ { 2 } / a = \sqrt { \overline { { \beta } } } / a ^ { 3 / 2 }$ Also $d t = { \sqrt { a / \mu } } r d u$ ,by (96). The period with respect to $\pmb { u }$ is

$$
T = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } a ( 1 - e \cos u ) d u = \frac { 2 \pi a ^ { 3 / 2 } } { \sqrt { \beta } } \cdot
$$

Hence, if we put $n = 2 \pi / T = \sqrt { \bar { \beta } } / a ^ { 3 / 2 }$ we have $n ^ { 2 } a ^ { 3 } = \beta$ .This is the third law of Kepler. $_ { n }$ is called the mean motion. Thus

$$
u - e \sin u = n ( t - t _ { 0 } ) = M .
$$

This is Kepler's equation. $M$ is the mean anomaly and $t _ { 0 }$ the epoch of the perihelion passage. From (98) we have $r ^ { 2 } d \psi / d t = n a ^ { 2 } \nu ^ { \prime } \overline { { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } }$ .Integrate this equation by writing $\psi = v + \varpi - \Omega$ and substituting (100） for $r$ ； then

$$
\tan { \frac { \upsilon } { 2 } } = \sqrt { \frac { 1 + \ell } { 1 - \ell } } \tan { \frac { \upsilon } { 2 } } \cdotp
$$

$v$ is called the true anomaly. $M , u ,$ and $v$ all equal zero at the perihelion and $\pi$ at the aphelion,and have a common period $T$

$$
r \cos v = a ( \cos u - e ) , \qquad r \sin v = a \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } \sin u .
$$
