$\mathcal { L } = \boldsymbol { \psi } + \boldsymbol { \Omega } = \boldsymbol { v } + \boldsymbol { \varpi }$ is called the longitude in the orbit. It is customary to write

$$
M = n t + \epsilon - \varpi , \qquad \lambda = n t + \epsilon = M + \varpi .
$$

$\lambda$ is the mean longitude or the mean longitude in the orbit, $\epsilon$ the mean longitude at the epoch,that is,for $t = 0$ .These angles are counted over two different planes, just like the angle $\boldsymbol { \varpi }$ ·

For the ecliptic we have rectangular coordinates $x _ { 1 } , \ x _ { 2 } , \ x _ { 3 }$ with the vernal equinox as the $\scriptstyle x _ { 1 }$ direction,and

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { x _ { 1 } = r ( \cos \psi \cos \Omega - \sin \psi \sin \Omega \cos I ) , } \\ & { x _ { 2 } = r ( \cos \psi \sin \Omega + \sin \psi \cos \Omega \cos I ) , } \\ & { x _ { 3 } = r \sin \psi \sin I . } \end{array}
$$

Substitute in (105) these expressions (105a) for $\pmb { r }$ cos $\psi$ and $r \sin \psi$ ：

$$
\begin{array} { r } { r \cos \psi = r \cos v \cos ( \varpi - \Omega ) - r \sin v \sin ( \varpi - \Omega ) , } \\ { r \sin \psi = r \cos v \sin ( \varpi - \Omega ) + r \sin v \cos ( \varpi - \Omega ) ; } \end{array}
$$

but first substitute in (l05a) the expressions for $\boldsymbol { r }$ cos $v$ and $r$ sin $v$ given in (103).We thus obtain

$x _ { 1 } = a ( \cos u - e ) [ \cos ( \varpi - \Omega ) \cos \Omega - \sin ( \varpi - \Omega ) \sin \Omega \cos I ]$ $- \left. a \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } \sin u [ \sin \left( \varpi - \Omega \right) \cos \Omega + \cos \left( \varpi - \Omega \right) \sin \Omega \cos \Omega ] \right\} \cos \Omega ,$ sI], $\begin{array} { r } { x _ { 2 } = a ( \cos u - e ) [ \cos ( \varpi - \Omega ) \sin \Omega + \sin ( \varpi - \Omega ) \cos \Omega \cos } \\ { + a \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } \sin u [ - \sin ( \varpi - \Omega ) \sin \Omega + \cos ( \varpi - \Omega ) \cos \Omega } \end{array}$ $x _ { 3 } = a ( \cos u - e ) \sin ( \varpi - \Omega ) \sin I + a \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } \sin u \cos ( \varpi - \Omega ) \sin$ in I; or, if we let

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { X = r \cos { ( v - M ) } } } \\ { { \ = a ( \cos { u } - e ) \cos { M } + a \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } \sin { u } \sin { M } , } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { Y = r \sin { ( v - M ) } } } \\ { { \ = - a ( \cos { u } - e ) \sin { M } + a \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } \sin { u } \cos { M } . } } \end{array}
$$

we obtain

$$
a ( \cos u - e ) = X \cos M - Y \sin M ,
$$

$$
a { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } \sin u = X \sin M + Y \cos M ;
$$

accordingly,

$x _ { 1 } = X [ \cos { ( M + \varpi - \Omega ) } \cos { \Omega } - \sin { ( M + \varpi - \Omega ) } \sin { \Omega } \cos { \Omega }$ 1 $- \ { \cal Y } [ \sin \left( M + \varpi - \Omega \right) \cos \Omega + \cos \left( M + \varpi - \Omega \right) \sin ^ { 2 } \left( M + \varpi - \Omega \right) \cos \Omega ] .$ n Ω cos I],   
x2= $\mathbf { \Omega } = X [ \cos { ( M + \varpi - \Omega ) } \sin { \Omega } + \sin { ( M + \varpi - \Omega ) } \cos { \Omega } \cos { I } ]$ 1 $+ \ Y [ - \sin { ( M + \varpi - \Omega ) } \sin { \Omega } + \cos { ( M + \varpi - \Omega ) } \cos { \Omega } \cos { \Omega }$ I],   
${ x _ { 3 } } = X \sin \left( { M + \varpi - \Omega } \right) \sin I + Y \cos \left( { M + \varpi - \Omega } \right) \sin I ;$ (107)   
or

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { x _ { 1 } = X \Big [ \cos ^ { 2 } { \frac { I } { 2 } } \cos \lambda + \sin ^ { 2 } { \frac { I } { 2 } } \cos { ( \lambda - 2 \Omega ) } \Big ] } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } ~ - Y \Big [ \cos ^ { 2 } { \frac { I } { 2 } } \sin \lambda + \sin ^ { 2 } { \frac { I } { 2 } } \sin { ( \lambda - 2 \Omega ) } \Big ] , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } ~ x _ { 2 } = X \Big [ \cos ^ { 2 } { \frac { I } { 2 } } \sin \lambda - \sin ^ { 2 } { \frac { I } { 2 } } \sin { ( \lambda - 2 \Omega ) } \Big ] } } \\ { { { } ~ + Y \Big [ \cos ^ { 2 } { \frac { I } { 2 } } \cos \lambda - \sin ^ { 2 } { \frac { I } { 2 } } \cos { ( \lambda - 2 \Omega ) } \Big ] , } } \end{array}
$$

$$
x _ { 3 } = X \sin I \sin ( \lambda - \Omega ) + Y \sin I \cos ( \lambda - \Omega ) .
$$

The constants appearing in (84) are related to the Keplerian constants $a , e , I , \Omega , \varpi , t _ { 0 }$ by

$$
h _ { 1 } = \mathrm { ~ - ~ } \frac { \beta } { 2 a } , \qquad h _ { 2 } = \sqrt { \beta a ( 1 - \mathrm { ~ } e ^ { 2 } ) } , \qquad h _ { 3 } = \sqrt { \beta a ( 1 - \mathrm { ~ } e ^ { 2 } ) } \cos { I } ,
$$

$$
\gamma _ { 1 } = - t _ { 0 } , \qquad \gamma _ { 2 } = \varpi - \Omega , \qquad \gamma _ { 3 } = \Omega .
$$

The constants $h _ { 1 } , h _ { 2 } , h _ { 3 } , \gamma _ { 1 } , \gamma _ { 2 } , \gamma _ { 3 }$ are determined by the method of Hamilton-Jacobi and are called the canonical constants. $h$ and $\gamma$ are canonically conjugate.

# 5.11CAUCHY's FIRST THEOREM

Suppose that $s$ can be expanded in a Laurent series for all values of $_ z$ in the ring domain,excluding O and $\infty$ , of a complex variable $z$ and for all values of $e$ in a domain enclosing $\epsilon = 0$ ：

$$
S = \sum _ { j \mathrm { ~ = ~ - ~ } \infty } ^ { + \infty } P _ { j } z ^ { j } .
$$

Then, by Cauchy's theorem on residues in the theory ofanalytic functions, we have

$$
P _ { j } = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } } \int ^ { ( 0 + ) } z ^ { - j - 1 } { \cal S } d z \qquad ( j \not \equiv 0 )
$$

for a closed integration contour surrounding the point $o$ in the positive sense.Take the variable $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ such that

$$
z = s \exp \left[ - \frac { e } { 2 } \left( s - s ^ { - 1 } \right) \right] .
$$

The right-hand member is convergent for all values in the domain enclosing $\epsilon = 0$ ; indeed, it can be developed in a uniformly convergent Laurent series in the ring domain of $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ , excluding $s = 0$ and $s = \infty$ .Then,

$$
d z = \left[ 1 - \frac { e } { 2 } ( s + s ^ { - 1 } ) \right] \exp \left[ - \frac { e } { 2 } ( s - s ^ { - 1 } ) \right] d s .
$$

The derivative $d z / d s$ does not become infinite except for $s = 0$ or $\infty$ Hence,

$$
P _ { j } = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } } \int ^ { ( 0 + ) } { s ^ { - j - 1 } U d s } ,
$$

where

$$
U = S \bigg [ 1 - { \frac { e } { 2 } } ( s + s ^ { - 1 } ) \bigg ] \exp \bigg [ { \frac { j e } { 2 } } ( s - s ^ { - 1 } ) \bigg ] .
$$

The integration contour for $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ is obtained by the transformation (112) of

the corresponding integration contour for $z ,$ But, as there is no singularity, the contour for $s$ can be deformed to the unit circle around $o$ .Thus,

$$
U = \sum _ { j = - \infty } ^ { \infty } P _ { j } s ^ { j } .
$$

Since

$$
P _ { j } = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } } \int ^ { ( 0 + ) } \frac { 1 } { j } z ^ { - j } \frac { d S } { d z } d z ,
$$

which, by (112),

$$
= \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } j } \int ^ { ( 0 + ) } s ^ { - j } \exp \left[ \frac { j e } { 2 } ( s - s ^ { - 1 } ) \right] \frac { d S } { d s } d s ,
$$

therefore,

$$
P _ { j } = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } } \int ^ { ( 0 + ) } s ^ { - j } V d s ,
$$

where

$$
V = \frac { 1 } { j } \frac { d S } { d s } \exp \left[ \frac { j e } { 2 } \left( s - s ^ { - 1 } \right) \right] .
$$

The result (114) and (115) is called Cauchy's first theorem.In other words, take the unit circles around $O$ as the integration contour both for $_ z$ and for $s _ { : }$ and put

$$
z = \exp ( \sqrt { - 1 } \ M ) , \qquad s = \exp ( \sqrt { - 1 } \ u ) ;
$$

then a coefficient of the Fourier series expansion of $s$ in the mean anomaly $M$ equals the corresponding coefficient of the Fourier series expansion of the function $U$ defined by (1l4a） in the eccentric anomaly $\boldsymbol { u }$ and equals the corresponding coefficient of the Fourier series expansion of $V s$ defined by (115a) in $\boldsymbol { u }$

As an example, take

$$
S = { \frac { a } { r } } = ( 1 - \epsilon \cos u ) ^ { - 1 } = \left[ 1 - { \frac { e } { 2 } } ( s + s ^ { - 1 } ) \right] ^ { - 1 } .
$$

Because of (114a), we have

$$
U = \exp \left[ { \frac { j e } { 2 } } \left( s - s ^ { - 1 } \right) \right]
$$

and

$$
P _ { j } = P _ { - j } = J _ { j } ( j e )
$$

with the Bessel function. Therefore

$$
\frac { a } { r } = 1 + 2 \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { \infty } J _ { j } ( j e ) \cos j M .
$$

Note that the singularities

$$
s _ { 1 } = \frac { e } { 1 - \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } \equiv \frac { 1 } { \omega } > 1
$$

and

$$
s _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 - \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } { e } \equiv \omega < 1 ,
$$

obtained from

$$
1 - \frac { e } { 2 } ( s + s ^ { - 1 } ) = 0 ,
$$

occur in the transition from the complex $s$ plane to the complex $z$ plane by the condition $d s / d z = \infty$ .The corresponding points in the $_ z$ plane are $z _ { 1 } = ( 1 / \omega ) \exp { ( - \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } ) }$ and zz = ω exp $( \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } )$ ， $z _ { 1 } > 1 > z _ { 2 } ,$ where $d z / d s = 0$ The expansions of $z \mathrm { ~ - ~ } z _ { 1 }$ and $z \mathrm { ~ - ~ } z _ { 2 }$ in the neighborhood of these points begin with the terms $( s - s _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 }$ and $( s \mathrm { ~ - ~ } s _ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 }$ ， respectively. On the other hand, the expansions of $s \mathrm { ~ - ~ } s _ { 1 }$ and $s \mathrm { ~ - ~ } s _ { 2 }$ are in integral power series of $\sqrt { z - z _ { 1 } }$ and $\sqrt { z - z _ { 2 } }$ , respectively.

Let $j , \ q$ be positive integers or zero and $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ be a positive or negative integer or zero,and consider

$$
\Big ( x + \frac { 1 } { x } \Big ) ^ { j } \Big ( x - \frac { 1 } { x } \Big ) ^ { q } = \sum _ { p = - \infty } ^ { \infty } N _ { - p , j , q } x ^ { p } .
$$

$N _ { - p , j , q }$ is called a Cauchy number, and we have

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle N _ { - p , j , q } = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } } \int ^ { ( 0 + ) } x ^ { - p - 1 } \biggl ( x + \frac { 1 } { x } \biggr ) ^ { j } \biggl ( x - \frac { 1 } { x } \biggr ) ^ { q } d x } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } } \int ^ { ( 0 + ) } ( - 1 ) ^ { q } x ^ { - p - j - q - 1 } ( 1 + x ^ { 2 } ) ^ { j } ( 1 - x ^ { 2 } ) ^ { q } d x } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } } \int ^ { ( 0 + ) } x ^ { - p + j + q - 1 } \biggl ( 1 + \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } \biggr ) ^ { j } \biggl ( 1 - \frac { 1 } { x ^ { 2 } } \biggr ) ^ { q } d x . } } \end{array}
$$

Insert cross cuts between $\infty$ and $^ { - 1 }$ , between O and $+ 1$ ， between $+ i$ and $+ i \infty$ ，and between $- i$ and $- i \infty$ , and let the integration contour surround the cross cut between O and $+ 1$ . It is easily seen that $N _ { - p , j , q } = 1$ for $- \phi + j + q = 0 , N _ { - p , j , q } = ( - 1 ) ^ { q }$ for $- \phi - j - q = 0 .$ $N _ { - p , j , q } = 0$ for $- \phi + j + q < 0$ or $- p - j - q > 0$ and $N _ { - p , j , q } = 0$ for an odd $- \phi + j + q > 0$ and for an odd $- p - j - q < 0$

By formal substitution $x = 1 / x ^ { \prime }$ in (117) we obtain

$$
N _ { - p , j , - q } = ( - 1 ) ^ { q } N _ { p , j , q } .
$$

From

$$
\begin{array} { l } { x ^ { - p - ( j + 1 ) - q - 1 } ( 1 + x ^ { 2 } ) ^ { j + 1 } \qquad } \\ { \qquad \equiv x ^ { - p + 1 - j - q - 1 } ( 1 + x ^ { 2 } ) ^ { j } + x ^ { - p - 1 - j - q - 1 } ( 1 + x ^ { 2 } ) ^ { j } , } \end{array}
$$

we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r } { N _ { - p , j + 1 , q } = N _ { - p + 1 , j , q } + N _ { - p - 1 , j , q } , } \\ { N _ { - p , j , q + 1 } = N _ { - p + 1 , j , q } - N _ { - p - 1 , j , q } . } \end{array}
$$

In particular, we can compute $N _ { p , 1 , q }$ when we know $N _ { p , \circ , q }$ ，because $N _ { - p , 1 , q } = N _ { - p + 1 , 0 , q } + N _ { - p - 1 , 0 , q }$ and we can compute $N _ { - p , j , q }$ successively by the above recurrence formula. The numerical values were given by Bourget (1863).

By formal substitution $x ^ { 2 } = y$ in (117), we obtain

$$
N _ { - p , 0 , q } = \frac { ( - 1 ) ^ { q } } { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } } \int ^ { ( 0 + ) } y ^ { - ( p + q ) / 2 - 1 } ( 1 - y ) ^ { q } d y .
$$

Since $q > 0 , y = 1$ is an ordinary point, but $y = 0$ is a singular point. Consider an integration contour that surrounds $y = 0$ in the positive sense,extends from $y = 0$ to $y = + 1$ along the real axis, surrounds $y = 1$ in the positive sense and returns to $y = 0$ along the real axis. If the integral taken from $y = 0$ to $y = 1$ be l,then the integral taken from $y = 1$ to $y = 0$ is equal to

$$
\exp \left[ - 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \left( - \frac { \not p + \not q } { 2 } - 1 \right) \right] ,
$$

because the contour surrounds $y = 0$ once in the positive sense (see Figure 2, Section 5.12). Hence, using Gausss beta function $B ( \xi , \eta )$ we obtain

$$
N _ { - p , 0 , q } = ( - 1 ) ^ { q } \frac { 1 - \exp \left\{ - 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \left[ ( p + q ) / 2 \right] \right\} } { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } }
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \quad \times \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } y ^ { - ( p + q ) / 2 - 1 } ( 1 - y ) ^ { q } d y } \\ & { = ( - 1 ) ^ { q } ( - 1 ) ^ { - ( p + q ) / 2 } \frac { \sin { \left[ \pi \left( \rho + q \right) / 2 \right] } } { \pi } B \Bigl ( - \frac { \dot { p } + q } { 2 } , q + 1 \Bigr ) } \\ & { = ( - 1 ) ^ { ( q - p ) / 2 } \frac { 1 } { \Gamma \left[ - \left( \dot { p } + q \right) / 2 \right] \Gamma \left[ 1 + \left( \dot { p } + q \right) / 2 \right] } } \\ & { \quad \times \frac { \Gamma \left[ - \left( \dot { p } + q \right) / 2 \right] \Gamma \left( q + 1 \right) } { \Gamma \left[ - \left( \dot { p } - q \right) / 2 + 1 \right] } } \\ & { = ( - 1 ) ^ { ( q - p ) / 2 } \frac { q ! } { \left[ \left( q + \dot { p } \right) / 2 \right] ! \left[ \left( q - \dot { p } \right) / 2 \right] ! } . } \end{array}
$$

Similarly, we can prove that

$$
N _ { - p , j , 0 } = { \frac { j ! } { [ ( j + p ) / 2 ] ! [ ( j - p ) / 2 ] ! } } \cdot
$$

Substitute $x = \exp \left( { \sqrt { - 1 } \ \theta } \right)$ in

$$
N _ { 0 , j , q } = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } } \int ^ { ( 0 + ) } x ^ { - 1 } \biggl ( x + \frac { 1 } { x } \biggr ) ^ { j } \biggl ( x - \frac { 1 } { x } \biggr ) ^ { q } d x ;
$$

then

$$
N _ { 0 , j , q } = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \cos ^ { j } \theta \sin ^ { q } \theta d \theta \cdot z ^ { j + q } ( - 1 ) ^ { q / 2 } .
$$

Divide the integration path into the path from O to $\pi$ and the path from $\pi$ to $2 \pi$ , and write $\pi + \theta ^ { \prime }$ instead of $\theta$ in the second integral. Then $j + q$ must be even.By dividing the integration path from O to $\pi$ into the paths from O to $\pi / 2$ and $\pi / 2$ to $\pmb { \pi }$ ，and writing $\pi / 2 + \theta ^ { \prime }$ instead of $\theta$ in the second integral, we see that $q$ must also be even. Hence,

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } & { N _ { 0 , j , q } = { \frac { ( - 1 ) ^ { q / 2 } 2 ^ { j + q } } { \pi } } 2 { \displaystyle \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 2 } } \cos ^ { j } \theta \sin ^ { q } \theta d \theta } \\ & { \qquad = { \frac { ( - 1 ) ^ { q / 2 } 2 ^ { j + q } } { \pi } } { \frac { \Gamma [ ( j + 1 ) / 2 ] \Gamma [ ( q + 1 ) / 2 ] } { \Gamma [ ( j + q ) / 2 + 1 ] } } } \\ & { \qquad = ( - 1 ) ^ { q / 2 } { \frac { 1 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 \cdot \cdot ( j - 1 ) \cdot 1 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 \cdot \cdot ( q - 1 ) } { [ ( j + q ) / 2 ] ! } } \cdot } \end{array} }
$$

This is a new formula due to Hagihara (1935).

For an application of Cauchy's numbers, consider

$$
\frac { a ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } + \sum _ { j } c _ { j } \cos j M .
$$

We see that

$$
c _ { j } = 2 \sum _ { k } \sum _ { q } \frac { j ^ { q } } { q ! } \left( \frac { e } { 2 } \right) ^ { k + q } N _ { - j , k , q } .
$$

Further,by applying this expansion to

$$
\frac { d v } { d t } = \frac { a ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } ,
$$

we obtain

$$
v - M = \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } \sum _ { j } c _ { j } { \frac { \sin j M } { j } } \cdotp
$$

This expression is called the equation of center. Thus the expansions in the Keplerian motion can be obtained simply by the use of Cauchy's first theorem and Cauchy's numbers.

Note that a function may have different mean values taken with respect to the mean anomaly $M _ { : }$ ，the eccentric anomaly $\pmb { u } _ { : }$ ，or the true anomaly $v$ ， respectively，although the three anomalies have equal periods.Kozai (l959,l962) has given the formula (see also Tisserand, 1889) :

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \cos { j \upsilon } d M = ( - e ) ^ { j } [ 1 + \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } ] ^ { - j } [ 1 + j \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } ] } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { = ( - \omega ) ^ { j } [ 1 + j \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } ] , } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\omega = { \frac { 1 - { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } } { e } } = { \frac { e } { 1 + { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } } } \cdotp
$$

Kozai has also given the formula

$$
\gamma = \alpha + 2 \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { \infty } a _ { j } \sin j \alpha ,
$$

with

$$
j a _ { j } = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \cos j \alpha ~ d \gamma \qquad ( j \neq 0 )
$$

where $\propto$ and $\gamma$ are any two of the three kinds of anomaly.

If $\alpha = u$ and $\gamma = M ,$ ，then, $M = u - e \sin u ;$

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \cos j u d M = - \frac { e } { 2 } ~ } } & { { ~ ( j = 1 ) , } } \\ { { { } } } & { { { } } } \\ { { { } = 0 ~ } } & { { ~ ( j > 1 ) . } } \end{array}
$$

If $\alpha = M$ and $\gamma = v$ ,then the coefficient of sin jM in the equation of center can be expanded in a power series of $^ { e }$ (Tisserand,1889).

If $\alpha = M$ and $\gamma = u$ ,then,

$$
u = M + 2 \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { \infty } \frac { 1 } { j } J ( j e ) \sin j M ,
$$

and the mean value is

$$
\frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \cos j M d v = J _ { j } ( j e ) .
$$

If $\alpha = u$ and $\gamma = v$ ,then

$$
v = u + 2 \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { \infty } \frac { 1 } { j } \omega ^ { j } \sin j u ,
$$

and

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \cos j u d v = \omega ^ { j } .
$$

If $\alpha = v$ and $\gamma = u$ ,then

$$
u = v + 2 \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { \infty } { \frac { 1 } { j } } \left( - \omega \right) ^ { j } \sin { j v } ,
$$

and

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \cos j v \ d u = ( - \omega ) ^ { j } .
$$

Numerical tables of the coefficients in the expansions for elliptic motions have been published by Cayley (l86l) and recently by Jarnagin (1965).

# 5.12CAUCHY's SECOND THEOREM

Put

$$
t = \exp { ( \sqrt { - 1 } \ v ) } , \qquad s = \exp { ( \sqrt { - 1 } \ u ) } , \qquad z = \exp { ( \sqrt { - 1 } \ M ) }
$$

and

$$
\omega = \frac { e } { 1 + \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } = \frac { 1 - \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } { e } , \qquad \eta = \frac { 1 + \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 1 + e ^ { 2 } } ,
$$

$$
\omega \eta = \frac { e } { 2 } ;
$$

then

$$
s = \frac { t + \omega } { 1 + \omega t } , \qquad t = \frac { s - \omega } { 1 - \omega s } \cdot
$$

This is a linear fractional transformation in the theory of modular functions. The point $s = 0$ is carried to $t = \infty$ ,and $s = \infty$ to $t = - 1 / \infty _ { \mathrm { ; } }$ by this transformation. The invariant points $t = \pm 1$ are defined by

$$
t = { \frac { t + \omega } { 1 + \omega t } } \cdot
$$

We have

$$
\frac { d t } { d s } = \frac { t } { s } \frac { 1 - \omega ^ { 2 } } { ( 1 - \omega s ) ( 1 - \omega s ^ { - 1 } ) } = \frac { ( 1 + \omega t ) ^ { 2 } } { 1 - \omega ^ { 2 } } = \frac { 1 - \omega s + \omega ( s - \omega ) } { ( 1 - \omega s ) ^ { 2 } } ,
$$

$$
z = t { \biggl ( } 1 + { \frac { \omega } { t } } { \biggr ) } ( 1 + \omega t ) ^ { - 1 } \exp { \biggl [ } { - } { \frac { 1 - \omega ^ { 2 } } { 1 + \omega ^ { 2 } } } { \biggl ( } { \frac { \omega t } { 1 + \omega t } } - { \frac { \omega t ^ { - 1 } } { 1 + \omega t ^ { - 1 } } } { \biggr ) } { \biggr ] } ,
$$

$$
\frac { r } { a } = \frac { 1 } { 1 + \omega ^ { 2 } } ( 1 - \omega s ) ( 1 - \omega s ^ { - 1 } ) = \frac { ( 1 - \omega ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } { 1 + \omega ^ { 2 } } \frac { 1 } { ( 1 + \omega t ) ( 1 + \omega t ^ { - 1 } ) }
$$

$$
\frac { d s } { d t } = \frac { 1 + \omega ^ { 2 } s } { 1 - \omega ^ { 2 } } \frac { r } { t } \frac { r } { a } ,
$$

$$
{ \frac { d z } { d t } } = { \frac { 1 + \omega ^ { 2 } } { 1 - \omega ^ { 2 } } } { \frac { z } { t } } \left( { \frac { r } { a } } \right) ^ { 2 } .
$$

The variable $_ z$ ,regarded as a function of $\mathfrak { s }$ ,has singularities at $s = 0$ and $\infty$ , and,as a function of $t _ { : }$ ,has a simple pole at $t = \infty$ and an essential singularity at $t = \omega ^ { - 1 }$ . Its reciprocal $1 / z _ { ; }$ ，regarded as a function of $s ,$ has a simple pole at $t = 0$ and an essential singularity at $t = - \omega$ . The radius vector $r _ { \mathrm { \Omega } }$ ,regarded as a function of $s$ ,has no singularity, but, as a function of $t _ { : }$ ,has simple poles at $t = - 1 / \omega$ and $- \boldsymbol { \omega }$ . The reciprocal $1 / r ,$ regarded as a function of $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ ,has simple poles at $s = \omega$ and $s = 1 / \omega$ ,but, as a function of $t$ ，has no singularity.Hence, in the transition from the $_ z$ plane to the $t$ plane there are essential singularities at $t = - \omega$ and $t = - 1 / \omega$ ，so that we construct in the $t$ plane two cross cuts extending from $- \infty$ to $- 1 / \omega$ and from $- \boldsymbol { \omega }$ to O.The second cross cut is contained in the unit circle with $O$ as the center.Hence,as in Cauchy's first theorem,the coefficient $P _ { j }$ in the expansion

$$
S = \sum _ { j = - \infty } ^ { \infty } P _ { j } z ^ { j }
$$

equals the coefficient of $t ^ { j }$ in the expansion of

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } \left( \frac { r } { a } \right) ^ { 2 } \left( \frac { t } { z } \right) ^ { j } S = \frac { ( 1 - \omega ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 3 } } { 1 + \omega ^ { 2 } } ( 1 + \omega t ) ^ { j - 2 } ( 1 + \omega t ^ { - 1 } ) ^ { - j - 2 } } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \mathrm { } \times \exp \left[ - j \frac { 1 - \omega ^ { 2 } } { 1 + \omega ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { \omega t } { 1 + \omega t } - \frac { \omega t ^ { - 1 } } { 1 + \omega t ^ { - 1 } } \right) \right] S } } \end{array}
$$

It also equals the coefficient of $t ^ { j }$ in the expansion of

$$
\frac { t } { j } \ : ( 1 + \omega t ) ^ { j } ( 1 + \omega t ^ { - 1 } ) ^ { - j } \exp \left[ j \frac { 1 - \omega ^ { 2 } } { 1 + \omega ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { \omega t } { 1 + \omega t } - \frac { \omega t ^ { - 1 } } { 1 + \omega t ^ { - 1 } } \right) \right] \frac { d S } { d t } .
$$

This is called Cauchy's second theorem.

In general, an expression of the form

$$
f = { \biggl ( } { \frac { r } { a } } { \biggr ) } ^ { \rho } s ^ { \beta } t ^ { \gamma } = { \biggl ( } { \frac { r } { a } } { \biggr ) } ^ { \rho } [ \cos { ( \beta u + \gamma v ) } + { \sqrt { - 1 } } \sin { ( \beta u + \gamma v ) } ]
$$

can be written

$$
f = \sum _ { n } a _ { n } z ^ { n } = \sum _ { n } b _ { n } s ^ { n } = \sum _ { n } c _ { n } t ^ { n } ,
$$

where $\alpha , \beta , \gamma$ are arbitrary， with the proviso that $\beta + \gamma$ must be an integer so that $f$ will admit the period $2 \pi$ with respect to one of the anomalies $M , u , v .$ 、It can be seen (Andoyer,1923) that

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { a _ { n } = \displaystyle \sum _ { j } b _ { j } J _ { n - 1 } ( n e ) \mathrm { , } } } \\ { { \displaystyle b _ { n } = \eta ^ { o } K _ { n - \beta - \gamma } ^ { \rho - \gamma , \rho + \gamma } = ( - 1 ) ^ { n - \beta - \gamma } \frac { \left( \eta ^ { \prime } \right) ^ { - \rho - 1 } } { \theta } K _ { n - \beta - \gamma } ^ { - \rho - \beta + n - 1 , - \rho + \beta - n - 1 } \mathrm { , } } } \\ { { \displaystyle c _ { n } = ( - 1 ) ^ { n - \beta - \gamma } ( \eta ^ { \prime } ) ^ { - o } K _ { n - \beta - \gamma } ^ { - \rho - \beta - \gamma - \rho + \beta } = \theta \eta ^ { \rho - 1 } K _ { n - \beta - \gamma } ^ { \rho - \gamma + n - 1 , \rho + \gamma - n - 1 } \mathrm { , } } } \end{array}
$$

with

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { C _ { p } ^ { m } = { \displaystyle { \frac { p ( \rlap / p - 1 ) ( \rlap / p - 2 ) \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot ( \rlap / p - m + 1 ) } { 1 \cdot 2 \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot m } } } , \qquad ( m > 0 ) , \qquad C _ { p } ^ { 0 } = 1 , } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { C _ { p } ^ { m } = ( - 1 ) ^ { n } \displaystyle { \sum _ { m ^ { \prime } , m ^ { \prime } } ^ { } } \omega ^ { m ^ { \prime } + m ^ { \prime } C _ { p } ^ { m ^ { \prime } } C _ { q } ^ { m ^ { \prime } } } } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { \ = ( - 1 ) ^ { n } ( 1 - \omega ^ { 2 } ) ^ { p + q + 1 } K _ { n } ^ { n - p - 1 , - n - q - 1 } , } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \epsilon = \sin \phi , \theta = \cos \phi = \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } , \theta = \cos ^ { 2 } \phi , \theta = \frac { \theta } { \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } , } } \\ { { \omega = \tan \left( \phi / 2 \right) = e / ( 1 + \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } ) , \theta = \frac { \theta } { \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } , \theta = \frac { 1 + \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } { 2 ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } , \theta = \frac { \theta } { \cos ^ { 2 } \phi } , \theta = \frac { \theta } { \sin ^ { 2 } \phi } , \theta = \frac { \theta } { \theta } , \theta = \frac { \theta } { \theta } , \theta = \frac { \pi } { \theta } , \theta = \frac { \pi } { \theta } , \theta = \frac { \theta } { \theta } , \theta = \frac { \pi } { \theta } , \theta = \frac { \theta } { \theta } , \theta = \frac { \pi } { \theta } , } } \\ { { \eta = \cos ^ { 2 } \left( \phi / 2 \right) = \frac { 1 + \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } { 2 ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } , \theta = \frac { \cos ^ { 2 } \left( \phi / 2 \right) } { \cos ^ { 2 } \phi } = \frac { 1 + \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } { 2 ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } , } } \end{array}
$$

the last summation is to be extended to all nonnegative integers $m ^ { \prime }$ ， $m ^ { \prime \prime }$ such that $m ^ { \prime } - m ^ { \prime \prime } = n$ If we put

$$
\left[ 1 - \frac { e } { 2 } \left( s + s ^ { - 1 } \right) \right] ^ { p } = \sum _ { n } H _ { n } ^ { p } s ^ { n } ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { b _ { n } = H _ { n } ^ { p } , \qquad c _ { n } = ( - 1 ) ^ { n } \theta ^ { 2 p } H _ { n } ^ { - p } , \qquad H _ { n } ^ { p } = \eta K _ { n } ^ { p } , } } \\ { { \nonumber } } \\ { { H _ { n } ^ { p } = H _ { - n } ^ { p } = ( - 1 ) ^ { n } \displaystyle \sum _ { m } \left( \frac { e } { 2 } \right) ^ { n + 2 m } C _ { p } ^ { n + 2 m } C _ { n + 2 m } ^ { m } . } } \end{array}
$$

Put $( \lambda , k ) = \Gamma ( \lambda + k ) / \Gamma ( \lambda )$ .The series

$$
F ( \alpha , \beta , \gamma , x ) = \sum _ { j = 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { ( \alpha , j ) ( \beta , j ) } { ( \gamma , j ) ( 1 , j ) } x ^ { j }
$$

is absolutely and uniformly convergent not only within the circle $| x | < 1$ ， but also on the circumference of the circle $| { \boldsymbol x } | = 1$ if the real part

$$
\Re ( \alpha + \beta - \gamma )
$$

is less than zero,and defines a function of $_ x$ provided that we exclude negative integral values of $\gamma$ If $\pmb { \alpha }$ or $\beta$ is a negative integer, then there are only a finite number of terms in the series (l21).The series $F ( \alpha , \beta , \gamma , x )$ is called Gauss's hypergeometric series.A general hypergeometric series is of the form $x ^ { \kappa } ( 1 - x ) ^ { \lambda } F ( \alpha , \beta , \gamma , x )$ , with real numbers $\kappa$ and $\lambda$ ,and satisfies Gauss's differential equation

$$
x ( 1 - x ) \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d x ^ { 2 } } + \left[ \gamma - ( \alpha + \beta + 1 ) x \right] \frac { d y } { d x } - \alpha \beta \gamma y = 0 .
$$

The solution of this differential equation is represented by linear combinations of the four functions listed below:

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { y _ { 1 } = F ( \alpha , \beta , \gamma , x ) = F ( \beta , \alpha , \gamma , x ) , } \\ & { y _ { 2 } = x ^ { 1 - \gamma } F ( \alpha - \gamma + 1 , \beta - \gamma + 1 , 2 - \gamma , x ) , } \\ & { y _ { 3 } = ( 1 - x ) ^ { \gamma - \alpha - \beta } F ( \gamma - \beta , \gamma - \alpha , \gamma , x ) , } \\ & { y _ { 4 } = x ^ { 1 - \gamma } ( 1 - x ) ^ { \gamma - \alpha - \beta } F ( 1 - \beta , 1 - \alpha , 2 - \gamma , x ) . } \end{array}
$$

There are 24 such contiguous functions including these four. Kummer divided them into groups of four functions according to their domain and saw that in any domain one of the corresponding functions is expressed

![](images/7a8ca78e4fb806e81d18e42c735f18ef5c792fc092693d07fa5d73f8ebf177ec.jpg)  
FIGURE 2

linearly by the rest of the four functions. For example,in the domain $| x | < 1$ we have $y _ { 1 } = y _ { 3 } , y _ { 2 } = y _ { 4 }$

Gauss made an integral representation of the solution of the differential equation (122) in the form (Schlesinger,1922; Klein,1933; Poole,1936; Goursat,1936;Bailey,1936)

$$
f ( x ) = { \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi { \sqrt { - 1 } } } } \int _ { c } w ^ { { \beta } - 1 } ( 1 - w ) ^ { \gamma - { \beta } - 1 } ( 1 - x w ) ^ { - { \alpha } } d w ,
$$

where the integration contour $C$ is the one taken twice around $x = 1$ in the positive and negative senses；that is, the integration contour is such that

$$
\Phi ( w ) = w ^ { \beta } ( 1 - w ) ^ { \gamma - \beta } ( 1 - x w ) ^ { - \alpha - 1 }
$$

recovers its initial value when we follow along the contour $C$ (Figure 2). Put $\phi ( w ) = w ^ { \beta - 1 } ( 1 - w ) ^ { \gamma - \beta - 1 } ( 1 - x w ) ^ { - \alpha }$ ,and assume that $\Re ( \beta ) > 0$ ， $\Re ( \gamma - \beta ) > 0$ ; then,

$$
f ( x ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } } \left\{ \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } + e ^ { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } ( \gamma - \beta ) } \int _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } + e ^ { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \gamma } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \ d \right.
$$

$$
+ ~ e ^ { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \beta } \int _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } \biggr \} \phi ( w ) ~ d w .
$$

We carry out the integration by noticing that $\Gamma ( z ) \Gamma ( 1 - z ) = \pi / \sin \pi z$ ； then we obtain

$$
\int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } w ^ { \beta - 1 } ( 1 - w ) ^ { \gamma - \beta - 1 } ( 1 - x w ) ^ { - \alpha } d w = B ( \beta , \gamma - \beta ) F ( \alpha , \beta , \gamma , x ) ,
$$

where the integration contour for $\begin{array} { r } { B ( \phi , q ) = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } w ^ { p - 1 } ( 1 - w ) ^ { q - 1 } } \end{array}$ dw is $C _ { i }$ just defined,and $B ( \boldsymbol { p } , \boldsymbol { q } ) = \Gamma ( \boldsymbol { p } ) \Gamma ( \boldsymbol { q } ) / \Gamma ( \boldsymbol { p } + \boldsymbol { q } )$ (Nielsen,1906；Whittaker and Watson,l937). The expression (l25） is an analytic function of $\beta$ 、Hence, we can drop the restriction $\Re ( \beta ) > 0$ ,by the principle of analytic continuation.

The function $f ( { \pmb x } )$ defined by (l24) remains unaltered if we make one of the transformations

$$
\begin{array} { l } { w = 1 - w _ { 1 } , } \\ { w = \displaystyle \frac { w _ { 1 } } { 1 - x + w _ { 1 } x } , } \\ { w = \displaystyle \frac { 1 - w _ { 1 } } { 1 - w _ { 1 } x } . } \end{array}
$$

Thus, we obtain Euler's transformation of hypergeometric functions

$$
\begin{array} { l } { F ( \alpha , \beta , \gamma , x ) = ( 1 - x ) ^ { - \alpha } F \Big ( \alpha , \gamma - \beta , \gamma , \displaystyle \frac { x } { x - 1 } \Big ) } \\ { \qquad = ( 1 - x ) ^ { - \beta } F \Big ( \gamma - \alpha , \beta , \gamma , \displaystyle \frac { x } { x - 1 } \Big ) } \\ { \qquad = ( 1 - x ) ^ { \gamma - \alpha - \beta } F ( \gamma - \alpha , \gamma - \beta , \gamma , x ) . } \end{array}
$$

After this intermediary of hypergeometric functions we apply Cauchy's second theorem. The coefficient $X _ { j } ^ { n m }$ of the expansion of

$$
\left( { \frac { r } { a } } \right) ^ { n } \exp \left( { \sqrt { - 1 } } \ m v \right) = \sum _ { j = - \infty } ^ { \infty } X _ { j } ^ { n m } z ^ { j }
$$

is called Hansen’s coeffcent. It is given by

$$
X _ { j } ^ { n m } = { \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi { \sqrt { - 1 } } } } \int _ { c } ^ { ( 0 + ) } { \Big ( } { \frac { r } { a } } { \Big ) } ^ { n } t ^ { m } z ^ { - j - 1 } d z .
$$

Writing $S = ( r / a ) ^ { n } t ^ { m }$ in Cauchy's second theorem, we obtain

$$
X _ { j } ^ { n m } = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } } \int _ { c } ^ { ( 0 + ) } \frac { s ^ { m } } { ( 1 + \omega ^ { 2 } ) ^ { n + 1 } } \left( 1 - \omega s \right) ^ { n - m + 1 } \biggr ( 1 - \frac { \omega } { s } \biggr ) ^ { n + m + 1 }
$$

$$
\times \exp \left[ \frac { j \omega } { 1 + \omega ^ { 2 } } \left( s - \frac { 1 } { s } \right) \right] s ^ { - j - 1 } d s ,
$$

$$
X _ { j } ^ { n m } = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } } \int _ { c } ^ { ( 0 + ) } \frac { ( 1 - \omega ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 n + 3 } } { ( 1 + \omega ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 n + 1 } } t ^ { m } ( 1 + \omega t ) ^ { - n + j - 2 } \Big ( 1 + \frac { \omega } { t } \Big ) ^ { - n + j } 
$$

$$
\times \exp \left[ j \frac { 1 - \omega ^ { 2 } } { 1 + \omega ^ { 2 } } \left( \frac { \omega t } { 1 + \omega t } - \frac { \omega t ^ { - 1 } } { 1 + \omega t ^ { - 1 } } \right) \right] t ^ { - j - 1 } d t ,
$$

where the integration contour is the same as was presented in the proof of Cauchy's second theorem.

Put $j = 0$ ， $s = \omega \sigma$ in (127); then,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { X _ { 0 } ^ { n m } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } } \int _ { c } ^ { ( 0 + ) } \frac { ( - 1 ) ^ { n + m + 1 } } { ( 1 + \omega ^ { 2 } ) ^ { n + 1 } } } } \\ { { \times \omega ^ { m } \sigma ^ { - n - 2 } ( 1 - \sigma ) ^ { m + n + 1 } ( 1 - \omega ^ { 2 } \sigma ) ^ { n - m + 1 } d \sigma } } \end{array}
$$

We deform the integration contour so that the contour makes a single circuit around $\sigma = 0$ and a single circuit around $\sigma = 1$ ，both in the positive sense.We have $\sigma = 1 / \omega ^ { 2 } > 1$ . If we suppose that $n + 2 > 0$ and $n + m + 1 > 0$ ，then $\sigma = 1$ is not a pole; $\sigma = 0$ is the only pole, and a function is multiplied by exp $[ - 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } ~ ( n + 2 ) ]$ after a single circuit around $\sigma = 0$ Hence,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \begin{array} { c } { { \mathrm {  ~ \psi ~ } _ { 0 } ^ { r n m } = \displaystyle \frac { ( - 1 ) ^ { n + m + 1 } } { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } } \frac { \omega ^ { m } } { ( 1 + \omega ^ { 2 } ) ^ { n + 1 } } \left\{ \exp \left[ - 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \left( n + \frac { \omega } { 2 } \right) \right] \right. } } \end{array} } } \\ { { \begin{array} { r l } { { } } & { { } } \\ { { } } & { { \times \displaystyle \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \sigma ^ { - n - 2 } ( 1 - \sigma ) ^ { n + m + 1 } ( 1 - \omega ^ { 2 } \sigma ) ^ { n - m + 1 } d \sigma } } \end{array} } } \\ { { \begin{array} { l } { { } } & { { } } \\ { { = \displaystyle \frac { ( - 1 ) ^ { m } \omega ^ { m } } { ( 1 + \omega ^ { 2 } ) ^ { n + 1 } } \frac { \Gamma ( m + n + 2 ) } { \Gamma ( n + 2 ) \Gamma ( m + 1 ) } } } \end{array} } } \\ { { \begin{array} { r l } { { } } & { { } } \\ { { \times { \cal F } ( m - n - 1 , - n - 1 , m + 1 , \omega ^ { 2 } ) . } } \end{array} } } \end{array}
$$

There are only a finite number of terms in the expansion of the hypergeometric function if $n + m + 1 > 0$ 、If we suppose that $n + 2 < 0$ and $n + m + 1 > 0$ ，then neither $\sigma = 0$ nor $\sigma = 1$ is a pole and $X _ { 0 } ^ { n m } = 0$ If $n + 2 < 0$ and $n + m + 1 < 0$ ,then $\sigma = 0$ is an ordinary point and $\sigma = 1$ is a singularity. Hence,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { X _ { 0 } ^ { n m } = \displaystyle \frac { ( - 1 ) ^ { m } \omega ^ { m } ( 1 - \omega ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 n + 3 } } { ( 1 + \omega ^ { 2 } ) ^ { n + 1 } } \displaystyle \frac { \Gamma ( - n - 1 ) } { \Gamma ( m + 1 ) \Gamma ( - m - n - 1 ) } } } \\ { { \times F ( n + 2 , m + n + 2 , m + 1 , \omega ^ { 2 } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Substitute in (l27) the expansion in Bessel functions

$$
\exp \left[ { \frac { j \omega } { 1 + \omega ^ { 2 } } } \left( s - { \frac { 1 } { s } } \right) \right] = \sum _ { p = - \infty } ^ { \infty } J _ { p } { \left( { \frac { 2 j \omega } { 1 + \omega ^ { 2 } } } \right) } s ^ { p }
$$

and put $s = \omega \sigma$ ; then,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle X _ { j } ^ { n m } = \sum _ { p = - \infty } ^ { \infty } J _ { p } \Big ( \frac { 2 j \omega } { 1 + \omega ^ { 2 } } \Big ) X _ { j p } ^ { n m } } , } \\ { { \displaystyle X _ { j p } ^ { n m } = \frac { ( - 1 ) ^ { n + m + 1 } } { ( 1 + \omega ^ { 2 } ) ^ { n + 1 } } \frac { \omega ^ { m + p - j } } { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } } } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ \times \int _ { c } ^ { ( 0 + ) } \sigma ^ { p - n - j - 2 } ( 1 - \sigma ) ^ { n + m + 1 } ( 1 - \omega ^ { 2 } \sigma ) ^ { n - m + 1 } d \sigma } . } \end{array}
$$

$\mathrm { I f } ~ \hbar - \mathscr { n } - j - 2 < 0$ and $n + m + 1 > 0$ ,then,

$$
= ( - 1 ) ^ { p + m - j } { \frac { \omega ^ { m + p - j } } { ( 1 + \omega ^ { 2 } ) ^ { n + 1 } } } { \frac { \Gamma ( n + m + 2 ) } { \Gamma ( - p + n + j + 2 ) \Gamma ( p + m - j + 1 ) } }
$$

$$
\times F ( m - n - 1 , \boldsymbol { \phi } - n - j - 1 , \boldsymbol { \phi } + m - j + 1 , \omega ^ { 2 } ) ;
$$

and $F$ consists of a finite number of terms. If $\not { p } - \mathscr { n } - \dot { \mathscr { j } } - 2 > 0$ and $n + m + 1 > 0$ ,then $X _ { j p } ^ { n m } = 0$ .If

$$
\dot { p } - n - \dot { j } - 2 > 0 \quad \mathrm { a n d } \quad n + m + 1 < 0 ,
$$

then

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { X _ { j p } ^ { n m } = \displaystyle \frac { \omega ^ { m + p - j } } { ( 1 + \omega ^ { 2 } ) ^ { n - 1 } } \displaystyle \frac { \Gamma ( \phi - n - j - 1 ) } { \Gamma ( p + m - j + 1 ) \Gamma ( - n - m - 1 ) } \nonumber } } \\ { { \times F ( m - n - 1 , \phi - n - j - 1 , \phi + m - j + 1 , \omega ^ { 2 } ) , } } \end{array}
$$

or, if we use (123),

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { X _ { j p } ^ { n m } = \displaystyle \frac { \omega ^ { m + p - j } ( 1 - \omega ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 n + 3 } } { ( 1 + \omega ^ { 2 } ) ^ { n + 1 } } \displaystyle \frac { \Gamma ( \phi - n - j - 1 ) } { \Gamma ( p + m - j + 1 ) \Gamma ( - n - m - 1 ) } } } \\ { { \times \displaystyle F ( \phi + n - j + 2 , - m - n - 2 , \phi + m - j + 1 , \omega ^ { 2 } ) , } } \end{array}
$$

which consists of a finite number of terms.

We can deduce similar formulas from (l28) (Hagihara, 1935).

# 5.13EXPANSION IN SEVERAL VARIABLES

Let $C$ and $C ^ { \prime }$ be two simple integration contours around $z = x$ and $z ^ { \prime } = x ^ { \prime }$ ， respectively, in the complex plane of two complex variables $_ z$ and $z ^ { \prime }$ ; and let $F ( z , z ^ { \prime } )$ be a single-valued continuous analytic function inside $C$ and $C ^ { \prime }$ . Then by the residue theorem of Cauchy.in the theory of analytic functions (Goursat， 1913；Osgood,1914,1924；Behnke and Thullen,1934; Bochner and Martin,1948; Forsyth,1914; Picard,1926; Picard and Simart, 1897),we have

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle F ( x , x ^ { \prime } ) = \frac { 1 } { ( 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } ) ^ { 2 } } \int _ { c } d z \int _ { c ^ { \prime } } \frac { F ( z , z ^ { \prime } ) ~ d z ^ { \prime } } { ( z - x ) ( z ^ { \prime } - x ^ { \prime } ) } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { \partial ^ { m + n } F ( x , x ^ { \prime } ) } { \partial x ^ { m } \partial x ^ { \prime n } } = \frac { m ! n ! } { ( 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } ) ^ { 2 } } \int _ { c } d z \int _ { c ^ { \prime } } \frac { F ( z , z ^ { \prime } ) ~ d z ^ { \prime } } { ( z - x ) ^ { m + 1 } ( z ^ { \prime } - x ^ { \prime } ) ^ { n + 1 } } . } } \end{array}
$$

If a single-valued continuous analytic function $S ( z , z ^ { \prime } )$ can be expanded in the double Laurent series

$$
S ( z , z ^ { \prime } ) = \sum _ { j = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \sum _ { j ^ { \prime } = - \infty } ^ { \infty } P _ { j j ^ { \prime } } z ^ { j } z ^ { \prime j ^ { \prime } }
$$

such that $0 < r < | z | < R < \infty$ ， $0 < r ^ { \prime } < | z ^ { \prime } | < R ^ { \prime } < \infty$ ，then, according to this theorem, the coefficient $P _ { j j ^ { \prime } }$ can be written

$$
P _ { j j ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 1 } { ( 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } ) ^ { 2 } } { \int _ { c } ^ { ( 0 + ) } \int _ { c ^ { \prime } } ^ { ( 0 + ) } S ( z , z ^ { \prime } ) z ^ { - j - 1 } z ^ { \prime - j ^ { \prime } - 1 } d z d z ^ { \prime } } .
$$

If we transform by

$$
z = s \exp \left[ - \frac { e } { 2 } \left( s - s ^ { - 1 } \right) \right] , \qquad z ^ { \prime } = s ^ { \prime } \exp \left[ - \frac { e ^ { \prime } } { 2 } \left( s ^ { \prime } - s ^ { \prime - 1 } \right) \right] ,
$$

then

$$
P _ { j j ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 1 } { ( 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } ) ^ { 2 } } \int _ { c } ^ { ( 0 + ) } \int _ { c ^ { \prime } } ^ { ( 0 + ) } U s ^ { - j - 1 } s ^ { \prime - j ^ { \prime } - 1 } d s d s ^ { \prime } ,
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { U = S \Bigg \{ 1 - \displaystyle \frac { e } { 2 } \left( s + s ^ { - 1 } \right) \Bigg \} \Bigg \{ 1 - \displaystyle \frac { e ^ { \prime } } { 2 } \left( s ^ { \prime } + s ^ { \prime - 1 } \right) \Bigg \} } } \\ { { \times \exp \Bigg \{ \displaystyle \frac { j e } { 2 } \left( s - s ^ { - 1 } \right) + \displaystyle \frac { j ^ { \prime } e ^ { \prime } } { 2 } \left( s ^ { \prime } - s ^ { \prime - 1 } \right) \Bigg \} . } } \end{array}
$$

This is an extension of Cauchy's first theorem to two variables (Hill, 1905).

Put $| z | = 1 , | z ^ { \prime } | = 1$ in these integration contours and write

$$
\begin{array} { l c r } { { z = \exp { ( \sqrt { - 1 } \ : M ) } , \ : \ : } } & { { z ^ { \prime } = \exp { ( \sqrt { - 1 } \ : M ^ { \prime } ) } , } } \\ { { \ : } } & { { \ : } } \\ { { s = \exp { ( \sqrt { - 1 } \ : u ) } , \ : \ : } } & { { s ^ { \prime } = \exp { ( \sqrt { - 1 } \ : u ^ { \prime } ) } ; } } \end{array}
$$

then,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { P _ { j j ^ { \prime } } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { ( 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } ) ^ { 2 } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } S \exp [ - \sqrt { - 1 } ( j M + j ^ { \prime } M ^ { \prime } ) ] d M d M ^ { \prime } } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { \displaystyle = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi ^ { 2 } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } U \exp [ - \sqrt { - 1 } ( j u + j ^ { \prime } u ^ { \prime } ) ] d u d u ^ { \prime } , \qquad \displaystyle ( \mathscr { m } ^ { \prime } - \mathscr { G } ^ { \prime } - \mathscr { G } ^ { \prime } ) d u d w ^ { \prime } , \quad \mathrm { ~ \ } } } \end{array}
$$

$$
U = S ( 1 - \epsilon \cos u ) ( 1 - \epsilon ^ { \prime } \cos u ^ { \prime } ) \exp { [ \sqrt { - 1 } ( j \epsilon \sin u + j ^ { \prime } \epsilon ^ { \prime } \sin u ^ { \prime } ) }
$$

Hence, the coefficient of the double Fourier expansion of $s$ in the mean anomalies equals the corresponding coefficient in the double Fourier expansion of $U$ in the eccentric anomalies (Hagihara, 1936).

The relation between $s$ and $\boldsymbol { u }$ can be generalized in the form

$$
M = u - \alpha \phi ( u ) ,
$$

replacing the Kepler equation. Put

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \cal F } ( u , u ^ { \prime } ) = \sum _ { p = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \sum _ { p ^ { \prime } = - \infty } ^ { \infty } B _ { p p ^ { \prime } } \exp \left[ \sqrt { - 1 } \left( \rlap / p u + \rlap / p ^ { \prime } u ^ { \prime } \right) \right] } } \\ { { = \sum _ { q = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \sum _ { q ^ { \prime } = - \infty } ^ { \infty } A _ { q q ^ { \prime } } \exp \left[ \sqrt { - 1 } \left( q M + q ^ { \prime } M ^ { \prime } \right) \right] ; } } \end{array}
$$

then,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { d _ { \mathrm { \tiny { \# z ^ { \prime } } } } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi ^ { 2 } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } F ( u , u ^ { \prime } ) \exp \left[ - \sqrt { - 1 } \left( q M + q ^ { \prime } M ^ { \prime } \right) \right] d M d M ^ { \prime } } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { \displaystyle = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi ^ { 2 } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } F ( u , u ^ { \prime } ) \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } \Psi } { \partial M \partial M ^ { \prime } } d M d M ^ { \prime } } } \\ { { \displaystyle = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi ^ { 2 } q ^ { \prime } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } F } { \partial u \partial u ^ { \prime } } \Psi [ u - e \sin u , u ^ { \prime } - e ^ { \prime } \sin u ^ { \prime } ] d u d u ^ { \prime } } } \\ { { \displaystyle = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi ^ { 2 } q \bar { q } ^ { \prime } } \sum _ { p = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \sum _ { \sigma } ^ { \infty } \sum _ { p ^ { \prime } = - \infty } ^ { p } p b \bar { p } ^ { \prime } B _ { p p ^ { \prime } } } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\Psi = - \frac { 1 } { q q ^ { \prime } } \exp { [ - \sqrt { - 1 } ( q M + q ^ { \prime } M ^ { \prime } ) ] } .
$$

Hence,

$$
A _ { q q ^ { \prime } } = \sum _ { p = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \sum _ { p ^ { \prime } = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \frac { p / p ^ { \prime } } { q q ^ { \prime } } B _ { p p ^ { \prime } } J _ { p - q } ( q \ell ) J _ { p ^ { \prime } - q ^ { \prime } } ( q ^ { \prime } e ^ { \prime } ) .
$$

In particular, if we denote the mutual distance of the two planets by $\Delta$ ，then $s ^ { 2 } s ^ { \prime } { } ^ { 2 } \Delta ^ { 2 } = R ( s , \ s ^ { \prime } )$ is a sextic integral polynomial in $\mathfrak { s }$ and $s ^ { \prime }$

(Hagihara，1936；Poincaré,1892，1907；Brown and Shook，1933;Andoyer 1923). Write

$$
\begin{array} { r } { F = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \Delta } = \sum _ { p = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \sum _ { p ^ { \prime } = - \infty } ^ { \infty } B _ { p p ^ { \prime } } \exp { [ \sqrt { - 1 } ( \rlap / { p } u + \rlap / { p ^ { \prime } } u ^ { \prime } ) ] } } \\ { = \displaystyle \sum _ { q = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \sum _ { q ^ { \prime } = - \infty } ^ { \infty } A _ { q q ^ { \prime } } \exp { [ \sqrt { - 1 } ( q M + q ^ { \prime } M ^ { \prime } ) ] } ; } \end{array}
$$

then

$$
B _ { p p ^ { \prime } } = - \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi ^ { 2 } } \int _ { C } \int _ { C ^ { \prime } } \frac { d s d s ^ { \prime } } { s ^ { p } s ^ { \prime } ^ { p ^ { \prime } } \sqrt { R ( s , s ^ { \prime } ) } } ,
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \cal A } _ { q q ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi ^ { 2 } q q ^ { \prime } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { 2 \pi } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } ( 1 / \Delta ) } { \partial u \partial u ^ { \prime } } \exp \left[ - \sqrt { - 1 } ( q M + q ^ { \prime } M ^ { \prime } ) \right] d u d u ^ { \prime } } } \\ { { \mathrm { } ~ = ~ - \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { 4 \pi ^ { 2 } q q ^ { \prime } } } \int _ { c } \int _ { c ^ { \prime } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } ( 1 / \Delta ) } { \partial s \partial s ^ { \prime } } \exp \left[ \sqrt { - 1 } q e \sin u + \sqrt { - 1 } q ^ { \prime } e ^ { \prime } \sin u \right. } } \\ { { \mathrm { } ~ \times \displaystyle { \frac { d s } { s ^ { q } } } \frac { d s ^ { \prime } } { s ^ { \prime \prime } } } } \end{array}
$$

This expression has been used by Poincaré (Poincaré, l898;Féraud, 1902).

In extending the theorems from two variables to more than two variables, put

$$
\exp \left[ \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { k } \frac { z _ { j } } { 2 } \left( t ^ { j } - t ^ { - j } \right) \right] = \sum _ { n = - \infty } ^ { \infty } J _ { n } ( z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { k } ) t ^ { n } ;
$$

then the Laurent series converges in a ring domain excluding $t = 0$ and $\infty$ Write $t ^ { - 1 }$ in the place of $t$ and add or subtract the corresponding expressions,and put $t = \exp { ( - \sqrt { - 1 } \phi ) }$ ; then

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { { \displaystyle \cos \left( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { k } z _ { j } \sin j \phi \right) = \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \left[ J _ { n } ( z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { k } ) + J _ { - n } ( z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { k } ) \right] \cos n \phi } , } } \\ { { { \displaystyle \sin \left( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { k } z _ { j } \sin j \phi \right) = \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } \left[ J _ { n } ( z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { k } ) - J _ { - n } ( z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { k } ) \right] \sin n \phi } } } \end{array}
$$

Differentiate the first equation of (l39) with respect to $\phi$ and put $\phi = 0$ ； then,

$$
\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { k } j z _ { j } = \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } n [ J _ { n } ( z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { k } ) - J _ { - n } ( z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { k } ) ] .
$$

Put $\phi = 0$ before carrying out the differentiation; then

$$
1 = \sum _ { n = \infty } ^ { \infty } J _ { n } ( z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { k } ) { \mathrm { . } }
$$

Multiply (l38) by the expression obtained by replacing $t$ by $t ^ { - 1 }$ in (138); then

$$
1 = C _ { 0 } + \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } C _ { n } t ^ { n } + \sum _ { n = 1 } ^ { \infty } C _ { - n } t ^ { - n } .
$$

This relation is independent of $t .$ Hence, $C _ { 0 } = 1 , \ C _ { n } = 0 , \ C _ { - n } = 0$ $( n = 1 , 2 , \ldots )$ . The relation $C _ { 0 } = 1$ is explicitly written

$$
1 = \sum _ { n = - \infty } ^ { \infty } J _ { n } ^ { 2 } ( z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { k } ) .
$$

Hence

$$
| J _ { n } ( z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { k } ) | < 1 .
$$

Multiply both sides of (138) by exp $( - \sqrt { - 1 } ~ 2 \pi \phi ) ~ d \phi / 2 \pi _ { ; }$ , integrate with respect to $\phi$ , and take the real part; then

$$
J _ { n } ( z _ { 1 } , \ldots , z _ { k } ) = { \frac { 1 } { \pi } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \cos \left( n \phi - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { k } z _ { j } \sin j \phi \right) d \phi .
$$

When there is only one $_ z$ , this reduces to the usual integral representation of Bessel functions.Put

$$
y _ { k - 1 } = m \phi - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { k - 1 } z _ { j } \sin j \phi ;
$$

then (140） becomes

$$
\begin{array} { r } { J _ { m } ( z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { k } ) = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \cos y _ { k - 1 } \cos \left( z _ { k } \sin k \phi \right) d \phi } \\ { + \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \sin y _ { k - 1 } \sin \left( z _ { k } \sin k \phi \right) d \phi . } \end{array}
$$

By the usual expansion formulas in Bessel functions,

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { \pi } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \cos y _ { k - 1 } d \phi = J _ { m } ( z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { k - 1 } ) ,
$$

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { \pi } } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } 2 \cos y _ { k - 1 } \cos 2 j k \phi d \phi
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \cos \left[ y _ { k - 1 } - 2 j k \phi \right] + \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \cos \left( y _ { k - 1 } + 2 j k \phi \right) d \phi } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \mathrm { } = J _ { m - 2 j k } ( z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { k - 1 } ) + J _ { m + 2 j k } ( z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { k - 1 } ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } 2 \sin y _ { k - 1 } \sin \left( 2 j - 1 \right) k \phi d \phi } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle = \frac { 1 } { \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \cos \left[ y _ { k - 1 } - ( 2 j - 1 ) k \phi \right] d \phi } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \quad - \frac { 1 } { \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \cos \left[ y _ { k - 1 } + ( 2 j - 1 ) k \phi \right] d \phi } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle = J _ { m - ( 2 j - 1 ) k } \big ( z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { k - 1 } \big ) - J _ { m + ( 2 j - 1 ) k } \big ( z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { k - 1 } \big ) . } } \end{array}
$$

From these expressions we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { z _ { k } ) = { \cal J } _ { 0 } ( z _ { k } ) { \cal J } _ { m } ( z _ { 1 } , \dots , z _ { k - 1 } ) } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { \infty } { \cal J } _ { 2 j } ( z _ { k } ) [ { \cal J } _ { m - 2 j k } ( z _ { 1 } , \dots , z _ { k - 1 } ) + { \cal J } _ { m + 2 j k } ( z _ { 1 } , \dots , z _ { k - 1 } ) ] } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { \infty } { \cal J } _ { 2 j - 1 } ( z _ { k } ) [ { \cal J } _ { m - ( 2 j - 1 ) k } ( z _ { 1 } , \dots , z _ { k - 1 } ) } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ - { \cal J } _ { m + ( 2 j - 1 ) k } ( z _ { 1 } , \dots , z _ { k - 1 } ) ] . } } \end{array}
$$

But, since

$$
J _ { 2 j } ( z _ { k } ) = J _ { - 2 j } ( z _ { k } ) , J _ { 2 j - 1 } ( z _ { k } ) = - J _ { - ( 2 j - 1 ) } ( z _ { k } ) ,
$$

therefore

$$
J _ { m } ( z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { k } ) = \sum _ { q _ { k } = - \infty } ^ { \infty } J _ { m - k q _ { k } } ( z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { k - 1 } ) J _ { q _ { k } } ( z _ { k } ) .
$$

By repeated use of this formula we finally obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 } , \dots , z _ { k } ) } } \\ & { = \sum _ { q _ { 2 } = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \sum _ { q _ { 3 } = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \quad } & { \sum _ { q _ { k } = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \ J _ { m - 2 q _ { 2 } - 3 q _ { 3 } - \cdots - k q _ { k } } ( z _ { 1 } ) J _ { q _ { 2 } } ( z _ { 2 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot J _ { q _ { k } } ( z _ { k } ) . } \end{array}
$$

This formula has been deduced by Jekhowsky (Appell,1915,1924,1925; Peres,1915; Akimoff, 1915,1916,1917,1924,1927; Jekhowsky,1916, 1916a,1917,1917a,1918,1920,1921,1927; Humbert,1920).

We now expand $y = \cos { \beta v }$ with respect to $_ x$ ， such that

$$
x = v - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { k } \alpha _ { j } \sin j v ,
$$

with $| \alpha _ { 1 } | + | \alpha _ { 2 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | \alpha _ { k } | < 1$ Since $y$ remains unaltered as u increases by $2 \pi / \hbar$ ，the function $y$ is periodic.By the Weierstrassian approximation theorem of Section 2.6,it can be written

$$
y = { \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } A _ { 0 } + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { \infty } A _ { j } \cos j x ,
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { A _ { j } \displaystyle = \frac { 2 } { \pi } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \cos { \phi } v \cos { j x } d x = \frac { 2 \phi } { \pi j } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \sin { \beta v } \sin { j \left( v - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { k } \alpha _ { i } \sin i v \right) } d v } } \\ { { \displaystyle \phantom { \frac { 2 p } { \pi j } = \frac { 2 } { \pi j } \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \cos { \beta v } \cos { j x } d x } = \frac { \beta } { j } \left[ J _ { j - p } ( j \alpha _ { 1 } , j \alpha _ { 2 } , . . . , j \alpha _ { k } ) - J _ { j + p } ( j \alpha _ { 1 } , . . . , j \alpha _ { k } ) \right] . } } \end{array}
$$

Hence,

$$
( \pm \sqrt { - 1 } p v ) = - \frac { \hbar \alpha _ { p } } { 2 } \pm \hbar \sum _ { j = - \infty } ^ { \infty } J _ { j \mp p } ( j \alpha _ { 1 } , . . . , j \alpha _ { k } ) \frac { 1 } { j } \exp { ( \sqrt { - 1 } j x ) } ,
$$

where in the summation $j = 0$ is excluded. Put $z = \exp { ( \sqrt { - 1 } \upsilon ) }$ ， $s = \exp { ( \sqrt { - 1 } \boldsymbol { x } ) }$ ; then Cauchy's theorem is generalized; that is, the coefficient $P _ { j }$ in the expansion

$$
S = \sum _ { j = - \infty } ^ { \infty } P _ { j } z ^ { j }
$$

equals the coefficient of $s ^ { j }$ in the expansion of

$$
U = S \left[ 1 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { l = 1 } ^ { k } l \alpha _ { l } ( s ^ { l } + s ^ { - l } ) \right] \exp { \left[ \frac { j } { 2 } \sum _ { l = 1 } ^ { k } \alpha _ { l } ( s ^ { l } - s ^ { - l } ) \right] } ,
$$

or equals the coefficient of $s ^ { j }$ in the expansion of

$$
V = \frac { 1 } { j } \frac { d S } { d s } s \exp \bigg [ \frac { j } { 2 } \sum _ { l = 1 } ^ { k } \alpha _ { l } \big ( s ^ { l } - s ^ { - l } \big ) \bigg ] ,
$$

where $_ z$ and $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ are related by

$$
z = s \exp \left[ - { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { k } \alpha _ { j } \big ( s ^ { j } - s ^ { - j } \big ) \right] .
$$

In the case where the number of variables in the arguments of the Bessel functions is infinite, it is sufficient to have

$$
| z _ { 1 } | + 2 | z _ { 2 } | + 3 | z _ { 3 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + k | z _ { k } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot < 1
$$

in order that the series be convergent. The formulas obtained in the foregoing study are also true for $k \to \infty$ . Such expansions were used by Hagihara (l93l） for the expansions of quasi-periodic motions in the relativistic trajectories of Sections 2.15-2.20.

# 5.14RADIUS OF CONVERGENCE

In Kepler's equation (101),

$$
u - e \sin u = M ,
$$

the singularity occurs when

$$
\frac { d M } { d u } = 1 - e \cos u = 0 \qquad ( 0 < \ell < 1 ) ,
$$

in representing the series for $\boldsymbol { u }$ as power series in $M$ or in $t \mathrm { ~ - ~ } t _ { 0 }$

On the complex plane for $u = z + \sqrt { - 1 } w$ we erect a rectangle of very large height between $z = m \pi$ and $z = ( m + 1 ) \pi$ perpendicular to the real axis.Briot and Bouquet (l875) proved that there are two roots in the rectangle for an even positive or an odd negative integer $_ m$ and no root for an odd positive or an even negative integer $\pmb { m }$ . For a very large $_ m$ the two roots whose real parts are between $2 m \pi$ and $( 2 m + 1 ) \pi$ tend asymptotically（Goursat,1913） to

$$
2 m \pi + \frac { \pi } { 2 } \pm \sqrt { - 1 } \left[ \log \left( \frac { 2 } { e } \right) + \log \left( 2 m \pi + \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) \right] .
$$

Moulton (1903) computed the singular points in the complex $\pmb { u }$ -plane to be

$$
z = 2 m \pi , w = \pm \cosh ^ { - 1 } \left( { \frac { 1 } { e } } \right) = \pm \log { \frac { 1 + \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } { e } }
$$

with an integer m. Using an arbitrary value $e _ { 0 }$ of $\pmb { \ell }$ $( 0 \leq e _ { 0 } \leq 1 _ { . } $ as center, draw a circle through the nearest singular point. The radius of this circle is the convergence radius of the expansion at $\ell = e _ { 0 }$

In Lagrange's theorem of Section 2.3 put

$$
t = e , \phi ( z ) = \sin u , z = u , a = M ;
$$

then a function $f$ of $\pmb { u }$ with Kepler's equation $\boldsymbol { u } = \boldsymbol { M } + e \sin \boldsymbol { u }$ can be expanded in an integral power series of $\pmb { e }$ in the form

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle f ( u ) = f ( M ) + \frac { e } { 1 } f ^ { \prime } ( M ) \sin M + \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + \frac { e ^ { n + 1 } } { ( n + 1 ) ! } \frac { \partial ^ { n } } { \partial M ^ { n } } [ f ^ { \prime } ( M ) \sin ^ { n + 1 } M ] + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
| e \sin u | < | u - M | .
$$

Suppose that $\pmb { \ell }$ and $M$ are given, what is the maximum value of $\pmb { e }$ satisfying this inequality? Let

$$
u = M + \epsilon { \mathrm { s i n } } u { \mathrm { \equiv } } M + \rho \cos \chi + { \sqrt { - 1 } } \rho { \mathrm { s i n } } \chi , = 1 \omega
$$

where ${ \boldsymbol { \rho } } =$ constant. From this,

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \sin u = \sin ( M + \rho \cos \chi ) \cosh ( \rho \sin \chi ) } \\ & { \qquad + \sqrt { - 1 } \cos ( M + \rho \cos \chi ) \sinh ( \rho \sin \chi ) . } \end{array}
$$

Hence,

$$
| \sin u | ^ { 2 } = \cos h ^ { 2 } ( \rho \sin \chi ) - \cos ^ { 2 } ( M + \rho \cos \chi ) .
$$

The worst situation occurs when $| \sin u |$ is a maximum, that is,when

$$
\sin \chi = \pm 1 , \qquad \cos \left( M + \rho \cos \chi \right) = 0 ,
$$

or $\chi = \pm \pi / 2 ,$ $M = \pm \pi / 2$ ，or when $| \sin u | = \cosh \rho .$ Hence， the required condition is

$$
\displaystyle e < \frac { \rho } { \cosh \rho } .
$$

The maximum of $\rho / { \mathsf { c o s h } } \ \rho$ occurs when

$$
\frac { d } { d \rho } \left( \frac { \rho } { \cosh \rho } \right) = 0 ,
$$

or when cosh $\rho = \rho$ sinh $\pmb { \rho }$ or

$$
\exp \left( 2 \rho \right) = \frac { \rho + 1 } { \rho - 1 } ,
$$

that is, $\rho = 1 . 9 9 6 7 \cdots$ Thus, if

$$
\ell = \tan \alpha = \frac { \rho } { \cosh \rho } = \frac { 1 } { \sinh \rho } ,
$$

then the maximum occurs when $\alpha = 3 3 ^ { \circ } 3 2 ^ { \prime } 3 . 0$ $e = { 0 . 6 6 2 7 4 3 4 \cdot \cdot \cdot } .$ For any value of $\boldsymbol { e }$ larger than this critical value,any expansion for an elliptic motion as an integral power series of $e$ is not convergent. This finding is due to Laplace.

Now compute the radius of convergence of an integral power series of $e$ in the complex $e$ plane,by putting $e = g + { \sqrt { - 1 } } h$ according to Charlier (1927).The singularities in the $M$ plane are obtained by the two equations

$$
\frac { d M } { d u } = 1 - e \cos { u } = 0 ,
$$

$$
u - M - e \sin u = 0 .
$$

Since

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \exp \left( \sqrt { - 1 } \ : u \right) = \frac 1 \epsilon \ : \mp \ : \sqrt { \frac { 1 } { \epsilon ^ { 2 } } - 1 } = \exp \left[ \sqrt { - 1 } \ : e \sin u + \sqrt { - 1 } \ : M \right] } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \mathrm { } = \exp \left[ \mp \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } + \sqrt { - 1 } \ : M \right] , } } \end{array}
$$

the singularities are defined by

$$
\gamma = 1 \mp \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } = e \exp { [ \mp \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } + \sqrt { - 1 } M ] } ,
$$

or by

$$
\left( { \frac { 2 } { \gamma } } - 1 \right) \exp { ( 2 \gamma ) } = \exp { ( 2 + 2 { \sqrt { - 1 } } M ) } .
$$

Write $\gamma = \epsilon \exp \left( { \sqrt { - 1 } } \theta \right)$ and take the real and imaginary parts; then the singularities are at the intersections of the two curves

$$
\frac { 4 } { \epsilon ^ { 2 } } + 1 - \frac { 4 } { \epsilon } \cos \theta = \exp \big ( 4 - 4 \epsilon \cos \theta \big ) ,
$$

$$
{ \frac { 2 } { \epsilon } } \sin \left( 2 M + \theta - 2 \epsilon \sin \theta \right) - \sin \left( 2 M - 2 \epsilon \sin \theta \right) = 0 .
$$

These equations are satisfied by

$$
\epsilon \cos \theta = 1 , \qquad \theta - \tan \theta = { \frac { n \pi } { 2 } } - M \qquad ( n = 0 , \pm 1 , \pm 2 , . . . ) ,
$$

which define a set of parallel straight lines. Other solutions are obtained by the equations

$$
\mu ^ { 2 } = \frac { 4 ( 1 - \lambda ) } { \exp \left[ 4 ( 1 - \lambda ) \right] - 1 } - \lambda ^ { 2 } ,
$$

$$
( \lambda - 1 ) ^ { 2 } = 1 + 2 \mu \cot { ( 2 M - 2 \mu ) } - \mu ^ { 2 } ,
$$

![](images/fa5936cf267e8a9653d4c639318c3217cd5c9a69195ebe9f43ec430fb08c1be1.jpg)  
FIGURE 3

where $\lambda = \epsilon \cos \theta _ { ; }$ $\mu = \epsilon \sin \theta$ ，The solution $\lambda = 1$ corresponds to the set of straight lines just obtained.We can make the curve defined by the second equation of (l45) pass through any point of the curve defined by the first equation. If we denote such values of $\lambda , \mu$ by $\lambda _ { 0 } , \mu _ { 0 }$ ,respectively, then the second equation is satisfied by

$$
\tan { ( 2 M - 2 \mu _ { 0 } ) } = \frac { 2 \mu _ { 0 } } { ( \lambda _ { 0 } - 1 ) ^ { 2 } - 1 + \mu _ { 0 } } \cdot
$$

Take the real and the imaginary parts of this equation by puting

$$
e = g + { \sqrt { - 1 } } h = \kappa \exp \left( { \sqrt { - 1 } } \tau \right) ;
$$

then,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \kappa ^ { 2 } \cos 2 \tau = - ( 1 - \lambda ) ^ { 2 } + \mu ^ { 2 } + 1 , } } \\ { { \nonumber } } \\ { { \kappa ^ { 2 } \sin 2 \tau = 2 \mu ( 1 - \lambda ) . } } \end{array}
$$

The solution $\lambda = 1$ corresponds to the part of the $g$ axis in the $\pmb { \ell }$ plane such that $\left| g \right| \geq 1$ . The points for $M = \pi / 2$ corresponds to point $B$ in Figure 3. Put $e = { \sqrt { - 1 } } h$ ; then,

$$
1 + { \sqrt { 1 + h ^ { 2 } } } = h \exp { ( { \sqrt { 1 + h ^ { 2 } } } ) }
$$

and the solution is $h = 0 . 6 6 2 7 \cdots$ ,which is equal to the maximum value of $\pmb { \ell }$ obtained in the foregoing discussion by means of Lagrange's theorem. All points on the singular curve of Figure 3 are singular points of the integral (l44) which is expanded in the form

$$
u = a _ { 0 } + a _ { 1 } ( e - e _ { 0 } ) + a _ { 2 } ( e - e _ { 0 } ) ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

The curve is the envelope of a one-parameter family of convergence circles with variable'radius centered at the point $\scriptstyle { e _ { 0 } }$

Elenevskaya (1953,1957,1962) has writen the equations (144), by separating the real and imaginary parts

$$
u = x + { \sqrt { - 1 } } y , \qquad e = \xi + { \sqrt { - 1 } } \eta ,
$$

in the form

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { M - x = - \xi \sin x \cosh y + \eta \cos x \sinh y , } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { y = \xi \cos x \sinh y + \eta \sin x \cosh y , } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { 1 = \xi \cos x \cosh y + \eta \sin x \sinh y , } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { 0 = \xi \sin x \sinh y - \eta \cos x \cosh y . } } \end{array}
$$

The intersections of these two curves in the $( \xi , \eta )$ -plane define the singular points of the integral of the first equation of (l44). The coordinates of these singular points are obtained from the last three equations of (146):

$$
\xi = \frac { y \cos x } { \sinh y } , \qquad \eta = \frac { y \sin x } { \cosh y } ,
$$

or

$$
\sin ^ { 2 } x = { \frac { y \cosh ^ { 2 } y - \sinh y \cosh y } { y } } \cdotp
$$

$_ { x }$ is a multi-valued function of $y$ ,but $\xi$ and $\eta$ are determined uniquely:

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \xi ^ { 2 } = \frac { y } { \sinh y } \big ( \cosh y - y \sinh y \big ) \mathrm { , } } } \\ { { \displaystyle } } \\ { { \eta ^ { 2 } = \frac { y } { \cosh y } \big ( y \cosh y - \sinh y \big ) \mathrm { . } } } \end{array}
$$

It can be shown that there are no singular points other than those lying on this curve and that any point on this curve is a singular point of the integral.

Elenevskaya (l962） extended the discussion to hyperbolic motions. She derived

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { M + x = \xi \sinh x \cos y - \eta \cosh x \sin y , } \\ & { } & { y = \xi \cosh x \sin y + \eta \sinh x \cos y , } \\ & { } & { 1 = \xi \cosh x \cos y - \eta \sinh x \sin y , } \\ & { } & { 0 = \xi \sinh x \sin y + \eta \cosh x \cos y . } \end{array}
$$

![](images/3c988bc206773fe54f9d14330240ea76a31a55f5168000385fc7b2609bc23bd2.jpg)  
FIGURE 4

From these we obtain

$$
\xi = \frac { y \cosh x } { \sin y } , \qquad \eta = \frac { y \sinh x } { \cos y } ,
$$

$$
\cosh ^ { 2 } x = { \frac { y \sin ^ { 2 } y + \sin y \cos y } { y } } \cdotp
$$

With $_ x$ eliminated, the equation of the singular curve for a hyperbolic motion is

$$
\xi ^ { 2 } = \frac { y } { \sin y } ( y \sin y + \cos y ) , \qquad \eta ^ { 2 } = \frac { y } { \cos y } ( \sin y - y \cos y ) .
$$

Figure 4 shows the singular curve.

If $\pmb { e }$ is close to unity, then it is possible to accelerate the convergence by restricting the range of variation of the angular variable. Such an expansion is made in powers of $( 1 - e )$ . We have obtained the singular curve for an elliptic motion by considering (l46) as a system of equations describing two curves and selecting from these the one independent of $M .$ The convergence circle of the expansion of the eccentric anomaly $\pmb { u }$ is centered at the point $\boldsymbol { e } _ { 0 }$ ; this point is inside the singular curve.The point of contact between the convergence circle and the singular curve is the point at which the singular curve is intersected by another curve independent of $M$ and determined by (l46). For a given value of $M _ { i }$ the power series in $( e \mathrm { ~ - ~ } e _ { 0 } )$ will cease to be convergent when $e$ is outside the circle in contact with the singular curve at this point.

When $\boldsymbol e _ { 0 } = 1$ , the expansion of $\boldsymbol { u }$ in powers of $1 \textrm { -- } e$ becomes divergent for any value of $e$ except l, at least for some values of the mean anomaly $M .$ In order to determine the values of $M$ for which the divergence of the expansion does not take place, draw a circle of an arbitrary radius about a point $\boldsymbol { e } _ { 0 } = 1$ . This circle intersects the singular curve at two points.For convenience, consider only the part of the curve lying in the first quadrant.Each point of intersection of the circle with the singular curve is a point at which the singular curve is intersected by one of the family of curves depending on $M$ and determined by (l46).We call this latter curve the anomaly curve. This point gives the value of $M$ for which the expansion of $\pmb { u }$ in powers of $1 \ : - \ : e$ is no longer convergent. Denote this value of $M$ by $M _ { c r }$

Consider the family of curves governed by (l46) in order to determine the functional relation $M _ { c r } = \phi ( e )$ . From (146) we obtain

$$
\xi = \frac { ( x - M ) \cosh y } { \sin x } , \qquad \eta = \frac { ( x - M ) \sinh y } { \cos x } ,
$$

so that the relation between $_ x$ and $y$ is

$$
\sinh ^ { 2 } y = \frac { \sin x \cos x - ( x - M ) \cos ^ { 2 } x } { x - M } .
$$

This shows that $y$ is a multi-valued function of $_ x$ .We choose one region in which $e = \psi ( u )$ is single-valued, that is,the strip of width

$$
- \infty < y < + \infty , \qquad - \pi \leq x \leq \pi .
$$

From (147a) and (148) we obtain the family of anomaly curves

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \xi ^ { 2 } = \frac { x - M } { \sin x } [ \cos x + ( x - M ) \sin x ] } , } \\ { { \displaystyle } } \\ { { \displaystyle \eta ^ { 2 } = \frac { x - M } { \cos x } [ \sin x - ( x - M ) \cos x ] } . } \end{array}
$$

![](images/df5959abf591486ce60c66c5c1b27d7ee987d8ef0387020b620dba768035a091.jpg)  
FIGURE 5

The curve of this family for $M = 0$ is identical with the singular curve of a hyperbolic motion.It shows that when $M = 0$ the expansion of $\pmb { u }$ in powers of $( 1 ~ - ~ e )$ is divergent for any value of $\boldsymbol { e }$ .Figure 5 shows the family of anomaly curves. For $M = \pi / 2$ the anomaly curve degenerates into'the straight line $\xi = 0$ ,since $_ x$ in the case can only take the value $\pi / 2$ .Hence, for $M > \pi / 2$ , the expansion of $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { u } }$ will be convergent for any value of $\pmb { e }$

Draw a circle of radius $R$ about the point $e _ { 0 } = 1$ ; at some point it touches one of the anomaly curves. Moreover, the curves of the family for which $M \leq M _ { c r }$ intersect the circle,but the curves for which $M > M _ { c r }$ remain outside the circle.The intersection of the circle with the $\xi$ -axis gives the real part of $e _ { \scriptscriptstyle \mathrm { : } }$ ,for which the expansion of $\boldsymbol { u }$ in powers of $1 \textrm { -- } e$ is convergent for all $M > M _ { c r }$ and divergent for all $M \leq M _ { c r }$ ，since for a given value of $\pmb { \ell }$ these points of contact are the singular points of the equation $u - M - e \sin u = 0 .$

Similarly, equations (147b) for a hyperbolic motion give

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \xi ^ { 2 } = \frac { M + x } { \sinh x } [ \cosh x - ( M + x ) \sinh x ] , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \eta ^ { 2 } = \frac { M + x } { \cosh x } [ - \sinh x + ( M + x ) \cosh x ] . } } \end{array}
$$

![](images/d550f34d4cb32b22ba493d2b9f28d46583daad77e4cac060d989ffa807b57a80.jpg)  
FIGURE 6

These curves are shown in Figure 6. For $M = 0$ the anomaly curves degenerate into the singular curve for an eliptic motion.

We study the general case of the convergence of the expansions of $\pmb { u }$ in powers of $( 1 ~ - ~ e )$ , by considering the expansion $u \mathrm { ~ - ~ } M \mathrm { ~ - ~ } e$ sin u as an integral of the differential equation

$$
{ \frac { d u } { d e } } = { \frac { \sin u } { 1 - e \cos u } } ,
$$

in which $u = x + \textstyle { \sqrt { - 1 } } y , e = \xi + \textstyle { \sqrt { - 1 } } \eta , M = a + \textstyle { \sqrt { - 1 } } b$ This gives as particular cases the singular and the anomaly curves for elliptic $( b = 0 )$ ）and hyperbolic $( a = 0 )$ ） motions. The singular curves are represented by

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \xi ^ { 2 } = \frac { \alpha } { \sinh \alpha } ( \cosh \alpha - \alpha \sinh \alpha ) \mathrm { , } } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \eta ^ { 2 } = \frac { \alpha } { \cosh \alpha } ( \alpha \cosh \alpha - \sinh \alpha ) \mathrm { ; } } } \end{array}
$$

and the anomaly curves by

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { \dot { \xi } } ^ { 2 } = \frac { \beta - c } { \sin \beta } [ \cos \beta - ( \beta - c ) \sin \beta ] , } } \\ { { \displaystyle } } \\ { { \eta ^ { 2 } = \frac { \beta - c } { \cos \beta } [ ( \beta - c ) \cos \beta - \sin \beta ] . } } \end{array}
$$

![](images/ef4ebd176fcc9ebd2580b22101444d81eddcd0f7cd202080742c1b12204b7ed5.jpg)  
FIGURE 7

$\pmb { \alpha }$ and $\beta$ are parameters, real for an ellptic motion and purely imaginary for a hyperbolic motion; $c = a$ for an elliptic and $c = b$ for a hyperbolic motion. Thus, the radius of convergence of the expansions of $\boldsymbol { u }$ in powers of $( 1 ~ - ~ e )$ for all $M$ above a certain critical value is $( 1 ~ - ~ e _ { c r } )$ for an elliptic and $( e _ { c r } \mathrm { ~ - ~ } 1 )$ for a hyperbolic motion.The relation between $e$ and the critical value of $M$ is given by (l50) and (150a). $M _ { c r }$ as a function of $e$ is shown in Figure 7.

We have seen that, when the eccentric anomaly $\boldsymbol { u }$ is expanded in a power series of $( 1 ~ - ~ e )$ ，a certain region in the neighborhood of the point $( e _ { 0 } \mathrm { ~ - ~ } 1 )$ is cut out from the domain of the convergence of the expansion of $\boldsymbol { u }$ .In order to study the motion in this region,Elenevskaya has used Fourier series in the case of an elliptic motion and Laurent series in the case of a hyperbolic motion.

The coordinates of a point moving according to Kepler's laws can be represented in the form

$$
{ \frac { f ( e , u ) } { ( 1 - e \cos u ) ^ { 2 } } } ,
$$

where $f ( e , u )$ is an integral function of $\pmb { u }$ .If we expand the trigonometric functions in series and then substitute $z = \exp { u }$ ，we can transform this expansion for the coordinates to

$$
{ \frac { ( - 1 ) ^ { \alpha } 2 ^ { \alpha } z ^ { \alpha } f ( e , u ) } { e ^ { \alpha } ( z - \beta ) ^ { \alpha } ( z - 1 / \beta ) ^ { \alpha } } } , \qquad \beta = \tan { \frac { \phi } { 2 } } , \qquad \phi = \sin ^ { - 1 } e .
$$

This function has no singularity apart from the poles of order $\pmb { \alpha }$ Hence, it can be expanded in a Laurent series within the ring domain between two circles $z = \beta$ and $z = 1 / \beta$ . The coefficients of such expansions are given by

$$
P _ { n } = \frac { ( - 1 ) ^ { \alpha } 2 ^ { \alpha } } { e ^ { \alpha } 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } } \int _ { c } \frac { f ( e , u ) z ^ { n + \alpha - 1 } } { ( z - \beta ) ^ { \alpha } ( z - 1 / \beta ) ^ { \alpha } } d z ,
$$

where $c$ is the circle of unit radius. Since

$$
\beta = \tan \frac { \phi } { 2 } = \frac { \sin \phi _ { 1 } + \sqrt { - 1 } \sinh \phi _ { 2 } } { \cosh \phi _ { 2 } + \cos \phi _ { 1 } } ,
$$

therefore

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { \beta } } = { \frac { \sin \phi _ { 1 } - { \sqrt { - 1 } } \sinh \phi _ { 2 } } { \cosh \phi _ { 2 } - \cos \phi _ { 1 } } } ,
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { e = \xi + \sqrt { - 1 } \eta = \sin \phi = \sin ( \phi _ { 1 } + \sqrt { - 1 } \phi _ { 2 } ) } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { = \sin \phi _ { 1 } \cosh \phi _ { 2 } + \sqrt { - 1 } \cos \phi _ { 1 } \sinh \phi _ { 2 } . } } \end{array}
$$

The singular points are determined by

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { z _ { 1 } = \displaystyle \beta = \exp \left( \sqrt { - 1 } \displaystyle _ { x _ { 1 } } \right) \exp \left( - y _ { 1 } \right) = \displaystyle \frac { \sin \phi _ { 1 } - \sqrt { - 1 } \sinh \phi _ { 2 } } { \cosh \phi _ { 2 } + \cos \phi _ { 1 } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle z _ { 2 } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { \beta } = \exp \left( \sqrt { - 1 } \displaystyle _ { x _ { 2 } } \right) \exp \left( - y _ { 2 } \right) = \displaystyle \frac { \sin \phi _ { 1 } + \sqrt { - 1 } \sinh \phi _ { 2 } } { \cosh \phi _ { 2 } - \cos \phi _ { 1 } } . } } \end{array}
$$

We separate the real and imaginary parts and take the ratios of corre-sponding parts. Then we construct the sum of the squares.We obtain the coordinates of the singular points

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { \tan x _ { 1 } = { \displaystyle \frac { \sinh \phi _ { 2 } } { \sin \phi _ { 1 } } } , } & { \exp \left( - y _ { 1 } \right) = { \displaystyle \frac { \sqrt { \sin ^ { 2 } \phi _ { 1 } + \sinh ^ { 2 } \phi _ { 2 } } } { \cosh \phi _ { 2 } + \cos \phi _ { 1 } } } } \\ { \tan x _ { 2 } = - { \displaystyle \frac { \sinh \phi _ { 2 } } { \sin \phi _ { 1 } } } , } & { \exp \left( - y _ { 2 } \right) = { \displaystyle \frac { \sqrt { \sin ^ { 2 } \phi _ { 1 } + \sinh ^ { 2 } \phi _ { 2 } } } { \cosh \phi _ { 2 } - \cos \phi _ { 1 } } } . } \end{array}
$$

Put

$$
z = \exp { ( - y ) } \exp { ( \sqrt { - 1 } \ : x ) } ,
$$

and transfer the coordinate origin to the point $z - 1$ .Expand(151）in powers of $( 1 - z )$ ； such expansions are convergent everywhere within the circle passing through the nearest singular point.

# 5.15ANDOYER'S EXPANSION

Let $f ( z )$ and $\phi ( z )$ be two holomorphic functions in the domain of a point $^ { a }$ and $C$ be a circle with center $^ { a }$ and radius $\pmb { r }$ such that along the circumference of the circle

$$
| \alpha f ( z ) | + | \beta \phi ( z ) | < r
$$

with small constants $\pmb { \alpha }$ and $\beta$ . The equation

$$
F ( z ) = z - a - \alpha f ( z ) - \beta \phi ( z ) = 0
$$

admits one and only one root $z = \zeta$ in the interior of the circle $C .$ Any holomorphic function $\Pi ( \zeta )$ of the root can be expanded in powers of $\pmb { \alpha }$ and $\beta$ by

$$
\frac { \Pi ( \zeta ) } { F ^ { \prime } ( \zeta ) } = \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } } \int _ { c } \frac { \Pi ( z ) ~ d z } { z - a - \alpha f ( z ) - \beta \phi ( z ) } ,
$$

which, by Lagrange's theorem (Section 2.3), gives

$$
{ \frac { \Pi ( \zeta ) } { - \operatorname { e f } ^ { \prime } ( \zeta ) - \beta \phi ^ { \prime } ( \zeta ) } } = \Pi ( a ) + \sum _ { m , n } { \frac { \alpha ^ { m } \beta ^ { n } } { m ! n ! } } { \frac { d ^ { m + n } } { d a ^ { m + n } } } \{ \Pi ( a ) [ f ( a ) ] ^ { m } [ \phi ( a ) ] ^ { n } .
$$

where the combination $m = n = 0$ is excluded in the summation. Put

$$
\Pi ( z ) = \Phi ( z ) [ 1 - \alpha f ^ { \prime } ( z ) - \beta \phi ^ { \prime } ( z ) ] ;
$$

then,

$$
\Phi ( \zeta ) = \Phi ( a ) + \sum _ { m , n } ^ { \prime } \frac { \alpha ^ { m } \beta ^ { n } } { m ! n ! } \frac { d ^ { n + m - 1 } } { d a ^ { m + n - 1 } } \{ \Phi ^ { \prime } ( a ) [ f ( a ) ] ^ { m } [ \phi ( a ) ] ^ { n } \} ,
$$

where the combination $m = n = 0$ is excluded in the summation.This gives Goursat's generalization of the Lagrange theorem from a single variable to two variables (Goursat, 1913).

Consider with Goursat (l915） two functions $z _ { 1 } ( x , y )$ and $z _ { 2 } ( x , y )$ ， vanishing for $x = y = 0$ Let $w = S ( x , y )$ be the integral series of $_ { x }$ and $y$ representing the root ${ \boldsymbol w }$ of the equation

$$
x \exp { ( w ) } + y \exp { ( - w ) } = w ,
$$

such that $S ( x , y )$ is convergent for $| x | < R , | y | < R ^ { \prime }$ and divergent for $| x | > R , | y | > R ^ { \prime }$ . If there are positive numbers $R , R ^ { \prime }$ ， $\rho$ such that

$$
R \exp \left( \rho \right) + R ^ { \prime } \exp \left( - \rho \right) \leq \rho , \qquad R \exp \left( - \rho \right) + R ^ { \prime } \exp \left( \rho \right) \leq \rho ,
$$

![](images/c89bcd8e2617ce26401afe8a9135f0ab3fd0f96a3d153fe8e787aa82c87d2a86.jpg)  
FIGURE 8

then (l52) admits only one root whose modulus is smaller than $\pmb { \rho }$ for $| x | < R$ ， $| y | < R ^ { \prime }$

To prove this theorem of Goursat (l9l5), describe a circle with radius $\pmb { \rho }$ and with the center as origin on the complex plane of w.We have $| x \exp { ( w ) } + y \exp { ( - w ) } | < \rho$ 、Along the circumference of the circle,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \left| x \exp { \left( w \right) } + y \exp { \left( - w \right) } \right| < R \exp { \left( \rho \cos { \phi } \right) } + R ^ { \prime } \exp { \left( - \rho \cos { \phi } \right) } , } \end{array}
$$

where $\phi$ is the argument of $\boldsymbol { \omega }$ .The maximum of the right-hand member is either $R \exp \left( \rho \right) + R ^ { \prime } \exp \left( - \rho \right)$ or $R$ $R \exp \left( - \rho \right) + R ^ { \prime } \exp \left( \rho \right)$ according as $R \gtrless R ^ { \prime }$ . If (153) are satisfied, then there is one and only one root ${ w _ { 1 } } ( x , y )$ whose modulus is smaller than $\rho$ .This root is a holomorphic function of $_ x$ and $y$ inside the circles $R$ and $R ^ { \prime }$ ， drawn with $x = 0$ and $y = 0$ as centers,respectively. It can be expanded by the generalized Lagrange theorem just proved.
