Let $\Pi ( w )$ be a holomorphic function inside the circle $\pmb { \rho }$ By the residue theorem (Section 2.3),we have

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { { \displaystyle \frac { \Pi ( w _ { 1 } ) } { x \exp { ( w _ { 1 } ) } + y \exp { ( - w _ { 1 } ) } } } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } } } \end{array} \qquad = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } } \int \displaystyle \frac { \Pi ( w ) \ d w } { w - x \exp { ( w ) } - y \exp { ( - w ) } } ; \qquad 
$$

the integral being taken along the circumference of the circle $\pmb { \rho }$

In particular, if

$$
\Pi ( w ) = w [ 1 - x \exp { ( w ) } + y \exp { ( - w ) } ] ,
$$

then

$$
w _ { 1 } ( x , y ) = \sum _ { m , n } { \frac { ( m - n ) ^ { m + n - 1 } } { m ! n ! } } x ^ { m } y ^ { n } ,
$$

where the combination $m = n = 0$ is excluded from the summation. The series (l54) is absolutely convergent if there exists a positive number $\pmb { \rho }$ such that

$$
x | \exp { ( \rho ) } + | y | \exp { ( - \rho ) } < \rho , \qquad | x | \exp { ( - \rho ) } + | y | \exp { ( \rho ) } < \rho
$$

Geometricall, consider the family of straight lines represented by

$$
X \exp \left( \rho \right) + Y \exp \left( - \rho \right) = \rho
$$

with the parameter $\rho , 0 \leq \rho \leq \infty$ .The envelope of these straight lines is the curve $\mathbf { \Gamma } \mathbf { \Gamma } \mathbf { \Gamma } \mathbf { \Gamma } \mathbf { \Gamma } \mathbf { \Gamma } \mathbf { \Gamma } \mathbf { \Gamma } \mathbf { \Gamma }$ represented by

$$
X = \frac { \rho + 1 } { 2 } \exp { ( - \rho ) } , \qquad Y = \frac { \rho - 1 } { 2 } \exp { ( \rho ) } .
$$

The point $( X , Y )$ starts at a point $\left( { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } , \ - { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \right)$ and describes the infinite branch $\$ 4 Q$ which is asymptotic to the $Y .$ -axis (Figure 8). The abscissa of the intersection $A$ of the curve with the $X$ axis is $1 / 2 . 7 1 8 2 8 \cdots$ The intersection of this curve with the bisectrix $o G$ is the point $B$ for which the value of $\rho$ is given by $( \rho - 1 )$ exp $( 2 \rho ) = \rho + 1$ .The envelope of the straight lines (l55） is symmetric with respect to the bisectrix $_ { O G }$ of the angle $X O Y$ .The series (l54） is absolutely convergent if the point with coordinates $( | x | , | y | )$ is in the region between the broken segment $A B A ^ { \prime }$ and the origin because, in the region OBA,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { X \exp \left( \rho \right) + Y \exp \left( - \rho \right) - \rho < 0 , } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { X \exp \left( - \rho \right) + Y \exp \left( \rho \right) - \rho < 0 , } } \end{array}
$$

Andoyer (1923） writes

$$
s _ { 1 } = \frac { e } { 2 } s , \qquad s _ { 2 } = \frac { e } { 2 } s ^ { - 1 }

and the function $s$ in Section 5.11 in the form

or

Suppose that

( \not p _ { 2 } - q _ { 2 } ) ! } , } } \end{array}

where $\mathbf { \nabla } \mathbf { \hat { \rho } } ^ { ! } = 1$ for $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } = 0$ and $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } ! = \infty$ for a negative integer $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { p } }$ For ${ \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { 1 } = { \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { 2 } = { \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { 1 }$

$$
a _ { p , p } = b _ { p , p } - b _ { p , p - 1 } - b _ { p - 1 , p } .

X ^ { \rho , \sigma } = \Big ( \frac { r } { a } \Big ) ^ { \rho } \Big ( \frac { t } { z } \Big ) ^ { \sigma } , \qquad Y ^ { \rho , \sigma } = \Big ( \frac { r } { a } \Big ) ^ { \rho } \Big ( \frac { t } { s } \Big ) ^ { \sigma } .

Suppose that a holomorphic function is expanded in double power series of

$$
z _ { 1 } = \frac { e } { 2 } \exp { ( \sqrt { - 1 } M ) } , \qquad z _ { 2 } = \frac { e } { 2 } \exp { ( - \sqrt { - 1 } M ) }
$$

in the form

$$
\sum _ { j _ { 1 } , j _ { 2 } } P _ { j _ { 1 } , j _ { 2 } } z _ { 1 } ^ { j } { \imath } z _ { 2 } ^ { i } { \imath } ^ { \phantom { \dagger } } .
$$

The convergence of this series is judged by the root of

$$
{ \boldsymbol { F } } = { \boldsymbol { w } } - z _ { 1 } \exp \left( { \boldsymbol { w } } \right) + z _ { 2 } \exp \left( - { \boldsymbol { w } } \right) = 0 , \qquad { \boldsymbol { w } } = { \sqrt { - 1 } } \left( { \boldsymbol { u } } - { \boldsymbol { M } } \right)
$$

for which

$$
\frac { \partial F } { \partial w } = 1 - z _ { 1 } \exp { ( w ) } - z _ { 2 } \exp { ( - w ) } \neq 0 .
$$

$F ( z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 } )$ admits a root $w = 0$ for $z _ { 1 } = z _ { 2 } = 0$ Other roots can be expanded in integral powers of $z _ { 1 }$ and $z _ { 2 }$ ,and are real. The expansion is absolutely convergent for $| z _ { 1 } | < \rho / 2 , | z _ { 2 } | < \rho / 2$ with a sufficiently small number $\rho$ ,if

$$
{ \frac { \partial F } { \partial w } } = 1 - z _ { 1 } \exp \left( w \right) - z _ { 2 } \exp \left( - w \right) \neq 0
$$

for all such values of $z _ { 1 }$ and $z _ { 2 }$

The critical case occurs if $F = 0$ and $\partial F / \partial w = 0$ are satisfied simultaneously. We have

$$
\begin{array} { r } { z _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( 1 + w ) \exp { ( - w ) } , \qquad z _ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( 1 - w ) \exp { ( w ) } . } \end{array}
$$

Put $w = a + { \sqrt { - 1 } } b$ ; then,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { | z _ { 1 } | ^ { 2 } = { \frac { 1 } { 4 } } [ ( 1 ~ + ~ a ) ^ { 2 } ~ + ~ b ^ { 2 } ] \exp { ( - 2 a ) } , } } \\ { { | z _ { 2 } | ^ { 2 } = { \frac { 1 } { 4 } } [ ( 1 ~ - ~ a ) ^ { 2 } ~ + ~ b ^ { 2 } ] \exp { ( 2 a ) } . } } \end{array}
$$

Then the foregoing condition for $z _ { 1 }$ and $z _ { 2 }$ is that

$$
\left| ( 1 + a ) ^ { 2 } + b ^ { 2 } \right| \exp { ( - 2 a ) } < \rho ^ { 2 } , \qquad \left| ( 1 - a ) ^ { 2 } + b ^ { 2 } \right| \exp { ( 2 a ) } < \rho ^ { 2 }
$$

should admit no common root.

Consider in the complex plane of w the equation

$$
| ( 1 + a ) ^ { 2 } + b ^ { 2 } | \exp { ( - 2 a ) } = \rho ^ { 2 } ,
$$

or

$$
b ^ { 2 } = \rho ^ { 2 } \exp { ( 2 a ) } - ( 1 + a ) ^ { 2 } .
$$

The equation $\rho ^ { 2 } \exp { ( 2 a ) } - ( 1 + a ) ^ { 2 } = 0 \ :$ viewed as an equation in $^ { a }$ ， admits three real roots since $0 < \rho < 1$ .One root is smaller than $^ { - 1 }$ ， the second is between $- 1$ and O,and the third is positive.The points satisfying $| ( 1 + a ) ^ { 2 } + b ^ { 2 } |$ exp(-2a)< p² are in the shaded area of Figure 9 on the complex plane of ${ \pmb w }$ .The area for which

$$
| ( 1 + a ) ^ { 2 } + b ^ { 2 } | \exp { ( 2 a ) } < \rho ^ { 2 }
$$

is symmetric to this area with respect to the $b$ -axis.Hence the condition for convergence is $O A _ { 1 } < O A _ { 3 }$ This is satisfied for small values of $\rho , \mathrm { \mathbf { u p } }$ to the value of $\rho$ such that

$$
\rho ^ { 2 } = ( 1 + a ) ^ { 2 } \exp { ( - 2 a ) } = ( 1 - a ) ^ { 2 } \exp { ( 2 a ) } .
$$

This value of $\pmb { \rho }$ is larger than unity for any positive value of $^ { a }$ ,because a = coth $^ { a }$ ， $\rho = \sqrt { a ^ { 2 } - 1 }$ This is equal to the value $\pmb { \rho }$ in (143).

# VARIATION OF CONSTANTS

In formula (70), Section 5.6, put

$$
H ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \kappa = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \left( \frac { y _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } ^ { \prime 2 } } { 2 m _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } ^ { \prime } } + \frac { y _ { 3 \kappa - 1 } ^ { \prime 2 } } { 2 m _ { 3 \kappa - 1 } ^ { \prime } } + \frac { y _ { 3 \kappa } ^ { \prime 2 } } { 2 m _ { 3 \kappa } ^ { \prime } } \right) - \sum _ { \kappa = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \frac { m _ { 3 } m _ { 3 \kappa } } { \left( x _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } ^ { \prime 2 } + x _ { 3 \kappa - 1 } ^ { \prime 2 } + x _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } \right) }
$$

$$
H ^ { \prime \prime } = \sum _ { \kappa = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \frac { m _ { 3 } m _ { 3 \kappa } } { ( x _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } ^ { \prime 2 } + x _ { 3 \kappa - 1 } ^ { \prime 2 } + x _ { 3 \kappa } ^ { \prime 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } - \sum _ { \tau , \nu = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { m _ { 3 \tau } m _ { 3 \nu } } { r _ { 3 \tau , 3 \nu } } ,
$$

$$
H = H ^ { \prime } + H ^ { \prime \prime } ,
$$

and suppose that any mass $m _ { i }$ other than $m _ { 1 } = m _ { 2 } = m _ { 3 }$ is of magnitude $\mu \ll 1$ . From (63), Section 5.6,

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle x _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } ^ { \prime } = x _ { 3 ( \kappa + 1 ) - 2 } - x _ { 1 } + \frac { m _ { 4 } x _ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + m _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } x _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } } { m _ { 1 } + m _ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot + m _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } } } } \\ { { - \displaystyle \sum _ { \tau = 2 } ^ { \kappa } \left( m _ { 3 \tau - 2 } x _ { 3 \tau - 2 } \right) \Biggl / \left( \displaystyle \sum _ { \tau = 1 } ^ { \kappa } m _ { 3 \tau - 2 } \right) ; } } \end{array}
$$

therefore the difference between $( x _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } ^ { \prime 2 } + x _ { 3 \kappa - 1 } ^ { \prime 2 } + x _ { 3 \kappa } ^ { \prime 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 }$ and $\pmb { r _ { 3 , 3 \kappa - 3 } }$ is of magnitude $\pmb { \mu }$ .Hence, the difference between the first sum on the right-hand side of $H ^ { \prime \prime }$ and the second sum extending to $\tau = 1$ ， $\nu = 2 , 3 , . . . , n$ is of magnitude $\mu$ .A term with $\tau \neq 1$ ， $\nu \neq 1$ is of magnitude $\mu ^ { 2 }$ Hence, $H ^ { \prime \prime }$ has a factor $\mu$ .Accordingly, $H ^ { \prime \prime }$ is of magnitude $\mu$ compared with $H ^ { \prime }$ , and can be regarded as the perturbation. At first we consider $H ^ { \prime }$ only and determine the intermediary orbit.

We have

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle { H ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { \kappa = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } H _ { \kappa } ^ { \prime } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { H _ { \kappa } ^ { \prime } = \sum _ { 2 m _ { 3 \kappa } ^ { \prime } } ^ { 1 } ( y _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } ^ { \prime 2 } + y _ { 3 \kappa - 1 } ^ { \prime 2 } + y _ { 3 \kappa } ^ { \prime 2 } ) - \frac { m _ { 3 } m _ { 3 \kappa } } { ( x _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } ^ { \prime 2 } + x _ { 3 \kappa - 1 } ^ { \prime 2 } + x _ { 3 \kappa } ^ { \prime 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 } } } . } } \end{array}
$$

Hence, the Hamiltonian function is separable,

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H _ { \kappa } ^ { \prime } } { \partial y _ { i } } , \frac { d y _ { i } } { d t } = \triangledown - \frac { \partial H _ { \kappa } ^ { \prime } } { \partial x _ { i } } \quad  &  \binom { i = 3 \kappa - 2 , 3 \kappa - 1 , 3 \kappa ; } { \kappa = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - 1 } .
$$

Referring to the notation of Sections 5.9 and 5.10, write $\beta _ { \kappa } = m _ { 3 \kappa } ^ { \prime } m _ { 3 } m _ { 3 \kappa }$ in place of $\beta$ and $m _ { 3 \kappa } ^ { \prime } h _ { 1 }$ in place of $h _ { 1 }$ ； this gives us a set of canonical variables derived from (109), Section 5.10,

$$
h _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } = - \frac { \beta _ { \kappa } } { 2 m _ { 3 \kappa } ^ { \prime } a } , \qquad h _ { 3 \kappa - 1 } = \sqrt { \beta _ { \kappa } a _ { \kappa } ( 1 - e _ { \kappa } ^ { 2 } ) } ,
$$

$$
h _ { 3 \kappa } = \sqrt { \beta _ { \kappa } a _ { \kappa } ( 1 - \ell _ { \kappa } ^ { 2 } ) } \cos I _ { \kappa } ,
$$

$$
\gamma _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } = - t _ { \kappa } , \qquad \gamma _ { 3 \kappa - 1 } = \varpi _ { \kappa } - \Omega _ { \kappa } , \qquad \gamma _ { 3 \kappa } = \Omega _ { \kappa } .
$$

Hence，by the principle of variation of constants stated in Sections 1.12-1.17, the differential equations for the variation of elements are

$$
\frac { d \gamma _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial h _ { i } } , \quad \frac { d h _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial \gamma _ { i } } \quad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n - 3 ) .
$$

Now transform the variables in (160). Omit the suffixes $\pmb { \kappa }$ .From the relations $n = \sqrt { \beta m ^ { \prime } } / a ^ { 3 / 2 }$ ， $h _ { 1 } = - \beta / 2 a$ of Section 5.10 we obtain $n = \sqrt { m ^ { \prime } } ( - 2 h _ { 1 } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } / \beta .$ . The Hamiltonian $H ^ { \prime \prime }$ is a function of $x _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n - 3 } ^ { \prime } .$ The latter variables are expressed in terms of elliptic motions,and hence are periodic functions of $M$ in the expansions described in Sections 5.9-5.15, in particular (l05) of Section 5.10.

In the present notation they are multiply periodic functions of arguments $n _ { \kappa } ( 1 \ + \ \gamma _ { 3 \kappa } )$ . But,as $n _ { \kappa }$ is a function of $h _ { 3 \kappa - 2 }$ ，the time $t$ appears explicitly in front of the periodic function in the right-hand member of (l6l).In order to avoid this inconvenience we make the following transformation:

First, put $l = n ( t + \gamma )$ ; then

$$
\frac { d h } { d t } = - n \frac { \partial H ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial t } \cdot
$$

Furthermore,

$$
\frac { d l } { d t } = n \bigg ( 1 + \frac { d \gamma } { d t } \bigg ) + ( t + \gamma ) \frac { \partial n } { \partial h } \frac { d h } { d t } \cdot
$$

Let $( \partial H ^ { \prime \prime } / \partial h )$ be the result of differentiating $H ^ { \prime \prime }$ with respect to $h$ by supposing that $h$ is not contained in $\pmb { n }$ .Then

$$
\frac { \partial H ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial h } = \left( \frac { \partial H ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial h } \right) + \frac { \partial H ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial l } \left( t + \gamma \right) \frac { \partial n } { \partial h } .
$$

If we put $d \gamma / d t = \partial H ^ { \prime \prime } / \partial h$ in the above equation for $d l / d t .$ ，then we obtain

$$
\frac { d l } { d t } = n + n \bigg ( \frac { \partial H ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial h } \bigg ) + n \frac { \partial H ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial l } \left( t + \gamma \right) \frac { \partial n } { \partial h } + \left( t + \gamma \right) \frac { \partial n } { \partial h } \frac { d h } { d t } .
$$

Hence

$$
\frac { d l } { d t } = n + n \bigg ( \frac { \partial H ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial h } \bigg ) .
$$

Put $L = { \sqrt { \beta a } }$ ; then, $h = ~ - \beta ^ { 2 } / ( 2 m ^ { \prime } L ^ { 2 } ) , n = \beta ^ { 2 } / ( m ^ { \prime } L ^ { 3 } )$ and

$$
- n { \frac { \partial H ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial l } } = { \frac { d h } { d t } } = { \frac { \beta ^ { 2 } } { L ^ { 3 } } } { \frac { d L } { d t } } = n { \frac { d L } { d t } } .
$$

Hence, $d L / d t = - \partial H ^ { \prime \prime } / \partial l .$ Furthermore, since

$$
\left( { \frac { \partial H ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial L } } \right) = \left( { \frac { \partial H ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial L } } \right) { \frac { \partial h } { \partial L } } = n \left( { \frac { \partial H ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial h } } \right) ,
$$

we have

$$
\frac { d l } { d t } = n + \Big ( \frac { \partial H ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial L } \Big ) ,
$$

where $\left( { { \partial { H ^ { \prime \prime } } } / { \partial { L } } } \right)$ denotes the result of differentiating under the supposition that $\scriptstyle { \pmb { L } }$ is not contained in $\pmb { n }$ .Put

$$
- \frac { \beta ^ { 2 } } { 2 m ^ { \prime } L ^ { 2 } } + H " = F ;
$$

then

$$
\frac { d L } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial l } , \frac { d l } { d t } = - \frac { \partial F } { \partial L } ,
$$

because

$$
- \frac { \partial F } { \partial L } = n + \frac { \partial H ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial L } .
$$

Now write

$$
F = \sum _ { \kappa = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \frac { \beta _ { \kappa } ^ { 2 } } { 3 m _ { 3 \kappa } ^ { \prime } L _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } - H ^ { \prime \prime }
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { L = \sqrt { \beta a } , \qquad G = \sqrt { \beta a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } , \qquad H = \sqrt { \beta a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } \cos { I } , } \\ & { \ l = n ( t + \gamma ) , \qquad g = \varpi - \Omega , \qquad h = \Omega , } \end{array}
$$

for each $\pmb { \kappa }$ ; then we have the canonical equations

$$
\frac { d L } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial l } , \frac { d G } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial g } , \frac { d H } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial h } ,
$$

$$
{ \frac { d l } { d t } } = \ - { \frac { \partial F } { \partial L } } , { \frac { d g } { d t } } = \ - { \frac { \partial F } { \partial G } } , { \frac { d h } { d t } } = \ - { \frac { \partial F } { \partial H } } .
$$

In particular, when $\mathscr { n } = 3$ ，

$$
m _ { 6 } ^ { \prime } = \frac { m _ { 3 } m _ { 6 } } { m _ { 3 } + m _ { 6 } } , \qquad m _ { 9 } ^ { \prime } = \frac { m _ { 9 } ( m _ { 3 } + m _ { 6 } ) } { m _ { 3 } + m _ { 6 } + m _ { 9 } } ;
$$

accordingly,

$$
\beta _ { 1 } = \frac { k ^ { 2 } m _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } m _ { 6 } ^ { 2 } } { m _ { 3 } + m _ { 6 } } , \qquad \beta _ { 2 } = \frac { k ^ { 2 } m _ { 9 } ^ { 2 } m _ { 3 } ( m _ { 3 } + m _ { 6 } ) } { m _ { 3 } + m _ { 6 } + m _ { 9 } } ,
$$

$$
F = \frac { \beta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { 2 m _ { 6 } ^ { \prime } L _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \beta _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { 2 m _ { 9 } ^ { \prime } L _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { m _ { 6 } m _ { 9 } } { r _ { 6 , 9 } } + \frac { m _ { 3 } m _ { 9 } } { r _ { 3 , 9 } } - \frac { m _ { 3 } m _ { 9 } } { \sqrt { x _ { 4 } ^ { \prime 2 } + x _ { 5 } ^ { \prime 2 } + x _ { 6 } ^ { \prime 2 } } } .
$$

The difference of the last two terms of $F$ is of magnitude $\mu ^ { 2 }$ ; the third term is of magnitude $\mu ^ { 2 }$ . The first two terms are of magnitude $\pmb { \mu }$ ; we say that they are of order 2 and l,respectively, with regard to the disturbing masses. $L , G , H$ are of order l with regard to $\mu$ .These canonical variables $L , G , H , l , g , h$ are called Delaunay's elements (Delaunay,l860,l867).The variables $a , e , I , t _ { 0 } , \varpi , \Omega$ are called the Keplerian elements.Write

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \boldsymbol { F } = F _ { 0 } + \mu \boldsymbol { F } _ { 1 } , } \\ & { \boldsymbol { F } _ { 0 } = \frac { \beta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { 2 m _ { \ell } ^ { 2 } L _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \beta _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { 2 m _ { 9 } ^ { 2 } L _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } , } \\ & { \mu F _ { 1 } = \frac { m _ { 6 } m _ { 9 } } { r _ { 6 , 9 } } + \frac { m _ { 3 } m _ { 9 } } { r _ { 3 , 9 } } - \frac { m _ { 3 } m _ { 9 } } { \sqrt { x _ { 4 } ^ { \prime 2 } + \ x _ { 5 } ^ { \prime 2 } + \ x _ { 6 } ^ { \prime 2 } } } . } \end{array}
$$

The term $\mu F _ { 1 }$ indicates the disturbing function and is multiplied by the factor $\mu$ of the order of the disturbing masses. The term $F _ { 0 }$ appears only in the right-hand member of dl/dt in (164).Let the solution of (164) for $\mu = 0$ be denoted by $\cdot \ : L ^ { \left( 0 \right) } , \ : G ^ { \left( 0 \right) } , \ : H ^ { \left( 0 \right) } , \ : l ^ { \left( 0 \right) } , \ : g ^ { \left( 0 \right) } , \ : h ^ { \left( 0 \right) }$ , and write

$$
n _ { i } = - \frac { 2 \beta _ { i } ^ { 2 } } { 3 m _ { 3 i } ^ { \prime } L _ { i } ^ { 3 } } = \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial L _ { i } } .
$$

Then we obtain $l _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } = n _ { i } ( t + \gamma _ { i } )$ ， with the integration constant $\gamma _ { i }$ ; the other elements are now all constants.Denote the initial values for $t = 0$ of these elements by ${ \cal L } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ { \cal G } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ { \cal H } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ { \cal l } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ g ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ { \cal h } ^ { ( 0 ) } ;$ ，and consider the quantities of magnitude $\pmb { \mu }$ .Then

$$
L _ { i } = L _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } + \mu \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial l _ { i } } d t , \qquad G _ { t } = G _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } + \mu \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial g _ { i } } d t ,
$$

$$
H _ { i } = H _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } + \mu \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial h _ { i } } d t ,
$$

$$
l _ { i } = l _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } - \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial L _ { i } } d t - \mu \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial L _ { i } } d t , \qquad g _ { i } = g _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } - \mu \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial G _ { i } } d t ,
$$

$$
h _ { i } = h _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } - \mu \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial H _ { i } } d t .
$$

The terms with the factor $\mu$ all represent the perturbation. Let $F _ { 0 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ be the result of substituting the values for $\mu = 0 \mathrm { i n } F _ { 0 }$ ; let $\Phi$ be the aggregate of terms of the third and higher orders with regard to $\delta L _ { i }$ ; let $C _ { i j }$ be a function of $L _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ and ${ \cal L } _ { j } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ with $C _ { i j } = C _ { j i }$ . Then,

$$
F _ { 0 } = F _ { 0 } ^ { ( 0 ) } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } n _ { i } \ : \delta L _ { i } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \sum _ { i , j } C _ { i j } \ : \delta L _ { i } \ : \delta L _ { j } + \Phi .
$$

Indicate the effect of the perturbation on the values for $t = 0$ by putting δ in front,and refer to the relation

$$
{ \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial L _ { i } } } = n _ { i } + \sum _ { j = 0 } ^ { n } C _ { i j } \ \delta L _ { j } + { \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial L _ { i } } } = n _ { i } + \mu \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } C _ { i j } \int { \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial l _ { j } } } \ d t + { \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial L _ { i } } } ;
$$

then

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \displaystyle \partial { \cal L } _ { i } = \mu \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial { \cal F } _ { 1 } } { \partial { \cal Z } _ { i } } d t , \qquad \displaystyle \partial { \cal G } _ { \mathrm { i } } = \mu \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial { \cal F } _ { 1 } } { \partial g _ { \mathrm { i } } } d t , \qquad \displaystyle \partial { \cal H } _ { \mathrm { i } } = \mu \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial { \cal Z } _ { \mathrm { i } } } { \partial { \cal Z } _ { \mathrm { i } } } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \displaystyle \partial l _ { i } = - \mu \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } C _ { i j } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } d t \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial { \cal F } _ { 1 } } { \partial { \cal { \cal L } } _ { j } } d t - \displaystyle \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial { \cal { L } } _ { i } } d t - \mu \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial { \cal F } _ { 1 } } { \partial { \cal { \cal L } } _ { i } } d t , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \hat { \delta } q _ { i } = - \mu \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial { \cal F } _ { 1 } } { \partial { \cal G } _ { i } } d t , \qquad \delta h _ { i } = - \mu \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial { \cal F } _ { 1 } } { \partial { \cal H } _ { i } } d t . } } \end{array}
$$

This result has been obtained by Poincaré (1905).

# 5.17POINCARE's ELEMENTS

We carry out the following transformation to Delaunay's elements:

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \Lambda = L , \qquad \Gamma = L - G , \qquad Z = G - H , } \\ { \lambda = l + g + h , \qquad \gamma = - g - h , \qquad z = - h . } \end{array}
$$

This is a contact transformaticn,as we see from any of the methods described in Sections 1.7-1.11,or from the relation

$$
\Lambda \lambda + \Gamma \gamma + Z z = L l + G g + H h .
$$

It is written in the Keplerian elements

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \Lambda = \sqrt { \beta a } , \qquad \Gamma = \sqrt { \beta a } ( 1 - \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } ) , \nonumber } } \\ { { \qquad \ } } \\ { { Z = \sqrt { \beta a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } ( 1 - \cos I ) , \nonumber } } \\ { { \qquad \lambda = M + \varpi , \qquad \gamma = - \varpi , \qquad z = - \Omega . } } \end{array}
$$

$\lambda$ is the mean longitude in the orbit, $\Gamma$ is of magnitude $e ^ { 2 }$ and $z$ is of magnitude $I ^ { 2 }$

Put

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \xi = \sqrt { 2 \Gamma } \cos \gamma , \quad } & { { } \beta = \sqrt { 2 Z } \cos z , } \\ { \eta = \sqrt { 2 \Gamma } \sin \gamma , \quad } & { { } q = \sqrt { 2 Z } \sin z . } \end{array}
$$

This is also a contact transformation because

$$
\xi d \eta = \cos \gamma \sin \gamma d \Gamma + 2 \Gamma \cos ^ { 2 } \gamma d \gamma = { \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } d ( \Gamma \sin 2 \gamma ) + \Gamma d \gamma .
$$

$\xi , \eta$ are called the eccentric variables, and $\phi , q$ the oblique variables.

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \Lambda = \sqrt { \beta a } , \qquad \xi = \sqrt { 2 \Lambda ( 1 - \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } ) } \cos \varpi , } \\ & { \beta = \sqrt { 2 \Lambda \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } ( 1 - \cos I ) } \cos \Omega , } \\ & { \lambda = M + \varpi , \qquad \eta = - \sqrt { 2 \Lambda ( 1 - \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } ) } \sin \varpi , } \\ & { q = - \sqrt { 2 \Lambda \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } ( 1 - \cos I ) } \sin \Omega . } \end{array}
$$

From these equations,

$$
\left( \frac { \xi } { \sqrt { \Lambda } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { \eta } { \sqrt { \Lambda } } \right) ^ { 2 } = 2 ( 1 - \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } ) = e ^ { 2 } + { \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 4 } } } e ^ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

Therefore,

$$
e ^ { 2 } = \left( \frac { \xi } { \sqrt { \Lambda } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { \eta } { \sqrt { \Lambda } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 4 } \left[ \left( \frac { \xi } { \sqrt { \Lambda } } \right) ^ { \overset { . } { 2 } } + \left( \frac { \eta } { \sqrt { \Lambda } } \right) ^ { 2 } \right] ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

Since ${ \sqrt { 2 ( 1 - { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } ) } } = e ( 1 + { \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 8 } } } e ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot )$ and

$$
\frac { \xi } { \sqrt { \Lambda } } = \epsilon \cos \varpi \bigg ( 1 + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { \infty } \alpha _ { j } \epsilon ^ { 2 j } \bigg ) , \frac { \eta } { \sqrt { \Lambda } } = - \epsilon \sin \varpi \bigg ( 1 + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { \infty } \alpha _ { j } \epsilon ^ { 2 j } \bigg )
$$

with numerical coefficients $\alpha _ { j }$ , therefore,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle e \cos \varpi = \frac { \xi } { \sqrt \Lambda } \biggl \{ 1 - \frac { 1 } { 8 } \left[ \biggl ( \frac { \xi } { \sqrt \Lambda } \biggr ) ^ { 2 } + \biggl ( \frac { \eta } { \sqrt \Lambda } \biggr ) ^ { 2 } \right] + \cdot \cdot \cdot \biggr \} , } } \\ { { \displaystyle e \sin \varpi = - \frac { \eta } { \sqrt \Lambda } \biggl \{ 1 - \frac { 1 } { 8 } \left[ \biggl ( \frac { \xi } { \sqrt \Lambda } \biggr ) ^ { 2 } + \biggl ( \frac { \eta } { \sqrt \Lambda } \biggr ) ^ { 2 } \right] + \cdot \cdot \cdot \biggr \} . } } \end{array}
$$

Hence $e \cos \varpi$ and $\textit { e s i n } { \varpi }$ can be expanded in integral power series of $\xi / \sqrt { \Lambda }$ and $\eta / \sqrt { \Lambda }$ Conversely, $\xi / \sqrt { \Lambda }$ and $\eta / \sqrt { \Lambda }$ can be expanded in integral power series of $\ell \cos \varpi$ and $\ e \sin \ \varpi$ Thus, $e \cos { \varpi }$ ， $\pmb { \ell }$ sin $\boldsymbol { \varpi }$ ， sin $( I / 2 )$ cos $\Omega$ ，sin $( I / 2 )$ sin $\Omega$ can be expanded in integral power series of $\xi / \sqrt { \Lambda } , \eta / \sqrt { \Lambda } , \phi / \sqrt { \Lambda } , q / \sqrt { \Lambda }$ The converse is also true.

Poincaré（l905；see also Charlier，1927；Picart,1906） treated the three-body problem in this manner.The coefficients of the expansion in the Keplerian elements are available in tabular form,as will be seen in a later chapter,but the coefficients of the expansion in Delaunay's or

Poincaré's elements are scarcely ever published (Noren and Walberg, 1910).So the expansion in either of these latter elements is not of much use,except in theoretical discussion. Delaunay himself has avoided making complete reference to his canonical variables in his lunar theory (Delaunay, 1860, 1867).

By the expansions for an elliptic motion in Sections 5.9-5.15 the expressions $u \mathrm { ~ - ~ } M ,$ cos $( u \mathrm { ~ - ~ } M )$ ，sin $( u \mathrm { ~ - ~ } M )$ can be expanded in integral power series of e cos $M _ { ; }$ esin $M .$ Similarly, $e ^ { 2 }$ cos $( u + M )$ ， $e ^ { 2 }$ sin $( u + M )$ can be expanded. Hence, the expressions $X , ~ Y$ in (106) of Section 5.10

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \frac { X } { a } = \cos \left( u - M \right) \frac { 1 + \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } { 2 } } } \\ { ~ } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + \varepsilon ^ { 2 } \cos \left( u + M \right) \frac { 1 - \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } { 2 e ^ { 2 } } - \varepsilon \cos M } , }  \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { Y } { a } = \sin \left( u - M \right) \frac { 1 + \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } { 2 } } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ - \varepsilon ^ { 2 } \sin \left( u + M \right) \frac { 1 - \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } { 2 e ^ { 2 } } + \varepsilon \sin M } , }  \end{array}
$$

can be expanded in integral power series of e cos $M$ $\pmb { e }$ sin $M$ because

$$
e ^ { 2 } = e ^ { 2 } \cos ^ { 2 } M + e ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } M .
$$

As

$$
e \cos M = e \cos \varpi \cos \lambda - e \sin \varpi \sin \lambda ,
$$

$$
e \sin M = e \sin \varpi \cos \lambda + e \cos \varpi \sin \lambda ,
$$

the expressions e cos $M$ ， $^ { e }$ sin $M$ are Fourier series in $\lambda$ with the Fourier coefficients expressed as integral power series of $\xi / \sqrt { \Lambda } , \eta / \sqrt { \Lambda }$ . We have similar Fourier expansions for $X , Y .$

The coefficients of $X , Y$ in (108) of Section 5.10 are of the form

$$
\pm \cos ^ { 2 } \frac { I } { 2 } \cos \lambda \pm \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { I \cos } { 2 } \sin \lambda + 2 h ) \qquad \mathrm { o r } \qquad \sin I _ { \sin } ^ { \cos } ( \lambda + h ) .
$$

Their coefficients are expanded in integral power series of

$$
\sin { \frac { I } { 2 } } \cos \Omega , \qquad \sin { \frac { I } { 2 } } \sin \Omega ,
$$

and accordingly they are expanded in integral power series of $\xi / { \sqrt { \Lambda } } _ { \mathrm { { ; } } }$ $\eta / { \sqrt { \Lambda } } , \rho / { \sqrt { \Lambda } } , q / { \sqrt { \Lambda } }$ Hence, $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , x _ { 3 }$ are expanded in integral power series of these latter quantities and are of the form $\begin{array} { r } { \sum A N \cos { ( j \lambda + b ) } . } \end{array}$ ， where: (i), $N$ is an integral power series of $\xi , \eta , \phi , q$ ； (ii), $A$ and $b$ depend only on $\Lambda$ ; and (ii), $j$ is a positive or negative integer. Thus using (170) we can write the expansion in the form

$$
\sum A ^ { \prime } \Gamma ^ { s _ { 1 } } Z ^ { s _ { 2 } } \cos { ( j \lambda + j _ { 1 } \gamma + j _ { 2 } z + b ^ { \prime } ) } ,
$$

where $A ^ { \prime } , \ b ^ { \prime }$ depend only on $\Lambda$ ,and $j , j _ { 1 } , j _ { 2 }$ are positive or negative integers. The integers $2 s _ { 1 } , 2 s _ { 2 }$ are positive or zero, such that

$$
2 s _ { i } \geq | j _ { i } | \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 ) .
$$

A similar expansion can be obtained with respect to the Keplerian elements.

The perturbative term $\mu F _ { 1 }$ of (167) can also be expanded in a similar form，because ${ x _ { 3 \kappa } } _ { - 2 }$ ， ${ x _ { 3 \kappa } } _ { - 1 }$ ， ${ x } _ { 3 \kappa }$ are expanded in integral powers of $\xi _ { \kappa } , \eta _ { \kappa } , \ p _ { \kappa } , \ q _ { \nu }$ Actually, $\mu F _ { 1 }$ ceases to be regular with respect to $x _ { 3 \kappa - 2 }$ ${ x } _ { 3 \kappa - 1 }$ ， ${ x } _ { 3 \kappa }$ only when any one of the mutual distances of the $\pmb { n }$ bodies becomes zero or when $( x _ { 3 \kappa - 2 } ^ { \prime 2 } + x _ { 3 \kappa - 1 } ^ { \prime 2 } + x _ { 3 \kappa } ^ { \prime 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 }$ becomes zero. For the moment we ignore such cases and leave their discussion to a later chapter. In such cases the word“perturbation’’ loses its meaning；we cannot consider the actual motion as a small deviation from the intermediary orbit (in the present case an eliptic motion)，so that a completely different mode of treatment should be adopted. For convenience we limit ourselves to the case of three bodies； then the expansions are represented in the series form

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \quad F = F _ { 0 } + \mu F _ { 1 } , \quad \quad \quad \quad ( \quad ) } \\ & { \quad \mu F _ { 1 } = \sum A _ { s _ { 1 } s _ { 2 } s _ { 3 } s _ { 4 } } \Gamma ^ { s _ { 1 } } Z ^ { s _ { 2 } } \Gamma ^ { \prime s _ { 3 } } Z ^ { \prime s _ { 4 } } \cos { [ j \lambda + j ^ { \prime } \lambda ^ { \prime } + j _ { 1 } \gamma + j _ { 2 } \gamma ^ { \prime } + j _ { 3 } z + j _ { 4 } z + j _ { 4 } z ^ { \prime } ] } . } \end{array}
$$

where

$\pmb { A }$ is a function of $\Lambda , \Lambda ^ { \prime }$ ， $\Gamma ^ { \prime }$ is of magnitude $e ^ { 2 }$ ，

and

$z$ is of magnitude $\scriptstyle { I ^ { 2 } }$

The representative term written here is of degree $2 ( s _ { 1 } + s _ { 2 } + s _ { 3 } + s _ { 4 } )$ with respect to $\boldsymbol { e }$ and $\boldsymbol { \mathbf { \mathit { I } } } .$ This is called the degree of the term. Note that $F _ { 0 }$ is a function of $\Lambda$ and $\Lambda ^ { \prime }$ only and is not multiplied by any quantity of the order of the disturbing masses.

The quantities $\Lambda$ and $\Lambda ^ { \prime }$ are constant multiples of $\sqrt { a }$ and $\sqrt { a ^ { \prime } }$ ， respectively; $F _ { 0 }$ is the sum of constant multiples of $L ^ { - 2 }$ and $L ^ { \prime } ^ { - 2 }$ As $\mu F _ { 1 }$ is of degree $^ { - 1 }$ with regard to the length, so is the function $F$ itself.

Hence,the coefficient $A$ of (173） is homogeneous of degree -1 with regard to $a , a ^ { \prime }$ , and homogeneous of degree $- 2$ with regard to $\Lambda , \Lambda ^ { \prime }$ .Let

$$
\mathcal D = j \lambda + j ^ { \prime } \lambda ^ { \prime } + j _ { 1 } \gamma + j _ { 2 } \gamma ^ { \prime } + j _ { 3 } z + j _ { 4 } z ^ { \prime } ,
$$

that is, the quantity under the cosine in (l73).We call $\mathcal { D }$ the argument. From (l04）of Section 5.10,we have $\lambda = n t + \epsilon , \ \lambda ^ { \prime } = n ^ { \prime } t + \epsilon ^ { \prime }$ If $j = j ^ { \prime } = 0$ ，then $t$ does not appear explicitly in the argument,and the corresponding term does not contain $t$ explicitly. Such a constant term is called a secular term. If $j n + j ^ { \prime } n ^ { \prime } = 0$ ， $j \neq 0$ ， $j ^ { \prime } \neq 0$ ，then a similar situation occurs; this is when $n { : } n ^ { \prime }$ is rational, and the corresponding term is called a critical term.

The function $F _ { \mathrm { : } }$ ,being a function of the mutual distances of the three bodies,does not depend on the sense of the longitude. Consequently, $F$ remains unaltered when we change the signs of $\lambda , \lambda ^ { \prime } , \gamma , \gamma ^ { \prime } , z , z ^ { \prime }$ simultaneously.Hence $b ^ { \prime }$ ,representing the phase of the argument, should be zero and each term should be of cosine form.The differences

$$
2 s _ { i } - | j _ { i } | \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 ) ,
$$

are even positive integers including O, where $s _ { i }$ is a positive integral multiple of $\textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } }$ Since ${ \pmb F }$ also remains unaltered by the reversal of the sense in which the inclination is taken, the sums $2 s _ { 3 } + 2 s _ { 4 }$ and $j _ { 3 } + j _ { 4 }$ should be even integers. Since $F$ remains unaltered by the rotation through δ in longitude of the coordinate axes around the origin，we obtain $j + j ^ { \prime } + j _ { 1 } + j _ { 2 } + j _ { 3 } + j _ { 4 } = 0$ 、Finally, the degree of a term of $F _ { 1 }$ is equal to or higher than the sum of the coefficients of $\lambda , \lambda ^ { \prime }$ in the argument; that is,

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { 2 s _ { 1 } + 2 s _ { 2 } + 2 s _ { 3 } + 2 s _ { 4 } \geq | j _ { 1 } | + | j _ { 2 } | + | j _ { 3 } | + | j _ { 4 } | } \\ & { \qquad \geq | j _ { 1 } + j _ { 2 } + j _ { 3 } + j _ { 4 } | \geq | j + j ^ { \prime } | . } \end{array}
$$

Brown and Shook (l933) have called a series of this nature a $\mathbfcal { D } ^ { \ast }$ Alembert series. If we use Poincaré's elements, then the argument is $j \lambda + j ^ { \prime } \lambda ^ { \prime }$ and the coefficient has the factor $\xi ^ { s } { \eta } ^ { s } { _ 1 } { p } ^ { s } { \sf { s } } q ^ { s } { _ 3 }$ for one of the planets,and the factor $\xi ^ { \prime s } { _ { 2 } } \eta ^ { \prime s } { _ { 2 } } \phi ^ { \prime s } { _ { 4 } } { q ^ { \prime s } } _ { 4 } ^ { \prime }$ for the other planet instead of $\Gamma$ ， $z$ .The degree of the term is $s _ { 1 } + s _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + s _ { 2 } + s _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } + s _ { 3 } + s _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } + s _ { 4 } + s _ { 4 } ^ { \prime }$ .Thus the differential equations are

$$
\frac { d \Gamma } { d t } = \mu \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial \gamma } , ~ \frac { d Z } { d t } = \mu \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial z } ,
$$

$$
\frac { d \lambda } { d t } = - \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial \Lambda } - \mu \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial \Lambda } , \qquad \frac { d \gamma } { d t } = - \mu \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial \Gamma } , \qquad \frac { d z } { d t } = - \mu \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial z } .
$$

A similar argument can be applied to other bodies.If the Hamiltonian function has the form (l56)and (157),then $F$ — given in (162)—is common to all bodies,and the differential equations are in canonical form.The element $\lambda$ ,as can be seen from (l74), varies rapidly compared with the other quantities, which vary slowly in the order of the disturbing mass $\mu$ . Note that $F _ { 0 }$ contains only $\Lambda$ and $\Lambda ^ { \prime }$ ; also, $\mu F _ { 1 }$ is a trigonometric series with the sum of integral multiples of $\lambda , \lambda ^ { \prime } , \gamma , \gamma ^ { \prime }$ ,Z, $z ^ { \prime }$ as argument, and each coefficient is an integral power series of $\Gamma , Z , \Gamma ^ { \prime } , Z ^ { \prime } { } _ { ; }$ ,multiplied by a function of $\Lambda$ ， $\Lambda ^ { \prime }$ .This is the fundamental form of the equations in the perturbation theory of celestial mechanics that will be the main topic of the following discussions.

Corresponding to (164a),we obtain from (l74) the expressions for the perturbation for time $t > 0$ ：

$$
\delta \Lambda = \mu \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial \lambda } d t , \qquad \delta \Gamma = \mu \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial \gamma } d t , \qquad \delta Z = \mu \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial z } d t ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \delta \lambda = - \mu \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } C _ { i j } \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } d t \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial \Lambda } d t - \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial \Lambda } d t - \mu \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial \Lambda } d t , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \delta \gamma = - \mu \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial \Gamma } d t , \qquad \delta z = - \mu \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial Z } d t . } } \end{array}
$$

These expressions are important for the discussion of the form of integrals in perturbation theory (Poincaré, 1905).

Each term in the solution of (174) or in (174a) in the form of trigonometric series corresponds to a term in the trigonometric series expansion of $\mu F _ { 1 }$ with the same argument. According to the method of integration, the degree or order of a term may differ from the degree or order of the corresponding term in the expansion of the disturbing function. Each term in the trignometric series for the expansion of the perturbation is called an inequality. Even though the expansion of the disturbing function may be convergent, it does not necessarily follow that the resulting solution for the perturbation is convergent. Indeed,the trigonometric series obtained in this manner is not,as a rule,uniformly convergent. Here we meet the greatest difficulty in perturbation theory. The theory at present resolves itself into the question; what is the best method of expansion for practical use, in which we can best approximate the rigorous solution by computing as few terms as possble from the series representation thus obtained? The series representation to be studied for such a purpose may be different for different planetary systems,for different satellite systems,or for artificial celestial vehicles. The same situation occurs when we use the Keplerian elements. As a matter of fact, the ordinary perturbation theory uses the Keplerian elements,as will be seen in Volume II.

# 5.18 KEPLERIAN ELEMENTS

In the Keplerian elements the expansion of the disturbing function is, as is easily verified,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \sum \mathcal { C } \cos \mathcal { D } , \qquad } \\ { \mathcal { C } = \mathcal { A } e ^ { q _ { 1 } } e ^ { \prime q _ { 2 } } ( \tan I ) ^ { q _ { 3 } } ( \tan I ^ { \prime } ) ^ { q _ { 4 } } , \qquad } \\ { \mathcal { D } = j \lambda + j ^ { \prime } \lambda ^ { \prime } - j _ { 1 } \varpi - j _ { 2 } \varpi ^ { \prime } - j _ { 3 } \Omega - j _ { 4 } \Omega ^ { \prime } , } \end{array}
$$

where $\mathcal { A }$ has the same character as $\Lambda$ in (173),so that: (i),it is homo-geneous of degree $^ { - 1 }$ with respect to the two semimajor axes $^ { a }$ and $a ^ { \prime }$ ； (ii), $q _ { i } - | j _ { i } |$ $( i = 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 )$ are even positive integers including O, and; (ii), $q _ { 3 , } + q _ { 4 } \mathrm { a n d } j _ { 3 } + j _ { 4 }$ are both even, ir $\scriptstyle { 1 j + j ^ { \prime } + j _ { 1 } + j _ { 2 } + j _ { 3 } + j _ { 4 } = 0 }$ These are the necessary characteristics of a $D '$ Alembert series.

The Delaunay elements also have this character,as is evident from (168).The expressions $1 - G ^ { 2 } / L ^ { 2 } , 1 / 2 - H / ( 2 G )$ in terms of the Delaunay elements are, respectively, $e ^ { 2 } , 4 \sin ^ { 2 } { ( I / 2 ) }$ and take the place of $\Gamma$ ， $z$ in terms of the Poincaré elements,which are, respectively, $e ^ { 2 }$ ， $\tan ^ { 2 } I$ in terms of the Keplerian elements.Hence,the expansion of the disturbing function is of the form

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { { \displaystyle \sum _ { } A ( L , L ^ { \prime } ) \Big ( 1 - \frac { G ^ { 2 } } { L ^ { 2 } } \Big ) ^ { s _ { 1 } } \Big ( 1 - \frac { G ^ { \prime 2 } } { L ^ { \prime 2 } } \Big ) ^ { s _ { 2 } } \Big ( \frac { 1 } { 2 } - \frac { H } { 2 G } \Big ) ^ { s _ { 3 } } \Big ( \frac { 1 } { 2 } - \frac { H ^ { \prime } } { 2 G ^ { \prime } } \Big ) ^ { s _ { 4 } } } } } \\ { { \times \cos { [ j l + j ^ { \prime } l ^ { \prime } + ( j - j _ { 1 } ) g + ( j ^ { \prime } - j _ { 2 } ) g ^ { \prime } } } } \\ { { + ( j - j _ { 1 } - j _ { 3 } ) h + ( j ^ { \prime } - j _ { 2 } - j _ { 4 } ) h ^ { \prime } ] } . } \end{array}
$$

In his lunar theory Delaunay used the noncanonical elements

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { a } & { { } \mathrm { f o r } \quad L ^ { 2 } / \beta , } \\ { \ e } & { { } \mathrm { f o r } \quad \Big ( 1 - \frac { G ^ { 2 } } { L ^ { 2 } } \Big ) ^ { 1 / 2 } , } \end{array}
$$

The second set of the Delaunay canonical equations (164) is replaced by

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { \partial F } { \partial L } = \frac { 2 } { n a } \frac { \partial F } { \partial a } + \frac { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } { n a ^ { 2 } e } \frac { \partial F } { \partial e } , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { \partial F } { \partial G } = - \frac { \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } { n a e } \frac { \partial F } { \partial e } + \frac { 1 - 2 \gamma ^ { 2 } } { 4 n a ^ { 2 } \gamma \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } \frac { \partial F } { \partial \gamma } , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { \partial F } { \partial H } = - \frac { 1 } { 4 n a ^ { 2 } \gamma \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } \frac { \partial F } { \partial \gamma } \qquad ( n ^ { 2 } a ^ { 3 } = \beta ) . } \end{array}
$$

Denote any one of the Keplerian elements by $E _ { i }$ ; then by the formulas of Section 1.14, with the Poisson brackets $( E _ { i } , E _ { j } )$ , we obtain the equations

$$
\frac { d E _ { i } } { d t } = - \sum _ { j } \left( E _ { i } , E _ { j } \right) \frac { \partial F } { \partial E _ { j } }
$$

for the variation of the Keplerian elements. Since these formulas are not canonical, we must refer as the standard to the elliptic motion of the body in question relative to the nth body-see (72),Section 5.7, on Poincaré's transformation $\gamma$ ，Then the following fundamental formulas (the most important in practical dynamical astronomy） are obtained for the variation of the Keplerian elements,similarly to (l63) and (164):

$$
\frac { d a } { d t } = 2 \sqrt { \frac { a } { \beta } } \frac { \partial R } { \partial \epsilon } ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \frac { d } { d t } } & { = - \frac { \omega _ { 0 } + \beta \delta } { \sqrt { \beta } } \frac { d } { d t } \frac { \nabla \cdot \mathbf { n } + \lambda \cdot \mathbf { d } \cdot \boldsymbol { d } \cdot \mathbf { d } \cdot \mathbf { d } \mathbf { \delta } } { \sqrt { \beta } } \frac { d } { \omega t } , } \\ { \frac { d } { d t } } & { = - \frac { 1 } { \omega _ { 0 } + \beta \cdot \mathbf { n } + \lambda \cdot \nabla \cdot \mathbf { d } \cdot \mathbf { d } \cdot \mathbf { d } \cdot \mathbf { \delta } } \frac { d \delta } { \omega t } - \frac { \lambda \cdot \mathbf { d } \cdot \mathbf { d } \cdot \mathbf { \delta } } { \sqrt { \omega t } } \frac { d } { ( 2 \pi \omega + \frac { \beta \delta } { \omega t } ) } \frac { d } { ( 2 \pi \omega + \frac { \beta \delta } { \omega t } ) } , } \\ { \frac { d } { d t } } & { = \frac { \omega _ { 0 } + \beta \cdot \mathbf { n } } { \omega _ { 0 } + \omega _ { \mathrm { m } } + \beta \cdot \mathbf { n } } \frac { d } { d t } , } \\ { \frac { d } { d t } } & { = \frac { \omega _ { 0 } + \beta \cdot \delta \cdot \mathbf { n } } { \sqrt { \beta } } \frac { d } { d t } + \frac { \omega _ { 0 } + \beta \cdot \mathbf { n } } { \sqrt { \beta } } \frac { d } { d t } , } \\ { \frac { d } { d t } } & { = - 2 \int _ { 0 } ^ { \infty } \frac { d } { d t } \frac { \partial \cdot \mathbf { n } } { \sqrt { \beta } } \frac { d } { d t } \frac { \partial \cdot \mathbf { n } + \lambda \cdot \mathbf { d } \cdot \mathbf { d } \cdot \mathbf { \delta } } { \sqrt { \omega t } } + \frac { \delta \cdot \mathbf { n } } { \sqrt { \beta } \cdot \mathbf { n } + \lambda \cdot \nabla \cdot \mathbf { d } \cdot \mathbf { \delta } } \frac { d } { \omega t } } \\ & { = \mathrm { i n } \omega _ { \mathrm { b } } + \frac { \omega _ { 0 } - \beta \cdot \mathbf { \delta } } { \sqrt { \omega t } } - \frac { \lambda \cdot \mathbf { d } \cdot \mathbf { \delta } } { \sqrt { \omega t } } \frac { d } { \omega t } \frac { d } { \omega t } \frac { d } { \omega t } \frac { d } { \omega t } } \\ &  = \kappa ^ { 2 } \sum _  m _ { 0 } < \frac  m _  0  \end{array}
$$

It is s n (173a), the mea nts of the trigonometric functions,and the semimajor axis $^ { a }$ is supposed to be present only in their coeffcients.Then $R$ is differentiated partially with respect to the Keplerian elements and $\mathbf { \boldsymbol { \epsilon } } ^ { \prime }$ substituted for $\epsilon$ in $\lambda$ ，such that $\epsilon + n t = \epsilon ^ { \prime } + \int \bar { n }$ dt. Here put

$$
\rho \equiv \int n d t = \sqrt { \beta } \int a ^ { - 3 / 2 } d t ;
$$

then

$$
\rho = - 3 \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } d t \int _ { 0 } ^ { t } \frac { 1 } { a ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial R } { \partial \epsilon } d t .
$$

Ordinarily $R$ is called the disturbing function.The first term of $R$ is called its direct part and the second term its indirect part.It is customary in the treatment of planetary motion to take the sun as the nth body,and in the treatment of satellite motion to take the planet as the nth body.

The variation of elements in (175) is expressed by the partial derivatives of the disturbing function $R$ with regard to the Keplerian elements. It is sometimes useful to express this variation by the components of the disturbing force.Let $s$ be the component of the disturbing force in the direction of the instantaneous radius vector; let $_ T$ be the component, in the instantaneous orbital plane, perpendicular to the radius vector in the sense of the motion; and let $W$ be the component perpendicular to the instantaneous orbital plane,making a right-handed system with radius vector and the instantaneous velocity vector. The partial derivatives of $R$ in these directions - that is,the components of grad $R$ — are the components of the disturbing force in those directions.As these vectors are expressed by the Keplerian elements,those partial derivatives can easily be transformed to the partial derivatives with respect to the Keplerian elements.Such formulas for the variation of the elements in terms of $S , \ T , \ W _ { \colon }$ ，can be directly derived even when there does not exist the potential $- R$ for the disturbing force.The result (Tisserand, 1889；Charlier，1927；Plummer，1918；Brown，1896；Brown and Shook,1933；Andoyer，1923；Moulton，1923；Dziobek,1888；Smart, 1953;Danjon, 1953; Brouwer and Clemence,l96l） is:

$$
\frac { d a } { d t } = 2 \sqrt { \frac { a ^ { 3 } } { \beta } } \{ S \tan \phi \cos v + T \sec \phi ( 1 + e \cos v ) \} ,
$$

$$
\frac { d \epsilon } { d t } = \sqrt { \frac { a } { \beta } } \cos \phi \{ S \sin v + T ( \cos v + \cos u ) \} ,
$$

$$
\frac { d I } { d t } = r W \cos { ( \upsilon + \varpi - \Omega ) } / ( \sqrt { \beta a } \cos { \phi } ) ,
$$

$$
\frac { d \Omega } { d t } = r W \sin { \left( v + \varpi - \Omega \right) } / ( \sqrt { \beta a } \cos { \phi } \sin { I } ) ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { d \varpi } { d t } = \biggl \{ - a S \cos ^ { 2 } \phi \cos v + r T \sin v ( 2 + e \cos v ) } \\ { \displaystyle \qquad + r W \sin \phi \tan \frac { I } { 2 } \sin \left( v + \varpi - \Omega \right) \biggr \} / ( \sqrt { \beta a } \sin \phi \cos \phi ) , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d \epsilon } { d t } = - \frac { 2 r S } { \sqrt { \beta a } } + 2 \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { \phi } { 2 } \frac { d \varpi } { d t } + 2 \cos \phi \sin ^ { 2 } \frac { I } { 2 } \frac { d \Omega } { d t } , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d n } { d t } = - 3 \{ S \sin \phi \sin v + T ( 1 + e \cos v ) \} / ( a \cos \phi ) . \qquad ( 1 7 6 \propto \frac { I } { 2 } ) , } \end{array}
$$

ont.)

These formulas are especially important in the numerical computation of the variation of elements.When the positions of the disturbing planets are known at each instant, the numerical values of S, T, $W$ may be computed from these positions and the differential equations of motion numerically integrated.Such a method of computing the perturbation by numerical processes is generally called the theory of special perturbation (Bauschinger, 1928; Stracke,1929).

# 5.19ANGULAR MOMENTUM INTEGRALS

The angular momentum of the $\pmb { n }$ -body system is invariant by Poincare's transformation $\beta$ ，as has been seen in Section 5.6.For these fictitious planets, the angular momentum,by (l60) of Section 5.16 and (84b) of Section 5.9, is equal to $h _ { 2 } = \sqrt { \beta a ( 1 ~ - ~ e ^ { 2 } ) }$ .The orbital plane is determined by the inclination $\boldsymbol { \mathit { I } }$ and the longitude of the node $\Omega$ with reference to the ecliptic.Hence, the components of the angular momentum (which is perpendicular to the instantaneous orbital plane) in the directions of the three coordinate axes are $\sqrt { \beta a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) }$ sin $I \sin \Omega , - \sqrt { \beta a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) }$ sin $I \cos \Omega$ ,and $\sqrt { \beta a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) }$ cos $\boldsymbol { \mathit { I } } .$ The six orbital elements at any given instant are computed from the six data for the coordinates and the momenta at that instant. If the actual motion is rigorously Keplerian, then the six orbital elements thus computed are all constant. If it is not Keplerian, then the values of these orbital elements vary from time to time,and those variations are given by the equations (l64), (174),or (175).Such orbital elements are called the osculating elements. The actual orbit touches the Keplerian orbit at the given instant. Also, the magnitude and the direction of the momenta are the same for the two orbits; so are the expressions for the coordinates and the momenta in terms of the orbital elements and t.The Keplerian elements for the actual orbit vary with time but those for the Keplerian orbit remain constant.Hence, if these expressions for the coordinates are partially differentiated with respect to $\pmb { t }$ (which appears explicitly) they give the velocity components. If they are multiplied by the mass, they give the momentum components.On this basis, the formulas for the variation of elements of Sections 5.17-5.19 are derived; the right-hand members of these formulas are supposed to be given in terms of the elements and t.

Thus the angular momentum just given is the same for the actual orbit and the osculating Keplerian orbit；hence, the components of the resultant angular momentum of the whole system are constant:

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \sqrt { \beta _ { j } a _ { j } ( 1 - e _ { j } ^ { 2 } ) } \sin I _ { j } \sin \Omega _ { j } = c _ { 1 } , } } \\ & { } & { - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \sqrt { \beta _ { j } a _ { j } ( 1 - e _ { j } ^ { 2 } ) } \sin I _ { j } \cos \Omega _ { j } = c _ { 2 } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \sqrt { \beta _ { j } a _ { j } ( 1 - e _ { j } ^ { 2 } ) } \cos I _ { j } = c _ { 3 } .
$$

In particular, for the three-body problem,with the osculating elements $a , e , I , \Omega , a ^ { \prime } , e ^ { \prime } , I ^ { \prime } ,$ $\Omega ^ { \prime }$ of the two fictitious bodies, we have

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \sqrt { \beta a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } \sin I \sin \Omega + \sqrt { \beta ^ { \prime } a ^ { \prime } ( 1 - e ^ { \prime 2 } ) } \sin I ^ { \prime } \sin \Omega ^ { \prime } = c _ { 1 } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
- \sqrt { \beta a ( 1 - \ell ^ { 2 } ) } \sin I \cos \Omega - \sqrt { \beta ^ { \prime } a ^ { \prime } ( 1 - \ell ^ { \prime 2 } ) } \sin I ^ { \prime } \cos \Omega ^ { \prime } = c _ { 2 } ,
$$

$$
{ \sqrt { \beta a ( 1 ~ - ~ e ^ { 2 } ) } } \cos I + { \sqrt { \beta ^ { \prime } a ^ { \prime } ( 1 ~ - ~ e ^ { \prime 2 } ) } } \cos I ^ { \prime } = c _ { 3 }
$$

Take the invariable plane of the three bodies as the standard plane and denote the values of $I , \ \Omega$ in this case by ${ { I } _ { 0 } } , \ { { \Omega } _ { 0 } }$ Put $c ^ { 2 } = c _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + c _ { 3 } ^ { 2 }$ and substitute $I _ { 0 } , \Omega _ { 0 } , I _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } , \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } , 0 , 0 .$ and $\pmb { c }$ for $I , \Omega , I ^ { \prime } , \Omega ^ { \prime } , c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } ,$ and $c _ { 3 }$ ， respectively, in (177).Divide the first and second equations side by side, to obtain tan $\Omega _ { 0 } = \tan \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$ .Reference to the third equation shows that $\Omega _ { 0 } = \Omega _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } + \pi$ .This last relation expresses the elimination of the nodes (Jacobi).

In terms of the Delaunay elements, (l77) is writen

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { \sqrt { G ^ { 2 } - H ^ { 2 } } \sin h + \sqrt { G ^ { \prime 2 } - H ^ { \prime 2 } } \sin h ^ { \prime } = c _ { 1 } , } \\ & { } & { - \sqrt { G ^ { 2 } - H ^ { 2 } } \cos h - \sqrt { G ^ { \prime 2 } - H ^ { \prime 2 } } \cos h ^ { \prime } = c _ { 2 } , } \\ & { } & { H + H ^ { \prime } = c _ { 3 } . } \end{array}
$$

If we take the invariable plane as the standard, then

$$
G ^ { 2 } - H ^ { 2 } = G ^ { \prime 2 } - H ^ { \prime 2 } , \qquad h = h ^ { \prime } + \pi , \qquad H + H ^ { \prime } = c .
$$

By these relations we can reduce the order of the differential equations (164).

According to the third equation of (178a),

$$
\frac { d H } { d t } + \frac { d H ^ { \prime } } { d t } = 0 .
$$

Hence, from (164),

$$
\frac { \partial F } { \partial h } + \frac { \partial F } { \partial h ^ { \prime } } = 0 .
$$

Write the expression (173b) for the expansion of ${ \pmb F }$ in the form

$$
F = \sum A \stackrel { \cos } { \sin } ( i l + i ^ { \prime } l ^ { \prime } + j g + j ^ { \prime } g ^ { \prime } + k h + k ^ { \prime } h ^ { \prime } ) ,
$$

and combine with (179).Then we see that $\pmb { k } + \pmb { k } ^ { \prime } = 0$ ，and that $h , h ^ { \prime }$ appear in $F$ only as $h - h ^ { \prime }$ . We have just seen that $h - h ^ { \prime } = \pi$ when we take the invariable plane as the standard.In this case $\pmb { F }$ depends on neither $h$ nor $h ^ { \prime }$ .Hence by solving (l78a) we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { G = \Theta , \displaystyle \qquad G ^ { \prime } = \Theta ^ { \prime } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle { \cal H } = \frac { c } { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 c } ( \Theta ^ { 2 } - \Theta ^ { \prime 2 } ) , \qquad { \cal H } ^ { \prime } = \frac { c } { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 c } ( \Theta ^ { 2 } - \Theta ^ { \prime 2 } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Substituting (164) in $d h / d t = d h ^ { \prime } / d t$ obtained from (178a), we obtain

$$
\frac { \partial F } { \partial H } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial H ^ { \prime } } .
$$

From (181), we obtain

$$
\frac { \partial F } { \partial \Theta } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial G } , \frac { \partial F } { \partial \Theta ^ { \prime } } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial G ^ { \prime } } .
$$

Hence, the differential equations (164) of the three-body problem are reduced to

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { { \frac { d { \cal L } } { d t } } = { \frac { \partial F } { \partial l } } , { ~ { \cfrac { d \Theta } { d t } } = { \frac { \partial F } { \partial g } } , } } } \\  { { \frac { d \overline { { { l } } } } { d t } } = - { \frac { \partial F } { \partial L } } , { ~ { \cfrac { d g } { d t } } = - { \frac { \partial F } { \partial \Theta } } , } } \\ { { { \frac { d { \cal L } ^ { \prime } } { d t } } = { \frac { \partial F } { \partial l ^ { \prime } } } , { ~ { \cfrac { d \Theta ^ { \prime } } { d t } } = { \frac { \partial F } { \partial g ^ { \prime } } } , } } } \\ { { { \frac { d { l ^ { \prime } } } { d t } } = - { \frac { \partial F } { \partial L ^ { \prime } } } , { ~ { \cfrac { d g ^ { \prime } } { d t } } = - { \frac { \partial F } { \partial \Theta ^ { \prime } } } , } } } \end{array}
$$

which is of the eighth order and has four degrees of freedom.We obtain the common direction of the ascending nodes from dh $/ d t = - \partial F / \partial H$ by substituting in (181） after solving (182).

In the plane three-body problem, $\begin{array} { r } { \pmb { I } = \pmb { 0 } , } \end{array}$ Put

$$
G = H = \Pi , ~ G ^ { \prime } = H ^ { \prime } = \Pi ^ { \prime } ;
$$

then the integral of the angular momentum reduces to one equation

$$
\Pi + \Pi ^ { \prime } = c ,
$$

and $\partial F / \partial g + \partial F / \partial g ^ { \prime } = 0$ Hence, $\pmb { F }$ depends on $g , \ g ^ { \prime }$ only through $g - g ^ { \prime }$ . Write

$$
K = \Pi , \qquad \kappa = g - g ^ { \prime } ;
$$

then

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { d K } { d t } = \frac { d \Pi } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial g } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial \kappa } , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { d \kappa } { d t } = - \frac { \partial F } { \partial \Pi } + \frac { \partial F } { \partial \Pi ^ { \prime } } = - \frac { \partial F } { \partial K } , } \end{array}
$$

and (164) is now of the form

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l } & { \frac { d L } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial l } , } & & { \frac { d L ^ { \prime } } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial l ^ { \prime } } , } & & { \frac { d K } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial \kappa } , } \\ & { \frac { d l } { d t } = - \frac { \partial F } { \partial L } , } & & { \frac { d l ^ { \prime } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial F } { \partial L ^ { \prime } } , } & & { \frac { d \kappa } { d t } = - \frac { \partial F } { \partial K } , } \end{array}
$$

which is of the sixth order and has three degrees of freedom. The longitude of the perihelion is determined from $d g / d t = - \partial F / \partial \Pi$ by (184) after we solve (184a).

Since $\Theta = L \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } }$ in (182)， we have $L ^ { 2 } > \Theta ^ { 2 }$ Similarly, $L ^ { \prime 2 } > \Theta ^ { \prime 2 }$ .But we know that

$$
\Theta \cos I + \Theta ^ { \prime } \cos I ^ { \prime } = c , \qquad \Theta \sin I + \Theta ^ { \prime } \sin I ^ { \prime } = 0 .
$$

Hence,

$$
| \Theta | + | \Theta ^ { \prime } | \geq | c | , \qquad | \Theta | - | \Theta ^ { \prime } | \leq | c | .
$$

If $\Theta$ is approximately equal to $L$ ,then $F$ is not regular with regard to 0.Since (173b) shows that $s _ { 1 } , s _ { 2 }$ are integral multiples of $1 / 2$ , the function $\pmb { F }$ can be developed in an integral power series not of $L - \Theta$ but of $\sqrt { L - \Theta }$ . In such a case we have only to use the variables $\xi , \eta$ given by (170) of Section 5.17. The function $F$ is regular with regard to $\xi , \eta$

Furthermore, if $| \Theta | + | \Theta ^ { \prime } | = | c |$ or $| \Theta | - | \Theta ^ { \prime } | = | c |$ holds approximately，that is,if $\pmb { I }$ and ${ \bf \nabla } { \bf { \mathit { r } } } ^ { \prime }$ are small (for example, if the relation $\Theta + \Theta ^ { \prime } = c$ holds approximately)，then ${ \pmb F }$ can be developed in an integral power series of $\phi , q , \xi , \eta , \phi ^ { \prime } , q ^ { \prime } , \xi ^ { \prime } , \eta ^ { \prime }$ of Section 5.17, that is,of $\sqrt { L - G }$ $\sqrt { L ^ { \prime } - G ^ { \prime } }$ ， $\sqrt { G - H } , \sqrt { G ^ { \prime } - H ^ { \prime } }$ .If ${ \boldsymbol { J } } = { \boldsymbol { I } } ^ { \prime } = 0$ ，then $G = H , G ^ { \prime } = H ^ { \prime }$ , and the last two of the four square roots become zero. The function $\pmb { F }$ is regular with regard to $G , G ^ { \prime }$ $\hat { z } ^ { \prime } , \sqrt { G - H } , \sqrt { G ^ { \prime } - H ^ { \prime } }$ But,as we have discussed in Section 5.17, $F$ remains unaltered when we change the signs of $\pmb { I }$ and ${ \mathbf { } } ^ { \prime \prime }$ , that is, when we increase $\Omega$ and $\Omega ^ { \prime }$ by $1 8 0 ^ { \circ }$ Hence, ${ \pmb F }$ remains unaltered when we change the signs of $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } \ q , \pmb { \mathscr { p } } ^ { \prime }$ ， and $q ^ { \prime }$ simultaneously, and,consequently, the signs of $\sqrt { G - H }$ and $\sqrt { G ^ { \prime } - H ^ { \prime } }$ simultaneously. Thus, $\pmb { F }$ is regular with regard to $G , \ G ^ { \prime }$ ， $\sqrt { ( G - H ) ( G ^ { \prime } - H ^ { \prime } ) }$ ,when $\pmb { I }$ and $\scriptstyle { I ^ { \prime } }$ are small. But, by (181),

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \sqrt { ( G - H ) ( G ^ { \prime } - H ^ { \prime } ) } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 c } \sqrt { [ ( \Theta - c ) ^ { 2 } - ( \Theta ^ { \prime } { } ^ { 2 } ] [ ( \Theta ^ { \prime } - c ) ^ { 2 } - ( \Theta ^ { 2 } ] } } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } = \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 c } ( \Theta + \Theta ^ { \prime } - c ) \sqrt { ( \Theta - c - \Theta ^ { \prime } ) ( \Theta ^ { \prime } - c - \Theta ^ { \prime } ) } . } } \end{array}
$$

Hence, $G , G ^ { \prime }$ ， $\sqrt { ( G - H ) ( G ^ { \prime } - H ^ { \prime } ) }$ are regular with regard to $\Theta , \Theta ^ { \prime }$ ， $\Theta + \Theta ^ { \prime } = c .$ Accordingly, $\pmb { F }$ is regular with regard to $\Theta , \Theta ^ { \prime } , \Theta + \Theta ^ { \prime } = c .$ If the relation $\Theta - \ \Theta ^ { \prime } = c$ holds approximately, then it can be similarly shown that $\pmb { F }$ is regular with regard to @, $\Theta ^ { \prime }$ ， $\Theta - \Theta ^ { \prime } = c$

In the plane three-body problem, $F$ has,by (173b),the form

$$
F = \sum A ( L ^ { 2 } - G ^ { 2 } ) ^ { s _ { 1 } } [ L ^ { \prime 2 } - ( c - G ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } ] ^ { s _ { 2 } } \cos { ( i l + i ^ { \prime } l ^ { \prime } + i g + i ^ { \prime } g ^ { \prime } ) }
$$

If $\tau \approx G$ and $L ^ { \prime } \approx G ^ { \prime }$ ,that is,if $\pmb { \ell }$ or $e ^ { \prime }$ is small, then $\pmb { F }$ is developed in integral powers of $\sqrt { L ^ { 2 } - G ^ { 2 } }$ ， $\sqrt { L ^ { \prime 2 } - G ^ { \prime 2 } }$ and looks as though it were not single-valued. This situation can be avoided by transforming to the variables $\xi , \eta , \xi ^ { \prime } , \eta ^ { \prime }$ by the transformation of Section 5.17.

In the variables (l69) of Section 5.17 the integrals of the angular momentum are

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { - \sqrt { ( \Lambda - \Gamma ) ^ { 2 } - ( \Lambda - \Gamma - Z ) ^ { 2 } } \sin z } \\ & { \phantom { - } - \sqrt { ( \Lambda ^ { \prime } - \Gamma ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } - ( \Lambda ^ { \prime } - \Gamma ^ { \prime } - Z ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } } \sin z ^ { \prime } = c _ { 1 } , } \\ & { - \sqrt { ( \Lambda - \Gamma ) ^ { 2 } - ( \Lambda - \Gamma - Z ) ^ { 2 } } \cos z } \\ & { \phantom { - } - \sqrt { ( \Lambda ^ { \prime } - \Gamma ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } - ( \Lambda ^ { \prime } - \Gamma ^ { \prime } - Z ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } } \cos z ^ { \prime } = c _ { 2 } , } \\ & { \phantom { - } \Lambda - \Gamma - Z + \Lambda ^ { \prime } - \Gamma ^ { \prime } - Z ^ { \prime } = c _ { 3 } . } \end{array}
$$

If we refer to the invariable plane as the reference plane, the integrals are

$$
z _ { 0 } = z _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } + \pi , \mathrm { ~ \ } \Lambda - \Gamma - Z _ { 0 } + \Lambda ^ { \prime } - \Gamma ^ { \prime } - Z _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } = c ,
$$

$$
( \Lambda - \Gamma ) ^ { 2 } - ( \Lambda - \Gamma - Z _ { 0 } ) ^ { 2 } = ( \Lambda ^ { \prime } - \Gamma ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } - ( \Lambda ^ { \prime } - \Gamma ^ { \prime } - Z _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } .
$$

The expansion of the disturbing function can be discussed as in the case of the Delaunay elements. It can be shown that $\pmb { F }$ is regular with regard to the variables (l69) although the expansion is formally of the form (173).

In the canonical variables (l7O) of Section 5.17, we find the expressions for the angular momentum by taking the invariable plane as the reference plane:

$$
\frac { \dot { p } } { q } = \frac { \dot { p ^ { \prime } } } { q ^ { \prime } } ,
$$

$$
\left( \Lambda - \frac { \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 } - \left( \Lambda - \frac { \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } + \rlap / p _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + \eta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right) ^ { 2 }
$$

$$
= \bigg ( \Lambda ^ { \prime } - \frac { \xi ^ { \prime 2 } + \eta ^ { \prime 2 } } { 2 } \bigg ) ^ { 2 } - \bigg ( \Lambda ^ { \prime } - \frac { \xi ^ { \prime 2 } + \eta ^ { \prime 2 } + \dot { p } _ { 0 } ^ { \prime 2 } + q _ { 0 } ^ { \prime 2 } } { 2 } \bigg ) ^ { 2 } ,
$$

$$
\Lambda - \frac { \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } + \dot { p } _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } + q _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { 2 } + \Lambda ^ { \prime } - \frac { \xi ^ { \prime 2 } + \eta ^ { \prime 2 } + \dot { p } _ { 0 } ^ { \prime 2 } + q _ { 0 } ^ { \prime 2 } } { 2 } = c .
$$

# 5.20OTHER KINDS OF CANONICAL ELEMENTS

We have, from (84) of Section 5.9,

$$
W = \int _ { r _ { 0 } } ^ { r } d r \sqrt { \frac { 2 \beta } { r } + 2 h _ { 1 } - \frac { h _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } } + h _ { 3 } \theta + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \phi } d \phi \sqrt { h _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - \frac { h _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } { \cos ^ { 2 } \phi } } .
$$

Put

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { P = \beta , } } & { { L = \sqrt { \beta a } , \ : \ : \ : \ : \ : P > 0 , } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } & { { } } \\ { { Q = \sqrt { \frac { \beta } { a } } , \ : \ : \ : \ : \ : \ : \ : U = \sqrt { \beta a } , \ : \ : \ : \ : \ : \ : Q U > 0 , } } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
V = \omega \sqrt { \beta a ( 1 - \epsilon ^ { 2 } ) } , \qquad G = ( 1 + \omega ) \sqrt { \beta a ( 1 - \epsilon ^ { 2 } ) } , \qquad H = G
$$

with a constant $\pmb { \omega }$ . Take $h _ { 1 }$ of (187) to be $L$ or V, $\pmb { h } _ { 2 }$ to be $G$ ,and $h _ { 3 }$ to be $H .$ Then, from (188) and (190),

$$
W = \int _ { r _ { 0 } } ^ { r } d r \sqrt { - \frac { P ^ { 2 } } { L ^ { 2 } } + \frac { 2 P } { r } - \frac { ( G - V ) ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } } + H \theta + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \phi } d \phi \sqrt { G ^ { 2 } - \frac { H ^ { 2 } } { \cos ^ { 2 } \phi } } .
$$

Take $h _ { 1 }$ to be $\boldsymbol { \tau }$ or $\displaystyle \bar { V } , h _ { 2 }$ to be $G$ and $h _ { 3 }$ to be $H .$ Then, from (188) and(190)，

$$
W = \int _ { r _ { 0 } } ^ { r } d r \sqrt { - Q ^ { 2 } + \frac { 2 Q U } { r } - \frac { ( G - V ) ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } } } + H \theta + \int _ { 0 } ^ { \phi } d \phi \sqrt { G ^ { 2 } - \frac { H } { \cos { ( \theta \cos { \theta } ) } } }
$$

The lower limit $\boldsymbol { r } _ { 0 }$ of the integrals in (191) and (192) is the value of $_ r$ that makes the expression under the root sign zero.As it has been supposed that $\mathcal { P } > 0$ ， $Q U > 0$ ,we have always $r > 0$ From the known relations

$$
{ \dot { p } } _ { r } = { \frac { \partial W } { \partial r } } , \qquad { \dot { p } } _ { \theta } = { \frac { \partial W } { \partial \theta } } , \qquad { \dot { p } } _ { \phi } = { \frac { \partial W } { \partial \phi } } ,
$$

where we substitute

$$
{ \bf n } \phi = \sin I \sin ( v + g ) , \tan ( \theta - h ) = \cos I \tan ( v + g ) ,
$$

$$
r = a ( 1 - e \cos u ) = \frac { a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } { 1 + e \cos v } , \qquad M = u - e \sin u = l ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle { \dot { P } _ { r } = \frac { \sqrt { \beta } e \sin v } { \sqrt { a ( 1 - \varepsilon ^ { 2 } ) } } = \sqrt { \beta a } \frac { e \sin u } { r } = m ^ { \prime } \frac { d r } { d t } , } } \\ { \displaystyle { \dot { P } _ { \theta } = ( 1 + \omega ) \sqrt { \beta a ( 1 - \varepsilon ^ { 2 } ) } \cos I = m ^ { \prime } r \cos \phi \frac { d \theta } { d t } , } } \\ { \displaystyle { \dot { P } _ { \phi } = ( 1 + \omega ) \sqrt { \beta a ( 1 - \varepsilon ^ { 2 } ) } \frac { \sin I \cos { ( v + g ) } } { \cos \phi } = m ^ { \prime } r \frac { d \phi } { d t } , } } \end{array}
$$

where $n ^ { 2 } a ^ { 2 } = \beta$ If we equate $\pmb { \omega }$ to zero in these formulas, they reduce to those for ordinary Keplerian elliptic motion. ${ \bf O r }$ ,if we keep $t$ in the expression for $\scriptstyle { \pmb { \mathscr { p } } } _ { \pmb { \tau } }$ unaltered but substitute $t / ( 1 + \omega )$ for $t$ in the expressions for $\pmb { \mathscr { p } _ { \pmb { \theta } } }$ and $\pmb { \hat { p } _ { \phi } }$ , then the formulas reduce to those for a quasi-Keplerian motion， the difference from a pure Keplerian motion being that the period for $\pmb { \gamma }$ is $2 \pi / n$ ,while the period for $\theta$ and $\phi$ is $2 \pi / [ n ( 1 + \omega ) ]$ ; that is, the time interval between two consecutive perihelion passages is longer than the period for the angles $\theta$ and $\phi$ for $\omega > 0$ 、In other words, the perihelion rotates in the positive sense at the rate

$$
\frac { n } { n ( 1 \mathrm { ~ + ~ } \omega ) } = \frac { d v } { d t } \bigg / \frac { d ( v + g ) } { d t } ,
$$

that is, $d g = \omega$ du (see Section 2.19).

With these expressions for the momenta $p _ { r } , p _ { \theta } , p _ { \phi } $ ，the Hamiltonian function $\boldsymbol { H }$ takes the form

$$
H = \frac { 1 } { 2 m ^ { ' } } \left( / { p } { } _ { r } ^ { 2 } + \frac { G } { r ^ { 2 } } \right) - \frac { \beta } { r } ,
$$

where an additional force function

$$
\omega \beta a \bigg ( 1 + \frac { \omega } { 2 } \bigg ) ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) / r ^ { 2 }
$$

is superposed on the ordinary force function $\beta / r$ .Thus the ordinary integral $t + \gamma _ { 1 } = \partial W / \partial h$ ， is replaced either by $\partial W / \partial L = l ;$ orby $\partial W / \partial U = u _ { ; }$ orby $\partial W / \partial V = \boldsymbol { v }$ ； the ordinary integrals $\gamma _ { 2 } = \partial W / \partial h _ { 2 }$ and $\gamma _ { 3 } = \partial W / \partial L$ are replaced by $\partial W / \partial G = g$ and $\partial W / \partial H = h _ { ; }$ respectively.

We will now consider three fundamental cases :

I. Suppose that $P$ and $V$ are constant parameters; then the canonical variables $l , g , h , L , G , H$ correspond to $\omega = 0$ and, hence, $V = 0$ This is the case of Delaunay’s elements.

II. Suppose that $Q$ and $V$ are constant parameters and $U$ is variable; then $u , g , h , U , G , H$ are the canonical variables. This was discovered by Levi-Civita (l9l3),Andoyer (l9l3),and de Sitter (1913);in this case $\omega = 0$ ,and,hence, $V = 0$

III. Suppose that $\mathcal { P }$ and $L$ ,or $Q$ and $U _ { : }$ or $\beta$ and $^ { a }$ are constant; then $v , \ g , \ h , \ V , \ G , \ H$ are canonical variables. This was discovered by Hill (1913),Andoyer (19l3),and de Sitter (1913).

In these three cases the Hamiltonian functions are, respectively:

$$
H = - { \frac { P ^ { 2 } } { 2 L ^ { 2 } } } + { \frac { P } { r } } + { \frac { V ( 2 G - V ) } { 2 r ^ { 2 } } } - { \frac { \beta } { r } } ;
$$

in cas I $H = { \frac { Q U } { r } } + { \frac { V ( 2 G - V ) } { 2 r ^ { 2 } } } - { \frac { \beta } { r } } ;$

in case III， H =β+ $H = { \frac { \beta } { r } } + { \frac { V ( 2 G - V ) } { 2 r ^ { 2 } } } - { \frac { \beta } { r } } = { \frac { V ( 2 G - V ) } { 2 r ^ { 2 } } } .$

If we put $P = \beta$ $V = 0$ in case I, then $H = - \beta ^ { 2 } / ( 2 L ^ { 2 } )$ . If we put $V = 0$ in case II, then $H = ( Q U - \beta ) / r$ .Case I (Delaunay) corresponds to the particular solution of the differential equations such that $U = \beta / Q$ In this case,

$$
\frac { d u } { d t } = \frac { Q } { r } = \sqrt { \frac { \beta } { a } } \frac { 1 } { r } ,
$$

and the other elements are constant in the intermediary orbit. In case III, $H = V ( 2 G - V ) / 2 r ^ { 2 }$ and case I corresponds to the particular solution for $V = 0$ .In this case III we have

$$
{ \frac { d v } { d t } } = { \frac { G } { r ^ { 2 } } } = { \frac { \sqrt { \beta a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } } { r ^ { 2 } } }
$$

and the other elements are constant. The force function can be discussed similarly,even if the term

$$
\omega \beta a \Big ( 1 + \frac { \omega } { 2 } \Big ) \frac { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } { r ^ { 2 } }
$$

is added to it. In cases I and II, the assumption $V = 0$ corresponds to $\omega = 0$ ，and the momenta $\not p _ { \tau } , \not p _ { \theta } , \not p _ { \phi }$ are the same for the intermediary and disturbed orbits; hence the elements are osculating.But they are not the same for the two orbits in case III. The elliptic orbit and the disturbed orbit have different tangents at their common point. Such properties have been studied by Martin (l959-62) in connection with the two-body problem of variable mass.

In general, there are an infinite number of elliptic orbits that have common points with a given orbit, common tangents at any of those common points,and a common focus.Case I is one of those in which the attracted point starting with the given velocity is attracted by the force $- \beta / r ^ { 2 }$ .This is called isodynamical. Case II is one of those in which the attracted point starts with the given velocity at the given point, and the energy $h _ { 1 } = - \beta / 2 a$ is the same as that for an elliptic motion. This is called isoenergetic. In general, if we suppose that $H = T - \beta / r = h _ { 1 }$ has the value $h _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ at $t = 0$ and $\beta _ { 0 }$ is determined by $T - \beta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } / r = h _ { 1 } ^ { \left( 0 \right) }$ ， then

$$
T - \frac { \beta } { r } = h _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } + \frac { \beta _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } } { r } - \frac { \beta } { r } \cdot
$$

Each of these two elliptic motions is osculating but differs from the other. Case III, however, corresponds to the motion with $a = U / Q$ . It is not Osculating, since the value of $^ { a }$ is prefixed. This is called isoprotometric.

The notion of a protometer appears in the perturbation theory of Gyldén (see Chapter lO)； the protometer corresponds to the semimajor axis of an elliptic orbit but is not the same, because the intermediary orbit is not elliptic.

In such a case,

$$
H = \frac { \beta ^ { 2 } } { 2 a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } \left[ 2 + 2 \epsilon \cos v - \epsilon ^ { 2 } \sin ^ { 2 } v - \frac { G ^ { 2 } } { \beta a ( 1 - \epsilon ^ { 2 } ) } ( 1 + \epsilon \cos v - \epsilon ^ { 2 } \cos v ) \right]
$$

and

$$
\frac { \partial H } { \partial v } = \frac { \beta } { a } \frac { e \sin v } { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } ( 1 + e \cos v ) \left[ \frac { G ^ { 2 } } { \beta a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } - 1 \right] .
$$

The last factor,and hence $\omega$ , has the magnitude of the disturbing mass. The relation $G ^ { 2 } = \beta a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } )$ should hold always,although $\pmb { a }$ varies. This is the concept of Gylden on the protometer.

Now we have, respectively,in cases II and III, corresponding to (169) in case I:

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \Lambda _ { 1 } = U , \qquad \Gamma _ { 1 } = U - G , \qquad Z _ { 1 } = G - H , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \lambda _ { 1 } = u + g + h , \qquad \gamma _ { 1 } = - g - h , \qquad z _ { 1 } = - h , } } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \qquad \Lambda _ { 2 } = V , \qquad \Gamma _ { 2 } = V - G , \qquad Z _ { 2 } = G - H , } \\ & { ~ \lambda _ { 2 } = v + g + h , \qquad \gamma _ { 2 } = - g - h , \qquad z _ { 2 } = - h . } \end{array}
$$

Furthermore, if we put

$$
\begin{array} { l c r } { { F = V - G , } } & { { \Psi = G , } } \\ { { f = v , } } & { { \psi = v + g , } } \end{array}
$$

then this is also a canonical transformation and was discussed by Hill (1913).

Recently Soudan (l955） extended the studies of Hill, de Siter, Levi-Civita and Andoyer on the canonical elements. Consider two absolute constants $\pmb { \mu }$ and $\pmb { \rho }$ Put

$$
\mu ^ { 2 } = n ^ { 2 } a ^ { 3 } , \qquad L = n a ^ { 2 } , \qquad Q = n a , \qquad U = n a ^ { 2 } ,
$$

$$

\begin{array} { l } { { L = n a ^ { 2 } , \qquad G = ( 1 + \rho ) n a ^ { 2 } \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } , \qquad H = G \cos I , } } \\ { { \nonumber } } \\ { { l = n ( t - t _ { 0 } ) , \qquad g _ { 1 } = g + \frac { \rho } { 1 + \rho } v , \qquad h = \Omega . } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { U = n a ^ { 2 } , \quad } } & { { G , \quad \quad H , } } \\ { { \ } } & { { \ } } \\ { { u , \quad } } & { { g _ { 1 } , \quad \quad h . } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { F = \rho n a ^ { 2 } \sqrt { 1 - { e ^ { 2 } } } , G = ( 1 + \rho ) n a ^ { 2 } \sqrt { 1 - { e ^ { 2 } } } , H = G \cos { I _ { , } } } \end{array}
$$

h；

or

which is the system studied by Hill.

$$

$\mathbf { V } ^ { \prime }$

$$
\begin{array} { l c r } { { \displaystyle { \cal L } = n a ^ { 2 } , \qquad } } & { { \displaystyle { \cal G } } } & { { \qquad H , } } \\ { { \displaystyle l , \qquad g _ { 2 } = g _ { 1 } + \frac { 2 ( u - l ) } { ( 1 + \rho ) \sqrt { 1 - e ^ { 2 } } } , \qquad k . } } \end{array}
$$

VI'.

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { { \cal N } = n a ^ { 2 } , \qquad G , \qquad H , } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \nu = \displaystyle \frac { 3 u - l } { 2 } , \qquad g _ { 1 } , \qquad h . } } \end{array}
$$

Several other systems can be considered, for example:

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l } { L = Q \sqrt { a } , } & { } & { G = ( 1 + \rho ) n a \sqrt { a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } } & { } & { H = G \cos { I } , } \\ & { } & { \mathrm { ~ } = ( 1 + \rho ) Q \sqrt { a ( 1 - e ^ { 2 } ) } , } \end{array}
