In (196) the eccentric longitude $\lambda _ { 1 }$ in the orbit and in (l97) the true longitude $\lambda _ { 2 }$ in the orbit are used as the variables,in contrast to the case (174) in which the mean longitude $\lambda$ in the orbit is used. Such canonical elements are not used in ordinary perturbation theory.But Le Verrier discussed the motion of the major planets according to the idea of Lagrange, basing it on the mean longitude in the orbit. In the past, theoretical investigations have been based on the mean longitude.But, owing to the diffculty even of the formal convergence of the series obtained by employing the mean longitude in the orbit, Hansen (1857) and Newcomb (l865,1874） have used the eccentric anomaly or the eccentric longitude in the orbit for the computation of the ephemerides of the major planets. Newcomb (1891, 1895) obtained the development of the disturbing function in terms of the eccentric anomaly. Gyldén (1893)and Brown (1930; see also Brown and Brouwer,1937; Brouwer, i925)discussed perturbation theory by employing the true anomaly or true longitude in the orbit. Brendel (l898-l91l） applied Gyldén's theory to the motion of asteroids and Brown applied it to the motions of the Trojan group of asteroids and the eighth satellite of Jupiter, followed by Hori (1957,1957a,1958) on the motion of the ninth satellite of Jupiter. Such theories will be discussed in detail in later chapters.

# Algebraic Integrals and Uniform Integrals

THEOREM OF BRUNS

# 6.1INTEGRALS INDEPENDENT OF TIME

In Section 4.10 we have shown that the order of the differential equations of the $\pmb { n }$ -bodyproblem can be reduced from $6 n$ to $6 n - 1 2$ by the use of Euler's classical ten integrals. Several attempts have been made to discover other integrals. Bruns (1887, 1887a) proved that there cannot be any integral that is algebraic with respect to the coordinates and momenta,other than the classical integrals.Poincaré (l889） further proved that there cannot be any integral that is uniform with respect to the Keplerian elements,other than the classical integrals.

We have shown in Sections 5.14 and 5.19 that the position coordinates and the momenta are uniform or regular, that is, single-valued and continuous,with respect to the osculating elements computed from these coordinates and momenta, except in the case of collisions of two or more bodies of the system.It will be shown in a later chapter that a similar result can be obtained on the solution itself of the equations of motion.This theorem of Poincaré has a part of its validity domain in common with the theorem of Bruns,although each has its own validity domain. The theorem of Poincaré presupposes that the right-hand members of the differential equations are developed in ascending powers of a small parameter $\mu$ ，described in Sections 5.16-5.20；that is,the disturbing mass must be assumed to be so small, that it is impossible to apply the result to the general $\pmb { n }$ -body problem- for example,to the system of three bodies with finite masses of comparable magnitude. These theorems become trivial in the case of two bodies,as the problem can then be solved by means of the classical integrals alone. Painlevé (1896,1896a,1896b,1897a,1898) extended the theorem and proved that there cannot be an integral that is algebraic with respect to the momenta alone other than the classical integrals.The proof of Bruns's theorem has been improved by Poincaré (1896；see also MacMillan,l913；Perchot and Ebert， 1898；Whittaker， 1928； Forsyth， 1900)．We propose to extend the theorem from the three-body to the $\pmb { n }$ -body case.

Consider a system of differential equations of order $6 n - 6$ for the $\pmb { n }$ -body problem. If we write $\mu _ { i }$ instead of $m _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ of Section 5.6,then the equations become

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial y _ { i } } , \quad \quad \quad \frac { d y _ { i } } { d t } = \mathrm { ~ - } \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { i } } ,
$$

$$
H = T - U , \qquad T = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { y _ { i } ^ { 2 } } { 2 \mu _ { i } } \quad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , N ; N = \cdot 3 n - 3 ) ,
$$

where $U$ is supposed to be expressed in terms of Jacobi's canonical variables $x _ { i }$ and $_ { T }$ in terms of their conjugate variables $y _ { i }$ only. Write any integral of(l） in the form

$$
f ( y _ { 1 } , . ~ . ~ . , y _ { N } , x _ { 1 } , . ~ . ~ . , x _ { N } ) = a ,
$$

with an arbitrary constant $^ { a }$ .Our problem is to prove that there cannot be an integral of the form (2) in which $f$ is algebraic with respect to $y _ { i }$ and $x _ { i }$ ，other than the classical integrals.

At first an integral (2) should contain $y _ { i }$ .If $f$ were independent of $y _ { i }$ then,by differentiating (2) with respect to $t$ ，we should see from

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial y _ { i } } = \frac { y _ { i } } { \mu _ { i } }
$$

that the relation

$$
0 = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } { \frac { \partial f } { \partial x _ { i } } } { \frac { y _ { i } } { \mu _ { i } } }
$$

should hold for all values of $t$ ,and hence that it should hold for any value of $y _ { i }$ .Thus, we should have $\partial f / \partial x _ { i } = 0$ $( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , N )$ ,and $f$ should also be independent of $x _ { i }$

Let $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ be the sum of the $n ( n - 1 ) / 2$ mutual distances ${ r } _ { 3 j , 3 k }$ $( j , k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ . Any $\boldsymbol { r } _ { 3 j , 3 k } ,$ ，and accordingly $U _ { : }$ ，is expressed in terms of ${ \mathfrak { x } } _ { 1 } , { \mathfrak { x } } _ { 2 } , \dotsc , { \mathfrak { x } } _ { N }$ and $\mathfrak { s } .$ .The function $f$ is algebraic with respect to $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { N } , y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { N }$ but is not necessarily rational. By rationalizing (2)and rewriting it in descending powers of the constant $^ { a }$ , we see that the coefficients are rational with respect to ${ { y } _ { i } }$ and $x _ { i }$ . It can be written in the irreducible form

$$
a ^ { l } + a ^ { l - 1 } \psi _ { 1 } ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { N } , s ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \psi _ { l } ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { N } , s ) = 0 ,
$$

where $\psi _ { j }$ are rational functions of $x _ { 1 } , \dotsc , y _ { N }$ and $s$ Differentiate (3) with respect to $t$ and refer to the Poisson brackets by the use of (1). Then

$$
a ^ { l - 1 } ( \psi _ { 1 } , H ) + a ^ { l - 2 } ( \psi _ { 2 } , H ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + ( \psi _ { l } , H ) = 0 ,
$$

where $( \psi _ { j } , H )$ are rational with respect to $x _ { 1 } , \dotsc , y _ { N }$ and s. Suppose that none of the Poisson brackets $( \psi _ { j } , H )$ is equal to zero. In order that (3) and (4) may hold simultaneously,equations (3) and (4) should have in common more than one root of $^ { a }$ .But we have assumed that (3） is irreducible,and this would contradict that assumption.Hence $( \psi _ { j } , H )$ must all equal zero.Thus,by Poisson's theorem of Section 4.3,all functions $\psi _ { 1 } , \psi _ { 2 } , . . . , \psi _ { l }$ are integrals of (1). Hence,any integral $f$ is an algebraic combination of other rational functions of $x _ { 1 } , \dotsc , y _ { N }$ and $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ Thus we need only consider integrals that are rational with respect to $x _ { 1 } , \dotsc , y _ { N }$ and $\pmb { \mathscr { s } } _ { : }$ ，

$$
f ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { N } , y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { N } , s ) = a .
$$

Equations (1) remain unaltered when $x _ { i } , y _ { i }$ ， $t$ are replaced, respectively, by $x _ { i } \alpha ^ { 2 } , y _ { i } \alpha ^ { - 1 }$ ， $\scriptstyle t \alpha ^ { 3 }$ with a constant $\pmb { \alpha }$ .Hence(5) is also an integral for any value of $\pmb { \alpha }$ But $f$ can be written as the quotient of two integral polynomials;after this transformation it should be of the form

$$
f = \frac { A _ { 0 } \alpha ^ { p } + A _ { 1 } \alpha ^ { p - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + A _ { p } } { B _ { 0 } \alpha ^ { q } + B _ { 1 } \alpha ^ { q - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + B _ { q } } ,
$$

where $A _ { 0 } , \dotsc , A _ { p } , B _ { 0 } , \dotsc , B _ { q }$ are integral polynomials of

$$
x _ { 1 } , . ~ . ~ . , x _ { N } , y _ { 1 } , . ~ . ~ . , y _ { N }
$$

Since df/dt should equal zero, we obtain from this expression for $f$ 一 when the coefficients of all powers of $\pmb { \alpha }$ are equated to zero —

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle 0 = { { B _ { 0 } } \frac { { d { A _ { 0 } } } } { { d t } } - { A _ { 0 } } \frac { { d { B _ { 0 } } } } { { d t } } , } } \\ { \displaystyle 0 = { { B _ { 1 } } \frac { { d { A _ { 0 } } } } { { d t } } + { B _ { 0 } } \frac { { d { A _ { 1 } } } } { { d t } } - { A _ { 1 } } \frac { { d { B _ { 0 } } } } { { d t } } - { A _ { 0 } } \frac { { d { B _ { 1 } } } } { { d t } } } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
0 = B _ { q } \frac { d A _ { p } } { d t } - A _ { p } \frac { d B _ { q } } { d t } \cdot
$$

As these $( q + p + 1 )$ equations are equivalent to

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { A _ { 0 } } } { \frac { d A _ { 0 } } { d t } } = { \frac { 1 } { A _ { 1 } } } { \frac { d A _ { 1 } } { d t } } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = { \frac { 1 } { A _ { p } } } { \frac { d A _ { p } } { d t } } = { \frac { 1 } { B _ { 0 } } } { \frac { d B _ { 0 } } { d t } } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = { \frac { 1 } { B _ { q } } } { \frac { d B _ { q } } { d t } } ,
$$

the quotients $4 _ { 1 } / A _ { 0 } , A _ { 2 } / A _ { 0 } , . . . , A _ { p } / A _ { 0 } , B _ { 0 } / A _ { 0 } , . . . , B _ { q } / A _ { 0 }$ are all integrals. Let $G _ { 1 , } \ G _ { 2 }$ be integral polynomials, such that they are each multiplied by a certain power of $\propto$ by a transformation from $x _ { i } , y _ { i } ,$ $t$ to $x _ { i } \alpha ^ { 2 }$ ， $y _ { i } \alpha ^ { - 1 }$ ， $\scriptstyle t \alpha ^ { 3 }$ Then any integral $f$ is an algebraic combination of integrals of the form

$$
{ \frac { G _ { 1 } ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { N } , s ) } { G _ { 2 } ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { N } , s ) } } = { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } } .
$$

If $G _ { 1 } / G _ { 2 }$ contains imaginary parts, then the real part and the imaginary part should be separate integrals. Hence we can suppose that (6) is real.

# 6.2 LEMMAI

It might occur that $G _ { 1 }$ is written as the product of irreducible integral polynomials of $y _ { 1 } , \dotsc , y _ { N }$ .In such cases the coefficient of each term of these polynomials is a rational function of $x _ { 1 } , \dotsc , x _ { N } , s .$ Let $G _ { 1 }$ be in the form $G _ { 1 } = \psi ^ { \lambda } \chi$ .From

$$
{ \frac { d } { d t } } \left( { \frac { G _ { 1 } } { G _ { 2 } } } \right) = 0
$$

we obtain

$$
\frac { d \psi } { d t } = \psi \Bigl ( \frac { 1 } { \lambda G _ { 2 } } \frac { d G _ { 2 } } { d t } - \frac { 1 } { \lambda \chi } \frac { d \chi } { d t } \Bigr ) \cdot
$$

It shows that $d \psi / d t$ and $\psi$ are integral polynomials of $y _ { i }$ and that $\psi$ is of degree one lower than d4/dt.Also, $\psi$ has no factor in common with either $G _ { 2 }$ or $\chi$ Hence, the factor of $\psi$ on the right-hand side of this equation is linear with respect to $y _ { i }$ .Denote it by $\omega$ ; then,

$$
\frac { d \psi } { d t } = \omega \psi .
$$

Similar relations should hold for each of the remaining irreducible factors of $G _ { 1 }$ . Let the factors of $G _ { 1 }$ be $\psi ^ { \prime } , \psi ^ { \prime \prime }$ 。...; then, $G _ { 1 } = \psi ^ { \prime \mu } \psi ^ { \prime \prime \nu } \cdot \cdot \cdot ,$ Put

$$
{ \frac { 1 } { \psi ^ { \prime } } } { \frac { d \psi ^ { \prime } } { d t } } = \omega ^ { \prime } , \qquad { \frac { 1 } { \psi ^ { \prime \prime } } } { \frac { d \psi ^ { \prime \prime } } { d t } } = \omega ^ { \prime \prime } , \ldots ;
$$

then

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { 1 } { G _ { 1 } } \frac { d G _ { 1 } } { d t } = \frac { \mu } { \psi ^ { \prime } } \frac { d \psi ^ { \prime } } { d t } + \frac { \nu } { \psi ^ { \prime \prime } } \frac { d \psi ^ { \prime \prime } } { d t } + \cdot \cdot \cdot . } \\ { = \mu \omega ^ { \prime } + \nu \omega ^ { \prime \prime } + \cdot \cdot \cdot = \omega . } \end{array}
$$

Since the integral polynomial $\omega$ is linear in $y _ { i }$ and rational in $x _ { i }$ and $s ,$ the polynomial $G _ { 1 }$ satisfies the equation $d G _ { 1 } / d t = \omega G _ { 1 }$ Hence, $G _ { 2 }$ should satisfy $d G _ { 2 } / d t = \omega G _ { 2 }$ .Thus, $G _ { 1 }$ and $G _ { 2 }$ satisfy the same equation. Let $\phi$ represent either of the two; $\phi$ is a real integral polynomial with respect to $x _ { i } , ~ s$ and satisfies (7). In the transformation from $x _ { i } , y _ { i }$ ， $t$ to $x _ { i } \alpha ^ { 2 } , y _ { i } \alpha ^ { - 1 } , t \alpha ^ { 3 } , \phi$ is multiplied by a certain power of $\pmb { \alpha }$ ； therefore, the polynomial $\omega$ is multiplied by $\propto ^ { - 3 }$ because of the relation

$$
\omega = \frac { 1 } { \phi } \frac { d \phi } { d t } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { 1 } { \phi } \left( \frac { \partial \phi } { \partial x _ { i } } \frac { y _ { i } } { \mu _ { i } } + \frac { \partial \phi } { \partial y _ { i } } \frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { i } } \right) .
$$

Hence there is no term independent of $y _ { i }$ in $\omega$ ， since such a term,if present, would be multiplied by an even power of $\pmb { \alpha }$

Thus $\pmb { \omega }$ is a linear homogeneous expression in $y _ { i }$ of the form

$$
\omega = \omega _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } + \omega _ { 2 } y _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \omega _ { N } y _ { N } ,
$$

where each $\omega _ { j }$ is homogeneous of degree $- 1$ in $\pmb { x } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \pmb { x } _ { N }$ and $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ Bya similar consideration, $\phi$ should be an aggregate of terms of even degrees in $y _ { 1 } , \dotsc , y _ { N }$ ; it is written

$$
\phi = \phi _ { 0 } + \phi _ { 2 } + \phi _ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

where $\phi _ { 0 }$ is the aggregate of the terms of the highest degree in $y _ { i } , \phi _ { 2 }$ the aggregate of those of degree two lower in $y _ { i }$ than $\phi _ { 0 }$ ，and so on. Each $\phi _ { j }$ is a polynomial in $( y _ { 1 } , . ~ . ~ . , y _ { N } , x _ { 1 } , . ~ . ~ . , x _ { N } , s )$ homogeneous in $( y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { N } )$ and also in $( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { N } , s )$

Next we shall show that, if $\phi _ { 0 }$ does not contain $s$ ，we can obtain an integral by multiplying $\phi$ by a suitable rational function of $x _ { 1 } , \dotsc , x _ { N } .$

For proof, we suppose that $\phi _ { 0 }$ does not contain $\pmb { S } .$ Equating the terms of the highest degree on both sides of

$$
\frac { d \phi _ { 0 } } { d t } + \frac { d \phi _ { 2 } } { d t } + \cdot \cdot \cdot = ( \omega _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \omega _ { N } y _ { N } ) ( \phi _ { 0 } + \phi _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { N } \frac { y _ { i } } { \mu _ { i } } \frac { \partial \phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } = ( \omega _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \omega _ { N } y _ { N } ) \phi _ { 0 } .
$$

In general, $\phi _ { 0 }$ may contain $y _ { 1 }$ as a factor. Put $\phi _ { 0 } = y _ { 1 } ^ { h } \phi _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ ; then

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { y _ { i } } { \mu _ { i } } \frac { \partial \phi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } } { \partial x _ { i } } = ( \omega _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \omega _ { N } y _ { N } ) \phi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } .
$$

Let the terms independent of $\dot { y } _ { 1 }$ in $\phi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$ be $\phi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime }$ ; then

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { y _ { i } } { \mu _ { i } } \frac { \partial \phi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime } } { \partial x _ { i } } = ( \omega _ { 2 } y _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \omega _ { N } y _ { N } ) \phi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime } .
$$

$\phi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime }$ may be a function of $x _ { i }$ only. In this case denote it by $R$ ; then

$$
\frac { 1 } { \mu _ { i } } \frac { \partial R } { \partial x _ { i } } = \omega _ { i } R .
$$

Hence,

$$
\frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { j } } \left( \mu _ { k } \omega _ { k } \right) = \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { k } } \left( \mu _ { j } \omega _ { j } \right) \qquad ( j , k = 2 , 3 , . . . , N ) .
$$

Next, $\phi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime }$ may contain one of $y _ { 2 } , \dotsc , y _ { N }$ ， for example, $y _ { 2 }$ . In this case we write $\phi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime } = y _ { 2 } ^ { k } \phi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime \prime }$ , where $\phi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime \prime }$ does not contain ${ \pmb y } _ { 2 }$ as a factor. We have

$$
\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { y _ { j } } { \mu _ { j } } \frac { \partial \phi _ { 0 } ^ { m } } { \partial x _ { j } } = ( \omega _ { 2 } y _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \omega _ { N } y _ { N } ) \phi _ { 0 } ^ { m } .
$$

Let $\phi _ { 0 } ^ { \pi }$ be the terms independent of ${ \pmb y } _ { 2 }$ in $\phi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime \prime }$ . Proceeding in a similar manner, either we finally obtain

$$
\frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { N } } \left( \mu _ { N - 1 } \omega _ { N - 1 } \right) = \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { N - 1 } } \left( \mu _ { N } \omega _ { N } \right) ,
$$

or there exists an integral polynomial $\psi$ with respect to

$$
x _ { 1 } , \dotsc , x _ { N } , y _ { N - 1 } , y _ { N } ,
$$

which is homogeneous in $x _ { 1 } , \dotsc , x _ { N } ;$ is homogeneous in $y _ { N - 1 } , y _ { N }$ ,is free from any factor $y _ { 1 } ^ { h } y _ { 2 } ^ { k } \ldots ,$ and satisfies

$$
\frac { y _ { N - 1 } } { \mu _ { N - 1 } } \frac { \partial \psi } { \partial x _ { N - 1 } } + \frac { y _ { N } } { \mu _ { N } } \frac { \partial \psi } { \partial x _ { N } } = ( \omega _ { N - 1 } y _ { N - 1 } + \omega _ { N } y _ { N } ) \psi .
$$

Put $\psi = a y _ { N } ^ { l } + b y _ { N - 1 } ^ { l } + c y _ { N } ^ { l - 1 } y _ { N - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot$ and equate the coefficients of $y _ { N } ^ { l + 1 } , y _ { N - 1 } ^ { l + 1 }$ on both sides; then

$$
\omega _ { N } = \frac { 1 } { \mu _ { N } a } \frac { \partial a } { \partial x _ { N } } , \qquad \omega _ { N - 1 } = \frac { 1 } { \mu _ { N - 1 } b } \frac { \partial b } { \partial x _ { N - 1 } } \cdot
$$

The coefficients $a , b , \ldots ,$ are polynomials of $x _ { i }$ . They may have a common factor $Q$ ,such that $a = a ^ { \prime } Q , b = b ^ { \prime } Q , . .$ .. Let

$$
\psi ^ { \prime } = a ^ { \prime } y _ { N } ^ { l } + b ^ { \prime } y _ { N - 1 } ^ { l } + c ^ { \prime } y _ { N } ^ { l - 1 } y _ { N - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

so that $\psi = Q \psi ^ { \prime }$ ; then, writing

$$
\begin{array} { r l } {  { \frac { 1 } { \psi ^ { \prime } } \Big ( \frac { y _ { N } } { \mu _ { N } } \frac { \partial \psi ^ { \prime } } { \partial x _ { N } } + \frac { y _ { N - 1 } } { \mu _ { N - 1 } } \frac { \partial \psi ^ { \prime } } { \partial x _ { N - 1 } } \Big ) } } \\ & { = \frac { 1 } { \psi } \Big ( \frac { y _ { N } } { \mu _ { N } } \frac { \partial \psi } { \partial x _ { N } } + \frac { y _ { N - 1 } } { \mu _ { N - 1 } } \frac { \partial \psi } { \partial x _ { N - 1 } } \Big ) - \frac { 1 } { Q } \Big ( \frac { y _ { N } } { \mu _ { N } } \frac { \partial Q } { \partial x _ { N } } + \frac { y _ { N - 1 } } { \mu _ { N - 1 } } \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { N } } } \\ & { = \Big ( \omega _ { N } - \frac { 1 } { Q \mu _ { N } } \frac { \partial Q } { \partial x _ { N } } \Big ) y _ { N } + \Big ( \omega _ { N - 1 } - \frac { 1 } { Q \mu _ { N - 1 } } \frac { \partial Q } { \partial x _ { N - 1 } } \Big ) y _ { N - 1 } } \\ & { = \omega _ { N } ^ { \prime } y _ { N } + \omega _ { N - 1 } ^ { \prime } y _ { N - 1 } , } \end{array}
$$

we obtain

$$
\omega _ { N } ^ { \prime } = { \frac { 1 } { \mu _ { N } a ^ { \prime } } } { \frac { \partial a ^ { \prime } } { \partial x _ { N } } } , \qquad \omega _ { N - 1 } ^ { \prime } = { \frac { 1 } { \mu _ { N - 1 } b ^ { \prime } } } { \frac { \partial b ^ { \prime } } { \partial x _ { N - 1 } } } ,
$$

and

$$
\frac { y _ { N } } { \mu _ { N } } \frac { \partial \psi ^ { \prime } } { \partial x _ { N } } + \frac { y _ { N - 1 } } { \mu _ { N - 1 } } \frac { \partial \psi ^ { \prime } } { \partial x _ { N - 1 } } = ( \omega _ { N } ^ { \prime } y _ { N } + \omega _ { N - 1 } ^ { \prime } y _ { N - 1 } ) \psi ^ { \prime } ,
$$

where the left-hand member is an integral polynomial in

$$
x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { N } , y _ { N - 1 } , y _ { N } .
$$

Suppose that $\acute { a }$ contains $x _ { N }$ ; then $\omega _ { N } ^ { \prime }$ would contain $\acute { a }$ or its power as its denominator,and $\psi ^ { \prime }$ would contain $\acute { a }$ or its power as a factor.But this is contradictory to our assumption that $a ^ { \prime } , b ^ { \prime } , \ldots$ ,have no common factor. Hence, $\pmb { a } ^ { \prime }$ does not contain $\pmb { x } _ { N }$ and $\omega _ { N } ^ { \prime }$ is zero. Similarly, $\omega _ { N - 1 } ^ { \prime }$ is zero. Thus

$$
\omega _ { N } = { \frac { 1 } { Q \mu _ { N } } } { \frac { \partial Q } { \partial x _ { N } } } , \qquad \omega _ { N - 1 } = { \frac { 1 } { Q \mu _ { N - 1 } } } { \frac { \partial Q } { \partial x _ { N - 1 } } } ,
$$

and accordingly

$$
\frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { N - 1 } } \left( \mu _ { N } \omega _ { N } \right) = \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { N } } \left( \mu _ { N - 1 } \omega _ { N - 1 } \right) .
$$

Thus, in either of the two alternative cases,

$$
\frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { j } } \left( \mu _ { k } \omega _ { k } \right) = \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { k } } \left( \mu _ { j } \omega _ { j } \right) \qquad ( k , j = 1 , 2 , . . . , N ) .
$$

Hence we can write $\omega _ { i }$ in the form

$$
\mu _ { i } \omega _ { i } = \frac { 1 } { R } \frac { \partial R } { \partial x _ { i } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , N ) ,
$$

where $R$ is a rational function of $\pmb { x } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \pmb { x } _ { N }$ Thus

$$
\omega _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \omega _ { N } y _ { N } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { y _ { i } } { \mu _ { i } } \frac { 1 } { R } \frac { \partial R } { \partial x _ { i } } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { 1 } { R } \frac { \partial R } { \partial x _ { i } } \frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } ;
$$

hence,

$$
\frac { 1 } { \phi } \frac { d \phi } { d t } = \frac { 1 } { R } \frac { d R } { d t } .
$$

Therefore,

$$
{ \frac { \phi } { R } } = { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t . } }
$$

It has thus been proved that, if the terms $\phi _ { 0 }$ of the highest degree in $G _ { 1 }$ and $G _ { 2 }$ do not contain $\pmb { \mathscr { s } } _ { : }$ ，then any algebraic integral in question can be obtained by multiplying an integral polynomial in $y _ { 1 } , \dotsc , y _ { N }$ that is rational with regard to $\pmb { x } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \pmb { x } _ { N }$ and $s _ { : }$ ，by a rational function of $\pmb { x } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \pmb { x } _ { N }$

# 6.3 LEMMA I1

Now we propose to prove that a function $\phi _ { 0 }$ that satisfies (8) does not contain the irrationality s.Bruns's original proof of this part contained an error,which was corrected by Poincaré.

Assume tentatively that there exists a $\phi _ { 0 }$ ， containing $s$ ，that satisfies (8). If we substitute $n ( n - 1 ) / 2$ values of $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ in $\phi _ { 0 }$ ,then $\phi _ { 0 }$ takes as many values, which are mutually independent. Let them be $\phi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } , \phi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime } . . . .$ The corresponding value of $\omega$ must satisfy the equation of the form

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { y _ { i } } { \mu _ { i } } \frac { \partial \phi _ { 0 } ^ { ( j ) } } { \partial x _ { i } } = \omega ^ { ( j ) } \phi _ { 0 } ^ { ( j ) } \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ( n - 1 ) / 2 ) .
$$

Write

$$
\Phi = \phi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } \phi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime \prime } \cdot \cdot \cdot , \qquad \Omega = \omega ^ { \prime } + \omega ^ { \prime \prime } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ;
$$

then

$$
\frac { 1 } { \Phi } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { y _ { i } } { \mu _ { i } } \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial x _ { i } } = \Omega ,
$$

where $\Omega$ is linear homogeneous in $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \dotsc , y _ { N }$ and the coefficients are rational in $\pmb { x } _ { 1 } , \pmb { x } _ { 2 } , \dots , \pmb { x } _ { N } ,$ Function $\Phi$ ，by definition rational in ${ \mathfrak { x } } _ { 1 } , { \mathfrak { x } } _ { 2 } , \dotsc , { \mathfrak { x } } _ { N }$ ，does not contain $s ,$ and is an integral polynomial in $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \dotsc , y _ { N }$ .Hence,in accordance with Lemma I we can make $\Omega = 0$ by multiplying $\Phi$ by a rational function of $\pmb { x } _ { 1 } , \pmb { x } _ { 2 } , \dots , \pmb { x } _ { N } .$ Thus, $\Phi$ must satisfy

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { y _ { i } } { \mu _ { i } } \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial x _ { i } } = 0 .
$$

As this is a partial differential equation for $N$ independent variables, $N - 1$ independent solutions are obtained according to the results in Sections 4.1 and 4.17. These solutions are

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } { \left( \frac { x _ { i } y _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } - \frac { x _ { 1 } y _ { i } } { \mu _ { i } } \right) } & { { } } & { ( i = 2 , 3 , . . . , N ) . } \end{array}
$$

Hence, $\Phi$ is a function of these $N - 1$ solutions and $N$ variables $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \dotsc , y _ { N }$ But each factor of $\Phi$ is the value of $\phi _ { 0 }$ for each root of ${ \pmb S } .$ Hence, if the two corresponding roots become equal, the corresponding two factors should coincide. Thus, $\Phi = 0$ should, as an equation for $y _ { 1 }$ ， have at least two equal roots. Let the condition for equal roots be

$$
F ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { N } ) = 0 ;
$$

then $\partial \Phi / \partial y _ { 1 } = 0$ Similarly, $\partial \Phi / \partial y _ { 2 } = { \bf \cdot \cdot \cdot } = \partial \Phi / \partial y _ { N } = 0$ Since $\Phi$ is homogeneous in $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \dotsc , y _ { N }$ ，the equation

$$
y _ { 1 } { \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial y _ { 1 } } } + y _ { 2 } { \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial y _ { 2 } } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + y _ { N } { \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial y _ { N } } } = 0
$$

is equivalent to $\Phi = 0$ Thus, $\Phi = 0$ is not independent of

$$
\frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial y _ { 1 } } = 0 , \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial y _ { 2 } } = 0 , . . . , \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial y _ { N } } = 0 .
$$

Suppose that we make smal variations satisfying $\Phi = 0$ ，such that

$$
\sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { N } \left( \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial x _ { i } } \delta x _ { i } + \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial y _ { i } } \delta y _ { i } \right) = 0 .
$$

For any set of values satisfying ${ \partial \Phi } / { \partial y _ { i } } = 0$ , this equation reduces to

$$
\sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { N } \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial x _ { i } } \delta x _ { i } = 0 ,
$$

and this is equivalent to

$$
\sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { N } { \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { i } } } \delta x _ { i } = 0 .
$$

Hence

$$
{ \frac { \partial F / \partial x _ { 1 } } { \partial \Phi / \partial x _ { 1 } } } = { \frac { \partial F / \partial x _ { 2 } } { \partial \Phi / \partial x _ { 2 } } } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = { \frac { \partial F / \partial x _ { N } } { \partial \Phi / \partial x _ { N } } }
$$

should be a consequence of $\partial \Phi / \partial y _ { i } = 0 ~ ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , N )$ . Since

we have

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { y _ { i } } { \mu _ { i } } \frac { \partial \Phi } { \partial x _ { i } } = 0 ,
$$

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { y _ { i } } { \mu _ { i } } \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { i } } = 0
$$

for values of $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \dotsc , x _ { N }$ that satisfy (9b). Therefore $F = 0$ and

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { y _ { i } } { \mu _ { i } } \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { i } } = 0
$$

are derivable algebraically from $\partial \Phi / \partial y _ { i } = 0 ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , N )$ . Here the actual values of ${ \mathfrak { x } } _ { 1 } , { \mathfrak { x } } _ { 2 } , \dotsc , { \mathfrak { x } } _ { N }$ are not essential in this process of elimination， so we replace $x _ { i }$ by $x _ { i } + ( y _ { i } / \mu _ { i } ) t$ in all these equations. Then

$$
F { \biggl ( } x _ { 1 } + { \frac { y _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } } t , x _ { 2 } + { \frac { y _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 2 } } } t , . . . , x _ { N } + { \frac { y _ { N } } { \mu _ { N } } } t { \biggr ) } = 0
$$

and

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { y _ { i } } { \mu _ { i } } \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { i } } F \biggl ( x _ { 1 } + \frac { y _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } t , x _ { 2 } + \frac { y _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 2 } } t , . . . , x _ { N } + \frac { y _ { N } } { \mu _ { N } } t \biggr ) = 0
$$

are algebraic combinations of $N$ equations

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle { \frac { \partial } { \partial y _ { i } } } \Phi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { N } , y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { N } ) } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } - \displaystyle { \frac { 1 } { \mu _ { i } } \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { i } } } \Phi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { N } , y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { N } ) } } \end{array}
$$

But one of the algebraic combinations is

$$
\Phi ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { N } , y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { N } ) = 0 ,
$$

because we can derive this last equation by adding the above $N$ equations after multiplying them by $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \dotsc , y _ { N } .$ ,respectively. We shall show that this is the result of the elimination just mentioned.

Denote the result of elimination by $\Psi$ . The equation

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \left( { \frac { \partial \Psi } { \partial x _ { i } } } \ \delta x _ { i } + { \frac { \partial \Psi } { \partial y _ { i } } } \ \delta y _ { i } \right) = 0
$$

must be a combination of

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { i } } \left( \delta x _ { i } + \frac { t } { \mu _ { i } } \delta y _ { i } \right) = 0
$$

and

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { 1 } { \mu _ { i } } \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { i } } \delta y _ { i } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { y _ { i } } { \mu _ { i } } \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } F } { \partial x _ { i } \partial x _ { j } } \left( \delta x _ { j } + \frac { t } { \mu _ { j } } \delta y _ { j } + \frac { y _ { j } } { \mu _ { j } } \delta t \right) = 0 .
$$

Since the last equation contains $\delta t$ , it cannot enter into the combination. Hence,

$$
\frac { \partial \Psi / \partial x _ { 1 } } { \partial F / \partial x _ { 1 } } = \frac { \partial \Psi / \partial x _ { 2 } } { \partial F / \partial x _ { 2 } } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = \frac { \partial \Psi / \partial x _ { N } } { \partial F / \partial x _ { N } } ,
$$

$$
\frac { \partial \Psi } { \partial y _ { i } } = \frac { t } { \mu _ { i } } \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { i } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , N ) .
$$

From the fact that these equations should be identical with equations (9b) already obtained for $\Phi$ we see that $\Phi = 0$ and $\Psi = 0$ are equivalent. Hence, $\Phi = 0$ is the result of eliminating $\pmb { t }$ from

$$
\begin{array} { r } { F \Big ( x _ { 1 } + \frac { y _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } t , x _ { 2 } + \frac { y _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 2 } } t , . . . , x _ { N } + \frac { y _ { N } } { \mu _ { N } } t \Big ) = 0 , } \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\frac { \partial } { \partial t } F \bigg ( x _ { 1 } + \frac { y _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } t , x _ { 2 } + \frac { y _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 2 } } t , . . . , x _ { N } + \frac { y _ { N } } { \mu _ { N } } t \bigg ) = 0 .
$$

Now we can write down explicitly the equation $F ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { N } ) = 0 _ { ! }$ ， which expresses the condition for equal roots in the equation for $\pmb { S }$ From this we can find all possible polynomials $\Phi$ ,and hence all possible polynomials $\phi _ { 0 }$ ，by factorizing $\Phi$ .The values of $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ formed from the $n ( n - 1 ) / 2$ mutual distances ${ \pmb r } _ { 3 j , 3 k }$ are $\pm r _ { 3 , 6 } \pm r _ { 3 , 9 } \pm \cdot \cdot \cdot \pm r _ { 3 n - 3 , 3 n } ,$ and there are $2 ^ { n ( n - 1 ) / 2 }$ such values of $\pmb { S } ,$ ，Hence, two roots of $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ become equal when we have either of the relations

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { r _ { 3 , 6 } = 0 , \quad } & { r _ { 3 , 9 } = 0 , \quad \quad \ldots , \quad \quad r _ { 3 n - 3 , 3 n } = 0 ; } \\ { \mathrm { } } \\ { : \pm r _ { 3 , 9 } , \quad } & { r _ { 3 , 6 } = \pm r _ { 3 , 1 2 } , \quad } & { \ldots , \quad \quad r _ { 3 n - 3 , 3 n - 6 } = \pm r _ { 3 n - 3 , 3 n } ; } \\ { \mathrm { } } \\ { : r _ { 3 , 9 } \pm r _ { 3 , 1 2 } = 0 , \quad } & { \ldots , \quad } & { r _ { 3 , 6 } \pm r _ { 3 , 9 } \pm \cdot \cdot \pm r _ { 3 n - 3 , 3 n } = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

We limit ourselves to the case $n = 3$ .Then there are eight values of $s$ Two roots become equal when we have either of the relations,

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { { r _ { 6 , 9 } = 0 , } } & { { \quad r _ { 9 , 3 } = 0 , \qquad r _ { 3 , 6 } = 0 , \qquad r _ { 9 , 3 } = \pm r _ { 3 , 6 } , } } \\ { { r _ { 3 , 6 } = \pm r _ { 6 , 9 } , } } & { { \quad r _ { 6 , 9 } = \pm r _ { 9 , 3 } , \qquad r _ { 6 , 9 } \pm r _ { 9 , 3 } \pm r _ { 3 , 6 } = 0 . } } \end{array}
$$

From $r _ { 6 , 9 } = 0$ we obtain $F = x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } = 0 \nonumber$ If we eliminate $t$ from

$$
F \biggl ( x _ { 1 } + { \frac { y _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } } t , x _ { 2 } + { \frac { y _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 2 } } } t , \ldots \biggr ) = 0
$$

and

$$
\frac { \partial } { \partial t } F \biggl ( x _ { 1 } + \frac { y _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } t , x _ { 2 } + \frac { y _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 2 } } t , \ldots \biggr ) = 0 ,
$$

then we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r } { ( x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } ) \Big ( \frac { y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { y _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { y _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } \Big ) = \Big ( \frac { x _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } + \frac { x _ { 2 } y _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 2 } } + \frac { x _ { 3 } y _ { 3 } } { \mu _ { 3 } } \Big ) ^ { 2 } . } \end{array}
$$

Hence, function $\Phi$ takes the form

$$
\small \Phi = \left( \frac { x _ { 1 } y _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 2 } } - \frac { x _ { 2 } y _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { x _ { 2 } y _ { 3 } } { \mu _ { 3 } } - \frac { x _ { 3 } y _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 2 } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( \frac { x _ { 3 } y _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } - \frac { x _ { 1 } y _ { 3 } } { \mu _ { 3 } } \right) ^ { 2 } .
$$

Since this cannot be factorized into real factors, there can be no real polynomial $\phi _ { 0 }$ in this case. From $\pmb { r _ { 9 , 3 } } = 0$ and from $r _ { 3 , 6 } = 0$ we obtain similar results.

From $r _ { 9 , 3 } = \pm r _ { 3 , 6 }$ we derive $\Phi$ in the form

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \Phi = \{ 2 ( x _ { 1 } x _ { 4 } + x _ { 2 } x _ { 5 } + x _ { 3 } x _ { 6 } ) + \frac { m _ { 3 } - m _ { 6 } } { m _ { 3 } } ( x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } ) \} } } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \times \{ 2 \Big ( \frac { y _ { 1 } y _ { 4 } } { \mu _ { 1 } \mu _ { 4 } } + \frac { y _ { 2 } y _ { 5 } } { \mu _ { 2 } \mu _ { 5 } } + \frac { y _ { 3 } y _ { 6 } } { \mu _ { 3 } \mu _ { 6 } } \Big ) + \frac { m _ { 3 } - m _ { 6 } } { m _ { 3 } } \Big ( \frac { y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { y _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { y _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } \Big ) \} } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { - \{ \frac { x _ { 1 } y _ { 4 } } { \mu _ { 4 } } + \frac { x _ { 4 } y _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } + \frac { x _ { 2 } y _ { 5 } } { \mu _ { 5 } } + \frac { x _ { 5 } y _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 2 } } + \frac { x _ { 3 } y _ { 6 } } { \mu _ { 6 } } + \frac { x _ { 6 } y _ { 3 } } { \mu _ { 3 } }  } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \quad  + \frac { m _ { 3 } - m _ { 6 } } { m _ { 3 } } \Big ( \frac { x _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } + \frac { x _ { 2 } y _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 2 } } + \frac { x _ { 3 } y _ { 3 } } { \mu _ { 3 } } \Big ) \} ^ { 2 } . } \end{array}
$$

This cannot be factorized into real polynomials $\phi _ { 0 }$ that are linear in $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \dotsc , y _ { N }$ .Hence, there can be no real polynomial $\phi _ { 0 }$ in this case. From $r _ { 3 , 6 } = \pm r _ { 6 , 9 }$ and from $r _ { 6 , 9 } = \pm r _ { 9 , 3 }$ we obtain similar results.

Lastly, the relation ${ r _ { 6 , 9 } } \pm { r _ { 9 , 3 } } \pm { r _ { 3 , 6 } } = 0$ is rationalized to

$$
( r _ { 6 , 9 } ^ { 2 } - r _ { 9 , 3 } ^ { 2 } + r _ { 3 , 6 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } - 4 r _ { 3 , 6 } ^ { 2 } r _ { 6 , 9 } ^ { 2 } = 0 .
$$

If $r _ { 6 , 9 } = 0$ ,then the case reduces to the last one (9d). $\Phi$ likewise cannot be factorized.Hence, there is no real polynomial $\phi _ { 0 }$ that contains $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$

Summarizing the result so far obtained: We have shown that any algebraic integral independent of $t$ is an algebraic function of the integrals $\phi$ ,each of which can be written in the form $\phi _ { 0 } + \phi _ { 2 } + \phi _ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot _ { ; }$ where $\phi _ { 0 }$ is a homogeneous polynomial of degree $k$ ,say,in $y$ and a homogeneous algebraic function of degree $l$ say,in $_ x$ ,and $\phi _ { 2 }$ is a homogeneous polynomial of degree $k - 2$ in $y$ and a homogeneous algebraic function of degree ${ \mathrm { \Omega } } l - 1$ in $\pmb { x } _ { \mathrm { : } }$ ,and so on.

# 6.4 INTEGRALS OF MOMENTUM AND ANGULAR MOMENTUM

By extending the proof in the case of three bodies to the general case of $\pmb { n }$ bodies we shall show that such an integral $\phi _ { 0 }$ is an algebraic combination of the classical integrals.

If we replace $\phi$ by $\phi _ { 0 } + \phi _ { 2 } + \phi _ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot$ in the equation

$$
\frac { d \phi } { d t } = 0 ,
$$

that is, in

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \left( \frac { y _ { i } } { \mu _ { i } } \frac { \partial \phi } { \partial x _ { i } } + \frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { i } } \frac { \partial \phi } { \partial y _ { i } } \right) = 0
$$

and,it being an identity, equate terms of equal degree, then we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle 0 = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { \partial \phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } \frac { y _ { i } } { \mu _ { i } } , } \\ { \displaystyle 0 = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { \partial \phi _ { 2 } } { \partial x _ { i } } \frac { y _ { i } } { \mu _ { i } } + \frac { \partial \phi _ { 0 } } { \partial y _ { i } } \frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { i } } , } \end{array}
$$

The first equation is a partial differential equation of the first order for $\phi _ { 0 }$ and the solution is

$$
\phi _ { 0 } = f _ { 0 } ( Y _ { 2 } , Y _ { 3 } , . . . , Y _ { N } , y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { N } ) ,
$$

where

$$
Y _ { i } = { \frac { x _ { i } y _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } } - { \frac { x _ { 1 } y _ { i } } { \mu _ { i } } } \qquad ( i = 2 , 3 , . . . , N ) .
$$

The expression for $\phi _ { 2 }$ takes the form $\phi _ { 2 } = f _ { 2 } ( x _ { 1 } , Y _ { 2 } , . . . , Y _ { N } , y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { N } )$ in terms of $\pmb { x } _ { 1 }$ ,Y,..., $Y _ { N } , y _ { 1 } , \dotsc , y _ { N }$ Refer to

$$
x _ { i } = \frac { \mu _ { 1 } Y _ { i } } { y _ { 1 } } + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } y _ { i } x _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { i } y _ { 1 } }
$$

and form

$$
\frac { \partial f _ { 2 } } { \partial \pmb { x } _ { 1 } } ;
$$

then we obtain from (10)

$$
\frac { y _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial f _ { 2 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { y _ { i } } { \mu _ { i } } \frac { \partial \phi _ { 2 } } { \partial x _ { i } } = - \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { \partial \phi _ { 0 } } { \partial y _ { i } } \frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { i } } \cdot
$$

The integral of this equation is

$$
f _ { 2 } = \chi ( Y _ { 2 } , Y _ { 3 } , . . . , Y _ { N } , y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { N } ) - \int \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { y _ { 1 } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { \partial \phi _ { 0 } } { \partial y _ { i } } \frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { i } } d x _ { 1 } .
$$

If we express

$$
X = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { \partial \phi _ { 0 } } { \partial y _ { i } } \frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { i } }
$$

in terms of $x _ { 1 } , Y _ { 2 } , . . . , Y _ { N } , y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { N } ,$ then

$$
X = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial y _ { i } } \frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { i } } + \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { N } \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { j } } \left( \frac { x _ { j } } { \mu _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { 1 } } - \frac { x _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { j } } \frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { j } } \right) .
$$

As $\int X d x _ { 1 }$ should be an algebraic function, there cannot be any logarithmic term in this integral; that is, it cannot be Abel's integral of the third kind (Section 2.20).Let $V$ be the expression of $U$ in terms of $x _ { 1 } , Y _ { 2 } , . . . , Y _ { N } , y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { N }$ ；then

$$
\frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { i } } = \frac { y _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial V } { \partial Y _ { i } } \qquad ( i > 1 ) .
$$

Since

$$
\frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { 1 } } = \frac { \partial V } { \partial x _ { 1 } } - \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { N } \frac { \partial V } { \partial Y _ { j } } \frac { y _ { j } } { \mu _ { j } } ,
$$

the terms of $X$ that might give rise to a logarithmic term in $\int X d x _ { 1 }$ are

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \displaystyle \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { N } \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { j } } \Big ( \frac { y _ { j } } { \mu _ { j } } \frac { V } { y _ { 1 } } + \frac { y _ { j } } { \mu _ { j } } \frac { x _ { 1 } } { y _ { 1 } } \sum _ { k = 2 } ^ { N } \frac { \partial V } { \partial Y _ { k } } \frac { y _ { k } } { \mu _ { k } } + \frac { x _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { j } \mu _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial V } { \partial Y _ { j } } \Big ) . } \end{array}
$$

Now $V$ is the sum of $n ( n - 1 ) / 2$ terms of the form

$$
( A + B x _ { 1 } + C x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { - 1 / 2 } .
$$

Take each of these terms separately in the above expression for $X$ and equate to zero the part of the transcendental function in $\int X d x _ { 1 }$ ; then we obtain the condition

$$
\gamma \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { N } \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { j } } \frac { y _ { j } } { \mu _ { j } y _ { 1 } } - \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { N } \sum _ { k = 2 } ^ { N } \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { j } } \frac { y _ { j } y _ { k } } { \mu _ { j } \mu _ { k } y _ { 1 } } \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \partial B } { \partial Y _ { k } } - \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { N } \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { j } } \frac { y _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { j } \mu _ { 1 } } \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \partial B } { \partial Y _ { j } } = 0
$$

In general, $\boldsymbol { r } _ { 3 j , 3 k } ^ { 2 }$ is of the form

$$
[ ( x _ { 3 j - 2 } - x _ { 3 k - 2 } ) ^ { 2 } + ( x _ { 3 j - 1 } - x _ { 3 k - 1 } ) ^ { 2 } + ( x _ { 3 j } - x _ { 3 k } ) ^ { 2 } ]
$$

and is the sum of expressions of the form $x _ { 3 j - 2 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 j - 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 j } ^ { 2 }$ and $x _ { 3 j - 2 } x _ { 3 k - 2 } + x _ { 3 j - 1 } x _ { 3 k - 1 } + x _ { 3 j } x _ { 3 k }$ For an expression of the form $x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } .$ ,the term in (156) and (157) of Section 5.16 can be written in the present case (after some computation) in the form

$$
( A + B x _ { 1 } + C x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { - 1 / 2 } ,
$$

where

$$
A = { \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } } ( Y _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + Y _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } ) ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { 1 } { 2 } B = \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } Y _ { 2 } y _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 2 } y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } Y _ { 3 } y _ { 3 } } { \mu _ { 3 } y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } , } \\ { C = 1 + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } y _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } y _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } . } \end{array}
$$

Hence the contribution to the above condition due to such a term is

$$
\sum _ { i = 2 } ^ { N } \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { i } } \frac { y _ { i } } { \mu _ { i } } - \left( \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 2 } } \frac { \mu _ { 1 } y _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 } } \frac { \mu _ { 1 } y _ { 3 } } { \mu _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } \right) = 0 ,
$$

or

$$
\sum _ { i = 4 } ^ { N } y _ { i } \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { i } } = 0 .
$$

Next, for an expression of the form $x _ { 3 j - 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 j - 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 j } ^ { 2 }$ we have

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \quad A = \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } ( Y _ { 3 j - 2 } ^ { 2 } + Y _ { 3 j - 1 } ^ { 2 } + Y _ { 3 j } ^ { 2 } ) , } \\ & { \frac { 1 } { 2 } B = \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } Y _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 3 j - 2 } } { \mu _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } Y _ { 3 j - 1 } y _ { 3 j - 1 } } { \mu _ { 3 j - 1 } y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } Y _ { 3 j } y _ { 3 j } } { \mu _ { 3 j } y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } , } \\ & { \quad C = \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } \Big ( \frac { y _ { 3 j - 2 } ^ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 3 j - 2 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { y _ { 3 j - 1 } ^ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 3 j - 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { y _ { 3 j } ^ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 3 j } ^ { 2 } } \Big ) . } \end{array}
$$

Hence the contribution to the above condition due to such a term is

$$
y _ { 3 j - 2 } = { \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 j - 2 } } } + y _ { 3 j - 1 } { \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 j - 1 } } } + y _ { 3 j } { \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 j } } } = 0 \qquad ( j = 2 , 3 , . . . , n - 1 ,
$$

Combining (12) with (11), we obtain the same $n - 2$ equations (12). By solving these equations we see that $f _ { 0 }$ is a function of

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \jmath _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { N } , Y _ { 2 } , Y _ { 3 } , x _ { 3 j - 1 } y _ { 3 j - 2 } - x _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 3 j - 1 } , x _ { 3 j } y _ { 3 j - 2 } - x _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 3 j } } \\ & { \phantom { \bigg . . . } ( j = 2 , 3 , . . . , n - 1 ) , } \end{array}
$$

or a function of

$$
y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { N } , Y _ { 2 } , Y _ { 3 } , Y _ { 3 j - 1 } y _ { 3 j - 2 } - Y _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 3 j - 1 } , Y _ { 3 j } y _ { 3 j - 2 } - Y _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 3 j } .
$$

Next, from an expression of the form

$$
( x _ { 1 } x _ { 3 j - 2 } + x _ { 2 } x _ { 3 j - 1 } + x _ { 3 } x _ { 3 j } ) \qquad ( j = 2 , 3 , . . . , n - 1 ) ,
$$

which is written

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { x _ { 1 } \bigg ( \frac { \mu _ { 1 } Y _ { 3 j - 2 } } { y _ { 1 } } + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } y _ { 3 j - 2 } } { \mu _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 1 } } x _ { 1 } \bigg ) + \bigg ( \frac { \mu _ { 1 } Y _ { 2 } } { y _ { 1 } } + \frac { y _ { 2 } x _ { 1 } } { y _ { 1 } } \bigg ) \bigg ( \frac { \mu _ { 1 } Y _ { 3 j - 1 } } { y _ { 1 } } + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } y _ { 3 j - 1 } x _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { 3 j - 1 } y _ { 1 } } \bigg ) } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \qquad + \bigg ( \frac { \mu _ { 1 } Y _ { 3 } } { y _ { 1 } } + \frac { y _ { 3 } x _ { 1 } } { y _ { 1 } } \bigg ) \bigg ( \frac { \mu _ { 1 } Y _ { 3 j } } { y _ { 1 } } + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } y _ { 3 } } { \mu _ { 3 j } } } \end{array}
$$

we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { A = \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } Y _ { 2 } Y _ { 3 j - 1 } } { y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } Y _ { 3 } Y _ { 3 j } } { y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } , } \\ & { B = \frac { \mu _ { 1 } Y _ { 3 j - 2 } } { y _ { 1 } } + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } Y _ { 3 j - 1 } y _ { 2 } } { y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } Y _ { 2 } y _ { 3 j - 1 } } { \mu _ { 3 j - 1 } y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } Y _ { 3 j } y _ { 3 } } { y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } Y _ { 3 } y _ { 3 j } } { \mu _ { 3 j } y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } , } \\ & { C = \frac { \mu _ { 1 } y _ { 3 j - 2 } } { \mu _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 1 } } + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } y _ { 2 } y _ { 3 j - 1 } } { \mu _ { 3 j - 1 } y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } y _ { 3 } y _ { 3 j } } { \mu _ { 3 j } y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } . } \end{array}
$$

Hence the corresponding condition is

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle _ { 2 } y _ { 3 j - 2 } - y _ { 1 } y _ { 3 j - 1 } ) + \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 } } \left( y _ { 3 } y _ { 3 j - 2 } - y _ { 1 } y _ { 3 j } \right) } } \\ { { + \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 j - 2 } } \left( \frac { \mu _ { 1 } y _ { 3 j - 2 } ^ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 3 j - 2 } } - y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \right) + \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 j - 1 } } \left( \frac { \mu _ { 1 } y _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 3 j - 1 } } { \mu _ { 3 j - 2 } } - y _ { 2 } y _ { 1 } \right) } } \\ { { + \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 j } } \left( \frac { \mu _ { 1 } y _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 3 j } } { \mu _ { 3 j - 2 } } - y _ { 1 } y _ { 3 } \right) = 0 } } \\ { { ( j = 2 , 3 , . . . , n - 1 ) . \quad ( 1 3 ) } } \end{array}
$$

Combining (13) and (12), we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle ( y _ { 2 } y _ { 3 j - 2 } - y _ { 1 } y _ { 3 j - 1 } ) \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 2 } } + ( y _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 3 } - y _ { 1 } y _ { 3 j } ) \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 } } } } \\ { { - y _ { 1 } \Big ( y _ { 1 } \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 j - 2 } } + y _ { 2 } \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 j - 1 } } + y _ { 3 } \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 j } } \Big ) } } \\ { { ( j = 2 , 3 , . . . , n - 1 ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Similarly, from an expression of the form

$$
( x _ { 3 j - 2 } x _ { 3 k - 2 } + x _ { 3 j - 1 } x _ { 3 k - 1 } + x _ { 3 j } x _ { 3 k } )
$$

we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { A = \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } Y _ { 3 j - 1 } Y _ { 3 k - 1 } } { y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } Y _ { 3 j } Y _ { 3 k } } { y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } , } \\ & { B = \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } y _ { 3 k - 2 } Y _ { 3 j - 2 } } { \mu _ { 3 k - 2 } y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } y _ { 3 j - 2 } Y _ { 3 k - 2 } } { \mu _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } y _ { 3 k - 1 } Y _ { 3 j - 1 } } { \mu _ { 3 k - 1 } y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } y _ { 3 j - 1 } Y _ { 3 j } } { \mu _ { 3 j - 1 } y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } } \\ & { \phantom { m m m m m m m m m m m m m } + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } y _ { 3 k } Y _ { 3 j } } { \mu _ { 3 k } y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } y _ { 3 j } Y _ { 3 k } } { \mu _ { 3 j } y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } , } \\ & { C = \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } y _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 3 k - 2 } } { \mu _ { 3 j - 2 } \mu _ { 3 k - 2 } y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } y _ { 3 j - 1 } y _ { 3 k - 1 } } { \mu _ { 3 j - 1 } \mu _ { 3 k - 1 } y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } y _ { 3 j } y _ { 3 k } } { \mu _ { 3 j } \mu _ { 3 k } y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } . } \end{array}
$$

Hence the corresponding condition is

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \displaystyle { y _ { 3 k - 2 } \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 j - 2 } } + y _ { 3 k - 1 } \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 j - 1 } } + y _ { 3 k } \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 j } } } } \\ { \displaystyle { + y _ { 3 j - 2 } \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 k - 2 } } + y _ { 3 j - 1 } \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 k - 1 } } + y _ { 3 j } \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 k } } } } \\ { \displaystyle { ( j , k = 2 , 3 , . . . , n - 1 ) . } } \end{array}
$$

We have seen that $Y _ { j }$ enters into these equations only in the combinations

and

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { ( Y _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 3 j - 1 } - Y _ { 3 j - 1 } y _ { 3 j - 2 } ) , ( Y _ { 3 j - 1 } y _ { 3 j } - Y _ { 3 j } y _ { 3 j - 1 } ) , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { ( Y _ { 3 j } y _ { 3 j - 2 } - Y _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 3 j } ) . } } \end{array}
$$

In these combinations, it is evident from (l5) that we can exchange the suffixes $j$ and $k$ Hence $Y _ { j }$ enters only in the combinations

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { n } \big ( Y _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 3 j - 1 } - Y _ { 3 j - 1 } y _ { 3 j - 2 } \big ) , \qquad \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { n } \big ( Y _ { 3 j - 1 } y _ { 3 j } - Y _ { 3 j } y _ { 3 j - 1 } \big ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { n } \big ( Y _ { 3 j } y _ { 3 j - 2 } - Y _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 3 j } \big ) . } } \end{array}
$$

On the other hand, the angular momentum of our dynamical system expressed in terms of $Y _ { i } ,$ according to Section 4.12,is

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { y _ { 1 } } H _ { 4 } = Y _ { 3 } y _ { 2 } - Y _ { 2 } y _ { 3 } + \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { n - 1 } \big ( Y _ { 3 j } y _ { 3 j - 1 } - Y _ { 3 j - 1 } y _ { 3 j } \big ) , } \\ { \displaystyle } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { y _ { 1 } } H _ { 5 } = - Y _ { 3 } y _ { 1 } + \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { n - 1 } \big ( Y _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 3 j } - Y _ { 3 j } y _ { 3 j - 2 } \big ) , } \\ { \displaystyle \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { y _ { 1 } } H _ { 6 } = Y _ { 2 } y _ { 1 } + \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { n - 1 } \big ( Y _ { 3 j - 1 } y _ { 3 j - 2 } - Y _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 3 j - 1 } \big ) , } \end{array}
$$

and each of the integrals $H _ { 4 } =$ constant, $H _ { 5 } =$ constant, $H _ { 6 } =$ constant satisfies partial differential equations (l4). Next, put

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { H _ { 1 } = \displaystyle - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } y _ { 3 j - 2 } , \qquad H _ { 2 } = \displaystyle - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } y _ { 3 j - 1 } , \qquad H _ { 3 } = \displaystyle - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } y _ { 3 j } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { y _ { 1 } } M _ { 1 } = H _ { 3 } \Big ( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \mu _ { 3 j - 1 } Y _ { 3 j - 1 } \Big ) - H _ { 2 } \Big ( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \mu _ { 3 j } Y _ { 3 j } \Big ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { y _ { 1 } } M _ { 2 } = H _ { 1 } \Big ( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \mu _ { 3 j } Y _ { 3 j } \Big ) - H _ { 3 } \Big ( \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { n - 1 } \mu _ { 3 j - 2 } Y _ { 3 j - 2 } \Big ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { y _ { 1 } } M _ { 3 } = H _ { 2 } \Big ( \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { n - 1 } \mu _ { 3 j - 2 } Y _ { 3 j - 2 } \Big ) - H _ { 1 } \Big ( \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n - 1 } \mu _ { 3 j - 1 } Y _ { 3 j - 1 } \Big ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Then $M _ { 1 } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t }$ ， $M _ { 2 } =$ constant, $M _ { 3 } =$ constant are integrals of our partial differential equations. They are not independent but connected by the relation

$$
H _ { 1 } M _ { 1 } + H _ { 2 } M _ { 2 } + H _ { 3 } M _ { 3 } \equiv 0 .
$$

The Jacobian of $H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , M _ { 2 } , M _ { 3 }$ with regard to $Y _ { 2 } , Y _ { 3 } , Y _ { 4 } , Y _ { 5 } , Y _ { 6 }$ is, apart from the factor $\mu _ { 1 } / y _ { 1 }$ ,equal to (19):

$$
\left| \begin{array} { l l l l l l } { - y _ { 3 } } & { y _ { 2 } } & { 0 } & { - y _ { 6 } } & { y _ { 5 } } \\ { 0 } & { - y _ { 1 } } & { y _ { 6 } } & { 0 } & { - y _ { 4 } } \\ { y _ { 1 } } & { 0 } & { - y _ { 5 } } & { y _ { 4 } } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { \mu _ { 3 } H _ { 1 } } & { - \mu _ { 4 } H _ { 3 } } & { 0 } & { \mu _ { 6 } H _ { 1 } } \\ { - \mu _ { 2 } H _ { 1 } } & { 0 } & { \mu _ { 4 } H _ { 2 } } & { - \mu _ { 5 } H _ { 1 } } & { 0 } \end{array} \right|
$$

and does not vanish. Hence, $Y _ { 2 } , Y _ { 3 } , Y _ { 4 } , Y _ { 5 } , Y _ { 6 }$ can be expressed in terms of $H _ { 4 }$ ， $H _ { 5 }$ $H _ { 6 }$ ， $M _ { 2 }$ ， $M _ { 3 }$ , and the remaining $\scriptstyle { Y _ { 7 } }$ ， $Y _ { 8 } , . . . , Y _ { N } .$

Suppose that $Y _ { 2 } , Y _ { 3 } , Y _ { 4 } , Y _ { 5 } , Y _ { 6 }$ in an integral $x$ of our partial differential equations are expressed in terms of $H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , M _ { 2 } , M _ { 3 }$ . If we equate the latter five quantities to zero, then $x$ can be considered independent of the functions $Y _ { 2 } , Y _ { 3 } , Y _ { 4 } , Y _ { 5 } , Y _ { 6 }$ .Combining (12) with (14),we obtain

$$
( y _ { 3 j - 2 } - y _ { 1 } ) \frac { \partial \chi } { \partial Y _ { 3 j - 2 } } + ( y _ { 3 j - 1 } - y _ { 2 } ) \frac { \partial \chi } { \partial Y _ { 3 j - 1 } } + ( y _ { 3 j } - y _ { 3 } ) \frac { \partial \chi } { \partial Y _ { 3 j } } = 0
$$

$$
( j = 3 , 4 , . . . , n - 1 ) .
$$

Put $k = 2$ in (15)and subtract it from (12); then

$$
( y _ { 3 j - 2 } - y _ { 4 } ) \frac { \partial \chi } { \partial Y _ { 3 j - 2 } } + ( y _ { 3 j - 1 } - y _ { 5 } ) \frac { \partial \chi } { \partial Y _ { 3 j - 1 } } + ( y _ { 3 j } - y _ { 6 } ) \frac { \partial \chi } { \partial Y _ { 3 j } } = 0
$$

$$
( j = 3 , 4 , . . . , n - 1 ) .
$$

From these two equations we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { \partial \chi } { \partial Y _ { 3 j - 1 } } } \\ { \overline { { y _ { 3 j - 1 } - y _ { 2 } } } \quad y _ { 3 j } - y _ { 3 } \bigg | = \frac { \frac { \partial \chi } { \partial Y _ { 3 j - 1 } } } { \left| y _ { 3 j } - y _ { 3 } \right. \ y _ { 3 j - 2 } - y _ { 1 } } } \\ { \overline { { y _ { 3 j - 1 } - y _ { 5 } } } \quad y _ { 3 j } - y _ { 6 } \bigg | \qquad \left| \begin{array} { l } { \frac { \partial \chi } { \partial Y _ { 3 j - 1 } } } \\ { \overline { { y _ { 3 j - 2 } - y _ { 3 } } } \quad \overline { { y _ { 3 j - 2 } - y _ { 3 } } } \bigg | } \\ { \overline { { y _ { 3 j - 1 } - y _ { 6 } } } \quad \overline { { y _ { 3 j - 2 } - y _ { 4 } } } \bigg | } \\ { \qquad \left. \frac { \partial \chi } { \partial Y _ { 3 j } } \right. } \\ { \left| \overline { { y _ { 3 j - 2 } - y _ { 1 } \quad y _ { 3 j - 1 } - y _ { 2 } } } \right| } \\ { \left. \overline { { y _ { 3 j - 2 } - y _ { 4 } \quad y _ { 3 j - 1 } - y _ { 5 } } } \right| } \end{array} \right| } \end{array}
$$

Write

$$
D \equiv \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { { y _ { 3 j - 2 } - y _ { 1 } } } & { { y _ { 3 j - 1 } - y _ { 2 } } } & { { y _ { 3 j } - y _ { 3 } } } \\ { { y _ { 3 j - 2 } - y _ { 4 } } } & { { y _ { 3 j - 1 } - y _ { 5 } } } & { { y _ { 3 j } - y _ { 6 } } } \\ { { y _ { 3 j - 2 } - y _ { 3 k - 2 } } } & { { y _ { 3 j - 1 } - y _ { 3 k - 1 } } } & { { y _ { 3 j } - y _ { 3 k } } } \end{array} \right| ;
$$

then (20) can be transformed, with constants $c _ { j }$ and $\pmb { c } _ { k }$ ,to

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \big ( y _ { 3 j - 2 } - y _ { 3 k - 2 } \big ) \frac { \partial \chi } { \partial Y _ { 3 j - 2 } } + \big ( y _ { 3 j - 1 } - y _ { 3 k - 1 } \big ) \frac { \partial \chi } { \partial Y _ { 3 j - 1 } } } } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \qquad + \big ( y _ { 3 j } - y _ { 3 k } \big ) \frac { \partial \chi } { \partial Y _ { 3 j } } = c _ { j } D , } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \big ( y _ { 3 j - 2 } - y _ { 3 k - 2 } \big ) \frac { \partial \chi } { \partial Y _ { 3 k - 2 } } + \big ( y _ { 3 j - 1 } - y _ { 3 k - 1 } \big ) \frac { \partial \chi } { \partial Y _ { 3 k - 1 } } } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \qquad + \big ( y _ { 3 j } - y _ { 3 k } \big ) \frac { \partial \chi } { \partial Y _ { 3 k } } = c _ { k } D . } \end{array}
$$

However,from (15) and (12) we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { ( y _ { 3 j - 2 } - y _ { 3 k - 2 } ) \displaystyle \bigg ( \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 j - 2 } } - \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 k - 2 } } \bigg ) } } \\ { { + \ ( y _ { 3 j - 1 } - y _ { 3 k - 1 } ) \displaystyle \bigg ( \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 j - 1 } } - \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 k - 1 } } \bigg ) } } \\ { { + \ ( y _ { 3 j } - y _ { 3 k } ) \displaystyle \bigg ( \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 j } } - \frac { \partial f _ { 0 } } { \partial Y _ { 3 k } } \bigg ) ~ ; } } \end{array}
$$

therefore,

$$
( c _ { j } - c _ { k } ) D = 0 ;
$$

that is,

$$
c _ { j } = c _ { k } = c .
$$

Hence,

$$
y _ { 3 j - 2 } \frac { \partial \chi } { \partial Y _ { 3 j - 2 } } + y _ { 3 j - 1 } \frac { \partial \chi } { \partial Y _ { 3 j - 1 } } + y _ { 3 j } \frac { \partial \chi } { \partial Y _ { 3 j } } = c \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { y _ { 3 j - 2 } } & { y _ { 1 } } & { y _ { 4 } } \\ { y _ { 3 j - 1 } } & { y _ { 2 } } & { y _ { 5 } } \\ { y _ { 3 j } } & { y _ { 3 } } & { y _ { 6 } } \end{array} \right|
$$

is zero because of (11). Thus, $c = 0$ Hence,(20) becomes

$$
{ \frac { \partial \chi } { \partial Y _ { 3 j - 2 } } } = { \frac { \partial \chi } { \partial Y _ { 3 j - 1 } } } = { \frac { \partial \chi } { \partial Y _ { 3 j } } } = 0 \qquad ( j = 3 , 4 , . . . , n - 1 ) .
$$

Thus $x$ does not contain $Y _ { 3 j - 2 } , Y _ { 3 j - 1 } , Y _ { 3 j } ( j = 3 , 4 , . . . , n - 1 )$ ; that is, $f _ { 0 }$ contains only $\boldsymbol { x } _ { i }$ in the combinations $H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , M _ { 2 } , M _ { 3 } ,$ Hence, if we eliminate $x _ { 2 } , x _ { 3 } , x _ { 4 } , x _ { 5 } , x _ { 6 }$ from $f _ { 0 }$ ，then the remaining

$$
x _ { 7 } , x _ { 8 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n - 3 }
$$

also disappear from $f _ { 0 }$ ·

When we express $Y _ { 2 } , Y _ { 3 } , Y _ { 4 } , Y _ { 5 } , Y _ { 6 }$ in terms of $H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , M _ { 2 } , M _ { 3 } ,$ the corresponding Jacobian should appear in the denominator. The Jacobian determinant of $H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , M _ { 2 } , M _ { 3 }$ with regard to $x _ { 2 } , x _ { 3 } , x _ { 4 } ,$ ${ x } _ { 5 } , { x } _ { 6 }$ is, apart from the factor $y _ { 1 } / \mu _ { 1 }$ ,equal to (19); that is,

$$
\Delta \equiv \mu _ { 4 } H _ { 1 } ( \mu _ { 6 } y _ { 1 } - \mu _ { 3 } y _ { 4 } ) { \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { y _ { 1 } } & { y _ { 4 } } & { H _ { 1 } } \\ { y _ { 2 } } & { y _ { 5 } } & { H _ { 2 } } \\ { y _ { 3 } } & { y _ { 6 } } & { H _ { 3 } } \end{array} \right| } .
$$

The expression for $f _ { 0 }$ in terms of $H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , M _ { 2 } , M _ { 3 }$ has a power of $\Delta$ in the denominator.Let it be

$$
G ( H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , M _ { 2 } , M _ { 3 } , y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { N } ) \Delta ^ { - p } .
$$

If we eliminate ${ \boldsymbol { x } } _ { 2 } , { \boldsymbol { x } } _ { 3 } , { \boldsymbol { x } } _ { 7 } , { \boldsymbol { x } } _ { 8 } , { \boldsymbol { x } } _ { 9 }$ instead of $x _ { 2 } , x _ { 3 } , x _ { 4 } , x _ { 5 } , x _ { 6 }$ by means of $H _ { 4 }$ $H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , M _ { 2 } , M _ { 3 } ,$ we obtain

$$
\Delta ^ { \prime } \equiv \mu _ { 7 } H _ { 1 } \cdot ( \mu _ { 9 } y _ { 1 } - y _ { 3 } y _ { 7 } ) { \left| \begin{array} { l l l } { y _ { 1 } } & { y _ { 7 } } & { H _ { 1 } } \\ { y _ { 2 } } & { y _ { 8 } } & { H _ { 2 } } \\ { y _ { 3 } } & { y _ { 9 } } & { H _ { 3 } } \end{array} \right| } .
$$

As $\Delta$ and $\Delta ^ { \prime }$ have $H _ { 1 }$ in common, the remaining factor of $\Delta$ or $\Delta ^ { \prime }$ in $f _ { 0 }$ cancels with the corresponding factor of the numerator,and only a power of $H _ { 1 }$ remains in the denominator of $f _ { 0 }$ . If we eliminate $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 3 } , x _ { 4 } , x _ { 5 } , x _ { 6 }$ instead of $x _ { 2 } , x _ { 3 } , x _ { 4 } , x _ { 5 } , x _ { 6 } ,$ and $M _ { 3 } , M _ { 1 }$ instead of $M _ { 2 } , M _ { 3 }$ , then $f _ { 0 }$ takes the form

$$
G ( H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , M _ { 3 } , M _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { N } ) \cdot H _ { 2 } ^ { - q } .
$$

Similarly, if we eliminate $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , x _ { 4 } , x _ { 5 } , x _ { 6 } .$ , then we obtain

$$
G ( H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , M _ { 1 } , M _ { 2 } , y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { N } ) \cdot H _ { 3 } ^ { - \tau } .
$$

Suppose that none of the exponents $\phi , q , r$ of the denominator vanish. There is no linear relation among the $H _ { 4 }$ ， $H _ { 5 }$ ， $H _ { 6 }$ ， $M _ { 1 }$ ， $M _ { 2 }$ except the linear relation $H _ { 1 } M _ { 1 } + H _ { 2 } M _ { 2 } + H _ { 3 } M _ { 3 } \equiv 0$ among $M _ { 1 }$ ， $M _ { 2 }$ ， $M _ { 3 }$ ； therefore the functions $H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , M _ { 1 } , M _ { 2 } , M _ { 3 } , y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { N }$ in $f _ { 0 }$ can be considered independent. If we express $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , y _ { 3 }$ by means of $H _ { 1 }$ ， $H _ { 2 }$ ， $H _ { 3 }$ ,and the remaining $y _ { i }$ ,then,

$$
f _ { 0 } = G ( H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , M _ { 1 } , M _ { 2 } , M _ { 3 } , H _ { 1 } , H _ { 2 } , H _ { 3 } , y _ { 4 } , y _ { 5 } , . . . , y _ { N } ) \cdot H _ { 1 } ^ { - m } .
$$

Insofar as $x _ { i } , y _ { i }$ $( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , N )$ are finite, $f _ { 0 }$ cannot become infinite. Hence, we arrange $f _ { 0 }$ in the order of descending powers of $H _ { 1 }$ in the form

$$
f _ { 0 } = \frac { f _ { 0 0 } } { H _ { 1 } ^ { m } } + \frac { f _ { 0 1 } } { H _ { 1 } ^ { m - 1 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

where $f _ { 0 0 } , f _ { 0 1 } , \ldots$ are polynomials in the variables they contain. If $H _ { 1 } = 0$ ,that is,if $H _ { 2 } M _ { 2 } + H _ { 3 } M _ { 3 } = 0$ ，then $f _ { 0 0 }$ is zero.Hence, $f _ { 0 0 }$ has the factor $H _ { 2 } M _ { 2 } + H _ { 3 } M _ { 3 }$ ,that is, $f _ { 0 0 }$ is of the form

$$
f _ { 0 0 } = ( H _ { 2 } M _ { 2 } + H _ { 3 } M _ { 3 } ) f _ { 0 0 } ^ { \prime } ,
$$

where $f _ { 0 0 } ^ { \prime }$ is another polynomial. Hence

$$
f _ { 0 } = \frac { f _ { 0 1 } - M _ { 1 } f _ { 0 0 } ^ { \prime } } { H _ { 1 } ^ { m - 1 } } + \frac { f _ { 0 2 } } { H _ { 1 } ^ { m - 2 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

By repeating this process we can always express $f _ { 0 }$ in the form of a polynomial in $H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , M _ { 1 } , M _ { 2 } , M _ { 3 } , y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { N }$ without denominator.

# 6.5ENERGY INTEGRAL

Now we shall prove that any integral contains $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \dotsc , y _ { N }$ only in the combination

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { y _ { i } ^ { 2 } } { 2 \mu _ { i } } ,
$$

that is, in thecombination $T$ of the kinetic energy.

As we have shown, the function $\phi _ { 0 }$ is a polynomial of the form

$$
\phi _ { 0 } = G ( H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , M _ { 1 } , M _ { 2 } , M _ { 3 } , y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { N } )
$$

and satisfies

$$
\frac { d \phi _ { 0 } } { d t } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { i } } \frac { d y _ { i } } { d t } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { i } } \frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { i } } \cdot
$$

Let $X _ { i }$ be the function $\partial U / \partial x _ { i }$ expressed in terms of

$$
x _ { 1 } , Y _ { 2 } , Y _ { 3 } , . . . , Y _ { N } , y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { N } ;
$$

then $f _ { 2 }$ is of the form

$$
f _ { 2 } = \chi ( Y _ { 2 } , Y _ { 3 } , . . . , Y _ { N } , y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { N } ) - \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { y _ { 1 } } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { i } } \int X _ { i } d x _ { 1 } .
$$

The second term on the right-hand side can be transformed to

\frac { \kappa } { 2 } \frac { \partial \mathcal { F } } { \partial x _ { \perp } } y _ { \perp } \right) + \frac { y _ { \perp } } { \mu _ { \perp } } \frac { \partial ^ { \perp } } { \partial y _ { \perp } } \frac { \partial \mathcal { F } } { \partial y _ { \perp } } \frac { \partial \mathcal { G } } { \partial y _ { \perp } } \mathrm { d } x _ { \perp } } \\ & { - \frac  \ \end{array}
$$

where $\Sigma _ { A }$ denotes the sum over $n ( n - 1 ) / 2$ values of the form

$$
( A + B x _ { 1 } + C x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) ,
$$

and $m _ { 3 l } m _ { 3 k }$ the product of the masses of the two bodies corresponding to the distance ${ r } _ { 3 l , 3 k }$ in the denominator of (156) and (157) of Section 5.16. As $\chi ( Y _ { 2 } , . . . , Y _ { N } , y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { N } )$ does not give rise to any term containing $( A + B x _ { 1 } + C x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } )$ in the same denominator,the quantities multiplying each $( A + B x _ { 1 } + C x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 / 2 }$ are of the same nature as the function $\phi _ { 2 }$ itself; that is, they are polynomials in $y _ { 1 } , \dotsc , y _ { N }$ when expressed in terms of $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { N } , y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { N }$ Hence,

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { y _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { 1 } } + 2 \displaystyle \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { N } \left( \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { j } } - \frac { \mu _ { 1 } y _ { j } } { \mu _ { j } y _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { 1 } } \right) } \\ { \times \frac { - A \displaystyle \frac { \partial B } { \partial Y _ { j } } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } x _ { 1 } B \displaystyle \frac { \partial B } { \partial Y _ { j } } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } B \displaystyle \frac { \partial A } { \partial Y _ { j } } + C x _ { 1 } \displaystyle \frac { \partial A } { \partial Y _ { j } } } { B ^ { 2 } - 4 A C } } \end{array}
$$

is a polynomial in $y _ { 1 } , \dotsc , y _ { N }$ when expressed in terms of

$$
y _ { 1 } , \dotsc , y _ { N } , x _ { 1 } , \dotsc , x _ { N } .
$$

First, take $x _ { 3 j - 2 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 j - 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 j } ^ { 2 }$ for $A + B x _ { 1 } + C x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }$ ； then，by thedefinition of $\textstyle { Y _ { i } }$ ，

$$

Puting these in (22),we eventually obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \frac { 1 } { y _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { 1 } } - \sum _ { k = 0 , 1 , 2 } \bigg ( \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { 3 j - k } } - \frac { \mu _ { 1 } y _ { 3 j - k } } { \mu _ { 3 j - k } y _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { 1 } } \bigg ) } } \\ & { \times \ [ x _ { 3 j - k } ( x _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 3 j - 2 } / \mu _ { 3 j - 2 } + x _ { 3 j - 1 } y _ { 3 j - 1 } / \mu _ { 3 j - 1 } + x _ { 3 j } y _ { 3 j } / \mu _ { 3 j } ) } \\ & { - \ \big ( y _ { 3 j - k } / \mu _ { 3 j - k } \big ) ( x _ { 3 j - 2 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 j - 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 j - 1 } ^ { 2 } ) } \\ & { \div } & { \big ( x _ { 3 j - 1 } y _ { 3 j } / \mu _ { 3 j } - x _ { 3 j } y _ { 3 j - 1 } / \mu _ { 3 j - 1 } ) ^ { 2 } + \big ( x _ { 3 j } y _ { 3 j - 2 } / \mu _ { 3 j - 2 } - x _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 3 j } / \mu _ { 3 j - 4 } \big ) } \\ & { } & { + \ \big ( x _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 3 j - 1 } - x _ { 3 j - 1 } y _ { 3 j - 2 } / \mu _ { 3 j - 4 } \big ) } \end{array}
$$

Next we take $x _ { 3 j - 2 } x _ { 3 k - 2 } + x _ { 3 j - 1 } x _ { 3 k - 1 } + x _ { 3 j } x _ { 3 k } ( j = 2 , 3 , . . . , n - 1 )$ ， and obtain similar expressions.For $x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 } ^ { 2 }$ we have

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { A = \frac { \mu _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } { y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } ( Y _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + Y _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } ) , } \\ & { B = 2 \bigg ( Y _ { 2 } \frac { y _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 2 } } + Y _ { 3 } \frac { y _ { 3 } } { \mu _ { 3 } } \bigg ) \bigg ( \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { y _ { 1 } } \bigg ) ^ { 2 } , } \\ & { C = 1 + \bigg ( \frac { y _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } } + \frac { y _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } } \bigg ) \bigg ( \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { y _ { 1 } } \bigg ) ^ { 2 } , } \end{array}
$$

and (22) becomes

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \displaystyle \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { 1 } } - \sum _ { r = 2 , 3 } \bigg ( \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { r } } - \frac { \mu _ { 1 } y _ { r } } { \mu _ { r } y _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { 1 } } \bigg ) } & { } \\ { \displaystyle } & { \qquad \times \frac { 2 x _ { r } \Big ( \frac { x _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } + \frac { x _ { 2 } y _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 2 } } + \frac { x _ { 3 } y _ { 3 } } { \mu _ { 3 } } \Big ) - 2 \frac { y _ { r } } { \mu _ { r } } ( x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } + x _ { 3 } ^ { 2 } ) } { \Big ( \frac { x _ { 2 } y _ { 3 } } { \mu _ { 3 } } - \frac { x _ { 3 } y _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 2 } } \Big ) ^ { 2 } + \Big ( \frac { x _ { 3 } y _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } - \frac { x _ { 1 } y _ { 3 } } { \mu _ { 3 } } \Big ) ^ { 2 } + \Big ( \frac { x _ { 1 } y _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 2 } } - \frac { x _ { 2 } y _ { 1 } } { \mu _ { 1 } } \Big ) ^ { 2 } } . } \end{array}
$$

As $G$ is a polynomial in $H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 }$ , the expression

$$
\left( { \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { i } } } - { \frac { \mu _ { 1 } y _ { i } } { \mu _ { i } y _ { 1 } } } { \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { 1 } } } \right) \qquad ( i = 2 , 3 , . . . , N )
$$

is a polynomial in $H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 }$ ; and $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , x _ { 3 }$ are contained only through $H _ { 4 }$ ， $H _ { 5 }$ ， $H _ { 6 }$ . Hence,(23) and (24) either have no term containing ${ \pmb x } _ { 1 } , { \pmb x } _ { 2 } , { \pmb x } _ { 3 }$ ，or have terms containing ${ \pmb x } _ { 1 } , { \pmb x } _ { 2 }$ or $\pmb { x } _ { 3 }$ . In neither case can the denominator be a factor of the numerator.Hence, in order that both (23)and (24) may hold, the condition is

$$
\frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { 2 } } - \frac { \mu _ { 1 } y _ { 2 } } { \mu _ { 2 } y _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { 1 } } = \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { 3 } } - \frac { \mu _ { 1 } y _ { 3 } } { \mu _ { 3 } y _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { 1 } } = 0 ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { 3 j - 2 } } - \frac { \mu _ { 1 } y _ { 3 j - 2 } } { \mu _ { 3 j - 2 } y _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { 1 } } = \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { 3 j - 1 } } - \frac { \mu _ { 1 } y _ { 3 j - 1 } } { \mu _ { 3 j - 1 } y _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { 1 } } } \\ { = \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { 3 j } } - \frac { \mu _ { 1 } y _ { 3 j } } { \mu _ { 3 j } y _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { 1 } } = 0 . } \end{array}
$$

These constitute $3 n - 4$ equations； indeed, they form a complete system in the sense of Section 4.9.From what was stated in Sections 4.1 and 4.17, we see,as the number of $y _ { i }$ is $N = 3 n - 3 ;$ ，that there exists one and only one independent integral. This must be

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { N } { \frac { y _ { i } ^ { 2 } } { 2 \mu _ { i } } } = T = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } .
$$

This is the total kinetic energy of the dynamical system with $N$ degrees of freedom. Hence no integral can contain $y _ { 1 } , \dotsc , y _ { N }$ independently, but must contain them only in the combination $T _ { s }$ ,and indeed the integral is a polynomial in $T .$

We are discussing differential equations (1） that have been reduced to the order $6 n - 6$ from the order $6 n$ by Poincaré's transformation $\beta$ of Section 5.6. The function $H _ { 4 }$ ， $H _ { 5 }$ ， $H _ { 6 }$ ， $M _ { 1 }$ ， $M _ { 2 }$ ， $M _ { 3 }$ of Section 6.4 are consequently expressed in terms of $6 n - 6$ variables $y _ { i } , x _ { i }$ .Thus the total momentum,total angular momentum,and total kinetic energy remain unaltered after Poincaré's transformation $\beta$ and are of the forms (17), (18),and (25),respectively.Hence these expressions represent the total momentum, total angular momentum,and total kinetic energy of the whole $\pmb { n } .$ -body system and hence are the classical integrals. The only supposition is that the nth fictitious body after the transformation is at rest； that is,

$$
y _ { 3 n - 2 } = y _ { 3 n - 1 } = y _ { 3 n } = x _ { 3 n - 2 } = x _ { 3 n - 1 } = x _ { 3 n } = 0 .
$$

Now $\phi _ { 0 }$ is homogeneous in $x _ { i }$ and $y _ { i }$ .The integrals $H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , M _ { 1 } , M _ { 2 } ,$ $M _ { 3 }$ are linear in $x _ { i }$ .The kinetic energy $T$ is quadratic in $y _ { i }$ and does not contain $x _ { i }$ .Hence,if $T$ is contained in $\phi _ { 0 }$ ,then $\phi _ { 0 }$ should be of the form

$$
\phi _ { 0 } = h ( H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , M _ { 1 } , M _ { 2 } , M _ { 3 } ) \cdot T ^ { m } ,
$$

where $\boldsymbol { h }$ is homogeneous in the variables it contains.

As we have shown, the function $f _ { 2 }$ is of the form

$$
f _ { 2 } = \chi ( Y _ { 2 } , . . . , Y _ { N } , y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { N } ) - \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { y _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { 1 } } U ,
$$

where

$$
\frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { y _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial G } { \partial y _ { 1 } } = \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { y _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial G } { \partial T } \frac { \partial T } { \partial y _ { 1 } } = m h ( H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , M _ { 1 } , M _ { 2 } , M _ { 3 } ) \cdot T ^ { m - 1 } .
$$

Hence

$$
= \chi ( Y _ { 2 } , . . . , Y _ { N } , y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { N } ) - m h ( H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , M _ { 1 } , M _ { 2 } , M _ { 3 } ) \cdot T ^ { m - 1 } ,
$$

Thus

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \phi = \phi _ { 0 } + \phi _ { 2 } + \phi _ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \\ { { \ = h ( H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , M _ { 1 } , M _ { 2 } , M _ { 3 } ) ( T ^ { m } - m T ^ { m - 1 } U ) } } \\ { { \ \quad \ + \chi ( Y _ { 2 } , . . . , Y _ { N } , y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { N } ) + \phi _ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot . . } } \end{array}
$$

Accordingly,anyintegral can be considered to consist of two integrals; the one is the combination $h ( H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , M _ { 1 } , M _ { 2 } , M _ { 3 } ) ( T - U ) ^ { m }$ of

the classical integrals, and the other is $\phi ^ { \prime } = \phi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } + \phi _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } + \phi _ { 4 } ^ { \prime } + \cdot \cdot \cdot _ { }$ where $T - U$ is the total energy and $\phi ^ { \prime }$ is such that

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \phi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } = \chi ( Y _ { 2 } , . . . , Y _ { N } , y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { N } ) , } } \\ { { \phi _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = \phi _ { 4 } - \displaystyle \frac { m ( m - 1 ) } { 2 ! } h ( H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , M _ { 1 } , M _ { 2 } , M _ { 3 } ) T ^ { m - 2 } U ^ { 2 } , } } \\ { { \phi _ { 4 } ^ { \prime } = \phi _ { 6 } + \displaystyle \frac { m ( m - 1 ) ( m - 2 ) } { 3 ! } h ( H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , M _ { 1 } , M _ { 2 } , M _ { 3 } ) T ^ { \pi } } } \\ { { \vdots } } \end{array}
$$

But $\phi ^ { \prime }$ is an integral of the same form as $\phi$ ，The difference is that its highest-degree term $\phi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$ with regard to $y _ { i }$ is of degree 2 less than the highest-degree term $\phi _ { 0 }$ of $\phi$ .We have shown that $\phi$ is composed of $\phi ^ { \prime }$ and the classical integrals.Similarly, $\phi ^ { \prime }$ is composed of the classical integrals and an integral of the same nature as $\phi ^ { \prime }$ but of degree 2 less than $\phi ^ { \prime }$ with regard to ${ { y } _ { i } }$ . By repeating this argument we show that $\phi$ is composed of the classical integrals and an integral $\phi ^ { ( n ) }$ of the same nature as $\phi$ but of degree either l or O with regard to $y _ { i }$ .If $\phi ^ { ( n ) }$ is of degree l, then the exponent $k$ of $_ T$ in

$$
\phi ^ { ( n ) } = \phi _ { 0 } ^ { ( n ) } = h ( H _ { 4 } , H _ { 5 } , H _ { 6 } , M _ { 1 } , M _ { 2 } , M _ { 3 } ) T ^ { k }
$$

is $k = 0$ Then $\phi ^ { ( n ) }$ is composed of the classical integrals. If $\phi ^ { ( n ) }$ isof degree O,then it is a function of $x _ { i }$ alone.However,we proved in the beginning that such an integral never exists.Hence, $\phi$ must be a combination of the classical integrals.
