Thus,we have obtained Bruns's theorem that any integral of the $\pmb { n }$ -body problem that is algebraic with respect to the coordinates and momenta and does not contain $t$ explicitly is an algebraic combination of the classical integrals.

# 6.6INTEGRALS CONTAINING TIME

We have proved Bruns's theorem for the integrals of the form (2）of Section 6.l on the assumption that the integral does not contain the time $t$ explicitly. Now we shall prove that the theorem is also true for integrals that do contain $t$ explicitly. We consider the original form of the differential equations of order $6 n$ for the $\tilde { n \cdot }$ body problem,

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial H } { \partial y _ { i } } , \frac { d y _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { i } } ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 6 n ) ,
$$

and suppose that there is an integral with an arbitrary constant

$$
f ( x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { 3 n } , y _ { 1 } , \dots , y _ { 3 n } , t ) = a ,
$$

in which $f$ is algebraic (though not necessarily rational) in $\pmb { x _ { i } } , \pmb { y _ { i } }$ and $t .$ We rationalize $f$ in the form

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { a ^ { m } + a ^ { m - 1 } \phi _ { 1 } ( x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { 3 n } , y _ { 1 } , \dots , y _ { 3 n } , t ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { + \phi _ { m } ( x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { 3 n } , y _ { 1 } , \dots , y _ { 3 n } , t ) = 0 , } } \end{array}
$$

where the coefficients $\phi _ { r } \left( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , m \right)$ are rational in $t$ and algebraic in ${ x _ { i } , y _ { i } }$ . We may consider this equation irreducible with regard to $t .$

Diferentiate (28) with respect to $t$ ; then,

$$
a ^ { m - 1 } \frac { d \phi _ { 1 } } { d t } + a ^ { m - 2 } \frac { d \phi _ { 2 } } { d t } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \frac { d \phi _ { m } } { d t } = 0 ;
$$

but $d \phi _ { \tau } / d t \ ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , m )$ is rational with regard to $t$ when expressed in terms of $x _ { i } , y _ { i }$ ，t.Equation (28) would not be irreducible unless this equation were identically zero. Therefore,

$$
\frac { d \phi _ { r } } { d t } = 0 \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

Hence, $\phi _ { \tau }$ itself is an integral and accordingly $f$ is rational in $t$ and is a combination of other integrals $\phi$ which are algebraic in ${ x _ { i } , y _ { i } }$

We decompose $\phi$ into linear factors in $t$ such that

$$
\frac { P ( t - \phi _ { 1 } ) ^ { m _ { 1 } } ( t - \phi _ { 2 } ) ^ { m _ { 2 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot ( t - \phi _ { k } ) ^ { m _ { k } } } { ( t - \psi _ { 1 } ) ^ { n _ { 1 } } ( t - \psi _ { 2 } ) ^ { n _ { 2 } } \cdot \cdot \cdot ( t - \psi _ { l } ) ^ { n _ { l } } } ,
$$

where $P , \phi _ { 1 } , . . . , \phi _ { k } , \psi _ { 1 } , . . . , \psi _ { l }$ are algebraic in $x _ { i } , \ y _ { i }$ Since this is an integral, we have

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \frac { 1 } { \overline { { { P } } } } \frac { \partial { P } } { d t } + \frac { m _ { 1 } } { t \mathrm { ~ - ~ } \phi _ { 1 } } \left( 1 - \frac { d \phi _ { 1 } } { d t } \right) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \frac { m _ { k } } { t \mathrm { ~ - ~ } \phi _ { k } } \left( 1 - \frac { d \phi _ { k } } { d t } \right) } } \\ { { - \frac { n _ { 1 } } { t \mathrm { ~ - ~ } \psi _ { 1 } } \left( 1 - \frac { d \psi _ { 1 } } { d t } \right) - \cdot \cdot \cdot - \frac { n _ { l } } { t \mathrm { ~ - ~ } \psi _ { l } } \left( 1 - \frac { d \psi _ { l } } { d t } \right) } } \end{array}
$$

If we replace

$$
\frac { d { \cal P } } { d t } , \frac { d \phi _ { 1 } } { d t } , . . . , \frac { d \phi _ { k } } { d t } , \mathrm { a n d } \frac { d \psi _ { 1 } } { d t } , . . . , \frac { d \psi _ { l } } { d t } ,
$$

respectively, by $( P , H ) , ( \phi _ { 1 } , H ) , . . . , ( \phi _ { k } , H )$ and $( \psi _ { 1 } , H ) , . . . , ( \psi _ { l } , H )$ using Poissn's theorem (Section 4.3), then this equation becomes an identity, that is,

$$
\frac { d P } { d t } = 0 , 1 - \frac { d \phi _ { 1 } } { d t } = 0 , . . . , 1 - \frac { d \phi _ { k } } { d t } = 0 ,
$$

$$
1 - \frac { d \psi _ { 1 } } { d t } = 0 , . . . , 1 - \frac { d \psi _ { \imath } } { d t } = 0 ;
$$

in other words, each of the expressions

$$
P , t - \phi _ { 1 } , . . . , t - \phi _ { k } , t - \psi _ { 1 } , . . . , t - \psi _ { l }
$$

must be an integral. Hence,any algebraic integral of the $\pmb { n }$ -body problem containing $t$ consists of an algebraic integral not containing $\pmb { t }$ and an integral of the form $t - \phi =$ constant, where $\phi$ is algebraic in ${ x _ { i } } , { y _ { i } }$

Now

$$
t + \frac { \Xi _ { 1 } } { \overline { { \mathrm { H } } } _ { 1 } } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ,
$$

where

$$
\Xi _ { 1 } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 j - 2 } x _ { 3 j - 2 } , \qquad \mathrm { H } _ { 1 } = { } - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 3 j - 2 }
$$

is the integral representing the motion of the center of masses of the $\pmb { n }$ bodies. Hence, any algebraic integral containing $t$ is composed of an algebraic integral not containing $t _ { : }$ ,an integral of the form

$$
\phi + \frac { \Xi _ { 1 } } { \mathrm { H } _ { 1 } } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ,
$$

where $\phi$ is an integral algebraic in $x _ { i } , \ y _ { i }$ ， and the classical integral $t + \Xi _ { 1 } / \mathrm { H } _ { 1 }$ . The first two of these three integrals are algebraic integrals not containing $t$

We can similarly discuss the classical integrals for the motion of the center of mass:

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { \displaystyle t + \frac { \Xi _ { 2 } } { { \cal H } _ { 2 } } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } , \quad } } & { { \Xi _ { 2 } = \displaystyle \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 j - 1 } x _ { 3 j - 1 } , \quad \quad { \mathrm { H } } _ { 2 } = - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 3 j - 1 } } } \\ { { \displaystyle t + \frac { \Xi _ { 3 } } { { \cal H } _ { 3 } } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } , \quad } } & { { \Xi _ { 3 } = \displaystyle \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { 3 j } x _ { 3 j } , \quad \quad \quad { \mathrm { H } } _ { 3 } = - \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n } y _ { 3 j } . } } \end{array}
$$

Hence,any algebraic integral containing $t$ is an algebraic combination of the classical integrals. The classical integrals are algebraic in $x _ { i } , y _ { i } , t .$ Thus it must be concluded that there does not exist any integral that is algebraic in $x _ { i } , y _ { i } , t ,$ other than the classical integrals.Thus, the theorem of Bruns has been proved also in the case of integrals containing $t$ explicitly.

# PAINLEVE'S EXTENSION

# 6.7PREPARATORY LEMMAS

Painleveé (1896,1896a,1896b,1897a,1898) extended Bruns's theorem to the case in which the integral contains algebraically the momentum only,while the coordinates are left arbitrary.

The equations for the $\pmb { n }$ -body problem constitute a system here denoted by (S):

$$
\vdots \quad { \frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } } = { \frac { \partial H } { \partial y _ { i } } } , \qquad { \frac { d y _ { i } } { d t } } = - \ { \frac { \partial H } { \partial x _ { i } } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , \ldots , 3 n ) ,
$$

where $\pmb { H }$ does not contain $t$ explicitly, and $U$ is the sum of the products of the masses and the reciprocals of the $n ( n - 1 ) / 2$ mutual distances. The sum of the $n ( n - 1 ) / 2$ mutual distances is denoted by $\pmb { S } .$ The force function $U$ is expressed by a rational function of $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n }$ and $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ Let $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ denote the system of differential equations for which $U = 0$ ， that is, for which no force is acting:

$$
( \mathrm { S } _ { 0 } ) : \qquad \frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { 1 } { m _ { i } } y _ { i } , \qquad \frac { d y _ { i } } { d t } = 0 \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n ) .
$$

Our problem is to discuss whether the system (S) admits an integral that is algebraic in $y = ( y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } )$ . Such integrals are assumed irreducible and of the form with an arbitrary constant $^ { a }$

$$
f ( y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } ) = a ,
$$

in which $t$ is contained only implicitly. Here we propose to extend the results of Sections 6.1 and 6.2.

Lemma 1: An integral of (S) that is algebraic with respect to $\pmb { y }$ is an algebraic combination of the integrals of (S) that are rational in $_ y$

Rationalizing (31),we obtain

$$
a ^ { m } + a ^ { m - 1 } \phi _ { 1 } ( x _ { i } , y _ { i } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + a \phi _ { m - 1 } ( x _ { i } , y _ { i } ) + \phi _ { m } ( x _ { i } , y _ { i } ) = 0 ,
$$

where the coefficients $\phi ( x _ { i } , y _ { i } )$ are rational in $\pmb { y }$ .Differentiating by referring to (30),we obtain

$$
a ^ { m - 1 } ( \phi _ { 1 } , H ) + a ^ { m - 2 } ( \phi _ { 2 } , H ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + ( \phi _ { m } , H ) = 0 ,
$$

where the Poisson brackets $( \phi _ { j } , H )$ are supposed to be nonzero and algebraic in $y$ .In order that (32） and (33) may hold simultaneously they should admit one or more than one common root.Then (33) would become reducible,which is contrary to the assumption.Hence $( \phi _ { j } , H )$ should all be equal to zero.By Poisson's theorem (Section 4.3） the functions $\phi _ { j }$ should all be integrals of (S). Thus the integrals $\phi _ { j }$ are rational in $\pmb { y }$

The highest degree of any term in a polynomial is called the degreee of the polynomial. An integral that is rational in $\pmb { y }$ is called a rational integral and an integral that is a polynomial in $\pmb { y }$ is called a polynomial integral. Write a rational integral $f$ in the form

$$
f = \frac { \Pi _ { 2 } + \Pi _ { 1 } } { K _ { 2 } + K _ { 1 } } ,
$$

where $\Pi _ { 1 }$ and $\pmb { K _ { 1 } }$ are of odd degree and $\Pi _ { 2 }$ and $\pmb { K _ { 2 } }$ of even degree. The system (S) remains unaltered by the change of $t , y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n }$ into $- t , - y _ { 1 } , . . . , - y _ { 3 n }$ .By this transformation $f$ istransformed to $f ^ { \prime } = ( \Pi _ { 2 } - \Pi _ { 1 } ) / ( K _ { 2 } - K _ { 1 } )$ and $f ^ { \prime }$ is still an integral of (S). The sum $f + f ^ { \prime }$ and the product $f f ^ { \prime }$ contain only even-degree terms in $\pmb { y }$ and are both integrals of (S).Hence we can limit our discussion to real and rational integrals of (S), in which the numerator and the denominator are of the same parity, that is, either both are of even degree or both are of odd degree. The sum of odd-degree terms and the sum of even-degree terms of a rational integral are also integrals.

Lemma 2:Any rational integral of (S) is an algebraic combination of real rational integrals that contain only terms of the same parity.

If a function $\Theta ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } , y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } )$ becomes zero when the variables ${ \pmb x } = ( { \pmb x } _ { 1 } , { \pmb x } _ { 2 } , . . . , { \pmb x } _ { 3 n } )$ and $y = ( y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \dotsc , y _ { 3 n } )$ in $\Theta$ are replaced by the solution of a differential equation for all values of $t$ ,then $\Theta = 0$ is called an invariantive relation according to Section 4.6.

Lemma 3: In order that a polynomial $P$ in $y$

$$
{ \begin{array} { r l } { P ( y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } ) \ ~ } & { } \\ & { \ } \\ & { \equiv \Pi ( y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } , s ) } \end{array} }
$$

be an invariantive relation for (S),it is necessary and sufficient that

$$
\frac { d P } { d t } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } \bigg ( \frac { \partial P } { \partial x _ { i } } \frac { y _ { i } } { m _ { i } } + \frac { \partial P } { \partial y _ { i } } \frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { i } } \bigg ) = \Omega ( y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } ) P ,
$$

where $\Omega$ is a polynomial in $y$ and should be linear, because the degree of the left-hand member is one higher than that of $P$

To prove the condition necessary, denote by $x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ a set of arbitrary values of $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n }$ for which the branch of $s = \phi ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } )$ under consideration is holomorphic, together with the corresponding branches of $\partial U / \partial x _ { i }$ (Section 2.20). Put $P \equiv \Pi [ y _ { i } , x _ { i } , \phi ( x _ { i } ) ]$ and suppose that $( y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } , x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } )$ is an arbitrary solution of the relation $\pmb { P } = 0$ . The equation $\Pi = 0$ is an invariantive relation for (S); that is, the solution $( y _ { i } ( t ) , x _ { i } ( t ) )$ of (S) with the initial condition $y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } , x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } , s _ { 0 } = \phi ( x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } )$ for $t = t _ { 0 }$ satisfies $P = 0$ identically. In particular, the value of $d P / d t$ for $t = t _ { 0 }$ that is,

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } \left( \frac { \partial P } { \partial { x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } } } \frac { y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } } { m _ { i } } + \frac { \partial P } { \partial { y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } } } \frac { \partial { U } } { \partial { x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } } } \right)
$$

is equal to zero. Hence $P ( y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } , x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } ) = 0$ should be related to

$$
\sum _ { i = 0 } ^ { 3 n } \biggl [ \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } } P ( y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } , x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } ) \frac { y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } } { m _ { i } } + \frac { \partial } { \partial y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } } P ( y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } , x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } ) \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } } U ( y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } , x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } ) \biggr ] = 0 .
$$

If a polynomial $\pmb { P }$ in $\pmb { y }$ is irreducible,then the left-hand member of this equation must have a factor $P ( y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } , x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } )$ ,and we obtain (34) which is written

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \log P = \Omega .
$$

Suppose,on the contrary, that $\pmb { P }$ can be decomposed into a product $P = P _ { 1 } P _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot P _ { l }$ of irreducible polynomials of degrees $\alpha _ { 1 } , \alpha _ { 2 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { l } ,$ respectively, of which the factors need not be mutually independent. Then, since each polynomial must satisfy (34) we have

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \log P _ { j } = \Omega _ { j } \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , l ) .
$$

Thus

$$
{ \frac { d } { d t } } \log P = { \frac { d } { d t } } \log \left( P _ { 1 } P _ { 2 } \cdot \cdot \cdot P _ { l } \right) = \Omega _ { 1 } + \Omega _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \Omega _ { l } \equiv \Omega ,
$$

or

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } \left( \frac { \partial P } { \partial x _ { i } } \frac { y _ { i } } { m _ { i } } + \frac { \partial P } { \partial y _ { i } } \frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { i } } \right) = \Omega P .
$$

To prove the condition sufficient, suppose that $P ( y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } , x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } ) = 0$ for $t = t _ { 0 }$ Since the solution $( y _ { i } ( t ) , x _ { i } ( t ) )$ of (S） satisfies

$$
\frac { d P } { d t } = \Omega P
$$

and hence

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } P } { d t ^ { 2 } } = \Omega \frac { d P } { d t } + P \frac { d \Omega } { d t } = \Big ( \Omega ^ { 2 } + \frac { d \Omega } { d t } \Big ) P ,
$$

the derivatives $d P / d t , d ^ { 2 } P / d t ^ { 2 } , \ldots .$ are all zero for $t = t _ { 0 }$ ,and we have $P \equiv 0$ identically by substituting this solution.

From this lemma it follows that, if $P _ { y }$ is the aggregate of the highestdegree terms of $P$ and if $\Omega$ is written $\Omega = \Omega _ { 1 } + \Omega _ { 0 } ,$ where $\Omega _ { 0 }$ is the part of $\Omega$ independent of $\pmb { y }$ ,then

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } \frac { \partial P _ { v } } { \partial x _ { i } } \frac { y _ { i } } { m _ { i } } = \Omega _ { 1 } P _ { v } ;
$$

in other words, $P _ { v } = 0$ is an invariantive relation for $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ .If $P$ contains only terms of the same parity, then $\Omega$ cannot contain $\Omega _ { 0 }$ and is linear homogeneous with respect to $\pmb { y }$ .If $\Omega$ is of the form

$$
\Omega = \frac { \partial \lambda } { \partial x _ { 1 } } \frac { y _ { 1 } } { m _ { 1 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \frac { \partial \lambda } { \partial x _ { 3 n } } \frac { y _ { 3 n } } { m _ { 3 n } } ,
$$

where $\lambda$ is a function of $_ { x }$ only, then $P e ^ { - \lambda }$ is also a polynomial integral of (S).

Finally, if $P / Q$ is a rational integral of (S),and if $P _ { v } , \ Q _ { \mu }$ are the aggregates of the highest-degree terms of $P$ and $Q$ ，respectively， then $P = 0 , Q = 0$ are invariantive relations of (S) and we have

$$
\frac { d } { d t } \log { P } = \Omega , \frac { d } { d t } \log { Q } = \Omega ^ { \prime } .
$$

However, $\Omega$ coincides with $\Omega ^ { \prime }$ ,because

$$
{ \frac { d } { d t } } \log \left( { \frac { P } { Q } } \right) \equiv 0 .
$$

Conversely, if $P$ and $Q$ satisfy the same equation (34), then $P / Q$ is an integral of (S). In this case the equations $P _ { v } = 0$ ， $Q _ { \mu } = 0$ are two invariantive relations for $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ , since

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } \frac { \partial P _ { \nu } } { \partial x _ { i } } \frac { y _ { i } } { m _ { i } } = \Omega _ { 1 } P _ { \nu } , \qquad \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } \frac { \partial Q _ { \mu } } { \partial x _ { i } } \frac { y _ { i } } { m _ { i } } = \Omega _ { 1 } Q _ { \mu } .
$$

The fraction $P _ { \nu } / Q _ { \mu }$ is an integral of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ . If the integral $P / Q$ contains only terms of the same parity, then $\Omega$ coincides with $\Omega _ { 1 }$ .If $\mathcal { P } _ { \textbf { v } }$ is a polynomial integral of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ , then $\Omega _ { 1 } = 0$ and $\pmb { P }$ and $Q$ are both polynomial integrals of (S). In other words, we may state

Lemma $4 \colon { \mathrm { I f ~ } } f = P / Q$ is a rational integral of (S) and the denominator and the numerator both consist of terms of the same parity, and if $P _ { \nu }$ the highest-degree term in $\mathcal { P }$ , is an integral of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ ，then $P$ and $Q$ are both polynomial integrals of (S).

# 6.8 PROPERTIES OF (S)

Consider a rational integral $f = P / Q$ of (S)，where $\pmb { P }$ and $Q$ are supposed to be relatively prime polynomials in $y$ .If a certain number of algebraic relations among the coefficients

$$
\alpha ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } ) , \beta ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } ) , . . . , \lambda ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } )
$$

of the polynomials $P$ and $Q$ ,their first order derivatives

$$
\frac { \partial \alpha } { \partial \pmb { x } _ { 1 } } , \hdots , \frac { \partial \lambda } { \partial \pmb { x } _ { 3 n } } ,
$$

and the given functions $\partial U / \partial x _ { i }$ ，are compatible,then the general solution may depend either on these functions or on arbitrary constants. We propose the following

Fundamental Theorem: If all functions $\partial U / \partial x _ { i }$ $^ { \prime } / \partial { x _ { i } } \ ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n )$ are holomorphic at $\pmb { x } _ { 1 } = x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } = x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ then $f = P / Q$ is either holomorphic or meromorphic at

$$
y _ { 1 } = y _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } = y _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \qquad x _ { 1 } = x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } = x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } .
$$

A function holomorphic at a point can be expanded in Taylor series convergent around that point.A meromorphic function is expressible as the quotient of two holomorphic functions at that point and accordingly admits no singularity other than poles (Section 2.2O).Iffcan be separated into several branch functions, then $f$ is said to be meromorphic when each branch function is meromorphic.When $f$ is neither holomorphic nor meromorphic at a singular point, that point is said to be a nonpolar singular point. $\operatorname { I f } f = P / Q$ is meromorphic at

$$
y _ { 1 } = y _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } = y _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \qquad x _ { 1 } = x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } = x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } ,
$$

then the coefficients $\alpha , \beta , \ldots , \lambda$ of $P$ ， $Q$ are meromorphic at

$$
x _ { 1 } = x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } = x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } .
$$

Conversely, if $\alpha , \beta , . . . , \lambda$ are meromorphic at $x _ { 1 } = x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } = x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ then $f = P / Q$ is meromorphic at

$$
y _ { 1 } = y _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } = y _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \qquad x _ { 1 } = x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } = x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } .
$$

Suppose that $x _ { 1 } = a _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } = a _ { 3 n }$ is a point at which the branch function of $\partial U / \partial x _ { i }$ is holomorphic.Assume that the branch functions corresponding to an integral $f$ were neither holomorphic nor meromorphic at that point. Then there would exist a point $( b _ { 1 } , . . . , b _ { 3 n } )$ of $( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } )$ in an arbitrary neighborhood of $( a _ { 1 } , . . . , a _ { 3 n } )$ at which the functions $\alpha , \beta , \ldots , \lambda$ would all be holomorphic.The function $f ( y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } )$ is either holomorphic or meromorphic at $y _ { 1 } = b _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } = b _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime } , x _ { 1 } = b _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } = b _ { 3 n } .$ ，whatever the values $b _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , b _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime }$ may be.Let the solution of (S） with the initial condition $x _ { i } = a _ { i } , y _ { i } = y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ $( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n )$ for $t = t _ { 0 }$ be

$$
x _ { i } = a _ { i } + \frac { y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } } { 1 } ( t - t _ { 0 } ) + \frac { 1 } { 2 ! } \frac { \partial } { \partial a _ { i } } U ( a _ { 1 } , . . . , a _ { 3 n } ) ( t - t _ { 0 } ) ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot
$$

$$
y _ { i } = y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } + { \frac { \partial } { \partial x _ { i } } } U ( a _ { 1 } , . . . , a _ { 3 n } ) ( t - t _ { 0 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot \equiv \chi _ { i } ( t , y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } )
$$

With a real positive number $\scriptstyle { R }$ the functions $\psi _ { i }$ and $\chi _ { i }$ are holomorphic with respect to $t$ and $y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ in the domain denoted by $( \mathbf { D } _ { \pmb { R } } )$ ：

$$
( \mathrm { D } _ { R } ) : \qquad | t - t _ { 0 } | < R , \quad | y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } | < R .
$$

As $\pmb { t }$ and $y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ vary within $( \mathbf { D } _ { R } )$ ， the variables $x _ { 1 } = \psi _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } = \psi _ { 3 n }$ coincide with the values in the neighborhood of $( a _ { 1 } , . . . , a _ { 3 n } )$ . In particular, these variables coincide, for a suitably chosen value $t _ { 1 } , a _ { i } ^ { \prime } \cot t , y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ ， with a value $( b _ { 1 } , . . . , b _ { 3 n } )$ for which $\alpha , \beta , \ldots , \lambda$ are holomorphic. Put $b _ { i } ^ { \prime } = \chi _ { i } ( t _ { 1 } , a _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , a _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , a _ { 3 n } ^ { \prime } )$ $( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n )$ ; then

$$
f ( y _ { 1 } , \dots , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { 3 n } )
$$

is either holomorphic or meromorphic at

$$
x _ { i } = b _ { i } , \qquad y _ { i } = b _ { i } ^ { \prime } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n ) .
$$

Let the general solution of (S) with the initial condition

$$
x _ { i } = x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \qquad y _ { i } = y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n ) ,
$$

be

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { x _ { i } = \Psi _ { i } ( t , y _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \dots , y _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } , x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \dots , x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } ) } \\ { y _ { i } = \Xi _ { i } ( t , y _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \dots , y _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } , x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \dots , x _ { 8 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } ) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , \dots , 3 n ) . } \end{array}
$$

The functions $\Xi _ { i }$ and $\Psi _ { i }$ are holomorphic at $t = t _ { 1 } , x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } = a _ { i } , y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } = a _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ and equal to $b _ { i }$ and $b _ { i . } ^ { \prime }$ , respectively.

Let the result of replacing $y _ { i } , x _ { i }$ by $\Xi _ { i } , \Psi _ { i }$ respectively in an integral

$$
f ( y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } )
$$

be

$$
F ( t , y _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } , x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } ) .
$$

This is either holomorphic or equal to the quotient of two holomorphic functions at $t = t _ { 1 } , x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } = a _ { i } , y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } = a _ { i } ^ { \prime }$ Since $f$ is an integral, equation (37) should be satisfied identically, that is,

$$
f ( y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } ) \equiv f ( y _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } , x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } ) ,
$$

and $\pmb { F }$ coincides with $f ( y _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \dots , y _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } , x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \dots , x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } )$ . Hence $F$ does not depend on $t .$ On the other hand, $f ( y _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \dots , y _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } , x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \dots , x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } )$ is either holomorphic or meromorphic at $y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } = a _ { i } ^ { \prime } , x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } = a _ { i }$ 、This contradicts the assumption. Hence the theorem is proved.

From this theorem we derive the following important

Corollary: An integral $f ( y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } )$ of (S)，which is rational in $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n }$ and is the most general of its degree $\nu$ , can contain only a finite number of arbitrary constants, and its nonpolar singularities coincide with the singularities of $\partial U / \partial x _ { i }$

The coefficient functions $\alpha , \beta , \ldots , \lambda$ cannot depend on any arbitrary functions.For, consider the differential relations connecting the functions $\alpha ( x _ { 1 } , . ~ . ~ . , x _ { 3 n } ) , \beta ( x _ { 1 } , . ~ . ~ . , x _ { 3 n } ) , . ~ . ~ . .$ Either the general solution of this system depends on a finite number of constants, or alternatively, for any given values of the first $j \left( j < 3 n \right)$ variables $x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , x _ { j } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ ， we can choose an arbitrary one of the unknown functions, for example,

$$
\alpha ( x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , x _ { j } ^ { ( 0 ) } , x _ { j + 1 } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } ) \equiv \alpha _ { 0 } ( x _ { j + 1 } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } ) .
$$

In the latter case it is reasonable to take for $\alpha _ { 0 }$ a function that admits $x _ { j + 1 } = x _ { j + 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } = x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ asa nonpolar singularity, where the values $\pmb { x } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , ~ . ~ . ~ . ~ , \pmb { x } _ { j } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \pmb { x } _ { j + 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , ~ . ~ . ~ . ~ , \pmb { x } _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ do not coincide with any of the singularities of $\partial U / \partial x _ { i }$ The integral $f$ then would admit a nonpolar singularity $x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ that is not a singularity of $\partial U / \partial x _ { i }$ This is contradictory,and $\alpha , \beta , \ldots , \lambda$ can depend only on a finite number of constants.

If the integral of (S) is a polynomial $f = P$ ，then the singularities of $f$ should coincide with those of $\partial U / \partial x _ { i }$ .The coefficients $\alpha , \beta , \ldots , \lambda$ of $P$ satisfy linear homogeneous relations with respect to $\alpha , \beta , \ldots , \lambda$ and their derivatives. The general solution of these relations depends on only a finite number of arbitrary constants of the form $c _ { 1 } f _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + c _ { j } f _ { j } .$ where $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , \ldots , c _ { j }$ are constants and $f _ { 1 } , \ldots , f _ { j }$ are particular solutions.

# 6.9 PROPERTIES OF $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$

In order to study the rational integrals of (S） we first discuss the system $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ . Let a rational integral of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ be $f ( y _ { 1 } , \dots , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , \dots , x _ { 3 n } )$ From the theorem just proved, the integral $f$ has only polar singularities in the finite region. The points $x _ { 1 } = \infty , . . . , x _ { 3 n } = \infty$ may be essential singularities of $f .$ It will be proved that in reality they are not essential singularities, but $f$ should be algebraic in $_ { x }$ and even rational in $_ x$

It is sufficient to prove that $x _ { 1 } = \infty$ , for example, is not an essential singularity $_ { \mathrm { o f } f }$ ;that is, $f ( y _ { 1 } , . ~ . ~ . , y _ { 3 n } , 1 / \xi _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . ~ . ~ . , x _ { 3 n } )$ is, as a function of $\xi _ { 1 }$ ，either holomorphic or meromorphic for $\xi _ { 1 } = 0$ . We carry out Appell's transformation, which we denote by $( \mathrm { T } )$ ：

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle x _ { 1 } = \frac { 1 } { \xi _ { 1 } } , ~ x _ { 2 } = \frac { \xi _ { 2 } } { \xi _ { 1 } } , ~ \ldots , ~ x _ { 3 n } = \frac { \xi _ { 3 n } } { \xi _ { 1 } } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle y _ { 1 } = - \eta _ { 1 } , ~ y _ { 2 } = \eta _ { 2 } \xi _ { 1 } - \eta _ { 1 } \xi _ { 2 } , ~ \ldots , ~ y _ { 3 n } = \eta _ { 3 n } \xi _ { 1 } - \eta _ { 1 } \xi _ { 3 n } , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d t } { d \tau } = \frac { 1 } { \xi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } . } } \end{array}
$$

Then $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ becomes $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } )$ ：

$$
( \mathrm { S } _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } ) : \qquad \frac { d \xi _ { i } } { d \tau } = \eta _ { i } , \qquad \frac { d \eta _ { i } } { d \tau } = 0 \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 3 n ) .
$$

To an integralfof $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ corresponds an integral $\phi ( \eta _ { 1 } , . . . , \eta _ { 3 n } , \xi _ { 1 } , . . . , \xi _ { 3 n } )$ of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } )$ .This laterintegral isational withresectto $\eta _ { 1 } , \dotsc , \eta _ { 3 n }$

By the fundamental theorem this function $\phi$ should be of the form $A / B$ around $\xi _ { 1 } = 0 , . . . , \xi _ { 3 n } = 0 _ { : }$ ，where $A$ and $B$ are polynomials in $\eta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \eta _ { 3 n }$ whose coefficients are expanded in positive ascending powers of $\xi _ { 1 } , . . . , \xi _ { 3 n }$ By substituting $y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$ for

$$
\eta _ { 1 } , . . . , \eta _ { 3 n } , \xi _ { 1 } , . . . , \xi _ { 3 n }
$$

in $\phi$ by means of $( \mathbf { T } )$ , that is, by writing

$$
\xi _ { 2 } = \xi _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } , \quad . \ . \ . , \quad \xi _ { 3 n } = \xi _ { 1 } x _ { 3 n } ,
$$

$$
\eta _ { 1 } = - y _ { 1 } , \quad \eta _ { 2 } = \frac { y _ { 2 } } { \xi _ { 1 } } - y _ { 1 } x _ { 2 } , \quad . . . , \quad \eta _ { 3 n } = \frac { y _ { 3 n } } { \xi _ { 1 } } - y _ { 1 } x _ { 3 n } ,
$$

the expression $A / B$ takes the form $A _ { 1 } / B _ { 1 } ,$ where $\ b { A } _ { 1 }$ and $B _ { 1 }$ are both polynomials of $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n }$ whose coefficients are expanded in positive ascending powers of $\xi _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$ The fraction $A _ { 1 } / B _ { 1 : }$ as a function of $\xi _ { 1 } ,$ admits $\xi _ { 1 } = 0$ as an ordinary point or as a pole, because it coincides with $f ( y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , 1 / \xi _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } )$ ·

Thus it has been shown that any integral of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ that is rational in $y$ should be rational in $\pmb { x } .$ 、Accordingly, it should be represented as the quotient $P / Q$ of two relatively prime polynomials $\pmb { P }$ and $Q$ ,in

$$
y _ { 1 } , \dotsc , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , \dotsc , x _ { 3 n } .
$$

We prove that $P$ and $Q$ are both integrals of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ . For this purpose we prove the following

Lemma : Suppose that $\Pi ( y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } ) = 0$ is an invariantive relation for $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ and is anireducible polynomial o $\Gamma y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } .$ Then $\Pi$ is an integral of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$

In fact, $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ admits integrals

$$
\begin{array} { c c } { { y _ { 1 } = y _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , } } & { { \ldots \cdot \cdot , } } & { { y _ { 3 n } = y _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } , } } \\ { { } } & { { } } \\ { { x _ { i } y _ { 1 } - x _ { 1 } y _ { i } = x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } y _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } - x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } } } & { { ( i = 2 , 3 , \ldots , 3 n ) . } } \end{array}
$$

From these integrals we can obtain $x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } , y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n }$ as functions of $x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } , x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ Substituting in $\boldsymbol { \Pi }$ ,we obtain $\Pi$ in the form

$$
\Pi _ { 1 } \big ( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } , x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } \big ) / \big ( y _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } \big ) ^ { r } ,
$$

where $\Pi _ { 1 }$ is a polynomial containing the factor

$$
\Pi ( y _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } , x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } ) \equiv \Pi _ { 0 } ,
$$

so that

$$
\Pi _ { 1 } \equiv \Pi _ { 0 } { \cal L } ( x _ { 1 } , y _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } , x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } ) .
$$

Suppose tentatively that $L$ contains $\pmb { x } _ { 1 }$ . Let

$$
y _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } , x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) }
$$

have arbitrary values that would make neither $y _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ nor $\Pi _ { 0 }$ vanish. Let $\bar { x } _ { 1 }$ be a value of $\pmb { x } _ { 1 }$ that would make $L$ vanish. The solution of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ with the initial condition that $x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } , y _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ have the aforesaid arbitrary values would make $\boldsymbol { \Pi }$ vanish for $x _ { 1 } = \bar { x } _ { 1 }$ ；accordingly $\Pi$ would become identically zero.Since $\Pi _ { 0 }$ should vanish with this initial condition, it is contradictory to assume that $\scriptstyle { \pmb { L } }$ contains $\pmb { x } _ { 1 }$ .Hence $L$ ， and accordingly $\Pi _ { 1 }$ ,does not contain $\pmb { x } _ { 1 }$ .Thus, $L \equiv 1$ ,and $\Pi =$ constant is an integral of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$

Suppose that

$$
P \equiv P _ { 1 } ^ { l } \cdot \cdot \cdot P _ { i } ^ { l _ { i } } , \qquad Q \equiv Q _ { 1 } ^ { m _ { j } } \cdot \cdot \cdot Q _ { j } ^ { m _ { j } } ,
$$

where $\mathcal { P }$ and $Q$ are relatively prime polynomials $\mathrm { f } y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } .$ Then $P _ { 1 } , . . . , P _ { i } , Q _ { 1 } , . . . , Q _ { j }$ are irreducible polynomials in

$$
y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } ,
$$

where $l _ { 1 } , . . . , l _ { i } , m _ { 1 } , . . . , m _ { j }$ are positive integers. The relation $P _ { 1 } = 0$ is an invariantive relation,and,according to the property just established, ${ \cal P } _ { 1 } =$ constant is an integral. Similarly, $P _ { 2 } , . . . , P _ { i } , Q _ { 1 } , . . . , Q _ { j }$ are integrals. Hence $\boldsymbol { P } =$ constant, $Q =$ constant are integrals of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ . Thus we arrive at the following

Theorem: Any integral of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ which is rational in $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n }$ equals the quotient of two relatively prime polynomials in $y _ { 1 } , . ~ . ~ . , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , . ~ . ~ . , x _ { 3 n } ,$ whose denominator and numerator are both integrals of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ ：

Hence,any polynomial integral of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ is also a polynomial in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$ ，Otherwise,the integral would be of the form $P / Q$ and $Q$ would be a polynomial in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$ and an integral of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ . We come to the important conclusion that an integral of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ which is algebraic in $y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n }$ is also algebraic in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$

We have seen that, if $\Pi = 0$ is an invariantive relation for $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ ,where $\Pi$ is a polynomial in $y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n }$ ， with coeficients rational in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } ,$ then $\Pi =$ constant is an integral of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ ; in other words, if

$$
\Phi ( y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } ) = 0
$$

is an invariantive relation for $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ ，where $\Phi$ is a polynomial whose coefficients are rational in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$ ，then there exists a function $\Lambda ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } )$ such that $\Lambda \Phi =$ constant is also an integral of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ . Note that this holds not necessarily only when the coefficients in $\Phi$ are rational.

We propose to examine the properties of an invariantive relation, denoted by (E), for $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ ：

$$
\Phi ( y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } ) = 0 ,
$$

where $\Phi$ is a homogeneous polynomial in $y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n }$ and is algebraic in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$ . Suppose that one of the coeffcients. of $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n }$ in the polynomial $\Phi$ is unity,and that the other coefficients are algebraic but not rational in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$

Geometrically, let $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$ be the rectangular coordinates of a point in a $_ { 3 n }$ -dimensional space and let

$$
x _ { i } = y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } t + x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , : . . , 3 n )
$$

be a straight line in that space. If $y _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ x _ { i } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ satisfy $\Phi = 0$ ， then this relation is satisfed all along the straight line $( \mathbf { D } )$ .Hence (E) represents a line complex, in which (E） defines a cone with the point $( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } )$ as vertex. Let the locus of points at which various branch functions of $\Phi$ interchange, that is, the bifurcation surface, be denoted $( \Sigma )$ ：

$$
( \Sigma ) \colon \quad \ \Sigma ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } ) = 0 ,
$$

which is the equation of an algebraic surface. This leads to the following

Theorem: The tangents to the surface $\pmb { \Sigma }$ form a part of the line complex (E).

It is suffcient to prove that if we choose arbitrarily the coordinate axes $0 \pmb { x } _ { 1 } , . . . , 0 \pmb { x } _ { 3 n }$ ，then the tangents to $( \Sigma )$ parallel to the $( 3 n - 2 )$ planes $x _ { 3 } = 0 , . . . , x _ { 3 n } = 0$ form a part of the complex (E). The coordinate axes do not belong to (E） because those axes are taken arbitrarily. Let $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ be the degree of $\Phi$ with respect to $\pmb { y }$ 、Take the coefficient of $y _ { 1 } ^ { p }$ as unity. Terms in $y _ { 1 } ^ { p } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n } ^ { p }$ are all present. The origin is an ordinary point for various branch functions. Consider two branch functions $\Phi _ { j }$ and $\Phi _ { \imath }$ of $\Phi$ Then $\Phi _ { j } - \Phi _ { l } = 0$ defines a cone $\mathbf { \Gamma } \mathbf { \Gamma } \mathbf { \Gamma }$ with $( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } )$ as vertex. The cone $\Gamma$ corresponding to $x _ { 1 } = 0 , . . . , x _ { 3 n } = 0$ contains $0 \pmb { x } _ { 1 }$ but no other coordinate axes. Let $\alpha ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } )$ be the coefficient of $\dot { y } _ { 2 } ^ { p }$ in $\Phi$ The two values $\alpha _ { j }$ and $\alpha _ { l }$ of $\pmb { \alpha }$ corresponding to these two branch functions $\Phi _ { j }$ and $\Phi _ { l }$ cannot coincide identically. If they coincide they should do so at the origin；then $\Phi _ { j } - \Phi _ { l }$ would be zero for $y _ { 1 } = y _ { 3 } = y _ { 4 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = y _ { 3 n } = 0$ and would vanish all along the $0 \pmb { x _ { 2 } }$ axis.

Let $P ( x _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } )$ be an arbitrary point of $( \Sigma )$ , at which the tangent plane is not parallel to the $0 { x } _ { 1 }$ axis. Suppose that

$$
x _ { 1 } = g ( x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } )
$$

is the equation of $( \Sigma )$ near $P$ ，where $\pmb { g }$ is holomorphic in $x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$ and equal to ${ \pmb x } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ for $x _ { 2 } = x _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } = x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ Suppose further that $j$ branch functions $\Phi _ { 1 } , . . . , \Phi _ { j }$ of $\Phi$ interchange in the region of $\pmb { P }$ Put $z = ( x _ { 1 } - g ) ^ { 1 / 3 }$ ; then $\Phi _ { 1 }$ is expanded in ascending powers of $_ z$ whose coefficients are polynomials in $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n }$ and holomorphic in $x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$ for $x _ { 2 } = x _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } = x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ If we give to $x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$ the values $x _ { 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } $ respectively， and vary $\pmb { x _ { 1 } }$ by making a circuit around the value ${ \pmb x } _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ ,then $\Phi _ { 1 }$ changes to $\Phi _ { 2 }$ , and one value ${ \pmb { \alpha } } _ { \bf 1 }$ of $\pmb { \alpha }$ changes to the value ${ \pmb { \alpha } } _ { 2 }$ of another branch. If we give to $x _ { 3 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$ in $\Phi$ the values $x _ { 3 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } ;$ ，respectively， andput $y _ { 3 } = y _ { 4 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = y _ { 3 n } = 0$ ，then Clairaut's equation (e) is obtained:

$$
0 = 1 + \alpha \big ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , x _ { 3 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } \big ) \Big ( \frac { d x _ { 2 } } { d x _ { 1 } } \Big ) ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

where the coefficient $\pmb { \alpha }$ admits the curve

$$
\Sigma ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , x _ { 3 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \dots , x _ { 3 n } ^ { ( 0 ) } ) = 0
$$

on the plane $( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } )$ as the locus of the branch point.Hence the tangents to this curve satisfy (e)； in other words, the tangents to $( \Sigma )$ parallel to the planes $x _ { 3 } = 0 , . . . , x _ { 3 n } = 0$ form a part of the line complex (E).

As a corollary to this theorem we note the following property:

The surface $( \Sigma )$ may be decomposed into several distinct surfaces, some of which may be planes. If $( \Sigma )$ consists only of planes, then the locus of the branch points of the right-hand member of (e) should consist of straight lines,which is contradictory.Hence, $( \Sigma )$ cannot consist only of planes. Thus, all tangents to $( \Sigma )$ form a part of (E), $( \Sigma )$ contains at least a nonplanar surface $( \Sigma ^ { \prime } )$ , and the tangents to $( \Sigma )$ form a complex contained in (E).

Consider the consequences of this theorem. Let $G$ be an irreducible polynomial in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$ and $s _ { \mathrm { { ; } } }$ and $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ be an algebraic function of $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$ defined by $G ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } , s ) = 0$ 、We propose to study the condition for the existence of an invariantive relation for $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ in the form

$$
\Phi ( y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } ) = 0 ,
$$

where $\Phi$ is a polynomial in $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n }$ whose coeffcients are rational in $x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } ,$ $s$ but irrational in $x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n }$

Let the bifurcation surface of $s ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } )$ be

$$
\Sigma ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } ) = 0 .
$$

If $( \Sigma )$ is composed of planes, then there is no invariantive relation of the form $\Phi = 0 .$ Let $\Sigma _ { 1 } , \Sigma _ { 2 } , \dots$ be irreducible nonplanar surfaces contained in $( \Sigma )$ . Then the tangents to $\Sigma _ { 1 } , \Sigma _ { 2 } , \ldots$ form the complexes

$$
{ } _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } ) = 0 , \psi _ { 2 } ( y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } ) = 0 , . . . ,
$$

where $\psi$ are irreducible polynomials in $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n }$ For the existence of an invariantive relation of the form $\Phi = 0$ it is necessary and sufficient that at least one of the $\psi$ can be decomposed into a product of polynomials in $y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n }$ that are rational in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } ,$ s.

Let $\phi ( y _ { 1 } , . ~ . ~ . , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , . ~ . ~ . , x _ { 3 n } )$ be one of the decomposed factors. If this condition is satisfied,then $\phi = 0$ is an invariantive relation of the form $\Phi = 0$ 、Hence the condition is sufficient.

We drop from $\Phi$ those polynomials in $y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n }$ that are rational in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$ and are contained in $\Phi$ as factors. The complex $\Phi = 0$ contains at least one of the complexes $\psi = 0$ .Let it be $\psi _ { 1 }$ . Since the point $( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } )$ is chosen arbitrarily,the cone $\Phi = 0$ and the cone $\psi _ { 1 } = 0$ must have a common part; in other words, the polynomials $\Phi$ and $\psi _ { 1 }$ in $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n }$ must have the largest common factor $\chi ( y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } )$ ， whose coefficients are expressed rationally in terms of the coefficients of $\Phi$ and $\psi _ { 1 }$ , and consequently as functions of $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } , s$ Furthermore, since it divides $\Phi$ , the factor $\chi$ is not rational in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$ . Hence the polynomial is of the form $\psi _ { 1 } = \chi \chi _ { 1 }$ and the polynomials $\boldsymbol { \chi }$ and $\chi _ { 1 }$ in $y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n }$ are rational in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } , $ . Thus the condition is necessary.

If the irrationality $s$ is real and the polynomial $\Phi$ is real in $( \mathbf { D } )$ ,as the variables $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n }$ vary in the real domain $( \mathrm { D } )$ of the $3 n$ -dimensional space $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$ then the coefficients of $G$ are real and the coefficients of the polynomials $\psi$ are real. $\chi$ is also real, together with $\Phi$ and $\psi _ { 1 }$ This gives us the following

Theorem : If the irrationality $s$ is real in the real domain of the space $( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } )$ ，then,in order that there may exist a real invariantive relation $\Phi = 0$ in $( \mathrm { D } )$ , it is necessary and sufficient that at least one of the polynomials $\psi$ should be decomposed into a product of polynomials in $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n }$ that are rational and real in $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } , s .$

If none of the polynomials $\psi$ can be decomposed in this manner, then an invariantive relation $\Phi = 0$ ，which is a polynomial in $y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n }$ and real and rational in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } , s ,$ should be of the form

$$
\Phi = \Lambda ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } , s ) P ,
$$

where $P$ represents a polynomial integral of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ and $\Lambda$ is rational in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } , s .$ This result plays an important role in the $\pmb { n }$ -body problem.

# 6.10 PROPERTIES OF THE INTEGRALS OF (S)

Let $f = P / Q$ be an integral of (S) that is rational in $y$ . We have seen in Section 6.8 that $f$ can contain only a finite number of arbitrary constants. We now prove that these constants can be so chosen that they are contained in $f$ algebraically.

Put

$$
y _ { 1 } = { \frac { 1 } { u } } , \qquad { \frac { y _ { 2 } } { y _ { 1 } } } = v _ { 2 } , . . . , \qquad { \frac { y _ { 3 n } } { y _ { 1 } } } = v _ { 3 n } ;
$$

then $P / Q$ becomes a rational function $f _ { 1 }$ of $u , v _ { 2 } , \ldots , v _ { 3 n }$ and can be expanded in the form

$$
f _ { 1 } = u ^ { \ i } R _ { 0 } + u ^ { \ i + 1 } R _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + u ^ { \ i + i } R _ { i } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

where $u ^ { l } R _ { 0 }$ is the quotient of the highest-degree terms in $P$ and $Q$ with respect to $y$ ,and $\mathbf { \xi } _ { l }$ denotes an integer, which may be negative. By Section 6.7 it is an integral $P ^ { \prime } / Q ^ { \prime }$ of $( \mathsf { S } _ { 0 } )$ where $P ^ { \prime }$ and $Q ^ { \prime }$ are relatively prime with regard to $_ y$ .The rational function $f _ { 1 }$ contains a finite number $h$ of independent constants. We can choose an integer $j$ sufficiently large so that the first $j + 1$ terms in the expansion of $f _ { 1 }$ , that is,

$$
F _ { 1 } = u ^ { l } R _ { 0 } + u ^ { l + 1 } R _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + u ^ { l + j } R _ { j } ,
$$

can be made to depend on just $h$ independent constants.

At first we prove that these constants can be chosen so that they are contained algebraically in $F _ { 1 }$ . The expression $u ^ { l } R _ { 0 } \equiv P _ { 1 } / Q _ { 1 }$ is defined by a system of differential equations $( \mathbf { D } _ { 1 } )$ which contain algebraically the coefficients of $P _ { 1 }$ and $Q _ { 1 }$ together with their derivatives. On the other hand,if $P ^ { \prime } / Q ^ { \prime }$ is a rational integral of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ ,then $P ^ { \prime }$ and $Q ^ { \prime }$ contain linearly a finite number of constants $a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \dotsc$ Substituting $P ^ { \prime }$ and $Q ^ { \prime }$ in $( \mathbf { D _ { 1 } } )$ for $P _ { 1 }$ and $Q _ { 1 }$ ，we obtain a certain number of algebraic relations among the constants $\ i _ { 1 } , \ i _ { 2 } , \ldots \ i $ Suppose that the first $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ constants, for example, are independent; then $P _ { \mathrm { 1 } } / Q _ { \mathrm { 1 } } \mathrm { o r } u ^ { \ i } R _ { \mathrm { 0 } }$ contain algebraically the independent constants $a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \dotsc , a _ { p } .$

If the following term $u ^ { l + 1 } R _ { 1 }$ of $F _ { 1 }$ does not contain any new constant, then the term can be obtained algebraically as a function of

$$
x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } , u , v _ { 2 } , \ldots , v _ { 3 n }
$$

of $R _ { 0 }$ and its derivatives, that is,as a function of

$$
y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } , a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { p } .
$$

Suppose that $u ^ { l + m } R _ { m }$ is the first term that contains constants other than $a _ { 1 } , \dotsc , a _ { p }$ ; then the frst $\pmb { m }$ terms of $F _ { 1 }$ depend algebraically on $a _ { 1 } , \dotsc , a _ { p }$ The term

$$
u ^ { \iota + m } R _ { m } \equiv \rho ( y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } , a _ { 1 } , . . . , a _ { p } , b _ { 1 } , . . . , b _ { q } )
$$

is defined by a system of differential equations $( \mathbf { D } _ { m } )$ which contains algebraically this function $\rho$ and its derivatives, $y , x , R _ { 0 }$ and its derivatives. Hence $( \mathbf { D } _ { m } )$ is algebraic with respect to $\rho$ ，its derivatives,and the constants $a _ { 1 } , \dotsc , a _ { p }$ . Suppose that the numerical values $b _ { 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \ldots , b _ { q } ^ { ( 0 ) }$ are given to $b _ { 1 } , . . . , b _ { q } ,$ respectively. Let $f ^ { \prime }$ be the corresponding function $f ,$ and $\rho ^ { \prime } = u ^ { l + m } R ^ { \prime }$ be the corresponding function $\pmb { \rho }$ .The difference $f - f ^ { \prime }$ is still an integral of (S). If $f - f ^ { \prime }$ is expanded in increasing powers of $\pmb { u }$ ,then the first term of the expansion is

$$
u ^ { l + m } ( R _ { m } - R _ { m } ^ { \prime } ) \equiv \rho - \rho ^ { \prime } ,
$$

which defines an integral of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ rational in $\pmb { y }$ .The difference $\sigma = \rho - \rho ^ { \prime }$ is defined by $( \mathbf { D } _ { m } ^ { \prime } )$ ,which is an immediate transform of $( \mathbf { D } _ { m } )$ , algebraic with respect to $\pmb { \sigma }$ , its derivatives, and $a _ { 1 } , . . . , a _ { p }$ Hence it is sufficient to substitute $\pmb { \sigma }$ for the rational integral of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ in order to see that $\sigma$ contains algebraically $a _ { 1 } , \dotsc , a _ { p }$ and the new constants, $a _ { p + 1 } , \dotsc , a _ { p + q } ,$ for example.

The procedure can be followed until all terms of $F _ { 1 }$ are disposed of. Let $u ^ { l + m + n } R _ { m + n }$ be the first term of ${ \cal F } _ { 1 }$ that algebraically contains constants other than $a _ { 1 } , \dotsc , a _ { p + q }$ . The term $u ^ { l + m + n } R _ { m + n } \equiv \rho _ { 1 }$ is defined by a differential system $( \mathrm { D } _ { m + n } )$ , where $\rho _ { 1 }$ appears algebraically with its derivatives and $a _ { 1 } , \dotsc , a _ { p + q } .$ Let $\rho _ { 1 } ^ { ' }$ be a particular solution of $( \mathrm { D } _ { m + n } )$ ； then $\sigma _ { 1 } = \rho _ { 1 } - \rho _ { 1 } ^ { \prime }$ is defined by a differential system $( \mathrm { D } _ { m + n } ^ { \prime } )$ which is algebraic with respect to $\sigma _ { 1 }$ , its derivatives, and $a _ { 1 } , \dotsc , a _ { p + q } .$ On the other hand, since $\sigma _ { 1 }$ is an integral of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ , we see that $\sigma _ { 1 }$ ,and hence $\pmb { \rho _ { 1 } }$ ， algebraically contains $a _ { 1 } , \dotsc , a _ { p + q }$ and the new constants suitably chosen.

Thus the $h$ independent constants $a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { h }$ contained in $F _ { 1 }$ appear algebraically if they are chosen in a suitable manner.

Next we prove that $f _ { 1 }$ contains $a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { h }$ algebraically.

Let $\displaystyle \overline { { F } } _ { 1 }$ be the value of $F _ { 1 }$ in which $a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { h }$ are given their numerical values $\bar { a } _ { 1 } , \dotsc , \bar { a } _ { h }$ Let $A ( u , v _ { 2 } , \ldots , v _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } )$ be a function of $\pmb { u }$ holomorphic at $u = 0$ .Consider all integrals $f _ { 1 }$ whose expansions are of the form $f _ { 1 } = \bar { F } _ { 1 } + u ^ { l + j } A$ None of these functions $f _ { 1 }$ can contain any constant, otherwise $f _ { 1 }$ would contain at least $h + 1$ constants. Denote by $( \Delta )$ the differential system that defines $f _ { 1 }$ . These equations are rational with respect to the coefficients $\alpha _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 1 } , \ldots$ and their derivatives in the function $f _ { 1 }$ ，which is rational in u $, v _ { 2 } , \ldots , v _ { 3 n }$ ，The condition that $f _ { 1 } - \overbar { F } _ { 1 }$ is divisible by $u ^ { l + j }$ is expressed by algebraic relations among $\alpha _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 1 } , \ldots ,$ in which $\bar { a } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \bar { a } _ { h }$ appear algebraically. Denote by $( \Delta ^ { \prime } )$ the whole system of conditions obtained by adding this condition to $( \Delta )$ . The most general solution $\alpha _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 1 } , . . . \circ \mathrm { f } \left( \Delta ^ { \prime } \right)$ cannot contain any constant. Hence $\alpha _ { 1 } , \beta _ { 1 } , \ldots$ contain $\bar { a } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \bar { a } _ { h }$ only algebraically. Since this is true whatever the constants $\bar { a } _ { 1 } , \ldots , \bar { a } _ { h }$ are,the integral $f _ { 1 }$ is an algebraic function of its h independent constants a1,..., an·

This theorem has an important consequence. We have seen that the integral is of the form $f = P / Q$ ，where $P$ and $Q$ are relatively prime polynomials in $y$ . Denote by $P _ { v } , \ Q _ { \mu }$ the aggregate of the highest-degree terms with regard to $y$ in $P$ ， $Q$ ,respectively.We have equated one of the coefficients of $P _ { v } , \ Q _ { \mu }$ to unity. We prove that the other coefficients of $P _ { v } , \ Q _ { \mu }$ are algebraic functions of $_ { x }$

The polynomial $P _ { v } , \ Q _ { \mu }$ need not necessarily be relatively prime. Denote by $\pmb { D }$ the largest common factor of these two polynomials. Writing $P _ { \nu } / Q _ { \mu }$ in the irreducible form $P ^ { \prime } / Q ^ { \prime }$ ，we see that $P ^ { \prime } / Q ^ { \prime }$ is an integral of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ rational in $_ x$ Hence,we can write $P _ { \nu } = D P ^ { \prime }$ ， $Q _ { \mu } = D Q ^ { \prime }$ ， where $P ^ { \prime }$ ， $Q ^ { \prime }$ are two polynomials in $y , x .$ .We factorize the polynomial $D ( y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } )$ into irreducible polynomials $D = D _ { 1 } ^ { \lambda } D _ { 2 } ^ { \mu } \cdot \cdot \cdot$ If $D _ { 1 } = 0$ contains at least one of the constants $a _ { 1 } , \ldots , a _ { h }$ —say ${ \pmb a } _ { 1 }$ - then $D _ { 1 }$ is algebraic with respect to $\pmb { x }$ Indeed, $a _ { 1 }$ is contained algebraically in $D _ { 1 }$ ， so that $D _ { 1 } = 0$ is written $d _ { 1 } ( y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } , a _ { 1 } ) = 0$ ，which is an irreducible polynomial in $a _ { 1 }$ and $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n }$ Since $d _ { 1 } = 0$ is an invariantive relation for $( \mathsf { S } _ { 0 } )$ , the ratios of the coeffcients of various powers of $a _ { 1 }$ in $d _ { 1 }$ are also integrals of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ .Hence, they are algebraic and rational with respect to $_ x$ Thus $D _ { 1 } = 0$ is algebraic with respect to $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } .$ As a result we can write

$$
\begin{array} { r } { P _ { v } = C ( x _ { i } ) \Pi ( y _ { i } , x _ { i } ) \Delta ( y _ { i } , x _ { i } ) , } \\ { Q _ { \mu } = C ( x _ { i } ) \mathrm { K } ( y _ { i } , x _ { i } ) \Delta ( y _ { i } , x _ { i } ) . } \end{array}
$$

where the polynomials $\Pi$ and $\mathbf { K }$ in $y$ are algebraic in $_ x$ and the polynomial $\Delta$ in $y$ contains no constant except one coefficient which is unity. This polynomial $\Delta$ is defined by a differential system which algebraically contains its coefficients $\alpha _ { j } ( x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } )$ , their derivatives,and $_ x$ but not any constant. From this evolves the theorem that the polynomials $P _ { \nu } , Q _ { \mu }$ in $y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n }$ become algebraic in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$ by being divided by a suitably chosen function $C ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } )$

Next, we prove that $P _ { \nu }$ ， $Q _ { \mu }$ can be regarded as rational in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } , s .$ For, suppose that, as the variables $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } , s$ related by the algebraic equation $G ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } , s ) = 0$ , after varying in a certain manner, return to their initial values, the polynomials $P _ { \nu } , \ Q _ { \mu }$ take $j$ different values $[ P _ { \nu } ^ { ( 1 ) } , Q _ { \mu } ^ { ( 1 ) } ] , . . . , [ P _ { \nu } ^ { ( j ) } , Q _ { \mu } ^ { ( j ) } ]$ .Denote the $j$ branch functions offcorrespond-ing to these $j$ branches $\operatorname { b y } f _ { 1 } , \ldots , f _ { j } .$ These $j$ functions are each integrals of (S)with respect to one of the branches for the corresponding values of $s .$ ，The symmetric functions

$$
{ \cal F } _ { 1 } = \Sigma f _ { 1 } , \qquad { \cal F } _ { 2 } = \Sigma f _ { 1 } f _ { 2 } , \qquad \dots , \qquad { \cal F } _ { j } = \Sigma f _ { 1 } f _ { 2 } , \dots , f _ { j }
$$

are also integrals of (S). Since the terms similar to $P _ { \nu } , \ Q _ { \mu }$ in these $j$ new integrals are rational in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } , s ,$ a rational integral of (S) is an algebraic combination of the rational integrals where the terms $P _ { v } , Q _ { \mu }$ are rational with respect to $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } , s$

According to Section 6.7 it is reasonable to consider those integrals that contain only terms of degrees of the same parity (both odd or both even） and are real in the same $3 n$ -dimensional real domain as that of $\partial U / \partial x _ { i }$ If $\partial U / \partial x _ { i }$ are real in this real domain $( \mathbf { D } )$ ，then all rational integrals of (S) are algebraic combinations of rational integrals which are real in $( \mathbf { D } )$ and contain only terms of degrees of the same parity,and their terms $P _ { v } , \ Q _ { \mu }$ are rational algebraic in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } , s$ .

Hence, we limit ourselves to considering such integrals. Let $\Sigma = 0$ be the bifurcation surface for the irrational function $s ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } )$ . Let $\Sigma _ { 1 } = 0 , \Sigma _ { 2 } = 0 , . . .$ be irreducible nonplanar surfaces contained in $\Sigma = 0$ Let $\psi _ { 1 } = 0$ ， $\psi _ { 2 } = 0 , . . .$ .be the equations, written as poly-nomials in $y _ { i }$ and $x _ { i } .$ ， for the line complexes of the tangents to $\Sigma _ { 1 } , \Sigma _ { 2 } , \dotsb .$

Suppose that none of the polynomials $\psi _ { 1 } , \psi _ { 2 } , \ldots$ can be decomposed into a product of polynomials in $y _ { 1 } , \dotsc , y _ { 3 n } .$ ，rational in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } , s$ and real within $( \mathbf { D } )$ . Put $P _ { y } = 0$ in the rational integral $f = P / Q$ we are considering；then $P _ { y } = 0$ defines an invariantive relation for $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ which is a polynomial in $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n }$ ，rational in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } , s$ and real within $( \mathbf { D } )$ . Let $\Lambda$ be a suitably chosen function； then $\Lambda ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } ) P _ { \nu }$ is an integral of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ according to Section 6.9.Hence, $\Lambda P$ and $\Lambda Q$ are polynomial integrals of (S),according to Section 6.7. Thus we arrive at the

Theorem:The algebraic functions $\partial U / \partial x _ { i }$ ，which are real in the $3 n \textmd { - }$ dimensional real domain $( \mathbf { D } )$ ,are supposed to be represented by rational expressions in terms of the irrationality $s ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } )$ . Let $\Sigma _ { 1 } , \Sigma _ { 2 } , \dots$ be the nonplanar bifurcation surfaces of $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ ；let $\psi _ { 1 } = 0$ ， $\psi _ { 2 } = 0 , . . . ,$ written in the form of polynomials,be the equations for the line complexes of the tangents to $\Sigma _ { 1 } , \Sigma _ { 2 } , \dotsb .$ If none of the polynomials $\psi _ { 1 } , \psi _ { 2 } , \ldots$ can be decomposed into a product of polynomials in $y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n }$ ,rational in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } , s$ and real in $( \mathbf { D } )$ ， then all rational integrals of (S） are algebraic combinations of the polynomial integrals of (S).

# 6.11 APPLICATION TO THE $\pmb { n }$ -BODY PROBLEM

Put

$$
\begin{array} { l } { r _ { 3 j , 3 k } = \sqrt { ( x _ { 3 j - 2 } - x _ { 3 k - 2 } ) ^ { 2 } + ( x _ { 3 j - 1 } - x _ { 3 k - 1 } ) ^ { 2 } + ( x _ { 3 j } - x _ { 3 k } ) ^ { 2 } } , \quad } \\ { \quad \quad s = \displaystyle \sum _ { j , k } r _ { 3 j , 3 k } \qquad ( j , k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ; \quad \quad } \end{array}
$$

then the function $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ has $2 ^ { n ( n - 1 ) / 2 }$ branches.To each of these branches corresponds one and only one value of $r _ { 3 j , 3 k }$ . The irrationality $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ satisfies an irreducible algebraic relation of degree $2 ^ { n ( n - 1 ) / 2 }$ with regard to $s ;$ that is, $G = 0$ ，The bifurcation surfaces of the function $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ are in a $3 n -$ dimensional space and defined by

$$
( x _ { 3 j - 2 } - x _ { 3 k - 2 } ) ^ { 2 } + ( x _ { 3 j - 1 } - x _ { 3 k - 1 } ) ^ { 2 } + ( x _ { 3 j } - x _ { 3 k } ) ^ { 2 } = 0 .
$$

The straight lines

$$
{ \bf \mu } _ { 1 j - 2 } = y _ { 3 j - 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } t + x _ { 3 j - 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \qquad x _ { 3 j - 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } = y _ { 3 j - 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } t + x _ { 3 j - 1 } ^ { ( 0 ) } , \qquad x _ { 3 j } ^ { ( 0 ) } = y _ { 3 j } ^ { ( 0 ) } t + x _ { 3 j - 2 } ^ { ( 0 ) } .
$$

are tangent to this surface and satisfy the relation

$$
\phi _ { j k } = ( \xi \xi ^ { \prime } + \eta \eta ^ { \prime } + \zeta \zeta ^ { \prime } ) ^ { 2 } - ( \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } + \zeta ^ { 2 } ) ( \xi ^ { \prime 2 } + \eta ^ { \prime 2 } + \zeta ^ { \prime 2 } ) = 0 ,
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l r l } & { \xi = x _ { 3 j - 2 } - x _ { 3 k - 2 } , } & & { \eta = x _ { 3 j - 1 } - x _ { 3 k - 1 } , } & & { \zeta = x _ { 3 j } - x _ { 3 k } , } \\ & { \xi ^ { \prime } = y _ { 3 j - 2 } - y _ { 3 k - 2 } , } & & { \eta ^ { \prime } = y _ { 3 j - 1 } - y _ { 3 k - 1 } , } & & { \zeta ^ { \prime } = y _ { 3 j } - y _ { 3 k } . } \end{array}
$$

The relation is written also in the form

$$
\psi _ { j k } = ( \xi \eta ^ { \prime } - \xi ^ { \prime } \eta ) ^ { 2 } + ( \eta \zeta ^ { \prime } - \eta ^ { \prime } \zeta ) ^ { 2 } + ( \zeta \xi ^ { \prime } - \zeta ^ { \prime } \xi ) ^ { 2 } = 0 ,
$$

where the $\psi _ { j k }$ are linear in $\xi ^ { \prime } , \eta ^ { \prime } ;$ ， $\zeta ^ { \prime }$ and cannot be decomposed into a product of linear factors in $\xi ^ { \prime } , \eta ^ { \prime } , \zeta ^ { \prime }$ ,which are real at the same time as $\xi , \eta , \zeta .$ Hence we obtain the following

Theorem: Any integral of the $\pmb { n }$ -body problem which is algebraic with respect to the momentum is an algebraic combination of polynomial integrals with respect to the momentum.

Denote such an integral by

$$
f = P _ { v } + P _ { v - 2 } + P _ { v - 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot = { \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } } ,
$$

where $P _ { v } , P _ { v - 2 } , P _ { v - 4 } , . . .$ are homogeneous polynomials in $y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } ,$ of degree $\nu , \nu - 2 , \nu - 4 , \ldots$ ，respectively. It is reasonable to suppose that this integral contains only terms of degrees of the same parity in $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n }$ . Since the term $P _ { \nu }$ is an integral of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ , it is a polynomial in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$ . We prove that $P _ { \nu - 2 }$ is also algebraic in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$

Let $P _ { v - 2 } ^ { \prime } , P _ { v - 2 } ^ { \prime \prime }$ be two of the branch functions of function $P _ { v - 2 }$ of $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } , s .$ ，These branch functions interchange as the variables $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } ,$ $\pmb { \mathscr { s } }$ resume their initial values after varying in a certain manner. $P _ { v - 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } \sim P _ { v - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ is a polynomial in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$ For,the two branch functions $\circ \mathrm { f } \ f - \ f ^ { \prime }$ and $f ^ { \prime \prime }$ — correspond to the two branch functions of $P _ { v - 2 }$ 一 $P _ { y \mathrm { ~ - ~ } 2 } ^ { \prime } , P _ { v \mathrm { ~ - ~ } 2 } ^ { \prime \prime }$ The branch functions $f ^ { \prime } , f ^ { \prime \prime } , \mathrm { a n d } f ^ { \prime } - f ^ { \prime \prime }$ are all integrals of (S). Since $P _ { \nu - 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } - P _ { \nu - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ is an integral of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ and is a polynomial in $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n } .$ , it is also a polynomial in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$

The functions $\partial U / \partial x _ { i }$ of (S) are of the form $\begin{array} { r } { \sum _ { j , l } A _ { j l } / r _ { 3 j , 3 l } ^ { 3 } } \end{array}$ where $A _ { j } { \iota }$ are linear in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$ The polynomials $P _ { v } , P _ { v - 2 }$ in $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n }$ satisfy identically

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } \frac { \partial P _ { v - 2 } } { \partial x _ { i } } \frac { y _ { i } } { m _ { i } } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } \frac { \partial P _ { v } } { \partial y _ { i } } \frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { i } } = 0 .
$$

If we substitute $\xi _ { 2 } , . . . , \xi _ { 3 n }$ defined by

$$
x _ { i } y _ { 1 } - x _ { 1 } y _ { i } = m _ { i } \xi _ { i } \qquad ( i = 2 , . . . , 3 n )
$$

for $x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n }$ the $P _ { y }$ and $P _ { v - 2 }$ become $\Pi _ { v }$ and $\Pi _ { v - 2 }$ ， respectively. Therefore the function $\Pi _ { \nu }$ is a rational fraction in $\xi _ { 2 } , . . . , \xi _ { 3 n } , y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n }$ and independent of $\pmb { x } _ { 1 }$ ,and is of the form

$$
M ( y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , \xi _ { 2 } , . . . , \xi _ { 3 n } ) \cdot m _ { 1 } ^ { \ i } y _ { 1 } ^ { - \ i } ,
$$

where $M$ is a polynomial. Also,

$$
\frac { \partial P _ { \nu } } { \partial y _ { i } } = \frac { \partial \Pi _ { \nu } } { \partial y _ { i } } + \frac { \partial \Pi _ { \nu } } { \partial \xi _ { i } } \frac { \partial \xi _ { i } } { \partial y _ { i } } = \frac { \partial \Pi _ { \nu } } { \partial y _ { i } } - \frac { x _ { 1 } } { m _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial \Pi _ { \nu } } { \partial \xi _ { i } } ~ ( i = 2 , 3 , . . . , 3 n ) ,
$$

$$
\frac { \partial P _ { v } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } = \frac { \partial \Pi _ { \nu } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } + \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { 3 n } \frac { \partial \Pi _ { \nu } } { \partial x _ { j } } \frac { \partial \xi _ { j } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } = \frac { \partial \Pi _ { \nu } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { y _ { 1 } } \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { 3 n } \frac { \partial \Pi _ { \nu } } { \partial \xi _ { j } } ( m _ { j } \xi _ { j } + x _ { 1 } y _ { j } ) .
$$

Hence, the second sum

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 3 n } { \frac { \partial P } { \partial y _ { i } } } { \frac { \partial U } { \partial x _ { i } } }
$$

on the left-hand side of (38) is of the form

$$
\sum _ { j , l } \big ( U _ { j l } x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + V _ { j l } x _ { 1 } + W _ { j l } \big ) r _ { 3 j , 3 l } ^ { - 3 } ,
$$

with

$$
r _ { 3 j , 3 l } ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } } ( u _ { j l } x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + v _ { j l } x _ { 1 } + m _ { j l } ) ,
$$

where $U , ~ V , ~ W$ are rational fractions in $y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , \xi _ { 2 } , . . . , \xi _ { 3 n } ,$ and $u , v , w$ are polynomials in the same variables.In particular,

$$
u _ { j l } = ( y _ { 3 j - 2 } - y _ { 3 l - 2 } ) ^ { 2 } + ( y _ { 3 j - 1 } - y _ { 3 l - 1 } ) ^ { 2 } + ( y _ { 3 j } - y _ { 3 l } ) ^ { 2 } .
$$

Since $\xi _ { i }$ are integrals of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ ，

$$
\frac { \partial \Pi _ { \nu - 2 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } \frac { y _ { 1 } } { m _ { 1 } } = \frac { \partial P _ { \nu - 2 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } \frac { y _ { 1 } } { m _ { 1 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \frac { \partial P _ { \nu - 2 } } { \partial x _ { 3 n } } \frac { y _ { 3 n } } { m _ { 3 n } } ,
$$

and (38) becomes

$$
{ \frac { \partial \Pi _ { \nu - 2 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } } = { \frac { 1 } { y _ { 1 } } } \sum _ { j , l } \left( U _ { j l } x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + V _ { j l } x _ { 1 } + W _ { j l } \right) \cdot r _ { 3 j , 3 l } ^ { - 3 } \equiv B ( x _ { 1 } ) .
$$

Let $\Pi _ { \nu - 2 } ^ { \prime } , \Pi _ { \nu - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ be the two branch functions of $\Pi _ { y - 2 }$ corresponding to the same values of $y _ { 1 } , \dotsc , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , \dotsc , x _ { 3 n } , s ;$ then $\Pi _ { \nu - 2 } ^ { \prime \prime } \ : - \ : \Pi _ { \nu - 2 } ^ { \prime }$ should be rational in $y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , \xi _ { 2 } , . . . , \xi _ { 3 n }$

We now prove that the Abelian integral

$$
I = \int B ( x _ { 1 } ) \ d x _ { 1 }
$$

formed by (39) cannot possess any period.

If this integral possesses a period $\omega ( y _ { 1 } , . ~ . ~ . , y _ { 3 n } , \xi _ { 2 } , . ~ . ~ . , \xi _ { 3 n } )$ ,then this period should be rational in $y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , \xi _ { 2 } , . . . , \xi _ { 3 n }$ If $y _ { i } , \ \xi _ { i }$ are given their numerical values $\bar { y } _ { i } , \ \bar { \xi } _ { i } ,$ then the branch points of the radicals

$$
{ r _ { 3 j , 3 l } = \frac { 1 } { y _ { 1 } } \sqrt { u _ { j l } x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + v _ { j l } x _ { 1 } + w _ { j l } } } ,
$$

are all different, unless $\bar { x } _ { i } , \bar { \xi } _ { i }$ are chosen in some particular manner. The periods of $\pmb { I }$ are the periods of various integrals

$$
I _ { j l } = y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \int \frac { U _ { j l } x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + V _ { j l } x _ { 1 } + W _ { j l } } { ( u _ { j l } x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + v _ { j l } x _ { 1 } + w _ { j l } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } d x _ { 1 } .
$$

Such an integral $I _ { j l }$ admits $2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } U _ { j l } / u _ { j l } ^ { 3 / 2 }$ as the unique period. Since $U _ { j l }$ and $\boldsymbol { u } _ { j l }$ are rational in $y _ { i } , \xi _ { j } ,$ and $\boldsymbol { u } _ { j l }$ is not a perfect square, this period can be rational with respect to $y _ { i } , \xi _ { j } ,$ when and only when $U _ { j l }$ is identically zero. Hence, the integral $\boldsymbol { \mathit { I } }$ cannot possess any period.

The integral $I ( x _ { 1 } , \xi _ { 2 } , . . . , \xi _ { 3 n } , y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } )$ is called a primitive function of $B ( x _ { 1 } )$ of (39). It is algebraic in $x _ { 1 }$ . It can always be so chosen that it is also algebraic in $\xi _ { 2 } , . . . , \xi _ { 3 n } , y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n }$ The difference

$$
\Pi _ { \nu - 2 } - I \equiv P _ { \nu - 2 } - J ( y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n } , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } )
$$

is an integral of $( \mathbf { S } _ { 0 } )$ .Since this difference is algebraic in $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n }$ it is necessarily algebraic in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$ ， according to the lemma of Section 6.9. Hence, $P _ { \nu - 2 }$ should be algebraic in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n }$

If $P _ { \nu - 2 }$ is not rational in $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } , s _ { ; }$ ， then it can be shown by an inference similar to Section 6.10 that $f$ is an algebraic combination of analogous integrals, where $P _ { \nu }$ and $P _ { \nu - 2 }$ are rational in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { 3 n } , s .$ Itis reasonable to suppose that $P _ { \nu - 2 }$ is rational in $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } , s ,$ and that $P _ { \nu - 2 }$ becomes infinite only for those values of $x _ { i }$ that make one of the quantities $\pmb { r _ { 3 j , 3 l } }$ vanish.

Furthermore, the system (S） remains unaltered when t, $x _ { i } , \ y _ { i }$ are changed to $\alpha ^ { 3 } t , \alpha ^ { 2 } x _ { i } , \alpha ^ { - 1 } y _ { i }$ ,respectively.By this transformation $f$ can be decomposed into a sum of integrals, each of which remains unaltered by this transformation. From this we may state the

Theorem : If the $\pmb { n }$ -body problem were to admit an integral that is algebraic with regard to the momentum,other than the classcal integrals, then there would exist at least one integral that is a polynomial in $y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { 3 n }$ ,of the form

$$
P = P _ { \nu } + P _ { \nu - 2 } + P _ { \nu - 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

where $P _ { j }$ is a homogeneous polynomial of degree $j$ in $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { 3 n }$ ，the coefficients of $P _ { y }$ are homogeneous polynomials all of the same degree $\lambda$ in $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n }$ ， and the coefficients of $P _ { \nu - 2 }$ are algebraic, homogeneous of degree $\lambda - 1$ in $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n }$ , and rational in $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n } , s .$

The conclusion that no such integral exists is a result of Bruns's theorem (Sections 6.1-6.6). Bruns used only the first two terms $P _ { v } , P _ { v - 2 }$ of the polynomial integral $P$ ， supposed to be algebraic in $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { 3 n }$ By expressing,on one hand, that $U _ { j l }$ are zero,and, on the other hand, that

$$
\frac { \partial \Pi _ { \nu - 2 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } = \sum _ { j , l } \frac { V _ { j l } x _ { 1 } + W _ { j l } } { ( u _ { j l } x _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + v _ { j l } x _ { 1 } + w _ { j l } ) ^ { 3 / 2 } } ,
$$

in the present terminology, Bruns proved that $P _ { y }$ should necessarily be a polynomial combination of the energy $T$ and the nine classical linear integrals.

Painleve's Theorem: Whatever the values of the masses may be, the differential equations of the three-body (or $\pmb { n }$ -body)problem do not admit, other than the classical integrals,any integral that is algebraic with regard to the momentum.

That the theorem is true also for an integral containing $t$ explicitly can be shown by the method used in the proof of Bruns's theorem.

# INTEGRALS OF THE RESTRICTED THREE-BODY PROBLEM

# 6.12POINCARE's RECURRENCE THEOREM

Suppose that two finite masses are moving in uniform circular motion around their center of mass,and consider the motion of a third body of infinitely small mass under the attraction of those two finite masses.It is assumed that the mass of the third body is so small that its motion does not affect the motion of the first two bodies. This is called the restricted problem of three bodies.We further assume that the third body moves in the plane of motion of the first two bodies.In this plane restricted problem of three bodies we take the $_ x$ -axis in the direction of the two finite masses and the $y .$ -axis in the same plane,perpendicular to the $\pmb { x }$ -axis.If we choose the units suitably in this rotating coordinate system, then the equations of motion of the third body are (refer to Sections 3.3-3.5)

$$
\frac { d ^ { 2 } x } { d t ^ { 2 } } - 2 \frac { d y } { d t } = \frac { \partial U } { \partial x } , \frac { d ^ { 2 } y } { d t ^ { 2 } } + 2 \frac { d x } { d t } = \frac { \partial U } { \partial y } ,
$$

$$
U = \mu _ { 1 } \Bigl ( \frac { 1 } { 2 } r ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { r } \Bigr ) + \mu \Bigl ( \frac { 1 } { 2 } r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } \Bigr ) ,
$$

$$
r ^ { 2 } = ( x - \mu ) ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } , \qquad r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = ( x + \mu _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } + y ^ { 2 } ,
$$

$$
\mu + \mu _ { 1 } = 1 , \qquad 0 < \mu < 1 .
$$

The known integral is

$$
\left( { \frac { d x } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } + \left( { \frac { d y } { d t } } \right) ^ { 2 } - 2 U = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ,
$$

which is called the Jacobi integxal. We now propose to prove that, if there exists an integral $\dot { f } ( x , y , d x / d t , d y / d t , t ) =$ constant， which is algebraic in the coordinates and the momenta and time,then it is an algebraic combination of (4l).This is a special case of Bruns's theorem but it is an independent theorem and we shall prove it de novo after Siegel (1936).

Substitute, in (40)

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { \dot { \boldsymbol { x } } = \boldsymbol { \xi } , \quad \quad \dot { \boldsymbol { y } } = \eta , \quad } & { \boldsymbol { \xi } = \boldsymbol { X } , \quad \quad \eta = \boldsymbol { Y } , } \\ { 2 \eta + \displaystyle \frac { \partial { U } } { \partial \boldsymbol { x } } = \Xi , \quad } & { - 2 \boldsymbol { \xi } + \displaystyle \frac { \partial { U } } { \partial \boldsymbol { y } } = \mathrm { H } ; } \end{array}
$$

then the equations take the form

$$
\frac { d x } { d t } = X , ~ \frac { d y } { d t } = Y , ~ \frac { d \xi } { d t } = \Xi , ~ \frac { d \eta } { d t } = \mathrm { H } .
$$

Neither $X , ~ Y , ~ \Xi $ nor $\mathbf { H }$ explicitly contains $x , y , \xi ,$ or $\eta$ ，respectively. Therefore,

$$
