\frac { \partial { X } } { \partial { x } } + \frac { \partial { Y } } { \partial { y } } + \frac { \partial { \Xi } } { \partial { \xi } } + \frac { \partial { \mathrm { H } } } { \partial \eta } = 0 .
$$

Hence, equations (40) admit an integral invariant $\iiint d x d y d \xi d \eta .$ as has been shown in Section 4.18. The Jacobi integral (41) can be written

$$
\xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } - 2 U = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } = C .
$$

Thus, in order that an integral, $f ( x , y , \xi , \eta ) =$ constant, of (40) may exist, the equation

$$
X \frac { \partial f } { \partial x } + Y \frac { \partial f } { \partial y } + \Xi \frac { \partial f } { \partial \xi } + \mathrm { H } \frac { \partial f } { \partial \eta } = 0
$$

should hold identically. We shall prove that,iffis algebraic in $x , y , \xi , \eta , t ,$ then $f$ is an algebraic function of $\xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } - 2 U .$

We can suppose that $f$ is rational in $\xi , \eta , x , y , r , r _ { 1 } , t ,$ as shown in the proof of Bruns’s theorem. Let $\pmb { c }$ be a function that does not contain $t$ explicitly, and $g ( t ) = t ^ { m } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , h ( t ) = t ^ { n } + \cdot \cdot \cdot$ be two relatively prime polynomials in $t _ { : }$ ，of which the coefficients are rational functions of $\xi , \eta , x , y , r , r _ { 1 }$ Write $f = c g ( t ) / h ( t )$ ; then

$$
{ \frac { \dot { c } } { c } } + { \frac { \dot { g } } { g } } + { \frac { \dot { h } } { h } } = 0
$$

should be satisfied identically by $\xi , \eta , x , y , t ;$ when $\dot { \xi } , \dot { \eta }$ are eliminated by (40a). Since $t$ is not contained explicitly in (40a),and since $\dot { c } / c$ is independent of $t$ ,and ${ \dot { g } } / g , { \dot { h } } / h$ are such that their denominators and numerators are relatively prime polynomials of $t$ ,equations (43) should hold term by term separately, that is,any one of $c , g , h$ should be an integral. Hence, it is sufficient to consider integrals in the form of polynomials in $t$

If we denote by $b _ { 0 } , b _ { 1 } , \ldots , b _ { m }$ rational polynomials of $\xi , \eta , x , y , r , r _ { 1 } ,$ then such integrals are of the form

$$
f = b _ { 0 } t ^ { m } + b _ { 1 } t ^ { m - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + b _ { m } ( m \geq 0 , b _ { 0 } \neq 0 ) .
$$

Choose a curve $C$ on the $_ { x y }$ plane (4la) such that no one of $b _ { 0 } , b _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } , \dotsc , b _ { m } ^ { - 1 }$ is zero at any point on $C .$ . Take a general point $x = x _ { 0 } , y = y _ { 0 }$ on this plane and choose $\xi _ { 0 } , \ \eta _ { 0 }$ so as to satisfy (4la). Suppose that none of $b _ { 0 } , b _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } , \dotsc , b _ { m } ^ { - 1 }$ is zero at $( x _ { 0 } , y _ { 0 } , \xi _ { 0 } , \eta _ { 0 } )$ .We shall now show that $m = 0$

The relation (42) holds in a four-dimensional space with rectangular coordinates $x , y , \xi , \eta ;$ ，and the integral of volume is invariant. In the rationality domain of $x , y , \xi , \eta ,$ let a state of motion defined by (40a) correspond uniquely to an orbit of the points in the finite portion of this four-dimensional space. Since the integral invariant of volume exists and the relation (42) is valid, the motion according to Poincaré is represented by the motion of molecules of incompressible fluid enclosed in a vessel of finite four-dimensional volume,as we have described in Section 4.18. Take a small volume $V _ { 0 }$ inside the vessel； consider the motion of a particular molecule in $V _ { 0 }$ ; let $_ { T }$ be a finite time interval. Suppose that the particular molecule that was in $V _ { 0 }$ at $t = 0$ occupies a volume $V _ { n }$ at $t = n T ,$ ，where $\pmb { n }$ is an integer. Since the fluid is incompressible,the volumes $V _ { n }$ $( n = 1 , 2 , 3 , . . . )$ are all equal to $V _ { 0 }$ .There should be a finite integer $m$ such that there is at least a common part between $V _ { 0 }$ and $V _ { m }$ .Hence,there should be a $V _ { n }$ such that it has parts in common with $V _ { 0 }$ an infinite number of times during the time interval from $t = 0$ to $t = + \infty$ . If we take $V _ { 0 }$ small enough, the orbit of the molecule comes as near as we please to the initial position in a sufficiently long interval of time. This theorem is called Poincare's recurrence theorem or the quasiergodic theorem and it will be discussed in detail in a later chapter (Hagihara, 1933).

According to this theorem there should be an upper bound to the time interval during which a moving point comes again to a point where it has once passed along its orbit. For, ifwe form (44) for those values $\operatorname { o f } x , y , \xi , \eta$ for which the moving point comes again very close to its initial point, and also for the corresponding value of $t _ { : }$ ,then the two resulting values of $f$ in (44) should be equal because (44) is an integral. But by our assumption, $b _ { 0 } , b _ { 1 } , \dotsc , b _ { m }$ have nearly the same values at these two epochs. Solving these two equations,we see the finite upper bound of the time interval of the two epochs.A similar argument holds true for $m < 0$ Hence, $m = 0$ .Thus we conclude that $f$ does not contain $t$ explicitly.

We have excluded in the above the cases where $x , y , \xi , \eta$ become infinite. $_ x$ and $y$ become infinite when the orbit goes to infinity.E and $\mathrm { H }$ become infinite in the finite region when $_ r$ or $\boldsymbol { r } _ { 1 }$ becomes zero, that is, in a collision.The condition for a colision is an integral of the problem; we have,however,excluded such cases.The condition for a collision is transcendental,as we shall show in a later chapter (Painlevé,1896,1897; Bisconcini, 1906;Levi-Civita,l903, l906).In order that the orbit may extend to infinity,the energy constant $C$ in (4la） must be infinite.We can exclude this case.There must also be a condition for a periodic orbit (as will be discussed in a later chapter).Since this condition is also an integral of the problem, we can exclude this case, too. Our question is whether an integral is algebraic with regard to $t _ { i }$ ，but an algebraic expression has no period.We also exclude orbits asymptotic to either of the straight-line equilibrium points, since special conditions are necessary for such motions.

# 6.13 LEMMA

We are looking for integrals that are rational functions o $\displaystyle  \{ x , y , \xi , \eta , r _ { 1 }$ Let $f = g / h$ ,where $g , h$ are relatively prime polynomials in $\xi , \eta$ Since the equation

$$
g { \frac { d h } { d t } } = h { \frac { d g } { d t } }
$$

is satisfied identically when the expressions for $d \xi / d t , \ d \eta \uparrow d t$ are substituted from (40a), the equation

$$
\frac { d g } { d t } = \xi \frac { \partial g } { \partial x } + \eta \frac { \partial g } { \partial y } + \Xi \frac { \partial g } { \partial \xi } + \mathrm {  ~ H ~ } \frac { \partial g } { \partial \eta }
$$

is a polynomial in $\xi , \eta$ and of degree one higher than $g$ with regard to $\xi , \eta$ The coefficients are rational in $x , y , r , r _ { 1 }$ Hence, $d g / d t$ belongs to the rational algebraic field $\Omega ( x , y , r , r _ { 1 } )$ .But dg/dt is divisible by $g$ ,according to (45). Hence, we have identically

$$
\frac { d g } { d t } = g ( u \xi + v \eta + w ) .
$$

Let $G$ be the highest degree term in $\xi , \eta ,$

$$
G \equiv a \xi ^ { k } \eta ^ { m - k } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + b \xi ^ { l } \eta ^ { m - l } ,
$$

where $a , \ldots , b$ are not identically zero. From (46) and (47) we obtain

$$
u = \frac { 1 } { a } \frac { \partial a } { \partial x } , \qquad v = \frac { 1 } { b } \frac { \partial b } { \partial y } ,
$$

$$
\xi \frac { \partial G } { \partial x } + \eta \frac { \partial G } { \partial y } = \Bigl ( \frac { 1 } { a } \frac { \partial a } { \partial x } \xi + \frac { 1 } { b } \frac { \partial b } { \partial y } \eta \Bigr ) G .
$$

If we denote polynomials in $x , y$ by $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , c _ { 3 } , c _ { 4 } ,$ ， then any quantity belonging to the algebraic field $\Omega ( x , y , r , r _ { 1 } )$ can be written in the form

$$
c _ { 1 } + c _ { 2 } r + c _ { 3 } r _ { 1 } + c _ { 4 } r r _ { 1 } .
$$

As $g , h$ are determined by $\Omega ( x , y , r , r _ { 1 } )$ , apart from a common factor, we can assume that $^ { a }$ has one of the forms $c _ { \bf 1 } , c _ { \bf 2 } r , c _ { \bf 3 } r _ { \bf 1 } , c _ { \bf 4 } r r _ { \bf 1 }$ ,that the coefficients $a , \ldots , b$ of $G$ are all polynomials,and that $\boldsymbol { r }$ and $r _ { 1 }$ have no common factor in the form of a polynomial in $x , y$ 、But any irreducible polynomial belonging to the field $\Omega ( x , y )$ ,which is included in $\pmb { a }$ ，should be included by (49) in all the coefficients in $G .$ 、A similar statement can be made for $\pmb { r }$ and $\pmb { r } _ { 1 }$ ; that is, the polynomial should be included in $\pmb { b }$ when it is included in a.Hence, $^ { a }$ should be a constant. As far as this argument is concerned, we can exchange $^ { a }$ and $b$ and we can conclude that $^ { b }$ is also a constant.

Because of (45), (47),and (48), $g$ must be a polynomial in $\xi , \eta$ with the coefficients belonging to the rational algebraic field $\Omega ( x , y , r , r _ { 1 } )$ and must satisfy the differential equation

$$
\frac { d g } { d t } = w g ,
$$

where w belongs to the rational algebraic field $\Omega ( x , y , r , r _ { 1 } )$

Next we shall show that $\pmb { w }$ in (50) is constant. Put

$$
g = g ^ { ( m ) } + g ^ { ( m - 1 ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + g ^ { ( 0 ) } ,
$$

where $g ^ { ( k ) } \left( k = 0 , 1 , . . . , m \right)$ , is a polynomial of degree $k$ in $\xi , \eta$ with the coefficients belonging to the feld $\Omega ( x , y , r , r _ { 1 } )$ . Suppose that $g ^ { ( m ) } \not \equiv 0$ From (46), (50),and (51) we obtain the following equations:

$$
\xi \frac { \partial g ^ { ( m ) } } { \partial x } + \eta \frac { \partial g ^ { ( m ) } } { \partial y } = 0 ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \xi \frac { \partial g ^ { ( m - 1 ) } } { \partial x } + \eta \frac { \partial g ^ { ( m - 1 ) } } { \partial y } + 2 \eta \frac { \partial g ^ { ( m ) } } { \partial \xi } - 2 \xi \frac { \partial g ^ { ( m ) } } { \partial \eta } = w g ^ { ( m ) } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
+ \frac { \partial U } { \partial x } \frac { \partial g ^ { ( k + 1 ) } } { \partial \xi } + \frac { \partial U } { \partial y } \frac { \partial g ^ { ( k + 1 ) } } { \partial \eta } = w g ^ { ( k ) } ~ ( k = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

$$
\frac { \partial U } { \partial x } \frac { \partial g ^ { ( 1 ) } } { \partial \xi } + \frac { \partial U } { \partial y } \frac { \partial g ^ { ( 1 ) } } { \partial \eta } = w g ^ { ( 0 ) } .
$$

We see from (52) that $g ^ { ( m ) }$ is a function of $\xi , \eta , x \eta - y \xi$ only. On the other hand, since $g ^ { ( m ) }$ is a polynomial in $\xi , \eta$ with the coefficients belonging to the field $\Omega ( x , y , r , r _ { 1 } )$ , the polynomial $g ^ { ( m ) }$ is a polynomial in $\xi$ ， $\eta , x \eta - y \xi$

Suppose that ${ \pmb w }$ is a function of $_ { x }$ and has a pole of order $h \not \equiv 1$ at $\begin{array} { r } { \pmb { x } = \pmb { x } _ { 1 } } \end{array}$ ，where $\pmb { x } _ { 1 }$ differs from $\mu \pm y { \sqrt { - 1 } }$ and $- \mu _ { 1 } \pm y \sqrt { - 1 }$ . Since $g ^ { ( m ) }$ is not factorizable, ${ \boldsymbol { w g } } ^ { ( m ) }$ has a pole of order $\pmb { h }$ at $\boldsymbol { x } = \boldsymbol { x } _ { 1 }$ .Expand $g ^ { ( m - 1 ) } = c ( { \pmb x } - { \pmb x } _ { 1 } ) ^ { s } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot$ in ascending powers of ${ \pmb x } - { \pmb x } _ { 1 }$ ; then

$$
\xi \frac { \partial g ^ { ( m - 1 ) } } { \partial x } + \eta \frac { \partial g ^ { ( m - 1 ) } } { \partial y } = c s \bigg ( \xi - \eta \frac { \partial x _ { 1 } } { \partial y } \bigg ) ( x - x _ { 1 } ) ^ { s - 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

As $s - 1 = - h$ ， $s \neq 0$ ，by (53)， therefore, $h > 1$ ， and $g ^ { ( m - 1 ) }$ has a pole of order $h - 1$ at ${ \boldsymbol { x } } = { \boldsymbol { x } } _ { 1 }$ . Assume that $g ^ { ( m - l ) }$ has a pole of order $l ( h - 1 )$ at $\boldsymbol { x } = \boldsymbol { x } _ { 1 }$ ；then by (54) $g ^ { ( m - l - 1 ) }$ has a pole of order $( l + 1 ) ( h - 1 )$ at $\boldsymbol { x } = \boldsymbol { x } _ { 1 }$ .But in that case the right-hand member of (55) has a pole of order $m ( h - 1 )$ and the left-hand member has a pole at most of order $( m - 1 ) ( h - 1 )$ . Hence, ${ \pmb w }$ as a function of $_ { x }$ can-not have poles anywhere but at $\infty$ ， $\mu \pm y { \sqrt { - 1 } } , - \mu _ { 1 } \pm y { \sqrt { - 1 } }$ ·

Next, assume that ${ \pmb w }$ has a pole of order $h$ at ${ \pmb x } = \infty$ and that $g ^ { ( m ) }$ is of degree $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ with regard to $_ { x }$ ； then, quite similarly we see that $g ^ { ( m - l ) }$ has a pole of order $l ( h + 1 ) + p$ at $x = \infty$ ,which contradicts (55).Further,if ${ \pmb w }$ becomes infinite as $r ^ { - h }$ at $x = \mu \pm y { \sqrt { - 1 } }$ ，then，as $\partial U / \partial x$ and $\partial U / \partial y$ become infinite as $r ^ { - 3 }$ for $h > 1$ , the polynomial $g ^ { ( m - l ) }$ becomes infinite as $r ^ { - l ( h - 2 ) }$ at ${ } x = \mu \pm y { \sqrt { - 1 } }$ and, hence, $h > 2$ Again, this contradicts (55)． Similarly，we see that ${ \pmb w }$ cannot become infinite faster than $r _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 }$ at $x = - \mu _ { 1 } \pm y { \sqrt { - 1 } }$

We can draw a similar conclusion by considering w as a function of $\pmb { y }$ Then ${ \pmb w }$ takes the form

$$
w = \frac { c _ { 1 } } { r r _ { 1 } } + \frac { c _ { 2 } } { r } + \frac { c _ { 3 } } { r _ { 1 } } + c _ { 4 } ,
$$

where $\pmb { \mathscr { c } } _ { 1 }$ is (at most) of the second degree in $x , y$ ; also, $c _ { 2 } , c _ { 3 }$ are (at most)

of the first degree; also, $\pmb { c _ { 4 } }$ is a constant. Since $g ^ { ( m - 1 ) }$ is a polynomial belonging to the field $\Omega ( x , y , r , r _ { 1 } )$ , it should'be of the form

$$
\begin{array} { r } { g ^ { ( m - 1 ) } = b _ { 1 } r r _ { 1 } + b _ { 2 } r + b _ { 3 } r _ { 1 } + b _ { 4 } , } \end{array}
$$

where $b _ { 1 } , b _ { 2 } , b _ { 3 } , b _ { 4 }$ are polynomials in $x , y$ Furthermore,

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { \xi \frac { c } { \partial x } \left( b _ { 1 } r r _ { 1 } \right) + \eta \frac { c } { \partial y } \left( b _ { 1 } r r _ { 1 } \right) = \frac { c _ { 1 } } { r r _ { 1 } } g ^ { ( m ) } ; } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \xi \frac { \partial } { \partial x } \left( b _ { 2 } r \right) + \eta \frac { \partial } { \partial y } \left( b _ { 2 } r \right) = \frac { c _ { 2 } } { r } g ^ { ( m ) } , } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \xi \frac { \partial } { \partial x } \left( b _ { 3 } r _ { 1 } \right) + \eta \frac { \partial } { \partial y } \left( b _ { 3 } r _ { 1 } \right) = \frac { c _ { 3 } } { r _ { 1 } } g ^ { ( m ) } . } \end{array}
$$

By introducing $\alpha = x \eta - y \xi , \beta = x \xi + y \eta$ in the place of $x , y ;$ we see that $g ^ { ( m ) }$ does not depend on $\beta$ From (56),we obtain

$$
\frac { \partial } { \partial \beta } \left( b _ { 1 } r r _ { 1 } \right) = \frac { c _ { 1 } } { r r _ { 1 } } \frac { 1 } { \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } } g ^ { ( m ) } .
$$

If we suppose tentatively that $b _ { 1 }$ is not identically zero, then $b _ { 1 } r r _ { 1 }$ becomes infinite at least as fast as $\beta ^ { 2 }$ .Hence $\partial ( b _ { 1 } r r _ { 1 } ) / \partial \beta$ becomes infinite at least as fast as $\beta .$ 、But the right-hand member of (59) does not become infinite, except for the particular cases we have excluded.Hence, $b _ { 1 } = 0$ ， $c _ { 1 } = 0$ Similarly, because of (57) and (58), $b _ { 2 } , b _ { 3 }$ are constants. Furthermore,

$$
b _ { 2 } ( \beta - \mu \xi ) = c _ { 2 } g ^ { ( m ) } , \qquad b _ { 3 } ( \beta + \mu _ { 1 } \xi ) = c _ { 3 } g ^ { ( m ) } .
$$

Since $g ^ { ( m ) }$ does not depend on $\beta$ ,we have $c _ { 2 } = 0$ ， $c _ { 3 } = 0$ .Thus, we have shown that ${ \pmb w }$ is a constant.

From Poincaré's recurrence theorem we can prove that the constant w is zero.Integrating (50),we obtain

$$
g = K e ^ { w t } ,
$$

in which $\kappa$ is constant along the trajectory. As a special case of Section 6.12 of Poincaré's recurrence theorem,there exists,among the motions with $g \neq 0$ in the neighborhood of the trajectory $x = x _ { 0 } , y = y _ { 0 } , \xi = \xi _ { 0 } ,$ $\eta = \eta _ { 0 }$ ，a motion that returns to the immediate neighborhood of the initial state of motion after the lapse of an arbitrarily long time. Suppose that $w \neq 0$ ; then the right-hand side of (6O) would tend to the limit zero or infinity as $\pmb { t }$ approaches infinity,and this contradicts the theorem. Hence, $w = 0$ ·

# 6.14PROOF OF SIEGEL's THEOREM

We now state Siegel’s theorem :An integral in the form of a polynomial in $\xi , \eta$ with the coefficients belonging to the algebraic field $\Omega ( x , y , r , r _ { 1 } )$ is a polynomial of only one variable $\xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } - 2 U .$

To prove this theorem,we divide an integral $g$ into parts homogeneous in $\xi , \eta$ and write

$$
g = g ^ { ( m ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + g ^ { ( 0 ) } .
$$

Putting $w = 0$ in (52), (53), (54),(55), we obtain

$$
\xi \frac { \partial } { \partial x } g ^ { ( m ) } + \eta \frac { \partial } { \partial y } g ^ { ( m ) } = 0 ,
$$

$$
\xi \frac { \partial } { \partial x } g ^ { ( m - 1 ) } + \eta \frac { \partial } { \partial y } g ^ { ( m - 1 ) } + ^ { . } 2 \eta \frac { \partial } { \partial \xi } g ^ { ( m ) } - 2 \xi \frac { \partial } { \partial \eta } g ^ { ( m ) } = 0 ,
$$

$$
+ \frac { \partial U } { \partial x } \frac { \partial } { \partial \xi } g ^ { ( k + 1 ) } + \frac { \partial U } { \partial y } \frac { \partial } { \partial \eta } g ^ { ( k + 1 ) } = 0 ( k = 1 , 2 , . . . , m - 1 ) .
$$

$$
\frac { \partial U } { \partial x } \frac { \partial } { \partial \xi } g ^ { ( 1 ) } + \frac { \partial U } { \partial y } \frac { \partial } { \partial \eta } g ^ { ( 1 ) } = 0 .
$$

If we introduce new variables, $\alpha = x \eta - y \xi , \beta = x \xi + y \eta ,$ instead of $x , y$ ,then we see by (6l), that $g ^ { ( m ) } = P ( \xi , \eta , \alpha )$ is a polynomial in the three variables $\xi , \eta , \alpha$ ,and that (62) takes the form

$$
( \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } ) \frac { \partial } { \partial \beta } g ^ { ( m - 1 ) } = 2 \bigg ( \xi \frac { \partial P } { \partial \eta } - \eta \frac { \partial P } { \partial \xi } + \beta \frac { \partial P } { \partial \alpha } \bigg ) ;
$$

hence,

$$
g ^ { ( m - 1 ) } = \frac { 2 \beta } { \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } } \bigg ( \xi \frac { \partial P } { \partial \eta } - \eta \frac { \partial P } { \partial \xi } \bigg ) + \frac { \beta ^ { 2 } } { \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } } \frac { \partial P } { \partial \alpha } + g _ { 1 } ( \xi , \eta , \alpha ) ,
$$

where $g _ { 1 }$ is independent of $\beta$ .Thus $g ^ { ( m - 1 ) }$ is quadratic in $\beta$ ,considered as a function of $\xi , \eta , \alpha , \beta$

From (63),

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { ( \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } ) g ^ { ( k - 1 ) } = \displaystyle \int \left[ 2 \Big ( \xi \frac { \partial g ^ { ( k ) } } { \partial \eta } - \eta \frac { \partial g ^ { ( k ) } } { \partial \xi } + \beta \frac { \partial g ^ { ( k ) } } { \partial \alpha } - \alpha \frac { \partial g ^ { ( k ) } } { \partial \beta } \Big ) \right. } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad \left. - \frac { \partial U } { \partial x } \left( \frac { \partial g ^ { ( k + 1 ) } } { \partial \xi } + x \frac { \partial g ^ { ( k + 1 ) } } { \partial \beta } - y \frac { \partial g ^ { ( k + 1 ) } } { \partial \alpha } \right) \right. } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad \left. - \frac { \partial U } { \partial y } \left( \frac { \partial g ^ { ( k + 1 ) } } { \partial \eta } + x \frac { \partial g ^ { ( k + 1 ) } } { \partial \alpha } + y \frac { \partial g ^ { ( k + 1 ) } } { \partial \beta } \right) \right] \ : d \beta } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad \left( k = 1 , 2 , . . . , m - 1 \right) . } } \end{array}
$$

If we define $g ^ { ( - 1 ) } = 0$ ,then,by (64), this formula is true also for $k = 0$ If we define $g ^ { ( - 2 ) } = 0 .$ ,then this formula does not give anything new for $k = - 1$ ,but it is true.It is sufficient to discuss the formula (66） for $k = m - 1$ and $k = m - 2$ Now, referring to

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { ( \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } ) r ^ { 2 } = ( \alpha - \mu \eta ) ^ { 2 } + ( \beta - \mu \xi ) ^ { 2 } , \nonumber } } \\ { { ( \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } ) r _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } = ( \alpha + \mu _ { 1 } \eta ) ^ { 2 } + ( \beta + \mu _ { 1 } \xi ) ^ { 2 } , } } \end{array}
$$

we integrate (66) by parts for $k = m - 1$ ; then we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle g ^ { ( m - 2 ) } = \frac { 1 } { \alpha - \mu \eta } \left[ y \frac { \partial P } { \partial \xi } - ( x - \mu ) \left( \frac { \partial P } { \partial \eta } + \mu \frac { \partial P } { \partial \alpha } \right) \right] \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { r } } } \\ { { + \frac { 1 } { \alpha + \mu _ { 1 } \eta } \left[ y \frac { \partial P } { \partial \xi } - ( x + \mu _ { 1 } ) \left( \frac { \partial P } { \partial \eta } + \mu _ { 1 } \frac { \partial P } { \partial \alpha } \right) \right] \frac { \mu } { r _ { 1 } } + g _ { 2 } , } } \end{array}
$$

where $g _ { 2 }$ is a polynomial biquadratic function of $\xi , \eta , \alpha , \beta . \operatorname { F o r } k = m - 2$ ， using (67) and (65),we obtain after some calculation

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { ( \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } ) g ^ { ( m - 3 ) } = \left( \eta \frac { \partial P } { \partial \xi } - \xi \frac { \partial P } { \partial \eta } - \mu \xi \frac { \partial P } { \partial \alpha } \right) \displaystyle \int \frac { \mu _ { 1 } } { r } d \beta } \\ & { \qquad + \left( \eta \frac { \partial P } { \partial \xi } - \xi \frac { \partial P } { \partial \eta } + \mu _ { 1 } \xi \frac { \partial P } { \partial \alpha } \right) \displaystyle \int \frac { \mu } { r _ { 1 } } d \beta + g _ { 3 } , } \end{array}
$$

where $g _ { 3 }$ belongs to the algebraic field $\Omega ( \xi , \eta , x , y , r , r _ { 1 } )$

In addition, put

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { \mu _ { 1 } \bigg ( \eta \frac { \partial P } { \partial \xi } - \xi \frac { \partial P } { \partial \eta } - \mu \xi \frac { \partial P } { \partial \alpha } \bigg ) = A , } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \mu \bigg ( \eta \frac { \partial P } { \partial \xi } - \xi \frac { \partial P } { \partial \eta } + \mu _ { 1 } \xi \frac { \partial P } { \partial \alpha } \bigg ) = B ; } \end{array}
$$

then, because of (68),

$$
A \int \frac { d \beta } { r } + B \int \frac { d \beta } { r _ { 1 } }
$$

belongs to the algebraic field $\Omega ( \xi , \eta , x , y , r , r _ { 1 } )$ . When we make a single circuit around the point

$$
\beta _ { 1 } = \mu \xi + \sqrt { - 1 } ( \alpha - \mu \eta ) \right. \left. - \mu _ { 1 } \xi \pm \sqrt { - 1 } ( \alpha + \mu _ { 1 } \eta )
$$

in the $\beta$ plane, then $\pmb { r }$ changes its sign and

$$
- A \int \frac { d \beta } { r } + B \int \frac { d \beta } { r _ { 1 } }
$$

also belongs to the algebraic field $\Omega ( \xi , \eta , x , y , r , r _ { 1 } )$ . When we make a single circuit around the point at infinity, then the two integrals increase by the amount $2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 }$ but the expressions (7O) and (71） remain unaltered. Hence $A + B = 0$ ， $- A + B = 0$ ; that is,

$$
A = 0 , \qquad B = 0 .
$$

We obtain from (69) and (72) the two differential equations

$$
\eta \frac { \partial P } { \partial \xi } - \xi \frac { \partial P } { \partial \eta } = 0 , \frac { \partial P } { \partial \alpha } = 0 .
$$

The second equation shows that the polynomial $P ( \xi , \eta , \alpha )$ depends only on $\xi , \eta$ ; the first equation shows that it depends only on $\xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 }$ .Hence, $P ( \xi , \eta , \alpha )$ as a homogeneous polynomial in $\xi , \eta$ should be of the form

$$
P = \kappa ( \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } ) ^ { k } ,
$$

with a constant $\pmb { \kappa }$ . Since $\kappa ( \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } - 2 U ) ^ { k }$ is an integral,

$$
g - \kappa ( \xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } - 2 U ) ^ { k }
$$

is an integral in the form of a polynomial in $\xi , \eta$ of lower degree than $g$ and with coeffcients belonging to the finite algebraic field $\Omega ( x , y , r , r _ { 1 } )$

By mathematical induction Siegel (l936) has proved that $\boldsymbol { g }$ isa polynomial of only one variable $\xi ^ { 2 } + \eta ^ { 2 } - 2 U ,$ and then proved the theorem in question.

# THEOREM OF POINCARE

# 6.15CASE OF NONVANISHING HESSIAN

We consider the canonical equations of the $\pmb { n }$ -body problem given in Sections 5.16-5.20:

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial y _ { i } } , \frac { d y _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { i } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

$$
F = F _ { 0 } + \mu F _ { 1 } + \mu ^ { 2 } F _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

where $F _ { 0 }$ does not contain ${ \pmb y } _ { i }$ but only $\pmb { x _ { i } }$ and $F _ { 1 } , F _ { 2 } , \ldots$ are functions of $y _ { i } , x _ { i }$ and periodic,with period $2 \pi$ with regard to each ${ \pmb y } _ { i }$ .The canonical variables ${ x _ { i } } , { y _ { i } }$ are the elements of the osculating elliptic motion discussed in Sections 5.16-5.17. Poincaré proved that the canonical equations (73) cannot have any uniform integral, that is,any integral which is singlevalued and continuous with respect to $x _ { i } , y _ { i }$ ，other than ${ \pmb F } =$ constant. This is called the theorem of Poincaré (1889,1892).

We assume at first that the Hessian of $F _ { 0 }$ with respect to $x _ { i }$ is not zero.

Suppose that $\Phi$ is uniform, that is, single-valued and continuous, with respect to $x _ { i } , \ y _ { i }$ and $\mu$ ,and periodic,with period $2 \pi$ in each $y _ { i }$ . The function $\Phi$ need not be uniform for all values of $x _ { i } , y _ { i }$ ,and $\pmb { \mu }$ . It should be uniform for all values of $y _ { i }$ ,but only for the values of $\mu$ sufficiently small and only for the values of $x _ { i }$ in a domain (D) which can be taken as small as we please.Under this condition the function $\Phi$ can be expanded in the form

$$
\Phi = \Phi _ { 0 } + \mu \Phi _ { 1 } + \mu ^ { 2 } \Phi _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

where $\Phi _ { \diamond } , \Phi _ { \imath } , . . . ,$ are uniform functions of $x _ { i } , y _ { i }$ and periodic in $y _ { i }$ We shall prove according to Poincaré that such a function $\Phi$ cannot exist.

The necessary and sufficient condition for $\Phi$ to be an integral is, from our result of Chapter 4, that the Poisson bracket be equal to zero, that is,

$$
( F , \Phi ) = 0 ,
$$

or

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { ( F _ { 0 } , \Phi _ { 0 } ) + \mu \{ ( F _ { 1 } , \Phi _ { 0 } ) + ( F _ { 0 } , \Phi _ { 1 } ) \} } \\ & { \phantom { ( F _ { 0 } , \Phi _ { 0 } ) + \mu \{ ( F _ { 1 } , \Phi _ { 0 } ) + ( F _ { 0 } , \Phi _ { 0 } ) + ( F _ { 1 } , \Phi _ { 1 } ) + ( F _ { 0 } , \Phi _ { 2 } ) \} } + \cdot \cdot } \end{array}
$$

or

$$
( F _ { 0 } , \Phi _ { 0 } ) = 0 ,
$$

$$
( F _ { 1 } , \Phi _ { 0 } ) + ( F _ { 0 } , \Phi _ { 1 } ) = 0 , \begin{array} { r l r } { { \displaystyle } } & { { } } & { { \displaystyle } } \\ { { \displaystyle } } & { { } } & { { \vdots } } \end{array}
$$

First we shall prove a lemma: that we can suppose that $\Phi _ { 0 }$ is not a function of $F _ { 0 }$

Assume tentatively that $\Phi _ { 0 } = \psi ( F _ { 0 } )$ . So far as the value of $x _ { i }$ is in the domain $( \mathbf { D } )$ ， $\psi$ is,in general, uniform. Indeed,we solve

$$
F _ { 0 } = F _ { 0 } ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } )
$$

in the form

$$
x _ { 1 } = \theta ( F _ { 0 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } ) ;
$$

then $\theta$ is uniform if $\partial F _ { 0 } / \partial x _ { 1 } \neq 0$ in $( \mathbf { D } )$ . Replacing $x _ { 1 }$ by this expression, we see that

$$
\Phi _ { 0 } ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } , y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { n } ) = \psi ( F _ { 0 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } , y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { n } ) ;
$$

hence $\psi$ is a uniform function of $F _ { 0 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { n } , y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { n }$ . Since we have assumed that $\psi$ depends only on $F _ { 0 } { \mathrm { : } }$ ，the function $\Phi _ { 0 } = \psi$ is uniform as a


<!-- chunk 0010: pages 631-700 -->
function $\mathsf { o f } F _ { 0 }$ .If $\partial F _ { 0 } / \partial x _ { 1 } = 0$ in $( \mathbf { D } )$ , then we have only to take one of the $\partial F _ { 0 } / \partial x _ { i }$ which is not zero in $( \mathbf { D } )$ and proceed similarly.

Now, if $\Phi$ is uniform, then $\Phi - \psi ( F )$ is also uniform,and $\Phi - \psi ( F )$ can be expanded in integral powers of $\pmb { \mu }$ and is divisible by $\pmb { \mu }$ ， since $\Phi _ { 0 } - \psi ( F _ { 0 } ) = 0$ 、The function $\Phi ^ { \prime }$ defined by $\Phi - \psi ( F ) = \mu \Phi ^ { \prime }$ is uniform and may be expanded in the form

$$
\Phi ^ { \prime } = \Phi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } + \mu \Phi _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + \mu ^ { 2 } \Phi _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

In general, $\Phi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$ is not a function of $F _ { 0 }$ .If $\Phi _ { 0 } ^ { \prime }$ is a function of $F _ { 0 }$ ,then we repeat the same operation and finally we arrive at an integral that is not a function of $F _ { 0 }$ for $\mu = 0$ .In fact,as we have assumed that $\Phi$ is not a function of $F _ { : }$ ，there should be at least one of the Jacobians of $\Phi$ and $\pmb { F }$ with respect to two of the variables $x _ { i } , y _ { i } ,$ that does not vanish.We call it $J .$ We have assumed that $\Phi _ { 0 }$ is a function of $F _ { 0 }$ .Hence, $J$ has a factor $\mu$ Let the factor be $\mu ^ { \mathcal { P } }$ ; then $J ^ { \prime }$ defined by $J = \mu J ^ { \prime }$ has a factor $\mu ^ { p - 1 }$ . By repeating this process $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ times we arrive at a Jacobian which has no factor $\pmb { \mu }$ and which corresponds to an integral not reducing to a function of $F _ { 0 }$ for $\mu = 0$ ，Hence,if there exists a uniform integral $\Phi$ ，which is independent of $\boldsymbol { \mathsf { F } }$ but such that $\Phi _ { 0 }$ is a function $\mathsf { o f } F _ { 0 }$ ,then we can find another integral of the same form which does not reduce to a function of $F _ { 0 }$ for $\mu = 0$ .Hence we can assume that $\Phi _ { 0 }$ is not a function of $F _ { 0 }$

Next we shall prove a second lemma: that $\Phi _ { 0 }$ contains none of the $y _ { i }$

Assume tentatively that $\Phi _ { 0 }$ contains $y _ { i }$ .As it is periodic in ${ \pmb y } _ { i }$ ,it should be of the form

$$
\Phi _ { 0 } = \sum A \exp \left[ \sqrt { - 1 } ( m _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + m _ { n } y _ { n } ) \right] \equiv \sum A \zeta ,
$$

where $m _ { i }$ is a positive or negative integer and $\pmb { A }$ a function of $\pmb { x _ { i } }$ As $F _ { 0 }$ does not depend on $y _ { i }$ and as $\partial F _ { 0 } / \partial y _ { i } = 0$ ，we obtain

$$
( F _ { 0 } , \Phi _ { 0 } ) = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial y _ { i } } .
$$

On the other hand,

$$
\frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial y _ { i } } = \sum \sqrt { - 1 } ~ m _ { i } A \zeta .
$$

Hence,(75) is written

$$
\sqrt { - 1 } \sum A \bigg ( m _ { 1 } \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + m _ { n } \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { n } } \bigg ) \zeta = 0 .
$$

Since this is an identity, we have

$$
A \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } { \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } } = 0 .
$$

Hence either

$$
A = 0 \qquad { \mathrm { o r } } \qquad \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } { \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } } = 0 .
$$

From the latter relation we obtain

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } { \frac { \partial ^ { 2 } F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } \partial x _ { k } } } = 0 \qquad ( k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

and this should occur either when $m _ { 1 } = m _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = m _ { n } = 0$ or when the Hessian of $F _ { 0 }$ is zero. As we have assumed that the Hessian is not zero, $A = 0$ except the terms for which $m _ { 1 } = m _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = m _ { n } = 0$ Hence, $\Phi _ { 0 }$ should reduce to only one term that does not depend on $\pmb { y }$ Hence, $\Phi _ { 0 }$ does not depend on $y _ { i }$

After these preparations we come to the proof of the theorem. Since $F _ { 0 }$ and $\Phi _ { 0 }$ do not depend on $\pmb { y }$ ,we obtain from (76)

$$
- \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } \frac { \partial F _ { 1 } } { \partial y _ { i } } + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 1 } } { \partial y _ { i } } = 0 .
$$

By our assumption, $F _ { 1 }$ and $\Phi _ { 1 }$ are of the form $\begin{array} { r } { F _ { 1 } = \sum B \zeta } \end{array}$ and $\Phi _ { 1 } = \Sigma C \zeta ,$ where $\boldsymbol { B }$ and $c$ are functions of $x _ { i }$ alone and

$$
\zeta = \exp { [ \sqrt { - 1 } ( m _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + m _ { n } y _ { n } ) ] } .
$$

Substitute these in (76); then,

$$
- \sum B \zeta \Bigl ( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } \Bigr ) + \sum C \zeta \Bigl ( \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } \Bigr ) = 0 .
$$

Since this is an identity, we have for any system ofintegers $m _ { i }$ the relation

$$
B \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } { \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } } = C \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } { \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } } ;
$$

indeed, the relation holds for all values of $x _ { i }$ .Now, if we choose $\pmb { x _ { i } }$ so as to satisfy

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } = 0 ,
$$

then the right-hand member of (77) vanishes. Hence, if $x _ { i }$ satisfies (78), we have either $\mathcal { B } = 0$ or

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } = 0 .
$$

As $F _ { 1 }$ , accordingly $\boldsymbol { B }$ ,is given by our problem we can find out, from (78)，whether $\mathcal { B } = 0$ is satisfied. In general, such a relation is not satisfied.Hence,(79) must hold.

Let ${ \pmb { \beta } } _ { 1 } , { \pmb { \beta } } _ { 2 } , \ldots { \pmb { \beta } } _ { n }$ be integers; choose $_ { x }$ such that

$$
\frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \beta _ { 1 } \partial x _ { 1 } } = \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \beta _ { 2 } \partial x _ { 2 } } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \beta _ { n } \partial x _ { n } } \cdot
$$

Then there should be an infinite number of sets ofintegers $m _ { 1 } , m _ { 2 } , \ldots , m _ { n }$ such that

$$
m _ { 1 } \phi _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } \phi _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + m _ { n } \phi _ { n } = 0 .
$$

The relation (78) must be satisfied for an infinite number of such sets of integers. Hence,

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } = 0 .
$$

This relation, together with (78), gives

$$
\frac { \partial F _ { 0 } / \partial x _ { 1 } } { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } / \partial x _ { 1 } } = \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } / \partial x _ { 2 } } { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } / \partial x _ { 2 } } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } / \partial x _ { n } } { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } / \partial x _ { n } } ;
$$

that is,the Jacobians of $F _ { 0 }$ and $\Phi _ { 0 }$ with respect to any two of the variables $\pmb { x _ { i } }$ are all zero. This is the case when the values of $_ x$ satisfy (80), that is, $\partial F _ { 0 } / \partial x _ { i }$ are all commensurable.As there is an infinite number of such values of $x _ { i }$ in any arbitrarily small domain,and as the Jacobian is continuous, it identically equals zero, that is, $\Phi _ { 0 }$ would be a function of $F _ { 0 }$ .This contradicts our assumption.Hence,(73） has no uniform integral other than ${ \pmb F } =$ constant.

In the proof we assumed that $B \neq 0$ .We must discuss the case when one or many or an infinite number of $B$ are zero.

Corresponding to any set of integers $m _ { 1 } , m _ { 2 } , \ldots , m _ { n }$ there is a coefficient $B$ A value of $B$ that corresponds to the value of $_ x$ satisfying (78) is called secular. When two sets of integers $m _ { 1 } , m _ { 2 } , \ldots , m _ { n }$ and $m _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , m _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , m _ { n } ^ { \prime }$ are related in the form

$$
\frac { m _ { 1 } } { m _ { 1 } ^ { ' } } = \frac { m _ { 2 } } { m _ { 2 } ^ { ' } } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = \frac { m _ { n } } { m _ { n } ^ { ' } } ,
$$

we say that the corresponding two coefficients $\boldsymbol { B }$ belong to the same class. In the above proof we have assumed that none of the coefficients $B$ is zero even when it is secular.For the validity of the theorem it is sufficient to know that there should be,in each class,at least one $B$ that does not vanish even when it becomes secular.

Suppose that the value of $B$ corresponding to the set $m _ { 1 } , m _ { 2 } , \ldots , m _ { n }$ is zero but that the value $B ^ { \prime }$ of $B$ corresponding to $m _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , m _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , m _ { n } ^ { \prime }$ is not zero. Choose the value of $x _ { i }$ such that

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } = 0 ;
$$

then

$$
\sum _ { i \mathop { = } 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } ^ { \prime } \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } = 0 ,
$$

and hence

$$
B \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } = 0 , \qquad B ^ { \prime } \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } ^ { \prime } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } = 0 ,
$$

simultaneously. We do not obtain

$$
\sum _ { i \mathop { = } 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } = 0
$$

from the first equation, but we do obtain

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } ^ { \prime } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } = 0
$$

from the second equation. This leads to

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i } { \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } } = 0
$$

and the proof is carried out similarly to the foregoing one.

The coefficient $\pmb { B }$ that becomes zero when it becomes secular is called singular; the coefficient $B$ that does not is called ordinary. Let $\pmb { \beta } _ { 1 } , \pmb { \beta } _ { 2 } . . . . . , \pmb { \beta } _ { n }$ be given integers.The coefficient $B$ corresponding to the values of $m _ { 1 } , m _ { 2 } , \ldots , m _ { n }$ that satisfy (8l) is said to belong to the same family.Take $q$ sets of integers $m _ { 1 i } , m _ { 2 i } , . . . , m _ { n i }$ $( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , q )$ . If there is no set of $q$ integers $a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \dotsc , a _ { q }$ that are not zero all at once, satisfying

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { q } a _ { i } m _ { k i } = 0 \qquad ( k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

then we say that the class of these $q$ integers is independent.When there are $n - 1$ independent ordinary classes, the family is said to be ordinary; otherwise,it is said to be singular.When there are $n - 2$ ordinary classes, the singularity is said to be of order l;when there are $n - q \mathrm { ~ - ~ } 1$ ordinary classes, the singularity is of order $q$ . If there are any domains of $_ { x }$ that satisfy (80),then the family defined by the integers ${ \pmb { \beta } } _ { 1 } , { \pmb { \beta } } _ { 2 } , \ldots , { \pmb { \beta } } _ { n }$ is said to belong to the domain (D). We can prove that there does not exist any uniform integral other than $F$ in any domain (δ) contained inside (D).

For the values of $_ { x }$ corresponding to the ordinary families the proof is quite similar. The Jacobians of $F _ { 0 }$ and $\Phi _ { 0 }$ with respect to two of the variables $x _ { i }$ become zero an infinite number of times in al domains (δ) contained in $( \mathbf { D } )$ . Hence, they are identically zero.

We can show that, if there are an infinite number of singular classes of order $q$ in any region (8） contained in $( \mathbf { D } )$ ， then there are at most $q + 1$ independent uniform integrals, including ${ \pmb F } _ { \pmb { \bot } }$

In fact, assume that there were $q + 2$ such integrals. Let them be

$$
F , \Phi ^ { ( 1 ) } , \Phi ^ { ( 2 ) } , . . . , \Phi ^ { ( q + 1 ) }
$$

and suppose that they become

$$
F _ { 0 } , \Phi _ { 0 } ^ { ( 1 ) } , \Phi _ { 0 } ^ { ( 2 ) } , . . . , \Phi _ { 0 } ^ { ( a + 1 ) }
$$

for $\mu = 0$ . Choose the set of values of $_ { x }$ corresponding to the singular family of order $q$ . There are $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } = \pmb { \mathscr { n } } - \pmb { \mathscr { q } } - 1$ ordinary classes belonging to this family. Let the corresponding set of integers be

$$
m _ { 1 k } , m _ { 2 k } , . . . , m _ { n k } \qquad ( k = 1 , 2 , . . . , p ) .
$$

Then, for these values of $x _ { i }$ ，

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i k } { \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i k } { \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } ^ { ( h ) } } { \partial x _ { i } } } = 0
$$

$$
( k = 1 , 2 , . . . , p ; h = 1 , 2 , . . . , q + 1 ) .
$$

The Jacobians of $q + 2$ functions (82) with respect to any set of $q + 2$ variables $x _ { i }$ are zero for the values of $x _ { i }$ in question; indeed, this occurs an infinite number of times in any domain (δ).Hence, these Jacobians would be zero, and the $q + 2$ integrals in question cannot be independent.

This consideration does not interest us, according to Poincaré,as such a case does not appear in any practical dynamical system.We shall nevertheless continue the discussion of such a case from time to time in later chapters.

# 6.16CASE OF VANISHING HESSIAN

Consider the case when the Hessian of $F _ { 0 }$ with respect to $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ is zero. The function $F _ { 0 }$ depends only on ${ \boldsymbol { x } } _ { 1 } , { \boldsymbol { x } } _ { 2 }$ and is independent of the remaining $x _ { 3 } , x _ { 4 } , \dotsc , x _ { n }$ In order to distinguish ${ x } _ { 1 } , \ { x } _ { 2 }$ from other $x _ { i }$ ， denote the variables $x _ { i }$ that are not contained in $F _ { 0 }$ by $z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 } , . . . , z _ { n - 2 } ,$ and their canonically conjugate variables by $u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , . . . , u _ { n - 2 }$

By the first lemma of Section 6.15 we can suppose that,if there exists a uniform integral $\Phi$ independent of ${ \pmb F }$ then $\Phi _ { 0 }$ is not a function of $F _ { 0 }$ . In the relation

$$
( F _ { 0 } , \Phi _ { 0 } ) = \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial y _ { 1 } } + \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 2 } } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial y _ { 2 } } = 0
$$

we put $\zeta = \exp \left[ { \sqrt { - 1 } } ( m _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + m _ { n } y _ { n } ) \right]$ and write $\Phi _ { 0 } = \textstyle \sum A \zeta _ { : }$ 、 where $\pmb { A }$ is a function of $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , z , u .$ Then,from this relation, we have an identity

$$
\sqrt { - 1 } \sum A \zeta \Bigl [ m _ { 1 } { \frac { \partial { \cal F } _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } } + m _ { 2 } { \frac { \partial { \cal F } _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 2 } } } \Bigr ] \equiv 0 .
$$

Hence

$$
m _ { 1 } \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } + m _ { 2 } \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 2 } } = 0
$$

cannot hold identically,unless both $m _ { 1 }$ and $m _ { 2 }$ are zero.Hence, we can show in a manner similar to that of Section 6.15 that $\Phi _ { 0 }$ depends neither on $y _ { 1 }$ nor on $y _ { 2 }$ .Put

$$
F _ { 1 } = \sum { B \zeta } , \qquad \Phi _ { 1 } = \sum { C \zeta } ,
$$

or

$$
F _ { 1 } = \sum B _ { m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } } \exp { [ \sqrt { - 1 } ( m _ { 1 } y _ { 1 } + m _ { 2 } y _ { 2 } ) ] }
$$

in (76)； then

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \phantom { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \cdot \sum { B \zeta \Bigl ( \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 2 } m _ { i } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } \Bigr ) } + \sum C \zeta \Bigl ( \displaystyle \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { 2 } m _ { i } \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { i } } \Bigr ) } \\ & { \phantom { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } + \sum \zeta \displaystyle \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n - 2 } \Big ( \frac { \partial B } { \partial z _ { j } } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial u _ { j } } - \frac { \partial B } { \partial u _ { j } } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial z _ { j } } \Big ) = } \end{array}
$$

should be an identity. Thus we can equate the coeffcient of any exponential $\boldsymbol { \zeta }$ to zero. Choose ${ \pmb x } _ { 1 } , { \pmb x } _ { 2 }$ such that

$$
m _ { 1 } \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } + m _ { 2 } \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 2 } } = 0 ;
$$

then

$$
- B \bigg ( m _ { 1 } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } + m _ { 2 } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 2 } } \bigg ) + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n - 2 } \bigg ( \frac { \partial B } { \partial z _ { j } } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial u _ { j } } - \frac { \partial B } { \partial u _ { j } } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial z _ { j } } \bigg ) = 0 .
$$

The coefficients $B _ { m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } } , B _ { m _ { 2 } ^ { ' } m _ { 1 } ^ { ' } }$ , such that $m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } - m _ { 2 } m _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } = 0$ , belong to the same class, and $B _ { m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } }$ belongs to the class $m _ { 1 } / m _ { 2 } . \ B _ { 0 0 }$ belongs to all classes.

Let ${ p , q }$ be integers such that $m _ { 1 } { : } m _ { 2 } = p { : } q$ ; put

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \zeta = \exp { [ \sqrt { - 1 } ( \phi y _ { 1 } + q y _ { 2 } ) ] } , D _ { \lambda } = B _ { \lambda p , \lambda q } \zeta ^ { \lambda } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { - \zeta \mathrm { H } = \displaystyle { \rlap / p \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } + q \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 2 } } } . } } \end{array}
$$

Choose the value of $x _ { i }$ such that

$$
\phi \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } + q \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 2 } } = 0 ;
$$

then for any positive or negative integer $\lambda$ , including 0,

$$
\mathrm { H } \frac { \partial D _ { \lambda } } { \partial \zeta } + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n - 2 } \left( \frac { \partial D _ { \lambda } } { \partial z _ { j } } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial u _ { j } } - \frac { \partial D _ { \lambda } } { \partial u _ { j } } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial z _ { j } } \right) = 0 .
$$

This relation holds in the following two cases. The first case occurs when

$$
\mathrm { H } = 0 , \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial z _ { j } } = 0 , \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial u _ { j } } = 0 ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , n - 2 ) .
$$

In this case,

$$
\frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 2 } } - \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 2 } } \frac { \partial \Phi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } = 0 ,
$$

which contradicts our assumption. The second case occurs when the Jacobian of arbitrary $2 n - 3$ of the expressions $D _ { \lambda }$ with regard to $2 n - 3$ variables $\zeta , z _ { j } , u _ { j }$ is zero. Thus only $2 n - 4$ among the $2 n - 3$ expressions $D _ { \lambda }$ are independent; in other words, if we choose the constant values of ${ \boldsymbol { x } } _ { 1 } , { \boldsymbol { x } } _ { 2 }$ so that they satisfy (85), then

$$
B _ { \lambda p , \lambda q } ^ { \lambda ^ { \prime } } B _ { \lambda ^ { \prime } p , \lambda ^ { \prime } q } ^ { - \lambda }
$$

depends only on $2 n - 4$ variables, that is, on $z _ { j }$ and $u _ { j }$ . If there were a uniform integral, then $2 n - 5$ among these expressions (87） should be independent; that is, there should be a relation among these $2 n - 4$ expressions (87).

Then what is the condition for the coexistence of the three independent uniform integrals $\boldsymbol { F } =$ constant, $\Phi =$ constant, and $\Psi =$ constant? Denote their respective values for $\mu = 0$ by $F _ { 0 } , \Phi _ { 0 } ;$ and $\Psi _ { 0 }$ As in the lemma in Section 6.15, we can consider that there is no relation among $F _ { 0 } , \Phi _ { 0 } $ and $\Psi _ { 0 }$ . Hence putting

$$
- \mathrm { H } ^ { \prime } \zeta = \beta \frac { \partial \Psi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } + q \frac { \partial \Psi _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 2 } } ,
$$

we obtain

$$
H ^ { \prime } \frac { \partial D _ { \lambda } } { \partial \zeta } + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { n - 2 } \left( \frac { \partial D _ { \lambda } } { \partial z _ { j } } \frac { \partial \Psi _ { 0 } } { \partial u _ { j } } - \frac { \partial D _ { \lambda } } { \partial u _ { j } } \frac { \partial \Psi _ { 0 } } { \partial z _ { j } } \right) = 0 .
$$

Thus from (85) we can derive both of the relations (86) and (88).This circumstance can arise either when there is a relation among $F _ { 0 }$ ， $\Phi _ { 0 } , \Psi _ { 0 }$ or when the Jacobian of arbitrary $2 n - 3$ expressions among the expressions $D _ { \lambda } ,$ together with all its first minor determinants,vanishes. As the first case contradicts our assumption,there should be two relations among arbitrary $2 n - 3$ of $D _ { \lambda } ,$ if $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 }$ is to satisfy (85);in other words, there are only $2 n - 6$ independent expressions among (87).But the expressions (87） are formed out of the coefficients of the given function $F _ { 1 }$ .Hence,we can judge directly whether there are one or two relations among the expressons (87). In general, there is only one such relation. Hence, there is no uniform integral other than $\pmb { F }$

When there is no relation among $2 n - 4$ expressions (87), we say that they are ordinary;when there are $q$ relations,we say that they are singular of order $q \mathrm { ~ - ~ } 1$ .When there are values of ${ x } _ { 1 } , { x } _ { 2 }$ that satisfy (85) in a domain of $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , z , u _ { \mathrm { { ~ \scriptsize ~ 2 ~ } ~ } }$ , we say that the class $\hbar / q$ belongs to the region (δ).If there are an infinite number of ordinary classes in the region (δ) of the domain $( \mathbf { D } )$ that include an infinite number of values of $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , z , u _ { \mathrm { { ; } } }$ then there exists no integral (other than $\boldsymbol { F }$ ）that is uniform with respect to $x , y , z , u$ and periodic with respect to $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 }$ for all values of $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 }$ and all values of $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , z , u$ in $( \mathbf { D } )$ so long as $\mu$ is sufficiently small. If there is an infinite number of singular classes of order $q$ in (δ), then there are no more than $q + 1$ uniform independent integrals including $F$

In the foregoing discussion we have assumed that the uniform integrals $\Phi$ in question are expanded in integral powers of $\pmb { \mu }$ But the theorem is true also for integrals $\Phi$ that are expanded in integral powers of $\sqrt { \mu }$ Suppose that $\Phi ^ { \prime }$ and $\Phi ^ { \prime \prime }$ are integral power series of $\pmb { \mu }$ ; write

$$
\Phi = \Phi ^ { \prime } + \sqrt { \mu } \Phi ^ { \prime \prime } .
$$

If $\Phi$ is an integral, then we have identically

$$
( F , \Phi ) = ( F , \Phi ^ { \prime } ) + \sqrt { \mu } ( F , \Phi ^ { \prime \prime } ) = 0 .
$$

As $( F , \Phi ^ { \prime } )$ and $( F , \Phi ^ { \prime \prime } )$ are both expanded in integral power series of $\mu$ ， we have separately $( F , \Phi ^ { \prime } ) = 0 $ $( F , \Phi ^ { \prime \prime } ) = 0 $ Thus, $\Phi ^ { \prime }$ and $\Phi ^ { \prime \prime }$ are both integrals. Since we have proved that there exists no uniform integral that can be expanded in an integral power series of $\mu$ ,we see now that there exists no uniform integral that can be expanded in an integral power series of $\sqrt { \mu }$

More generally, suppose that

$$
\theta _ { 1 } ( \mu ) , \theta _ { 2 } ( \mu ) , . . . , \theta _ { p } ( \mu )
$$

are arbitrary functions of $\mu$ and that there exists no relation of the form

$$
\phi _ { 1 } \theta _ { 1 } ( \mu ) + \phi _ { 2 } \theta _ { 2 } ( \mu ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \phi _ { p } \theta _ { p } ( \mu ) = 0
$$

among these functions, where the functions $\phi _ { 1 } , \phi _ { 2 } , . . . , \phi _ { p }$ are expanded in integral power series of $\mu$ . Suppose that one of the functions $\phi _ { i }$ say $\phi _ { 1 }$ ,does not have $\mu$ as a factor. Then, solving (89),we obtain

$$
\theta _ { 1 } ( \mu ) = - \frac { \phi _ { 2 } } { \phi _ { 1 } } \theta _ { 2 } ( \mu ) - \frac { \phi _ { 3 } } { \phi _ { 1 } } \theta _ { 3 } ( \pounds ) - \cdot \cdot \cdot - \frac { \phi _ { p } } { \phi _ { 1 } } \theta _ { p } ( \mu ) ,
$$

where $\phi _ { 2 } / \phi _ { 1 } , . . . , \phi _ { p } / \phi _ { 1 }$ can be expanded in integral powers of $\pmb { \mu }$ .Hence, we can eliminate one of the functions $\theta _ { 1 } ( \mu ) , \theta _ { 2 } ( \mu ) , . . . , \theta _ { p } ( \mu )$ by substituting this expression for $\theta _ { 1 } ( \mu )$ . Now we can suppose that there is no relation of the form (89) among the functions $\theta _ { p } ( \mu )$ . Thus $\Phi$ is considered to be of the form

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \Phi = A _ { 0 1 } \theta _ { 1 } ( \mu ) + A _ { 1 1 } \mu \theta _ { 1 } ( \mu ) + A _ { 2 1 } \mu ^ { 2 } \theta _ { 1 } ( \mu ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \\ { { \ } } \\ { { \qquad + A _ { 0 2 } \theta _ { 2 } ( \mu ) + A _ { 1 2 } \mu \theta _ { 2 } ( \mu ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \end{array}
$$

$$
~ + ~ A _ { 0 p } \theta _ { p } ( \mu ) + A _ { 1 p } \mu \theta _ { p } ( \mu ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

where $\pmb { A }$ is a function independent of $\mu$ but a function of $_ x$ and $y$ only. Rewrite it in the form

$$
\Phi = \Phi _ { 1 } \theta _ { 1 } ( \mu ) + \Phi _ { 2 } \theta _ { 2 } ( \mu ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \Phi _ { p } \theta _ { p } ( \mu ) ,
$$

where $\Phi _ { 1 } , \Phi _ { 2 } , . . . , \Phi _ { p }$ are expanded in integral powers of $\pmb { \mu }$ .If $\Phi$ is an integral, then

$$
( F , \Phi ) = \theta _ { 1 } ( \mu ) ( F , \Phi _ { 1 } ) + \theta _ { 2 } ( \mu ) ( F , \Phi _ { 2 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \theta _ { p } ( \mu ) ( F , \Phi _ { p } ) = 0
$$

should hold identically. Hence,

$$
( F , \Phi _ { 1 } ) = ( F , \Phi _ { 2 } ) = \cdot \cdot \cdot = ( F , \Phi _ { p } ) = 0 .
$$

Otherwise $( F , \Phi _ { i } ) ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , p )$ would be expanded in integral powers of $\pmb { \mu }$ and (91) would acquire the form (89).This would contradict our assumption. Hence, $\Phi _ { 1 } , \Phi _ { 2 } , . . . , \Phi _ { p }$ are separately integrals of the problem. Since we have proved that there exists no uniform integral that can be expanded in an integral power series of $\pmb { \mu }$ ，we see that there exists no uniform integral that can be represented in the form (90).

This proof is valid even when $\Phi$ is expanded with respect to the base field of a denumerably infnite number of linearly independent functions $\theta _ { 1 } ( \mu ) , \theta _ { 2 } ( \mu ) , \ldots ,$ which can be expanded in integral powers of $\mu$ ·

The foregoing theorem of Poincaré deals with integrals that are uniform with respect to $y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { n } , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } ,$ ，while Bruns's theorem deals with integrals that are algebraic with respect to $y _ { 1 } , \dotsc , y _ { n } , x _ { 1 } , \dotsc , x _ { n } .$ . Painleve (1900) generalized the theorem of Poincaré to integrals that are uniform only with respect to $y _ { 1 } , \ldots , y _ { n }$ (Sections 6.7-6.11). When he generalized Bruns's theorem, Painlevé used the theorem that an integral which is rational in the momenta has no singularity other than poles for such values of $x _ { 3 j - 2 } , x _ { 3 j - 1 } , x _ { 3 j }$ that do not satisfy

$$
\begin{array} { r } { ( x _ { 3 j - 2 } - x _ { 3 k - 2 } ) ^ { 2 } + ( x _ { 3 j - 1 } - x _ { 3 k - 1 } ) ^ { 2 } + ( x _ { 3 j } - x _ { 3 k } ) ^ { 2 } = 0 } \\ { ( j , k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) . } \end{array}
$$

Corresponding to this is the theorem that a real uniform integral has no singularity other than those for the values satisfying $x _ { 3 j - 2 } = x _ { 3 k - 2 } ,$ $x _ { 3 j - 1 } = x _ { 3 k - 1 }$ ， ${ \mathfrak { x } } _ { 3 j } = { \mathfrak { x } } _ { 3 k }$ $( j , k = 1 , 2 , . . . , n )$ in the real domain of $x _ { 3 j - 2 } , x _ { 3 j - 1 } , x _ { 3 j }$ .Painlevé used this latter theorem in his generalization of the theorem of Poincaré.Then he proved that there exists no integral that is real and uniform in the momenta other than the classical integrals, and thus completed the generalization of Poincaré's theorem.

# 6.17APPLICATION TO THE $\pmb { n }$ -BODY PROBLEM

In the restricted problem of three bodies,Poincaré (l889,1892) established the basis of his theory of the periodic solutions and the characteristic exponents which will be discussed in later chapters,and proved the theorem that there exists no integral uniform in the Keplerian elements other than the classical integrals, in the following manner.

Consider the plane case of the three-body problem,and suppose that we reduce the differential equations of order six with the variables $L , L ^ { \prime } , K , l , l ^ { \prime }$ ， $\pmb { \kappa }$ in the form (184a), Section 5.19.Let the expansion of the disturbing function be

$$
F _ { 1 } = \sum _ { m , m ^ { \prime } } B _ { m m ^ { \prime } } \exp { [ \sqrt { - 1 } \ ( m l + m ^ { \prime } l ^ { \prime } ) ] } ;
$$

then the coefficient $B _ { m , m ^ { \prime } }$ is a function of $L , L ^ { \prime } , K , \kappa .$ . Form the expression

$$
B _ { \lambda p , \lambda q } ^ { \lambda ^ { \prime } } B _ { \lambda ^ { \prime } p , \lambda ^ { \prime } q } ^ { - \lambda } \qquad ( \lambda , \lambda ^ { \prime } = 0 , \pm 1 , \pm 2 , . . . )
$$

with two relatively prime integers, and take the values of $L , L ^ { \prime }$ such that

$$
\dot { p } \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial L } + q \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial L ^ { \prime } } = 0 ,
$$

that is,such that the mean motions are in the ratio $- \pmb { \mathscr { p } } \colon \pmb { q }$ .The disturbing function is,as has been seen in Sections 5.16-5.18,of degree $- 1 / 2$ with regard to $L , L ^ { \prime }$ ， and homogeneous of degree $^ { - 1 }$ with regard to $a , a ^ { \prime }$ · Suppose that $a ^ { \prime } > a$ ， Then the disturbing function,apart from the common factor $\acute { a } ^ { \prime - 1 }$ ,is a function of ${ \mathbf { } } a / a ^ { \prime }$ or $L / L ^ { \prime }$ only. Since the coefficient $B _ { m m } .$ of (87) can accordingly be considered as a function of this ratio only, it is uniquely determined by (85a). In order that there may exist a uniform integral other than the energy integral, there should be,as $n = 3$ and $2 n - 4 = 2$ ,one relation between two of the expressions (87) of Section 6.16; in other words, (87) should be a function of the secular term $B _ { \infty }$ . We know (as we shall see in Chapter 7 from the property of the expansion of the disturbing function） that there is no such relation among the coefficients.Hence, there exists no uniform integral of the form

$$
\Phi ( L , L ^ { \prime } , K , l , l ^ { \prime } , \kappa ) = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t }
$$

other than the energy integral.

The above proof, however, is not yet complete. We must examine an integral of the form

$$
\Phi ( L , L ^ { \prime } , G , G ^ { \prime } , l , l ^ { \prime } , g , g ^ { \prime } ) = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } .
$$

Take the canonically conjugate variables $L , L ^ { \prime } , G , G ^ { \prime } , l , l ^ { \prime } , g , g ^ { \prime }$ of (164) in Section 5.16 for the three-body problem;then (87) depends only on $L , L ^ { \prime } , G , G ^ { \prime } , g , g ^ { \prime }$ Take for $L , L ^ { \prime }$ the values satisfying (85a)； then (87) depends only on $G , G ^ { \prime } , g , g ^ { \prime }$ . In order that there may exist a uniform integral other than the energy integral, there should exist, as $n = 4$ and $2 n - 4 = 4$ ,one relation among four of the expressions (87). Since (87) is a function of $G , G ^ { \prime } , g - g ^ { \prime }$ only,nothing prevents there being such a relation. Thus, there is one uniform integral other than the energy integral: one of the integrals of angular momentum.In order that there may exist two uniform integrals, there should be one relation among three of the expressions (87); that is, all of the expressions (87) should be expressed in terms of only two variables.But the actual expansion of the disturbing function shows that this is not the case.Hence, there exists no uniform integral other than the energy integral and the angularmomentum integral in the plane three-body problem.

·We now turn to the general three-body problem,and consider the twelve canonically conjugate variables $L , L ^ { \prime } , G , G ^ { \prime } , H , H ^ { \prime } , l , l ^ { \prime } , g , g ^ { \prime } , h , h ^ { \prime }$ as in (l64) of Section 5.16.If we choose the values of $L$ ， $L ^ { \prime }$ so as to satisfy (85a), then (87) still contains eight variables $G , G ^ { \prime } , H , H ^ { \prime } , g , g ^ { \prime }$ ， $h , h ^ { \prime }$ 、 In order that there may exist $q$ uniform integrals other than $F _ { : }$ ， there should be one relation among $2 n - 3 - q = 3 - q$ of the expressions (87).We have proved in Section 5.19 that these expressions depend only on the five variables $G , G ^ { \prime } , g , g ^ { \prime } , h - h ^ { \prime }$ .Hence, there should be one relation among $9 - 3 = 6$ of the expressions (87). Thus, $q = 3$ ; nothing prevents there being three independent uniform integrals, that is, the three integrals of the angular momentum discussed in Section 5.19. There is no relation among $9 - 4 = 5$ of the expressions (87). Hence, in the general three-body problem, there exists no uniform integral other than the energy integral and the threeintegrals of the angular momentum.

Let us next see in detail that there is no relation among an arbitrary set of $2 n - 4$ of the expressions (87).

The disturbing function is expanded in the form

$$
F _ { 1 } = \sum { B _ { m m ^ { \prime } } \exp { [ \sqrt { - 1 } \ ( m l + m ^ { \prime } l ^ { \prime } ) ] } } ,
$$

where $B _ { m m ^ { \prime } }$ is a function of $L , L ^ { \prime } , G , G ^ { \prime } , g , g ^ { \prime } , H , H ^ { \prime } , h , h ^ { \prime }$ . It can be seen that the same is true for the expansion in the Keplerian elements as was described in Section 5.18.The expansion is written in the form

$$
F _ { 1 } = \sum C _ { m m ^ { \prime } } \exp \{ \sqrt { - 1 } \ [ m ( l + g + h ) + m ^ { \prime } ( l ^ { \prime } + g ^ { \prime } + h ^ { \prime } ) ] \} ,
$$

where

$$
B _ { m m ^ { \prime } } = C _ { m m ^ { \prime } } \exp { [ \sqrt { - 1 } ~ ( m g + m ^ { \prime } g ^ { \prime } + m h + m ^ { \prime } h ^ { \prime } ) ] } .
$$

Here the mean longitude is denoted by $l + g + h = l + \varpi . \ C _ { m m ^ { \prime } }$ is a function of ten variables, $a , a ^ { \prime } , e , e ^ { \prime } , I , I ^ { \prime } , \varpi , \varpi ^ { \prime } , \Omega , \Omega ^ { \prime }$ . Since the Jacobian

${ \mathrm { o f } } L , L ^ { \prime } , G , G ^ { \prime } , H , H ^ { \prime } , g , g ^ { \prime } , h , h ^ { \prime }$ with regard to $a , a ^ { \prime } , e , e ^ { \prime } , I , I ^ { \prime } , \varpi , \varpi ^ { \prime } , \Omega , \Omega ^ { \prime }$ is not zero, the result of our discussion on $B _ { m m ^ { \prime } }$ is valid also for $C _ { m m ^ { \prime } }$ ,and (87) is replaced by

$$
C _ { \lambda p , \lambda q } ^ { \lambda ^ { \prime } } C _ { \lambda ^ { \prime } p , \lambda ^ { \prime } q } ^ { - \lambda } \qquad ( \lambda , \lambda ^ { \prime } = 0 , \pm 1 , \pm 2 , . . . ) .
$$

In order that there may exist a uniform integral, there should be one relation among $2 n - 4$ of the expressions (87a).

In ordinary perturbation theory the coefficient $C _ { m m } .$ is expanded in powers of $\ : e , e ^ { \prime } , I , I ^ { \prime } \ :$ . The degree of a term in the expansion is at least $| m + m ^ { \prime } |$ as has been shown in Sections 5.16-5.l8. We arrange the terms of $C _ { m m ^ { \prime } }$ according to the degree and let it be

$$
C _ { m m ^ { \prime } } = C _ { m m ^ { \prime } ; 0 } + C _ { m m ^ { \prime } ; 1 } + C _ { m m ^ { \prime } ; 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + C _ { m m ^ { \prime } ; p } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ;
$$

where $C _ { m m ^ { \prime } : p }$ is of degree $| m + m ^ { \prime } | + 2 p$ . We call $C _ { m m ^ { \prime } ; 0 }$ the principal part of $C _ { m m ^ { \prime } }$ .The term $C _ { 0 0 ; 0 }$ depends on $a , a ^ { \prime }$ . Suppose for the moment that $a , a ^ { \prime }$ are constant; ignore $C _ { 0 0 : 0 }$ , since it is now a constant. We call

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { C _ { 0 0 ; 0 } + C _ { 0 0 ; 1 } + \displaystyle \sum C _ { m m ^ { \prime } ; 0 } } } \\ { { \times \exp \{ \sqrt { - 1 } [ m ( l + g + h ) + m ^ { \prime } ( l ^ { \prime } + g ^ { \prime } + { } ^ { . }  } } \end{array}
$$

the principal part of the expansion of $F _ { 1 }$

From $( 8 7 a )$ we form a matrix with an infinite number of elements, such that, corresponding to a positive, negative,or zero value of the index $\lambda$ of each row, the first element of the row with index $\lambda$ is $\lambda C _ { \lambda p , \lambda q }$ and the other elements of the same row are the partial derivatives of $C _ { \lambda p , \lambda q }$ with regard to $e , e ^ { \prime } , I , I ^ { \prime } , \varpi , \varpi ^ { \prime } , \Omega , \Omega ^ { \prime }$ .In order that there may be one relation among $2 n - 4$ of $( 8 7 \mathsf { a } )$ , all the determinants obtained by taking $2 n - 3 = 9$ rows from this matrix must be zero.But, as the terms of $C _ { m m ^ { \prime } }$ are at least of degree $| m + m ^ { \prime } |$ ，the first element $\lambda C _ { \lambda p , \lambda q }$ of the row with index $\lambda$ begins with the term of degree $| \lambda \boldsymbol { p } + \lambda \boldsymbol { q } |$ ,while the partial derivatives of $C _ { \lambda p , \lambda q }$ with regard to e, $e ^ { \prime }$ ，I, $I ^ { \prime }$ are of degree $| \lambda p + \lambda q | - 1$ ,and its partial derivatives with regard to $\varpi , \varpi ^ { \prime } , \Omega , \Omega ^ { \prime }$ are of degree $| \lambda \boldsymbol { p } + \lambda \boldsymbol { q } |$ . The first element of the row with $\lambda = 0$ is zero. The expansion of the partial derivatives of $C _ { \boldsymbol { \mathfrak { o } } 0 }$ with regard to $\varpi , \varpi ^ { \prime } , \Omega , \Omega ^ { \prime }$ begins with a term of degree 2,and the expansion of those with regard to $\ell , e ^ { \prime } , I , I ^ { \prime }$ begins with a term of degree l. Our determinant is expanded in integral powers of $e , e ^ { \prime } , I , I ^ { \prime }$ .Denote by $\Delta$ the determinant formed of the rows with indices $\lambda _ { 1 } , \lambda _ { 2 } , . . . , \lambda _ { 2 n - 3 }$ ； then the terms of its expansion are at least of degree

$$
| { \mathfrak { p } } + q | : ( | \lambda _ { 1 } | + \cdot \cdot \cdot + | \lambda _ { 2 n - 3 } | ) - 4 .
$$

Denote this degree by $\pmb { \alpha }$ If $\lambda _ { 2 n - 3 } = 0$ ,then we denote

$$
| { \boldsymbol { p } } + { \boldsymbol { q } } | \cdot ( | \lambda _ { 1 } | + \dots + | \lambda _ { 2 n - 4 } | ) - 2
$$

by $\pmb { \alpha }$ . Since $\Delta$ is identically zero,a term with degree $\pmb { \alpha }$ must also be zero. Such terms are obtained by replacing each $C _ { \lambda p , \lambda q }$ of $\Delta$ by its principal part $C _ { \lambda p , \lambda q ; 0 }$ .If $\lambda = 0$ ,then we replace it by $C _ { 0 0 : 1 }$ . The determinant $\Delta _ { 0 }$ thus obtained should be identically zerc. We add $C _ { 0 0 : 1 }$ corresponding to $\lambda = 0$ to the expression

$$
( C _ { \lambda p , \lambda q ; 0 } ) ^ { \lambda ^ { \prime } } ( C _ { \lambda ^ { \prime } p , \lambda ^ { \prime } q ; 0 } ) ^ { - \lambda } \qquad ( \lambda , \lambda ^ { \prime } = 0 , \pm 1 , \pm 2 , . . . ) .
$$

This is of degree 2 with regard to $e , \ e ^ { \prime } , \ I , \ I ^ { \prime }$ .The equation $\Delta _ { 0 } = 0$ represents the fact that there is at least one relation among an arbitrary set of $2 n - 4 = 8$ of (87b) thus obtained.Hence,in order that there may exist a uniform integral, there should be one relation among these $2 n - 4$ expressions.While (87) is an infinite series, (87b) is rational with regard to $\ell , e ^ { \prime } , I , I ^ { \prime }$ and the cosines of $\varpi , \varpi ^ { \prime } , \Omega , \Omega ^ { \prime } .$ .The question whether there is such a relation among the coefficients of the expansion will be discussed in Section 7.30 on the expansion of the disturbing function.

In the case of the $\pmb { n }$ -body problem we write the differential equations (164),Section 5.16, for the $n - 1$ fictitious bodies. The order of the differential equations is $6 n - 6$ Hence,we take $6 n - 6$ as the value of $\pmb { n }$ in the above discussion. If we choose $L _ { 1 } , . . . , L _ { n - 1 }$ so as to satisfy

$$
m _ { 1 } \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + m _ { n - 1 } \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { n - 1 } } = 0 ,
$$

then (87） contains $n - 3$ variables $L _ { j }$ It also contains $n - 1$ sets of variables $G _ { j } , H _ { j } , g _ { j } , h _ { j } .$ Thus,it is a function of

$$
4 ( n - 1 ) + n - 3 = 5 n - 7
$$

variables altogether.How many relations are there among the $5 n - 6$ variables of (87), (87a),or (87b)? In order that there may exist $q$ uniform integrals other than $F _ { \cdot }$ ，there should be one relation among an arbitrary set of $5 n - 6 - q$ variables of (87).As we have discussed in Section 5.8, the order of the differential equations is reduced to $6 n - 1 0$ Excluding the $n - 1$ variables $L _ { j { \mathrm { : } } }$ ，we must express the integrals as functions of $6 n - 1 0 - n + 1 = 5 n - 9$ variables. Thus $q = 3$ .These integrals are the three integrals of the angular momentum.Hence, the differential equations written in the form (l64) of Section 5.16 for the $_ { n }$ bodies have no uniform integral other than those of the energy and the angular momentum.

# 6.18DISCUSSION OF POINCARE's THEOREM

We have given in Section 6.l2 an exceptional case where there exists an algebraic integral in the restricted three-body problem.When two of the three bodies in the three-body problem collide at a finite distance at the end of a finite interval of time, the differential equations cease to be regular,as will be discussed later.Otherwise, the coordinates of the three bodies can be determined uniquely by Cauchy's theorem on the existence of the solution of a differential equation,and the solution can be expanded in integral powers of $t \mathrm { ~ - ~ } t _ { 0 }$ around an arbitrary initial condition for $t = t _ { 0 }$ (Painlevé, l897).There are two independent conditions for such a collision,which reduce to one in the case of the plane problem.The conditions for a collision in the two-body problem are algebraic, but the conditions for the three- and $\pmb { n }$ -body problems are transcendental according to Painlevé (1897，1900，i900a，1900b). Suppose that a collision occurs when

$$
\chi ( t , x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { n } , y _ { 1 } , . . . , y _ { n } ) = 0
$$

is satisfied. Since this is an invariantive relation of the motion, it should be an algebraic combination of the known classical integrals, according to Bruns's theorem.But the conditions for a collision should be satisfied when we increase $t$ by a constant amount； they should be unaltered when we increase the coordinates and the momentum by a constant amount.

We limit ourselves to the consideration of the plane three-body problem.Let the total kinetic energy of the whole three-body system around the center of mass be $T _ { i }$ ，the total angular momentum $W _ { \perp }$ and the force function $U$ ; then $T - U$ and $W$ remain unaltered by the above change in coordinates and momentum.Hence, the condition for a collision does not depend on the coordinates and the momentum of the center of mass but should be a relation between $W$ and $T - U ,$ The equations of motion remain unaltered by changing $t$ to $\alpha ^ { 3 } t , \ x _ { \vdots }$ to $\alpha ^ { 2 } x _ { i }$ ， and $y _ { i }$ to $\alpha ^ { - 1 } y _ { i }$ with a constant $\pmb { \alpha }$ ; hence, the condition for a collision also remains unaltered by this transformation- in which $T - U$ is multiplied by $\alpha ^ { - 2 }$ and $W$ is multiplied by $\pmb { \alpha }$ .Let the condition be

$$
W = \phi ( T - U ) ;
$$

then,

$$
\alpha W = \phi [ \alpha ^ { - 2 } ( T - U ) ] = \alpha \phi ( T - U ) .
$$

Hence, the condition should be of the form

$$
\phi ( T - U ) = \lambda / { \sqrt { T - U } } ,
$$

or

$$
W ^ { 2 } ( T - U ) = \lambda ^ { 2 }
$$

with a constant $\lambda$ .Whatever the value of $\lambda$ ，this corresponds to a motion of hyperbolic type; that is, it is the case when the three bodies recede to infinite distances from each other as $t$ approaches $\pm \infty$ .Hence, it cannot be the condition for a collision.The condition should be transcendental.This is the argument of Painlevé, (l897, 1897a). On the other hand, $W$ should be finite even at the time of a collision,as has been proved by Sundman (l907, l909,1913)and as we shall see in a later chapter.But, since the momentum becomes infinite at the time of a collision，this relation is contradictory.Hence,according to Bruns's theorem, the condition for a collision cannot be algebraic；it should be represented by transcendental functions at least with respect to the momentum.

It was Levi-Civita and Bisconcini who obtained the condition for a collision explicitly (Levi-Civita,1903,1906；Bisconcini, 1906；see also Kiveliovitch,1926;Rosenblatt, 1928; Chazy,1932).According to these authors there exists (as will be shown in Chapter 22) a uniform integral in the case of a collision,but it is neither for $t$ nor for the Keplerian elements - with regard to which the integral is uniform.The theorem of Poincaré deals with the Keplerian elements； hence, it expresses the uniformity of the integral in the neighborhood of those values of $x _ { i } , y _ { i }$ that represent the state of motion belonging to the same osculating elliptic elements. As has been remarked by Levi-Civita, the theorem of Poincaré does not deny the existence of uniform integrals for a part of the trajectory.It does not deny the existence, in the neighborhood, of a state of motion very different from an elliptic orbit (Section 14.16).

In the case of a simultaneous collsion of all three bodies at a point, the total angular momentum of the system must be zero, as proved by Sundman (l9o9). This is an algebraic invariantive relation. The above discussion can be applied to the $\pmb { n }$ -body problem,as we will demonstrate in Chapter 22.

Consider the state of motion for $t \to \pm \infty$ . When two of the three mutual distances of the three bodies become infinite of order 1 with regard to $t$ and one of the mutual distances is bounded, the motion is said to be elliptic-hyperbolic (Chazy, l922).When two of the three bodies move in orbits of elliptic type around the center of mass and the third body moves in an orbit of parabolic type around the center of mass, that is,when two of the mutual distances becomes infinite of order $\scriptstyle { \frac { 2 } { 3 } }$ and the remaining one is bounded,we say that the motion is elliptic-parabolic.When all three of the mutual distances are bounded, we say that the motion is oscillatory.In these three cases the energy constant $\boldsymbol { h }$ of the whole system is negative,and the region $h < 0$ in the 16-dimen-sional space is divided into four subspaces. Three of these four subspaces contain the region $h = 0$ in their interiors.Each orbit in these subspaces is elliptic-hyperbolic； two of the mutual distances become infinite of order l with regard to $t$ as $t \to \pm \infty$ ; the third mutual distance, which remains the same all the time,is bounded.The three mutual distances are either bounded or oscillatory inside the subspace bounded by three analytic surfaces.

When the three mutual distances become infinite, of order $\frac { 2 } { 3 }$ ，1,1, respectively, as $t$ increases indefinitely, the motion is said to be parabolichyperbolic.When all three mutual distances become infinite of order 1, the motion is said to be hyperbolic. When the three bodies collide at infinity, the order of infinity is $\scriptstyle { \frac { 2 } { 3 } }$ and the motion is said to be parabolic. The configuration of the three bodies in the immediate neighborhood of a simultaneous collision is that of either the equilateral triangular solution of Lagrange or the straight-line solution of Euler,as has been remarked in Chapter 3.In the first two cases the point at infinity $t = \infty$ is a transcendental singular point,but in the case of a colision it is an algebraic singularity of order two. These are the cases when the total energy constant $h$ of the whole system is positive.

The trajectories should be inside a subspace of eight dimensions embedded in the twelve-dimensional space, but in the case of a collision they should be inside a six-dimensional subspace. Two conditions are necessary for a collision and they have been shown (Chazy, l9l8,1922, 1929,1932,1933) to be transcendental with regard to $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , y _ { 3 } , y _ { 4 } , y _ { 5 } , y _ { 6 }$ The region in which the trajectories of the three bodies lie is divided into five regions,except for a simultaneous collision of the three bodies. The exterior region corresponds to hyperbolic motions,three of the five regions to ellptic-hyperbolic motions,and the remaining ffth either to oscillatory motions for which the three mutual distances are all bounded or to motions for which two of the mutual distances are arbitrarily large while the third is bounded. There are motions with elliptic osculating orbits in the fifth region.As $t$ increases indefinitely, each of the functions $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } , y _ { 3 } , y _ { 4 } , y _ { 5 } , y _ { 6 }$ tends to a finite limit.The limits are uniform functions of the values of the twelve variables $x _ { i } , y _ { i }$ $( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , 6 )$ for a certain epoch.Hence, the squares of the sum and the difference of the limiting values of each of these six $y _ { i }$ are two uniform integrals in the domain of hyperbolic motions；each of those integrals represents a family of surfaces,and one of them passes through an arbitrary point in that domain.
