In the domain of elliptic-hyperbolic motions we denote by $y _ { 4 } , y _ { 5 } , y _ { 6 }$ the momentum of the third body (receding to infinity) relative to the center of mass of the two bodies remaining in a bounded finite domain,and define the eliptic elements by six out of the twelve osculating elements and the hyperbolic elements by the remaining six; then these elements tend to finite limits in each of the trajectories,and each of them defines a uniform integral in the corresponding domain. Those uniform integrals that can exist simultaneously in both of these two domains define a family of surfaces,only one of which can pass through an arbitrary point in the whole domain. Among these surfaces, the surface bounding one of the five domains is a particular one. It can be shown that the integrals corresponding to this bounding surface cannot be reduced to the energy integral or the angular-momentum integrals (see Chapter 22).

As will be discussed later in detail, this theorem of Poincaré deals with uniform integrals with respect to the twelve elements belonging to the two osculating elliptic motions. Such integrals are uniform in the domain of another two-dimensional space and do not enter in this theorem of Poincaré.

The theorem of Bruns, on the other hand, differs from the theorem of Poincaré and can be applied to such integrals. One such integral is neither an algebraic combination of the classical integrals,nor an algebraic combination of $y _ { i }$ only,as in the extension of the theorem by Painlevé.The bounding surfaces of the five domains of these trajectories are not algebraic surfaces.

Painlevé (1900,1900a,1900b) studied the cases in which the solution can be represented by meromorphic functions of $t$ with polar singularities. He has given algebraic differential equations that admit uniform integrals, besides the known functions, such as exponential, elliptic,Abelian,and uniform integrals of a linear differential equation including Fuchsian and automorphic functions. The critical points are called fixed if they do not depend on the integration constants; otherwise they are called movable. Painlevé,extending the works of Poincaréand Picard,examined differential equations that admit the general integrals only with fixed critical points.He considered differential equations of the first, second,and third order. In the case of the third order he found a new class of singularities, for example, those with a perfect set of discontinuities (see Chapter 22).

# 6.19INTEGRALS WITHOUT PARAMETERS

Although the theorem of Poincaré looks as if it were more general than the theorem of Bruns, it has a narrower validity domain; it is valid only for vanishingly small values of $\mu$ .However, it is wider than Bruns's in the sense that it is not limited to algebraic integrals. The theorem of Poincaré cannot be valid if we take off the restriction that the integrals should be arranged in integral powers of $\mu$ or expanded in the sequence of base functions of an algebraic feld of $\mu$ . It may happen to be false when the masses have particular values.

We suppose that in the system of differential equations

$$
\frac { d \xi _ { i } } { d t } = \Xi _ { i } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

the right-hand members $\Xi _ { 1 } , \Xi _ { 2 } , . . . , \Xi _ { m }$ do not contain $t$ explicitly, are expanded in integral powers of $\xi _ { 1 } , \xi _ { 2 } , . . . , \xi _ { m } ,$ and are not all zero for $\xi _ { 1 } = \xi _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = \xi _ { m } = 0$ . Then there may exist $m - 1$ independent integrals that do not contain $t$ explicitly and can be expanded in convergent integral power series of $\xi _ { 1 }$ ， $\xi _ { 2 } , . . . , \xi _ { m }$ (Cherry,1924,1924a,1926, 1927,1928;Woltjer,1924; Fermi, 1923).

Let the values of $\Xi _ { 1 } , \Xi _ { 2 } , . . . , \Xi _ { m }$ for

$$
\xi _ { 1 } = \xi _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = \xi _ { m } = 0
$$

be, respectively, $a _ { 1 } , a _ { 2 } , \ldots , a _ { m }$ and at least one of them, for example $a _ { 1 }$ ， be different from zero. Replacing $\xi _ { i }$ by

$$
\xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } = \xi _ { i } - { \frac { a _ { i } } { a _ { 1 } } } \xi _ { 1 } \qquad ( i = 2 , 3 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\frac { d \xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } } { d t } = \Xi _ { i } - \frac { a _ { i } } { a _ { 1 } } \Xi _ { 1 } = \Xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } ~ ( i = 2 , 3 , . . . , m ) .
$$

As

$\Xi _ { i } ^ { \prime } = 0 \qquad ( i = 2 , 3 , . . . , m ) \quad \mathrm { f o r } \quad \xi _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } = \xi _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = \xi _ { m } ^ { \prime } = 0$ and

$$
\frac { \partial ( \xi _ { 1 } , \xi _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } , \xi _ { 3 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , \xi _ { m } ^ { \prime } ) } { \partial ( \xi _ { 1 } , \xi _ { 2 } , \xi _ { 3 } , . . . , \xi _ { m } ) } = 1 ,
$$

(93) can be written

$$
\frac { d \xi _ { 1 } } { d t } = \lambda + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { m } a _ { j } ^ { ( 1 ) } \xi _ { j } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } a _ { 1 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) } \xi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + a _ { 1 2 } ^ { ( 1 ) } \xi _ { 1 } \xi _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ,
$$

$$
\frac { d \xi _ { i } } { d t } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { m } a _ { j } ^ { ( i ) } \xi _ { j } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } a _ { 1 1 } ^ { ( i ) } \xi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + a _ { 1 2 } ^ { ( i ) } \xi _ { 1 } \xi _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot
$$

We shall solve these equations in ascending power series of

$$
\xi _ { 1 } , \xi _ { 2 } , . . . , \xi _ { m }
$$

of the form

$$
\xi _ { i } = \xi _ { i } ^ { ( 1 ) } + \xi _ { i } ^ { ( 2 ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

Substituting (94) in (93a) and equating the terms of the same degree, we first obtain from

$$
{ \frac { d \xi _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) } } { d t } } = \lambda , \qquad { \frac { d \xi _ { j } ^ { ( 1 ) } } { d t } } = 0 \qquad ( j = 2 , 3 , . . . , m )
$$

the solution

$$
\xi _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) } = \lambda ( t + c _ { 1 } ) , \qquad \xi _ { j } ^ { ( 1 ) } = c _ { j } \qquad ( j = 2 , 3 , . . . , m ) .
$$

Substituting these in the second-degree terms

and solving

$$
\frac { d \xi _ { i } ^ { ( 2 ) } } { d t } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { m } a _ { j } ^ { ( i ) } \xi _ { j } ^ { ( 1 ) } \qquad ( j = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

$$
\frac { d \xi _ { i } ^ { ( 2 ) } } { d t } = a _ { 1 } ^ { ( i ) } \lambda ( t + c _ { 1 } ) + \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { m } a _ { j } ^ { ( i ) } c _ { j } ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\xi _ { i } ^ { ( 2 ) } = { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } a _ { 1 } ^ { ( i ) } \lambda ( t + c _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } - \sum _ { j = 2 } ^ { m } a _ { j } ^ { ( 1 ) } c _ { j } ( t + c _ { 1 } ) \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

The integration constants are determined so that $\xi _ { i } ^ { ( 2 ) }$ is homogeneous of the second degree in $t + c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , \ldots , c _ { m } ;$ and vanishes together with $t + c _ { 1 }$ . By repeating this procedure we can determine $\xi _ { i } ^ { ( s ) }$ ， $( s = 2 , 3 , \ldots )$ ， so as to make them vanish together with $t + c _ { 1 }$ . If we substitute these in (94),we obtain the solution in the form of an integral function of $t + c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , . . . , c _ { m }$ satisfying (93a).

Suppose that $\mathrm { H } _ { 1 }$ ， $\operatorname { H } _ { 2 } , \ldots , \operatorname { H } _ { m }$ are the majorant functions, respectively, of $\Xi _ { 1 } , \Xi _ { 2 } , . . . , \Xi _ { m }$ in $\eta _ { 1 } , \eta _ { 2 } , \ldots , \eta _ { m }$ ； that is,the coefficient in the expansion of $\mathrm { H } _ { k }$ with regard to $\eta _ { 1 } , \eta _ { 2 } , . . . , \eta _ { m }$ is larger than the corresponding coefficient in the expansion of $\Xi _ { k }$ with regard to $\xi _ { 1 } , \xi _ { 2 } , \ldots , \xi _ { m }$ in the absolute values within the domain of $\xi _ { i } , \eta _ { i }$ in question. Consider

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle \frac { d \eta _ { 1 } } { d t } = { \mathrm { H } } _ { 1 } = \mu + \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { m } b _ { j } ^ { ( 1 ) } \eta _ { j } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } b _ { 1 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) } \eta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 1 2 } ^ { ( 1 ) } \eta _ { 1 } \eta _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d \eta _ { i } } { d t } = { \mathrm { H } } _ { i } = \sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { m } b _ { j } ^ { ( \mathrm { i } ) } \eta _ { j } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } b _ { 1 1 } ^ { ( \mathrm { i } ) } \eta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + b _ { 1 2 } ^ { ( \mathrm { i } ) } \eta _ { 1 } \eta _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad ( i = 2 , 3 , . . . , m ) , } } \end{array}
$$

and try to expand the solution, similarly to (94),in the form

$$
\eta _ { i } = \eta _ { i } ^ { ( 1 ) } + \eta _ { i } ^ { ( 2 ) } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , m ) .
$$

Solving these equations in the same manner as we did for (93), we obtain

$$
\eta _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) } = \mu ( t + d _ { 1 } ) , \qquad y _ { j } ^ { ( 1 ) } = d _ { j } \qquad ( j = 2 , 3 , . . . , m ) ,
$$

$$
\eta _ { i } ^ { ( 2 ) } = { \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } } b _ { 1 } ^ { ( i ) } \mu ( t + d _ { 1 } ) ^ { 2 } + b _ { 2 } ^ { ( i ) } d _ { 2 } ( t + d _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + b _ { m } ^ { ( i ) } d _ { m } ( t + d _ { 1 } )
$$

$$
( i = 2 , 3 , \cdots , m ) .
$$

Choose the integration constants $t + d _ { 1 } , d _ { 2 } , . . . , d _ { m }$ so that they are positive, as are $t + c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , . . . , c _ { m } .$ ,and satisfy

$$
| t + c _ { 1 } | < t + d _ { 1 } , | c _ { 2 } | < d _ { 2 } , . . . , | c _ { m } | < d _ { m } .
$$

Comparing (95) with (99), we have

$$
\big | \xi _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) } \big | < \eta _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) } , . . . , \big | \xi _ { m } ^ { ( 1 ) } \big | < \eta _ { m } ^ { ( 1 ) } .
$$

Comparing (96) with (100), we have

$$
\Big | \xi _ { 1 } ^ { ( 2 ) } \Big | < \eta _ { 1 } ^ { ( 2 ) } , . . . , \big | \xi _ { m } ^ { ( 2 ) } \Big | < \eta _ { m } ^ { ( 2 ) } .
$$

In general, $\eta _ { i } ^ { ( s ) }$ is larger than the corresponding $\xi _ { i } ^ { ( s ) }$ in absolute value. Hence, if $t + d _ { 1 } , d _ { 2 } , . . . , d _ { m }$ are chosen so that (98) is convergent, then the series (94) is absolutely and uniformly convergent.

Now let $M$ and $\propto$ be sufficiently large positive constants， and the expansion of

$$
\frac { M ( \eta _ { 1 } + \eta _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \eta _ { m } ) } { 1 - \alpha ( \eta _ { 1 } + \eta _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \eta _ { m } ) }
$$

be the functions $\mathrm { H } _ { 1 } - \mu , \mathrm { H } _ { 2 } , . . . , \mathrm { H } _ { m }$ of （97）with $\mu > | \lambda |$ ； then,writing $\eta _ { 1 } + \eta _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \eta _ { m } = \zeta ,$ we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \frac { d ( \eta _ { 1 } - \eta _ { j } ) } { d t } = \mu \qquad ( j = 2 , 3 , . . . , m ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \frac { d \zeta } { d t } = \frac { \mu + \zeta ( m M - \alpha \mu ) } { 1 - \alpha \zeta } . } } \end{array}
$$

We can choose $M$ and $\propto$ so that they satisfy $m M - \alpha \mu = 0$ and are both sufficiently large positive constants. Then the solution of these equations are

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \zeta - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \alpha \zeta ^ { 2 } = \mu t + \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } , \qquad } \\ { \eta _ { 1 } - \eta _ { j } = \mu t + \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } \qquad ( j = 2 , 3 , . . . , m ) . } \end{array}
$$

Determine the constants so that $\eta _ { 1 } = 0 , \eta _ { 2 } = d _ { 2 } , . . . , \eta _ { m } = d _ { m }$ for $t + d _ { 1 } = 0$ ; then

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \zeta - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \zeta ^ { 2 } = \mu ( t + d _ { 1 } ) + d - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \alpha d ^ { 2 } \qquad ( d = d _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + d _ { m } ) } } \\ { { \nonumber } } \\ { { \eta _ { 1 } - \eta _ { j } = \mu ( t + d _ { 1 } ) - d _ { j } \qquad ( j = 2 , 3 , . . . , m ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Hence, the value of $\zeta$ equal to $d$ for $t + d _ { 1 } = 0$ is

$$
\zeta = \frac { 1 } { \alpha } [ 1 - \sqrt { 1 - 2 \alpha \mu ( t + d _ { 1 } ) - 2 \alpha d + \alpha ^ { 2 } d ^ { 2 } } ] .
$$

This function can be expanded in integral powers of $t + d _ { 1 } , d _ { 2 } , . . . , d _ { m }$ which is convergent for sufficiently small values of

$$
| t + d _ { 1 } | , | d _ { 2 } | , . . . , | d _ { m } | .
$$

Thus $\eta _ { 1 } , \eta _ { 2 } , \ldots , \eta _ { m }$ can be expanded in integral power series of the same nature by (lOl) and are of the same form as (98),which is absolutely and uniformly convergent. Hence,(94） is absolutely and uniformly convergent so long as $| t + c _ { 1 } | , | c _ { 2 } | , \ldots , | c _ { m } |$ are sufficiently small.

Thus,we have obtained the solution of (93a) in the form (94) so that it is zero for $t + c _ { 1 } = c _ { 2 } = \cdot \cdot \cdot = c _ { m } = 0$ and

$$
{ \frac { \partial ( \xi _ { 1 } , \xi _ { 2 } , . . . , \xi _ { m } ) } { \partial ( c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , . . . , c _ { m } ) } } = \lambda .
$$

Hence, $t + c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , . . . , c _ { m }$ are convergent as integral power series of $\xi _ { 1 } , \xi _ { 2 } , \ldots , \xi _ { m }$ insofar as the latter variables are sufficiently small. Hence

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \lambda ( t + c _ { 1 } ) = \xi _ { 1 } - \frac { \xi _ { 1 } } { \lambda } \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } a _ { 1 } ^ { ( 1 ) } \xi _ { 1 } + a _ { 2 } ^ { ( 1 ) } \xi _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + a _ { m } ^ { ( 1 ) } \xi _ { m } \right) + \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } = \Phi _ { 1 } ( \xi _ { 1 } , \xi _ { 2 } , . . . , \xi _ { m } ) , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { c _ { j } = \xi _ { j } - \frac { \xi _ { 1 } } { \lambda } \left( \frac { 1 } { 2 } a _ { 1 } ^ { ( j ) } \xi _ { 1 } + a _ { 2 } ^ { ( j ) } \xi _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + a _ { m } ^ { ( j ) } \xi _ { m } \right) + \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } = \Phi _ { j } ( \xi _ { 1 } , \xi _ { 2 } , . . . , \xi _ { m } ) ( j = 2 , 3 , . . . , m ) . } } \end{array}
$$

Each term of the second and the higher degrees has $\xi _ { 1 }$ as a factor. Since $c _ { 1 } , c _ { 2 } , \ldots , c _ { m }$ are arbitrary constants, $\Phi _ { 2 } , . . . , \Phi _ { m }$ are $m - 1$ independent integrals. Thus, we have obtained $m - 1$ independent integrals of (93).

Next we consider a set of Hamiltonian canonical equations with $m = 2 n$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle \frac { d \xi _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial \eta _ { i } } , \qquad \frac { d \eta _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial F } { \partial \xi _ { i } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) , } \\ { F = F _ { 0 } + F _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot } \end{array}
$$

If we apply Poincaré's contact transformation

$$
\xi _ { i } = \sqrt { 2 x _ { i } } \cos y _ { i } , \qquad \eta _ { i } = \sqrt { 2 x _ { i } } \sin y _ { i } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , n ) ,
$$

then the differential equations are transformed to the same form as equations (73) in the theorem of Poincaré but without the parameter $\mu$

In order that $F _ { 0 }$ shall not contain variables other than $x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , \ldots , x _ { n }$ as in the form of Poincaré, $F _ { 0 }$ must be a power series of

$$
\xi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \eta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } , . . . , \xi _ { n } ^ { 2 } + \eta _ { n } ^ { 2 } ,
$$

and the derivatives of $F _ { 0 }$ must not be zero; furthermore, the coefficients $\dot { B } _ { m _ { 1 } \cdots m _ { n } }$ in the expansion of ${ \cal F } _ { 1 }$ must not have a small divisor $\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { n } m _ { i }$ $\partial F _ { 0 } / \partial x _ { i }$ .These conditions are satisfied by

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { F _ { 0 } = \lambda _ { 1 } ( \xi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \eta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \lambda _ { n } ( \xi _ { n } ^ { 2 } + \eta _ { n } ^ { 2 } ) } } \\ { { \mathrm { } + \nu _ { 1 } ( \xi _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \eta _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \nu _ { n } ( \xi _ { n } ^ { 2 } + \eta _ { n } ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } . } } \end{array}
$$

Indeed, if we take such an $F _ { 0 }$ ，then there exist $2 n - 2$ independent integrals as given by (l02） besides ${ \pmb F } =$ constant.Hence, there exist $2 n - 2$ independent integrals without the parameter $\mu$ for the equations of Poincaré.Thus the theorem of Poincaré fails when we consider integrals that do not contain the smal parameter $\mu$

Whittaker (l916,l937） considered the differential equations of form (103）for a dynamical system of two degrees of freedom,in which the Hamiltonian function $F$ is expanded in integral powers of $\sqrt { \xi _ { 1 } } , \sqrt { \xi _ { 2 } }$ and in a trigonometric series in $\eta _ { 1 } , \eta _ { 2 }$ of the form

$$
\sum A \xi _ { 1 } ^ { s } { } ^ { _ { 1 } / 2 } \xi _ { 2 } ^ { s } { } ^ { _ { 2 } / 2 } \cos { ( j _ { 1 } \eta _ { 1 } + j _ { 2 } \eta _ { 2 } ) } ,
$$

with the frst term $s _ { 1 } \xi _ { 1 } + s _ { 2 } \xi _ { 2 }$ where $\pmb { A }$ is a constant, $s _ { 1 } , s _ { 2 }$ are positive integers, and $j _ { 1 } , j _ { 2 }$ are positive or negative integers including zero, $s _ { 1 } + s _ { 2 }$ being the degree of the term. Thus $F$ is written

$$
F = s _ { 1 } \xi _ { 1 } + s _ { 2 } \xi _ { 2 } + F _ { 3 } + F _ { 4 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot .
$$

Whittaker discovered the so-called adelphic integral for this system of differential equations (discussed in Section 14.15).The integral takes three different forms according as: (i) $s _ { 1 } / s _ { 2 }$ is irrational； (ii), $s _ { 1 } / s _ { 2 }$ is rational and equals the ratio of two integers $m / n$ ,and there is no term of the form cos $( n \eta _ { 1 } - m \eta _ { 2 } )$ in $F _ { 3 }$ ； or (iii), $s _ { 1 } / s _ { 2 }$ equals the ratio of two integers $m / n$ but there is a term cos $( n \eta _ { 1 } - m \eta _ { 2 } )$

Fermi (l923,1923a） tried to relate the theorem of Poincaré to the quasi continuity of the trajectories and to base on it the foundation of the kinetic theory of gases of Boltzmann and Maxwell. Maxwell based his theory on the assumption of the so-called continuity of path of the trajectories, the assumption that a dynamical system passes through all phases permitted by the law of energy conservation in a sufficiently long interval of time (Jeans,l92l).The modern ergodic theorem has been refined from this assumption of quasi continuity of the dynamical trajectories (Hagihara, l933; discussed in Chapter 26).

Fermi, by adopting the above proof of the theorem of Poincaré as it stands， proved the validity of quasi ergodicity for a nondegenerate dynamical system of two degrees of freedom without perturbatin. A system is nondegenerate if: (i),from the relation

$$
{ \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 1 } } } \Big / { \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 2 } } } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t }
$$

the relation

$$
\frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 3 } } \Big / \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x _ { 4 } } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ,
$$

for example, does not follow； (i),if $\Sigma _ { i } m _ { i } ( \partial F _ { 0 } / \partial x _ { i } )$ does not vanish on any surface $F _ { 0 } =$ constant, where $m _ { i }$ are integers, not all of them zero; and (iii), the vanishing of the coefficient $B _ { m _ { 1 } \cdots m _ { n } }$ of Section 6.15 does not follow from $\Sigma _ { i } m _ { i } ( \partial F _ { 0 } / \partial x _ { i } ) = 0$

Born and Pauli (l922) expanded the perturbation in a quantummechanical system in the present form with the angular and linear variables of Section 2.7 as the canonically conjugate variables. They tried to express the perturbation in a multiple Fourier series of the angular variables by expanding it with a smal parameter. The perturbation in this case is the imposed electric feld and the magnetic field.Born and Brody (l921） based the discussion on a different form of expansion. Epstein (l922) used the expansion of Delaunay (Chapter 9). Since according to the theorem of Poincaré there exists no uniform integral, it is possible to give the quantum condition to a quantum-mechanical system with perturbations.Owing to the fact that such series expansions are not uniformly convergent (as will be shown in Chapter l5),the quantum-mechanical calculation presents a grave and fundamental difficulty. According to Born and Pauli the broadening of spectral lines of a perturbed system due to electromagnetic forces is negligible compared with the broadenings due to pressure and the Doppler effect. It is the conditionally periodic motion discussed in Chapter 2 that enables us to escape from this difficulty,but, as we have argued in Section 2.7, it is necessary to have a heavy and severe condition for that purpose (See Section 4.24;also Arnold,l963).Whether such a condition is actually satisfied in any quantum-mechanical system, whether we should adopt such a condition as an axiom in quantum mechanics, is a question for quantum mechanicians.Here appears the important role that should be played by the theory of adiabatic invariants discussed in Section 4.23. However,we have there assumed the quasi ergodicity of the dynamical system; so the question is raised again. On the other hand, a quantum-mechanical system without perturbation is degenerate in general; so the same difficulty appears. It was presumably for this reason that quantum mechanicians in the past assumed the general invalidity of classical dynamics in quantum theory.

Arnold (963) recently solved the problem by means of quasi-periodic functions, along with the difficulty of the degenerate cases, making the severe assumption that the series representing the functions are almost everywhere convergent-by excluding the domain of Lebesgue measure zero, as will be discussed in a later chapter.

In the light of modern developments in wave mechanics and quantum mechanics，especially the theory based on Bohr's complementarity principle, our mode of thinking has changed. However,even now it is evident that only a first approximation is treated in the computation of the perturbation; indeed, the method for the second approximation fails completely owing to the nonconvergence of the process. We are in quantum mechanics confronted with the same grave difficulty of nonconvergence that makes its first appearance in classical dynamics.

# KOWALEWSKIS TOP MOTION

# 6.20RIGID-BODY ROTATION

Despite the theorems of Poincaré and Bruns,Kowalewski (1888,1890) has found an algebraic integral other than the classical integrals in the motion of a rigid body. We shall discuss this case briefly.

The motion of a rigid body suspended at its center of mass has been completely solved by Euler.Denote the three moments of inertia around the center of mass by $A , B , C .$ The motion under gravity of a rigid body, such that $A = B$ ， suspended at a point on the principal axis of inerta corresponding to $C$ ，has been completely solved by Lagrange. The motion in the case $A = B = 2 C$ has also been solved completely by using elliptic functions and hyperelliptic functions,no matter where the center of mass is located.This is called the Kowalewski case. In such cases there exists an integral algebraic in the coordinates and the momentum, which is not an algebraic combination of the classical integrals (energy and angular-momentum).Poincaré has proved that there cannot exist any uniform integral,which is not an algebraic combination of the classical integrals,for a general initial condition,if we exclude the above cases of Euler, Lagrange,and Kowalewski. Several authors have discussed the case of $A = B = 2 C / n$ ， $\pmb { n }$ being an integer (Husson, 1905, 1906,1908,1932；Liouville,1897；Hesse，1890；Kotter，1892,1893； Staude,1894；Nekrassoff, 1896；Kolossoff, 1902,1903；Stäckel,1908; Oseen，1911；Marcolongo，1902，1906，1910；Levi-Civita，1901; Lazzarino,1919; see also Klein and Sommerfeld, i921; Halphen, 1888; Leimanis, 1965).

The quantum-mechanical analogues of these top motions have been studied by Casimir (l93l) in general, by Kramers and Ittmann (1929, 1930)and O.Klein (1929) for Euler's case, by Reiche and Rademacher (1926,1927) and Kronig and Rabi (1927) for Lagrange's case, and by Laporte (l933) for Kowalewski's case.

We shall first show, following Poincaré (l892),that there can exist a uniform integral in the case $A = B$

Suppose that the product of the distance between the point of suspension and the center of mass multiplied by the gravitational force is infinitesimally small and write the equations of motion of the rigid body in the form

$$
\frac { d x _ { i } } { d t } = \frac { \partial F } { \partial y _ { i } } , \frac { d y _ { i } } { d t } = - \frac { \partial F } { \partial x _ { i } } ( i = 1 , 2 , 3 ) ,
$$

$$
F = F _ { 0 } + \mu F _ { 1 } ,
$$

where $F$ is the total energy, $F _ { 0 }$ the kinetic energy, $\mu F _ { 1 }$ is the product of the distance from the center of mass to the horizontal plane passing through the point of suspension,multiplied by the gravitational force. $[ \mathsf { f } \ \mu = 0$ ,then the center of mass coincides with the point of suspension and the motion of the rigid body is that of Poinsot. Since $\mu$ is supposed to be small, the motion is the Poinsot motion,to the first approximation. In the Poinsot motion the ellipsoid of inertia makes a rolling motion by always touching a fixed plane, without slipping. Let $P$ be the foot of the perpendicular from the point of suspension to this fixed plane,and $Q$ be the point of contact at a certain epoch.This point of contact describes a fixed curve upon the ellipsoid of inertia. This is called the polhode. Suppose that after a lapse of time $\pmb { \tau }$ the same point $Q$ of the polhode comes to a point $Q ^ { \prime }$ at which the ellipsoid of inertia touches the fixed plane.Denote the angle $Q P Q ^ { \prime }$ by $\pmb { \alpha }$ and put $n = 2 \pi / \tau , n ^ { \prime } = \alpha / \tau ; n$ and $\scriptstyle { \pmb { n } } ^ { \prime }$ correspond to the mean motions in the Keplerian elliptic motion. Denote the coordinates of a point of the rigid body by $x , y , z$ ； let the point of suspension be the coordinate origin,and take the $_ z$ axis as the vertical. With integration constants $\epsilon , \epsilon ^ { \prime }$ ，write $l = n t + \epsilon , l ^ { \prime } = n ^ { \prime } t + \epsilon ^ { \prime }$ .Let $\xi _ { : }$ ， $\eta , \psi$ be functions of $n , n ^ { \prime }$ ， $\boldsymbol { l }$ and periodic with period $2 \pi$ in l. With the new integration constants $\theta , \phi$ ，

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { x = \cos { \theta } ( \xi \cos { l ^ { \prime } } - \eta \sin { l ^ { \prime } } ) - \sin { \theta } \cos { \phi } ( \xi \sin { l ^ { \prime } } + \eta \cos { l ^ { \prime } } ) } \\ & { \phantom { x x } + \psi \sin { \theta } \sin { \phi } , } \\ & { y = \sin { \theta } ( \xi \cos { l ^ { \prime } } - \eta \sin { l ^ { \prime } } ) + \cos { \theta } \cos { \phi } ( \xi \sin { l ^ { \prime } } + \eta \cos { l ^ { \prime } } ) } \\ & { \phantom { x x } - \psi \cos { \theta } \sin { \phi } , } \\ & { z = \sin { \phi } ( \xi \sin { l ^ { \prime } } + \eta \cos { l ^ { \prime } } ) + \psi \cos { \phi } . } \end{array}
$$

Let $( x , y , z )$ be the coordinates of the center of mass; then $\pmb { F } _ { 1 }$ is reduced to $_ z$ ,apart from a constant factor.

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle F _ { 1 } = \sum B _ { m , 1 } \exp { [ \sqrt { - 1 } ( m l + l ^ { \prime } ) ] } + \sum B _ { m , 0 } \exp { [ \sqrt { - 1 } m l ] } } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + \sum B _ { m , - 1 } \exp { [ \sqrt { - 1 } ( m l - l ^ { \prime } ) ] } , } } \end{array}
$$

where the coefficients $B$ depend on $n , n ^ { \prime } , \phi$ Refer to the general discussion of Section 6.15. Choose $n , n ^ { \prime }$ such that

$$
\dot { p } \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial x } + q \frac { \partial F _ { 0 } } { \partial y } = 0 ;
$$

then $B$ depends only on $\phi$ .Hence, there must be a relation between two of the coefficients. Since $D _ { \lambda }$ defined in Section 6.15 depends only on $\phi$ and $\zeta _ { : }$ ，there must be one relation among arbitrary $2 n - 3 = 3$ of these $D _ { \lambda }$ ，where we put ${ \cal D } _ { \lambda } = B _ { \lambda p , \lambda q } \zeta ^ { \lambda }$ .The series described in Section 6.15 are all singular of order 1; hence, there may exist a uniform integral distinct from the energy integral.Actually, there is the angular-momentum integral.In order to see whether there exists a third integral we look for the classes that are singular of order 2.In that case there must necessarily be two relations among an arbitrary three of $D _ { \lambda }$ ； indeed, the condition is also sufficient. Hence, $D _ { \lambda }$ should be a function of only one of such coefficients.We list several classes :

1. Class $1 / 0$ which contains all coefficients of the form $B _ { m , 0 }$ . This class is singular of order 2.For, we have $B _ { m , 0 } = C _ { m , 0 } \cos \phi _ { \mathrm { ~ ~ } }$ where $C _ { m , 0 }$ depends only on $n , n ^ { \prime }$ ,and,as $n , n ^ { \prime }$ are supposed to be constant, $C _ { m , 0 }$ is considered as a constant. Thus, $D _ { \lambda } = C _ { \lambda , 0 } ( \cos { \phi } ) \zeta ^ { \lambda }$ . In order that $D _ { \lambda }$ shall be a function of only one of the coefficients,all coefficients $C _ { \lambda , 0 }$ except one should vanish, or $\psi$ should be reduced to the form exp $( { \sqrt { - 1 } } m l )$ . For satisfying $\pmb { \dot { p } } \partial F _ { 0 } / \partial x + q \partial F _ { 0 } / \partial y = 0$ ，however，we must equate $\pmb { n }$ to zero. The Poinsot motion for $n = 0$ is a uniform rotation about one of the principal axes of inertia, and $\psi$ is a constant independent of $\mathbf { \xi } _  l . $ ，Hence，the values of $C _ { \lambda , 0 }$ other than $C _ { \boldsymbol { \circ } , \boldsymbol { \circ } }$ are all zero for such special values of $n , n ^ { \prime }$ Thus this class is singular of order 2.

2. Classes of the form $m / 1$ contain only three coefficients: $B _ { m , 1 } , B _ { 0 , 0 } ,$ $B _ { - m , - 1 }$ . These classes are singular oforder 2 only if $B _ { m , 1 } = B _ { - m , - 1 } = 0$ ； in other words, there should be no terms exp $( { \sqrt { - 1 } } m l )$ in the expansion of $\xi + \sqrt { - 1 } \eta , \xi - \sqrt { - 1 } \eta$ in positive and negative powers of

$$
\exp { ( \sqrt { - 1 } \it { l } ) } ,
$$

as $\xi , \eta$ are supposed to be real. Generally, this does not happen unless the ellpsoid of inertia is an ellipsoid of revolution. When two axes of the ellipsoid are equal, then

$$
\pounds = A ^ { \prime } \cos l + B ^ { \prime } \sin l + C ^ { \prime } , \qquad \eta = A ^ { \prime \prime } \cos l + B ^ { \prime \prime } \sin l + C ^ { \prime \prime } ,
$$

where $A ^ { \prime } , B ^ { \prime } , C ^ { \prime } , A ^ { \prime \prime } , B ^ { \prime \prime } , C ^ { \prime \prime }$ are constants. Hence, unless $m = 0 , 1 \mathrm { o r } - 1$ ， we have $B _ { m , 1 } = - B _ { - m , - 1 } = 0$ Hence the classes $m / 1$ , except $1 / 1 , 0 / 1$ ， $- 1 / 1$ , are singular of order 2.

3. The other classes, reducing to only one coefficient $B _ { \circ , \circ }$ , are singular of order 2.

Summarizing,we have seen that if the ellipsoid of inertia is an ellipsoid of revolution then the singularity is of order 2,except for the classes $1 / 1 , 0 / 1 , - 1 / 1$ Hence,there may exist a third uniform integral; indeed, there may exist an algebraic integral, provided that the Jacobian of these three integrals is zero for $n ^ { \prime } = 0$ or $n ^ { \prime } = \pm n$ . In the case of Lagrange, in which the point of suspension is on the $C$ axis of the ellipsoid of inertia, the last condition is not necessry since $\xi , \eta$ are then reduced to constant. If the ellipsoid of inertia is not an ellipsoid of revolution, then there are an infinite number of classes that are not singular of order 2, that is,the classes $m / 1$ . Considera domain $( D )$ which contains an infinite number of sets of values of $n , n ^ { \prime } , \phi ,$ $\theta$ and suppose that in any such set the value of $\scriptstyle { \pmb { n } } ^ { \prime }$ is not an integral multiple of $_ { n }$ .None of the classes $m / 1$ belong to this domain. In this case there may exist a third uniform integral, provided that the Jacobian of these three integrals vanishes when $n ^ { \prime }$ is an integral multiple of $_ { n }$ .Hence we can conclude that the third integral cannot be algebraic in general.

# 6.2ISOLUTION IN ALGEBRAIC INTEGRALS

Husson (1906,1908) has proved,independently of Poincaré,on the basis of Abel's theory of algebraic functions, that any algebraic integral with arbitrary initial conditions is an algebraic combination of the classical integrals (except in the cases of Euler, Lagrange, and Kowalewski)，and that consequently there cannot exist any algebraic integral other than the classical integrals in the general case of top motions (except in the aforesaid cases). Liouville's proof (l897) of his theorem on a basis similar to the proof by Bruns was incomplete. Painlevé (1900) criticized Kowalewski's proof.

Let the origin $o$ be the point of suspension；take coordinates in the directions,fixed in the rigid body,of the principal axes of inertia with regard to the point of suspension. Let the center of mass be at a point $G$ ； let the unit of length be such that the numerical ratio of the external force mg on the body to the distance $o G$ equals l. Let the direction cosines of the direction of gravity in these coordinates be $\gamma , \gamma ^ { \prime } , \gamma ^ { \prime \prime }$ Then the equations of motion of the rigid body around $O$ are

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { A { \frac { d { \underline { { \phi } } } } { d t } } = ( B - C ) q r + y _ { 0 } \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } - z _ { 0 } \gamma ^ { \prime } , \quad } } & { { { \frac { d \gamma } { d t } } = r \gamma ^ { \prime } - q \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } , } } \\ { { \ } } & { { \ } } \\ { { B { \frac { d q } { d t } } = ( C - A ) r { \underline { { \hat { p } } } } + z _ { 0 } \gamma - x _ { 0 } \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } , \quad } } & { { \frac { d \gamma ^ { \prime } } { d t } } = { \hat { p } } \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } - r \gamma , }  \\ { { \ } } & { { \ } } \\ { { C { \frac { d r } { d t } } = ( A - B ) { \hat { p } } q + x _ { 0 } \gamma ^ { \prime } - y _ { 0 } \gamma , \quad } } & { { \frac { d \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } } { d t } } = q \gamma - { \hat { p } } \gamma ^ { \prime } . }  \end{array}
$$

In order to separate the variables in the manner of Sections 1.15 and 1.16, transform by

$$
\begin{array} { l l } { { y _ { 1 } = a \phi + \sqrt { - 1 } \ b q , \quad } } & { { y _ { 2 } = a \phi - \sqrt { - 1 } \ b q , } } \\ { { \nonumber } } \\ { { z _ { 1 } = \gamma + \sqrt { - 1 } \ \gamma ^ { \prime } , \quad } } & { { z _ { 2 } = \gamma - \sqrt { - 1 } \ \gamma ^ { \prime } , } } \end{array}
$$

with

$$
a = \sqrt { A ( A - C ) } , \qquad b = \sqrt { B ( B - C ) } ,
$$

and put $- { \sqrt { - 1 } } \ t$ in place of $t .$ Then

$$
{ \frac { y _ { 1 } } { l t } } = - { \sqrt { \frac { ( A - C ) ( B - C ) } { A B } } } r y _ { 1 } - \left[ x _ { 0 } { \sqrt { \frac { B - C } { B } } } + { \sqrt { - 1 } } y _ { 0 } { \sqrt { \frac { A - C } { A } } } \right]
$$

$$
+ \frac { z _ { 0 } } { 2 } \left[ \sqrt { \frac { B - C } { B } } \left( z _ { 1 } + z _ { 2 } \right) + \sqrt { \frac { A - C } { A } } \left( z _ { 1 } - z _ { 2 } \right) \right] ,
$$

$$
\frac { d y _ { 2 } } { d t } = \sqrt { \frac { ( A - C ) ( B - C ) } { A B } } r y _ { 2 } + \bigg [ x _ { 0 } \sqrt { \frac { B - C } { B } } - \sqrt { - 1 } y _ { 0 } \sqrt { \frac { A - C } { A } } \bigg ]
$$

$$
+ \frac { z _ { 0 } } { 2 } \left[ \sqrt { \frac { B - C } { B } } \left( z _ { 1 } + z _ { 2 } \right) - \sqrt { \frac { A - C } { A } } \left( z _ { 1 } - z _ { 2 } \right) \right] ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \displaystyle C \frac { d r } { d t } = \frac { A - B } { 4 \sqrt { A B ( A - C ) ( B - C ) } } \left( y _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } - y _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \right) - \frac { x _ { 0 } } { 2 } \left( z _ { 1 } - z _ { 2 } \right) } } \\ { { \displaystyle ~ + \frac { \sqrt { - 1 } y _ { 0 } } { 2 } \left( z _ { 1 } + z _ { 2 } \right) } , } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { d z _ { 1 } } { d t } = - r z _ { 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \frac { y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } } { \sqrt { A ( A - C ) } } + \frac { y _ { 1 } - y _ { 2 } } { \sqrt { B ( B - C ) } } \right] \gamma ^ { \prime } , } \\ & { \frac { d z _ { 2 } } { d t } = r z _ { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \frac { y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } } { \sqrt { A ( A - C ) } } - \frac { y _ { 1 } - y _ { 2 } } { \sqrt { B ( B - C ) } } \right] \gamma ^ { \prime } , } \\ & { \frac { d \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } } { d t } = \frac { 1 } { 4 \sqrt { A ( A - C ) } } \left( y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } \right) ( z _ { 1 } - z _ { 2 } ) } \\ & { \qquad - \frac { 1 } { 4 \sqrt { B ( B - C ) } } \left( y _ { 1 } - y _ { 2 } \right) ( z _ { 1 } + z _ { 2 } ) . } \end{array}
$$

There exist tree integrals-the energy integral,theangular-momentum integral, and the relation among the direction cosines:

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \frac { ( y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } { 4 ( A - C ) } - \frac { ( y _ { 1 } - y _ { 2 } ) ^ { 2 } } { 4 ( B - C ) } + C r ^ { 2 } } } \\ { { - \left[ x _ { 0 } ( z _ { 1 } + z _ { 2 } ) - \sqrt { - 1 } y _ { 0 } ( z _ { 1 } - z _ { 2 } ) + 2 z _ { 0 } \gamma ^ { \prime } \right] = \mathrm { c o n s i } } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { { \displaystyle \frac { \cal A } { 4 \sqrt { { \cal A } ( A - C ) } } ~ ( y _ { 1 } + y _ { 2 } ) ( z _ { 1 } + z _ { 2 } ) ~ - \displaystyle \frac { \cal B } { 2 \sqrt { { \cal B } ( B - C ) } } ~ ( y _ { 1 } - y _ { 2 } ) ( z _ { 1 } - z _ { 1 } ) } } } \\ { { { { } } } } \\ { { { { } } } } \\ { { { z _ { 1 } z _ { 2 } + \displaystyle { \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } } ^ { 2 } = \mathrm { c o n s t a n t . } } } } \end{array}
$$

Multiply $y _ { 1 } , z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 } , \gamma ^ { \prime \prime }$ by a constant parameter $\lambda$ Denote the integration constants on the right-hand sides ofthe three equations (lO6)after this substitution by $h _ { 1 } , h _ { 2 } , h _ { 3 }$ ,respectively; these are supposed to be independent of $\lambda ,$ ，Denote the resulting equations - after this substitution in (105）and (106)-by (l05a) and (106a),respectively.Since the righthand members of (lO6a) are regular for $\lambda = 0$ ，there exists the solution of (106a) arranged in positive integral power series of $\lambda$ insofar as $\lambda$ is sufficiently small,according to Poincaré's extension of Cauchy's existence theorem of the solution of a differential equation (discussed in a later chapter).We eliminate $t$ from these equations and try to obtain $y _ { 1 } , y _ { 2 } ,$ $z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 } , \gamma ^ { \prime \prime }$ as functions of $r .$ . In the case treated by Lagrange,we obtain $r =$ constant, because $x _ { 0 } = y _ { 0 } = 0$ , so we disregard this case.Equations (106a) give $y _ { 2 } , z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 }$ as functions of $y _ { 1 } , \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } , r ,$ and (l05a) are replaced by two differential equations of order l for determining $y _ { 1 } ( r ) , \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } ( r )$ . The derivatives $d y _ { 1 } / d r$ and $d \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } / d r$ are algebraic with regard to $h _ { 1 } , h _ { 2 } , h _ { 3 } , \lambda , y _ { 1 } ;$ $\gamma ^ { \prime \prime } , r ,$ and regular where $\lambda = 0$

If there exists an algebraic integral $f ( y _ { 1 } , z _ { 1 } , z _ { 2 } , \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } , y _ { 2 } , r ) =$ constant, which does not depend on $t$ explicitly other than the classical integrals for the equations (lO5), then there exists an algebraic integral

$$

$$

for equations (l05a) for any value of $\lambda$ Substitute for $y _ { 2 }$ ， $z _ { 1 } , \ z _ { 2 }$ from (106a)； then we obtain two differential equations of the first order for $y _ { 1 } ( r ) , \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } ( r )$ in the form $F ( h _ { 1 } , h _ { 2 } , h _ { 3 } , \lambda , y _ { 1 } , \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } , r ) =$ constant, where $F$ is algebraic in each of the arguments. Here $\boldsymbol { F }$ must contain $y _ { 1 }$ and $\gamma ^ { \prime \prime }$ Otherwise $_ r$ would become a constant； then,either the case would reduce to Lagrange's, or $F$ would not contain $y _ { 1 } , \gamma ^ { \prime \prime }$ ， $r$ and the integral $f =$ constant would be one of the combinations of the classical integrals.

If we multiply ${ \pmb F }$ by a power of $\lambda$ such that $\lambda = 0$ is neither a zero point nor a pole of $\boldsymbol { F }$ considered as a function of $\lambda$ ,then thealgebraic function $F$ of $\lambda$ can be expanded in positive integral ascending powers of $\lambda$ or $\lambda ^ { 1 / p }$ in the neighborhood of $\lambda = 0$ .The coefficients of various powers of $\lambda$ in this expansion are algebraic in $h _ { 1 } , h _ { 2 } , h _ { 3 } , y _ { 1 } , \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } ,$ r. The first equation of (1l2） expresses $\pmb { y _ { 2 } }$ as a function of $r _ { : }$ and the second and the third equations express $z _ { 1 } , \ z _ { 2 }$ as irrational functions of $\gamma ^ { \prime \prime }$ .To avoid this inconvenience, we introduce in place of $\gamma ^ { \prime \prime }$ a new variable $v$ such that the differential equations become rational:

where $a _ { 1 } , \ a _ { 2 } , \ a _ { 3 } , \ a _ { 4 }$ are nonzero constants. It is assumed that none of $A - B , B - C , C - A$ is zero.

Thus $v ( r ) , y ( r )$ are expressed algebraically in terms of $y _ { 1 } , \gamma ^ { \prime \prime }$ ,r.Hence, in order that there may exist a new algebraic integral, the expressions $v ( r ) , y ( r ) , r$ should be related by algebraic equations. After integrating (116)we obtain

with integration constant $k$ . Therefore, the two expresions

$$
w = \left( \frac { r - a } { r + a } \right) ^ { 1 / ( 2 \alpha _ { 0 } ) } , \qquad z = \int { \left( a _ { 3 } \alpha _ { 3 } + a _ { 4 } z _ { 0 } \frac { r } { \sqrt { r ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } } \right) \left( w + \frac { h ^ { \prime } } { w } \right) d r } ,

and $\boldsymbol { r }$ are related by algebraic equations, where $h ^ { \prime }$ is an arbitrary number. We shall prove that this condition is satisfied only if the center of gravity coincides with the point of suspension (Euler's case).

$A , B , C$ are all different. (104) is symmetric with respect to $\ p , \ q , \ r .$ Hence,we may suppose that the value of $C$ lies between those of $\pmb { A }$ and $\boldsymbol { B }$ Then $\scriptstyle \alpha _ { 0 }$ is imaginary and $w ( r )$ is never algebraic, so that it should be $z ( r )$ that is related to $_ w$ and $r$ through an algebraic equation. Moreover, this equation should be valid for all values of $h ^ { \prime }$ and ${ \pmb w }$ does not contain $h ^ { \prime }$ .Hence,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { J _ { 1 } = \displaystyle \int \left( a _ { 3 } \alpha _ { 3 } + a _ { 4 } z _ { 0 } \frac { r } { \sqrt { r ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } } \right) w ~ d r , } } \\ { { \displaystyle J _ { 2 } = \displaystyle \int \left( a _ { 3 } \alpha _ { 3 } + a _ { 4 } z _ { 0 } \frac { r } { \sqrt { r ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } } \right) \frac { d r } { w } } } \end{array}
$$

should be expressed algebraically in terms of $\boldsymbol { r }$ and ${ \pmb w }$

When the representative point $\boldsymbol { r }$ describes a small closed contour around $r = a$ on the plane for the values of $\boldsymbol { r }$ ,the value of $_ { w }$ is multiplied by a constant. Hence,

$$

$$

should be expressed algebraically in terms of $\boldsymbol { r }$ and ${ \pmb w }$

# 6.22ABEL's LEMMAS

In order to discuss the integrals of (l17) we refer to Abel's three lemmas (Husson, 1906,1908).

Abel's First Lemma: Let $P ( x )$ be a rational algebraic fraction,and $Q ( x )$ be a polynomial or a rational fraction in $_ x$ - that is, the quotient of two polynomials or two rational polynomials in $_ x$ 一 such that $Q ( x )$ has as simple poles the zero points or the poles of $P ( x )$ . Form

$$
J ( x ) = \int { \frac { Q } { y } } d x
$$

with

$$
y ( x ) = [ P ( x ) ] ^ { \alpha } ,
$$

where $\propto$ is a real irrational number or an imaginary number. If $\pmb { J } ( \pmb { x } )$ is expressed algebraically in terms of $y$ and $\pmb { x } _ { ; }$ ，then the function can be represented by

$$
J ( x ) = \frac { f ( x ) } { y ( x ) } + \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } ,
$$

where $f ( x )$ is either a polynomial of $_ x$ of degree one higher than $Q ( x )$ or a constant.

This lemma follows from the properties of algebraic functions described in Section 2.20.The integral $J ( x )$ is uniform for finite values of $\pmb { x }$ except the zero points and the poles of $P ( { \pmb x } )$ . Let $x = a$ be a zero point of $P$ of order $_ m$ ; then

$$
y ( x ) = ( x - a ) ^ { m \alpha } y _ { 1 } ( x ) ,
$$

where $y _ { 1 } ( x )$ is uniform at $x = a$ and $1 / y _ { 1 } ( x )$ is also uniform at $x = a$ Then we can write, with a constant $E \leq 1$ ，

$$
{ \begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle { J ( x ) = \int { \frac { Q ( x ) } { ( x - a ) ^ { m \alpha } } } { \frac { d x } { y _ { 1 } } } } } \\ { \displaystyle { = \int { \frac { 1 } { ( x - a ) ^ { m \alpha + E } } } \left[ b _ { 0 } + b _ { 1 } ( x - a ) + b _ { 2 } ( x - a ) ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot \right] d x . } } \end{array} }
$$

The result of integration gives

$$
J ( x ) = \frac { 1 } { ( x - a ) ^ { m \alpha + E - 1 } } \left[ b _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } + b _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ( x - a ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot \right] + C ,
$$

with fixed constants $b _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } , b _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots$ and an arbitrary constant $C$ Hence,in the neighborhood of $x = a$ ，

$$
y ( x ) J ( x ) = \phi ( x ) + C [ P ( x ) ] ^ { \alpha } ,
$$

where $\phi ( x )$ is holomorphic.If $J ( x )$ is algebraic in terms of $_ x$ and $y$ ,then $J ( x ) - C$ is also algebraic, so that we can put $C = 0$ Let

$$
y ( x ) J ( x ) = f ( x , y ) ;
$$

then, $f ( x , y ) = \phi ( x )$ in the neighborhood of $x = a$ As $_ x$ makes a circuit from a point $\boldsymbol { x } = \boldsymbol { x } _ { 0 }$ near $x = a$ by describing the whole circumference of a circle with $x = a$ as center in the $( x , y )$ -plane,the function $\phi ( x )$ again takes the initial value, but $y ( x )$ takes an infinite number of values in geometrical progression.If the algebraic function $f ( x _ { 0 } , y )$ depends on $y$ ， then it should take the same value only for a finite number of values of $y$ ,according to Section 2.20.Hence, for $f ( x , y ) = \phi ( x )$ to be possible, $f$ should not depend on y. Thus, $y ( x ) J ( x ) = f ( x )$ ,where $f ( x )$ is an algebraic function of $_ { x }$ . The function $f ( x )$ ceases to be holomorphic only when $y ( x )$ or $\pmb { J } ( \pmb { x } )$ ceases to be holomorphic, that is, at the zero points or the poles of $\pmb { P }$ , except in the case where $x = a$ is a zero point.

If $x = b$ is a zero point of $P ( x )$ other than $x = a$ ,then a similar proof can be carried out. If $x = a$ is not a zero point,but is a pole, then the proof can be obtained simply by changing minto $- { m }$ .Hence thealgebraic function $f ( x )$ is holomorphic for any finite value of $_ x$

The behavior at $x = \infty$ can be studied,as in Section 2.20, by the substitution $x = 1 / z$ If $P$ and $Q$ are polynomials of degree $_ n$ and $\pmb { n } _ { 1 }$ ， respectively, then,

$$
y = z ^ { - n \alpha } y _ { 2 } ( z ) , ~ Q = z ^ { - n _ { 1 } } y _ { 3 } ( z ) ,
$$

where $y _ { 2 } ( z )$ and $y _ { 3 } ( z )$ are holomorphic and nonzero at $z = 0$ .Hence

$$
J ( x ) \mathop { = } \ z ^ { n \alpha - n _ { 1 } - 1 } ( b _ { 0 } ^ { \prime } + b _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } z + \cdot \cdot \cdot ) + C ^ { \prime } ,
$$

and accordingly

$$
f ( x ) = y ( x ) J ( x ) = z ^ { - n _ { 1 } - 1 } \psi ( z ) + C ^ { \prime } z ^ { - n \alpha } y _ { 2 } ( x ) ,
$$

where $\psi ( z )$ is holomorphic and nonzero for $z = 0$ 、Since the function $z ^ { n \alpha }$ has $z = 0$ as a transcendental singularity, $C ^ { \prime }$ must be zero. If $n _ { 1 } > - 1$ ，then the point at infinity is a pole of order $n _ { 1 } + 1$ of $f ( x )$ Since $f ( x )$ is holomorphic for finite values of $_ { x }$ ，thisalgebraic function is a polynomial in $_ x$ of degree $n _ { 1 } + 1$ .If $n _ { 1 } < - 1$ ，then the point at infinity is a zero point of $f ( x )$ ，which reduces to zero identically,and $J ( x )$ cannot be expressed algebraically in terms of $y$ and $_ x$

Abel's Second Lemma: If there exists an algebraic relation among Abel's transcendental functions $\pmb { J } ( \pmb { x } )$ and $J ^ { \prime } ( x )$ and the variable $\pmb { x } _ { i }$ ,then the relation is of the form $J ^ { \prime } = k J + \phi ( x )$ ，where $k$ is a constant and $\phi ( x )$ is an algebraic function.

Suppose that the integrals $\boldsymbol { J }$ and $J ^ { \prime }$ are related by an algebraic equation $f ( J , J ^ { \prime } ) = 0$ as in Section 2.20. Solving this equation for $J ^ { \prime }$ ， we obtain $J ^ { \prime } = \psi ( J , x )$ . Since $\boldsymbol { J }$ is not an algebraic expression， the integral $\boldsymbol { J }$ increases by a period $\pmb { \omega }$ as $_ x$ returns to its initial value, by describing the suitably chosen closed curve $C$ on the Riemann surface of $_ { x }$ ,and $J ^ { \prime }$ increases bya period $\omega _ { 1 }$ at the same time, where

$$
\omega _ { 1 } = \psi ( J + \omega , x ) - \psi ( J , x ) .
$$

But this period $\pmb { \omega } _ { 1 }$ does not contain $\boldsymbol { J }$ if $\boldsymbol { J }$ and $_ x$ are regarded as the independent variables， because $\boldsymbol { J }$ could be solved as an algebraic expression of $_ x$ if $\omega _ { 1 }$ contained $J .$ Hence

$$
\frac { \partial \psi ( J + \omega , x ) } { \partial J } - \frac { \partial \psi ( J , x ) } { \partial J } \equiv 0 ,
$$

and ${ \partial \psi ( J , x ) } / { \partial J }$ has the period $\omega$ . Consequently we can write

$$
J ^ { \prime } = \psi ( J , x ) = J \alpha ( x ) + \beta ( x ) ,
$$

where $\pmb { \alpha }$ and $\beta$ are algebraic expressions. Thus,

$$
\frac { d J ^ { \prime } } { d x } = J \frac { d \alpha } { d x } + \alpha \frac { d J } { d x } + \frac { d \beta } { d x } ,
$$

in which $\boldsymbol { J }$ is transcendental and the functions other than $\boldsymbol { J }$ are algebraic. Accordingly $d \alpha / d x \equiv 0$ ，that is, $\alpha = k =$ constant.Similarly,from $d \beta / d x = d J ^ { \prime } / d x - \alpha ( d J / d x )$ ，we see that $d \beta / d x$ is rational in $_ { x }$ and so is $\beta ( x )$

Corollary : If one of the two integrals $\boldsymbol { J }$ and $J ^ { \prime }$ ，

$$
J = \int \frac { y ~ d x } { x ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } , \qquad J ^ { \prime } = \int \frac { d x } { y ( x ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } ) } , \qquad y = ( x ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } ) ^ { p / m } ,
$$

is not algebraic, where $\beta , m$ are relatively prime positive integers - that is,if $2 \hbar / m$ is not an integer - then it is impossible to have an algebraic relation among $J , J ^ { \prime }$ ， $\pmb { x } _ { : }$ unless $2 \phi / m$ is an integer,that is,one of the integrals $\boldsymbol { J }$ or $J ^ { \prime }$ is algebraic.

In fact, if $J$ and $J ^ { \prime }$ are transcendental functions, then

$$
J ^ { \prime } = k J + \phi ( x , y )
$$

by the second Lemma. As $_ x$ describes a closed contour $C$

$$
\omega _ { 1 } = \int _ { C } { \frac { d J } { d x } } d x = k \int _ { C } { \frac { d J } { d a } } d x = k \omega .
$$

As $_ x$ makes a circuit around $x = a$ in the region of the point $x = a$ ,the variable $_ x$ returns to its initial value, but $y$ is multiplied by

$$
\exp \Big ( 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \frac { \not p } { m } \Big ) ,
$$

$$
\textnormal { d } \frac { d J ^ { \prime } } { d x } \textnormal { b y } \exp \Big ( { - 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } } \frac { \beta } { m } \Big ) , \mathrm { a n d } \frac { d J } { d x } \mathrm { b y } \exp \Big ( { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } } \frac { \beta } { m } \Big ) .
$$

relation between the periods is

$$
\omega _ { 1 } \exp \left( - 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \frac { \rlap / p } { m } \right) = k \omega \exp \left( 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \frac { \rlap / p } { m } \right) .
$$

Thus we see that $2 \pmb { \mathscr { p } } / m$ should be equal to an integer $_ n$

Abel’s Third Lemma: Consider the integral $K ( x ) = \int J ( x ) \rho ( x , y ) \ d$ x with a rational function $\rho$ of $_ x$ and $y$ ,and an Abel’s transcendental function $\pmb { J } ( \pmb { x } )$ ，where $y$ is defined by $f ( x , y ) = 0$ as an implicit function of $_ x$ If $J , \ K ,$ $_ x$ are related by an algebraic equation， then Abel's integral $\int \rho ( x , y )$ dy is expressed algebraically in terms of $J$ and $_ { x }$

In fact, the algebraic relation among $J , K$ $_ x$ must contain $\kappa$ Ifit did not contain $\kappa$ then $J$ would be algebraic. Hence we obtain an equation $K = \phi ( J , x )$ that is algebraic in $\boldsymbol { J }$ and $_ x$ ，Suppose that,as $_ x$ describes a closed curve $C _ { i }$ ，the integral $\boldsymbol { J }$ increases by $\omega$ and $K ( \pmb { x } )$ becomes $K _ { 1 } ( x )$

Then

$$
K _ { 1 } ( x ) - K ( x ) = \phi ( J + \omega , x ) - \phi ( J , x ) = \psi ( J , x ) .
$$

But

$$
\frac { d K _ { 1 } } { d x } - \frac { d K } { d x } = ( J + \omega ) \rho - J \rho = \omega \rho ( x , y ) .
$$

Hence

$$
\psi ( J , x ) = \omega \int \rho ( x , y ) d x .
$$

Since $\psi$ is algebraic both in $J$ and $\pmb { x }$ ，the Abelian integral $\int \rho ( x , y ) d x$ can be expressed algebraically in terms of $J$ and $_ x$

On the basis of Abel's lemmas,we seek the condition that integrals $J _ { 3 } , J _ { 4 } , J _ { 5 } , J _ { 6 }$ should be expressed algebraically by $r$ and w. If $\alpha _ { 3 } \neq 0$ ， then, according to the first lemma,

$$
\int \frac { d r } { w } = \frac { b r + c } { w } + \mathrm { c o n s t a n t }
$$

with two constants $b$ and $c .$ .Differentiating this expression with respect to $\pmb { r }$ would give an impossible relation

$$
\frac { w ^ { \prime } } { w } = \frac { a } { \alpha _ { 0 } } \frac { 1 } { r ^ { 2 } - a ^ { 2 } } = \frac { b - 1 } { b r + c } .
$$

Hence

$$
\alpha _ { 3 } = x _ { 0 } \sqrt { \frac { B - C } { B } } + \sqrt { - 1 } y _ { 0 } \sqrt { \frac { A - C } { A } } = 0 .
$$

Exchanging $y _ { 1 }$ and $y _ { 2 }$ we have

$$
x _ { 0 } \sqrt { \frac { B - C } { B } } - \sqrt { - 1 } y _ { 0 } \sqrt { \frac { A - C } { A } } = 0 .
$$

Either $B = C , A = C ;$ or $x _ { 0 } = y _ { 0 } = 0$ (Lagrange's or Euler's case).

Put

$$
w _ { 1 } = \left( \frac { r - a } { r + a } \right) ^ { 1 / ( 2 \alpha _ { 0 } ) - 1 / 2 }
$$

in $J _ { 6 }$ ; then,according to the first lemma,

$$
\int { \frac { 1 } { w _ { 1 } } } { \frac { r d r } { r - a } } = { \frac { b r + c } { w _ { 1 } } } + \mathrm { c o n s t a n t }
$$

for $z _ { 0 } \neq 0$ . Differentiate this with respect to $r$ ; then

$$
( r + a ) [ ( 1 - b ) r + a b ] = a \Big ( 1 - \frac { 1 } { \alpha _ { 0 } } \Big ) ( b r + c ) ,
$$

that is, $b = 1$ ， $c = a$ and $a = a ( 1 - 1 / \alpha _ { 0 } )$ .But this last equation is valid only when $A = C$ or $B = C$ (Lagrange's case).

Hence,in order that there may exist an algebraic integral other than the classical integrals, in the case when $A , B$ ， $C$ are all different, $x _ { 0 } , y _ { 0 } ,$ and $z _ { 0 }$ must all equal zero; that is, the center of mass coincides with the point of suspension. This is Euler's case.Thus the theorem is proved.

Suppose that this relation contains $J _ { 1 }$ . In Abel’s first lemma the function $f$ should be of the form $f = c x + d$ for $n _ { 1 } = 0$ ，that is,for the particular case $Q = 1$ . Differentiating $y ( x ) J ( x ) = f ( x )$ ,with this $f ,$ gives

Let $C / A$ be an irreducible fraction $C / A = p / m$ . Then $\boldsymbol { J } _ { 1 }$ should be expressed algebraically in terms of $r$ and $y _ { 2 }$ ，or in terms of $r$ and $\boldsymbol { J }$ By the corollary to Abel's second lemma this is possible only when $2 C / A$ is an integer, regardless of whether $\boldsymbol { J }$ is algebraic or transcendental.If $C / A$ is an integer, then the transcendental function $\boldsymbol { J } _ { 1 }$ cannot be expressed as an algebraic function of $r$ and of an algebraic function $J .$ If $2 C / A$ is an odd integer, then $J _ { 1 }$ is an algebraic function.Hence $F$ contains $\gamma ^ { \prime \prime }$ only when $2 C / A$ is an odd integer.

Put $2 C / A = 2 n - 1$ and find the condition for which $2 J _ { 2 }$ is expressed algebraically in terms of $_ r$ and $y _ { 2 }$ ,or in terms of $r$ and $J .$ First suppose that $\epsilon \neq 0$ Since $J$ is transcendental,

by Abel's third lemma.

Let $\phi , \phi _ { 1 }$ be algebraic functions and $\alpha , \alpha _ { 1 }$ be nonzero constants; then

Hence,in neither of these cases can $F$ contain $\gamma ^ { \prime \prime }$ Thus $y _ { 2 } ( r )$ is an algebraic expresson.By (l21),the function ${ \pmb y } _ { 2 }$ consists of a term containing the arbitrary constant and a term containing the integral. Thus $( r ^ { 2 } \mathrm { ~ - ~ } a ^ { 2 } ) ^ { 1 - ( C / A ) }$ and $( 2 C / A ) J _ { 4 }$ tan $\epsilon$ should be algebraic expressions. To begin with, $C / A$ should be rational. $\mathrm { I f } \ \epsilon = 0$ , then the second expression vanishes and it is obviously algebraic.For $\epsilon \neq 0$ the integral $\boldsymbol { J } _ { \boldsymbol { 4 } }$ should be algebraic, and $C / A$ rational. Hence $J _ { \mathbf { 4 } }$ is the integral $J$ in the second lemma, and $C / A$ should be an integer. Thus, in order that there may exist other algebraic integrals besides the classical integrals, either $C / A$ should be rational and $\epsilon = 0$ ,or $C / A$ should be an integer.

Husson (1906,1908) further examined the next term in the expansion in powers of $\lambda$ and showed that there exists no algebraic integral, by excluding the case of Kowalewski $2 C / A = 1$ .Thus it is proved that there can be no algebraic integral other than the classical integrals with arbitrary initial conditions, except for the cases of Euler,Lagrange, and Kowalewski.

Burgatti (l906,19l0) has proved the above theorem of Goriatchoff for the nonexistence of algebraic integrals,unless transcendental, other than the classical ones,by a more elementary method than Husson's.

Kowalewski (l888；Kotter, l893） obtained the explicit form of the solution in her case by putting $z _ { 0 } = 0$ ; that is, $\epsilon = 0$ , and, in particular, $y _ { 0 } = 0$ ， $C = 1$ . Then $A = 2$ ,and (l04) are of the form

$$

$$

$$
\frac { d r } { d t } = \gamma ^ { \prime } , \frac { d \gamma ^ { \prime \prime } } { d t } = q \gamma - \rlap / p \gamma ^ { \prime } .

The five roots of $W ( s ) = 0$ are all different. The inversion of these integrals gives hyperelliptic functions (Section 6.25).

Suppose that $\begin{array} { r } { e _ { 3 } < k _ { 2 } < e _ { 2 } < e _ { 1 } < k _ { 1 } , } \end{array}$ and define

d u _ { 1 } = \frac { d y _ { 1 } } { \sqrt { R ( y _ { 1 } ) } } , \qquad d u _ { 2 } = \frac { d y _ { 2 } } { \sqrt { R ( y _ { 2 } ) } } ;

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \wp ^ { \prime 2 } ( w ) \ : = \ : - \ : 4 l _ { 1 } ^ { 3 } + g _ { 2 } l _ { 1 } - g _ { 3 } = - l _ { 0 } ^ { 2 } , } \\ { \qquad \quad s _ { 1 } = \wp ( u _ { 1 } + u _ { 2 } ) , \qquad s _ { 2 } = \wp ( u _ { 1 } - u _ { 2 } ) , } \end{array}

we obtain, eventually,

$$
\small p = - \sqrt { - 1 } \frac { L _ { 1 } P _ { 1 } + M _ { 1 } P _ { 2 } + N _ { 1 } P _ { 3 } } { L P _ { 1 } + M P _ { 2 } + N P _ { 3 } } , \qquad q = \frac { E } { L P _ { 1 } + M P _ { 2 } + N P _ { 3 } } ,
