where

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \Gamma _ { \nu _ { 1 } } = \frac { \sigma _ { 1 } \left( u _ { 1 } + u _ { 2 } \right) } { \sigma \left( u _ { 1 } + u _ { 2 } \right) } \frac { \sigma \left. u _ { 1 } - u _ { 2 } \right. } { \sigma \left( u _ { 1 } - u _ { 2 } \right) } \quad \mathrm { ~ ( ~ i = ~ 1 , ~ 2 , ~ 3 ) , ~ } } \\ & { L = \left( \varepsilon _ { 2 } - \varepsilon _ { 3 } \right) \frac { \sigma _ { 1 } \left( w \right) } { \sigma \left( w \right) } , \quad \quad M = \left( \varepsilon _ { 3 } - \varepsilon _ { 1 } \right) \frac { \sigma _ { 2 } \left( w \right) } { \sigma \left( w \right) } , } \\ & { N = \left( \varepsilon _ { 3 } - \varepsilon _ { 2 } \right) \frac { \sigma _ { 3 } \left( w \right) } { \sigma \left( w \right) } , } \\ & { L _ { 1 } = \left( \varepsilon _ { 2 } - \varepsilon _ { 3 } \right) \frac { \sigma _ { 2 } \left( w \right) } { \sigma \left( w \right) } \frac { \sigma _ { 3 } \left( w \right) } { \sigma \left( w \right) } , \quad \quad M _ { 1 } = \left( \varepsilon _ { 3 } - \varepsilon _ { 1 } \right) \frac { \sigma _ { 3 } \left( w \right) } { \sigma \left( w \right) } \frac { \sigma _ { 4 } \left( w \right) } { \sigma \left( w \right) } , } \\ & { N _ { 1 } = \left( \varepsilon _ { 2 } - \varepsilon _ { 2 } \right) \frac { \sigma _ { 1 } \left( w \right) } { \sigma \left( w \right) } \frac { \sigma _ { 3 } \left( w \right) } { \sigma \left( w \right) } , } \\ & { E = \left( \varepsilon _ { 3 } - \varepsilon _ { 3 } \right) \left( \varepsilon _ { 3 } - \varepsilon _ { 1 } \right) \left( \varepsilon _ { 3 } - \varepsilon _ { 2 } \right) . } \end{array}
$$

The remaining unknown functions are computed as follows:

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { r = \displaystyle \frac { 2 } { q } \displaystyle \frac { d p } { d t } = - \sqrt { - 1 } \displaystyle \frac { L P _ { 2 3 } + M P _ { 1 3 } + N P _ { 1 2 } } { L P _ { 1 } + M P _ { 2 } + N P _ { 3 } } , } } \\ { { } } \\ { { \gamma ^ { \prime } = - \left( 2 \displaystyle \frac { d q } { d t } + r \beta \right) = \displaystyle \frac { L _ { 1 } P _ { 2 3 } + M P _ { 1 3 } + N _ { 1 } P _ { 1 2 } } { L P _ { 1 } + M P _ { 2 } + N P _ { 3 } } , } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { P _ { i } = \sqrt { ( s _ { 1 } - a _ { i } ) ( s _ { 2 } - a _ { i } ) } , } } \\ { { \nonumber } } \\ { { P _ { i j } = \frac { P _ { i } P _ { j } } { s _ { 1 } - s _ { 2 } } \left[ \frac { \sqrt { R ( s _ { 1 } ) } } { ( s _ { 1 } - a _ { i } ) ( s _ { 1 } - a _ { j } ) } - \frac { \sqrt { R ( s _ { 2 } ) } } { ( s _ { 2 } - a _ { i } ) ( s _ { 2 } - a _ { j } ) } \right] } } \end{array}
$$

A similar computation can be carried out for $\gamma ^ { \prime } = d r / d t .$

$$
2 \gamma = 2 ( \not p ^ { 2 } + q ^ { 2 } ) + r ^ { 2 } - 6 l _ { 1 } .
$$

These functions are expressed in terms of $s _ { 1 }$ and $s _ { 2 }$

The next step is to express these functions in terms of $t .$ To solve (132), put

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { u _ { 1 } = \displaystyle \int _ { a _ { 1 } } ^ { s _ { 1 } } \displaystyle \frac { s \ d s } { \sqrt { W ( s ) } } + \displaystyle \int _ { a _ { 2 } } ^ { s _ { 2 } } \displaystyle \frac { s \ d s } { \sqrt { W ( s ) } } = t + c , } } \\ { { u _ { 2 } = \displaystyle \int _ { a _ { 1 } } ^ { s _ { 1 } } \displaystyle \frac { d s } { \sqrt { W ( s ) } } + \displaystyle \int _ { a _ { 2 } } ^ { s _ { 2 } } \displaystyle \frac { d s } { \sqrt { W ( s ) } } = c _ { 1 } , } } \end{array}
$$

where $a _ { 0 } < a _ { 1 } < a _ { 2 } < a _ { 3 } < a _ { 4 }$ are, respectively, $e _ { 3 } < k _ { 2 } < e _ { 2 } < e _ { 1 } < k _ { 1 }$ of the five roots of $W ( s ) = 0$ Denote the integrand of the first line by $F _ { 1 } ( s ) / \sqrt { W ( s ) }$ and that of the second line by $F _ { 2 } ( s ) / \sqrt { W ( s ) }$ , and write

$$
\Omega _ { i j } = \int _ { a _ { 2 j - 1 } } ^ { a _ { 2 j } } \frac { F _ { i } ( s ) ~ d s } { \sqrt { W ( s ) } } , \qquad \sqrt { - 1 } ~ \Omega _ { i j } ^ { \prime } = \int _ { a _ { 2 j - 2 } } ^ { a _ { 2 j - 1 } } \frac { F _ { i } ( s ) ~ d s } { \sqrt { W ( s ) } } \qquad ( i , j = 1 ,
$$

$$
u _ { 1 } = 2 \Omega _ { 1 1 } v _ { 1 } + 2 \Omega _ { 1 2 } v _ { 2 } , \qquad u _ { 2 } = 2 \Omega _ { 2 1 } v _ { 1 } + 2 \Omega _ { 2 2 } v _ { 2 } ,
$$

or

$$
v _ { 1 } = g _ { 1 1 } u _ { 1 } + g _ { 1 2 } u _ { 2 } , \qquad v _ { 2 } = g _ { 2 1 } u _ { 1 } + g _ { 2 2 } u _ { 2 } .
$$

Take Riemann's $\vartheta$ function (Section 6.24)

$$
\vartheta ( v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } ) = \sum _ { \nu _ { 1 } , \nu _ { 2 } } ^ { - \infty } \exp \big \{ \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \big [ \nu _ { 1 } ( 2 v _ { 1 } + \nu _ { 1 } \tau _ { 1 1 } + \nu _ { 2 } \tau _ { 1 2 } )  \\  + \nu _ { 2 } ( 2 v _ { 2 } + \nu _ { 1 } \tau _ { 2 1 } + \nu _ { 2 } \tau _ { 2 2 } ) \big ] \big \} ,
$$

where

$$
\tau _ { i j } = 2 \sqrt { - 1 } ( g _ { 1 i } \Omega _ { 1 j } ^ { \prime } + g _ { 2 i } \Omega _ { 2 j } ^ { \prime } ) , \qquad \tau _ { i j } = \tau _ { j i } .
$$

Then, with arbitrary integers $/ _ { 1 } , / _ { 2 } .$ we have

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } & { } & { \vartheta ( v _ { 1 } + \rlap / p _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } + \rlap / p _ { 2 } ) = \vartheta ( v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } ) , \qquad } \\ & { } & { \vartheta ( v _ { 1 } + \ r _ { 1 i } , v _ { 2 } + \ r _ { 2 i } ) = \vartheta ( v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } ) \exp \left[ - \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \ ( 2 v _ { i } + \tau _ { i i } ) \right] } \\ & { } & { \qquad \quad ( i = 1 , 2 ) . } \end{array}
$$

In addition,write

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \tau _ { i } = n _ { 1 } \tau _ { i 1 } + n _ { 2 } \tau _ { i 2 } , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \vartheta ( v _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } / n _ { 1 } , n _ { 2 } ) = \vartheta ( v _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 1 } , v _ { 2 } + \tau _ { 2 } ) } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { \displaystyle \qquad \times \exp \left\{ \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \left[ n _ { 1 } ( 2 v _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 1 } ) + n _ { 2 } ( 2 v _ { 2 } + \tau _ { 2 } ) \right] \right\} } } \end{array}
$$

and choose a set of integers $m _ { 1 } ^ { \lambda } , m _ { 2 } ^ { \lambda } , n _ { 1 } ^ { \lambda } , n _ { 2 } ^ { \lambda }$ such that

$$
{ \displaystyle \vartheta ( \boldsymbol { v } _ { 1 } , \boldsymbol { v } _ { 2 } ) _ { \lambda } = \vartheta ( \boldsymbol { v } _ { 1 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } m _ { 1 } ^ { \lambda } , \boldsymbol { v } _ { 2 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } m _ { 2 } ^ { \lambda } / \frac { 1 } { 2 } n _ { 1 } ^ { \lambda } , \frac { 1 } { 2 } n _ { 2 } ^ { \lambda } ) ; }
$$

then $P _ { i }$ and $P _ { i j }$ ,and accordingly $\ p , \ q , r , \gamma , \gamma ^ { \prime } , \gamma ^ { \prime \prime }$ , are all represented by functions of the form

$$
\frac { \vartheta _ { i } ( c t + c ^ { \prime } , c _ { 1 } t + c _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ) } { \vartheta ( c t + c ^ { \prime } , c _ { 1 } t + c _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ) } .
$$

Thus the top motion of Kowalewski can be solved completely by the elementary method of integration； that is, by finding the necessary

number of integrals. What the theorem of Poincaré states is that the differential equations of celestial mechanics cannot be solved by such an elementary method of integration (Golubev, 1953).

# 6.24RIEMANN $\vartheta$ -FUNCTIONS

In Section 2.1O we have described quasi-periodic functions that can be expanded in trigonometric series with linear functions of the independent variable as arguments.A similar periodicity with a similar form of expansion (not in the real but in the complex domain） can be obtained for the Riemann $\vartheta$ -functions (Riemann，1892；Weierstrass,1894; Wirtinger，1895；Baker，1897，1907；Krazer，1903；Landau，1918; Hecke,1928;Coble,1929).

The series

$$
\sum _ { m = - \infty } ^ { + \infty } \exp { ( a m ^ { 2 } + 2 b m + c ) }
$$

is absolutely convergent for finite values of $b$ and $\pmb { c }$ if the real part $r$ of $\smash { a = r + \sqrt { - 1 } s }$ is negative. The function represented by

$$
\vartheta ( u ) = \sum _ { m = - \infty } ^ { \infty } \exp { ( a m ^ { 2 } + 2 m u ) }
$$

is called a $\vartheta$ -function of a complex variable $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { u } }$ ,and $^ { a }$ is called the modulus. We have

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \displaystyle \vartheta ( u + \pi \sqrt { - 1 } ) = \vartheta ( u ) , \qquad \vartheta ( u ) = \vartheta ( - u ) , } } \\ { { \displaystyle \vartheta ( u + a ) = \exp \left( - a - 2 u \right) \cdot \vartheta ( u ) , } } \end{array}
$$

Repeating these formulas, we obtain

$$
\vartheta ( u + \kappa a + \lambda \pi { \sqrt { - 1 } } ) = \exp { ( - \kappa ^ { 2 } a - 2 \kappa u ) \cdot \vartheta ( u ) }
$$

with arbitrary integers $\kappa$ and $\lambda$ If for finite values of a single-valued continuous function of a complex variable $\boldsymbol { u }$ the equations

$$
G ( u + \pi { \sqrt { - 1 } } ) = G ( u ) , \qquad G ( u + a ) = \exp { ( - a - 2 u ) } \cdot G ( u )
$$

are satisfied,or if the function satisfies

$$
G ( u + \kappa a + \lambda \pi \sqrt { - 1 } ) = \exp { ( - \kappa ^ { 2 } a - 2 \kappa u ) } \cdot G ( u ) ,
$$

then $G ( u )$ and $\vartheta ( u )$ differ only by a factor independent of $\pmb { u }$ This function $\vartheta ( u )$ is called a Riemann $\vartheta$ -function.

When this class of function is generalized, the $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ -ple infinite series

$$
\sum _ { m _ { 1 } , \cdots , m _ { p } } ^ { - \infty \cdots + \infty } \exp \left( \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { p } a _ { \mu \nu } m _ { \mu } m _ { \nu } + 2 \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } b _ { \mu } m _ { \mu } + c \right)
$$

with finite values of $b _ { 1 } , \dotsc , b _ { p } ,$ $c$ is absolutely convergent, if the quadratic form

$$
\sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { p } r _ { \mu \nu } x _ { \mu } x _ { \nu }
$$

formed of the real parts $r _ { \mu \nu }$ of $a _ { \mu \nu }$ is negative-definite.

A function defined by

$$
\vartheta ( u _ { 1 } , u _ { 2 } , \ldots , u _ { p } ) = \sum _ { m _ { 1 } , \cdots , m _ { p } } ^ { - \infty \cdots + \infty } \exp \Big ( \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { p } a _ { \mu \nu } m _ { \mu } m _ { \nu } + 2 \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } m _ { \mu } u _ { \mu } \Big )
$$

with the above conditions for $a _ { \mu \nu }$ is called a $\vartheta$ function of complex variables $u _ { 1 } , \dotsc , u _ { p }$ . Such a function satisfies the set of equations (A):

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \vartheta ( u _ { 1 } , . . . , u _ { \nu } + \pi \sqrt { - 1 } , . . . , u _ { p } ) = \vartheta ( u _ { 1 } , . . . , u _ { \nu } , . . . , u _ { p } ) , } } \\ { { \vartheta ( u _ { 1 } + a _ { 1 \nu } , . . . , u _ { p } + a _ { p \nu } ) } } \\ { { \qquad = \vartheta ( u _ { 1 } , . . . , u _ { p } ) \exp { ( - a _ { \mu \nu } - 2 u _ { \nu } ) } \qquad ( \nu = 1 , 2 , . . . , p ) . } } \end{array}
$$

More generally, the function (l33a) satisfies

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \vartheta \Big ( u _ { 1 } + \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { p } \kappa _ { \nu } a _ { 1 \nu } + \lambda _ { 1 } \pi \sqrt { - 1 } , . . . , u _ { p } + \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { p } \kappa _ { \nu } a _ { p \nu } + \lambda _ { p } \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \Big ) } } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { = \exp \Big ( { - \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { p } a _ { \mu \nu } \kappa _ { \mu } \kappa _ { \nu } - 2 \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } \kappa _ { \mu } u _ { \mu } } \Big ) \cdot \vartheta ( u _ { 1 } , . . . , u _ { p } + \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } a _ { \mu } \kappa _ { \mu } ) , } \end{array}
$$

with integers $\kappa _ { 1 } , . . . , \kappa _ { p } , \lambda _ { 1 } , . . . , \lambda _ { p }$ The $2  { p }$ sequences on the left-hand side in the foregoing $2  { p }$ equations $( \mathbf { A } )$ for a $\vartheta$ -function such as

$$
\begin{array} { r l r l r l r l r l r l r l } { \pi { \sqrt { - 1 } } } & { { } } & { 0 } & { { } } & { \qquad } & { 0 } & { { } } & { { } \ a _ { 1 1 } } & { { } } & { { } } & { { } } & { \ a _ { p 1 } } \\ { 0 } & { { } } & { \pi { \sqrt { - 1 } } } & { { } } & { } & { { } } & { 0 } & { { } } & { { } \ a _ { 1 2 } } & { { } } & { { } } & { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \end{array}
$$

are said to give sequences for the simultaneously varying values of $u _ { 1 } , \dotsc , u _ { p }$ Such sequences are called the periodicity moduli of a $\vartheta$ -function. Conversely, a function $G ( u _ { 1 } , \ldots , u _ { p } )$ satisfying

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { G \Big ( u _ { 1 } + \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { p } \kappa _ { \nu } a _ { 1 \nu } + \lambda _ { 1 } \pi \sqrt { - 1 } , . . . , u _ { p } + \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { p } \kappa _ { \nu } a _ { p \nu } + \lambda _ { p } \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \Big ) } } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { = \exp \Big ( - \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { p } a _ { \mu \nu } \kappa _ { \mu } \kappa _ { \nu } - 2 \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } \kappa _ { \mu } u _ { \mu } \Big ) \cdot G ( u _ { 1 } , . . . } \end{array}
$$

with arbitrary integers $\kappa _ { 1 } , \kappa _ { 2 } , . . . , \kappa _ { p } , \lambda _ { 1 } , . . . , \lambda _ { p }$ differs from $\vartheta ( u _ { 1 } , . . . , u _ { p } )$ by a factor independent of $\boldsymbol { u }$

The periodicity moduli are related uniquely with an arbitrary sequence $c _ { 1 } , \ldots , c _ { p }$ by

$$
\mathrm {  ~ \sigma ~ } _ { 1 } = \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } g _ { \mu } a _ { 1 \mu } + h _ { 1 } \pi \sqrt { - 1 } , \quad \mathrm {  ~ \sigma ~ } \cdot \cdot \cdot , \quad \mathrm {  ~ \sigma ~ } \cdot \mathrm {  ~ \sigma ~ } _ { \mu } = \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } g _ { \mu } a _ { p \mu } + h _ { p } \pi \sqrt { - 1 }
$$

with real numbers $g _ { 1 } , \dotsc , g _ { p }$ ， $h _ { 1 } , . . . , h _ { p }$ These $2 \phi$ real numbers are called the period characteristics of $c _ { 1 } , \ldots , c _ { p }$

Define

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \left[ \begin{array} { l } { g _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot g _ { p } } \\ { h _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot h _ { p } } \end{array} \right] ( u _ { 1 } , . . . , u _ { p } ) = \displaystyle { \sum _ { m _ { 1 } , \cdot \cdot \cdot , m _ { p } } ^ { - \infty } } \exp \left[ \displaystyle { \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { p } a _ { \mu \nu } ( m _ { \mu } + g _ { \mu } ) ( m _ { \nu } + g _ { \nu } ^ { \mu } ) ^ { \frac { p } { 2 } } } \right] ( \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } a _ { \mu \nu } + g _ { \mu \nu } ) ( m _ { \nu } + g _ { \mu } ) ( m _ { \nu } + g _ { \nu } ^ { \mu } ) ( m _ { \nu } + g _ { \mu } ) ( m _ { \nu } + g _ { \nu } ^ { \mu } ) , } \\ { + \displaystyle { 2 \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } ( m _ { \mu } + g _ { \mu } ) ( m _ { \mu } + h _ { \mu } \pi \sqrt { - 1 } ) ^ { \frac { p } { 2 } } } , } \end{array}
$$

This $\vartheta \cdot$ -function satisfies

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \vartheta \Big [ \frac { g _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \cdot g _ { p } } { h _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot h _ { p } } \Big ] ( u _ { 1 } , \cdot \cdot \cdot , u _ { p } ) } } \\ & { } & { \quad = \vartheta \Big [ u _ { 1 } + \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } g _ { \mu } a _ { 1 \mu } + h _ { 1 } \pi \sqrt { - 1 } , \cdot \cdot \cdot , u _ { p } + \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } g _ { \mu } a _ { p \mu } + h _ { p } \pi \mathcal { V } } \\ & { } & { \quad \times \exp \Big [ \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { p } a _ { \mu \nu } g _ { \mu } g _ { \nu } + 2 \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } g _ { \mu } ( u _ { \mu } + h _ { \mu } \pi \sqrt { - 1 } ) \Big ] , } \end{array}
$$

and is equal to $\vartheta ( u _ { 1 } , . . . , u _ { p } )$ if all $g$ and $h$ are zero. This $\vartheta$ -function is denoted by $\vartheta [ _ { h } ^ { g } ] ( ( u ) )$ to simplify the notation.

If the period characteristics of $c _ { 1 } , \ldots , c _ { p }$ are $g _ { 1 } , \dotsc , g _ { p } ,$ $h _ { 1 } , \ldots , h _ { p } $ then we denote the sequence $u _ { 1 } + c _ { 1 } , . . . , u _ { p } + c _ { p }$ by $( u \mathrm { ~ + ~ } \{ \mathfrak { a } \} )$ . This function satisfies

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { \mathscr { g } } { h } \bigg ] \left( \left( u + \binom { \kappa } { \lambda } \right) \right) = \vartheta \bigg [ \frac { \mathscr { g } } { h } \bigg ] ( ( u ) ) \exp \bigg [ - \displaystyle \sum _ { u = 1 } ^ { \frac { p } { \gamma } } \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { \frac { p } { \gamma } } a _ { \mu \nu } \kappa _ { \mu } \kappa _ { \nu } - 2 \sum _ { u = 1 } ^ { \frac { p } { \gamma } } \kappa _ { \mu } u _ { \mu } } \\ { + \ : 2 \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } ( \lambda _ { \mu } g _ { u } - \kappa _ { \mu } h _ { \mu } ) \pi \sqrt { - 1 } } \end{array}
$$

Conversely, a function $G ( ( \boldsymbol { u } ) )$ satisfying

$$
\begin{array} { r l r }   { G \Big ( \Big ( \boldsymbol { u } + \{ \begin{array} { c } { \kappa } \\ { \lambda } \end{array} \} \Big ) \Big ) = G \big ( ( ( \boldsymbol { u } ) \cdot ) \big ) \exp \Big [ - \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { p } a _ { \mu \nu } \kappa _ { \mu } \kappa _ { \nu } - 2 \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } \kappa _ { \mu } \boldsymbol { u } _ { \mu } } \\ & { } & { \qquad + \ : 2 \displaystyle \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } ( \lambda _ { \mu } g _ { \mu } - \kappa _ { \mu } h _ { \mu } ) \pi \mathcal { V } } \end{array}
$$

differs from $\vartheta [ _ { h } ^ { g } ] ( ( u ) )$ only by a factor independent of $\boldsymbol { u }$ With real numbers $g _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , g _ { p } ^ { \prime } , h _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , h _ { p } ^ { \prime }$ this function satisfies

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \vartheta \Big [ _ { h } ^ { g } \Big ] \Big ( \Big ( \boldsymbol { u } + \Big \{ \begin{array} { c } { g ^ { \prime } } \\ { h ^ { \prime } } \end{array} \Big \} \Big ) \Big ) \ } & { = \displaystyle g \Big [ _ { h } ^ { g } + \boldsymbol { g } ^ { \prime } \Big ] ( ( \boldsymbol { u } ) ) \exp \Big [ - \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } \sum _ { \nu = 1 } ^ { p } a _ { \mu \nu } g _ { \mu } ^ { \prime } g _ { \nu } ^ { \prime } } } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \qquad - \ 2 \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { p } g _ { \mu } ^ { \prime } ( \boldsymbol { u } _ { \mu } + \lambda _ { \mu } \pi \sqrt { - 1 } + h _ { \mu } ^ { \prime } \pi \sqrt { - 1 } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\vartheta \biggl [ \frac { g } { h + \lambda } \biggr ] ( ( u ) ) = \vartheta \biggl [ \frac { g } { h } \biggr ] ( ( u ) ) \exp \biggl ( 2 \sum _ { \mu = 1 } ^ { \mathcal { P } } \lambda _ { \mu } g _ { \mu } \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \biggr ) ,
$$

$$
\vartheta \biggl [ { g \atop h } \biggr ] ( - u _ { 1 } , . . . , - u _ { p } ) = \vartheta \biggl [ - { g \atop h } \bigr ] ( u _ { 1 } , . . . , u _ { p } ) .
$$

If the period characteristics $g _ { 1 } , . . . , g _ { p } , h _ { 1 } , . . . , h _ { p }$ are half-integers, denote them by ${ \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \alpha _ { 1 } , \dots , { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \alpha _ { p } , { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \beta _ { 1 } , \dots , { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \beta _ { p } }$ with integers $\alpha _ { 1 } , . . . , \alpha _ { p } ,$ $\beta _ { 1 } , . . . , \beta _ { p }$ Let $\alpha \cdot \beta = { \alpha } _ { 1 } \beta _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + { \alpha } _ { p } \beta _ { p }$ ，the scalar product of two vectors $\alpha = ( \alpha _ { 1 } , . . . , \alpha _ { p } )$ and $\beta = ( \beta _ { 1 } , . . . , \beta _ { p } )$ ,and write

$$
\Theta ( u ; \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \beta ; \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \alpha ) = \vartheta \Big [ \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \alpha _ { 1 } \cdot \cdot \cdot \frac { 1 } { 2 } \alpha _ { p } \Big ] \big ( \pi \sqrt { - 1 } u _ { 1 } , . . . , \pi \sqrt { - 1 } u _ { p } \big ) .
$$

We have

$$
\Theta ( - u ; { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \beta ; { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \alpha ) = \exp { ( \pi \sqrt { - 1 } ~ \beta \alpha ) } \cdot \Theta ( u ; { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \beta ; { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \alpha ) .
$$

$\Theta ( u ; { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \beta ; { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \alpha )$ is an odd or an even function according as whether $\beta \alpha$ is odd or even.

Put

$$
m _ { i } + \alpha _ { i 1 } m _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \alpha _ { i p } m _ { p } ^ { \prime } \equiv \Omega _ { m m ^ { \prime } } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , p )
$$

with $2  { p }$ constants $m _ { 1 } , . ~ . ~ . , m _ { p } , m _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , . ~ . ~ . , m _ { p } ^ { \prime }$ ，and denote the matrix products by $\scriptstyle { a m ^ { \prime } }$ ，mx, $m ^ { \prime } \beta$ ， $_ { a \alpha }$ ,for example； then, from the $2 \hbar$ formulas (A) for $\vartheta ( u _ { 1 } , . . . , u _ { p } )$ we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { \Theta ( u + \Omega _ { m m ^ { \prime } } ; \frac { 1 } { 2 } \beta ; \frac { 1 } { 2 } \alpha ) } } \\ { { = \exp { [ - 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \ m ^ { \prime } ( u + \frac { 1 } { 2 } a m ^ { \prime } ) + \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \ ( m \alpha - m ^ { \prime } \beta ) ] } \cdot \Theta ( u ; \frac { 1 } { 2 } \beta ; \frac { 1 } { 2 } \alpha ) } } \\ { { \Theta ( u + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Omega _ { \beta ^ { \prime } \alpha ^ { \prime } } ; \frac { 1 } { 2 } \beta ; \frac { 1 } { 2 } \alpha ) } } \\ { { = \exp { [ - \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \ \alpha ^ { \prime } ( u + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \beta + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \beta ^ { \prime } + \frac { 1 } { 4 } a \alpha ^ { \prime } ) ] } \cdot \Theta ( u ; \frac { 1 } { 2 } \beta + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \beta ^ { \prime } ; \frac { 1 } { 2 } \alpha + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \beta ^ { \prime } ; \frac { 1 } { 2 } \alpha ) } } \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { - \pi \sqrt { - 1 } ~ \alpha ^ { \prime } ( u + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \beta + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \beta ^ { \prime } + \frac { 1 } { 4 } a \alpha ^ { \prime } ) ] \cdot \Theta ( u ; \frac { 1 } { 2 } \beta + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \beta ^ { \prime } ; \frac { 1 } { 2 } \alpha + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \alpha ^ { \prime } ) , } \\ & { ~ \Theta ( u ; \frac { 1 } { 2 } \beta ; \frac { 1 } { 2 } \alpha ) = \exp \left[ \pi \sqrt { - 1 } ~ \alpha ( u + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \beta + \frac { 1 } { 4 } a \alpha ) \right] \cdot \Theta ( u + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Omega _ { \beta \alpha } ) , } \\ & { + ~ m ; \frac { 1 } { 2 } \alpha + m ^ { \prime } ) = \exp \left( \pi \sqrt { - 1 } ~ m \alpha \right) \cdot \Theta ( u ; \frac { 1 } { 2 } \beta ; \frac { 1 } { 2 } \alpha ) . } \end{array}
$$

Let the function $\Theta ( u ; 0 ; 0 )$ be abbreviated to $\Theta ( u )$

As we have seen in Section 2.20, the normal Riemann integral of the first kind $W _ { \tau } ( x ; c )$ possesses the periods $0 , 0 , \ldots , 0 , 1 , 0 , \ldots , 0 , a _ { r 1 } , \ldots , a _ { r p }$ in the rth row, and the imaginary part of the quadratic form

$$
a _ { 1 1 } m _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + a _ { r r } m _ { r } ^ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + 2 a _ { 1 2 } m _ { 1 } m _ { 2 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + 2 a _ { r s } m _ { r } m _ { s } + \cdot \cdot \cdot
$$

is positive for all constant values of $\displaystyle m _ { 1 } , \ldots , m _ { p }$ .Hence these @-functions are convergent.

Let $e _ { 1 } , \ldots , e _ { p }$ be the definite constant; let $_ m$ be a fixed place and $_ { x }$ a varying place, both on the Riemann surface. Consider the function $\Theta ( u )$ as a function of the variables $u _ { \tau } = W _ { \tau } ( x ; m ) + e _ { \tau }$ $( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , p )$ formed of the normal Riemann integrals of the first kind $W _ { 1 } ( x ; m ) , \ldots ,$ $W _ { p } ( x ; m )$ .This function satisfies

$$
\begin{array} { r c l } { { } } & { { \Theta ( u + k ) = \Theta ( u ) , } } & { { } } \\ { { } } & { { } } & { { } } \\ { { \Theta ( u _ { r } + a _ { r } k ^ { \prime } ) = \exp \left[ - 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } k ^ { \prime } ( u + \frac { 1 } { 2 } a _ { r } k ^ { \prime } ) \right] \cdot \Theta ( u ) , } } & { { } } \end{array}
$$

where $k = ( k _ { 1 } , \ldots , k _ { p } )$ ， $k ^ { \prime } = ( k _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , k _ { p } ^ { \prime } )$ ，and $a _ { r }$ is the rth row of the matrix $a _ { \tau s }$ . Such a function $\Theta ( u )$ ,consisting of the integrals of the first kind (Section 2.2O),does not become infinite as a function of $_ x$ ，while it possesses just $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ simple zero points.If a zero point is ${ \mathfrak { s } } .$ -ple,then it is counted as $s$ simple zero points.Denote the places of these simple zero points by $z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { p }$ ， The Riemann surface can be partitioned so that $\log \Theta ( u )$ is uniform by drawing sets of cross cuts, where a set consists of cross cuts of the $\pmb { A }$ type, those of the $B$ type,and the closed curves each enclosing a zero point (Section 2.2O). Let each of these cross cuts correspond to one of the zero points $z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { p }$ .When we complete the circuit of all sets of these cross cuts,we obtain

$$
\frac { 1 } { 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } } \int \log \Theta ( u ) d u _ { s } = 0 ,
$$

because we have then completed the whole circuit along the whole boundary of the partitioned regions. The expression

$$
k _ { s } = \sum _ { r = 1 } ^ { p } \int _ { { \cal B } _ { r } } \left[ W _ { \tau } ( x ; m ) + { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } a _ { r r } \right] d W _ { s } ( x ; m )
$$

depends only on the place $\pmb { m }$ . We determine the places $m _ { 1 } , . . . , m _ { p }$ so that

$$
W _ { s } ( m _ { 1 } ; P _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + W _ { s } ( m _ { p } ; P _ { p } ) \equiv k _ { s } , \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , . . . , p )
$$

with the periods as moduli. Then we can make the $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ places $\begin{array} { r } { { m _ { 1 } } , \dotsc , { m _ { p } } , } \end{array}$ which depend only on the places of $_ m$ , correspond to an arbitrary place $_ m$ such that the places of the zero points of the function

$$
\Theta [ W ( x ; m ) - W ( z _ { 1 } ; m _ { 1 } ) - \cdot \cdot \cdot - W ( z _ { p } ; m _ { p } ) ] ,
$$

as functions of $_ { x }$ are $z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { p } ,$

Replacing $_ x$ with $z _ { p }$ ， we see that

$$
\Theta [ W ( m _ { p } ; m ) - W ( z _ { 1 } ; m _ { 1 } ) - \cdot \cdot \cdot - W ( z _ { p - 1 } ; m _ { p - 1 } ) ]
$$

vanishes identically at all places $z _ { 1 } , \dotsc , z _ { p - 1 }$ . Thus the function

$$
\Theta [ W ( x ; m ) - W ( z _ { 1 } ; m _ { 1 } ) - \cdot \cdot \cdot - W ( z _ { p } ; m _ { p } ) ]
$$

vanishes if and only if either $_ x$ coincides with one of the places $z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { p } ,$ or the places $z _ { 1 } , . . . , z _ { p }$ are the zero points of the $\phi$ polynomials (Section 2.20). Denote by $r$ a set of $r _ { 1 } , \ldots , r _ { p }$ for which $\Theta ( r ) = 0$ If $\Theta [ W ( x ; z ) + r ]$ does not vanish at any place of $\pmb { x }$ ,then there exists one and only one set of places $z _ { 1 } , \dotsc , z _ { p - 1 }$ for which

$$
r \equiv W ( m _ { p } ; m ) - W ( z _ { 1 } ; m _ { 1 } ) - \cdot \cdot \cdot - W ( z _ { p - 1 } ; m _ { p - 1 } ) .
$$

In general, if $\Theta [ W ( x _ { 1 } ; z _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + W ( x _ { q } ; z _ { q } ) + r ]$ is identically zero for all places $x _ { 1 } , z _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , z _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { q } , z _ { q } ,$ and if

$$
\Theta [ W ( x ; z ) + W ( x _ { 1 } ; z _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + W ( x _ { q } ; z _ { q } ) + r ]
$$

is made not identically zero with regard to $_ x$ by suitable choice of $_ z$ and is made not identically zero with regard to $_ z$ by suitable choice of $_ { x }$ ， then we can determine the set $\zeta _ { 1 } , \dotsc , \zeta _ { p - 1 }$ such that

$$
r \equiv W ( m _ { p } ; m ) - W ( \zeta _ { 1 } ; m _ { 1 } ) - \cdot \cdot \cdot - W ( \zeta _ { p - 1 } ; m _ { p - 1 } ) ,
$$

in which $q$ out of these $\zeta$ places are arbitrary.We can further find the places $\xi _ { 1 } , \ldots , \xi _ { p - 1 }$ such that

$$
- r \equiv W ( m _ { p } ; m ) - W ( \xi _ { 1 } ; m _ { 1 } ) - \cdot \cdot \cdot - W ( \xi _ { p - 1 } ; m _ { p - 1 } ) ,
$$

in which $q$ out of these $\xi$ places are arbitrary. In such a case,

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { 2 W ( m _ { p } ; m ) + W ( m _ { 1 } ; \zeta _ { 1 } ) + W ( m _ { 1 } ; \xi _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot } } \\ { { \ \qquad + W ( m _ { p - 1 } ; \zeta _ { p - 1 } ) + W ( m _ { p - 1 } ; \xi _ { p - 1 } ) } } \end{array}
$$

or

$$
( m ^ { 2 } , \xi _ { 1 } , . . . , \xi _ { p - 1 } , \zeta _ { 1 } , . . . , \zeta _ { p - 1 } ) \equiv ( m _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } , m _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } , . . . , m _ { p } ^ { 2 } ) ,
$$

which means that the residue for a closed contour describing a double circuit around the place $_ m$ and a single circuit around each of these places is equal to the residue for a closed curve describing double circuits around each of the places $m _ { 1 } , . . . , m _ { p }$ Let these $2 p - 2$ places $\xi _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , \xi _ { p - 1 } ^ { \prime } , \zeta _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , \zeta _ { p - 1 } ^ { \prime }$ be the places of the zero points $a _ { 1 } , \dotsc , a _ { 2 p - 2 }$ of the $\phi$ -polynomial (Section 2.20). Then we can determine $\displaystyle m _ { 1 } , \ldots , m _ { p }$ from $m$ such that

$$
( m ^ { 2 } , a _ { \bar { 2 } } , . . . , a _ { 2 p - 2 } ) \equiv ( m _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } , m _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } , . . . , m _ { p } ^ { 2 } ) ,
$$

or, if we regard $c _ { 1 } , \ldots , c _ { p }$ as independent places, there is a set of places $m _ { 1 } , . . . , m _ { p }$ such that

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { 2 [ W _ { s } ( m _ { 1 } ; c _ { 1 } ) ~ + ~ \cdot ~ \cdot ~ + ~ W _ { s } ( m _ { p } ; c _ { p } ) ] } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { { } \equiv 2 W _ { s } ( m ; c _ { p } ) ~ + ~ W _ { s } ( a _ { 1 } ; c _ { 1 } ) ~ + ~ \cdot ~ \cdot + ~ W _ { s } ( a _ { 2 p - 2 } } } \end{array}
$$

Denoting the right-hand member by $A _ { s } ,$ we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r } { _ { s } ( m _ { 1 } ; c _ { 1 } ) \ + \cdot \cdot \cdot + W _ { s } ( m _ { p } ; c _ { p } ) \equiv \frac { 1 } { 2 } A _ { s } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } ( h _ { s } + g _ { 1 } a _ { s 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + g _ { p } a _ { s p } ) \quad } \\ { \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , p ) , } \end{array}
$$

with integers $g _ { 1 } , . . . , g _ { p } , h _ { 1 } , . . . , h _ { p }$ If the set $m _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , \ldots , m _ { p } ^ { \prime }$ is the set of integers of $\displaystyle m _ { 1 } , \ldots , m _ { p }$ satisfying (134), then

$$
\begin{array} { r } { W _ { s } ( m _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } ; m _ { 1 } ) \ + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \ W _ { s } ( m _ { p } ^ { \prime } ; m _ { p } ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Omega _ { \beta \alpha } \qquad ( s = 1 , 2 , \ldots , p ) , } \end{array}
$$

with the previous notation $\Omega _ { \alpha \beta }$ (Section 2.2O). If we can find, with the suitably chosen integers $\alpha _ { 1 } , \ldots , \alpha _ { p } , \beta _ { 1 } , \ldots , \beta _ { p } $ ，more than one set of values $n _ { 1 } , . . . , n _ { p - 1 }$ satisfying

$$
- { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \Omega _ { \beta \alpha } \equiv W ( m _ { p } ; m ) - W ( n _ { 1 } ; m _ { 1 } ) - \cdot \cdot \cdot - W ( n _ { p - 1 } ; m _ { p - 1 } ) ,
$$

then there are an infinite number of such sets. Suppose that there are $\infty ^ { q }$ of these; then, $\beta \alpha \equiv q + 1$ (mod 2).

If the function $\Theta [ W ( x ; z ) + r ]$ does not vanish identically for the values of $r$ satisfying $\Theta ( r ) = 0 _ { : }$ ， whatever may be the values of $_ x$ and $_ z$ then Riemann's normal integral of the third kind can be written

$$
\Pi _ { \alpha \beta } ( x ; z ) = \log \Big \{ \frac { \Theta [ W ( x ; \alpha ) + r ] } { \Theta [ W ( x ; \beta ) + r ] } \div \frac { \Theta [ W ( z ; \alpha ) + r ] } { \Theta [ W ( z ; \beta ) + r ] } \Big \} .
$$

In fact, the function

$$
\exp \left[ - \Pi _ { \alpha \beta } ( x ; z ) \right] \cdot \frac { \Theta [ W ( x ; \alpha ) + r ] } { \Theta [ W ( x ; \beta ) + r ] } \frac { \Theta [ W ( z ; \beta ) + r ] } { \Theta [ W ( z ; \alpha ) + r ] }
$$

is uniform on the Riemann surface which is cut along the circuits $A$ and $B$ and is neither zero nor infinite at points other than $z$ This function takes an equal value along a complete circuit around any closed cross cut of the $\pmb { A }$ or $B$ type.Hence, this function is also uniform on the Riemann surface before the cross cuts are made; that is,it does not depend on $_ x$ The function has its value corresponding to $x = z$ ，that is, it equals unity.

If we make the place $x _ { p }$ tend to the place $z _ { p }$ in the equation

$$
\Theta [ W ( x _ { 1 } ; z _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + W ( x _ { q } ; z _ { q } ) + r ] = 0 ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { p } \Theta _ { i } ^ { \prime } [ W ( x _ { 1 } ; z _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + W ( x _ { q - 1 } ; z _ { q - 1 } ) + r ] \Omega _ { i } ( z _ { q } ) = 0 ,
$$

where $\Theta _ { i } ^ { \prime } ( u ) = \partial \Theta ( u ) / \partial u _ { i }$ and $\Omega _ { i } ( x ) = 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \ D _ { x } W _ { i } ( x ; a )$ with an arbitrary constant $^ { a }$ .This relation is valid for any place of

$$
x _ { 1 } , z _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , z _ { 2 } , . ~ . ~ . , x _ { q - 1 } , z _ { q - 1 } .
$$

Since $\Omega _ { 1 } ( z _ { q } ) , . . . , \Omega _ { q } ( z _ { q } )$ are independent, $\Theta ^ { \prime } ( u )$ vanishes for

$$
u = W ( x _ { 1 } ; z _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + W ( x _ { q - 1 } ; z _ { q - 1 } ) + r .
$$

Similarly $\Theta ^ { \prime \prime } ( u )$ vanishes for

$$
u = W ( x _ { 1 } ; z _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + W ( x _ { q - 2 } ; z _ { q - 2 } ) + r .
$$

Hence the function $\Theta ( u )$ and its derivatives up to the order $q$ vanish for $u = r$ Conversely,it can be shown that

$$
\Theta [ W ( x _ { 1 } ; z _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + W ( x _ { q } ; z _ { q } ) + r ]
$$

vanishes for $u = r _ { \mathrm { { \scriptsize ~ 2 } } } r$ ，whatever the values of $x _ { 1 } , z _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { q } , z _ { q } ,$ if and only if $\Theta ( u )$ and its derivatives up to the order $q$ are zero.

If from the linearly independent integrals of the first kind

$$
V _ { 1 } ( x ; m ) , . . . , V _ { p } ( x ; m )
$$

the normal integral of Riemann is obtained by means of

$$
W _ { i } ( x ; m ) = \lambda _ { \mathrm { i } 1 } V _ { 1 } ( x ; m ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \lambda _ { i p } V _ { p } ( x ; m ) ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , p ) ,
$$

then $\Theta ( u )$ is given by

$$
\boldsymbol { u } _ { i } = \lambda _ { i 1 } \boldsymbol { U } _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \lambda _ { i p } \boldsymbol { U } _ { p } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , p )
$$

as a function of $U _ { 1 } , . . . , U _ { p } ,$ which we write $\vartheta ( U )$ . Then the $\phi$ -polynomial which has the places $n _ { 1 } , . . . , n _ { p - 1 }$ as zeros of the second order takes the form

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { p } \vartheta _ { i } ^ { \prime } ( \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { \overline { { \Omega } } } _ { k k ^ { \prime } } ) \psi _ { i } ( x ) ,
$$

where $\psi _ { 1 } ( x ) , . . . , \psi _ { p } ( x )$ are the $\phi$ polynomials corresponding to

$$
V _ { 1 } ( x ; m ) , . . . , V _ { p } ( x ; m ) ,
$$

and $\scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } { \overline { { \Omega } } } _ { k k ^ { \prime } }$ is a set of the half-periods of these $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ functions $V _ { i } ( x ; m )$ . Since the half-periods $\Omega _ { \boldsymbol { k } \boldsymbol { k } ^ { \prime } }$ have been defined so that

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { 2 \lambda _ { i 1 } \omega _ { 1 s } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + 2 \lambda _ { i p } \omega _ { p s } = \left\{ 0 \atop 0 \right. \qquad ( r \neq s ) , }  \\ & { 2 \lambda _ { i 1 } \omega _ { 1 s } ^ { \prime } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + 2 \lambda _ { i p } \omega _ { p s } ^ { \prime } = a _ { i s } \qquad ( r , s = 1 , 2 , . . . , p ) , } \end{array}
$$

we have

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \bar { \Omega } _ { k k ^ { \prime } } = k _ { 1 } \omega _ { i 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + k _ { p } \omega _ { i p } + k _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \omega _ { i 1 } ^ { \prime } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + k _ { p } ^ { \prime } \omega _ { i p } ^ { \prime } } \\ { ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , \phi ) . } \end{array}
$$

Consider in general the sum over the values from $- \infty$ to $+ \infty$ of $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ integers $n _ { 1 } , . . . , n _ { p }$ of the function

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { u ; h ; g ) \equiv \vartheta ( u ; h ) ~ } } \\ { { ~ } } \\ { { ~ = ~ \displaystyle \sum \exp \left[ \kappa u ^ { 2 } + 2 k u ( n + g ) + b ( n + g ) ^ { 2 } + 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \ : h ( n + g ) \right] ~ } } \end{array}
$$

where $\kappa$ and $\pmb { b }$ are symmetric matrices with $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ rows and $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ columns, $k$ is an arbitrary matrix with $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ rows and $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ columns,and the quadratic form $b m ^ { 2 }$ is supposed to be negative for real values of $m _ { 1 } , . . . , m _ { p }$ .The letters $\boldsymbol { g }$ and $h$ denote the characteristic sequences. In the place of $b$ we put $\pi { \sqrt { - 1 } } a$ with a symmetric matrix $^ { a }$ ,and denote by $\boldsymbol { U }$ the $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ quantities $k u$ ; then,

$$
\vartheta ( u ; h ) = \exp { ( \kappa u ^ { 2 } ) } \cdot \Theta ( U ; h , g ) .
$$

Let $\omega , \omega ^ { \prime } , \eta , \eta ^ { \prime }$ be matrices with $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ rows and $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ columns ; let

$$
\Omega _ { P } = 2 \omega P + 2 \omega ^ { \prime } P ^ { \prime } , \qquad H _ { P } = 2 \eta P + 2 \eta ^ { \prime } P ^ { \prime }
$$

with row matrices

$$
P = ( P _ { 1 } , . . . , P _ { p } ) , \ P ^ { \prime } = ( P _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , . . . , P _ { p } ^ { \prime } ) .
$$

Then the expression in the generalized form

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { \vartheta ( u + \Omega _ { P } ; h ) = \exp \big \{ H _ { P } ( u + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Omega _ { P } ) - \pi \sqrt { - 1 } { P P ^ { \prime } + 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } ( P g - P  } } } \\ & { } & { \mathrm { ( } \times  \exp \{ - 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } { P g } \} \cdot \vartheta ( u , P + h )  } \end{array}
$$

is valid under the condition that

$$
\eta = 2 \kappa \omega , ~ \eta ^ { \prime } = 2 \kappa \omega ^ { \prime } - 2 \bar { k } , ~ k \omega = \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \pi \sqrt { - 1 } , ~ k ^ { \prime } \omega ^ { \prime } = \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } b ,
$$

where $\bar { k }$ denotes the transposed matrix of $k$ . In such a case, (135) becomes

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \vartheta ( u + \Omega _ { P } ; h ) = \exp { [ \lambda _ { P } ( u ) - 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } P ^ { \prime } h ] } \cdot \vartheta ( u ; h + P ) , } \\ & { ~ \lambda _ { P } ( u ) = H _ { P } ( u + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Omega _ { P } ) - \pi \sqrt { - 1 } P P ^ { \prime } . } \end{array}
$$

Condition (136) expresses $\omega , \omega ^ { \prime } , \eta , \eta ^ { \prime }$ in terms of $\kappa , k , b$ . The remaining condition is that

$$
H _ { P } \Omega _ { Q } - H _ { Q } \Omega _ { P } = 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } ( P Q ^ { \prime } - P ^ { \prime } Q ) .
$$

If the matrices $P$ and $P ^ { \prime }$ consist of integers, then (137) becomes

$$
\vartheta ( u + \Omega _ { m } ; h ) = \exp { [ \lambda _ { m } ( u ) + 2 \pi \sqrt { - 1 } ( m g - m ^ { \prime } h ) ] } \cdot \vartheta ( u ; h ) ,
$$

where $m , \ m ^ { \prime }$ are row matrices consisting of integers. The function $\vartheta ( u ; h )$ defined in this manner is an odd or an even function according to whether $4 g h$ is odd or even.Put

$$
h = \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \ a , \zeta _ { i } ( u ) = { \frac { \partial } { \partial u _ { i } } } \log \vartheta ( u ) ;
$$

then, from

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \vartheta ( u + \Omega _ { m } ) = \exp { [ H _ { m } ( u + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Omega _ { m } ) - \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \ : m m ^ { \prime } ] } \cdot \vartheta ( u ) , } \end{array}
$$

we obtain

$$
( u + \Omega _ { m } ) - \zeta _ { i } ( u ) = 2 ( \eta _ { i 1 } m _ { 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \eta _ { i p } m _ { p } + \eta _ { i 1 } ^ { \prime } m _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \eta _ { i p } ^ { \prime } m _ { p } ^ { \prime } ) ;
$$

in particular,

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \zeta _ { i } ( u _ { 1 } + 2 \omega _ { 1 r } , . . . , u _ { p } + 2 \omega _ { p r } ) = \zeta _ { i } ( u ) + 2 \eta _ { i r } , } \\ & { \zeta _ { i } ( u _ { 1 } + 2 \omega _ { 1 r } ^ { \prime } , . . . , u _ { p } + 2 \omega _ { p r } ^ { \prime } ) = \zeta _ { i } ( u ) + 2 \eta _ { i r } ^ { \prime } . } \end{array}
$$

Denote by $\mu _ { i } ( x )$ the derivative $d u _ { i } ( { \boldsymbol { x } } ; { \boldsymbol { a } } ) / d { \boldsymbol { x } }$ and by $\bar { \nu } _ { \tau i }$ a minor of the determinant formed of $\mu _ { i } ( x _ { r } )$ . Then we obtain an important relation:

$$
\begin{array} { l } { - \zeta _ { \mathrm { i } } [ u ( x ; m ) - u ( x _ { 1 } ; m _ { 1 } ) - \cdot \cdot \cdot - u ( x _ { p } ; m _ { p } ) ] } \\ { + \ \zeta _ { \mathrm { i } } [ u ( a ; m ) - u ( x _ { 1 } ; m _ { 1 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot - u ( x _ { p } ; m _ { p } ) ] } \\ { = L _ { \mathrm { i } } ( x ; a ) + \displaystyle \sum _ { r = 1 } ^ { p } \bar { \nu } _ { r \mathrm { i } } [ ( x _ { r } , x ) - ( x _ { r } , a ) ] \frac { d x _ { r } } { d t } . } \end{array}
$$

Thus $\tilde { \nu } _ { \tau i }$ depends on the places $x _ { 1 } , \dotsc , x _ { p }$ exactly as the quantity $\nu _ { \tau i } ,$ which is a minor of the determinant $\left\| \dot { \mu } _ { i } ( x _ { \tau } ) \right\|$ ， depends on the places $c _ { 1 } , \ldots , c _ { p }$ (Section 2.20). The right-hand member has been already derived.

From (138), differentiating with regard to $_ x$ denoting $- \partial \pmb { \zeta } _ { i } ( u ) / \partial u _ { j }$ by $\wp _ { i j } ( u )$ , and making $^ { a }$ coincide with $\pmb { x }$ ,we obtain

$$
\sum _ { j = 1 } ^ { p } \mu _ { j } ( x ) \wp _ { i j } ( U ) = D _ { z } L _ { i } ( x ; a ) + \sum _ { s = 1 } ^ { p } \nu _ { s i } D _ { z } \Bigl [ ( x _ { s } , x ) \frac { d x _ { s } } { d t } \Bigr ] ,
$$

where $U = u ( z ; m ) - u ( z _ { 1 } ; m _ { 1 } ) - \cdot \cdot \cdot - u ( z _ { p } ; m _ { p } )$ (Baker,1897).

Equation (73a) of Section 2.20 in the general case can be writen in the form

$$
y ^ { 2 } = H ( x - a _ { 1 } ) ( x - a _ { 2 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( x - a _ { p } ) ( x - c _ { 1 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( x - c _ { p + 1 } )
$$

and defines the Riemann surface. There exist an even number of the Riemann $\vartheta$ -functions that vanish when the variable vanishes. One of the branch places, $^ { a }$ for example, is at infinity.Let $b$ and $b ^ { \prime }$ be any two of the branch places； then the $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ integrals $u _ { 1 } ( b ; b ^ { \prime } ) , . . . , u _ { p } ( b ; b ^ { \prime } )$ are the $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ simultaneous components of a half-period, so that, for example

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { u _ { r } ( b ; b ^ { \prime } ) = m _ { 1 } \omega _ { r 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + m _ { p } \omega _ { r p } + m _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \omega _ { r 1 } ^ { \prime } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + m _ { p } ^ { \prime } \omega _ { r p } ^ { \prime } } } \\ { { \equiv \frac 1 2 \Omega \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , \ldots , p ) } } \end{array}
$$

with integers $m _ { 1 } , . ~ . ~ . , m _ { p } , m _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } , . ~ . ~ . , m _ { p } ^ { \prime }$ independent of $\boldsymbol { r }$ If, $b$ remaining any branch place, $b ^ { \prime }$ is taken to be each of the other $2 \phi + 1$ branch places in turn，then the $2 \phi + 1$ half-periods $u ( b ; b ^ { \prime } )$ thus obtained consist of $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ odd half-periods and $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } + \mathbb { 1 }$ even half-periods.Thus,if the branch places $b ^ { \prime }$ for which $u ( b ; b ^ { \prime } )$ is an odd half-period be denoted by $b _ { 1 } , \dotsc , b _ { p } ,$ then necessarily $\begin{array} { r } { \vartheta [ u ( b ; b _ { 1 } ) ] = 0 , . . . , \vartheta [ u ( b ; b _ { p } ) ] = 0 } \end{array}$ ,and we can take the places $b _ { 1 } , \dotsc , b _ { p }$ for the places $m , m _ { 1 } , \ldots , m _ { p }$ In particular, if the branch place $^ { a }$ is taken for $b$ and the branch places are located as in Figure l,then each of $u ( a ; a _ { 1 } ) , . . . , u ( a ; a _ { p } )$ is an odd half-period. Hence the function $\vartheta [ u ( x ; a ) - u ( x _ { 1 } , a _ { 1 } ) - \cdot \cdot \cdot - u ( x _ { p } ; a _ { p } ) ]$ has the places $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { p }$ as its zero places,where it is assumed that the function $\vartheta [ u ( x ; a ) ]$ does not vanish identically. This assumption is evidently justified (Baker,1897).

Let $b , b ^ { \prime } , b ^ { \prime \prime } , \ldots$ .be the different branch places other than a.From the $2  { p } + 1$ half-periods $u ( b ; a )$ with respect to the branch places $b$ (not with respect to the branch places $^ { a }$ )，we can derive $\binom { 2 p + 1 } { 2 }$ half-periods of the form $u ( b ; a ) + u ( b ^ { \prime } ; a )$ ，and $( ^ { 2 p + 1 } )$ half-periods of the form $u ( b ; a ) + u ( b ^ { \prime } ; a ) + u ( b ^ { \prime \prime } ; a )$ , and so on; in general, $\binom { 2 p + 1 } { p }$ half-periods in the form of the sum of $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ half-periods of the type $u ( b ; a )$ . The sum of these half-periods makes

$$
{ \binom { 2 \phi + 1 } { 1 } } + { \binom { 2 \phi + 1 } { 2 } } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + { \binom { 2 \phi + 1 } { \phi } } = 2 ^ { 2 p } - 1
$$

in number,the number of incongruent half-periods.The half-periods that are identically zero are not included in the sum.None of them are congruent to each other.

When, in general, the half-periods are of the form

$$
{ \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \Omega _ { m m ^ { \prime } } \equiv m _ { 1 } \omega _ { \tau 1 } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + m _ { p } \omega _ { \tau p } + m _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } \omega _ { \tau 1 } ^ { \prime } + \cdot \cdot \cdot + m _ { p } ^ { \prime } \omega _ { \tau p } ^ { \prime } ,
$$

we write

$$
\binom { k _ { 1 } ^ { \prime } } { k _ { 1 } } k _ { 2 } ^ { \prime } k _ { \bar { \nu } } ^ { \prime } \qquad k _ { p } ^ { \prime } \biggr )
$$

instead of writing $m _ { r } = k _ { r } , ( \mathrm { m o d } 2 ) ; m _ { s } ^ { \prime } = k _ { s } ^ { \prime } , ( \mathrm { m o d } 2 ) ;$ and we call that symbol the characteristic symbol,in which $k _ { s } , k _ { s } ^ { \prime }$ (equal to O or 1） are the remainders when $m _ { s } , ~ m _ { s } ^ { \prime } ,$ ，respectively,are divided by 2.Each of the $2 ^ { 2 p } - 1$ possible characteristic symbols,other than the one that has all its elements zero,can be obtained as the sum of not more than $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ chosen from the $2  { p } + 1$ so-called fundamental characteristic symbols.The sum of these $2 \pm 1$ fundamental characteristic symbols is equal to the symbol of which all the elements are zero in mod 2. Of these $2 \phi + 1$ symbols, $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ are associated with the odd half-periods $u ( a ; a _ { 1 } ) , . . . , u ( a ; a _ { p } )$ ； the remaining $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } + \mathbb { 1 }$ are associated with the even half-periods. For example, for $\pmb { \hat { p } = 2 }$ ，

$$
, \qquad { \binom { 0 } { 0 } } \quad { \overset { 1 } { 1 } } ) , \qquad { \binom { 0 } { 1 } } \quad { \overset { 0 } { 1 } } ) , \qquad { \binom { 1 } { 0 } } \quad { \overset { 0 } { 1 } } ) , \qquad { \overset { 0 } { 0 } } \qquad { \overset { 1 } { 0 } } \quad { \overset { 1 } { 0 } } ) ,
$$

Put

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { 1 } { 2 } m _ { s } = M _ { s } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } k _ { s } , ~ \frac { 1 } { 2 } m _ { s } ^ { \prime } = M _ { s } ^ { \prime } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } k _ { s } ^ { \prime } , } \end{array}
$$

where $M _ { s } , M _ { s } ^ { \prime }$ are integers and each of $k _ { s } , k _ { s } ^ { \prime }$ is either l or O,and write

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { \lambda = [ 2 \eta ( M + { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } k ) + 2 \eta ^ { \prime } ( M ^ { \prime } + { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } k ^ { \prime } ) ] [ u + \omega ( M + { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } k ) + \omega ^ { \prime } ( M ^ { \prime } + { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } k ) } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { - \pi \sqrt { - 1 } ~ ( M + { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } k ) ( M ^ { \prime } + { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } k ^ { \prime } ) ; } } \end{array}
$$

then,

$$
\vartheta ( u ; { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } k , { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } k ^ { \prime } ) = \exp { [ - \lambda - \pi \sqrt { - 1 } \ M k ^ { \prime } ] } \cdot \vartheta ( u + { \textstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \Omega _ { m m ^ { \prime } } ) .
$$

Denote the left-hand side by $\vartheta ( u / _ { 2 } ^ { 1 } \Omega _ { m m ^ { \prime } } )$ . If

$$
{ \scriptstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \Omega _ { m m ^ { \prime } } = u ( b _ { 1 } ; a ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + u ( b _ { s } ; a ) ,
$$

then we write it $\vartheta [ u / u ( b _ { 1 } ; a ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + u ( b _ { s } ; a ) ]$ . An odd half-period is represented by

$$
\begin{array} { r } { \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Omega \equiv u ( a _ { p } ; a ) - u ( n _ { 1 } ; a _ { 1 } ) - \cdot \cdot \cdot - u ( n _ { p - 1 } ; a _ { p - 1 } ) , } \end{array}
$$

and an even half-period, for which $\vartheta \big ( \textstyle { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \Omega ^ { \prime } \big )$ does not vanish, is represented by

$$
{ \scriptstyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } } \Omega ^ { \prime } \equiv u ( b _ { 1 } ; a _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + u ( b _ { p } ; a _ { p } ) .
$$

The zero places of $\vartheta [ u ( x ; z ) / \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Omega ]$ are $z$ and $n _ { 1 } , . . . , n _ { p }$ .The zero places of $\vartheta [ u ( \pmb { x } ; a ) / \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Omega ^ { \prime } ]$ are $b _ { 1 } , \dotsc , b _ { p }$

For $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } = 2$ there is no even $\vartheta$ -function that vanishes when the argument vanishes. There are six odd $\vartheta$ -functions. One of them— $\vartheta _ { \alpha a _ { 1 } } ( u )$ ，for example- is of half-period $\begin{array} { r l } { u ( a ; a _ { 1 } ) = { \frac { 1 } { 2 } } ( { \frac { 1 } { 1 } } } & { { } 0 ) } \end{array}$ , and the corresponding half-period $u ( a _ { 2 } ; a ) \ : - \ : u ( n _ { 1 } ; a _ { 1 } )$ is such that $n _ { 1 } = a _ { 2 }$ 、There are ten even $\vartheta$ -functions. One of them一 $\vartheta ( u )$ ，for example—is of half-period $\frac { 1 } { 2 } \bigl ( \begin{array} { l l } { 0 } & { \mathrm { ~ \small ~ \alpha ~ } } \\ { 0 } & { \mathrm { ~ \small ~ \alpha ~ } } \end{array} \biggr )$ ; substituting the corresponding half-period $u ( b _ { 1 } ; a _ { 1 } ) + u ( b _ { 2 } ; a _ { 2 } )$ ， we see that $b _ { 1 }$ and $b _ { 2 }$ must equal $a _ { 1 }$ and $a _ { 2 }$ ,respectively.Another function $- \vartheta _ { a c } ( u )$ ，for example—is of half-period $u ( a ; c ) = ( { \ O } _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \quad { \ O } _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } )$ and the corresponding half-period is such that $b _ { 1 }$ and $b _ { 2 }$ must equal $c _ { 1 }$ and $c _ { 2 } ,$ respectively.

If the sum of the characteristics $q _ { 1 } , . . . , q _ { n }$ differs from the sum of the characteristics $r _ { 1 } , . . . , r _ { n }$ by characteristics consisting only of integers where $n \geq 2$ ，then

$$
f ( u ) = \frac { \vartheta ( u ; q _ { 1 } ) \vartheta ( u ; q _ { 2 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot \vartheta ( u ; q _ { n } ) } { \vartheta ( u ; r _ { 1 } ) \vartheta ( u ; r _ { 2 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot \vartheta ( u ; r _ { n } ) }
$$

is a periodic function of $\pmb { u }$ If $\pmb { u }$ is given by

then the $\begin{array} { r l } & { u _ { r } = u _ { r } ( x _ { 1 } ; a _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + u _ { r } ( x _ { p } ; a _ { p } ) \qquad ( r = 1 , 2 , . . . , p ) , } \\ & { \circ \mathrm { { f u n c t i o n } } f ( u ) \mathrm { ~ i s ~ r a t i o n a l ~ i n ~ } x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { p } . \operatorname { A c c o r d i n g l y } \frac { \vartheta ^ { 2 } \left[ u / u ( b ; a ] \right) } { \vartheta ^ { 2 } ( u ) } } \end{array}$ is rational in $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { p }$ . This function, regarded as a function of $\pmb { x } _ { 1 }$ has its zero places $b$ of the second order and its infinity places $^ { a }$ of the second order.Hence, it is a product of $( b - x _ { 1 } )$ with a function independent of $x _ { 1 }$ . Since this function is symmetric with respect to $x _ { 1 } , \dotsc , x _ { p } ;$ it equals

$$
\sqrt { ( b - x _ { 1 } ) ( b - x _ { 2 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( b - x _ { p } ) } = \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { K } } \frac { \vartheta [ u / u ( b ; a ) ] } { \vartheta ( u ) } ,
$$

with a constant $K _ { ☉ }$ ，This function may be interpreted as single-valued of the places $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { p }$ on the Riemann surface which is dissected by the $2  { p }$ cross cuts that are the period loops.

Put $u = u ( x _ { 1 } ; a _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + u ( x _ { p } ; a _ { p } )$ and let $q$ denote in turn all possible half-integer characteristics except the identically zero one; then all the $2 ^ { 2 p } - 1$ ratios $\vartheta ( u ; q ) / \vartheta ( u )$ are expressed algebraically in terms of $x _ { 1 } , \dotsc , x _ { p }$ That is,if $\Delta ( x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { p } )$ is the product of all the differences of $x _ { 1 } , \dotsc , x _ { p }$ and $\phi ( { \boldsymbol { x } } ) = ( { \boldsymbol { x } } - b _ { 1 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( { \boldsymbol { x } } - b _ { k } )$ ，then the ratios can be expressed by

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { \vartheta [ u / u ( b _ { 1 } ; a ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + u ( b _ { k } ; a ) ] \vartheta ^ { k - 1 } ( u ) } { \vartheta [ u / u ( b _ { 1 } ; a ) ] \cdot \cdot \cdot \vartheta [ u / u ( b _ { k } ; a ) ] } } \\ & { \phantom { m m m m m m } = C \frac { \displaystyle \Big \| \frac { x _ { r } ^ { \lambda } y _ { r } } { \phi ( x _ { r } ) } , \frac { x _ { r } ^ { \lambda - 1 } y _ { r } } { \phi ( x _ { r } ) } , . . . , \frac { y _ { r } } { \phi ( x _ { r } ) } , x _ { r } ^ { \mu } , x _ { r } ^ { \mu - 1 } , . . . , 1 \Big \| } { \Delta ( x _ { 1 } , x _ { 2 } , . . . , x _ { p } ) } } \end{array}
$$

with constant $C _ { i }$ ，where the numerator is the determinant obtained by putting $r = 1 , 2 , \ldots$ for each row,and where

$$
\lambda = \frac { k } { 2 } - 1 , ~ \mu = \phi - 1 - \frac { k } { 2 } ,
$$

for $k$ even, and

$$
\lambda = \frac { k - 3 } { 2 } , \qquad \mu = \phi - \frac { k + 1 } { 2 }
$$

for $k$ odd. In particular, for $k = 2$ we have $\lambda = 0$ ， $\mu = \pmb { \mathscr { p } } - 2$ giving

$$
\frac { \vartheta [ u / u ( b _ { 1 } ; a ) + u ( b _ { 2 } ; a ) ] \vartheta ( u ) } { \vartheta [ u / u ( b _ { 1 } ; a ) ] \vartheta [ u / u ( b _ { 2 } ; a ) ] } = C \sum _ { r = 1 } ^ { p } \frac { y _ { r } } { ( x _ { r } - b _ { 1 } ) ( x _ { r } - b _ { 2 } ) } \frac { 1 } { R ^ { \prime } ( x _ { r } ) } ,
$$

where

$$
R ( x ) = ( x - x _ { 1 } ) ( x - x _ { 2 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( x - x _ { p } )
$$

and $C$ is a constant.

After this preparation we discuss the problem of inversion (Section 2.20) of the $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ equations represented by

$$
u _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } ; a _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + u _ { i } ( x _ { p } ; a _ { p } ) \equiv u _ { i } \qquad ( i = 1 , 2 , . . . , p ) .
$$

The solution according to (139), is given by the $2  { p } + 1$ equations

$$
\frac { \vartheta ^ { 2 } [ u / u ( b ; a ) ] } { \vartheta ^ { 2 } ( u ) } = A ( b - x _ { 1 } ) ( b - x _ { 2 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( b - x _ { p } ) .
$$

From any of these $\pmb { \mathscr { p } }$ equations the variables $x _ { 1 } , \dotsc , x _ { p }$ can be expressed as single-valued functions of the arbitrary arguments $u _ { 1 } , . . . , u _ { p }$ When the constant $A$ is determined we obtain

$$
\frac { \vartheta ^ { 2 } [ u / u ( b ; a ) ] } { \vartheta ^ { 2 } ( u ) } = \pm \frac { ( b - x _ { 1 } ) ( b - x _ { 2 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( b - x _ { p } ) } { [ \exp { ( \pi \sqrt { - 1 } P P ^ { \prime } ) } \cdot f ^ { \prime } ( b ) ] ^ { 1 / 2 } } ,
$$

$$
f ( x ) = ( x - a _ { 1 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( x - a _ { p } ) ( x - c ) ( x - c _ { 1 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( x - c _ { p } ) ,
$$

where exp $( \pi { \sqrt { - 1 } } \ P P ^ { \prime } )$ is $+ 1$ or $^ { - 1 }$ according as $u ( b ; a )$ is an odd or even half-period. Put $a _ { 1 } , \dotsc , a _ { p }$ in turn in the place of $b$ ; then it can be seen that $x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { p }$ are the roots of

with

$$
\sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { p } \epsilon _ { i } \sqrt { - \frac { Q ( a _ { i } ) } { P ^ { \prime } ( a _ { i } ) } } \frac { \vartheta ^ { 2 } [ u / u ( a _ { i } ; a ) ] } { a _ { i } - x } = \vartheta ^ { 2 } ( u ) ,
$$

$$
\begin{array} { l } { P ( { \boldsymbol x } ) = ( { \boldsymbol x } - { \boldsymbol a } _ { 1 } ) ( { \boldsymbol x } - { \boldsymbol a } _ { 2 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( { \boldsymbol x } - { \boldsymbol a } _ { p } ) , } \\ { Q ( { \boldsymbol x } ) = ( { \boldsymbol x } - { \boldsymbol c } ) ( { \boldsymbol x } - { \boldsymbol c } _ { 1 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( { \boldsymbol x } - { \boldsymbol c } _ { p } ) , } \end{array}
$$

since

$$
{ \frac { ( x - x _ { 1 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( x - x _ { p } ) } { P ( x ) } } = 1 + \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { p } { \frac { ( a _ { i } - x _ { 1 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( a _ { i } - x _ { p } ) } { ( x - a _ { i } ) P ^ { \prime } ( a _ { i } ) } } ,
$$

where $\epsilon _ { i }$ stands for $+ 1$ or $- 1$ such that

$$
\frac { \vartheta ^ { 2 } [ u / u ( a _ { i } ; a ) ] } { \vartheta ^ { 2 } ( u ) } = \epsilon _ { i } \frac { ( a _ { i } - x _ { 1 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( a _ { i } - x _ { p } ) } { \sqrt { - P ^ { \prime } ( a _ { i } ) Q ( a _ { i } ) } } .
$$

This method of solution is due to Weierstrass.

Next we solve the inversion problem by a different method. Denote by $h _ { \tau }$ the sum of the homogeneous products of $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { p } , r$ together, without repetition,and put

$$
( x ; x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { p } ) = x ^ { p - i } - h _ { 1 } x ^ { p - i - 1 } + h _ { 2 } x ^ { p - i - 2 } - \cdot \cdot \cdot + ( - 1 ) ^ { p - i } h _ { p - i } .
$$

If we consider the integrals

$$
\int _ { \mu } ^ { x } { \frac { d x } { y } } , \qquad \int _ { \mu } ^ { x } { \frac { x d x } { y } } , \qquad \cdot \cdot \cdot , \qquad \int _ { \mu } ^ { x } { \frac { x ^ { p - 1 } d x } { y } }
$$

in the place of $u _ { 1 } ( x ; \mu ) , . . . , u _ { p } ( x ; \mu )$ , then we see that

$$
\begin{array} { c } { \bar { \nu } _ { k i } = y _ { k } \frac { \chi _ { p - i } ( x _ { k } ; x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { p } ) } { F ^ { \prime } ( x _ { k } ) } \bigg / \frac { d x _ { k } } { d t } , } \\ { F ( x ) = ( x - x _ { 1 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( x - x _ { p } ) . } \end{array}
$$

We can prove from (138) that

$$
\begin{array} { l } { - \zeta _ { i } ( u ) = L _ { i } ( x ; \mu ) + L _ { i } ( x _ { 1 } ; \mu ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + L _ { i } ( x _ { p } ; \mu ) } \\ { \qquad - \frac { y \chi _ { p - i } ( x ; x _ { 1 } , \cdot \cdot \cdot , x _ { p } ) } { 2 R ^ { \prime } ( x ) } - \cdot \cdot \cdot - \frac { y _ { p } \chi _ { p - i } ( x _ { p } ; x , x _ { 1 } , \cdot \cdot \cdot , x _ { p - 1 } ) } { 2 R ^ { \prime } ( x _ { p } ) } } \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array} { l } { R ( t ) = ( t - x ) ( t - x _ { 1 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( t - x _ { p } ) , } \\ { \qquad u = u ( x ; a ) + u ( x _ { 1 } ; a _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + u ( x _ { p } ; a _ { p } ) . } \end{array}
$$

If we make $_ { x }$ tend to the place $^ { a }$ at infinity in this formula, then

$$
\begin{array} { l } { { - \zeta _ { \mathrm { i } } [ u ( x _ { 1 } ; a _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + u ( x _ { p } ; a _ { p } ) ] } } \\ { { \mathrm { } } } \\ { { \mathrm { } = L _ { \mathrm { i } } ( x _ { 1 } ; a _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + L _ { \mathrm { i } } ( x _ { p } ; a _ { p } ) - \displaystyle \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { p } \frac { y _ { k } \chi _ { p - i + 1 } ( x _ { k } ; x _ { 1 } , \ldots , x _ { p } ) } { 2 F ^ { \prime } ( x _ { k } ) } } } \end{array}
$$

which is an odd function. This equation enables us to write $\zeta _ { i } ( u )$ for any value of $\boldsymbol { \mathscr { u } }$

For $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } = 1$ this formula reduces to the relation for the elliptic functions

$$
\begin{array} { c } { { - \zeta _ { 1 } [ u ( x _ { 1 } ; a _ { 1 } ) ] = { \cal L } _ { 1 } ( x _ { 1 } ; a _ { 1 } ) , } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { - \zeta _ { 1 } [ u ( x ; a ) + u ( x _ { 1 } ; a _ { 1 } ) ] = { \cal L } _ { 1 } ( x ; a _ { 1 } ) + { \cal L } _ { 1 } ( x _ { 1 } ; a _ { 1 } ) } } \\ { { { } } } \\ { { - { \displaystyle \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { y - y _ { 1 } } { x - x _ { 1 } } } + \mathrm { c o n s t a n t } . } } \end{array}
$$

Referring to the known formulas for the elliptic functions,for which $u = u ( x ; a )$ ，giving

$$
y = - \wp ^ { \prime } ( u ) , \qquad u ( x ; a _ { 1 } ) = \omega + \omega ^ { \prime } - u ,
$$

so that

$$
\zeta _ { 1 } [ u ( x ; a _ { 1 } ) ] = - L _ { 1 } ( x ; a _ { 1 } ) = - \int _ { a _ { 1 } } ^ { x } \frac { x d x } { y } ,
$$

and

$$
d \zeta ( u ) = \frac { x d x } { y } , \qquad \zeta _ { 1 } [ u ( x ; a _ { 1 } ) ] = - \zeta ( u ) ,
$$

we obtain the well-known addition theorem

$$
\zeta ( u + v ) = \zeta ( u ) + \zeta ( v ) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \frac { \wp ^ { \prime } ( u ) - \wp ^ { \prime } ( v ) } { \wp ( u ) - \wp ( v ) } .
$$

As a particular case of (138) for $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } = 2$ ，

$$
\begin{array} { l } { \displaystyle { - \zeta _ { p } [ u ( x _ { 1 } ; a _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + u ( x _ { p } ; a _ { p } ) ] = L _ { p } ( x _ { 1 } ; a _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + L _ { p } ( x _ { p } ; a _ { p } ) } } \\ { \displaystyle { \quad \quad = \frac { \lambda _ { 2 p + 1 } } { 4 } \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { p } \int _ { a _ { k } } ^ { x _ { k } } \frac { x ^ { p } d x } { y } . } } \end{array}
$$

Denote

$$
- \frac { \partial } { \partial u _ { i } } \left. \zeta _ { r } ( u ) \quad \mathrm { b y } \quad \wp _ { r i } ( u ) \right\}
$$

then,

$$
\wp _ { r j } ( u ) = { \frac { 1 } { 4 } } \lambda _ { 2 p + 1 } \sum _ { k = 1 } ^ { p } { \frac { x _ { k } ^ { p } \chi _ { p - i } ( x _ { k } ; x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { p } ) } { F ^ { \prime } ( x _ { k } ) } } .
$$

Finally, we can derive the formula

$$
{ \scriptstyle { \frac { 1 } { 4 } } } \lambda _ { 2 p + 1 } x _ { k } ^ { p } = \sum _ { i = 1 } ^ { p } x _ { k } ^ { i - 1 } \wp _ { p i } ( u ) .
$$

Let $\lambda _ { 2 p + 1 } = 4$ .Then $x _ { 1 } , . . . , x _ { p }$ satisfying the inversion problem

$$
u \equiv u ( x _ { 1 } ; a _ { 1 } ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + u ( x _ { p } ; a _ { p } )
$$

are the roots of

$$
F ( x ) = x ^ { p } - x ^ { p - 1 } \wp _ { p , p } ( u ) - x ^ { p - 2 } \wp _ { p , p - 1 } ( u ) - \cdot \cdot \cdot - \wp _ { p , 1 } ( u ) = 0 ,
$$

giving

$$
h _ { r } = ( - 1 ) ^ { r - 1 } \wp _ { p , p - r + 1 } ( u ) .
$$

Since $F ( x _ { k } ) = 0$ , then, by writing

$$
\frac { \partial \wp _ { p q } ( u ) } { \partial u _ { r } } = \wp _ { p q r } ( u ) ,
$$

we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l r } {  { y _ { k } = \psi ( x _ { k } ) , } } \\ & { } & \\ & { } & { \psi ( x ) = - \frac { \partial F ( x ) } { \partial u _ { p } } = x ^ { p - 1 } \wp _ { p , p , p } ( u ) + x ^ { p - 2 } \wp _ { p , p , p - 1 } ( u ) + \cdot \cdot \cdot + \wp _ { p , p , 1 } } \end{array}
$$

This formula gives the complete solution of the inversion problem.

Writing the right-hand member of (140) as

$$
\frac { 1 } { \mu _ { i } } ( a _ { i } - x _ { 1 } ) \cdot \cdot \cdot ( a _ { i } - x _ { p } ) ,
$$

we see that

$$
\frac { \vartheta ^ { 2 } [ u / u ( a _ { i } ; a ) ] } { \vartheta ^ { 2 } ( u ) } = a _ { i } ^ { p } - a _ { i } ^ { p - 1 } \wp _ { p , p } ( u ) - a _ { i } ^ { p - 2 } \wp _ { p , p - 1 } ( u ) - \cdot \cdot \cdot - \wp _ { p , 1 } ( u )
$$

Denote the expression $\vartheta \big [ u / { \bf \Pi } _ { 2 } ^ { 1 } ( { \bf \Pi } _ { 1 } ^ { 0 } \mathrm { ~  ~ \Pi ~ } _ { 1 } ^ { 1 } ) \big ]$ by $\pmb { \sigma } ( \pmb { u } )$ , and put

$$
\wp _ { i j } ( u ) = - \frac { \partial \sp 2 } { \partial u _ { i } \partial u _ { j } } \log \sigma ( u ) , \qquad v = u + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Omega _ { m m ^ { \prime } } ;
$$

then, for $\pmb { \hat { p } } = 2$ we obtain

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \frac { [ \vartheta ( 0 ) ] ^ { 2 } \mu _ { 1 } \mu _ { 2 } } { a _ { 1 } - a _ { 2 } } \frac { \sigma ( u + v ) \sigma ( u - v ) } { \sigma ^ { 2 } ( u ) \sigma ^ { 2 } ( v ) } } \\ & { \qquad = \wp _ { 1 1 } ( u ) - \wp _ { 1 1 } ( v ) + \wp _ { 1 2 } ( u ) \wp _ { 2 2 } ( v ) - \wp _ { 1 2 } ( v ) \wp _ { 2 2 } ( u ) . } \end{array}
$$

This is the generalization of the formula for the eliptic functions in the case $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } = 1$ ,for which $\sigma ( u ) / u = 1$ when $u = 0$ ,that is, of the formula

$$
\frac { \sigma ( u + v ) \sigma ( u - v ) } { \sigma ^ { 2 } ( u ) \sigma ^ { 2 } ( v ) } = \wp ( v ) - \wp ( u ) .
$$

There are for $\pmb { \mathscr { p } } = 2$ six odd $\vartheta$ -functions :

$$
\vartheta _ { a a _ { 1 } } ( u ) , \vartheta _ { a a _ { 2 } } ( u ) , \vartheta _ { a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } } ( u ) , \vartheta _ { c _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } } ( u ) , \vartheta _ { c c _ { 1 } } ( u ) , \vartheta _ { c c _ { 2 } } ( u ) ;
$$

and ten even $\vartheta$ -functions :

$$
\begin{array} { r l } & { \vartheta ( u ) , \vartheta _ { a c } ( u ) , \vartheta _ { a c _ { 1 } } ( u ) , \vartheta _ { a c _ { 2 } } ( u ) , \vartheta _ { a c _ { 1 } } ( u ) , \vartheta _ { a _ { 1 } c _ { 1 } } ( u ) , \vartheta _ { a _ { 1 } c _ { 2 } } ( u ) , \vartheta _ { a _ { 2 } c _ { 1 } } ( u ) , \vartheta _ { a _ { 2 } c _ { 2 } } ( u ) , } \\ & { \qquad \vartheta _ { c a _ { 2 } } ( u ) , \vartheta _ { c a _ { 1 } } ( u ) . } \end{array}
$$

Denote the functions $\vartheta ( u )$ and $\vartheta ( u ^ { \prime } )$ by $\vartheta$ and $\vartheta ^ { \prime }$ , respectively; then we can derive, with a constant $C _ { i }$ ，

$$
\begin{array} { r l } { C \frac { \vartheta [ ( u + u ^ { \prime } ) / u ( a _ { 1 } ; a ) ] } { \vartheta ( u + u ^ { \prime } ) } = } & { - [ \epsilon ( \vartheta \vartheta _ { a a _ { 1 } } \vartheta _ { a _ { 1 } c _ { 1 } } ^ { \prime } \vartheta _ { a c _ { 1 } } ^ { \prime } + \vartheta ^ { \prime } \vartheta _ { a a _ { 1 } } ^ { \prime } \vartheta _ { a c _ { 1 } } \vartheta _ { a c _ { 1 } } ) } \\ & { \qquad + \ \vartheta _ { a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } } \vartheta _ { a a _ { 2 } } \vartheta _ { a _ { 2 } c _ { 1 } } ^ { \prime } \vartheta _ { c c _ { 2 } } ^ { \prime } + \ \vartheta _ { a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } } ^ { \prime } \vartheta _ { a a _ { 2 } } ^ { \prime } \vartheta _ { a _ { 2 } c _ { 1 } } \vartheta _ { c c } } \\ & { \qquad + \ [ \vartheta ^ { 2 } \vartheta ^ { \prime 2 } + \vartheta _ { a a _ { 1 } } ^ { 2 } \vartheta _ { a a _ { 1 } } ^ { \prime 2 } + \vartheta _ { a a _ { 2 } } ^ { 2 } \vartheta _ { a a _ { 2 } } ^ { \prime 2 } + \vartheta _ { a _ { 1 } a _ { 2 } } ^ { 2 } \vartheta _ { a a _ { 2 } } ^ { \prime } \vartheta _ { a c _ { 1 } } ^ { \prime } } \end{array}
$$

This is an extension of the addition theorem for the eliptic functions

$$
\~ \mathsf { s n } \left( u + u ^ { \prime } \right) = \frac { \mathsf { s n } ~ u \mathrm { c n } ~ u ^ { \prime } \mathrm { d n } ~ u ^ { \prime } + \mathsf { s n } ~ u ^ { \prime } \mathrm { c n } ~ u \mathrm { d n } ~ u } { 1 - k ^ { 2 } \mathsf { s n } ^ { 2 } u \mathsf { s n } ^ { 2 } u ^ { \prime } } .
$$

This second solution of the inversion problem is due to Konigsberger.

Unfortunately this solution of the inversion problem is of little use in conjunction with the separation of variables in the Hamilton-Jacobi equation, except in the case of Kowalewski's top motion.

# Retrospect of Volume I

At this stage let us recapitulate the results of our discussion.

The elementary method for solving a system of differential equations of order $_ { 6 n }$ for the $_ { n }$ -body problem lies in the discovery of as many independent integrals as the order of the differential equations. So far, the known classical integrals are: one for energy， three for angular momentum,six for the center of mass,one for the elimination of the node,and one for the elimination of time-in all, twelve integrals. The first ten are called the classical Eulerian integrals.Lie's theory on the continuous group of transformations is applied, to reduce the order of the differential equations.The concepts·of contact transformation,involution, function groups,and distinguished functions are introduced for the theoretical discussion of the reduction.The order of the differential equations is reduced to $6 n - 1 2$ by the known classical integrals.The problem is to see whether any further integrals exist,apart from some rigorous particular solutions of the problem treated in Chapter 3.The theorem of Bruns as extended by Painlevé states the nonexistence of new integrals that are algebraic with regard to the momenta.The theorem of Poincaré denies the existence of new integrals that are uniform functions with regard to the Keplerian elements. Then what would be the functional form of the new integrals ? In search for such new integrals we must refer to transcendental functions, or try to solve the equations on the basis of transcendental processes.

The existence of the solution will be proved in a later volume basing it on Weierstrass's theory of analytical functions.The whole field of celestial mechanics at present is occupied by various attempts to solve the equations by an infinite process of iteration,which we treat in several chapters of a future volume. The usual method is to expand the solution in the form of an infinite multiple Fourier series proceeding in powers of the small disturbing masses, with linear functions of time as arguments. Although such a series expansion resembles the one in the theory of quasi-periodic or almost periodic functions,we know little about the condition for which the solution is a quasi-periodic function, so that the series employed in the usual perturbation theory in celestial mechanics cannot be said to represent the solution we are seeking. Can we possibly attack the problem from the function-theoretic standpoint of Poincaré and Painlevé? On the other hand,as will be demonstrated in one of the subsequent volumes,Poincaré has proved that none of the infinite processes ever proposed for solving the problem is uniformly convergent, notwithstanding all the efforts that our predecessors have concentrated on this method of solution.Is it possible to predict the future behavior of the motion on a purely qualitative basis - thus abandoning our original aim of modifying the laws of mechanics through comparing observations with theoretical computations carried out on a nonrigorous mathematical foundation? At this pessimistic stage Poincaré, followed by Birkhoff, founded his whole theory of topology and applied it to the equations of celestial mechanics,in order to study the state of motion in an infinite time interval. These will be the main topics of the future volumes.
